Volume Twenty-One: Continuation of the Record of Panyang (Three) - Twenty-Three Entries** **Volume Twenty-Two: Continuation of the Record of Panyang (Four) - Twenty-Two Entries** **Volume Twenty-Three: Continuation of the Record of Panyang (Five) - Twenty-Six Entries** **Ji Yun, Two Poems** A thousand lifetimes of effort are worn away; the words on paper pass by like fleeting clouds. I planned to build a library, but now I am old; one should only talk about ghosts as Su Dongpo did. The causes and effects are verified without error; collecting trivial notes is like a cart full of ghosts. Tell the disciples of the Luo gate; minor officials were originally not part of the Confucian tradition. **Self-Titled by the Rejected Daoist** **Preface by Zheng** (The preface by Sheng has already been posted.) Ji Wenda, the distinguished scholar from Hejian, has long served in the imperial library. His grand literary works are renowned as the masterpieces of his generation. Some say he enjoys wit and humor, and that his playful, angry, or mocking remarks all become articles. Now, when we examine the notes he has compiled, we find them sincere and well-intentioned, with strict adherence to form. The main purpose of his work is to encourage and admonish, and it seems to align with the wisdom of the sages. Though it is a novel, it is still akin to a history. Ji himself says, "I do not confuse right and wrong as in *Biyunxia*." I do not harbor grievances as in *Zhou Qin Xingji*. I do not depict talented scholars and beautiful women as in *Hui Zhen Ji*. I do not describe licentiousness as in *Mibin*. I hope that it will not be rejected by the virtuous. This is still a humble statement from Ji. His grandson Shufu has come to serve in Lingnan. Many people have requested this book, so it has been reprinted. Shufu is sincere, cautious, and knowledgeable. He excels in his official duties and does not tarnish his family’s reputation.

On the spring day of the Jia (乙) year of the Yin (未) cycle in the 15th year of the Daoguang era (1835), Longxi Zheng Kāixī recorded this. Preface to the Summer Record of Luanyang In the summer of the Ji (己) year of the You (酉) cycle during the Qianlong era (1789), I was engaged in compiling and arranging rare books in Luanyang. At that time, the proofreading and organization were long finished, and I was only supervising the officials who labeled the books on the shelves. With long days and nothing to do, I recorded what I had seen and heard, writing down my thoughts as soon as they came to me, without any fixed form or structure. These are short stories and minor officials' tales, knowing they have no relation to serious writing, but street talk and alley gossip may be beneficial for encouragement and punishment. I entrusted the records to a copyist to keep them, and I named the work "Preface to the Summer Record of Luanyang." Volume Five: Records of Summer in Luanyang A woodworker named Zheng Wu was involved in a case of betrayal and moral transgression, where a debt ghost demanded repayment. In his village, a newly widowed woman harbored a heart set on winning or losing, and there was a case involving the collection of taxes. A ghost dog appeared, and someone returned to the hall at night. Zhang Fu died in place of another, and a woman treated wealth as life. Ghostly figures appeared in an ancient temple, and secret play pictures were found. At the Guandi temple, a ghost was encountered, and the concept of reincarnation was discussed. The god of literary success and official rank was mentioned, as well as a man who had a fox as a concubine. Strange fires were reported, and Fan used a spirit board for divination. A woman took her mother-in-law's place in death, and Nie Songyan of Changshan was an elder scholar. Wang De'an was a man of letters, and paper chariots and horses were used in rituals. The god at the Wen Chang temple was invoked, and Yellow Robe's allegory was recounted. A beautiful girl was seen in a tombyard, and a woman's spirit saw a ghost in broad daylight. A donkey sighed deeply, and Shi Nan Gong, the skilled archer, was mentioned. A ghost was found under a tree, and a woman was adorned with colored clothes and golden bracelets. Two children thieves frightened each other, and a poem inspired by a deity was recited. Cao Er, the diviner in Zhengding, was a vicious woman in Gaocun. A falling dragon was reported, and a woman met a ghost in broad daylight. A young girl's divine words were heard, and two ghosts enforced justice. Si'er, the righteous dog, was mentioned, and a fox could change form. A flying sky wild fork was seen, and a sheep's false servant's spirit was encountered. A cow monster was reported, and four bodies were found in a dry well. A giant snake like a pillar was seen, and Zheng Yi, the True Person, was mentioned. The thunder god was invoked, and a large tree blocked the road. A great whirlwind was reported, and a giant turtle was seen. A wild fox listened to scriptures, and Li Bai dreamed of a pen that bloomed. Wandering spirits were encountered.

《Notes from the Reading and Observing Studio》 (Qing) Ji Yun Original Preface Writing conveys the Way, and scholars cannot fail to speak of it. Is the Way so deep and hidden, secret and untransmittable, like the mind-seal of Buddhism or the oral formula of Daoism? The natural principles of all things are the Way. Therefore, the Way is present in heaven and earth, like mercury poured onto the ground, each drop forming a perfect circle; like the moon reflected in water, seen everywhere. From the great task of governing the state and pacifying the world to the small details of one matter, one thing, one action, one word, the Way is present in all of them. Writing is one aspect of the Way. The greatest of writings are the Six Classics, which are the repository of the Way; descending to the histories of various dynasties, the writings of the various schools of thought, and the collections of many authors, these are another aspect of writing, and their words are sufficient to elucidate the Way; further descending to the writings of minor officials and novels, it seems to have nothing to do with the Way. Yet the *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) in the *Han Shu* (Book of Han) lists them as a category, and the bibliographies of all dynasties also record them. Is it not because of the absurd and heretical ones? Though they may not be numerous, those that are close to the right have not been without benefit to the hearts of people and the ways of the world. Mr. Hejian, with his learning and literary talent, bears the great reputation of the world, and by nature is solitary and upright. He does not like to engage in empty talk about the mind and nature to establish a school, nor does he like talented people who are unrestrained in poetry societies and drinking clubs, flaunting the elegance of the literati.

Therefore, in the leisure of retirement, he was solely immersed in classical texts. As old age made him reluctant to engage in research, he instead collected strange and unusual stories, occasionally writing notes to express his thoughts. The five books *Luan Yang Xia Xia Lu* and others contain all manner of strange and bizarre tales, and speak freely and unrestrainedly on every subject, yet their main intention ultimately returns to sincerity and correctness, aiming to guide people in what to be encouraged or discouraged from. Hence, books that indulge in sensuality and guide toward desire, those that pride themselves on beautiful women and talented scholars, though once as popular as paper in high demand, eventually faded into obscurity, while the Master’s books were repeatedly printed on wooden blocks and remained beloved for a long time. This is a clear demonstration of the difference between appearance and substance. However, many reprints have been made, and errors have indeed proliferated; moreover, some have arbitrarily added titles, as was the case with Ming dynasty editions of *Lengzai Yewa*, which troubles readers. Shiyan, who has long been a student of the Master, once printed *Guwang Tingzhi* of the Master and attached a preface at the end of the book. The Master considered this a wise commentary. Recently, the various editions have become increasingly blurred and worn, so he requested permission from the Master to compile the five books into a single volume, while still preserving the original order of each. He painstakingly proofread by lamp, without fearing labor, and then asked the Master to review it once more before proceeding with the printing. Though the Master’s writings need not rely on this edition to be preserved, the textual errors, though rare, are less significant than the Master’s fundamental purpose of guiding the world, and perhaps this effort is not without some small benefit. In the eighth month of the year Gengshen of the Jiaqing era, a student from Beiping, Sheng Shiyan, respectfully composed this preface.

Luan Yang Summer Notes, Volume One In the summer of the Gengyou year of the Qianlong era, I was tasked with organizing secret books in Luan Yang. By then, the work of proofreading and arranging had been completed for a long time, and my duties were limited to supervising officials, labeling books, and placing them on shelves. With the long days and nothing to do, I began recording my observations and memories, writing down whatever came to mind, without any set format. These were mostly short stories and minor works, knowing they had no direct relation to scholarly writing. However, the gossip and common talk from the streets and alleys might be useful for moral instruction or punishment. So I entrusted it to the copyists to keep it, and I named it "Luan Yang Summer Notes." Censor Hu Musiting said that in his village, there was a man who kept a pig. Whenever he saw a neighbor, the pig would glare at him with fierce eyes, roar wildly, and charge forward as if to bite him, but it would behave normally around others. The neighbor was initially very angry and wanted to buy the pig to eat its meat. Later, he suddenly realized, "This must be the past-life enmity mentioned in the Buddhist scriptures? There is no unresolved grudge in the world." He then bought the pig with a fair price, sent it to a Buddhist temple to be a long-term pig, and after that, when he saw the pig again, it would lower its ears and approach affectionately, no longer displaying its former aggressive behavior. I once saw a painting by Sun Zhong depicting a taming tiger, with a poem written by Li Yan from Baizhou: "A sage rides a fierce tiger, controlling it like a swift horse. Is it inherently gentle? The power of the Dharma tames its ferocity. Thus, we know that all beings in the world can be connected through compassion. Let us preserve our hearts as firm as gold and stone, and not be overly fearful or suspicious." This poem can serve as an explanation for this event.

In Cangzhou, there was a scholar-official named Liu Shiyu, who had a study occupied by a fox. During the day, the fox would converse with people and throw tiles and stones at them, but no one could see its form. The prefect of the Plain, Magistrate Dong Sir Ren, was a virtuous official, and upon hearing of the incident, he went to drive the fox away. As he was eloquently explaining the principle that humans and spirits should not cross paths, suddenly a clear voice from the eaves said: "You, as an official, love the people and do not take money, so I dare not attack you. However, your love for the people is merely for fame, and your refusal to take money is just to avoid future troubles, so I also do not fear you. Rest now, do not speak too much to exhaust yourself." Magistrate Dong returned in a state of disarray and remained angry and unhappy for several days. Liu had a servant woman who was very coarse and stupid, but she alone was not afraid of the fox. The fox also did not attack her. Sometimes, during conversations, Liu would mention her to the fox. The fox said: "She may be a lowly servant, but she is truly a filial woman; even ghosts and spirits would avoid her, let alone us." Liu then ordered the servant woman to live in this room, and on that day, the fox left.

Master Aitang said he heard that there was once a scholar who was walking at night when he suddenly encountered his deceased friend. The scholar, who was usually straightforward and upright, did not fear the encounter. He asked his friend where he was going, and the friend replied, "I am a soul collector from the underworld, and I am going to the village of South Village to summon someone. We happen to be going the same way." They walked together to a dilapidated house. The ghost said, "This is a scholar's residence." The scholar asked how he knew, and the ghost explained, "In general, people are busy with their affairs during the day, and their inner spirits are obscured. Only when they sleep, with no thoughts in their minds, do their original spirits become clear. The books they have read inside them emit light, with each word radiating光芒, which rises from their pores and appears as a splendid and colorful light, like brocade. Scholars as great as Zheng Xuan and Kong Yingda, or as talented as Qu Yuan, Song Yu, Ban Gu, and Sima Qian, have light that illuminates the sky and competes with the stars and the moon. Lesser scholars have light that reaches several zhang or several chi, with varying degrees of brightness. Even the least bright scholars have a faint glow like a single lamp illuminating the windows. This light is not visible to humans but only to ghosts and spirits. I can see that the light from this room is several chi high." The scholar asked, "I have studied all my life. How bright is the light from my studies?" The ghost hesitated for a long time and said, "Yesterday, I passed by your school, and you were napping during the day. I saw that inside you was a detailed commentary on classical texts, five or six hundred examination papers, seventy or eighty classical texts, and thirty or forty strategic writings. Each word turned into black smoke, covering the roof of the school. The sounds of the students reciting were muffled, as if they were in thick clouds and fog. I did not see any light, so I did not dare to speak falsely." The scholar angrily scolded the ghost, and the ghost laughed and left.

Mr. Li Youdan from Dongguang once visited the ruined garden of the former prime minister in Wanping, where he saw two poems under the corridor. The first poem read: "The west wind blows through the broken window frames, the autumn grasses rustle in the empty courtyard, moonlight filters through the eaves, illuminating half of the wall with moss and green algae." The second poem read: "Sparse stars twinkle faintly, occasionally a cloud drifts across the Milky Way, I sit by the railing listening to the drumbeats from the watchtower, counting until the fifth strike." The ink traces were dim and faded, seemingly not written by a human hand. Mr. Dong Qujiang, whose name is Yuan Du, was from Pingyuan. He passed the imperial examination in the year 1772 (the year of the Water Dragon in the Chinese zodiac) and entered the Hanlin Academy. After graduating, he was appointed as a county magistrate and later became a professor. He resigned due to illness and returned home. As a young man, he dreamed of someone giving him a fan, on which there were three absolute verses: "Zeng drank water from the pool on the day of the Western Garden, his literary talent moved his old friend, in the end, his feelings remained unchanged, the moonlight and flower shadows fell on the flags." "Horses came together from the southern part of the city within a distance of fifteen feet, willows lined up like red scales, golden hooks and embroidered brocade, doubting the talent of Chen Wang who was said to have eight dou of talent." "Drums and flutes sounded in the painted candle-lit tower, who was it that personally played on the small cool island, how many young girls are blooming in the spring breeze, all turned into a segment of sorrow in the autumn river." The language was difficult to understand. Later, there was no sign of any verification, and the reason remained unclear.

Pingding Scholar-Official Wang Xieqin once served as a literary official in Yulin. One night, he stayed at a temple and heard people discussing poetry in the library upstairs. He found it surprising that there were few literati in this place, so he listened carefully. However, he still could not understand what they were saying very well. Later, the conversation moved to the corridor below the library, and it became clearer. One person said, "Tang Yanqian's poetry may not be high in style, but 'the fields of millet and hemp are abandoned, giving rise to the border atmosphere; the grass and trees are cold in spring, stirring up the sounds of war,' these are excellent lines." Another person said, "I once wrote, 'the sunlight on the shady desert is as white as snow, and the sand particles on a windy day enter the clouds and turn yellow.' This is a scene one can only see if they have been to the border regions." Another person said, "I also wrote, 'the mountains sink into the border atmosphere, emotionless green; the river carries the cold sounds of autumn throughout the ages.' I think these lines capture the desolate atmosphere of the border regions well." They recited and appreciated these lines together for a long time. Suddenly, the temple bell rang, and there was silence. When morning came, Wang Xieqin got up and found the door locked and covered with dust. The lines "the mountains sink into the border atmosphere, emotionless green" were later found in General Ren's posthumous writings. General Ren, whose name was Ren, died in battle while leading troops in the Jinchuan campaign. The lines "the sunlight on the shady desert is as white as snow, and the sand particles on a windy day enter the clouds and turn yellow" are still unknown as to who wrote them. Even if the spirits of these people still exist, they could join General Ren in traveling together, which is certainly not an ordinary ghost.

In the southern bank of the river in Cangzhou City, there was a ruffian named Lü Si, who was fierce and violent, doing whatever he pleased, and people feared him as much as they would wolves and tigers. One evening, as dusk approached, he and a group of young ruffians went to the outskirts of the village to cool off. Suddenly, they heard a faint rumble of thunder, and rain and wind were about to arrive. They saw a young woman entering an ancient temple by the riverbank. Lü said to his companions, "She can be molested." By then, it was already night, and the sky was dark with clouds. Lü suddenly rushed in and covered her mouth, and the others stripped her clothes and molested her. Suddenly, lightning flashed through the window, and they saw that the woman resembled his wife. He quickly let go and asked her, and it turned out to be true. Lü was greatly angered and wanted to throw his wife into the river. She shouted loudly, "You want to molest someone, and now someone has molested me. The principles of heaven are clear. Do you still want to kill me?" Lü was speechless and hurriedly looked for their clothes, but they had already been swept away by the wind into the river.

In a state of confusion and without a plan, he took the naked woman back home. As the clouds dispersed and the moon became bright, the entire village erupted in laughter and gathered to inquire about the situation. Unable to respond, he ultimately threw himself into the river. It turned out that his wife had returned to her parents' home for a visit, planning to return in a month, but she was unaware that a fire had broken out at her parents' house, leaving no place for her to stay, so she returned early. He was unaware of this and thus faced this difficulty. Later, his wife dreamed that he came to her and said: "My sins are heavy, and I will be eternally trapped in the realm of hell. Because I was filial to my mother in life, the officials of the underworld recorded this and allowed me to take on a snake's body. I am now going to be reborn. Your new husband will soon arrive. Treat your new in-laws well. The moral code of the underworld severely punishes filial impiety, so do not fall into the punishments of the underworld." On the day of her remarriage, a red snake appeared at the corner of the house, looking down with a loving gaze. The wife remembered her dream and raised her head to ask it. Soon, she heard the sound of drums and music outside, and the snake leaped and jumped on the roof several times before suddenly leaving.

A servant named Zhou Hu from the Zhou family of Xian County was bewitched by a fox, and for over twenty years they lived together like a married couple. The fox once told Zhou Hu: "I have refined my form for over four hundred years. In past lives, there was a karmic debt to be repaid to you. If one day is not fulfilled, I cannot ascend to heaven. When the karmic debt is fulfilled, I must leave." One day, the fox was joyfully smiling yet tearfully sorrowful, and said to Zhou Hu: "On the nineteenth day of the month, my karmic debt will be fulfilled, and I must part. I have already arranged a wife for you; you can marry her." The fox then gave Zhou Hu white gold to prepare the wedding. From then on, their intimacy and affection exceeded what was usual, and they were always inseparable. On the fifteenth day, the fox suddenly woke up and said goodbye. Zhou Hu was surprised that the fox had left early, and the fox wept: "Karmic debt cannot be reduced or increased by a day; only the timing depends on circumstances. I have left this three-day karmic debt for another reunion." Several years later, the fox indeed returned, and they enjoyed three days together before parting. As the fox left, she wept: "From now on, it is a final farewell." Chen Deyin commented: "This fox was wise in preserving its blessings; those who cherish blessings should be like this." Liu Jizhen, however, said: "Three days later, a separation is inevitable; why linger?" This fox, having refined its form for four hundred years, had not yet reached the state of ultimate liberation. Those who face situations should not be like this. I believe both scholars' views have their own merits and appropriateness.

Xian County Magistrate Ming Chen, a native of Ying Shan, once sought to clear a wrongful case but was concerned that his superiors would not agree, leaving him in doubt and indecision. There was a scholar named Wu Banxian who was a gatekeeper in Confucian studies, and he had a friend who was a fox, often speaking of minor omens and predictions, which were often accurate. Ming Chen sent him to consult the fox, who spoke seriously: "Your Excellency is the parent of the people; you should only consider whether it is a wrongful case or not, not whether it is approved or not. Do you not remember what Governor Li said?" The gatekeeper returned to report, and Ming Chen was greatly alarmed. He then mentioned that Governor Li, when he was not yet prominent, once crossed the river with a Taoist priest. Just then, there was someone arguing with the boatman, and the Taoist priest sighed and said: "Life is fleeting; still counting a few coins?" Soon, the person was swept overboard by the sail and drowned. Governor Li was surprised by this. A wind arose in midstream, and the boat was about to capsize. The Taoist priest performed the Yu step and recited incantations, and the wind stopped, allowing them to cross safely. Governor Li bowed twice to thank for being saved. The Taoist priest said: "The person who fell into the river was destined to die; I could not save him. You are a noble person, and being saved from danger is also fate. I could not help but save you; why thank me?"

Li Gong bowed again and said, "I will accept this teaching and embrace my fate for the rest of my life." The Taoist priest replied, "It is not entirely so. One should accept one's fate in terms of personal fortune and misfortune, but if one does not accept one's fate, one will compete fiercely and push others aside, going to any lengths. Even if Li Linfu and Qin Hui did not betray virtuous people, they still became prime ministers, but it would only add to their own crimes. However, when it comes to the welfare of the nation and the people, one cannot speak of fate. The creation of talents by heaven and earth, and the establishment of officials by the court, are all for the purpose of correcting the course of destiny. If one holds power and passively entrusts one's fate, why would heaven and earth create such talents, and why would the court establish such officials? The gatekeeper at the morning gate said, 'To know that something is impossible but still do it.' Zhuge Liang said, 'To serve with utmost dedication until death.' Success or failure, gain or loss, are not things that can be predicted in advance. This is the wisdom of sages and scholars regarding how to shape one's destiny. You should understand this." Li Gong humbly received the teaching and bowed to ask the Taoist priest's name. The Taoist priest said, "To say it might scare you." He then disappeared mysteriously after walking down the boat for several dozen steps. In the past, in the capital city, Li Gong had spoken of this matter. I do not know how this fox could know.

Zhen Suxian from North Village once dreamed of reaching the underworld, where he saw King Yama recording the deeds of the deceased. A woman from a neighboring village arrived before the throne, and the king changed his expression and bowed, offering her a cup of tea and ordering the underworld officials to quickly send her to a good place. Zhen privately asked the underworld official, "What merit does this old farmer's wife have?" The official replied, "This woman has never had selfish thoughts that harm others. Even wise scholars and officials may not escape selfishness, but those who are selfish inevitably harm others. Various schemes and tricks arise from this, and various grudges and sins are caused by this, even leading to a lasting bad reputation and spreading poison in all directions. All these evils stem from this single thought. This village woman can control her selfishness, and scholars who study and teach often feel ashamed in comparison. No wonder the king treated her with respect." Zhen, who usually had a calculating mind, woke up feeling alarmed upon hearing this.

Zheng then recounted that before this old woman arrived, an official in formal attire entered with a proud demeanor, claiming that wherever he went, he only drank one cup of water, and thus had no shame before the spirits. The king smiled and said: "Officials are appointed to govern the people. Even down to post station attendants and gatekeepers, there are matters of benefit and harm that require handling. Simply not taking money does not make one a good official. Sitting like a wooden puppet in the hall, not even drinking water, would not be better than you, would it?" The official then argued: "I may have no merit, but I have no fault." The king replied: "You seek to preserve yourself at every turn. In certain cases, you avoided suspicion and remained silent, not speaking up, which does not harm the people? In certain matters, you feared the complexity and did not raise the issue, which does not harm the state? What is the purpose of the triennial assessment? To have no merit is to have committed a crime." The official became greatly embarrassed, and his sharpness diminished. The king then slowly turned and smiled, saying: "I found your arrogance amusing. To be fair, you are still a decent official of the third or fourth rank. In your next life, you will not lose your status. I hereby order you to be sent to the Wheel King." From these two events, one can see that the subtle flaws in human nature are known even to the spirits. Even the wisest among us, in a single thought, cannot escape reproach. "Your heart is in your room," indeed, is it not so? During the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, in the year Renzi, the wife of a high-ranking official had never shown any signs of marital discord. Suddenly, a bolt of lightning struck her through the window, passing through her heart and exiting through her left side. Her husband was also struck by the lightning, and his back to his buttocks were burned black. He barely had any breath left, but after some time, he regained consciousness. Looking at his wife's corpse, he wept and said: "My nature is strong and resolute. I may have argued with my mother at times, but you only privately expressed your depression, hiding your tears behind the lamp. Why did the lightning strike you by mistake?" This was because he did not understand that the law holds the mastermind accountable, and that the moral order applies equally in both the visible and invisible worlds.

The Cloudless Monk, whose origins are unknown, was a hermit during the reign of Emperor Kangxi. He stayed at the Zisheng Temple in Hejian, where he spent his days in silent meditation and did not respond to conversation. One day, he suddenly ascended to his meditation seat, struck the table with a ruler, and passed away peacefully. Upon looking at the table, there was a verse: "Cutting off hair and leaving home to purify the six dusts, focusing on one's own liberation. Benevolence towards people and love for all things are endless tasks, originally rooted in the teachings of Duke of Zhou and Confucius. Buddhism is close to the teachings of Mencius, and this monk is also close to the teachings of Yang Zhu." In Ningbo, there was a man named Wu who enjoyed visiting brothels. Later, he became close to a fox spirit and had secret meetings with her. Despite this, he still frequented the brothels. One day, the fox spirit said to him, "I can transform into anyone. For any person you desire, I can mimic their appearance. With just a thought, they will appear instantly, no different from buying laughter with gold." He tried it, and indeed, the transformations happened in an instant, indistinguishable from the real thing. He no longer left his home. Once, he said to the fox spirit, "Sleeping among flowers and lying among willows is truly pleasing to the heart, but it is all an illusion, and there is always a barrier in the mind." The fox spirit replied, "Not so. The pleasures of the senses are fleeting like lightning and fire. How could it be that only I transform into someone else? They too are illusions. Even I am an illusion. All the famous courtesans and beautiful women of the past thousand years are illusions."

White poplars and green grass, yellow earth and green mountains—none of these are not ancient sites of singing and dancing; holding rain and clouds, burying fragrant flowers and precious stones, parting cranes and phoenixes—all within the span of a moment, the meeting of two beauties, whether measured in moments, days, months, or years, there will always come a time of final separation; when they finally part, whether after decades or mere moments, it is the same as letting go at the edge of a cliff, turning everything into nothingness in an instant. Being close to beautiful women, are they not all like a spring dream? Even if deeply fated to meet and stay together for life, the red cheeks do not stay, and white hair has already invaded, one person's body is no longer the same as before. Then the black eyebrows and rosy cheeks of the past can also be called illusions. Why is it only my resemblance to someone specific that is considered an illusion? Wu suddenly had an epiphany. After several years, the fox girl left, and Wu completely stopped engaging in promiscuous behavior.

Jiaohé and Rú'ài, Zhang Wenfu from Qingxian county, both are old scholars. They both teach students in Xian. Once they walked together between the southern and northern villages under the moon, a bit far from the school, in a desolate and quiet wilderness with dense vegetation. Zhang was frightened and wanted to return, saying, "There are many ghosts in the cemeteries, how can one stay for long?" Soon an old man came with a walking stick, greeted them and invited them to sit, saying, "How can there be ghosts in the world? Have you not heard of Ruan Zhan's argument? You two are scholars, why believe in the false teachings of Buddhism?" He then explained the principles of the rise and fall of qi (vital energy) according to Cheng and Zhu. He clarified and proved his points, making them clear and coherent. Both listened and nodded in agreement, praising the true understanding of the Song scholars, and exchanged responses with each other. They forgot to ask his name. Just then, several carriages came from afar, the sound of the oxen's bells rang out. The old man stood up quickly and said, "A person from the underworld, long has been silent. Without the argument against ghosts, I could not have kept you two for a whole night. Now I am about to leave, I will tell you the truth, do not be surprised or offended." In an instant, he disappeared. There are very few literati in this area, only the tomb of Mr. Dong Kongru is nearby. Perhaps it was his spirit?

Tang Sheng from Hejian was fond of playing tricks and mocking others, and the locals still talk about him today. He is known as "Tang the Howler." There was a village schoolmaster who often argued that there were no ghosts, once saying, "Yan Zhan encountered a ghost, how could such a thing be true? It was just idle talk fabricated by Buddhist monks." One night, Tang sprinkled dirt on the schoolmaster's window and made a mournful sound, knocking on the door. The schoolmaster was terrified and asked who it was. Tang replied, "I am the good function of the two qi." The schoolmaster was greatly frightened, covering his head and trembling, and he sent two of his disciples to guard the door all night. The next day, he was so exhausted that he could not get up. When his friends came to visit, he only groaned and said, "There is a ghost." Later, when they found out it was Tang's doing, they all laughed. However, from then on, the fox spirit became very active, throwing stones and tiles, shaking the doors and windows without a break. At first, they thought it was Tang returning, but upon closer inspection, they realized it was a real fox spirit. They could not bear its harassment and eventually abandoned their school and left. After being so frightened, they were also filled with shame and their spirits were weakened, allowing the fox to take advantage of their vulnerability. "Evil spirits arise from human actions" — this is what it means.

A certain scholar-official from Tianjin, accompanied by several young friends, went for a spring outing in the suburbs. They were all young and frivolous. Seeing a young woman riding a donkey under the shade of willows, they mocked her for being alone and invited the group to chase after her, engaging in flirtatious and teasing banter. The young woman did not respond and whipped the donkey to run quickly. Two or three of them caught up first, and the young woman suddenly dismounted and spoke in a gentle voice, as if she was pleased. Soon, the scholar-official and three or four others caught up, and upon closer inspection, it was revealed that the woman was his wife. However, his wife did not know how to ride a donkey, and there was no reason for her to be in the suburbs that day. He was both suspicious and angry, and approached her to scold her. His wife continued to laugh and joke as before, and the scholar-official, filled with rage, raised his hand to slap her face. His wife suddenly leaped onto the back of the donkey, transformed into a different form, and pointed at him with a whip, saying: "When you see another man's wife, you engage in all sorts of lewd behavior; when you see your own wife, you become angry like this. You have read the classics, yet you cannot understand the meaning of 'forgiveness.' How can you be listed on the imperial examination roll?" After finishing her words, she rode away. The scholar-official's face turned as pale as ashes, and he almost froze standing on the side of the road, unable to move. He finally realized that he had encountered some kind of spirit or demon.

Tian Baiyan of Dezhou said: There was a commander named E, who was traveling through the mountains between Yunnan and Guizhou. He saw a Daoist pressing a beautiful woman against a stone, intending to cut out her heart. The woman cried out in anguish for help, and E quickly rode forward, striking the Daoist’s hand. The woman let out a sudden, sharp cry and transformed into a flash of fire, flying away. The Daoist stamped his foot in frustration and said, “You have ruined my matter! This demon has seduced and killed over a hundred people, so I captured and executed it to eliminate the danger. But because it has absorbed much essence over the years, it has become spiritual. If you behead it, its spirit will flee, so only by cutting out its heart will it die. Now that you have let it go, you have once again caused endless suffering!” To spare the life of a tiger and release it into the deep mountains, without knowing how many lives of deer and gazelle have been torn apart by its fangs! He placed his dagger into a box, filled with resentment, and crossed the stream. This is the parable passed on to Baiyan, which is precisely what is meant by the saying: “What is the difference between one family weeping and an entire road weeping?” To tolerate corrupt officials, thinking it to be a hidden virtue, people often call such a person loyal and kind. Yet the poor, who are forced to sell their children and wives, have not even given it a second thought. Then, what use is this so-called “kind person”?

A clerk named Wang from Xian County was skilled in official writing and adept at cunningly taking people's wealth. However, every time he accumulated some wealth, an unexpected event would deplete it. One night, a young monk from the city god temple was walking in the corridors when he encountered two officials holding ledgers and discussing something. One of them said, "He has accumulated more this year; what method should be used to deplete it?" The other replied, "One Cuiyun is sufficient; there is no need for indirect or complicated methods." The temple often encountered ghosts, and the young monk, having seen them many times, was not afraid. However, he did not know who Cuiyun was or who was being depleted. Soon after, a courtesan named Cuiyun arrived, and Wang became deeply infatuated with her, depleting his wealth by eight or nine parts. He then contracted a severe illness, received thorough medical treatment, and by the time he recovered, he was completely ruined. People calculated the total wealth he had accumulated over his lifetime, which could be counted on the fingers, and it amounted to approximately thirty to forty thousand gold pieces. Later, he suddenly developed a mental illness and died, ultimately dying without a coffin for burial.

Chen Yunting, a secretary, reported that a messenger from Taiwan was staying at an inn when he saw a beautiful woman climbing the wall to peep. He shouted and searched but saw nothing. In the middle of the night, there was a clear sound, and then a tile was thrown onto the pillow. He shouted and asked what kind of demon it was, daring to insult the imperial envoy. A clear voice from outside the window said, "Your destiny is heavy, and I couldn't avoid you. I fear divine punishment and have been trembling until now. Now you have conceived evil thoughts while sleeping, mistakenly taking the daughter of a post official, planning to take her as a concubine in the future. When the heart moves, the gods and spirits know it. Calling evil with evil, I cannot be blamed for it, so I threw the tile to report back. Why are you angry?" The messenger was greatly ashamed and left before dawn, hurriedly packing and departing. Suddenly, a fox spirit appeared and spoke during the day at the residence of Ye Luting, an inspector. It forced Ye to give up his residence, causing disturbance and mocking him, to the point where the cups and plates danced by themselves, and the tables and beds moved by themselves. Ye reported this to Zhang the True Person, who entrusted the matter to a judge. First, he wrote a talisman, which was just spread out when it broke. Then he sent a document to the city隍, but it was also ineffective. The judge said, "This must be a celestial fox. Only by presenting a petition can it be done." So he built a Daoist altar for seven days. By the third day, the fox was still cursing, but by the fourth day, it finally pleaded for peace with gentle words. Ye did not want to make things difficult and prayed that the matter would not be completed. The True Person said, "The petition has already been presented and cannot be recalled." By the seventh day, there was suddenly a loud sound of fighting, and the doors and windows were broken and fell. By dusk, it had not yet stopped. The judge then issued a summons to other gods for help, and it was finally captured. It was then stored in a jar and buried outside the Guangqu Gate.

I once asked the true person about the reason for driving and commanding spirits, and he replied: "I also do not know why, but I simply follow the law and carry it out." Generally speaking, spirits are all subject to the power of the seal, and the talismans are in the hands of the judicial officer. The true person is like a superior officer, and the judicial officer is like a clerk; the true person cannot make talismans without the judicial officer, and the judicial officer's talismans are not effective without the true person's seal. Sometimes they work, and sometimes they do not, much like official documents and memorials, which may be approved or rejected, and cannot always be carried out. This statement is quite reasonable. I also asked, if in an empty house in a deep mountain, suddenly encountering a spirit or demon, can you still subdue it? He said: "It's like a high-ranking official passing by, and bandits naturally hide." If they are ignorant and rampant, suddenly attacking the double flags, even if holding the military token, unable to call for reinforcements, one is also helpless in the moment. This statement is also quite solid. Therefore, all these miraculous claims are all fabrications.

During his tenure as governor of Taian, Zhu Ziyin, the transport commissioner, heard that a scholar ventured deep into the sacred depths of Mount Tai. Suddenly, a voice emerged from the stone wall, saying, "Where is the incense burning? Could it be that a reincarnated person has come here?" There was a sudden, thunderous sound, and the stone wall opened, revealing magnificent jade towers and pearl palaces rising at the peak. An elderly scholar, dressed formally, descended to greet him. The scholar was startled and asked, "Where are we?" The elder replied, "This is the Hall of Incense." The scholar inquired about the meaning of the Hall of Incense, to which the elder responded, "The story is long; please sit and let me explain." In ancient times, Confucius refined and established teachings that have endured for millennia. Profound and subtle principles were passed down from generation to generation. During the Han Dynasty, scholars were close to the ancient times, and their explanations and annotations allowed them to glimpse the heart of the sage ancestors. They remained pure and uncorrupted, free from the habits of forming factions and competing for fame, each diligently tracing the origins of their teachings. This tradition continued unchanged into the Tang Dynasty. By the Northern Song Dynasty, thirteen volumes of commentaries and explanations were compiled, and the sage ancestors approved of this work. Concerned that new interpretations might emerge and gradually lead to the loss of these teachings, the great scholars of the time built this hall to store them.

At the center is the original manuscript, housed in a case of five-colored jade, to honor the sacred teachings; it is accompanied by official editions from successive dynasties, each in a case of white jade, to illustrate the merit of the emperors in promoting and venerating the classics—all facing south. To the left and right are privately published editions by various scholars; each time a new edition is completed, the finest and most carefully printed copy is selected, arranged in chronological order and stored in this hall, enclosed in cases of blue jade, as a reward for the diligence of those who seek out ancient texts—all facing east and west, marked with coral tags and secured with golden locks. In the eastern and western side halls, the tables are made of sandalwood and the cushions are of brocade. The spirits of the great scholars come once a year to inspect, and they sit together in this hall. Behind the third column are the interpretations of the classics by scholars prior to the Tang dynasty, bound in woven silk and collected into a single vault. Beyond this, even if a scholar's writings fill the body and their fame surpasses the ages, they must store their works in their own sacred mountains and may not take a single step into this gate. This is the will of the sage. When the books reach the hours of Zi and Wu, each word and sentence emits a rich fragrance—hence the name "Scent of the Classics" (Jingxiang). For the great cycle of the universe turns, and the two forces of yin and yang intermingle. Yin begins at the midpoint of Wu, and yang is born at the midpoint of Zi. The heart of the sage is in harmony with heaven and earth. The great scholars who expound the teachings of the sage, their profound insights also connect with heaven and earth, hence they are in resonance. Yet only those who have passed on this learning can hear it; others cannot.

World scholars who have studied the Thirteen Classics have taken various approaches: some have studied late into the night, diligently and reverently throughout their lives, while others have scrutinized and demanded rigorously, attacking from all angles. This reflects the differing inclinations of their natures and understanding. Four generations ago, your ancestor was a woodblock engraver who once carved half of the *Rites of Zhou* by hand. The lingering influence of his work allowed me to learn of your arrival, so I invited you to tour the halls and entertained you with tea and fruit. Before parting, I said: Take good care of yourself; this place is not easily accessible. As a scholar, you turned around and saw only towering mountains reaching into the sky, with no sign of human presence. This story is absurd and seems to be a parable by a Han scholar. Han scholars specialized in the study of classical texts' meanings and interpretations, while Song scholars valued moral and philosophical principles. It seems that Han studies were more basic, while Song studies were more refined. However, without understanding the meanings and interpretations, how could one know the moral and philosophical principles? Generally using slander and defamation, regarding something as worthless, once one has crossed a river, they burn the boat, implying that the tools of learning are discarded once the goal is achieved. Thus, critics of Song scholars began to emerge. I wrote in the preface of the *Poetry Section* in the *Four Treasuries Collection*: Song scholars criticized Han scholars not for the sake of interpreting the classics, but merely to outdo them.

The Criticism of Song Scholars Critics of the Song scholars are not motivated by a desire to interpret the classics; rather, they are dissatisfied with the Song scholars' criticism of the Han scholars. Wei Yingwu of Suzhou once wrote in his poem: "Water is naturally still, and there is no sound in the stone; how can they interact so violently, causing thunderous echoes to reverberate through the empty mountains?" This is what it refers to. Speaking frankly, the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes) began to change old interpretations from Wang Bi onwards, becoming the sprout of Song learning, but the Song scholars did not criticize this aspect. The *Xiao Jing* (Classic of Filial Piety) has clear words and meanings, and the Song scholars only argued about the modern and ancient forms of words and sentences, which are not related to the main point. Let's set them aside for now and not discuss them. As for the commentaries and annotations on the *Shang Shu* (Book of Documents), the *San Li* (Three Rites), the *San Zhuan* (Three Transmissions), the *Maoshi* (Book of Songs), and the *Er Ya* (Dictionary of Ancient Terms), all are based on ancient meanings, and it is beyond the capability of the Song scholars. The *Lun Yu* (Analects of Confucius) and the *Mengzi* (Mencius) were studied with great effort by the Song scholars, who carefully examined each word and sentence, which is beyond the reach of the Han scholars. The Han scholars valued teachers and had a clear lineage, while the Song scholars valued introspection and delved deeply into their studies. The Han scholars sometimes adhered strictly to old texts and trusted them too much, while the Song scholars sometimes relied on their own judgment and dared to alter the classics. Considering their gains and losses, they are roughly equal. Only the learning of the Han scholars requires reading and studying ancient texts to make any statement, while the learning of the Song scholars allows anyone to engage in empty talk. Like grass and sweet flag growing together, there are indeed things that do not fully satisfy people's hearts. This is the source of ridicule and criticism. Such fabricated words are not made without reason.

Cao Zhuxu, the Agrarian Minister, recounted that his cousin traveled from She to Yangzhou, passing by a friend's house during the height of summer. The friend invited him to sit in the study, which was very spacious and cool. At dusk, Cao wanted to stay there for the night, but his friend warned him that there was a ghost in the house and it was unsafe to stay at night. Despite the warning, Cao insisted on staying. In the middle of the night, something slimy and paper-thin crept through the door crack, entering the room and gradually unfolding into a human form, revealing itself to be a woman. Cao was not afraid at all. Suddenly, the figure pulled out her hair and stuck out her tongue, assuming the form of a hanged ghost. Cao laughed and said, "It's still hair, just a bit messy." The ghost's tricks were exhausted, and it suddenly disappeared. On the return journey, they stayed at another place overnight. In the middle of the night, the door crack moved again, and as soon as the ghost's head appeared, Cao spat and said, "Another thing to ruin the mood?" The ghost did not enter. This incident is similar to the story of Ji Zhongshan. Tigers do not eat drunk people because they do not fear. Fear causes the heart to be confused, and confusion causes the spirit to scatter. When the spirit scatters, the ghost can take advantage. Without fear, the heart remains calm, and calmness ensures the spirit is whole. When the spirit is united, evil influences cannot affect it. Therefore, those who recorded Ji Zhongshan's story said his spirit was clear and pure, and the ghost felt ashamed and left.

董 Qiujiang said, when Mr. Mo'an was the total漕official, his office had two shrines dedicated to the earth god and the horse god. Only the earth god had a consort. His youngest son, relying on his talent and being arrogant, thought that the earth god, an old man, should not have a beautiful wife; the horse god, being young, was the perfect match. He moved the statue of the female deity to the horse god's shrine. Soon after, he fainted and lost consciousness. Upon hearing of this, Mr. Mo'an personally prayed for the statue to be returned, and his son recovered. He also heard that in the school office of Hejian, there was an earth god with a consort represented by a female statue. A trainee official said that the school should not have statues of women, so he built a small shrine elsewhere and moved the statue there. The earth god spoke through his young grandson, saying: "Your reasoning may be correct, but your heart is selfish. You are only trying to expand your own space. I will not accept this." The trainee official was speaking eloquently about ancient rituals when suddenly his hidden motives were revealed, and he was greatly shocked. He then avoided staying in that position for the rest of his term. These two events are quite similar. Some might say that the trainee official moved the temple according to propriety, while Mr. Qiujiang was very disrespectful to the deity and should be severely punished. I believe that Mr. Qiujiang was just a young man acting recklessly. The trainee official, however, had hidden selfish motives, benefiting himself while pretending to act for the public good, leaving others with no excuse. It was only through the deity revealing his hidden plot that people realized he could not truly correct the rituals of worship. According to the principle of judging by intentions as in the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, the trainee official should be punished more severely than Mr. Qiujiang.

Magic tricks are all about quick hands. Yet, there is also real object-moving magic. I recall when I was young, in my maternal grandfather Mr. Xuefeng's house, a magician placed a cup of wine on the table, raised his hand and touched it, and the cup sank into the table, with its rim level with the table surface, yet touching the table did not reveal the bottom of the cup. After a short while, it was taken out, and the table remained as before. This might be a method of obstructing vision. He then raised a large bowl of fish slices, threw it into the air, and it disappeared. When asked to retrieve it, he said: "It cannot be done." It was in the drawer of the bookcase in the study, which you all can take for yourselves. At the time, there were many guests, and the study was filled with ancient artifacts, already locked up. Moreover, the drawer was only two inches high, while the bowl was three or four inches tall, so it could not possibly fit. I doubted it and called for the key to open it and see, and the bowl was placed on the table, containing five Buddha's hands instead. The dish originally containing Buddha's hands had been replaced with fish slices, hidden in the drawer. Is this not object-moving magic? What is logically impossible can sometimes occur in reality, cases like this are similar. Yet, it is also a matter of logical possibility. Fox spirits and mountain demons, stealing people or objects, are not extraordinary; those who can summon or control fox spirits and mountain demons are also not extraordinary; once they can summon or control, they can command them, and once they can steal people or objects, they can also retrieve things for others, what is there to be extraordinary about that?

Old servant Zhang Shou said, in the past, he served a certain official. One morning, he saw one official arrive, followed shortly by another, both of whom were close friends. Their demeanor suggested they were secretly passing messages. Soon after, they all left, and the host also departed, returning only at dusk. The vehicle and horse were extremely tired, unable to bear the exhaustion. Soon, the two officials returned, and under the light, they engaged in various gestures: whispering, nodding, shaking hands, frowning, and clapping, but the content of their discussion remained unknown. At two drums after midnight, the observer heard continuous laughter from the north window, but no one inside the room heard it. While the observer was puzzled, a long sigh was suddenly heard, followed by the words "Why be like this." The guests and host were startled, opened the window to look, and saw the smooth mud after the rain with no traces of people. They suspected it was the observer's dream, but the observer had been standing outside the southern veranda under the flower stand, not sleeping and not speaking, and ultimately, the reason remained unknown.

Er Tian, a scholar from Yongchun, chanced to rest on the road near Nine-Li Lake. A boy riding a cow came by, traveling very quickly. He stopped briefly in front of Er Tian and recited aloud: "Coming through wind and rain, going through mist and clouds, the slanting sunlight illuminates the green peaks, this is my return path." Er Tian was surprised that a village boy could come up with such words. As he pondered and wanted to ask, the boy's hat shadow appeared and disappeared among the pines and cypresses, already half a li away. Er Tian wondered whether the boy was a fairy playing a trick or a village schoolchild who had heard someone recite it and happened to remember it. Lin Pei, a teacher from Putian, returned to the north after completing his term in Taiwan. On the way, he reached the southern part of Zhuozhou and got out of his carriage to take a break. He saw a poem written on the wall of a dilapidated house with a piece of magnetic material: "Caravans of mules, each with a bell, clear morning, braving the cold to pass by a post station, I leisurely enjoy the snowy landscape, the donkey's hooves slowly tread through the green mountains." The signature read "Luo Yang Mountain Man." After reading the poem, Lin Pei said to himself, "The poem has a slight charm, but what is Luo Yang?" Someone inside the house responded, "The language seems to be from Huguang." When Lin Pei entered to see, there was only dust and fallen leaves. Realizing he had encountered a ghost, he was frightened and quickly got back into his carriage. He remained depressed and uncomfortable, and soon after, he died.

Li Luyuan Ji, a scholar from Jingzhou and a juren (presented scholar) of the Kangxi era, was my brother-in-law. He was erudite and skilled in poetry. One day, while waiting for his appointment, he composed a couplet in a dream: "Luanhe Jizhong, Emei Quzuo." Upon waking, he could not understand its meaning. Later, he obtained the position of a magistrate in Hunan and died in office, exactly in the place where Qu Yuan is said to have wandered and composed his poetry. My grandmother, Lady Zhang, kept a small spotted dog. The maidservants were troubled by the dog stealing meat and secretly strangled it. One of the maids, named Liu Yi, constantly saw the dog coming to bite her in her dreams and would mutter in her sleep. Lady Zhang learned about this and said, "If the maidservants all killed the dog, why does Liu Yi alone bear the grudge? This must be because Liu Yi also stole meat and was not convinced of the dog's innocence." When she investigated, it turned out to be true. In the examination hall of Tingzhou, Fujian, there were two ancient cypress trees in front of the hall, relics from the Tang dynasty. It was said that these trees had a spirit. On the day I visited the hall, an official told me that I should pay respects to the trees. I replied that tree spirits do not cause harm, so I could simply observe them. Moreover, paying respects to tree spirits was not part of the official rituals, and as an envoy, I should not bow to them. The trees had dense and towering branches, visible through several layers of buildings. That night, under the bright moonlight, I walked up the steps and saw two red-capped figures on the tree branches, bowing and saluting me, gradually disappearing. I called my secretary to see them, and he still saw them. The next day, I bowed to the trees and had a couplet inscribed on the gate of the shrine: "The dark green of the towering trees remains unchanged, the red-capped figure might be you." This event was quite unusual. Yuan Mei once recorded this incident in his *New Qi Heng*, but his account was slightly different, likely due to misreporting.

A gentleman from Dezhou named Song Qingyuan recounted that there was a Taoist priest named Lü, whose origins were unknown, but who was skilled in magical arts. He once stayed at a rural household in the mountains during the time when the purple wisteria was in full bloom, and guests gathered to admire it. A vulgar scholar spoke words that were crude and base, talking incessantly, which greatly spoiled the mood. A young man, of a light and flippant nature, was even more disgusted and scolded the scholar to not speak more. The two were about to roll up their sleeves in anger, but an elderly scholar tried to mediate. Neither would listen, and anger showed on their faces. Everyone in the room felt unhappy. The Taoist priest whispered to a child to fetch paper and ink, drew three talismans, and burned them. Suddenly, the three people stood up and moved around the courtyard several times. The vulgar scholar went to sit in the southeast corner and muttered to himself, and it was heard that he was talking about family matters with his wives and concubines. Soon he looked back and forth as if trying to reconcile, then his face brightened as he defended himself, soon made a show of taking responsibility, knelt on one knee, then on both knees, and kept bowing his head repeatedly; looking at the young man, he was sitting on a flower stand in the southwest corner, his eyes wandering and looking around, speaking softly and gently, soon laughed and joked, soon humbly thanked, soon sang softly from the *Huan Sha Ji*, kept cooing, clapping his hands, displaying all kinds of licentious behavior; the elderly scholar sat upright on a stone bench and lectured on the chapter about Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Wen of Jin from the *Mengzi*, analyzing each word and sentence, gesturing and looking around, as if talking to four or five people, suddenly shook his head and said 'no,' suddenly glared and said 'still don't understand?' and kept coughing incessantly.

The crowd was shocked into laughter. The Taoist priest shook his hand to stop them. When the wine feast ended, the Taoist burned three talismans, and the three then sat in a dazed, fixed state. After a short while, they woke up, claiming they had unknowingly fallen asleep, and apologized for their rudeness. The crowd secretly laughed and dispersed. The Taoist said, "This is a small trick, not worth mentioning." Ye Fashan led Emperor Xuanzong of Tang into the Moon Palace using this talisman. At the time, they mistakenly thought it was a real immortal, and the conservative scholars thought it was nonsense. They are all like frogs at the bottom of a well. Later, at an inn, the talisman captured the soul of a concubine of a passing noble. The concubine woke up and got into the carriage, recognizing the path and the entrance. She told the noble to hurry and catch her, but she had already escaped. Is this why the Zhou Rituals prohibit strange people?

The Old Scholar from Jiahe and Runchu, during the provincial examination in the year Yimao of the Yongzheng era. They arrived late at Shimen Bridge, where all the inns were full. There was only one small house with windows facing the stables, and nobody was willing to stay there, so they temporarily unpacked and settled down. The horses were restless, and they could not sleep at night. After everyone had gone to sleep, they suddenly heard the horses talking. The scholar loved to read miscellaneous books and remembered a story from a collection of tales from the Song dynasty about a cow speaking under a dam. Knowing it was not a ghost, they held their breath and listened. One horse said, "Today I finally understand the pain of hunger. Where did the grass and bean money I cheated during my life go?" Another horse said, "We were mostly reborn from stable hands. The dead know, but the living do not understand. It is worth sighing." All the horses began to weep. One horse said, "The judgment in the afterlife is not very fair. Why did Wang Wu become a dog?" Another horse said, "The messenger of the underworld once said that his wife and two daughters were all promiscuous, they stole all his money and gave it to their lovers, and they deserve half the punishment." One horse said, "Indeed, punishments vary in severity. Jiang Qi fell into the role of a pig and suffered being slaughtered. It is worse than our fate." Then he coughed lightly, and the conversation stopped. The scholar often used this story to warn stable hands.

My one concubine, throughout her life, never uttered abusive words. She herself claimed to have seen her grandmother, who was known for her abusive language, suffer no illness, but suddenly her tongue rotted up to the throat, rendering her unable to eat, speak, or move, and she twisted in pain for several days before dying. There was a man who, one day, rose late and called for his wife and concubines, but they did not come. He asked a young maid, who told him that they had both followed a young man south. He drew his sword and chased after them, intending to cut them in two, but the young man suddenly disappeared. An old monk, dressed in a red robe, holding a begging bowl in one hand and a staff in the other, blocked his sword and said, "Have you not yet gone to sleep? Your greed, jealousy, and cunning are too great, and you are able to make others unaware of them. The gods and spirits despise hidden evil, so they have decreed that these two women should do this to punish you. What fault do they have? After speaking, the monk also disappeared. The man returned home silently. The two women said, "We did not know the young man, nor did we like him, but suddenly we felt bewildered as if in a dream and followed him." The neighbors also said, "These two women were not promiscuous, and they did not get along with the young man, so how could they follow him? Moreover, promiscuous women would avoid being caught, so how could there be someone walking slowly and waiting to be caught in broad daylight? This must be a case of divine punishment." However, the man's evil deeds remain unnamed, truly concealing evil.

All things are predestined, how can this not be true? In the spring of the year Wu Zi, I wrote a poem for a painting depicting foreign cavalry engaged in hunting, saying: "White grass stretches to the sky, wild animals are fat; drawing a bow, the arrow flies like a horse; when shall I drink the blood of a yellow sheep? Setting up a hunting party on the snowy Tianshan mountains." In August of that year, I eventually joined the army in the Western Regions. Later, Dong Wenke once made a painting of me seeking verses in the autumn forest for me. When I arrived in Urumqi, there was a deep forest to the west of the city, with old trees reaching the clouds, stretching for dozens of miles. General Wu built a pavilion in the middle of it, naming it Xiu Ye. Walking there, it was just like the scene in the previous painting. In the year Xin Mao, I returned to the capital, and then wrote a quatrain for myself: "Autumn leaves turn yellow, the bones of trees become greenish; I sing alone and feel too isolated; who knew it was an omen of my early western journey? Old trees, cold clouds, and the Xiu Ye pavilion."

A physician from Nanpi, skilled in treating ulcers, was highly proficient in his craft. However, he secretly used toxic medicines and demanded exorbitant fees, and if his desires were not satisfied, the patient would surely die. His methods were secretive, and other doctors could not understand them. One day, his son was struck dead by lightning. Although the physician is still alive, no one dares to call upon him. Some say that the physician had killed many people, so why did heaven strike the son instead of the father? This seems to be an excessive punishment. Generally, punishment for a crime does not extend to the family unless it is a capital offense, and evil does not lead to extreme calamity unless it is severe. By striking the son, heaven makes clear that misfortune can extend to future generations. Anzong Kuan said: In the past, when Wu Sangui rebelled, there was a diviner who was highly skilled in the Six Rens (a form of divination). He was about to go to join Wu Sangui when he met someone who also said he intended to join Wu Sangui. They stayed in the same lodging that night, and the other man slept under the western wall. The diviner said to him, "Do not sleep here; this wall will collapse at midnight." The man replied, "Your skill is not deep enough; the wall will collapse outward, not inward." And indeed, at night the wall collapsed outward. I believe this is a fabricated story. If this man could predict the direction of the wall's collapse, why could he not foresee Wu Sangui's inevitable defeat?

There was a monk who traveled to the home of Su Libu Cigong, a high-ranking official in the Ministry of Personnel. The monk was skilled in magic, producing wonders without end. He claimed to have studied under the same master as Lü the Taoist. Once, he molded clay into a pig, chanted over it, and it gradually wriggled; he chanted again, and it suddenly made a sound; he chanted once more, and it leaped up. He then handed it over to the cook to slaughter and serve the guests, but the taste was not very good. After eating, the guests all vomited, and what they vomited was all mud. There was a scholar who, due to rain, stayed overnight. He secretly asked the monk, "According to the *Taiping Guangji*, a magician chanted over a tile and gave it to someone, and the wall was instantly opened, allowing one to secretly reach someone's inner chamber. Can your teacher's technique achieve this?" The monk replied, "This is not difficult." He picked up a tile and chanted over it for a long time, saying, "Hold this and you can go, but do not speak; if you speak, the technique will be dispelled." The scholar tried it, and the wall indeed opened. He arrived at a place and saw the one he admired just taking off her makeup and getting ready to sleep. He kept the monk's warning and did not speak, and went straight to the bed and engaged in intimate relations. The woman also happily engaged in intimate relations and fell asleep. Suddenly opening his eyes, he saw himself sleeping on his wife's bed. They were just about to question each other when the monk came to the door and rebuked him, saying, "Lü the Taoist, with a single wrong thought, has already been struck by lightning. Are you going to involve me too?" The monk continued, "This small trick was just a joke on you; fortunately, it did not harm your great virtue. Do not even think of it again in the future." After that, he sighed and said, "This single thought has already been recorded by the officials of fate. Although there is no great punishment, it may still be detrimental to your official record." The scholar indeed suffered setbacks. Later, he obtained a position as a schoolmaster but ultimately remained poor.

During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, Hu Weihua of Xian County gathered people by burning incense and plotted rebellion. His stronghold was located along the route from Dacheng and Wen'an, about 300 miles from the capital, Beijing; and along the route from Qing County and Jinghai, about 200 miles from Tianjin. Hu Weihua planned to divide his forces into two: one would move unexpectedly to reach Beijing simultaneously; the other would occupy Tianjin, plunder ships at sea, and if successful, the troops in Tianjin would advance boldly, but if unsuccessful, they would retreat to Tianjin, board ships, and sail into the sea. While he was setting up fake officials, the plot was already exposed. The imperial army captured him, surrounded him, and launched a fire attack, leaving no one alive. Initially, Hu Weihua's father was wealthy and enjoyed helping the poor and needy, and he did not commit great evils. In a neighboring village, the elderly scholar Zhang Yueping had a daughter of exceptional beauty, almost the most beautiful woman in the country, and Hu Weihua was captivated by her. However, Zhang Yueping was upright and rigid, with no intention of allowing her to become a concubine, so he invited her to teach.

The coffins of Yueping's parents were in Liaodong and could not be brought back, causing him to be constantly sad. Whenever he mentioned it, he would donate money to have them escorted back and also give them a burial plot. There was a body found in Yueping's fields, which was his enemy, and the authorities investigated it as a case of murder. He defended himself with every possible means and was eventually acquitted. One day, Yueping's wife took their daughter to visit her parents, and they had three young sons. Yueping went home to guard the house, planning to return in a few days. He secretly had his accomplices lock the door at night and burn down his house, and all four of them perished in the fire. He pretended to be shocked and grieved, arranged for the funeral, and often helped his wife and daughter, eventually coming to rely on him for support. Someone wanted to marry the daughter, and her mother would always consult her about it, but she would secretly discourage her from accepting. Over time, the man gradually revealed his intention to seek the daughter as a concubine. The mother was grateful for his kindness and wanted to agree, but the daughter initially refused. One night, she dreamed that her father said, "If you don't go, I will never fulfill my wishes." The daughter then accepted the fate. After a year, she gave birth to Weihua, but soon fell ill and died. Weihua ultimately destroyed the family line.

There is a place about thirty to forty miles from my home where someone abused and killed the servants and their family and then took in the daughter. The daughter was clever and cunning. She managed his diet, clothing, and daily needs, doing everything to his satisfaction. Anything that could win her favor, she would do, being licentious and intimate with him in every way. People privately commented that she had forgotten her grudge. As she bewitched him deeply, he listened only to what she said. At first, she led him into luxury, squandering about seven to eight tenths of his property. She also sowed discord among his family members, making those inside the family like enemies. Later, she often told him stories from *Water Margin* about Song Jiang and Chai Jin, calling them heroes and encouraging him to associate with outlaws. Eventually, he was executed for murder. On the day of his execution, the woman did not cry for her husband but secretly carried a cup of wine to offer it to her parents' graves, saying, "My parents always dreamed of tormenting me, filled with resentment and seeming to want to hit me. Do they know now?" People then realized that she had harbored a desire for revenge. Someone said, "What this woman did, not only humans but even ghosts cannot predict. How deep her schemes are!" However, it is not considered a case of cunning and deceit. The *Spring and Autumn Annals* judge based on intent; fundamentally, they are enemies who cannot coexist under the same sky.

When I was in Urumqi, the military officials prepared dozens of official documents and held a brush of ink, asking for a judgment, saying: "For all those who die as guests here, their coffins are returned to their hometowns, and they are given official documents as a rule. Otherwise, their souls cannot enter the border. To be valid in the underworld, it is not signed with red ink, and the seal is also in black." Looking at the text, it is very crude and absurd. I said, "This is just the scribes fabricating an excuse to extort money," and informed the general to abolish this practice. After ten days, someone reported that ghosts were crying in the ruins and graves west of the city, and they cannot return home without the documents. I dismissed it as false; after another ten days, someone reported that the ghosts were crying even closer to the city, and I dismissed it as before; after another ten days, there was a sound outside my wall, and I still thought it was the scribes' fabrication; after several more days, the sound reached my window, and at that time, the moon was bright and clear, so I got up to look, but there was actually no one there.

A colleague, the Censor Cheng, remarked: "The reasoning you uphold is correct, and even a general could not override it." Yet the wails of the ghosts were indeed heard by all; those who could not be vindicated, in truth, resented you. Why not try granting them once, merely to temporarily silence the tongues of slanderers? If the ghosts still wailed as before, then you would also have a defense. Reluctantly, he followed the suggestion. That night, all was quiet. Another military official, Song Jilu, was in the seal room when suddenly he fainted and, after a long while, awoke and said he had seen his mother arrive. Soon afterward, a military officer presented an official document. Upon opening it, it was reported that Jilu’s mother had come to visit her son, but had died on the way. What strange things exist in the world! Scholars merely discuss the ordinary. I once composed a hundred and sixty poems on Urumqi, one of which reads: "White grass rustles, meeting cold clouds, Who divides the passes and borders? Ghostly spirits come and go with official documents, The original ghost of Changli has yet to be heard." These are the very two events I have just described.

Fàn Héngzhōu said that once, while crossing the Qiantang River, he boarded a boat with a monk who immediately placed his sitting mat and leaned against the mast, without exchanging greetings. When Fàn Héngzhōu spoke to him, the monk answered vaguely, his eyes fixed elsewhere, and his mind seemed disinterested. Fàn Héngzhōu found him arrogant and did not speak to him again. At that time, the west wind was blowing fiercely, and Fàn Héngzhōu happened to compose two lines of poetry: "White waves toss the boat's bow, travelers fear the stone wind." He was still thinking of the second line, reciting it several times, when the monk suddenly closed his eyes and softly recited: "How can the red-sleeved maiden still lean on the highest tower?" Fàn Héngzhōu did not understand what he meant and tried to speak to him again, but the monk did not respond. By the time the boat was tied to the dock, a young girl was standing on the highest floor of a building, wearing a red sleeve. Fàn Héngzhōu was greatly surprised and asked her repeatedly. She replied, "I just happened to see her." However, the water and mist were vast, and the houses were obscured, making it impossible to see clearly. Fàn Héngzhōu suspected that the monk had some kind of foresight and wanted to show him respect, but the monk had already taken his staff and left. Fàn Héngzhōu was puzzled and unable to understand, saying, "This is yet another Lùò Bīnwáng." As for Zhang Gōng Yuè of Qīngyuán, when he was serving as an official in Zhengzhou, Henan, there was an old mulberry tree in his office that was so thick that two people could not encircle it. It was said to house a divine being, and Zhang Gōng Yuè hated it and cut it down. That night, his daughter saw a person by the lamp. The person's face, hands, feet, and clothes were all a deep green, and he spoke in a stern voice: "Your father is too overbearing. I am showing you a warning." She screamed and called the maidservants, but the spirit had already gone mad. Later, she returned to Gē Tài Pú Xiānzhōu and did not live long. To drive away evil spirits and destroy idolatrous shrines is the kind of thing that Dí Liánggōng and Fàn Wénzhènggōng would do. If virtue is not sufficient to overcome it, it is rare not to fail.

Master Qian Wenmin said: Is not the dispensation of calamity and blessing by Heaven akin to the rewards and punishments of a ruler?
