Is not the discernment of ghosts and spirits akin to the detailed deliberations of officials? Suppose there were a memorial stating: "A certain official has maintained an unblemished character and has achieved notable accomplishments in office, yet his entrance and pathways face an inauspicious direction, and his construction violated an inauspicious day. Should he be demoted and punished? Would the responsible official approve or reject this?" Suppose again there were a recommendation stating: "A certain official has many blemishes in his character and has performed poorly in office, yet his entrance and pathways face a favorable direction, and his construction took place on a favorable day. Should he be promoted? Would the responsible official approve or reject this?" If officials would certainly reject the first case, then why would ghosts and spirits approve of the second? Therefore, I ultimately do not accept the theory of yang zhai (the selection of auspicious sites for dwellings). This analogy is extremely clear, used to question the image or concept, and there is no room for argument. Yet I have indeed seen houses of misfortune. In the capital, there is a house diagonally opposite Gugusi Temple on the southern side of the road, for which I performed mourning rites five times; on the northernmost road of Fenfang Lüli Street, there is another house for which I performed mourning rites seven times. The house near Gugusi Temple was once occupied by Master Cao Zongcheng, who studied in Fujian. As soon as he moved in, two of his servants died suddenly in one night, and out of fear he moved away; the house on Fenfang Lüli Street was once occupied by Professor Shao Dashi, who often saw strange phenomena during the day, yet remained resolute and fearless, ultimately dying within it. What explanation is there for this? Master Liu Wenzheng said: Choosing a burial site is found in the classics, choosing a day for burial is found in the rites. If there were no auspicious or inauspicious signs, why would the sage perform divination? It is merely that this is not known to the present-day diviners. This is a balanced and fair statement.

Pan Ban of Cangzhou was skilled in painting and calligraphy, and he self-titled himself Yellow Leaf Daoist. Once, he stayed overnight at a friend's study and heard a small voice from the wall say, "You will not have anyone to share your bed tonight, so I will come to you." Pan was greatly frightened and moved out. The friend said, "The room has had this spirit before. It is a charming and graceful woman who does not cause harm." Later, the friend privately told his close associates, "Will Pan ultimately be trapped in the life of a scholar? This spirit is neither a ghost nor a fox, and its nature is uncertain. It does not appear to coarse or vulgar people, nor to wealthy or powerful people, but only to talented scholars who are in distress, offering them companionship in bed." Indeed, Pan suffered misfortunes and died. After more than ten years, he suddenly heard crying in the study at night. The next day, a strong wind broke an old apricot tree, and the spirit then disappeared. My maternal grandfather, Mr. Zhang Xuefeng, once joked, "This spirit is excellent, its awareness surpasses that of those in silk and brocade."

Tán Fēngyá Guānglù said that during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, a scholar from Fongting, while reading in a study house, saw a stone in the grass that was broken and worn, with only a few dozen characters remaining, occasionally forming a couplet or a complete sentence, which seemed to be the tombstone of a deceased young woman. The scholar, who was fond of such things, thought that the tomb must be nearby, and so he often placed tea and fruit on the stone and wished upon it with intimate words. After more than a year, he saw a beautiful woman walking alone in a vegetable patch, holding wild flowers, and looking at him with a smile. The scholar approached her, flirting with his eyes and eyebrows, and was about to lead her into the bushes, but the woman stood still, looking straight at him, as if lost in thought. Suddenly, she slapped herself and said, "For over a hundred years, my heart has been as calm as an ancient well, yet now it is moved by a wayward youth?" She stamped her feet several times and then disappeared. He then realized that she was the ghost of the tomb. Pán Xiūzhuàn Jìshí said: "It is said that one can judge a person only after they are dead, but in this case, it is known that even after death, it is still difficult to make a final judgment." This was a soul of purity, yet due to a momentary lapse, she almost lost her way. Zhu Xi's poem says: "There is nothing in the world as dangerous as human desire, how many people have been misled by this throughout their lives, truly."

A scholar named Jin Ying, who had passed the imperial examination but had not yet been appointed to an official position, recounted that in Nanjing, a scholar was staying in an abandoned garden of an old family. One moonlit night, a beautiful woman peered through his window. He immediately recognized that she was either a ghost or a fox spirit, but he loved her beauty and did not fear her. He invited her into the room, and she responded with grace. However, she never spoke a word, and when he asked her, she did not answer. She only smiled and cast her gaze around. For about a month, he could not understand her reason. One day, he took her hand and firmly questioned her. She then took a pen and wrote, saying: "I was a concubine of a certain Hanlin scholar in the Ming dynasty, unfortunately died young. Because I was skilled in spreading slander, I caused the family to be like water and fire. The underworld punished me, and I was condemned to be a mute ghost. I have been in this state for over two hundred years. If you can write ten copies of the Diamond Sutra, you can rely on the power of the Buddha to be lifted out of the sea of suffering, and you will be grateful in every life." The scholar followed her request and wrote the ten copies of the Diamond Sutra. On the day when the writing was completed, she came to the scholar and bowed twice, and then took a pen and wrote again, saying: "By reciting the Diamond Sutra, I have been freed from the realm of ghosts. However, my sins from my previous life were severe, and I can only take my karma with me to the next life. I still need to be a mute woman for three more lives before I can speak again."

Records of the Hall of Reading the Small Details, Volume 2, Panyang Summer Records, Part 2 When Mr. Dong Wenke was a junior official in the Ministry of Works, he recounted that in his past life in Fuyang, a village elder sat near his home, hearing the sound of reading and said, "You are a person of high status, please let me meet you." After carefully observing him several times, the elder asked about his birth chart and pondered for a long time, saying, "Your fate and appearance are both of the highest rank. You will become a county magistrate in a certain year, temporarily serve as the magistrate of a larger county in another year, be officially appointed to a position in yet another year, and be promoted to a deputy governor in another year, then to a prefectural governor, and from there to a provincial administrator, and then to a provincial governor, and finally to a governor-general. Take good care of yourself, and one day you will know that my words are not mistaken." After this initial meeting, the narrator did not see the elderly villager again, and the predictions made did not come true. However, when the narrator examined their life in detail, the positions they held matched the predictions, but through different paths. The predictions were both verified and unverified, depending on the interpretation, but the prediction about becoming a governor-general remains uncertain.

After the public official was appointed as the Minister of Rites in the year of his age, his rank remained unchanged. According to the calculation of the Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches, sometimes the predictions were astonishingly accurate, sometimes completely inaccurate, or sometimes partially accurate and partially inaccurate. I have often pondered deeply on the most certain cases I have heard or seen, and found that the eight characters indicating fortune, nobility, poverty, and wealth are only roughly like this, with slight variations in their addition and subtraction, surplus and deficit. The wife of Mr. Zou Xiaoshan from Wuxi and the wife of Mr. Chen Misha from Anzhou had the same Heavenly Stems and Earthly Branches in their eight characters. Mr. Zou Xiaoshan served as the Deputy Minister of Rites, while Mr. Chen Misha served as the Governor of Guizhou, both holding the second rank. In terms of title, the Governor was not as prestigious as the Deputy Minister; in terms of salary, the Deputy Minister did not receive as much as the Governor, but they complemented each other in this way. Both ladies enjoyed long lives. Mrs. Chen was widowed early, but in her later years she remained healthy and content; Mrs. Zou lived harmoniously with her husband until old age, but in her later years she lost a child and her household finances were also thin, which also complemented each other in this way. This may be due to the difference in their places of birth, one in the north and the other in the south, or the difference in their times of birth, one early and the other late. My sixth nephew and my servant Liu Yunpeng were born only a wall apart, with their windows facing each other, and both cried at the same time when they were born, not only in the same hour and minute, but even in the same second. My nephew died at the age of sixteen, while Liu Yunpeng is still alive today. Is it not that the fortune assigned to this life is only this much: my nephew grew up in wealth and privilege, and his fortune was exhausted first; Liu Yunpeng grew up in poverty and lowly status, and his fortune has not yet been exhausted? The fullness and emptiness, growth and decline of fortunes are indeed like this, and let those who understand fate elaborate further.

My great-uncle, Guangji, served as the garrison commander of Zhenfan at the beginning of the Kangxi era. He had a wife who was the wife of a scholar named Li. She constantly abused her concubine, stripping off her lower garment and beating her whenever she was angry, almost every day. There was an old woman in the village who could enter the underworld, known as a "wandering soul guide." She advised his wife, saying: "You have an ancient grudge with this concubine, but she should only be flogged 200 times. Now, your jealousy is intense, and you have beaten her more than ten times over, adding to your debt. Moreover, a virtuous woman should not be stripped and humiliated, even by law. If the matter goes too smoothly, you will offend the gods and spirits. You are kind to me, and I secretly see the records of the underworld, so I dare not remain silent." The wife smiled and said: "Old woman, you're just talking nonsense, do you want me to appease the spirits and get money?" Coinciding with the fall of the general, and the rebellion led by Wang Fuchen, rebel forces rose up like bees. Li died in battle, and the concubine was obtained by General Han. He liked her intelligence and wisdom, and she became the favored concubine. General Han had no official wife, and the household affairs were then managed by the concubine. The wife was captured by the rebels. The rebels were defeated and captured, and the spoils were distributed among the soldiers. She was given to General Han. The concubine kept her as a servant and made her kneel in the hall, saying to her: "If you can obey my commands, every morning you must first kneel in front of the dressing table, strip off your lower garment, and receive five lashes on the ground, then you can serve. Otherwise, you will lose your life."

Otherwise, if you are the wife of a bandit, there is no prohibition against killing you; you should be cut into small pieces and fed to dogs and pigs. The wife, fearing death, loses her resolve and bows her head, wishing to follow your teachings. However, I do not want her to die too soon, so the whipping is not too severe, only to make her feel pain. After a year, she dies from another illness, and the number of whippings is just right. This woman is truly stubborn, dull, and shameless. She is also hated by the gods and spirits, who have secretly taken her soul. Mr. Han does not hide this and cites it to illustrate the principle of cause and effect. Therefore, people know the details. Mr. Han also says that this is still a case of swapping positions openly. During the late Ming dynasty, he once traveled in the regions of Xiang and Deng, and lived with the Taoist master Zhang Yunhu in the same lodging. Zhang Yunhu knew well that the host's wife was excessively cruel to her concubine, and he had long been dissatisfied with this. He privately said, "Taoism has a method of borrowing forms. When one has not yet completed cultivation but their vital energy has already declined, and they cannot achieve the elixir of immortality, then they borrow a strong and vigorous body and switch places with it while it is asleep. I have learned this method, let's try it." The next day, the family suddenly heard the wife speaking in the concubine's room, and the concubine speaking in the wife's room. When they came out of the house, the one speaking like the wife was the concubine, and the one speaking like the concubine was the wife. The concubine obtained the wife's body but just sat quietly; the wife obtained the concubine's body and was very unwilling. They argued and quarreled, and the relatives could not decide. They reported it to the authorities, who were angry and called it absurd. They flogged the husband and expelled them, but there was nothing they could do. However, according to appearance, the wife was actually the concubine. Not in her position, she could not exercise authority. They eventually separated the house and lived apart until the end. This matter is especially strange.

It is said that there was a private teacher who, on a moonlit summer night, led his disciples to cool off on the field ridge outside the Temple of King Xian of Hejian. They discussed proposed titles for the Three Hundred Poems, their voices clear and resonant like bells and drums. He then ordered the children to recite the *Xiaojing* (Classic of Filial Piety), and after they finished, they discussed it further. Suddenly, lifting his head, he saw faint figures beneath the two ancient cypress trees at the temple gate. He tried to approach them, and their shapes were quite unusual, leading him to realize they were spirits or ghosts. However, he privately thought that there could be no evil spirits or monsters in front of the Temple of King Xian. He asked them their names, and they replied, "Mao Chang, Guan Changqing, and Yan Zhi, because we came to pay respects to King Xian." The private teacher was very happy and bowed twice to request teachings on the classics. Mao and Guan together said, "We have heard what you have spoken, but it is all beyond our understanding, so we cannot respond." The private teacher bowed again and said, "The meaning of the *Shijing* is profound and subtle, difficult to teach to someone of limited understanding. May Mr. Yan give a lecture on the *Xiaojing*?" Yan Hui, facing inward, said, "What your children recited is missing and disordered, completely different from the version I taught, so I have nothing to say." Soon, he heard the king's command saying, "There seems to be someone outside speaking drunkenly, disturbing the ears for a long time, please drive them away." I believe this is similar to what Mr. Aitang said about scholars encountering ghost officials, all of which are witty remarks by erudite scholars to mock common scholars. However, it is also true that wind comes through a hole and turtledoves build nests where there is milkweed.

My deceased grandfather, the Lord of Yao'an, was known for his stern and severe nature, and his household never entertained people of low status. One day, he conversed with a man dressed in tattered clothes, and he called my brothers and me to greet him. He said, "This is the great-grandson of Cao Manshu. We have not heard from each other for a long time, and now I see him." During the late Ming dynasty, your great-grandfather was eleven years old, wandering among soldiers, and survived thanks to Cao Manshu. Cao Manshu then carefully planned for his survival, and he warned my brothers and me, "What should be repaid out of righteousness, there is no need to discuss cause and effect, but cause and effect will not be wrong." In the past, there was a certain official who received a second chance at life, and after becoming wealthy and powerful, he ignored the descendants of those who had helped him, treating them as strangers on the road. Later, when he was ill, just as he was about to take medicine, he suddenly saw the person who had saved him hand him two unsealed letters. Upon reading them, they turned out to be the letters he had written asking for help in the past. He overturned the cup on the ground and said, "I died too late." That night, he passed away. Cao Manshu, the governor of Sichuan, recounted that this certain official was a censor during the Ming dynasty. Once, he performed a divination session to ask about his lifespan, and the spirit predicted that he would die on a specific date. The date was not far off, and he was always worried, but at the appointed time, he was unharmed. Later, he entered the new dynasty and reached the rank of Nine Ministers. Just as his colleague's family was performing a divination session, the same spirit descended again. The official asked the spirit why his prediction had not come true, and the spirit replied, "If you don't die, what can I do?" The official pondered deeply and suddenly decided to leave, for the date the spirit had predicted was indeed March 19, 1644, the day the Ming dynasty fell.

When Mr. Shen Jiao Yuan was the headmaster of Aofeng Academy, he showed me the old inkstone of Zhao Zhongyi from Gaoyi. The front of the inkstone bears six characters: "Eastern Sky Not Yet Bright." The back has an inscription that reads: "The faint glow of the waning moon, the steady light of the White Star, the rooster crows three times, the fifth watch has struck. At this moment, submitting a memorial to attack the eunuchs, if successful, you will be rewarded; if not, you will be demoted together." This was the inkstone used to draft the memorial to impeach Wei Zhongxian. At the end, there is a small line of text inscribed by the disciple Wang Duo: "This line was not engraved, but the black mark deeply etched into the stone, only visible when wet." When water is used to clean it, the five characters become clearly visible. It is said that initially, Wang Duo was ordered to write this inscription, but before it could be engraved, difficulties arose. Later, in a place of exile, it was engraved, with the instruction to the craftsman not to engrave this line. However, after over a hundred years, it cannot be washed away, which is quite remarkable. Some say: Zhao Zhongyi was strict in his hatred of evil. The notes of the Fishery Mountain Man say that Wang Duo's character and calligraphy have declined. Therefore, Zhao Zhongyi must have seen this beforehand, removing his name and rejecting him. It cannot be washed away, indicating that he was once rejected by Zhao Zhongyi. Heaven, earth, and spirits often reveal their subtlety in one matter, warning people, and this might be true.

In the year of Gengwu during the Qianlong era, the official treasury lost some jade artifacts. An investigation was conducted among the garden households, and when the garden household member Changming was being questioned, he suddenly spoke in the voice of a child, saying: "I did not steal the jade artifacts, but I did kill a person, and I am the spirit of the person I killed." The magistrate was greatly shocked and transferred the case to the Ministry of Punishment. At that time, Mr. Yao'an was the Deputy Minister of Jiangsu, and along with Mr. Yu Wenyi and others, they interrogated Changming. The spirit said: "My name is Ergé, I am fourteen years old, my home is in Haidian, and my father is Li Xingwang. Last Lantern Festival, Changming took me to watch the lanterns and return home. It was deep into the night when everyone was quiet, and Changming teased and flirted with me. I was trying to resist and said I would tell my father when I returned, but Changming then strangled me to death with a sash and buried me under the riverbank. My father suspected Changming had hidden me, complained to the city patrol, and sent the case to the Ministry of Punishment. Because there was no evidence, they decided to investigate for the real culprit."

My soul has always followed Changming, but when we were only a few feet apart, I felt as if I was standing in front of a raging fire and could not get close. Later, the heat slightly decreased, and I gradually approached to two or three feet, then to about a foot. Yesterday, I did not feel any heat at all and was able to attach myself to him. He also said that during the initial interrogation, his soul followed him to the Ministry of Punishment, pointing to the door, which was the Guangxi Department. According to the date mentioned, the old case was indeed found. When asked about the body, he said it was near the riverbank, beside the third willow tree, and digging it up also found it, which was not yet decayed. His father was called to identify it, and he cried out in great sorrow, saying, "It is my son." Although the matter was fantastical and elusive, all the evidence was true. During the interrogation, when his name, Changming, was called, he suddenly seemed to wake up from a dream and spoke in Changming's voice. When the name Erge was called, he suddenly seemed to be drunk and spoke in Erge's voice. After several rounds of mutual debate, he finally confessed. His father and son chatted about family matters, and everything was clear, leaving no doubt about the case. The true situation was reported, and the judgment was made according to the law. On the day the order was issued, his soul was very happy. He had been making a living by selling sweet rice cakes and suddenly shouted "selling sweet rice cakes" loudly. His father cried, saying, "I haven't heard this in a long time, it sounds exactly like his voice when he was alive." He asked his son where he would go, and he replied, "I don't know, just go." From then on, whenever he was asked about Changming, he no longer spoke in Erge's voice.

In Nankai, Deputy Magistrate Zhang Shou Chang, who served as the Inspector of Kaifeng and Guizhou, was reviewing a legal case file one night. He muttered to himself in deep thought: "For someone who has committed suicide by cutting their own throat, the knife wound should indicate a serious injury but instead shows a minor one. Why is the wound appearing minor but the outcome is severe?" Suddenly, he heard a deep sigh behind him. He turned around but saw no one. He sighed and said, "How terrifying it is to handle legal cases! This case is fortunate not to have been mishandled, but how can one ensure that mistakes will not be made in the future?" He then took sick leave and returned home.
A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

My maternal grandmother recounted that after the death of Cao Huazhun, his family buried a jade belt from the previous dynasty as part of his funeral rites. Several years later, a white snake was always seen in front of the tomb. Later, the tomb was eroded by water, and the coffin rotted and decayed. On the day of reburial, all other precious items were still there, but the jade belt was gone. The snake's body had patterns resembling a belt, and it seemed to have transformed into the shape of a belt. Could it be that the fierce spirit of the jade belt had transformed into the snake, finding寄托 in the jade?

My great-grandfather, Mr. Zhang Xuefeng, was a man of high moral integrity. His study was meticulously arranged with writing tools and furniture, and his collection of books and historical texts was neatly organized. He always locked the door and only opened it when he was personally present. The garden in the courtyard was lush and shaded, with thick green moss covering the ground. The servants dared not step on the ground unless on a mission, for fear of violating the strict rules. One day, when my great-grandfather was away, my uncle, Jianting Gong, who was around eleven or twelve years old, secretly went to the courtyard to cool off under a tree. He heard what sounded like someone moving inside the room and suspected that my great-grandfather had already returned. Holding his breath, he peered through a crack in the window and saw a woman sitting on a bamboo chair, dressed up like a painting. Opposite the chair was a large mirror, about five feet high. The reflection in the mirror was a fox. Afraid and daring not to move, he peeked at what she was doing. Suddenly, the woman saw her own reflection and quickly stood up, circling the mirror and cursing it. The mirror became foggy like mist. After a long time, she returned to her seat, and the traces of her cursing on the mirror gradually disappeared. When she looked again, it was a beautiful woman. Afraid of being seen, he tiptoed back. Later, he whispered this story to his grandfather, the former Yao An. The former Yao An used to teach his grandsons about the chapter on self-cultivation in the *Great Learning*. He cited this incident and said: "A clear mirror is empty, so nothing can hide its reflection. But if it is obscured by demonic energy, it still loses its true form. How much more so with personal biases and preconceptions, which are already obstructed?" He continued: "Not only are personal emotions an obstacle, but even impartiality can be an obstacle. Righteous and virtuous people, when manipulated by villains, can be provoked to act against their principles. Their stubbornness can lead to a complete reversal of right and wrong. In the past, the officials under Bao Zheng's command acted as if they were playing with power, and prisoners who should have been flogged were not flogged. This is also a case of demonic energy obscuring the mirror. Therefore, to have a sincere heart and genuine intentions, one must first understand things and gain knowledge."

A flower-selling old woman said: In the capital city, there was a residence near an abandoned garden that used to be full of foxes. One beautiful woman, at night, climbed over a short wall and engaged in intimate relations with a neighbor's young man. Afraid that the affair would be exposed, she initially lied about her name. As their affection grew, she thought they would not abandon each other, and so she claimed to be a fox maiden from the garden. The young man was pleased with her beauty and did not doubt or reject her. After some time, suddenly from the woman's house, tiles were thrown and she shouted: "I have lived in the garden for a long time. Sometimes children play and throw bricks or stones, disturbing the neighbors, but there is no scandalous or seductive behavior. Why do you defame me?" The matter was then exposed. Strange indeed, foxes often disguise themselves as humans, but this woman disguised herself as a fox. Those who are skilled at seduction are compared to foxes, but this fox was chaste towards humans.

A wandering scholar supported himself through painting and calligraphy, and in the capital city, he took a concubine whom he loved very much. Whenever he attended banquets or gatherings, he would carry fruits and snacks to give to her, and they got along very well. Soon, he fell seriously ill and spoke to her, saying: "I have no family, and you have nowhere to go; I have no relatives, and you have no support. I make a living through writing and painting. After I die, you will have to marry another, it is the way of the world, and it is also the law of nature. I leave no debts to burden you, and you also have no parents or siblings to hinder you. You can act according to your own will and refuse even the smallest dowry. But promise to visit my grave once a year, and I will have no regrets." The concubine wept and accepted his words, and the man who took her as his concubine also kept his promise and loved her very much. However, the concubine always felt depressed and remembered the favors of her former husband. At night, she must dream of the former husband sharing the same bed, and in her sleep, she sometimes mumbles softly. Her husband noticed this and secretly invited a sorcerer to use talismans to suppress her dreams. The dream talk stopped, but her illness gradually worsened until she was on the brink of death. As she lay dying, she pressed her forehead against the pillow and said: "The feelings for the former husband are deep, truly cannot be forgotten. You know this well, and I do not hide it."

Last night, I had another dream: "I have been long separated from you, but now I can come back. Your illness is as it is, why don't we return together?" I had already agreed to it. If you can grant an exceptional favor, return my body to that grave, and I will in life after life, repay you with gratitude. This is an unreasonable request, please consider it. After speaking, he suddenly disappeared. The man was also a brave hero, and he said with a sigh: "The soul has already gone, why leave this shell behind?" Yang Yuegong could reunite the broken mirror of Lechang, can I not reunite it in the afterlife? He finally agreed. This was an event during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng in the years 1724 (Jia Yin) and 1725 (Yi Mao). I was around eleven or twelve years old at the time, and I heard it from others, but I forgot their names. I believe that remarriage betrays the former husband, and marrying with divided loyalty betrays the new husband. This woman has no clear stance. Mr. He Zishan also said: "To die out of longing, how is that different from dying in sacrifice?" Mr. He Lian'an said: "The *Spring and Autumn Annals* hold the wise accountable, one cannot judge women by the standards of gentlemen. One can pity their circumstances and sympathize with their intentions."

The Butcher Xu Fang, carrying two jars of wine, was traveling at night and became tired, resting under a large tree. The moon was as bright as day, and he heard a mournful sound from afar. A ghost emerged from a cluster of graves, with a terrifying appearance. He then hid behind a tree, holding his burden to defend himself. The ghost arrived at the jar, jumping and dancing with great joy, and immediately opened it to drink. After finishing one jar, it still wanted to open the second jar, but just as the seal was halfway opened, it already fell down. Xu was very angry and, seeing that it had no other skills, suddenly lifted his burden to strike it, as if hitting empty space. He then continued to strike it painfully, gradually relaxing and falling to the ground, transforming into a cluster of thick smoke. Fearing it would change form, he struck it over a hundred times. The smoke spread flat on the ground, gradually dispersing and spreading out, leaving a trace like light ink, like light grain, gradually more dispersed and thinner, until there was none left. It had already completely disappeared. I say that ghosts are the residual energy of humans. Energy gradually dissipates, which is why the *Zuo Zhuan* says that new ghosts are large and old ghosts are small.

In the world, there are those who claim to see ghosts, but none have heard of encountering ghosts from the era of Fuxi and Huangdi, for they have already vanished. Alcohol disperses the vital energy, so traditional Chinese medicine uses alcohol to treat conditions like promoting blood circulation, inducing sweating, relieving depression, and expelling cold. These ghosts, with their remaining vital energy, are dispersed by a full jar of alcohol, and the strong yang energy from the alcohol stirs up and vaporizes the weak yin energy, so their complete disappearance is natural. They vanish in intoxication, not from physical force. When hearing this story, one who abstains from alcohol said: "Ghosts are adept at illusion. Because of alcohol, they end up being beaten while sleeping. Ghosts are what people fear, but because of alcohol, they end up being trapped by people. Those who indulge should reflect on this." One who indulges in alcohol said: "Ghosts, though invisible, possess consciousness and still experience human emotions like joy, anger, sorrow, and pleasure. Now, in a deep, unconscious sleep, they vanish into nothingness, returning to their true nature. The true enjoyment of alcohol lies in this transformation. Buddhists regard nirvana as the ultimate state of bliss, but how can those who are constantly striving understand this? Is this not what Zhuangzi meant by 'this is one right, and that is another right'?" In Xian County, a cow gave birth to a unicorn, which was so shocking that it was killed. The magistrate Liu Zhenglian collected and buried it, and erected a stele inscribed with "Sighting of the Unicorn at the Altar." Liu was indeed a good official, so what is so humble about this action? The unicorn is a benevolent creature, but it is not of the ox species. A calf that grows a unicorn's horn is likely the result of a dragon's influence during a thunderstorm.

Dong Wenke, before he had passed the imperial examinations, stayed in an empty house. He often heard of strange phenomena, but he did not believe them. One night, he lit a lamp and waited. After the third watch, a cold wind suddenly blew, and the door and gate opened by themselves. A group of ghostly figures, resembling neither human nor ghost, entered in a disheveled manner. Seeing Dong, they were greatly startled and said, "This house has ghosts!" They all fled in a panic. Dong grabbed a stick and chased after them. The ghosts called out, "The ghosts are chasing us! Run quickly!" They scrambled over the wall and fled. Dong often spoke of this incident and laughed at himself, saying, "I don't know why they called me a ghost." The old city resident Jia Hanheng, who often studied classics under Dong, once mentioned the *Taiping Guangji* account of a wild horse wanting to eat the corpse of Consort Geshu Han. Geshu Han was sleeping nearby, and the wild horses whispered to each other, "A noble person is here, what shall we do?" Geshu Han thought to himself, "If they call me a noble person, hitting them should do no harm." So he got up and hit them, and the wild horses scattered. The word "ghost" sounds similar to "noble," so perhaps the ghosts called Dong a noble person, but he was unsure if he had heard correctly. Dong laughed and said, "Perhaps that is so." In the autumn of the Gengwu year, Dong bought a copy of *Piya* and found a folded green sheet of paper inside with a poem written on it: "Sorrowful smoke lingers low over the double red doors, a cold wind gently rattles the window of the jade maiden. Faint greenish light appears from ancient walls, soil patterns erode gold-colored spots. Insects scurry in the grass as dew falls, deep in the night a lotus stands quietly. A wet firefly flies over an empty pond, dim light reveals a withered flower weeping." The poem was titled "A Poem by the Beautiful Cloud Fairy Descending the Altar" and was recorded by Zhang Ning. It was written by someone who performed the divination ritual. I believe this poem was written by a ghost, not a fairy.

Mr. Zhang Xuan'er of Cangzhou composed a quatrain in a dream, which reads: "On the river, autumn tides crash against the shore; a solitary boat moors near midnight. A red-painted building is surrounded by willows, and somewhere, a flute is played under the bright moon." In his self-commentary, he wrote: "If dreams are not thoughts, how can they inspire poetry? If dreams are thoughts, how can one dream of a place they have never been to?" The reason is unclear, so he decided to record and preserve it. Mr. Yao Biefeng from Tongcheng, whom he had never met before, recently arrived from Jiangnan and met him at Li Ruidian's home. Among his recent works, this poem was found. When asked about the date, it was revealed that the poem was written more than a year after his dream. Upon opening the box and showing the old draft to him, both were greatly surprised. There are truly inexplicable things in the world; Song Dynasty Confucian scholars always spoke of reason, but where can this reason be found? Mr. Li Shu Liu, also known as Chengfang from Haiyang, was also from the Dingmao year. In my study, I have a painting of Tao Yuanming picking chrysanthemums, painted by Lan Tian Shu. Dong Qiujiang remarked, "How much he resembles Li Shu Liu," and upon careful examination, I found this to be true. Later, when Li Shu Liu entered the capital by public carriage, he asked to take this painting with him, saying that all the small portraits he had ever made did not compare to this. This matter is also inexplicable.

West of Jingcheng, there are several neglected graves that are about to be leveled. When I was a child and passed by them, the old servant Shi Xiang pointed and said: "These are the descendants of Zhou, who extended their lineage for three generations through one good deed." During the end of the Chongzhen period of the Ming dynasty, severe drought and locusts ravaged Henan and Shandong provinces, exhausting all grass roots and tree bark, leading to cannibalism. Officials could not stop it, and women and children were bound and sold in the market, called "vegetable people." Butchers bought them, treating them like discarded sheep and pigs. The ancestor of the Zhou family returned from a business trip in Dongchang and stopped at a shop for lunch. The butcher said: "The meat is all gone, please wait a moment." Suddenly, two girls were dragged into the kitchen, and he shouted: "The guest has been waiting a long time, you can fetch a hoof first." Zhou rushed out to stop it and heard a long cry. One girl had her right arm severed, twisting on the ground, while another was trembling with no color, seeing Zhou and crying out in distress. One begged for quick death, and the other begged for help. Zhou's heart was moved with compassion and he paid to redeem them. One had no chance of survival, so he quickly stabbed her heart to end her suffering; the other he took home, because he had no son, he took her as a concubine, and she eventually gave birth to a son. The son had a red thread on his right arm, from under his armpit around his shoulder blade, just like the girl who had her arm cut off. Later, the lineage lasted for three generations before ending. All say that Zhou originally had no son, and these three generations were extended by one good deed.

A young woman from Qing County, known for her flirtatious nature, followed her husband closely in all his activities, never leaving his side. They laughed and joked together without regard for others' opinions, and sometimes in the summer nights, they slept together in a melon field. People viewed her as promiscuous, but when it came to others, she was as cold as iron. If anyone tried to seduce her, she would firmly reject them. Later, she was attacked by bandits and suffered seven wounds, but she continued to curse them until she died, remaining chaste until the end. Everyone was amazed by her loyalty and chastity. Old scholar Liu Zuo said: "This is what is meant by someone who is naturally good but lacks education. Because she is so loyal to her husband, she would rather die than be unfaithful; because she lacks knowledge of proper etiquette, her desires influence her behavior and appearance, and her intimate affection is evident in her actions." Mr. Xin Tongfu said: "Zengzi once said that those who avoid suspicion are lacking in substance. This woman has no ulterior motives, so she walks her path with confidence and does not doubt herself. This is why she could remain loyal until death. I have seen those who set themselves apart, but they are not like her." Yao An Gong said: "Liu Zuo's statement is correct, and Mr. Xin's words are a passionate defense. Later, her husband was guarding his bean fields at night and was alone in the thatched hut when he suddenly saw his wife, who was as affectionate as ever. She said: 'The underworld officials have judged me to be virtuous and will place me in the next life as a county magistrate. I do not want to go, so I asked to be a wandering spirit so I can always be with you. The underworld officials pitied me and agreed.' The husband was moved to tears and vowed never to marry again. From then on, she would hide during the day and come at night, for nearly twenty years. Even children sometimes saw her. This was an event that occurred near the end of the Kangxi era, and Yao An Gong could name the person and the location, but I have forgotten them now."

A Learned Scholar from Xian County Mr. Han, a learned scholar from Xian County, was known for his upright and principled character. He always acted in accordance with propriety and was highly respected in his village, being considered its leading scholar. One day, he fell ill with a cold, and in a daze, a ghost appeared before him and said, "The City God has summoned you." Mr. Han thought that his life was coming to an end and that refusing would be futile, so he followed the ghost. They arrived at an official office, where the City God checked the records and said, "There was a mistake because of the same surname. I will punish this ghost with twenty strokes and send him back." Mr. Han felt unjustly treated and asked, "Human life is the most precious. How can you send such a foolish ghost, leading to a mistaken arrest? If the error is not discovered, will it not result in an unjust death? What does it mean to be wise and upright!" The City God smiled and said, "I thought you were stubborn, and indeed you are. Just as the movement of celestial bodies cannot be without precession, how can ghosts and spirits be exempt? To recognize an error immediately is to be wise; to recognize an error and not excuse oneself is to be upright. How could you understand these things? Considering your pure actions and words, I will temporarily forgive you. Do not act so impulsively and rashly in the future." Mr. Han suddenly awoke and recovered. This story is attributed to Han Zhangmei.

An Ancestor's Servant and a Modern Tale of Deception My ancestors once had a small servant named Dae Yue, who was around thirteen or fourteen years old. He would often accompany villagers to fish in the river, and one day, he caught a large fish, nearly two feet long. Just as he was holding it up to show everyone, the fish suddenly swung its tail and struck him on the left cheek, knocking him into the water. The crowd was surprised that he did not get up, so they tried to help him, but blood was seeping out. There was a broken bowl in the mud, with a sharp edge like a blade, which had pierced his temple. Previously, his mother had dreamed that this servant was tied up on a chopping block by someone, being butchered like a sheep or a pig, and seemed to have lingering resentment. Upon waking, she hated the dream and always warned him not to fight with people, but she did not expect that he would be struck by a fish. Was this event due to a past life debt, as Buddhism suggests? Liu Shao, the Lesser Grand Preceptor of Qing Yuan, recounted a story about a relative who had a suspicion involving Yuan Zhen's meeting with the truth. The woman was pregnant, and her mother discovered it. She fabricated a story, saying that a giant would come at night, pressing her body very heavily, and was dark and black in color. The mother said, "This must be a clay figure causing mischief." She gave her daughter colored silk, instructing her to secretly tie the giant's feet when he came. The daughter secretly gave the silk to the one she loved, tying it on General Zhou's feet at Guandi Temple. The mother searched and found it, beating his feet almost breaking them. Later, they had another secret meeting, and suddenly, they saw General Zhou strike his waist, and both the man and woman lay stiffly and could not get up. Everyone said, "This is a punishment for defaming the gods." Monopolizing the benefit and shifting the disaster onto others, the skill is clever, but the clever ones are hated by the creator. Countless machinations, in the end, return to affect oneself, this is the way of heaven. The gods hate their danger, not their defamation.

Luo Liangfeng of Yangzhou, who could see ghosts with his eyes, said: "Ghosts exist everywhere where there are people." The violent and malevolent spirits, those who died prematurely and have lingered for many years, are usually found in secluded rooms and empty houses. These are not to be approached, as they can cause harm. The restless spirits, however, are more active in the morning when the yang (positive) energy is strong, often found in the shadows of walls. In the afternoon, when the yin (negative) energy is stronger, they scatter and wander, able to pass through walls without using doors or windows. When they encounter people, they avoid them, fearing the yang energy of humans. These spirits are found everywhere but do not cause harm. He also said: "Ghosts tend to gather in densely populated areas, while in remote or open places, they are rarely seen. They enjoy gathering around kitchens and stoves, as if attracted by the smell of food. They also like to enter latrines, but the reason for this is unclear. Perhaps they prefer places where human activity is sparse?" Luo Liangfeng mentioned that he had painted a picture titled "Ghostly Scenes," which he suspected was more of an artistic creation than a factual representation. One of the ghosts in the painting had a head that was dozens of times larger than its body, making it particularly seem like a fantasy. However, he heard from his predecessor, Yao'an Gong, about Chen Gong of Yaojing, who once slept with the window open on a summer night. The window was a full zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length) wide, and suddenly a large face appeared, as wide as the window, peering through it. Its body was nowhere to be seen. Chen Gong quickly drew his sword and stabbed its left eye, and it disappeared immediately. An elderly servant who was also present saw the face emerge from the ground beneath the window. They dug a hole a zhang deep but found nothing. Chen Gong confirmed that such ghosts do exist. "Mysterious and obscure," Luo Liangfeng concluded, "how can I verify this?"

Servant Liu Si, in the summer of the year Rénchén, requested leave to return home for a visit, driving his own ox cart with his wife. When they were about thirty to forty miles from home, halfway through the night, the ox suddenly refused to move. His wife,惊呼 from within the cart, cried out, "There is a ghost! Its head is as large as a jar, standing in front of the ox." Liu Si looked closely and saw a short, dark woman wearing a broken chicken cage on her head, dancing and shouting, "Come, come!" Out of fear, he turned the cart around, but the ghost leaped again in front of the ox, shouting, "Come, come!" This continued, with the ghost circling them in all directions, until the rooster crowed. Suddenly, the ghost stood up and laughed, saying, "The night is cool and I have nothing to do; I’m just borrowing you two for entertainment. It was just a bit of fun. After I leave, be careful not to curse me, for if you do, I will return." The ghost continued, "This chicken cage belongs to a certain family in the previous village; please deliver it back to them." After speaking, the ghost threw the chicken cage onto the cart. At dawn, they arrived home, and both husband and wife were dazed, as if drunk. Soon after, the wife fell ill and died, and Liu Si also ended up in a state of destitution, abandoned by all. It seems the ghost had taken advantage of their weakened state.

Xian County Gazetteer also records this. According to the records, Liu Wuzhou was from Shanhou Maoyi, so his tomb should not be here. It is suspected to be the tomb of Liu Xuan of the Sui dynasty. Liu Xuan was from Jingcheng, and the One-Volume Gazetteer of China records his tomb in the east of Xian County, eighty li away. Jingcheng is eighty-seven li from the city, which roughly matches the distance. There was a fox inhabiting the tomb, which sometimes played tricks on drunk people. There was a man named Chen Shuang in the village, who was a drunkard. Hearing about this, he became furious and said, "How dare the beast do this!" He went to the tomb and began to scold and curse. At that time, there were many farmers working in the fields, and they all saw their father sitting angrily by the side of the tomb. Chen Shuang jumped around and shouted, "Why are you so drunk? Why are you cursing your father?" Chen Shuang looked closely and saw that it was indeed his father. He was greatly frightened and kowtowed, and his father walked directly back home. Chen Shuang followed him and begged, and he caught up with him outside the village. Only then did he fall to the ground and explain his situation. Suddenly, a group of women surrounded him and laughed, saying, "Why is Chen Shuang kneeling to his wife?" Chen Shuang looked up and saw that it was indeed his wife. He stood there in shock, and his wife also walked directly back home. Chen Shuang was in a daze when he returned home, but his father and wife had not actually left. He then realized that they were all illusions created by the fox. He was so ashamed that he did not leave his house for several days, and those who heard about it could not help but laugh. I say that if Chen Shuang had not scolded the fox, how could he have been tricked by the fox? Chen Shuang had his own reasons for being tricked. If the fox had not played tricks on people, how could it have been scolded by Chen Shuang? The fox also had its own reasons for being tricked. Everything is entangled and confused, all because of a moment of delusion. Therefore, the Buddha says that all beings should be cautious in their actions.

Fang Gui, son of an exile from Urumqi, once herded horses in the mountains. One day, a horse suddenly ran away. Following the trail, he went to look for it. Across the ridge, he heard a very loud neighing sound. Following the sound, he arrived at a secluded valley, where he saw several creatures resembling both humans and animals. Their entire bodies were covered with scales like ancient pines, and their hair was fluffy like a plume. Their eyes were bulging, pure white, like two eggs embedded. The creatures pressed the live horse and bit its flesh. The herders carried guns for self-protection. Fang Gui was stubborn and naughty, so he climbed a tree and fired his gun. The creatures all went into the deep forest. The horse had already been half-eaten. Afterward, he never saw them again, and he never knew what they were.

Rui Shuzi Tieya lived in a house where a fox resided in one of its upper rooms. The room was always locked. Sometimes at night, the fox would prepare food in the kitchen and entertain guests in the study; the family members were familiar with this and were not surprised. The fox could handle any situation involving thieves or fires, protecting the owner in all circumstances. They had coexisted peacefully for a long time. Later, Rui sold the house to Scholar Li Lianyi, who did not believe in superstitions or nonsense. He personally went to open and look at the room, and found three bays on the upper floor, which were extremely clean. In the center, there was a piece as large as a mat, laid with wooden boards, neat and resembling a table or bed. There was nothing else to see. At that time, the house was being rebuilt, and they decided to destroy the building as well, so that the fox would have nothing to rely on, and there were no other strange occurrences. Just after the house was recently completed, suddenly flames rose up, and in an instant, the house was completely destroyed. The neighboring houses with thatched roofs were not affected. Everyone said it was done by the fox. Scholar Liu Qingyuan said: This house should have been burned on that day. If it was not meant to be burned, how could the fox dare to set fire? I say that if demons could strictly follow laws, then there would be no thunderbolts from heaven to punish them. The law prohibits killing people, but many do not dare to kill, and those who do kill are sometimes punished. This is indeed uncertain. Wang Shaosikou Lanquan said: During the time when Meng Wutang was the supervisor of education in the southern region, behind his office, there was a high hill. Strange lights were always seen at night, and it was said that a pheasant and a snake lived on it, both having lived there for a long time and able to become spirits. Meng, being young and full of vigor, gathered shovels and baskets to level the hill. The crowd hesitated and did not lift their hands. Meng was just angry and supervised, when suddenly a piece of matting was blown by the wind and covered his head. He quickly removed it, and another piece covered him, both from the cool pavilions in the office. Meng felt something was unusual and stopped the work. It still stands today.

Old servant Wei Zhe heard his father recount a tale from the early Shunzhi period. There was a certain young man, about eighty to ninety li from our home, whose name has been forgotten. He and his wife died one after another. After three or four years, his concubine also passed away. One of the household's hired workers, traveling at night to avoid the rain, stayed under the eaves of the East Peak Temple. As if in a dream, he saw the young man wearing shackles standing before the courtyard, with his wife and concubine following. A deity dressed like the City God bowed and spoke to the Peak God: "This young man defiled two people, which is a crime; he saved two lives, which is also a merit. They should be offset." The Peak God angrily replied: "These two people were afraid of death and endured shame, so they can still be pardoned. The young man saved two people, but it was to defile them. Only punishment should be given; why speak of offsetting merit and crime?" He waved them out, and the young man and his wife and concubine also left. The worker trembled and dared not speak. At dawn, he returned home and told the family, but none could understand. An old servant wept and said: "Strange, is he recorded for this matter? This matter is known only to my father and me, because of deep gratitude, we vowed never to speak. Now separated by two dynasties, only now dare we recount. The two main wives were actually not women. In the previous dynasty's Tianqi period, Wei Zhongxian killed Yu Fei. All the palace maids and eunuchs under her were secretly captured and sent to the Eastern Depot, where they died very painfully."

There were two eunuchs, one named Fu Lai and the other Shuang Gui, who had fled and gone into hiding. Because they had once been acquainted with the host, and the host was then traveling in the capital, they sought refuge with him at night. The host led them to a secret room, and I peered through a crack in the wall. The host spoke to the two eunuchs, saying: "Your voices, laughter, and appearances are between those of men and women, slightly different from ordinary people. If you go out, you will surely be captured. If you change into women's clothes, you will be beyond recognition. However, two unmarried women lodging with a household will arouse suspicion and eventually lead to failure. Your bodies are already androgynous and originally not different from women. If you are willing to become my wife or concubine, you will have no chance of failure." The two eunuchs, having no other options, thought deeply for a long time and ultimately agreed. The host then provided them with women's clothing and pierced their ears, gradually allowing them to wear earrings. He also bought softening medicine and secretly bound their feet. After several months, they suddenly became two beautiful women. The host then took them back home in a cart, lying that they were married in the capital. Both Fu Lai and Shuang Gui were fair and gentle, with no trace of a man's appearance. The matter was far beyond expectations, and no one suspected them. They were only surprised that they did not know how to do needlework, which they attributed to their pride and laziness due to their favored status. The two eunuchs were deeply grateful for the host's life-saving kindness, and after the situation was resolved, they were content to live together for the rest of their lives. However, in reality, the host used clever words to entice and coerce them, not out of pity for their plight, but rather, they should be punished by the gods of fate. Indeed, people can be deceived, but ghosts and gods cannot be deceived!

In the year Qianlong Jǐmǎo (1789), I presided over the provincial examination in Shanxi, and two candidates passed. One was ranked forty-eighth, but when filling out the preliminary list, the co-examiner Wànquán Lǚ Lìng Bāng accidentally placed the candidate's papers in his clothes, and they could not be found afterward; the other was ranked fifty-third, and during the filling of the preliminary list, the mysterious wind extinguished the candles three or four times, forcing the replacement of his papers. After the results were announced and the sealed papers were inspected, it was discovered that the candidate whose papers were lost was Fàn Xuéfū, and the candidate affected by the mysterious wind was Lǐ Téngjiāo. I strongly suspected that these two candidates had been punished by the spiritual realm. However, in the following year's Qínghuáng Gēngchén (1790) provincial examination, both candidates passed. Fàn still maintained his rank of forty-eighth, and Lǐ passed the imperial examination in Xīnchǒu (1801). Thus, I realized that success in the imperial examinations is determined by fate, and it cannot be achieved even if one tries hard the previous year. What are those who are busy with trivial matters doing? Even if one seeks and obtains success, it must be what is destined, and even if one does not seek it, one will still attain it.

The late Mr. Yao’an recounted that during the imperial examination of the Gengshen year in the Yongzheng era, he was assigned the same examination room as Mr. Tang, a scholar from Xiongxian. One night, Mr. Tang suddenly saw a woman ghost with disheveled hair, who pulled the curtain and with her hands tore his scroll, the paper fluttering like a swarm of swallowtail butterflies. Though Mr. Tang was naturally upright and resolute, he was not frightened. He sat calmly and asked her, “I know nothing of my past life, but in this life I have truly not harmed anyone. Why have you come to me?” The ghost, startled, stepped back and replied, “Are you not number forty-seven?” Mr. Tang replied, “I am number forty-nine.” The ghost had failed to count two empty rooms. After gazing at him for a long time, she bowed in apology and left. Soon afterward, the person in room forty-seven cried out that someone named Jia had suddenly fallen ill. This ghost was clearly confused, and Mr. Tang may be said to have suffered an unmerited misfortune. Fortunately, he had a clear conscience, so in that moment of urgency he dared to question her, and only one scroll was torn. Otherwise, the consequences could have been disastrous. Mr. Gu Demao, the magistrate, once told me himself that he was an official of the Underworld, though I did not fully believe him. Yet his words carried some reason. Once, while staying at the home of Master Qiu Wenda, he told me: “The underworld highly values chaste women, but there are different levels of virtue. Those who are attached to their children or their property and thus do not leave the world are the lowest. Those who cannot entirely escape the stirrings of desire but are able to restrain themselves through propriety and righteousness are of the next level. But those whose hearts are as still as a dry well, with no ripples of emotion—those who do not see wealth and honor, do not know hunger and cold, and do not calculate gain and loss—are the highest. Such people are rare, one in a thousand. When one such woman is found, even ghosts and spirits will show reverence. One day, it was widely announced that a chaste widow had arrived. The King of the Underworld changed his expression, and all the spirit officials stood up, raising their robes in anticipation. They saw an old woman come forward in a lifeless, puppet-like manner, her steps gradually rising as though climbing a staircase.”

Upon his arrival, he finally passed over the roof ridge, and no one knew where he went. The King of Hell looked regretfully and said, "This has already ascended to heaven and is no longer in my register of the dead." He then said, "Excellent officials are also divided into three ranks: those who fear the law are the lowest rank, those who value reputation and integrity are the second rank, and those devoted to the royal house, who only concern themselves with the state's finances and people's welfare, unaware of good or bad fortune and praise or blame, are the highest rank." He further said, "The underworld dislikes restlessness and competition, considering various evil deeds arising from this, therefore, it often hinders them, making it not worth the effort. The more cunning human hearts become, the more ingenious the workings of ghosts and gods become. However, it does not highly value reclusion, believing that heaven and earth produce talents originally intended to benefit worldly affairs. If everyone were like Chao and Xu, then floods would still be raging, and there would be no place to hang a gourd or drink milk from a calf, nor would it be possible." He then said, "The laws of the underworld are like the *Spring and Autumn Annals* in holding the virtuous accountable and encouraging good deeds. Scholars who are overly rigid can harm matters and are also recorded as faults; a petty person who benefits others in one matter will also be rewarded with a small good deed." The world does not understand this meaning, therefore, many doubt that cause and effect may be wrong.

Grand Secretary Yong Ning, whose courtesy name was Ning, fell gravely ill and was quite debilitated. He invited a doctor for diagnosis and treatment, but the illness did not quickly improve. He then changed to invite another doctor and asked for the prescription from the previous doctor, but could not find it. Yong Ning thought the maid had misplaced it elsewhere and ordered her to search, threatening to beat her if she couldn’t find it. As he leaned on his pillow to rest, a ghostly figure suddenly appeared kneeling by the lamp and said, "Do not beat the maid; this prescription was kept by me. I am the prisoner whom you, as an Inspector, acquitted and saved." When asked why he had kept the prescription, the ghost replied, "Among doctors, there is mutual jealousy. They must change the previous doctor’s prescription to show their own expertise. The medicine you are taking is correct; it’s just that the first dose hasn’t fully taken effect yet. If the next doctor sees the prescription, they will surely oppose it to establish their own method, and then you would be in danger. That’s why I secretly took it." Yong Ning was in a state of confusion and did not think it was a ghost. After a while, he realized the truth, and sweat broke out in fear. He then claimed the previous prescription was lost and could no longer be remembered, and asked the next doctor to prescribe something new. When they saw the medicine, it was still the same as the previous doctor’s. After taking several doses, the illness suddenly disappeared. When Yong Ning was stationed in Urumqi, he personally told me about this incident and said, "This ghost truly understands human affairs."

My cousin, Master Bi'an, said that in Suching there was a private teacher who taught the doctrines of Cheng Hao and Zhu Xi. One day, a wandering monk came to beg for food outside the study hall, and the sound of his wooden fish was loud and continuous from morning until noon. The teacher grew tired of him and went out to scold him, saying, "You are a heretic, and foolish people might be deceived by you. Here are all the disciples of sages; why do you have such delusions!" The monk bowed and replied, "Monks who beg for food and clothing are just like scholars who seek wealth and status. Both have lost their original nature. Why must you make things difficult for me?" The teacher became angry and hit himself with a stick. The monk stood up, saying, "Too much mischief," and left, leaving a cloth bag on the ground. The teacher thought he would return, but he did not come back that evening. When they checked the bag, it was filled with loose coins. The students wanted to take them, but the teacher said, "Wait until he doesn't come back for a long time before we decide what to do. We must count them clearly to avoid disputes." As soon as they opened the bag, swarms of bees surged forth, stinging the teacher and students, causing their faces to swell. They shouted and tried to save themselves. The neighbors were all surprised and asked what had happened. The monk suddenly burst into the room and said, "Are the sages plotting to hide someone's money?" He picked up the bag and left. As he was leaving, he clasped his hands and said to the teacher, "A heretical person occasionally offended the sages; please forgive me." The spectators laughed. Some said it was magic, while others said the teacher was against Buddhism and always criticized the monk. The monk deliberately put bees in the bag to play a trick. Master Bi'an said, "I witnessed this with my own eyes. If there were many bees in the bag beforehand, there would have been signs of movement visible outside the bag. At that time, I did not see anything. Those who said it was magic were closer to the truth."

Zhu Qinglei said that there was a person who fled to a deep mountain to avoid enemies. One night, when the moon was bright and the wind was clear, he saw a ghost lingering under a white poplar tree. He crouched down in fear and dared not stand up. The ghost suddenly saw him and asked, "Why don't you come out?" Trembling, he replied, "I am afraid of you." The ghost said, "The most terrifying thing is a person; what do I have to fear? What caused your misfortune—was it people or ghosts? It is the same either way." I believe this is Zhu Qinglei's allegorical story. There was a huge python in the treasury of the Censorate, and it sometimes came out at night. When I served as the Chief Censor, I saw it twice. The footprint of the python in the dust was about two inches wide, and estimating its body, it should be about five inches in diameter. The wall had no cracks, the door had no gaps, and the window frames were less than two inches wide. It is unknown how it entered and exited. Generally speaking, things that exist for a long time can change their form. Fox spirits can come and go through window gaps, and their original form cannot fit through those gaps. The officials in the hall said that its appearance foretells good or bad luck, but it has never been verified. It is just a superstition.

The realms of the living and the dead are distinct, and for matters that humans can handle, the gods and spirits do not need to intervene further, as this shows respect. However, when the realms of the living and the dead are governed by the same principle, and humans cannot manage certain matters, the gods and spirits may step in to handle them on their behalf, demonstrating their mysterious and unpredictable nature. According to Go Taifu Xianzhou, there was a servant who once fell asleep drunk on the altar of the city god. The city god took him away and flogged him twenty times, leaving blue marks all over his thighs. Go Taifu witnessed this with his own eyes. In the village of Dusheng, eighteen li from my home, there was a man who, greedy for the wealth of a rich household, sold his adopted daughter-in-law as a concubine. Although she had not yet married, she had lived with her husband for several years and morally should not remarry. Considering the matter unchangeable, they secretly agreed to escape together. Her in-laws discovered and pursued them, and the two of them arrived at night at the village's earth deity shrine in my village, with nowhere to rest. They embraced and wept. Suddenly, a voice from inside the shrine said: "Those pursuing you are about to arrive. You can hide under the god's altar." Soon after, the temple attendant stumbled home drunk, lying down outside the door. The in-laws arrived and asked about their whereabouts. The temple attendant mumbled in his drunken state: "Are they a young man and woman? How old are they? What do they wear? Which road did they take?" The in-laws hurriedly followed the direction he pointed, and the two of them thus escaped. They begged for food at the adopted daughter-in-law's parents' house, and her parents wanted to take the matter to court, so she was not sold. At that time, there was no one in the shrine. The temple attendant said: "I did not know about this matter at first, nor do I remember saying those words. It must all be the power of the earth deity."

During the year 1760, a fire in Beijing's Yangmeizhu Xie Street destroyed nearly a hundred buildings. Among the ruins, a dilapidated house stood alone, unscathed. Its walls, neatly aligned like a boundary line, were the only remnants. Inside, a widowed daughter-in-law remained by her sick mother-in-law's side, demonstrating the utmost filial piety and fraternal love, which can reach the divine. The Yu family was an old and respected clan from Sucheng. During the reign of Wei Zhongxian, who held power through underhanded means, he regarded princes, marquises, generals, and prime ministers as mere dirt. However, because he grew up in Sucheng, he was influenced by the Yu family, looking at them with the same reverence as the Wang and Xie families from the Jin dynasty. He arranged a marriage for his nephew, insisting on marrying a Yu family girl. Just as the youngest son of the Yu family was taking the provincial examination, Wei Zhongxian hosted a banquet and forcibly invited him home to discuss the matter. Yu thought: if he agreed, the disaster would come later; if he refused, the disaster would come immediately. He could not make a quick decision and said that it was difficult to act without his father's permission. Wei Zhongxian replied: "This is easy. You should quickly perform the ceremony, and I can immediately bring your father here." That night, Yu's father dreamed of his deceased father, who supervised him as usual and gave him two questions: one was "Confucius said yes," and the other was "return to purity." While he was thinking, there was a sudden knock at the door, and he woke up, receiving a letter from his son, suddenly realizing the truth. He then wrote back agreeing to the marriage and added that his illness was severe, urging his son to return quickly.

Sùnìng is four hundred miles from the capital. By the time the messenger returned, the sky had just begun to brighten, and the play had not yet ended. Yú Shēng hastily packed his belongings, and by the time he reached the road, officials who had come to greet him were already lined up with accommodations prepared. Upon arriving home, both father and son claimed illness and did not go out. That year was the first year of the Tianqi era (Jiazi). Three years later, Zhongxian fell from power, and Yú Shēng was finally spared from calamity. After the affair was settled, Yú Wēng sat in a small carriage and roamed the outskirts, saying: "For three years I secluded myself, only to gain this day of admiring flowers and drinking wine." How perilous it was! When Yú Shēng was about to depart, Zhongxian presented him with a small portrait, saying: "Let her first wife recognize my face." The Yú family is related to mine by marriage; when I was still a child, I saw this painting. His appearance was tall and vigorous, yet refined and slender. His complexion was pale, with a faint reddish hue. His cheekbones were slightly visible, his cheeks slightly narrow. His eyes were as if drunk. Above the area beneath his eyes, a light reddish tint, like a slight swelling, was visible. He wore a crimson robe, and on the table beside him, nine golden seals were displayed in plain sight.

A Taoist priest at the Earth God Temple in Dulin Town dreamed that the Earth God spoke, saying: "This place is bustling and busy, and I have failed to protect it. As a result, the pestilence spirits mistakenly entered the homes of filial sons and chaste wives, injuring the children. Now I have been demoted and removed. The new Earth God is strict; you should treat him well, for he might not be as lenient as I was." The priest thought the spring dream was unreliable and did not take it seriously. A few days later, he was drunk and sleeping beside the deity's seat and caught a cold illness, nearly dying. In the garden of Governor Guo Tong of Jingzhou, during his time as an official in Shuopu, one night a retainer woke up and saw a bright moon filling the window. He saw a woman sitting next to his table and was greatly frightened, calling his servant. The woman waved her hand and said, "I have lived here for a long time, but you have not seen me. Now I have not been able to avoid you, why are you so terrified?" The retainer called out more urgently, and the woman smiled and said, "If I really wanted to harm you, could the servant save you?" She then quickly got up, like a gentle breeze shaking the window paper, and disappeared through the window.

The Confucian scholar Wu Mingjing from Yingzhou said that his fellow townsman, the respected and honest scholar Lin Sheng, was a straightforward and honest person. Lin Sheng used to read in the temple, which was spacious, and many people rented rooms there. Lin Sheng was solitary and aloof, and they never interacted. One day, at midnight, unable to sleep, he took a walk under the moonlight, and suddenly a guest came to exchange greetings and small talk. Lin Sheng, feeling lonely, invited the guest into the room to talk, and the conversation was very meaningful. They happened to discuss matters of cause and effect. Lin Sheng said: "The sages and virtuous people do good deeds without any ulterior motive. If they do good deeds with an ulterior motive, even if the actions are right, their hearts are not pure, and it is purely human desire. Therefore, the Buddhist concept of merit-making, the gentlemen do not follow." The guest said: "Your words are purely the words of a Confucian scholar. However, using these principles to discipline oneself is acceptable, but using them to discipline others is not; using them to discipline gentlemen is still acceptable, but using them to discipline all people in the world is absolutely not. The teachings of the sages aim to make people do good deeds. For those who cannot do good, they are guided and encouraged to achieve it; for those who are unwilling to do good, they are driven and urged, and thus the system of punishment and reward arises. Those who do good because they seek rewards, the sages only acknowledge their good deeds, but do not blame them for seeking rewards. Those who do good because they fear punishment, the sages also acknowledge their good deeds, but do not blame them for avoiding punishment."

If one uses punishment and reward to make people follow the natural order, and yet holds them accountable for desiring rewards and fearing punishments as human desires, it is said that not being motivated by punishment and reward is unvirtuous; being motivated by punishment and reward, yet again, is also said to be unvirtuous, leaving people at a loss as to what to do. Moreover, desiring rewards and avoiding punishments, which are already considered human desires, and then being motivated by punishment and reward, would lead people to believe that the sages actually guide the people through human desires. Is there any reason for this? It is because there are few highly intelligent people in the world and many ordinary people. Therefore, the sages' use of punishment and reward is to teach the average and below-average people; the Buddhist concept of cause and effect also serves the same purpose for the average and below-average people. Although the foundational teachings of Confucianism and Buddhism differ, when it comes to guiding people to do good, their intentions align. The master clings to Dong Zhongshu's theory of seeking profit and calculating merit to refute the Buddhist concept of cause and effect, intending to use the sages' punishment and reward system to refute it as well? The master only sees Buddhist monks enticing people to give alms, calling it doing good and receiving blessings; sees ordinary people observing the fast and burning incense, calling it doing good and receiving blessings. Those who do not do so are called not doing good and will surely be punished, thus concluding that the Buddhist concept of cause and effect is merely misleading the masses, without realizing that the Buddhist notions of good and evil are no different from those of Confucianism. The retribution for good and evil is also no different from that of Confucianism. Lin Sheng does not agree with this, and still wishes to further express his own views. As the sun was about to rise, the guest got up to leave, but was firmly persuaded to stay, only to suddenly remain motionless, turning out to be a clay statue of a judge in the temple.

My clan-uncle, the Master of Leiyang, said that once there was someone who encountered officials of the underworld and asked if fate is all predestined. They replied, "Yes." However, fate is specifically about the numbers of poverty, prosperity, longevity, and shortness of life, similar to the premonitions of food supplies described in Tang novels, which are the divination methods of the occultists. If everyone were to record these trivial matters, even if the whole earth were a shelf, it could not hold these records. He then asked if the fixed numbers could be changed. They replied, "Yes, great virtue can change it, great wickedness can change it." He asked who determines and changes it. They replied, "The person themselves determines and changes it; the spirits have no power." He asked why there are instances of retribution and non-retribution. They replied, "In this world, good and evil are judged in one lifetime, and so are blessings and misfortunes; in the underworld, good and evil span past lives, and so do blessings and misfortunes, hence sometimes it seems inconsistent." He asked why the rewards differ. They replied, "Each according to their innate nature." Using the analogy of human affairs, if a Shangshu (minister) is promoted one rank, they become the Prime Minister; if a Dianshi (local official) is promoted one rank, they are still just a Zhubu (lower official). If there is a grade increase, it cancels out the demotion; if there is no increase, the demotion is permanent. Thus, the same action can lead to different outcomes.
