He asked why people are not made aware of this beforehand. They replied, "It is not possible. If people knew in advance, human affairs would cease; Zhuge Liang would be seen as overly busy, and the six Tang ministers as fated." He asked why people are sometimes made aware of it. They replied, "If not shown occasionally, people would rely on the absence of spirits and act without restraint, doing whatever is obscure and hard to know." The former Yaoan Master once recounted this, saying, "This may be what Master Leiyang discussed, attributed to officials of the underworld, but judging by reason, it is likely no more than this."

The late Yao An Gong had a servant who appeared cautious and honest but was very cunning. One day, taking advantage of the master's urgent need, he flattered and extorted several dozen gold pieces. His wife was also resentful and self-righteous, seeming inviolable, but secretly had a lover. She had long wanted to escape with him, troubled by lack of funds. Once she obtained the money, she stole it and fled with him. They were captured after more than ten days, and both the husband and wife's adultery was exposed. My brothers were very pleased by this. Yao An Gong said: "How did such a coincidence lead to this outcome! There must be supernatural forces at play." He then asked, "Is such supernatural intervention merely for the sake of human satisfaction?" He continued, "It is meant as a warning." Therefore, when encountering such situations, one should be vigilant rather than joyful. Jia and Yi were friends. Jia lived in Xia Kou, and Yi lived in Bo Zhen, 30 miles apart. Yi's wife visited Jia's home, and Jia got her drunk and kept her overnight. Yi knew but could not speak up and instead thanked Jia. Jia's wife, after her boat capsized while crossing a river, was carried by the swift current to Yi's doorstep, where she was rescued by someone Yi recognized. He took her back and also got her drunk, keeping her overnight. Jia knew but could not speak up and also thanked Yi. Their neighbor's wife secretly knew about this and joined her hands in prayer, reciting Buddhist chants: "Is this so? I know I should be afraid." Yi's son was involved in a false lawsuit and was called back urgently. He advised, "You should be like this neighbor's wife."

During the evening, a man named Mao Gong Zhenyao, who was acting as the deputy governor of Hejian, recounted a story. He said that one of his fellow villagers was walking in the mountains late in the evening when he sought shelter from the rain in an abandoned temple. Upon entering, he saw that there was already a man sitting under the eaves. Upon closer inspection, he realized that it was his deceased uncle. Startled, he wanted to avoid the apparition, but his uncle quickly stopped him and said, "I have something to tell you, so I am here. I will not harm you, so do not be afraid. After my death, your aunt has been beaten unjustly by your grandmother, enduring without complaint but harboring resentment and hatred. She secretly curses and slanders her in private places. I serve as a guard in the underworld and have seen the earth gods report this multiple times. Entrust you with a message, warn her to repent and reform. If she does not repent, she will not escape being cast into hell." After speaking, he disappeared. The villager returned home and told his aunt, who, although she denied it, was visibly shaken and looked uncomfortable, confirming the truth of the ghost's words.

毛公又言，有人夜行，遇一人状似里胥，锁絷一囚，坐树下。因并坐暂息。囚啜泣不已，里胥鞭之，此人意不忍，从旁劝止。里胥曰：此桀黠之魁，生平所播弄倾轧者，不啻数百。冥司判七世受豕身，吾押之往生也。吾何悯焉。此人悚然而起，二鬼亦一时灭迹。 阅微草堂笔记卷三滦阳消夏录三 俞提督金鳌言，尝夜行辟展戈壁中——戈壁者，碎沙乱石不生水草之地，即瀚海也。遥见一物，似人非人，其高几一丈，追之甚急，弯弧中其胸，踣而复起，再射之始仆。就视，乃一大蝎虎，竟能人立而行。异哉。

During the time of the Changji rebellion, the rebels were captured and executed in the western woods of Dihua City—Dihua is the old name for Urumqi, now established as a prefecture. The woods stretch for dozens of miles and are locally known as "tree nests." This was the eighth month of the year 1888 (Wu Zi). Later, dark, ghostly clouds appeared in the woods, moving swiftly and causing those who traveled at night to become lost. I believe these were the spirits of the rebellious and wicked, gathered to form evil apparitions, much like the lingering poison left behind by dead snakes and lizards. It is not surprising that they caused such disturbances. Generally, evil and malevolent spirits dissipate when confronted with righteous and strong energy. Several troops were sent to ambush and shoot the apparitions at night, and they dispersed and vanished upon being shot. The Guandi Temple in Urumqi had a horse, donated by a merchant to serve as an offering to the deity. The horse grazed in the mountains and forests and did not return to its stable. Every time during the new moon and full moon when the deity was worshipped, it stood outside the temple gate before dawn, as if carved from clay. It always stood in the same spot without deviation. Even during the short month, it arrived on time. After the worship, it disappeared, and no one knew where it went. I believe the Taoist priest led it to the temple gate, and the deity is credited with this action. In the first day of the second month of the year 1890 (Geng Yin), I arrived at the temple slightly early and actually saw the horse slowly walk from the snowy desert and stand quietly at the temple gate with its ears down. There were no human tracks in the snow, and this is also strange.

Huai Town is located 55 miles east of Xian County, which is also known as Huaijia Town in the Jin History. There was a family named Ma who suddenly experienced strange occurrences. At night, they would either have tiles and stones thrown at them, hear ghostly moaning sounds, or see sudden flames appear in deserted places. These disturbances continued for more than a year without stopping, and prayers and rituals also had no effect. They then bought a new house and moved. Subsequent tenants who rented the house experienced the same disturbances and moved away soon after. Because of this, no one dared to rent the house again. An elderly scholar, who did not believe in these things, bought the house for a low price and chose a day to move in. Finally, there was complete silence and nothing else, and it was thought that his virtue could overcome the evil spirits. Later, a cunning thief came to the door and started quarreling. It was then discovered that the strange occurrences in the house were all caused by the old scholar bribing the thief to do it at night, and it was not a real ghost or demon. The late Yao An Gong said: Ghosts are merely illusions. The old scholar’s actions were as deceptive as those of a ghost, so one could call him a real ghost. In the seventh month of the year Jihou, Yao An Gong met a monk at Yuanjia Kou. The monk clasped his hands and greeted him, saying: We have been apart for 73 years. I hope we can meet again soon. The inn happened to be selling only vegetarian food, so they had a meal together. When asked about his age, the monk took out a document from his bag, which was issued in the second year of Chenghua during the Ming Dynasty. When asked how many generations of monks had been passed down, the monk quickly put the document back in his bag and said: You doubt me, so there’s no need to say more. Before the meal was finished, the monk left, and the truth about him remained uncertain. Yao An Gong once mentioned this to Yun as a warning: Scholars and officials are often curious, and they are often deceived by such people. Even if the person is a true immortal or Buddha, I would rather miss them than be deceived.

At my family's artificial mountain, there is a small pavilion where a fox has lived for over fifty years. People do not go up, and the fox does not come down. But one can sometimes see the windows and doors open and close by themselves. To the north of the pavilion is a place called Lüyi轩 (Green Intention Pavilion), with old trees providing dense shade, making it a good place for cooling off in summer. In the seventh month of the year Wuchen, suddenly one night, the sounds of a zither and chess were heard. The servant ran to tell Yao An Gong, who knew it was the fox's doing and did not mind at all. He just told the servant, "It's better than your drinking and gambling." The next day, he told me, "If a guest of the sea has no intention, then the white heron can be approached." They have been living peacefully for a long time, so it's best to ignore what you can't see or hear. And until now, there has been no other unusual event. In the spring of Dinghai, I brought my family to the capital because our old house near Tiger Bridge had not been redeemed, and we temporarily stayed at Mr. Qian Xiangshu's vacant house. There is also a fox living in the cloud pavilion, but the place is locked and filled with miscellaneous items, and people do not go up lightly. I playfully stuck a poem on the wall saying: "Hastily moving my family, I met you by chance; let's share the upper and lower floors equally. Being obsessed with poetry is a scholar's habit; even if you sing all night, no one will listen." One day, the lady servant opened the lock to get something and suddenly cried out in surprise. When I went to see, on the dust on the floor, there were drawings of lotus flowers, with stems and leaves, all having brushwork. So I placed paper and ink on the table and stuck another poem on the wall saying: "Immortals indeed love this pavilion; their literary talent and charm surpass mine. I have just received thirty sheets of Wu paper; can you draw lotus flowers on each one?" After several days, when I checked, he had not taken up the brush. I told Qu Wen Da Gong, and he laughed and said, "The fox at Mr. Qian Xiangshu's house should be somewhat refined."

A man from Hejian named Feng Shunan was barely able to handle writing, wandering aimlessly in the capital for over ten years. Whenever he encountered opportunities, he achieved no success. When seeking help from others, they usually promised but did not deliver. Poor, sorrowful, and depressed, he prayed for a dream at the Temple of the Immortal Lu. In his dream, a person told him, "You should not blame others for their coldness; this is the result of your own actions. In your past life, you enjoyed using empty words to gain the reputation of a wise person. When encountering good deeds, you knew you could not accomplish them but still encouraged others repeatedly, making people feel your support. When encountering bad people, you knew they could not be forgiven but still repeatedly defended them, making people feel your rescue. Although you neither harm nor benefit others, the kindness is attributed to you, and the resentment is directed at others. Your cunning has gone too far. If the matter slightly involves you, you retreat as quickly as possible, watching others suffer without lifting a finger, even though it would be easy to help. Can this heart still be questioned? Therefore, thinking about it, people may seem close to you on the surface but distant in heart, caring outwardly but ignoring you inwardly, which is appropriate or inappropriate? The spirits hold people accountable; minor mistakes can still be atoned for through good deeds, but if the fault lies in one's intentions, it is not tolerated by the laws of the underworld. This life is over; strive to improve in the future." In the end, he died of cold and hunger.

Mr. Shi Songtao, whose given name was Mao, was from Huazhou and reached the position of Grand Master of the Imperial Academy. He was a close friend of my late Yao'an public. When I was around 14 or 15 years old, I remember him talking to my late Yao'an public about an incident: a certain official once beat a servant to death. Later, a simple-minded maid approached him and argued, saying, "The servant was guilty and deserved to die, but the master's killing of the servant was unjust. The master's high rank and generous salary were not due to the servant's loyalty? The master's selling of official positions and accumulation of vast wealth were not due to the servant's bribes? The master's confusion of right and wrong, deciding life and death, were not due to the servant's abuse of power? How can the master be guilty of betraying the country, yet blame the servant for betraying the master?" The master continued to complain and protest. Later, the official did not have a good end. My late Yao'an public sighed and said, "We definitely will not do such things, but we advance and retreat together with others, living off our salaries without doing much work, and often blame the servants for not doing their jobs. Are we not secretly disapproving of our own behavior?"

Li from Shucheng, who traded dates between neighboring counties, secretly enticed the young wife of his host to return with him. When he arrived home, he found that his wife had already fled with someone else. He was relieved to say, "Fortunately, I brought this woman with me; otherwise, I would be a widower." People had calculated the time when his wife would leave with someone else, which coincided with the day this woman had climbed the wall to escape. He was informed of this but still did not realize it? Later, this woman became unhappy living in a rural household and followed a young man to flee, leaving him lost and confused. Afterward, his husband tracked him to Shucheng and wanted to sue Li. Li claimed that the woman had gone elsewhere and there was no evidence, so he firmly denied it. In the midst of the dispute, they heard that there was a person who could communicate with spirits through a ritual. The crowd said, "Why not ask the spirit for judgment?" The spirit judged with a poem saying: "The dream of the mandarin ducks was good, both were happy. Do you remember that Luo Fu already had a husband? Today, we meet and need a smile. Clearly, it's just copying the old pattern." His husband silently returned. There were people in the neighboring counties who knew about the matter, saying that this woman was also originally lured by her husband.

Madam Man, the nurse of my younger brother, had a daughter named Lizhi, who married into a nearby village household. One day, upon hearing that her mother was ill, Lizhi did not wait for her husband to accompany her, but rushed here in a disheveled state. It was already night, and the crescent moon faintly glowed. Looking back, she saw someone chasing her urgently, and she judged it to be an act of sexual assault. Being in the open field, there was no one to call for help, so she hid herself behind an ancient tomb and a white poplar tree, placing her hairpins and earrings in her bosom, untying her sash and tying it around her neck, loosening her hair, sticking out her tongue, and staring fixedly ahead in preparation. As the man approached, she suddenly invited him to sit. Upon a close look, he realized it was a hanged ghost, and he was shocked, collapsing and unable to rise. Lizhi, however, ran wildly and escaped. When she finally entered the house, the whole family was greatly frightened. After calmly inquiring, they learned the truth, and were both angry and amused, then began to plan to question the neighbors. The next day, it was widely spread that a young man from a certain household had encountered a ghost and been afflicted by a malevolent spirit, and that the ghost was still following him, causing him to go mad and speak incoherently. Later, medicine, herbs, and talismans were all ineffective, and he became epileptic for life. This may have been due to the aftermath of terror, when demons seized the opportunity to strike—though this is uncertain; or it may have been all hallucinations created by the mind—though this is uncertain; or it may have been that a righteous god punished evil, and the ghost took his soul—though this is also uncertain. Nevertheless, all of these possibilities can serve as a warning against madness.

Governor Tang, holding the jade seal, once examined a murder case, and the trial was ready for judgment. One night, holding a candle and sitting alone, he suddenly heard faint weeping, as if it was approaching the window. He ordered a young maid to look, and she fell unconscious. The governor himself opened the curtain, and there was a ghost covered in blood kneeling on the steps, loudly rebuking him. The ghost bowed its head and said, "The one who killed me is someone, but the county magistrate wrongly accused someone else. My revenge will not be avenged, and I cannot rest in peace." The governor replied, "I know." The ghost then left. The next day, the governor personally questioned the case, and the witnesses described the victim's clothing and shoes, which matched what the ghost had said. The governor's confidence grew stronger, and he ultimately changed the verdict according to the ghost's words. The county magistrate defended himself in many ways, but the governor finally said, "Even if the Southern Mountain were to move, this case would not be overturned." His advisor suspected there might be another reason, and gently inquired about it. The governor then fully explained the beginning and end of the matter, but there was nothing more to be done. One night, the advisor asked to see him and said, "Where did the ghost come from?" The governor replied, "It came to the steps." The advisor asked, "Where did the ghost go?" The governor replied, "It suddenly leaped over the wall." The advisor said, "All ghosts have form but no substance; they should disappear suddenly, not leap over the wall. Therefore, I searched the place where it leaped over the wall. Although the tiles were cracked, after the recent rain, there were faint traces of mud on the roof, leading to the outer wall. I pointed this out to the governor and said, "This must be the work of a prisoner who bribed a thief." The governor thought deeply and realized it, and still followed the original verdict. He concealed the matter and did not pursue it further.

To the south of Jingcheng, there was a ruined temple with no one living around it. Inside, there was only one monk accompanied by two disciples who managed the temple's incense and fire. They were all dull and stupid like country bumpkins, unable to show proper respect to others. However, they were extremely deceitful. They secretly bought pine resin, refined it into powder, and at night, rolled paper into scrolls, lit them on fire, and scattered them in the air. The flames shot out in all directions, and when people saw this and asked about it, the monk and his disciples would lock the door and sleep soundly, all saying they didn't know anything about it. They also secretly bought theatrical costumes of Buddha, making them into the shapes of Bodhisattvas and Arhats. At moonlit nights, they might stand on the roof ridge or hide behind the trees at the temple gate. When people saw this and asked, they would also claim they had not seen anything. When people reported what they saw, the monk would clap his hands and say, "Buddha is in the Western Paradise. What business does Buddha have in this ruined temple?" The authorities were just banning the White Lotus Society. We have no grudge against you. Why would you make such a statement to harm me? People increasingly believed it was a manifestation of Buddha, and daily offerings increased. However, the temple gradually fell into disrepair, and they refused to repair even a single tile or beam. They would say, "People in this region enjoy spreading rumors. Whenever they talk about this, there are many strange and supernatural occurrences. If we were to make it more grand, those who deceive the public would have even more excuses." After accumulating for over ten years, they gradually became wealthy. Suddenly, thieves watched their house, and the monk and his disciples were beaten to death. The thieves took all their wealth and left. The authorities inspected the remaining bags and chests and found items like pine resin and theatrical costumes, finally realizing their deceit. This was an event from the end of the Chongzhen era of the Ming dynasty. My great-grandfather, Master Houzai, said, "This monk used not being deceptive as a form of deception. It was also very clever. However, the deception they gained ultimately led to their own destruction. Even calling it extremely foolish would be acceptable."

A scholar favored a young male lover, and they loved each other like husband and wife. When the child fell ill and was near death, he showed countless forms of sorrowful attachment. Even after his breath had ceased, he still held the scholar's wrist, and it took breaking his hand to let go. Afterward, the scholar saw him in dreams, under the lamp and moonlight, and even during the day. The ghost always stayed about seven or eight feet away, not speaking when asked, not coming forward when called, and retreating when approached. Because of this, the scholar became mentally distressed, and using talismans and exorcisms proved ineffective. His father suggested seeking refuge in a temple, hoping that evil spirits would not enter the Buddha's realm. When the scholar arrived, the ghost appeared as before. One old monk said: "All these demonic obstacles arise from the mind. Is it truly this child? Is it something your mind has summoned rather than this child? Is it a mere illusion of your mind? If you can empty your mind, everything will vanish." Another old monk said: "The master is using high-level teachings for someone of lesser capacity; he lacks the strength of will, how can his mind be emptied? It's like saying only the symptoms of the illness without prescribing the medicine." He then told the scholar: "Evil thoughts entangle like weeds taking root; it's like an object stuck in a hole; use a wedge to push it out, and when the hole is filled, the object will naturally come out. You should contemplate what happens after this child dies: his body gradually becomes stiff and cold, then bloated, then stinks, then decays, then maggots begin to move, and finally his internal organs shatter."

The flesh and blood were in disarray, taking on various colors. Its face gradually changed in appearance, in color, and in form, resembling a demon like a rakshasa, and a sense of terror arose. Further, if this child were to grow up, day by day it would become increasingly robust and grand, losing its charming demeanor, gradually developing facial hair, eventually having a long beard like a halberd, turning its face pale and dull, its hair turning gray, its temples as white as snow, becoming bald and toothless, hunching over and suffering from chronic cough, covered in mucus and other bodily fluids, making it unclean and repulsive, and a sense of disgust would arise. Further, if this child were to die first, then I would think of it, and if I were to die first, its beautiful appearance would surely attract someone, who would use temptation and threats to lead it away, and it might not remain chaste like a virgin, and once led away to the bed, all the lewd words and lewd behaviors I had directed at it while alive would be turned towards this person, and they would indulge in pleasure.

In the past, all kinds of close affections faded away like clouds dissipating, leaving no residue, and then feelings of resentment and anger arose. If one thinks again that the child is still there, or they might act arrogantly due to favoritism, causing me distress; or if we accidentally cause offense, leading to criticism; or if my wealth is insufficient to satisfy their demands, suddenly developing a different heart, becoming cold and indifferent in demeanor; or if they see wealth and status, abandoning me and going elsewhere, meeting me like strangers on the road, then feelings of resentment and hatred arise. With these thoughts rising and falling, arising and ceasing within the heart, then the mind has no spare time. When the mind has no spare time, all roots of love and desire have nowhere to take root, and all obstacles and hindrances naturally dissipate. This phenomenon arises from what was taught, sometimes seen and sometimes not for several days, and after several more days, it finally disappears. When the illness subsides and I go to visit, then there are no such two monks in the temple. Some say that an ancient Buddha appeared in transformation, others say that they are from the ten directions and remain permanently, coming and going like clouds, meeting by chance like floating algae on water, having already moved on to another place with their staff.

The wet nurse of the deceased grandmother, Liao, recounted that in the area of Ma Luo Po in Cangzhou, there was a woman who made a living by selling noodles. She used leftover noodles to feed her mother-in-law. Being poor, she could not afford to keep a donkey, so she would constantly grind flour herself, grinding until the fourth drumbeat each night. After her mother-in-law passed away, when she returned from visiting her grave, she met two young girls on the road. They greeted her with a smile and said, "We have lived together for over twenty years. Do you recognize us?" The woman was startled and did not know what to say. The two girls said, "Do not be surprised, Sister-in-law. We are foxes. We are moved by your filial piety and have helped you grind flour every night. We did not expect to be praised by the Heavenly Deity. Due to this merit, we have achieved enlightenment. Now that your duty to care for your mother-in-law is complete, we sisters have also ascended to immortality and are leaving. We respectfully come to bid farewell and thank you for your assistance." After they finished speaking, they left as swiftly as the wind, and in an instant, they were gone. When the woman returned home and tried to turn the mill again, she could hardly manage it; it was no longer as effortless as before. Urumqi, which means "beautiful pasture" in Mongolian, was named during the Qing dynasty, more than two decades before the pacification of the Western Regions. There was a man in this place whose name was Urumqi. He recounted that at birth, his father dreamed that his grandfather said, "The child you will bear should be named Urumqi," and pointed out the characters to show. He woke up without understanding what was said, but the dream was very vivid, so he named the child Urumqi. He did not expect that the child would indeed come to this place, and wondered if he would end his life here. Later, he was transferred to the position of head of the seal room and indeed died in office. Throughout his service, he never left this place. All things are predestined, is it not true?

It is said in Urumqi that there was a servant named Bara. During the campaign, whenever he encountered the enemy, he fought bravely. Later, an arrow pierced his left cheek, and the arrowhead emerged from behind his right ear. He still fought with a sword, killing another enemy, and both fell together. Later, because of some matter, he went to the house of Gulu, which is located between Urumqi and Tengger. In a dream, Bara paid a visit, dressed neatly and properly, and did not look like a low-ranking servant. In the dream, he forgot that Bara was already dead. He asked where Bara had been and where he was going. Bara replied, "Because I was on an errand here, I happened to meet the owner, just to express my long-held affection." He asked why Bara had received a position, and Bara said, "Loyalty, filial piety, integrity, and righteousness are what God values. Those who sacrifice their lives for the country, even if they are low-ranking servants, will be given a position in the afterlife if they had no serious sins in life. Those who had committed serious sins in life will also have their past crimes forgiven and be reborn as humans. I am now a general under the God of Bogeda Mountain, with the rank of a cavalry officer." He asked where Bara was going, and Bara said, "Changji." He asked what the mission was, and Bara said, "I carry official documents, but I don't know what they are about." He woke up suddenly, and the sound of Bara's voice seemed to still be in his ear. It was the month of June in the year Wuzi. By August 16th, there was a rebellion in Changji, and it seems that the spirits were not allowed to reveal this in advance.

When the city of Changji was being constructed, they dug the soil to more than five feet deep and found a red, finely embroidered women's shoe. The shoe was exquisitely crafted and had not completely decayed. In my "Miscellaneous Poems of Urumqi," I wrote: "Digging the soil to great depths, the rhythmic sounds of labor fill the air. A strange event causes everyone to look at it in unison, a crescent moon partially obscured by flowers." This poem describes the discovery of the shoe. The soil was dug to more than five feet deep, which means that the shoe must have been buried for at least several decades. Yet, it remained largely intact. The Uighur women do not practice foot binding, so why would their shoes be shaped like a bow, only about three inches long? There must be a reason for this, but it is unknown now.

Guo Liu, a farmer's wife from Huai Town, was unsure whether her husband's or father's surname was Guo. It is said that she was called Guo Liu. During the years of 1724 and 1725 in the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, a great famine struck, and her husband, foreseeing that he would not survive, left to beg for food everywhere. On the verge of departure, he bowed deeply before her and said, "Both my parents are old and ill; I leave this burden to you." The woman was naturally beautiful, and the young men in the village, seeing that she lacked food, tempted her with money, but she refused them all. She supported her in-laws only through women's work, but eventually, she could no longer sustain them. She then gathered the neighbors and knelt before them, saying, "My husband has entrusted his parents to me, but now my strength is exhausted. If I do not make other plans, we will all die. If the neighbors can help me, I ask for their assistance; if they cannot, then I will sell myself like a flower, so do not laugh at me—among the villagers, it is said that women who lean on doors are selling flowers." The neighbors hesitated and mumbled, slowly dispersing. She then wept bitterly and told her in-laws, openly associating with various young men, secretly accumulating funds for illicit relations, and keeping another woman. Yet, she guarded herself very strictly, not letting outsiders see her face. Some said she would seek a high price, but she did not argue. After more than three years, her husband returned.

The period of mourning had just concluded, and she was immediately introduced to her in-laws. They said, "Our parents are still alive, and now we return you." She then led the woman she had arranged for her husband to marry, saying to him, "I have already been defiled and cannot bear the shame of facing you again. I have already arranged for you to marry another woman, and I am now giving her to you." Her husband was shocked and did not immediately respond, so she said, "Let me prepare a meal for you first," and then went to the kitchen and took her own life. The magistrate came to examine the situation and found her eyes wide open and unblinking. The magistrate ordered her to be buried in the ancestral tomb but not in her husband's tomb, saying, "Not being buried in her husband's tomb indicates a severance from him. Being buried in the ancestral tomb shows that she is not severed from her in-laws." Her eyes remained wide open, and her in-laws lamented, saying, "She was originally a chaste woman, and we brought her to this state. As children, we cannot support our parents, and instead, we sever the bond with those who have taken care of our parents? Moreover, as men, we cannot support our parents and instead leave a young woman to fend for herself. The common people know our hearts. Whose fault is it that we have severed this bond? This is our family matter, and the magistrate does not need to be involved." After speaking, her eyes closed. At the time, the people of the county had various opinions, and my grandfather, the Yongfu, said, "Chastity and filial piety are both important. Chastity and filial piety cannot both be fully upheld. This matter can only be decided by a sage, and I dare not offer any opinion."

When the official known as the "御史" was executed according to the law, one of the judges fell into a doze during the day and, in a daze, saw the spirit of the deceased. Startled, he asked, "Do you have any grievances?" The spirit replied, "I, as a '言官,' accepted bribes and sold official documents, which according to the law merits my execution. What grievance could I possibly have?" The judge asked, "If you have no grievances, why have you come to see me?" The spirit answered, "I have grievances against you." The judge said, "There are seven or eight judges in this case, and among them, there are two or three who were old acquaintances of mine. Why do you hold a grudge against me alone?" The spirit explained, "I and you have had a longstanding conflict, nothing more than competition in our pursuits. We are not enemies who cannot share the same sky. When I was on trial, you avoided involvement due to a conflict of interest, but you appeared to have a smug and self-righteous expression; when my verdict was handed down, you offered insincere words of comfort, yet there was a subtle hint of disdain. It was others who condemned me to death according to the law, but you added to my suffering by harboring resentment and hastening my death. In times of hardship, this is the most hurtful thing, so how could I not feel resentful?" The judge, filled with fear and shame, apologized, "Then, are you going to seek revenge against me?" The spirit replied, "I died according to the law; how could I seek revenge against you? Your heart is like this, and it is not a path that will bring you blessings. I do not need to seek revenge against you, but I have an injustice to express, so that you may know it." After speaking, the spirit seemed to be both asleep and awake, and when he opened his eyes, the spirit had vanished. The tea on the table was still slightly warm. Later, a close friend of the judge saw him in a state of bewilderment, and in private, inquired about it. The judge then recounted the entire story, sighing deeply and saying, "I am fortunate that I did not add insult to injury; his resentment was still so great." The friend remarked, "Indeed, Confucius said, 'Grieve and show compassion, do not rejoice.' Is this not true?" When the friend told this story to others, he also sighed deeply, saying, "Even with a slight bias, if one is guilty of a crime, they will not accept it; how much more so if one is not guilty?"

程 Bianxiu of Fish Gate said: How great is the harm of hatred to people! Song Xiaoyan, on the verge of death, sent a note to his friend saying: "White bones can turn to dust, but the wandering soul will not disperse. At the Mirror of Karma in the underworld, I await your arrival to meet." I personally witnessed this. His friend, also on the brink of death, patted the bed with his hand and said: "Song Gong, sit down." I also personally witnessed this.

It is said that an envoy returned from a mission and stayed at the official residence. At the time, the courtyard chrysanthemums were in full bloom, and he lingered under the flowers, seeing a young boy hidden among sparse bamboo. The boy was about 14 or 15 years old, elegant and refined, like a well-dressed girl. He asked to find out that the boy was the son of the host, called him over to talk, and found him very clever and quick-witted. The envoy took a fan and gave it to him, and the boy glanced back and forth with longing, seeming to agree. The envoy also admired his cleverness and grace, spoke softly and lingered with him. Just when the attendants were not around, the boy knelt and tugged at the envoy's robe, saying, "If you do not despise me, I will not dare to deceive you. My father is in prison for a false charge, and if you say one word, my father can be saved. If you are willing to help, I will not hesitate to sacrifice myself." Just as he reached into his sleeve to take out the legal documents, suddenly a strong wind blew, and all six windows were blown open, almost exposing the boy to the gaze of the attendants. The envoy realized something was amiss, hurriedly waved him away, and said, "Wait until evening to discuss further." Immediately, he hurriedly ordered the carriage to depart. Later, it was discovered that it was a case involving a wealthy local who had killed someone, and the situation could not be resolved quickly. The officials bribed the envoy to bring him to their home, secretly bought a young male lover, pretended it was his son, and also bribed the attendants to get close using a plan similar to Qin's weak grass, not anticipating the manifestation of the spirit of the wronged. Qiu Wenda once said, "This envoy was occasionally too involved, almost fell into their trap." Scholars and officials must be careful with their words and actions. If at that time he had been as stern as Bao Zheng, there would have been no opportunity for them to take advantage.

In the late years of the Ming dynasty during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, in Meng Village, there was a large band of robbers engaged in plundering. They saw a woman who was beautiful and also captured her parents, binding them. The woman refused to be defiled, so they bound her parents and subjected them to the torture of burning. The parents cried out in extreme pain, and they ordered the woman to follow the bandits. The woman requested that her parents be released, and only then would she comply. The bandits knew she was deceiving them and would force her to be defiled before releasing her. The woman then fiercely struck the bandit's cheek and died with her parents, leaving their bodies in the wilderness. Later, the bandits fought against the soldiers, and the horses approached the bodies, shying away and refusing to advance. Thus, they became trapped in the mud and were captured. The woman also had a spirit. Regretfully, her name and family cannot be verified. Those who discuss this matter say that a woman in her family should obey her parents' commands. Her parents commanded her to follow the bandits, to establish her own reputation, and watch her parents suffer. The woman seemed overly cruel. Some say that fate has its ups and downs, and following the bandits is not comparable to being betrothed. Her parents commanded her to become a prostitute; should she also become a prostitute? The woman seems to be innocent. The late Yao An Gong said: this matter is the opposite of what Guo Liu Zheng did. Both have arguments that can be made, but in their hearts, they could not firmly believe. Not eating the liver of a horse is not a sign of not knowing the taste.

Liu Yu, whose name is unknown, was from Cangzhou. He was often engaged in literary exchanges with my great-grandfather, the Venerable Zhai, who was known as "Thick-Zhai." Liu Yu was solitary and reclusive, fond of discussing ancient systems, but in reality, these ideas were impractical and could not be implemented. Once, he commissioned Dong Tian Shi to paint a picture and asked my great-grandfather to inscribe a poem on it. One of the paintings depicted a man sitting alone at the base of an autumn tree, with no one to keep him company. The poem read: "Sitting alone at the root of the autumn tree, isolated with no one to keep company, not knowing what book he is reading, only seeing ancient beards and mustaches. Only worrying about what he is holding, perhaps it is a diagram of the well-field system." This was a criticism of him. Later, he accidentally obtained an ancient military treatise and studied it for years, believing he could lead ten thousand soldiers. At that time, there happened to be a bandit group, and he trained local militia to fight against them. The entire team was defeated, and he almost captured by them. He then obtained an ancient hydraulic engineering book and studied it for years, believing he could turn a thousand miles of land into fertile fields. He drew diagrams and presented his ideas to the local official, who was also interested and ordered him to test his plan in a village. The irrigation channels were just completed when a large amount of water came, overwhelming his efforts.

Water flowed through the channel and into the area, nearly turning people into fish. From this, he became depressed and dissatisfied, often walking alone in the courtyard, shaking his head and muttering to himself: "Could the ancients have deceived me?" Thus, every day he repeated these six words a thousand or a hundred times. Soon after, he fell ill and died. Afterwards, on clear nights with the wind and moon bright, his spirit was often seen walking alone beneath the cypress trees before his tomb, shaking his head, and reciting the same six words. If someone laughed at him, he would vanish. The next day, if one waited for him, he would appear again. Those who cling rigidly to the past are foolish—how foolish can one be to reach this point? Lord Wen Qin once taught Yun: "Being filled with books can be harmful; even having no books at all can also be harmful." The national master of Go does not discard old strategies, yet does not cling rigidly to them; the national physician does not blindly adhere to ancient prescriptions, yet does not abandon them entirely. Therefore it is said: "To make the divine clear and manifest depends on the person." It is also said: "One can teach people the rules, but cannot make them skillful."

The evil of Wei Zhongxian during the Ming dynasty is unprecedented in history. Some say he knew his downfall was certain and secretly kept a mule that could travel seven hundred miles a day to prepare for fleeing; he also secretly kept someone who looked like him to prepare for his death. Later, in Fucheng's Youjiadou, he indeed used this to secretly flee. I believe this is groundless talk. Considering the way of heaven, if divine principles are not false, Wei Zhongxian could not avoid punishment; considering human affairs, Wei Zhongxian governed for seven years, who does not know him? If he hid in the homes of his old party members, friends among the unworthy, when power fails, they separate, and they would simply bind and surrender him; if he hid in remote and desolate places, among those who farm and herd, suddenly an eunuch appears with strange words and appearance, shocking the eyes and ears, within three days, he would surely fail; if he fled far beyond the borders, Yan Shifan once communicated with Japan, and Chou Luan once associated with the Altan Khan, but Wei Zhongxian did not do so, mountains and seas are deep and obstructing, and customs barriers are isolated, where could he go from there?

In the past, there was speculation about Emperor Jianwen's escape, and later generations remained uncertain. However, Emperor Jianwen's loss of virtue was not widely known, and the people's hearts had not turned away. Former officials and elderly scholars still harbored thoughts of their former ruler. Prince Yan raised arms and usurped the throne, slaughtering loyal officials, and was not supported by the people. Successively, they hid in disguise, which might be reasonable to assume. The cruel tyranny of Wei Zhongxian was as high as the sky, and his evil spread throughout the world. Everyone wanted to see him punished. At that time, there were still fifteen years until the fall of the Ming dynasty. How could he have remained hidden for fifteen years? Therefore, the claim of his private escape, I do not believe. Prince Yue Fang of Wen'an said: At the beginning of the Qianlong reign, in the county school, there was a sudden thunderstorm that struck the altar, swirling around the Confucian temple, with lightning flashes shooting out like a red snake, entering and exiting the main hall more than ten times. The school supervisor Wang Zhuo stood up and said: There must be something unusual. He braved the rain to take a look and saw a large centipede on the altar of the revered teacher. He pulled it out and threw it in front of the steps. A thunderclap sounded, the centipede died, and the sky cleared. Upon inspection, there were red characters 'Wei Zhongxian' written on its back. This story, I believe it.

In the deep mountains of Urumqi, herders often sight small beings about a foot in height, with men, women, the elderly, and children all present in full. When the red willow flowers bloom, they gather willow branches, twist them into small rings, and place them atop their heads. They dance in groups, their sounds soft and melodic, like a song being performed. Sometimes they come to the herders’ tents to steal food; if caught, they kneel and weep. If bound, they refuse to eat and die; if released, they at first dare not move quickly, and after advancing a few feet, they look back. If chased and scolded, they still kneel and weep. Once they are somewhat distant, believing they cannot be pursued, they suddenly leap across ravines and over mountains to escape. Yet their nests or places of dwelling remain forever untraceable. These beings are neither forest spirits nor mountain beasts, but rather belong to the *Jiao Jiao* race. Not knowing their name, because they resemble children and like to wear red willow rings, they are called *Hong Liu Wa*. County Magistrate Qiu Tianjin, during his inspection of the pastures, once captured one of them and preserved it for return. Upon close examination of its eyebrows, hair, and beard, they were no different from those of humans. This confirmed the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*’s mention of the *Jing Ren*, which is indeed real. Where there is the extremely small, there must also be the extremely large; the *Texts of Lie Yukou*’s reference to the Kingdom of Long Bo is also clearly real.

Outside the frontier, there grows the snow lotus, in the high mountains covered with snow, its appearance resembling today's chrysanthemum, named as a lotus. It always grows in pairs, the male being slightly larger, and the female smaller. However, they do not grow together, nor do they share the same root, always maintaining a distance of about ten feet. If you see one, look for the other, and you will always find it. Like *tu si* and *fu ling*, they are transformed by a single essence, with their energies connected. Whenever you see this flower, approach it silently, and you will find it. If you point it out, it will retreat into the snow, leaving no trace. Even if you search through the snow, you will not find it. Plants have consciousness, and the principle behind this is inexplicable. The locals say that the mountain god cherishes it, perhaps this is true? This flower grows in the most frigid places, yet its nature is extremely hot. The two forces have a bias, but not an extreme. The external accumulation of cold leads to the internal formation of pure yang. The *Kan* trigram has a single yang line surrounded by two yin lines, and the *Bo* and *Fu* trigrams have a single yang line between five yin lines, representing its nature.

Using alcohol as a tonic can lead to excessive heat in the blood and erratic behavior, and when combined with love potions, the disaster is especially severe. The balance of yin and yang in nature allows all things to thrive; similarly, the balance of yin and yang in the human body ensures the harmony of all meridians. Therefore, the *Huangdi Neijing* states: "Excess leads to harm, while moderation brings control." Since Zhu Danxi proposed the theory that yang tends to be excessive and yin tends to be deficient, doctors have lost their original purpose and often use bitter and cold medicines to weaken vital energy. Zhang Jiebin and his followers corrected excesses but went too far in the opposite direction, thus becoming overly focused on tonifying yang. The use of herbs like ginseng, astragalus, cinnamon, and aconite also led to negative consequences that could result in death. They did not understand the *Yi Jing*’s support for yang, nor did they heed the warning in the topmost line of the *Qian* hexagram against the dragon in excess. Desires and cravings increase daily, and many are weak and frail. Tonic remedies easily show minor effects, and those who believe in them become numerous. Therefore, I say that those who excessively deplete yang are like Han Fei’s legalist school, and those who excessively tonify yang are like Shang Yang’s methods for strengthening the state. At first, they all seem effective, but once the habit is formed, it cannot be reversed. The damage to the root is the same. The efficacy of snow lotus does not cure the root problem, which is also this principle.

In the "Sequel to the Holy Teaching of the Three Baskets" commissioned by Emperor Taizong of Tang, it describes a realm of wind disasters and ghostly hardships, which seems to refer to the region around Turpan. Lone travelers in the desert often hear their names called out; if they respond, they follow and never return. There is a wind tunnel in the southern mountains, as large as a well, from which the wind does not come out at regular intervals. Each time it does, the wind can be heard from tens of miles away, with the sound of waves preceding it by one or two moments before the wind arrives. The road it crosses is no more than three or four miles wide, and it can be quickly avoided by running. If one cannot avoid it, many vehicles are tied together with large ropes into a single unit, yet they still experience strong vibrations and rocking like a boat in a surging river. If a single vehicle encounters it, people, horses, and baggage all become as light as a leaf, floating away, and no one knows where they go. The wind always blows from south to north, and after several days, it blows from north to south, like the to-and-fro of breathing.

I was in Urumqi when I received a report from Pizhan, which stated that a military officer named Lei Ting, on a certain day, was carried by the wind across the northern mountains, leaving no trace. Additionally, the magistrate of Changji reported that on a certain day at noon, a person fell from the sky, who turned out to be the prisoner Xu Ji from Tengnu'er, carried by the wind. Shortly after, the county magistrate of Tengnu'er reported that Xu Ji had escaped on that day, and according to the time, he flew and traveled over two hundred li from the fourth hour to noon. Such an event is not unusual in that place, but it would be a strange occurrence elsewhere. Xu Ji said that when he was carried by the wind, he felt as if he were drunk or dreaming, his body spinning like a wheel, his eyes unable to open, his ears filled with the sound of a thousand drums, and his nose and mouth blocked by something, making it impossible to breathe. He struggled for a long time before he could finally take a breath. According to the *Zhuangzi*, the wind is called the "great block's sigh," and it can go anywhere, without a specific path. It is the temporary concentration of air that causes such unusual events. Just as the fire gas occasionally gathers in Bashu and becomes a fire well, or the water vein occasionally gathers in Khotan and becomes the source of a river.

Mr. He Lian'an recounted that during the late Ming dynasty, there was a scholar who was walking alone through dense wilderness when he heard the clear sound of books being read. Surprised that such a thing could exist in the wilderness, he searched for the source and found an old man sitting in a desolate graveyard, with more than ten foxes around him, each holding a book and squatting. The old man stood up to welcome him, and the foxes all held their books and stood up. The scholar thought that since they understood reading, they would certainly not cause harm. He then bowed and gestured to invite them to sit on the ground. He asked what the purpose of reading was, and the old man replied: "We are all those who cultivate immortality." There are two paths for foxes seeking immortality. One path involves gathering vital energy, worshipping stars, gradually becoming spiritual and changing form, and then accumulating good deeds to achieve immortality, which is the path from demon to immortality. However, if one strays into evil, it violates heavenly laws, making this path quick but dangerous. The other path involves first transforming one's form to become human, and once human, then practicing internal alchemy, which is the path from human to immortality. Although breathing exercises and guiding techniques are not accomplished in a day or two, with persistent effort, they naturally lead to perfection. This path is slower but safer. However, the form does not change by itself; it changes with the heart. Therefore, one should first read the books of sages and virtuous people, understand the principles of the three bonds and five constants, and when the heart changes, the form will also change. The scholar asked to see the books, which were all Five Classics, Analects, Book of Filial Piety, and Mencius. But there were only the texts of the classics without annotations.

Why not explain the classics without interpretation, how can one teach or discuss them? An old man replied: "We read books merely to understand principles. The words of sages and virtuous people are inherently not difficult to understand. Through oral transmission and textual clarification, one can grasp their meaning. Why bother with annotations?" The young scholar found his views eccentric and could not respond. He then asked about the old man's age, to which he replied: "I do not remember. But I do recall that when I studied the classics, there were no printed books in the world." The scholar further inquired if there were any differences in worldly affairs across several dynasties. The old man said: "They are not very different. Before the Tang dynasty, there were only Confucian scholars. After the Northern Song dynasty, I often heard that someone or another was a sage, which was a slight difference." The scholar could not fathom his meaning and bowed to take his leave. Later, while traveling, he encountered the old man again and tried to speak with him, but the old man turned his head and walked away. This story is likely a parable of the master. The master once said: "Using the study of classics to pursue official examinations leads to superficial and fragmented interpretations, where the words become more beautiful but the classics more barren; using the study of classics to establish sectarian positions results in excessive and contradictory arguments, where the explanations become more detailed but the classics more barren. The meaning is as precise as a seal. He also said: "All clever methods must have some inherent instability. If one takes steady and grounded steps, even if there are minor mistakes, one will not suffer serious setbacks. This is the same principle as the two paths of cultivating immortality."

A Diviner Who Performs Spirit Writing There was a diviner who came from southern Jiangnan. The spirit that appeared during the divination session called itself "Recluse of the Sleeping Tiger Mountain." It did not speak of good or bad omens but only engaged in poetic exchanges with people and could also paint. Its paintings were simple, featuring just a few strokes of bamboo and orchids, but they were complete in themselves. Its poetry was clear and simple, yet not vulgar. Once, after a divination session, it composed a poem: Love the brilliant red flowers reflected in the water, A white heron pauses and lingers for a moment, The flower spirit finds my green collar strange, I rest on moss and lichen, sleeping soundly. It also composed a poem about boats and carts: A shallow stream flows gently, about two feet deep, How delightful is the journey of a light boat to and fro, Turning back, the spring mud on the shore is slippery, It worries me to see the tired ox pulling a slow, clumsy cart, A small cart with creaking wheels is pulled by a black ox, Carrying wine, I leisurely walk through the fields, Do not envy the wealthy with their golden reins, The two wheels turn smoothly, steady as a boat. Most of its other poems were similar in style. When asked for its name, it replied: "Why should a person from the outside world leave a name?" If pressed, it would create a name on the spot.

甲 and 乙 studied the talisman together, and when they summoned it, it came. However, many of the characters were illegible, as the person fanning the divining board was not skilled. One day, 乙 burned the talisman, but the spirit did not descend. After several days, when they summoned it again, it still did not come. Later, the spirit descended at 甲's home. 甲 asked 乙 why it had not descended before, and the spirit judged: "Human life is fundamentally based on filial piety and fraternal love. If one is ashamed of these two virtues, they cannot be considered a human. This person recently divided property with his brother and concealed a thousand gold pieces. He also falsely claimed that his father had a long-standing debt that should be shared by the brothers, in reality keeping his brother's payment for himself. I, though a recluse, do not interfere in worldly affairs, but I cannot associate with such people. Please convey my message and do not disturb me again." The spirit also instructed 甲: "You recently obtained new fruits and favored your children while forgetting the orphaned and the poor, causing them to weep all night. Although it was unintentional, it stemmed from a discriminatory mindset. If you do this again, I will not come." The former Governor of Yaoan said: "I saw his poems and believed they were written by a spirit; considering his arguments, it seems he is truly a spirit."

The Governor-General of Guangxi, Tian Gong Gengye, married Lady Meng, who passed away early. When he was serving as the governor of Liangzhou, one moonlit night, he sat alone in his office, and in a dream, Lady Meng descended gracefully from a tree branch. They exchanged the usual greetings and concerns as if they were still together in life. She said, "I was originally a heavenly maiden, destined by fate to be your wife, and now I return because my karmic connection is complete. Now we meet again, it is because our karmic connection is not yet finished." He asked her about his future career, and she replied, "Your career will not stop at this point; you will advance further." When he inquired about his lifespan, she said, "This is difficult to predict, but you will not die in familiar places, nor in your official residence, nor on the road, nor in a battlefield. When the time comes, you will know." He then asked if they would meet again after death, and she responded, "That depends on you. If you strive to reach the heavens, you will see me, otherwise, you cannot." Later, when he led a campaign against rebellious Miao tribes and returned, he died on the battlefield. Hui Zao, a servant of Tian Gong, was known for his flirtatious nature and fondness for spying on women. One day, outside the village, he encountered a young girl whom he recognized but did not know her name or residence. He tried to talk to her, but she only responded with a glance and then walked away. As he continued to watch, the girl looked back as if inviting him, and he followed her. As he approached, she whispered, "There are many people around, I am afraid we might be seen." She instructed him to wait a little distance away until she arrived home and would meet him outside her eastern house near a tied-up ox and a stone roller. As she moved further away, and as evening approached, she reached Li's depression, twenty miles from her home. The weather cleared after rain, with mud up to the knees and swollen, painful feet. Seeing the girl enter her eastern house, he secretly pleased and rushed towards it.

A woman stood with her back turned, but when she suddenly turned her face, it transformed into a demon-like appearance, with sharp teeth and curved claws, a face as dark as indigo, and dull, lifeless eyes like a dim light. Startled, she ran away. The demon quickly pursued her, and she ran madly for more than twenty miles. She reached Xiangguo Village, and it was already the beginning of the hour of the pig (around 9 PM). Recognizing the door of her father-in-law's house, she knocked frantically without stopping. As soon as the door opened, she suddenly rushed in, knocking over a young girl and falling down herself. The women inside shouted in anger, each holding a clothes-beating stick and beating her legs. Breathless and unable to speak, she only called out "me, me." Soon, an old woman with a lamp came out, and they realized it was her son-in-law. They laughed together in surprise. The next day, he was carried home in a cart and lay in bed for several months. When Zao came and went, people only saw him going and returning on his own, without seeing any demon or young girl. Was this not the result of summoning evil with evil, with demons taking advantage and mocking him? My elder brother Qinghu said: From then on, Zao dared not indulge in licentious behavior again, and when meeting women on the road, he would bow his head. Even if called divine retribution, it is acceptable.

Ten miles from my home, there was a blind man surnamed Wei. On the night of the Horse year's New Year's Eve, he visited the usual singers and musicians to bid farewell to the old year, giving them food, and carried it back on his own. Halfway, he lost his footing and fell into a dry well. Already in a remote and isolated path, and every household was celebrating the New Year, with no one on the road, he called out until his throat was dry, but no one responded. Fortunately, the temperature at the bottom of the well was warm, and there was also bread and dried meat to eat. When very thirsty, he chewed on watermelons, and thus survived for several days without dying. Just then, a butcher named Wang Yisheng was driving pigs home, about half a mile away from the well. Suddenly, the rope broke, and the pigs escaped and ran wildly into the fields, also losing their footing and falling into the well. He pulled the pigs out with a hook. Then he saw the blind man, who was barely breathing. The well was not on the butcher's usual path, it was as if it were fated or ordained. My elder brother Qinghu asked about the situation in the well. The blind man said: At that time, all thoughts were empty, his heart was as dead. Only thinking of his elderly mother who was ill, awaiting the blind son to care for her. Now even the blind son cannot be counted on, at this time, she is probably starving to death, he felt a pain that penetrated his liver and spleen, it was unbearable. My elder brother said: Not for this one thought, Wang Yisheng's pigs would not have broken the rope.

Qi Da was a notorious bandit from Xian County, who once went on a robbery spree with others. One of the bandits saw Qi Da's wife and forced himself on her. He threatened her with a knife, but she refused to comply, and he tied her hands behind her back and bound her to a chair. He then stripped her of her clothes and called two other bandits to hold her feet. Qi Da was on guard, a term bandits used for being on lookout duty. Hearing his wife's cries, he leaped down from the roof and rushed in with a knife, shouting: "Who dares to do such a thing? I will not live with you!" He was ready to fight fiercely, his eyes like those of a hungry tiger. In an instant where there was no room for a hair, he managed to save his wife. Later, the other bandits were all captured and executed, but Qi Da could not be caught. The bandits said that when the officials came to arrest them, Qi Da was actually hiding under the horse trough. The soldiers all said that they searched multiple times, but they only saw a bundle of rotten bamboo, about a thousand or so stalks, covered in dust and filth, as if discarded many years ago. Zhang Mingjing Qinglan said that in one temple, there was a fox living in the hall where the scriptures were kept. Many monks stayed in the hall. One day, during a scorching summer, a monk who was packing his belongings became annoyed by the noise and moved his bedding upstairs to the hall. Suddenly, the monks heard a fox on the beam say: "Everyone, please go back to your rooms. I have many family members, and we will move down to the hall." The monks asked: "We have been living in the hall for a long time. Why do you suddenly want to take it over?" The fox replied: "The monk is there." The monks asked: "Are you afraid of the monk?" The fox said: "The monk is a follower of the Buddha. How could I not be afraid?" The monks then asked: "Are we not monks?" The fox did not answer. The monks insisted: "You think you are monks, but what about us?" Zhang Mingjing's older brother Mao Yuan heard this and said: "This fox is too black and white. However, it can also make people of the three teachings reflect deeply."

Jiā saw Yǐ's wife and found her attractive, and he told Bǐng about it. Bǐng said, "His husband is coarse and fierce and can be plotted against. If you do not hesitate to spend money, I can resolve this matter for you." Bǐng then chose a dissolute youth from the town, lured him with money, and instructed him, saying, "You should sneak into Yǐ's house during the day and deliberately make Yǐ hear something, so that when he is caught, you should admit that you wanted to steal. During the day, when it is not the time for theft, your appearance and clothing should not look like those of a thief, so he will surely suspect adultery and not confess. The officials will interrogate you again before you confess. Your crime will not be more than being sentenced to the stocks or flogging, and I will devise a plan to prevent the case from being concluded, so you will suffer no harm." The youth followed Bǐng's instructions, and the case indeed did not come to a conclusion. However, Yǐ eventually divorced his wife. Bǐng worried that Yǐ might regret it, so he incited Yǐ's wife's family to sue Yǐ. He also secretly bribed the witnesses to ensure they would lose the case, causing them to become angry and marry their daughter to Jiā. Yǐ also severed ties and allowed his wife to marry Jiā. Jiā bought her at a high price as a concubine. Bǐng then taught the youth to turn against Jiā, exposing Jiā's secret plans, and instructed Jiā to bribe to settle the matter. In total, Bǐng embezzled a thousand gold pieces from these actions.

Just heard about a family temple gathering, where efforts were made to prepare offerings to compete with the gods in order to seek blessings. The next day, the temple caretaker dreamed of a god who said, "Where did this money come from? It is a grand ceremony to honor me." The god warned, "Do not let them enter the temple tomorrow. An offering made in an improper manner is not accepted by the gods, how much more so an offering made in an unrighteous way?" When C arrived, the temple caretaker refused him entry based on the god's words. C, however, was angry and did not believe the warning. As soon as he reached the steps, the bearers stumbled, and all the offerings were destroyed. He then returned in a state of alarm. After more than a year, A died. The townspeople, due to a shared plot, often visited C's house. They lured his daughter away, causing C to die of heartbreak. His wife, with her wealth, remarried. The girl reached Dezhou, where people interrogated and found evidence of adultery. She was sent back to her hometown by official document, beaten, and sold by the government. At the time C had died, B had not yet married, and C's wife ended up marrying him. This is a story that satisfies the mind, but there is no such thing. The townspeople later became beggars, and the girl became a prostitute, which is indeed a real occurrence.

A Man from Yidu Named Li Ciwan Said: When Mr. Qiu Gu was traveling south, he temporarily stayed in a garden pavilion of a family. One night, after lying down, he wanted to compose a poem and was just beginning to think deeply when he heard a voice from outside the window say: "Sir, have you not yet slept? Your clear and beautiful words have intoxicated my heart for over a decade. Now that I am fortunate enough to stay in this room, I have secretly listened to your discussions. Although it has been a month, I still regret not being able to question and challenge you. I fear that if you depart suddenly, I will not be able to fully express my thoughts, and that will be a lifelong regret. Therefore, I do not hesitate to be presumptuous and wish to listen to your scholarly discussions from behind the window. Sir, can you not refuse?" Mr. Qiu Gu asked, "Who are you?" He replied, "The secluded pavilion is deep, and the heavy doors are closed at night. I myself have decided that it is not a place where people can come. Sir, your mind is broad and untroubled. I believe you are not afraid, and there is no need to delve deeply. He asked, 'Why do you not enter the room to meet me?' He replied, 'Sir, your mind is broad and untroubled, and I am also tired of formalities. As long as we can have a spiritual connection, why must we be in the same physical space?' Mr. Qiu Gu then daily engaged in conversation with him, and his understanding of the six principles of poetry became quite profound.

For several nights, I once, while drunk, playfully asked: "Listening to your discourse, you are neither god nor immortal, nor ghost nor fox — are you perhaps a *wooden guest* of the mountains, one who understands poetry?" After speaking, there was silence. Peering through a crevice, I saw a crescent moon faintly bright, and a shadow, swaying loosely, swept past the eaves of the waterside pavilion and vanished. The old trees in the garden towered high, and I suspected it was a *wood spirit*. The *Ciwǎn* then said again: When *Qiugǔ* spoke with the spirit, a guest secretly listened. The spirit said of *Yuyang Shansheng*'s poetry: "Like famous mountains and beautiful waters, strange trees and secluded flowers — yet not an inch of land to cultivate grain; like carved railings and curved pavilions, pleasant halls and pools — yet no bedroom to shelter from wind and rain; like bronze vessels and ritual objects, colorful and full of the table — yet no cauldron or steamer to cook food; like embroidered silks and brocades, skillfully woven by divine looms — yet no fur or coarse cloth to guard against cold and heat; like dancing robes and singing fans, the twelve golden hairpins — yet no housewife to manage the kitchen; like the *Liangyuan* and *Jingu* gardens, where refined guests filled the hall — yet no good friends to offer advice." *Qiugǔ* greatly applauded this. He also said that poetry of the late Ming dynasty was filled with vulgar sounds and mixed tunes, hence *Yuyang* rescued it with freshness; modern poetry, its floating sounds increasingly growing, hence the master rescued it with candor. The trend is inherently connected; there is no partial victory. I secretly think the two schools should be balanced and complement each other. If combined, both are beautiful; if separated, both are harmed. *Qiugǔ* was quite dissatisfied with this view.

In Urumqi, there was a Taoist priest who sold medicine in the marketplace. Some people claimed that he possessed magical powers. Observers noted that at night, he would stay at an inn, and before going to sleep, he would always check his satchel, take out a small gourd, and pour out two black objects. Immediately afterward, two young girls would appear to sleep with him, but they would disappear in the morning. When questioned, the Taoist denied their presence, saying there were none. I recalled a past memory from the *Zhuoying Lu* (Record of Ceasing Plowing), which stated: "These are living souls he has captured. The spell can be broken by eating horse meat." Coincidentally, a horse had died in the central camp, and officials secretly instructed the innkeeper to ask if there was any horse meat available. The Taoist refused, saying, "How could one eat horse meat?" I became increasingly suspicious and planned to take action. My colleague Chen, who was known as "Tieqiao," advised me: "You have not personally seen the Taoist carrying young girls, nor have you personally seen him not eating horse meat. Acting on unverified rumors and hastily launching an investigation seems inappropriate. Outsiders should not be allowed to stay in the frontier region, and it would suffice to instruct the authorities to expel him from the area." I then decided to stop my plans. Later, General Wen, upon hearing about the matter, said: "Pursuing this too aggressively is excessive. If fear of punishment leads to false confessions, and the matter is serious yet lacks concrete evidence, what should be done? Simply expelling him is insufficient. If he is moved to another place, it might lead to complications, and if he claims to have lived in Urumqi for a long time, it is unclear who would be responsible. Suspicious individuals should be questioned and searched at checkpoints. If there is concrete evidence, they should be handed over to the authorities; if there is no concrete evidence, they should be sent back to their hometown to avoid misleading the people. Is this not a better approach?" Both I and Chen agreed with General Wen's judgment.

Zhuang Mùkè, a scholar, was once traveling with his father, Shū Shí, when they docked at the riverbank. One night, he lost his footing and fell into the river. The boatman did not notice. While drifting, he heard a voice say, "You can be rescued at the Fujian Academy. This has significance; do not act carelessly." Unbeknownst to him, he was already hanging from the stern of the boat and was saved by calling for help. Later, Zhuang Mùkè went on to oversee the Fujian Academy. When he was appointed, he told me about this incident and said, "Am I not returning?" I encouraged him with the doctrine of destiny, but he eventually died in office. His brother, Fanggēng, a high-ranking official, experienced a similar event during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng in the year 1730 while staying in the capital. An earthquake struck, and he was pressed into a small alley. Just as two walls collapsed, they supported each other like a human-shaped tent, and he sat in it for a day and night before being dug out. Is it not that life and death are fated? Mr. Hé Lì’ān recounted that when he was thirteen or fourteen years old, he returned to the capital with his father after his father's retirement. The boat was crowded, so he spread a mat on top of a large box and slept there. In the middle of the night, he felt a cold hand touch him, like ice, and he was tormented for a long time before waking up. This happened every night afterward. He thought it was a ghostly illusion and took medicine, but it was ineffective until he landed on shore. Later, he learned that the box belonged to his servant. The servant's mother had died in the official residence and was buried outside the city. On the eve of their departure, the servant secretly burned her coffin and wrapped her bones in clothing, hiding them in the box. Because someone slept on top of it, the spirit could not rest, causing these strange phenomena. Thus, it is true that traveling spirits follow the bones back.

Master Li An also recounted that a friend named Nie traveled deep into the Western Mountains to pay respects at a grave and returned as the day grew short and cold. Fearing the danger of tigers, he struggled and rushed forward, and upon seeing a dilapidated temple nestled in the mountainside, he hurriedly ran inside. By then, it was already dusk, and he heard a voice from a corner of the wall saying, "This is not a place for the living; benefactor, you should leave quickly." Realizing it was a monk, he asked, "Why are you sitting here in the dark?" The monk replied, "The Buddha's teachings do not lie; I am a hanged ghost, waiting here for a replacement." Nie's hair stood on end in fear. He then said, "Better to die by a ghost than by a tiger. I will stay the night with you, Master." The ghost said, "You can stay if you wish, but the paths of the living and the dead are different. You cannot withstand the invasion of the ghostly influence, and I cannot withstand the influence of the living energy. Both of us will be restless." They agreed to take corners and not come too close. Nie asked from afar why the ghost was waiting for a replacement. The ghost said, "The Supreme Deity favors life and does not wish for people to take their own lives. If a loyal minister sacrifices his life for his country or a chaste woman remains faithful even in death, these acts are equivalent to a natural death, and there is no need to wait for a replacement. However, for those who are driven by circumstances and have no way to survive, pitying their plight, they too are sent to the cycle of rebirth."

A thorough review of one's entire life, according to good and evil deeds, one receives retribution, and does not necessarily wait for a replacement. If there is even a slight chance of survival, or if one cannot bear a small anger, or uses it to burden others, venting one's anger, and hastily hangs oneself, then it greatly goes against the heart of heaven and earth to nurture life. Therefore, it must make them wait for a replacement to show punishment. Thus, they are often imprisoned and stagnate, sometimes for a hundred years. Is there not someone who lures others to take their place? The ghost says: I cannot bear it. When people hang themselves, those who die for righteousness and loyalty, their souls rise from the top. Their death is quick; those who die out of anger and jealousy, their souls do not descend from the heart, and their death is slow. In the moment before death, the blood vessels surge backward, the skin and flesh seem to tear apart inch by inch, the pain is as if being cut into pieces, the chest, diaphragm, stomach, and intestines feel as if they are burning with intense heat, unbearable, in this manner for about ten moments, the body and spirit finally separate. Thinking of this intense suffering, those who see the hanged person try to stop them from returning, would they dare to lure others? The ghost says: If one holds this thought, they will surely ascend to heaven. The ghost says: I dare not hope for that. Only by sincerely reciting Buddha's name and hoping for repentance. Soon, dawn is approaching, asking them does not respond, carefully looking there is nothing to see. Later, the ghost always goes to the grave, must carry food, paper money, and offerings, and a whirlwind circles around the left and right. One year later, the whirlwind did not appear, thinking that a single good thought has freed them from the realm of spirits.

Mr. Wang, the Half-Sage, once visited his fox friend. The fox greeted him with a smile and said, "You dreamed last night of visiting Fan Zhu’s home, and you were so joyful there." Fan Zhu was a renowned courtesan of the county. Wang recalled that he indeed had such a dream and asked how the fox could know. The fox replied, "Humans are born with yang energy; yang is drawn upward, and the energy constantly rises to the top of the head. When one sleeps, the spirit gathers in the heart, and the spiritual light reflects with the yang energy, like a mirror reflecting an image. Dreams arise from the heart, and their images all appear within the yang energy.
