They come and go, appear and vanish, suddenly changing form into little figures no more than an inch or two tall—like paintings, like plays, like the wriggling of insects. Even matters too secret to speak of are fully revealed, and even ghosts and gods can see them. Foxes who are spiritually sensitive can also see them, though they cannot hear the words spoken. Yesterday, I happened to pass by your home, and so I saw your dream."

It is also said that a person's good or bad intentions are revealed in their yang energy. When a good thought arises, it is like a line of intense flame in one's energy; when a bad thought arises, it is like a line of thick smoke in one's energy. When thick smoke covers the head, there is still a faint light, indicating a person in the animal realm; when even that faint light is absent, one is in the hell-like state of mud-lire. The king asked how a person with thick smoke covering their head, truly a dream-like shadow, could be seen again. The response was that the human heart is inherently good, but evil thoughts can cloud it. When one sleeps without any thoughts, the heart returns to its original state, and the yang energy remains bright. At the moment of waking, before thoughts have arisen, the light is still present. As thoughts gradually arise, the mind becomes gradually dimmer, and when all thoughts arise, the mind becomes completely dark. Since you do not read, try asking a scholar. The "nightly energy" that Mencius referred to is precisely this. The king was startled and said: "Gods and spirits observe and judge, even in dreams and sleep."

Thunder originating from the earth, I witnessed it at the White Crane Ridge in Fujian. The ridge is fifty li high, and during overcast rain, when looking down, dense clouds cover only the middle of the mountain. A column of gas rises from the clouds, shooting straight up. At the tip of the gas, a sudden burst of flames scatters, accompanied by a loud bang, similar to a cannon explosion. As for thunder that strikes objects, it comes directly from the sky. In the summer of Wu Wu, I and my elder brother Mao Yuan were staying and studying in the three-story house in Cui Village. Opening the windows to look around, one could see for several miles. It was raining with thunder, and I saw a person coming from the south, about half a li away from the village. Suddenly, he knelt down, and the cloud and gas descended, obscuring him from sight. Suddenly, a thunderclap, a flash of fire lit up the eyes, as if it were close by. The cloud had already gathered and risen. Soon, it was announced that Li Shanren from Gaocun had been struck by lightning. Following the crowd, I went to see. His entire body was burned black. He knelt with his hands clasped, looking up at the sky. There were red characters on his back, not in seal script, not in clerical script, not in cursive script, not in regular script, with strokes intertwined, making it impossible to read a few words. This person held vegetarian fasts and worshipped Buddha, with no good deeds or bad deeds. It is unknown whether it was due to past karma or hidden sins. His nephew Li Shiqin said: That morning, he insisted on going to Cui Village. In fact, he had nothing to do, but he came in the rain, and thus met this disaster. Someone said: That day, Cui Village had a big market—people in Cui Village traded, with a big market every six and eight days. It is likely that the ghosts and deities drove him there, and everyone saw it.

During my service in the Ministry of War, there was a clerk who had been bewitched by a fox. He was emaciated and weak, so he sought a talisman from Master Zhang, a Taoist master, to cure his illness. Suddenly, he heard voices from the eaves saying, "As an official, you took money unjustly and should face punishment. I was reborn as your benefactor in a previous life, so I used beauty to enchant you and drained your vitality, intending for you to die of a wasting disease. Now I am being driven away, meaning your karma is too heavy to be saved. You should strive to do good deeds, hoping for a chance to change your fate." From then on, the clerk recovered. However, he did not reform and was later executed for forging official seals and embezzling horse taxes. A hall clerk who knew about this later told me. My late mother, Lady Zhang, had a maid named Xiu Luan. One night, while sitting on the steps, she called Xiu Luan, and two Xiu Luans appeared from the east and west corridors. Their appearance and clothing were identical, even the way their right sleeves were folded and left sleeves partially rolled up were the same. She was greatly startled and almost fell; upon looking again, only one remained. She asked, and it turned out one came from the west corridor. She also asked if she saw anyone from the east corridor, and she said she didn't. This happened in July. By November, she passed away. Perhaps her lifespan was nearly over, so the spirit dared to manifest.

**The Nun from the Chaozhou Temple in Cangzhou** A nun from the Chaozhou Temple in Cangzhou, surnamed Dong, was preparing offerings for the Bodhisattva's birthday. Just as she was nearly finished, she suddenly felt slightly tired and leaned against a table to rest briefly. In a daze, she dreamed of the Bodhisattva's birthday. The Bodhisattva said to her, "If you don't offer food, I will also not be able to endure hunger; if you offer food, I will not be any more satisfied. Outside the temple, there are several beggars who cannot find food and are starving and on the verge of death. If you stop offering food and give it to them, the merit will be ten times greater than offering it to me." She suddenly woke up, opened the door, and saw that indeed, it was not mistaken. From then on, every year after offering the food, she would give it to beggars, saying, "This is the Bodhisattva's intention." My late mother said that in Cangzhou, there was a sedan chair bearer named Tian. His mother was suffering from ascites and was on the verge of death. He heard that a doctor in Jinghe Town had a miraculous medicine, but they were over a hundred miles apart. At dawn, he ran madly away and by dusk, he had run madly back, breathing only slightly. But that night, the Wei River flooded, and boats dared not cross. So he looked up to heaven and cried out loudly, with tears following his voice. The crowd felt sorry for him but could do nothing. Suddenly, a boatman untied the rope and called out, "If there is divine will, this person will not drown. Come, come, I will take you across." He vigorously rowed, cutting through the white waves. In the blink of an eye, he had reached the eastern bank. The onlookers all joined their palms and recited Buddhist mantras. My late father-in-law said, "This boatman's faith was so strong, surpassing that of scholars."

The *Yuewei Caotang Notes*, Volume Four, *Lanyang Xiaxia Lu* Four A spirit known as the "Nestled Tiger Hermit" descended and communicated through a medium at the home of Tian Baiyan. The guests burned incense and prayed, while one reckless scholar sat alone, leaning against a table. He said, "Wandering scholars and entertainers are merely skilled at performing tricks for amusement; how could there truly be immortals who listen to people’s calls every day?" The medium then wrote a poem to descend from the altar: "The *titi* bird cries incessantly in the autumn, as I turn back to the willows of Changtai, lush and overgrown. There was a promise of blossoms, but my heart is broken; the clouds disperse, leaving no trace, and even dreams become bewildering. Standing briefly, I secretly play with my golden bracelet; slightly drunk, I laughingly urge the precious objects east and west. Is the pipa still as it was in the past? I ask the wife of the merchant from Xunyang." The reckless scholar was greatly startled and could not help but bow. It was because several days before, he had secretly sent a poem to an old courtesan, which he had not yet saved in a draft. The spirit then judged, "Fortunately, this has not been disseminated; if it were, it would lead to another scandal like the story of *Bufenyan*. This woman has already become respectable, yet you are spying on someone’s private life. Bai Juyi, the *Xiangshan Hermit*, merely wrote an allegory; have you brought it to life in reality? Generally, romantic and scandalous stories are the seeds of hell. I saw this recorded in the books of the underworld officials yesterday, so I am able to remember it. The vast waves of karma—turning back is the shore. The spirit is talking excessively, but it is truly motivated by good intentions. Sir, do not be surprised by the excessive talk." The reckless scholar stood by the table, pale with fear, and died a year later. Among all the mediums I have seen, only this spirit does not speak of auspicious or ominous signs, but rather enjoys pointing out others’ faults. Is it a righteous and upright spirit? My late father, Yao An Gong, always disliked excessive worship, but when he encountered this spirit, he would always bow deeply and say, "So strict and proper, even ghosts should respect it."

Yao An, before he passed the imperial examinations, encountered a diviner. He asked if he would achieve official success. The judgment said, "Your future is promising." He then asked when he would pass the examinations, and the judgment said, "You will have to wait a thousand years to pass." He thought this might mean he would enter officialdom through some other means. When he finally passed the imperial examinations in the year "癸巳" to celebrate the emperor's birthday, he realized the meaning of the thousand-year prediction. Afterward, he served as the magistrate of Yao'an Prefecture in Yunnan Province and requested leave to return home to care for his parents, and he did not return to office. Both the prediction of a promising future and the thousand-year prediction came true. Generally, illusions are quick and skillful, but in the case of divination, there is a real basis for it. However, these are all spirits who are skilled in writing. The spirits they claim to be, whether gods or immortals, are often false claims. Even those who claim to be from a specific dynasty or person, when questioned about their own works, often say they have forgotten due to the passage of time and cannot answer. The diviners themselves, when they encounter someone who can write, produce good writing; when they encounter someone who can compose poetry, they produce good poetry; when they encounter someone who cannot write or compose poetry, they may still produce a complete piece, but it is slow and clumsy. I can compose poetry but not write, and my elder brother Tanju can write but not compose poetry. When I perform divination, my poetry is quick but my writing is sloppy; when Tanju performs divination, his writing is neat but his poetry is shallow and unrefined. In truth, neither of us had any intention of this. It is also because they rely on human spirit to be effective. What is meant is that spirits are not inherently effective; they depend on human spirit to be effective. The divination tools, the yarrow stalks and tortoise shells, are originally dry grass and rotten shells, but they can know auspicious and inauspicious omens, and they also depend on human spirit to be effective.

My maternal grandfather lived on the east bank of the Wei River, where he had a tower called Duofan, which faced west. The lower floor of the tower had a door facing east, leading to a separate courtyard that was not connected to the tower. Previously, a servant's wife named Shi Jinjie had hanged herself in this courtyard. As a result, the place had been abandoned for a long time and was not locked. One day, some young servants, unaware of the past, had a secret meeting in the middle of the night. They heard what sounded like footsteps outside, resembling someone with leprosy walking, and were afraid of being seen, so they crouched down and did not move. They peeped through a crack in the door and saw a hanged ghost stepping up the stairs, sighing softly under the moonlight. Both of them were terrified, and they froze inside the door, afraid to go out. The door was blocked by the two of them, and the ghost also did not dare to enter. They stood off against each other for a long time. Suddenly, a dog saw the ghost and barked, and other dogs heard the sound and gathered to bark as well. Thinking there was a thief, people came with torches and weapons. The ghost disappeared, and the servants' affair was exposed. The servant girl felt ashamed and could not bear it, and by evening, she also went to the courtyard to hang herself. She was found and saved, but she attempted to hang herself again, and finally, she returned to her parents. Thus, they realized that the ghost was not afraid to enter the room but wanted to expose the servants' affair, causing them to feel ashamed and hang themselves to seek redemption. My grandmother said, "This woman was born with a cunning nature, and even after death, she remains the same. Her downfall is indeed appropriate." My great-grandmother said, "If this maid had not done this, how could the ghost have taken advantage? The blame cannot be placed on the ghost."

When Mr. Xin Tongfu was serving as the magistrate of Yiyang, an old man submitted a petition stating: "Last night, while staying outside the eastern city gate, I saw five or six hanged ghosts entering through a gap in the gate. I feared they were seeking someone to take their place. I request that you issue an edict advising the people not to abuse servants and maids, not to demand debts, and to avoid disputes, hoping that this would prevent the ghosts from causing harm." The magistrate became furious and beat the old man before dismissing him. The old man did not resent or regret the beating and, upon reaching the steps, said, "Alas, these five or six lives cannot be saved." A few days later, there were reports of four suicides in the city. The magistrate was greatly alarmed and immediately called the old man to ask about it. The old man replied that he had been dazed and did not remember what happened, but now he realized he had submitted the petition, wondering if he had angered the spirits by being beaten. The news spread quickly, and people began to prepare themselves. Indeed, two people who attempted suicide were saved. One was a woman who had been abused by her mother-in-law, who deeply regretted her actions; the other was under pressure due to unpaid debts, and the creditor burned the debt document, allowing both to live. Thus, it became clear that although fate might be predetermined, human effort could still save some lives. It also became known that human life is very precious, and even if spirits foresaw someone's death, they would still rely on human intervention to save them if there was any chance. This is because the flow of events is like the harsh winter winds and snow, and the heavens and earth must also follow this course. However, protecting oneself with a coat against the snow and sheltering in a small house from the wind are matters left to human action, and they are not forbidden.

Xian County's Shi, whose name is unknown, was a man who did not concern himself with trivial matters but was straightforward and upright, looking down on those who were petty or corrupt. One day, he happened to return from a gambling place and saw a villager and his wife hugging their child and crying. The neighbor said, "They owe a wealthy family money and have sold their wife to pay it off. They have been together for a long time, and the child is still breastfeeding. They intend to abandon her and leave, hence their sadness." Shi asked how much they owed, and the neighbor replied, "Thirty gold coins." He then asked how much they had sold her for, and the neighbor replied, "Fifty gold coins, to be a concubine for someone." Shi asked if they could be redeemed, and the neighbor replied, "The contract has just been signed, but the money has not been paid yet. Why can't they be redeemed?" Shi immediately gave the seventy gold coins he had won at the gambling place to them, saying, "Thirty gold coins to pay off the debt, and forty gold coins to make a living. Do not sell yourselves again." The couple were very grateful to Shi. He cooked chicken and invited them to drink. When they were drunk, the husband held the child and went out, signaling to his wife with his eyes, intending to have her sleep with him as repayment. She nodded in agreement.

The conversation became somewhat casual, and Shi spoke in a serious tone: "I have spent half my life as a thief and the other half as a constable, and I have killed people without hesitation. If I were to defile a woman in an emergency, I truly could not do it." After finishing his meal, he waved his arm and left directly, saying nothing more. Half a month later, the village where he lived was engulfed in a fire at night. It was the time when the autumn harvest was just finished, and every household had their thatched roofs and corn fences filled with straw. Within moments, the flames spread all around, and it seemed impossible to escape. Shi and his family sat in the dark, waiting for death. Suddenly, he heard a voice from the roof calling out: "The East Peak has an urgent decree, and the entire Shi family is to be erased." A cracking sound was heard, and the back wall partially collapsed. Shi then leaped out with his wife on his left and his son on his right, as if he had wings. After the fire was extinguished, it was counted that nine people in the village died from epilepsy. The neighbors all clasped their hands and said: "Yesterday we still secretly laughed at your foolishness, but we did not expect that seventy gold coins would redeem three lives." I believe that this event was protected by the god of fate, with four parts of the merit coming from donating the gold and six parts from resisting temptation.

During the time when Yao An served as an official in the Ministry of Punishment, seven people were caught robbing together outside Dading Gate. Five of them had already been captured. Only Wang Wu and Jin Daya remained at large. Wang Wu fled to Yinxian County, where the path was blocked by a deep ditch. Only a small bridge could accommodate one person at a time. A strong ox was lying across the road with its eyes glaring. Approaching it would cause it to charge violently. He backed away to look for another route and then suddenly encountered a patrol officer. Jin Daya fled to Qinghe, where north of the bridge, a herder boy drove two oxen together, causing them to fall into the mud. The oxen fought angrily. Qinghe is close to the capital, and someone who recognized them told the village headman to bind and deliver them to the authorities. Both were Hui Muslims and were butchers by profession. But both were foiled by the oxen. Is it not because of the cruel slaughter? Even animals harbor resentment. It is the result of malevolent spirits using their own kind to seek revenge? If not, encountering an ox and being knocked down is still a common occurrence. Without reason, blocking the bridge, who made it do this?

Cao Mengquan says that Mr. Sun Eshan once fell ill in a boat in Gaoyou. Suddenly, he seemed to be taking a walk to the shore, and his mood was unusually refreshing and pleasant. Soon, someone led him away. Dazed and forgetting what to do, he did not ask questions and followed to a house. The path was very elegant and clean, and he gradually entered the inner room. There, he saw a young woman just giving birth. He wanted to retreat and hide, but the person behind him slapped him on the back, and he became unconscious and unaware. After a long time, he gradually woke up. Then, his form had shrunk, and he was wrapped and placed in a silk swaddling cloth. He knew it was a rebirth and already nothing could be done. When he wanted to speak, he felt cold air entering through the fontanelle, and immediately clenched and could not speak. He looked around the room at tables, beds, and objects, and couplets, calligraphy, and paintings, all of which were clear. By the third day, a maid carried him to bathe, but dropped him accidentally to the ground. Again, he became unconscious and unaware. When he woke up, he was still lying in the boat. His family said he had been dead for three days, but because his limbs were soft and his chest was still warm, they were not daring to wrap him up.

The teacher quickly took a piece of paper and wrote down what he had seen and heard, then sent a messenger through a certain route to deliver it to a certain door, instructing them not to excessively punish the maid. He then slowly and thoroughly explained the matter to the family. On that day, his illness was cured, and he went directly to that house, where he found the maids and nurses as if they were old acquaintances. The owner, an elderly person without children, looked at each other, sighed with regret, and marveled, but that was all. Recently, in a dream, the Censor-in-Chief Jianxi also had this event, and he remembered the details of the road and door. Upon investigation, it was confirmed that on that day, the child was born and died immediately. Recently in the office, the Imperial Academy scholar Shiqian spoke very detailedly about the situation. Generally, it is similar to what Mr. Eshan said, but only Mr. Eshan remembered the journey there but not the return, while Jianxi remembered both the journey there and back. Moreover, on the way, he met his deceased wife arriving at home, and when entering the room, he saw his wife and daughter sitting together, which was a small difference. According to the theory of reincarnation, it is something that Confucian scholars reject, but in reality, it often occurs. The cause and effect, the logic is not false. Only these two gentlemen temporarily entered the cycle of rebirth, then returned to their original form, and appeared for no reason like a fleeting bubble, which cannot be explained by logic. Beyond the six directions, sages preserve it but do not discuss it, and it is sufficient to leave the doubts unaddressed.

Second cousin, the honorable Mr. Canchen said: Once upon a time, there was a county magistrate who encountered a murder case that could not be resolved, and the case became increasingly complicated. He then prayed for a dream at the City God Temple. In his dream, a god led him to a ghost with a ceramic pot on its head, containing bamboo plants, which were green and beautiful. Upon waking, he checked the case and found someone with the surname Zhu. The pronunciation of 'Zhu' is the same as 'bamboo,' so he must be it. Despite thorough investigation, there was no trace. He then checked the case and found someone named Jie. He privately thought that 'bamboo has joints,' so it must be him. Despite thorough investigation, there was no trace. However, both suspects were nearly dead. With no other options, he reported the doubtful case to higher authorities and requested a separate investigation to find the killer, but ultimately, they could not find him. In cases of doubt, one should approach with an open mind and thorough investigation to possibly uncover the truth. The practice of praying to gods and seeking dreams is merely to intimidate the ignorant and coax them into revealing the truth. If one relies on the vague and uncertain nature of dreams and adds speculative guesses, and uses them as a basis for a reliable verdict, it is rarely correct. Throughout history, cases of praying for dreams to solve crimes, I believe, are all post-facto fabrications.

In the year of Renzi (壬子) during the Yongzheng era, in the month of June, a heavy thunderstorm struck at night, and a villager in the west of Xian County was struck by lightning. The county magistrate, Ming Gong Sheng, went to investigate and ordered the body to be placed in a coffin and buried. After more than half a month, he suddenly arrested one person and interrogated him, asking, "Why did you buy gunpowder?" The man replied, "To catch birds." The magistrate pressed further, "Using a gun to shoot sparrows, a small amount would be a few coins, a larger amount would be up to two or three pounds, enough for one day's use. Why did you buy 20 to 30 pounds?" The man answered, "To prepare for use over several days." The magistrate continued, "You have not used the gunpowder for a full month, and the amount used would not exceed one or two pounds. Where are the rest of the gunpowder now?" The man had no answer, and after being tortured and interrogated, he confessed to plotting murder due to adultery. Both the man and his wife were executed. Someone asked, "How did you know it was this person?" The magistrate replied, "Gunpowder cannot be made to look like lightning without dozens of pounds, and the mixture must contain sulfur. It is now the height of summer, not the time for setting off firecrackers during festivals. The number of people buying sulfur is countable. I secretly sent someone to the market to see who was buying the most sulfur, and everyone said it was a certain craftsman. I also secretly checked who the craftsman was selling the medicine to, and everyone said it was a certain person. That is how I knew." Someone else asked, "How did you know the lightning was fake?" The magistrate explained, "Lightning strikes people from above, not causing the ground to split. If it destroys a house, it also comes from above. Today, the thatched grass and roof beams flew up, and the surface of the earthen bed was also peeled off, indicating that the fire started from below. This place is 5 to 6 li away from the city, and the thunder and lightning were the same. That night, the thunder and lightning were intense, but they were all coiled in the clouds, with no signs of a direct strike. At that time, his wife had already returned to her parents' home, making it difficult to interrogate her. Therefore, it was necessary to first find this person before the wife could be interrogated. This magistrate can be called astute."

Ge Taifu of Xianzhou reported that in the year 1778 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a person was struck by lightning and died on the bridge outside the west gate of Hejian. The person remained upright and kneeling, holding a paper package, which the lightning did not interrupt. Upon examination, the contents of the package were found to be arsenic. The reason for this was unknown. Shortly after, the person's wife arrived and saw the body. She did not cry and said, "If I had known this earlier, I would have regretted it later." She explained that the deceased had previously spoken harshly to their mother and had suddenly conceived a wicked thought to purchase arsenic to poison her mother to death. She had wept and pleaded with him for an entire night, but he did not listen.

My Cousin Xusheng told me that in the south of our village, there used to be a fox girl who often seduced young men. She was known as "Second Miss." One of our relatives had once intended to capture her alive, but he did not speak of it. One day, he saw a beautiful woman in an abandoned garden and suspected that she was the same one. He sang romantic songs playfully, and she looked around happily. He picked a flower and threw it in front of her. Just as she was about to pick it up, she suddenly stepped back several steps and said, "You have evil thoughts; I will leave through the broken wall." Later, two students were studying in the monks' room of Dongyue Temple. One lived in the south room and was close to her, while the other lived in the north room and did not see her. The student in the south room often wondered why she came so late and teased her, saying, "Are you lifting the sleeve of Fuhu and patting the shoulder of Hongya?" The fox girl replied, "You do not disdain me as an outsider, so I will show myself to those who please me. The student in the north room has a heart as cold as stone; how dare I approach him?" The student in the south room then said, "Why not climb over the wall to take a look? It may not be three years before you consent. If you change your ways, you can avoid being as unapproachable as Cheng Yi." The fox girl answered, "A magnet can only attract a needle. If the spirits are not similar, it will not move. Do not trouble yourself; it will only bring shame." At that time, we were both serving alongside Grandfather Yao An. He said, "I have heard of this before, and it happened at the end of the Shunzhi era. The one in the north room seems to be our ancestor, Leiyang Gong. Leiyang Gong was an elderly man who failed in the imperial examinations and had no other merits except for his simple and honest heart, which even the fox girl found unattractive. Those who are deceived by demons have already harbored evil thoughts."

The Deceased Maternal Grandmother's Maternal Family, the Cao Clan, had an old woman who could see ghosts. When my grandmother visited her parents' home, she discussed matters of the afterlife with the old woman, who said: "I saw a ghost yesterday at a certain house, and it could be called utterly foolish. Yet, the situation is pitiful, and it also moves one's heart and soul. The ghost's name is [name], and he lived in a certain village. His family was also relatively well-off, and he died at the age of twenty-seven or twenty-eight. After he died, a hundred days later, his wife invited me to accompany her, and I saw that he always sat under a lilac tree in the courtyard. Sometimes he heard the sound of his wife crying, sometimes the sound of his child crying, and sometimes the quarreling sounds of his elder brothers and sisters-in-law with his wife. Although the intense heat of the sun could not bring him closer, he would still listen intently from the window, and his sorrowful expression was palpable. Later, when he saw the matchmaker come to the wife's room, he was startled and jumped up, looking around anxiously. When he heard that the proposal was not accepted, he showed a slight smile. Soon after, when the matchmaker returned and went back and forth between the wife and her elder brothers and sisters-in-law, he followed her anxiously, as if something had been lost. On the day of the engagement, he sat under the tree, staring directly at the wife's room, his tears streaming down like rain. From then on, whenever the wife went in or out, he would follow her, and his feelings of attachment became even stronger. On the night before the wedding, when the wife prepared her trousseau, he walked back and forth outside the eaves, sometimes leaning on a pillar and weeping, sometimes deep in thought, and when he heard the sound of coughing from inside the room, he would peer through a crack secretly. He was busy and restless all night. I sighed and said: 'Why should a foolish ghost behave like this?' As if he did not hear me.

The person who is performing the marriage ceremony enters, holding a torch in front and proceeding, standing aside and leaning against the wall, still raising his head to look at the woman. I leave with the woman and look back, seeing him following us far away to the house of the person performing the ceremony. He is stopped by the gatekeeper, kowtows and begs pitifully, and is then allowed to enter. Upon entering, he hides in a corner, looks at the woman, performs a ritual, and stands motionless as if drunk. The woman enters the room and moves closer to the window. His state is just like when he was tidying up the chest of drawers. Even after the candle is extinguished and they go to bed, he does not leave. Driven by the madness of the inner demon, he rushes out in a disheveled state. At that time, I am asked by the woman to return to see the child and follow him back. I see him entering the woman's room directly, looking at every place where the woman sat or slept. Soon, I hear the child crying for the mother, and he rushes out, circling the child, holding hands, and making a helpless gesture.

The sister-in-law came out and slapped the boy with one hand, then pounded the ground and struck her chest, making a gesture of gnashing teeth from afar. I could not bear to see it and went home directly, unsure of what happened afterward. Later, I privately told my wife about it, and she bit her teeth in self-reproach. In the village, there was a young widow considering remarriage who, upon hearing this, swore by her life to say: "I cannot bear to let the deceased be in such a state." Alas! A gentleman does not betray others, regardless of whether they are alive or dead. A petty person always betrays others, and does not care whether they are alive or dead. The sentiment of ordinary people is that their feelings exist when the person is alive and disappear when the person is dead. If one thinks about the state of the deceased, one cannot help but feel sorrow. Scholars see flattery and sacrilege seeking blessings, and see superstition and falsehood spreading confusion, thus they repeatedly hold the view that there are no spirits, missing the profound intention of the ancient kings to set up spiritual guidance for teaching. Only to make the ignorant men and women boldly without any restraint, still not as much as the words of this village woman, which can move people to feel about life and death.

Wang Lanquan, the magistrate of justice, reported that the younger wife of Hu Zhongcheng Wenbo, a high-ranking official, died for a day and then revived. She did not recognize her family members and would not allow her husband to approach her. Upon inquiring the reason in detail, it was found that the soul of a woman from the Chen family had possessed her body and returned to life. When asked about her residence, it was discovered that they were only dozens of miles apart. Her relatives were called, and they all recognized her clearly. However, the woman did not want to stay with the Hu family. The Hu family held up a mirror to make her look at herself, and she saw that her appearance was not the same. Thus, she had no choice but to stay with the Hu family as husband and wife. This incident is similar to an event recorded in the "Five Elements" chapter of the Ming History involving a peony. At the time, the officials ruled the case based on physical evidence rather than spiritual claims, as physical evidence is more reliable, while spiritual claims lack proof. If the decision had been based on the soul's return, there would likely have been fraudulent claims of possession, so precautions were taken to prevent such occurrences.

There was a Shanxi merchant staying at the Xincheng Inn in Beijing. His clothes, servants, and horses were all luxurious, and he claimed to be applying for a title through a donation. One day, an old man came to visit, but the servants did not let him in. The old man waited outside the door and was eventually allowed to see the merchant. The merchant's expression was cold and indifferent, and after one cup of tea, there was no further small talk. The old man gradually revealed his request for help. The merchant suddenly became angry and said, "At this time, I don't even have enough to donate, how can I have the extra strength to help you?" The old man was not satisfied and, in front of everyone, explained that the Shanxi merchant had once been in dire poverty and that the old man had supported him for over a decade. The old man had also lent him a hundred taels of silver, which helped the merchant become a wealthy businessman. Now, the merchant had been dismissed from his position and was living in exile. Upon hearing this, the merchant was overjoyed, as if he had been reborn. The old man had no extravagant expectations; he merely hoped to receive back the amount he had previously lent, enough to clear his debts and return to his hometown to be buried there. After finishing his story, the old man wept continuously. The Shanxi merchant seemed not to hear him. Suddenly, a fellow lodger from Jiangxi, who happened to be present, said to the Shanxi merchant, "Is what this old man said true?" The Shanxi merchant, with a twisted face, replied, "It is indeed so. It is only regrettable that I cannot repay him."

Yang said, "You will soon be in a position to lend money, so do not worry about where to borrow it. If someone is willing to lend you a hundred gold, to be repaid within a year without any interest, would you be willing to repay it?" The Western merchant strongly replied, "I am very willing." Yang said, "You just write the bond, and the hundred gold is mine." The Western merchant, pressured by public opinion, had no choice but to write the bond. Yang took the bond, opened his chest, and handed over a hundred gold to the Western merchant, who reluctantly handed it to the old man. Yang then prepared a feast and invited both the old man and the Western merchant to drink. The old man was very happy, but the Western merchant finished the drinking ceremony in a hasty manner. The old man thanked and left, and Yang moved out a few days later. From then on, they never heard from each other again. Later, the Western merchant found that the hundred gold was missing from his chest, but the lock and seal were intact, leaving no room for questioning. He also lost a half-sleeved fox fur, but found a pledge ticket worth two thousand in his chest. The amount matched what Yang spent on the feast. It then became clear that Yang was a magician, and he had played a trick on them. All the people in the same lodging secretly praised him. The Western merchant felt ashamed and moved away, and no one knew where he went. Mr. Chiya, the son of Mr. Chiya, enjoyed composing poetry. He once wrote a poem for the Qixi Festival: "In an instant, the flutes and drums of the mortal world have ceased, and the lamb lamps are extinguished at midnight, leaving the sky green." He also wrote a poem for the Ullambana Festival: "On both banks, the red sand spins and dances, and the sudden wind does not settle until midnight." Mr. Chiya saw these poems and said with a worried look, "Why did you suddenly speak in ghost language?" Indeed, he passed away shortly after. Therefore, Mr. Liu Wending wrote a preface for his posthumous collection: "At the time of the River Drum, he spoke in words that were green like flames at midnight; during the Ullambana Festival, he spoke in words that were red like the sand on both banks. The poetry was already harshly criticized, as your talent surpassed that of Zhong Jun's age; the elegies were inappropriate, but I happened to be in the year of the horse province."

A Farmer Named Chen Si, on a summer night, was guarding his melon field in Tuanjiao. From afar, he saw several shadowy figures under an old willow tree and suspected them to be thieves. He feigned sleep to listen more closely. One of them said, "Do you not know if Chen Si has already fallen asleep?" Another replied, "Chen Si will join us in a few days; there is no need to fear him. We saw it in the city god's record yesterday while serving at the earth god temple." A third said, "You do not know? Chen Si has been granted an extension of his life." The others asked why, and he explained: "A family lost two thousand coins, and their servant girl was beaten hundreds of times but did not confess. Her father, angered by her behavior, said, 'Having a daughter like this is worse than having none.' If she had stolen, he would have hanged her. The girl said, 'If I do not confess, I will die; if I do, I will also die.' She cried out in despair. Chen Si’s mother, moved by her plight, secretly pawned her clothes to raise two thousand coins and returned them to the owner, saying, 'I, an old woman, acted foolishly and took the money, thinking the owner had plenty and would not miss it. I did not expect to cause such trouble for this girl. I am truly ashamed. The money has not yet been spent, and I dare to confess under threat of death, to avoid making a grudge in the next life. I have no face to stay here; please let me leave.' The girl was thus spared. The earth god, pleased with her self-sacrifice to save others, reported this to the city god. The city god then informed the Eastern Peak, who, upon checking the records, found that this woman would otherwise have died of old age, losing her son and starving to death. For this virtue, it was decreed that Chen Si would borrow longevity from his next life, allowing him to care for his mother in this one. You did not know about this yesterday. Chen Si, who had secretly resented his mother for being driven away for stealing money, was now at peace. Nine years later, after his mother’s death and the completion of her funeral, he passed away peacefully without illness.

My father-in-law, Mr. Zhou Lu, said that in the southern part of Dongguang, there was a family named Liao who raised funds to build a public cemetery, and the villagers helped them accomplish this task. More than thirty years have passed since then. In the early years of the Yongzheng reign, a major epidemic broke out in Dongguang. Liao had a dream where over a hundred people stood outside the door, and one person stepped forward and said, "The plague spirit is coming. Please burn over ten paper flags and over a hundred silver-paper-covered wooden swords. We will fight against the plague spirit to repay the kindness of the entire village." Liao, being a good-hearted person, temporarily made and burned them. A few days later, all night long, the villagers heard the clamor and sounds of battle in the fields, which continued until dawn. Indeed, the entire village had no one infected with the plague. In the Shaha Bridge area, there was a merchant named Zhang who traded in the capital. He married a woman who had the grace of a noble family. Zhang himself had a thousand gold pieces worth of property, and his management was also very orderly. One day, a high-ranking official with a grand entourage arrived. Zhang was riding in a sedan chair carried by eight men, with a yellow canopy. The official asked, "Is this Zhang's house?" The neighbors replied, "Yes." The official then ordered his attendants, "Zhang is innocent, but tie up his wife and bring her here." The attendants immediately tied up the woman and brought her out. Zhang, seeing the official's powerful presence, dared not resist. The official ordered the woman's clothes to be removed and her buttocks to be lashed thirty times, then left with a haughty air. The villagers followed to watch, and they disappeared instantly into the shadow of the trees, leaving only a rolling whirlwind heading southwest. While the woman was being flogged, she only knelt and confessed to being guilty of a capital crime. Later, when people asked her why, she wept and said, "I was originally a concubine of a certain Minister of Ceremonies. While he was alive, I intended to secure his favor and vowed never to remarry. Now that his spirit appears during the day, there is nothing more I can say."

Wang Tuzi, known as Wang the Bald, lost his parents at a young age and was unaware of his original surname. He was raised by his aunt's family and adopted the surname Wang. He was ruthless, cunning, and good-for-nothing, causing children to run away and even dogs and chickens to be unsettled wherever he went. One day, he returned home from Gaochuan drunk with his companions. At night, as they passed through the cluster of graves in the southern part of Nanhengzi, they were surrounded by a group of ghosts. His companions trembled in fear and crouched on the ground, but Wang Tuzi alone fought vigorously. One ghost scolded, "Wang Tuzi, you are ungrateful! I am your father. How dare you assault me!" Wang Tuzi did not recognize his father and was confused. Another ghost scolded, "I am also your father. How dare you not bow to me!" The ghosts then all shouted together, "Wang Tuzi, you neglected to honor your mother, leading to her starvation and wandering here, where she became our common wife. We are all your fathers!" Wang Tuzi became angry and swung his fists in a whirlwind. His strikes felt like hitting empty air, and he jumped and struggled until the rooster crowed, exhausted and unable to move, he fell into the bushes. The ghosts all laughed and said, "Wang Tuzi, your heroism is spent today. This is a relief for the people of our village. If you do not repent, you will still face us here in the future." From then on, his boldness and vigor were diminished. One night, he took his wife and children and fled, and no one knows what happened to him. This story is trivial and not worth mentioning, but it shows that the fierce and violent will inevitably meet their match. What people cannot control, even ghosts envy and jointly control.

In the summer of the year of the Earth Pig (Wu Zi), rumors spread in the capital that a flying insect was injuring people at night. However, there were no actual reports of people being harmed by the insect, nor were any insects seen; only pictures were shown. The insect was described as resembling a large silkworm moth with claws, and some enthusiasts might identify it as a type of arrow shooter. According to legend, a mythical creature known as "short-range arrow shooter" shoots sand to harm others, but it does not fly and sting people. This explanation is particularly mistaken. Upon traveling to the Western Regions, I learned that the insect depicted was actually the Pixin's waxworm. This insect is born with intense heat, and it sees people and flies after them. If water is sprayed at it, it becomes soft and falls. If the water fails to reach it and it hits someone, one should quickly chew the root of madder and apply it to the wound to heal. Otherwise, the poison will penetrate the heart and cause death. Urumqi has a lot of madder plants, and in the south of the mountain, in the settlements of Pixin, officials often issue documents to obtain madder for those who are harvesting crops to prepare against this insect.

The Hu Feng Academy in Urumqi once had a female criminal who hanged herself on the window frame. The headmaster, the former magistrate of Ba County, Chen Zhiyi, one night was reading by the light of a burning candle when he heard a rustling sound from the ceiling inside the window. Looking up, he saw two slender feet slowly hanging down from a crack in the paper, gradually revealing the knees and thighs. Chen, knowing of the incident, sternly said, "You have hanged yourself out of anger and shame, will you harm me? I am not your enemy, will you haunt or bewitch me? I have never entered the brothels, and you cannot bewitch me either. If you dare to come down, I will punish you with the rod." The feet slowly retreated back up, and a faint sound of sighing was heard. Soon, the woman's face appeared from the crack, looking very beautiful. Chen spat upwards and said, "Even in death, you are not ashamed!" and then retreated back into his room. Chen extinguished the candle and went to bed, waiting with a hidden blade for her to come, but she did not come down. The next day, Chen Tiqiao from Xianyou visited him, and when they talked about this matter, there was a sound like tearing silk on the ceiling. She was not seen again. However, his servant slept in an outer room, often muttering in his sleep, and after a long time, he fell ill. On his deathbed, Chen said he had accompanied him for two thousand miles, and cried bitterly. The servant waved his hand and said, "There was a beautiful woman who used to come to me privately, and now she has invited me to be her husband. This journey will be very enjoyable; do not be sad." Chen stamped his foot and said, "I relied on my courage and strength, and did not move to another place, and now you are suffering because of me. How harmful is arrogance!" Later that year, Yang Fenyuan from Luan'an took over the management of the academy and moved to another room, saying, "Mencius said, 'Do not stand under a dangerous wall.'"

De Langzhong Hen, during the summer, took a stroll outside Urumqi City and, on his way, came to Xiuyeting to cool off. He sat there for a while when suddenly he heard a loud voice saying, "You should return. I am about to host a feast." Frightened, he hurriedly returned and told me, "Am I about to die? I saw a ghost during the day." I replied, "Seeing a ghost without cause is not a good omen. If you see a ghost in a place where ghosts are expected, it is like seeing people in a house—it is no wonder." The pavilion was located in the deep woods to the west of the city, where the trees were so tall that the sun could not be seen. It was a place where travelers' coffins were temporarily laid and where criminals who had been executed were buried. Such places often became sites of strange occurrences. A certain gentleman from Wuyi was enjoying flowers with relatives and friends in front of the scripture hall of a Buddhist temple. The area was very open, but the hall often had strange occurrences, and no one dared to sit there after nightfall. This gentleman, who considered himself a scholar of the Way, remained calm and did not believe in such things. As they drank and grew warm, he spoke enthusiastically about the principle of "all things being one" from the *Western Inscription*. Everyone listened attentively, and they did not notice that it had already grown late. Suddenly, a stern voice from the hall shouted, "Now is a time of famine and plague, and many people are dying. As a local official, you should have thought of initiating acts of kindness early, such as distributing porridge and medicine. If not, at least you should take advantage of this good night to close your doors and sleep, which would still be better than nothing. Instead, you are engaging in empty and impractical discussions here, talking about the unity of all things. Do you know if you can make food or medicine by talking until dawn? And I will throw a brick at you to see if your so-called 'righteousness' can withstand it!" Suddenly, a brick flew down from the hall, making a sound like thunder. The cups, plates, and tables all shattered. The gentleman hastily fled, saying, "If I do not believe in the teachings of Cheng and Zhu, this is why this demon is a demon." He walked slowly, sighed deeply, and left.

Cangzhou painter Bo Kui, courtesy name Qi Zhan—his surname is this "Bo" character, and he claims descent from Bo Zhouli. A friend once joked with him, saying: "Your abilities do not match those of your second-generation ancestor, the Grand Minister. Recently, his descendants do not know how to write, yet they still claim the surname 'Bai.'" He once painted a picture of a beautiful woman, just outlining the contours when he was interrupted by other matters and left it unfinished, locking it away in his study. After two days, he wanted to complete it, but found that the small color palettes on the desk were scattered chaotically, and the brushes were wet and stained. To his surprise, the painting was already completed. The spirit and vitality of the figure were vivid and extraordinary. Bo Kui was greatly frightened and showed it to his maternal uncle, Zhang Gong Mengzheng, from whom he learned painting. Zhang Gong said: "This is beyond your reach, and it is also beyond mine. It must have been a divine play." At the time, the city guard commander Yong Gong Ning was quite fond of painting and bought it at a good price. Yong Gong later promoted to the position of Vice-General in Sichuan and took the painting with him. A few days before he was about to resign, the figure in the painting suddenly disappeared, leaving only a faint human shadow. The paper color was as fresh as new, while the rest of the painting, the trees and rocks, remained dull and old. This was likely a sign of impending failure. However, the reason why the figure could disappear remains unknown.

Tenant farmer Zhang Tianxi once saw a skull in a field and played by urinating into its mouth. The skull suddenly leaped up and exclaimed, "Humans and ghosts have different paths; how dare you deceive me! And I am a woman, while you are a man, so you have disrespected me, which is especially unacceptable." Gradually rising higher, it directly touched his face. Zhang Tianxi was terrified and ran back home, but the ghost followed him there. At night, the ghost would scold and berate him continuously on the walls and eaves, causing Zhang Tianxi to develop a high fever and become dazed and unconscious. The whole family prayed, and the ghost's anger seemed to lessen. Someone asked about the ghost's name, surname, and hometown before death, and the ghost stated everything. Everyone bowed and said, "Then it should be your great-grandmother; why harm your descendants?" The ghost sighed deeply and said, "Is this still my home? How long ago did we move here? Who are you all?" After explaining the details, the ghost sighed again and said, "I had no intention of coming here; the other ghosts wanted to use this opportunity to find food and persuaded me to come. There are several of them near the sick person and several outside the door. You can prepare a bowl of water and wait for me to send them away properly. Ghosts are often hungry, and if they cause disasters without reason, they fear divine punishment, so they often create trouble when they encounter matters, seeking offerings and sacrifices. You should be careful to avoid such things in the future and not fall into their traps." Following the ghost's instructions, the ghost said, "They have already dispersed. The foul smell in my mouth is unbearable. You can go to the original place and find my bones, wash them, and bury them." The ghost then moaned a few times and fell silent.

A Tenant Farmer Named He Dajin One night, He Dajin, a tenant farmer, was guarding his wheat fields. An elderly man came to sit with him. Dajin thought that there was no such person in the village and assumed the man was a traveler resting briefly. The elderly man asked for water, and Dajin gave him some from a jar. Then, the man inquired about Dajin’s name and his ancestors. With compassion, the man said, “Do not be afraid. I am your great-grandfather. I will not harm you.” He then asked detailed questions about the family, expressing joy and sorrow at times. Before leaving, he instructed Dajin: “Apart from occasionally attending rituals to seek sustenance, we spirits have no other affairs. We constantly think of our descendants, and this longing grows stronger with time. The only trouble is the separation between the living and the dead, which prevents us from hearing news of you. If we hear that your descendants are thriving, we rejoice for several days, and all the spirits come to congratulate us. If we hear that they are declining, we grieve for several days, and all the spirits come to offer condolences. Our longing for our descendants is perhaps ten times greater than that of the living. Now that I hear your family is still warm and well-fed, I will dance and sing for several more days.” He looked back several times, giving Dajin earnest advice before departing. The respected official Yao An later commented, “He Dajin is a dull-witted man, and he surely could not have fabricated such words.” Hearing this, one’s heart naturally feels a sense of reverence for the past.

In the year 1776 (Qianlong 41st year), a scholar from Fujian traveled to the capital for the imperial examinations. Arriving at the end of the year, he was in a hurry and unable to find a place to stay, so he rented an old house in a dilapidated temple north of the Altar of the God of Agriculture. After more than ten days, in the middle of the night, someone outside the window said, "Mr. Scholar, are you awake? I have something to tell you." They had lived in the room for a long time and initially let the scholar stay because he was a hardworking student seeking fame, having traveled thousands of miles to do so. However, they later noticed that the scholar often went out at night, which they initially did not mind, thinking he was just visiting relatives and friends as a newcomer to the capital. Recently, they noticed that the scholar often returned drunk and began to doubt his character. They also heard him talking with a monk, indicating that he spent his days drinking and watching plays, leading them to conclude that he was a playboy. The speaker had moved behind the Buddha's seat to avoid the scholar's disruptive behavior, finding his daily activities inconvenient and unable to tolerate letting a playboy stay. They warned the scholar that if he did not move out the next day, they would throw tiles and stones at him. The monk in the opposite room also heard this and advised the scholar to move elsewhere. Since then, no one dared to rent the house, and anyone who asked about it was told the story.

Mr. Cangling, whose name was Dan, was the younger brother of Mr. Qianju. Mr. Qianju was gentle and easygoing, while Mr. Cangling was open, bold, and generous, yet he stood upright in conduct and was consistent in character. In the village, there was a woman who had been abused by her mother-in-law and hanged herself. Mr. Cangling, seeing that both families were of the scholar-gentry class, advised the woman's father and brothers not to pursue legal action. That night, he heard distant weeping sounds approaching, gradually entering the house and reaching the window, with the ghost wailing and lamenting, its words filled with deep sorrow and resentment for Mr. Cangling's decision to stop the litigation. Mr. Cangling rebuked it, saying, "Your mother-in-law abused you to death, but the law does not provide for punishment. Even if you pursued legal action, it would not satisfy your wishes. Moreover, litigation would require an inspection, which would inevitably involve exposing oneself, thereby bringing further shame to both families." The ghost continued to wail and lament. Mr. Cangling said, "Between ruler and subject, there is no need for litigation; between father and son, there is no need for litigation. People pity your unjust death and can blame your mother-in-law for her cruelty. But as a wife, you desire to sue your mother-in-law, which is a thought that violates the principles of righteousness. Even if you appeal to the gods, they will not support you." The ghost then fell silent and departed. Mr. Qianju said, "Mr. Cangling's words can be told to women throughout the world, but not to mothers-in-law." The respected elder, Mr. Yao'an, said, "Mr. Cangling's words are about speaking to a son about filial piety; Mr. Qianju's words are about speaking to a father about kindness."

During his travels to the capital, Dong Qujiang shared a lodging with a friend, not because they were companions but to save on lodging and food expenses. The friend pursued wealth and status, often staying out late. Dong Qujiang slept alone in his study, where he sometimes heard the sounds of pages being turned and objects being handled. Knowing that the capital was known for its fox spirits, he did not find this unusual. One night, he placed an unfinished poem on the table, and it seemed as though he heard someone reciting it. When he asked, there was no response. By morning, he found that several lines had been marked with circles and points. Despite calling out to the unseen presence many times, it never responded. When his friend returned to their lodging, the night was quiet, and the friend was quite pleased, believing that his good fortune protected him from evil spirits. Once, Li Qingzi came to stay, and after the wine had run out, both Dong Qujiang and his friend went to bed. Li Qingzi walked through the empty garden under the moonlight and saw an old man with a child standing under a tree. He knew it was a fox spirit and hid to watch them. The child said, "It's very cold, let's go back to the room." The old man shook his head and said, "Dong Qujiang living in the same room is not a problem, but this gentleman's commonness is overpowering; how can we live together? Rather than sit in the cold wind and cold moonlight." Li Qingzi later revealed what he saw to other friends, and gradually, the story spread. The fox spirit held a grudge against Li and eventually excluded him, leaving him to return home in a disheveled state with his backpack.

My eldest daughter married the Lu family of Dezhou. The place they live is called Jijia Village. I once saw a man lying by the stream, dressed in rags, groaning. Upon closer inspection, I found that one of his hair follicles had a louse, all its mouths facing inward, and its hind legs hooked onto the ragged cloth. It was impossible to remove it; doing so would cause excruciating pain. There was nothing that could be done, so we just watched him die. This was probably a retribution for past sins, wasn't it? Mr. Wang, the scholar of the Pavilion, rented a house on Yama Temple Street. The courtyard had a jujube tree, which was over a hundred years old. Every night when the moon was bright, one saw a red-clothed woman sitting with her feet hanging down on a crooked branch, her toes pointing towards the moon, completely ignoring people. If you approached her, she disappeared; if you backed away and looked again, she was still in the same place. They once had two people, one standing under the tree and one inside the room. The person in the room saw the person under the tree reach out to touch her feet, but the person under the tree could not see anything. When they looked at her, looking down at the ground, the tree had a shadow, but the woman did not. When they threw tiles and stones at her, there was no obstruction in the air; when they shot at her with a gun, she scattered and disappeared, and after the smoke and flames passed, she quickly returned to her original form. The owner of the house said that the strange phenomenon began when he bought the house, but it did not harm anyone, so people lived there in peace. Wood spirits and flower fairies are things that always exist. Generally, they are prone to change, but this one remained still and silent, sitting on a branch, and the reason for this was not clear. Mr. Wang was concerned that she might cause trouble, so he moved to another place to avoid her. Later, the owner of the house cut down the tree, and the strange phenomenon disappeared.

Liao Old Lady was from Qing County; her maternal family name was Zhu, and she served as the nursemaid to my late mother. She was widowed before reaching thirty and vowed never to remarry, spending the rest of her life in service to my late mother. She passed away at the age of ninety-six. Her character was strict and upright; whenever something needed to be said, she would speak boldly and directly, arguing with my late mother. My late father, Lord Yao'an, also did not treat her as a common servant. I and my younger brothers and sisters all ate and slept under her care. She was attentive to every detail, whether we were hungry or full, cold or hot. Yet if we slightly violated the rules, we would be scolded or forbidden from doing so. She disciplined the servants and maids with little tolerance for leniency, and so they all secretly resented her. She was in charge of the household keys and the kitchen, and no one could obtain even the smallest private favor from her; in the end, there was nothing to be done about her. Once, she took a young boy with her from a relative's house to inquire after someone and returned by evening when a sudden downpour began. They were driven to take shelter in a ruined garden and a dilapidated house, but the rain did not cease throughout the night. From afar, they heard voices outside the wall saying: "I was just coming to your house to take shelter from the rain—why are you sitting in the rain under the tree?" Then they heard the person under the tree reply: "Do not speak further—the chaste widow of the Liao family is inside the house." After that, it fell silent. Later, the boy accidentally recounted the event, and all the servants and maids said: "A person who is not reasonable, even ghosts will hate and avoid." Alas! Did the ghosts truly hate and avoid her?

An's cousin, whose name is forgotten, has a fox as a friend. They often converse in the fields and gardens. An sees the fox, but others do not. The fox claims to have been born at the beginning of the Northern Song dynasty. An asks about historical events of the Song dynasty, and the fox replies, "I know nothing of them." All who learn the Dao must wander beyond the world, severing all worldly ties and focusing solely on refined cultivation. If one has any knowledge or experiences in the world, one's heart will inevitably have judgments of right and wrong. If there are judgments, there will be likes and dislikes. If there are likes and dislikes, emotions such as joy, anger, sorrow, and happiness will inevitably arise, consuming one's vital energy. The spirit is exhausted and the body is worn out. How could they still exist today? After achieving the Dao, they come and go among people, viewing all cunning and deceit as plays, and all gains and losses, victories and defeats, and even the rise and fall of dynasties as illusions. If they were not paying attention at the time, how could they remember every detail? Even meeting you is also a predestined fate.

Yet for hundreds of years, those who meet like you are countless, mostly like meeting floating萍 (萍 is a type of floating plant) and dissipating like smoke and clouds. The jokes and laughter of the past are mostly not remembered. Thus, those I have not met can be known from this. At the time, there was an incident at the Three Officials Temple in Bagli Village where a scorpion was struck by lightning. How can it be that objects that have long been spiritual are often struck by lightning? Is longevity also something the Creator abhors? There are two aspects to this: internal alchemy and external elixirs, both require great effort and hardship to achieve enlightenment, like farming to become wealthy, it is naturally appropriate; if seducing, causing nightmares, stealing vital energy, shortening others' lives, and extending one's own, such actions are no different from banditry, and are not tolerated by heavenly law. And if one indulges in magical illusions, causing harm to living beings, heavenly law will not tolerate this either; if one preserves the original spirit, maintains life, causes no harm to others, and does not compete in the world, then a long-lived creature is just like a long-lived person, how could it offend the Creator? Uncle Mr. Shizhai heard this and said, "What this fox says are all the basic principles of Laozi, but using them for self-cultivation is sufficient."

A scholar from Zhejiang Province had a dream in which he arrived at an official hall, which was said to be the Temple of the City God. A ghost official told him, "Now a certain official is accusing his friend of being ungrateful and is bringing you as a witness. Please think if you have ever had such a thing happen." The scholar recalled the incident and found it to be true. Soon he heard the City God ascend the throne. The ghost official reported, "Someone is accusing someone of being ungrateful. The witness has arrived. Please investigate and judge." The City God presented a case to the scholar, who answered truthfully. The City God said, "These people form cliques for their own benefit and seek advancement through alliances. They regard agreement and disagreement as affection and aversion, and affection and aversion as right and wrong. When they are isolated, they cling to others to seek help; when they are equally matched, they push each other out and devour each other. They change their minds as often as the weather, in a moment, countless changes. Originally, it is the way of small-minded people to interact. How can one hold them to the standards of a gentleman? To bring weapons into the house is a natural consequence. The investigation is clear. They can be driven away." The City God looked at the scholar and said, "Is it not possible that the ungrateful one will escape punishment? As you sow, so shall you reap. The cause and effect will compensate each other. Flowers bear fruit, and the fruit bears more flowers. The cause and effect will generate each other. The ungrateful one will be followed by another ungrateful person. Without waiting for the intervention of ghosts and spirits." The scholar woke up suddenly. After several years, it turned out exactly as the god had said.

A lady from Fujian loved to eat cats. Whenever she obtained a cat, she would first store lime in a jar, place the cat inside, and pour boiling water over it. The lime would erode the cat's fur, causing it to fall off completely, and the blood would concentrate in the organs, making the meat as clear as jade. She claimed that the meat tasted ten times better than young chicken. She set up nets and traps every day, capturing and killing countless cats. Later, the lady fell ill and began to make cat-like sounds, eventually dying after about ten days. My father-in-law, Lu Guanxian, once told me about this. He also mentioned a man from Jingzhou, a government official's son, who enjoyed torturing cats and dogs by breaking their legs and twisting them backward, watching them struggle and scream for amusement. He killed many animals in this way. His children were born with feet that pointed backward, a physical deformity. Another servant of mine, Wang Fa, was skilled with a bird gun and killed dozens of birds every day. His only son, named Jinan, was born when they were traveling to Jinan. By the time he was eleven or twelve, he suddenly developed sores all over his body, each containing a small iron particle, which no one could explain how they got there. Despite numerous treatments, he could not be cured and eventually died, leading to the end of the family line. The karmic consequences of killing are indeed very heavy, as this story clearly shows.

I have often wondered at those who practice good deeds, all of whom observe fasting daily as if following a law, yet cannot abstain from killing in their daily lives. The Buddhist practice of fasting, is it because eating vegetables and fruits constitutes a virtue? It is precisely because eating vegetables and fruits means not taking life. Today, people merely say that certain days are fasting periods for Guanyin, and other days are fasting periods for Dizang Bodhisattva. On these days, they fast, and the Buddha is greatly pleased. But on non-fasting days, they fill the kitchen with cooking and slaughtering, spread delicacies on the sacrificial table, and engage in cruel and harsh slaughter, while the Buddha remains indifferent. Is there such a thing in this world? Moreover, the emperor does not kill oxen without cause, the minister does not kill sheep without cause, and the scholar does not kill dogs and pigs without cause, all in accordance with ritual. Scholars follow the teachings of the sages, and thus they absolutely should not give up meat. However, even outside of sacrificial ceremonies, killing at certain times is also absolutely inappropriate. For the sake of a morsel, one suddenly takes a life; for the sake of a broth, one suddenly takes dozens or even hundreds of lives; for the infinite fear and suffering, and infinite cruelty and pain of sentient beings, to satisfy my fleeting pleasure. How can this be consistent with the intention behind daily fasting? Mr. Dongpo (Su Shi) once held this view, and I believe it to be the middle way. I wish to discuss this with those who practice good deeds.

Beyond the six directions, sages refrain from discussing. Yet even within these six directions, there are matters that cannot be discussed. When a person dies, according to the Confucian view, the soul ascends and the spirit descends; according to Buddhist teachings, the ghost is also taken to the underworld and cannot return to the world of the living. However, there is a belief in the world called "returning of the ghost." Common and vulgar practitioners claim to have a book that can predict the day, hour, and direction of the ghost's departure, which is absurd in the extreme. Yet I once saw from the window of a building across the courtyard, a ghostly apparition like a white smoke, emerging from the chimney and slowly heading southwest. There was not a single difference between what was predicted and what actually happened. I also had two occasions where I personally opened the lock and carefully examined the place where ashes were scattered, and the handprints and footprints were indistinguishable from when the person was alive, and those close to me could recognize them. What can be said about this? Fortune and misfortune are determined by fate, and life and death are governed by destiny; even the wisest cannot compete with the forces of nature. Yet there are practices of poisoning and haunting, clearly recorded in legal codes. I have not witnessed the former, but I have seen the latter many times.

Those who practice this art are no more than blind fortune-tellers, witches, and builders. Yet they truly can bring fortune or calamity, life or death, and their effects are clearly verifiable. This suggests that the power of heaven and earth, and of ghosts and spirits, is left entirely in their hands, without restraint. What can be said about this? There must be a reason behind it, but people simply do not know it. The Song scholars, when faced with things they could not understand, made arbitrary judgments, denying that such things existed. Is this not like forcing a fixed peg into a zither—stiff and inflexible? Master Li Youdan said: The Song scholars based their discussions of the heavens on theory, claiming they had grasped the fundamental principles of nature and the yin-yang forces. They spoke with certainty about the sun, moon, and five visible planets, as if they were pointing them out with their fingers. Yet the Song calendars changed repeatedly and became increasingly inaccurate. Only after Guo Shoujing's time, when observations were tested against actual measurements and verified through eclipses, did it become clear that the scholars of Lian, Luo, Guan, and Min had no real understanding of this matter. Even Kangjie, who was most skilled in mathematics, relied only on odd and even numbers, and the shapes of circles and squares, to speculate about influences and effects—without truly understanding them through astronomical calculations. Thus, the higher their theories, the more they fell into misinterpretation and misunderstanding. The seven celestial bodies move in a way that can be observed and recorded, yet even these cannot be accurately judged by mere reasoning. How much more so, then, is it impossible to grasp the metaphysical principles of Taiji and Xiantian, which lie beyond the realm of the visible? The ancient sages once said: A gentleman, when he does not know something, remains silent and does not pretend to understand.

A Female Witch Named Hao Hao, the witch, was a cunning and deceitful village woman. I saw her when I was young at my aunt's house in Cangzhou. She claimed that a fox spirit had possessed her body, predicting good or bad fortune for people, knowing every detail of household matters, and thus many believed her. In reality, she spread among her followers, befriended maids and elderly women, spied on secret matters on their behalf, and sold her deceit. Once, a pregnant woman asked Hao whether she would give birth to a boy or a girl, and Hao promised a boy, but she later gave birth to a girl. The woman questioned the accuracy of the spirit's prediction. Hao angrily said, "You should have given birth to a boy. On a certain day, your family gave you twenty buns, and you gave six to your in-laws, hiding fourteen for yourself. The underworld punished you for your filial impiety, turning your son into a daughter. Don't you understand yet?" The woman did not know that this matter had been spied on beforehand, and thus she was terrified and confessed her guilt. Her skill in fabricating stories was similar to this. One day, while burning incense and summoning the spirit, she suddenly sat upright and spoke clearly, saying, "I am a genuine fox spirit. We, though living among people, actually cultivate qi and refine our forms, and would never associate with village old women or meddle in people's trivial affairs. This woman plots endlessly, using our names to deceive and gain wealth. Therefore, today I truly possess her body to reveal her deceit to everyone." She then listed her hidden sins and the names of her followers, and upon finishing, Hao suddenly woke up as if from a dream, fleeing in panic. Later, no one knew what happened to her.

The mother of the concubine, Madame Shen, recounted that in Gaochuan there was a beggar who lived with his mother and wife in a dilapidated temple. In the summer months, he would gather a small amount of wheat, instruct his wife to grind it into flour to feed his mother. However, the wife hid the good flour and poured out dirty water, making bread with the coarse flour to give to the mother to eat. That night, a heavy thunderstorm occurred. In the darkness, the wife suddenly let out a loud cry. The beggar got up to look and saw a large snake enter through her mouth, biting her heart and causing her death. The beggar dug a grave and buried her. Madame Shen personally saw the snake's tail hanging between her chest and abdomen, about two feet long. There were two village schoolteachers who lived near each other and both considered themselves to be scholars of the Way. One day, they invited each other to a lecture, with more than ten students serving as their audience. They were engaged in a debate about human nature and the heavens, analyzing the relationship between reason and desire, speaking with solemn and serious expressions, as if they were addressing sages. Suddenly, a gentle breeze blew, carrying a piece of paper that fell to the steps below, swirling and dancing without stopping. The students picked it up and looked at it, and it turned out to be a letter in which the two men had been secretly plotting to seize the land of a widow. Perhaps the gods detested their deceit, and thus ingeniously exposed their wickedness? Although many people employ such tactics, they have not all been caught. It is said that after this letter was revealed, their plan failed, and the widow's land was ultimately preserved. This was likely due to the widow's steadfast virtue, which moved the spirits of the underworld, and thus they manifested this miraculous event to secretly protect her.

Li Xiao-lian recorded this story: In Lixian County, there was a haunted house. One day, an elderly scholar and several guests stayed in it. During the night, they heard a rustling sound outside the window. The scholar shouted, "Evil cannot interfere with righteousness, and demons cannot overcome virtue. I have studied Daoist philosophy for thirty years; what have I to fear from you?" It seemed as though a woman's voice replied from outside the window, "You have been preaching Daoist philosophy for a long time. Although I am of a different kind, I have also read some Confucian texts. The essence of sincerity lies in being cautious when alone. Your every word and action must follow ancient rituals. Is it truly for the purpose of self-cultivation, or is there a slight tendency toward seeking fame? You write numerous discourses and engage in debates with other scholars. Is it truly for the purpose of elucidating the Way, or is there a slight tendency toward winning?
