Self-cultivation and elucidating the Way are the way of heaven, but seeking fame and winning are the selfish desires of man. If one cannot overcome selfish desires, what is the point of studying? This matter should not be resolved through words alone. At midnight, one should first ask oneself how one stands. Then, whether evil can interfere or demons can overcome, one should already know it clearly. Why should one resort to verbal or physical threats?" The scholar broke out in a sweat like rain, shivering and unable to respond. Soon, he heard a slight laugh from outside the window, "You dare not answer, yet you still do not deceive your own heart. Let me allow you to sleep." There was another rustling sound, and it flew away over the eaves.

When a certain official died, the ancient artifacts he had collected were unknown in value to his widow and orphans, who asked a friend to appraise them. This friend deliberately set a high price, causing the items to remain unsold for a long time. When the family was in dire straits, he then bought them at a cheap price. Two years later, this friend also died, and his widow and orphans, unaware of the value of his collected artifacts, once again asked a close friend to appraise them. This friend imitated the previous strategy and took the artifacts for himself. Some said: "The way of heaven favors return; nothing goes without coming back. Those who imitate this wisdom deserve lesser punishment." I say this is a satisfying remark, but it cannot be taken as a principle. The thief has committed a crime, and then steals again — can it be said that his crime is less than theft? The butcher Xu Fang was the same man previously mentioned as the one who encountered a drunken man at night. When he slaughtered a donkey, he first dug a trench in the ground and placed a board over it, then bored four holes around the board and trapped the donkey’s feet within them. When a customer wished to buy meat, depending on the amount purchased, he would pour boiling water from a pot over the donkey’s body to remove its hair and cook the meat, then flay it and take the meat, claiming that it must be done this way to be crisp and delicious. After one or two days, the donkey died once the meat was all removed. While still alive, its mouth was clamped shut so it could not make a sound, and its eyes glared with rage, glowing like two torches — a sight so grim it was unbearable. Yet Xu remained calm and unconcerned. Later, he fell ill, his entire body ulcerated with no intact skin. His appearance was just like the donkey he had slaughtered — writhing on his bedding, unable to die, he cried out in anguish for forty to fifty days before finally passing away. During his illness, he deeply regretted and reproached himself, instructing his son Zhixue to quickly change his profession. After Xu’s death, Zhixue changed his trade to butchering pigs. I still saw him when I was young, but now I hear nothing of his descendants; it seems their line has long been extinguished.

The Recluse of Bian Suixuan said that there was once a man who entered the underworld and saw an old scholar standing under the eaves, looking very frightened. A magistrate from the underworld, who seemed to be an old acquaintance, greeted him politely and then smiled, saying, "Master, you always held the view that there are no ghosts. But I wonder what you are today?" The other ghosts all laughed, while the old scholar cowered in fear. East Guang Ma Dahuan once spent a summer night sleeping naked in the library of Zisheng Temple. He felt someone shaking his arm and said, "Get up, don't desecrate the Buddhist scriptures." When he woke up, he saw an old man beside him. He asked, "Who are you?" The old man replied, "I am the guardian spirit of this collection." Ma Dahuan, who was naturally free-spirited, was not afraid. The moon was bright like daylight, so he invited the old man to sit and talk. He asked, "Why do you guard this collection?" The old man replied, "It is my destiny." Ma Dahuan asked, "The Confucian classics fill countless shelves, yet I have never heard of any spirit guarding them. Does Heaven favor Buddhist scriptures?" The old man answered, "Buddhism teaches through the supernatural, and people may believe or not believe. Therefore, it needs a spirit to guard it. Confucianism teaches through human conduct, and all people should respect and guard it. Since everyone knows to respect it, it does not need a spirit's help, and Heaven does not favor Buddhist scriptures." Ma Dahuan then asked, "Then does Heaven view the three teachings equally?" The old man replied, "Confucianism focuses on self-cultivation and governing others; Daoism emphasizes stillness and gentleness; Buddhism stresses concentration and compassion. Their principles are different and cannot be the same. However, in teaching people to do good and benefiting the world, they are similar. Their ultimate goals are roughly the same. Heaven cannot help but allow them to coexist."

In summary, Confucianism is the foundation for establishing the destiny of the people and takes its root in the self; Buddhism and Taoism are self-centered studies and extend their influence to things with leftover effort. Therefore, those who clarify human ways are primary, and those who clarify divine ways are secondary. One cannot solely use Buddhism and Taoism to govern the world, as this is a case where the different are the same and the same are different. Confucianism is like grains; without it for one day, one is hungry, and without it for several days, one must die. Buddhism and Taoism are like medicine; they explain grievances and eliminate distress in matters of life and death, gain and loss, joy, anger, sorrow, and happiness, and are more effective than Confucianism in this regard. Their teachings on karma and retribution are used to shock and move the ignorant, and they are easier to accept than Confucianism. However, one should stop when the illness is cured; one cannot take them as a regular medicine, as it may lead to an imbalance. Some ritual scholars talk abstractly about the mind and nature, mixing with the Buddha and Laozi, while others attack them as if they were enemies. Both are limited views. What about the Taoist priests and Buddhist monks who indulge in absurdities? If we do not strongly criticize them, will it not harm the world? One says: This is just discussing the root cause. As for the later developments, not only do Buddhism and Taoism cause problems, but Confucianism does too. Even if the public is drunk and sleeps naked, it is unlikely to be in accordance with the rituals of Zhou Gong and Confucius. Greatly embarrassed, he thanked him and continued talking until dawn, then left. He did not know who the spirit was, but someone said: 'It was a fox.'

All trades and crafts each have a god as their ancestor: entertainers worship Guan Zhong, because he had 300 concubines; actors worship Tang Xuanzong, because he was associated with the imperial music bureau. These are the most classic examples. Low-ranking officials worship Xiao He and Cao Can, carpenters worship Lu Ban, and these have some meaning. As for shoemakers who worship Sun Bin and blacksmiths who worship Laozi, such practices are more questionable. Servants worship Zhong Sanlang, performing secret rituals at night, and it is not known what god he is. Yan Jiezi of Qufu said: it must be a transformation of the name of the Wolf of Zhongshan. My grandfather Yao'an said: this is not necessarily true, nor is it necessarily false. Taking something out of context, it is not entirely without benefit. My uncle Yian Gong had a pawnshop in the west city. A small building was occupied by foxes, and at night their voices could often be heard, but they did not harm anyone, and over time they became accustomed to each other. One night, there was a loud quarreling and beating sound on the second floor, and everyone went to listen. Suddenly, a voice cried out in pain: "The people downstairs, all should understand reason. Is there a wife who beats her husband?" Just then, one person was being beaten by his wife, and the scratches on his face were still not healed. The crowd laughed and said: "This is indeed possible, nothing to be surprised about." The foxes on the second floor also laughed, and their fight was resolved. Those who heard it could not help but laugh. Yian Gong said: this fox used laughter to dispel anger, and can still be considered good.

Xu Si, a farmer from the village of Tian, was the fourth son. After his father's death, his stepmother gave birth to a younger brother who was extremely wicked and rebellious. Their family owned over a hundred acres of land. When dividing the property, the younger brother claimed he needed it to support their mother and took eight-tenths of it, and Xu Si reluctantly agreed. The younger brother then chose the most fertile part of the land, and Xu Si also agreed. Later, when the younger brother had spent all his share, he again demanded from his elder brother, so Xu Si gave him the entire share and rented land to farm himself, remaining calm and unperturbed. One night, after drinking with neighbors from another village, he passed through an orchard of jujube trees and encountered a group of ghosts throwing dirt. He trembled in fear and dared not move. The ghosts made eerie sounds and gradually approached. When they were face to face, the ghosts started and retreated, saying, "This is Xu Si, the brother who gave up his property," and suddenly turned into black smoke and dispersed. The monk Mingyu from the White Robe Temple said that once a monk on Mount Wutai often dreamed of going to hell, seeing various transformations. An elder monk taught him to recite scriptures with deep intent, but his dreams became more vivid, and he gradually grew weak. Another elder monk said, "It must be that before you became a monk, you committed evil deeds. After becoming a monk and gradually understanding the cause and effect, you realized you would surely fall into hell, which caused you fear. With this fear, you created these images, so the more you recited the scriptures, the more vivid the illusions became." Buddhism is vast and allows for repentance; all evil deeds should be eliminated at the moment of thought. If one puts down the butcher's knife, one becomes a Buddha immediately. Have you not heard this? Upon hearing this, the monk immediately made a vow to the Buddha, became brave and diligent, and from then on, he had no more dreams.

Inspector Shen's wife and husband both passed away, and his young son lived on the charity of relatives. They were in extreme poverty and had no means. His wife, who had married into the Taichang family, heard and felt pity. She secretly sent maidservants to give clothes, and later the Taichang found out and said, "This is still within the bounds of human feelings and natural reason, so do not prohibit it." Qicangzhou from Qiantang mentioned that there was a widow who was ill and could not cook for herself, so she called out for a neighbor's old woman to cook for her, but the old woman could not come often. Suddenly a young girl pushed open the door and said, "I am a girl who has just moved in next door." She heard that her sister was suffering from hunger and poverty and had always been unable to bear it, so now she had told her parents and wished to prepare food for her sister and take care of her illness. From then on, she came to her house every day for several months. The widow recovered and was about to go to her door to thank her parents. The girl wept and said, "I dare not deceive you, I am actually a fox. I was closest to my husband when he was alive, and now I am moved by our old feelings and pity for my sister's hardship, so I have come under a false name." She placed several bars of silver on the bed and wept as she left. The two stories are quite similar. Therefore, turning one's head away without feeling, not only is it not as good as this wife, but it is not even as good as this fox.

Wu Shidu Jieyun said, in the year Gui Chou, there was a senior scholar whose surname he could not recall, perhaps it was Mr. Wang Yanfu, but his memory of this was not very clear. He once rented a residence on Haiseng Temple Street, behind which was a dilapidated house with three rooms. It was said that there was a ghost in the house, making it uninhabitable, but the ghost did not cause harm; one only occasionally heard sounds. One night, there were voices of scolding in the house. Crouching in the corner of the wall to listen, he found that it was two wives arguing over seating positions, one claiming to have come first, the other claiming to be older, and they continued to argue incessantly. The senior scholar could not help sighing and said, "Still not at rest even after death?" Listening again, the voices became silent. In households where a husband lives with a wife and concubines, enduring and living in peace is rare, occurring perhaps once in ten; getting along happily is even rarer, occurring perhaps once in a thousand or a hundred. This is because there is still a distinction in status to restrain them. As for two wives standing together, there has never been one who got along, nor one who lived in peace. Without a distinction in status to restrain them, they will not submit to each other, and it is natural. What is surprising about noisy quarreling?

Yuewei Caotang Biji* Volume 5, *Pulang Xiaoxialu* 5 Zheng Wu, whose origin is unknown, took his mother and wife to live temporarily in a river area, supporting himself by carpentry. On the verge of death, he instructed his wife: "I originally had no place to stand, and you are also inept at needlework. I estimate that the old mother would surely die of cold and hunger. Now I make an agreement with you: if there is someone who can take care of my mother for me, you will then marry him, and I will not regret it after I die." The wife followed the agreement, and the mother survived through this arrangement. If the care was slightly neglected, there would be a sound in the room, like the sound of broken porcelain or snapped bamboo. One year passed without the cotton clothes being made, and the mother cried and shivered from cold. Suddenly, a loud sound like drums shaking the walls occurred. This continued for seven or eight years until the mother's death, when it ceased. Tenant farmer Cao Zili, barely able to read, not very well. Suddenly fell ill with a cold, in a confused state, was led away by a servant. On the way, he met another servant, who realized it was a mistaken arrest. They argued for a long time, and he was ordered to be sent back. Passing by a place with stone walls surrounding an area of about one li, inside, thick smoke was rising, and purple flames were blazing. Six characters on the door, as large as a winnowing basket, could not be fully recognized. But he remembered the strokes and returned, based on the remembered strokes, deduced that it seemed to be a place for those who betray their hearts and morals.

It is commonly said that deceased children are known as "debt ghosts," and this belief is indeed prevalent. According to Lu Nanshi, Zhu Yuanting's son fell ill with consumption, suffering for a prolonged period, and groaned to himself, saying, "I still owe me nineteen gold coins." Soon after, a doctor administered ginseng, which was prepared but not consumed before the patient passed away. The price of the ginseng was exactly nineteen gold coins. This incident occurred recently. Some might say that within the vast world, in the course of a single day, there are countless deceased children, and how could there be so many who have accumulated debts from past lives? Life and death are cyclical, and cause and effect are intertwined, like the sands of the Ganges River, whose quantity cannot be calculated; like the clouds in the sky, whose transformations cannot be imagined. This is truly difficult to confine to a single pattern. However, considering the general trend, grievances and karmic debts often arise from material wealth. The *Laozi* says, "In the world, people rush about for profit; in the world, people gather together for profit." Throughout a person's life, it seems that everyone is preoccupied with this pursuit. Yet, the resources of heaven and earth are limited. When one gains, another loses; when one is abundant, another is in deficit. From this arises scheming, and from scheming arises enmity and friendship. Karmic retribution extends over three lifetimes. Observing the multitude of those who seek profit, one can infer that there are not a few who seek retribution. Sima Qian once said, "Resentment and hatred are profound in their impact on people!" A gentleman would rather believe in their existence, perhaps to provoke deep reflection.

A woman who had recently lost her husband was seduced by a man who bribed a neighbor woman to entice her. One night, he entered her room, closed the door, and was about to sleep when suddenly the light turned green and dim, shrinking to the size of a bean. Suddenly, there was a loud explosion, and red flames spread out, forming a circle about two feet in diameter, as large as a mirror. A human face appeared in the center, which was her former husband. Both men and women fell to the bed, and family members were shocked and saw the incident, which was exposed. Some people wonder why this ghost is particularly vengeful when many widows have fallen from virtue. I say that ghosts have strength and weakness, and people have periods of strength and decline. This was a strong ghost, and it happened during a period of weakness for both of them, so it could cause harm. There are countless others who harbor grudges in the underworld, with grievances spanning several generations. It is not that spirits die with the body. Some people suspect that it was due to some supernatural entity causing these strange phenomena. This may be true, but supernatural entities do not arise on their own; they arise due to human actions. They are also the result of the dark and toxic energy of spirits, which secretly attract and empower evil spirits. Otherwise, why hasn't anyone heard of the ghost of Liqiu in the room of Tao Ying?

Luo Yangshan, an official in the Ministry of Rites, was obstructed by his colleagues at every turn, facing difficulties like walking through thorns. His naturally slow and stubborn temperament gradually turned into resentment and illness. One day, feeling deeply depressed and sitting idle, he suddenly dreamed of reaching a mountain where flowers bloomed and water flowed, with clear skies and fresh air. He felt mentally refreshed and his worries dissipated. He strolled along the stream and came across a humble thatched cottage, where an elderly man invited him to sit down briefly. Their conversation was pleasant, and the old man asked why Luo had a sickly appearance. Luo explained his troubles. The old man sighed and said, "This has a past cause, which you do not understand. Seven hundred years ago, you were Huang Quan of the Song dynasty, and I was Xu Xi of the Southern Tang dynasty. Xu Xi's painting style was originally superior to Huang Quan's. Huang Quan feared losing his favor and used clever words to suppress Xu Xi, causing him to suffer and die with lingering resentment. After that, we were unable to meet again in our cycles of rebirth, but now our karmic connections have brought us together, allowing you to satisfy your old grudge. What was done to you is what you did to him; why are you still resentful?"

The general principle that everything returns, which is the way of heaven; the principle that there is always a response to action, which is the nature of humanity. Once a cause is sown, it will eventually bear fruit. The attraction of karma, like a magnet drawing a needle, either does not happen or, if it does, it is irresistibly drawn; the accumulation of resentment, like a stone containing fire, either does not ignite or, if it does, it erupts suddenly; the persistence of unresolved issues, like a hidden disease, will inevitably flare up; the eventual meeting of karmic forces, like the cyclical meeting of the sun and moon, will inevitably intertwine. Therefore, all kinds of harmful actions are ultimately self-harm. In my past lives, I had an acquaintance with you, and because you did not understand, I explained the reasons for your suffering. You and the other party have already experienced the consequences. From now on, be careful not to sow new causes. Luo suddenly understood and completely let go of his desire for victory. Within a few days, all his old ailments were completely cured. This is what I heard from Mr. Huo Yishu when I was about ten years old. Some say: this is the story of Wei Gong Yanpu, and the master might have mistakenly remembered it. I do not know the exact details, but I have recorded it here.

Mr. Tian Baiyan said that during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there was a case involving the collection of grain taxes in the southern part of China, where several officials were executed according to the law. Several years later, one of them communicated with a spirit through a divination board at his friend's house, saying that he was in the underworld suing a certain official. The friend was surprised and asked, "That official was a virtuous and upright person, and he served as the governor-general of the two provinces of Jiangsu and Anhui for more than ten years before this case. Why is he being sued without cause?" The spirit wrote, "This case is not a recent matter. When it first began, if one official had been dismissed and one or two subordinates had been exiled, the problem could have been resolved before it even started. That official cultivated a reputation for being broad-minded and kind, but he neglected the problem, allowing it to fester and eventually burst open, leading to our downfall. We caused harm to the people and corrupted the state, and we cannot blame the current enforcers of the law. To trace the root of the disaster, if not this official's lawsuit, then whose lawsuit is it?" After writing this, the divination board remained motionless, and no one knew what the final judgment would be in the underworld. The Golden Man said, "A small stream that is not blocked will eventually become a river; a hair that is not trimmed will grow into a tree. The ancient sages had far-sighted vision. What the ghost said is not without reason."

In a certain village, there was a man named Jiang who was about to die. He instructed his wife not to remarry. The wife wept and promised. Later, a woman of great beauty offered a high price to buy her as a concubine. As she was about to get on the carriage, the dog she kept suddenly stood on its hind legs and barked furiously. With both paws, it grabbed and bit her face, splitting her nose and blinding one of her eyes. With her appearance ruined, the buyer abandoned her. Afterward, no one else coveted her. This was an event that occurred during the years of the Kangxi emperor's reign, and some old people still remember it. Everyone said: "How righteous this dog is! It loves its master through virtue; how wise this dog is! It can attack the root of the problem." I say that the dog could not possibly have seen this; it must be the ghost of its deceased master possessing it. Mr. Aitang once returned home after drinking at night, and his horse suddenly became frightened and galloped away. The grass and trees were dense, and the ditches and ridges were uneven, nearly causing him to fall several times. Suddenly, a person emerged from the side of the road, one hand pulling the reins and the other supporting him down. They said: "My mother was once rescued by you; now I am saving you from the danger of a broken bone." When he asked for the person's name, they vanished in an instant. Mr. Aitang recalled that he had never experienced such a thing in his life, not knowing why the ghost would say so. Is this the greatest merit that the Buddha's teachings speak of as being done without thought?

Zhang Fu, a peddler from the town of Dulín, was engaged in his livelihood when one day he got into a dispute with a wealthy and influential man from the village. The wealthy man struck him with a cane and pushed him off a stone bridge. At that time, the river was frozen, and the sharp edges of the ice were like blades, causing his skull to break open. He was barely clinging to life. The village official, who was biased toward the wealthy man, immediately reported the incident to the authorities. The official was motivated by the prospect of financial gain, and the case was handled very quickly and harshly. Zhang Fu secretly sent his mother to speak to the wealthy man, saying, "If you pay for my life, what good will it do me? Can you take care of my elderly mother and young children? While I am still alive, I will tell the authorities that I fell off the bridge accidentally." The wealthy man agreed. Zhang Fu had a basic understanding of writing and was still able to write his own statement despite the pain. The living confession was solid and convincing, and the officials could do nothing about it. After Zhang Fu's death, the wealthy man ultimately broke his promise. His mother repeatedly appealed to the authorities, but the authorities could not act because the living confession was solid, and the wealthy man could not be held accountable. Later, while the wealthy man was drunk and traveling at night, he also fell off a horse and died by the bridge. Everyone said: "This was retribution for breaking his promise to Zhang Fu." The Grand Secretary of Yao commented: "How difficult it is to handle cases, especially in cases of murder. There are those who take the blame for others, willingly dying in place of another; there are those who bribe to settle cases, willingly selling out their loved ones. Such situations are suddenly and unexpectedly hard to question. As for the person who was killed, if he had written with his own hand that it was not this person who killed him, even if the legendary judge Gao Yao heard this, he could not convict them. If it were not for the failure to keep the promise and not pay, leading to being struck down by a ghost, then they would have been acquitted due to money. Legal cases are full of changes and complexities, there is nothing they do not contain! How can those who handle criminal cases simply rely on reason to decide?"

Master Yao An recounted that there was a man named Sun Tianqiu, who regarded wealth as life itself. With bare hands, he accumulated a fortune of a thousand gold pieces, yet even when his wife and children were starving, he treated them as strangers, enduring cold and hunger himself, and never lightly spent a single coin. When his illness grew severe, he laid out all his accumulated wealth before his pillow, touching each item with his own hands, and said, “Is it truly not mine?” Then, weeping, he passed away. Before Sun’s death, he was harassed by fox spirits. They would repeatedly steal his wealth, leaving him in dire straits, almost to the point of death, only for him to later find it again in some other place—this happened many times. There was also a man named Liu, who, like Sun, regarded wealth as life itself. He too was harassed by foxes. On one New Year’s Eve, Liu gave several gold pieces to every poor relative and friend he had. This was unlike his usual behavior, and soon it was learned that the foxes had stolen over two hundred gold pieces from his private chest, yet in return, Liu received dozens of thank-you letters. The reason for this was that Sun’s wealth had been earned through hardship, and the foxes found his stinginess strange, so they merely played a trick on him. Liu’s wealth, however, had mostly come from cunning and exploitation, so the foxes ultimately dispersed it. Their handling of the matter was indeed appropriate.

During my tenure as the educational supervisor in Fujian, my secretary from Xinhu told me that his friend, who used to be in the private office of a certain official, stayed at an ancient temple because of a meeting for inspection. The moonlight was dim, and he saw a shadow under the window of the official. The shadow lingered for a long time and slowly ascended the bell tower. Knowing in his heart that it was a ghost or demon, but having always been brave, he finally followed and went to find it. Upon arrival, the door of the bell tower was locked, and there seemed to be two people talking upstairs. One said, "Why did you return empty-handed?" The other replied, "This place rarely sees officials. Now that two officials are staying together, I will wait until it is quiet to plead my case." Just now, I secretly listened to what they said. It was either methods of flattery and conformity or methods of smoothing over and patching up. These are not sufficient to handle my affairs. Therefore, I returned disappointedly. After speaking, there seemed to be a sighing sound. Listening again, it was completely silent. The next day, the host secretly warned, indeed changing color and shaking his hand, and warned against meddling. But did not know what his grievance was. I think this friend of his had an interest in the host, therefore fabricated this story, to describe his skill at avoiding, mocked by ghosts. If we consider this matter alone, the ghost was not seen, and the words were not heard. Vague and obscure, without concrete evidence. Even if it were the Lord of Hell or Judge Bao, they would have no way to intervene, yet to blame it on the official?

Dong Qiuyuan from the Plain reported that in Haifeng, there was a temple where foxes were often present and would throw tiles and stones to tease people. A scholar rented a room in the east wing to teach his students. Upon hearing about the foxes, he went to the Buddha hall himself to scold them. For several nights, everything was quiet, and the scholar looked proud of his action. One day, the east host came to visit and during the act of bowing and greeting, a scroll suddenly fell from his sleeve. Upon picking it up, they found it was a secret play scroll. The east host remained silent, and the next day, none of the scholar's students arrived. The foxes had not harmed people, but people had provoked the foxes, and ultimately, they were punished by the foxes. A gentleman should be cautious and prepared for small people. To provoke without cause, one rarely avoids failure. In the Guan Di temple, all the statues depict Zhou General, but his name is not found in historical records. According to the Han Shou Ting Hou Temple stele by Yuan Luzhen, there is already a reference to riding the Red Hare accompanied by Zhou Cang, indicating that this tradition has been long established. The spirit of Zhou General is also very prominent. A village woman named Liu Pochē said that her husband once fell asleep drunk in front of the Guan Di altar and dreamed that Zhou General kicked him awake. A blue mark appeared on his left thigh, which took half a month to disappear.

If ghosts do not reincarnate, then from ancient times to the present, the number of ghosts increases daily, and the earth will not be able to contain them; if ghosts do reincarnate, then when one dies, another is born, and soon they change form and depart; yet in the world, there are no ghosts, and common people like merchants and farmers often reincarnate, suggesting that they all reincarnate. In desolate paths and abandoned tombs, ghosts are often seen, which suggests that some do not reincarnate. My cousin An Tianshi once fell ill, and his soul reached the underworld, where he asked the official in charge of records about this. The official said: there is reincarnation, and there is no reincarnation. Those who reincarnate fall into three categories: those who receive rewards for good deeds, those who receive punishments for sins, and those who receive rewards or punishments for debts of gratitude or resentment; those who do not reincarnate also fall into three categories: sages, virtuous people, immortals, and Buddhas do not reincarnate, those in the Avici Hell do not reincarnate, and those who have neither sin nor merit wander freely in the void, existing as long as their residual energy remains and ceasing to exist as it gradually dissipates. Like dewdrops and bubbles, they appear and disappear suddenly; like wildflowers and weeds, they flourish and wither on their own. Such beings have no place in the cycle of reincarnation. Sometimes, homeless spirits attach themselves to living people and cause pregnancy, which is called "stealing a life." Those with high moral standing who are monks or Taoists may take over another body to continue their existence, which is called "taking possession." These are all occasional transformations that do not fall within the normal rules of reincarnation. As for divine beings descending to assist enlightened times, or demons and monsters multiplying and causing widespread destruction, these are determined by cosmic forces and are not subject to the laws of reincarnation. An Tianshi, who did not believe in reincarnation, after recovering from his illness, often told people: according to what he said, it is clearly logical.

The Astrologer Yu Chuntan, skilled in fortune-telling, often made astonishingly accurate predictions. Once, during a brief journey to Xiangyang and Hanyang, he shared a boat with a scholar. They conversed amicably, but Yu Chuntan found it strange that the scholar neither slept nor ate for an extended period. He suspected the man to be a spirit or ghost. One night, he secretly questioned him. The scholar replied, "I am neither a spirit nor a ghost, but the god of literature and official rank, Wenchang. I am on a mission to Nan Yue, and we have a karmic connection, so I have been able to spend several days with you." Yu Chuntan then asked, "I consider myself quite knowledgeable in destiny, yet I once predicted that a certain person would achieve great success, but it never came to pass. As the recorder of official rank, you should know the reason." The scholar said, "That person's destiny was indeed destined for greatness, but their excessive ambition reduced it by seven-tenths." Yu Chuntan remarked, "Ambition in officialdom is a common trait. Why is the divine retribution so severe?" The scholar explained, "In officialdom, those who are strong and bold will inevitably abuse power; those who abuse power will be cruel and stubborn. Those who are weak will cling to their positions; those who cling to their positions will be cunning and deep. Moreover, those who abuse power and cling to their positions will be restless and competitive. When they compete, they will inevitably push each other out. When they push each other out, they do not consider whether someone is virtuous or not, but rather whether they belong to the same faction. They do not consider whether something is right or wrong, but only whether it benefits them or not. The consequences of this are too numerous to describe. Such behavior is worse than greed and cruelty. It not only reduces their official rank but also their lifespan." Yu Chuntan secretly remembered these words, and two years later, the person indeed died.

In the family of Mr. Zhang Xuan'er, there was a man who took a fox girl as his concubine. They built a separate quiet room for her to live in, with beds, curtains, and utensils no different from those of humans. However, she had her own maids, and did not use Zhang's slaves. The room was spotless, but after sitting for a long time, one would feel a chilling sense of coldness, and one would sometimes hear laughter and speech, but could not see her form. Zhang was a prominent family, and during family gatherings and banquets, they often asked to see her, but she refused. One day, Zhang insisted strongly, and she said: "Only a certain lady from a certain family can see me, others absolutely cannot." When they entered the room to meet her, her demeanor was elegant and refined, and she appeared to be in her thirties. When asked about the reason for the coldness in the room, she said: "The lady is just feeling anxious herself, the room is naturally like that." Later, Zhang asked her about the reason for seeing her alone, and she said: "Humans are yang, ghosts are yin, and foxes are between humans and ghosts, but they are also yin. Therefore, they always come out at night. During the day, when yang is at its peak, they dare not lightly interact with humans. The lady's yang energy has already weakened, so I can see her." Zhang was startled and said: "If you stay with me day and night, will I become weak?" She replied: "There is a special reason for this. Generally, foxes enchant humans in two ways: one is through bewitchment, and the other is through karmic affinity. Those who are bewitched by yin will fall ill if their yang is eroded, and will die if it is completely eroded. Those with karmic affinity have a natural connection, their energies will naturally harmonize, and yin and yang will merge, allowing for long-term peace. However, ninety percent of foxes enchant through bewitchment, and only ten percent through karmic affinity. Those who bewitch will also claim to have karmic affinity, but one can tell the truth from the false by whether they harm or do not harm the person. Those who later saw her indeed did not live long."

Lu and Jia lived next door to each other, with Lu being wealthy and Jia being poor. Lu wanted to buy Jia's house and set a low price for it, intending to sell it to someone else after acquiring it. He also obstructed Jia's attempts to sell the house to others. Over time, Jia became increasingly desperate due to financial difficulties and had no choice but to sell the house to Lu at a reduced price. Lu renovated the house, making it look brand new. On the day of completion, he held a grand banquet and offered sacrifices to the gods. Just as the paper money was lit, a sudden strong wind blew it onto the beams, causing a sudden fire. The flames spread rapidly, and within moments, not a single beam remained. The old house was completely destroyed by the fire. When the fire started, everyone tried to help put it out. Lu stopped them by putting his hands to his chest, indicating deep emotion or regret. In the firelight, he saw Jia's deceased father, whom he believed was responsible for the fire, and thought it futile to try to save the house. He expressed deep regret for his actions. Lu immediately called Jia's son and gave him twenty acres of fertile land as compensation. From then on, Lu changed his ways, became good, and lived a long life.

In Cangzhou, the Fan family conducted a *fújī* session, and a certain official from the river engineering department was present. The spirit that descended was Guan Yu. Suddenly, large characters were inscribed: "You have come forward, but your written confession is filled with excuses. If you are insincere even before a deity, how much more so before a person. Mistakenly harming someone is a fault, but justifying it is evil. Heaven pardons faults but punishes evil. Will it listen to your clever excuses?" The man cowered on the ground, trembling with fear, and sweated profusely. From then on, he was sullen and dissatisfied as if something had been lost, and after several months, he died of illness, yet no one knew what he had confessed to. In the village of Chuxi, there was a farming family where a woman and her mother-in-law slept together. One night, during the rain, the wall collapsed, and the mud fell in a shower. The woman, hearing the noise, quickly got up and leaned against the wall, urgently calling out for her mother-in-law to wake up. The mother-in-law, crawling down from the bed, fell to the floor, and the woman was crushed beneath her. The woman’s body lay exactly where her mother-in-law had been sleeping. She was truly a filial woman, but because she was of lowly status, no one reported her to the authorities, and her name and surname were eventually forgotten. It was said that after the woman’s death, her mother-in-law wept bitterly. One day, a neighbor told the mother-in-law: "I dreamt last night that your wife came to me in a ceremonial robe and said, 'Tell my mother-in-law not to cry for me. Because I died in her place, I have now become a deity.'" The elders of the village all said, "I also dreamt the same thing last night." Some said that if the woman truly became a deity, why didn’t she show her mother-in-law a dream? This was likely a fabrication by the villagers to console her grief. I believe that those who are loyal, filial, chaste, and righteous will become deities after death. The way of Heaven is clear, and there is a long history of evidence. This story can be believed to be true. Even if one person fabricated the story and others followed along, Heaven sees as the people see, and Heaven hears as the people hear. If the people believe it to be true, Heaven will also believe it to be true. Why should we doubt its authenticity?

Longshan Nie Songyan, a practitioner of seal engraving, traveled to the capital. He once stayed with me and told me about a friend from his hometown who befriended a fox. Whenever there was a gathering of guests and friends for a banquet, he would invite the fox to sit with them. They would eat, drink, and laugh together, just like any human. However, one could only hear the fox's voice and not see its form. Sometimes, people would force the fox to reveal itself, saying, "If you cannot see each other, how can you be considered friends?" The fox replied, "True friendship is based on the heart, not on appearance. Human hearts are unpredictable, more treacherous than mountains and rivers, and full of hidden traps and dangers. By this, the true nature of people is concealed. You all judge people based on their appearance, thinking it makes you closer, but if you cannot see their true form, you think they are distant. Is this not contradictory?" **Tian Baiyan said, "This fox has deep experience with the world."**

A respected scholar from Sùnìng named Wang De'an, who passed the imperial examination in the year 1686 (the 12th year of the Kangxi era), was once a teacher to the author's grandfather. During the summer, Wang De'an visited a friend's house and was impressed by the cool and refreshing garden and pavilions, wishing to stay overnight. However, the friend declined due to the presence of ghosts at night. Wang De'an then recounted an incident he had witnessed: a man named Cén Shēng from Jiangnan once stayed at Zhang Diézhuāng's house in Cāngzhōu. The wall was adorned with a painting of Zhōng Kuí, which was as tall as a person, and a self-striking clock was placed in front of it. Cén Shēng, drunk, went to bed without noticing these items. At midnight, he woke up from his drunkenness and found the moonlight as bright as daylight. He heard the sound of gears turning and was already very surprised. Suddenly, he saw the painting and thought it was a strange ghost. He picked up an inkstone from the desk and struck it upward, causing a loud noise that shook the windows and doors. The servants pushed open the door to see what happened and found the ink splattered everywhere, with Cén Shēng's head and face covered in ink. The clock in front of the painting, along with a jade vase and a porcelain tripod, were all broken. Everyone who heard the story laughed uncontrollably. Wang De'an commented that people often claim to see ghosts, but it is actually due to their own fear. He questioned where the ghost was and, as soon as he finished speaking, a voice suddenly responded from the corner of the wall: "The ghost is right here, planning to pay a visit at night. Please do not hit me with the inkstone again." Wang De'an remained silent and left. Later, he told his students about this incident, explaining that ghosts do not speak to people during the day and that it must have been a fox spirit. He feared that his virtue was not strong enough to withstand the supernatural and thus avoided such places. Ultimately, Wang De'an held the belief that there were no ghosts.

Burial objects, known as *mingqi*, are part of ancient Chinese funeral rites. Later generations began using paper carriages and horses for funerals. Meng Yunqing, an ancient poet, wrote in his *Gu Wan Ge* (Ancient Lament): "In the darkness, what do you need? Fulfill my wishes as a living person." This is merely to ease one's grief. However, when my eldest son, Ru Jie, was at the critical stage of his illness, his daughter burned a paper horse. Ru Jie died and then revived, saying, "My soul left my body, feeling lost and directionless. I met the old servant Wang Lian Sheng, who led a horse to send me back. I regret that the horse's foot was broken, causing a bumpy ride. The servant who burned the horse wept, saying, 'It is my fault.' Indeed, the foot was broken when the fire was lit." Another case involves my sixth aunt's mother-in-law, Chang Shi, who, in her final moments, mumbled to herself, "I just went to see the new house, which was quite good, but the eastern wall was damaged, what can I do?" Those attending to her illness went to check her coffin and found that the left side was rotten, with a small hole. Neither the craftsmen nor the supervisors had noticed this. Mr. Li You Dan recounted a story about a poor scholar who failed the imperial exams and burned his exam papers, appealing to the Temple of the God of Literature. That night, he dreamed that a god told him, "You have studied for half your life, yet you still do not know that success and failure are determined by fate?" I once served under my late father, Yao An Gong, and mentioned this story to him. My father was displeased and said, "You Dan, who is preparing for the imperial exams, can pass on this story. But you, who are in charge of the exams, should not spread such talk." In the Hall of Gathering Brightness, there is a couplet written by the Chancellor Xiong Xiao Gan: "The glorious regulations are always kept in my sleeves, only the white jade tablet. The clear documents, where can the red robe be seen before the curtain?" Have you not seen this?

Li Yudian, a respected elder from Haiyang, recounted that there were two students studying at a Buddhist temple. One night, as they were engaged in intimate behavior, a large, luminous mirror suddenly appeared on the wall, about a zhang in diameter, as bright as day, and clear enough to see every detail. They heard a voice from the eaves say, "Buddhism is vast and does not harbor anger, but look at your reflection in the mirror and see what you look like." I thought that with a secret and intimate agreement, there must be no one around, so who saw the reflection? The two students would not have spoken to themselves, so how did they hear the voice? Nevertheless, the event is reasonable and should not be dismissed as impossible. Li Yudian also recounted that there was an old scholar who set up his study in an abandoned garden. One night, he heard the sound of recitation from outside the wall, followed by the sound of debate, then quarreling, then cursing, and finally the sound of fighting. The garden behind was empty and uninhabited, and he realized that the commotion was caused by ghosts. As he trembled with fear, the argument had moved to the window. One ghost spoke with great confidence, saying, "He criticized my writing, and I feel deeply wronged. Now, let us both seek correction from you, sir." The ghost then recited several hundred words, striking a beat with his hand after each sentence. The other ghost was in pain and mocking the first. The old scholar was too afraid to speak, and the first ghost spoke sharply, "Sir, what do you think?" The old scholar hesitated for a long time and then said, "A chicken rib is not worth your punch." The first ghost laughed and left, while the other ghost continued to come and go outside the window, breathing heavily. The commotion ended at dawn. This story was told to me by Fa Huangchang of Jiaozhou, and I believe it might be a parable.

Meng Shengwen, a man from Tianjin with a distinguished appearance and sharp wit, was greatly favored by Mr. Zhang Shilin. One day, after returning from a tomb-sweeping ceremony, he met Meng Shengwen at a roadside tavern. He saw a new poem written on the wall, which read: "The gentle breeze stirs, and the spring clothes sway. Strolling leisurely in search of fragrance, I return leisurely. The redness of peach blossoms reflects a person's smile, Willow branches entwine as swallows fly in pairs. Wandering along winding paths, I admire fragrant grass, Feeling melancholic as the sun sets behind tall trees. Remember this place where I lingered today, The thatched door is beside the wine shop." He asked Meng Shengwen about the poem, but Meng Shengwen evaded the question. When pressed, he admitted that he had just seen a beautiful woman by the roadside, whose beauty was unparalleled, and he stayed there hoping she would appear again. Mr. Zhang asked where she was, and Meng Shengwen pointed to the location. Mr. Zhang was greatly startled, saying, "This is the ancestral burial ground of a certain family, which has been neglected for a long time. How could there be such a person?" They went together to investigate, and indeed found a mound of grass with no trace of human activity. During my time in Urumqi, one day, I received a report that a military officer named Wang had been dispatched to transport military equipment to Ili, leaving his wife alone at home. That day, after noon, the door did not open, and she did not respond when called. There must be some other reason. I ordered the Diwuhu Tongzhi (County Magistrate) Mu Jintai to go and investigate. They broke down the door and entered, finding two people lying naked, embracing each other, both with their bellies cut open and dead. The man's origin was unknown, and no one recognized him. After questioning the neighbors, there was no clear lead, and the case was to be treated as an unresolved mystery. That night, the woman's body suddenly groaned, and the guards were startled. They looked, and she had come back to life. The next day, she could speak. She confessed that she had been in love with the man since childhood and continued to meet him secretly after marriage. Later, when she followed her husband to guard the frontier in the Western Regions, the man could not forget her and came to find her again. Just as he arrived at the door, he was led inside.

Therefore, the neighbors were all unaware. Considering the temporary meeting and eventual separation, they made an agreement to die together. When they received the knife cuts, they experienced extreme pain and lost consciousness, like waking from a dream, and their souls had left their bodies. They urgently sought that person, but did not know where they had gone. They stood alone in the desert, with white grass and yellow clouds, boundless in all directions. Just when hesitating, they were bound by a ghost and taken to a government office, where they were severely questioned and humiliated. They said that although shameless, their life was not yet over. They were scolded and given one hundred strokes with an iron stick, and then sent back. The stick was made of iron, and they could not endure the pain, and fainted. When they gradually regained consciousness, they revived. Looking at their thighs, indeed they had overlapping marks from the stick. The commander-in-chief, Ba Gong, said: "This person has already been punished by the underworld; immoral crimes can be exempt from severe punishment." I, in my miscellaneous poems from Urumqi, have said: "Swans and mandarin ducks ultimately do not fly together, the old wish of heaven and earth is broken. White grass swaying, burying the coffin of a traveler, a lifetime of sorrow in the Huashan region." This is a reflection on this matter.

Zhu Qingyun said, once he was strolling by the waterside with Gao Xiyuan. At that time, the spring ice had just begun to melt, and the clear, green water spread out. Gao remarked, "I recall a line from a late Tang poet: 'Purple like fish scales, green like duck feathers.' Though no word directly mentions the spring water, the image of the sunny waves sliding like a jade tablet is vividly before our eyes. I regret not remembering the poet's name." Zhu pondered deeply but did not respond. Suddenly, he heard a voice behind an old willow tree say, "This is actually a line from the early Tang poet Liu Xiyi, not a late Tang poet." He rushed to see but found no one there. Zhu was startled and said, "Ghosts appear in broad daylight!" Gao smiled and said, "Such a ghost, seeing it is quite good, but perhaps it doesn't want to be seen." They bowed three times to the tree and continued their walk. Upon returning, Zhu checked Liu Xiyi's poems and indeed found these two lines. I happened to mention this to Dai Zhen, who then told me about two scholars arguing under a candlelight, debating the Zhou and Xia calendars, with intense back-and-forth. Suddenly, a sigh came from outside the window, saying, "The author of the Zuo Zhuan was a Zhou person; he could not possibly not know the Zhou calendar. Why do the two gentlemen need to argue so much?" Looking out the window, only a little servant boy was sound asleep. Considering these two incidents, Confucian scholars often engage in textual criticism, discussing ancient matters and writing extensively, sometimes up to fourteen thousand words. Who knows if there are no people mocking from the unseen realm?

聂松岩说，即墨的于生，骑着一头驴前往京城，在半路上休息在高岗上，将驴拴在树上，倚靠石头小睡片刻，忽然看到驴抬起头四处张望，深沉地叹息道：“已经几十年没有到过这个地方了，青山依旧，但村庄已经不再是旧时的模样了。”于生一向好奇，听到驴的叹息后立刻起身说：“这是宋处宗的长鸣鸡啊。”他每天骑着驴一起交谈，不再担心长途的寂寞。他向驴拱手致意并说话，驴却只顾吃草不理睬，他反复劝说，约定与驴结为忘形之交，驴也好像听不见。他生气地狠狠鞭打驴，驴跳起来狂吼，最终还是无法开口说话，结果断了一条腿，被卖到了屠夫的店铺。他只好徒步返回，这件事真是极其可笑。难道是在梦中听错了吗？还是这头驴前世有冤孽，被某种东西附身，以此激怒他而将其杀死呢？

San Shu Yi Nan Gong had a strong servant named Bi Si, who was skilled in archery hunting and could draw a bow with the strength of ten men. He constantly hunted quails in the wild. All quail hunters must do so at night. First, they insert straw into the ground to resemble rows of crops, and then spread a net on top. They use a cow horn to make a curved tube and imitate the sound of quails by blowing it. When the quails have gathered, they first slightly startle them, so they gradually retreat into the straw. Then, they startle them loudly, causing them to fly up in a panic, and they all touch the net. When blowing the pipe, the sound is mournful and sobbing, often mistakenly attracting ghosts. Therefore, they must build a fire to protect themselves and carry weapons to prepare for it. One night under the bright moon, they saw an old man come and bow, saying, "I am a fox. My descendants have had conflicts with the foxes of the north village, and the whole clan fought with weapons. Their side captured one of my daughters, and every time they fight, they tie her hands behind her back and drive her out to humiliate me. I also captured one of their concubines, as a form of retribution."

Thus, the enmity grew stronger, and they agreed to fight a decisive battle tonight here. Hearing of your righteousness and bravery, they asked for a helping hand, and would be grateful until death. The one holding the iron ruler is that one, and the one holding the knife is me. Bi Fang, hearing the story, happily went along, and entered the dense undergrowth. The two armies met, and the two foxes fought fiercely without stopping, until they embraced and fought hand-to-hand. Bi Fang, having carefully aimed, drew the bow and shot, hitting the fox from the north village, causing it to fall. Unaware that the bow was strong and the arrow sharp, it passed through the abdomen, and the old man was killed through the armpit. The two armies, in panic, seized the bodies and fled, abandoning prisoners. Bi Fang untied the two foxes and told them: "Pass this on to your clan; the two families' victories and defeats are balanced, and the feud can be resolved." Before this, the north village heard the sound of battle every night, but from now on it became silent. This is similar to the story of Li Bing. However, Li Bing fought the river god to protect against disasters, while these foxes fought out of private grudges, fighting endlessly until both were injured. Is this not something that should be stopped?

Yao An Gong, during his time in Yunnan, had officials who reported that under the mandarin orange tree in his official residence, there was a woman in red attire standing gracefully under the moonlight. When she saw people, she would slowly disappear into the ground. The officials considered excavating to see what was beneath the tree. Yao An Gong carried a cup of wine to pour under the tree and made a wish: "You hide when you see people, which shows you have no ill intent. Why do you need to repeatedly appear, leading to your own harm?" After this, she no longer appeared. There was also a spacious study that had been unused for a long time. Uncle An Gong Wu Zhang, who was with Yao An Gong in Yunnan, once slept naked in the study one summer day. In his dream, a person bowed and said: "Although we belong to different realms, there are still distinctions between genders. Why did you not act with propriety?" He woke up suddenly and decided not to return to the study. Yao An Gong once said: "The spirit under the tree could be reasoned with, and the spirit in the study could also be reasoned with. This region is remote, located among countless mountains, with simple and unrefined customs, untouched and unrefined. Therefore, even supernatural beings here are simple and good-natured."

Once, when I was two or three years old, I saw four or five small children dressed in colorful clothes and wearing gold bracelets, playing with me. They all called me brother, and it seemed they loved me very much. After a while, they all disappeared. Later, I told this to my father, the Marquis of Yao'an. He thought about it for a long time and then sighed, saying: "Your late mother was grieved by not having a son, so she often had the nun and servant tie colored silk to clay figures from the temple, bring them back and place them in the bedroom, give them each a milk name, feed them fruit and snacks daily, and treat them no differently from feeding a child. After her death, I had them buried in an empty courtyard behind the house, and those must be the figures. Fearing that later they might become evil, I planned to dig them out, but after many years, the location was lost." She was my late mother's sister. After the annual memorial service for the deceased, my mother-in-law took a nap, and in her dream, she saw my late mother push her with her hand and say: "My sister is too careless; a sharp blade should not be entrusted to child's play." She awoke in shock, and then I was sitting beside her, pulling out the dagger from my father's leather belt. Thus, I realized that the soul returning to receive the sacrifice was indeed a real event. This is why the ancients treated the dead as if they were alive.

My paternal cousin Wang Bibo's wife passed away, and a fortune-teller claimed that on a certain day at the *zi ke* hour, the soul would return in a ritual known as *hui sha*. The entire family fled the house to avoid it. A thief, pretending to be the ghost, climbed over the wall and entered. Just as he opened the chest and seized hairpins and earrings, another thief, also pretending to be the ghost, arrived. The ghostly wails grew louder and closer, and the first thief, frightened, fled out. They met in the courtyard, each thinking the other was the real ghost. Both were seized with terror, lost their composure, and collapsed to the ground. At dawn, the family returned in mourning, only to suddenly see them lying there. They were greatly startled, and upon careful inspection, realized they were thieves. They gave them ginger soup to revive them, then bound them in ghost costumes and sent them to the authorities. People gathered along the road to watch, and none could help but laugh heartily. Based on this incident, the belief in *hui sha* must surely be false. Yet I have personally seen the signs of *hui sha* many times. The nature of ghosts and spirits remains obscure, and ultimately, one cannot know how they truly are. Zhu Tianmen of Yidu once said that in the summer of the year Jiazi, he and several friends gathered by the side of Ming Lake in the evening, hiring courtesans to accompany them with wine. As they were just getting into the height of their drinking, one of the courtesans, who had never learned to read, suddenly took up a brush and wrote a quatrain: A night of drizzling rain, The high tower fears the morning cold. Have the peach blossoms withered? Call the maid to draw the curtain and look. She then tossed the poem before one of her companions. After reading it, the man turned pale and fell to the ground; the courtesan also collapsed. After a while, the courtesan recovered, but the man did not. Later, he asked all his close associates, but no one could explain the cause.

In the years 1743 and 1744, there was a person practicing feng shui or divination by乩 who came from Zhengding. This person did not talk about good or bad fortune, only made paintings and calligraphy, and one might suspect it is a fake. However, when one saw the colorful landscape scroll he made for Cao Mutang and the drunken Zhong Kui portrait, the brushwork and ink were not vulgar. One also saw a couplet he gave to Dong Qujiang, which said: "Golden-hearted guests are still warm, white-haired and returning home, dreams more often." This couplet very much resembles Dong Qujiang's character. A tenant farmer's wife was very fierce and often scolded wind and rain, and scolded gods and spirits. When she disagreed with someone, she rolled up her sleeves and exposed her arms, carried two clothes-beating sticks, and shouted and jumped around like a fierce female tiger. One day, on a rainy day, she went out to steal wheat, and suddenly a windstorm and heavy hailstones like goose eggs hit her, injuring her and causing her to fall to the ground. Then, a wind blew a five-dou basket and it fell on her head, but she was not killed. Does heaven also fear her arrogance? Someone said: although she is violent, she treats her grandmother well, and when her grandmother scolds her, she immediately submits, and when her grandmother slaps her on the cheek, she kneels and accepts it. Therefore, there is a reason why she was not killed. Confucius said: "Filial piety is the constant law of heaven and the proper order of earth. Isn't that so?"

In the summer of the year of the Pig, a dragon fell in the north of Gaochuan, and many villagers saw it with their own eyes. Mr. Yao'an ordered his carriage to go and see, but the dragon had already ridden the wind and rain away. The traces of its winding and grasping movements, which trampled and ruined two mu of crops, were still clearly visible. The dragon is a divine creature; why did it fall? Some say it was sent to rain and made a mistake, leading to its exile by heaven. According to the world, dragons can cause rain, but Song scholars say that rain is the qi of heaven and earth, not caused by dragons. I believe that the rites say that timely rain is sent from heaven, and mountains and rivers produce clouds. Therefore, the Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals says that clouds touch the stones and emerge, meet at a distance of one inch, and can rain over the whole world in a single morning, only the clouds of Mount Tai. This is the foundation of the Song scholars' view. The Commentary on the Words of the Yi says that clouds follow the dragon. Therefore, the rain-praying method of Dong Zhongshu involves summoning the dragon with a clay dragon. This is the basis of the common belief. Generally, there is rain from heaven and rain from dragons. The rain that comes slowly with clouds and falls gently is rain from heaven; the rain that comes with strong winds and thunder and does not last long is rain from dragons. Observing those who touch or offend the dragon's pool, they can instantly cause wind and rain. Can the qi of heaven and earth come together so quickly? Those who wash and recite Buddhist mantras can also instantly cause wind and rain. Can the qi of heaven and earth be so fixed in time? Therefore, both meanings must be presented together for the theory to be complete. Adhering rigidly to one view is a failure to understand the changes.

A villager named Wang, who was plowing in the fields, became tired and rested his head on a piece of earth to sleep. Suddenly, he saw a sedan chair coming from the west, accompanied by many servants and horses. The person seated in the sedan chair was his deceased uncle, Yi Nan Gong. Surprised that his uncle, who was ill, was traveling, Wang hurriedly approached to inquire about his health. They talked for a long time, after which the sedan chair headed northeast. Upon returning, Wang learned that his uncle had already passed away. The number of servants and horses he had seen matched the number of paper items burned. Shen Chonggui's wife, a servant, heard the story directly from Wang. A month later, Wang also fell ill and died, leading to the belief that meeting a ghost during the day was a sign of a bad omen or decline.

My third daughter was engaged to the son of Ge Xianzhou, the Taifu, and was ten years old when she died on the summer solstice of the year Gengxu. The day before, her illness had worsened, and at that time, I was on official duty at Fangze. My daughter suddenly spoke to herself, saying, "Today is the eighth day; I will leave at the third watch tomorrow, and I will still be able to see my father." When I asked how she knew, she closed her eyes and said nothing. On the ninth day, the ceremony was completed, and I returned home, and indeed, I saw her death. At the time of her death, there was a foreign clock hanging on the wall, and it precisely rang eight times, which is also strange. The cook named Yang Bo, who had a basic knowledge of writing, was with Yao An Gong in Yunnan when he suddenly dreamed of two ghosts holding a gao ticket to arrest him. The name on the ticket was Yang Bo, and he argued, saying, "My name is Yang Yi, not Yang Bo; you must have made a mistake." The two ghosts both said, "There is a dot above the character, which is a simplified form of the character '义'." Yang Yi argued again, saying, "I have never seen the character '义' written this way; it must still be the character '皏,' with a mistake of a drop of ink." The two ghosts could not force him and left. The person sleeping beside him heard his mumbling, and it was very clear. Soon, Yao An Gong retired and returned home, and Yang Yi followed him to Pingyi. He dreamed again of two ghosts holding a ticket to come, and the ticket clearly wrote the character "杨义" in regular script. Yang Yi still refused, saying, "I have already returned to the north, and I should belong to the City God of Zhili; why should the City God of Yunnan arrest me?" They argued loudly for a long time. The person sleeping beside him called him, and he woke up, saying to himself, "The two ghosts were very angry and seemed to not give up." The next day, he walked to the Shengjing Archway in the south of Yunnan, and indeed, his horse stumbled, and he fell to the ground and died.

While I was stationed in Urumqi, I kept several dogs. In the year 辛卯 (1761), after being granted a ring and returning eastward, I had a black dog named Si'er, who clung to me with deep affection, refusing to leave no matter how I tried to send him away. We eventually reached the capital together. Along the way, the dog guarded my traveling trunk with great vigilance. Unless I was present, even the servants could not take anything from it. When someone approached, it would stand on its hind legs and bark angrily. One day, we passed through the seven mountain passes of Biezhuan (七达坂), which are known for their steep and winding paths, called a natural barrier. There were four vehicles, with half on the north side of the pass and half on the south side. It was already dusk, and we could not all pass through. The dog lay alone on the summit, watching over the vehicles, and would rush to investigate any shadows. I composed two poems to commemorate this: "Return home without needing you to send letters, endure wind and rain, and follow me. When the servants are deeply asleep at night, guard the several vehicles traveling east. Every day, endure hunger while walking through the empty mountains, traverse the rugged, snow-covered paths for a hundred and twenty miles. I have no official position, so what do I have to be attached to? Pity that you are so devoted." These poems record the actual events.

After arriving in the capital for over a year, one night he died from poisoning. Some say that the slaves were troubled by his strict night watch, so they plotted to kill him and attributed the death to a thief, which seems likely. I collected and buried his bones, intending to build a tomb and inscribe it as the Tomb of the Four Righteous Dogs. I carved a stone monument, sculpting the images of the four slaves who went out to the border, kneeling before the tomb, each with their names engraved on their chests: Zhao Changming, Yu Lu, Liu Chenggong, and Qi Laiwang. Some say that placing these four slaves beside the dog, the dog might not care. I then stopped. I only inscribed the title of the room where the slaves lived, calling it the Dog Master Hall. When Scholar Zhai first gave me this dog, the night before, I dreamed that my former servant Song Yu knelt and said, "Thinking of the master who has served far away in the army, now comes to serve, the next day I got this dog, clearly knowing it was Song Yu reborn." However, when Song Yu was alive, he was cunning and deceitful, the leader among the servants. Why is he a dog now, and why does he show loyalty? Is he aware that he fell due to his evil deeds, repenting and turning to good deeds? Can also be called good at correcting mistakes.

The divine can transform into form, so the foxes with spiritual powers can move back and forth through a small gap, but it is merely transforming its own form. Ceng Mengquan said that a servant woman in his household was bewitched by a fox, who would strip her of her clothes at night, leaving her naked, and carry her out through the window, placing her under the eaves, where they would play and frolic together. When her husband brandished a knife to confront the fox, the door lock could not be opened, or if he closed the door to wait, the fox could still tightly shut it, scolding angrily from inside the window. One day, he secretly hid a bird gun, intending to shoot the fox through the window, but when the time came, the gun was nowhere to be found. The next day, he found it inside a money cabinet. The gun was nearly five feet long, but the cabinet opening was only a little over a foot wide, so it is unclear how it got inside, indicating that the fox can also transform into other forms. The Song scholars often spoke of investigating things, but such phenomena, how can they be reasoned about? Yao An Gong once said: "Foxes dwell in ruins and cemeteries, but they can transform into houses and buildings, which people see as real, but it is unknown how the foxes perceive themselves." "Foxes have fur and skin, but they can transform into cosmetics, which people see as real, but it is unknown how the foxes perceive themselves." "It is unknown how other foxes perceive the transformations of this fox." This is truly something that cannot be investigated or reasoned about.

Patsang Dondup, the captain of Urumqi, reported that when this region was initially pacified, he once patrolled deep into the southern mountains — since Urumqi is north of the Tian Shan, the mountains are referred to as the southern mountains. As dusk approached, he seemed to see human figures across a ravine, suspecting them to be raiders (玛哈沁). In the Oirat (Ööled) language, the term "玛哈沁" refers to raiders, and this term was adopted in military circles. He hid in the dense undergrowth to closely observe the scene and saw a person in military attire sitting on a large flat rock, with several soldiers standing guard, all looking fierce. Their speech was too far away to be understood, but he saw a soldier emerge from a stone cave and call out six beautiful and fair-skinned women. They were all dressed in fine silk, with their hands bound behind their backs, trembling and kneeling. The soldier led them one by one in front of the seated person, stripped them of their lower garments, and whipped them until they bled, their cries of pain echoing through the forest. After finishing, the soldier left immediately. The six women, trembling, knelt to send him off, but they could not see his shadow, and then they wept and returned to the cave. The distance was within the range of an arrow, but the ravine was deep and the cliffs steep, with no way to reach it. They sent a strong archer to shoot arrows at a tree on the opposite cliff. Two arrows stuck in the tree, serving as a marker. The next day, after circling around for dozens of miles, they found the location. The cave entrance was covered with dust. They entered with torches, and the cave was winding and about four zhang deep, with no signs of any passage. He did not know what deity he had encountered the previous day or what the women were. This was the strangest thing he had ever seen in his life. Consulting the *Taiping Guangji*, he found a story of an old monk seeing celestial beings pursuing flying demons (飞天野叉), and the demons were beautiful women. What Patsang Dondup saw seemed to be of the same kind, did it?

In the kitchen, the six domestic animals are a common sight, a usual practice. However, killing them excessively turns it into a sinful act. Killing someone who should not be killed can also bring retribution. According to the military officer in Urumqi, General Ru Daye, the commander of Jimsa, he sent a servant into the mountains to search for snow lotus, but the servant became lost and could not return. One night, the general dreamed that the servant came back covered in blood, saying: "In a certain mountain, I encountered the Mahaqin, who ate me in pieces. My remains are still under the third pine tree south of the bridge. Please go and find them." The commander sent a military officer to search the tree, and indeed, there was blood all over the place. However, upon inspection, they were all sheep bones. It turned out that the stable hands had stolen an official sheep and killed it there. There was still a suspicion that the servant might have died elsewhere. Two days later, the servant was found by a hunter and brought back. It was then known that the sheep had taken on the servant's spirit to expose the stable hands' crime.

Lao, an elderly woman from Qing County, served as a cook in my household during the years 1777 and 1778 under the reign of Emperor Qianlong. She recounted that in her village, there was a farmer who lived near an ancient tomb. The farmer kept two oxen that would occasionally climb onto the tomb and trample it. One night, the oxen dreamt of someone scolding them. The villagers, being ignorant and crude, paid no heed to the dream. Soon after, strange occurrences began in the farmer's home. At night, two large creatures, as big as oxen, would appear, kicking, jumping, and throwing things around, breaking jars and pots in the courtyard. This happened for several nights. One night, a large stone roller was moved onto the roof and fell with a loud crash, sending flames flying and shattering a stone used for pounding clothes into several pieces. The farmers became very angry and borrowed many firearms to defend themselves. When the creatures appeared, the farmers attacked them together, and both creatures fell down with a single sound. The farmers were overjoyed and quickly lit a fire to reveal the creatures, which turned out to be their own oxen. After this, the strange occurrences ceased, and the household gradually declined. They believed their oxen had become evil spirits and caused their own destruction, a skillful manipulation indeed. However, it was also due to the oxen's wild and fierce nature that they could be manipulated so easily.

In Xian County, east of Shuangta Village, there were two elderly monks who shared a hermitage. One evening, two elderly Taoist priests knocked on the door to ask for lodging. The monks initially refused, but the Taoists said, "Buddhism and Taoism may be two religions, but monastic life is one. Why are your horizons so narrow?" The monks then allowed them to stay. The next day, the door would not open, and calls went unanswered. Neighbors climbed over the wall to look inside and found that the four people were all gone, yet the monks' room was undisturbed. The Taoists' travel bags, which contained several dozen gold coins, were also there. Everyone was greatly alarmed and reported it to the authorities. The county magistrate, Mr. Su, came to investigate. A shepherd boy said there was a dead person in a dry well more than ten miles south of the village. Mr. Su rushed to see it and found four bodies piled on top of each other. However, none of them were injured. Mr. Su said, "Nothing was missing, so it was not theft. They were all old, so it was not adultery. They were just passing by for lodging, so it was not a grudge. They had no injuries, so it was not murder. How could four people die together? How could four bodies be moved together? How could they leave the locked door and travel so far to the dry well? This is beyond reason and logic. I can interrogate people, but not ghosts. Since there is no one to interrogate, the case should be concluded as a mystery." He directly reported to the higher authorities, who also had no objections, and the case was resolved as he suggested. Mr. Sheng, a capable county magistrate from Yingshan, once said, "When I arrived in Xian, I heard about this case. I thought about it for years, but could not solve it. When faced with such cases, one should accept that they cannot be solved. If one tries to be clever, it will lead to many problems." People say Mr. Su is foolish, but I actually admire his simplicity.

The Left Tradition says, "Deep mountains and great marshes truly give birth to dragons and snakes." Little slave Yu Bao was the son of an exile from Urumqi. He was initially assigned to the military garrison farm in Tengnagel. Once, he ventured into a valley to chase after a lost sheep and saw a giant snake as thick as a pillar, coiled at the top of a high hill, basking in the sun and drying its scales. Its entire body was adorned with a dazzling array of colors, like a pile of embroidered silk. On the top of its head was a horn about a foot long. When a flock of pheasants flew by, the snake opened its mouth and sucked them in, even though they were several dozen feet away, and they all fell gracefully, as if arrows were being shot into a target. He knew that the sheep had been swallowed by the snake, so he took advantage of the moment when it was not seen and followed the stream to escape back. He was so terrified that he almost lost his soul. The military officer Wu Tulin then explained, "This snake is extremely poisonous, but its horn can detoxify, and it is what is known as the 'absorbing stone.' Those who see this snake should carry several pounds of realgar, burn it on the windward side, and it will immediately become weak and unable to move. Take its horn, saw it into pieces.
