At the beginning of a boil or abscess, place a piece on the top of the sore, and it will then stick like a magnet to iron, and cannot be removed. Wait until the toxins are absorbed out, and it will then fall off by itself. Place it in human milk to extract the toxins, and it can be used again. Those with mild toxicity will turn the milk green, those with moderate toxicity will turn it blue-gray, and those with severe toxicity will turn it blackish-purple. Those whose milk turns blackish-purple will absorb it after four or five times, and then it is completely absorbed. One or two times will cure it. I remember that my cousin Mao Yuan had an absorbing stone at home, which was very effective in treating boils and abscesses. Its nature was neither wood nor stone, and until now I realized it was a snake's horn."

The Zhengyi Taoist priest is capable of creating talismans for inducing labor, which are commonly found in many households. However, this seems strange, as it is not related to the usual Taoist practices of summoning rain or driving away demons. It is difficult to understand why a Taoist priest would be involved in such matters. Some say that Taoist texts mention two demons, one called *Yuwang* and the other *Jingyi*, who can cause difficult childbirth. If one writes their names on paper, the demons will depart. Could the talisman be designed to control these two demons? After all, there are countless women in childbirth across the four seas and within the land, numbering as many as the grains of sand in the Ganges. Are there only these two demons in the world? Or does each place and each household have its own pair of demons, all named *Yuwang* and *Jingyi*? If there are only these two demons, they would have to travel extensively to cause harm, which would be quite a burden for them. If each place and each household has its own pair of demons, then there would be far more times when women are not giving birth than when they are. With trillions of demons, they would have nothing to do but wait idly for women to give birth, which would make them seem redundant and useless. Another explanation is that there are many reasons for difficult childbirth, and *Yuwang* and *Jingyi* are just one of them. We cannot be certain that they are the cause, nor can we be certain that they are not. Therefore, the practice of summoning and investigating the cause is undertaken.

This is also one interpretation. However, considering a possible event that might occur, and calling generals to test and examine every day, if the general arrives and finds a ghost, he will drive it away; if the general arrives and it is not a ghost, he will return empty-handed. Will this not be disrespectful to the gods? Even if the gods do not mind disrespect, and one talisman for one general, this means training countless generals to wait for the beacon fires of King You. The Supreme Deity will add another god with one talisman for a real person, like all the talismans for one general. Then this general, even with a thousand hands and a thousand eyes, will be tired from running around, and the Supreme Deity will set up countless incarnations of gods for the task of chasing shadows and catching the wind. Can they or cannot they? However, the senior Mr. Zhao Luquan has a talisman passed down from the Ming Dynasty, drawn by the True Man of High Conduct, who refined his vigorous energy. When tested, it works as effectively as an echo. Mr. Zhao Luquan is not a liar, this is beyond my ability to measure.

A popular belief holds that Zhang Sanfeng's servants are all gods and spirits. Once, during a conversation with a guest, the tea server was identified as the Thunder God. The guest showed no respect, and upon returning, thunderstorms followed, nearly causing an accident. This is a tale from Qi and Dong, a legendary and unreliable story. I recall one day when I accompanied Zhang Sanfeng to a ritual, and as we were about to enter, he forgot his ceremonial bead and asked to borrow one from me. I joked, "The Thunder God's commands are executed the fastest. Why not send one to fetch it?" Zhang Sanfeng laughed at my jest. However, during my time in the governor's office in Fuzhou, an old servant named Wei Cheng caused disturbances every night. One night, drunk and angry, I shouted, "My master has always been friends with the Heavenly Master. Tomorrow, I'll send a letter, and the Thunder God will arrive immediately!" And with that, the disturbances ceased. Thus, even fox spirits and ghosts are familiar with this saying. As for my servant Wang Tingzuo, one night he returned from Cangzhou on horseback. Upon reaching Chang's brick river, the horse suddenly refused to move forward. In the darkness, he saw a large tree blocking his path, something he had never encountered before. He reined the horse to pass by, but the tree began to rotate around him, spinning in all directions. After a few moments, the horse grew tired, and Wang Tingzuo became increasingly disoriented. Soon, two carpenters named Guo and Han, whom he knew, came from the east. They saw Wang Tingzuo standing in a trance-like state and were puzzled. Wang Tingzuo pointed to the tree and told them about it. At that time, the two carpenters were already drunk. They shouted together, "The Buddha hall is missing a beam, and we've been searching for a large tree. Now that we've found one, we can't let it go!" They each grabbed an axe and saw and rushed to cut down the tree. Suddenly, the tree transformed into a whirlwind and disappeared. The *Yinfu Jing* (Book of the Hidden Symbol) states, "The control of birds lies in their qi." Wooden spirits fear craftsmen, just as fox spirits and ghosts fear hunters. The accumulated power and presence of craftsmen are enough to intimidate and subdue them. It is not necessary for them to have superior strength.

Su Zi Yu of Ningjin reported: In the summer of the Dingmao year, a mother-in-law and daughter-in-law from the Zhang family were harvesting wheat together. Just as they had gathered it into a pile, a great whirlwind came from the west and blew it all to pieces. The woman became angry and threw her sickle at the wind, spilling several drops of blood onto the ground. As they were about to search for what had been lost, the woman leaned against a tree and suddenly seemed faint. Her soul was seized by unseen forces and taken to a shrine. The deity there angrily scolded her, saying: "How dare you, a fierce woman, injure my official? Take your punishment at once!" The woman, known for her strong character, boldly replied: "Our family is poor and has only planted a few acres of wheat to sustain our lives. Under the scorching sun, my mother-in-law and I toiled hard to cut the wheat, only for it to be scattered by this strange wind. We believed it to be a demon or spirit, so I threw the sickle at it, not realizing that I had injured your great king’s messenger. Furthermore, the messengers have their own official roads to travel—why should they cross through the people’s fields and destroy their wheat? To be punished for this is truly unacceptable." The deity then nodded in thought and said: "Her words are just; she may be sent away." The woman awoke, and the whirlwind returned, gathering the wheat back into one place. When recounting this story, Wang Renshi of Wuqiao said: "We do not know what kind of deity this was, but it did not unjustly favor its favorites. It may be called upright." He continued: "Yet, to first listen to a superficial complaint and nearly have the woman punished—calling this wise, however, is not yet so." Ge Litian of Jingzhou said: "The woman appealed her grievance, and the deity immediately discerned it. This is also wisdom." He added: "But if the one who laments does so with sorrow, and the one who listens is confused, what would you call that?" Su Zi Yu replied: "Renshi never stops blaming people; Litian’s words are correct."

Governor Zhang Baonan of Sichuan was the maternal uncle of the author's grandmother. His mother, the author's great-grandmother, had a fondness for turtle dishes. One day, a cook caught a large turtle. As soon as the cook cut off its head, a small person about four or five inches tall emerged from the turtle's neck, running around the turtle. The cook was so startled that he fell to the ground; the others helped him up, but the small person had already disappeared. When the turtle was cut open, the small person was found inside, already dead. The author's grandmother once examined it. The author's mother, who was still young at the time, also witnessed it. The small person was adorned like the figures in the tribute picture of the Hui people, with a yellow hat, blue robe, red belt, and black boots, all with clear and distinct patterns like a painting. Its face and limbs were also finely carved. The tutor, Mr. Cen, recognized it and said, "This is called the 'turtle treasure.' If a person obtains it, they must cut their own arm and place it in the turtle's body; the turtle will then feed on human blood to survive. If a person has this treasure in their arm, they can see precious metals and gems underground through the earth, but they will die when their blood runs out. The descendants can also cut their arms and place the treasure in the turtle to maintain wealth for generations." The cook, upon hearing this, was greatly regretful. Every time he thought about it, he would hit himself in the cheek. The author's great-aunt, Lady Cao, said, "According to Mr. Cen, it is a matter of risking one's life for wealth. If a person is willing to risk their life, there are many other ways to gain wealth, why cut an arm to feed the turtle?" The cook never understood and eventually died out of self-reproach.

A monk named Gu Shu, whose origin and name are unknown, lived in a dilapidated temple in Jingcheng during the late Ming Dynasty. My great-great-grandfather, known as Houzhai Gong, once gifted him a poem. One night, as Gu Shu recited scriptures by the light of a lamp, he heard a faint rustling sound outside the window, as if people were moving around. He shouted to ask who it was, and a clear voice responded: "I am a wild fox, come here to listen to the scriptures." Gu Shu then asked why the fox did not go to a temple where the most splendid religious gatherings took place, and the fox replied: "They recite scriptures in places with people, while you recite in a place without people." Later, Gu Shu recounted this encounter to Houzhai Gong, who said: "If you told me these words, it is also reciting in a place with people." Gu Shu remained in deep thought for a long time. As for Li Bai's dream of his brush pen blooming with flowers, it was merely a dream-like vision in the land of sleep. General Ma Fugong, the road commissioner of Fujian, had a fondness for calligraphy and writing. Whenever he had a little free time, he would practice calligraphy. One day, as he hung his large brush pen on a rack, it suddenly emitted a flame of light several feet long, pouring from the tip of the brush down to the ground, then rolling back up in a billowing swirl. After a while, the flame subsided. All the soldiers in the office saw this, and General Ma drew a small portrait of it. I once wrote a poem for it. However, General Ma eventually died in office, so it was an omen of misfortune rather than good fortune.

Shi Yitang, a lesser official of the Ministry of War, was the second son of Prime Minister Wenjing Gong. While living at home, he suddenly experienced dizziness and confusion, feeling as if his soul had left his body. Someone supported him into a sedan chair and carried him for several miles. Another sedan chair then appeared from behind, and those in it called out urgently, asking to see him. It was his father, the Prime Minister Wenjing Gong. Shi Yitang stepped down from the sedan chair and bowed to pay his respects. His father said to him, "You still have descendants who have not yet been born. You should not go to the other world at this time. Have the bearers take you back." He suddenly woke up, and at that time, he was seventy-four years old. The following year, he had a son, and two years later, he had another son, just as his father had predicted. This account was told to me personally by Shi Yitang when he was seventy-eight years old and came to the capital. From the "Yue Wei Cao Tang Ji" (Notes from the Reading of a Tiny Hut), Volume Six, "Luan Yang Xiu Xia Lu" (Luan Yang Summer Notes), Part Six: When the Hui people of Ush were about to rebel, there was a high mound on the west side of the city, which was said to be the grave of their ancestral founder. Every evening, a giant figure would stand on the mound, with a face over a foot wide, looking intently toward the east as if waiting for something. After the rebels were defeated, the figure no longer appeared. Some said that the giant knew that a calamitous fate was approaching and was waiting to gather the souls of the descendants. Others said that the giant was looking east to warn the descendants that soldiers were coming from the east, urging them to prepare early. Still others said that since the Hui people faced eastward, the giant's gaze inwardly indicated that the descendants should not rebel. All these interpretations are uncertain, but it is clear that the giant was an omen of the impending doom of Ush.

The monk Mingxin from Hong'en Temple said that in Shangtianzhu, there was an old monk who once entered the underworld and saw fierce ghost soldiers driving thousands of people outside a large public office. All of them had their clothes stripped off and were bound with their hands behind their backs. An official sat facing south, and officials holding books called out names, selecting one by one, fine and coarse, measuring fat and lean, like a butcher selling sheep and pigs. The monk found it strange and thought about it. He saw an official a bit farther away, who was an old patron, and therefore, he joined his palms and asked, "What are they all?" The official replied, "They are various heavenly demons who all take humans as food. The Buddha used great divine power to subdue the demon king and renounced the Five Precepts. However, their clans gather in groups, sometimes rebellious, sometimes obedient. They say since the beginning of time, the demon group eats humans, just as humans eat grain. The Buddha can stop humans from eating grain, then we will not eat humans. Argued like this, even the demon king could not control them. The Buddha saw the vast waves of karma, sinking into the cycle of rebirth without return, and the hell of no interruption could no longer contain them. Therefore, he wrote down and sent to Yama, wanting to transfer these prisoners from the underworld to feed them. Their stomachs would be full, and can avoid harming living beings. The ten kings discussed together, because it concerns the fate of the people, nothing is better than local officials, easiest to do good, can also cause great harm. Various wrongdoings, most are not done by themselves. The mirror of the underworld's records, there is a place for the punishment. The most harmful to the people are officials, servants, relatives of officials, and servants of officials."

These four kinds of people have no official responsibilities but wield official power. Officials may concern themselves with their own performance evaluations, while these individuals know only how to seek profit, clinging to grass and trees, relying on their position to make a show of strength. They are capable of extracting marrow and pouring fat, making people swallow their tears and shed blood. Within the four continents, these four kinds of evil deeds are most numerous. Therefore, my hells are cleared to feed their cauldrons, with the white-skinned, the soft and brittle, and the fat and rich, serving as the food of the demon king. Rough materials are used to feed the many demons. Thus, they are first distinguished, and then dispatched. Among them, those with slightly lighter karma, once subjected to slicing, cutting, and roasting, are immediately reduced to nothing. Those with heavier karma have their remaining bones thrown aside, blown by the wind of their karma, returning to their original form, and again offered to the knife and cutting board, from two or three times to a thousand times, varying endlessly. Those with the heaviest karma even change form multiple times in a single day, with cutting and roasting never ceasing. The monk exclaimed, "It would be better to shave one's head and leave the world, so as to avoid such worries." The official replied, "That is not the case. Their power can harm others, but their strength can also help others. At the assembly on Mount Ling, there were originally officials, even among these four kinds of people, there were never any who did not enjoy freedom in the lotus realm." When he finished speaking, the monk suddenly realized that his nephew was in a county magistrate's office, and he hurriedly wrote a letter urging him to return, advising him to change his career. This matter was told by the monk to his nephew, and Mingxin heard it while in the temple. Although the words seem absurd and appear to be an allegory, the way of the gods sets up teachings, making people know fear, and it is also the earnest intention of warning the world, and cannot be bound by the precept against false speech.

A blind musician from Cangzhou named Liu Junrui, who was skilled in playing stringed instruments, once visited my home and shared a story. He mentioned that a man surnamed Lin had once been invited to perform. One evening, just before dusk, someone came to his door and called out, saying, "A certain official's boat is moored by the riverbank. We have heard that you are good at performing narrative songs, so we invite you to come and try it. There will be a generous reward." The caller immediately urged him to take his lute and led him by his bamboo cane to the boat, which was about four or five miles away. Upon arriving at the boat, after exchanging pleasantries, the host instructed him, "It is too hot inside the boat. Sit on the shore and perform, and I can listen to it from my window." Lin, lured by the reward, performed with all his might. Around the third watch of the night, his fingers ached and his throat was dry, but he could not get any water. Listening carefully, he noticed that the people around him were a mix of men and women, laughing and talking loudly. He felt that this was not the household of a scholar-official, nor was it a place by the water. He stopped playing and wanted to leave. The crowd angrily said, "What kind of blind scoundrel are you, daring not to follow orders?" They beat him with their hands, causing him unbearable pain. He then begged to perform again. After a while, he heard the people gradually disperse, but he still dared not stop. Suddenly, he heard someone call from beside his ear, "Mr. Lin, why are you still here before sunrise, performing in a place full of graves, taking the early coolness under the tree?" Startled, he asked in surprise. It turned out to be a neighbor who had gotten up early to sell things and passed by. Realizing he had been tricked by a ghost, he returned in a panic. The man surnamed Lin was always known for his cunning and was nicknamed "Lin Ghost." Those who heard the story all laughed and said, "Today a ghost met another ghost."

Former Yao An Gong said: In our village, there was a man named Bai Zhong who, by chance, bought a book of ghost-commanding spells. Hoping to use it to practice the method of moving objects, or perhaps earn a living, he followed the instructions in the book to prepare various ritual objects. On a moonlit night, he dressed as a Taoist priest and went to a desolate area between graves to test the spells. Sitting at a table facing the book, he recited the spells. Indeed, he heard sounds from all sides. Suddenly, a strong wind arose, blowing the book away and causing it to fall among the grass. A ghost leaped out and grabbed the book. The other ghosts came out in a commotion and said, "You relied on the spells to command us, but now that the spells are lost, we no longer fear you." They gathered and attacked together. Bai Zhong stumbled and fled, with stones and debris flying at him like a sudden rain. He barely managed to reach home. That night, he suffered greatly from malaria and lay ill for more than a month, suspecting that it was also due to ghostly interference. One day, he complained to Yao An Gong, feeling both ashamed and angry. Yao An Gong said, "Fortunately, your magic didn't work, so it only became a laughing matter. If unfortunately, your magic had worked, how would you know it wouldn't bring disaster? This is your good fortune. What more do you have to complain about?"

The house of my cousin Yu Dun was originally an old garden in the southern part of the village. Before the house was built, there were no residents around it. One night, while Tian Da, the gardener, was sleeping in a small room next to the well, he heard a quarreling voice outside the wall. He initially thought the voices were villagers and asked, "Who are you, and why are you disturbing me at night?" One of the voices replied, "I have a matter to seek your fair judgment. I don't know where this guest ghost came from, but it forcibly entered my home and harassed my wife. Is such a thing possible in the world?" The other voice said, "I brought money to the ancestral temple. This woman saw me laughing and invited me into the room. Then, someone suddenly entered and took my money. Is such a thing also possible in the world?" Tian Da realized they were ghosts and remained silent, not daring to respond. Both ghosts said, "This place cannot resolve our matter. We should take it to the land god." They left noisily towards the northeast. The next day, Tian Da went to the temple of the land god and asked the caretaker, but found no trace of the incident. Everyone doubted that Tian Da was lying. Li Mingru from Linqing said, "It's not surprising. I think the ghost woman probably resolved the matter." The people laughed heartily.

In the year 1779, I was with Dongguang Li Yunju and Huo Yangzhong, studying together in the Cloud Study Hall. One evening, we casually discussed ghosts and spirits. Cloud Raise believed in them, while Nourish Zhong did not believe in them. While we were debating, Cloud Raise's servant suddenly said, "There are indeed strange things in the world. If I hadn't experienced it myself, I wouldn't have believed it either." He continued, "Once, I passed by the cemetery in front of the City God Temple and stumbled, breaking open a coffin. At night, I dreamed that the City God had me taken away. He said someone was accusing me of destroying their room. I knew it was about the broken coffin and argued with him. 'Your room should not have been in the way,' I said. 'It was not me who invaded you.' He replied, 'The road naturally led to my house. It was not my house that caused it to be in the way.'"

The City God smiled and looked at me and said, "Everyone walks this path, so I cannot blame you. Everyone steps on it without breaking it, why did you break it? I cannot completely release you, you must pay with underworld money." He then said, "The ghost cannot repair its own coffin, you cover it with a board and pile earth on top." The next day, as instructed by the god, I still burned underworld money. A whirlwind carried away the ashes. One night, passing by the place again, I heard someone calling me to sit. I knew it was the ghost from before, and hurriedly returned home. The ghost laughed loudly, the sound was like that of an owl. Thinking about it now, I still feel my hair stand on end. Yang Zhong said to Yun Ju, "Your servant helps you, one mouth cannot feed two." However, I cannot consider what others see as what I see. If the judge interrogates, will they believe everything they see directly? Or will they rely on the testimony of many? To see everything directly is not possible. To rely on the testimony of many is not to consider what others see as what I see. How do you handle it, we laughed together and ended it.

Lin Qingbiao, the professor from Putian, said that when Zheng Chenggong occupied Taiwan, a strange monk from Guangdong arrived by sea. His martial arts were unparalleled; he sat with his arms exposed, and when struck with a blade, it felt like hitting iron or stone. He also knew the art of divination and meteorology, and when he discussed military strategy, he spoke eloquently and logically. Zheng Chenggong was recruiting heroes and treated him with great respect. After a while, he became increasingly arrogant and haughty, and Zheng Chenggong could no longer tolerate him and suspected him of being a spy. He wanted to kill him but feared he could not succeed. His general Liu Guoxuan said: "If you must eliminate him, the task is mine." He then went to the monk and treated him courteously. Suddenly, he asked: "Master, you are a person of Buddhist enlightenment, but what if you encounter Maudgalyayana? Would you still be affected?" The monk replied: "Master Shenliao, my mind has been like a piece of mud for a long time." Liu Guoxuan joked: "I want to test your spiritual power with the double body of Liu and Wang. Can I make the people more confident?" He then selected ten or so beautiful young boys and girls who were skilled in seduction, spread out bedding and pillows, and allowed them to engage in intimate acts around him. Their gentle and seductive attitudes were extremely alluring. The monk talked and laughed as if he had not seen or heard anything. After a while, he suddenly closed his eyes and stopped looking. Liu Guoxuan drew his sword and struck, and the monk's head fell off instantly. Liu Guoxuan said: "This skill is not due to any supernatural forces, but rather the practice of qi to strengthen oneself. When the mind is calm, the qi gathers; when the mind moves, the qi disperses. This monk's mind was initially calm, so he dared to observe everything. When he closed his eyes, it showed that his mind had moved and he was trying to control it, hence the blade struck and he could not defend himself." The explanation was quite insightful, but it is unclear how a violent and cunning youth could understand such subtleties. His ability to navigate the treacherous waters of Taiwan for over a decade was indeed remarkable.

Zhu Xi, known as Mr. Hui'an, once walked with Wu Gong, the hermit, in the southern part of the city. They sat under a tree to discuss the Yi. Suddenly, a voice came from behind them, saying: "What you two are discussing is the Yi of diviners, not the Yi of Confucians." Surprised by its sudden appearance, they asked where it had come from. The voice replied: "I have been sitting here already; you two just haven't noticed me." When asked for its name, it said: "I am Cui Yin from Jiangnan." Today, I stayed at an inn outside the city walls, and it was still early in the evening when I went out for a walk to clear my mind. The hermit appreciated my refined manners and engaged me in a close and intimate conversation about the differences between the Yi of diviners and the Yi of Confucians. Cui Yin said: "The sages created the Yi to speak of human affairs, not heavenly principles. It is for the common people, not for the sages. Sages act from the heart without overstepping the boundaries, so they have no doubts and do not need divination. Only the common people are often confused by the complexities of life and find themselves at a loss when faced with choices. Therefore, the sages use the waxing and waning of yin and yang to show the advancement and retreat of human affairs, enabling people to know how to approach or avoid things. This is the core purpose of the Confucian interpretation of the Yi. All things and events in the world can be understood through the lens of yin and yang, and later scholars have expanded on these ideas, each clarifying a different aspect."

Yang Jian Wang Zong Zhuan, elucidating the study of the mind, this is the *Yi* of the Chan school, derived from Wang Bi; Chen Tuan and Shao Yong, this is the *Yi* of the Daoist school, derived from Wei Boyang; the *Yi* of the technical school, developed by Guan Zhong and Guo Pu, originating from Jiao Hong and Jing Fang, which is what the two gentlemen spoke of. The *Yi* philosophy is vast and encompasses everything, revealing wisdom and benevolence, with principles that are fundamentally consistent. Later generations have forgotten its origins and instead elevate secondary interpretations as the true essence, implying that the sages who composed the *Yi* did so only for a select few of the most intelligent, not for the teachings of countless generations or the understanding of countless people. A classic is the constant, referring to the unchanging principles of the universe; a classic is also the path, referring to the way that people follow. How could it be that the first of the Six Classics should be made obscure, making it incomprehensible to people? The two gentlemen were pleased with his eloquence, and they continued talking until the moon rose, but when questioned about their origins, they spoke of distant times and foreign cultures. They apologized and said, "Are you a scholar who has retreated into seclusion?" Cui Wei smiled faintly and said, "If you are a recluse, you would not have time to hide your talents and retreat from the limelight, how could you be known? If you are a scholar, you would not have time to turn inward and discipline yourself, how could you engage in teaching? What the world calls scholars and recluses are all tangled and chaotic, and I am fleeing from this very thing. You may stop now, do not dirty my ears!" Suddenly, he let out a loud shout, and the leaves of the trees flew in all directions. He had already vanished, and it was then that they realized they had not seen a real person.

A Scholar Named Xu Nanjin from Nanpi, Known for His Fearlessness Mr. Xu Nanjin from Nanpi was known for his remarkable courage. While studying in a Buddhist temple, he shared a bed with a friend. One night, he saw two torches burning on the north wall. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was a face emerging from the wall, as large as a winnowing basket, with the two torches being its eyes. His friend trembled in fear, but Mr. Xu calmly put on his clothes and said, "I was about to read, but the candle had burned out. Your arrival is very welcome." He then took a book and began reading aloud, his voice clear and loud. Before finishing a few pages, the eyes gradually faded. He tapped the wall and called out to it, but it did not respond. On another night, while using the restroom, a young servant carried a candle with him. Suddenly, the same face emerged from the ground, smiling at them. The servant dropped the candle, which fell to the ground. Mr. Xu quickly picked it up and placed it on the ghost's head, saying, "The candle had no stand, but your arrival is again very welcome." The ghost looked up without moving. Mr. Xu said, "Where can you not go? Why are you here? There are people who follow their nose to the sea; are you one of them?" He then took a piece of dirty paper and tested the ghost's mouth. The ghost vomited violently, roared wildly several times, and extinguished the candle before disappearing. From then on, the ghost was no longer seen. Mr. Xu once remarked, "Ghosts and demons are all real, and sometimes they can be seen. Only by examining one's life and facing everything without fear can one's heart remain calm."

Dai Dongyuan recounted that during the late Ming dynasty, there was a man named Song who was choosing a burial site. He traveled to a deep mountain in She County, and as the sun was setting, a storm was about to come. He saw a cave at the foot of a cliff and threw himself into it for temporary shelter. He heard a voice from inside the cave say, "There is a ghost here; you should not enter." Song asked, "How did you enter?" The ghost replied, "I am a ghost myself." Song then requested to see the ghost, to which the ghost responded, "If we meet, the clash of yin and yang energies will cause you to experience slight chills and fever. It would be better for you to protect yourself with your own fire and have a conversation at a distance." Song then asked, "You must have a tomb; why are you living here?" The ghost replied, "During the reign of Emperor Shenzong, I was a county magistrate. I disliked the officials who competed for wealth and advancement, so I resigned and returned to the countryside. After my death, I prayed to Yama, not to be reborn in the human world, and in my next life, my rank and salary were changed to an official in the underworld. I did not expect that in the underworld, there would also be competition and rivalry, so I resigned again and returned to my tomb. Living among the ghosts in the tomb, coming and going in a noisy and crowded manner, it was too much for me. I had no choice but to seek refuge here. Although it is cold and rainy, it is desolate and unbearable. Compared to the storms of the official world and the traps of life, it is like being born in the heaven of the Thirty-Three Heavens." The ghost continued, "In the silent and lonely mountains, I have forgotten the years. The time since I separated from the ghosts is unknown, and the time since I separated from humans is even more unknown. I am happy to have freed myself from all worldly ties and to be in harmony with the natural order. I did not expect to encounter humans again. Tomorrow I will move to another place. O Fisherman of Wuling, do not visit the Peach Blossom Spring again." After speaking, the ghost did not respond further. Song asked for the ghost's name, but the ghost did not answer. Song, who had brought writing tools with him, took out his ink and brush and wrote the words "Ghost Hide" in large characters on the cave entrance before returning home.

Wang Jinguang of Yangqu related that Zhao Gong, the governor of Jining, had two literary assistants, one surnamed Qiao and the other surnamed Che. They jointly hired a sedan chair to return to their hometown. Zhao Gong playfully composed a couplet using their surnames: "Qiao and Che, two literary friends, each riding half a sedan chair." The characters "轿" and "轿" in the couplet both contain the character "半," which is a clever pun. At the time, Zhao Gong was practicing the summoning of spirits, and he presented the couplet to the medium for a response. The medium wrote: "This is a real person and a real event, not something that can be forced or fabricated." Half a year later, Zhao Gong summoned the spirits again, and the medium suddenly wrote: "I have now found the answer to the previous couplet: 'Lu and Ma, two scholars, pulling a donkey together.'" The medium added: "Four days from now, between the hours of Chen and Si, go to the south gate to wait for them." On the appointed day, Zhao Gong sent a servant to spy on the situation. Sure enough, there were two scholars named Lu and Ma, who were pulling a donkey together, carrying the examination papers of newly successful candidates to the provincial capital to sell them. Zhao Gong smiled and said, "Indeed, it is a clever response. However, the two scholars are deeply humiliated by this. It is like an arrow on the bowstring; once it is there, it must be shot. Even the immortals could not help but smile."

My ancestors had a place called Changli, which is now divided among my younger brother's family. It is said that before the division of property, there was a stack of firewood in the field that had been there for many years. A fox was said to live there, and no one dared to disturb it. Once, a tenant farmer named someone, who was drunk, lay down next to it. His peers warned him not to anger the spirits, but he did not listen and instead cursed loudly. Suddenly, he heard someone say, "You are drunk, I won't hold it against you, just go home and sleep." The next day, he went to the garden to guard the melons. His wife carried food to him. She saw from afar a red-clothed woman sitting with her husband in the smoke. Seeing the woman, she was startled and hastily jumped over the wall. His wife, who was naturally jealous and domineering, thought her husband had an affair. Furious and unable to bear it, she suddenly struck him with her load. He explained, "Unable to clear myself, I suffered severe beating." His wife, tired of striking, stopped briefly but continued to curse him. Suddenly, they heard a loud laugh from the tree tops, and they realized it was the fox playing a trick on them.

Wu Hui Shu reported that in his village there was a wealthy family with only one son who was suffering from a severe illness. Ye Tian Shi diagnosed him and said, "The pulse shows signs of a ghostly ailment, which cannot be treated with medicine." He then invited a Daoist priest from Mount Fangshan to perform a ritual. By midnight, a chilling wind arose, and the candles on the altar all dimmed to a greenish hue. The priest drew his sword, closed his eyes, and seemed to see something. He then brushed off his clothes and abruptly left, saying, "The mischief of demons and spirits can be expelled by my methods. As for past-life grievances, although there are ways to resolve them, whether or not they are resolved depends on the person involved. If it involves moral and familial obligations, it violates heavenly laws, and even presenting a green tablet in worship cannot reach the celestial realm. This curse is because your father neglected a young brother, and your brother left two orphaned nephews. You consumed everything like silkworms and whales, leaving nothing for them, and treated the orphaned children as if they were strangers. You did not provide them with food, warmth, and comfort, and let them suffer in pain and discomfort. Your father endured pain in the afterlife and appealed to the underworld. The underworld official issued a decree, taking your son to repay the grievance. Although I have skills, I can only expel ghosts from people, not drive away a father from his son." Indeed, the son soon passed away, and later, the family had no son. They eventually adopted a nephew as heir.

The Temple of Huochi is located forty li east of Hejian. There was a farmer named Yu, whose family was moderately prosperous. One night, Yu went out, and several bandits leaped down from the roof, wielding large axes to break the door, making a clattering sound. The family consisted only of women and children, who cowered in fear, waiting for what would happen. Suddenly, the two oxen that Yu had raised, suddenly roared and rushed in, fighting the bandits with their horns. The bandits attacked with sticks and knives, but the oxen fought even harder. The bandits, injured, fled in a disordered and hasty manner. It happened during the year of Qianlong's reign, the year of Guihai (1763), when there was a major famine in Hejian. Many farmers could not provide fodder for their cattle and sold them at the butcher's market. These two oxen, upon reaching the butcher's door, mourned and lay down, refusing to move forward. Yu, seeing this, felt pity and pawned his clothes to get money to redeem them, enduring the cold and returning home. The oxen's loyalty to fight to the death was indeed fitting. However, the bandits were inside the house, while the oxen were in the stable. How did the oxen know there was an alarm? Moreover, oxen are not agile creatures, and the outer door was tightly closed. How could they jump over the wall? This must have been the work of someone or something. It was not the work of ghosts or spirits, but who else could it be? This was told to me by Liu Dongtang during the winter of 1765 (the year of the Earth Ox) during the annual examination in Hejian. Liu Dongtang, who was from the Temple of Huochi, said he had seen the two oxen, each covered with several wounds.

The *zhī* plant is called a lucky herb, yet it is not necessarily a sign of good fortune. During the Yuan dynasty, the provincial governor of Jinghai, while serving in Gansu, had nine *zhī* plants grow in his office, and he therefore gave himself a nickname. However, he was soon dismissed from his position. My uncle, An Gong Wu Zhan, had his coffin kept in the house, and suddenly a *zhī* plant grew on it. From then on, the descendants declined. Now there are no young children left. It seems that misfortune and good fortune are about to emerge, and the energy moves first. Unusual omens, the principle does not come in vain. Whether they are good or bad, they cannot be predicted. My elder brother Qinghu said: People know that omens come from ghosts and gods, and human affairs respond to them. But they do not know that omens actually come from human affairs, and ghosts and gods respond to them. It is also not impossible to predict.

The Grand Secretary Wu Gong Mi Tai said, once while in Tibet, he saw a cliff with no path, where there were naturally occurring Sanskrit characters of the Great Compassion Mantra on the stone, each character clearly visible, not something that could be done by human effort, nor could it have been reached by human footsteps. He once mentioned the name of the mountain, but the Sanskrit name was difficult to remember, and he has forgotten it now. He never spoke falsely in his life. Knowing that it was indeed not a fabrication, the vastness of heaven and earth contains everything. Song Dynasty Confucian scholars often dismissed things that seemed impossible according to reason, and would immediately conclude that they must not exist. They did not understand that 'everything exists' is itself a principle. There are two types of lamas, one called the Yellow Sect, and the other called the Red Sect, distinguished by their clothing. The Yellow Sect teaches morality and the principle of cause and effect, sharing the same origin as the Zen school but having different branches. The Red Sect specializes in magical arts. The Minister of the Board of Propaganda and Pacification, Liu Gong Baohu, said, when he was stationed in Tibet, he once offended a Red Sect lama. Some said that the lama would seek revenge when he climbed the mountain. He had his sedan chair and attendants go ahead, but secretly rode a horse behind them. When they reached halfway up the mountain, a horse suddenly leaped up, crushing the sedan chair, turning it to powder. This is what Liu Gong himself said. When I was serving in Urumqi, someone had lost their horse. A Red Sect lama took a small wooden frame and chanted for a long time. Suddenly, it folded and turned repeatedly, like a see-saw, allowing the person who lost the horse to proceed. They reached a valley, and the horse was there. This is what I personally witnessed. Examining the magicians in the Western Regions who swallow swords and fire, such practices have existed since the Han Dynasty. This is likely a tradition passed down from ancient times, not part of the original Buddhist practices. Therefore, the Yellow Sect calls the Red Sect 'demons,' or it is said that they are the Brahmins, as mentioned in the Buddhist scriptures, they are heretical teachers and outsiders. This seems to be closer to the truth.

The mountains around Balikun, Pizhan, and Ürümqi are known for having many foxes, but none are known to harm people. However, there was an incident in Gengkete where a child was injured by a black shadow while trying to catch a fox at night. People generally believe that the fox was causing harm, but it might just be due to the child's fear and dizziness. Since the time of the Turkic and Uyghur peoples, hunting has been a common activity. Today, people in the region engage in various activities such as settling in remote areas, garrisoning, clearing land, and foraging beyond the borders, searching for food and gathering resources. Foxes are often injured and do not live long, so they cannot become spirits. Alternatively, the remote location and lack of knowledge about spiritual practices might explain why foxes do not become spirits. This shows that customs and habits must be learned and practiced to be maintained; human nature follows what is learned and practiced, and if not, it cannot be sustained. The Daoist idea that transforming nature and creating artificial things is not without merit is relevant here. Yao An Gong, a historical figure, once said that in remote southern regions, even ghosts are good, which aligns with the idea that remote areas have different customs.

Liu Gongjian, the Deputy Commander of the Qing Dynasty, recounted that he once visited Ili, where there was a skilled spirit medium. The spirit claimed to be Zhang Yue, the Duke of Yan from the Tang dynasty, and engaged in composing poetry and prose with others, which was recorded and compiled into a volume. The spirit had a strong liking for drinking and would always burn paper money and pour out a large cup of wine as an offering whenever it descended from the altar. Liu Gongjian wondered why Zhang Yue would appear in the harsh, snowy climate of Ili. He read some of the poems and found them to be shallow and vulgar, suggesting that they might be the work of a lowly person who died in the cold and is now seeking sustenance by pretending to be Zhang Yue. A local person named Zhang was deeply cunning and deceitful, and even close relatives and friends could not get a word of truth from him. He was quick-witted in speech and often deceived others, earning him the nickname "Bald-Necked Horse." One day, while traveling with his father at night, they became lost and saw several people sitting around. They called out to ask which direction they should go, and the people all replied, "Go north." As a result, they fell into deep mud. When they called out again, the people all replied, "Turn east." They nearly drowned and were stuck in the mud, unable to move. They heard the people laughing heartily and said, "Bald-Necked Horse, do you now know the error of lying to others?" The voice was near their ear, but they could not see anyone, realizing that they had been deceived by a spirit medium.

Evil spirits arise from human actions, and such cases often occur. According to Li Yunju, there was a man who was extremely timid, and another man wanted to play a trick on him. The servant of the timid man had hands as black as ink, and he was instructed to hide in the room. They secretly agreed that the man would sit under the moon with someone, and when he cried out in fear of a ghost, the servant would suddenly extend one hand from the window. On the agreed time, the man cried out, and a hand shot out, as large as a winnowing basket, with five fingers stiff like a pestle. Both the host and guest were shocked, and the servants exclaimed, "Is this the real ghost?" They entered with torches and sticks, and found the servant lying unconscious in the corner of the wall. When they revived him, he said that in the darkness, it seemed like something was blowing air at him, and he became dazed. My uncle, Huan'an, said that two people were studying together in a Buddhist temple. One of them stood in front of the other in the dim light, pretending to be a hanged ghost. The other man was so terrified that he almost fainted, and quickly called out, "It's me, don't be afraid!" The man said, "I knew it was you, but what is behind you?" When he looked back, he saw a real hanged ghost. When deceit arises, the ghost responds with deceitful intent. This can also be compared to the metaphor of the mantis and the sparrowhawk.

When I was eight or nine years old, I was at my maternal uncle's house, Mr. Shizhai An, and I heard Mr. Su Donggao say that a certain magistrate of Jiaohé had embezzled thousands of government funds. He sent his servant to return the funds, but the servant reported that the boat had capsized on the Yellow River. The magistrate secretly sent his servant Chongtai to carry the funds back, and Chongtai then stole the funds and headed north. He was traveling to Yanzhou when he was robbed and killed by bandits. My maternal uncle gasped and said, "How terrifying! This is what humans can do, but it is also what ghosts and spirits can do." He then questioned, "Do ghosts and spirits necessarily have to appear in broad daylight, with a mirror of karma on their left and a book of the dead in their right hand, directing all beings through the six realms of rebirth to see the retribution for good and evil?" He concluded, "This is enough to deserve a place on the Iron List of the Underworld." Mr. Su Donggao replied, "If the magistrate had not embezzled the funds, why would the servant have to cover them up; if the servant had not covered them up, why would Chongtai have followed suit; if Chongtai had not followed suit, why would the bandits have robbed and killed him? This is still what humans can do, not what ghosts and spirits can do. If what you say is true, the magistrate should be punished, so he sent the servant to embezzle the funds; the servant should be punished, so he sent Chongtai to follow suit; Chongtai should be punished, so he was robbed and killed by bandits. If ghosts and spirits sent him, and the retribution person followed suit, isn't that absurd?" My maternal uncle said, "This is a skillful argument, but it is not the right principle." However, keeping this idea in mind can also serve as an encouragement in a world of conformity, urging people to stand on their own.

Liu Yizhai, while serving as a censor, once rented a house along the Western Riverbank. Every night, several people were heard striking the drum, their sounds clear and continuous throughout the night, perfectly synchronized with the city's watch drums. When looked at, however, there was nothing visible causing the disturbance. The noise prevented him from getting any sleep. Because of his stubborn nature, Liu Yizhai wrote an article himself, pointing out the "crimes" of the disturbances, and posted it prominently on the wall to drive them away. That night, everything became quiet. Liu Yizhai proudly claimed that his action was no less effective than Han Yu's driving away of crocodiles. I remarked that his literary and moral qualities seemed not to match those of Han Yu, but his strong and passionate nature, combined with his lifetime of integrity, made him boldly fearless of ghosts. Furthermore, he had struggled to move into this house, exhausted and unable to move again, with no other choice but to hold out against the ghosts to the death. For him, this was like a cornered beast still fighting; for the ghosts, it was like not chasing a cornered enemy. Do you not remember the *Taiping Guangji* records of Zhou Ji arguing with ghosts over a house, and the ghosts being intimidated by his wooden strength and leaving? Liu Yizhai laughed and slapped my back, saying, "Wei Shou is frivolous, but you understand me."

During my tenure as the provincial education commissioner in Fujian, there was a building called the Pen-Bearing Tower in my official residence, flanked by two pagodas. The envoy resided on the lower floor, while the upper floor had double-layered, winding walls that were not very visible except at noon. Formerly, it was inhabited by a mountain demon, although not seen in the form of a one-legged creature walking backward, sounds were often heard at night. I recalled a line from Du Fu's poetry about a mountain spirit hiding during the day, realizing that ghosts and demons avoid light and seek darkness, likely due to the secluded and hidden nature of the rooms. Therefore, all the walls were removed, so that windows were opened on all sides, allowing the green mist of three mountains to be seen clearly. The signboard was inscribed as "Floating Green Pavilion," and the couplet read: "The landscape bends but does not obscure the wide view; the open windows allow the peaks to be seen." From then on, the mountain demon moved to the southeast corner of the Conference Hall, which had been abandoned for a long time. Since it caused no harm to people, it was allowed to remain hidden. It was not taken to extremes. When Xu Gong Jingxi served as the salt commissioner of Fujian, fires often broke out from within the chests in his office, and the locks remained intact. One night, the demon secretly cut the hair of his concubine, causing particularly severe mischief. Afterward, Xu Gong was dismissed and returned home, but he did not have time to leave before he died. The mountain spirit knows the events of a year, so it took advantage of his departure to mock him. When Xu Gong was in power, the spirit remained silent and hidden. When his decline began, the spirit attacked without reason. Is this why such spirits are considered evil?

When wild rice was abundant in my hometown, every night there would be a creature in the fields that was indistinguishable in its head and feet, moving by flipping and crashing into the ground with a sound like pounding. The farmers, accustomed to seeing it, did not find it strange, and they called it the Spirit of the Wild Rice. It was said that this spirit often drove away ghosts from the fields. When this spirit appeared, all the ghosts returned to their places and dared not roam the fields. This spirit is not recorded in ancient books, but it is certainly not an evil spirit. My cousin Mao Yuan once saw it at Li's Hollow under the moonlight. He observed closely and saw that its shape was like a cloth bag. Every time it flipped, one end would touch the ground, and it moved slowly and heavily. My ancestor, Gong, who was favored, had his original wife, Lady Chen, who died early. His second wife, Lady Zhang, returned to the household on her wedding day. She sat alone in the room and saw a young woman step in by pulling the curtain. The woman sat directly beside the bed, wearing a dark robe, yellow shirt, and light green skirt. Her manner was dignified and refined. The new wife did not feel comfortable greeting her and thought it might be one of my cousins or sisters-in-law, or perhaps an aunt or sister. The woman chattered about the family's affairs and successes, the good and bad of the maids and servants, speaking in detail and with care. After a while, a servant brought in tea, and the woman left. Later, after several days, I found it strange that there was no such person in the family. I carefully described her clothing, and it was the same as Lady Chen's funeral attire. There are many examples of the living and the dead being jealous of each other in historical records. Lady Chen had already passed away, but she still worried that the new wife was not familiar with managing things. She appeared to give instructions, regardless of whether in the realm of the living or the dead. What kind of heart does this show? Now, my descendants who have passed the imperial exams and held official positions are all Lady Chen's offspring.

Great-great-grandfather loved Elder Gentleman Tang, who had a good reputation among scholars during the late Ming dynasty. He made a special effort to study the teachings of Zheng Xuan and Kong Yingda, dedicating himself to Confucian studies without distinction between winter and summer, always reading until midnight. One night, he dreamed of a place called Gongxie, with a sign reading "Cultural Ceremony." Inside, about ten people were handling official documents, and he felt vaguely familiar with them, as if they were old acquaintances. Upon seeing him, they all expressed surprise and said, "You are still delayed by seven years before you should return; it is still too early." He suddenly woke up, startled, and realized he did not have long to live. From then on, he spent his days associating with those outside the normal social order. Once, he chanced upon a Taoist, and their discussion was quite harmonious. The Taoist invited him to drink together, but after the Taoist left, he encountered a servant named Hu Mende on the way, who said, "Recently, a book was forgotten to be given to your master. You can take it back." The subject looked at the book, which contained magic spells for driving spirits and commanding ghosts. He shut himself in his room to practice, mastering the art completely. From time to time, he used it for dramatic purposes to pass the time. Seven years later, during the Chongzhen Dingchou year, he indeed died from illness. Half a day after his death, he revived and said, "I used the Five Thunder Method frivolously and received punishment from the underworld. They have ordered me to retrieve this book, so you can quickly burn it." After burning it, he died again, but half a day later, he revived once more, saying, "The underworld has checked and found three pages missing. They have ordered me to retrieve them." He looked in the ashes and indeed found three pages not fully burned. He burned them again, and then he died. This story is recorded in the family genealogy by Yao'an Gong. The subject heard it from his great-grandfather, who in turn heard it from his great-great-grandfather, who was the one who personally burned the book. Who would say that there are no ghosts and spirits?

The place where my clan resides is called Jingcheng, an ancient county of the Song dynasty. The site of the city can still be vaguely discerned, and sometimes, at the break of dawn, one might see the shadow of a city in the mist, with its walls and turrets clearly visible, resembling a mirage. This phenomenon is often recorded in other books, but no one understands its cause. I believe that anything with form must have a spiritual energy. The thick earth is where the earth's spiritual energy gathers, like a person's soul and spirit. This city, spanning several miles, is massive in form. It has been gathering spiritual energy for over a thousand years since the Han to the Song dynasties, like a person who accumulates much and uses it widely, its soul and spirit are uniquely strong. Therefore, even though its form has changed, the accumulated spiritual energy cannot be dissipated in a day. Occasionally, this phenomenon still appears as a city, just as a ghost remains in human form after a person's death. However, not all ancient cities appear in this form, and those that do are not commonly seen. What is the reason for this? When a person dies, there may be a ghost, or there may not be. When a ghost exists, it may be seen or not seen, just as it is.

Mr. Nan Gong Bao Jing said: In his hometown, there was a scholar named Tchen who studied in a shrine during the summer night. He slept naked under the eaves. In a dream, a deity summoned him to the front of the seat and scolded him severely. Tchen defended himself, saying, "There were several peddlers sleeping on the hall first, and I took refuge under the eaves. Why do I instead incur blame?" The deity replied, "Peddlers are acceptable, but you are not. They are stupid like deer and pigs, not worth arguing with. You read, yet do not know propriety?" It is said that the *Spring and Autumn Annals* holds high standards for the virtuous, and the principle is like this. Therefore, a gentleman in the world should follow the customs when it is appropriate, without unnecessarily seeking to be different; he should not follow the customs when it is inappropriate, nor blindly conform. The world often justifies unreasonable things by saying that someone did it. Without considering whether the matter is right or wrong, they only consider whether the matter exists. Since ancient times, what has never happened? Can people use each occurrence as an excuse?

Yuyang Shanren recorded a story about Zhang Xun's concubine seeking revenge in a reincarnation, but I do not agree with it. She said: "You are a loyal minister, what crime have I committed? And yet you killed me to feed your soldiers." When the city was about to fall, Zhang Xun had already resolved to sacrifice himself. If he was to die for his country, should I not also die for my master? Throughout history, there have been countless loyal ministers who upheld their principles, sacrificed their clans, and even their wives and children. If everyone could seek revenge, there would be no moral order in the world. If we allowed such demands, there would be no divine reason in the world. Wang Jing's mother accepted her fate with a smile, facing the sword. Who was she? This might be a case of evil spirits causing trouble, pretending to be a historical figure to seek offerings. It is also possible that officials during the late Ming dynasty, caring for their families and properties, clung to life and fabricated such words to justify themselves. Scholars writing books should preserve moral teachings, and even in collections of strange stories, they should not include words that go against reason.

My cousin Mr. Mu'an said that in the south of Jingcheng, people often saw a creature just before sunrise, riding a whirlwind eastward. Its body was not visible, but its head was raised more than ten feet high, with a long, writhing mane. No one knew what kind of monster it was. Some said it was the stone horse in front of Feng Dao's tomb, which had become a demon after many years. Investigating where Feng Dao lived, it is now called Xiangguo Village, and his wife's family is now called Furen Village, both near Jingcheng. Therefore, my great-great-grandfather's poem says: "The green annals leave only a few lines, scholars ultimately yield to princes. Liu Guangbo's tomb is nowhere to be found, but the Xiangguo and Furen Villages each have their own estates." Its tomb cannot be accurately identified by the county records. South of Beicun, there is a place called Shirenwa, where incomplete stone statues still exist. The locals point to it as Feng Dao's tomb. Perhaps there is some tradition behind it? Dong Kongru once stumbled drunk at night and turned to its side. Suddenly, a cold wind swept horizontally, sand and gravel flew everywhere, and it seemed to have a low growl. Dong Kongru shouted: "Old Longle, you are foolish and shameless, how can you still have a spirit after seven or eight hundred years. This is definitely a demon relying on it, dare to act recklessly again. And I will urinate on you every day." After he finished speaking, the wind stopped.

South Village's Dong Tian Shi, whose name is unknown, was a scholar of the late Ming Dynasty and a close friend of the author's great-great-grandfather. In the collection "Hua Wang Ge Sheng Gao," there are four poems mourning Tian Shi, which say: "Understanding every aspect of someone has always been difficult, yet throughout life, the two elders looked at each other with mutual respect. A sudden letter came, startling him into dropping his chopsticks; he mourned until the moment of burial in a remote village. Unfinished drafts were not collected, and the remaining funds were only enough to sell worn-out scholar's caps. Two pieces of coarse cloth could suffice for burial, as Qian Lou, despite his poverty, did not fear the cold. Indignation fills his chest like the Five Sacred Mountains, and his conversation, sharp and skillful, can quickly gain the upper hand. Not encountering Huang Zu is truly a blessing, and one might wonder at Ji Kang's disdain for worldly affairs. Sometimes he opens a window to invite the moonlight in, and with a cane, he walks everywhere to avoid being seen by others."

It is likely that in this vast world, there is no one who can understand or share in this sentiment, so let us try to ride a celestial chariot to the Purple Clear. With a tattered cloak hanging like worn-out clothes and frost on both temples, I nourish myself with ice and snow, moistening an empty stomach. A life that only experiences the coldness of autumn and winter, until death, one does not know the fragrance of silk and brocade. In the cold, one indulges in village wine, breaking one's own vows, and in old age, living in a monastery is to return to one's true home. Now, in this final sleep, there are no more matters to attend to, and there is no need for the blue rope to be busy with mourning. For twenty years, we have agreed to escape the dust of the world, but the heavens and earth, devoid of emotion, have caused this person to perish. In a chaotic world, one escapes to Buddhism for temporary relief, and in old age, crying for a friend is even more bitter and painful. The rivers and mountains are turbulent, connected by the aura of war, and teeth and hair are as gray as the waves of the river,寄托着病弱的身体. In the underworld, if there is a spirit, it should remember me, and a solitary tomb under a white poplar tree also saddens the spirit. From this, one can imagine the life of Tian Shi. The county records do not include a biography for him, perhaps because they have not seen the poetry of his ancestor. It is said that after Tian Shi's death, someone saw him riding a donkey up Mount Tai, but when called, he did not respond. Soon, he was obscured by an old tree and disappeared. Could it be that he attained immortality through a corpse transformation? Or was his appearance merely similar? Considering his solitary and reclusive nature, he seems to be close to the realm of the immortals.

In the collection of the First Emperor, there is a poem titled "Kuai Zai Xing," which reads: A single laugh astonishes heaven and earth, this joy is unprecedented in ancient times. All my life I did not understand drinking, but if I do, I can drink a full dou. Once an old man flattered the eunuchs, upright men all lost their heads. Who could have known that times would change and people would turn their hands? The prosperity of the Jin Gu Garden of yesteryear, today the decline of the Changtai willows. How clever is the heart of creation, this punishment is better than shackles. When drunk, talking about old matters, the cause and effect are not coincidental. Writing drunkenly with wet ink, as if gods and ghosts are moving my arm. Names are not written to preserve loyalty and sincerity. It was during the tenth year of the emperor's reign, the year of the Dragon in the Chinese zodiac, the second month of summer in the lunar calendar, the twenty-ninth day of the month. Six people were present, and the one who wrote it was an old man from Hejian. This was written for the concubines of Xu Xianchun who have fallen into the brothels. At the time, there were those who swore to die, and at night they dreamed that Xu Xianchun came with blood on him, saying: I died without being able to cover my guilt, so heaven has shown you the punishment after death. If you do not comply, my sin will be even heavier. The concubines often mentioned this to their guests. Therefore, the line 'cause and effect are not coincidental' is said.

My Fourth Uncle, Lifu Gong, once went to Hecheng to visit a friend. He saw a rider galloping swiftly toward the northeast, who suddenly hung a willow branch and fell. The crowd rushed to look, and found him already dead. After a while, a woman came weeping and crying, saying: "My mother-in-law is ill and has no medicine. I walked for a day and night, went to my mother’s family, and borrowed several items of clothing and ornaments, but did not expect them to be taken by a horse-riding bandit." The crowd led her to look at the fallen rider, who by then had revived. The woman cried out, "This is the man!" His bundle had been thrown by the roadside. They asked about the number of clothes and ornaments in the bundle; the fallen rider could not answer what the woman had said. When they opened it, everything matched exactly, and the fallen rider then confessed his guilt. The crowd declared that daylight robbery was a crime deserving of execution by hanging, and they were about to take him to the authorities. But the fallen rider knelt and begged for his life, offering to give the woman the dozens of gold pieces in his chest as a ransom to redeem himself. The woman, because her mother-in-law was gravely ill, did not wish to involve herself in court proceedings, so she took the gold and let him go. My uncle said, "The speed of retribution is none faster than in this matter." Every time I think of it, I feel that everywhere there are ghosts and spirits.

Qi Shun Ting, a descendant of the previously recorded notorious bandit Qi Da, was the most fierce and bold. He could tie a rope to the hilt of a knife and throw it to injure someone from a distance of two or three zhang. His gang nicknamed him "Flying Knife." His neighbor was Zhang Qi, whom Qi Shun Ting treated as a slave. He forced Zhang Qi to sell his house and stable and made his gang threaten him by saying, "If you don't move quickly, disaster will come immediately." Zhang Qi, having no choice, carried his wife and daughters out in a hurried manner, not knowing where to go. He then went to a temple to pray and said, "An unfortunate person being forced by a notorious bandit, trapped and with no way out, respectfully placed a staff before the deity, looked at the direction, and went that way." The staff fell pointing northeast, so he wandered and begged until reaching Tianjin. He married his daughter to a stove worker and helped him dry salt, barely supporting themselves. After three or four years, Qi Shun Ting's robbery of rations was discovered, and soldiers surrounded and captured him. He escaped at night under the cover of wind and rain, thinking that his gang members were on merchant ships, and was going to join them and sail away. He lurked during the day and traveled at night, stealing melons and fruits for food, luckily without being caught. One night, starving and thirsty, he saw a dim light in the distance and tried knocking on the door. A young woman stared at him for a long time and suddenly called out, "This is Qi Shun Ting." The edict for his arrest had already been urgently sent to Tianjin, and a reward was immediately offered for his capture. The workers heard the sound and gathered. Qi Shun Ting, with no weapon, lowered his head and surrendered. The young woman was Zhang Qi's daughter. If he had not forced Zhang Qi to move here, then Qi Shun Ting would have changed his clothes, and no one would have recognized him. The place was only a few miles from the seaport, and he would have sailed away.

Wang Lan Zhou once bought a boy while on a boat. The boy was about thirteen or fourteen years old, very refined and elegant, and had a basic understanding of characters. He said his father had died, the family had fallen on hard times, and they had failed to find relatives. They were traveling south on a boat, having sold all their belongings, so he had been sold to work as a servant. When Wang spoke to him, the boy was shy and bashful like a new bride, which already seemed strange. By the time they went to bed, the boy had undressed and lay down beside Wang. Wang had originally bought the boy to serve him, with no other intentions, but the boy's actions made Wang feel conflicted and unable to control his own desires. Later, the boy lay down on his pillow and wept silently. Wang asked, "Are you unwilling?" The boy replied, "I am not." Wang then asked, "If you are unwilling, why did you come to me first?" The boy said, "When my father was alive, he kept several young servants, none of whom refused to serve him. Those who initially hesitated were often beaten." Wang said, "What was the point of buying you if you act so confusedly?" The boy explained, "I knew that as a servant, I was supposed to serve my master. If I did not, I would be beaten. So I could not help but offer myself." Wang suddenly pushed the pillow away and said, "This is truly frightening." He quickly called the boatman to row faster. That night, he caught up with the boy's mother and brother and returned the boy to them, giving them fifty gold pieces as compensation. Feeling uneasy, Wang then went to the Mencius Temple to pray and confess his sins. In a dream, a guardian deity said to him, "You repented and corrected your actions in an instant. The underworld has not yet recorded your deeds, so there is no need to seek forgiveness from the Buddha."

When Mr. Ge Dongchang served as an official in the Hanlin Academy, his grandfather, Mr. Fu Zhai, bought a dark green robe from the market. One day, he locked the door and went out, but upon returning, he lost the key and feared it had been mistakenly left on the bed. Looking through the window, he saw the robe standing upright like a person. Then, hearing a startled cry, it fell to the ground. The crowd suggested burning it. At that time, Mr. Liu Xiaogu, who was staying in the same lodging, said: "This must be the clothing of a deceased person, with a spirit attached to it." Spirits are composed of yin energy, and they disperse when exposed to sunlight. So they placed the robe under the scorching sun and repeatedly exposed it for several days. Then, they placed it back in the house and observed it closely, and it no longer caused any disturbances. Moreover, Mr. Ge Dongchang had early baldness and always used a wig to maintain his braid. When he was about to be dismissed from office, the wig suddenly stretched out, winding like a snake's tail. Soon after, he retired to the countryside. This too was the hair of the deceased, affected by waning energy and undergoing transformation.

Xu Kāihòu, a scholar from Deqing, was also a Renxu predecessor. When he first entered the academy, every night while he was reading, there were reading sounds in an empty house behind his residence, echoing with the sounds of recitation. Upon closer listening, the sounds were of regulated verse compositions typical of the imperial examination system. When he opened the door, he saw nothing. One night, he stole quietly and held his breath, peeking inside, and saw a young man wearing a blue half-sleeve and a blue silk shirt, holding a scroll and sitting with his back to the moon, nodding and reciting, as if savoring the words. He did not appear to be a ghost or a spirit causing mischief. Later, there were no significant consequences. A story from a Tang dynasty novel tells of a fox spirit taking the imperial examinations, with two questions or topics, all in four-character rhyming phrases, the writing quite ancient and obscure. Perhaps this fox spirit was also taking the examinations? This story was told by Gō Dōngcháng, a respected elder and scholar, and mentions that Gō and Xu were both Jinshi in the same year. A Taoist priest at the Eight Sacrifices Temple in Urumqi, over eighty years old, one night, spread out 7,000 coins on a mat and died lying on it. The community discussed using these coins to fund the priest's burial. One night, the priest appeared in a dream to Wú Yùlín, an official in the workhouse, saying, "I guarded the official temple, and the coffin should be provided by the government. These coins are what I have earned through hard work. I beg you to place them in the coffin, and I will retrieve them in my next life." Wú Yùlín felt compassion and agreed to the priest's request. After the burial, he sighed and said, "Burying the coins with me, and burying me in the wilderness, is like collecting and burying bones, and the bones will surely be exposed." I replied, "Even if you bury the coins with the coffin, and someone takes them from the coffin, they will become a malevolent spirit. Who would risk their life to argue with a ghost over 7,000 coins? It will surely be fine." Everyone laughed pleasantly. However, Wú Yùlín's argument was correct.

In the spring of the year of the Rabbit, I returned from Urumqi to Barkol. My old servant, Xian Ning, was sleeping on horseback in a dense fog and became separated from the group. Mistakenly following the hoofprints of wild horses, he entered a chaotic mountain area and became lost, unable to find a way out. Believing himself to be doomed to die, he chanced upon a corpse lying at the foot of a cliff. Apparently, the corpse was that of a fugitive who had frozen to death while fleeing. The fugitive had food tied to his back in a cloth bag, which Xian Ning used to satisfy his hunger. Therefore, he prayed and wished, "I will bury your bones. If you have any spirit, please guide my horse." He then moved the body into a cave in the rock. Encountering a tight passage of rocks, he wandered aimlessly, allowing the horse to walk. After traveling more than ten miles, he suddenly found a path out of the mountains and found himself in the territory of Hami. Comrade Xu, a military officer in Hami, was an old acquaintance from Urumqi. Therefore, Xian Ning went to Comrade Xu's office to wait for me. I arrived two days later. Meeting as if we had been separated for centuries, it is unknown whether the ghost had any spirit and guided him out, or whether a benevolent thought from a god saved him, or whether it was just a lucky coincidence. Comrade Xu said, "I would rather attribute the credit to ghosts and gods, to encourage those who cover and bury bones."

Dong Qujiang, the respected elder, recounted that when Gu Xiajun completed the compilation of the *Yuan Poetry Selection*, there was a child of five or six years old in the household who suddenly raised his hand and pointed outward, saying, "There are hundreds of people in formal attire bowing at the door." Ah! Even ghosts are fond of fame. I believe that by rescuing the obscure and supporting the submerged, gathering the scattered and abandoned, through the power of commendation, we illuminate the darkness. They would surely feel gratitude in the underworld, which is only natural. However, there are those who connect through reputation and call upon disciples, exhausting resources and causing harm, elevating each other to a sacred status. This is not limited to the late Ming period, where there is much false praise; even the members of the Yuequan Poetry Society suffer from the influence of superficiality. Because there are many who form factions, and those who privately vie for fame trample on each other, even after death, it is difficult to reach a definitive judgment, let alone during the days of literary and drinking gatherings, where mutual praise is common. The *Wenxuan* of Emperor Wu of Liang included He Xun, but did not include a single word. The ancients had a far-sighted view.

My second daughter, who married into the Yuan family of Changshan, lives in a place called Jiaojiaqiao. This year, when she returned to her parents' home, she mentioned that about two or three miles from where she lives, there was a peasant woman returning to her husband's home. Her father accompanied her back to her in-laws' house. On the way, the woman entered a tomb forest to relieve herself and stayed there for a long time before returning. Her father noticed that she looked and behaved somewhat differently, and her voice was also not the same. He secretly suspected something but had no way to bring it up. After arriving home, the woman's husband privately told his parents, "My wife has been living peacefully for a long time, but now I see her and feel uneasy. Why is that?" His parents dismissed his words as nonsense and sent him back to bed. The couple's room was separated from their parents' by a wall. That night, they suddenly heard a commotion. They got up quietly and listened, and heard their son crying out loudly. The family rushed in, breaking down the door, and saw a creature like a black donkey rushing out, accompanied by flashes of fire. It leaped away in a single bound. When they looked at their son, he was left with only a pool of blood. At dawn, they went to look for the woman, but could not find her. They suspected that she had been eaten by the creature. This story is very similar to the account of a "Luo Sha" ghost in *Taiping Guangji*. Perhaps it was a ghost?
