From this, we can see that Buddhist scriptures are not entirely false, and novels and unofficial histories are not entirely fabricated.

In the region of Hejian, there was a woman who was inclined to licentiousness, yet she was extremely unattractive. Every day, she would dress up and lean on the door, but no one paid attention to her. Later, her husband followed Gao Ye Fei to serve in Tianchang, where he was highly trusted and entrusted with important duties. He seized wealth through force and cunning, sending back a large amount of money each year. The woman, relying on his wealth, enticed young men, and her door became like a market. However, when Gao Ye Fei was punished, her husband fled back home, leaving all their bags and chests empty. Even household items were sold off, and they were almost entirely depleted. Only a plain woman remained, with venereal sores all over her body. People thought that without a substantial fortune, this woman had no reason to remain chaste. Isn't this the way of heaven?

My great-grandfather, Grand Master Zhan Yuan, my uncle, Lord Jun Zhang, and my elder brother, Lord Xu Sheng, all died from palpitations and insomnia. Xu Sheng's son, You Yun, also suffered from this illness. One day, while renovating the house, a carpenter looked at the corner of the building and laughed, saying, "There is something here. If you break through, you will find bricks arranged like a small shrine, with an old lamp stand inside." He said that this object could cause insomnia, and it was a magical item from the time of the altar. You Yun was cured from that day on. In the spring of Ding Wei, my cousin Ru Lun told me about this, and I wondered what the reason for this was? However, considering that this object hidden in the wall could control the life and death of the owner, the belief that a house can have auspicious or inauspicious qualities is worth believing. Dai Hu Cao Lin, who served in the inner court with his calligraphy, once dreamed of the underworld, where he met an old friend who was an official. They stayed and talked, and he happened to open his register, only to see his own name written in red cursive script, resembling the character for "犀" (rhinoceros). The official then seized and covered it. He seemed slightly angry, but when asked, he did not answer. Suddenly, he woke up in a state of panic, unable to understand the cause. He happened to tell Qu Wen Da Gong about this, who thought deeply and said, "This is probably a simplified record in the underworld, like a brief summary of the department. The two characters '户' and '中' written together resemble the character '犀'. Will you end up as a Langzhong in the Ministry of Household?" Later, it turned out as Qu Wen Da Gong predicted.

Mr. Huo Yishu of Dongguang, during the year Jia Chen of the Yongzheng era, passed the local examination but remained in the capital without success. He sought guidance through a dream at the shrine of Lü Dongbin, where a deity showed him a poem: "Six-petaled plum blossoms inserted into his head, who would stop before death? Look at the noble crane in the clouds, flying to the Three Dukes and enduring nine autumns." By the fifth year of Yongzheng, when the hat's top was first standardized, its copper disk with six petals resembling a plum blossom made him realize the meaning of the first line. He privately thought that the crane symbolized the highest rank, and the Three Dukes were the position of the prime minister. If this line had come true, the last two lines must also come true. Later, he rose from the position of Zhongshu Sheren to become the Governor of Fengtianfu, but was exiled to a military post called Kuisutu, which was actually the third ducal position. In official documents, the word "dai" was abbreviated to "dai," perfectly matching the poem's language, and indeed, he returned after nine years. While in the frontier, he adopted the pseudonym "Cloud Crane," using the phrase from the poem. Later, he told this story to Mr. Yao'an. Mr. Yao'an said: "The character 'Huo' has the top part of 'yun' (cloud) and the lower half of 'he' (crane), subtly hinting at your surname, not a mere coincidence." Mr. Huo sighed and said: "It's not just that. In my early years, I had abundant energy and was eager to advance, thinking I could achieve high office immediately, but I ultimately fell and failed, due to the second line of the poem, which the deity warned me about. It's regrettable that at that time I did not think about it."

Anciently, divination was done using tortoise shells, and Confucius systematized the *I Ching* (Book of Changes), highly praising the virtue of the yarrow stalks. However, tortoise shell divination gradually fell out of use, and the *Fire Pearl Forest* began to use coins instead of yarrow stalks, though it still required six tosses. The *Ling Qi Jing* started to form hexagrams with a single toss, but it still required arranging the results. By the time of the oracle sticks at the shrines, one could draw them in a single pull, making the process even simpler. Shrines generally have oracle sticks, but none are more effective than those of Guan Yu. Among Guan Yu's oracle sticks, none are more effective than those at the shrine near the Zhengyang Gate. During the year, from New Year's Day to New Year's Eve, and throughout the day, from dawn to dusk, the sound of shaking the tubes is always clear and continuous. One tube is not enough, so multiple tubes are placed. The situation is chaotic and mixed-up, with many forms changing rapidly, leaving no time for verification, nor can it be reasoned about. Even with a thousand hands and eyes, it cannot be fully responded to. Yet, the oracle sticks drawn are as accurate as if spoken directly. Why is this so? The most extraordinary case occurred during the imperial examination in the year Ren Shen of the Qianlong era (1772). A southern scholar fasted and washed to pray on the first day of the third month, requesting to be shown the exam questions. He drew an oracle stick that read: "Looking at the hidden parts, strange to you; a laughing dagger in a boat; the fence is broken, but there is nothing to worry about; a bird is born with a care for its feathers." The question related to the *Mencius* text was: "Cao Jiao asked: Can all people become Yao and Shun?" This corresponds to the first line; the question related to the *Analects* text was: "The Master smiled gently and said: Why use a butcher's knife to kill a chicken?" This corresponds to the second line; the question related to the *Doctrine of the Mean* text was: "Therefore, heaven, in producing all things, must develop their nature according to their materials." This corresponds to the fourth line. Truly, it is beyond comprehension.

Mr. Sun Xuchuan once recounted that his friend once suffered from a cold illness, and in a state of drowsiness, he felt his soul and breath soaring, drifting with the wind, until he arrived at an official residence. Upon closely examining the interior, he saw only ghosts and spirits, realizing it was the underworld. He saw someone enter through a side door and decided to follow him, finding no one to rebuke or forbid him. He then sat with the crowd under the eaves, and no one questioned him either. Peering at the hall, he saw litigants swarming, with the King of the Underworld checking records on his left and holding a brush in his right hand. Some cases were settled with just a few words, while others took dozens or hundreds of words to resolve, much like the criminal courts of the mortal world. When the conch shell signaled the proceedings, all submitted without further words. Suddenly, he saw a respected official enter in full regalia, and the King of the Underworld invited him to sit, asking what the dispute was about. The official then complained about the ingratitude of his former disciples and officials, naming several dozen individuals, speaking with great resentment. The King of the Underworld seemed unconvinced, waiting for him to finish, then bowed and said: "These people compete aggressively and push others out of the way, devising countless schemes. The heavenly way is clear and evident, and they will ultimately suffer divine punishment. However, it is acceptable for the gods to punish them, but it is not appropriate for you to blame them. Those who plant peaches and plums will reap the fruit, while those who plant thorns will reap thorns. Have you not heard this? The people you admire are mostly those who flatter power. Once the power is gone, you then hold them to the standards of righteousness, which is like trying to find fire in ice. You are mistaken in this. How can you have time to blame others?" The official was disappointed and hesitated for a long time before retreating. His friend, who had been acquainted with the official, wanted to approach and inquire, but suddenly heard a shout behind him. Turning around, he was startled awake.

Master Wang, the long-serving servant of Grand Secretary Dong Wenke, was of a modest and cautious nature, skilled in managing the household entrance, and for several decades had never offended a single person — this was the so-called "Master Wang the Monk." He once recounted following Grand Secretary Dong to the abandoned garden of General Bo, where on a moonlit night he sat on a stone to cool off. From a distance, he saw one person fleeing in a state of panic, while another intercepted and stopped him, grasping his arm and sitting with him beneath the tree. The one who had been stopped said, "I thought you had ascended to heaven long ago — how is it that we meet here?" He then first recounted the depth of their former friendship, and then accused the other of having betrayed him in matters of trust. "In one matter, you took advantage of my urgent need and used difficult words to pressure me, how much did you profit in the middle? In another matter, you deceived me by pretending I did not understand, exaggerating the numbers to trick me — how much did you gain from that?" In this way, he recounted dozens of such incidents, striking him on the cheek for each one. His anger surged forth, as if he wished to devour him.

A sudden old man emerged from the grass and said, "He has now fallen into the realm of hungry ghosts. Why must you harm each other?" He added, "Debts must be repaid, but why must you be so hasty?" One of them became even angrier and said, "Now that he is a hungry ghost, how can he repay the debt?" The old man replied, "When karma is complete, then the debt will be repaid. The laws of the underworld state that all borrowed money with principal and interest, if there is good fortune in the next life, will be repaid; if there is no good fortune, it will be exempted, because it is limited by strength. If wealth is taken by coercion or enticement, even through countless rebirths, it must still be made up for. If there is no good fortune to offset, then one must be repaid by becoming a domestic animal, or if one lifetime is not enough to offset, then it must be repaid over several lifetimes. Is not the pig Mr. Dong ate tonight his eleventh incarnation as a servant?" The anger of the other person seemed slightly calmed. They then let go and each went their own way. The old man suspected it was an earth deity. What was said concerning the servant, Mr. Wang still saw it, indeed, he was the most cunning. In the same year, during a meeting at the provincial governor's office in Fujian, while the food was still being served, a servant brought a small child past the hall. The child was terrified and would not move forward, saying, "There are countless strange ghosts, all of them over ten feet tall, shouldering the beams and pillars." The others heard the cries and rushed out to ask what was happening. As they looked up, they saw dust falling from the ceiling, making a sound like scattering beans. They quickly jumped out, but the beam had already collapsed and fallen to the ground. Everyone raised their hands in relief, saying that the ghosts and spirits were protecting them. The governor of Huguang, who was then serving as a provincial inspector, upon hearing this story, sighed and said, "Since there are ghosts and spirits everywhere protecting us, there must be ghosts and spirits everywhere observing us."

The Collected Notes of the Reading and Observing Pavilion, Volume Seven: As I Have Heard, Part One Previously, I had begun to write the *Luan Yang Xia Yu Lu* (Notes of the Summer at Luan Yang), but it was still in the rough draft stage when it was suddenly stolen and published by a bookseller, which was not my intention. However, learned and refined gentlemen may not consider this a mistake, and some have informed me of new additions. Therefore, I supplemented and patched together old news, and thus formed another four volumes. Ouyang Xiu once said: "Things tend to gather around what one likes; is it not true?" Therefore, it is known that having a particular preference will inevitably lead to a gradual spread and not stopping. Matters of the world often occur like this, and one can also engage in deep and long-term thinking about them. On the 21st day of the seventh month in the 56th year of the Qianlong era (1791), during the sexagenary cycle of Xin Hai. A man from Taiyuan named Zhe Sheng Yu Lan recounted that in his hometown, there was a practitioner of fengshui who descended from the altar and wrote a poem in large characters: "Heroes of a generation left to the passing waves, great ambitions are vainly grasping the spear of Lu Yang. The imperial court has urgent military strategies, the heavens and earth are indifferent to the many bones of war. Old fortifications bring new grass and trees in spring, wandering spirits at night review the old mountains and rivers. The elite sons of the ten counties of Chen, what is the bitter recitation of Du the Old?" The poem was signed as "the defeated general of Shi Garden." All were shocked, knowing it was General Sun of Bai Gu. In the battle of Shi Garden, the defeat was due to the emperor's urgent order to fight, and the fault does not lie with the general. The poem compares itself to Fang Guan's chariot warfare, taking it as a personal mistake. The heart of a virtuous gentleman sees Wang Huazhen and others overturning the chariot and misleading the country, still trying to shift blame onto others. Truly like the three lights in the netherworld. A man from Datong named Du Sheng Yi Ci also recorded this poem, with "vainly grasping" becoming "betrayal," "spring growth" becoming "spring addition," and "what is meant" becoming "what is ultimately meant," with all four characters differing. Due to occasional differences in transcription, the main idea remains the same.

Mr. Xu Nangjin recounted that during the year 1705 of the Kangxi era, he passed through Manhe in Fucheng. The summer rain had turned the ground muddy, and his horse was too tired to proceed. He rested under a tree by the roadside and sat down to take a brief nap. In his drowsy state, he saw a woman bowing and speaking: "I am Lady Tang, the wife of Huang Baoning. I was here, forced by violent men, and fought to the death, but was ultimately killed by several swords. Although the authorities captured and executed the perpetrators, they did not honor me because I had already been defiled. The officials of the underworld pitied my loyalty and chastity, allowing me to remain in this place as the leader of the souls who died violently. It has been over forty years now. A beggar woman from a distant land, traveling alone, suddenly encountered three strong men who tied her to a tree and committed vile acts. There was nothing she could do except curse them and beg for death. Her biting her lip in shame was due to her lack of strength, not a lack of virtue. The judges were excessively harsh, which is unjust, isn't it? You look like a scholar and must understand the law. Please speak up for me." In his dream, he wanted to ask about her hometown, but he suddenly woke up. Later, he asked the scholars of Fucheng, but none knew of this incident. He also asked the old officials, but they could not find any records, as at that time, she was not considered a virtuous woman, and her story had been forgotten for a long time.

In the capital city, there was a specific temple where a fox once dwelled. A Taoist priest conducted a ritual and collected a substantial amount of money. After the ceremony concluded, the priest and his disciple were accounting for the income and expenses in front of the deity's seat and lamp. They discovered that there was still a shortfall of several coins. The master accused the disciple of embezzlement, while the disciple claimed that the master had made a mistake in the calculation. The sound of coins clinking continued until the third drumbeat, when the dispute still had not been resolved. Suddenly, a voice from the beam above said, "The coolness of early autumn makes me tired, and I wish to sleep. Why are you still arguing here?" The voice then questioned, "These missing coins, weren't you planning to buy aphrodisiacs and keep them in your pocket, then go to Liu Erjie's house in the alley behind the temple? She demanded a gold ring, and you gave it to her while drunk? How could you have forgotten?" The disciple turned away and covered his mouth. The Taoist priest then quietly gathered his records and left. Wei Fu, a barber who was staying in the temple, personally heard the conversation. He described the voice as sounding like that of a small child or young couple.

The Drought Demon and the Fox The drought demon wreaks havoc, as described in the *Clouds* poem of the *Book of Songs*, which is a matter documented in classical texts. The *Classic of Mountains and Seas* actually identifies the drought demon as a female, seemingly due to the language of the poem. However, according to its description, it is merely an evil spirit. In modern times, the drought demon is believed to be a zombie, and digging it up and burning it often brings rain. Rain is the joyous union of heaven and earth, but can the aura of a single zombie really fill the entire universe and prevent communication? Rain can also be made by dragons, but can the tricks of a single zombie really drive away divine beings and make them fear and retreat? What explanation can resolve this? Additionally, the belief that foxes can avoid lightning strikes has been prevalent since the Song Dynasty, as seen in various accounts. Is the fox innocent? If thunder strikes it at a fixed time, is it an excessive punishment, for the way of heaven is not like this. Is the fox guilty? Why can't it be punished at any time, but it is limited to a specific day and time, allowing it to know in advance and avoid it, even temporarily avoiding it, but after that time, it will never be pursued again, which is a lenient punishment. The way of heaven is not like this, so what explanation can resolve this? I happened to read a recent book called *Night Talks Collection*, where I saw the account of burning the drought demon and two accounts of foxes avoiding disasters. I noted my doubts and await those who study the principles of nature to explain it.

A house west of Tiger Fang Bridge, once the residence of Zhang Gongzi Wei from Nanpi, is now inhabited by Liu Yunfang, the Deputy Censor. Inside the house, there is a well that yields sweet water only at noon and midnight, but not at other times. The reason for this is unclear. Some say that yin energy rises at noon and yang energy is born at midnight, and the well water is in harmony with the earth's energy. However, the primordial energy is pervasive throughout heaven and earth, so why do other wells not respond to the earth's energy, while this one does? Western scholars, who are most adept at the study of natural phenomena, have recorded in *The Records of the Foreign Countries* that in a certain place, the water experiences twelve tides in a day, perfectly synchronized with the sundial. A man who wanted to understand the reason behind this phenomenon built a hut by the water and measured it day and night, but ultimately could not comprehend it, and in his frustration, he drowned himself. This well is of a similar nature. According to Zhang Du's *Records of the Inner Chamber*, it is said that after a person dies for several days, a bird will emerge from the coffin, known as the "sha." During the Tahe period, there was a man named Zheng who caught a large, blue-gray bird that was about five feet tall, but it suddenly disappeared. When he asked the people in the village about it, one person replied: "There was someone in the village who died several days ago, and a fortune-teller said that today the 'sha' would leave. The family watched and saw a large, blue-gray bird emerge from the coffin. Is the bird you caught the same one?" This bird is the modern-day "sha" spirit. Xu Xuan's *Records of the Divine* states: Peng Huzi, a young and strong man, once claimed there were no ghosts or gods. After his mother's death, a fortune-teller warned him: "On a certain day, the 'sha' will return, and there will be another death. You should leave to avoid it." The entire family, including the women and children, fled, but Peng Huzi stayed behind.

At night, someone pushed open the door, and the child, in a panic and without a plan, quickly hid in a large jar nearby, covering his head with a board. The mother felt her son on the board, and someone asked if there was no one under the board. The mother replied, "No." This is what is now called the "returning of the煞." Folklore says that children who die before their teeth come in do not have a煞, but those who have teeth do. Shamans can predict the date of the煞 in advance. My servants, Sun Wenju and Song Wen, are both proficient in this art. I once examined their books and found that they specifically use the year, month, day, hour, and celestial stems and earthly branches to calculate, but there is no special mystery to it. On a certain day when the凶煞 occurs, they use a certain charm to ward it off, but it is merely deceptive words to take money. Sometimes, in narrow living quarters where there is no place to avoid the煞, there are methods of suppression. They make it stay hidden and not come out, called "斩殃," which is especially absurd. However, my servant Song Wen, after his wife's death, summoned a shaman to perform斩殃, and since then, in the room where he lives, there is always a sound at night, and his young children often see its form, so it seems not entirely false. The vastness of heaven and earth contains everything, and the principles of the visible and invisible are inexhaustible. There is no need to twist the words, nor is it necessary to forcefully refute the theory.

Those who have died, their souls are registered in the records of the underworld. However, the Earth is said to be nine thousand miles in circumference and three thousand miles in diameter, with territories that cannot be counted. The population should be a hundred times greater than in China, and the number of ghosts should also be a hundred times greater. Why, then, do those who visit the underworld see only ghosts from China, and not a single ghost from outside the borders? Does every place have its own Yama? The official named De Mao, who oversees the affairs of the underworld, was asked about this but could not provide an answer. Those who do not die are recorded in the books of immortals. Yet, the legendary immortals such as Chi Song and Guang Cheng are known from ancient times. Why do the immortals encountered in later times all come from recent history? The records of Liu Xiang and others do not mention these immortals. Could it be that they will eventually pass away, as Zhu Xi suggested about Wei Boyang? The Taoist master Lou, who leads the Taoist organization, was also asked about this but could not provide an answer.

A Local Man Named Yan Xun, Suspected That His Wife Was Having an Affair with His Cousin, So He Took a Gun and Killed His Cousin, Then Returned Home and Killed His Wife. He drove the knife into her chest, and it made a clattering sound like striking iron or stone, yet it could not wound her. Some said: This was because the ghosts and spirits pitied her unjust death and protected her from the afterlife. But there are many who die unjustly—why do the spirits not protect them all? It must be that she had other good deeds, so she was quietly guarded and protected. Mr. Xuekun of Jingzhou was the son of Mr. Qianju. He was simple, honest, and unpretentious, upholding the traditions of his family, and he had great faith in the Daoist teachings. Once, he told his cousin Maoyuan: In the past, while at a certain temple, I saw monks using the idea of "fields of merit" to entice people to donate, in order to fund feasts and meat. Because of this, I wrote a treatise advising against giving alms. That night, I dreamed of a god, resembling the "Galam" of that religion, who spoke to me calmly and earnestly: "Do not do this. According to Buddhist teachings, compassion is vast and all beings are equal. Are these monks and nuns not one of all beings? We feed the birds and crows, and cherish the insects and mice, desiring their lives. These people rely on alms to live; if you make them hungry and cause them to die, have you not regarded them as less valuable than birds and mice? Among them, those who have broken the precepts and fallen into hell are indeed numerous. But because there is an owl, do you destroy all birds? Because there is a broken tiger cub, do you destroy all beasts? Is this reasonable? In terms of worldly law, if the land is insufficient to be distributed, it is unavoidable that people must find their own means of sustenance."

Monks and nuns are also one kind of common people, and their begging is also one way of seeking food. If one must consider their not farming or weaving as a drain on the state and the people, are they the only ones who do not farm or weave and thus drain the state and the people? Why don't you write articles one by one to ban them? Moreover, the world is vast, and this group is not limited to tens of thousands. If one were to cut off their source of clothing and food, the weak and frail will turn to the ditches; the fierce and cunning will take desperate risks. How will you deal with the aftermath? Han Yu criticized Buddhism, saying that the widowed, the orphaned, the elderly, the lonely, and the disabled have support. You have no strategy to support them but only to shorten their lives, which is not only not the intention of Buddhism but also not the intention of Confucius and Mencius. Four horses cannot catch up with the tongue. You should consider this. In my dream, I wanted to debate with you, but suddenly I woke up, and the words are still vividly remembered. What do you think of your argument? After pondering for a long time, Mao Yuan said: Your argument is correct, but his perspective is broad. However, human sentiments have always been this way, how can your one argument stop it? This spirit of incessant argument is quite common.

In the same year, Jinmen Gao, a native of Wu County, once anchored at night between Huaiyin, where he saw two old men meet on the shore. They sat down in a thatched pavilion by the water. One of the old men asked, "What have you been up to recently?" The other replied, "The owner has been escaping the heat in his garden, and I enter his waterside pavilion every day to watch a living secret play, which abounds with charms and is very interesting to watch. The fifth concubine is especially beautiful and charming. I saw her cut her hair with the owner as a vow, and in the future, she will become Guan Panpan in the Swallow Tower. She also agreed to be reincarnated as Yuxiao to serve Wei Gao again. The owner was moved to tears by this. However, I heard by chance that she and her mother secretly discussed, saying that the owner is old and should store money and silk early, as a plan to leave and play the pipa. Do you think these people are trustworthy?" They sighed deeply for a long time. Another old man then said, "I heard that her legitimate wife is very virtuous. Is that true?" The first old man shook his head and said, "In the world, very jealous people. Why say virtuous? Jealous and noisy and contentious, is to drive fish away from deep water."

This woman, when妾媵 came, treated the weaker ones with kindness, allowing them free movement and indulgence in promiscuous behavior, no longer restraining or controlling them, causing them to fall into debauchery, and their husbands left out of shame. She treated the stronger ones with proper etiquette, outwardly honoring them as equals but secretly opposing the husband, causing them to cultivate arrogance and defiance, and their husbands were unable to bear it and thus left. For those who could not be tamed by these two methods, she secretly incited and provoked each other, ensuring mutual destruction or failure, and such cases were numerous. Fortunately, if the conflict did not immediately lead to failure, and within the household, frequent complaints and quarrels were heard, causing the husband to enter the妾's room with words of complaint and a sad expression; entering the wife's room, he would have a gentle voice and a pleasant expression. His decision to stay or leave was known without asking. This is the kind of jealous person in the world, how can they be considered virtuous? The door was high, and listening to what was said, I agreed with the reasoning, but did not understand why she went into the water pavilion every day. As I was pondering this, an official boat sounded its bell and came, ready to anchor, and the two old men turned and were no longer seen. I then realized that they were not ordinary people.

My elder brother Qinghu said, "Those who drink salted water will have their blood congealed and die, and there is no medicine to cure it." In the village, there was a woman who drank some of this salted water and, in a state of panic and unable to act, suddenly an old woman forcefully entered the room and said, "Quickly take the soy milk ground by the neighbor who sells tofu and pour it into her. The salted water will meet the soy milk, and it will coagulate into tofu instead of blood." She then said, "I am an old fox from the village. I once heard this remedy from a fairy." After speaking, she disappeared. When they tried it, the woman indeed revived. Liu Juanzi's *Liu Juanzi Guiyi Fang* (Liu Juanzi's Legacy of Prescriptions) includes remedies allegedly given by ghosts, so this can be called a fox's remedy. The hired worker Qin Er Yan once drove a carriage from Li Family Ditch to Huai Town. On the way, he encountered someone holding a gun shooting at sparrows, and the horses all became frightened and ran away. Er Yan fell off the carriage in panic and lay horizontally in the road. He thought there was no chance of survival, but the horses suddenly stopped moving. By evening, he returned home and bought wine to celebrate. At night, with his companions, he talked about the strange event. He heard a voice from outside the window say, "Do you think the horses stopped on their own? It was we two who pulled on their reins." Er Yan opened the door to look out, but found no trace of anyone. The next day, he brought wine and meat to the place where he fell and offered a sacrifice. My late grandfather, the former governor of Yaoan, heard about it and said, "If ghosts seek food in such a way, why should they be blamed?"

A Local Resident Named Wang Wuxian — When he was young, he heard his name being called, and it was these two sounds, but he did not know if they were these two characters. He was an elderly school teacher. Once, he passed by an ancient tomb at night and heard the sound of whips and beatings, along with a reprimand: "You do not read or recognize characters, so you cannot understand principles. What cannot you do in the future? When you offend the heavenly laws, it will be too late for you to regret." He wondered who could be teaching disciples in the deep night and desolate wilderness, so he listened carefully and found that the sound came from a fox den. Wang Wuxian sighed deeply and said, "I did not expect to hear such words here." My uncle, the Righteous Southern Master, had a pawnshop in the western city. A hired worker named Chen Zhong was in charge of buying vegetables. His peers all said that he had extra profit and should share it with everyone. Chen Zhong denied it. The next day, the chest was locked and not opened, but the thousands of coins he had saved were only nine hundred left. There used to be a fox on the upper floor, which often talked to people through the window. Suspecting the fox, he went to ask it, and it clearly replied, "The nine hundred coins are what you are entitled to for your wages. I dare not take them. The rest have been embezzled daily and were not yours to begin with. Today is the Dragon Boat Festival, and I have already bought some bamboo leaves, wine, meat, chicken, fish, and various fruits and vegetables, as well as rice wine and realgar, all in the empty room downstairs. You should cook and prepare them early. If you delay, the heat will spoil them." He opened the door and saw that all the items were indeed there, but there was nothing to dispose of, so he finally ate them with others. This fox could be considered mischievous, but it was also quite pleased with itself.

The character "亥" has two heads and six bodies, marking the beginning of the art of character divination (拆字). During the Han dynasty, prophetic texts (图谶) often used the techniques of combining and separating strokes (离合点画). It was not until the Song dynasty, with figures like Xie Shi, that this practice became a specialized skill. Yet, it often yielded strange and accurate results. In the year Qianlong Jia Xu (1774), after I passed the palace examination but before the announcement of the results, I was at the home of Minister Dong Wenke when I met by chance a scholar from Zhejiang who was skilled in character divination. I wrote the character "墨" (ink), and the scholar said, "The dragon's head does not belong to you." When I split the character "里" (a measure of distance), it became "二甲" (second rank), and the lower part, four dots, suggested the fourth position in the second rank. However, he said, you will certainly enter the Hanlin Academy, for the four dots resemble the lower part of "庶" (common) and the upper part of "吉" (auspicious), making you a "庶吉士" (trainee scholar of the Hanlin Academy). Indeed, this came to pass. Later, in the autumn of Wu Zi (1778), I was punished for leaking confidential information, and my imprisonment was urgent. Each day, I was guarded by a military officer, and one officer surnamed Dong claimed to be skilled in character divination. I wrote the character "董" (Dong) and had him interpret it. He said, "You will be sent far away on military service—this is a journey of thousands of miles." I then wrote my name for him to interpret. He said, "The lower part is the character '口' (mouth), and the upper part is the radical of '外' (outside), meaning '口外' (beyond the mouth, or the frontier). The character '日' (sun) in the west becomes '夕' (evening), which suggests the Western Regions." I asked if I would be allowed to return, and he said, "The shape of the character resembles '君' (you) and also '召' (summon), so you will surely be granted release." When I asked in which year, he said, "The character '口' forms the outer frame of the character '四' (four), but it is missing two strokes, which means the time remaining is not enough for four years." This year is Wu Zi, and four years later will be Xin Mao (1781). The character "夕" is also a component of "卯" (the fourth earthly branch), which also aligns with the prediction. Indeed, I was sent to serve in Urumqi and returned to the capital in the sixth month of Xin Mao. This was due to the movement of the spirit, which moved the ghosts and spirits; the emergence of qi (vital energy) manifested in the form and image. This is no different from divination by counting yarrow stalks or burning tortoise shells—both follow the same principle. It seems miraculous, yet it is not truly supernatural.

The Doctor Hu Gongshan, of unknown origin, some say originally surnamed Jin, was actually an agent of Wu Sangui. After Wu Sangui's defeat, he changed his name, but there is no evidence to confirm this, and no one knows the details. I saw him when I was six or seven years old; he was over eighty years old, light and agile like a monkey, with martial skills unparalleled. Once while traveling by boat, at night he encountered bandits. He had no weapon, only holding an inverted tobacco pipe, moving swiftly like the wind, he stabbed several people in the nose, causing them to fall. However, he was very afraid of ghosts and never dared to sleep alone all his life. He said that when he was young, he once met a zombie. He punched it, and it felt like hitting wood or stone. He almost got caught by it, but fortunately, he jumped onto the top of a tall tree. The zombie circled the tree and jumped around, until dawn, it held onto the tree and did not move. When a group of bell-trotting animals passed by, he finally dared to look down. It had white hair all over its body, red eyes like cinnabar, fingers like curved hooks, and teeth protruding from its lips like sharp blades. He was so terrified that he almost lost his soul. Another time, he stayed at a mountain inn. At night, he felt something wriggling in his bed, suspecting it was a snake or a mouse. Soon it started to struggle and push, growing larger and larger, emerging next to the pillow. It turned out to be a naked woman, who hugged him with both arms, like being tied with a thick rope, kissing him and breathing on him, the smell of blood filled his nose, and he fainted. The next day, he was revived with water. From then on, he was extremely frightened. After dusk, when he heard wind or saw moonlight, he would tremble and step back.

The Magistrate of Nanpi, Ju Gong Zhan, spent twenty years in the administrative offices of the state and county, practicing the handling of official documents and giving gifts of money or goods without a year passing. Having amassed wealth, he then followed the example to obtain an official position, thinking it would be as easy as driving a light cart on a familiar road. When he took office, however, he was stupid and lifeless like a wooden chicken, turning red in the face and speaking haltingly whenever the two parties in a dispute argued, unable to utter a single word. In the presence of superiors, his responses to advances and retreats were all in disarray. After more than a year, he was dismissed due to his lack of ability. On the day of his resignation, he dreamed of a man with unkempt hair and a dirty face who bowed deeply and said, "You have been dismissed from your post; I shall take my leave from you." He woke up suddenly, feeling his mind and spirit suddenly clear. With no means of returning to his former life, he resumed his old profession, and then became sharp and decisive, making judgments as smoothly as water flows. Was the figure in the dream his old grudge, or perhaps the poverty demon sent by Han Yu?

Duke Qiu Wenda, when serving as an official responsible for speech or advice and as a tutor to the crown prince, encountered the day and, at the fifth watch, headed to the Imperial Garden. Along the way, he noticed a high willow tree with lights surrounding it, seeming to have some other matter. Upon arriving, he found a guard hanged from the tree. The crowd tried to save him, and after a long time, he regained consciousness. He explained that he had stopped briefly to rest and saw a small room by the roadside with lights. A young woman was sitting by a round window, calling out to him. He jumped through the window and entered, only to find his neck already hanging. It is likely that the ghost of the hanged person transformed to seek a substitute. Such incidents are common in many places, but this ghost is even more peculiar. To the northwest of the Temple of Agriculture and to the south of the Pavilion of Literature, there is a collection of stagnant water, where water ghosts often lure people. When I was thirteen or fourteen years old, I saw a man enter the water without reason, already half-submerged. The crowd shouted and pulled him back, and he was forcibly returned. He sat in a dazed state for a long time, gradually regaining his senses. He asked what had troubled him and why he had drowned himself. He said that he had no particular trouble but was very thirsty and saw a teahouse, went to ask for a drink, and still remembered the sign hanging above the door, which was a white board with green characters, saying "Duiying Pavilion." The naming of the teahouse has a literary meaning. Who wrote it, and who inscribed it? This ghost is even more peculiar.

Shandong Liu Shanmo, my fellow examinee from the Dingmao year, was known for his cunning and cleverness, and everyone jokingly called him Liu Guigǔ. Liu was naturally witty and would sometimes refer to himself by this nickname. Thus, the name Guigǔ became widely known, while his given name or other titles were less recognized. In the Qianlong era, during the Xinwei year, Liu rented a small house in a military camp. Tian Baiyan happened to pass by and chatted with him. Looking around, Tian sighed and said, "This was once the old residence of Fengyan Zhang San. The courtyard remains unchanged, but the fragrant earth of the burial mound has been there for over twenty years." Liu was startled and said, "Since I moved into this house, I have repeatedly dreamed of a beautiful woman wandering through the halls and corridors. Could she be the same person?" Tian asked for details, and Liu confirmed it was true. After deep thought, Liu pounded the table and said, "What kind of lustful spirit dares to haunt Liu Guigǔ? If it appears in its true form, I will beat it severely." Tian replied, "When this woman was alive, she was truly a Guigǔzi, manipulating situations in countless ways and bewitching many. What is there to say about a fake Guigǔzi? The capital is vast; why must one necessarily live with ghosts?" He strongly advised Liu to move elsewhere. I also once visited Liu at this place and remembered that it was across from the residence of Ge Jiezhou, with about six or seven households nearby. Now, I cannot point to the location.

Historian Songtao said, when he first served as an official in the Ministry of Revenue, he lived in the Annan Camp, next door to a widow. One night, a thief broke into her house, having already dug through the wall. Suddenly, the thief cried out, "There's a ghost!" and fled in a panic over the wall. Nobody knew what he had seen. Could it be that the gods, moved by his loneliness, secretly protected him? Another day, after the elder Gōdong had finished his meal, he sat on the steps to admire chrysanthemums. Suddenly, he heard a loud cry: "There's a thief!" The voice was muffled, like a cow bellowing inside a jar. The whole family was startled, and soon he kept calling out repeatedly. Listening carefully, it turned out to be in the furnace pit under the eaves. He quickly called a guard to come and look, and they found a dark figure of a starving man, who stood up with his head held high and knelt. He said he had sneaked in two nights ago and hid in this pit, hoping to steal out when it was dark. He hadn't expected a light rain at the second watch of the night, and the lady ordered the two jars of pickled vegetables to be moved, placing them on the pit's boards, so he couldn't get out. He still hoped that when the rain stopped, they would move the jars down, but they didn't move them for two days. He was starving and couldn't bear it anymore. He thought that if he came out, he would be caught, and the punishment would be no more than a beating. If he didn't come out, he would end up as a starving ghost. So he started making noise to call for help. The story is very strange, but it is actually what one would expect according to human nature. Recording it is also enough to provide a laugh.

Li Qixin, a minor official in Hejian Prefecture, had a basic understanding of written language. One day, he asked someone, "What are the creatures called 'xiāo niǎo' and 'pò jìng'?" Someone replied, "The 'xiāo niǎo' eats its mother, and the 'pò jìng' eats its father; they are both symbols of filial impiety." Li clapped his hands and said, "That's right. I was suffering from a cold illness, and in a delirious state, my soul reached the underworld. I saw two officials sitting side by side, and one official held a document and said, 'In a certain place, a fox bit its grandson to death. Beasts are without knowledge, so it is difficult to hold them accountable by human reason. Now, we only discuss compensation, not imposing the crime of filial impiety.' The left official said, 'Foxes are different from other beasts. Those who have refined their form into humans should be judged by human law; those who have not refined their form should still be judged by animal standards.' The right official said, 'That is not correct. Beasts may differ from humans in other aspects, but in terms of familial bonds, they share the same nature as humans. Ancient kings punished the 'xiāo niǎo' and 'pò jìng' because they are not beasts but are exempt from punishment. They should be judged for filial impiety and sent to hell.' The left official nodded in agreement, saying, 'Your words are correct.' Suddenly, an official came down with a document and slapped Li with his hand, causing him to wake up in a state of shock. Everything he said was remembered clearly, but he did not understand the meaning of 'xiāo niǎo' and 'pò jìng.' He secretly suspected they were unrighteous birds and beasts. The story is unusual, so even the underworld is troubled by deliberation."

Understanding the complexities of legal cases is difficult to pin down to one perspective. According to what I have seen, there are cases that fall outside the usual legal framework. For example, someone leaves home, is falsely reported dead, and their parents sell their wife as a concubine. The husband returns, pressured by his parents, unable to sue. He sneaks into the home of the person who took his wife, waits for an opportunity to see her, and then takes her away. After a year, they are captured. If considered not adulterous, she has already remarried; if considered adulterous, she is still with her original husband. The official has no legal precedent to follow. Another type of bandit, called "gandan," do not steal but rob other thieves. They wait for other thieves to go out, either attacking their hideouts or ambushing them on the road, taking their stolen goods. One day, they fight each other, and both are brought to the official. If considered not thieves, they are actually robbers; if considered thieves, they are stealing from other thieves, and the official still has no legal precedent to follow. There is also a case where a woman becomes pregnant through adultery, is punished, and the official, following the law, rules that the child should go to the adulterer. After the child is born, the original husband, filled with hatred, kills the child. The adulterer sues for murder of his child. Although there is a legal precedent, it ultimately feels that the adulterer's claim is logical but lacks emotion, while the original husband's actions are emotional but lack logic, making it impossible to maintain fairness. It is unknown how the officials in the afterlife would judge such cases.

Outside the Fengyi Gate, there stood an ancient pine tree in the Feng family's garden, which many of the earlier scholars and literati had written poems or inscriptions about. Mr. Qian Xiangshu, a scholar, still saw the pine tree when he was alive, but it has now been cut down and turned into firewood. According to He Huafeng, it was said that when the pine tree was still alive, during calm winds and bright moonlight, one might hear the sounds of stringed instruments and flutes. One day, a prominent figure happened to visit the place and went there with his guests at night to see the pine tree. After the second drumbeat (indicating late at night), there was the sound of a pipa (a Chinese lute) coming from what seemed like inside the tree trunk or from its top. After some time, a soft, slow singing began: "People say winter nights are cold, But I say winter nights are good. The quilt is warm like spring, And there's no need to worry about the dawn." The prominent figure shouted in disbelief or anger: "What kind of old spirit dares to sing such lewd words to me?" The music stopped abruptly. Shortly after, the sound started again. The song began with new lyrics: "My lover is like a peach or plum blossom, And I am like a pine or cypress tree. Peach and plum blossoms fade easily, But pines and cypresses remain unchanged." The prominent figure nodded in agreement: "This is somewhat closer to elegance." As the lingering sound swayed, a faint whisper could be heard outside the tree: "This old man is surprisingly easy to deal with. However, when he speaks these words, it brings joy, and there is a sudden sound as if a string has snapped." Upon listening again, everything became silent.

Tenant farmer Bian Jinbao, resting by the side of the field, laid down on a block of earth for a brief sleep. In a drowsy state, he heard voices saying: "What happened in the government office yesterday?" One person replied: "Yesterday, they examined the second wife of someone and gave her a hundred iron rods. Although she had a sickly appearance, her eyebrows and eyes were still beautiful like a painting, and her skin was smooth as congealed fat. Every time she received a rod, she cried out in pain, her voice winding and turning, like the sound of a flute played by the wind, making people's hearts break. My hands trembled, and I could not strike, almost receiving a whipping myself." The questioner sighed and said: "Only because she is so alluring, she has bewitched her husband, inflicted suffering on his previous wife and children, and committed many evil deeds." Jinbao privately wondered what kind of government office would use iron rods, and he wanted to get up and ask about it. Stretching and rubbing his eyes, he found himself in a desolate place with wild vegetation and smoke, looking around and seeing only silence and emptiness.

The townsfolk of Jiahan often recounted that Zhang Er You and Zhang San Chen were brothers. Zhang Er You passed away first, and Zhang San Chen treated his nephew as if he were his own son, managing their land and property, and planning their marriages, all with great effort and dedication. When the nephew fell ill with a serious disease, Zhang San Chen took care of him, almost neglecting sleep and meals. After the nephew's death, Zhang San Chen was often distracted and seemed to be missing something, and people praised his affectionate nature. Several years later, Zhang San Chen fell gravely ill and, in a delirious state, muttered to himself, "How strange!" Soon after, he arrived in the underworld, where his older brother accused him of killing their nephew and cutting off his ancestral worship, asking, "Is this not an injustice?" From then on, Zhang San Chen would occasionally mumble incoherently. One day, he regained some consciousness and said, "It is my fault." His older brother, in the presence of Yama, rebuked him, saying, "This child was not beyond education. As your uncle, you were only a step away from being a father. You knew how to raise him but not how to educate him, allowing him to do whatever he wanted. Fearing to go against his wishes, you let him indulge in promiscuity, leading to his death from a terrible illness. Who else could have killed him?" Zhang San Chen was at a loss and had no response. He realized it was too late to regret his actions and struck himself with a stick before dying. Zhang San Chen's actions were also difficult for people of his time, but it was the *Spring and Autumn Annals* that held the wise to high standards. Nevertheless, one cannot say that Zhang Er You was harsh. Wang Zhi Xin from Pingding was a scholar I selected in the year Ji Mao. He asked me to inscribe his stepmother's tombstone, mentioning that his mother gave birth to a brother named Zhi Pu and a half-brother named Zhi Bi. In daily life, they were treated equally in terms of food, clothing, and other matters. When they made mistakes, they were scolded and punished, and the three brothers were treated equally. How virtuous, these few words sum it up.

The record of Qian Zunwang's diligent pursuit of knowledge in his book "Mingqiu Ji" states: After Zhao Qingshang's death, his descendants sold his inherited books, and in Wukang Mountain, ghostly cries were heard even during the day. Everything that gathers must eventually disperse; why is this not understood? The former residence of Ming Shouning Hou in Xingji was mostly sold off, leaving only the main hall standing. Later, the wood from the residence was sold to my ancestor. On the day of dismantling, the carpenters also heard crying sounds coming from the columns. The souls of those who are devoted to their passions have a similar fate. I once said to Dong Qujiang, "The great mountains and rivers of the earth, even the Buddha considers them as bubbles and shadows. How much more so for these small matters! After I am gone, if my books, instruments, and playthings are scattered among people, and connoisseurs point out and admire them, saying, 'These were once Ji Xiaolan's possessions,' this would also be a good story. What is there to regret?" Dong Qujiang replied, "You say this, but your desire for fame still exists." I would say that to pass the time and entertain myself, I cannot help but do so. When I am no longer here, what does it matter? Let them be eaten by insects and mice, or left to dust and mud. Therefore, my books have no seals, and my inkstones have no inscriptions. Just like beautiful flowers and bright moons, famous waters and renowned mountains, when they happen to come into my possession, I enjoy them. Once they pass by like clouds and mist, I no longer ask whose property they are. Why bother engraving names and inscriptions for future generations? This view is particularly free-spirited.

Government officials who commit immoral acts against female servants are punished only by the loss of their salary. This is because such crimes occur within the confines of a household, making them difficult to detect; the law is profound and subtle, aiming to prevent the gradual development of false accusations and retaliation. However, violent coercion and forceful actions bring severe hidden retribution. Mr. Dai Suotang recounted: During the late reign of the Kangxi Emperor, a man from a prominent family defiled a female servant. The servant's breath was obstructed, leading to a condition similar to esophageal cancer. At the time, the woman was pregnant. On the verge of death, the servant touched her belly and said, "Is it a boy or a girl? Can you avenge me?" Later, a daughter was born. When she grew up a bit, she was very intelligent and beautiful. The man from the prominent family then took her as a concubine and had a son. The son suffered from a condition similar to diabetes and died young. The daughter, unrestrained in behavior, eventually involved in a public lawsuit, greatly damaging the family's reputation. Over ten years, the woman wore white mourning clothes and carried the coffin, while the daughter wore a green robe and appeared in court. Mr. Dai Suotang saw all of this with his own eyes, as if it happened just a few days ago. Isn't this the result of accumulated resentment, producing such a remarkable person as a form of retribution? Mr. Dai Suotang also recounted: Someone tried to seduce a female servant, but she did not respond. The master became angry and said, "If you dare to refuse again, I will beat you to death." She cried and told her husband. He was deeply intoxicated and, in a rage, said, "If you dare to defy me, I will stab you in the chest." The woman angrily said, "Whether to comply or not, death is the result. Better to die first." She eventually hanged herself. The officials came to investigate, but the body showed no signs of injury, and there were no witnesses. She died next to her husband, and there was no one to blame, so they could not investigate further. However, from then on, the room where she hanged herself, even on sunny days, was still dim and misty. At night, there was a sound like tearing silk. In front of lamps and under the moon, often saw black mist swaying like human shadows. Tracing it revealed nothing. Like this for over ten years, until the master died. Before the master's death, day and night, people were stationed around the sickbed, suspecting that he saw something.

The military official Wu Tulin of Ürümqi reported that his cousin once traveled to Jing County to visit a friend, but encountered a night rain and sought shelter in a ruined temple. The walls were in disrepair, the ground overgrown with weeds, and there was no one living nearby. Only the temple gate remained sturdy enough to provide shelter, so he stayed there temporarily, waiting for the rain to cease. At that time, the clouds were as dark as ink, and in the darkness he heard a woman's voice saying: "A vengeful ghost bows, requesting a single set of paper clothing, so that the white bones may carry gratitude." The man was terrified and could not move, but judging that there was no way to escape, he forced himself to rise and ask her. The ghost wept and said: "I was once a village girl. I happened to be passing through this temple alone when a monk blocked my path and detained me. I cried and scolded, refusing to comply, and was killed in anger. At that time, my clothes had already been stripped from me, and I was buried naked. Now, over a hundred years have passed. Though I am in the afterlife, I still have a sense of modesty and shame. Without a single thread of clothing, I am ashamed to face the gods. Therefore, rather than bear this grievance, I have hidden my form and not appeared. Now, I have met a noble gentleman. If you would take a few sheets of colored paper, cut them into a skirt and tunic, and burn them at the temple gate, so that the ghost may be covered, then I may ascend to the underworld and be reborn in the cycle of reincarnation. Only you, please have mercy and rescue me." The man, trembling with fear, nodded in agreement, and the crying sound then ceased. Later, he was unable to return to that place, and so the paper was never burned. He often said that he had broken this promise, causing this ghost to suffer hatred in the netherworld, and he always felt uneasy and troubled.

According to Dao Guang, there was a scholar who passed by the Yue Temple at night. The red doors were tightly closed, but someone emerged from the temple. Knowing it was a divine being, he prostrated himself and called out to the Supreme Sage. The person reached out and supported him, saying, "I am not a great god, but an official in charge of the mirror on the right platform, carrying documents here." When asked about the meaning of the mirror, he replied, "It is the mirror of karma, which reflects only the good and evil of one's actions. But down to the subtlest thoughts in one's heart, emotions are deceitful, myriad in form, arising and disappearing without constancy, harboring unmeasurable depths, profound and secretive, leaving no trace to be discerned. Often, outwardly they appear like noble creatures, but inwardly they tread in the realm of ghosts. What is hidden and not yet manifest cannot be reflected by the mirror of karma." After the Northern and Southern Song dynasties, this technique became more sophisticated, with more elaborate concealment and smoothing over. Sometimes they remained unblemished for life. Therefore, the celestial beings agreed to move the mirror of karma to the left platform to reflect true villains, and add the mirror of the heart to the right platform to reflect false gentlemen. The circular light reflects each other, and the inner sanctum becomes clear.

There are those that are twisted and crooked, those that are skewed, those as black as ink, those curved like hooks, those messy and disordered like a wall of feces, those turbid and muddy like sediment, those with multiple layers of obstacles and barriers, those with twisted and winding veins, those thorny like a bush, those sharp and dangerous like a sword or knife, those stinging like a scorpion or wasp, those ferocious like a tiger or wolf, those showing the shadow of a hat and carriage, those emitting a metallic or valuable aura, and even those subtly and mysteriously revealing a secret or erotic image. Yet, when one looks at their form, they all appear dignified and upright. Those that are round and lustrous like pearls, and clear and pure like crystal, are but a few in a thousand. I stand by the mirror, recording these observations, and report them every three months to the God of Mount Hua to determine punishment or reward. Generally, the more famous one is, the stricter the scrutiny; the more skillful one is, the heavier the penalty. During the 240 years of the Spring and Autumn period, the evil deeds were varied and numerous. Only when the temple of Bo Yi was shaken did heaven specially send a warning to the Xian family, due to concealment. You should remember this. The scholar bows and receives instruction, returns to ask for a calligraphy inscription by Dao Guang, and names the room "Observing the Mind."

There was a singing child holding a fan with a painted rooster's crest, who asked Li Luyuan to inscribe it at the banquet. Li Luyuan playfully wrote a quatrain saying: Purple and red, surpassing the evening sky, Even in the wind, it sways gracefully. In vain do butterflies flutter countless times, This was originally not a flower. All admired its clever double entendre. After Li Luyuan went to take up his post in Hunan, there was a person who used spirit writing and asked to inscribe the rooster's crest. And immediately wrote this poem. I was startled and said, "Is this not by Li Luyuan?" The spirit writing suddenly stopped. The person who used spirit writing left in a hurry. Yan Jiezǐ sighed and said, "Even immortals steal verses." Someone said, "The person who used spirit writing was actually a fraud and had already been caught stealing verses many times."

My cousin Yuan Ju told me that he once heard Liu Xinting talk about two stories. In one, a farmer was bewitched by a fox spirit. He hired a magician to exorcise the fox, and the fox was captured and was about to be boiled in an oil pot. The farmer knelt and begged for mercy, and the magician released the fox. Afterward, the farmer fell ill and could not be cured by doctors. One day, the fox returned, and the farmer was filled with a mixture of sadness and joy. However, the fox seemed cold and indifferent. It said to the farmer, "You have been thinking of me so much, but it was only because you were attracted by my appearance. You did not know that it was my illusion. If you see my true form, you will be too terrified to even think of escaping." Suddenly, the fox jumped down, revealing its gray fur and long tail, snorting heavily, with eyes glaring like torches. It leaped onto the roof and howled several times before leaving. The farmer then recovered from his illness. This fox can truly be said to repay kindness. In the other story, another farmer was also bewitched by a fox spirit. He hired a magician to exorcise the fox, but the method did not work, and all the charms were torn apart by the fox. The magician was about to strike the fox on the altar when an old woman, who appeared to be the fox's mother, stopped him and said, "Creatures care for their kind, and people protect their associates. This magician's skill may be shallow, but hurting the fox too much might provoke retaliation from other magicians. It would be better for you to go to your husband and sleep, and let the fox escape." The farmer listened to her advice and let the fox go. This fox can truly be said to think far ahead.

In the year 1683, my late father, Yao'an Gong, studied at the brickyard—where bricks made from soil, once used as tribute, had been produced, marking the site of an old brick factory. One day, he broke off a cherry blossom and placed it in water. Later, the flower fell, and two cherries formed like beans, gradually growing larger and larger until they turned red and ripe, indistinguishable from those growing on a tree. That year coincided with the Emperor's birthday, when special examinations were held, and my father passed the provincial examination. Mr. Wang De'an, who lived there at the same time, inscribed the name "Rui Xing Xuan" on the plaque. After my father's death, the estate was inherited by my younger brother, Dongbai. In 1744, I returned from Fujian and inquired about the plaque, only to find that it no longer existed. I intended to ask Liu Shiguan to write a new inscription and rebuild the house, writing an inscription and carving it into a stone niche on the wall to preserve the legacy of our ancestors. Due to procrastination, this was not accomplished, and I do not know when this wish will be fulfilled.

The respected Mr. Yao'an once recounted that at the beginning of the Yongzheng era, there was a tenant farmer named Dong in Li Family Hollow. After his father's death, Dong left behind an old and lame ox, which he intended to sell to a butcher's shop. The ox escaped and went to its father's tomb, where it lay motionless on the ground. Neither pulling nor beating could make it move; it only wagged its tail and bellowed. When villagers heard of this, they came in a continuous stream to see. Suddenly, an elderly neighbor of Dong's named Liu arrived angrily and hit the ox with a stick, saying, "Your father fell into the river, what business is it of yours? If he had drowned and been eaten by fish and turtles, wouldn't that be great? You caused him to be brought out, prolonging his life by over a decade. He was cared for, treated with medicine when ill, buried in a coffin, and left this one tomb, which needs to be sacrificed to every year, becoming an endless burden for the Dong family descendants. You have committed a great sin." He continued, "To die is your fate, why are you mooing?" It turned out that Dong's father had once fallen into a deep river, and the ox had jumped in after him, pulling his tail to save him. Dong initially did not know about this incident. Upon hearing it, he was deeply ashamed and slapped his own cheek, saying, "I am not a human," then quickly led the ox back. A few months later, he died of illness and wept as he buried the ox. This old man had a very humorous style, coincidentally matching the story of Dongfang Shuo saving Emperor Wu of Han's wet nurse.

My father-in-law, Wang Zifú, was from an old family in Wengan. When the family was still prosperous, a pig's head suddenly fell off the hook and hit the ground at the butcher's shop, jumping and running. The townspeople shouted and chased it, but it went straight into his home and stopped. From then on, the family gradually declined and lost its prosperity, until they could only afford thin porridge, and now there are no descendants left. This was told by my father-in-law's sister. Also, my father, Yao An Gong, said that in a relative's family—whose name I have forgotten after many years, but he called them "your uncle" when telling me about this—early in the morning when opening the door, a rabbit walked slowly into the house, not afraid of people, and went all the way to the inner bedroom and lay down on the bed. So they cooked and ate it. Within a few years, most of the family members died, and the house was also torn down. These were all called by the declining energy. Wang Ju Zhuang said that there was a scholar who stayed overnight at Poyang Lake, walking under the moon to cool off, and arrived at an inn where he met several people who introduced themselves as locals. They bought some wine and had a small drink. After chatting and laughing, they started talking about ghosts, sharing strange stories and new insights, many of which were unexpected. One person said, "These are all strange, but none is more strange than what I saw." He said that once in Beijing, to avoid the noise, he lived in a flower gardener's house in Fengtai, where he met a scholar and had a conversation with him. He said that the flowers here are very beautiful, but there are many hateful ghosts in the cemeteries. The scholar replied that ghosts also have refined and vulgar ones, and cannot be dismissed altogether. He said that once he visited the Western Hills and met someone who discussed poetry with many profound insights.

Reciting his own works, there were lines that said: "In the deep mountains, the sun appears late; in the ancient temple, autumn comes early." Another line read: "The bell sounds disperse through the village, and the lights reveal human dwellings." Yet another: "The monkey's cry is cut off by the water, and human voices penetrate the deep mist." Another: "The trees at the edge of the forest brighten the distant waters, and the sun hangs over the corner of the tower." Another: "Moss stains the sickbed, and the rain brings the dusk into the dim lamp." Another: "The owl, after many years, can speak like a human; the wraiths roam the deep mountains during the day." Another: "The empty river reflects the tears of the lotus, and in the ruined garden, the soul of the butterfly seeks spring." All these lines were graceful and appealing. Just as he was about to ask about their lodging, suddenly there was a ringing sound, and they vanished abruptly. Could this ghost still be hateful? I loved their free-spirited nature and wanted to keep them to share a drink. The person stood up, shook their clothes, and said: "To escape your hatred, it is already a great blessing; how dare I enter the luxurious kitchen again?" With a smile, they disappeared. I then realized that the one who spoke of ghosts was themselves a ghost. The scholar then playfully remarked: "Such extraordinary beauty, unheard of in ancient times, yet the story of the Yangyan goose cage, where miracles within miracles occur, is a tale of transformation. How can we know that the one speaking of this ghost is not also a ghost?" Several people changed color at once. A sudden gust of wind arose, the lights dimmed, and everything transformed into a thin mist and light smoke, scattering in all directions.

In the fourth month of the Gengwu year, my late mother, in her critical state of illness, said to her descendants: "I have heard that in the afterlife, family members reunite at the moment of death, and today I have indeed experienced this. I am fortunate to have lived without any shame. While you are still alive, cherish your family ties and kinship, and always keep in mind the places where you will meet again in the afterlife." Yao An said: "Exceptionally intelligent and unique individuals may know everything, but they do not know that people die; those with great strategic and leadership abilities may plan everything, but they cannot plan for the time of death. If they knew that people die, all their actions would necessarily lead to a sense of futility and cessation; if they could plan for the time of death, all their actions would necessarily be halted out of fear. It is a pity that they seek understanding in the vastness of the universe, yet miss the obvious truths right before their eyes."

A Southern scholar, known for his literary talents, traveled among the high officials. Once, he accidentally obtained a Han dynasty jade pendant, which was bright and white with blood stains that penetrated to the bone, and he used it as a paperweight. One day, while staying at the home of a certain official, he was writing by lamplight when he heard a sound from the window crevice. Suddenly, a hand reached in, and he suspected it was a thief. He took an iron如意 to strike, but saw that the hand was slender like spring onions, and hesitated. He peeped through the paper and saw a green-faced罗刹鬼, which frightened him so much that he fell to the ground. When he recovered, the jade pendant was already missing. He suspected it was a fox charm and illusion and no longer pursued the matter. Later, he saw it in the market and asked about its origin. It had passed through several owners, but he could not find its origin. After a long time, it was known that it was taken by a servant of a certain official who pretended to be a ghost. Dong Qiujiang jokingly said: "He knows you are a censor who cares for flowers, so he dared to show these soft hands. If he met people like us who are unrefined, he would certainly not dare to take it and cut off his wrist." I said this slave pretended to be a ghost, one reason is to make him not dare to seize it, and one reason is to make him not pursue it. In the dim light, with one hand breaking the window, fearing being beaten, he pretended to be a woman's hand, making him know it was not a thief; and leading him to see the evil form, making him know it was not a human. His planning is also very meticulous. Because these people serve their masters, even though they are as dull as a mallet. When they commit crimes and violate laws, ingenious plans emerge one after one, like a ghost or a venomous creature, generally speaking, all the same, not just this one person or one event.

Zhu Zhuping, a censor, once had a small gathering at the home of Yan Lici, a high-ranking official. During the drinking session, Zhu Zhuping said with a sigh: "Uprightness is a duty of a gentleman. But if one relies on their uprightness to bully others, it is especially suspect and shows pretentiousness." In the past, a certain official, while serving as a censor, lived in this house. During a gathering, someone mentioned fox spirits, and the official severely scolded them. A few days later, under the moonlight, a thief was seen climbing over the wall. Despite searching inside and outside, there was no trace, causing disturbance all night. By morning, an old man was seen lying in the hall, getting up and saying: "The hot and humid summer—this is the sixth month according to the *Huangdi Neijing*; Wang Taifu's commentary reads it with a rising tone. Du Fu's line 'Long summer village, everything is secluded' reads it with a level tone. It seems the annotators occasionally did not check this." He continued, "I temporarily came here to cool off, causing the host to be uneasy all night, and I feel deeply ashamed." He then smiled and disappeared, indicating that the host had unreasonably intruded on the fox, and the fox played a trick on him. Is this not self-inflicted humiliation?

A Divination Session at Zhu Tianmen's House At Zhu Tianmen's house, a divination session using a *ji* was held, and many curious people came to watch. One arrogant scholar, who was proud of his calligraphy and painting skills, looked down on others and acted as if no one else was around. He even took off his shoes and socks to scratch his feet, and with a sneer, he said to the divination session, "Please write a poem for me." The divination session immediately wrote: "Turning back, the years have passed swiftly, How many times have the vicissitudes of life brought us to this moment? I have seen the Inner Historian of Kuaiji, Who personally led his guests to Shanyin." The crowd asked, "So, have you seen the Right General (Wang Xizhi)?" The divination session replied, "Not only the Right General, but I have also seen Tiger Head (Gu Kaizhi)." The scholar stood up and said, "These two masters were so distinguished that I have personally witnessed their greatness. At this moment, all the great men of the past and present are here. How different are they, really?" The divination session then wrote, "Although these two masters were unparalleled in their artistry, their work was subtle and refined, and their elegance and depth might make some feel less inspired. Guan Fu, who was known for his bold and sometimes disrespectful behavior, was a different kind of person altogether. It is better to separate two good things than to combine two bad ones." The crowd understood what the divination session was referring to and exchanged glances, smiling. They turned to see the scholar, who had already put on his shoes and was about to leave. Who knows what kind of spirit was conducting this divination session, making such cruel and playful remarks? Once, Chen Yunting, a magistrate from Huian, wrote a poem titled "Cold Mountain Old Tree" about this scholar: "A worn and tired old painter in the world, His life filled with resentment, like Xu Xi. Without reason, he paints desolate and cold scenes, Depicting the rugged texture of autumn mountains, his hair already gray. Drunk and rude, he disregards social norms, Who would have known that his bold spirit belongs to a wild and unrestrained person? In the future, if the *Xuanhe Catalogue* is continued, The painter today is like Guan Fu." The divination session's reference to Guan Fu must have been pointing to this scholar. But how did this spirit know about this poem?

A Tale of Uncle Zhang Gong's Dream When I was young, I heard from Uncle Zhang Gong about a story involving a student of the Imperial Academy in Cangzhou. This scholar lived by the riverbank, and one night, a clerk arrived with a name card, knocking on the door to announce that the new governor was passing through. The clerk said that the governor had heard of the scholar's family as a prominent household in the area and invited him to meet on the boat. Just as the host was attending a funeral at a relative's house, which was about ten miles away, the gatekeeper brought the card and hurriedly informed the scholar. The scholar quickly ordered his carriage to return, but by the time he arrived, the boat had already left. He then prepared the carriage, horses, and gifts, and chased along the riverbank day and night, traveling over two hundred miles. By the time he reached the border of Dezhou, Shandong, he asked people along the way, but there was no such official, nor was there such a boat. He returned in a disheveled state. For several days, he felt lost and dreamlike, as if in a trance. Some suspected that his family had much wealth, and bandits wanted to lure and capture him, but he fortunately avoided it by being elsewhere. Others suspected that he viewed poor relatives and friends as enemies, and that he was not hesitant to spend much money to cultivate powerful connections. There were fox spirits in the nearby village, and they especially hated him and played tricks on him. All these suspicions lacked evidence. However, the local community spread the news widely, and everyone said that a certain college student had encountered a ghost. My maternal grandfather, Xuefeng Gong, said, "It was neither fox, ghost, nor bandit; it was done by poor relatives and friends." This statement is close to the truth.

A popular belief holds that the spot where a magpie and a snake fight is a lucky piece of land, and marking the grave site there will bring great wealth and honor, known as a dragon and phoenix site. When I was around eleven or twelve years old, such an event occurred in the fields of a family named Kong in Huai Town. My maternal uncle, An Gong Shi Zhai, witnessed it himself.
