Essence and blood are transformed and generated, containing latent heat, which can still cause a toxic outbreak of measles, damaging one or two out of ten. How much more so when heat-inducing medicines are used to form a fetus, the accumulated toxins will be several times greater, hence every time there is a measles outbreak, not one in a hundred survives."

People lament the short life of someone who dies young, but they do not know that the day of their birth already harbors the inevitability of death. If life is like non-life, what value is there in the seed? This principle is very clear, but past sages did not understand it. The hermit has a heart for saving others and cannot use this knowledge to deceive people. These theories and principles are all things that medical practitioners are unwilling to say, or is it truly that a divine spirit is behind it? I have also heard that Mr. Liu Jizhen once discussed medicine with a spirit medium, who said: "You like to use ginseng to treat deficiency, but there are many different types of deficiency, and the nature of ginseng is specialized and cannot treat all conditions. In terms of the organs, ginseng only reaches the upper and middle jiao, but not the lower jiao; in terms of the nutritive and defensive qi, it only reaches the qi aspect and not the blood aspect. For kidney and liver deficiency and yin deficiency, using ginseng to supplement, could it be of any help? Not only would it be ineffective, but would it not even exacerbate the condition by causing excessive yang to burn? And in ancient prescriptions, there was a distinction between raw and cooked ginseng, but today, ginseng is harvested and immediately steamed, so where can one find raw ginseng? In ancient times, ginseng came from Shangdang, possessing the central earth energy, so its nature is warm and thick, first entering the middle palace. Now Shangdang's energy has been exhausted, and only Liaodong ginseng is used, possessing the eastern spring energy, so its nature is to promote growth and first rises to the upper part. Even in terms of medicine, each has its own application, I hope you will consider this carefully." Mr. Liu Jizhen strongly disagreed, and I, not being a doctor, have included this account for those who specialize in this field to decide.

A native of She named Jiang Ziyuan, who resided temporarily in Chengjiabang, Xian County, made a living as a doctor and had a remedy for arsenic poisoning. When used, it cured immediately, but he insisted on demanding a heavy sum of money. If the demand was not met, he would watch the patient die. One day, he died suddenly and appeared in a dream to his host, saying, "I have caused nine deaths due to my greed for profit." The dead complained to the underworld authorities, who sentenced him to die from arsenic poisoning in nine lifetimes. He was about to be reincarnated and bribed the ghost soldiers to come and see you, presenting this formula to you. If you use it to save one person, he will reduce his karmic retribution by one lifetime. After speaking, he cried and left. You said, "I regret it too late." The formula consists of one ounce of *fangfeng* (防风), ground into powder and taken with water. There is no other secret ingredient. Additionally, Shen Zhan Fenggong said, "Mixing *shiqing* (石青) with cold water can instantly cure arsenic poisoning." Shen Zhan Fenggong never spoke falsely, so his formula should also be effective.

The Elder Scholar Liu Tingsheng recounted that in the eastern city there was a hunter who, in the middle of the night, awoke from sleep and heard the paper window creaking softly. Soon after, he heard a rustling sound beneath the window. Putting on his clothes, he shouted in surprise and asked who was there. A voice suddenly replied: "I am a ghost. I have something to ask of you. Please do not be afraid." When asked what the matter was, the ghost said: "Foxes and ghosts have never lived together since ancient times. The tombs where foxes dwell are not the tombs of ghosts. My tomb is about three li north of the village. While I was away, the foxes gathered their clan and took up residence there, driving me out and preventing me from entering. If I were to fight them, I am but a scholar, and I would certainly lose. If I were to sue them before the earth gods, even if I were to prevail, they would eventually retaliate, and I would still lose. Only if you, when you go hunting, take a detour of half a mile and pass by that place several times, will they be terrified and move elsewhere. However, if you should encounter them, do not immediately kill them, for fear that the matter may be discovered, and they will again harbor resentment toward me." The hunter followed the ghost’s advice, and later dreamed that the ghost came to thank him. Indeed, the situation is like the magpie’s nest occupied by the jay — the matter is inherently just. Yet, when one’s strength is insufficient to overcome the usurper, one should avoid conflict. When one’s strength is enough to overcome the usurper, one should think carefully and not exhaust one’s strength, nor seek an easy victory nor an excessive one. This is why he ultimately prevailed. A weak person facing oppression, like this ghost, is admirable.

Uncle Zhang Gong Jian Ting said that Magistrate Wang of Cangzhou had a beloved daughter who was suffering from a serious illness. One night, the family entered the study and suddenly saw her standing alone under the moonlight in the shade of flowers. Startled, they returned, suspecting it to be a fox spirit taking a human form. They commanded the dog to attack it, and it disappeared suddenly. Soon, the sick girl in the room said, "Just now in my dream, I went to the study to look at the moon, feeling very pleasant. I didn't expect the dog to come, and I nearly couldn't escape. I still feel frightened and sweating." They realized what they saw was her living spirit. The doctor, upon hearing this, said, "Her physical and spiritual states are separated, and even the greatest doctors cannot help." She soon died. In Fujian, there is a type of bamboo that is square-shaped, and in Yan Mountain, there is a type of persimmon that has a slightly square shape. These are different types. In Yidu, Shandong, there is a square-shaped cypress tree, but it is a rare occurrence, as other cypress trees are not square-shaped. When I was eight or nine years old, I saw four pots of chrysanthemums in my maternal grandfather's hall, all with perfectly square petals,整齐如裁剪 (neatly trimmed). It was said that they obtained the chrysanthemums from the family in Tianjin named Zha, called "Golden Seal," and my grandfather in Yao'an requested the roots to be brought back. The following year, the petals gradually became rounder, and by the second year, they were fully round. Someone said that the square petals were a special variety, and that there are methods to change the color of flowers, such as using indigo to make lotus seeds blue or ink to make the roots of lilies black. This is just one possible explanation. My servant Song Yu, when his illness was critical, suddenly opened his eyes and said, "Have your brothers come? What is the limit?" He then muttered to himself, "Eighteenth day is also fine." At the time, a scholar who taught in our household heard this and laughed, saying, "It's delirious talk." On the eighteenth day, he indeed died. The scholar laughed again, saying, "It was just a coincidence." Shen Tiechan, who was eating with him, put down his chopsticks and sighed, saying, "You can truly be called a devoted follower of Cheng and Zhu."

How many extraordinary virtues and heroic deeds have been lost to obscurity, unrecorded? Gao’an Gong heard this from Yuntai Gong: During the late Ming period, while fleeing from chaos, he saw a couple escaping together. The husband seemed to be carrying money, and a bandit, brandishing a knife, pursued them urgently. The wife suddenly turned around and stood firm, waiting for the bandit to arrive, then suddenly embraced his waist. The bandit struck her with his blade, and blood poured from her like a stream. Yet she held on tightly, not releasing her grip until she expired and fell. By then, her husband had long since fled. Alas, his name and surname are unknown. He also heard this from Zhenfan Gong: During the late Ming period, five provinces in northern China suffered from a great famine, so severe that human flesh was sold like meat, and officials could not stop it. A traveler staying at an inn between Dezhou and Jingzhou saw a young woman lying naked on a chopping block, her hands and feet bound, while someone was drawing water to wash her. The terrified and trembling state of the woman was unbearable to witness. Moved with pity, the traveler paid double the price to redeem her, released her bonds, and helped her dress. When he touched her breast, the young woman angrily said: "I am grateful to you for giving me a second life. I would have no regrets even if I were to serve you as a servant for the rest of my life. But to be a concubine is absolutely unacceptable. I only refused to serve two husbands, hence I was sold here. Why have you so quickly treated me so lightly?" She then took off her clothes and threw them to the ground, remaining naked on the chopping block, closing her eyes and accepting her fate. The butcher, filled with hatred, cut off a piece of her thigh flesh while she cried out in agony, but she never showed any regret. Alas, her name and surname are also unknown.

The mother-in-law of the Prince of Suxing, who is also the aunt of the Prince of Yao'an, recounted that in her hometown, there was a widow who, along with her mother-in-law, was raising an orphaned son who was about seven or eight years old. The widow was known for her beauty, and many matchmakers came to propose marriage for her, but she refused. Just when the child fell gravely ill with smallpox, a certain doctor was invited to diagnose and treat him. The doctor privately told a neighbor that he could cure the illness, but only if the widow would agree to his advances. Both the widow and her mother-in-law were furious and scolded him. However, as the child's condition worsened, they, overwhelmed by their love for him, wept and reluctantly agreed to the doctor's demands. Unfortunately, the treatment was too late, and the child died. Overwhelmed with regret, the widow hanged herself. People believed that her suicide was due to her grief over the child's death and did not suspect any other reason. Her mother-in-law also kept the matter secret and did not speak about it openly. Soon after, the doctor died, followed by the child, and then the house caught fire, leaving nothing behind. The widow eventually became a courtesan and occasionally mentioned the events to her lovers.

The Commoner Xiangke recounts that a scholar stayed in Mount Kuaiji one night, and across a ravine, he heard voices of recitation. He listened intently, and it seemed as though they were discussing ancient interpretations. The next day, he crossed the ravine to search for the source of the voices but found no trace of them. He wandered for several days, hoping to encounter something. Suddenly, he heard a voice from the top of a tree, saying, "You love antiquity so much, let us meet here." As he turned around, a stone chamber suddenly opened. Inside, there were dozens of people sitting in rows, all closing their books and shaking off their clothes, ready to greet him. The scholar looked at the tables and saw that they were filled with commentaries on classical texts. The person sitting at the head of the table bowed and said, "In ancient times, the profound teachings of Confucius were passed down by scholars of the classics. Although the old texts still exist, and the cultural heritage has not been lost, new interpretations are constantly emerging, and people who love antiquity are rare. The Sage Confucius fears that the tradition might gradually disappear over time. Therefore, he gathers the spirits of the deceased and summons them. All the spirits of scholars from all generations who still have influence have gathered here to study ancient texts. They will be reborn in the human world to continue the tradition, in order to transmit ancient learning and preserve the legacy of Confucian teaching. Please record what you have seen and heard, and tell your fellow scholars, so that they may know that the true foundation of Confucius and Mencius lies here, not elsewhere." The scholar wanted to ask something, but suddenly he woke up from a dream, sitting beneath an old pine tree. Upon hearing this, Xiangke packed provisions and went to the mountain, climbing through dense vegetation for more than a month, but returned without seeing anything. This story is similar in essence to the tale of Jingxiangge told by Zhu Ziyin. Some say that Xiangke enjoys discussing ancient meanings and has written a work called *Gu Jing Jie Hou* (Uncovering Hidden Knowledge from Ancient Classics), so the scholar might have played a trick on him to amuse him. Others say that Xiangke fabricated this story to claim that he was one of those born into a certain role or identity.

Yao An Gong, while serving in the Ministry of Punishment, had a colleague named Wang Gong Shou Kun who said, "I had a dream last night where I saw a person standing covered in blood, but I could not recognize him. Why did he come here?" Chen Gong Zuomei then said, "This person is always worried about accidentally killing someone, feeling uneasy and guilty, so it is just an image created by his mind. There is no such ghost, so how could you recognize him? Moreover, seven or eight people are involved in reviewing a case document. Why did the dream only appear to you? Do not doubt yourself." Buddha Gong Lun then said, "That is not correct. Colleagues are part of the same whole. If someone sees something in their dream, it is as if they see it in everyone's dream. We are responsible for handling cases across the land, but we cannot consider all prisoners. We rely on written confessions to decide life and death. How could we recognize the person? You should be cautious, and we all should be cautious." Yao An Gong then said, "I agree with Buddha Gong Lun's view." Lü Taichang Huanhui said, "In the capital, there was a wealthy family that married off their daughter. Both the bride and groom were exceptionally beautiful, and their relatives all regarded them as if they were gods and goddesses. Observing their demeanor, the couple seemed very much in love. The next day, at dawn, the door remained closed, and calls went unanswered. Peering through a window, it was found that they had hanged themselves side by side. Upon inspection, their bedding showed signs of having been used. The maidservants and servants all said, 'She had already taken off her wedding clothes last night. Why did she put on her formal attire again before dying?' How strange! Even Gao Yao, the legendary judge, would not be able to understand this case."

Song, the village official, known as "East Village Tai Sui," was attracted to a neighboring child's beauty and used every trick to seduce and have intimate relations with him. The child's father discovered this and forced the child to commit suicide. The incident was kept secret and no one knew about it. One night, Song dreamed that he was taken to the underworld, where he was accused by the child's ghost. Song defended himself, saying, "I loved you out of compassion, not with any harmful intent. Your death was caused by your father, and it was unexpected." The child's ghost retorted, "If you hadn't seduced me, how could I have been defiled? If I hadn't been defiled, how could I have died? The root of this disaster is you, who else could it be?" Song continued to defend himself, saying, "Although I lured you, it was your choice to follow me. Who could resist a glance and a smile? I did not force you, so it is unreasonable to blame me." The underworld official angrily rebuked him, saying, "A child, being innocent, led you into a trap. You blame the fish for being baited, is that fair?" With a loud clap of his hand, Song was startled awake. Later, the official was caught for corruption, and Song's name was implicated in the scandal. Facing potential disaster, Song realized it was retribution for his actions and recounted his dream to his close friends. When the case was finally decided, he was only sentenced to hard labor. Some might say that dreams are unreliable, but three years later, when he was released, the child's father, still harboring resentment, took advantage of the child's mother being alone and enticed her with heavy bribes. The mother, seeing the opportunity for wealth, betrayed her son. Fearing public gossip, Song was shamed and committed suicide. Thus, his earlier escape from severe punishment was not a coincidence but a sign of impending retribution, showing that one's actions and their consequences are inseparable, like shadows following their forms.

An Old Servant Named Zou Ming Said: Once, while serving at the Danyang County Office, I was out at midnight to relieve myself. Passing by an empty room, I heard sounds of lewdness between men and women. Assuming it was a secret tryst by the inner court servants, I feared it might bring trouble, so I secretly returned. Later, on a moonlit night, I heard the same sounds again. Peering through a crack in the window, I found no one from the inner court present. The weather was bitterly cold, and the person was naked, not wearing even a single thread of clothing. I suspected it to be a fox spirit. I coughed lightly outside the window, and the figure suddenly disappeared. Once, I mentioned this to a companion, and a fireman said: "This was once the residence of a former official's retainer. The retainer had a box of ivory carvings depicting secret sexual scenes, with a mechanism inside that allowed it to move. It was always kept in a pillow case and occasionally taken out for play. One day, it went missing, and it was suspected to have been hidden by a colleague, but no trace was found afterward. Could this object be causing mischief?" We searched the room thoroughly but could not find it. Since it did not harm anyone, we did not pursue it further. It probably often lingered in the bedding area, absorbing human essence, and over time, transformed into something else.

At my maternal grandfather's house, Snow Peak Zhang Gong, the peonies were in full bloom. One night, the household servant Li Gui saw two women leaning on the railing. One said, "The moonlight is exceptionally beautiful." The other replied, "There are very few of these flowers here; only the Tong family garden and a few here." Li Gui realized they were fox spirits and threw a tile to strike them, but they suddenly disappeared. Soon after, bricks and stones flew around, and all the window frames were damaged. Snow Peak Gong went to see it himself, clasping his hands and saying, "The elegant affair of appreciating flowers and the refined person who enjoys walking under the moon—why should one compete with a lowly person, ruining the scenery?" After speaking, it became silent. Gong sighed, "This fox is not vulgar." Tenant Zhang Jiubao said, once during summer, after finishing hoeing the fields, the sky was about to darken. He sat with others on the embankment and saw a streak of fire like a red snake flying from the southwest, suddenly falling to the ground. It turned out to be a fox, pale white, wounded and bleeding, lying down and panting. He quickly raised his hoe to strike it, but the fox managed to leap up again, transforming into a streak of fire and flying northeast. Later, when he was pulling a cart selling goods to Zaoqiang, he heard that a woman from a certain family had been bewitched by a fox. A Taoist was invited to exorcise the spirit, and the fox was captured and sealed in a jar. Some children secretly tore off the talisman, wanting to see what the fox looked like, and it broke free and flew away. When asked about the date, it was the same day the fox had fallen. The Taoist's spells could be said to have worked, but they were no match for the curiosity of children. Throughout history, many efforts have been nearly successful, only to be undone by the hands of the ignorant, just like this.

An Old Servant Liu Qi once spoke of his sister's husband, who one night lay alone in a room, with his bed by the northern window. In the middle of the night, he felt a hand touching him, suspecting it was a thief. Startled, he looked closely and saw an arm reach from the southern window, about a zhang long. Being naturally brave, he immediately grabbed it. Suddenly, another arm broke through the window and slapped his cheek, causing unbearable pain. As he turned to resist, the arm he had grabbed was already pulled away. He heard a loud voice from outside the window say, "Do you now fear?" He then remembered that last evening he was cooling off under the trees and had claimed to his peers that he did not fear ghosts. Why should ghosts want people to fear them? If they can make people fear, what glory is there for ghosts? For the sake of one word, seeking to provoke and win, this ghost can truly be called troublesome. Qiu Wenda once said, "Making people fear me is not as good as making them respect me." Respect comes from the heart and cannot be forced. It is a pity that this ghost did not hear these words.

The Taoist priest named瑶华, a member of the imperial family, recounted that a Mongol prince, an imperial son-in-law, once shot a fox. Later, the fox appeared with red shoes on its feet and hips that were no different from those of a woman. Another story was told by Yunjiao, a former official named Shen, about Li Jingshi, a former official who had a relationship with a fox spirit. Li Jingshi's grandfather suspected that the fox spirit was a neighboring girl and spread ashes on the path leading to the courtyard, hoping to catch it. The footprints left behind were those of an animal, but at the door of the study, they resembled delicate human feet. The fox spirit that the Mongol prince shot showed no other unusual traits; however, the fox spirit that Li Jingshi befriended lived with him for several years before departing. Li Jingshi asked when they would meet again, and the fox spirit replied that it would return when Li Jingshi reached the rank of a third-grade official. Many people knew this prediction, and it eventually came true. The narrator's uncle's brother, Zhang Xuetang, recounted that when he was seventeen or eighteen years old, he gathered with several friends on a moonlit night. At that time, crabs were just beginning to be fat, and new wine was ready. During a convivial gathering, a man suddenly stood before the table, wearing a straw hat and a blue robe, and removed his shoes. He bowed and said, "Although I am humble and unrefined, I greatly enjoy drinking wine and eating crabs. May I join you at the end of the table?" The guests were startled and unsure, so they hesitantly invited him to sit. When they asked for his name, he laughed and did not respond. Instead, he drank heartily and ate voraciously, saying nothing. After he had eaten and drunk his fill, he suddenly stood up and said, "Our meeting today is also fated. The future is uncertain, and I do not know when we will meet again to repay your kindness." With those words, he leaped up, and the tiles of the roof made no sound as he vanished. Looking at the chair where he had been sitting, they found a gold coin shining brightly, roughly equivalent to the cost of the gathering.

Some say he was a fairy, others say he was a magician, and still others say he was a notorious bandit. I find the theory that he was a notorious bandit to be the most plausible. When I was young, I saw people like Li Jinliang, whose skills could reach such a level. I also heard about Dou Er Dong, a notorious bandit from Xian County. His brother was called Da Dong, but his name is unknown, and he is known by his childhood name. Sometimes, his name is written as Dou Erdun. He was known for entering homes at night, waiting until women were asleep, threatening them with force and forbidding them to speak, rolling up their bedding together, and carrying them over several houses. When the dawn bell was about to ring, he would still roll them up and return them. The victims felt as if they were in a dream. One night, a woman from her family hid in the room, waiting for him to return them. He suddenly attacked, swinging a knife to fight with one hand and throwing the woman onto the bed with the other, as swift as a whirlwind and lightning, leaving no trace. Perhaps he was a descendant of the swordsmen of the Tang Dynasty?

Books on the art of *Qimen Dunjia* are widely available. However, most of them do not contain genuine teachings; true teachings are merely a few key phrases passed down orally, not written down. Mr. Qingyuan from Dezhou said that he once visited a friend—Qingyuan once mentioned his name, but he has forgotten it after so many years. Qingyuan said that the weather was muddy after rain, so he borrowed a mule from someone to go there, and the friend's residence was not far away. The friend invited him to stay overnight and said, "The moon is bright tonight, would it be good to watch a play?" He then took out more than ten benches, arranged them in the courtyard, and enjoyed a candlelit dinner with Qingyuan in the hall. After the second drumbeat, they saw a person jump over the wall and enter, circling in front of the steps. Whenever they encountered a bench, the person would stumble, and after much effort, they finally managed to jump over. Initially, they moved in a straight line, jumping back and forth many times, then turned around and moved backward; they jumped back and forth again many times, becoming exhausted and falling down. By dawn, the friend led them to the hall, asked where they had come from, and the person knelt and said, "I am indeed a thief. After entering the house, I only saw many short walls, and the more I tried to jump over them, the more I couldn't. I became trapped and had to leave, but the more I tried, the more I couldn't. I became exhausted and was caught. Life and death are in your hands." The friend laughed and let him go. He told Qingyuan, "I predicted that a thief would come yesterday, so I played a trick with a small skill." When asked what the trick was, he replied, "It is the *Qimen Dunjia* method. Others who receive it might bring misfortune, but you are truly upright and cautious. If you wish to learn, I will teach you." Qingyuan declined and thanked him, and the friend sighed, "Those who wish to learn cannot receive the teaching, and those who can receive it do not wish to learn. This art will eventually become extinct." Feeling a sense of loss, he sadly sent the thief back.

There was a man from an old, distinguished family. A fortune teller predicted that he would achieve great wealth and status, and a physiognomist also said the same. However, by the time he was on the verge of old age, his official rank only reached the sixth grade. One day, he consulted a spirit through divination, asking why his official career had been so difficult. The deity responded: "The fortune teller and the physiognomist were both correct, but because of the partiality of your mother-in-law, your official rank and salary were reduced to this extent." He respectfully asked, "Partiality is indeed unavoidable, but why would it go so far as to reduce my official rank?" The deity replied: "The rites say that a stepmother is like a mother, so one should treat children from a previous marriage as one's own. An illegitimate son wears mourning clothes for three years for a legal mother, so one should also treat illegitimate children as one's own. However, human nature is treacherous and evil, and people often set up their own boundaries. Legitimate children and illegitimate children are clearly like oil and water that do not mix. When selfishness arises, various schemes and tricks are employed. From daily life to wealth, land, and houses, there is not a single thing where legitimate children are favored and illegitimate children are neglected. This has already offended the Creator."

Even or possibly to sow discord and slander, to secretly plot schemes, to scold, slander, and bully, disregarding reason and law, causing those who suffer to swallow their words, and those who observe to gnash their teeth, yet continuously claiming to have suffered from those born to them. How can it be seen that the fairness of heavenly justice is not present? Those who rely on their age, devour their younger brothers; those who rely on their mother's favor, oppress their older brothers. Without their mother's support, how can they not be treated like fish and meat? [PROPER_NOUN] said: This is despite jealous talk, yet it cannot be said that there is no such logic. The world's affairs are ever-changing, those who manage the household should handle matters calmly.

My clan-ancestor, Huang Tugong, said: During the reigns of Shunzhi and Kangxi, the world had just been pacified, and people's hearts were not yet united. A certain person A secretly served as a spy for Wu Sangui and brought B, who was strong and cunning, into the conspiracy. Afterward, the traitors were punished, and A repented and abandoned his rebellious thoughts. The secret letters back and forth were mostly found at B's place. There was no mention of B's name in the letters, but B threatened to expose A's secrets, and the crime would result in the destruction of the entire clan. Unwillingly, A gave his daughter to B as a wife and she lived in B's household. B became more arrogant after achieving his goal, losing all sense of humanity. He forced himself upon almost all of A's women, even A's mother was not spared, and even A's young brother, aged thirteen or fourteen, was not spared. All of them cried and were humiliated, trembling in fear of displeasing B. A was depressed and unable to cope, often avoiding home. One day, while walking in the fields, A met an old man and conversed with him. A was surprised that there was no such person in the nearby villages. The old man said, "I am not deceiving you; I am a heavenly fox. You certainly have committed a crime, but B has also been too harsh on you, which I find unfair. Now, I will steal your secret letters and return them to you. He will have nothing to hold over you, and he will leave on his own."

He then took out more than ten sheets and gave them to Jia, who verified that they were indeed genuine, and immediately tore them up and swallowed them. Returning home, he told Yi the truth. Fearing that Jia's daughter might steal them, Yi secretly buried them in an iron pot elsewhere and secretly went to check. Indeed, they were no longer there, so he staggered away with the girl. The girl daily quarreled with him, and soon after, they divorced. Later, the matter gradually came to light, and both families were shunned by their fellow townspeople, each carrying their family far away. Indeed, the chaos at the end of the Ming dynasty was extreme, and the holy dynasty purged the great furnace, rescuing the people from the fires. Jia had lived on the land for more than thirty years. When Wu Sangui rebelled, he had already personally killed the Prince of Gui, and he could not be called the three households of Chu. Therefore, Jia secretly communicated with Wu Sangui, and he could not be called the stubborn people of Yin. Even if the whole family were executed, it would not be unjust. Yi then defiled his family, and compared to harming the virtuous, his crime seems not to have diminished. However, Yi was originally a co-conspirator, and their crimes were originally equal. Furthermore, he wielded weapons to coerce and indulged in his wickedness, and his crime was indeed one degree higher than Jia's. Although he later suffered retribution, there is no proof, but the heavenly way is clear; surely there is no chance of escape.

Yao An Gong was studying at the home of his uncle, Chen Gong De Yin, when one morning he heard people talking loudly. They said that a tenant farmer named Zhang Min, who had been guarding a melon field outside the village the previous night, had already lost his soul and remained speechless by the next morning. Despite trying every possible method to revive him, he only regained consciousness in the evening. He recounted that after midnight, he saw a distant light in the woods, which gradually moved closer. By the time it reached the melon field, it turned out to be a giant, over ten zhang tall, holding a bamboo cage as large as a room, standing in front of the melon field. The giant looked down for a long time, and Zhang Min was terrified and fainted, not knowing when it left. Some said it was a shadow spirit, while others believed it was the night guardian god. According to the *Records of Strange Things*, the incantation of the night guardian god is "Basha Binyin Di," reciting which can ward off bad dreams and stop fear. It should not appear in such an unusual form to scare people. I suspect the shadow spirit is more likely.

Yao'an Gong also recounted that one evening, along with several friends, he stayed overnight in his uncle's study. After the candles were extinguished and they went to bed, suddenly a loud sound like a giant cannon was heard in front of the bed. The roof tiles shook, everyone in the room trembled, and they were speechless. Some people were deaf for several days. At that time, it was the tenth month of winter, and there should not have been thunder. There was no flame or impact, and it was not like thunder. His contemporary, Gao Zhen'er, said: "This is a drum demon, not a good omen. The host should cultivate virtue to ward it off." Deyin Gong also lived in constant fear every day, being cautious about everything. That year, there was a hanged death in the family, with no other incidents, perhaps due to the power of vigilance. Yao'an Gong heard from his great-grandfather Runsheng Gong that in Jingcheng, there was a man named Jiang Sanmang, who was brave and simple-minded. One day, he heard someone talk about how Song Dingbo sold ghosts for money. He was very pleased and said: "Now I know that ghosts can be bound. If I bind a ghost each night and spit on it to turn it into a sheep, in the morning, I would take it to the slaughterhouse market to sell. It would be enough to provide for one day's worth of alcohol and meat expenses." So he carried a club and rope every night, secretly wandering among the graves, like a hunter waiting for foxes and rabbits, but never met any ghosts. Even in places known for ghosts, he pretended to be drunk and lay down to lure them, but saw nothing. One night, he saw several phosphorescent lights jumping towards him through the forest. Before reaching the door, they scattered, and he returned in disappointment. For over a month, he did not succeed and stopped. Ghosts often take advantage of people's fear. Jiang Sanmang firmly believed that ghosts could be bound and looked down upon them in his heart. His boldness and determination were enough to scare away ghosts, so they avoided him.

Yi Du's Zhu Tianmen reported that there was a scholar who temporarily resided at Yunju Temple in the capital city. He often saw a young boy around fourteen or fifteen years old frequently coming and going at the temple. The scholar, being a licentious person, enticed the boy into intimacy and kept him overnight. At dawn, a guest pushed open the door and entered. The scholar felt embarrassed and ashamed, but the guest acted as if he had not seen anything. Soon after, a monk brought in tea, and he too acted as if he had not seen anything. The scholar suspected something unusual was happening. After the guest left, he embraced the boy and firmly asked him about it. The boy said, "Please do not be afraid, I am indeed a spirit of the apricot flower." The scholar was startled and asked, "Are you trying to bewitch me?" The boy replied, "Spirits and demons are different. Mountain demons and malevolent ghosts cling to grass and trees to cause mischief, which is what we call demons. An old tree that has lived for a thousand years, with its essence and vitality accumulating internally over time, eventually forms a shape, much like the Taoist concept of forming a sacred embryo, which is what we call a spirit. Demons harm people, but spirits do not harm people." The scholar then asked, "Flower spirits are mostly female, why are you male?" The boy answered, "Apricot flowers have male and female varieties, and I am a male apricot." The scholar asked again, "Why do you remain hidden and inactive?" The boy replied, "It is due to past karma." The scholar then asked, "How can humans and plants have a connection?" The boy hesitated for a long time and said, "It is because they cannot refine their form without borrowing human essence and energy." The scholar said, "Then you are trying to bewitch me." He pushed away his pillow and quickly got up. The boy also left angrily. The scholar stopped himself in time, which can be called great wisdom. That person was Zhu Tianmen's disciple, but Zhu Tianmen refused to mention his name.

Shen Tiechan, whose given name was Zhao Ding, was from Yangqu. He passed the provincial examination in the year Gengchen and served as a magistrate. He served in my household for the longest time. In the autumn of Gengshu, he was on probation in Shaanxi. Suddenly, he sent a letter to bid farewell to me, the words of which were vague and elusive, filled with melancholy and suppressed emotion, and I did not understand what they meant. Yet Shen Tiechan was certainly not an unsuccessful person, so I could not fathom the reason. Soon after, news of his death arrived. Later, I met Shao Er Yun, the Zan Shàn, and then learned that Shen Tiechan had suffered from illness for several months in Xi'an. After recovering, he went into the mountains to hunt. Upon returning, he saw two round objects like balls in front of him, spinning like windmills, even with his eyes closed. A few days later, they suddenly burst open, and two little maids came out from the middle, claiming that they were immortals who had invited him. His soul unconsciously followed them. Upon arriving, they were in jade towers and pearl palaces, where a woman of unparalleled beauty spoke and offered herself as a match. Shen Tiechan firmly declined, excusing himself by saying he was unaccustomed to living in such a place. The woman, in slight anger, shouted him out, and he suddenly awoke.

After a month or so, the subject saw two round objects emerging from his eyes as before. The two small maids also invited him to go as before, and they had already constructed a new residence, secluded and winding, which was quite charming. The subject asked where they were, and was told it was "Foshang." He was then asked to inscribe the name of the hall, and he wrote "Foshang Fragrant Realm" in the Eight-Style script. The woman repeated her earlier request, and the subject was unable to resist, leading to a romantic commitment. From then on, he frequently dreamed of this place. Over time, the woman also appeared during the day. The subject, named Tietan, was forbidden from interacting with his loved ones, leading to a worsening illness. At the time, a Taoist practitioner named Li administered red pills, which caused the subject to vomit and die. It was then realized that the earlier letter was a manifestation of a heart ailment. Tietan was exceptionally intelligent, skilled in poetry, and proficient in the Eight-Style script, and he was known for his wide network and influence. However, he was known for his libertine lifestyle and charismatic demeanor. In middle age, he developed a fascination with immortality, leading to a spiritual or psychological crisis. He ultimately died in a state of confusion. The passage suggests that supernatural phenomena arise from human desires and that illusions are created by the mind. Tietan's high aspirations and love for the unusual ultimately led to his death, indicating a sense of tragedy and regret.

The Old Residence at Cui Zhuang To the west of the main hall, there are three rooms facing north and three facing south, surrounded by flowers and bamboo, densely shaded and rather secluded. When my ancestors were still alive, the servant Zhang Yunhui went at night to fetch tea and saw a young woman with hanging hair secretly hiding under a tree, facing the wall and standing with her back to the corner. He thought she was a maid in the household, meeting secretly in this secluded spot. He suddenly seized her arm, intending to take advantage of her. The woman suddenly turned her face, pale as if powdered, yet with no eyes, ears, mouth, or nose. She let out a piercing scream and fell to the ground. When the others came with candles, she was nowhere to be seen. Some said there had long been a ghost in the place; others said Zhang Yunhui had suffered a momentary vision problem; still others claimed it was actually a clever maid who had been suddenly stopped and could not escape, having covered her face with a white cloth and pretended to be a ghost in order to flee. None knew the true nature of the matter. From then on, doubts remained unresolved, and those who stayed in this courtyard always felt a sense of dread. At night, strange sounds were often heard. People avoided living there, and so ghosts and fox spirits entered. Another building, a small house to the right of the eastern wing, was also said to be haunted. Though it did not cause harm, female servants sometimes saw it. One day, Lord Yao'an examined some discarded books and caught two badgers under a basket. The people said, "This must be the spirit!" Lord Yao'an replied, "Badgers, when their heads are bent by a child, are certainly not spirits." Yet the room had no human footprints, and the fact that wild animals made it their den suggests that there was indeed a spirit. This also fits the meaning of the saying "where there is a hole, the wind comes"—that is, these stories have some basis in reality. Later, the western hall was divided and given to my cousin Yuan to live in, and now belongs to my cousin Ruo Tong. The building was divided and given to my elder brother Jinghu, and now belongs to my nephew Ruo Fen. As my nephews and cousins grew in number, the house had no empty space, and the spirits all left on their own without being driven away.

Jia and Yi were friendly with each other, and Jia invited Yi to manage his household affairs. When Jia became a military governor, he also appointed Yi to assist in official governance, and he followed Yi's advice without question. Over time, all his wealth was embezzled by Yi, and Jia began to scold Yi slightly. Yi used Jia's private secrets against him and suddenly turned on Jia. Jia was overwhelmed with anger and then filed a complaint with the city god. That night, Jia dreamed that the city god spoke to him: "Yi is so wicked and treacherous. How could you trust him without doubt? Jia replied, "Because everything he did was exactly as I wanted." The god sighed and said, "If someone can do everything you want, it is very frightening. You don't fear him, but instead you are pleased with him. If not you, who else would be deceived? His evil deeds are about to reach their limit, and he will eventually face consequences. As for you, you are bringing disaster upon yourself. There is no need to file a complaint." This is what Jia personally told Yao'an Gong. The incident occurred during the late reign of Emperor Yongzheng. Jia was from Yunnan, and Yi was from Vietnam.

The "Du Yang Miscellaneous Records" recounts the story of Li Fuguo's fragrant jade and its supposed protective properties, which are indeed quite extraordinary and often doubted to be mere fiction or absurd tales. Yet, there actually exists something called "fragrant jade." My grandmother once had a green jade pendant shaped like a fan, said to be a relic of Cao Huachun, stolen from the imperial treasury of the Ming dynasty. It was made in a simple and rough manner, with two dragons intertwined, and had several spots of blood, the color of which was like melted wax. When rubbed warm and smelled, it gave off a fragrance of sandalwood; if not rubbed warm, it did not smell. I suspect the jade of Li Fuguo was not much different. The recorder embellished the words. My grandmother often secretly requested it, and the grandmother said: "I will give it to you when I die." After the grandmother passed away, the uncle suspected it was in the grandmother's place, and the grandmother also suspected it was in the uncle's place. The aunt of the Wei family said: "The mother wore it on her body all the time, it must have been taken back to the grave." The maids who took care of the sick all said they did not see it at the time of the burial. Therefore, there was another suspicion that it was in the aunt of the Wei family's place. Now, the aunt has been dead for a long time, the Wei family has declined greatly, and all their precious collections have been sold off. There has been no sign of this object being sold, and we still do not know where it has gone.

A guest brought a piece of Chai kiln porcelain and demanded several hundred gold coins for it. He claimed that if embedded in a helmet, it could deflect fire weapons on the battlefield. However, it was unknown whether this claim was true. I said, "Why not suspend this object with a rope and shoot it with a musket ball? If it can deflect fire, it will not break, and the price of several hundred gold coins would not be excessive; if it breaks, then the claim that it can deflect fire is false, and it would be unreasonable to demand such a high price." The seller refused, saying, "You are not an expert in appraisal, and it would be very unbecoming." He then took it away, and later it was heard that he sold it to a wealthy family for a hundred gold coins. A gentleman can be deceived by following the proper path, but it is difficult to deceive him by going against the right way. Cannon fire strikes horizontally, like thunderbolts descending from the sky. How could a mere piece of tile withstand it? Moreover, the beauty of Chai kiln porcelain lies in its true colors, which are exquisite. Ultimately, it is man-made, not divine, so how could it retain its miraculous properties after being broken? In my poem about old tile inkstones, I wrote: "The ruins of the copper province's platform have collapsed, leaving no trace, yet how many tiles remain like this? Scholars often have a penchant for the unusual, knowing in their hearts that it is false but deceiving themselves." The Chai kiln porcelain is of this kind.

Beyond Jiayuguan, there is the Kuo Shitutu Ridge, which serves as the boundary between Hami and Barkul. The term "Kuo Shitutu" translates to "Stone Inscription Mountain." On the mountain ridge, there is an inscription from the time of Emperor Taizong of Tang, commemorating the conquest of Gaochang by General Hou Junji. The guards have encased the inscription in brick and stone, preventing anyone from reading it, claiming that if one reads it, wind and snow will immediately arrive. This has been tested many times, and it has never failed. It is said that the spirits of the mountain, trees, and stones are active, displaying strange phenomena to demand sacrifices, and there is indeed a reason for this. In Barkul, there is another inscription from the time of Emperor Shun of Han, commemorating the defeat of Huanyuanwang by General Fei Cen, located on a lake ten miles west of the city. People can copy this inscription without any other unusual occurrences. However, it is said that the lake is inhabited by a cold dragon, and the city should not fire the night cannon. If the night cannon is fired, the cold dragon will be disturbed, and the sky will become extremely cold. This phenomenon cannot be explained by logic.

The Elder Li is of unknown origin. He claimed to be several hundred years old, but there is no record to verify this. His words were fragmented and remote, likely those of an enlightened or awakened figure from the previous dynasty. He once visited the home of my respected teacher, Mr. Qian Wenmin, and I had the chance to see him. He used talismans and medicine to treat illnesses, and sometimes there were minor successes. Mr. Qian Wenmin's second son lived in the Shuiyue An temple in the capital. One night, after drinking and returning home, he saw dozens of ghosts blocking the road and became delirious, cutting his own abdomen. I went with Chen Yuzhai and Ni Yujiang to visit him. His body was covered in blood and flesh, barely alive, and it seemed that he had no chance of survival. Suddenly, Elder Li arrived and took him away. After treating him for half a month, the wound healed, and people found this quite extraordinary. However, Mr. Qian Wenmin mistakenly believed in the practice of "Zhuoyou" (a form of exorcism) and had a wart removed, which led to complications and his death. Elder Li's treatment failed to save him, as the art of talismanic incantations and alchemical practices is sometimes effective, sometimes not. My respected teacher, Mr. Liu Wenzheng, said, "There must be immortals, but not the current medicine-selling Taoist priests; there must be Buddhas and Bodhisattvas, but not the current preaching Zen monks." This is truly a fair and balanced view for all time.

Yang Pian, the minor official, was a student of mine who I selected during the Jiaqing era's examination. His physiognomy and calculations of the eight characters and five planets were all accurate. When he served in the Ministry of Justice, he worked with Ruan Wushan. Suddenly, he told others, "According to my method, Wushan should become the Deputy Minister of Justice within half a month, but now the position is not vacant, what is the reason for this?" The next day, after the daily assembly, he privately told his colleagues, "The position of Du Gong is vacant." Subsequently, Du Ningtai indeed went on a military campaign in the Ili region. One day, he hurriedly requested leave, returned home, and bid farewell to me. I asked why he was in such a hurry, and he said, "I have only one son to take care of my elderly father, and now it is predicted that my son will die in a certain month. I am afraid that my father will be overwhelmed with grief, so I am in a hurry to return." At that time, it was not yet the time of death. Later, when I asked the locals, they confirmed what he had said, which is particularly strange. I once asked him about the belief of the school of thought that fate is predetermined and the belief of the school of thought that fate can be changed, who is right. He replied, "If one can find a favorable burial site, it is fate; if one mistakenly buries in an unfavorable site, it is also fate. The principle is the same." This statement can be said to grasp the truth.

A man exiled to Jichang, Peng Qi, had a seventeen-year-old daughter and a wife, both suffering from tuberculosis. His wife died first, and the daughter was also near death. Peng had government land to farm but could not take care of his daughter, so he abandoned her in the forest, leaving her to die or survive on her own. Her cries of pain and sorrow moved anyone who heard them to pity. Another exile, Yang Ao, spoke to Peng and said, "You are very cruel. How could anyone do such a thing? I am willing to take her back and nurse her. If she dies, I will bury her, and if she lives, I will marry her." Peng replied, "That is very good," and immediately wrote a contract and gave it to Yang. After half a year, the daughter did not recover. On her deathbed, she told Yang, "I am deeply grateful for your high virtue, which touches my heart. Because of our promise of marriage and the generous consent of my parents, I have no fear of suspicion. I have eaten, slept, and been cared for by you without any hesitation."

Though his body was sickly and emaciated, he had never yet shared a bed with her, and this filled him with deep shame and regret. If I were to die and there were no spirits, what more could I say? But if my soul and spirit are aware, I will surely find a way to repay her. He wept and died. Yang buried him with tears. After the burial, every night he dreamed of the woman coming to him in intimate affection, as if she were truly alive. When he awoke, he saw nothing; though he called her in the night, she never appeared. But as soon as he closed his eyes, she lay relaxed and exposed before him. After many such encounters, he came to know within the dream that it was indeed a dream. He questioned her about why she refused to appear in her true form. She replied: "I have heard from the spirits that humans are yang and spirits are yin. If yin invades yang, it will surely harm a person. Only when one sleeps does yang retreat and yin enter, allowing one to meet with spirits. Though the spirit may meet, the physical form does not touch, and thus there is no harm." This was the event of spring in the year Dinghai. By spring of the year Xinmao, four years had passed. After I returned, I did not know what had ultimately become of them. The story of Lu Chong and the golden bowl, I have heard of in ancient times; the tale of Song Yu and Yao Ji, I have seen only once by chance. As for daily encounters occurring entirely within dreams, this is something rarely seen in the written records.

A woman from the Meng family returned from visiting her ancestral graves during the Qingming Festival, feeling thirsty, she sought a drink at a nearby household. There, she saw a girl standing under a tree, whose appearance was exceptionally graceful and charming. After giving her water to drink, the girl invited her to sit together, and they enjoyed a very warm and friendly atmosphere. The woman asked about the girl's parents and siblings, and the girl's responses were well-organized and logical. The woman then jokingly asked if the girl had already been betrothed, offering to be her matchmaker. The girl blushed and retreated, and the woman could not call her out again. By then, it was already evening, and the woman left without saying goodbye. Half a year later, someone proposed marriage for the woman's son, and upon inquiry, it turned out to be the same girl. Overjoyed, the woman hurriedly arranged the marriage. After the wedding, the woman patted the girl's shoulder and said, "I haven't seen you for a few months, and you've grown even more mature." The girl was surprised and didn't know how to respond. Upon further questioning, she learned that the girl had lost her mother at the age of ten and had been raised by her maternal relatives for five or six years. The marriage was only arranged after the exchange of gifts. The woman had not visited her home during the Qingming Festival. The girl's family was of a different surname and was somewhat impoverished. If it were not for the woman personally seeing the girl's intelligence and wisdom, the marriage might not have been arranged. It was unclear what kind of ghost or spirit had taken the form of a person to facilitate the marriage, and it was also unclear why the spirit would do so. There are things that cannot be explained by reason, and this is one of them.

Su Dounan from Jiahe returned from the metropolitan examination in the year 1733 during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, arriving at Bai Gou River, where he met a friend at an inn. The friend had just been dismissed from office and, after drinking heavily, complained and lamented the lack of retribution for good and evil. Just then, a man hurriedly dressed, tied his horse to a tree, and sat down opposite them. After listening for a long time, he bowed to the friend and said, "Do you doubt that cause and effect are not fair? Those who indulge in lust will surely suffer, and those who are addicted to gambling will surely fail. Those who rob by force will surely be punished, and those who kill will surely be held accountable. Although they both indulge in lust, their constitutions differ in strength; although they both gamble, their skills differ in proficiency. The situation cannot be equalized. The principle should be treated differently. The subtleties are very fine. There is mutual compensation for merits and faults; sometimes what seems like no retribution is actually retribution. If the sins and merits are not fully accounted for, sometimes there is retribution but it is not immediate. The comparison is even finer than a hair's breadth. You base your doubts on what you see now and question the clarity of heavenly justice. Isn't this unreasonable? And how can you blame the heavens? Your fate was supposed to be through a minor official background, reaching a seventh-rank position. Because you are cunning and crafty, and skilled in espionage, skilled in avoiding danger and adept at pushing others aside, you were demoted to an eighth-rank position. When you were promoted to an eighth-rank position, you thought that your cleverness and subtlety had brought you from a ninth-rank position. But you didn't realize that it was because of your cleverness and subtlety that you were demoted from a seventh-rank position. He whispered something in your ear and then asked you to speak loudly. Have you forgotten? You were startled and broke out in a cold sweat, asking how he could know such fine details. He smiled and said, 'Not only do I know, but everyone in the three realms knows.' He turned his head and mounted his horse. All you could see was a cloud of yellow dust, and soon it disappeared."

During the Qianlong era, in the years 1752 and 1753, villagers often fell ill with strange diseases. Men developed tails from their coccyx bones, resembling deer antlers or coral branches; women suffered from vaginal prolapse, looking like grapes or mushrooms. Those who could treat them were healed instantly with a single cut, but without treatment, they would die. Rumors spread that a sorcerer had poisoned wells with medicine, causing people to drink the contaminated water and fall ill, thereby profiting from their suffering. At the time, Yǒng, a scholar in the Grand Council, was the governor of Hejian. Some suggested capturing the healers and punishing them. Yǒng replied, "This matter is indeed suspicious, but there is no concrete evidence. Each village has only three or four wells. By strictly guarding and monitoring them, the sorcerer will have no opportunity to act. If we arrest the healers, no one will dare to treat this condition again, and many may die. All matters should be carefully considered for their consequences, and we should not act too hastily." He refused to allow the arrest. The disease eventually subsided. Some in the county regarded Yǒng's actions as calming the situation, while others accused him of allowing wrongdoing. Later, when I was in Urumqi, the scarcity of cattle led to high prices, causing hardship for farmers. To control prices, I banned slaughtering, which initially reduced the prices. However, cattle traders heard that cattle were cheap and stopped coming, leading to even higher prices the following year. After lifting the ban, prices gradually stabilized. In another instance, there were hundreds of illegal gold miners in remote mountains. Arresting them might cause unrest, but allowing them to continue would foster a larger problem. I devised a plan to cut off their supply lines, which led to their dispersal. However, after dispersing, they became outlaws, causing ongoing security issues. It took half a year of management to resolve the situation. Thus, I realized that in all matters, one often knows only one aspect and not the other. Actions that bring immediate benefits can often lead to long-term problems. Only then did I truly appreciate Yǒng's foresight and wisdom, as his later words proved to be far-sighted and wise.

*Yuewei Caotang Biji* Volume Nine: "Thus I Have Heard" Part Three Wang Zaiyang once said that he stayed overnight at a friend's vegetable garden and heard people outside the window say: "The wind and snow are very cold; you can temporarily take shelter in an empty house." He then heard another person say: "The back wall is partially collapsed; what shall we do if thieves enter?" "Those who eat at someone's table must also serve their duties." It was implied that these were the night watchmen among the servants. When dawn broke and the door was opened, there was no trace of anyone. Only two dogs were lying down under the broken part of the wall, covered in snow up to their bellies. Jiaxiang's Zeng Yinghua commented: "This is a parable by Zaiyang, intended to shame those servants who are ungrateful." I believe that dogs, without being coerced, remain vigilant and faithful at night, and endure cold and hunger to stay loyal to their masters. No servant in the world can match their loyalty. This is enough to make people feel ashamed, whether the dogs can speak or not.

According to the clear account of the grandson Han, there was a man from the Zhao family in Nanpi who was bewitched by a fox. The fox attached itself to his body and always spoke to people from the folds of his clothes. Once, he hung a small image of Zhong Kui on the wall. That night, he heard jumping sounds in the room and thought the fox had been driven away. However, the next day, the fox continued to speak as before. When asked if he had ever seen Zhong Kui, the fox replied: "Zhong Kui is very terrifying. Fortunately, his body is only a foot long, and his sword is only a few inches long. When he goes to bed, I get down; when he gets down, I go to bed. He cannot hit me at all." Thus, is the painting truly magical? Is the magic of the painting truly as depicted? If the painting were a tiny image, would it also wield a needle-thin sword and gently cut away evil? This is truly inexplicable. In the summer of 1788, hundreds of laborers in Xian County were repairing the city wall and tearing down old battlements. They threw broken bricks down to the base of the wall, where hundreds of other laborers carried them in wicker baskets. After the food was cooked, they beat drums and gathered for meals. While they were eating, one of the laborers named Xin Wu told the others: "Just now while transporting bricks, I suddenly heard a loud voice by my ear, saying, 'Killing someone means paying with your life, owing money means repaying it. Do you know this?' Looking back, I saw nothing, which is truly strange." Soon, everyone worked together, and the bricks fell like hail. One brick hit Xin Wu, causing his skull to split and he died. There were shouts of alarm and confusion, but the name of the person who struck him was never found, and the authorities could not investigate or judge. The labor supervisor was ordered to pay ten thousand coins for the coffin and burial. It turned out that Xin Wu's past life owed the striker a debt, and the striker's past life owed Xin Wu money. Karma and fate entangled, and eventually they balanced each other out. Without the prior warning of ghosts and deities, how could it not be seen as a coincidence?

Zhu Tongyu said that in his village, there was an old family with a book tower that was always locked. Whenever it was opened to be inspected, footprints of a woman were always seen on the dust, very small, measuring just over two inches long. Knowing them to be those of a ghost or spirit, the family remained silent and unseen for many years, and no one knew what kind of strange phenomenon this was. A local man named Liu, who was light-hearted and unrestrained, foolishly hoped for a meeting like Wang Xuan. He prayed to the owner of the book tower and stayed alone upstairs, preparing tea, fruits, wine, and food, burning incense and making fervent wishes, going to bed with a lit candle, and holding his breath in anticipation. However, he saw and heard nothing. Gradually, he felt a cold, eerie atmosphere piercing into his flesh and bones, able to see and hear but unable to speak or move. After a while, the cold seeped into his lungs and chest, like lying on layers of ice and snow, painful to endure. Until dawn, he could finally speak, still feeling frozen stiff. Since then, no one dared to stay in the book tower again. The ghost's behavior was hidden and subtle, showing the refined and profound qualities of a gentleman. Even in handling Liu, the ghost remained calm and composed.

The Rebirth of Gu Feixiong, as recorded in Duan Chengshi's *Xiyang Zazu* and Sun Guangxian's *Beimeng Suoyan*, is also mentioned in the collected works of Gu Kuang, Gu Feixiong's father, suggesting that the story is not fabricated. Recently, Shen Yunzhao, a former Minister of Rites, wrote a memorial for his mother, Lady Lu, detailing that upon her marriage, she had just completed her first year when her husband passed away. She gave birth to a posthumous child who lived only three years before dying. Lady Lu mourned deeply, saying, "I am now a widow, but I have you to rely on. Now that you are gone, I cannot bear to see our family lineage end here." At the time of the child's burial, she marked his arm with red ink and vowed, "If heaven does not abandon our family, let this be a sign of his rebirth, which occurred in December 1739 during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng." In the same month, a relative living nearby gave birth to a child with a red mark on his arm. Lady Lu adopted him, considering him her heir and the future Minister of Rites. When I served as Minister of Rites, I worked alongside Shen Yunzhao, who recounted this story in great detail. While Buddhist texts often contain fantastical stories that their followers exaggerate to manipulate others into giving alms, the concept of reincarnation is well-supported by evidence. Fate sometimes reveals signs through individual events to demonstrate moral teachings. Shen Yunzhao's story is an example of using reincarnation to illustrate the virtue of enduring hardship. Confucian scholars often deny the existence of ghosts and spirits, but how can they truly know?

The actor Fang Jun Guan, in his youth, was renowned for his beauty and talent, earning the admiration of the literati. In his old age, he sold ancient artifacts and occasionally traveled to the capital. Once, gazing at his reflection, he sighed, "How could anyone believe that Fang Jun Guan has come to this state? Who would think that I, who once captivated audiences with my dance and song, have fallen so far?" Ni Yu Jiang, moved by this, wrote a poem: "Wandering the rivers and lakes, my temples now speckled with silver, I recall the days when I played the red ivory plectrum to the music. Where has the butterfly of Zhuangzi gone? Only a single branch of withered flowers remains, filled with melancholy." This poem was written for Fang Jun Guan. Fang Jun Guan himself claimed to be the son of a Confucian scholar. At the age of thirteen or fourteen, while studying in a village school, he suddenly dreamed of being surrounded by music and lanterns, entering a room. Looking down, he saw himself dressed in a brocade skirt and adorned with pearls and jade, his feet gracefully bent, resembling those of a newlywed wife.

Startled and confused, she did not know what to do, yet was seized by many hands and could not control herself. She was finally carried into the curtain and sat shoulder to shoulder with a man, both terrified and ashamed, and awoke trembling with sweat. Later, she was lured by a reckless man and ultimately lost herself in the place of music and dance, then realized that all things were predestined. Yu Jiang said: "Wei Xima asked the Master of Music about a dream, and the Master of Music said it was a thought. You have likely accumulated this thought, and thus had this dream; once you had this thought, the dream led to this downfall. Indeed, cause arises from effect, and effect is created by the mind. How can one entrust it to fate?" I say that these people, sunk in degradation and filth, should also be karmic retribution from a previous life, experienced in this life. It cannot be said that there is absolutely no hidden fate. What I said is merely a discussion on returning to the root and clearing the source. Later, Su Xingcun heard this and said: "Xiaolan uses the theory of the three lives to explain causality, warning about the future; Yu Jiang uses the theory of one thought to explain causality, warning about the present. Though each clarifies one meaning, I ultimately believe Yu Jiang’s view can help people not to let their minds run wild."

My clan ancestor, Mr. Huang Tu, once said that he visited a friend in the northern peak. One summer night, he went for a walk outside the village and, without realizing, went slightly far. He heard a groaning sound in the millet field and followed the sound to look. There was a boy lying naked, and he asked what he was suffering from. The boy said that in the evening twilight, he passed by here and met a young girl with braided hair. She invited him to talk, and he was pleased by her beauty, so he teased her. The girl said that her parents were all out and invited him to sit at home. She led him to a deep part of the millet field, where there was a house with three rooms, empty and without anyone. The girl closed the door and brought out fruits to eat together. Their laughter and conversation were harmonious, and he undressed and climbed onto the bed. When he embraced her to the pillow, the girl suddenly transformed into a man with a terrifying appearance and brutally and violently acted. Terrified and unable to resist, he ultimately endured the violation, being trampled and tormented until he fainted. After a long time, he gradually regained consciousness and found himself lying in a desolate place with weeds, and the house and its surroundings had disappeared. It was a demon who was attracted to the boy's appearance and transformed into a girl to seduce him. Seeing profit and rushing towards it, he was ultimately ensnared by it, which was inevitable.

Respected Master Zhao Hengshan, when he was young, studied at West Lake. He chose a quiet temple building and set up his bed there. One night, he heard a rustling sound inside the room, as if someone was moving around. He shouted and asked, "Are you a ghost or a fox? Why are you disturbing me?" Gradually, he heard a hesitant response: "I am both a ghost and a fox." He then asked, "If you are a ghost, you are a ghost; if you are a fox, you are a fox. Why are you both a ghost and a fox?" After a long pause, the response came again: "I was originally a fox who had lived for hundreds of years. My internal alchemy had reached completion, but unfortunately, I was killed by a fellow fox who stole my alchemical essence. My spirit became trapped and now I am a ghost of a fox." He asked why he did not seek justice from the underworld. The response was: "All alchemical essences created through breathing and stretching exercises are like blood and qi attached to the body, fused into one, and not from the outside, so no one can steal them. However, those created through the practice of extracting essence from others are like stolen wealth, not rightfully mine, and thus can be taken by killing. I seduced people to take their essence, causing much harm. The one who kills will die, and death is the punishment. Even if I appeal to the gods, they will not listen. Therefore, I prefer to remain here in a state of depression." He asked what he was doing in the temple building. The response was: "I was originally hiding my form and practicing the method of transforming through the Great Yin. Because the sun's rays were scorching, my spiritual essence became unsettled, so I came out to beg for mercy, seeking a balance between the spiritual and material worlds." After he finished speaking, only the sound of his head hitting the ground was heard. When asked again, he did not respond. The next day, Master Zhao moved out. He once recounted this experience to his disciples, saying: "To take what is not yours will ultimately lead to your own destruction. How terrifying it is!"

From my elder brother Wan Zhou's account, there was a farm woman in Jiahe who would always ride a mule to visit her parents' home whenever she returned. The mule was very strong and well-trained, knowing the way without needing to be led. If her husband was busy, she would ride it herself, and it never failed her. One day, she returned slightly late, and it was a cloudy and moonless night, making it hard to tell directions. Suddenly, the mule ran wildly and carried her straight into a sweet sorghum field, where thick foliage and dense undergrowth made her lost and unable to return. By midnight, they reached a ruined temple, where only two beggars were staying under the eaves. With no other options, she had to stay with them for the night. The next day, the beggars escorted her back home, and her husband felt ashamed. He decided to sell the mule to a butcher's shop. That night, he had a dream where someone told him: "This mule in a past life stole your money, and you caught him urgently, but he escaped and was spared. You instructed the captors to detain his wife, who was detained for one night. Now, as the mule, he is repaying the stolen money by carrying your wife into the ruined temple, which is detaining your wife as repayment. Why create an enmity for the next life?" He awakened with a start and deeply repented. That very night, the mule suddenly died.

When the servant Ren Yu was at the critical moment of his illness, the watchers heard a sound like a cow's roar outside the window, and Yu died suddenly in terror. The next day, they discussed the unusual event, and Yu's wife wept, saying, "This young man had previously stolen and killed several cows, but no one knew about it." There was an elderly man who had served in the legal system for over forty years. After falling ill and nearing death, he experienced a ghostly apparition under the moonlight. He reflected, "I have always been kind-hearted and honest, vowing never to unjustly kill anyone. Why has this ghost come to haunt me?" That night, he dreamed of several people, their bodies covered in blood, crying out, "You know the deep-seated grudges from harsh and cruel actions, but you do not understand that even kindness can lead to resentment. The weak and vulnerable suffer greatly at the hands of the powerful, and their souls cry in pain, holding a grudge in the afterlife, hoping for justice and retribution. You only see the suffering of the living, not the sorrow of the dead. You manipulate legal documents to unjustly excuse the guilty, allowing the wicked to escape punishment and leaving the dead with unresolved injustices. Imagine yourself in their position, suffering unjustly, and observe how the judgment of the case is manipulated to the detriment of the innocent. Is this not a cause for gratitude or resentment? If you think that allowing evil to go unpunished is a good deed, who else should the wronged dead hold a grudge against if not you?" The elderly man awoke in fear, told his son about the dream, and struck himself, acknowledging his mistake. He died before he could settle down to sleep.

Liu Taishi Guoshi of Cangzhou was a man of broad and open-minded disposition, possessing the refined and unworldly character of the Jin dynasty scholars. He was on friendly terms with Old Man of Sweet Mountain and Mountain Man of Lotus Bay, though their intellectual inclinations differed. In his later years, while living at home, he supported himself by teaching students, but only accepted those who were poor and solitary, allowing them to present their gifts as disciples. His tuition was meager, and his gourd and ladle often went empty, yet he remained calm and unperturbed. Once, he bought more than a dou of rice and stored it in a jar, but after over a month, the rice had not diminished, which greatly surprised him. Suddenly, he heard a voice from under the eaves saying, "I am a celestial fox, who admires your noble conduct. I have been secretly adding to your rice each day. Do not be surprised." Liu questioned him, saying, "Your intention is indeed good, but you cannot farm, so where does this grain come from? I cannot drink from the spring of theft. Please do not do this again." The fox sighed and departed.

My deceased nephew, Rubei, courtesy name Lihan, once dreamed that someone recited poetry to him. Upon waking, he remembered a couplet: "The spring of grass and flowers is like a dream, and the night of heavy rain and wind feels like an endless year." He told me about it. I was surprised that it was not a good omen, and indeed, he died in the seventh month of the leap year "Wu Chen" (1738). After his death, his wife, Zhang from Wuchang, raised her brother's son as her heir. She maintained a strict and virtuous life for over thirty years, never once sleeping without taking off her clothes. Even the servants and maids still talk about it. I then realized that the couplet was a harbinger of widowhood and solitary living. During the years of "Bing Wu" and "Ding Wei" in the reign of Emperor Yongzheng (1736-1737), there were wandering refugees begging for food who passed through Cui Village. The couple was both suffering from an epidemic. On the verge of death, they held a contract and cried out in the market, wishing to sell their young daughter as a servant and using the money from selling the daughter to buy two coffins. My grandmother, Lady Zhang, buried the couple and took in the daughter, naming her Lian Gui. The contract listed the father as Zhang Li and the mother as Huang, but did not specify their hometown. When asked, they were already unable to speak. Lian Gui said that her family was from Shandong, with a house near a postal road, where there were often officials and their carriages passing by. It was about a month's journey from there, but she could not name the specific county. She also said that last year she had been betrothed to a family named Hu who lived next door. The Hu family was begging for food elsewhere and their whereabouts were unknown. After more than ten years, no relatives came to search for her. She was then married to a stablehand named Liu Deng. Liu Deng said he was from Xintai, Shandong, originally having the surname Hu, but after his parents died, he was raised by a family surnamed Liu and thus took their surname.

I remember my parents arranged a marriage for a girl, but I did not know her surname. She turned out to be of the surname Hu, and Xintai is also where the postal road passes. Wandering refugees begging for food, their journey could take over a month, and what Liangui said matches. I strongly suspected it was like the mirror of Yuezhang, separated and then reunited, but there was no clear evidence. My late uncle, Mr. Sufu, said: "This story could be embellished slightly, and it could even be included in a legendary tale." Unfortunately, this girl was as dull as a deer or pig, knowing only to eat well and sleep soundly, not suitable for embellishment, it is regrettable. The recluse Mr. Bianduan said: "The Qin people are not dead, so we believe Fu Sheng was falsely accused; the old man from Shu still exists, knowing how much Zhuge Liang was wronged." This is from Liu Zhiji's *Shitong*. The story of Fu Sheng is recorded in *Luyang Qialan Ji*, and the story of Zhuge Liang is found in the biography of Mao Xiuzhi in the *Wei Shu*. Pu Er'tian, in his commentary on *Shitong*, believed it was not detailed, it was likely an oversight in research. Historical records cannot avoid embellishment, even more so for legendary tales.
