The *Xicloud Record* describes Mu Suhui as beautiful like a fairy, and Wu Lintang said his ancestor saw her when he was young, short but plump, just an ordinary woman. Then, in legendary tales, the so-called beautiful women, half are fictional. This maid, though coarse, if a well-intentioned person follows the score to write lyrics, to perform on stage, one day on the red carpet, wouldn't they not be as charming as singing birds and blooming flowers? What the master has said, still不免 to fully trust the book.

Niezongyan said, in a temple in Jiaozhou, there was a vegetable garden behind the scripture hall. One night, the monk opened the window to cool off, and the moon was bright like a painting. He saw a person lingering under an old tree. Suspecting that the person was stealing vegetables, the monk called out to ask who he was, and the person bowed deeply and replied, "Master, do not be surprised, I am a ghost." The monk asked why the ghost did not return to his grave. The ghost replied, "Ghosts have their own groups, each following their kind. I was originally a scholar, but unfortunately, I was buried in a crowded cemetery, where I could not associate with lowly people like horse doctors and summer gardeners. These people also disliked me because I was not of their kind, and thus found it hard to fit in. I prefer to avoid the noise here." After finishing speaking, he slowly disappeared. Later, the monk often saw him from afar, but when he called to him, the ghost did not respond.

The Office of the Provincial Examiner in Fuzhou was originally the office of the tax eunuch of the previous Ming dynasty. Eunuchs were cruel and oppressive, often secretly killing innocent people, and to this day, strange phenomena are still frequently seen. When I was overseeing education in Fujian, the servants were often frightened at night. In the summer of the year Jia Yin (甲寅), my late father, the Governor of Yao'an, came to the office and heard that there was a ghost in a certain room. He moved his bed into that room and remained calm all night. I once took the opportunity to gently advise him, saying, "Please do not risk your precious body against ghosts." He then taught me, saying: "Scholars who say there are no ghosts are talking nonsense and forcing words. However, ghosts must fear people; darkness cannot overcome light. If they dare to harm people, it must be because light is insufficient to overcome darkness. The strength of light does not depend on physical strength or fierce temperament. In a person's heart, kindness is light, and cruelty is darkness. Honesty is light, and deceit is darkness. Fairness is light, and selfishness is darkness. Therefore, in the *I Ching*, light represents gentlemen, and darkness represents petty people. If one's heart is upright and great, then their spirit is purely strong and bright. Even if there are evil spirits, they will be like a dark room where a large furnace burns a fierce flame, melting away the frost. You have read quite a lot. Have you ever seen in history or biographies any upright and learned people being attacked by ghosts?" I bowed twice and accepted his teaching. To this day, whenever I recall his teachings, I feel a sense of awe as if he were standing beside me.

A young man from the Shao family in Shuzhou was known for his playful and flirtatious nature. He heard that there was a beautiful fox spirit in an ancient tomb in Huazhen and would often go to spy on her. One day, he saw her sitting on the ridge of a field. Just as he was about to make amorous advances, the fox spirit spoke sternly: "I have been practicing breathing exercises and physical cultivation for over two hundred years and have vowed never to seduce anyone. Do not entertain any foolish thoughts. Moreover, those who seduce others, do they truly enjoy it? They only take away their essence. When the essence is drained, the person dies. No one has ever been able to escape this fate. Why do you throw yourself into a trap?" She waved her sleeve, and a cold wind blew suddenly, dust flying into his eyes. She was already gone. An earlier respected scholar named Yao An, upon hearing this, said: "This fox can speak such words. I predict that she will certainly be reborn in heaven."

Xian County brothers Li Jinliang and Li Jingu were both notorious bandits. One night, Li Jinliang dreamed that his father said to him: "Bandits have their successes and failures. Do you know this? Wealth obtained from corrupt officials through threats and coercion; wealth taken from powerful and influential villains through force and deception; wealth gained through secretive and unfair means; wealth acquired through coercion or deceit; wealth obtained through cunning servants or laborers who exploit their positions; wealth gained through excessive interest rates and exploitation; and any wealth obtained through stingy and calculating means. This type of wealth can be taken without harm. Those who commit serious crimes, even if they kill, will not be harmed, for they are already condemned by heaven. If a person is inherently good and obtains wealth ethically, they are blessed by heaven. But if they violate this, they go against the will of heaven, and going against the will of heaven will eventually lead to failure. Your brothers previously robbed a chaste woman, causing the mother and child to cry out in injustice, and the spirits to look upon them with anger. If you do not repent and change your ways, disaster will not be far off. After a year or so, both Li Jinliang and Li Jingu were indeed punished by law. When Li Jinliang was arrested, he knew he would not escape, and he recounted this story to Shi Zhenru, a clerk in the criminal department. Shi Zhenru is from my hometown and once told this story to Yao An Gong, saying that bandits also have their own code of conduct. He also recounted the words of another notorious bandit, Li Zhihong: 'For thirty years, I have been riding and galloping, and I have robbed and plundered much. I have seen many others rob and plunder. About two or three out of ten bandits fail, while seven or eight out of ten succeed. But if one defiles or harms women, counting with my fingers, there has never been a single bandit who did not fail. Therefore, I constantly warn my followers, for heaven punishes those who indulge in excess, and this is an unchanging truth.'

In the summer of the Xinmao year, I returned from military service in Urumqi and rented a house on the eastern side of Zhu Chao Street, where I became a neighbor to Mr. Long, Chief Censor Chengzu. The house had five rooms in its second courtyard, and the southernmost of these rooms had a curtain that would constantly rise a foot or more, as if pushed by the wind. The curtains in my other four rooms did not behave this way. I could not understand the cause. When my young children entered the room, they would immediately cry in fear, saying that there was a fat monk sitting on the bed, smiling at them. How could a black-robed monk or a fierce ghost occupy a human dwelling? This was especially puzzling. Moreover, after the third drumbeat of the night, I often heard the sound of a woman weeping from Mr. Long’s residence. Those in Mr. Long’s house also heard it, and they said the sound came from this house, though the matter remained unclear. Nevertheless, I knew with certainty that this was not a good place to live, and so I moved to the Double Tree Study behind Master Zhanan’s residence. Those who lived in these two houses both met with misfortune. Mr. Baihuan, the Nine Judicial Officer, died suddenly without illness, and this occurred precisely in Mr. Long’s house. Thus, the saying of a haunted house is no mere superstition. My former teacher, Mr. Chen Baiya, once said: "Those who live in a good house are not necessarily good, and those who live in an evil house are not necessarily evil. Just as a gentle and warm breeze may not necessarily cure illness, yet a severe cold and harmful force may cause disease upon the slightest contact. A good medicine may not necessarily quickly restore health, but a strong remedy, once taken, may cause complete diarrhea." This, too, is a matter of real principle, and one should not stubbornly contend with fate. Mencius once said: "Therefore, one who understands fate will not stand beneath a sheer cliff."

From the words of Guo Shizhou in Luoyang, there was an elderly couple in a neighboring county who, for two hundred gold coins from a wealthy family, sold their widowed daughter-in-law as a concubine. On the appointed day, they forced her to dress in colorful clothing and pushed her onto the carriage. When the woman refused to go, they tied her hands with a red cloth and the matchmaker pushed her onto the carriage. The onlookers sighed deeply in indignation, but the woman had no relatives to speak up for her. As the driver started the carriage, the woman let out a cry, and suddenly a storm erupted. The three horses became frightened and ran uncontrollably, not heading towards her home but towards the county town. They flew over muddy ruts as if on a broad road, not tipping over even on narrow paths and dangerous bridges, and finally stood firm at the county hall, where the matter was thus thwarted. This is why it is known that the daughter-in-law called down lightning from heaven, and it is not a vain word in the classics. My cousin Yao Gong Jieran said: Vengeful spirits seeking justice are not uncommon in the classics, nor are they unheard of in hearsay. In the fifth month of the Guiwei year, on my way back from Gengjia An in Yanshan to Cui Village, I personally witnessed it. The man was about fifty years old, wearing a straw hat and a bright shirt, and was carrying a sleeping mat on a donkey, tied to a willow tree by the riverbank, leaning against the tree and sitting. I also tied my horse to rest. Suddenly, the man leaped up, gesturing as if defending himself, saying, "You took my life; I take yours. Why do you fight so?" He held on for a long time, his words gradually becoming indistinct and incomprehensible. Suddenly, he leaped into the water and was swallowed by the waves. There were more than ten people who saw it, all of them clasping their hands and reciting the Buddha. Although we did not know what injustice he was avenging, it was clear that he was taking a life for a life, as he himself had said.

In the summer of the year of the Earth Rat, a young female servant named Yu'er died of consumption. Soon after, she revived and said, "Yin officials have sent me back to demand money." Paper money was bought and burned for her, and she died again. Shortly thereafter, she revived once more and said, "The silver color was insufficient; the yin officials would not accept it." More refined gold and silver foil folded into ingots were then bought and burned, and she died without reviving again. Reflecting on the year of the Water Rat during the Yongzheng era, the author recalled that his deceased younger brother Yinggu, on the brink of death, had experienced something similar. Yet, does burning yin money truly have any use? If yin officials demand such things, what duties do yin officials perform? Hu Mutting, a high-ranking official, recounted that in his hometown, there was a man who was born to serve as a yin official. He described the affairs of the yin realm in great detail, though he could not recall everything. His account largely matched what was recorded in the classics. He particularly mentioned the concept of the six realms of rebirth, stating that it does not require the yin officials to send souls, as each person naturally follows their karmic path based on their actions in life, much like water flows to wet things and fire seeks dry things. The way of the yin realm is very reasonable, yet this aspect has never been mentioned by those who discuss ghosts. Foxes seduce humans primarily for the purpose of replenishing their own energy, not for the sake of seeking beauty. However, there are occasional instances where they do so for pleasure. The author's cousin An Hubei recounted that a man once spent the night in a deep forest and heard voices among the grass. One voice said, "Do you love the young boy from a certain family? Has everything gone smoothly?" This situation, driven by excessive yang energy, could severely deplete one's yin energy and corrupt the path. The other voice replied, "Thank you for your advice. I was indeed captivated by his beauty and could not forget him. However, though the boy is physically attractive, he has no evil thoughts. In my dreams, I tried to entice him with various sensual temptations, but he remained unmoved. I could do nothing about it and have since abandoned this desire." The man sensed something unusual and secretly went to look, only to see two foxes jumping away.

Tianzhou Ren Zitian, also known as Da Chun, was renowned for his extensive memory and profound scholarship, especially in the detailed annotations of the Three Ritual Classics and the study of the Six Principles of Chinese characters. In the year 1769 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, he achieved the first place in the second tier of the imperial examination, but his career remained unremarkable until his later years when he was appointed as a Censor. He died before taking up his post. Since the founding of the dynasty, only three individuals who achieved the first place in the second tier of the imperial examination did not enter the Hanlin Academy. Ren Zitian was indeed one of them. He recounted that when he was around fifteen or sixteen years old, he once wrote palace poems on a fan for his stepfather's concubine. His stepfather suspected him, leading to the concubine's suicide by hanging. Her soul appealed to the underworld, and Ren Zitian fell ill, his spirit also following her to the underworld. He was interrogated for several days and judged by officials in the underworld multiple times. It was determined that his actions were unintentional, but he was still found guilty of unintentional manslaughter and had his official salary reduced, thus his career was fraught with difficulties. Scholar Jia Dunfu remarked: "The judge of this case was Gu Demao, a scholar-official. The two did not know each other before, but when they met one day, they acted as if they had known each other for a long time. They were sitting together and personally witnessed the recounting of events in the underworld. When Ren Zitian recounted these events, he was still trembling."

Respected Elder Yang Huaiting from Jimo said that in Jining, there was a young boy who was favored by a fox. At night, they would share the same bed and pillow. By the time he was over twenty years old, he still had no night without the fox. Some advised him to grow a beard, but when his beard grew a little, the fox would cut it off while he was sleeping and apply makeup to him. They tried to exorcise the fox with talismans and magical inscriptions, but none of it worked. Later, when the Taoist master Zhengyi passed through Jining by boat, the boy submitted a petition requesting exorcism. The master issued a decree to the city god, and the fox then came to the master to plead its case. The fox did not reveal its form, but others heard its words. It said that in a past life, it was a woman, and this boy was a monk. One night, he passed the temple gate and was kidnapped and locked in a cave. For seventeen years, he endured humiliation in secret, and he died with a heavy heart. In the underworld, the judge ruled that this monk had completed his punishment in hell and would repay the debt in the next life. Coincidentally, the fox fell into the form of a fox due to another sin and hid in the mountains and forests for over a hundred years, unable to meet the monk. Now, it has achieved spiritual transformation and happened to meet the monk's reincarnation as this boy, and thus was able to repay the debt. After seventeen years, it will leave on its own and does not need to be exorcised. The Taoist master could do nothing about it. It is unknown whether the fox left after the period was up. However, based on what the fox said, we can see that people have debts, even after several lifetimes, they must still be repaid.

Xiang Jun Tingmo, who passed the imperial examinations in the same year as I, told me that he once stayed at the home of a certain scholar-official in the Hanlin Academy. They would often discuss learning together. One day, an official from the same district who served outside the capital came to visit and brought some gifts. The scholar-official stated his lifelong simplicity and frugality, saying that he did not need such things. Seeing his aloof and haughty demeanor, the official hesitated and took the gifts back. After sending off the guest, the scholar-official paced in front of the hall, feeling despondent and lost, as if something were missing. This state of mind lasted for several moments. When his family asked him to come inside for lunch, he was greatly annoyed. Suddenly, he heard a few people giggling. He looked around but saw no trace of them. Upon searching, he found the sound coming from above the ceiling. It was a fox spirit.

Chen Shao, a subordinate official to the Grand Censor with the courtesy name Gengyan, served in the Hanlin Academy when he was troubled by a demon. To avoid the demon, he moved to a new residence, but the demon followed him there. The demon often threw small notes containing private matters that only it knew, causing Chen Shao great fear. He began to worship the demon devoutly. One day, the demon threw a note criticizing Chen Shao for treating his nephew poorly and warned, "If you do not provide generous financial support, disaster will come." This led others to secretly suspect his nephew. They agreed to spy on him, and one night, they heard the sound of breaking objects. They rushed out and caught the nephew, who was indeed the culprit. Chen Shao, known for his kind and generous nature, especially towards his family, simply said, "If you need money, you can tell me. Why do you act like this?" He laughed and sent his nephew back to sleep. From then on, the demon no longer troubled him. Later, Wu Pu, a scholar in the imperial examination system, suddenly suffered a fire, and no one knew where it came from. He moved twice but was burned twice. I thought this might be similar to Chen Shao's experience. Wu Pu said, "I also suspected it, but during the third move to a meeting hall in Quanzhou, I was sitting in the hall with guests when suddenly a blazing flame shot down from the ceiling. It was not something a person could climb, nor could they enter it. It must have been truly caused by a demon."

Cheng Yeyuan, a scholar, resided in the old house of Cao Zhuxu. One night, due to negligence, a fire broke out, and many paintings, calligraphy, and ancient artifacts were destroyed. Among them was a scroll of Chu Huannan's copy of the "Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion," which had been pawned for five hundred gold coins. Just as he was considering how to retrieve it when a dealer came to redeem it, he suddenly found the scroll amidst the ashes. The box and cover were damaged, but the scroll itself was not even slightly harmed. His cousin Zhang Guiyan, who was staying at Cheng's house, witnessed this. Is this what Bai Juyi meant when he said that everywhere and always, there are divine beings protecting things? Or is it that destruction and preservation each have their predetermined fate, and this scroll was spared from the fire disaster? Nevertheless, the event is indeed remarkable and will be a delightful story for future connoisseurs. Ke Yufeng, a contemporary of Cheng, was a censor. Once, while staying overnight at a friend's house in the inner city, he heard a sound like a duck's call coming from the eastern room. He was startled and looked closely. At that moment, the moonlight filled the window, and he saw a black smoke trail emerging from the gap in the eastern room's door, touching the ground and moving along, about ten feet long, twisting like a giant python. The head of the smoke was a woman with neatly coiled hair. She looked up, her eyes wide, and continued making the duck-like sound. Ke Yufeng, who was known for his courage, slapped the bed and shouted at it. The smoke slowly retreated and disappeared back through the door gap. In the morning, he informed the owner, who said that there was a known supernatural occurrence here, which occasionally appeared every few years, causing no harm and bringing no other good or bad omen. Someone else said that before the house was bought, the previous owner had a servant girl who died in this room, and the exact cause is unknown.

A skilled gambler, known as the leader of a group, easily took people's money as if reaching into a bag, without using force. His followers closely supported each other, communicating through subtle gestures and expressions, employing countless tricks, coordinating like arms and fingers, and connected like breath. Wealthy salt merchants, like fish swallowing bait or pheasants encountering a lure, were easily preyed upon. For nearly ten years, he amassed vast wealth, sending his son to trade in Changlu to plan for a ten percent profit. The son was also cunning, but promiscuous and fond of seeking beauty. Those who fell into his schemes and ruined their families held intense hatred for him. The son asked to go with him and was secretly guided to visit the brothel, indulging in the attire of courtesans, so engrossed that he forgot to return, consuming nine-tenths of his wealth. The leader slightly heard about this and went himself to inspect, finding it already beyond repair. Those who discuss say that even if it is human planning, it also has heavenly will. The enemy's movement of this thought, perhaps the spirit inspired his heart? Otherwise, why was he foolish before and wise after?

故城刁飞万言，其乡有与狐女生子者，其父母怒谇之，狐女涕泣曰：舅姑见逐，义难抗拒，但子未离乳，当且携去耳。越两岁余，忽抱子诣其夫曰：儿已长，今还汝，其夫遵父母戒，掉首不与语。狐女太息，抱之去。此狐殊有人理。但抱去之儿，不知作何究竟？将人所生者仍为人，庐居火食，混迹闾阎欤？抑妖所生者仍为妖，幻化通灵，潜踪墟墓欤？或虽为妖，而犹承父姓，长育子孙，在非妖非人之界欤？虽为人，而犹依母党，往来窟穴，在亦人亦妖之间欤？惜见首不见尾，竟莫得而质之。 In the village of Gucheng, there was a man named Diao Feiwan who had a child with a fox woman. His parents scolded him severely for this union. The fox woman wept and said, "My in-laws have driven me away, and it is difficult to resist out of moral obligation. However, since the child is still nursing, I will take it away for now." After two years, she suddenly took the child to its father and said, "The child has grown up, and I am returning it to you." The husband, following his parents' instructions, turned away and did not speak to her. The fox woman sighed deeply and took the child away. This fox woman was remarkably human-like. But what will happen to the child that was taken away? Will it remain human, live in a house, eat cooked food, and blend in with ordinary people? Or will it remain a demon, change forms and possess spiritual powers, and hide in graveyards? Perhaps, even if it is a demon, it still carries the father's surname, raises descendants, and exists in a realm neither demon nor human? Even if it is human, it still relies on its mother's family, moves between dens, and exists in a space between human and demon? Unfortunately, the beginning is seen but the end is not, and it cannot be verified.

The scholar-official Jiang Xinyu, a fellow examinee, recounted that in his hometown there was an old, abandoned house where one often saw a beautiful woman dressed up, appearing at the wall. Wu, a bold and fearless martial artist, decided to bring a blanket and spend the night alone inside, hoping to meet someone. By midnight, the house was silent, and he patted his pillow and spoke to himself: "People say there is a fox girl in this house. Where has she gone now?" A small voice from outside the window replied: "Madame Liu knows you are here today and has gone to the stream to watch the moon." He asked, "Who are you?" She replied, "I am Madame Liu's servant." He then asked, "Why are you avoiding me alone?" She said, "I don't know why, but she said she fears meeting this 'abdomen-bearing general,' and I don't understand what that means." Later, Wu often asked people, "What rank is the 'abdomen-bearing general' in the military?" Everyone laughed heartily. Afterward, he asked the locals, "Is there really such a person and such a thing? However, I wandered all night and saw nothing. The story is embellished by Jiang Xinyu. Jiang Xinyu enjoys humor, and perhaps it is true."

My mother, Lady Zhang, once hired an elderly woman from Fangshan to manage the cooking. She lived deep in the Western Mountains. The woman recounted that in her village, there was a man who, out of extreme poverty, had abandoned his home in search of food. Having never ventured outside before, he became lost after walking for half a day. The rocky path was treacherous, and the overcast sky made it difficult to see. Not knowing which way to go, he sat under a withered tree, waiting for daylight to determine the directions. Suddenly, a person emerged from the forest, followed by two or three others. They were fearsome and tall, unlike ordinary people. He realized they were either mountain spirits or demons and understood that hiding was not possible. He prostrated himself in worship, crying out his suffering. The person, moved with compassion, said, "Do not be afraid, I will not harm you. I am a divine tiger. I am here to provide food for the tigers. When the tigers eat people, you take their clothes, and you will survive." He led the man to a place, shouted loudly, and the tigers gathered. The person raised his hand to give orders, but the language was incomprehensible. Soon, they all dispersed, leaving only one tiger hidden in the dense undergrowth. Soon after, someone carrying a load passed through the forest, and the tiger leaped to attack, but suddenly avoided and retreated. A short while later, a woman appeared, and the tiger ate her. The man found some money in her clothes, took it, and gave it to the person, who then told him, "Tigers do not eat people; they only eat animals. Those who eat people are people who have become like beasts."

Generally, those whose inherent goodness has not been completely extinguished will have a divine light above their heads, and tigers will avoid them; whereas those whose inherent goodness has been completely extinguished will have their divine light disappear entirely, and they will be no different from beasts, allowing tigers to prey upon them. A man who was once cruel and lawless, yet still took care of his widowed aunt and orphaned niece, ensuring they did not suffer from hunger and cold, due to this one act of kindness, his divine light shone brightly like a ball, so the tiger did not dare to eat him. A woman, however, abandoned her husband and eloped with another man, mistreating the children of her former husband to the point where she left no part of her body intact. She also stole money from her new husband to give to the daughter of her former husband, which she was carrying in her arms. Because of these various evils, her divine light completely disappeared, and the tiger no longer saw her as human, so it ate her. Now that she has met me, she also treats her stepmother well, giving up her own food to feed her, and there is a divine light about a foot high above her head, so I can guide her—not because she kneels and begs for mercy. Strive to cultivate good deeds, and you will still have future good fortune. I then pointed out the way back, and after one day and night, she finally reached home. The father of the old woman Zhang was related to this person, so he knew the details. At that time, a servant's wife who mistreated her seven-year-old orphaned nephew heard what the old woman Zhang said and somewhat restrained herself. The sages use the divine way to teach, and there is indeed a reason for this.

Phosphorus is the cause of ghost fire. The *Book of Miscellaneous Records* claims it is formed from battlefield blood, which is incorrect. How could there be battlefield blood everywhere? Ghosts are the residual energy of deceased humans. Ghosts belong to the yin, while residual energy belongs to the yang. When yang energy is blocked by yin, it condenses into light. Just as rain, which is extremely yin, can turn into fireflies, and sea mist, also extremely yin, can ignite into ghostly flames. Ghost fire is often seen in autumn and winter but hidden in spring and summer, because the qi (vital energy) condenses in colder months and disperses in warmer ones. If it appears in spring and summer, it is not in abandoned houses or dilapidated buildings, but rather in deep valleys and secluded ravines, where yin energy is always concentrated. It is often found in open fields, wetlands, and marshes, where yang energy is trapped within yin environments, such as the earth and water, which are both yin in nature. My brother Qinghu once encountered this phenomenon with his friend Shen Fenggong during a night journey, when phosphorus fire appeared on a high mountain ridge, glowing green like a torch, a sight never before witnessed. Li He, the Tang Dynasty poet, wrote in his poem: "Old trees turned into wood spirits, and their laughter rises from the nests of blue fire." I suspect he may have witnessed this strange phenomenon, which inspired his poetic imagery. Perhaps my brother's experience was caused by wood spirits.

A merchant offered a large inkstone for sale, its color a pure green with red spots like blood. When tested, it was slippery and did not accept ink. On the back, a long poem was engraved, saying: "The First Emperor, in his fury, lashes the stubborn stone; the bloodstains on the stone are as red as rouge. Through many changes of time, worn by water and sand, it remains a full foot in size. Falling petals stick to the fallen red, the soft grass looks tender and green. The sea people filter out silver waves, the merman laments, and the dragon maiden is moved. Why is it forced to serve as an inkstone? As if using a beautiful woman to serve as a mirror. Its smooth fat is not suitable for grinding, it becomes a meat press and is discarded." A guest asked about the meat press, and the judge replied: "It is mentioned in *Dream Brook Essays*." Its sound is hard to appreciate, as ancient people lamented; who is to blame for using it without considering its abilities? The jade toad on the desk of Mi Fu should shed tears for you. Later, on the ninth day of the ninth month in the year 1719, the Flower-Eating Taoist descended from a spirit medium. He occasionally asked to inscribe on the stubborn inkstone, immediately wrote a long poem, and engraved it on the back of the inkstone to record the strange event. The inscription is signed "Yi Dian," but his surname is not mentioned, and it is unknown who he is. The Flower-Eating Taoist is also unknown. His words are filled with grief and depression, not like the language of a deity. It is suspected to be a wandering talented ghost. The merchant asked ten gold coins for it, but was paid only four and refused to sell. When asked again later, it was said that a county magistrate from Sichuan had bought it.

A servant named Ji Chang, originally surnamed Wei, adopted the master's surname following the story of Huang Douzi. From a young age, he enjoyed reading and became quite proficient in literary arts and calligraphy. He was exceptionally shrewd and never missed an opportunity to gain an advantage. Later in life, he contracted a rare and severe illness that left him blind, deaf, mute, and paralyzed. His entire body became paralyzed, and he felt no pain or sensation. He lay on his back on a bed, appearing as lifeless as a piece of wood or stone, but his breathing continued. It was known that he was still alive, and food was placed in his mouth at regular intervals, which he could still swallow. Medical examination revealed that his pulse was steady, and there were no visible symptoms of illness, leaving even famous doctors helpless. He lived like this for several years before passing away. The monk Guo Cheng remarked, "This illness causes the body to die while the mind remains alive, a condition not recorded in ancient medical texts. Could it be a karmic retribution?" However, this servant had not committed great evils; he was merely self-interested and always had a plan. The monk concluded, "Being too clever can be a curse, as it is often met with divine retribution." The wife of the servant Li Fu was exceptionally fierce and rebellious. She constantly defied her in-laws, cursing them openly and secretly. When warned about the consequences of filial impiety, she dismissed the warnings, claiming, "I observe the Guanyin fast and chant the Guanyin mantra. The Bodhisattva Guanyin has the power to erase my sins. What can the King of Hell do to me?" Later, she fell ill with a severe disease, enduring countless torments, but she still blamed it on minor infractions in her religious practices, saying, "This is because I did not rinse my mouth after chanting and used kitchen fire for my incense. It is not due to any other reason." How foolish she was!

Governor Cai Baisheng, who once presided over matters in the underworld, was asked by Zhongcheng Official Zhu Shijun about the benefits of Buddhist repentance. Cai replied: "Ordinary grievances, Buddhism can place litigants in a good place, if they get what they want, their grievances will naturally resolve. It is like the concept of mediation in human society; but for grave offenses and deep-seated grudges, which cannot be reconciled in human society, they are not something that can be repented of through Buddhist practices, even Shakyamuni Buddha has no power to deal with them." These words are simple and close to reason. Confucian scholars say that Buddhism is definitely nonexistent, while Buddhists say that all kinds of sins can be eliminated, both are wrong. My family lives only a hundred miles from the sea, therefore, the ancient name for Hejian was Yingzhou. The terrain slopes towards the east, gradually becoming higher, so the coastline is extremely steep, the tide cannot go out, and water cannot enter. All nine rivers are in Hejian, and the Great Yu did not directly lead the rivers into the sea, but led them north for hundreds of miles, and they entered the sea at Jieshi. For this reason, in the sea, every few years or several decades, from afar, one can see water and clouds in a shimmering void, with red light illuminating the sky, it is called burning sea. There are often broken rafters and beams that follow the tide up, people pick them up as firewood. After a few days, people will often say that certain craftsmen were summoned by the gods to build the dragon palace, however, no one has personally seen someone describe the appearance of the dragon palace, it is only hearsay. I believe it is likely that large ships at sea caught fire, and the water's reflection illuminated the sky, with no obstacles in the air, so it can be seen from thousands of miles away. Beams and columns are found on ships, and they are not necessarily palace materials.

A certain constable from Xian County, once tasked with capturing a notorious bandit, had already apprehended him. The bandit's wife was beautiful, and the bandit begged to let her serve as a concubine before releasing him to escape. The constable refused, and later, due to accumulated corruption and embezzlement, he was sentenced to death. Two days before his execution, the prison wall collapsed, crushing him to death. The prison official Ye, who was responsible for maintaining the prison, was punished with severe flogging for not repairing the wall in time. Prior to this, Ye had a dream where he stood in the courtyard and heard the officials discussing the constable's case. One official commanded, "One good deed cannot cover a thousand evils, and a thousand evils cannot cover one good deed. Death cannot be avoided, but a reduced sentence is possible." Soon after, an official emerged with documents in hand, but Ye did not recognize him, nor could he identify the official. Realizing he was not in the county office, he woke up and secretly congratulated the constable, believing he would receive a reduced sentence. Unbeknownst to him, the gods had spared his head, allowing him to retain his life. When considering the constable's life, his only good deed had spared him from execution. The ways of heaven are clear; it does not deny people the chance to reform in their later years. In Wujia, Wu Lintang recounted that a relative of his encountered a fox spirit. Though the man was not ill, he often appeared listless and spiritless, causing his parents great concern. Hearing of a wandering monk who could exorcise such spirits, they sought his help. The monk said, "This spirit has a predestined connection with the young man and does not intend to harm him. The young man is simply indulging too much. However, I fear the spirit will not harm him, but the young man may harm himself. He should be sent away properly." That night, the monk went to the man's home, sat cross-legged, and chanted Buddhist mantras. The family, from afar, saw what appeared to be a woman in embroidered robes bowing repeatedly in the candlelight. The monk raised his fly whisk and said, "Leave your unfinished karmic ties for a joy in the next life. Is that not acceptable?" Suddenly, the woman vanished, and the spirit was never seen again.

Lin Tang recognized him as an extraordinary person and asked him about matters of meeting with immortals. The monk replied: "In ancient texts, there are those who use allegory, those who use pseudonyms, those who express grievances, those who enjoy talking about strange and fantastic tales, those who embellish tales of elegance and romance, making them into beautiful stories, and those who have no real basis but express their feelings through beautiful language, like poets who write love poems. Mostly, they are false (eighty-nine out of a hundred), and only a few are true (eleven out of a hundred). Among these few true ones, most are talented ghosts, fox spirits, flower fairies, and tree demons, and not a single one is an immortal. Those who claim to be immortals must use deceptive words. Immortals are upright and wise, while gods are serene and tranquil. How could there be immortals listed on the red platform, residing in the purple palace, with promiscuous women and idle maidens mixed among them, constantly engaging in licentious meetings? Lin Tang admired his profound insight, which was unheard of in ancient times. When he was telling this story, Lin Tang did not mention his name. Later, he asked Lin Tang's son Zhong Qiao, who said: 'When I saw this monk, I was only five or six years old, and at that time, I did not hear his name mentioned. Now there is no one to ask anymore. I only remember his voice, which sounded like that of a person from Hangzhou.'

A Divination Session at Li Shaoting's Home During a divination session at Li Shaoting's home, the spirit identified itself as Qiu Chuji. The spirit wrote rapidly, its strokes as swift as wind and rain, and the handwriting resembled the wild cursive script of the famous calligraphers Zhang Xu and Huaisu. A guest bowed and requested a formula for the elixir of immortality. The spirit responded: "Immortals possess the secrets of the elixir, not formulas. A formula refers to the alchemical practice of refining metals and minerals. The *Zhenyuan Miaodao Yaolue* speaks of furnaces, tripods, lead, and mercury, all of which are symbolic names, not literal instructions. Alchemists have misinterpreted and exaggerated these texts, leading to endless harm. Metals and minerals are inherently dry and intense, and when heated, they cause excessive heat to surge through the body, causing the blood vessels to swell. Thus, physical strength may seem to increase dramatically, but the true vital energy is depleted, and the hidden dangers are profound. Consider the cultivation of flowers: if they are fertilized with sulfur, they may bloom prematurely in the cold, but after blooming, the tree will wither. When heat rises from below, it causes the essence to surge upward. Once the essence is exhausted, the tree withers immediately. Why indulge in fleeting desires for a few years, risking one's precious life? The guest was deeply moved and rose in alarm. Later, Li Shaoting shared this with Tian Baiyan, who remarked: "Divination spirits often claim to be famous figures. This spirit's words are so wise; perhaps it truly is Qiu Chuji."

A divination session was held at Wu Yunyan's home, and the spirit that appeared claimed to be Qiu Changchun. A guest asked: "Was *Journey to the West* truly written by the immortal master, to illustrate the profound principles of alchemy?" The spirit replied: "Yes." The guest then inquired whether the book was written during the Yuan dynasty, noting that it mentioned Ming dynasty institutions such as the Imperial Bodyguard in the Kingdom of Sacrifice and Competition, the Office of Ceremonial in the Kingdom of Red and Purple, the Eastern City Military Police Bureau in the Kingdom of Annihilation, the Imperial Academy under Emperor Taizong of Tang, and the Secretariat of the Hanlin Academy. The spirit suddenly stopped moving, and further questions were unanswered. It was understood that the spirit had no more answers and retreated. Therefore, *Journey to the West* was likely attributed to a Ming person, without doubt. My aunt from the Wang family of Wen'an, the fifth sister of my late mother, recounted that when she was unmarried, while sitting in the *Duofan* building, she saw a boat docked by the river. Inside was a middle-aged woman from a high-ranking family, leaning against the window and crying, with onlookers gathered around. A wet nurse opened the back door to take a look and reported that the woman was the wife of a certain prefectural governor. She had been napping on the boat when she dreamt that her deceased daughter was being bound and slaughtered, her cries of pain vivid and terrifying. She awoke in a state of shock, and the sound of her cries still echoed in her ears, as if coming from a nearby boat. She sent a maid to investigate and found that a piglet was being slaughtered, its blood draining into a bowl, the process not yet complete. In her dream, she had seen the girl's feet bound with rope and her hands tied with a red sash. Upon checking the piglet's front paws, she found it to be true, and she was overcome with grief, weeping uncontrollably. She then paid double the price to redeem and bury the piglet. Her servants whispered that the girl had died at the age of sixteen. While alive, she was extremely gentle and compliant, but she only liked eating chicken. At every meal, it must be provided; if it was not, she would not lift her chopsticks. Every year, she would have about seven or eight hundred chickens slaughtered. This, they said, was the karma of killing.

A scholar from Jiaohe was walking alone in the fields at dusk when he saw what appeared to be a woman hiding in the millet fields. Suspecting it was a loose woman meeting a secret lover, he went to see it, but found nothing. He suspected she had hidden herself in the deep underbrush and no longer pursued her. Upon returning home, he suffered from a severe fever and began to speak deliriously, saying, "I am a hungry ghost. Because you have a fortune-teller's appearance, I dare not offend you, so I hid myself in the grass. I did not expect you to look at me, and to come to look for me, so now that we have feelings for each other, I will ask you for food. I ask for a small offering, and then I will depart." His family prepared paper money, food, and wine, and he suddenly recovered. The scholar Su told me, "This gentleman had no evil intentions, but because he happened to be doing something, he was taken by this ghost. The unscrupulous always look for opportunities to harm the virtuous, so one must be cautious in words and actions."

The Changes of Fortune Are Fleeting, Even for Ghosts and Spirits Cheng Yumen, the compiler, said: Wang Wen Zhuang Gong, when accompanying the rites at the northern suburbs, would always lodge temporarily in a cemetery outside An Ding Gate. The cemetery was known to be haunted. Wang Wen Zhuang Gong did not see anything. One year, he saw something under the lamp, and half a year later, Wang Wen Zhuang Gong passed away. Can even the mountain ghosts know the course of a year? Shen Tiechan of Taiyuan said: In the past, while traveling north from Suzhou, his ship's rudder was damaged, and he anchored south of Xingji. The shore was desolate, with no one in sight, and at night, he heard someone reciting poetry among the grass. He knew it was a ghost, and together with his friend, they listened closely. The ghost recited dozens of lines, with a faint, intermittent voice that was hard to distinguish. Shen Tiechan only heard one line: "The cold stars shine with sharp rays," while his friend heard two lines: "At midnight, the stone statues speak; under the dark moon, the ghostly carriage comes." Zhang Wanzhi, a secretary, rented a house, and someone said there was a fox in it. On the day he moved in, the pens and inkstones in his study began to move on their own, and he lost a red envelope. As he was asking around, a coin suddenly fell onto the table with a clear, metallic sound, as if compensating for the value of the lost envelope. Soon, someone shouted that the lost envelope was stuck to the wall of an empty room behind the house. Zhang Wanzhi went to see and found four characters written in neat calligraphy on the door of the inner chamber, warning to "Stop here." He said, "This fox is cunning; I fear it may cause trouble in the future." So he moved out. He heard that the house was on Baoganshi Street and suspected it was the residence of Wang Qianxi.

Mr. Li Yudan said that a house in Dongguang was haunted by a fox. One day, the fox suddenly threw bricks and tiles, damaging the pots and jars. The owner of the house cursed the fox, and that night, someone knocked on the window and said: "Are you asleep? I have something to say. Neighbors and fellow townspeople live door-to-door, and it is common for little children to sometimes offend each other. If it can be forgiven, it should be. If it cannot be forgiven, inform their parents or elders, and they will handle it. To suddenly add harsh words is not reasonable. Moreover, we come and go without form, and our movements are unpredictable, things beyond your sight and hearing, and beyond your ability to guard against. If you raise your arms in defiance, what hope do you have? You will certainly not be able to compete. Think carefully about this." The owner of the house got up and apologized, and from then on, they lived in peace. There was an incident where a minor dispute among servants led to a serious fight, nearly resulting in a lawsuit. Mr. Li Yudan commented: "This greatly reminds me of the fox in the household." The governor's residence in Beihé had a five-bay building that had been occupied by bats for many years. The size of the bats was unknown, but there was one large white bat, as big as a carriage wheel, which was the leader. The bats could change into strange forms, and all the governors who had served there had locked the doors and not used the building. Governor Li Qing of Fujian invited Master Zhengyi to exorcise the bats, and they were indeed driven away. Soon after, Governor Li died, and the bats returned. From then on, no one dared to question the bats. I believe that Master Tang Wen Zheng's act of driving away the Wutong gods was to eliminate harm to the people. The bats lived in one building and caused no harm to people, so Governor Li's action was truly something that could have been avoided but was not. As for his sudden death, it was coincidental, and one cannot say that the bats caused his death. Life is determined by fate, how could demons or spirits control one's lifespan?

When I was seven or eight years old, I saw a servant named Zhao Ping, who was overly confident in his courage. An old servant named Shi Xiang shook his hand and said, "Do not rely on your courage; I have already been defeated by it." In my youth, I was very bold. I heard that there was a haunted house in a certain family where no one dared to live, so I took bedding and lay down inside it. By midnight, there was a sudden, loud noise. The ceiling split open, and suddenly a human arm fell down, jumping and throwing itself continuously. Soon another arm fell, followed by two feet, then the body, and finally the head. All over the room, it leaped wildly like monkeys. I was startled and did not know what to do. Soon it had combined into a single person, with knife marks and stick marks, bloody and profuse. It raised its hand and came directly to grab my neck. Fortunately, it was a summer night and I was cooling off, with the window not closed. I quickly jumped out of the window and ran wildly, escaping. From then on, my heart and courage were shattered, and I still dare not sleep alone up to now. You continue to rely on your courage, will you not inevitably suffer the same fate as me? Zhao Ping did not agree, saying, "Old man, you made a great mistake. Why not catch a part of it first, so that it cannot form a shape?" Later that night, when I returned home drunk, I was indeed blocked by many ghosts, pushed into a sewage pit, and almost drowned.

"Zhong Shangting, my fellow scholar, told me that while I was serving as an official in Ningde, one of my assistants was seriously ill. He was taking medicine when he suddenly saw two ghosts. The ghosts said, 'There is a specific case in the underworld waiting for you to be judged. You do not need to take your medicine.' My assistant asked, 'This has already been fifty years. Why has it not yet been resolved?' The ghost replied, 'The laws of the underworld are extremely strict, but they are also applied with great caution. If there is any doubt, even if the matter is known, without sufficient evidence, the case will not be concluded. Therefore, it often waits for several decades.' My assistant then asked, 'Does this not cause unnecessary delay?' The ghost said, 'Such cases are rare.' That night, he indeed died. Thus, it seems that karmic retribution sometimes does not manifest immediately, perhaps due to such circumstances? In novels, there are often stories of living souls being summoned to appear in the underworld. The timing of these summonses may vary based on the severity and urgency of the case. Ultimately, whether early or late, the divine order is always clear and reliable, and this is indeed credible."

A woman from the Tian family falsely claimed that her family was associated with a fox spirit. Many women would burn incense to ask about auspicious or inauspicious signs, and she profited from this. Soon, a group of fox spirits gathered, demanding food and drink. Their offerings were exhausted but still insufficient, and they smashed jars and pots, burned clothes, and the family pleaded in despair but could not be dismissed. Frightened, they fled to another place. As they were about to leave, they heard laughter from the roof, and a voice said, "Will you dare to pretend and collect money again?" After that, the fox spirits were silent, and the family did not move. However, much of their previous wealth was depleted. This is what I heard my grandmother say when I was young. There was also a Taoist priest who claimed to serve the Lord of the Gods. When throwing coins to predict events, it sometimes proved accurate, and prayers were also popular. He happened to dislike a few people and blocked them from bringing a prostitute into the temple. He secretly asked an actor to dress up as a spirit attendant of the Lord of the Gods. During a night ritual, he suddenly jumped down from the roof, sat down, and scolded them for deceiving the people, ordering the spirit attendants to bind them and holding an iron staff to interrogate them. The Taoist priest was frightened and confessed, fully admitting their deceitful practice of collecting money. Everyone laughed loudly. He removed his robe, sang loudly, and left. The next day, when people looked for the Taoist priest, he had already disappeared. This was the event in the seventh month of the year Jia Yin during the Yongzheng era. I was staying at Shahe Bridge with my late grandfather, the Governor of Yaoan, and heard the host of the inn say.

A scholar from An County named Song Bantang, who once served as an official in Yinzhou, recounted that there was a man from Yinzhou who was skilled in writing but arrogant and failed the imperial exams. While ill, he dreamed of reaching a grand government office and, upon examining his appearance, realized he was in the underworld. He met an official who was an old acquaintance and asked if this illness would lead to his death. The official replied, "Your life is not yet over, but your official salary is exhausted, and you will not be long before you come here." The man explained that throughout his life, he made a living by teaching and did not engage in excessive waste, so why did his official salary exhaust first? The official sighed and said, "Exactly because you received payment for teaching but lacked diligence in teaching, the underworld said that you ate without earning it, and then belongs to wasted resources. They canceled out the salary you should have received and compensated for what you had already received, therefore, your life is not yet over, but your official salary is exhausted." It is said that the duty of a son to his parents, a subject to his ruler, and a wife to her husband, status and duties should be respected. To benefit others through teaching and mislead students, condemnation is also the heaviest. Those with official salaries will have their official salaries reduced, and those without official salaries will have their food allowances reduced. Even the smallest amounts are calculated without error. The world merely sees talented scholars who are either poor or short-lived, and often says that the will of heaven is difficult to understand. How can one know that self-inflicted sins throughout one's life are often due to this? The man was disappointed and awakened, and his illness indeed did not recover. On his deathbed, he mentioned it to warn his close relatives. Old acquaintances heard about the matter and said.

The Daoist Pang Douchu, from Xiong County, was once a guest at Gao Honglu's home in Xian County. When Yao An was young, he saw Pang Douchu pick up chess pieces and arrange them on the table, where they crisscrossed and intertwined, not very distinguishable in the middle, but neatly arranged and countable as eight gates on the outside. When a small mouse was thrown into the formation, entering through the gate of life, it twisted and turned to find a gap and came out; entering through the gate of death, it circled all day, unable to come out. Thus, Yao An believed the fish belly formation diagram, definitely not an empty talk. However, Pang Douchu said this was just a play. As for the rise and fall of a nation, it depends on fate; the victory or defeat of an army, it lies in human planning. All divination and calculation are useless. From ancient times to the present, there are those who have accomplished things using the method of Ren Dun and the stars and birds, such as talismans and spells, the world has many such methods, and they also have times when they are effective. However, for thousands of years, during times of war and division, is there no record of such things? Nor have I heard of any emperor, general, or chancellor dying due to the curses of enemy states. Others can be inferred similarly. Yao An said: These words are not something a diviner can say, and this principle is not something a diviner can know.

My maternal uncle, Mr. An, told me the following story: There was a tenant farmer named Liu Ziming who was moderately prosperous. A fox lived in his granary for many years without causing any trouble. The fox would only be disturbed during seasonal festivals when Liu would offer it five cups of wine and several eggs. Whenever there was a fire or a thief, the fox would knock on the doors and windows to alert Liu. They had been living in harmony for a long time. One day, Liu suddenly heard the fox laughing continuously. He asked the fox why it was laughing, but the fox did not answer and laughed even more. Angered, Liu scolded the fox, who then replied: "I am laughing at how I have formed a close bond with my fellow foxes, yet I despise my own brothers. I am also laughing at how I treat my wife's previous husband's children well, yet I despise my own stepchildren. What business is this of yours, and why do you get so angry?" Liu was deeply ashamed and had no response. Soon after, the fox began to recite the Analects of Confucius: "Words of strict advice, can one not follow them? But it is better to put them into practice. Gentle words, can one not be pleased by them? But it is better to ponder and understand them." After sighing several times, the fox fell silent. From then on, Liu began to change his ways. Later, I told this story to Shao Angu, who said: "What the fox said is something even close friends would find difficult to express, and the fox managed to say it. The fox's humorous approach is more effective than straightforward advice in conveying a message. Dongfang Shuo could not have done better. If you go to Liu's granary, you should bow three times at the door."

In the region of Manas, there was a woman who had been exiled and was sent to a penal colony. She went into the mountains to gather firewood when she was suddenly captured by the Mahaqin — the Mahaqin being nomads from the Oirat tribe, without a leader or a clan, moving in groups of dozens or a few individuals, appearing and disappearing in the deep mountains, eating whatever they encountered, whether it was birds, beasts, or humans. The woman was seized and stripped of her clothes, tied to a tree, with a fire lit beside her. Just as they were about to cut off a piece of flesh from her left thigh, a sudden sound of gunfire echoed, and loud voices of people filled the air, accompanied by the sound of horse hooves shaking the forest and valleys. Thinking that official soldiers had arrived, the Mahaqin abandoned their prey and fled. It turned out that the soldiers were merely tending horses, accidentally shooting a young pheasant with a bird gun, which mistakenly hit the tail of a horse. One horse jumped, causing all the horses to be startled, and they followed the lead horse into the mountains, shouting and chasing after them. If they had delayed even a moment, this woman would have been reduced to a bloody mess. Was this not as if someone had made it happen? From then on, the woman maintained a long-term vegetarian diet and once told others: "I am not worshipping Buddha for blessings. The greatest suffering in the world is being cut into pieces, and the greatest terror is being bound and waiting to be cut into pieces. Every time I see animals being slaughtered, I always remember the times when I was suffering, and I think about how other beings must also suffer and be terrified. So I can't bring myself to eat meat." These words can also be spoken to those who feast on meat.

A servant named Liu Qi raised a cow and a dog. Whenever the cow saw the dog, it would charge, and whenever the dog saw the cow, it would bite. Their fights often resulted in continuous bleeding, but the cow only charged this particular dog and did not attack other dogs; similarly, the dog only bit this cow and did not attack other cows. Later, they were tied to separate places. If the cow heard the dog's barking or the dog heard the cow's lowing, both would raise their heads and stare intently. After the former Governor of Yao'an served in the Ministry of Revenue, I followed him to the capital, and I do not know what happened to these two animals afterward. Some say that animals, which cannot speak, can remember their past lives. This cow and this dog must be the old grudge mentioned in Buddhist scriptures, still recognizing each other? I believe that the concept of old grudges is certainly true, but the idea that they can remember their past lives seems unlikely. Among my relatives, there are sisters-in-law who dislike each other. The sister-in-law gets along well with all the younger sisters-in-law except this one, whom she treats like an enemy; similarly, this younger sister-in-law gets along well with all the sisters-in-law except this one, whom she treats like an enemy. Can this be because they remember their past lives? Hatred and resentment stem from one's nature. When they meet again, it is like medicines that counteract each other, even if they are dry roots and rotten grass, inherently lifeless, their nature still causes them to fight. The entanglement of cause and effect means that every action is reciprocated. Three lifetimes are as fleeting as a moment; can one take pleasure in minor grievances?

From what Master Zhang, the cousin of my father, said, there was a man named Zhang Gong who lived during the Ming and Qing dynasties. He was the father-in-law of my tenth-generation ancestor, Zan Qi Gong. Once, he agreed with the people of his hometown to sue a county official collectively, riding a horse to the county seat. As they passed by his ancestral tomb, a whirlwind struck the horse's head, causing it to fall. Those accompanying him carried him back home, where he suddenly developed chills and fever, becoming confused and waking up intermittently. In a daze, he seemed to see ghostly figures and was about to invoke a shaman to ward off evil spirits. Suddenly, he sat up and spoke in the voice of his deceased father: "You suddenly prayed, and the one who struck your horse was me. All lawsuits are futile. If you are in the wrong, how can you prove it? If you are right, the public opinion is already clear, and everyone will feel sorry for you—this is a victory. Why sue? Moreover, the officials and clerks involved in lawsuits cause even greater harm. If you lose, the trouble is immediate; if you win, officials come and go, and their descendants will surely seek revenge in the future. This is a problem for later days. This is why I stopped you from going." After speaking, he lay back down and broke out in a sweat. Upon waking up, he felt completely recovered. Later, all those who had joined the lawsuit failed, and only then did he believe it was not just delirious speech. This story was told to my great-grandfather, Master Zhan Yuan, who never got involved in lawsuits with others, because he adhered to this warning.

There is a divination technique known as the "circle of light," where a piece of white paper is hung on a wall, and talismans are burned to summon spirits. A child of five or six years old is then asked to look at the paper, and the child will invariably see a large circular mirror appear on it. The figures in the mirror are distinctly visible, revealing future events, much like the shadows in divination. However, while the shadows in divination hint at the image, the circle of light clearly shows the form. Pang Douxu was skilled in this technique. A certain scholar had a close relationship with Pang Douxu and once harbored feelings for a woman. He secretly asked Pang Douxu to perform the circle of light divination to see if his feelings would be reciprocated. Pang Douxu was shocked and said, "How can such a matter be taken lightly with the spirits? But he insisted. Reluctantly, Pang Douxu performed the ritual. The child stared for a long time and said, "I saw a pavilion, with a couch set inside. A woman named San Niangzi and a young man were sitting on it." San Niangzi was the deceased concubine of the scholar. As he was scolding the child for speaking nonsense, Pang Douxu burst out laughing and said, "I saw it too. There was also a sign on the pavilion, but the child did not recognize the characters." The scholar angrily asked what the characters said, and the child replied, "The four characters 'What you do not want done to yourself, do not do to others.'" The scholar fell silent and left without a word. Some said that what Pang Douxu burned was not actually a talisman, but he had first lured the child with food and taught him to say those words. This seems to be close to the truth. Although it was a cruel joke, it did not lose the meaning of a friend advising and correcting another.

My late mother once recounted that during her time, our family often saw a strange figure at night, dancing in front of the building. When people approached, it would flee. Under the moonlight, we peeked through the window crack and saw it wearing a somber green robe, moving clumsily like a giant turtle. We could see its hands and feet but not its head, leaving us puzzled as to what kind of apparition it was. My great-uncle, the Purple Balance Master, sent several strong servants, armed with knives, sticks, and ropes, to lie in wait outside the door. They waited for it to emerge and then suddenly pounced. The figure stumbled and fled into the stairwell. We held a torch to investigate and found a green brocade cloth wrapped around a silver boat in the corner of the wall, with four wheels on either side. It turned out to be a toy used by children in the heyday of our great-grandparents' family. We then realized that the green robe was the cloth and the hands and feet were the four wheels. When melted, it yielded more than thirty taels of gold. An old woman said, "When I was a servant, this object was lost in the room, and my peers were severely punished. We don't know who stole it and placed it here, turning it into a spirit." According to the *Search for the Supernatural*, Confucius said, "All creatures, including domestic animals, turtles, snakes, fish, and plants, can become spirits or monsters, hence they are called the Five You. Each of the five directions has its own creature. You means old, so old things become strange." Kill it, and there is no need to worry. Thus, objects that are old and transform into strange forms are a common occurrence in the natural order.

There are indeed cases of couples reincarnated like Wei Gao and Yu Xiao. Jingzhou's Li Xiyi recounted that during the imperial examination held in the year of Yi-Chou, he encountered a scholar from Guizhou who described a child born in a commoner's family. The child, just able to speak, immediately said, "I was the daughter of a certain family in my previous life, and the wife of another. My husband's name was so-and-so, his courtesy name was so-and-so. When I died, he was so many years old, and this year he should be so many years old. The place where he lives is only about four or five days' journey from our home." This statement gradually became known, and by the time the child was around fourteen or fifteen years old, his former husband learned of this claim and came directly to inquire. They met and wept, and the details of their previous life matched perfectly. That night, they ended up sharing a bed together. His mother could not stop them. Doubting, she listened secretly and found that after the candles were extinguished, they were already talking in the manner of lovers. The mother was angry and drove away her former husband. This son became depressed and refused to eat. His former husband also lingered in an inn and refused to leave. One day, when precautions were slightly lax, they managed to escape together. No one knew what became of them. How strange this is, it has never been heard of in ancient times. This is what is called emotion arising but not stopping at propriety.

East Guang Huo Congzhan reported: A girl from a wealthy household, aged five or six, was kidnapped and sold while out at night to watch a play. After five or six years, the kidnappers were caught, and during their confession, they admitted to drugging the girl. Officials were summoned to inquire, and she was finally allowed to return home. Upon her return, her skin was covered with marks from whips, sticks, scissors, needles, branding irons, burns, claws, and teeth, as if carved into her flesh. Her mother held her and wept for several days. Whenever the story was mentioned, tears would stain her sleeves. The girl herself had said that her mistress was cruel and inhumane, and as a child, she did not know what to do, trembling in fear and awaiting death. As she grew older, she could not bear the suffering. She once considered suicide, but in a dream, an old man told her, "Do not act rashly. After being branded twice and whipped a hundred times, your karmic retribution will be complete." Indeed, one day she was tied to a tree and whipped, but just as the hundred lashes were about to be completed, county officials arrived with official documents. Her mother was extremely cruel to her maidservants; those who trembled in fear while standing by rarely escaped without bloodstains. When she turned her gaze, no one dared to meet her eyes. Thus, the gods showed retribution through her daughter, but she did not repent. Later, she developed an abscess on her neck and died. Her descendants are now in decline. Huo Congzhan also said that a woman from an official family, when her maid made a mistake, did not whip or beat her, but instead stripped her of her clothes and made her lie on the ground naked, claiming it was like using a reed cane to show disgrace. The maid later developed epilepsy and, whenever she was left unsupervised, would dance naked.

According to Master Ru Ai's account, his servant returned from a village where he had been drinking, fell asleep on the road, and upon waking found his clothes damp with dew and the moon high in the sky. In the moment of stretching and yawning, he saw a man shivering and standing behind a tree. He called out to ask who he was, and the man replied, "Do not be afraid, I am a ghost. The ghosts around here like to tease drunkards, so I have come to guard you." The servant asked why he would protect him, despite having no prior acquaintance. The ghost said, "Have you forgotten? After my death, someone slandered my wife, and you spoke up to clear her name of the false accusations. Therefore, I am grateful from the depths of the afterlife." With that, the ghost vanished. The servant did not get a chance to ask who he was, nor did he remember having done such a thing himself. It seems that even an offhand remark can be heard in the afterlife. Then, how much more so for those who deliberately spread false words, would they not clench their fists and gnash their teeth in the unseen world?

The Tomb of the Contributing King of Hejian is located eight li east of Xianxian County. In front of the tomb is a shrine, and in front of the shrine stand two cypress trees, said to be from the Han dynasty, though their exact origin is uncertain, and they may have been planted by later generations. To the left and right of the tomb are two accompanying tombs, which the county records identify as belonging to Mao Chang on the left and Guan Changqing on the right. However, there is also a tomb of Mao Chang in Renqiu, whose identity is equally unclear. Some say that Mao Chang was posthumously honored as the Marquis of LeShou during the Song dynasty, and since Xianxian is the ancient land of LeShou, the tomb of Mao Gong in Renqiu might actually belong to Mao Heng, which seems plausible. My maternal uncle, An Gong Wuzhan, recounted that during the Kangxi era, a group of bandits coveted the jade fish buried in the tomb, so they planted melons in front of the tomb, secretly excavated the soil, and dug tunnels underground. As they approached the tomb, they probed with a long needle, and a white vapor shot out along with the needle, making a sound like thunder, which knocked all the bandits to the ground, causing them to flee without daring to dig further. Some argue that the tomb had been sealed for two thousand years, and the earth's energy had been pent up for a long time, so it erupted when the bandits disturbed it, without the need for any supernatural intervention. I believe that the King's contributions to the Six Classics deserve the protection of spirits and deities. Many people have dug into ancient tombs, but why do the earth's energies not erupt in other places after such a long time?

Ghosts and spirits speak from within a person's body; I have personally witnessed or heard of three such cases. The first case involves Li Yishan, a compiler from Yunnan, who, during a fújī ritual, engaged in reciprocal singing with a fox spirit. Several sisters of the fox spirit then entered his body, speaking with him from time to time. Despite the efforts of the Taoist deity 正一真人 to expel them, they could not be driven away, and he suffered from epilepsy for life. I witnessed this while serving in the Hanlin Academy. The second case is about Zhang Wenhao, a friend from Wuanping, who, while serving as an official in Nanruiguang, shared a room with a secretary named Shi at an inn. A guest came to visit Shi, and they conversed all night. By morning, the guest and his servant had disappeared, and suddenly, words were heard coming from Shi's abdomen. After worshipping the Big Dipper and attempting to drive the spirit away, it temporarily left but soon returned to his body, remaining there until Shi's death. It is suspected that this was an ancient grudge, as told by the high-ranking official Jin Tingtao. The third case involves a nun from Pinghu, who had a ghost in her abdomen that spoke of good and bad omens, often proving accurate. Donations poured in, and the ghost claimed to owe the nun money from a previous life, using this as repayment. This is similar to the story of Tián Bù recorded in *Beimeng Suoyan*. People listened closely to the sound coming from under the nun's armpit and also heard her speak. It is suspected that this was a 樟柳神. This was told by the high-ranking official Shen Yunjiao.

The State of Jin killed a spy from the State of Qin, and after six days, he revived. Perhaps this was due to hanging or beating, which is why he could be revived. But it is unknown what state he was in before he revived. Interpreting the classics has its form; it cannot be like trivial notes in novels. Tenant farmer Zhang Tianxi once died for seven days. His mother heard knocking sounds from the coffin, opened it, and found him revived. She asked him what he had seen after his death. He replied, "I saw nothing, did not know I had been dead for seven days, but suddenly felt as if I had fallen asleep and then woken up." At that time, an elderly scholar staying at my family's residence heard of this and clapped his thigh and jumped with joy, saying, "The saints of Cheng and Zhu!" Matters concerning ghosts and spirits, Confucius and Mencius were still hesitant to deny their existence. Only these two masters dared to make a definitive judgment. If it is truly as they said, it is not something that only saints can do. I think Tianxi was in a state of qi stagnation and coma, unconscious and disoriented. His family mistakenly thought he was dead, but he was not truly dead. The story of the Duke of Guo's son is recorded in the *Records of the Grand Historian*; this old man has not seen it, has he?

Rulers use punishment and reward to encourage good behavior, sages use praise and criticism to encourage good behavior, and when punishment and reward fall short, and when praise and criticism fail to reach, then Buddhism uses cause and effect to encourage good behavior. The methods are different, but the intent is the same. Buddhist monks use the doctrine of sin and merit to coerce simple people, not distinguishing between good and evil based on moral character, but distinguishing between good and evil based on whether one gives alms or not. The idea of "fields of merit" becomes prevalent, and the original intent of the Buddha is obscured. There is someone who travels with the Messenger of Death and asks the underworld official about the benefits of the Blood Bowl Repentance Sutra. The underworld official says: "There is no such thing." When men and women come together, all things are born. It is the natural qi of heaven and earth, the ceaseless mechanism of yin and yang. Birth must lead to reproduction, and reproduction must result in impurity. Even virtuous and noble mothers cannot avoid this. It is not a self-inflicted sin. If it is considered a sin, then eating and drinking cannot be done without excretion, and the mouth and nose cannot avoid spitting and sneezing. These are also impurities, and should they also be considered sins? Those who say this, because the easiest to deceive are women, and what women cannot avoid is childbirth. To consider this a sin, and to insist that this sin must be repented, and the wealth of the inner chambers is all used for meritorious deeds. You who come and go in the underworld should have seen and heard. Where is the blood pool indeed? Who indeed falls into the blood pool? Yet you still doubt and ask? After the Messenger of Death, he told others, but no one believed his words. The weight has accumulated and cannot be returned, and this is what it means.

A Commentary by Ming Yu In the West Mountain, there was a monk who, while walking in the countryside during spring, suddenly had a thought. Just as he was standing idly and thinking deeply, a young woman suddenly made eye contact with him. They gradually began to speak softly, and she said her home was not far from there. Her husband had been out for a long time, and tonight she would light a lamp outside the forest to guide him. After giving him instructions, they said goodbye. The monk went as scheduled and indeed saw a flickering light, no more than half a li away. He followed it through the forest and across the stream, but could not catch up. Soon after, the light appeared and disappeared, sometimes to the left and sometimes to the right. He rushed and turned, and the path became lost. Tired and unable to walk, he fell and lay under an old tree. When dawn came and he carefully observed, he was still in the original place. He went back to the forest, where he saw moss and green grass, with overlapping footprints. Then he realized he had been circling this tree all night, like a cow circling a millstone. Knowing that his heart's desire had created a demon, he quickly went to his master to confess, and afterward there was no other trouble. Another story is about a monk from Shandong who often saw a beautiful woman looking down from the scripture hall. He knew in his heart it was a demon, but thought the demon was quite good. He went straight to meet her, but saw nothing. He called but did not come out. Like this for over a hundred times, he finally became confused and fell ill with a heart disease, until he died. On the verge of death, he confessed. This might be a karmic debt from past lives, to take revenge on him. But both monks ultimately failed themselves, not defeated by demons or spirits.

Fuwai Shu said: A doctor named Wuyi, who was usually cautious and kind, had an unusual experience one night. An elderly woman brought a pair of gold bracelets to buy an abortion pill. The doctor was greatly alarmed and firmly refused her. The next night, the woman brought two more pearl flowers, and the doctor was even more frightened, strongly dismissing her.
