Half a year later, the doctor had a dream in which he was taken by officials of the underworld. They told him that someone was accusing him of killing someone. When they arrived, a woman with unkempt hair, wearing a red scarf around her neck, wept and told him that she had tried to buy the pill but was refused. The doctor said, "Medicine is meant to save lives, not to kill for profit. Your own misdeeds have nothing to do with me." The woman replied, "When I tried to buy the pill, my fetus was not yet formed. If I had gotten the pill, I could have avoided giving birth, thus saving my life. This would have broken an unaware mass of blood, but saved a life that was about to end. Since I could not get the pill, I had to give birth, which led to my child being strangled and suffering greatly. I was also forced to hang myself. So, you tried to save one life but ended up harming two." The underworld official sighed and said, "What you say considers the circumstances, but what he adheres to is reason. Since the Song Dynasty, people have been rigidly adhered to one principle without considering the practical consequences, and this is not unique to this person alone. You may go now." The doctor heard a sound as he touched the table and suddenly became alert and understood.

惠叔又言，有疫死还魂者，在冥司遇其故人，褴褛荷校，相见悲喜，不觉握手太息曰：君一生富贵，竟不能带至此耶？其人蹙然曰：富贵皆可带至此，但人不肯带尔。生前有功德者，至此何尝不富贵耶？寄语世人早作带来计可也。李南涧曰：善哉斯言，胜于谓富贵皆空也。 Huishi spoke again, recounting a story of someone who died of an epidemic and returned to life, only to meet an old acquaintance in the underworld. The acquaintance was in tattered clothing and carrying a yoke, and they met with both sadness and joy. Unconsciously, they held hands and sighed, saying, "With your wealth and status in life, why couldn't you bring them here?" The person responded with a frowning expression, saying, "Wealth and status can indeed be brought here, but people refuse to bring them." Those who have done good deeds in life, here they are not without wealth and status, the person added. "I would advise the world to make plans to bring them early," they said. Li Nanjian commented, "What wise words these are, surpassing the saying that wealth and status are empty."

Yue Wei Grass Hut Notes, Volume Ten, As I Have Heard, Part Four Changshan Nie Songyan said that in Mr. Zhang Maojun's family in Anqiu, there is a book tower that is occupied by a fox. Whenever it converses with people, if there are any hidden things among the maids, servants, and children, it will expose them to the public. The whole family fears it like a god, trembling and not daring to commit any wrongdoing. This is also a speaking rule, an invisible historian. However, if cunning people respect and serve it, it will conceal its faults and not speak frankly. It is clever but lacks integrity. This is why it is a fox. The old nun Dong from Cangzhou's Chahua Temple said that once, in the middle of the night, she woke up and heard the sound of the bell in the Buddha hall, as if someone was worshipping. The next day, she told her disciples, saying: "My teacher is ringing in my ears." At night, it happened again, so she secretly got up and tiptoed to look. The Buddha's light was faintly green, and she could vaguely distinguish objects. She saw the one striking the bell, who was her deceased teacher, and a young woman kneeling before the Buddha, whispering softly, her face turned inward, and she did not recognize who it was. Upon careful listening to her wishes, it was for her husband's illness and seeking blessings. She was terrified and lost her composure, touching something with a red mark, and the dark atmosphere suddenly grew dim, and when the light returned, she could no longer see anything. My late grandfather, Zhang Gong from Xuefeng, said: This young woman has already entered the yellow soil (died), but she still worries about her husband's illness, and hearing this makes one feel more affection for marital love. The old nun said that recently, an old woman selling flowers, at night passing by a certain family's tomb, suddenly saw the spirit of a certain lady standing under a tree, waving her hand to beckon her. With no way to avoid it, she trembled and bowed to pay her respects.

A certain lady said: Every night I have been here, waiting for a familiar person to deliver a letter, on the verge of despair. Now I see you, go back and tell my daughter and son-in-law that all the plots, both gods and spirits have fully known, do not waste your efforts in vain. I am in the underworld, receiving severe punishment. Those who died before me are now being insulted by everyone, having nowhere to hide, only able to hide by this tree every day, enduring bitter rain and cold wind, countless pains. I don't know how many generations have suffered, and been reborn. I hear that the small lord has lost all the wealth he stole, only then can he hope for a way to be reborn. Also, the son-in-law had several secret letters, placed them in a small lacquered box while he was ill, ordered that they be taken out and destroyed to prevent future accusations. Repeatedly warned and sobbed before dying, the old woman secretly told the daughter. The daughter angrily said: 'Are you trying to defend the small lord?' When she opened the box and saw the previous letters, she was then startled. Later, the daughter's family gradually declined. Those among the relatives who knew the matter all joined their palms and said: 'The way for Lady X is near.'

The Commander of Urumqi, General Ba Gongyanbi, said that during his campaign in Yarkand, he dreamed of a place at the foot of a mountain where there were six or seven rows of tents, but no soldiers or guards were seen. Dozens of people were coming and going, many resembling civilian officials. He tried to look around and met a former chief of the imperial guards, whose name was five characters long. The official called out with a rolling tongue sound, but General Ba could not remember it now. They shook hands and exchanged greetings, and he asked the official why he had passed away and what he was doing there. The official replied, "I was appointed as a ghost official due to my lifetime of honesty and simplicity. I am currently recording the names of those who died in battle." General Ba saw several books on the official's table, which were yellow, red, purple, and black. He asked if these books were divided by flags. The official smiled and said, "How can there be purple or black flags? Although there used to be black flags, they were changed to blue because black was hard to distinguish at night. This is just to distinguish the order of A and B."

The rankings and distinctions are as follows: those who are loyal to the country and act bravely without regard for personal safety are recorded in the yellow register; those who strictly follow military orders and do not yield even unto death are recorded in the red register; those who follow the crowd in battle and perish after many battles are recorded in the purple register; those who flee in panic, with no way to survive, and are trampled and killed in the pursuit are recorded in the black register. Asked if it is possible to distinguish between soldiers who die simultaneously, with blood splattering and bodies lying scattered, and to do so without error, the response was that only officials in the underworld can discern such distinctions. Generally, when a person dies, their spirit remains, and their vital energy is still present. Those recorded in the yellow register have a spirit that is like a blazing fire, full of vitality; those recorded in the red register have a spirit that is like a rising smoke, steady and unshaken by the wind; those recorded in the purple register have a spirit that is like lightning in the clouds, flickering and moving. Among these three categories, the highest are the deities, and the lowest still find a favorable path. As for those recorded in the black register, their spirit is weak and lifeless, like dead ashes without flame. In the eyes of the court, they are all equally honored for their loyalty and righteousness, but in the underworld, they are regarded as ordinary ghosts and no longer counted among the worthy. Ba Gong listened attentively, deeply moved and impressed, and was about to question his own future when he was suddenly awakened by the sound of cannon fire. Later, he often told his subordinates, saying: "Whenever I am on the battlefield, I remember these words, and I feel that sacrificing myself in battle is as light as a feather."

The *Night Lamp Collection* records the story of *Guanzǐ* by *Xie Meizhuang*, but it does not know that *Guanzǐ*'s real name is *Lu Zhirén*, because it had not seen *Xie Meizhuang*'s self-written biography of *Guanzǐ*, relying solely on hearsay. During the *Xu Gui Su Tu* period, *Governor Huo* altered a book, and the carriage driver *Wang Er* was involved in a matter similar to *Guanzǐ*'s. Later, he died in the frontier regions, and *Governor Huo* cried in great sorrow. One night, *Governor Huo* suddenly heard a voice from outside the tent say: "The sheep have been stolen; quickly go to the northwest to chase them." He went out to see and indeed it was true. Hearing his voice, it was clearly the spirit of *Wang Er*. *Governor Huo* had a servant who was about to return home. On that day, he witnessed this strange event and therefore unpacked and did not leave, telling his fellow servants: "I fear that the spirit of *Wang Er* in the afterlife will mock people."

A Blind Man from Cangzhou named Mr. Cai, every time passing by the south mountain's pavilion, would immediately be invited by an old man to play and sing, and they would drink together. Gradually, they became familiar with each other, and the old man would also sometimes come to Mr. Cai's house to drink together. The old man said his surname was Pu and that he was from Jiangxi, having come here to trade porcelain. After a long time, Mr. Cai realized that the old man was actually a fox spirit. Despite this, they had a deep bond, and the fox spirit did not hide its true nature, nor did Mr. Cai fear it. There was a case involving slanderous gossip about a woman's private life, and opinions were divided. Mr. Cai happened to mention this to the fox spirit, saying, "Since you are so enlightened, you must know the truth." The fox spirit angrily replied, "We who practice the way do not interfere in people's trivial matters. The secret places of the bedroom, the private meetings of men and women, are ambiguous and hard to clarify, and suspicions are easy to arise. One dog barks at a shadow, and soon all the dogs join in barking. Even if it is true, what business is it of outsiders? To satisfy someone's mouth for a day is to bring shame to a person's descendants for generations. This already harms the harmony of heaven and earth and invites the jealousy of ghosts and gods. Especially when it is like a snake in a cup or a bow in a shadow, vague and without basis, but embellished and exaggerated, as if seen with one's own eyes, it is unbearable for people and impossible to prove. Often leading to deep depression and unspoken suffering, dying with a grievance. The poisonous resentment is especially difficult to dissipate over many lifetimes. If there are spirits, how can there be no karmic retribution? Fear that on the mountains of knives and trees of swords, there will be a place set aside for such a person. You are usually simple and honest, and even hearing this should make you cover your ears. To investigate the truth, what do you intend to do? Is it not enough to be blind? Do you still want to plow your tongue? He threw down his cup and left. From then on, he never came back. Mr. Cai felt ashamed and regretful, slapping himself on the cheek, and always told this story to warn others, not hiding it from anyone.

Zhang Gong, my maternal uncle, recounted that in the western part of Wu Family Village, where he lived, a beggar died on the road. The dog he kept stayed to guard the body without leaving. At night, wolves came to eat the corpse. The dog fiercely bit to prevent them from advancing. Soon, many wolves gathered. The dog exhausted and fell down, and was all eaten by them. Only the head remained, still with its eyes wide open in anger, all as if they were about to burst. A tenant who was guarding his melon field saw this. Another time, in Urumqi, Cheng Yimen had a thief enter his room one night. The thief had already climbed over the wall and was about to leave when the dog he kept chased and bit its feet. The thief drew a knife and slashed at it. Until it died, the dog still bit and did not let go, thus was captured. At that time, Cheng Yimen had a servant named Gong Qilong, who just betrayed and turned on his master. Everyone said: The home of Governor Cheng has two anomalies, one with a human face but a beast's heart, and one with a beast's face but a human heart.

During my time in Urumqi, the cavalry officer Sa Yin Choktu told me that in the past, while he was guarding the frontier outpost at Jiangshan Kou, one morning just before dawn, a crow cawed outside his door. Disliking the bad omen, he drew a sharp arrow and shot at it. The arrow made a loud noise and passed over the back of a nursing cow. The cow was startled and ran away, and he called several soldiers to chase after it. They entered a mountain valley and met two plowmen. One of the soldiers bumped into one of the plowmen, causing him to fall down. They supported and examined him, finding no serious injury, but only a limping foot. They asked if his home was not far and carried him back together. As they entered the room and had not yet settled down, they heard a child calling repeatedly that there was a thief. They went out together to help capture the thief, and it turned out to be the escaped convict Han Yun, who was just climbing over the wall to steal melons. They therefore arrested him. If the crow had not cawed outside the door, then Sa Yin Choktu would not have shot; if Sa Yin Choktu had not shot, the cow would not have been startled and run away; if the cow had not been startled and run away, they would not have bumped into the plowman and caused him to fall down; if they had not bumped into the plowman and caused him to fall down, several soldiers would not have arrived at his home; only a small child would have seen someone stealing melons, and the situation necessarily could not have bound him. Then they led each other, finally making him be bound and executed. This crow's coming, is it not that something relied on it? Probably Yun was originally a notorious bandit who had robbed and killed many people. At that time, although nothing was seen, it was actually the same as the cause and effect of Liu Gang encountering a ghost.

佐领额尔赫图说，过去他守卫吉木萨卡伦时，夜里听到团焦外传来呜呜的声音，人们出去追赶，声音就渐渐退去；人们停下，声音也停下；人们返回，声音又再次出现，如此持续了几个夜晚。一个戍卒胆大，最终拿起刀跟随声音，沿着声音的方向进入山中，来到一具僵尸面前，声音便消失了。查看后，发现有野兽啃咬的痕迹，尸体已经很久枯干了。戍卒回去报告，大家心中明白这是鬼魂在寻求安葬。于是准备了棺材将其安葬，之后声音便不再出现。灵魂已经离去，身体还有什么？这个鬼魂依附于遗体，实在是无法避免自缚。然而，蝼蚁鱼鳖的谈论，源自庄子的远见。怎能要求所有生物都像太上忘情一样？从这件事来看，可知棺材和殓衣必须谨慎处理，这是孝子的心意；遗骨必须妥善安葬，这是仁人的政策。圣人通晓鬼神的性质和状态，怎会说灵魂上升、精神下降，便在黑暗中无知无觉呢？

The magistrate of Xian County, on the eve of his death, had a servant who heard voices in the study at night. The voices said that he had enjoyed luxurious living for several years, and his salary was exhausted. His father complained to the underworld officials, asking to borrow a year's salary from his next life to resolve unfinished matters, but it was unknown if it would be granted. Shortly after, the magistrate died suddenly. Dong Wenke once said: "Heaven's way disapproves of extremes in everything. Therefore, excessive luxury and extreme frugality can both lead to misfortune. However, when tested repeatedly, the punishment for excess is lighter for the wealthy but heavier for the noble, and the punishment for frugality is lighter for the noble but heavier for the wealthy. For the wealthy, excess merely depletes their own wealth, but for the noble, excess inevitably leads to greed for power, and when it becomes severe, it is easier to obtain. For the noble, extreme frugality merely preserves their own wealth, but for the wealthy, extreme frugality inevitably leads to stinginess and calculation, and when it becomes severe, it involves many tricks. Scholars and officials should always deeply reflect, knowing that what benefits oneself will harm others. Leaving some room for everything is the way to invite good fortune."

Xiao Nu Yubao reported that in a family in the village of Tenagel, a cow suddenly entered their herd. The cow was very fat and healthy, and for a long time, no one came looking for it. Even when asked, no one reported losing a cow. They decided to keep it. The daughter of the family, who was around thirteen or fourteen years old, once rode this cow to visit her relatives' house. The cow reached halfway but did not follow the path, carrying her across mountains and valleys, straight into the chaotic mountains. The cliffs were steep, and the valleys deep; falling would surely result in death. She only clung to the cow's neck and cried out. The woodcutters and herders heard the sound and followed to see, but they were already at the top of a thousand peaks, gradually disappearing into the mist. Perhaps fed to tigers and wolves, or left in streams and ravines, all of which is unknown. Everyone blamed the father for greedily taking the cow, leading to great harm. I say that this cow and this girl were destined to have an old grudge; even if they had driven it away, there would still be some way for it to seek revenge. In the old city, Diao Feiwan said that in a village, there were two village teachers. After the rain, they walked together to the earth god temple, sat on the steps and talked, staying for a long time. The ground in front of the temple was clean like a palm. Suddenly, they saw something rise up like writing, and they all stood up to look at it. Then they saw sixteen characters drawn with a stick in the mud: "Not taking advantage of the coolness, teaching students, mixing into the school, is not this shameful?" The temple had no residents, and a fox was living there, annoyed by the two people talking for so long. At the time, the imperial examination was adding regulated verse, and Feiwan joked, "Writing something offhand, even in four-character rhyming, I am ashamed of this fox."

Fei Wan then said, "There was a scholar who was the most courageous. He always sought to see ghosts but never succeeded. One night, after the rain had stopped and the moon was bright, he ordered a small servant to carry a jar of wine to a cluster of tombs. He looked around and called out, 'This is a good night for a solitary walk, but it is quite lonely. Friends in the netherworld, would any be willing to join in a drink with me?' Soon he saw flickering phosphorescent light, appearing and disappearing among the grass. He called again, and they made a moaning sound, staying about a zhang away, all stopping and not advancing. He counted their shadows, about ten or so. He poured wine from a large cup, sprinkling it. All bent down to smell the aroma. One ghost praised the wine as excellent, asking for more. While sprinkling, he asked, 'Why don't you reincarnate?' They replied, 'Those with good roots are reborn, those with evil deeds are sent to hell. We thirteen, our sins are not yet complete, four are waiting to be reborn; nine are bound by karma and cannot be reborn.' He asked, 'Why don't you repent and seek liberation?' They replied, 'Repentance must be done before death, after death there is no place to exert effort.' After the wine was finished, he raised the jar to look at them, and they staggered away. One ghost turned back and said earnestly, 'Hungry ghosts who have been satisfied with wine, have nothing to repay the kindness, so I offer you a word of advice: repentance must be done before death.'"

A Scribe of the Hanlin Academy named Yi Shi, during his campaign in Ili, fought fiercely to break through enemy encirclement, suffering seven spear wounds. After two days and nights, he revived, and after riding hard for another day and night, he still managed to catch up with the main army. When I was in the Hanlin Academy with Bo Xizhai, I saw scars on him and inquired in detail about his experiences. He said that when he was wounded, he felt no pain, but suddenly fell into a deep sleep. Gradually regaining consciousness, he found his soul had left his body, surrounded by swirling wind and sand, unable to distinguish east from west. He clearly knew he was dead, but then suddenly thought of his young children and poor family, feeling heart-wrenchingly painful. He felt his body like a leaf, floating and wanting to fly. Suddenly, he thought of dying in vain and not wanting to, and swore to become a vengeful ghost to kill the enemy. Then, he felt his body like an iron pillar, unshakable by the wind. Standing still and hesitating, he was about to climb straight up the mountain to look for the enemy troops. Suddenly, he woke up from a dream, already lying stiff in the battlefield blood. Bo Xizhai sighed and said: "To hear such a situation, one feels that dying in battle is not to be feared. Then, loyal ministers and heroes, indeed, are easy to be. Why should people fear and not do it?"

In the village, there lived a family named Gu, whose members were engaged in the business of slaughtering cattle. The number of cattle they killed was incalculable. Later, the elderly man of the family became blind in both eyes, and the elderly woman, as she neared death, experienced severe skin damage and endured extreme pain. She claimed that the underworld had mimicked the methods of slaughtering cattle to mutilate her, and she cried out for over a month before finally passing away. Madame Shen, the mother of the concubine, personally witnessed these events. The sin of killing is extremely grave, and cattle contribute significantly to agriculture; thus, the act of killing them is particularly severe. The "Mingxiang Ji" (Records of Divine Signs) records the story of Yu Shao of the Jin dynasty, which already contains the advice to diligently practice and avoid killing, and if one cannot completely stop, at least refrain from slaughtering cattle. This is the oldest prohibition against killing cattle. The "Xuanshi Zhi" (Records of the Xuan Room) states that if demons and humans live together, epidemics will arise, but those who do not eat beef are spared. The "Youyang Zazu" (Miscellaneous Records of Youyang) also records this. Today, those who do not eat beef are indeed not affected by epidemics, and it is true that novels are not entirely without basis.

Tianwen Gong of Hai Ning said, "Once, while at someone's house, I encountered a spirit descending from the altar through divination, and it was Duke Li Wen Zhen of An Xi. The Duke bowed and asked about the way of dealing with the world. Duke Li Wen Zhen replied: 'When successful, do not become too overjoyed; when unsuccessful, do not speak too hastily, then always maintain ultimate good fortune.' The Duke recited it throughout his life and once taught his disciples, saying: 'When successful, do not become too overjoyed; those who slightly understand the gains and losses can do it; when unsuccessful, do not speak too hastily, even the wise may not be able to. Speaking hastily is not just about grumbling; indifferently and without regard, they make statements of broad-mindedness, such statements bring more disaster than grumbling does.' I then remembered what was recorded in the remaining drafts of my great-grandfather's Flower King Pavilion: Mr. Shengyang of Song, whose courtesy name was Dazhuang, a scholar from Hejian, was my great-grandfather's brother-in-law. He gave a poem, saying: 'The mad slave still retains his old ways, broad-mindedness is just a form of complaint.' This is similar to what the Duke discussed, almost like overlapping circles."

There was a woman from the Oirat tribe who became a commoner's wife in Urumqi. After several years, she became a widow. The woman had natural beauty, and matchmakers frequently knocked on her door. She declined, saying, "If I were to marry, I would marry, but my husband died without leaving children, and my father-in-law is old. Where would I go and whom would I rely on? Please wait until I have finished taking care of my father-in-law, and then we can discuss." Some men wanted to marry into her family and take care of her father-in-law, but she declined again, saying, "The nature of men cannot be guaranteed. In case they do not get along with my father-in-law, it would be too late to regret it. It is also not possible." She then worked hard and labored, ensuring her father-in-law was well-fed, warm, and content, even better than when he had sons. After six or seven years, her father-in-law died of old age. After completing the funeral arrangements, she cried bitterly and bid farewell to the grave, changed into mourning clothes, and left in a carriage. Those who commented on her felt pity for her lack of chastity, but could not help but call her filial. The Grand Secretary Yong was stationed in that area at the time, and upon hearing this, he sighed and said, "This is what is meant by having natural beauty but lacking education."

The Nine Sayings of Wang Fu of New City A friend of Wang Fu, whose name is not given, was appointed as a county magistrate in Guizhou. He borrowed money from a Western merchant, who then coerced and exploited him, resorting to every kind of trickery. Pressed by the time limit, the magistrate was forced to compromise and accommodate, but the Western merchant only brought up more complications. Their dispute continued until midnight, when the magistrate finally endured his pain and signed the contract. The contract amounted to a hundred gold pieces, but the actual amount he received was less than thirty gold pieces. After the Western merchant left, the magistrate held the money in a chest and sat alone, sighing deeply. Suddenly, he heard a voice from above the eaves saying: "There is no such injustice in the world. You are too weak and cowardly, causing people to be filled with anger. My original intention was to rob you, but now I will punish the Western merchant, and thus vent the frustration of officials throughout the world." The magistrate was frightened and dared not respond. Soon, a sound was heard from the corner of the house, and the figure had already leapt over the wall and left. The next day, it was heard that the Western merchant had been robbed, and all his new and old loan contracts had been swept up and taken away. This thief was especially full of chivalric spirit. However, the Western merchant had also gone too far, offending the natural order, and so ghosts and gods cleverly arranged for this encounter.

According to Xu Wenmu, there was a relative of his who had just been appointed to a new official position and prepared a grand feast with sacrificial food and wine to honor his ancestors. There was a shaman who could see ghosts and whispered to someone, saying: "When the ancestors of the family received the sacrifice, their faces were sad and gloomy, as if they were about to cry, while the ghost of Mr. A from the neighboring alley was sitting on the roof ridge of the opposite house, with feet raised and laughing." Only then did he realize the reason for his ancestors' sorrow and weeping. The joy of Mr. A, however, remained a mystery. After some time, someone who knew his secret affairs revealed that Mr. A's daughter was beautiful and that he had sent an old woman to seduce her with gold and jewels, leading to multiple nights together. People did not know, but the ghosts did. Who would say that one can act improperly in the unseen world?

Wang Mei, the Scholar-Official, recounted that west of Jiaohe City lies an ancient tomb, surrounded by dense and tangled forests, said to harbor supernatural beings. Those who dared to enter often fell ill with chills and fever. Woodcutters and herders avoided the area. An elderly scholar, upright and proud, would pass through this area on his way from his residence to the county town, stopping to rest each time. He would sit arrogantly, showing no fear, and over the years, he saw nothing unusual. One day, he sat by the tomb, exposing himself to cool off, and upon returning home, he went mad, speaking incoherently: "Once I thought you were a noble spirit, so I let you act recklessly, not daring to disrespect you. But you have betrayed my trust, knowing that your previous respectful demeanor was merely superficial. Now I no longer fear you." His family repeatedly prayed for his recovery, and he remained confused for several days. From then on, he lost his spirit and courage, moving quickly and humbly whenever he passed the area. From this, we can see that supernatural beings are not to be feared if one's heart is pure; even if they try to harm one, they will not dare to retaliate. We can also see that supernatural beings are very fearsome; if one's actions are flawed, even if hidden, they can still be seen.

A Student from Xiaoshan, Wang Hui Zu, courtesy name Huan Zeng, who passed the imperial examination in the year Yiwu of Qianlong's reign, is currently the magistrate of Ningyuan County in Hunan Province. Before passing the examination, he worked for a long time in a government office and wrote a two-volume work titled "Advisory Words on Governance." The work contains several recent events that are quite sufficient to serve as a lesson and warning. One of them says: Mr. Sun Jingxi, whose given name is Erzhou, was the magistrate of Wujiao when a retainer named Ye, one evening, while drinking, lay down on the ground and remained unconscious for two hours before waking up. The next day, he closed the door and wrote a yellow paper petition, went to the City God Temple to pray, and no one could understand the reason. After six days, he again lay down as before, remained unconscious for a long time, and then woke up, asking to move out of the office. He said that eight years ago, in the government office in Guantao, Shandong, a scholar reported that a young rascal was harassing his wife. Originally, he intended to ask the master to punish the young rascal without the need for the wife to testify. However, the interrogator, Mr. Xie, wanted to see the wife's appearance, urged to summon her for questioning, which led to the wife committing suicide, and the young rascal was also punished. Now, the young rascal has filed a complaint in the underworld, arguing that if the wife did not die, the young rascal would not have a reason to die, but the wife's death was due to the internal interrogation. The City God of Guantao sent a document to summon him. Yesterday, he submitted a petition to argue, stating that the wife should have testified, and the instigator was Mr. Xie. Recently, another document was sent, arguing that the intention of the interrogation was to see her appearance, not to clear her name. Though the idea originated with Mr. Xie, the pen was actually in the hands of Mr. Ye. Mr. Xie has already been summoned, and Mr. Ye cannot be spared. I am surely not exempt from punishment. He died the next night.

Governor-General of Zhejiang, Tong Gong, said: "During the autumn review in the year of Jiazi (乙亥) of the Qianlong era, I secretly went out to inspect the officials' handling of affairs. All were sound asleep, except one room where lamps and candles were lit. I peeped through a hole in the window and saw an official busy with paperwork. In front of the desk stood an old man and a young woman. I was very surprised and puzzled, so I looked at it cautiously. I saw the official first copy a note, then destroyed the draft and rewrote it. The young woman bowed and stepped back. Then he took out another scroll and pondered for a long time, wrote a note, and the old man also bowed and stepped back. I inquired of this official, and he explained that the first case was of a death in Taizhou caused by adultery. Initially, a deferred verdict was proposed, but later because the person was a scholar, compromising his character and causing a life to be lost, the verdict was changed to a confirmed death. The later scroll was about a case in Ningbo where repeated beatings led to death. Initially, a confirmed death was proposed, but later because of a legitimate debt collection, the death was due to retaliation, the verdict was changed to a deferred decision."

Knowing the young widow as the valiant spirit who sacrificed her life, and the old man as the ancestral spirit of the repeatedly imprisoned. One account goes: In the county seat of Xiushui, there is a tower called Love-Longing Tower. The wooden ladder has been destroyed for a long time, and during rainy weather, the sound of ghosts crying is often heard. An old official said that during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, the magistrate's mother was skilled at reciting Buddhist mantras, and thus this tower was built. In the early years of Emperor Yongzheng's reign, a magistrate brought a clerk named Hu with him. In the hot summer, he did not want to see people and stayed alone in the tower, with all his official documents and food lowered up and down using a rope. One day, he heard a loud, mournful cry from the top of the tower. His attendants hurriedly climbed the ladder up, and they found Hu naked and covered in blood, having stabbed himself in the abdomen, and shattered his entire body, as if carved. He said that previously in the office of a certain county in Hunan, there was a case where an adulterer killed the husband, and the adulteress reported it to the authorities. He feared that the master would be blamed for not noticing, and reported it as a capture. The woman was executed by dismemberment. Soon after, he saw a god leading the woman to him, stabbing a blade into his abdomen. He did not know anything else.

A man called out all night and died in the morning. One account states: A certain person from Wu Prefecture was well-known for his skill in managing finances. Occasionally, he was treated with disrespect by a colleague, who then secretly accused him of sleeping with someone and reported his private affairs to the higher authorities. This ultimately resulted in a major case, and the man later bit his own tongue and died. Another account tells of Zhang from Wuxi, who worked in the office of Governor Qu of Guian. There was a case where an adulterer killed the original husband. Qu wanted to release the woman because she was not involved in the plot, but Zhang spoke loudly, saying: "Zhao Dun did not punish the assassin, which is equivalent to killing the king; Xu Zhi did not taste the medicine, which is equivalent to killing his father. The *Spring and Autumn Annals* has a law of punishing intent, so this cannot be tolerated." The woman was ultimately sentenced to death. Later, Zhang dreamed of a woman with loose hair holding a sword, who approached him and said: "I did not deserve to die; why did you help so quickly?" She stabbed him with a blade, and when he woke up, he felt severe pain at the site of the wound. From then on, she tormented him every night, until he died. Another account says: Mr. Han Qiang from Xiaoshan was young and skilled in writing legal documents but had been long stuck in the examination hall and had no son, having given up on further advancement. In the year 1723 (Yongzheng's 11th year, Gui Mao), while working in the office of Governor of Gong'an County, he dreamed that a god said: "You have caused too much trouble with your writing, so all your good fortune and descendants are being taken away. Now that you have administered justice with kindness and leniency, I will reward you with success in the exams and a son. Return quickly." He did not believe it. The next night, he dreamed the same thing again. It was already the beginning of July, and he answered that it was too late for the exam. The god said: "I can send you there." After waking up, he hurriedly packed his belongings. The river journey was favorable, and on August 2nd, he finally arrived in Hangzhou. He entered the exam hall as a reserve candidate and passed. The following year, he indeed had a son. Huan Zeng was sincere and had an ancient style; what he said must not be false.

A Record of a Case Involving the Extinction of a Family Line During the time when Yang Yan-geng of Pinghu served as an assistant in the county of Yuxiang, the county magistrate also served as the magistrate of Linjin. There was a case of doubt that had not been resolved for a long time. After further investigation, it was found that the younger brother had beaten his older brother to death. At night, after drafting the final judgment document, he had not yet extinguished the candle before going to sleep. Suddenly, he heard the sound of a hook on the bed moving, and the curtain was slightly opened. He thought it was the wind. A short while later, the sound repeated, and the curtain was hanging on the hook. There was an elderly man with white hair kneeling in front of the bed and bowing his head. When he shouted at the elderly man, he disappeared. There was a sound of paper flipping on the desk. He quickly got up to look, and it was the judgment document he had drafted. He reviewed it repeatedly in detail. The crime was indeed not unjust. However, the family had been single-handedly passed down for four generations, and only when his father was born did the family have two sons. One of them died an unnatural death, and the other one confessed to the crime. The ancestral worship of five generations would be cut off. He destroyed the draft and kept the doubt as it was. It is considered better to maintain doubt. I say, according to the law of the state, those who destroy the family lineage must be punished. From a human perspective, the extinction of the family line is also pitiable. Both life and death are problematic, and benevolence and righteousness are both obstructed. If one must bend the rules to find a solution, the killer should be punished with death. The冤of the deceased has been avenged, but avenging one's own冤by cutting off the ancestral worship of one's ancestors. If the older brother were aware, he would certainly not want it. If he were to wish it, it would be devoid of human sentiment. Even if not punished, it is not unjust. This is one argument. Some say that emotions are personal matters, and the law is a matter for the whole world. If there are only two brothers, and the younger brother kills the older brother, out of pity for the extinction of the family line, they are not punished. There would be many who steal property and kill their brothers. What law can correct the family lineage? This is also not an invalid argument. Without the wise minister Gao Tao, this case is indeed difficult to decide. It should be kept for the wise to decide.

Yao An Gong said, "In the past, when I was at the home of my uncle, Tengong De Yin, there was a sudden downpour. It lasted from the fourth hour to the seventh hour before it stopped. The rain was all water used to soak flax. At the time, there was an elderly scholar teaching in the household, and the people then asked, 'What is the reason for this rain?' The scholar turned away and faced the wall, saying, 'The Master does not discuss the strange.'"

D Liu Xiangwan said, when he was once a guest in Shanxi, he heard that there was an old Confucian scholar who passed by an ancient tomb. A companion mentioned that there was a fox inside, and the old scholar cursed it, but nothing else unusual happened. The old scholar was known for his frugal lifestyle, wearing no fur in winter, no dark clothing in summer, eating no elaborate dishes, and drinking no strong liquor. His wife and children often went hungry, but he accumulated wealth bit by bit and eventually saved forty taels of gold, which he melted into four ingots, sealed them away, and claimed to others that he had no grain. After cursing the fox, the gold he had stored began to behave strangely: sometimes it would suddenly appear on the roof or at the top of a tree, requiring him to climb a ladder to retrieve it; sometimes it would appear in mud or shallow water, forcing him to wade through it to find it; sometimes it would be thrown into the latrine, requiring him to reach in and wash it out; sometimes it would move to a different location, and he would have to conduct a thorough search to find it; sometimes it would disappear for several days, only to fall from the air; sometimes it would appear when he was sitting opposite a guest, suddenly appearing in the brim of his hat; sometimes it would appear when he was bowing to someone, suddenly falling out of his sleeve with a clinking sound. The gold's movements were unpredictable and beyond comprehension. One day, the four ingots suddenly leaped into the air, flying like butterflies, hitting and touching each other, rising higher and farther away, as if about to fly away. Out of necessity, the scholar burned incense and prayed, and the ingots finally threw themselves into his chest. From then on, the gold no longer bothered him, and his demeanor as a teacher had already ended. When he recounted this story, one friend said, "I have heard that virtue can overcome evil spirits, but I have not heard that cursing can overcome evil spirits. It is naturally appropriate that the gold reached him." Another friend said, "If the great Confucian scholars Zhou, Zhang, Cheng, and Zhu had cursed, the gold would not have appeared. It is a pity that he had an ancient appearance but not an ancient heart." Another friend said, "The great Confucian scholars Zhou, Zhang, Cheng, and Zhu would not have cursed lightly. It is because he lacked virtue within that he was so troubled." Liu Xiangwan nodded in agreement, saying, "These words truly hit the mark."

香畹又言，一孝廉颇善储蓄，而性啬。其妹家至贫，时逼除夕，炊烟不举，冒风雪徒步数十里，乞贷三五金，期明春以其夫馆谷偿，坚以窘辞。其母涕泣助请，辞如故。母脱簪珥付之去，孝廉如弗闻也。是夕有盗穴壁入，罄所有去，迫于公论，弗敢告官捕。越半载，盗在他县败，供曾窃孝廉家，其物犹存十之七，移牒来问，又迫于公论，弗敢认。其妇惜财不能忍，因遣子往认焉。孝廉内愧，避弗见客者半载。夫母子天性，兄妹至情，以啬之故，人如陌路，此真闻之扼腕矣。乃盗遽乘之，使人一快，失而弗敢言，得而弗敢取，又使人再快，至于椎心茹痛，自匿其瑕，复败于其妇，瑕终莫匿，更使人不胜其快。颠倒播弄，如是之巧。谓非若或使之哉。然能愧不见客，吾犹取其足为善，充此一愧，虽以孝友闻可也。 Xiangwan further recounted that a scholar-official named Xianlian was quite adept at saving money but was also very frugal. His sister's family was extremely poor, and as the New Year approached, they had no fire for cooking. She braved the wind and snow, walking for dozens of miles on foot, to borrow three or five gold coins, promising to repay the next spring with the grain from her husband's fields. However, he firmly refused with a plea of poverty. Her mother wept and pleaded for her, but he still refused. Finally, the mother removed her hairpin and earrings and gave them to her to leave, but the scholar-official ignored her. That night, a thief dug a hole in the wall to enter and took everything, but out of public opinion, they dared not report it to the authorities. Half a year later, the thief was caught in another county and confessed to stealing from the scholar-official's home. Seven-tenths of the stolen goods were still there, and a notice was sent to inquire. Again, out of public opinion, they dared not admit it. His wife, unable to bear the loss, sent their son to identify the thief. The scholar-official felt deep shame and avoided seeing guests for half a year. Siblings have a natural bond, and brothers and sisters have deep affection, but due to his frugality, they became like strangers. Truly, this is something to make one clench their fists in anger. Then the thief took advantage of the situation, making people feel a sense of relief, but they dared not speak of the loss, nor could they take the recovered items, which made people feel even more relieved. It was a painful and humiliating experience, and he tried to hide his flaws, but they were exposed by his wife, and his flaws could no longer be hidden, making people feel even more relieved. The thief's cunning and manipulation were so skillful. Is it not as if fate had orchestrated it? However, his ability to feel shame and avoid seeing guests shows that he is worthy of being considered good. If he can expand this sense of shame, he could become known for filial piety and brotherly love.

Lu Jiyu, the editor-in-chief, suffered from a severe illness and mistakenly consulted a practitioner who followed the methods of *The Complete Works of Jingyue*, who administered ginseng, leading to his immediate death. His mother, overcome with regret, wept bitterly, and whenever she spoke, the wooden walls of the house would creak and crack. At night, she would sometimes walk around the bed calling out to her mother, clearly recognizing the voice of Jiyu. It is said that this was to prevent her from grieving excessively in her old age. Alas, even in death, he still remembered his mother!

Mr. Ju Tinghe, a respected elder from Haian, recounted that a lady from an official family was on her deathbed. She held a young boy in her left hand and a young girl in her right, weeping as she passed away. Only after her grip was forcefully broken did they release her, and her eyes remained wide open, not closing even after her death. Afterward, under the light of lamps and moon, her form would often be seen at a distance. Calling her did not respond, asking her did not answer, calling her did not come, and approaching her did not see her. Sometimes she would not appear for several nights, and sometimes she would appear multiple times in one night. Sometimes she was seen in front of someone, but that person did not see her; sometimes she was seen in one place, and then in another place. Generally, she appeared like bubbles, like empty flowers, like lightning, like flashes of light, disappearing in an instant and reappearing in a blink. Although she did no harm, everyone felt as if the deceased lady was still present in their minds. Therefore, the stepmother did not dare to have any feelings of differentiation towards the children, and the maids, servants, and attendants also did not dare to have any feelings of contempt or disrespect towards the children. When the children reached adulthood and got married, the ghost gradually stopped appearing, but after several years, she might appear once. Therefore, the entire family always lived in constant fear, as if she were always nearby. Some suspected that she was a fox spirit or a ghost, which is another possibility. However, fox spirits and ghosts disturb people, whereas this one did not approach them, and moreover, what purpose would a fox spirit have in persisting for over a decade, constantly creating this illusion? It seems that the extreme attachment of her spirit did not dissipate, and those who were her children understood their parents' hearts, and their love for her became even stronger after her death. One can also feel a sense of sorrow and emotion.

庭和又言，有兄死而吞噬其孤侄者，迫胁侵蚀，殆无以自存。一夕，夫妇方酣眠，忽梦兄仓皇呼曰：起起，火已至，醒而烟焰迷漫，无路可脱，仅破窗得出，喘息未定，室已崩摧，缓须臾则灰烬矣。次日，急召其侄，尽还所夺，人怪其数朝之内，忽跖忽夷。其人流涕自责，始知其故。此鬼善全骨肉，胜于为厉多多矣。 庭和又说道，有一个兄弟去世后，他的妻子却吞噬了他孤儿侄子的财产，通过胁迫和逐渐侵蚀的方式，几乎使侄子无法生存。一天晚上，这对夫妇正酣睡时，突然梦见已故的哥哥慌张地喊道：“快起来，火已经来了！”醒来后，他们发现烟雾弥漫，无路可逃，只能打破窗户逃生，刚喘过一口气，房子就已经倒塌了，稍有迟疑就会化为灰烬。第二天，他们急忙召来侄子，归还了所有夺走的东西，人们奇怪他在几天之内竟然从恶人变成了善人。那人流着泪自责，这才明白其中的原因。这个鬼魂善于保全家庭成员之间的关系，比作恶要好得多。 高淳令梁公钦，官户部额外主事时，与姚安公同在四川司，是时六部规制严，凡有故不能入署者，必遣人告掌印，掌印遣牒司务，司务每日汇呈堂，谓之出付，不能无故不至也。一日，梁公不入署，而又不出付，众疑焉，姚安公与福建李公根侯，寓皆相近，放衙后，同往视之。则梁公昨夕睡后，忽闻砰訇撞触声，如怒马腾踏，呼问无应者，悸而起视，乃二仆一御者，裸体相搏，捶击甚苦，然皆缄口无一言。 高淳县令梁公钦，在担任户部额外主事时，与姚安公同在四川司任职。当时六部的规章制度非常严格，如果有事不能按时到岗，必须派人报告给掌印，掌印再通知司务，司务每天汇总呈报给上级，这称为“出付”，不能无缘无故缺席。有一天，梁公没有去上班，也没有提交报告，引起了众人的怀疑。姚安公与福建的李公根侯住得较近，下班后，他们一同前往查看。结果发现，梁公昨晚睡觉后，突然听到砰砰的撞击声，就像愤怒的马匹在狂奔，他呼喊询问，却没有得到任何回应，心中恐惧，起来一看，原来是两个仆人和一个驾车的人，赤身裸体地互相搏斗，被打得很惨，但他们却一声不吭。

At that time, all the neighbors were asleep, and there was no one else in the lodging. They could do nothing but sit and watch them fight. Until the bell rang and they both fell down. Until dawn when they woke up, their wounds were like scales, and their faces were all damaged. When asked, they did not know what happened. Only remembered that evening they sat by the back door to cool off. Saw several dogs jumping around on the ruins of an old house from afar. Playfully threw bricks at them. Barked and jumped. After going to bed, such a change occurred. Perhaps the dogs were actually foxes, and they were not recognized under the moonlight. Liang Gong is from Taihe. He is a fellow townsman with the True One of Zhengyi. He was going to report it. Yao An Gong said: Foxes play by themselves, what do they have to do with people? For no reason to hit them, the blame is not on them. To take the side of the wrongdoer and attack the righteous, is not in accordance with reason. Li Gong also said: Whenever servants or slaves argue with others, they should first control themselves. Even if the argument is just, it should not be allowed to go unchecked. If they act recklessly because they have something to rely on, how much more so if they are in the wrong? Liang Gong then stopped.

During the year of Jia Wei in the Qianlong era, a scholar was walking along the west street of Yongguang Temple before the metropolitan examination. He saw a beautiful woman standing outside a door and was quite pleased with her. He entrusted a matchmaker to negotiate and took her as a concubine with three hundred gold pieces, and thus stayed at her family's residence. They also got along very well. Upon returning home after the examination, he found the windows broken and the walls dusty, with no one there. Filth was piled up, as if the house had been abandoned for many years. He inquired among the neighbors, who said that the house had been empty for a long time, and that the family had only lived there for a month before leaving one night, with no one knowing where they went. Some said it was a fox, as there are similar stories in novels. Others said the woman was a lure, having stolen money and fled far away, pretending to be a fox. If a fox pretends to be a human, this is also cunning; but if a human pretends to be a fox, isn't this even more cunning? I have lived in the capital for fifty to sixty years and have seen many such cases, and this is just one example.

汪御ist, Quanxiang, said that a cloth merchant named Han met a fox woman. She gradually became weak, and her companions sought talismans to exorcise and banish her, but she would temporarily leave and still return. One night, she slept with Han, and suddenly sat up, saying, "Do you have strange thoughts? Why do you suddenly feel the harshness piercing and making you uneasy?" Han replied, "I have no other thoughts. Only my neighbor Wu, forced by debt, sold his son as a singing boy. I could not bear that the descendant of a scholarly family should sink to a lowly status, so I donated forty taels of silver to redeem him, which is why I have been restless and unable to sleep." The fox woman suddenly pushed aside the pillow and said, "If you have this thought, you are a good person. Those who harm good people will suffer great punishment. I will leave from now on." She kissed him with her lips, exhaled for a long time, then waved her hands and left. Han thereafter became strong and healthy as before. Mr. Dai Suotang said: I once saw a great official on April 8th in a Buddhist temple performing repentance and releasing living beings. He happened to walk under the flowers and met a wandering monk who joined his hands in prayer and asked, "What are you doing here?" The official said, "I am doing good deeds." The monk asked again, "Why are you doing good deeds today?" The official said, "It is Buddha's birthday." The monk asked again, "If you do good deeds on Buddha's birthday, shouldn't you do good deeds during the remaining 359 days?" The official released living beings today, which is immediate merit, but do not know if the number of animals killed in the kitchen year after year is enough to match this number? The great official could not answer. The abbot scolded instead, "Noble person, you protect the Dharma, enhancing the glory of the Three Jewels. How dare I, a poor monk, speak falsely?" The wandering monk walked and laughed, saying, "The purple-robed monk does not speak, but the poor monk cannot help but speak." He waved his arm and left directly, and no one knew where he went.

An old monk sighed quietly and said: "This layperson is indeed very unwise, yet in our Dharma, this is akin to suddenly hearing the lion's roar." In former times, the monk Mingyu of Wutai Mountain once said: "To constantly recite the Buddha's name, evil intentions will not arise; it is not merely reciting the Buddha's name a few times a day that constitutes merit. To observe vegetarianism every day, the karmic debt from killing is permanently erased; it is not merely abstaining from meat for a few days each month that counts as merit." One may indulge in rich and fatty foods morning and evening, yet on certain days each month refrain from eating meat — and be called a virtuous person. Then, if corruption is openly practiced, and ceremonial vessels are not adorned, yet on certain days each month one refuses to accept money — can this be called the conduct of an upright official? This seems to align with the words of this wandering monk. Li Xingfu, the Chief Censor, then said: "This is what that religion says." Scholars and officials who abstain from meat for life may find it impossible to uphold, but if they observe a monthly vegetarian fast for several days, then during those days the karmic debt is somewhat reduced; if several people observe such a fast, then for those people the karmic debt is somewhat reduced. Is this not better than not observing any fast at all? This is also a matter of differing wisdom and compassion, each clarifying a different meaning. Yet one does not know whether Mingyu, if he were still alive, would have any objections to this.

Dong E Luo, the Chief Clerk of the Prince's Mansion — according to the Eight Banners Genealogy, he was originally a member of the Donggou clan, but he himself wrote his name as Dong E. In official documents and records, his name is also written as Dong E. As stated in the *Gongyang Commentary*, the name follows the owner's preference. Exiled to Manasi, a relative of the Urumqi branch, one day he went to Urumqi, travelling at night to avoid the heat, stopping to rest his horse under a tree, where he met a man kneeling halfway. He asked about his well-being, and the man said he was a soldier named Liu Qing. They talked for a long time, and Dong E Luo got on his horse to leave. Liu Qing said, "I have a trivial matter to ask you to convey a message: the official servant Xi'er in the seal room owes me three hundred coins. I am very poor now, and it should be returned." The next day, he saw Xi'er and told him what Liu Qing had said. Xi'er was terrified and sweating profusely, his face ashen, and he asked curiously why this was happening. He then realized that Liu Qing had been sick and died a long time ago. At the time of his death, Chen Zhushan, out of sympathy for his diligence and prudence, gave three hundred coins to Xi'er to buy wine and meat to offer as a sacrifice to Liu Qing. Xi'er, thinking Liu Qing had no relatives, took all the money for himself. No one knew about this, and he did not expect that the ghost would come to seek justice. Chen Zhushan never believed in cause and effect. At this point, he was startled and said, "This is not a lie, and this statement is not fabricated." He thought that people only fear being known for their evil deeds, and where people cannot know, they can do as they please. Now he realized that the theory of no ghosts is not reliable. Therefore, those who hide their evil deeds should be worried.

After the pacification of Changji, the military captured the children and women of the rebel party and distributed them as rewards among the generals. A certain officer in Urumqi was in charge of this task. He selected the four most beautiful captives, taught them to sing and dance, applied perfumed oils and cosmetics, dressed them in colorful attire with bright jewelry, and they displayed myriad postures and expressions, like delicate young girls. All who saw them were captivated. Later, he was promoted to the position of deputy general at Jinta Temple. When the appointed time to depart arrived, the captives checked their clothing. Suddenly, four pairs of embroidered shoes leaped out of a chest, filled the hall with dancing, like a swarm of butterflies flying. They were struck with a stick, but they still wriggled as if wanting to move, making a soft, continuous sound. Those who understood were surprised by the ominous sign. Traveling to Pizhan, he whipped an officer, and was impeached by the governor-general, sentenced to garrison duty in Ili. Ultimately, he died in exile.

In a place of utmost danger and urgency, sometimes something extraordinary suddenly appears. In an unreasonable and emotionless matter, there must be another reason. Acting beyond the usual rules or norms, one cannot be rigidly fixed and decided. An old woman from my hometown, for no apparent reason, led dozens of other elderly women to a neighboring village, forced her way in, and kidnapped the girl away. Thinking it was an act of provocation, they had never had contact before; thinking it was a case of bride kidnapping, the old woman had no son. The villagers were shocked and surprised, and no one could understand the reason. The girl's family took the matter to court, and the official issued a warrant to arrest her. The old woman had already taken the girl and fled, and no one knew where they went. The other maids and women also scattered and fled, involving many people. After much investigation and questioning, finally, one person confessed the truth: The old woman's son was dying of consumption. The old woman comforted him, saying, "Your death is your fate, what a pity, he didn't leave a grandson, so that our ancestors would become ghosts of hunger." The son groaned and said, "A grandson is not guaranteed, but there is hope. I had an affair with a girl from a certain family, and she is eight months pregnant. But I fear that she will be killed when she gives birth."

After the child's death, the old woman muttered to herself for more than ten days. Suddenly, she took this action, perhaps to take the girl and preserve the fetus. The official sighed and said, "Therefore, it is not necessary to investigate. She will return on her own after two or three months." At the appointed time, she indeed brought her grandson to confess. The official had no recourse, and he only sentenced her according to a lighter law, proposing a flogging and redemption. This matter is like a rabbit rising and a hawk falling; if slightly delayed, it will be lost. This old woman is also quick like a god. According to the quiet and calm statement, when she took the girl away at night, she used three carriages to carry maids and old women, and herself traveled in four different directions. Therefore, it is impossible to determine her location. She did not follow the official road, crossing and turning, with branches upon branches, so it is impossible to know her direction. She traveled by day and rested by night, not staying for even one day. She waited until she gave birth to rent a house, so it is impossible to trace her residence. Her planning is especially meticulous. The girl was abandoned by her parents upon returning, and she then accompanied the old woman to raise the orphan, and never remarried. Because she initially involved herself in the affairs of the Zhen and Wei rivers, the imperial decree did not reach her, and now, her clan is not recorded either.

Li Qingzi recounted that once, while staying at his friend's study, just as dawn was breaking, two mice suddenly began to run and chase each other wildly. The room was filled with a whirlwind-like motion, as if the mice were bouncing like balls, knocking over jars, vases, and other household items. Everything they touched was overturned, and the loud crashing sounds of breaking objects made people feel uneasy for a long time. One mouse leaped several feet into the air, only to fall back to the ground, repeating this action twice before collapsing. Upon closer inspection, it was found that the mouse had blood streaming from all its orifices, and no one knew the cause. They quickly called the household servants to check and repair the damaged items. It was discovered that dozens of love pills were hanging to dry in the room, and the mouse had eaten more than half of them. They realized that the mouse had accidentally ingested the love pills, leading to excessive and uncontrolled behavior. The female mice could not bear the advances and fled, while the male mice had no outlet for their energy and died from internal heat. The friend came out to see the scene, both shocked and amused. He then said in a startled voice, "How could it be so extreme? I now understand the danger." He threw all the stored love pills into water. He reflected, "Dry and hot medicines, when further refined, are both powerful and deeply toxic. I have seen many failures caused by such medicines. Even wise people cannot escape the effects of sulfur. Perhaps my friend was not meant to die, so he suddenly understood the danger after seeing the mouse."

According to Zhang Zuo's *Chao Ye Qian Zai*, during the Tang Dynasty, Liu Rengui, the governor of Qingzhou, was demoted to commoner status due to losing too many ships in maritime transport. He then served in the Liaodong region, where he fell ill and lay down near the city of Pyongyang. He lifted the curtain to watch the soldiers attacking the city, and a soldier came straight to the front and sat with his back to him. Despite being ordered to leave, the soldier remained. A short while later, arrows were shot from the city walls, and one hit Liu Rengui in the center of the chest, killing him. Without that soldier, Liu Rengui would have been hit by a flying arrow. When the university scholar and official led a campaign against the Uighurs, he was the leading commander. He was supervising the troops attacking the city and, being very thirsty, returned to his tent to drink. Just then, a bodyguard also came to ask for a drink, so he offered the mat and sat down. Just as he picked up the bowl, the enemy suddenly fired a large cannon, and a lead ball penetrated his chest, killing him. If this person had come a moment later, he would certainly have been killed. This scholar himself told me that the events were strikingly similar to those involving Liu Rengui. Later, when he led a campaign against the Dajin川 region, he was ultimately killed in battle at Mu Guo Mu. Knowing that a person's life and death are determined by their place, even if one is destined to die in battle, if not in the right place, they may still escape danger. However, those who shrink back and seek to avoid, are they not just avoiding more often?

As for humans and different categories of beings, foxes exist among them. The boundary between the visible and the invisible realms is distinct, and foxes occupy this space. The paths of immortals and demons diverge, and foxes are found in the space between them. Therefore, it is reasonable to consider meeting a fox as a strange occurrence, but it is also reasonable to consider it as a common occurrence. There is no reliable evidence of foxes before the Three Dynasties, but the *Records of the Grand Historian* in the "Biography of Chen She" mentions the use of a torch to make a fox cry, saying: "The Great Chu will rise, and Chen She will be king." This must have been a supernatural event at the time, and it was used to incite rebellion. Wu Jun's *Miscellaneous Records of the Western Capital* recounts that King Guangchuan of the Han dynasty broke into the tomb of Luan Shu and injured a fox within, and later dreamed of an old man reporting a grievance. This suggests that foxes initially transformed into human form during the Han dynasty. Zhang Zuo's *Collection of Gossips from the Court and the Common People* states that since the early Tang dynasty, the common people have often worshipped fox spirits, and a contemporary proverb says: "Without fox spirits, a village cannot be complete." This indicates that foxes were most prevalent during the Tang dynasty. *Taiping Guangji* records twelve volumes of fox-related stories, and nine-tenths of them are from the Tang dynasty, which can be used as evidence. Various records differ, but the details of their origins and development are best explained by Mr. Liu Shitui. It is said that an old scholar in the south of Cangzhou had a friend who was a fox, and Liu Shitui introduced them. The fox was short and small, with a face resembling that of a person in their fifties or sixties. Its clothing was neither ancient nor modern, resembling that of a Taoist. It bowed and greeted with calmness and humility. After the exchange of pleasantries, Liu Shitui asked about the purpose of the visit.

The teacher stepped back and said, "When commoners interact with the nobility, the accounts vary widely, and many things remain unclear. Hearing that you are open-minded and do not hide anything, I ask to dispel my doubts." The fox laughed and said, "Heaven creates all things and gives them names. Foxes are called foxes, just as humans are called humans. Calling a fox a fox is no different from calling a human a human; why should there be any shame? Among us foxes, there is great diversity, just as there are good and bad people among humans. Humans do not hide their own faults, so why should foxes hide theirs? Please speak frankly." The teacher asked if there were different types of foxes. The fox replied, "All foxes can cultivate the Dao, but the most intelligent ones are called *bi* foxes. This is like how there are fewer farmers who read books and more Confucian scholars who do." The teacher then asked if all *bi* foxes are born with intelligence. The fox answered, "This depends on their species; those born to unenlightened foxes remain ordinary, while those born to enlightened foxes can transform themselves." The teacher asked, "If they have achieved the Dao, they must retain their youth, yet stories depict old foxes. Why is this?" The fox explained, "Achieving the Dao means achieving human-like qualities. They experience the same life cycle as humans, including eating, drinking, love, birth, aging, sickness, and death. However, ascending to the heavens is a different matter. It is like among a thousand people, one or two may seek government service. Some practice alchemy and breathing exercises to gain fame through diligent study, while others use charm and seduction as a shortcut to success."

Those who wander on the fairy islands and ascend to the heavenly courts must refine their form and practice breathing exercises in order to achieve this. The seduction and exploitation of others, causing harm, is often prevalent, and such actions often violate the heavenly laws. Who oversees the prohibitions, rewards, and punishments? It is said that minor rewards and punishments are overseen by superiors, while major rewards and punishments are overseen by earthly ghosts and spirits. If there were no prohibitions, they could move about without form or trace, and nothing would be impossible. Why are these seductive and exploitative practices not included in the prohibitions, and why must they wait until they harm others before being punished? It is like someone skillfully luring people to give money, making them happy to assist, which the law does not prohibit, but when it comes to stealing or killing, that is a different matter. The *Lieh Tzu* records a tavern woman who has never been punished by the underworld. Why do I hear of foxes giving birth to humans, but not humans giving birth to foxes? He smiled slightly and said, "This is not worth discussing." It is because there is something to be gained, but nothing to be given. Why do they not fear the jealousy of the Cowherd when they give gifts to others? He smiled again and said, "You are too loose in your speech; you do not know the truth of the matter." In general, women can choose their own partners, but once married, they dare not overstep the boundaries. If giving gifts or picking flowers occasionally goes beyond propriety, human emotions and natural laws are generally the same, so it can be understood by analogy.

Whether one lives in a house or in the wilderness, what is the reason? It is said: those who have not yet attained enlightenment are still like animals, and it is beneficial to live far from people; nothing but the mountains and forests are convenient. Those who have attained enlightenment live in harmony with others, and it is beneficial to live close to people; nothing but cities are convenient. Those with high spiritual attainment can live in both cities and mountains, like wealthy and noble families, with their power, can obtain everything, and they can reside in remote villages or bustling metropolises. It is the same. The master then freely discussed the main points, only encouraging people to study the Way, saying: "We have struggled for hundreds of years to transform into human form, and you are now in human form, and your progress is nearly complete, yet you live aimlessly and eventually decay like plants, it is truly a pity." The master had a vast knowledge of the Tripitaka and discussed Zen with them, then said: "The status of Buddhism is extremely high, however, if one's practice is not sufficient, once reborn, they will lose their true nature, it is better to seek immortality first, as it is more reliable. I have often met virtuous teachers, and I dare not change my path because of novelty." The master said upon parting: "Today's meeting is also a matter of good fortune, do you have a word of advice for me?" After hesitating for a long time, he said: "Since the Three Dynasties, one might not seek fame, this is what I say to the lower class. Since ancient times, the sages have been calm and composed, without the slightest pretense, the scholars of Lu and Min, with their raised brows and glaring eyes, they create such tangles, please remember this, sir." The master was taken aback and lost his composure. Because the master's demeanor was too severe, he sometimes went too far.

Qiu Wenduo said that he once heard from Shi Dongcun: There was a cavalry officer who had some knowledge of books and enjoyed discussing literary meanings. One night, while on duty at Xuanwu Gate, he went out to cool off and walked to the east of Liqiao. He saw two figures leaning on the wall, talking to each other. Knowing in his heart they were fox spirits, he held his breath and watched them. One of them pointed north with one hand and said: "This was the former Ming dynasty Shou Shan Academy, now it is a Western Catholic church. They calculate celestial phenomena, make instruments, truly ingenious and cannot be surpassed. Their teachings transform Buddhist scriptures and connect them with Confucian principles. I went there before and secretly listened; every time they talked to a point without a conclusion, they would always resolve it by invoking the Lord, so they ultimately could not succeed. But observing their actions, their intentions are also very clever." The other one said: "You say they are clever, but I find them too foolish. They follow the orders of their king, came by sea, only want to convert China to their religion. Considering the situation, how could it be reasonable? Since the time of Matteo Ricci, they continuously come one after another, if they do not achieve their wish, they will not stop."

Isn't this foolish? One might say: Not only are these people foolish, but even those who built the Shou Shan Academy are extremely foolish. Evil eunuchs hold power in the state, secretly looking for opportunities to attack virtuous men, openly criticizing and attacking them, while the virtuous men gather to engage in idle talk, instead being given the label of 'party' or 'faction,' caught in one net, and what blame can there be? Moreover, the 3,000 disciples of Confucius, only Confucius could do it, Mencius thought he could not match Confucius, the ones he taught were only a few, such as Gong Sun Chou and Wan Zhang. The scholars of Lu and Min, without Confucius's moral character, yet they also gathered thousands and tens of thousands of students, eagles and phoenixes gather together, factions compete with each other, thus, they formed factions, and the state perished; the Donglin scholars did not learn from past mistakes, and they pursued empty fame and suffered real disasters. Today, when we visit the remains of the past, how can one not criticize the virtuous ones? As we were sighing together, suddenly turning back, we saw someone, who vanished mysteriously. Dongcun said: The world rushes to them like ducks, yet the foxes and ghosts outside the world are secretly dissatisfied. Are humans mistaken? Are the foxes and ghosts mistaken?

Mr. Wang Xiyuan, while serving as the governor of Hejian Prefecture, heard that people often met ghosts at night along the Balingzhuang River in Xian County. However, the county official Feng Dabang, when he passed by, the ghosts dared not appear. Some people claimed that they had met ghosts and pretended to be Feng Dabang, and the ghosts would also retreat. Mr. Wang, upon hearing this, said, "One county official can make the ghosts fear, there must be a reason for this. I will investigate secretly and punish him." Someone explained, "There is no such thing, it is just what the people have fabricated." Mr. Wang replied, "There are many county officials, but only Feng Dabang is mentioned, there must be a reason for this." He issued an order to arrest Feng, and Feng was frightened and fled. This was an event during the years of Gengwu and Xinwei. After leaving the prefecture for several years, Feng has not yet returned, and now we do not know what has become of him.

In the village, there was a man named Cui who sued a powerful local figure. Although his case was just, he could not prevail. Overwhelmed by anger, he almost wanted to take his own life. One night, he dreamed that his father told him: "People can be deceived, but gods are hard to deceive. People have allies, but gods have none. The more injustice in the world, the more justice in the afterlife. Those who seem to get away with their schemes today will all be trembling in court before a mirror ten years from now. I am a tea server in the underworld and have seen the registrar record it. Why are you so angry?" Cui, upon waking, had all his grievances disappear and no longer spoke a word. There was also a skilled litigator who, one day, was writing a legal document for someone, intending to implicate many people. The threads of the case were entangled, and he could not untangle them. He wanted to sit quietly and think, so he forbade visitors and even had his wife move to another room. His wife had previously been attracted to a neighbor's son, but there was no opportunity for them to meet. After waiting for over a year, she finally found a chance. Later, whenever he tried to think, his wife would make noise to disturb him, and he would shout at her to leave, following this pattern. The neighbor's son took advantage of the opportunity to come, and he also followed this pattern, and throughout his life, he did not fail. After his death, over a year later, his wife became pregnant privately and was accused by an enemy. The official investigated the cause of the affair, and she then fully confessed the truth. The official sighed and said, "This man was skilled with his pen, but how does he know that the Creator is even more skilled?"

A case that cannot be decided is not necessarily beyond reason or morality. The more it seems to lie within reason and morality, the more difficult it is to clarify. At the time when my disciple Wu Sheng Guanxian served as magistrate of An Ding, I returned from military service in the Western Regions and stayed at his office. There, I heard that a young boy and girl, both about sixteen or seventeen years old, had cried out their grievances before the carriage. The boy said: "This girl was my child bride; her parents have died, and they now wish to abandon me and marry her to someone else." The girl said: "I am her biological sister; her parents have died, and they wish to take me as their wife." When asked for their surnames, they could still remember, but when asked about their hometowns, they said that their parents had been wandering beggars, moving from place to place every day, and they no longer remembered where they were from. When asked about those who had traveled with them, they replied: "They have only just arrived here a few days ago, and then both parents died. We do not know the full story, but we only heard them refer to each other as brother and sister. However, in poor families, a child bride and her husband are also commonly called brother and sister, making it impossible to distinguish." An old official then suggested: "This matter is like grasping at wind and shadows—there is no real evidence. We cannot resort to coercion either. Whether we decide to separate them or unite them, we cannot be certain we will be correct. However, if we mistakenly decide to separate them, it would only be a mistake in breaking a marriage, a minor error. But if we mistakenly decide to unite them, it would be a grave disruption of human relationships." He then asked, "Why not decide to separate them?" After much deliberation, there was no clear way to proceed, so we ultimately followed the old official's advice. This made me recall a time when Yao An Gong served in the Ministry of Justice, when the textile official Hai Bao had just had his property confiscated by the state, and three soldiers were stationed to guard his residence.

The house had several hundred rooms. On a deep night, with wind and snow blowing, the three of them firmly locked the outer doors and gathered together in a secluded bedroom to keep warm. They lit lamps and drank together, becoming drunk. Afterward, by chance, the lamp was extinguished, and in the dark, the three of them struck each other, leading to a mutual brawl. They fought until midnight, each exhausted and lying prostrate. By dawn, one of them was dead. The other two, one named Dai Fu and the other named Seventy-Five, were also seriously injured, though fortunately they survived. During the interrogation, both claimed that the death resulted from a mutual beating, and they said there was no grudge between them. In the darkness of that night, when they felt someone twisting, they twisted in return; when they felt someone striking, they struck back. They did not know who was twisting or striking them, nor did they know who they were twisting or striking. The severity of the injuries, and which injury was caused by whom, not only could the two not know—even if the dead person were to be questioned, he could not know either. Since one life does not require two people to be punished, the official could arbitrarily point to one person, and it would be acceptable. If one were to investigate and determine it was a specific person, even with strict interrogation and punishment, it would only result in false confessions. In the end, they could do nothing, and the matter remained unresolved for over a month. Eventually, Dai Fu died of illness, and the case was concluded on that basis. Yao An Gong once said: In this case, assigning blame to the one who started the conflict could be sufficient to conclude the trial.
