But upon examining their statements, the one who started the conflict was actually unaware. Even if they were pressured and interrogated, it would be no better than just randomly pointing at someone. To this day, upon repeated reflection, no clear method of investigation or interrogation has been found. How difficult it is to be a judge of criminal cases!

Wen An Wang Yue Fang said that in his hometown there was a female shaman who could see ghosts. Once she visited a noble household and whispered to a servant woman, saying, "There is a female ghost in front of Madam's bed. She is wearing a greenish-gray robe with blood stains on her chest. Her neck is hanging but not completely severed, and her head is bent backward, hanging upside down from behind. The sight is extremely terrifying. Is she about to become ill?" Soon after, the servant woman developed a sudden and intense fever. She informed the shaman, who prepared paper money and food to send to the ghost. The servant woman instantly recovered. I once believed that cold, heat, and sunstroke can all cause illness, so why must there definitely be a ghost causing trouble? One shaman said, "Illnesses caused by cold, heat, and sunstroke develop gradually and fade away gradually. Ghostly illnesses, however, are sudden and severe, and they stop suddenly. This is the difference." The shaman's observations are accurate and reliable. This statement seems to have some merit.

Chen Shili recounted that a young man from a prominent family was accompanied by several guests to watch a play at the Jiu Ru Lou (Nine Happinesses Pavilion). They were drinking merrily when one of the guests suddenly collapsed due to illness. The others supported and administered first aid, and the guest suddenly sat up, opened his eyes, and looked straight ahead. He began by beating his chest and weeping bitterly, rebuking his son for his dissolute lifestyle. Then, he clenched his fists and scolded the guests for their bad influence, his words and expressions both stern, as if ready to attack or devour them. The son recognized his father's voice and manner, prostrated himself in fear and trembling, looking as if he had lost his color. The other guests all shrank back and fled secretly, some stumbling and falling, injuring their foreheads. Everyone present sighed deeply. This incident occurred in the year 1734 during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, and Chen Shili witnessed it with his own eyes, but he chose not to reveal the names of those involved. His former teacher, Wenqin Gong (A Scholarly and Diligent Master), said: "Families that do not entertain guests will have children who do not associate with scholars and officials, and thus, the children only see maids, servants, and other lower-class individuals, setting a poor example. Families that entertain too many guests will have corrupt friends and bad influences mixed in, befriending and influencing the children, causing endless harm." Through repeated observation and experience over the years, Chen Shili knew that his teacher's words were indeed very effective.

A military leader from the frontier region reported that an old farmer was guarding an orchard at night when he saw a shadow outside the orchard, suspecting it might be a thief. He watched discreetly, and soon a person came from the east and asked, "What are you doing here?" The person replied, "When I was present during the death of someone, I overheard a curse, which I have carried for over twenty years. Now, that person has also been captured, and I am waiting for them to pass by in chains." The depth of human resentment and hatred is indeed profound. Another story involves two individuals, A and B, who had a conflict, but A's wife was unaware of it. After A's death, his wife considered remarriage, and B offered a large sum of money to marry her. Three days after the wedding, they went to visit A's relatives and then took a detour to A's grave. B confronted the wife, asking if she recognized his wife, which enraged her, causing her to nearly harm herself. As they were being restrained, a sudden gust of wind blew, and dust and sand blinded their eyes. Both the husband and wife seemed to lose their senses. After being helped back, they alternated between confusion and waking moments, never fully recovering. The old servant Zhang Cai from my uncle's household, a close relative, witnessed this event. It is said that responding to grievances directly is not forbidden by sages, but excessive retaliation is not condoned. The *Huangdi Neijing* states, "Excessive behavior harms the family." A proverb says, "Excessive pride leads to downfall." B's excessive behavior and pride have led to their inevitable downfall.

The sutra chanted by the monk, its words are quite rustic, yet when one hears the mantras used to summon spirits and offer them food, they are indeed passed down from the Buddha. While I was in Urumqi, I once discussed this matter with fellow scholars, some saying it was true and others doubting it. Bai Liu, a former notorious bandit now serving in the printing office, suddenly said: "It is not a lie. Once, I encountered a wealthy family performing the ritual of offering food to spirits. I wanted to see if there was an opportunity to steal something when they were busy, but there was no chance to take advantage of the situation. I hid on the eaves of a tall building and saw from below the ringing of bells and chanting of mantras. There were countless black shadows, about two or three feet tall, some climbing over the walls, others entering through the holes, moving back and forth, swaying, filling all empty places. When the rice was scattered, they suddenly gathered and dispersed, moving forward and backward, like circling and fighting over the food, and the postures of reaching up and picking up the food, vaguely visible, their color like light smoke, their shape roughly human-like, but their facial features and limbs could not be distinguished. Therefore, if ghosts still seek food, how can one not believe in their existence?"

The Stele of Pei Cen, Governor of Dunhuang, Defeating the Huyan King, located in the Guandi Temple at Bariqin Lake, was discovered buried in the soil by garrison troops who were farming and cultivating the land. The events described on the stele are not recorded in the *History of the Later Han*, yet its phrases are ancient and obscure, and its calligraphy is simple and unadorned, certainly not something fabricated by later generations. Due to its remote location in the Western Regions, no one has made a stone carving copy, and the edges and corners remain intact. In the year Gengyin of the Qianlong period, Liu Cunren, a guerrilla commander — this is his title, and his name has been forgotten — from Wujin, made a wooden copy. He sprinkled gunpowder on it and burned it to create a mottled appearance, making it look exactly like an ancient stele. Both versions have been passed down through the ages, and appreciators generally consider the old stone version to be new and the new wooden version to be old. When confronted with this, they stubbornly refuse to believe it. Even with contemporary objects and witnesses, the truth can be reversed, how much more so with things from thousands of years ago? The hexagrams and numbers of the *Yi Jing*, the short prefaces of the *Shi Jing*, and the three commentaries on the *Chunqiu* — some may have seen the sages in person, some were written not long after the ancients, and the transmission of knowledge from teacher to student is clear. The Song scholars said: "People before the Han dynasty did not know, but we know it through reason." It is similar to this.

In the 14th year of the Kangxi reign (1675), a lion was presented as a tribute from the Western countries. Many senior officials and scholars in the imperial academy wrote poems about it. It is said that the lion soon escaped, moving as swiftly as the wind, breaking free from its cage at the fourth watch (9 AM) and being out of the Jiayu Pass by the fifth watch (10 AM). This story is considered unreliable, akin to "Qi Dong speech" (a term for false or unverified information). During Emperor Kangxi's southern tour, he returned via the Wei River, and the lion was transported by boat. The author's paternal grandmother, Lady Cao, once saw the lion from a window in the Dufan Pavilion on the boat. The lion's body was like that of a yellow dog, its tail like that of a tiger but slightly longer, and its face was round like a human's, unlike the narrow faces of other animals. It was tied to a mast on the front of the boat, and a pig was tied and used as food for it. The pig made no sound when near the boat but cried out when on land. When placed in front of the lion, the lion sniffed it, and the pig died out of fear. When the boat was about to set sail, the lion suddenly roared, its sound like thousands of bronze gongs striking all at once. Horses in the stables, separated by walls, trembled and cowered. The sound continued for some time after the boat had moved, confirming the lion's status as the king of beasts. The lion was initially brought to the imperial court, and Vice Minister Li of the Ministry of Personnel, a contemporary of the famous painters Gu Kaizhi and Lu Tanwei, painted a picture of it. The painting was originally in the collection of the Scholar's Study (Boxi Zhai) and was given to the scholar's ancestor by Vice Minister Li. Later, it was sold to the author, who asked a connoisseur to inscribe a label. Since Vice Minister Li did not sign his name, the connoisseur noted that there had been a similar incident in the Yuan dynasty and titled the painting "True Form of a Lion by a Yuan Dynasty Artist." The scholar said that Vice Minister Li's painting skill was not inferior to that of Yuan dynasty artists, and the title was fitting.

In the year 1760 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, the elder Gē Jièzhōu performed a séance, and the spirit that appeared identified itself as Zhang Ziluan from the Tang dynasty. The spirit claimed to be visiting Liu Changqing on Yingzhou Island and traveling together to Tianmu Mountain. Someone asked the spirit a question, and it responded by writing a poem: > From a distant land I come, > Sometimes I see Yingzhou Island, > The evening breeze is cool as the sun sets, > A lone goose flies into the clouds, far away. The poem suggests a sense of detachment and transcendence, implying that the spirit is not involved in human disputes. Gē Jièzhōu engaged in a discussion about poetry with the spirit, and the spirit responded enthusiastically. The spirit wrote three couplets about the famous scenic spots it visited: Poshishan, Tianmushan, and Lushan, before departing. At the time, Gē Jièzhōu was compiling the county gazetteer of Xian County, so he included the spirit's writings at the end of the gazetteer. The first part of the poem about Poshishan is a five-character regulated verse with eight rhymes, all of which are harmonious. Starting from the ninth rhyme, the poem suddenly shifts to the style of "Xinglu Nan" by Bao Shenzun and "Shudao Nan" by Li Bai, both known for their difficult and challenging styles. This style was not common among Tang poets and does not fit well within the traditional poetic forms. The poem uses the rhymes "dong," "dong," "geng," and "qing" together, imitating the style of Han Yu's "Cǐ Rì Zú Kě Xī," which uses seven rhymes in a single section. This suggests that the spirit has only a superficial knowledge of ancient texts. It is likely that the spirit is a ghost with limited literary knowledge who is pretending to be a Tang dynasty figure.

In the east of the county, fifteen li away, lies Hecheng, which was formerly known as Leishou County during the Sui dynasty. A villager from the west, while digging, discovered a mirror that was more than a zhang in width. Half of the mirror had already been broken. Those who saw it each took a piece and placed it in their rooms, where it emitted light every night. This phenomenon occurred in several households. It is also a divine mirror, similar to those associated with Wang Du, and it reflects the phases of the moon. Despite being broken, it still exhibits extraordinary phenomena. Some wonder why the mirror was so large. I believe it must have been from the palace of the Prince of Hejian. In a letter written by Lu Ji to his brother Lu Yun, he mentioned that there was a large mirror in the Renshou Hall of the Jin dynasty, which was more than a zhang in width and reflected human images. This practice continued into the Jin dynasty.

In the years of Qianlong's reign, during the years Jiaoyou and Gengchen, a tombstone of Zhang Junping from the Tang dynasty was unearthed in Xian County. It was written by Liu Sheng, who passed the Mingjing examination, in the seventh year of the Dazhong period. The calligraphy is still quite good, but the text is particularly vulgar. I showed the rubbing to the elder Li Lianyi, who said: "He said that ancients were superior to modern people in everything. Is this not a Tang text?" People generally boast about their fame and titles. If we examine the facts, those skilled in writing are always praised for the Jin dynasty, but there must also have been very poor writing. Those skilled in poetry are always praised for the Tang dynasty, but there must also have been very bad poems. It does not mean that all servants in the Jin dynasty were like Wang Xizhi and Wang Xianzhi, or that all butchers and vendors in the Tang dynasty were like Li Bai and Du Fu. Xishi and Dongji are actually from the same family; Daozhi and Liuxia are actually brothers. How can it be that all are beautiful or all are virtuous? A connoisseur gets a Song dynasty inkstone, even if it is slippery and does not take ink, it is still treasured like a precious gem. Gets a Han dynasty seal, even if it is flawed and does not form a proper pattern, it is still valued more than pearls and jade. Ask what is the value, he says: it is valued for its antiquity. Su Dongpo's poem says: tastes differ from the common ones in sour and salty flavors. Is this what it means?

An Elderly Scholar from Jiahe Named Liu Zuo Liu Zuo, an elderly scholar from Jiahe, was known for his cautious and kind nature, and was revered as a respectable elder. He taught in my family for over twenty years. His elder brother, Mao Yuan Tanju, and his younger brother, Dongbai Xixuan, were both his disciples. Once, after returning from the annual examinations in Hejian, he encountered rain on his journey and sought shelter in a civilian's home. The host said, "My house has only two rooms, but they are still suitable for staying. However, there is usually a spirit here, whether it be a fox or a ghost. If you can face it without fear, then please unpack your belongings." Reluctantly, Liu Zuo decided to stay. After extinguishing the candle, there was a loud, rumbling sound from the ceiling, like the galloping of angry horses. Liu Zuo got up, dressed in his formal attire, and bowed deeply, saying, "A humble scholar who is out of place, I have accidentally stayed here tonight. Do you wish to harm me? I am not your enemy. Do you wish to play a trick on me? We have never been close friends. Do you wish to drive me away? I will definitely not be able to leave tonight."

The Ming dynasty will also not be able to last, so why go through all this trouble? Soon, a voice from the ceiling, like that of an old woman, said: "The guest's words are indeed reasonable; you all should not act too rashly." The sound of footsteps, tapping and clattering, headed towards the northwest corner, and in an instant, silence returned. Mr. Junzhuo once told his disciples: "When encountering unexpected adversity, remain calm and composed; there might be a resolution. If one were to scold them angrily at that time, one might not avoid throwing bricks and tiles." Another time, Mr. Liu Jingnan rented a dwelling. On the night of moving in, he was greatly disturbed by foxes. Mr. Jingnan scolded them, saying: "I paid money to rent the house; how can you occupy someone else's nest?" The fox answered in a loud voice: "If you occupied it first and I came later to dispute it, the fault would be mine. I have lived in this house for fifty or sixty years, and everyone knows it. Why did you have to rent a house here and live together? It is bullying by force; how could I let you?" The next day, Mr. Jingnan moved away. Mr. He Lian'an said: "The fox encountered by Mr. Junzhuo could be reasoned with, and the fox encountered by Mr. Jingnan could reason with others." My elder brother Qinghu said: "It is easy to bend the fox, but it is difficult to bend oneself before the fox."

Taoism has a practice called the "Yin Transformation Method," which claims that after being buried for several hundred years, one can be reborn. Although there is only this claim, there is no recorded instance of such an event. In ancient times, bodies were preserved with mercury, preventing them from decaying, and this practice is well-documented. Dong Qujiang said: "For those who should have their bodies executed, even if buried for many years, their bodies do not decay. When Lü Liulang's bones were burned, his coffin was opened, and his appearance was still lifelike, and there was still a trace of blood when he was cut." This is believed to be a form of divine punishment. A certain person, a relative of Dong Qujiang, witnessed this event at the time. He was an official in Zhejiang and was sent to handle the matter, and he saw it with his own eyes. However, these preserved bodies do not become spirits that cause harm. Those that do cause harm are called "zombies." There are two types of zombies: one that is a newly buried body that suddenly rises to attack, and another that has been buried for a long time and has not decayed, transforming into a demon-like creature that may appear at night and attack people. Some say that the "dry devil" (a mythical creature causing droughts) is similar to these zombies, but the details are unclear.

When a person dies, the body and spirit separate. If the spirit does not attach to the body, how can it have consciousness or movement? If the spirit does attach to the body, it would be a form of resurrection, but why does it not return as a person but instead become a demon? Moreover, when a recently deceased person's body convulses, their parents and children may hold them tightly, with their fingers digging into the flesh and bones. How could this be if the spirit were not present? How could this be if the spirit had just left? If the spirit does not recognize its loved ones, it is likely that some evil entity has possessed it, or that it is influenced by malevolent energy, rather than being a wandering spirit. The *Xin Qi Xie* of the esteemed Yuan Mei records a story of a scholar's corpse from Nanchang who saw a friend at night, initially begging, then showing gratitude, then feeling sorrow and longing, and finally transforming and attacking. It is said that the soul of a person is good and spiritual, while the spirit is evil and foolish. When the soul first arrives, it is still present, and the spirit follows it. When the soul departs, after the heart's affairs are complete, the soul disperses, and the spirit lingers. When the soul is present, the person is alive; when the soul leaves, the person is no longer the same. The movement of corpses and shadows is caused by the spirit, and only those with virtue can control it. The statement is precise and contains profound reasoning. However, this is merely a narrow view, and I still doubt there might be another reason.

Ren Zitian said that there was a man in his village who was walking at night. Under the moonlight, he saw two people sitting together in the area between the tomb path and the cypress trees. One was a young man, about sixteen or seventeen years old, graceful and attractive. The other was an old woman with long white hair hanging down her neck, bent and carrying a walking stick, seeming to be over seventy or eighty years old. They were leaning on each other and laughing, as if they were very fond of each other. The man was surprised and wondered what kind of lecherous old woman would be intimate with a young man. As he approached, the figures gradually faded away. The next day, he asked whose tomb it was. He then learned that someone had died young, and his wife had been a widow for more than fifty years, and they were buried together there. The poem says: "Living in separate rooms but dying in the same grave. This is the ultimate expression of love." The ritual text says: "The Shang people buried their dead separately; the Zhou people buried them together." Indeed, the sages understood the rituals of the visible and invisible worlds, therefore they could understand the emotions of ghosts and spirits through human emotions. If one does not understand human emotions, how can one know the meaning of rituals?

My cousin Zhaoxian told me that there was a scholar who was studying in a temple when he encountered a Buddhist ritual known as "release from the hungry ghosts." He observed the dignified and orderly manner of the ritual participants and their commands, which seemed to command ghosts and spirits. He sighed and said, "The underworld respects this religion even more than Confucianism." In the dim light of the lamps, an old man appeared beside him and said, "Managing the world relies solely on sages and virtuous people. Buddhism and Taoism are used to supplement what Confucianism cannot achieve. Therefore, the underworld values sages and virtuous people even more than Buddhism and Taoism. However, what is valued are genuine sages and virtuous people. If one falsely claims to be a sage and virtuous person, they secretly anger heaven, and their punishment is even greater than that of false Buddhists and Taoists. In ancient times, such cases were rare, but in the past four or five hundred years, the number of prisoners has been increasing daily, and an additional prison has been created. Followers of Buddhism and Taoism merely cleverly present rewards and punishments to entice people to give alms. Apart from evil groups gathering followers and plotting against the state, there is not one in a thousand who falsely claims to be a god or Buddha. Confucian scholars who consider themselves sages and virtuous people are everywhere. They can deceive the common people, but the principles of the gods are hard to deceive. Therefore, they live in luxury and die, being cast into the Avīci hell. This is because they harm people's hearts and are detested by sages and virtuous people." The scholar was shocked and asked, "How do you know about these underworld affairs?" In a single finger snap, the old man had disappeared.

甲 and 乙 had a long-standing grudge, and 乙 plotted day and night to undermine 甲. 甲 knew about this, so he secretly sent his ally 某, who entered 乙's household through an indirect route. All the plans 某 made for 乙 were perfectly calculated. Whenever 乙 took any action, 甲 secretly assisted with the expenses, saving costs while achieving double the effect. After one or two years, 某 was greatly trusted by 乙, and those who had been trusted and relied upon by 乙 stepped back. Then, taking advantage of an opportunity, 某 persuaded 乙, saying: "In the past, 甲 secretly seduced my wife, but I dared not speak of it, though I harbored deep resentment. Because I lacked the strength to resist, I dared not provoke him. I heard that you also have a grudge against 甲, so I have served you like a loyal dog and horse, dedicating myself to you in gratitude for your kindness, and also for this very purpose. Now there is an opportunity to attack 甲; let us plan together." 乙 was overjoyed beyond expectation, and he gave a large sum of money to 某 to plot against 甲. 某 then used 乙's money to bribe on behalf of 甲, reaching and influencing indirectly in every possible way.

After the well was completed, A fabricated evil deeds against B and presented the names of witnesses to C, ordering them to prepare a document. When it came to court, all the matters were mere fabrications, and even the witnesses turned against him. He was convicted of false accusation and sentenced to military service. Extremely angry and resentful because of the long-standing grudge, A dared not act again, as all his lifetime secrets were in his hands. Finally, he died of suppressed anger. At the time of death, he swore to seek justice in the underworld, but after many years, there was no retribution. Those who commented said that the trouble started with B, and A and B could not coexist. Then A took desperate measures, which were merely self-defense tactics. The fault does not lie with A. The person was originally a spy for A, each being loyal to their own cause, and did not betray B. Therefore, the spirits did not intervene. This matter occurred at the end of the Kangxi era. The *Yue Jue Shu* records Zigan saying to King of Yue: "He who has designs on others and lets them know, is in danger." How can this not be true?

A fellow townsman named Fan Hongxi befriended a fox, who was skilled in drinking, and Fan was equally adept at it. They swore brotherhood and often drank together until they fell asleep in a drunken stupor. Suddenly, the fox stopped coming for a long time. One day, they met in a field of millet. Fan asked why the fox had abandoned him. The fox turned his head and said, "Even biological brothers will turn against each other; what of sworn brothers?" He then left without a word. It turned out that Fan was involved in a lawsuit with his younger brother. In his poem "The White-haired Lament," Yang Tiéyá wrote: "Bought a concubine for a thousand gold pieces, promised her body but not her heart; the gentleman has his own wife, yet he sings the White-haired Lament every night." This is exactly what the fox saw. In the county of Xian, a local police officer named Fan Chang and his colleague caught a notorious bandit. The bandit escaped, but they tied up the bandit's wife in an official inn, which was actually a private residence used by the police for interrogations. His colleague hugged and teased her, and the woman, fearing beatings, remained silent and did not move, only lowering her head and weeping. The rope was already loosened. Fan suddenly saw this and angrily said, "Who doesn't have women, and who can guarantee that women won't suffer hardships? Falling into the hands of others, you dare to act like this. I will report this to the authorities right now." His colleague was afraid and stopped. It was the 17th day of the 7th month in the 4th year of the Yongzheng era, at the hour of Xu. Fan's daughter, who had married a farmer's wife, was kidnapped by the bandit that night. She was already stripped and tied up, on the verge of being raped, but was also stopped by another bandit who scolded her. It was actually at the hour of Zi. There was only one hour of Hai between the two events. The next day, when Fan heard the news, he looked up at the sky, unable to move his tongue.

The Mansion Granted to Duke Qiu Wendada was located within the Stone Tiger Lane, inside the Xuanwu Gate. Before Duke Qiu Wendada, the site had been the Right Wing Imperial Clan School, which in turn had previously been the residence of Prince Consort Wu, and before that, it was the mansion of Zhou Yanru, a grand scholar of the Ming dynasty. Having been in existence for many years, the compound was also steep, deep, and remote, so it was not uncommon for strange occurrences to happen, though they did not harm people. In the western wing of the main hall, there were two small rooms called the Pavilion of Good Spring, where Duke Qiu Wendada would receive guests in a leisurely manner. Behind the north wall, there was another door leading to two more small rooms where servants slept at night. After falling asleep, they were often carried out by spirits, and it was unknown whether these were ghosts or fox spirits, so no one dared to stay there. Only the musician Qian was not afraid, and he experienced no other strange phenomena. Qian had a facial rash and looked extremely old and ugly. Jiang Chun'ong joked, "Your appearance is even more terrifying than a ghost; the ghost must have been scared away." One day, Qian locked the door and went out. Upon returning, he found a fine rain hat on the table, beautifully crafted and as new as if it had never been worn. They passed it around, and everyone was astonished and laughed. From this, they knew that the entity was a fox spirit and not a ghost, but no one dared to take the hat. Qian said, "I am old and frail, often met with contempt. Since the Grand Administrator, and especially when Duke Qiu Wendada served as the Minister of Works, few have shown pity or compassion. My hat is indeed worn out; this fox must feel pity for my poverty." Pleased, he put it on, and the fox no longer took it away. Was this hat truly a gift to Qian? What was the intention of the fox in giving it to Qian? This is truly inexplicable.

Once, I shared a night with Du Shao Sikou Ningtai at the Nan Shiqiao. We heard two families' sedan chair bearers talking to each other: "A strange thing happened yesterday. My cousin Zhu, who was guarding a tomb in Haidian for someone, went into the city and did not return. His wife was alone in the house and heard a fighting sound under a tree in the garden. She broke the window paper to secretly look and saw two people with raised arms fighting. An old man raised a staff to separate them but could not stop them. Suddenly, they fought and fell to the ground, and both revealed themselves as foxes. They jumped and struggled, knocking the old man down as well. The old man got up again and held one fox with one hand. He shouted, 'Wicked son, you are ungrateful. Zhu's fifth wife can help me.' Zhu crouched in fear and did not dare to come out. The old man stomped his foot and said, 'I will report this to the earth god.' He left in anger. The next night, she heard the sound of bells ringing all over the garden, as if there was a search. She felt the clay bottle on the table move slightly and looked at it in surprise. The bottle whispered, 'Please do not speak. I will repay the favor.' Zhu angrily said, 'If you do not repay the favor to your parents, how can you repay me?' He threw the bottle onto the stone base of the monument outside the door, and it shattered with a loud noise. Immediately, he heard a sound of distress, thinking that the fox had been captured. One sedan chair bearer said, 'Fighting and knocking down one's parents, what a big deal, and even being captured by the earth god, it is truly terrifying.' Ningtai looked at me and laughed, saying, 'Only a sedan chair bearer could say such a thing.'

There was an elderly woman named Zhang Aoye who said she had once served as a messenger between the living and the dead, but she had now been released from that duty. She recounted that in the past, when she visited the underworld, she once asked an official there if practicing Buddhism was beneficial. The official replied that Buddhism simply encourages people to do good, and that good deeds bring their own rewards, not because of divine intervention. He further explained that if one were to offer food or other items to Buddha in hopes of receiving blessings, it would be no different from a clean official accepting bribes, which is not the case. She then asked if repentance was beneficial, and the official responded that true repentance requires determination and effort, and that one should strive to make amends for past wrongs. He noted that many people today practice repentance merely to avoid punishment, which is not truly beneficial. Zhang Aoye remarked that such reasoning is not something a typical fortune-teller would say, suggesting that she might have learned it from a higher source or through personal experience.

*Yue Wei Cao Tang Ji* (Notes from the Studio of Reclining in the Shade), Volume Eleven, *Huai Xi Za Ji* (Miscellaneous Records of the Huai Xi) During my second term as a censor, I often handled legal cases with the judiciary, and thus spent many days lodging in the Western Garden. I borrowed a few rooms from my father-in-law's family and named them the "Old House of Huai Xi." After my official duties, I would retire to eat and rest there. The place was several dozen miles from the city, and apart from my subordinates reporting matters, there were very few visitors. The days were long, and I often had spare time, sitting quietly and doing nothing. I had previously written two books, *Puan Yang Xiao Xia Lu* (Records of Summer in Puan Yang) and *Ru Shi Wo Wen* (As I Heard It), which were published by a bookshop. Because of this, friends gathered, and we often shared strange stories. I then placed a notebook in this place, and when it was my turn to be on duty, I would recall and write down various things. If it was not my turn, I would stop. Sometimes I could not recall everything, and I would stop then. As time passed, I had another four volumes. Sun Shuxin compiled them into one volume and titled it *Huai Xi Za Ji*. Its style is still like the previous two books. From now on, perhaps I will be lazy and stop writing? Then I will consider it as the third record of *Hui Chen*. Or perhaps I will be too old and busy to continue? Then I will consider it as the *Yi Jian* (Record of the Strange) of the third volume. In the sixth month of the Ren Zi year, the Observer of Chessman recorded it.

The *Book of Sui* records that Princess Lanling died to accompany her deceased husband, ranking her first in the *Biographies of Exemplary Women*. This somewhat deviates from historical norms — Zu Junyan's proclamation against the Sui mentions that Princess Lanling was forced into a relationship and died, aiming to greatly vilify Emperor Yang. One should rely on historical texts for accuracy. Zhang Zuolin, a local doctor from Cangzhou, recounted a story about a woman in his hometown. After her first husband died, she remarried within a year. Two years later, her second husband also passed away, and she then swore never to remarry again, ultimately remaining faithful to her vows for life. Once, when she asked a neighbor about an illness, the neighbor suddenly glared at her and spoke in the voice of her former husband, saying, "You were willing to remain faithful to him, but not to me, why is that?" The woman responded resolutely, "You did not treat me as your first wife, and in three years, we never had a heartfelt conversation. How could I remain faithful to you? He did not regard me as a second-rate wife, and in two years, our affection and righteousness were deep. How could I not remain faithful to him? You do not reflect on yourself, yet you dare to blame others?" The ghost was speechless and retreated.

This case is similar to that of Princess Lanling. It reflects the principle of reciprocity, as expressed by Yu Ru: "If common people treat me as a common person, I will repay them as a common person; if a noble treats me as a noble, I will repay them as a noble." However, among the five fundamental relationships, only friendship is based on righteousness, and there is no need to calculate repayment; this is a manifestation of benevolence. Even if repayment is considered, it aligns with moral integrity. The relationship between siblings is so natural that it does not require repayment, let alone the hierarchical relationships of ruler-subject, father-son, and husband-wife, which are governed by the principles of the three bonds. Wang Shizhen wrote a poem about Yu Ru's bridge, which reads: "The water by the bridge of the noble, a thousand years of unending sorrow; as if I hear Zhu Lishu, dying to serve Duke Ju of Qi." He believed that this could encourage virtue, and indeed, it does. However, Zhu Lishu, feeling unrecognized and exiled, took his own life to shame the ruler for not knowing his loyal minister—this story is recorded in Liu Xiang's *Shuo Yuan*, and it carries a sense of resentment, merely comparing right and wrong with the ruler, not to protect the state. While the story is commendable, the poem's message may not align with moral principles. Is this a mistake in the records?

Wang Jinying of Jiangning, styled Ju Zhuang, was a scholar whom I selected in the year Renwu (1642) during my role as an examiner. He enjoyed writing poetry, though his talent was somewhat lacking in strength. However, his style was refined and elegant, not overly conventional, and closely resembled the subtle and refined poetry of the late Song Dynasty's Four Spirits. Once, he painted a portrait of himself with chrysanthemums, and I playfully imitated his style to write a poem for it, with lines such as "taking his name from the chrysanthemum, entering the painting with the flowers." Ju Zhuang was delighted, and thus we could understand his preferences. He compiled several volumes of poetry commentary, but it remained incomplete. Over time, his manuscripts were lost, perhaps scattered by the passage of seasons. I still remember one entry from his commentary, which described a dilapidated house in Jiangning where faint traces of writing were found on the walls. After brushing away the dust and examining it closely, five quatrains were discovered. The first one read: "New green grows while faded red becomes scarce, a beauty's tears wet her silken robe. Butterflies do not care if spring returns, only flying where the mustard flowers turn yellow." The second one read: "Year after year, the swallows of the Six Dynasties return, but the Zhuque Bridge has fallen and no flowers bloom. There is no need to be sorrowful, asking about the Wang and Xie families, for Liu Lang, once gone, never returned." The third one read: "Ruined pools and abandoned halls are overgrown with grass, where young men used to sing and dance. After the drum beats of the谯楼 (a watchtower) ceased, the gentle wind blew through the climbing vines." The fourth one read: "Moss and algae surround the crumbling walls, within which lie the spirits of peach leaves and roots. In the deep night, the moonlight covers the steps, but alas, the delicate shoes leave no trace." The fifth one read: "On Tomb-Sweeping Day, orioles cry everywhere, but the spring breeze does not reach the withered willow branches. Only the twin stone beasts by the stream might remember when you once hung golden threads." The handwriting was bold and impressive, yet no name was given. It is uncertain whether these words were written by a human or a spirit. I believe these quatrains were composed by former officials or scholars of the Ming dynasty after the fall of the Southern Ming.

Dong Qiuyuan said, when he was a scholar-official in Juye, there was a gatekeeper who managed the memorial hall for filial piety and chastity, and he brought his family to live near the hall. One day during the autumn sacrifice, the gatekeeper got up at night to sweep and clean, while his wife was still asleep. In his dream, he saw dozens of women linking arms and entering the hall. He knew that spirits had descended, but he was not afraid. Suddenly, he recognized two poor old women among them. After careful examination, he confirmed that they were indeed the ones he knew. He was surprised and asked why they had not been honored, yet were also there. One of the old women replied: In the world, honors cannot reach every poor village and humble house. Those who are forgotten and unrecognized exist everywhere. The spirits pity their suffering. Even if the hall does not have a place set for them, they are still invited to come and partake. Some hide their flaws and filth, and falsely claim to be fragrant. Even if they have a place set in the hall, they are not allowed to enter. That is why we two are here. This story is quite unusual. However, considering the principles of the spirit world, it seems appropriate. Another official named Wei in the ritual office of Xian County, on his deathbed, muttered to himself: I serve in a minor office, and I thought I had never done anything wrong. I did not expect that a poor woman would request an honor, and demand the usual fee. The punishment in the afterlife is so severe. These two stories complement each other. Indeed, loyalty, filial piety, chastity, and righteousness move heaven and earth and stir the spirits.

My cousin Xingzhi told me about a story: A farmer's wife and her sister-in-law were both exceptionally beautiful. One moonlit night, they went out to cool off and slept under the eaves. Suddenly, they saw a ghost with red hair and a green face emerge from behind the cowshed, twirling, leaping, and jumping as if it were going to attack and devour them. At that time, all the men were outside guarding the fields, so the sisters-in-law were terrified and dared not speak. The ghost seized them one by one and forced them to endure its foul actions. Just as it was about to leap onto a short wall, it suddenly let out a cry and fell to the ground. Seeing that it remained motionless for a long time, they dared to call for help. The neighbors rushed over to see what was happening. They found that one ghost was inside the wall, which turned out to be a young rascal from the village who had already fainted and was unconscious. The other ghost stood upright outside the wall, which was actually a clay figure from the village god's shrine. The elders of the village said that the village god was truly powerful and decided to offer a sacrifice the next morning. A young man then said in disbelief: "Majia always goes out to carry manure at dawn. I was just playing a prank by grabbing the clay figure from the shrine and placing it by the roadside to scare him, just for a laugh. I never expected to encounter this false ghost and mistakenly thought it was a real one, which frightened him so much that he fell. How powerful is the village god?" An old man replied: "Majia carries manure every day. Why didn't you play a prank on him another day, but chose this day? There are many ways to play a prank. Why did you suddenly grab this clay figure? Why did you place it outside this house's wall? There was a spirit actually possessing it, and you didn't know." They then collectively offered money for a sacrifice. The young rascal was carried away by his parents and lay in bed for several days, never recovering.

In the southwest of Taiguxian, Shanxi province, fifteen li from the village of Baicheng, there stands a temple dedicated to the Tuhu Shén (Confused God). The local people observe this deity with great reverence, believing that even slight disrespect can lead to adverse weather conditions such as wind and hail. However, no one knows which historical figure the deity represents or why it bears this name. Later, upon checking the general history, it was revealed that the deity is actually the Fox Tu (狐突) temple. It was ordered to be built in the third year of the Zhongtong era during the Yuan Dynasty (1262 AD), and its original name was the Temple of the Fox Tu God Who Brings Good Fortune. The words "fox" and "confused" sound similar in Chinese, and in northern dialects, they are pronounced similarly. Therefore, the name "Tu" (突) was changed to "Tu" (涂). This is yet another example of a phonetic play, similar to the case of Du Shiyi.

Natural images are often found in stones. Jiang Shao-shu, in his *Yunshi Xuan Biji*, recounts seeing a stone that formed a Taiji diagram, much like a spiral pattern in the stone's texture, with black and white sections. Yan Jiezi once saw an inkstone from Yingde with a white vein forming the words "mountain high, moon small," clearly and distinctly. The vein penetrates directly through the back of the stone, vaguely resembling the reverse side of the characters, but it is blurry and scattered, lacking the dots, strokes, and hooks of writing. Upon close inspection, it is neither carved nor inlaid, nor stained; it is truly a natural formation, isn't it extraordinary? Mountains and the earth both exist, and stones and mountains both exist. Could it be that since the beginning of creation, they had already known about Cheng Miao's clerical script? Or about Su Shi's *Chibi Fu*? Even if we say that the mountain gave birth to this stone in the Song Dynasty, who then made these characters, and who inscribed these words? Thus, the skill of heaven is limitless, the essence coiled and condensed, forming its own text. Such phenomena are beyond the understanding of ordinary logic. The *Hetu Luoshu* (River Map and Luo Book) are said to have originated in the Northern Song Dynasty, but they were not seen before the Tang Dynasty. The *Hetu* consists of fifty-five black and white circles, and the *Luo Shu* consists of forty-five black and white circles. According to Kong Anguo's commentary on the *Analects*, the *Hetu* is the same as the *Bagua* (Eight Trigrams). (Kong Anguo's commentary on the *Analects* is no longer extant; this passage is quoted in He Yan's collected commentary on the *Analects*.)

）Is it that the Confucian school originally had no diagram of fifty-five points? How did Chen Tuan obtain it? As for the Luo Shu, since it is called a book, it should have writing, yet it also has forty-five circles, similar to the He Tu. Therefore, it should be called the Luo Tu rather than the Luo Shu. How does the *Xici* (Commentary on the Appended Phrases) distinguish it as a book? Liu Xiang, Liu Xin, and Ban Gu all called the Luo Shu to have writing, and Kong Yingda's *Shangshu Zhengyi* (Commentary on the Book of Documents) meticulously records the number of characters. (In the *Hongfan* (Great Pattern) section of the *Shangshu* (Book of Documents), the *Shu* (Book of Documents) fully records this chapter, stating that these sixty-five characters are all from the Luo Shu. Considering the brevity and essential nature of heavenly words, there must be no sequential numbering. The twenty-seven characters including "敬用農用" (revere and use agriculture) were added by Yu. The eighteen characters including "敬用" (revere and use) were believed by the elder Liu and Gu to already exist on the turtle shell, totaling thirty-eight characters. The younger Liu believed that "敬用" and others were all arranged by Yu, and the turtle shell only had twenty characters.) Although the number of characters differs, it is sufficient to show that from the Han to the Tang dynasties, the Luo Shu did not have a false diagram of black and white dots. By observing this stone mountain, we know that the stone patterns form characters, and it is clearly not a mistake. It is not advisable to cling to Lu Bian's later theory. (The theory that the nine rooms of the Mingtang (Hall of Brightness) follow the pattern of the turtle shell first appeared in Lu Bian's commentary on the *Dai Liji* (Great Records of Rites) during the Northern Qi dynasty. Zhu Xi mistakenly recorded Zheng Xuan's theory.) Thus, the Taiyi Nine Palaces Diagram is truly what the divine Yu received. (The Luo Shu used by modern practitioners is the Taiyi Nine Palaces method, derived from the *Yi Lü* (Book of Changes Apocrypha) *Gan Zao Du* (Tilling the Earth), which is what the *Han Shu* (Book of Han) *Yi Wen Zhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) calls the Taiyi school, and it should be clearly understood that it is not called the Luo Shu.)

My cousin Liu Xiangwan recounted that in his days serving in Fujian, he heard of a young woman who was naturally quiet and reserved. After her death, she was buried at the foot of a mountain. Every night when the moon was bright, people would see her spirit tied with her hands behind her back and bound to a tree from afar. As one approached, she would become invisible, and no one could understand the reason for this. I said, "This must be a sign. People do not understand why she is being punished, but they are made to see her punishment, showing what is unknown to them, which only the spirits and deities know." Tian Fengya, a high-ranking official, recounted that a boy around fourteen or fifteen years old would often moan in his sleep. They suspected he was ill, but he denied it. Later, he would sometimes mumble incoherently, and even when called, he would not wake up. His words were quite clear, and upon careful listening, they were all lewd and indecent. His moaning was also influenced by obscene sounds. However, when asked, he would not speak. They realized he was possessed by a spirit and submitted a petition to the village god. That night, he dreamed of the village god saying, "There is indeed a spirit. It is beyond my power to control." So, they submitted a petition to the city god. The next morning, a clay statue of a horse attendant in the city god's temple had its head fall off for no apparent reason. They then understood what the village god meant by saying he lacked the power to control it. Yet, it was just a minor official, and it might not have been someone the city god loved. Even if the city god loved him, being a just and wise deity, he would not bend the law to protect a minor official. By submitting a petition, the spirit was punished in the afterlife, and the city god's intentions became clear. The village god, however, was cautious and fearful, enduring in silence and not daring to speak, wondering how he viewed the city god. How did the city god view this village god?

Three notes by Governor Zhao: one about a night encounter with a fox girl, who approached and teased him, then suddenly disappeared. Soon after, a flying tile struck and knocked off his hat. The next day, upon waking, he saw a poem written in fine script on the window paper, which read: "Deep courtyard, branches full of flowers, only butterflies should collect nectar. Grass under the grass, insects, you have weeds here." The language was unusually light, yet the style was elegant and charming, fitting that she did not like wealthy young men. Tian Baiyan said, "I once practiced feng shui divination with friends, and the spirit identified itself as 'True Mountain Man,' a reclusive gentleman from the end of the Song dynasty. According to records, 'True Mountain Man' has a collection of poems, which is now recorded in the Four Treasuries. The exchange of poetry was harmonious, but then an announcement was made about the arrival of certain guests, and the divination tool suddenly stopped. One guest had too much worldly experience and was too familiar with social interactions, so they would certainly use flattery in their conversation. 'Cloud and water wanderer' is not skilled in social interactions, so it would be better to avoid them. Another guest had too much mental detail and was too formal in their manners, constantly scrutinizing every word and being overly critical. 'Free-spirited wanderer' cannot tolerate such strict demands, so they would prefer to escape quickly rather than face such demands." Later, Yao An Gong heard this and said, "This spirit is indeed a reclusive and upright person, but their capacity is not broad."

My paternal uncle, Mao Yuan, recounted that during the provincial examination in the year 1766 (Qianlong's 21st year), he was assigned to sit in the autumn number examination room. When another person entered the room, the examination guard asked for his name and place of origin. The guard then bowed and congratulated him, saying: "Last night, I dreamt that a woman held a branch of apricot flowers and placed it on the examination room. She told me: 'Tomorrow, someone from County X will arrive, and he will say that apricot flowers are here. Your name, surname, and place of origin match perfectly, is this not a good omen?'" The person was startled and pale, and he did not participate in the examination, claiming illness and leaving. A villager who knew the story said: "This man once had a young servant named Apricot Flower, whom he forced and abandoned, and she eventually wandered off, dying with resentment." My cousin's son, Shu Sen, recounted that a man from Shanxi entrusted his assets to his younger brother while going out to do business. While away, he married a woman and had a son. After more than ten years, the woman fell ill and died, and he brought the son back. The brother feared that he would demand the return of the assets, so he falsely accused the son of being adopted from a different surname and therefore not entitled to inherit the father's property. The dispute remained unresolved, and they took the case to court. The judge was incompetent and did not investigate the business matters, but instead relied on the ancient method of blood testing to determine kinship. Fortunately, the blood matched, and the judge beat and expelled the brother. The brother did not believe in the blood test and pricked blood from his own son, which did not match. He then appealed the decision, arguing that the county magistrate's judgment was not based on solid evidence. The villagers disliked the brother's greed and jealousy, and they all said: "The woman had been intimate with him secretly for a long time, and the son is not his biological child, so the blood should not have matched."

The testimonies were clear and numerous, with clear evidence, finally confirming the adulterous affair. The woman's lover was arrested and interrogated, and he confessed with a bowed head. The younger brother was ashamed to the point of being unable to bear it, and he eventually expelled the woman and her son, fleeing away in disgrace. All assets were returned to the elder brother, and those who heard were pleased. According to *The Biographies of Eminent Men of Yu Nan*, there is a historical anecdote about blood tests to determine kinship. This method has been known since the Han dynasty. However, I have heard from experienced officials that blood from kin must always mix, in general. But in winter, placing a container on ice and snow to make it extremely cold, or in summer, wiping the container with salt and vinegar to make it have a sour and salty taste, then the blood that is dropped will coagulate upon entering the container, even if the individuals are close relatives, they may not mix. Therefore, blood tests are not sufficient to establish a reliable verdict. If this official had not performed the blood test, the younger brother would not have appealed, and if the younger brother had not appealed, the affair between the woman and her lover, and the child they had, would not have been exposed. This seems to be as if someone had caused it, and it cannot be entirely blamed on this official for adhering to ancient practices.

The Python at the Yushi Yuan, as I have recorded in the *Pulangyang Xiaxia Lu* (Record of Summer at Pulangyang), I have seen its traces twice, and it is not an imaginary or nonexistent creature. The officials and staff were afraid of it, and none dared to venture deep into the warehouse. In the second month of the Renzi year, I was ordered to repair the Yushi Yuan office, and upon opening the warehouse for inspection, I saw nothing at all, realizing that the emperor's presence had subdued all spirits. The head of the Yushi Yuan, General Shumu, then mentioned that the tomb of the cabinet scholar Zha Gong also had a large python, which was often seen far away as it moved in and out, exposing its scales. In front of the tomb were two locust trees, about ten paces apart, with the snake's head and tail hanging from each tree, its body resembling a rainbow spanning across. After selecting a grave site for his mother, he offered sacrifices and prayers, and indeed, the snake led its thousands of relatives away in a winding manner. After the burial, it returned. When it left, it moved like the wind, but gradually shrank until it was only a few feet long, demonstrating its ability to grow and shrink, already possessing the skills of a divine dragon. Thus, I understood that the python at the Yushi Yuan, whose girth was like a pillar, could still enter and exit through a gap of just an inch, much like this. In that same month, while at the home of Maguan in Shanxi with Vice-Chancellor Wang Jiaoxue, I encountered an official from the Imperial Household Department who said that the Western Ten Warehouses, where sulfur was stored, also had two pythons. Both had a horn on their heads and golden scales. When the lock was about to be opened, they would first sound a drum or bell. The most unusual thing was that every time the lock was opened, sulfur would pile up inside the door, like a fake mountain, sufficient for use, and it would replenish itself when taken. It seems these pythons did not want people to enter the warehouse, and no one dared to do so either. Some say they are the guardian spirits of the warehouse, and this might be true. The *Shan Hai Jing* (Classic of Mountains and Seas) records the gods of various mountains, many of which have snake bodies and bird heads, taking on various strange forms, not necessarily in human shape.

My elder brother Qinghu once told me about a man named Wang Zhensheng. In his old age, he lost his beloved son and was grief-stricken to the point of wanting to die. One night, he passed by his son's grave, lingering there in sorrowful attachment and unable to leave. Suddenly, he saw his son sitting alone on the hilltop. He hurriedly approached him, and the ghost did not flee. However, when he tried to take his hand, the ghost pulled away. When he spoke to the ghost, it appeared indifferent and seemed not to want to listen. Surprised, he asked the ghost why. The ghost smiled and said, "The relationship between father and son is a predestined bond. When the bond is severed, you are you, and I am I. Why should we continue to seek each other out?" Then, the ghost turned its head and left. From that moment on, Wang Zhensheng's grief and longing suddenly vanished. Someone remarked, "If Xihe could understand this meaning, he would not have lost his sight." My elder brother replied, "This is the deepest feeling of a filial son, creating this transformation to cut off his father's sorrowful thoughts, much like the intention behind Xi Chao's secret letter. It is not the true principle. If people hold this view, treating father and son, brothers, and spouses equally as if they were strangers meeting by chance, will they not gradually become more superficial?"

An official took in a concubine, who was graceful and beautiful in appearance, with charming words and laughter, and good at pleasing others. However, when she sat alone, she appeared contemplative and as if lost in thought. One grew accustomed to it and no longer found it surprising. One day, she claimed to be ill, locked the door, and lay down during the day. The official peeped through a hole in the window paper and saw her applying makeup, meticulously arranging her hairpins, bracelets, dresses, and skirts, then setting out wine and fruit as if for some kind of offering. Bursting in to ask, the concubine, with a troubled expression, clasped her sleeves and knelt to say, "I was once the favored concubine of a certain scholar-official. The scholar-official was about to die, and I anticipated that his wife would not tolerate me. Worried that I might be sold into a brothel, so I was sent away first. At parting, she gave me earnest private instructions, saying, 'You may not resent marrying me, but if you marry someone who treats you well, I will be even more comforted. But on my anniversary, you must in a private room, dress up and privately offer a sacrifice to me. If my spirit comes, you will see the sign of incense smoke swirling around you.'" The official said, "Xu Yuan did not betray Li Yu, and the Song emperor did not punish him. What harm is there in listening to you?" The concubine bowed twice, lit incense, and her tears fell into the tray. The smoke indeed swirled three times around her cheeks, gradually winding its way to her feet. Wen Tingyun's poem "Damo Zhi Qu" speaks of grinding musk into dust, the fragrance remains, and forcing a lotus to break into threads, it is hard to sever. Is this what it means? Even if she plays the pipa with another, she has already repaid her old favor. Yet her body leaves while her heart remains. Isn't this better than sleeping on the same bed but dreaming different dreams?

In Jiaohe, a memorial archway was erected for a chaste widow. Her relatives and acquaintances gathered for the occasion. One of her distant sisters, who had teased her since childhood, playfully asked her, "You have now lived a life of chastity until old age. But during these forty years, were there ever any moments of romantic or pleasurable thoughts during the beautiful spring days and moonlit nights?" The widow replied, "People are not plants or trees; how could we be without emotions? But I felt that propriety could not be violated, and righteousness could not be betrayed. I was able to control myself and not act on those feelings." One day, after completing the Qingming Festival rites to honor her ancestors, she suddenly became dizzy and began to mutter incoherently. She was helped back home and did not regain consciousness until nightfall. When she awoke, she told her son, "Just now, I had a vision of your father, who said he would soon come to meet me. He also consoled me greatly, saying that the spirits know everything we do in this world. Fortunately, I have lived a life without blemish. Otherwise, how could I face him in the afterlife?" Six months later, she indeed passed away. This story was recounted by Wang Xiaolian Mei Xu. Mei Xu commented, "The Buddhist prohibition against evil thoughts is a fundamental practice that only the noble can achieve. Ordinary people are often preoccupied with various thoughts, but if they fear and dare not act on them, they are still virtuous." The widow's descendants were somewhat reluctant to speak of this matter, and I, too, do not dare to mention her family name. However, her words are as clear and honest as the bright sun in the clear sky, truly a statement of integrity. Why should such a noble truth be concealed?

During the time when Yao'an Gong was supervising the South New Granary, one of the granary rooms' rear wall collapsed unexpectedly. Upon digging, nearly a bushel of dead mice was found, with the larger ones shaped almost like cats. It is because the mice had burrowed beneath the wall, multiplying daily, and their burrows also expanded daily until the wall below was completely hollow. The structure could no longer bear the weight and collapsed. His colleague, Fu Gong Hai, remarked, "When they destroy others' houses to expand their own, they almost forget that their own house is supported by the walls of others." I say that figures like Li Linfu and Yang Guozhong still do not understand this principle; what fault do they find in the mice? My great-great-grandfather, Runsheng Gong, once met a monk in Xiangyang who was originally a retainer of Wei Dengxiang. The monk provided a detailed account of the banditry, and together they lamented the difficulty of changing fate. The monk said, "According to me, fate is shaped by human actions, not by heaven." In the final years of the Ming dynasty, the brutality of killings, rapes, and looting was so severe that the bloodshed of Huang Chao's rebellion, which spanned three thousand miles, pales in comparison. From the mid-period of the Ming dynasty onwards, officials were generally corrupt and cruel, gentry were violent and domineering, and the common people were rife with theft, fraud, and deceit. As a result, deep-seated resentment and anger accumulated, angering the gods, and after centuries of accumulated grievances, it erupted in a single day. From what I have seen and heard, those who suffered the most severe calamities were those who had committed the worst evil deeds.

Can this be called fate? In the past, while in the rebel camp, I saw one of the captives, a nobleman, bound and kneeling before the tent, while the captor and his concubines drank wine. The captor asked if he dared to be angry, and the nobleman replied, "No." The captor then asked if he was willing to serve, and the nobleman replied, "Yes." The captor then released the bindings and had him serve wine on the side. Some of the spectators sighed with pity, and an elderly captive who had fallen into the hands of the enemy said, "I now understand the concept of cause and effect. His ancestor once teased a servant woman, and when the servant spoke out against it, he was beaten and tied to a locust tree, with others made to watch as he lay with the woman. This is just one example, but the principle can be extended to many others." A powerful man in the audience said, "A big fish swallows a small fish, a fierce bird preys on a flock of birds; the gods do not punish such actions, so why should they punish humans?" The monk shook his head and said, "Those are fish and birds, but are humans fish and birds too?" The powerful man then stood up and left in anger. The next day, he invited guests to visit the temple where he resided, intending to humiliate the monk. After removing a scroll from the wall, he wrote twenty characters on it: "You need not speak, nor do I need to explain. The ground floor is empty, but the upper floor has a bright moon." It is suspected that these words were meant to criticize the powerful man's private affairs. Later, the powerful man's entire family was destroyed.

A court official capsized a boat in the Wei River, and a concubine drowned. When her body was found, each of her hands held a handful of millet. Everyone found it strange, but an old man by the riverbank said, "There is nothing to be surprised about. Anyone who sinks in water sees darkness above and light below. In panic and confusion, they must try to escape from the brighter area, and they all scoop up soil. Therefore, to check if someone drowned, one should examine whether their fingernails have soil or not. If they do, it indicates they were thrown into the water." This means that there was a boat carrying millet that sank earlier, and the millet had not yet rotted, so she scooped it up. This argument is very detailed. However, the reason why it is darker above and brighter below cannot be explained. According to Zhang Heng's *Lingxian*, the sun is like fire, and the moon is like water. Fire emits light outward, while water reflects light inward. According to Liu Shao's *Renzhi*, fire and the sun emit light outward and cannot be seen from within; metal and water reflect light inward and cannot emit light outward. Therefore, it is naturally darker above and brighter below, which is the inherent nature of water.

Cheng Nianlun, also known as Sihao, came to the capital during the Qianlong era, from the years of Guiyou to Jiaxu, and was renowned as a national chess master. Mao Xiangzhu of Rugao remarked, "Both of us are second-rate players; there is no one who is truly the best, so we immediately boast of being the best." One day, his disciple Huwushu and others performed a divination session, asking the spirit if it was skilled at chess. The spirit replied that it could. They then asked if it would be willing to play against ordinary people, and the spirit said yes. At the time, Cheng Nianlun was staying at my home, and they decided to play together. In chess, moves are recorded by the pieces, while in Xiangqi, moves are recorded by the lines. When playing with the spirit, they used the Xiangqi method, such as placing a piece on the ninth vertical line and third horizontal line, and the spirit would respond with "nine three," and so on. Initially, after a few moves, Cheng Nianlun was completely confused and didn't understand, thinking the spirit's moves were unpredictable. He feared losing his reputation and pondered deeply, until his back was sweaty and his hands were trembling, before he finally dared to respond with one move, still feeling anxious. After a while, he seemed to feel no other strange occurrences and then freely attacked. The spirit ended up losing the entire game, and the entire room was in an uproar. The divination board suddenly wrote in large characters, "I am a spirit, temporarily here to play, posing as Zhang Sanfeng. Because I have a basic understanding of chess, I answered rashly. I did not expect to be trapped by this gentleman. I am now leaving." Huwushu sighed and said, "Even on the streets of Chang'an, ghosts can deceive people." I jokingly said, "After one defeat, they reveal the truth. Still a dull ghost on the streets of Chang'an."

Mr. Shen Qianju of Jingzhou, whose courtesy name was Xu, was a contemporary of Yao An Gong in the year 1634. He had a gentle and easygoing nature, and throughout his life, he never showed a displeased face. Yet, he was lofty and unique, refusing even the smallest thing, and had the demeanor of an ancient recluse. He always wore coarse cloth robes and ate coarse food. Once, his students offered him sacrificial meat, and he took it to the market to exchange for tofu, saying, "I did not do this to be different, but because I am not used to eating meat." Once, he returned from the annual examination in Hejian and had a child guide a donkey. When the child was tired, he would then ride and guide it himself. In the late afternoon, he encountered rain and sought shelter in a dilapidated shrine. The shrine had only one room, with nothing inside, and the ground was dirty and unsuitable for sitting. He then took a door panel and lay it horizontally in front of the door. In the middle of the night, he woke up and heard a small voice from the shrine saying, "I want to leave to avoid you, but you are blocking the door." Mr. Shen replied, "You can stay inside the door, and I can stay outside; we will not harm each other, why avoid each other?" After a while, the voice said again, "There is a difference between men and women; you should let me out." Mr. Shen responded, "Being inside or outside the door is already a difference; if you come out, there will be no difference." He then turned over and slept soundly. In the morning, a villager saw him and was startled, saying, "There is a fox here, which often flirts with young men, and anyone who enters the shrine is hit with stones. Why are you so calm?" Later, he happened to talk about this with Yao An Gong, who laughed and said, "There was a fox that wanted to flirt with Mr. Qianju, which is also a strange thing." Yao An Gong joked, "Even if the fox flirts with everyone in the world, it would never flirt with you. It must be a strange and unusual form that the fox has never seen, so it is afraid and wants to escape." One can imagine the character of Mr. Shen from this.

Mr. Dong Qujiang, the senior, said that during the provincial examination of the Ding Mao year during the Qianlong era, he was residing in a Buddhist temple in Jinan. In his dream, he arrived at a place where he saw an old tree with a dilapidated hut beneath it, leaning and about to collapse. Inside the hut, a woman dressed up and sitting by the door appeared red with sorrow and green with despair, pitifully oppressed. He suspected he had mistakenly entered someone's inner room and stopped, daring not to enter. The woman suddenly bowed to him from afar, her tears streaming down and wetting her sleeves, but finally said nothing. His heart palpitated, and he woke up. After several nights, the dream recurred, and the woman looked even more sorrowful, bowing her head over a hundred times. He wanted to force a question from her, but suddenly woke up, still unable to understand. He told the fellow lodgers, but nobody could understand. One day while taking a walk in the temple garden, he saw an old coffin under the eaves, already rotten. Suddenly, he looked up at the tree and found it exactly as seen in the dream. He asked the temple monk, who said it was the beloved concubine of a certain official, left here temporarily, awaiting someone to come and retrieve her. For decades now, there had been no news, and the monk dared not move or bury her, being at a loss for a long time. Mr. Dong suddenly understood clearly, since he was on good terms with the magistrate of Licheng. They then collected money and bought half an acre of land, informed the authorities, and had her reburied. Thus knowing that the dead find peace in the earth, leaving them in limbo is not what their spirits desire.

Zhu Qinglei said that Gao Xiyuan once dreamed of a guest visiting, with a name card that read "Simashi Xiangru." He was startled and woke up, not understanding what omen this was. A few days later, he accidentally found a jade seal named "Simashi Xiangru." It had ancient luster, with intricate and beautiful seal script, truly carved by a Kunwu knife. He always wore it, never leaving it off his body, and only those closest to him could see it once. When he was in charge of the salt field, Lu Zhenya, the governor of Lianghuai, was in office. During a casual meeting, Lu Zhenya asked to see it. Xiyuan left his seat, half-kneeling, and spoke seriously: "Fenghan has always made friends, and everything he owns can be shared with friends, except for two things: this seal and his wife." Lu Zhenya laughed and sent him away, saying, "Who would take your things? How foolish you are!" Xiyuan's painting skills were extremely high, but later he contracted a minor illness, causing his right arm to wither. He then used his left arm to paint, although it was stiff and stubborn, it became even more interesting. His poetry style was also free and unrestrained, although he held a minor official position, he died in obscurity. Among the literati and officials of recent times, he could still follow the style of his predecessors. Yang Tiayan's literary works were strange and beautiful, although he was called a "literary demon," it did not harm his reputation. Only the shoe cup incident, which was indecent and obscene, can be said to be the most unrefined, but it was written about and became a good story. Later, reckless and frivolous young men competed to imitate it, thinking it was the style of a distinguished scholar, which is hard to understand. It is said that in a large family, during a mid-year ancestral worship, as they were setting up the wine on the table, suddenly a cup broke with a sound like firecrackers, splitting in half. No one understood why. After a while, it was known that a few days before, his son had invited a courtesan and used this cup to imitate Yang Tiayan's story.

The Fairy Butterflies of the Taichang Temple, the Prosperous Pines of the Guozijian, and the Grace of the Emperor's Words are all well-known. The Golden Catalpas of the Hanlin Academy, with branches that can be embraced by several people and burls and knots like a rockery, are also known to some. However, the Longevity Grass of the Ministry of Rites is less widely recognized. This grass blooms with red flowers in spring, adorned like fire opals, and in autumn produces fruit resembling pearls, yet it is not recorded in the Compendium of Flowers or the Compendium of Wild Vegetables, and its name is unknown. Some say it is the "Old Public Road of the Field" — this grass is planted on the borders of two fields to mark boundaries; if the plow does not reach it, it does not grow laterally, but if the plow slightly invades it, it spreads without end, even beyond the invaded area, hence its name. Upon careful examination, its leaves are shaped like saw teeth, somewhat similar, but its flowers are different, so this explanation is incorrect. It is located north of the hall, in front of the office steps, and west of the passage, and it is believed to have originated from the early days of the nation. Over time, it has gradually become a climbing plant. Now it has divided into two branches, with branches spreading out like a lattice, and it stands as a sturdy old tree. Mr. Cao Dishan named it the Everlasting Grass. When I served as the Minister of Rites, I built wooden railings to protect it. My disciple, Governor Chen Mei, who was then an official in the Ministry of Rites, was ordered to make a painting of it. This is because the profound influence and harmony that sustain life, even a single blade of grass or insect, each finds its way to thrive in this manner. The Ministry of Rites also has Interconnected Catalpa Trees, located under the southern eaves of the斋戒处. Mr. Zou Xiaoshan, who served as a Grand Secretary, once painted a picture and composed a poem about them, which is still stored in the treasury. However, these are simply two catalpa trees of different sizes, growing side by side with their branches and trunks intertwined. They are not truly interconnected.

Daoism speaks of prayers to avert misfortune, Buddhism of repentance, and Confucianism of cultivating virtue to overcome evil. The two former address the symptoms, while Confucianism addresses the root cause. An ancestor of mine, the Lord of Leiyang, kept several sheep. One day, a sheep suddenly stood upright and danced. The crowd thought this was an omen of ill fortune and intended to kill the sheep. The Lord of Leiyang said, "How could a sheep dance? There must be something supernatural at play." He recalled the story of a stone speaking in the state of Jin, as recorded in the *Zuo Zhuan*, which clearly illustrates the principle of divine warning. The disaster has already occurred; what good would killing the sheep do? The disaster has not yet occurred, and the spirits are warning me through this event. The only solution is to cultivate virtue. Is it necessary to kill the sheep? From then on, every word and action was conducted as if in the presence of sages. Later, in the twelfth year of the Shunzhi reign (1655), he was selected as a boshi (tribute scholar), and in the fifteenth year of the Yongzheng reign (1727), he passed the second level of the imperial examinations, eventually becoming a tongpan (assistant governor). Throughout his life, he never suffered any misfortune. My third elder brother, Xiaodong, once returned from the imperial examinations in the year of Dingwei during the Yongzheng reign (1727). He encountered a beggar woman whose mouth grew out of her neck, yet she ate and drank like a normal person. Was she a monster? I replied, "This is merely a case of being affected by a strange qi; she is not a monster." Even physical anomalies like extra toes or fingers, which are unusual, cannot be called monsters. I have seen a pig with two bodies and one head, a cow with an extra foot on its back, and at the temple fair, a person whose right hand was as large as a winnowing basket and whose fingers were as thick as hammers, while the left hand was normal. Every day, this person used his right hand to write and sell paintings. If someone who talks about omens were to see this, they would surely say, "This is a sign of disaster for pigs, cows, and people, foretelling some calamity." Or they might say it is a sign of some specific event. However, all the anomalies I have seen have ultimately had no verifiable consequences. Therefore, I have the least faith in the *Spring and Autumn Annals* interpretations of yin and yang and the *Book of Changes* interpretations of the five elements in Han scholarship, and I have the least faith in the *River Map* and *Lo Shu* and the *Heavenly Principles* in Song scholarship.

Mr. Sun Duanren, my mentor, was a man of profound and elegant writing, and he had a strong liking for alcohol. His writing, whether he was drunk or sober, was the same, and the officials in the imperial academy considered him a close second to Li Bai, the legendary poet who wrote a hundred poems after drinking a dou of wine. When he was overseeing education in Yunnan, one moonlit night, he drank alone under the bamboo. Suddenly, he saw a person staring at the wine cup, as if enjoying it.
