He knew it was a ghost, but he was not afraid. He simply placed his hand on the cup and said, "There is not much wine today, so I cannot share it with you." The person then shrank back and disappeared. After waking up, he regretted his words and thought, "If he came to seek wine, he must not be an ordinary ghost. He would even offer me wine, which shows that he does not look down on me. How could I offend his intention to visit?" He then bought three large bowls of good wine and set up a small table under the bamboo at night. The next day, he checked and found the wine unchanged. He sighed, "This gentleman is not only refined but also aloof. If he is slightly teased, he will not even taste a drop." One of his subordinates said, "Ghosts and spirits only enjoy the aroma of food or drink. How can they really drink?" Mr. Sun responded with a sigh, "Then we should enjoy drinking while we are still alive, not just enjoy the aroma of food or drink after death." My nephew Yu Shan, who was in the educational office in Fujian, told me about this. It made me feel that the scholars of the Wei and Jin dynasties are not far removed from us in spirit.

Yu Qishi of Qiantang, whose name I have momentarily forgotten, seems to be You Shen. In the year 1773 during the Qianlong era, I happened to see his poem "Wild Mooring, Unable to Sleep," which reads: "The wild reeds and withered grass stretch across the cold wilderness where the water meets the level ground, and around me the night insects chirp incessantly. Even those who usually sleep deeply find it hard to rest soundly; I stand alone, leaning on a withered pine tree, gazing at the bright moon." I remarked, "Du Fu wrote, 'The child of Ba is restless, at midnight there are passing boats,' and Zhang Ji wrote, 'Outside the city walls of Suzhou, the cold Hanshan Temple, at midnight the bell tolls reach the guest boat.' Both poets used the technique of writing from another's perspective, capturing the experience of not sleeping at midnight, rather than describing the boats of Ba or the bells of Hanshan Temple. You have used this method skillfully, truly mastering the art of '夺胎' (borrowing and transforming). However, what Du Fu and Zhang Ji described were scenes before their eyes, while you suddenly speak of ghosts—doesn't this seem a bit abrupt?" Yu Qishi replied, "That night, I truly saw a figure standing under the moonlight, leaning against a tree, who seemed to be a scholar. I intended to engage in conversation to break the silence, but at a distance of more than ten steps, the figure gradually disappeared. Hence, these words." Zhong Xinhu joked, "The chickens and dogs in the clouds pass by Liu An, and the flute music in the moon returns to Emperor Yang of Sui." The Tang people called such poems '见鬼诗' (ghost poems), but even they were considered somewhat fabricated. As for your work, it truly lives up to this name."

Ho Zhenyi's book records: According to the Grand Agricultural Official from the Sea Region, there was a young man from a distinguished family who was reading in the family tomb. Outside the tomb, there were dozens of households, all of whom were caretakers of the tomb for wealthy families. One day, he saw a beautiful woman through a gap in the wall, showing only half her face. He was about to look more closely when she had already retreated. A few days later, he saw her again outside the wall, picking wildflowers. She would often gaze intently at the inside of the wall, or even climb up to the gap, showing half her body, as if she were admiring him like the neighbor admiring Cao Yin. He was deeply captivated by her, but privately thought that the people living in this place were common folk, and there should not be such a beautiful woman among them. Moreover, everyone he had seen wore simple clothes, yet this woman alone was elaborately dressed. He suspected she might be a fox spirit or a ghost, so although he followed her with his eyes and sent his glances, he did not speak to her. One night, standing alone under a tree, he heard two women whispering outside the wall. One said: "Your beloved is strolling under the moonlight right now, why don't you go to him?" The other replied: "He is still suspicious that I am a fox spirit or a ghost, so why would I just go and scare him?" The first woman then said: "On a clear day like this, how could there be any fox spirits or ghosts? What a foolish child, not understanding things this way." The young man, upon hearing this, secretly rejoiced, lifted his robe to step out, but suddenly realized: "If she claims not to be a fox spirit or a ghost, then she must indeed be one." In the world, no small person would claim to be a small person; not only would they not claim it, but they would also fiercely denounce themselves as small people to prove they are not. This must be the trick of the demon. He waved his arm and returned. The next day, he secretly investigated, and indeed, there were no such two women. Those two women also did not come again.

Wu Lintang said, during his travels in the Qin and Long regions in the past, he heard of a hunter near the foot of Shaohua Mountain who saw two people lying exhausted under a tree. When called, they could still force themselves to get up. He asked them why they were so tired and lying there, and one of them replied: "We are all fox spirits. Initially, I lost my way while traveling at night and sought lodging at a mountain home. There was a young woman there who was extremely beautiful. She waited for an opportunity to flirt with me. I could not control my thoughts and we engaged in intimate behavior. Her parents saw us and severely scolded me. I knelt and bowed, and thus avoided being beaten. Later, I heard her parents talking incessantly, as if they were discussing something. The next day, they ultimately took me as their son-in-law, but only agreed to let me go on duty every five days to take care of their daughter in the mountains, and return every five days. I also accepted this arrangement. After half a year, I suffered from consumption and coughed at night, unable to sleep. I took a walk in the forest and heard laughter and talking. Curiously, I went to investigate and saw several houses with people. My wife was surrounded by them, sitting on a stone watching the moon. I could not bear the anger and, with great effort, wanted to fight. The other person also angrily said, 'Scoundrels dare to look at my wife,' and also got up to fight. Fortunately, the other person was also ill and weak, and we fell together. My wife sat calmly on the stone and laughed, saying, 'You two should not fight. I clearly tell you, I actually move between the two families, all pretending to go on duty, to let you rest for five days, to store energy for the purpose of replenishing.'

The speaker's plans have been revealed, and your abilities have been exhausted; there is no further use for you. I am leaving. Suddenly, I disappeared, leaving the two of you lost and unable to find your way out, so you fell and starved here. Fortunately, you encountered us and were rescued. The other person spoke similarly. The hunter fed you with dried rice, and you could barely walk. He led you to see the place, and the two of you exclaimed together: "Previously, the walls and columns were solid earth, the beams and pillars were made of wood, the doors could open and close, and the windows could be opened and closed. All were solid and not illusions. Why are they all now earth caves? The courtyard was once flat and clean, but now, outside the earth caves, the terrain is rugged and impassable. The caves are not wide; the fox could fit, but how can we two people fit? Are our forms also being transformed? One of you saw a broken porcelain on the opposite cliff and said, 'This was what I held to climb the stairs, and it broke when I dropped it. Now, with the sheer cliffs, how did I go up and down at that time?' Looking around and wandering, everything seemed like a dream. The two of you greatly hated the fox girl and asked the hunter to go into the mountains to catch her. The hunter said, 'Meeting by chance and becoming a good couple—there is no such easy thing in the world. If something is too easy, there must be something not so easy. Fish swallow the hook because they are greedy for the bait, and the orangutan is pricked with blood because it loves alcohol. You two should blame yourselves, why blame the fox?' The two of you then silently and sadly stopped.

Lin Tang reported that there was a young man who was bewitched by a fox, and he gradually became weak and exhausted. The fox still visited him frequently. Later, they had intercourse, but the young man was too exhausted to satisfy the fox. The fox then put on her clothes and wanted to leave. The young man cried and tried to hold her back, but the fox paid no attention to him. She angrily blamed him for being unfeeling, and the fox also became angry and said: "I have no marital obligations to you; I came here only to replenish myself. Your vital essence has been depleted, so what do I have to gain by staying?" This is like a relationship based on power or wealth: when power is lost, the relationship ends; when wealth is gone, the relationship dissipates. When we flattered each other, it was for power and wealth, not out of genuine affection for each other. You have cut off communication with those families you once served, yet you blame me alone? Her voice was very harsh, and those who were tending to the sick young man all sighed. The young man then turned away, facing inward, and remained silent. Wang Xuchu said that he once saw a person performing a spirit-writing ritual, and the spirit claimed to be Zhang Ziyang. When asked about the *Wuzhen Pian*, the spirit could not answer. It only declared that the *Wuzhen Pian* contained the secrets of the golden elixir and that such knowledge should not be lightly transmitted. Later, a servant woman stole money and fled. The master asked if the servant could still be caught, and the spirit replied: "In your past life, you lured people with money, bought their wives, and then enticed them to gamble, taking their wealth. In this life, the person who lured your wife away and stole your money is paying back for buying her; and the thief is paying back for taking your money. Fate is already determined, and even if you try to catch the thief, it will be futile. It is better to let it go." Wang Xuchu said: "A true immortal would not lie, but if this reasoning is accepted, all thieves and criminals would claim their actions are predestined. It is better not to pursue them, to avoid encouraging such behavior."

The spirit tablet could not respond. Someone who doubted said: "Those who practice spirit-writing often associate with cunning and wicked youths. How can we be sure that someone hasn't hidden the servant's wife and taught her to say these words?" They secretly sent someone to investigate, and at dusk, the servant indeed went to a crooked alley, climbed onto the roof ridge to spy, and found them gathered and shouting "Lu," with the servant's wife adorned and serving wine. They secretly called the patrol officers to surround her residence, and she submitted with a lowered head and was bound. The law prohibits shamans and sorcerers, as evil people hide among them. Lan Daohang once used this method to defeat Yan Song. Those who discuss it do not consider it wrong, because they hate Yan Song. However, officials like Yang and Shen shed blood and broke their heads but could not contend, while a single Daoist calmly talks and laughs, and thus controls their fate, showing the power of the shaman. Fortunately, the one he targeted was Yan Song; if he had targeted the virtuous officials, even if it were Han, Fan, Fu, or Ouyang, could they resist? Therefore, the technique of the spirit tablet, scholar-officials occasionally play with it, compose and respond to poems, treat it like watching a play, then it is acceptable. If used to predict auspicious or ominous signs, a gentleman should fear its ultimate outcome.

My uncle, Mr. Mei'an, said: In the town of Huai, there was a family with an empty house consisting of five rooms, set apart as a courtyard, used to store miscellaneous items. Children often went there to play, jumping and trampling, causing quite a disturbance. When they were locked out, they would steal over a short wall to enter. So, he wrote a large notice and stuck it on the door, saying, "This room is inhabited by a fox spirit. Do not defile it; this is merely to scare the children." A few days later, at night, he heard a voice from outside the window: "Grateful for your invitation, I have now moved in. I will help you guard this courtyard." Afterward, anyone who entered was immediately hit by bricks and tiles, and even servants and laborers transporting miscellaneous items were afraid to go near. Over time, the courtyard fell into disrepair and eventually became completely dilapidated. The fox spirit then left. This is what is meant by "evil spirits are caused by human actions." I once had a farm in the south of Cangzhou, called Shangheya, which I have since sold. There was an old pavilion named Shuiming with five rooms, overlooking the Wei River below, where sails and masts passed under the railing. It was a good place for sightseeing, comparable to the Duofan Pavilion at my grandfather's house, Mr. Xuefeng Zhang. My great-grandmother, the respected lady, would often stay there during the summer to cool off, and the grandsons would take turns serving her. One day, I pushed open the window and looked south, seeing dozens of men and women boarding a ferry boat. The rope was already untied, and one person suddenly punched an old man, causing him to fall into shallow water near the shore. His clothes and shoes were all wet, and he was sitting up, angrily cursing, as the boat had already started rowing away. At that time, the Wei River was flooding, with floodwaters rushing down, surging with sound. A grain ship with two sails came downstream, as fast as an arrow shot from a bow. It collided with the ferry boat, breaking it into pieces like a piece of wood, and dozens of people drowned. Only this old man survived. He then turned his anger into joy, clasped his hands, and recited the Buddha's name.

When asked where he was going, he replied: "Yesterday, I heard that my cousin had received twenty gold coins and sold his daughter-in-law-to-be to become a concubine, with the contract being finalized today. I quickly pledged my land to get the same amount of money and carried it to redeem her." The crowd all said in unison: "This is one strike, as if directed by a god." They quickly arranged for a boat to take him across. At the time, I was ten years old, and I only heard that he was from Zhaojiazhuang, but I regretted not asking for his name and surname. This was the year of the Ox during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng. Furthermore, my late mother-in-law said that in Cangzhou, there was a man who forced his younger brother's wife into marriage and sold his two nieces to a brothel. The villagers were all indignant. One day, he carried gold and traded green beans, sailing a large boat to Tianjin. In the evening, he docked by the riverbank and sat on the boat's railing, washing his feet. Suddenly, a salt boat from the west bank, with its rope breaking, swept past. The sides of the boats touched, and from the knees down, his bones and muscles were torn apart like they were cut. He cried out for several days before dying. My maternal grandfather's servant heard this and hurriedly went to report: "Mr. A suffered such a terrible fate, truly a strange and unusual event." My maternal grandfather calmly said: "This event is not strange; if it had not happened this way, it would have been strange." This was the period between the years of the Dragon and the Snake during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng.

The King of Jiahe, Hongxu, reported that in Gaocun, there was a man named Liu who lived in a house with seven rooms. He himself occupied three rooms in the middle, while the three rooms in the east wing were used to store the coffin of his deceased wife because they lacked a burial site. The two rooms in the west wing were occupied by his young son and his sister. One night, he heard the child crying very loudly but did not hear the sister speaking. He suspected that she was in the kitchen and had not returned, so he looked through the crack in the window to see if the light was out. Under the bright moonlight, he saw a black smoke-like figure winding its way out from under the door of the east wing, circling around the window of the west wing, and lingering there for a long time. When the sister woke up and comforted the child, the black smoke-like figure slowly disappeared back into the east wing. He realized that it was the spirit of his deceased wife. From then on, every month on the night he heard the child crying, he would secretly get up to look, and he always saw the same thing. He told his sister about it, and she was moved to tears. How sad it is, the heart of a parent, even after death, does not forget their child! Can a child truly honor their parents in the same way?

My esteemed teacher, Lü Gong Anzhai of Guilin, told me that when he took up his post as the magistrate of his hometown, he dreamed of his mentor, Master So-and-so, who looked gaunt and seemed deeply troubled. The magistrate greeted him with concern, saying, "Your coffin has not yet returned, which is the fault of all your disciples. However, I have not forgotten you, and now, thanks to your patronage, I have obtained an official position. I will struggle to arrange for a proper burial." It turned out that Master So-and-so had died in a military outpost and was still temporarily buried in a monastery. When Lü Gong Anzhai first became the magistrate, there were people who had tried to cultivate previously uncultivated land. He mistakenly reported them as taxable, and many people came to complain. Although he knew their claims were just, he was afraid of being criticized by the officials and did everything he could to protect himself, preventing them from making their case. As a result, they continued to suffer, and the local earth deity reported the matter to the East Peak deity. The East Peak deity said that the mistake was due to carelessness, even though there was no selfish motive. However, he feared that reporting the matter would hinder promotion, so his sin was equivalent to acting out of self-interest. Consequently, the East Peak deity ordered Lü Gong Anzhai's soul to be detained here until the floating taxes were reduced, after which he could return. Lü Gong Anzhai was suffering from hunger and cold, and he could not bear to speak of it. Reflecting on his brief period of office, he realized that the benefits he had gained were minimal, while his actions had led to endless suffering. He truly could not help but shed tears of sorrow and pain. Fortunately, you have come to this position. If you remember our lifelong acquaintance and make a request for the floating taxes to be waived, then I can re-enter the cycle of rebirth and escape the realm of the dead. Even if my remains are left to the ants, I will not regret it. The magistrate then checked the old records and found that this was indeed the case. Later, he managed to request the reduction of the floating taxes, and in a dream, he saw Master So-and-so coming to bid him farewell.

According to the *Jiaohé* and *Fangyan*, those who talk about ghosts are often fantastical, yet there are also things that seem reasonable and credible. In the seventh month of the year 1725 during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, I moored my boat to the south of Jinghai. The moon was dim and hazy, and I strolled along the shore. I saw two people sitting under a willow tree, talking to each other. I approached them, and they happily invited me to sit. I listened carefully to what they said, and it was all about matters of the afterlife. I doubted that they were ghosts, and I shrank back and wanted to flee. They stopped me and said, "Do not be surprised. We are not ghosts. One is a messenger of death, and the other is a ghost-seer." I asked how they could see ghosts, and they replied, "We are born like this. No one knows why." I then asked how the messenger of death became a messenger, and they replied, "In a dream, suddenly I was conscripted. No one knows why either." We talked until the second drumbeat, and they mostly detailed the accounts of retribution. I then asked, "Does the underworld judge cases according to Confucian principles? Or Buddhist principles?" The ghost-seer said, "I can see ghosts, but I cannot speak with them. I do not know this." The messenger of death said, "You do not need to ask about this. Just ask your own heart. If your conscience is clear, that is what the underworld calls good. If your conscience is troubled, that is what the underworld calls evil. Right is right and wrong is wrong, both in the world and the afterlife. How can we distinguish between Confucianism and Buddhism?" Their words were simple and easy to understand, and they did not sound like the words of a sorcerer or fortune-teller.

In the village, there was a man who claimed to see ghosts. He said that ghosts are always restless and agitated, as if they are engaged in some activity, but he did not know what they are pursuing. They also have emotions such as joy, anger, sorrow, and pleasure, but he did not know the source of these emotions. Generally, ghosts compete with one another, just as people do. However, weak yin cannot match strong yang, so ghosts generally fear humans. Those who do not fear humans are either driven away by the presence of humans in their domain, or they seek to harm humans in order to be worshipped and offered sacrifices. Some are rebellious and powerful spirits with lingering evil energy, acting recklessly and violently, avoiding those with strong energy but daring to invade those with bad luck. There are also spirits of the wronged or vengeful dead who petition the gods to seek revenge for their accumulated hatred, but they are not included in this category. If someone is driven by desire, lustful ghosts will respond; if driven by a killing intent, malevolent ghosts will respond; if driven by anger, vengeful ghosts will respond. These are all self-invited, and they are not included in this category either. I once visited the graves during the Qingming Festival and saw young women going out for a spring outing. Those who were seductive and displayed their beauty were followed by various ghosts who laughed, while those who were composed, virtuous, and calm had no ghosts around them. I also once saw several ghosts in a school. When Mr. Bao, the school official, emerged — he was from Nangong and served as the magistrate of Xian County, recorded in the county gazetteer's section on upright officials — the ghosts cowered and hid among the grass. When another school official emerged, the ghosts jumped and moved freely. Thus, whether ghosts dare to harm someone depends largely on the person themselves.

Se Madame, the mother of the concubine, said that in Yanzhou there was a man named Liu who suffered from urinary retention and tried every medicine without success. One night, he dreamed that a deity told him: "Take the ash of the copper head boiled in wine to relieve the blockage." When asked what the copper head was, the deity replied: "It is what you call the cricket." He tried it and indeed recovered. I believe this case involves damp-heat accumulation, where damp-heat is used to treat damp-heat, leveraging the cricket's property of facilitating downward movement. If the bladder's qi cannot be transformed, one must seek the root cause, which this substance cannot address. The Deputy Censor Liang Tiezhuang said that a night traveler saw something near a bamboo grove, resembling a person but not quite human, moving clumsily and feeling its way. When he shouted at it, it did not respond. He realized it was a spirit or demon, so he picked up stones and threw them at it. The creature turned into black smoke, shrunk back into the grove, and made a whistling sound, saying: "Due to past karma, I have fallen into the realm of hungry ghosts, where I am both blind and deaf, enduring countless hardships. How can you bear to torment me further?" He then left it alone and went away. In my *Luan Yang Xiao Xia Lu* (Record of Summer in Luan Yang), I wrote about a story told by Wang Juzhuang about a female ghost who received a punishment of muteness for her skill in slandering and scheming. This ghost received a punishment of being both deaf and blind—could this be a retribution for being too clever?

The respected teacher, Mr. Wang Wenduan, once said that there were those who wished to harm members of a rival faction but struggled to find a good plan. A cunning individual secretly discovered their plot and prepared a poison, presenting it and saying, "This medicine will cause death upon ingestion, but the condition at the time of death will be no different from that of someone who died of illness. Even if the bones are boiled to examine them, the results will still be the same." The conspirator was greatly pleased and kept the person around to drink with him. Upon returning home, the person died that very night. It turned out that the poison had been given as a means to eliminate evidence. The teacher sighed deeply and said, "The one who offered the poison killed to curry favor, but ended up killing himself. The one who used the poison killed to silence witnesses, but the truth could never be silenced." What is the point of such schemes? At that time, the respected elder Zhang Fanchuan was also present and added, "There was once a man who liked young male companions and was attracted to a noble's son. He thought there was no way to win him over, so he secretly instructed his favorite concubine to arrange through a matchmaker to invite the noble's son to a villa, planning to seize and forcibly defile him. On the appointed day, they heard that he had arrived and rushed to capture him. Suddenly, they lost their footing and fell into a lotus pond on a wooden bridge, nearly drowning. They shouted and helped him out, but by then the noble's son had already fled, and the concubine was disheveled. It was because the noble's son was handsome and graceful, and the concubine also found him attractive. Later, without reason, the concubine was released, and the maidservants and matchmaker began to talk about the matter. Those who plot are disliked by the gods and spirits, and this is not unfounded."

The Flower Seller, Old Woman Gu, carried an old ceramic vessel for sale. It resembled a brush-wash basin but was slightly shallow, with a glaze-like color on its inside, outside, and bottom. It looked like a piece from the Ge kiln but lacked the crackle glaze. The center was flat like an inkstone, revealing only the clay body, with edges clearly delineated and not varying by even a hair's breadth. It was not at all worn away, and its purpose was unknown, so it was returned as useless. Later, I came across the *Guangyi Zhi*, which recorded the story of Ji Hu seeing a red brush and cup on a Taoist's desk in a stone chamber. The *Qianxunzi* also recorded, as seen by He Rang, the story of a heavenly fox possessing a red cup, brush, and inkstone. The *Yishi* recorded the story of Ye Fashan holding a red bowl to draw talismans. Thus, I realized that before the Tang dynasty, there were no red inkstones. For marking texts, red ink was ground in cups; for large brushes, red ink was stored in bowls. Cups were slightly smaller and had a wide mouth to facilitate the cleaning of the brush; bowls were slightly larger and had a narrower mouth to hold more concentrated ink. The old woman's vessel was indeed a red cup, previously unseen by connoisseurs. I quickly called her back to ask where this cup went. She said: "I bought it for thirty coins, and it was said to come from a well. Because it was deemed useless, I sold it for twenty coins at a miscellaneous goods stall. It has been nearly a year, and I cannot find out where it is now." I deeply regretted this. The world often pays high prices for fake items, while genuine ancient artifacts are sometimes rejected. I am not seeking items with carved bamboo, lacquer, or broken patterns, yet I have already lost such an opportunity. How many more hidden treasures are there? Later, I found another red cup with the same design, which was taken by Governor Chen Wangzhi. Thus, I realized that such items are still common in the world, just unrecognized by people.

Master Jie, the owner of the Wild Garden, said that among his close friends and relatives, there was one who was not afraid of ghosts. Upon hearing of a haunted house, he would often go there to stay, and someone mentioned a pavilion behind a temple on West Mountain where strange and supernatural phenomena were frequently seen. That year, coinciding with the provincial examination, he rented a room there. Every night, unusual and bizarre shapes would surround his bed and desk, but he dealt with it calmly, though it could not harm him either. One clear moonlit night, he pushed open the window and looked around, and he saw a beautiful woman standing under a tree. She laughed and said, "Frightening me so I don't move, are you trying to bewitch me? What kind of ghost are you? Come closer." The woman also laughed and said, "You don't recognize me, but I am your great-grandmother. I died and was buried in this mountain. I heard you are fighting with ghosts every day. You have been studying for over ten years. Are you just trying to gain a reputation for not fearing ghosts? Or are you thinking of striving for success in your studies to bring glory to your ancestors and benefit your family? You fight at night and sleep during the day. The exam is approaching, and your studies are completely neglected. Is this what your parents intended when they sent you into the mountains with provisions? Even though I am in the netherworld at my mother's home, I cannot be indifferent. So I am telling you straightforwardly. Think about it." After speaking, she disappeared. He privately thought what she said made sense, so he packed up and returned home. Upon returning, he asked his parents in detail, and there was no such great-grandmother. He deeply regretted and stamped his feet, saying, "I was deceived by a clever ghost." He was determined to go back, but his friend said, "The ghost did not dare to fight openly. But it transformed its shape and spoke kindly to persuade you. The ghost fears you now. Why do you need to pursue a cornered enemy?" So he stopped. This friend can truly resolve disputes. However, what the ghost said was true. True principles cannot be stopped, but clever words can. This can teach us how to soften stubbornness.

Previously, I recorded the story of a giant python at the tomb of Master Zha, according to the words of Grand Censor Shumulu. On the tenth day of the third lunar month in the year Renzi (1762), Jiang, the Junior Minister of Agriculture, invited me to see the peach blossoms. I happened to sit next to Master Zha, and I inquired about the details, confirming that Shumulu's words were true. Master Zha then said: There is another unusual story that Shumulu does not know. The wife of the tomb keeper, Lady Liu, always slept with the python, which coiled around her bed, nearly filling it. Whenever the python came, it would drink strong liquor from a large bowl. The python would raise its head and sniff, and the liquor would decrease by a considerable amount, leaving only a tasteless water. Lady Liu would use the python to treat illnesses, and it was often effective. Once, someone wanted to buy the python and gave Lady Liu eight thousand coins. They took the python while it was drunk. Afterward, Lady Liu suddenly went mad, saying, "I treated you well, but you sold me. I will take away your soul," and she beat herself continuously. Her brother rushed to tell Master Zha, who went to see her, but he could do nothing. After a few moments, she died. It is common for supernatural beings to possess female shamans, and it is also common to suffer misfortune by offending such beings. However, selling a supernatural being for money is quite unusual, and it is even more extraordinary that someone would pay money to buy such a being. This python is still in its place, outside the western gate of the city, and the locals call it the Red Fruit Garden.

In various places, there are institutions for nurturing infants and caring for the poor. However, in Cangzhou, there was a unique institution specifically for blind people, which was not under official control. A blind man named Liu Ruirui said: "Once, a candidate named Teng passed through Cangzhou, but he was in dire financial straits, with no one to borrow from, and found himself at a dead end, about to throw himself into the river. A blind man felt sorry for him and emptied his bag to help him on his way. The candidate went to the capital, eventually obtained an official position, and was recommended to become the governor of a state. He could not forget the blind man, so he spent several hundred gold coins out of his own pocket to repay the kindness, but he could not find the blind man. No one even knew his name. Therefore, he donated money to build this institution to care for the blind. Both the blind man and this candidate can be called ancient people." Liu Ruirui also mentioned that the blind people left a room, where they burned incense and worshiped Mr. Chen daily. I suggested that Mr. Chen should also have a seat beside the blind people. Liu Ruirui hesitated and said, "How can blind people sit beside an official?" I replied, "If he is worshipped for his official position, then the blind people naturally cannot sit beside him; but if he is worshipped for his righteousness, then the blind people's righteousness is equal to that of an official, why should they not sit beside him?" This event took place during the Kangxi period. Liu Ruirui told me about it during the Qianlong years of 1785-1786. He could still name those who lived in the institution, but it has been more than thirty years, and it is unknown whether it still exists or has been abandoned.

During the late Ming dynasty, when military chaos prevailed, Zeng Bazu, my great-great-grandfather, was just eleven years old when he was captured and taken to Linqing. There, he met Li Shoujing, an old tenant farmer, who helped him return home by transporting him in a single-wheeled cart. They faced many dangerous situations, nearly dying several times, but Li Shoujing never left him until the end. At the time, Song Tai Fu Ren, a respected elder, was present. She rewarded Li Shoujing with money. Li first bowed in thanks, then placed the money on the table and said, "Seeing the old master wandering, my heart could not bear it. How could I come for a reward?" He bowed and wept before leaving, and never came again after that. Li Shoujing was simple and straightforward. Among his peers, there were those who acted wickedly, and he always fiercely contended with them, which led to him being driven away by many people. Yet, in times of trouble, he remained true to his heart, just as he had been. As for premonitions, no one knows why they occur, like the dawn breaking through the dawn glow, or the ground becoming damp before rain. These events seem to be connected. Since I was four years old until now, I have never been separated from my brush and ink. On the second day of the third month of the year Renzi, I happened to be in the office and jokingly told the officials: "In the past, Tao Qian wrote his own funeral dirge. I also wrote a couplet for myself: 'Floating and sinking in the sea of officialdom like a seagull, life and death in the world of books like a bookworm.' After a hundred years, let the gentlemen write epitaphs to remember me, and that will be enough." Liu Shian'an, a high-ranking official, said, "The first line is very unlike you. If it were to eulogize Lu Ershan, it would be appropriate." Three days later, the news of Lu Ershan's death arrived. Was this not a sign of premonition?

Mr. Shen Cangling said, there was a scholar who lived in a separate study, and outside the wall of his residence, there was an abandoned grave whose occupant's identity was unknown. The gardener mentioned that at night, there were sometimes reciting sounds, but after listening for several nights, nothing was heard. One night, the reciting sound was suddenly heard, and the scholar quickly took wine to pour over the grave, saying, "In the depths of the grave, you struggle to compose poetry, surely you are a poet. Though separated by life and death, our essences are not different. Would you appear to have a conversation with me?" Soon, a shadow emerged slowly from the shade of the trees, and suddenly turned away. The scholar bowed and prayed earnestly, again and again, and faintly heard a voice from outside the tree saying, "Grateful for your appreciation, I dare not doubt you because of my unusual form. I intended to engage in a refined conversation, to break the long-standing loneliness. Upon seeing your graceful appearance, you are splendidly attired and exude wealth and nobility, unlike my humble clothing. We are not of the same kind or spirit. Each person has their own aspirations, and I dare not approach you. Only you understand and tolerate me." The scholar left feeling disappointed, and from then on, not even the reciting sound was heard. I said, "This is just Mr. Shen's playful allegory." These words were neither personally heard nor witnessed by anyone else. Could this scholar be mocked by a ghost, still dare to describe himself? Mr. Shen smiled and said, "The words of Chu Ji under the locust tree, the noise in Hun Liangfu's dream, who heard them? You alone question me."

Qiu Xiaolian, also known as Er Tianyan, recounted that in the mountains of Yongchun, there was an abandoned temple, all reduced to ashes. It is said that initially, a monk lived there. The monk was skilled in spells, and his disciples sometimes saw a mountain spirit at night. They asked the monk to banish it. The monk replied: "Humans are humans, and spirits are spirits; they do not interfere with each other. Humans act during the day, and spirits act at night; they do not harm each other. All things coexist, each finding its own way. Should spirits not prevent humans from appearing during the day, while humans prevent spirits from appearing at night?" Over time, the spirits began to bother humans even during the day, and the monk's quarters had no place of peace. They began to use spells, but by then the climate had already formed, and the followers had become numerous, and they could no longer be controlled. Angered, the monk went on a journey, seeking someone skilled in exorcism to return with him. He ascended the altar, summoned generals, and the thunder and fire descended, destroying the spirits and the temple. The monk lamented: "It is my fault. My spells were initially sufficient to overcome them, but I did not want to overcome them. My spiritual power was not enough to overcome them, yet I foolishly wanted to overcome them. Seeking the false reputation of benevolent transformation, leading to this collapse and destruction. Nurturing a sore and causing harm, that is what I am."

Flying Carriage Liu Ba was the driver of Sun Shu-san's grandson. He drove the carriage with the utmost force of the whip and the full strength of driving, always overtaking those traveling together until he passed them. Hence, he earned this name. He did not care whether the horses were strong or weak, whether they were hungry or full, or whether they lived or died. Over the years, he had killed many horses for several masters. One day, while driving Sun Shu-san to visit his relatives, he returned with an empty carriage. In the middle of the road, the horse ran off, was hit by the wheel, and fell into the rut. His injuries were not severe, but he eventually became unconscious and lost consciousness. He was carried back home, and by then his breath had already stopped. Those who love to win will inevitably meet their downfall, and those who are unkind will also inevitably meet their downfall. Dong Ye Ji was famous throughout the country for his skillful driving, but he ultimately lost control of the carriage due to his excessive exertion of the horses' strength. How much more so for this laborer! To end one's own life is not a misfortune.

My ancestor, the Guanglu, had a village east of the Wei River in Cangzhou. The land was often waterlogged, with its sides sloping like the character for "person" (人), hence it was named "Renshi Wang" (人字汪). Over time, the local dialect mispronounced "renzi" (人字) as "yinzi" (银子), meaning "silver coin," and further evolved "wang" (汪) to "wa" (洼), which sounds like "wa" (娃), meaning "child." The original meaning has been completely lost. The land was poor and the people were impoverished, with the area becoming increasingly dilapidated. Eight miles south of the village was a place called "Lang'er Kou" (狼儿口), where the local dialect pronounced "lang'er" (狼儿) in a way that sounded similar to "la" (辣), meaning "spicy." The Guanglu said, "Being opposite a place named 'Lang'er Kou' (狼儿口) is not favorable for prosperity," so he changed the village gate to face north. Five miles directly north was a place called "Muguku Kou" (木沽口), where the local pronunciation of "gu" (沽) lies between the sounds of "guo" (果) and "ge" (戈). After changing the direction of the gate, the "Renshi Wa" (人字洼) area gradually became prosperous, while the "Muguku Kou" (木沽口) area gradually became dilapidated. Is this a shift in the local geomantic energy? Or is the theory of "guxu" (孤虚), a concept in traditional Chinese geomancy, truly valid?

In the area known as Renzi Wangchang, there was a pile of accumulated firewood, commonly referred to as a "duò" (pile), which had been there for many years. The local people believed that it contained a spirit, and offending it often led to disasters. People would pray to it for illnesses, and sometimes it would be effective, but no one dared to pick even a single stem or leaf from it. During the year 1725 of the Yongzheng era, a great famine struck. A high-ranking official donated six thousand stones of grain and cooked porridge to provide relief. One day, the firewood ran out, and they wanted to use this pile, but no one dared to move it. The official went himself and prayed, saying, "If you have a spirit, you must be able to understand reason. Now thousands of people are starving to death, do you not have compassion? I plan to move you to guard the granary and take this firewood to save the starving. I believe you will not refuse." After the prayer, he ordered the crowd to pull it out, and there was no change. When the firewood was exhausted, they found a bald-tailed giant snake, coiled up and motionless. They carried it into the granary with a large basket, and soon it disappeared. From then on, it ceased to be spiritual. However, up to now, for about sixty to seventy years, no one dared to secretly enter and steal grain because of the agreement to guard the granary. Things that are extremely poisonous cannot help but be subdued by reason; evil cannot overcome virtue, and this is what it means.

According to Sun Shubao, there was a man named Shi from Hantian who was extremely poor. When his father was about to die, the family had only one blue cloth robe left, which was intended for his burial. His mother said, "Our family has not had enough food for a long time. If we sell this robe for rice, we can still live for more than a month. Why should we bury it with him?" Shi could not bear to part with the robe and ultimately used it for his father's burial. Many people knew about this incident. Later, someone lost a silver bracelet and searched everywhere without finding it. Shi suddenly found the bracelet in the soil. Everyone said, "This is heaven compensating you for your robe and commending your filial piety." The person who lost the bracelet redeemed it for 6,000 coins, which exactly matched the value of the robe. This is a recent event. Some people said it was just a coincidence. I say that if you consider it a coincidence, then Wang Xiang would never have caught the fish again, and Meng Zong would never have grown bamboo again. The interaction between the earthly and spiritual realms always reveals its message through a single event; how can you know it?

A Man from Jingzhou Named Li Qinglin Reported: There was a teacher named Liu who instructed young students in an ancient temple. One evening, under the faint light of the moon, he heard a rustling sound outside the window. Peering through the gap, he saw what appeared to be two shadows in the wall's opening. Startled, he called out, thinking there were thieves. Suddenly, a voice from the other side of the wall spoke: "We are not thieves; we have come to make a request of you." Surprised, he asked what they wanted. They replied: "Due to our past karma, we have been reborn in the realm of hungry ghosts for nearly a hundred years. Every time we hear the sound of cooking in the temple kitchen, our hunger burns like fire. Observing that you seem to have a compassionate heart, we humbly ask if we might receive a small offering of leftover food and cold porridge?" He wondered why they did not seek liberation from the temple monks through Buddhist scriptures and rituals, which are sufficient to help souls in the afterlife. They explained: "When ghosts encounter liberation, it is also due to their past causes. In our past lives, we strived for official positions. When powerful, people fawned over us; when fallen, we were treated like strangers. When we were successful, we did not help the poor or those in distress, nor did we create good karma. Now that we are fallen, how can we encounter such good fortune? Fortunately, we had abundant wealth and did not cherish it too much. We still occasionally helped our poor and old friends, so we sometimes received pity and a small amount of assistance. Otherwise, we would be like Maudgalyayana's mother in the great hell, where food turns into fierce flames when it reaches our mouths, even the power of the Buddha could not help."

The man felt deeply moved with compassion for him and agreed to his request. The ghost expressed gratitude and wept as he left. From then on, the man would often offer leftover food and wine to the wall outside, and it seemed as if there were spiritual responses, though the ghost remained invisible and silent. After more than a year, one night, the man heard a voice from outside the wall say, "For a long time, I have benefited from your kindness; now I come to bid you farewell." The man asked where he was going, and the ghost replied, "My two companions and I have no way to escape, but we have been thinking of doing good deeds to save ourselves. There are many wild birds in this forest. If anyone tries to shoot or trap them, we first frighten them so they fly high; if anyone sets nets, we drive them away so they do not enter. With this one thought, we have touched the divine, and now we have been sent to rebirth." The man once told people about this, saying, "Even a fallen ghost still has the power to help others; how can people excuse themselves by saying they cannot?" When my clan brother Zhonghan was the magistrate of Jingde County, there was a fierce tiger near the city that had injured several hunters and could not be captured. The townspeople pleaded, "Unless we hire the hunter from Huizhou named Tang, we cannot eliminate this problem." Hui Ning's Dai Dongyuan said, "During the Ming Dynasty, there was a man named Tang who was newly married and killed by a tiger. His wife later gave birth to a son and said to him, 'If you cannot kill a tiger, you are not my son. If any of my descendants in future generations cannot kill a tiger, they too are not my descendants.' Therefore, the Tang family has been able to capture tigers for generations." So the magistrate sent an official with money to hire the hunter. Upon returning, the official reported that the Tang family had selected two of their most skilled hunters, who were on their way. When they arrived, one was an elderly man with white hair and beard, who coughed occasionally, and the other was a young boy, about sixteen or seventeen years old. The magistrate was greatly disappointed and ordered food to be prepared. The old man, noticing the magistrate's dissatisfaction, half-knelt and said, "I have heard that this tiger is not more than five miles from the city. Let me go ahead and capture it; it won't be too late for you to give me food."

The old man then ordered the laborers to lead the way. Upon reaching the entrance of the valley, the laborers were too afraid to proceed. The old man laughed and said, "I am here, are you still afraid?" Halfway into the valley, the old man turned to the boy and said, "This beast seems to be still sleeping. You call it awake." The boy made the sound of a tiger roaring, and sure enough, a tiger emerged from the forest, pouncing directly at the old man. The old man held a short-handled axe, about eight or nine inches long, with a handle half its length, and raised his arm, standing firm. The tiger leaped at him, but he turned his head to let it pass, and the tiger jumped over his head, already bleeding and falling to the ground. When they looked, from the chin to the coccyx, all had been struck by the axe and split open. Then the old man generously rewarded and sent the laborers away. The old man said he had practiced his arm for ten years and his eyes for ten years. His eyes did not blink even when brushed with a feather duster, and his arm was so strong that even a strong man could not move it when suspended and pulled down. As the philosopher Zhuangzi said, "Practice tames the gods. Skillful people cannot surpass the door of those who practice," indeed. I once saw Shi She-ren Si-Biao writing in the dark with a brush, and it was no different from writing with a candle. I also heard of the high-ranking official Li Wen-ke Gong from Jinghai, who cut one-inch square paper into a hundred pieces, wrote a character on each, and folded and reflected each piece toward the sun, with not a single stroke out of place. All is just practice, there is no other trickery.

Li Qing's son reported that there was a fox living in a common household in Shandong for several generations. The fox did not reveal its form nor was its voice heard. At night, if there was a fire or a thief, it would knock on doors and shake windows to alert the owner. If the house needed repairs, silver coins would clang down onto the table, and the repairs would be made. The amount given was always twice the cost of the repairs, as if in exchange. During festivals, small gifts would be placed outside the window, and sometimes food would be returned to the window, disappearing instantly. The fox never bothered people, but children would sometimes tease it by throwing broken tiles and stones into the window, and the fox would throw them back out. If someone wanted to see the fox throw something back, they would keep throwing, and the fox would keep throwing back, never getting angry. One day, the fox suddenly spoke from the eaves, saying: "Although you are a farmer, your children are filial and friendly, and your family members are all gentle and harmonious. You are constantly protected by benevolent spirits, so I have stayed here for a long time to avoid a thunder disaster. Now that the great disaster has passed, I respectfully thank the owner and I am leaving." From then on, the fox was gone, and there has never been a fox that lived in a human home as carefully and disciplined as this one. Did it understand the principle of harmonizing with light from Laozi? By being careful and disciplined, it preserved itself and avoided accusations or punishment, seeing things one level higher than others.

My cousin Yu Dun, the son of my elder brother Mao Yuan, accompanied me in examining the books at Wen Yuan Ge in the third month of the Rénzǐ year. We stayed together in the old house in the western part of Huai Hai, which belonged to my son-in-law Peng Xu and which I had renovated to serve as a place for rest before attending court sessions. Yu Dun mentioned that Mao Yuan had a red lacquer rattan pillow, purchased in Cui Zhuang, a social gathering place, many years ago. One summer, whenever he used the pillow, he heard a buzzing sound, thinking it was tinnitus from hard work. After ten days or more, the sound gradually became more intense, resembling the flapping of wings of a flying insect. Another month or more later, the sound could be heard outside, without needing to lie down on the pillow to hear it. Suspecting something unusual, he cut the pillow open and found a slender-waisted wasp, which flapped its wings and emerged. There were no gaps as small as a needle or mustard seed in the pillow, so how could the wasp have left offspring inside? If it had left offspring before being lacquered, why did it take several years to hatch? Some say it was born through transformation, but bees are born from pupae, not through transformation. Even if it was born through transformation, why didn't it transform in other places, but in the pillow? Other pillows didn't transform, but this one did. The pillow provided no food or drink, so how could the wasp survive for over two months? If it wasn't cut out, would it have died? This is a mystery that is difficult to understand.

Yu Dun recounted another story: Lin Yumen, the magistrate of Yexian, was his teacher. Lin Yumen himself said that his grandfather, who was over eighty years old, had become senile and no longer recognized people. He could not walk either, yet he still ate well. He would sit motionless in a room, deeply distressed and unhappy. His descendants would often carry him on a chair to the outside to look around, hoping it would cheer him up. One day, he sent a servant to fetch something and sat alone waiting. When the servant returned, both the servant and the chair were missing. The whole family was filled with grief and fear, not knowing what to do. They wrapped food and went out in all directions to search for him, but found no trace. There happened to be a friend coming from Laoshan, who met Lin Yumen on the road and called out from afar: "Are you not looking for your grandfather? He is now in a temple in the mountains, and he is fine." They rushed to visit him, and indeed, he was there. The place was hundreds of miles away from Yexian. The monks did not know how he had arrived. Lin Yumen's grandfather only felt that two people had carried him flying, but he did not know who they were. This incident is extremely strange but not really strange. It seems that the mountain spirits and fox spirits were toying with the old man, treating it as a game.

Mr. Gao Tingmo, a xiao-lian (a Confucian examination degree), with the courtesy name Shi Zhi, was the eldest son of his predecessor Jie Zhou. He possessed a natural brilliance and clarity of mind, and his poetry, calligraphy, and style were all influenced by his father. Among his respected elders, he alone was my disciple. I expected him to achieve great things, but at the age of forty or so, he was first appointed as a school official. Later, he developed a mental illness, which came and went intermittently, and he died prematurely, which deeply saddened me. Once, I spoke with his cousin Shu Jue, who told me that before his death, he was reading late at night and, inspired by the scene, composed the line: "Autumn enters the dim window, the lamp is faint." He had not yet completed the couplet when a friend of his suddenly pulled back the curtain and entered. He invited him to sit and talk, and then told him this line. His friend replied: "Why not complete it with 'The soul returns to the old home, the moon is bleak and clear.'" Shi Zhi was startled and said: "Why are you speaking like a ghost?" In an instant, his friend disappeared. Shi Zhi then realized that it was not a human, but a ghost responding to the signs of his decline. Thus, Shi Zhi soon passed away as well, and his experience was quite similar to the story in *Ling Guai Ji* about Cao Tang's poem about a Buddhist temple in Jiangling, which includes the line "Beneath the water lies a spring, the spring is vast and serene."

Cao Mutang, the Chief Censor, recounted that there was a man who encountered ghosts while traveling at night. He fought fiercely with them, and soon a large group of ghosts gathered, some throwing sand and gravel, others pulling and dragging his limbs. Struggling on all sides, he suffered severe blows, falling over many times, yet his anger and resentment grew even more, still fighting to the death without stopping. Suddenly, on a slope, an old monk holding a lamp called out: "Venerable one, please stop. This place is the lair of ghosts. Though you are a brave warrior, you are already surrounded. The guest (you) and the host (the ghosts) have different forms, and the numbers are vastly different. With the courage of one person's blood and qi, to face the endless transformations of these beings, even if you were a hero like Guan Yu or Wu Song, you would not have a chance of winning. How much less so for those who are not as brave as Guan Yu or Wu Song? To know when to retreat is the act of a hero. Why not temporarily endure this, and follow the old monk to stay in a desolate temple for the night?" The man suddenly realized, and struggled to escape, following the light of the lamp. The ghosts gradually moved away, and the old monk also disappeared. He sat down to rest until dawn, and then found his way back home. This monk, whether he was a human or a ghost, can be considered a good teacher.

People from Haidian captured a large bird that resembled a grey goose in appearance, but had a long beak and sharp mouth, with bulging eyes that looked fierce and intimidating. It did not resemble any known birds, and no one dared to buy it. Mr. Jin Hai Zhu, who was living in Chenghuai Garden at the time, bought it alone and cooked it. The taste was not very good, and just after eating a few pieces, he felt his chest and diaphragm as cold as ice and snow, hard as iron and stone. Even washing it down with wine did not bring warmth. He became weak and lay down for several days before recovering. Some say that Zhang Du's *Xuanshi Zhi* records a popular belief that after someone dies, an animal will emerge from the coffin, called "kill." There was a man named Zheng Sheng who once lived in Xichuan and went hunting with a local official. They caught a large, grey bird that was over five feet tall. When they examined it, it suddenly disappeared. The villagers said that someone had died a few days ago, and the fortune-teller said that on this day the bird would leave. The family watched and saw a large, grey bird emerge from the coffin. Another record in *Yuanhua Ji* states that Wei Pang borrowed accommodation from a family and shot down and killed a ghost, cooked it, and ate it, finding the taste extremely sweet and delicious. The bird that Mr. Jin ate might have been transformed from a ghost, hence the cold and solidifying energy. At the time, Ni Yujiang was also living in the same place. Upon hearing this, he laughed and said, "This is another case of a scholar from Mount Zhongnan."

From Huangcun to Fengyi Gate, commonly known as Nanximen (South West Gate), it is a distance of forty li. The water sources and streams are interconnected, forming a network of flowing water. After heavy rain, the ground becomes muddy and wet, causing significant hindrance to vehicles and horses. There was a man named Li Xiu who was driving an empty carriage back from Gu'an. He saw a young man, about fifteen or sixteen years old, who was beautiful like a good girl, stumbling through the mud, looking very exhausted. By this time, the sun was setting, and the young man saw Li Xiu passing by and had a look of wanting to hitch a ride, but he was embarrassed and did not speak. Li Xiu, being naturally flirtatious, teased him with words, inviting him to ride in the same carriage. The young man hesitantly climbed in. Along the way, Li Xiu bought fruits and snacks to feed him, and the young man did not refuse much. Gradually, they became more affectionate, occasionally engaging in playful teasing, with the young man's smile being somewhat awkward and forced. After traveling several li, Li Xiu noticed that the young man's appearance seemed slightly older, but he did not pay much attention to it. After traveling another ten li, the evening grew dark, and Li Xiu felt that the young man's eyes and eyebrows also seemed to be changing. As they approached the west gate of Nan Yuan, the young man had a broad forehead and high cheekbones, with a beard and mustache. Li Xiu was surprised and felt dizzy, but he dared not ask further questions. When they reached an inn and got out of the carriage, the young man was now completely white-haired, having become an old man. He shook hands with Li Xiu and said farewell: "I am deeply grateful for your kindness, but my old age and declining appearance make me unsuitable for sharing a bed tonight. I am ashamed to have let you down." He smiled and left, and Li Xiu could not understand what kind of strange thing it was. Li Xiu's cousin, who worked as a cook in the household, once heard Li Xiu tell this story and regretted his youthful indiscretion, which led to being mocked by fox spirits.

Wen An Wang Yue Fang said, there was a man named Yang Sheng who was handsome. Worried about possibly encountering violence, he diligently studied martial arts and, by the time he was sixteen or seventeen, could already fight off dozens of people. He went to Tongzhou to take an exam and temporarily stayed in the capital. Once, while wandering alone at Tao Ran Pavilion, he encountered two foreigners who forcibly invited him into a tavern. Knowing their intentions, he temporarily agreed to drink and eat with them and deliberately demanded rare and delicious food. The two foreigners were very pleased and lured him to an empty temple. They grabbed him and sat him down, then suddenly embraced him. Yang Sheng pinned one person with one hand, knocked both to the ground, and stepped on their backs with his feet. He untied their belts and bound them behind their backs, then drew a knife and threatened them, saying, "Anyone who moves will die." He stripped them of their lower garments and humiliated them, while also scolding them, saying, "You are nearly thirty years old—how could you possibly satisfy me? Nevertheless, you have defiled many people. I am avenging the weak children." He then released their bindings and walked away with a flippant attitude. Later, when walking with Yue Fang, he met one of the foreigners on the road. He looked at him and smiled, and the man covered his face and fled in shame. Yang Sheng then explained the entire incident to Yue Fang. Yue Fang said, "Those who take lives must return lives, and those who steal must return the stolen goods—this is the law. This is what should be compensated. However, those who commit adultery are subject to punishment, but there is no law that requires them to be subjected to adultery. This is not something that should be compensated. What you did can be called satisfying one's desires, but it cannot be called reasonable."

According to the descendant of Sun Shulin, Ge Zhongfang, a scholar from the village of Nancun, went to the village of Zunzu, which is locally called "Hazel Nut Village." The name "Zunzu" is a corruption of "Zhen" (hazelnut) due to the overlapping sounds of "Zun" (to follow) and "Zhen" (hazelnut). Similarly, the nearby bridge, called "Nianzu Bridge," has also been corrupted over time to "YanZuo." During the funeral of the Cao family, Ge Zhongfang heard that a neighbor's chicken had laid an egg. At night, the egg emitted light. Ge Zhongfang and several guests went to see the egg. It was already dusk, and under the lamp, the egg appeared no different from any other. When they removed the lights, the egg indeed emitted a faint glow that surrounded it like a shallow bowl. They placed the egg in a corner of the room and stood outside the door to look at it. The entire room was illuminated as if it were daytime. One of the guests said, "This chicken was affected by a dragon, hence the egg has such strange changes. We fear that it might break and something emerge, which would be bad for the owner." The next day, Ge Zhongfang returned home, but he did not know what happened in the end. According to Mu Hua's *Ode to the Sea*, "The yang ice does not melt, and the yin fire burns secretly." This is because the yang energy is accumulated within the yin and eventually bursts out. The *Records of Strange Things in Lingnan* also mentions that fish and clams born in the sea emit light when placed in a dark place.

*岭表录异* also states that the head of the yellow wax fish glows at night like a lantern, and its flesh also shines piece by piece. It is born of water and shares the same nature as water. Only in seawater can there be fire, and only in marine creatures can there be light. Where water accumulates, it is where the accumulation of yin (cold) solidifies. Therefore, rivers cannot retain yang (heat) energy, only the sea can retain it. During the summer months, rotting grass transforms into fireflies due to layered yin and accumulated rain, and yang energy vaporizes and transforms into insects. Trees in the northern frontier that glow at night do so because of icy valleys and snowy rocks, where yang energy gathers and adheres to the trees. Fireflies die soon, and when night-glowing trees are transplanted into pots, they do not glow after one or two years. When the energy is released, it gradually dissipates. Only chicken eggs glow at night, and the reason is not understandable. The theory that dragons are affected is not necessarily true. According to Duan Chengshi's *Yuyang Zazu*, toxic mushrooms in Lingnan glow at night and kill very quickly. It is likely due to the accumulation of miasma, and due to heat and warmth, they manifest as yang fire. These eggs or the miasma might accidentally gather in chickens, or chickens might eat many toxic insects, and over time, they accumulate. Like toxic mushrooms that have a light, it is also not known.

From my cousin Yu Dun's words, I heard from Renqiu Liu Zongwan that there was a Manchu person going to Renqiu to collect rent. It happened that the villagers were performing a play at night, and he watched until the second drumbeat before dispersing. On his way back, thirsty from the journey, he saw a tea shop by a tree and tied his horse there. The owner came out and said that the fire had gone out, and there was only cold tea. After entering the room for a long time, the owner brought out half a cup of tea, which was deep red and viscous in color, with a faint fishy smell. He drank it all and then asked for more, saying that the bottle was empty. The owner replied that he would look for any leftovers and instructed him to wait there for a moment, not to peek. After waiting for a long time without seeing the owner come out, he peeped through the door crack and saw a naked woman hanging, with her abdomen cut open and supported by wood, while holding a cup and scraping her blood. Frightened, he exited and rode the horse quickly, hearing the sound of someone demanding payment for the tea following him, which did not stop along the way. By the time he reached his lodging, he was already dazed and fell down. The host heard the horse's sound and came out to see, helping him inside, and he woke up the next day, describing the entire incident. Together they went to trace it, to the place where the horse was tied, finding only a flat wasteland with old trees and many graves. On a thorny bush, a snake was hanging, with its belly cut open and supported by a grass stem. This is similar to the story in Pei Xing's novel about Lu Han encountering a concubine who killed a snake to make wine, but in that story, the concubine kept the guest, intending to find a mate. Why would this ghost sell tea? The ghost needs money for the afterlife, so why would it demand payment from people?

It is said by Mr. Tian Xianggu that in the southwest of Jinghe Town, there is a small village with about thirty to forty households. One night, a man named Mr. Zhou heard barking dogs and put on his clothes to go outside to look. Under the dim moonlight, he saw what appeared to be a giant sitting on the roof of his house. He was terrified and shouted, causing his neighbors to come out. They examined the scene more closely and realized that it was their cow standing on its hind legs with its head raised, and they did not know how it managed to do so. In a moment, the news spread, and men and women from the village all came to see the strange event. Suddenly, a house caught fire, with intense flames and strong winds, and the entire village was almost completely reduced to ashes. It was then known that the cow's appearance was a harbinger of fire (回禄). Mr. Yao An said: "It was the time of harvest, and the beans, straw, and grain were stored in the simple, flammable structures of the village, stretching out and touching each other. The farmers worked hard, and every household was sleeping soundly at midnight. Suddenly, they were burned, and the village would have been completely destroyed. The benevolent heart of heaven caused the villagers to wake up from their sleep due to the cow's appearance, so why was it considered a demon instead?"

A candidate for the imperial examination from the same prefecture, before he passed the exam, was unrestrained and free-spirited, frequently visiting brothels. However, those who waited at the door looked at him indifferently. Only one courtesan named Pei Shu (this prostitute had lost her name, and this was a playful term used in the neighborhood). She alone appreciated him and said, "This man is not destined to remain poor and lowly." At the time, she invited him for intimate drinking and pooled money at night to support his studies. When he took the examination, she also donated money to prepare his outfit and arranged for his daily necessities. The candidate was grateful and clasped arms with her, swearing an oath, "If I achieve success, I will take you as my wife." Pei Shu replied, "I value you highly because it is strange that the sisters only recognize wealthy men. I want people to know that even in the world of beauty and luxury, there are discerning eyes. As for a lifelong commitment, I dare not hear of it. My nature is promiscuous, and I cannot be a virtuous woman. If I am already married and still indulge in romantic affairs, how can you bear it; if I am confined in the inner chambers, it is like being imprisoned. How can I bear it? It is better to start with mutual affection but end in separation than to maintain a long-lasting longing." Later, when the candidate became a county magistrate, he invited her repeatedly, but she did not respond. After middle age, visits became less frequent, and she never once visited his office. She can truly be called a remarkable woman. If Han Xin could understand this sentiment, there would be no regret of betrayal after achieving success.

Jiaozhou's Fa Nanye, wandering in Chang'an, was extremely poor and distressed. One day, while at the seat of Li Fukan, the censor, he spoke of having once seen two poems at a lodging house in Lükou. The first poem read: "Wandering in rivers and lakes for fourteen springs, an older woman still living in the world of courtesans, a dream of love in a western pavilion, the bright moon peering through the window laughs at me." The second poem read: "Holding feelings unable to express them with a pipa, how many times lowering the head to comb the crow-like hair, thank the noble young man from Western Sichuan, willing to hold a red candle to admire the remaining flowers." Without the year, month, and name, it is unknown who wrote them. I said: This person is merely expressing their own hardships, but these fifty-six characters are equivalent to a whole *Pipa Xing*.

Yidu Li Sheng Wenyuan, younger brother of Nanjian, shared his love for antiquity as deeply as his brother, but surpassed him in knowledge and eloquence. Unfortunately, he died young, and Nanjian asked me to write an epitaph for his tomb, but I was too hurried to complete it, and the details of his life were lost, and it remains a regret to this day. One day, at my residence 'Yun Jing She,' we discussed ancient rites, and he then mentioned a story he had heard: there was a scholar in Boshan who, while walking through the forest at night, saw a high-ranking official sitting under a pine tree. He called out to him to speak, and upon closer inspection, it was his deceased maternal uncle, Mr. Mou. He had no choice but to approach and pay respects, and he inquired about family matters in great detail. The scholar then asked why the deceased official was there, given that it is said the body is buried in the wild while the spirit relies on the ancestral tablet in the temple, and his uncle had a family shrine. The official replied, "This is due to the ancient text that does not advocate for tomb sacrifices. The temple is a place for sacrifice, and the ancestral tablet is the place of worship. The spirit comes to be present based on this place and this position. If the spirit always resides in the temple and is always attached to the ancestral tablet, it would mean that ancestors and descendants, humans and ghosts, live together. And if there is a temple and an ancestral tablet, it is only for those with titles and salaries to say so."

In a single town or village, among ten thousand families, only a few can build a temple; among a thousand families, only a few can establish a shrine; among a hundred families, only a few can set up ancestral tablets. If the spirit resides in the tablet and not in the tomb, then countless millions of poor and lowly families have ancestors who are spirits without a place to dwell—can this be true? None knows the nature of ghosts and spirits better than the sage. The ritual of *mingqi* (ritual objects for burial) has existed since the time of the Xia dynasty. If the spirit resides in the tablet and not in the tomb, then the ritual objects should be placed in the temple; yet they are all buried in the tomb. Thus, the objects are used to serve the spirits, yet they are placed in places where the spirits do not come—would the sage act in such a contradictory way? The people of Wei buried the body in a separate pit, which was the practice of the Shang dynasty; the people of Lu buried the body in a single pit, which was the practice of the Zhou dynasty. Confucius favored the Zhou. If the spirit is not in the tomb, then whether the tomb is divided or united makes no difference—what is the difference between good and bad in this case? The *Rites* say: "One cannot bear to read the father's writings after his death, for the hand's moisture—his handwriting—still remains." One cannot bear to use the mother's cup after her death, for the moisture of her mouth still remains. Even the smallest object is treated in this way. Yet we treat the physical remains of our ancestors as nothing, while planting a few inches of wood and saying, "This is the spirit of our father and mother"—is this not a failure to understand the proper categories? The temple bell is about to sound; I shall now part from you. Now you have seen me; from now on, you need not be deceived by shallow scholars. Rise quickly, for the east is already bright—look, you are now standing before the path to the tomb.

Chen Yuzhai said that there was a man who rented a residence in a Taoist temple and became intimate with a fox girl, who would visit him every night without fail. But after several days of her absence, he could not fathom the reason. One night, she entered through the curtain with a smile, and when asked why she had been absent, she replied: "A new Taoist priest has arrived at the temple, and everyone regards him as a god. I feared he might possess supernatural powers, so I temporarily avoided him. Tonight, I transformed into a small mouse and peered through a crack in the wall. It turned out that he was merely making grandiose claims to deceive the world, hence I returned." When asked how she knew he lacked spiritual power, she explained: "False immortals and false Buddhas have only two tricks: one is to remain silent, making people uncertain, and the other is to act madly, making people doubt that they have something to rely on. However, true silence must be pure, calm, and peaceful; anyone who pretends to be silent is false. True reliance on madness must be carefree and unrestrained; anyone who acts anxiously is false. This is similar to your scholars, who pretend to be famous. Some may act eccentrically and coldly, making people suspect they are reclusive, while others may drink excessively and curse, making people suspect they are mad. Both are the same trick. This Taoist priest acts anxiously, which is enough to show that he is incapable." At the time, they were drinking together at Mr. Qian Jiaxuan's house, and Mr. Qian said: "This fox's eyes are like a mirror, but her words are too sharp, leaving no room for maneuver."

The Cook Cao, whose son is a monk, reported that he once saw a wealthy family from the eastern part of Guangdong organizing a vegetarian meal at a temple. They said that their wife had died nineteen years ago and appeared as a shadow under the lamp one night. She said, "Since I arrived in the underworld, I have never stopped thinking of you. I still hope that we can meet once after a hundred years, but unexpectedly, I am now assigned to the cycle of rebirth. From now on, it will be vast and endless, with no more meeting time. Therefore, I defied the rules of the underworld, bribed the escort, and came to say goodbye." Her husband was shocked and grieved, about to speak, when suddenly a whirlwind entered the room, swept her away, and he still faintly heard her sobbing. Therefore, he organized a vegetarian meal and performed penance to accumulate merit for the next life. This husband and wife can be said to be two who do not betray each other. The *Changheng Ge* (Long Resentment Song) says: "As long as the heart is as firm as gold and jade, we will meet in heaven and on earth." How can one know that this thought might also plant the cause for the next life?

The *Guiyuan Congtan* records that Li Weigong presented a square bamboo cane to a monk at Ganlu Temple, saying that this bamboo comes from the Daxuan Kingdom, being solid and square, with nodes and teeth, and emerging from all four sides. However, square bamboo is commonly found in Fujian and Guangdong today, not considered an exotic item, and Daxuan is now part of Kazakhstan, under the jurisdiction of the Ministry of Works. The land there does not produce bamboo, so how could there be such a thing as square bamboo? Furthermore, the *Gujin Zhi* records that the Wusun Kingdom had a fruit called Qingtian core, as large as a six-sheng gourd, which could turn water into wine when hollowed out. However, Wusun is now part of the Ili region, and when asked about it by the Oirat Mongols, they all said they had never heard of it.
