Additionally, the *Duyang Zabian* records that Yuan Zai built the Yunhui Hall in his private residence, using a fragrant grass called Yunxian, which comes from the Yutian Kingdom. Its scent is as pure as jade, does not decay when buried in the ground, and when ground into powder, can be used to coat the walls, hence the name Yunhui. Yutian is now part of Hetian, and no one there has ever heard of this plant. Only in the Western Regions is there a grass called Manugeng, similar to Atractylodes, which Tibetan monks burn to offer to the Buddha and consider it quite precious. However, it is not white and cannot be used to coat walls, all of which are fictional or speculative accounts.

Li Xingtang said: There was a young man whose father had been away on business for a long time and had not returned. Left without supervision, the youth was lured by a usurer into gambling and lost hundreds of gold pieces. The usurer proposed to pay the debt on his behalf in exchange for the youth's forced agreement to write a contract transferring his house. With no choice, he agreed. Worried that he would have no way to face his mother and wife, he did not return home, but instead went into the woods at night and hanged himself. Just as he was tying the noose, he heard the thundering of hooves. Turning around, he saw that it was his father returning. Startled, he asked why he had made such a plan. Judging that he could not hide the truth, he told him what had happened. The father was not at all angry, saying, "This is a common occurrence—why go so far as to take your own life? My earnings from this trip should be enough to cover the debt. You may return home, and I will go myself to repay the money and retrieve the contract." At the time, the gambling session had not yet ended. The father suddenly burst in through the door. He was known to all present, and he called out their names one by one. First, he reprimanded them for luring the youth into gambling, and then accused them of forcing him to sign the contract. The crowd was stunned and had no words to defend themselves. Then he said, "Now that my son has written the house contract, I also cannot take this matter to court through the gambling route. But today, I will repay you the money. You may distribute it among the others tomorrow, and return me the house contract—will that be acceptable?" Seeing that they were at fault, the usurers agreed. The father then took the money from his waist and handed it over to the usurer, who verified the amount. As soon as the contract was obtained, he immediately burned it by the lamp and left in anger. His son returned home, prepared a meal, and waited until morning, but his father did not return. He went to the usurer’s place to investigate and was told, "He has already burned the contract and left." Only then did they begin to suspect that something else was at play. The next day, the usurer opened his chest and found that the money was all paper money. The gold had been personally collected, and everyone had witnessed it. Unable to prove their innocence, they were forced to pay out of their own pockets. They began to suspect they had encountered a ghost. After more than ten days, the news of death finally arrived—the son had already died several months ago.

Li Qiaofeng said that there was a fishing boat moored under a shrine outside the Yungjin Gate in Hangzhou. He heard people speaking loudly inside the shrine. Soon, the god rebuked, saying, "You lot of wild spirits, why do you disgrace the scholars? You should be punished with a cane." Then he heard a defense: "When it's quiet and the moon is bright, the various spirits temporarily wander by the water, slightly easing their sorrows. These two scholars are alone discussing learning and poetry, chattering incessantly. We don't understand what they're saying, and we really find it annoying. We whisper to each other, subtly showing our dissatisfaction, and gradually withdraw. We don't dare to offend in any way." The god remained silent for a moment, then said, "Discussing literature is a refined activity, but it should be done in the right place and with the right people. Please stop, gentlemen." Soon after, phosphorescent lights like fireflies emerged from the shrine, and he heard them chuckling continuously, dispersing and going away. Dong Lai was from Cangzhou. His mother was born in the year Ren Shen of the Kangxi era and lived to be 111 years old by the year Ren Zi of the Qianlong era. She was still strong and healthy, able to eat well. She often encountered imperial edicts of grace, and the village headman wanted to report to the authorities to receive grain and silk, but she always firmly declined and did not wish to. Last year, someone wanted to petition for a tablet and a monument for her, but she also firmly declined and did not wish to. When asked why she refused, she said with a sigh, "I am a poor widow with a hard and thin fate. It is precisely because of my repeated hardships that I have been favored by the deities, living this long. If I seek excessive blessings, then the time of death will come." This old woman's insight is unusually high. Considering her life, she certainly had no excessive or unnecessary pursuits. Thus, she maintained a peaceful and tranquil demeanor, nourishing natural harmony, and preserving this long life.

**Notes from the Reading and Observing Pavilion, Volume Twelve, Huai Xi Miscellaneous Records, Part Two** Anzhong Kuang said: There was a man who was walking alone through the wilderness when he met two people who seemed to be scholars reciting poetry as they walked. One of them dropped a book from his sleeve, and this man picked it up. The characters in the book were very crude and difficult to read, with the sweeping strokes in the calligraphy barely formed. Only parts of it could be recognized—some were talismans, some prescriptions, some New Year couplets, all mixed together in disorder. Occasionally there were passages from the classics, ancient prose, or lines of poetry. Before he had finished reading, the two men suddenly ran back to take it from him, and then vanished in an instant. He suspected they were fox spirits. A strip of paper then flew down into the grass. After they had gone far away, he retrieved it and saw the words on it: “In the *Shijing*, the character *yú* is pronounced *wū*; in the *Yijing*, the character *zhuì* is written without a dot on the left side.” I said this was a metaphor for the crude and unrefined person who enjoys discussing literature and art. But if such a person can apply himself earnestly to these pursuits, is this not better than indulging in gambling, idling, or carousing? If scholars could encourage such people, they would certainly achieve something. Yet instead, they look down on them, dismiss them with contempt, and laugh at them—this shows they have not considered how the sage treated the two boys from Huxiang and Que Party. The scholars who are overly rigid and haughty make people content with self-abandonment. They all seek only to enhance their own reputations, treating the moral state of society and the human heart as something external and unimportant.

Master Ning Xun of Jingzhou was skilled in grinding glass and mixing it with lacquer to create large, bold calligraphy known as "bipei shu," which had a texture resembling stone patterns. He would always carry his skill to wealthy households, enjoying the hospitality of others. Whenever he heard of a gathering, he would eagerly join, often arriving last. One day, he attended a social event in Wuqiao and sold his calligraphy couplets and plaques, earning some money. Suddenly, a dozen people approached him and said, "We want you to dedicate a month to our project, creating several calligraphy characters, which we will give to our relatives and friends as gifts, hoping to receive a little benefit. Please join us for a meal tonight, and tomorrow we will escort you to a certain place." Master Ning was delighted and joined them in a tavern, where they indulged in food and drink until late into the night. When the owner urged them to close the doors, the dozen people suddenly disappeared. Left alone, Master Ning had no choice but to pay for the meal. Disappointed, he returned home, unaware that he had been deceived by magic or a fox spirit. Li Lu Yuan commented, "This gentleman deserved this retribution."

A certain gentleman favored a young, attractive male servant who was gentle and compliant, without the airs of a commoner, and did not take advantage of his favor to act recklessly. Suddenly, the boy cried for several days, his eyes swollen. The gentleman, puzzled, questioned him, and the boy, speaking with a sigh, said, "I have been engaging in sexual relations with you every day without realizing it. Last night in the lodging, a certain person had sexual relations with another boy. I secretly watched through a small hole, and what I saw was extremely unpleasant and hard to describe, very different from a woman lying down. Therefore, I thought, I, a man, have been treated in such a way. It is too late to regret, so I feel ashamed and angry to the point of wanting to die." The gentleman tried to explain and persuade him in many ways, but the boy remained discontented and could not let go of his feelings. Later, he eventually fled away. Someone said that he had already changed his name and was studying in school. Mei Yujin has a work called *Qingni Lianhua Ji* (The Lotus in the Mud), and this boy is also similar to the lotus in the mud, isn't he? Another incident involves a slave named Zhang Kai, who initially served as a servant in Cangzhou. Later, he heard the sound of a criminal weeping in the night, his heart moved, and he left to sell himself to the former Yao An Gong. He was over forty years old with no children.

One day, when his wife was about to give birth, Kai looked sorrowful and said, "Is it a daughter?" Soon after, it turned out to be a daughter. When asked how he knew this, he replied: "When I was a servant, there was someone who accused his wife of having an affair with the neighbor Zhang Jiu. Everyone knew the accusation was false, but the matter was too ambiguous to clear her name. At that time, the official sent me to arrest Zhang Jiu. I reported, saying, 'Zhang Jiu was arrested for unpaid taxes on the fifth day, and on the eighth day he was beaten fifteen times and sent away. Now I do not know where he is; I beg you to grant me more time.' The official checked the tax records, and they were indeed correct. He then scolded the accuser, saying, 'On the seventh day, Zhang Jiu was still under arrest. How could he have gone to your wife’s room?' He beat the accuser and sent him away. In truth, it was a different Zhang Jiu; I used this to cover up and avoid punishment. Last year I heard that this woman had died. Last night I dreamed that she bowed to me, and I knew that she had been reborn as my daughter. Later, this daughter married a merchant. As Kai and his wife grew old and ill, they ultimately relied on her filial care to live out their days. Yang Jiao Shan has a record called *The Demon Becomes a Buddha*. Is this servant also close to being a *demon who becomes a Buddha*?"

Feng Pingyu said: There was a man named Zhang Sihxi, who, being poor, worked as a laborer and wandered to Wanquan Mountain, where he met an elderly couple who left him in charge of tending their garden. They admired his diligence and took him as a son-in-law, giving him their daughter. After several years, the elderly couple said they were going to visit their eldest daughter in the frontier regions, and Zhang Sihxi also took his wife with him to another place. Over time, he gradually realized that she was a fox. Ashamed to be paired with a being of a different kind, he waited for her to be alone, then secretly drew his bow and shot her, striking her left thigh. The fox-woman pulled the arrow from her wound with her hand, then leapt directly before Zhang Sihxi, holding the arrow and accusing him, saying: "You are too unfaithful, which greatly makes one resentful. Although other foxes seduce people and engage in casual, unchaste unions, I was married to you by the command of my parents, through proper rites, and we had the duties of husband and wife. Bound by the Three Bonds, I dare not hold a grudge against you. Since you have cast me aside, I also dare not stay and bother you." She took Zhang Sihxi’s hand, wept bitterly, and after a while, suddenly vanished.

Si Xi returned, and after several years, he fell ill and died without a coffin for burial. Suddenly, a fox girl burst into tears from outside, paid respects to her aunts and uncles, and recounted the entire story. She said, "I have not yet married, so I dare to come." Her mother was moved by her, scolded Si Xi for being wicked, and the fox girl bowed her head and remained silent. A neighbor's wife, indignant, also joined in the scolding. The fox girl glared and said, "It is acceptable for parents to scold their child. How can you scold someone's husband in front of his wife?" She shook off her clothes and finally left, and no one knew where she went. After she left, five taels of silver were found at Si Xi's corpse, and thus he was able to be buried. Later, Si Xi's parents became poor, and often in pots and chests, they unintentionally found money and rice, which was also due to the fox girl. All said that this fox girl not only underwent a physical transformation but also transformed human hearts. Some said that although the fox knew etiquette, it did not go this far. It was probably Ping Yu who deliberately wrote this story to shame those who are not as good. Yao An Gong said, "Ping Yu, though a village elder, has a sincere and honest heart. He has never spoken a word of falsehood in his life. When talking to him, he speaks hesitantly and does not speak much. He is not one who fabricates words."

Lu Baiji, an observer, reported that in Chiping County, a couple died one after the other, leaving behind a son who was just one year old. His brother and sister-in-law both neglected to care for him, and he was on the verge of starving to death. Suddenly, a young woman pushed open the door and entered, taking the child into her arms. She scolded her brother and sister-in-law: "Your brother and his wife's bodies are still cold, how can you be so heartless? It would be better to give the child to me, and I can find a place for him to live." She took the child and left, and no one knows where she went. The neighbors all saw it with their own eyes. Those who knew the story said: "When her brother was alive, he often had a close relationship with a fox woman. Perhaps she did not forget her old feelings and came to see the orphan." This is also similar to the case of Zhang Sihxi's wife.

Urumqi has many narrow alleys and secluded lanes, where the sound of the square chime can be heard, from the first drum of the watchtower to the temple bell about to ring, the lights always flicker. Those who indulge in debauchery do whatever they please, and the officials do not prohibit it, nor can they. There was a cloth merchant from Ningxia named He, who was young, handsome, and accumulated wealth in the thousands, not very stingy, but did not like to visit the brothel district. Instead, he kept over ten female pigs, feeding them to be extremely fat and washing them to be extremely clean, and every day he closed the door and indulged in them repeatedly. The pigs also rubbed against each other and leaned on each other, as if they were close to their male counterparts. The servants and slaves always secretly watched him, but he did not notice. Suddenly, his friend, drunk, playfully questioned him, and he felt ashamed and jumped into a well to die. The local official, Di Hua Ting Tongzhi Mu Jintai, said, "If I did not personally investigate this case, even if Master Sima (referring to Sima Guang) told me, I would not believe it." I wrote a mixed poem about this place, saying, "Is there anything as shocking as a stone breaking through the sky? Later, those who love beauty surpass even Duke Deng. Why did Mr. He die for his passion? Only then did I believe that Liu Lang loves charming pigs." This is about this event. There are people whose eccentricities go to such lengths, thus I know that judging all things by reason does not capture all their variations. Even judging all things by emotion does not capture all their variations.

Zhang Yike, whose origin is unknown, took his wife to seek food in the frontier and worked for a Western merchant. The Western merchant favored his wife and spent money extravagantly, and within a few years, his wealth was exhausted and returned. Zhang Yike then depended on the family for food, but his wife grew tired of him and scolded him, demanding he leave. Zhang Yike said, "Without that person, I would not have this day; it would be unlucky to betray him." He firmly refused to leave. One day, his wife took a stick to chase the Western merchant, and Zhang Yike angrily cursed her. She also cursed him back, saying, "He does not love me; he favors my beauty. I do not love him either; I benefit from his wealth. To trade wealth for beauty, beauty has been obtained, and I originally owe nothing to him. To trade beauty for wealth, wealth does not last, and he cannot hold me accountable. If not dismissed, what is the point of keeping him?" Zhang Yike became even more angry and finally drew a knife to kill her. He first gave a hundred gold coins to the Western merchant and then surrendered himself to the authorities.

Another person, whose name is forgotten, also took his wife out of the frontier. His wife died of illness, and as a result, he was unable to return and began to beg. Suddenly, a Western merchant invited him to a shop and gave him fifty gold coins. Finding it too generous, he insisted on asking the reason. The Western merchant secretly said, "I was closest to your wife, and you don't know." "Your wife was about to die and privately entrusted you to me. I couldn't bear to betray the dead, so I funded your return home." This man angrily threw the money to the ground and finally fought with the merchant, leading them to court. The two events were only a month apart. At that time, Chancellor Wen Gong was stationed in Urumqi. One day, he entertained his subordinates at the Xiu Ye Pavilion. During the conversation, the former magistrate of Zhushan, Chen Tiqiao, said, "One does not change friendships based on wealth, and one does not break promises based on life or death. These are even though they are common people, they embody ancient virtues." The Chancellor frowned and said, "Ancient virtues are indeed true, but what about Zhang Yike? Can he be praised?" Later, the one who killed his wife was sentenced to execution, but the judgment was very lenient. The one who gave the gold was sentenced to flogging, but it was not mentioned that he should be publicly shamed with a枷. The Chancellor pondered for a long time and said with a sigh, "All are illegal, but human feelings have been thin for a long time. If the officials act this way, then it is acceptable."

Jiaxiang Ceng Yinhua said: One evening, under the clear autumn moon, he strolled with several friends outside the fields. Suddenly, a rolling whirlwind came from the southeast, containing over a dozen ghosts. They were pulling and dragging each other, beating and cursing one another, and still, some of their words could be distinguished, as if they were arguing over the differences between Zhu and Lu. Could the calamity of the gatehouse have reached the underworld? Farewell, farewell, again farewell, the sorrowful path at the gate. Step by step, again step by step, still holding affection despite twists and turns. A glance back filled with emotion, seeing the willow tree in front of my window. North of the willow is a tall building, with pearl curtains half drawn. Yesterday, I was the girl in the upstairs room, talking to a parrot behind the curtain. Now, I am the person outside the wall, with red tears staining the silk handkerchief. Outside the wall and upstairs, no more than ten paces apart, how can it be so close, as if separated by thousands of mountains. How sad to be so utterly separated, from now on, forever apart. The departing crane lingers in vain, who cares about the mournful cry. Lingering as the day turns late, determined to return. Tearing the Xiang skirt, crying while writing a letter to the husband. Pitifully, a single foot of silk, each word stained with blood, each word a painful cry, old love tugs at the heart. If you can take back what has been spilled, I willingly accept punishment. Otherwise, die before you, better than being abandoned alive. Death has no other words, wish to be buried in your family's soil. If I could become a heart-wrenching flower, still be able to live in your family.

Rightly seen in the Yongle Encyclopedia, titled "Li Fangshu's Blood-Stained Poem." It does not specify the dynasty, nor does it detail Li Fangshu's life, and it is unknown whether it was written by Li Fangshu himself, like the poem of Dou Xuan's wife, or if it was written by someone else on his behalf, like the poem of Jiao Zhongqing's wife. There is no surviving copy of it; while I was proofreading the Four Treasuries, I happened to come across it. I loved its tender and melancholic nature, without a single trace of resentment or anger, it could move even ghosts and gods to tears. I had a clerk transcribe it onto a sheet of paper, but it was lost after some time. Now, while on duty in Luanyang, I reviewed my old books and suddenly found it in a small box. Lost for several hundred years, it finally reappeared in the world. Is it not the soul or ghost of a loyal spirit, with a spirit that connects with the three luminaries, that cannot be erased? Lü Ershan, Deputy Censor, said: "This poem follows the example of Han Qiwang's granddaughter's poem. Since she was at the end of the Song dynasty, then Li Fangshu must have been a Song dynasty person. By analogy, it is likely."

My uncle, Mr. An Shizhai, one evening went to bed and heard a knocking sound outside his door. When he asked, there was no response, and when he looked, he saw nothing. After several nights, the same thing happened again, and after more nights, it occurred in other rooms as well, happening more than ten times with no other explanation. Later, a thief was caught in the village, who claimed he had entered a certain family's home more than ten times, each time because the people were asleep, so he returned. When asked, the dates matched. It was then known that the ghosts were warning about the thief. Therefore, auspicious signs are not necessarily good omens, and strange phenomena are not necessarily disasters; it depends on the person. In the second year of Yongle during the Ming Dynasty, prominent families from Jiangnan were relocated to the capital region. Our founding ancestor, Jiao Po, moved from Shangyuan to Jingcheng in Xian County. Later, descendants multiplied and branched out to live in Cui Village, three miles east of Jingcheng. Now, the locals say that many officials and scholars come from Cui Village, so they all call themselves Cui Village Jis, showing their prosperity. But my clan calls itself Jingcheng Jis, to remember our roots. Jiao Po's former residence was in Jingcheng, between Cui Village, and destroyed by war for a long time. The site now belongs to my cousin Uncle Mu'an, who studied the classics with me and passed the provincial examination in 1776, planning to build a house and move there. My late grandfather, Yao'an, pre-emptively inscribed a couplet saying: "The place where our founding ancestor first settled, this day the descendants rebuild the family." Later, the room was not built, and my grandfather passed away in August 1644, leaving behind his orphaned children. He chose this place as the most auspicious and then cut off other land to exchange with Uncle Mu'an for the burial site. The earlier couplet seemed like a self-fulfilling prophecy. All things are predestined, isn't that true?

Concubine Shen, whom I named Mingjuan, was the daughter of a family originally from Changzhou who had settled in Hejian. Her father made it his home there. She had two sisters, and Mingjuan was the younger one. She was exceptionally intelligent and graceful, unlike any ordinary woman. She often told her sister in private, "I cannot be a wife in a peasant's household, and noble families would never take me as a wife. Perhaps I can be a concubine in a wealthy family?" Her mother overheard this and eventually supported her wish. Mingjuan was clever and kind, never offending anyone. When she first came to me, she paid her respects to Lady Ma, who said, "I heard that you voluntarily chose to be a concubine. It is not easy to be a concubine." Mingjuan bowed and replied, "If I did not want to be a concubine, it would indeed be difficult. But since I wish to be a concubine, what difficulty could there be?" Thus, Lady Ma always loved her as if she were a cherished daughter and once told me, "A woman should die before the age of forty; people still mourn and cherish her. But to grow old in a blue skirt and white hair, like a lonely sparrow or a rotting mouse, is something I do not wish for." And so, she died as she wished, on the twenty-fifth day of the fourth month in the year Xin Hai, at the age of thirty.

Initially, he could only read and write, following me in checking and organizing books. After a long time, he began to roughly understand the meaning of texts and could also compose poetry using simple language. On the verge of death, he gave a small portrait to his daughter, recited a poem orally, and asked me to write it down: "Thirty years have been like a dream, the likeness was handed over to the daughter to keep, in the future, tell the story of my life, recognize me as the fifth lady of Suzhou." He passed away peacefully. When the illness was severe, I was on duty at the Summer Palace, stayed in an old house in the west of槐西 in Haidian. One night, I had two vague dreams of him, thinking it was due to lingering thoughts. Later, I learned that on that night he fainted, after two hours, he regained consciousness, and told his mother: "Just had a dream of arriving at the residence in Haidian, there was a loud noise like thunder, and woke up in fright." I remembered that night, indeed, a vase hanging on the wall fell to the ground, and I then realized that his soul had indeed come. Therefore, I wrote on his portrait: "Partly similar, partly different, perhaps the fragrant soul returned under the moon, a fleeting glimpse in a spring dream leaves no trace, the most touching part is the vague and indistinct nature." I also wrote: "Even until death, the silkworm still has silk, the wandering soul of the beautiful maiden does not need to be doubted, a sound shattered the dream of the pear blossom, exactly at the time the copper vase fell to the ground." This is a record of this event.

Separated by thousands of miles, people from Yan and Zhao discuss the customs of Yunnan and Guizhou, yet claim that those living in those regions do not know as accurately as they do—Is this true or false? Born decades later, young children discuss the affairs of the elderly, yet claim that those who have seen the person do not know as accurately as they do—Is this true or false? Zuo Qiuming, serving as the official historian of the state of Lu, personally saw the sage, and his account of the Spring and Autumn period indeed has a clear origin and development. By the mid-Tang Dynasty, scholars like Lu Chun began to hold different views, and after Song Dynasty scholar Sun Fu, scholars engaged in heated debates, with various theories contending, all claiming that the Zuo Zhuan is not credible, while their own theories are credible—How is this any different from that? The study of the Han Confucians was practical, while the Song Confucians were more concerned with reputation; if they did not propose new ideas, they could not attract attention; if they did not reject old theories, they could not propose new ideas. The interpretation of the classics can all be debated verbally, but the events in the Spring and Autumn period are clearly defined, difficult to alter. Thus, they claimed that Zuo Qiuming was from Chu, from the early Warring States period, or from Qin, and the claim that he was a historian of Lu and personally saw the sage was shaken. Since he was not a historian of Lu and did not personally see the sage, the events in the text are all unreliable, and thus they could say whatever they wanted. By the late Song Dynasty, Zhao Pengfei wrote the "Spring and Autumn Canon," even not knowing that Chengfeng was the birth mother of Duke Xi, how can one still discuss titles and determine praise or blame? Cheng Duanxue of the Yuan Dynasty furthered this criticism, being particularly aggressive.

Occasionally at the Five Clouds, which is the Pavilion of the Original Heart, I examined and corrected the annotations of the *Spring and Autumn Annals* by *端学*. *周书昌* then mentioned that a certain scholar had treasured this book as a great treasure. One day, while traveling with a friend to Mount Tai, they happened to discuss the meaning of the text, highly praising the scholar's interpretation of the return of *叔姬* to her husband's family, analyzing it with great precision. That night, the scholar dreamed of an anciently dressed woman with a grand retinue, who sternly questioned him: "I am the eldest daughter of King Wu of Zhou, the goddess of the Eastern Mountain. The Supreme Deity recognized my difficult and complete virtue, following in the footsteps of *共姜*, and made me a noble deity under *太姬*. For over two thousand years, you have been spreading the false words of a petty scholar, accusing me of being unfaithful to *纪季* and slandering me, which is truly heart-wrenching. I returned to *Ji* in the seventh year of Duke Yin, and to *Shun* in the twentieth year of Duke Zhuang, a span of thirty-four years, well over fifty years. As an elderly widow, how could I know that *季* would surely be pleased with me? Crossing the border to follow him, according to the rules of the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, only the wives of feudal lords are recorded, just as only officials are recorded. I, as a concubine, am not typically recorded in the annals, but because of my unwavering loyalty, Confucius made a special note of me. What basis does *程端学* have to fabricate such a slanderous accusation? If you spread this again, I will cut off your tongue and command the deity to strike you with a bone club." The scholar woke up screaming and immediately destroyed the book. I jokingly told *书昌*: "You are devoted to Song learning, and yet you say this." *书昌* replied: "I take what is good and do not hide what is bad." This is indeed a fair and balanced opinion.

The Temple of General Yang Ye (杨令公祠) is located inside the ancient pass of Gu Bei Kou, where it serves as a place of worship for the Song dynasty general Yang Ye. According to the *Chu Ping Shan Shui Ji* (昌平山水记) by Gu Tinglin, the *History of Song* states that Yang Ye died in battle at the northern pass of the Great Wall, likely in the region of Yunzhong, not at the ancient pass of Gu Bei Kou. However, when examining *Wang Zeng Xing Cheng Lu* (王曾行程录), it is noted that there was already a temple dedicated to Yang Ye inside the ancient pass of Gu Bei Kou. This suggests that the Liao people, who valued Yang Ye's loyalty and bravery, built a temple in his honor. Wang Zeng, who was personally involved in the conflict with Yang Ye and was sent on a mission, was only a few decades away from the events, making it unlikely that he did not know the true location of Yang Ye's death. The *History of Song*, compiled during the late Yuan dynasty by Toghtoa (托克托), who was far removed from the events of Yang Ye's life, seems less reliable than earlier sources like *Wang Zeng Xing Cheng Lu*. The name "托克托" was initially transcribed as "脱脱" but was corrected to "托克托" for accuracy, as per the *Three Histories National Language Explanation* (三史国语解).

In proofreading rare and secret texts, I have visited the Summer Palace no fewer than four times: in winter during the year Dingwei, in autumn during Wu Shen, in summer during Ji You, and in spring during Ren Zi. I have thus beheld the beauty of all four seasons. Each time, I would take a boat to Wenjing Pavilion, where the mountain's form and the water's spirit all arose naturally; the color of the trees and the sound of the springs were not of the mundane world. Whether cloudy or sunny, morning or evening, the scenery took a thousand forms and ten thousand appearances. Even a single bird or flower was as if painted by an artist. Most remarkable of all was the fine grass that ran along the slopes and valleys, soft and smooth like green felt, no more than a few inches high, and as uniform as if cut by a shears—no single stem was uneven in length. The garden attendants called it "Regulation Grass." Only a few steps beyond the palace walls, however, it immediately began to spread and flourish. Could this not be a fine plant born by heaven itself to accompany the imperial tour? Mr. Li Youdan said: There was a man named Zhang Zike who taught students in the village, living in quiet solitude with few visitors. Once, while walking in the fields, he met a gentleman of great refinement and elegance. They exchanged names and became quite friendly. The gentleman said he lived in a nearby village and had no one with whom he could converse, adding that meeting Zhang was like hearing footsteps in an empty valley. So they went together to the school, where the students were reading the *Classic of Filial Piety*. The gentleman asked Zhang: "This book has both the current and the ancient versions. Which one is correct?" Zhang replied: "Sima Zhen has spoken of this in detail." He continued: "Recently, while reading the *Lüshi Chunqiu*, I saw that the chapter on the feudal lords in the *Examine the Minute* section is in the current version. As seen by people of the Warring States period, where else could there be an ancient version?" The man was delighted and said: "You are truly a learned man!" From then on, he often visited the school. Zhang wished to visit him in return, but the man always declined, citing poverty and lack of accommodation. The man and his wife lived in a dilapidated house, with no room to entertain guests. Zhang, therefore, ceased his attempts.

One night, he suddenly asked Zhang if he feared ghosts. Zhang replied, "Spirits that have not yet departed from their physical form are still ghosts, while those who have already departed are merely people. Although I have not seen them, I feel there is nothing to fear." The person blushed and said, "Since you do not fear them, I will not deceive you. I am a ghost, born into a noble family, unable to follow the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts or compete for money and grain. Gratefully being part of the same kind, may I ask for a meal from you?" Zhang, whose relationship with the person was deep, also had no doubts or fears. He immediately prepared food and invited him to come several times, discussing and studying books and documents, which were very organized. They occasionally discussed the principles of Taiji and Wuji. The person became indignant and said, "There is a saying that the ways of heaven are distant, while human affairs are near. The Six Classics discuss all human affairs. Even though the *Yi Jing* explains yin and yang, it also uses the ways of heaven to clarify human affairs. To talk about the ways of heaven instead of human affairs is already too abstract and vague. Furthermore, discussing what comes before the primordial, empty words lead to disputes; what use is there in this? You pay attention to ancient meanings, that's why I come to you for food. Is this how you see things?" He brushed off his clothes and stood up, immediately disappearing. Zhang waited again at the place where they met, but did not see him again.

When I was supervising education in Fujian, an official in the educational bureau reported that during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, a concubine of the provincial examination examiner fell to her death from a building, with no other apparent cause, and it was initially thought that she had accidentally stumbled. Later, someone revealed the story, saying that the concubine was originally from Shandong, around fourteen or fifteen years old, and had married a poor man for a few months. The couple were very fond of each other, inseparable, but due to a famine that year, they could not support themselves, and her mother-in-law sold her to those who trafficked women. The couple held each other, cried all night, and bit their arms to mark their commitment before parting. The husband could not forget her, begging for food along the way and rushing to catch up with the traffickers. He secretly followed them to the capital, sometimes catching a glimpse of her in the carriage. Being young and timid, he feared being scolded, so he dared not approach to look at her, only shedding tears. After entering the official matchmaker's house, he would often wait by the side of the door, occasionally catching a glimpse of her, and they made a pact not to die. They hoped that one day, whether in heaven or on earth, they would eventually meet again. Later, he heard that she had been taken as a concubine by the provincial examination examiner, so he became his private secretary's servant and accompanied him to Fujian. However, they were separated by inner and outer boundaries, with no way to communicate, and his wife was unaware of this. One day, she died of illness.

A woman learned about her husband through the details provided by maidservants, including his name, hometown, appearance, and age, and finally came to understand. At the time, she was sitting at her desk in the upstairs room, standing silently for a long time. Suddenly, she recounted her entire story to the crowd, cried out loudly, and then leaped to her death. The local official chose not to publicize the event, so the story did not spread, but the truth cannot be hidden. Generally, women who die for their husbands have two reasons: one is to uphold moral and social norms, choosing to die rather than be disgraced, which is rooted in the teachings of propriety; the other is to endure shame and live on, clinging to life at any cost, hoping for a reunion like the broken mirror reunited, to be restored to their former state. Until all hope is lost and circumstances are dire, they finally choose to die to prove their resolve, which stems from their emotions. This woman did not die at the hands of traffickers, nor did she die in the home of a matchmaker; she waited until her virtue was tarnished and her beauty faded, after receiving news of her former husband's death, and then she died. Indeed, it was too late. However, her resolve to die had been firmly set for a long time; it was only that her lingering affection for him made her unable to sever ties. In her heart, she did not consider whether to die or not as a betrayal of her husband's kindness, but rather saw the delay as a failure to meet his expectations. One can pity her fate, grieve for her intentions, and regret her emotional mistakes. But to rigidly apply the moral principles of the *Spring and Autumn Annals* and criticize those who are not well-versed in the classics, is it truly a way to be kind to others?

In the seventh month of the Year of the Ox, I had a small gathering at Song Mengquan's home, where we casually discussed matters of fox spirits. Nie Songyan said, "Do you know about a certain incident involving a nobleman? In the past, during the provincial examination in Jinan, I heard of a man named Ji Sheng, whose hometown I cannot recall whether it was Shouguang or Jiaozhou. Once, late in the evening, he encountered a woman walking alone in the muddy and slippery conditions. He asked her for help in supporting herself, thinking that she must be a fox spirit. Tentatively, he tried to be intimate with her, believing that this would be sufficient to understand the nature of demons and spirits. He then told her, 'I recognize you. Do not deceive me. However, getting a wife like you is also desirable. After everyone is quiet, you can come to my study. Do not play around here, as it only causes unnecessary complications.' The woman laughed and left. That night, she indeed came, and they engaged in intimate acts for several nights. He gradually became entranced by her and decided to reject her, telling her not to come again. The fox spirit, feeling resentful, refused to leave. The man sternly said, 'Do not do that. Matters between men and women, the authority lies with the man. If a man seeks a woman and she does not want him, he can still force her. If a woman seeks a man and he does not want her, his heart is as cold as iron, and even if she uses force, it is useless. Especially since you came to steal my essence. Not out of genuine affection. I do not want to betray your feelings. You've seen many people, it's hard to talk about chastity. I also don't want to corrupt your chastity. Start a relationship and end it. What a gentleman dislikes, he talks about for others, not for people like you. Why cling to this, it's just a waste of time.' The fox spirit left with no words to say. Thus, I realized that once ensnared by temptation, one clings to it until death, and those who cannot be driven away by talismans are ultimately due to the entanglement of desire, unable to cut it off. If one remains indifferent and unmoved, what could she take from him that would make her stay?"

Fa Nan Ye recounted another story: In a village, a few ruffians heard that a certain family's abandoned grave was home to a fox that could transform its shape to charm people. At night, they carried and placed traps at the entrance of the den and indeed caught two female foxes. To prevent them from changing form, they quickly stabbed their thighs with a needle and tied them with a rope, holding a knife and threatening them, saying, "If you can indeed transform into a human, serve us wine. Otherwise, we will immediately dismember you." The two foxes howled and jumped around, as if they did not understand. The ruffians grew angry and stabbed one of them to death. The other one then spoke in human language, saying, "I have no clothes or shoes, and when I transform into a human, what form will I take?" They again held a knife to her neck, and she gracefully transformed into a beautiful woman, naked with not a single thread of clothing. The crowd was greatly pleased and took turns being disrespectful, embracing her and making her serve wine, but they kept pulling the rope, never letting go. The fox spoke softly and pleaded to have the rope removed. As soon as they let go, she quickly disappeared. On their way back, before reaching the door, they saw a distant firelight, and several houses were in ruins. They knew it was the fox's revenge. The fox does not disturb people, but people disturb the fox. Those who do much wrong will surely face retribution.

Mr. Tian Baiyan recounted an incident: His second wife was a young and beautiful woman who was bewitched by a fox. Despite investigations and punishments, there was no evidence. Later, a highly virtuous Taoist priest summoned a divine general, captured the fox, and brought it to the altar, demanding a written confession. Everyone heard the fox say, "I was born in the region of Yu, and I occasionally beat my wife. She secretly escaped here and became intimate with someone. I hold a deep grudge against her, so I took revenge." The man remembered that he indeed had this experience in his childhood, but it had been more than ten years. The Taoist priest said, "A grudge that has already become deep should naturally be repaid immediately. Why has it taken so long up to now? Perhaps you know this matter through investigation and are using it as an excuse?" The fox replied, "The previous wife was a chaste woman, and I feared incurring heavenly punishment, so I dared not approach her. This woman is frivolous, and I was able to seduce and become intimate with her. Cause and effect balance each other, and spirits do not blame. What can the teacher blame?" The Taoist priest pondered deeply for a long time and asked, "How many days has the certain person been intimate with your wife?" The fox replied, "More than a year." The Taoist priest then asked, "How many days have you been intimate with this woman?" The fox replied, "More than three years." The Taoist priest became angry and said, "The repayment is excessive, and the blame is also on you. If you do not go away, I will summon you to be handed over to the Thunder Department." The fox then admitted guilt and left. Mr. Qingyuan, the father of Mengquan, said, "This shows that the thoughts of right and wrong are known by all demons and spirits; the principle of repayment and giving is something that spirits cannot take away."

Qingyuan Mr. also told a story: There was a maid named Zhu, who was of simple nature. As she grew older, she gradually became clever and cunning, and her facial features became graceful and charming. Therefore, she was taken as a concubine, and she had a lot of cunning. She managed everything in an orderly manner, handling even the trivial details of daily life. Family members dared not deceive her even the slightest bit; if they did, it would surely fail. She was also skilled at accumulating wealth. All that she sold or traded, the price would surely be high the following year. Zhu gradually became prosperous, and he favored her exclusively in his bedroom. One day, she suddenly asked Zhu, "Do you know who I am?" Zhu laughed and said, "Are you crazy?" Then, as a joke, he mentioned her nickname and said, "Are you not so-and-so?" She replied, "No, I have been gone for a long time. Now I am the wife of someone in a certain place, and I have a child who is already seven or eight years old. I originally was a fox woman. Nine generations ago, you were a great merchant, and I was your accountant. You treated me well, but I embezzled more than three thousand gold from you. As a result, I was punished in the afterlife and was reborn as a fox. I refined my form for several hundred years and fortunately achieved enlightenment. However, due to this, I am still burdened and cannot ascend to immortality. Therefore, because of this maid's escape, I transformed her appearance to serve you. Calculated over more than ten years, what I earned was enough to offset what was owed. Now I have left by disintegrating my body. After I leave, I will definitely show my fox form. You can give a certain servant to bury me. He will definitely tear my body apart to take the skin. Do not blame him. Four generations ago, when he was a starving corpse, I had not yet achieved enlightenment and once ate his corpse. Let him tear me apart, perhaps the grudge can be resolved." Soon, she transformed into a fox and lay on the ground. A beautiful woman, several inches tall, emerged from the top of her head and gradually departed. Her appearance was completely different. Zhu could not bear it and buried her himself. Eventually, the servant secretly dug up her body, peeled and sold her skin. Zhu knew it was a karmic debt and sighed deeply.

According to Sun Shulín, a descendant, there was a man named He from Gaochuan who was extremely poor. On the eve of the New Year, he had no means to survive the year, so he went to his relatives and acquaintances to borrow money but received nothing. He could only buy wine to entertain himself. Depressed and bored, he drank to drown his sorrows and became very drunk before returning home. It was already dusk, and he met an old man carrying a bag who was struggling to walk. The old man asked He to carry the bag to Gaochuan and offered to pay him for it. He agreed. The bag was very heavy, and He thought to himself that he had no money to survive the year, and if he stole the bag and ran away, the old, feeble man would certainly not be able to catch up. So, He ran as fast as he could, and the old man called out from behind but could not catch him. He ran madly for seven or eight li and just barely made it home. He quickly closed the door and entered, called for a light, and looked at the bag. Inside was a newly cut piece of poplar wood, weighing over thirty catties. He then realized that he had been tricked by a ghost. Perhaps because of his greedy and cunning nature, he had long been hated by ghosts, so they took advantage of his predicament to mock him. Otherwise, why would the ghost play tricks on him alone among many passersby? At that time, he had not yet seen something desirable, so he had not yet developed a desire to steal, so why did the ghost wait in the middle of the road?

There is a story about a man from Duzhuang named Zhang Ziyi, who had a strong liking for drinking. When he was in his fifties, he died of a cold illness. Just as they were about to bury him, he suddenly revived and said, "I have recovered from my illness. Just now I went to the underworld and saw three large jars of wine stored there, all labeled with my name, Zhang Ziyi. One of them had already been opened and still contained half a jar. These must all be my food; I must drink them all before I can die." And indeed, he recovered. He continued to drink freely for over twenty years. One day, he told his close friends, "I will probably die soon. Last night I dreamed of going to the underworld again and saw that the three jars of wine were all gone." After several days, he indeed died without any illness. Then again, the supplementary records and biographies record the story of Li Weigong eating sheep. Is it true?

The Scholar Wang Jintang of Baodi said that the old city wall of Baodi had collapsed and deteriorated, with water eroding and rain penetrating, creating many holes. Evil spirits then made their lair within it. After the city wall was repaired, the old walls were destroyed, leaving the spirits without a place to rely on. They dispersed to empty houses and ancient temples, spreading out to torment people, and many men and women were seduced by them. Suddenly, a Taoist priest arrived and taught people to take forty-nine black beans, recite spells, and refine them for seven days to strike the evil spirits, who died instantly upon contact. Wang Jintang's house had many empty rooms, and the spirits took over. One servant woman was also seduced by them. When the Taoist's refined beans were thrown at the spirits, a strong wind suddenly arose, as if many people were shouting, "The lady was wounded, and she died." Upon inspection, a large snake was found, with wounds resembling those caused by bullets or lead balls. Wang Jintang asked the Taoist, "Since spirits that seduce women must be male spirits, why did this snake call itself 'Lady'?" The Taoist replied, "This is a female snake. Snakes can seduce people using both their heads and tails to emit a kind of spirit energy, not necessarily through direct contact." Soon after, someone who only heard the wind suddenly felt like they were having a nightmare, realizing that their spirit was being sucked away, and their essence was overflowing, confirming the Taoist's words. Another person suddenly saw an evil spirit and threw the beans, which were in a paper wrapper, without time to unwrap them, and the spirit was also wounded and fled. Another person was seduced by a female spirit and was given the beans, but he was too captivated by her beauty and refused to strike, and thus met his end. Evil spirits tormenting people is a common occurrence, but when they gather together and unleash their poison at once, it is an extraordinary evil that the heavens cannot tolerate. This Taoist priest neither came before nor after, but just at this time, perhaps it was the work of a deity.

A certain Censorial Official's wife passed away. After the coffin was closed, offerings were made. Suddenly, a white dove flew into the curtain, but upon searching, nothing was seen. In the commotion, smoke and flames erupted from the coffin, consuming the roofs and pillars in an instant. It was said that when she was alive, she ruled her subordinates strictly. Whenever she bought female slaves, after the contract was signed and they entered the door, she would make them kneel and first give them hundreds of words of instruction, which she called "teaching." Then she would strip them and bind their hands behind their backs, whipping them a hundred times, which she called "testing the punishment." If they turned or cried out, the whipping would become even more severe. When they were beaten until they could no longer speak or move, making a sound like striking wood or stone, only then would she say they had learned to fear. After that, she would put them to work. Lady Chen, the wife of a former Censorial Official from An Prefecture, was my late mother's aunt. I once visited her home and she often said: "Her servants and maids, when they marched in and out, were as orderly as if a great general were training an army." I also once visited a relative's home, and he was my father-in-law. Entering his inner chamber, I saw two whips hanging on either side of the door, with bloodstains on the tassels and handles that were polished to a shine. I heard that whenever he was about to go to bed, he would tie each of the maids to a stool, then cover them with a quilt to prevent them from running away or harming themselves. Later, when he died, his thighs were covered with festering sores that exposed the bone, just like the marks of a beating.

Criminal court case files often involve deaths resulting from injuries sustained during beatings, which lead to fatal illnesses. According to the law, within the period of responsibility for such injuries (known as "gu"), the perpetrator must be held accountable. Once, Grand Master of Ceremonies 吕含晖 published a secret formula. The formula consisted of three ingredients: Schizonepeta tenuifolia, beeswax, and fish bladder. The fish bladder was stir-fried until it turned yellow, with each ingredient weighed at five qian (approximately 3.125 grams). Three leaves of mugwort were added to a bowl of high-quality alcohol, and the mixture was boiled in a double boiler for the duration of burning one incense stick. Drinking the hot concoction would induce sweating and lead to immediate recovery. However, for 100 days after the treatment, one must abstain from eating chicken meat. Later, his son 慕堂 passed the imperial examination in the year 庚午, and people believed this was a reward for his father's publication of the secret formula.

The *Yuyang Zazu* records a dice charm that goes: "I Di Mi Di Mi Jie Luo Di." If one recites it a hundred thousand times, all six sides of the dice will respond to the caller's wishes. When tested, it sometimes works and sometimes does not. I believe this is akin to reciting nonsensical words to treat illness. Generally, when the spirit is concentrated, the energy responds to it, and when the energy is affected, spirits move it. This is what is meant by "true sincerity can even move metal and stone." If one firmly believes in it, sincerity will arise, and sincerity will certainly bring about movement. If one merely tries it out, there will be no sincerity, and without sincerity, there will be no movement. All practices of holding and refining, whether they be this charm or others, follow this principle. An old servant named Lan Gui once said that when he first arrived in the capital, he stayed with someone at the Fuqing Guild Hall. Outside the gate were many tombs. One night, it was pitch black, and he heard loud, noisy sounds, crying, and the voices of several people trying to persuade someone. Thinking there was no one here, he concluded it must be spirits fighting. He peered through a crack in the door but saw nothing. Holding his breath and listening intently, after a few moments, he saw a man moving a coffin, mistakenly taking someone else's. The woman in the coffin had a husband, and her grave was nearby. The husband believed his wife had been abducted by the man and demanded that the man's wife be given to him in compensation. The woman did not agree and began to quarrel. Just then, the guards passed by, ringing their bells, and the commotion ceased. It is unknown what ultimately happened, and it is also unknown what will happen when the woman whose coffin was mistakenly taken is eventually buried. Therefore, the belief that spirits attach to the living rather than the dead is not necessarily true.

虞 Dun had a tenant farmer named Sun, who was skilled with a bird gun and never missed his shots. Once, he saw an oriole and was ordered to capture it. Sun asked, "Should I capture it alive or dead?" When asked how lead bullets could decide its fate, he replied, "To capture the dead one, shoot it directly; to capture the living one, startle it into flight and hit its wing." He was told to capture the living one, so he raised his gun and fired. The oriole indeed fell, and upon inspection, it was found that one of its wings was broken. Such was his skill and precision. Coincidentally, there was a person who could recite a spell for releasing animals. They made a pact: "If I recite the spell three times, your hundred shots will miss." When tested, it was indeed so. This was tried many times, and every time it proved effective. However, the words of the spell were crude and almost laughable. It is hard to understand how it could have such an effect. Similarly, all the spells I have heard that can restrain or control are mostly crude and vulgar like this. Yet, they all work, and none can explain why they do.

Mr. Cai Geshan said: While proofreading the Siku Quanshu, I lost my salary several times due to errors in the text. However, there was one instance where the act of proofreading proved particularly useful. My young grandson once accidentally swallowed a nail. The doctor tried to induce vomiting or treat the condition with traditional medicines, but the nail did not come out. Gradually, he became weaker. Later, while proofreading the *Su Shen Liang Fang*, I found a specific remedy for treating children who have swallowed metal objects. It stated that one should peel the outer layer of newly formed charcoal, grind it into a fine powder, mix it with three bowls of porridge, and feed it to the child. The nail would then come out on its own. Following the method described, the nail indeed came out wrapped in charcoal. Thus, I realized that even miscellaneous or lesser-known books can be useful. This book is not widely available, but it is fully preserved in the Yongle Dadian. When I was heading a book office, I commissioned Wang Shiting to organize and compile the text into a coherent volume. "Su Shen" refers to Su Dongpo (Su Shi) and Shen Kuo (Shen Kuo). Both were interested in medicine, and their discussions were compiled by others during the Song dynasty to form this book.

Ye Shoufu, an old doctor from Dezhou, frequently visited our home, and I still saw him when I was young. I remember him speaking with my grandfather, Yao An Gong, about how he often traveled from Pingyuan to Haifeng. One night, they lost their way, and all the attendants were confused. Rain and wind were about to come, and there were no villages or settlements in any direction. They saw an abandoned temple and decided to seek temporary shelter. The temple door was slightly open, and the door had faintly visible large white-powdered characters. They lit a fire to see, and the message read, "This temple has many ghosts; travelers should not stay." With no way to advance or retreat, Ye Shoufu pushed the door and bowed twice, saying, "A traveler encounters rain, seeks the protection of the deity, will leave once the rain stops, and dares not linger long." They heard a voice from the ceiling board saying, "Appreciate your courtesy, but today I am very drunk, cannot receive guests, what to do. You may sit by the eastern wall; the western wall is a scorpion den, fear being stung. Do not drink the water dripping from the eaves, fear it might contain snake saliva. The sour pears behind the hall are ripe, you can pick and eat them." Ye Shoufu's hair stood on end, and he was silent and dared not speak. When the rain slightly stopped, he hurriedly bowed and thanked before leaving, like escaping from a tiger's mouth. Yao An Gong said, "The sign on the door must have hurt many people, but you were unharmed and received detailed instructions. It is because of your courtesy, nothing can resist courtesy. Because of your sincerity, nothing can resist sincerity. Even different species have no difference. You are not only experienced in medicine, but also experienced in dealing with the world."

Zhū Dǎojiāng says, a scholar from Xīntāi was taking the provincial examination. He was still half a day's journey from Jīnán when he and several friends set out early in the morning for a cool breeze. As they traveled, two donkeys were seen chasing each other, one in front and one behind, but they did not pay much attention to them. As it became lighter, they realized the donkeys were carrying two women. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be an elderly woman, about fifty or sixty years old, fat and dark-skinned, and a young woman about twenty years old, who was very beautiful. The scholar looked at her frequently, and the young woman suddenly turned back and exclaimed, "Is this your brother?" The scholar was surprised and at a loss for words. The young woman said, "I am your cousin's younger sister. Our family's rules prohibit us from seeing each other, so you don't recognize me, but I have often peeked at you through the curtains, so I know you." The scholar remembered that he had a cousin who married in Jīnán, and they began to converse. He asked why she was traveling so early in the morning. She replied, "Yesterday, my younger brother-in-law and I went to visit my aunt who is ill. We originally planned to return today, but my aunt has legal matters to attend to, and my younger brother-in-law has to go to the capital, so he cannot return immediately. I am returning early to prepare my luggage." She gazed affectionately, displaying a charming demeanor, and subtly revealed her fondness from when they first met as teenagers. When they reached a fork in the road, he invited her to a nearby inn for a meal, and she happily followed.

Agreed with fellow travelers to wait for them at a certain place until late. When the bell rang, they did not come, and there was no news the next day either. Searching along the branching paths to find them, their donkey was found in the wild fields, with the saddle still not removed. Thoroughly searching among the villages, no one knew of these two women. Further inquiries led to the home of his cousin, who had died more than half a year ago. Whether she was bewitched by a ghost, devoured by a monster, or lured by a thief, all are unknown. Thus, this person is gone forever. This is also a warning for young, frivolous people. At that time, Fangke Village was present and spoke of hearing a similar story during his travels in Qin and Long, where a man was later buried together with his wife. When the tomb was opened, there was a male corpse inside, and it is unknown what the two souls did when they met underground. According to the *Jiao's Yi Lin*, "Two men sharing one wife, none suitable for her, if this is the divination." Dai Zongyuan was also present and said, "The *History of the Later Han* still records cases of three men sharing one wife, why do you have such narrow knowledge?" I jokingly said, "The two of you, stop shouting, did you forget about Princess Shan Yin who had thirty male lovers?" However, those women were not afraid of their husbands, this ghost privately hid the young man, not considering the future burial, it was inevitable to indulge in desires and forget the consequences. Dai Zongyuan sighed and said, "Indulging in desires and forgetting the consequences, is it only this ghost?"

A traditional saying attributes the concept of "young male lovers" (娈童) to the Yellow Emperor, according to Qian Jian Shi Xinmei. Although Qian could name the book, I have forgotten it now—it is likely a fabricated claim. The term "naughty children" (顽童) first appears in the *Shang Shu* (Book of Documents), but this is derived from the *Pseudo-Wei Guwen* (False Ancient Texts) attributed to Mei Ze, which is not reliable. The *Yi Zhou Shu* (Lost Book of Zhou) mentions "beautiful men breaking old customs" (美男破老), which might refer to this practice. The *Zhou Li* (Rites of Zhou) discusses cases involving individuals who are not fully male, noting that they are naturally unable to engage in sexual relations. However, from ancient times to the present, there have been no legal cases involving such individuals; the text is concise and straightforward, suggesting it refers to this matter. Generally, women's sexual behavior is driven by natural desires, while young male lovers do not have such intentions; they are all young and deceived, or coerced by threats or promises. It is said that a wealthy family enjoyed the company of young boys and feared that they might refuse out of shame, so they bought many beautiful boys under ten years old. When playing with these boys, they made them hold candles and stand by, gradually becoming accustomed to various sexual acts, seeing them as normal. After three or four years, when the boys grew older, they were ready for sexual relations, like ships sailing downstream. A monk who was part of the household advised them: "Such things are common in the world, and I cannot stop you from doing them. However, if it is voluntary, it is similar to keeping courtesans, and the fault is still minor. But if you deliberately manipulate and corrupt the innocence of a child, you may incur divine anger, and I cannot condone it." Later, the family suffered misfortune. Indeed, using manipulation to achieve such ends is detested by nature, and it is even more so when applied to children.

The Wangmang River in Dongguang, also known as the Husu River, dries up during droughts and rises during floods, often causing difficulties for those who must cross it. My uncle, Ma Gong Zhou Lu, recounted that at the end of the Yongzheng era, a beggar woman was holding her child in one hand and supporting her sick grandmother with the other. As they reached the middle of the river, the grandmother suddenly fell and collapsed. The woman threw the child into the water and struggled to carry the grandmother out. The grandmother scolded loudly, saying, "I am an old woman of seventy, what harm is there in my death? The Zhang family has been waiting for this child to continue the family lineage, why did you throw the child into the water to save me? You are cutting off the family's ancestral line!" The woman cried and remained silent, kneeling for a long time. Two days later, the grandmother died of grief over the loss of her grandson. The woman wept uncontrollably and sat in a daze for several days before dying. Her identity is unknown, but it is known that she is a member of the Zhang family. A commentator noted, "In comparing the child and the grandmother, the grandmother is more important, but in comparing the grandmother and the ancestors, the ancestors are more important. If the woman had a husband or brothers, she would have saved the child."

A Widow in Dire Circumstances Having lost both her husband and parents, she was a widow living in poverty, with only a single orphaned son. This was the situation her mother-in-law expected of her. The woman died with regret. Yao An Gong said: Scholars never stop criticizing others. Swift currents that disappear quickly if not seized — is this a time for deep thought and long planning? The situation does not allow for both; abandoning the child to save the mother-in-law is in accordance with heavenly principle and the comfort of the heart. If the mother-in-law dies and the child survives, would she not feel guilty forever? Wouldn't there also be those who blame her for loving the child more than the mother-in-law? And the child is still young, whether he will survive is uncertain. If the mother-in-law dies and the child also fails to survive, how much more regret would there be? This woman's actions far beyond ordinary feelings. Unfortunately, the mother-in-law died, and the woman followed her in death, which is also pitiful. Still, they nitpick and criticize, claiming it as a profound scholarly pursuit, isn't this like bones holding grudges and carrying hatred to the grave? Sun Fu wrote *Spring and Autumn Annals: Exegesis on the Veneration of the King*, within 240 years, there is only criticism and no praise. Hu Zhitang wrote *Readings on History: Personal Insights*, no one is perfect from the Three Dynasties onward. Although they argue, it is not what I wish to hear.

De Guo Shizhou recounted that Zhu Mingjing Jingyuan had a friendship with a fox spirit. One day, while drinking at Jingyuan's home, he became so drunk that he fell asleep under a flower. When he awoke, Jingyuan asked him, "I have heard that nobles often change form after drinking, so I covered you with a quilt and guarded you myself. Yet you did not change at all, why is that?" He replied, "This depends on the depth of one's spiritual power. Those with shallow spiritual power can only transform their form, so they change when drunk, asleep, or in moments of panic; those with deep spiritual power can transcend their form, like the Taoist concept of *shi jie* (尸解), where they return to their true human form, thus there is no change. Jingyuan wished to learn the Way from him, but he said, "You cannot do it. It is easier for humans to cultivate the Way than for animals, as human energy is pure while animal energy is mixed. Achieving the Way is easier for animals than for humans, as animal minds are single-minded while human minds are complex. To refine the body, one must first refine one's energy; to refine one's energy, one must first refine the mind, which is the commander of one's spirit. When the mind is calm, energy gathers and the body becomes stable; when the mind is restless, energy disperses and the body weakens. The teachings of Guangchengzi to the Yellow Emperor are the secret essence of Taoism, not the fables of Zhuangzi. In the deep mountains and secluded valleys, unseen and unheard, one focuses the mind and guides the breath, harmonizing with the natural rhythms of heaven and earth, experiencing a hundred years as if it were a single day. Can humans truly do this?" Zhu then stopped.

Recalling a certain censor from the same year of study during the year Ding Mao, he once asked his favored performers: "There are many of you, but you alone excel. Why is that?" They replied: "We take our bodies as women and must also transform our hearts into those of women. Only then can we display gentle and charming demeanor, which melts the hearts of those who see us. If even a trace of a man's heart remains, then there will be a trace that is not like a woman, and how could we compete for the favor of beautiful women? As for performing on stage as a chaste woman, they must have a pure heart, and even in jest, they do not lose their chastity; as a promiscuous woman, they must have a licentious heart, and even when sitting solemnly, they cannot hide their licentiousness; as a noblewoman, they must have a dignified heart, and even in plain clothes, their noble aura remains; as a lowly woman, they must have a subdued heart, and even in elaborate dress, their lowly demeanor is evident; as a virtuous woman, they must have a gentle and compliant heart, and even when very angry, they do not show sudden anger; as a fierce woman, they must have a stubborn and rebellious heart, and even when logically defeated, they do not yield with submissive words."

Other joys, sorrows, anger, and pleasures, as well as grudges, love, and hatred, are all experienced by putting oneself in another's shoes, not treated as games but as real, and others view them as genuine. Others may perform feminine roles without truly feeling them, or perform various feminine behaviors without having the corresponding feelings, which is why I alone excel. Li Yudian said: This statement is vulgar and unworthy of mention, yet the principle is extremely refined. This matter may be small, but it can be used to illustrate something great. In the world, there is no case where the mind is not focused on a matter and yet that matter can reach its utmost; nor is there a case where the mind is entirely focused on a matter and yet that matter fails to reach its utmost. When the mind is entirely focused on one art, that art must be excellent; when the mind is entirely focused on one duty, that duty must be accomplished. Whether it is a small task like the ball of a minor official or the wheel of Bian Que, or a great endeavor like the governance of the four seas by Gao Yao, Kuai, Ji, and Xi, the principle is the same. This can be mutually illuminating with the theories of refining one's energy and mind.

Stone Isle recounted another tale: A scholar had a garden pavilion where he sat alone on a rainy night. Suddenly, a woman entered by drawing the curtain and said, "My home is outside the wall. I have been watching Song for a long time and now came to meet you in the rain." The scholar asked, "The rain is so heavy, why are your clothes and shoes not wet?" The woman was at a loss for words and admitted she was a fox. He then asked, "There are many young men here, why did you choose me?" She replied, "Fate." He questioned further, "Who recorded this fate, who oversees it, and who told you about it? Who were you and I in our previous lives? What event led to our connection, and in which dynasty and year?" The fox was unable to answer hastily and hesitated for a while before saying, "You have not sat here for a thousand days, but today you are here. I have seen thousands of people, but only you I find pleasing. It is clear that it is fate, please do not refuse." The scholar responded, "Those with fate will naturally be attracted to each other. I am just sitting here, and you just came. Since I feel no attraction, it is clear that there is no fate. Please do not stay." As the woman hesitated, she heard someone call from outside the window, "You are not understanding the situation, why must you insist on this stubborn person?" The woman waved her sleeve and extinguished the lamp before leaving. Some say this was the story of Young Master Tang Wen Zheng. I think a fox spirit would not dare to approach Master Tang; it is likely that there was such an event, but it was attributed to Master Tang.

Urumqi has many wild cattle, which resemble common cattle but are taller and larger, gathering in groups of hundreds. Their horns are sharp like spears. When they move, the strong ones lead and the weak ones follow. If attacked from the front, they will charge and attack fiercely, unable to be stopped by guns or cannons, even well-trained soldiers cannot surround them in formation. If attacked from the back, they will not look back, always having one largest one in the middle, like a king among bees, and they follow its movements. Once the leader fell into a deep ravine, the entire herd followed and perished together. There are also wild donkeys and wild horses that move in groups, but they are not as fierce and violent as wild cattle, running away when they see people, looking like real donkeys and real horses. However, when saddled and bridled, they cannot stand up. Sometimes there are marks on their backs where the saddles have left scars, where the saddle has rubbed, the fur turns white after healing, called 'saddle flowers.'

There are creatures with hooves embedded in iron, some say these are ridden by mountain gods, their origins are unfathomable. After a while, it is known that they are domesticated mules and horses that have fled into the mountains and become wild, joining the same herd. Mule meat is fatty and tender and can be eaten, but horse meat is not commonly consumed. There are also wild sheep, as mentioned in the *Hanshu* (Book of Han) in the section on the Western Regions, known as *xi yang*. The taste of wild sheep is no different from that of ordinary sheep. There are also wild pigs, as fierce as wild cattle, with fur and skin extremely tough, arrows cannot penetrate them, their teeth are sharper than sharp blades, and horse hooves touch them and break in half. In Jimsa Mountain, there is an old wild pig as large as a cow. People who approach it are often injured, and it often leads its clan of hundreds, emerging at night to destroy crops. Captain Erhertu led seven dogs into the mountains to hunt, and they suddenly encountered the old wild pig. The seven dogs were immediately eaten, and then it bared its teeth at people, and they whipped the horses to gallop and escaped. I planned to plant trees as a fence and set up large cannons inside, waiting to attack it. Some said if the shot missed, then its teeth would pull down the fence like pulling down rotten wood, and the people inside the fence would be in danger. I then abandoned the plan. There are also wild camels, with only one hump, and their meat is cut into pieces and extremely fatty and delicious. Du Fu's poem *The Beautiful Lady* mentions "purple camel's hump" as a delicacy, referring to this. Nowadays, people consider two-humped camels as one of the eight delicacies, losing their true meaning.

Yang Huaiting said, a man from Jimo went to Laoshan and stayed overnight in a mountain cottage. The house he lived in had a back door, and outside the door was a short wall surrounding a vegetable garden. By the time the sun was setting, he opened the door to cool off and saw a beautifully dressed woman on the wall, with beautiful eyebrows and eyes, only her face visible, as if she were smiling. Just as he was staring, he heard a group of children outside the wall shouting: "A big snake is coiled around a tree, and its head is on the wall." Then he realized it was a snake demon in disguise, trying to lure and suck his blood. He hurriedly closed the door and didn't know when it left. If he had approached, it would have been dangerous.

Qian Sheng, the lute player, once stayed at the house of Mr. Qiu, a high-ranking official. They had daily close interactions, and he forgot to ask about his name and hometown. Qian Sheng mentioned that in his hometown, there was a person who was extremely poor, and he gave all his earnings to his widowed sister-in-law, who eventually died a virtuous death. One day, while sitting by the candlelight, he saw a small face through a window gap, as small as a coin, with bright eyes looking inward. He quickly reached out and grabbed it, and it turned out to be a jade child about four inches long, skillfully made with earthy erosion marks. The pawnshop put it in a box, but it disappeared the next day, causing them to fear that the owner might come to redeem it. Qian Sheng heard about this and said, "This is a monster. I accidentally grabbed it, how could I force people to pay for it?" He detailed the beginning and end of the story and returned the pawn ticket. The pawnshop was moved by this and often called him to work, paying double the wage, and provided seasonal assistance, eventually leading to a modest prosperity.
