Mr. Qiu said, "This is heaven rewarding him for his brotherly love." Otherwise, why didn't it disappear in his home, only to disappear when it reached the pawnshop? To generously return the pawn ticket is especially difficult for human nature, but it is just a minor aspect of his character. There is no one who is shrewd and cunning yet loving to their brothers, nor is there anyone who is loving to their brothers yet shrewd and cunning.

A woman from Wang Qingcha, who often served as a ghost guide—this is the same as the ghost mentioned in the *Luanyang Xiasu Lu* who escorted a woman to remarry—was approached by a noblewoman's concubine who asked, "What is the cause and effect of our status as concubines?" The ghost replied, "According to the laws of the underworld, minor good and bad deeds can offset each other, but major good and bad deeds cannot be concealed. You all have accumulated minor good karma, hence you are born into wealthy and noble families, but you also have some bad karma, which is why you have a slight deficiency. If you increase your good deeds in this life, your bad karma will be repaid, and your good karma will continue, making your next life even more perfect. If you increase your bad deeds, your good karma will be lost, and your bad karma will continue, making your next life uncertain. However, increasing good deeds does not mean just burning incense and worshipping Buddha. True good deeds are filial piety towards your parents and respect for your legitimate spouse, and maintaining a harmonious family. One of the concubines then asked, 'Is having children or not having children predestined? Please check and tell me. If it is not recorded in the underworld's registry, I will not dream any more foolish dreams.' The ghost said, 'There is no need to check. Just act as if you will have children, and even if it is recorded that you will not, it will be changed to say you will. If you act as if you will not have children, even if it is recorded that you will, it will be changed to say you will not.' My maternal grandfather, Zhang Xuefeng, who was married into the Wang Qing Cao family, was strict and upright throughout his life and most disliked the six types of women, but he often spoke with her, saying, 'Although what this old woman says is not necessarily all true, she never encourages women to give alms or worship idols, which is commendable.'

Rou Ting, a scholar in the Hanlin Academy, once visited a friend in Handan. When he arrived, his host was not home, so he temporarily stayed at the City God Temple. Just then, a seller of melons rested his load and lay down in front of the deity's seat. An old man selling thread was staying inside the temple. The old man warned the seller of melons, "You should not do that; the god is divine." The seller of melons replied, "How can the god be in this dilapidated house?" The old man said, "I often go out at night to cool off and hear voices in the hall. Tiptoeing and listening stealthily, I heard a fox pleading with the god. The general meaning is that a fox from a neighboring house seduced a young man. Just before the young man died, the fox still wanted to take his essence. His family was very angry and set up hunters with guns and arrows to attack it. The fox was frightened, revealed its true form, and ran away. The crowd shouted and followed behind. The fox did not go to its own den but went to a den about a mile away. The people set up nets outside the den, smoked it with fire, and all in the den were killed. But this fox took advantage of the opportunity and escaped. Therefore, it accused them of shifting blame."

The city god said, "He killed someone and you suffered misfortune, which is appropriate to sue about. But do your descendants also have someone who flatters others?" After a long pause, the fox replied, "Yes, there is." "Have they killed anyone?" The city god asked again. After another long pause, the fox replied, "Perhaps there is." "How many people did they kill?" The city god asked. The fox did not respond, so the city god became angry and ordered the fox to be struck on the cheek. The fox then replied, "Actually, it was dozens of people." The city god said, "To kill dozens of lives and be punished by dozens of lives is just and fitting. This vengeful spirit has taken possession of this fox, using it as a tool, so why would you sue?" The city god then commanded the records to be checked and shown to the fox, and the fox wept and left. How can you say that the gods do not care? It is now clear that misfortune does not arise without reason, even if it seems to be an unjustified calamity, there must be a cause for it. However, those who discuss things based on their immediate context cannot always understand the underlying reasons.

Wang Zhushi Kanggu remarked that there was once a person who practiced divination by fumigation at West Lake. The spirit that descended wrote a poem on the altar: "I have returned from Tianmu Mountain, riding a crane to view Longjing. The sun has set halfway, its slanting rays illuminating a solitary flying shadow. A fleeting cloud passes over the peaks." The poem was not signed. One guest whispered, "When the sun is halfway down, it is actually a reflection, as described by Sima Xiangru as 'transcending the inverted view.' How can it be called a slanting ray?" Suddenly, the divination board shook violently, and after a long time, it seemed as if the spirit was angry. It wrote in large characters: "Child, you are disrespectful." It then ceased to move. I thought the guest's argument was quite reasonable. Why was this spirit so protective of its own words? Has it not heard of the ancient concept of a "one-word teacher"? Yushi Qiqi recounted that he once lived in a small room in the office of Yao, the military governor. Every night, when he was about to wake from sleep, he would vaguely see a shadow by his table. When he opened his eyes, there was nothing to be seen. He began to suspect that he was experiencing visual illusions. However, this should not happen every night. Later, he pretended to sleep to observe the situation and saw that it was a servant girl, who slowly emerged from the corner of the wall. He listened carefully for a long time before she dared to take a few steps. When he moved slightly, she would shrink back. He then realized that an earthbound spirit was lingering in the room, unable to leave, yet fearful of humans and unwilling to approach. It must be quite pitiful. He privately thought that since the spirit was not causing harm, there was no need to get too close and make it uncomfortable. It would be better to move out. As soon as he had this thought, he faintly saw the spirit bowing from a distance. It is clear that when the human heart moves, even ghosts and spirits know. How could the saying "ten eyes, ten hands" not be true? The next day, he moved out under the pretense of an excuse. Later, when he was in my service, he revealed the truth, saying, "I did not want to frighten the master." I said, "You have always been meticulous, but you have not fully resolved this matter of the spirit. In the future, there will surely be someone who will live here and owe the spirit a bow."

My cousin Zhaoxian told me that when my uncle Zhonghan was serving in Jingde, there was someone who dug and encountered an ancient tomb. The coffin and bones were all turned to ashes, but only the heart remained, still red with blood. Afraid, he threw it into the water. There was a stone about a foot square, still showing writing. My uncle Zhonghan heard about it and went to see it. The villagers feared it would bring trouble, so they smashed it and sank it, denying that it ever happened. It became a rumor in the neighborhood. After he was dismissed from office, he finally managed to obtain a copy. The text said: "A white jade has a flaw, and in the netherworld, one suffers shame. The soul breaks in the waters, the bones are buried at the foot of the mountain. I make this oath, to curse the darkness at the bottom of the tomb. A thousand years later, if someone uncovers this, are you unfaithful? Then dissolve into mud and silt. If you bear a grudge, your heart will never die." At the end, it is inscribed: "Renshen March, by Old Farmer Plowman for his fifth daughter." It is said that his daughter died unjustly, and this was written in her memory. The heart remains uncorrupted, knowing that the injustice is real. But the old man had no name, the daughter had no husband or family, the dates had no year, and we do not know who they were, so we cannot trace their story. Thus, the miracle remains unproclaimed, and it is truly a pity.

Xu Wenmu said, at the end of the Kangxi era, Li Lutin, who sold ancient artifacts, was a respected elder. He was skilled in the Six Calendar and only cast a divination for himself in the morning, never willing to cast one for others. He said, "Revealing too much about the future is something the gods dislike." Someone compared him to Shao Yong, and he replied, "My talent is only six or seven parts." Once, he cast a divination predicting that on a certain day, a divine figure would come with a bamboo staff, drink wine, compose a poem, and then leave. He waited with incense burning, and indeed, someone brought a carved bamboo statue of Lü Dongbin to sell, leaning against a wine flask, with the poem "Morning Journey to the Northern Sea" inscribed on it. How could Shao Yong make such a mistake? At over fifty years old, Li Lutin had no son and only kept a concubine. One day, Xu's father visited and heard the concubine weeping and muttering, "What is this matter that is used to play with me? Is he testing me?" Xu's father also heard Li Lutin strongly defend himself, saying, "This is a true statement, not a joke." Xu's father asked the reason for the quarrel, and Li Lutin said, "The matter is very strange. Today, I cast a divination predicting that two guests would come to buy ancient artifacts. One was her previous husband in a past life, still with a one-night relationship; the other was her future husband, and they would become good friends within half a year, living together in the same room. I told her, and she became furious immediately. Fate is unchangeable, and though I did not weep, she did; though I did not conceal it, she did. Isn't she a foolish woman?" After half a year, Li Lutin indeed died, and his concubine was sold to a family of a scholar-official. The wife could not tolerate her, and after one night, she was sent away. She was then sold again to a family of a mid-level official, and they lived peacefully.

On the day of his first marriage, Pang Xuaye dreamed of arriving at a place where he saw a woman dressed in green with a high coiled hairstyle. A person beside her pointed and said, "This is your wife." Upon waking, he felt repulsed by the dream. Later, when he remarried to a woman from the Yin family, she was exactly the same as the woman from his dream. Therefore, in the *Congbi Shanfang* collection, there is a mourning poem that reads: "Foolishly speak of karmic causes and effects, Who among those before us is truly destined by karma? On the day we first tuned our zithers together, Strange indeed that the konghou player entered my dream." This records the event. There are two known instances of the konghou player appearing in dreams. The first is recorded in *Xian Chuan Shi Yi*, where Xue Zhao captured the daughter of Lu Changyuan and showed her to Cui Yu; the second is recorded in *Yi Shi*, where Lu’s second uncle captured the daughter of the Liu family and showed her to Li Sheng. In both cases, an unmarried woman was used as a performer to entertain at banquets, which is especially cruel and mischievous. In recent times, I have heard of Daoists such as Lü who also possess this skill; the details are discussed in *Luanyang Xiaxia Lu*.

Yè Lǚtíng said that his ancestor still met Liu Shiqiu. One night, after drinking, a close friend pressured Liu Shiqiu to summon a fairy. Liu Shiqiu ordered a room to be swept, hung a bamboo curtain on the door, and lit two torches on the table. Everyone moved their seats to sit in the courtyard, while Liu Shiqiu took the Yu step and recited a spell, then took a boundary ruler and tapped the table once. Inside the curtain, indeed stood a graceful woman. The friend saw that it was his concubine and rose up to beat her. Liu Shiqiu quickly tapped the boundary ruler once, and saw the firelight wriggling like a lightning bolt, which had already passed through the curtain. Laughing and telling his friend, he said: "We have been friends for twenty years. How could there be anyone who truly uses your concubine as a joke? I just summoned a fox girl, created an illusion to provoke your anger for laughter." The friend hurried back to look, and the concubine was still embroidering. Such as this kind of play, perhaps it lies in the realm of neither too close nor too distant. I then thought of Li Shaojun inviting Lady Li, only allowing distant viewing, but not allowing close proximity, perhaps it was also summoning a spirit or demon, creating such an illusion.

Fee Changfang investigated and punished many ghosts, but later lost his talisman and was killed by the ghosts. Ming Chongyan died, stabbed through the chest, and the cause of his death could not be determined. People also say that dealing with ghosts is too difficult, and that ghosts can turn against them, leading to their downfall. This is often the case for those who rely on magic. Liu Xiangwan recounts that there was a monk skilled in spells who was lured by a fox to a desolate wilderness. The foxes formed a large group, howling and attacking the monk. The monk wielded a golden mallet and struck down an old fox in human form, then broke through the encirclement and escaped. Later, he encountered the old fox on the road. The fox fell to the ground in worship and said, "In the past, you spared my life, and I deeply repent. Now I wish to皈依 and take the Five Precepts." The monk wanted to touch the top of the fox's head, but the fox suddenly threw an object over the monk's face and disappeared. The object was neither silk nor leather, had the color of amber, was as sticky as lacquer, and was tightly adhered, causing dizziness and discomfort that were unbearable. The monk peeled it off with great effort, but the face skin was completely peeled, causing pain and dizziness that nearly fainted. After the scab fell off, there was no longer a human appearance.

Another wandering monk put up a sign on his door that read "Chase away foxes." A fox came to tempt the monk, and the monk recognized it as a demon. He shook a bell and recited Sanskrit mantras, causing the fox to flee in fear. A month later, an old woman knocked on his door and said that her home was near a graveyard, and she was being harassed by a fox every day. She begged the monk to go and exorcise the fox. The monk took out a small mirror and shone it on her, realizing that she was clearly a human. He followed her, and she led him to the riverbank. Suddenly, she grabbed his bag and threw it into the river, taking all his talismans, scriptures, and ritual objects with it. The old woman then fled into the fields of millet, and he could not trace her. Just as he was in despair, pieces of tile and rubble began flying at him, damaging his face. Fortunately, he relied on the Sanskrit mantras to protect himself, and the fox could not get close. He returned in a disheveled state. The next day, he fled in shame. After some time, he realized that the old woman was a local villager whose daughter had a close relationship with the fox. The daughter had bribed the old woman with gold to steal the monk's talismans. All these events showed that even though the monk's skills were sufficient to overcome the fox, he was ultimately outwitted by it. The fox had a plan, while the monk was unprepared; the fox had allies, while the monk had no help. How much more so if one's skills are not enough to defeat such a supernatural being and one rashly confronts it?

A relative named Wuzhan An, speaking on behalf of his family, recounted the story of a carpenter from Liufu Village. The carpenter consulted a diviner about his marriage prospects. The diviner teased him, saying, "Go to a certain place to the southwest, about a hundred li away, where a man named a certain person is about to die. His wife will soon marry you. Hurry there to find him, and you will succeed." The carpenter believed the diviner and traveled to the location, where he stayed at an inn in a village. There, he met a man who asked where the certain person lived. The man inquired, "Why do you want to find him?" The carpenter told him the truth, not realizing that the man he was speaking to was the certain person himself. Upon hearing this, the man became enraged and drew his dagger to attack the carpenter. The carpenter fled into the inn's backyard and escaped over the wall. The man suspected that the innkeeper was hiding the carpenter inside and tried to search the room, but the innkeeper refused. A fight ensued, and in the struggle, the innkeeper was killed. The man was subsequently tried and executed for the crime. As for the carpenter's name, surname, and place of residence, none of these details were ever discovered. After a year or so, an elderly woman accompanied by a man and a woman passed through Xian County. They claimed to be the woman's uncle and her widowed sister-in-law. The elderly woman suddenly died, and her nephew, unable to afford the funeral, decided to arrange a marriage for his sister-in-law. With no other options, she reluctantly agreed.

The craftsman had not yet married, and the crowd arranged a marriage for him. Later, when he inquired about his former husband, it turned out to be Mr. A. How strange! The diviner did not jest, and the craftsman did not go. Because the craftsman did not go, there was no way to fight with Mr. A. If there was no way to fight with Mr. A, then the master would not die. If the master did not die, then Mr. A would not be held accountable. If Mr. A was not held accountable, then this woman could not marry this craftsman. Without any cause, waves were created, and eventually, they were entangled with each other, becoming spouses. Is it not the will of fate? It is also said that there is a diviner at the West Fourth Gate of Beijing, who sets up his stall daily in the street. During the month of June in the year 1730, he suddenly predicted his own death on the eighteenth day, only one or two days away. He thought he had no way to die, but the divination was very clear. So on that day, he locked himself inside and did not go out, to see how he would die. Unaware that there was suddenly an earthquake, the house collapsed and he was buried under it. If he had not predicted it, that day he would certainly set up his stall in the main street, how could he have been buried? This is also fate, which cannot be avoided, but it turned out that his foresight was misleading.

Painter Zhang Wunian resided in the Cherry Alley of the Capital. His study was unique; it used a large sheet of wide paper as a window, with not a single dot on it, to ensure it was bright. On the night of the full moon, there was always a woman's shadow in the center of the paper. When he opened the door to look, he saw nothing, but the shadow remained as before. Since it did not bring any harm or misfortune, he chose to let it be. One night, he observed closely and felt that the figure was alive, as if it had entered a painting. Playfully, he drew around it with a brush, and from then on, the shadow no longer appeared. However, sometimes a woman would appear on the wall to look down. Suddenly realizing that this ghost wanted a portrait, Zhang understood that it had first shown him its form and now its appearance. When he spoke to it, it did not respond, and when he looked at it, it did not blush or avoid his gaze. After a long while, it disappeared. Zhang then completed the drawing of the eyebrows, eyes, and clothing patterns, creating a picture of a lady. That night, he heard a voice from outside the window say, "My name is Tingting." When he asked again, it was silent. He then inscribed on the paper. Later, the artwork was bought by a magistrate. Some say it was Li Zhongshan, others say it was a fox, not a ghost, which is closer to the logic of the matter. Some say there was no such thing, and Zhang Wunian just fabricated the story. This is also uncertain. However, fragrant spirits and talented ghosts always want to leave a name for posterity. From now to ancient times, the habit is the same, it is also reasonable.

During his tenure as a Langzhong (middle-level official) in the Jiangsu Department of the Ministry of Justice, Yao An Gong handled a case transferred from the Western City involving a young man who had forcibly defiled a young girl. The boy was sixteen years old, and the girl was fourteen. It is said that the boy, after visiting the Western Peak, saw the girl picking vegetables in a garden and proceeded to coerce and threaten her. Patrol guards heard the girl's cries for help and apprehended the boy. The interrogation was not yet complete when both sets of parents submitted statements, claiming that the girl was the boy's未婚妻 (unmarried wife) and that the incident was a misunderstanding. According to the law, there was a provision for consensual adultery between an unmarried wife and her partner, but no provision for rape. While deliberating, the girl's testimony also changed, stating that it was merely a joke. Consequently, the boy was given a light reprimand and dismissed. Some speculated that the girl's parents had received a large bribe, and the girl herself was attracted to the boy's graceful appearance and his wealthy family, leading her to fabricate this false statement to resolve the dispute. Yao An Gong remarked, "This is uncertain, but the matter involves only marriage, unlike cases where bribes are used to cover up murder, leading to innocent people being wrongfully punished. The act of rape was not completed, and there is no evidence to verify it. There is no proof of the bribe, making it difficult to substantiate. The girl has admitted to it, her parents have supported her, and the matchmaker and witnesses have provided clear evidence. The neighbors have no objections, and there is no contradiction in the testimonies of both parties. A gentleman can be deceived by appropriate means, but one cannot arbitrarily apply severe punishment, such as sending a young boy to a distant military outpost."

During the summer, a certain official retired from court and took a maid to a quiet room for a daytime nap. Just as the gatekeeper reported something, he asked where the master was. A young servant intentionally played with the gatekeeper and casually replied, "The master is just embracing your wife in sleep somewhere." The woman arrived and, angry, scolded him. The master came out and, after questioning, beat and chased away this young servant. After three or four years, the gatekeeper's wife died. Just as this maid lost favor due to a slight, the master forgot his previous words and ultimately married her to the gatekeeper. Afterward, recalling it, he sighed deeply and said, "Is it by chance?" According to Li Huating of Wenshui, a hundred miles from his home, there was an abandoned temple said to have a demon, and no one dared to live there. There were over a dozen sheep traders who took shelter there from the rain. At night, they heard a moaning sound and, in the dark, saw an object that was fat and round, indistinct, limping and coming, moving very slowly and heavily. All were good-for-nothing youths, not at all frightened, and together they threw broken bricks to hit it. The sound was loud and metallic, and it gradually retreated as if to retreat, realizing it was powerless. They shouted and chased it to the side of the temple gate's broken wall, where it stood firmly without moving. Upon closely examining it, it was a broken bell, inside of which were many broken bones, thought to be what it had eaten. The next day, they told the locals to melt it and cast it into an object, and from then on, the strange occurrences ceased. This object was extremely dull, but it also annoyed people, ultimately destroying itself. Perhaps it saw a good illusion of the strange, with something causing harm and imitating it. A maid from my family, from Shan Guozhuang in Cangzhou, said that this village was a haven for thieves. Someone saw that thieves were profitable and followed them. The pursuers were urgent, and other thieves fought and escaped, but this person was captured and punished. Perhaps it was also like this bell.

A Relative of Uncle An, named An Gong, recounted the following: There was a man named Liu who had an extremely close relationship with a fox spirit. Liu was poor, and the fox often provided for his clothing and food. Liu also owed a large sum of money to a wealthy family and was in danger of having his daughter pawned to settle the debt. The fox stole the pawn ticket, and the matter was resolved. The fox would sometimes visit Liu's home, and Liu's wife and children would converse with it, but only Liu could see its true form. The fox had charmed a wealthy family's daughter, and no talismans or spells could exorcise it. The wealthy family offered a hundred gold pieces to anyone who could exorcise the fox. Liu's wife knew about the fox's relationship with Liu and was greedy for the large sum of money. She encouraged Liu to wait for an opportunity to kill the fox. Liu felt guilty for betraying the fox, but his wife scolded him, saying, "If it can charm someone else's daughter, why can't it charm your daughter?" She reminded him that the fox had given five gold pieces to make a winter coat for Liu's daughter, and there must be some intention behind it. Liu could not ignore this problem and secretly bought arsenic, bought wine to wait, and planned to lure the fox. The fox, however, already knew about Liu's plan and gathered with Liu and several neighbors. From the eaves, the fox called out Liu's name, first recounting the depth of their friendship, then mentioning the long period of support, and finally revealing each of Liu's hidden plans: "I am not incapable of causing you harm, but we have been acquainted for a long time, and I cannot easily become your enemy." The fox then threw down a piece of cloth and a bundle of cotton from the eaves, saying, "Yesterday, your child cried from cold and hunger, and I promised to make a blanket for them. I cannot break my promise to a child." The others felt unjustly treated and scolded Liu. The fox said, "Choosing friends without discrimination is also my fault. The world is like this, and there is no need for deep resentment. I will just make you aware of this." The fox sighed and left. After this, Liu was no longer respected by his fellow villagers, and no one was willing to help him even with a small amount. He left his family at night, and no one knows his final destination.

My uncle, Zhang Gong, recounted that when the Tong family's garden in Cangzhou was still in use, it was surrounded by water on three sides, with dense and shady trees. It was a place where people often gathered for banquets. The garden keeper frequently heard ghosts singing at night, their voices echoing through the garden. The song went: "The leaves are green and lush, the flowers are layered and deep. I cannot see clearly, but there is a shadow of a beautiful woman in the middle. I only see her embroidered gold robe and her skirt made of water-red silk." This continued for several years. Later, a courtesan was insulted by her guests, and in anger, she hanged herself on a tree. The color of her clothes matched exactly what was described in the song. No one could understand the reason behind this. Some said that this hanged ghost was waiting for a successor, knowing in advance who would come to replace her, and therefore sang happily. In Qing County, there was a farming family. The husband was sick and unable to work, and they were on the verge of starvation. He considered selling his wife to save both of them. His wife said, "If I go, how will you survive? Even if we sell the money, we will still starve to death. It would be better for me to stay and take care of you. This way, we can manage our food, medicine, and health, and perhaps hope for a new life. I would rather become a courtesan." She stayed with him for more than ten years. Later, when she was dying of illness, she died and then came back to life. She said, "I suddenly found myself in the underworld. The official said that courtesans should be reborn as sparrows or pigeons. But because I never forgot my husband, I can still be reborn as a human."

The Concubine Guo The concubine Guo was the daughter of a man from Datong who had settled in Tianjin. At the time of her birth, her mother dreamt of a vendor selling colorful talismans for the Dragon Boat Festival, and she bought one branch, which was then used to name her. At the age of thirteen, she came to me, giving birth to several children, none of whom survived, but she had one daughter who married Lu Yinwen of Dezhou, the son of the local official Huigui. Huigui was skilled in astrology and once predicted that she would not live past forty, and indeed she died at thirty-seven. When I was in the Western Regions, the concubine was already suffering from tuberculosis. I prayed to Guan Yu by drawing lots, asking if we would meet again. The sign I drew said: "Magpies on the eaves bring good news, knowing you have a heart to return from afar, the embroidered curtain will be tied again with the ribbons of the mandarin ducks, but the falling leaves and frost will soon bring the cold." It was said that I would return in the autumn or winter, and I was very pleased. At the time, my student Qiu Er Tian, who was staying with me, heard this and said, "If you see her, you will see her, but the last line is not a good omen." After my return in June of the year Xinmao, the concubine's illness had improved, but by September, it suddenly worsened, and she gradually grew weaker until she passed away. After her death, I went through her belongings and wrote two poems in remembrance: "The wind and flowers still dot the old silk robes, filled with sorrow as the peony petals fall, just as the poet Bai Juyi once said, spring follows the beautiful maiden, just as she leaves." The concubine died on the thirtieth day of the third month, just as spring was ending. "The pleated skirt of the Xiang river flutters against the painted railing, in the wind, I still remember her graceful steps. Knowing that the divine prophecy had already been made, I still lament the fragility of the lotus, which cannot withstand the cold." "It may not last long like this, the lotus cannot withstand the cold," a poem by Hanshan, using the meaning of the sign.

It is said that the practice of fortune-telling began with Li Xuzhong. His method uses the year, month, and day but not the hour, as it is based on the epitaph written by Changli (Han Yu) for Li Xuzhong. His book is recorded in the *Yiwenzhi* (Catalogue of Arts and Literature) of the *Songshi* (History of Song), but it has been lost for a long time. Only the *Yongle Dadian* (Great Canon of the Yongle Era) contains three volumes of Li Xuzhong's *Ming Shu* (Book of Destiny), which is still a complete work. What it says actually includes discussions of the eight characters (Ba Zi), not that the hour is not used. Some suspect it was fabricated by Song dynasty people, but it cannot be proven otherwise. However, examining the epitaph of Li Xuzhong, it states that he was most proficient in the Five Elements. He recorded the year, month, and day of a person's birth, the day and hour they were born, the relationships between the stems and branches, and the cycles of growth and decline, birth and death, all of which he carefully considered. He deduced a person's lifespan, wealth, status, and fortune or misfortune, etc. There are twelve hours in a day, therefore a day is divided into twelve hours. When the sun reaches a certain hour, it is that hour. Therefore, the hour is also called a day and hour. The *Guoyu* (Discourses of the States) states that the positions of stars and day-hours are all in the northern sky. The *Shijing* (Book of Songs) mentions the weaving maiden and her daily tasks. Kong Yingda's commentary explains that the weaving maiden moves through seven hours from morning to evening, hence the term 'seven shifts'. The *Chuci* (Songs of Chu) mentions auspicious days and hours, with Wang Yi noting that 'day' refers to the days of the week (甲乙辰), and 'hour' refers to the hours of the day (寅卯以辰), making the distinction clear. Based on this, it seems that the four characters '所直日辰' should be connected to the preceding '年月日' as a single phrase. Later people incorrectly connected it to the following text, thus leading to the idea that the hour was not used.

In compiling the "General Catalog of the Four Treasuries," I also noted that the method of divination by the four pillars of destiny without considering the time element still follows an old tradition. I now record this here to acknowledge my oversight. Regarding the Five Elements astrology, it is traditionally said to originate from Zhang Guo, but his theory is not found in canonical texts. According to the *Liezi*, one's fate is determined by the stars; if it encounters good fortune, it is good; if it encounters bad fortune, it is bad. Once fate is determined, neither spirits nor wise men can change it. Wang Chong in *Lunheng* states that heaven imparts its qi (vital energy), and the stars distribute their essence. Those who contain qi grow, and those who receive nobility become noble, and those who receive lowliness become lowly. Nobility may have varying ranks, and wealth may have varying amounts, all of which are bestowed by the positions of the stars. Therefore, speaking of fate through the stars has existed since ancient times and does not necessarily start with Zhang Guo. Han Yu in his *Three Stars* says: "The day I was born, the moon was in the constellation of the Southern Dipper. The Ox raises its horns, and the Dipper opens its mouth." Du Mu in his self-written epitaph states: "I was born under the constellations of the Horns and the Stars. In the Horns, it is the eighth house, called the House of Illness and Calamity, also known as the Eighth House of Destruction. Jupiter is there, followed by Mars and Saturn." Yang Xuan says: "Jupiter is in the constellation of the Bow, which in the Horns is the eleventh house of fortune and virtue. Jupiter is a great benefactor, and the gentleman has no worries." I say: "The governor of Hu did not serve for a full year before being promoted to the position of Shiren. Jupiter's blessings in the Horns are sufficient. Mars and Saturn returned to death in the Horns, as they should." The Five Elements astrology originally arose in the Tang Dynasty, and the method is also not different from today. Astrologers attribute their methods to Zhang Guo, and it is not without reason. But the books they attribute to him are all crude and vulgar, and are lower in quality than Li Xu Zhong's book on destiny. They are definitely not texts from the Tang Dynasty.

Huo Yangzhong reported that there was a painting of a fairy riding a deer hanging on the wall of an old house, inscribed with the name Zhao Zhongmu. It is uncertain whether this inscription is accurate. Zhao Zhongmu, whose name was Zhongmu, was the son of Zhao Mengfu. Whenever the room was empty, the figures in the painting would move along the wall, as if in a shadow puppet show. One day, someone pre-tied a long rope to the back of the painting and hid to observe. When the figures moved far enough, they quickly pulled the painting out. The figures then appeared on the wall with vivid colors, gradually fading and eventually disappearing after half a day. It was suspected that they had completely vanished. I once believed that paintings lack form and spirit, and that the idea of them becoming animated is unlikely. The ancient texts referred to "painting demons," suggesting that they might be caused by some entity. Later, I read "Lindeng's Records of Curiosities," which described an incident where Yuan Zhao of the Northern Wei dynasty captured a "painting demon" from the Yunmen Huanghua Temple. Yuan Zhao questioned the demon, saying, "You are originally void, created by a painting, how can you have such a demonic form?" The demon replied, "The form is originally the painting, which represents reality, and thus has a spirit. Moreover, the painting has a spirit that can communicate. This is why I was moved and transformed; I am truly guilty." Its words seemed reasonable.

The cavalry officer Sa Yin Choktu was friends with a fox. One day, the fox came in a hurry and said, "There is a ghost haunting my home. I intend to borrow your family tomb for my relatives to reside in." The cavalry officer asked, "Why don't you care for each other? I've heard of foxes haunting humans, but not of something haunting a fox. What kind of demon is this?" The fox replied, "It is a heavenly fox, with magical powers that are beyond comprehension. It appears and disappears like ghosts and lightning, making it unpredictable and elusive. When it haunts humans, they cannot guard against it, and when it haunts foxes, even foxes cannot see it." The cavalry officer asked, "Why don't they care for each other?" The fox said, "Humans and humans are of the same kind, yet the strong oppress the weak, and the wise deceive the foolish. How can they care for each other? A demon meeting another demon is quite strange. The trend of the world is that one thing is constantly surpassed by another, and the ingenuity of the world is that new things keep emerging without end. How can one aspect alone explain all of this?" In the thirteenth volume of *Yue Wei Cao Tang Bi Ji* (Notes from the Reading of the Grass Hut), titled *Hui Xi Zhi Za San* (Miscellaneous Notes from the Hui Xi Collection), it is recorded that in the year of Dingmao, Guo Tonglin, a contemporary of the same year, went to the capital for the imperial examination in the year of Wuchen. He stayed at a lodging house in a new post station. Sitting alone under the lamp, reciting poetry, he heard someone say from outside the window, "You are a scholar, there is a poem on the west wall, I would like to ask for your opinion." When he went out to look, he saw nothing. He then brushed away the dust and looked closely at the west wall, where he found an eight-line poem written by a traveler who was ill. The poem was very sad and vulgar, not very well-formed. Could it be that the person who liked to write on walls, even after death, the habit is not forgotten? Or does he want Guo Tonglin to pass on his name so that people know that a certain person died in this place, hoping that his family can bring back his bones?

Slave Ceng Yu had been married three times. His first wife was separated from him immediately after the wedding ceremony and eventually divorced. His second wife was forced to have twins, which was undesirable due to the burden of raising them and the lack of sufficient milk, leading Ceng Yu to seek a method to prevent childbirth. He mistakenly believed a woman named Wang Ao's advice, ground a stone into powder, and consumed it, leading to the stone accumulating in his intestines and causing his death. When Ceng Yu fell seriously ill, he muttered as if arguing with someone, regained consciousness briefly, and whispered to his third wife: "I took my first wife out, and my parents had already arranged for her to be married to someone else. The wife was unaware of this, and I had led her to a secluded place where we engaged in intimate activities. She thought my intentions had changed and happily complied, even sleeping together until dawn. When the wedding procession arrived, the wife left in anger. However, the matchmaker had already informed the second husband that we had not slept together, and my mother and brothers also confirmed this. When the second husband discovered that the wife was not a virgin, he doubted and criticized her, leading to her death from sorrow." The second wife initially refused to take the stone powder, but Ceng Yu severely beat her until she complied. After her death, Ceng Yu feared she would become a malevolent spirit and bribed a witch to perform a ritual to ward off evil. Now, Ceng Yu sees the second wife in a dream-like state and believes he will not recover. Indeed, he died shortly after. Another slave named Wang Cheng is described as eccentric. He would suddenly command his wife to kneel and be whipped, then resume playing with her. Sometimes, while whipping her, he would suddenly stop and play with her again, saying he could continue the whipping.

Still, he scolded her and made her kneel to receive the whip. Generally, within one day and night, his moods would alternate between joy and anger several times, and his wife feared him as much as a tiger. When he was happy, she dared not but force herself to be cheerful; when he was angry, she dared not but accept it willingly. One day, she cried and complained to her deceased mother-in-law, who called Wang Cheng to ask the reason. Wang Cheng knelt and reported, saying, "I do not know myself, nor am I free. I suddenly feel her lovable, and then suddenly feel her hateful." The deceased mother-in-law said, "This is beyond human reason, perhaps it is the old grudge that Buddhism speaks of?" Worried that his wife might take her own life, she decided to send her away. Later, it was heard that Wang Cheng died of illness, and his wife eventually wore a red robe. The husband is the head of the household, a natural law, but respect ultimately does not match that of a lord, and kinship ultimately does not match that of a father. Therefore, the wife is also trained together, having equal status. Then, their interaction should be fair in both emotion and reason. Song Yu's second wife died by mistake, and the crime was only that she was too fierce. His first wife, having already been divorced and remarried, had broken the bond of gratitude and righteousness, and should no longer be considered as husband and wife. She merely seduced someone else's unmarried wife. Thereby causing her death is an appropriate retribution. Wang Cheng was cruel and violent, but he did not cause his wife's death. Living in the same room means becoming part of heaven. After his death, she did not wear mourning clothes but instead wore festive clothes, which is against reason and norms, and her suffering abuse is not worthy of pity.

Wu Hui shu said that in Tai Lake there was a fisherman’s family who had a daughter to be married. When their boat reached the center of the lake, the wind and waves suddenly rose, and the helmsman lost his composure. The boat was already tilting dangerously and about to capsize; the others all clung to one another in tears. Suddenly, the bride burst through the curtain and came out, one hand on the rudder and the other pulling the sail rope. Breaking through the waves, she steered the boat forward, directly reaching the groom’s house before the auspicious hour had even passed. The people of Dongting Lake passed this story on as a marvel, though some criticized it as going beyond the rites. Wu Hui shu said, "This was a girl from a fishing family, who every day held the pole and oar at the bow of the boat. One cannot expect her to necessarily be a Song Boji." He also heard that in my county there was a daughter of the Jiao family, whose county of origin is unknown. She had already been betrothed, but someone plotted to make her a concubine, spreading slanderous rumors. The groom’s family wished to dissolve the marriage, and her father brought a lawsuit before the authorities. The conspirators had already set a deep trap—there was not only solid evidence and witnesses, but even someone who had confessed to being her lover. Seeing the situation was urgent, the girl finally asked a neighborly old woman to guide her to the groom’s house. She ascended the hall and bowed to her mother-in-law, saying: "I am not a wife in the conventional sense, and whether I am chaste or not has clear proof. Rather than expose my shame before the official matchmaker and still be falsely accused, I would rather do so before you." She then closed the door, removed her clothes, and asked her mother-in-law to examine her. The lawsuit was immediately resolved. This case was even more beyond the rites than that of the bride who steered the boat. Yet in times of crisis and survival, there are things one must do despite their unorthodox nature. Scholars who lecture on principles often demand death as a punishment for such actions, but this is not a balanced view.

Yang Yuting said, in the depths of Mount Laoshan, there was a person sitting alone among wood and stone, his body having become the same color as the wood and stone, yet his breathing continued, and his eyes were bright and he could still see. This was a child who had been refined but was trapped and could not emerge. Neither dead nor alive, what value is there in cultivating the Dao, when it is actually better to be as free as a ghost? Generally, true immortals have the bones of immortals, their nature inherently pure and empty; they have the karma or fate of immortals, receiving the secret teachings. Without the true transmission, rashly attempting to ascend, many have suffered harm because of this. This person is a clear example. Someone suggested: "Cut open the top of his head with a knife, and he should be released through military means." This is also words based on guesswork; how easy is it to talk about?

In ancient times, high-ranking officials would worship the five deities; nowadays, households only worship the Kitchen God, or the Door God, or the Well God, or the Toilet God, or the Central Madness God, some worshipping and others not. However, it is unclear whether there is one Kitchen God for the entire world, one per city or village, or one per family? If there were one Kitchen God for the whole world, like the Fire God, it would surely be recorded in the ritual texts, but there is no such record today; if there were one per city or village, like the City God or Village God, there would surely be specific rituals for each place, but such rituals are not seen everywhere. Therefore, it seems that each family has its own Kitchen God. Yet, it is also unclear how many Kitchen Gods there are given the vast number of households, as numerous as the sands of the Ganges. Who creates and assigns these countless Kitchen Gods? Are there too many gods? Households often move and their fortunes change frequently. What happens to the idle or newly created Kitchen Gods? The constant creation and reassignment of gods must be quite burdensome. These questions are truly beyond understanding, yet encounters with the Kitchen God still occur from time to time. When I was young, I saw an incident at my maternal grandfather Zhang Gong of Xuefeng's house. A kitchen maid had the habit of sweeping dirty objects into the stove. One night, she dreamed of a black-clothed figure rebuking her and slapping her cheek. Upon waking, her cheek swelled into a large abscess, which grew to the size of a cup within a few days. The pus drained internally, coming out through her mouth, and with every breath, it entered her throat, causing her to vomit and nearly die. She made a vow and prayed sincerely, and only then did she recover. What does this mean? Someone said: "When a household establishes a ritual, there must be a spirit associated with it. If the ritual continues, the spirit remains; if the ritual ceases, the spirit disappears. These spirits are not individually appointed by the Emperor." This explanation might be correct.

Mr. Sun Yefei stayed overnight at a mountain house and heard a sound described as "了鸟" — which, according to Li Shangyin, refers to the clinking of iron on a door. He asked who it was, and a faint voice from outside replied, "I am neither a ghost nor a demon; the neighbor girl has something to say." Mr. Sun asked, "Who called you a ghost or demon, and why did you first deny being one? Isn't this trying to cover up the truth even more?" After that, there was silence. Cui Chonghui, a native of Fenyang, made a living by selling silk and had been traveling to the remote areas of Shanggu Cloud for many years. One year, he lost over ten taels of silver, and his fellow merchants complained. Overwhelmed with anger, Cui Chonghui cut open his own abdomen with a knife, and his intestines protruded several inches. His breath was nearly gone, but the host, seeing him still alive, quickly called the village head and his wife. They asked if he had any grievances, and he replied, "I am poor at trading and have lost the host's capital; I am truly ashamed, so I do not wish to live, and this has nothing to do with others." He asked them to quickly move him back, so that a capital case would not trouble others. The host was moved by his words and gave him several dozen taels of silver for funeral expenses. Cui Chonghui was dying slowly. A doctor stitched his intestines back into his abdomen, applied medicine, and formed a scab, and he gradually recovered, but feces came out through the knife wound, and his anus was closed.

After he had fallen into extreme poverty, he was forced to sell his wife. Those who had previously sold silk with him felt pity for him and each gave him silk, which he used to twist into thread to support himself. Gradually, he became moderately prosperous again and remarried, having children. By the years of Qianlong's reign, from the year Ji Si (1793) to Ji Wu (1794), he died at the age of seventy. A fellow villager named Liu Bing wrote a biography for him, and an official named Cao Shouzhi recorded it and showed it to me. I have summarized the main points. Losing money through trading is a common occurrence, but losing more than ten taels of silver and self-inflicting harm can be said to undervalue one's life. However, his original intention was that he originally had no private interest, but his actions resembled making a profit through improper means. He could not clarify his heart and chose to prove himself through death, showing his self-respect throughout his life. On the brink of death, in front of the crowd, he explained clearly to the village headman, making the government officials have no doubt. He strongly advised his wife, ensuring that his relatives had no reason to sue, demonstrating loyalty and kindness. His intention was not particularly unusual, but rather a reflection of the will of heaven. The matter seems strange but is not actually strange.

Master Zifǔ of Wén'ān Wang recounted that in Bǎzhōu, a family of officials married a woman. Just as the bride was about to remove her fan, the groom lost his voice and ran out in a panic. When asked why, he said, "The bride had a green face and red hair, resembling a strange ghost, and I was so terrified that I ran away." The bride was of average beauty, and no one could understand why she appeared that way. They forced the groom to return to the bridal chamber, but he saw the same thing again. Despite the parents' insistence, he eventually found an opportunity to hang himself. Since the wedding rites were not completed, the bride was supposed to return to her family. At the time, the guests were still present, and the father led his daughter to bow to all the guests, saying, "My daughter is indeed not beautiful, but how could she have caused such a shock and led to her own death?" The *Yōugài Lù* (Record of Strange Events) records a similar story about Lu Sheng marrying the daughter of a magistrate from Hóngnóng, but in that case, the groom did not die. This incident must be due to a past grudge or karma, and it cannot be explained by ordinary logic. From a scholarly perspective, one might say that the groom had a mental illness, causing him to see things that were not there.

Li Zaiying, the grandson of Han San, the Governor-General, was an official under me at the Ministry of Rites. His demeanor was clear and bright, and I expected him to achieve great things. However, shortly after his marriage, he died prematurely. It is said that at the time of his wedding, when the new bride worshipped the gods, the mirror in her hand suddenly fell to the ground and broke into two pieces. She was already startled by the bad omen, and soon afterward, ghostly sounds chirped all night long, suggesting that his impending death was a premonition. A certain official rented a house at Huafang Bridge. Someone said there was a fox in the house, but it was not a problem; if one moved in and offered a sacrifice, it would be peaceful. The person was frugal and did not comply, and there was no other abnormality. Later, he took a concubine. On the day she first arrived, she sat alone in the room, and outside the window, through the gap in the curtain, dozens of people whispered and commented on her beauty and ugliness. She felt embarrassed and dared not look up. Then she extinguished the candle and went to bed, and the entire room filled with continuous laughter. Every action she made, they would loudly announce what she was doing, and this continued for several nights. He complained to the True Person of Zhengyi, and the judge Wang said: "If a spirit harms a person, it can be punished. If it only plays pranks, it does not harm anyone. It is like mutual jesting, which does not lead to any serious trouble, and is not forbidden by the law. How can one trivial matter of indecency be brought before the gods?" The person had no choice but to offer wine and food and pray, and that night, everything was quiet. He sighed and said, "Now I understand that the etiquette of socializing cannot be neglected."

Wang Fujiu said: In a household in Fenghuang Village, a child held his mother's shoes while playing and left them under the flower stand in the garden. His father picked them up, and the wife was greatly scolded and questioned, unable to prove her innocence, and intended to hang herself. Suddenly, a great disturbance caused by fox spirits occurred in the household, where the wife's personal belongings were stolen and thrown away in other places. This went on for more than a month before it stopped. The suspicion about the lost shoe was not debated and was resolved. It seemed as if the fox spirits were secretly untangling the knot for this woman. No one could understand the reason. Someone said: Her mother-in-law was strict, and there was a maid who had a secret pregnancy, so the wife secretly stole the key to the garden and let her escape. There was such a hidden merit, so the deity sent the fox spirits to save her. Another person said: If it was divine protection, why didn't the deity send the fox spirits to retrieve the shoe first, wouldn't there have been no trace? Wang Fujiu said: The deity was using the trace to demonstrate cause and effect. I also agree with Wang Fujiu's words.

Hu Taixu, a military governor, had the ability to see ghosts. He once used this ability to inspect his servants' homes while repairing a house. In all the rooms, he saw ghosts entering and leaving, except for one room that was completely quiet. When he asked about it, someone said, "That is where I live." However, this servant was clumsy and had no merit, and his wife was also just an ordinary woman. After the servant died, his wife remained chaste for the rest of her life. It is said that a "烈妇" (heroic woman) might act out of a moment of passion, but a "节妇" (chaste woman) must have a firm resolve to endure hardship for many years. The righteous energy in her heart has accumulated for a long time, which is why the ghosts dared not approach her. Another person who could see ghosts said, "In every household, there are always ghosts coming and going. Whenever there is intimate or promiscuous behavior in the bedroom, the ghosts gather to watch, pointing and laughing, but people do not see or hear them." Ghosts sometimes avoid certain people, and those people are either "烈妇," "节妇," "孝妇," or "贤妇." This aligns with what Hu Taixu said, showing a consistent pattern of righteousness and virtue.

Zhu Dingyuan recounted that one evening, a scholar sat to enjoy the cool breeze. Suddenly, he heard a noise from the roof and was startled, looking up to see two women fighting from the eaves. They fell down and shouted loudly, asking, "Sir, you are a scholar. Is it proper for two sisters to share one husband?" The scholar held his breath and dared not speak. The women pressed him further, and he trembled and stammered, saying, "I am a human being, only know human customs. Ghosts have ghost customs, foxes have fox customs. It is not something I know." The two women spat and said, "This person is indecisive and can't handle things. We should ask someone who can handle things." They continued to fight and left. Su Weidao was known for his ambiguous approach, truly a good strategy for self-preservation. However, those who shirk responsibility and cause trouble often get punished. Worldly experience is too deep, self-interest is too cunning. Often, they avoid things that don't need to be avoided, thus, they also fail to do things that should be done. They often miss opportunities, leaving behind a source of disaster, leading to irreparable damage. This scholar was ridiculed by the foxes, a small matter, indeed.

According to Zhu Qinglei, there was a young man from a family in Jinan who fell in love with his neighbor's daughter. They often looked at each other, and after some time, he began to show signs of stealing fragrant things, which made the girl's father suspicious. At night, he hid on the wall, looking around left and right, and secretly watched their comings and goings. Then he saw a young man in the girl's room, and in the young man's room, there was a girl. Their clothing and appearance were all the same, and he then realized that both the man and the woman were bewitched by fox spirits—this was truly the trick of the fox. Zhu Qinglei said: "According to what I saw, those who like to meddle should arrange a match, and it would also be a good story." However, he heard that both sets of parents were very angry, and they each invited a witch to drive away the fox spirits. At the time, he was packing to go north, and he doesn't know how it turned out in the end.

A person who sees ghosts said: "In a household, when a stepchild is adopted, if the child is of a different surname, even if they are the son of a daughter or the nephew of a wife, only the biological children come to offer sacrifices during ancestral worship, while the adopted children do not. However, if the adopted child is of the same clan, even if they are beyond the fifth degree of kinship, they come to offer sacrifices. Biological children also come, but they sit to the side and do not take precedence. Only when Yu adopted Zhang's son did the adopted child come to offer sacrifices. Over time, it was discovered that several generations ago, a woman from the Yu family was pregnant when she married Zhang, making Zhang the ancestor of the Yu family. What is the meaning of this?" I said: "This meaning is easy to understand. Events far apart are connected, like the collapse of a copper mountain in the west causing a bell in the east to respond. Amber attracts small seeds but not needles, and a magnet attracts needles but not seeds. Those with a single lineage have a shared spirit, while those with two lineages do not share the same spirit. Seeing this, one naturally feels a sense of unity within the clan, and a sense of reverence for ancestors also naturally arises. The body splits into four limbs, and each limb further splits into five fingers, making twenty-five parts in total. I can feel the pain and itching of all twenty-five parts because they are all part of one body. No one is closer than one's wife and concubines. If my wife or concubines do not tell me, I will never know their pain or itching. It is as if there are two separate bodies."

Song Zigan said, an old scholar taught at a rural school. Next to the school, there was a pile of firewood, where a fox lived. The villagers dared not approach it, but the unruly students would sometimes defile it. One day, the old scholar went to attend a funeral and promised to return the next day. The children, taking advantage of his absence, made a platform out of desks and painted with red and black to perform a play. The old scholar suddenly returned and severely beat each of them, causing them to bleed, and left angrily. Everyone thought the children were either eleven or twelve years old at most, or just seven or eight years old, and all found the teacher too strict. The next day, the old scholar returned and said he had not actually come back the previous day. They then realized that the fox was seeking revenge. Some wanted to sue the local deities, some wanted to remove the pile of firewood, and some wanted to go and insult the fox. One person said, "The children were indeed disrespectful, but the punishment was not excessive; it was just too harsh. I have heard that to overcome evil spirits, one should use virtue. Competing with force will never lead to victory. Retaliating against each other in a cycle of revenge, I fear the disaster will not stop here." Everyone then stopped. This person can be called level-headed and far-sighted.

In the second year of the Yongzheng era (1725), a tenant farmer named Zhang Tianshi in his household gave birth to a goose with one body and two heads. Some people thought it was a monster. Mr. Fenggong said: "It is not a monster. Just as humans can be twins, eggs can also have double yolks. Eggs with double yolks will produce chicks with malformed heads, which I have seen many times." Discussing this with his cousin Yu Dun, Yu Dun said: "Generally, geese with one male and one female will produce ten chicks from ten eggs. If there are two males and one female, they will produce only eight or nine chicks, because the male energy is mixed. If there is one male and two females, they will also produce only eight or nine chicks, because the male energy is weak. Chickens and ducks are not affected by this, as each species has its own nature." Reflecting on this, I thought about how geese and ducks cannot incubate their own eggs, and humans use chickens to do so. In the beginning of creation, birds were first formed through a gaseous process before being born from eggs. This is self-evident—everything is first formed through a gaseous process before physical reproduction. The debate about whether the chicken or the egg came first is well-known, but no one has thought about this. I wonder how the species survived if chickens did not incubate the eggs. This is truly something that cannot be understood after much thought.

Dong You, the Han official, said that when he was staying at a friend's house in Shandong, he heard that a girl from his neighbor had been bewitched by a fox spirit. The girl's father tracked down the fox's lair and, after using every possible means, caught a small fox. He made an agreement with the fox, saying, "If you can release my daughter, then I will release your son." The fox agreed, and after releasing its son, the fox still came to visit. The girl's father scolded the fox for breaking the agreement, and the fox apologized, saying, "Many people deceive each other, so why should we be blamed for this?" The girl's father was very angry and ordered his daughter to pretend to persuade the fox to drink, while secretly placing poison in the drink. The fox, after being poisoned, transformed and left unsteadily. After one night, tiles and debris flew around the house, and the windows and doors shook. A group of foxes gathered and shouted, demanding revenge. The girl's father shouted sternly, telling the beginning and end of the story. It seemed like an old fox was speaking, saying, "Alas, they only see people deceiving each other and follow suit, not knowing that divine justice returns. Those who are good at deceiving will eventually meet deception. The master's arguments are just, and offending him would be disastrous. You all should follow me back." After the old fox finished speaking, everything became silent. This fox's perspective exceeded its son's far.

Ji Lianfu said, in his old house in Taixing, there was a five-bay pavilion at the back, rarely visited by people. Ji Lianfu chose it for its seclusion and often stayed alone there. One night, just as he opened the door, he saw a black thing on the wooden platform, resembling a person but not quite, with long, coarse, hair-like covering that looked like a straw cloak. It extinguished his lamp and let out a long roar, rushing toward him. Another time, while staying at his uncle's house in Yangzhou, in a drowsy state, he saw a red-clothed woman pushing the door open. He knew it was a ghostly thing and struggled to get up and scolded it. The woman knelt down, as if making a plea, and soon she slowly left the room. The next day, he asked the host, who confirmed that indeed, a woman had hanged herself in this room, and at that time, she was causing disturbances. Dark and secluded rooms are often where ghosts and spirits hide. The black thing was likely an incomplete spirit or demon, having been lurking for a long time, and it was too sudden to avoid that night. The hanged ghost knelt long, perhaps seeking release from its state of being. Ji Lianfu was in his prime and full of energy, so neither could approach and stay. Local shamans say that all those who hanged themselves wore red clothes, so their ghosts can enter and exit rooms, and the gods and spirits do not prohibit it. Because women are not buried in red clothes, red is a yang color, still resembling a living soul. This saying is not known to have originated from where, but women believe it very deeply. So those who die with resentment often wear red clothes when they hang themselves, to seek to cause disturbances. This ghost wore red clothes, must also be due to this.

My elder brother Qinghu told me that in the home of my two aunts who married into the Lu family in Cangzhou — both of my twin aunts married into the Lu family, and I do not know if this refers to the home of my second aunt or fifth aunt — there was a large tree outside the gate. The geomancers said it was inauspicious, and the people discussed cutting it down, but they had not yet decided. One night, he dreamed of an old man saying, "We have been neighbors for two or three hundred years. How could we harm each other?" Upon waking, he realized it was the spirit of the tree, who said, "If not cut down soon, it will become an evil spirit." The decision was then made to cut it down. If the tree had not spoken on its own, the matter might still not have been resolved. There are cases in the world where one prevents disaster in advance, makes up for and smooths over the situation, but instead, it triggers the disaster. This is often the case. I heard about Li Taipu Jingtang, who, during a certain examination, reviewed the test papers. Suddenly, a scholar came to present his credentials, but Li Taipu refused to see him. However, he privately wondered, "Is there a flaw in the paper?" The next day, he checked it and found that it had already been reviewed and had no markings. He then carefully rechecked it and finally found the mistake, which caused the examination to be postponed. If this scholar had not sought an audience, it would have been missed.

A Servant Named Wang Jing, Son of Wang Liansheng, was once the son of Wang Liansheng. I had an old pawnshop in Cui Village, which I had run for a long time, but it had suffered significant financial losses. The family gathered funds to revive it and called upon Wang Jing to be in charge of the night watch. One night, he hanged himself on the upper floor. Although his mother and brother could not guess the reason, a guest worker named Hu Xingwen lived near the building, and his wife was gravely ill. Suddenly, Wang Jing's spirit possessed her and spoke, recounting the faults of his mother and brother. He said, "I died because of gambling debts. Why did you demand so much for my coffin and burial expenses? It made me feel betrayed. This is to show that it was not my intention." Someone asked if he blamed those who demanded payment. He replied, "I do not blame them. If they owed me, could I not demand payment?" Another asked if he blamed those who encouraged gambling. He replied, "I do not blame them either. The hand was my own. If I did not gamble, could they have held my hand and made me gamble? I only waited for someone to take my place." At first, people thought the sick woman was delirious, but later she recounted her life, greeted old acquaintances, and spoke in a voice just like Wang Jing's. All present remarked, "This spirit did not lose its true nature and will not ultimately fall into the realm of ghosts."

Li Yudian recounted that there was once a young man from a noble family who was lost in the deep mountains at night, unable to find his way. Seeing a cave, he decided to rest there and found the respected elder, Mr. So-and-so, inside. Initially afraid to enter, he was eventually persuaded by the elder's earnest invitation, believing there was no harm, and thus approached to pay his respects. They exchanged pleasantries and inquired about each other's well-being as if they were old acquaintances. He briefly asked about the elder's family affairs, and they shared a mutual sense of sorrow and regret. Curious, he asked where the elder's honorable burial site was, wondering why he was wandering here alone. The elder sighed deeply and said, "In my lifetime, I had no significant faults, but I merely followed others' plans when studying and fulfilled my duties as expected when serving in office, achieving nothing noteworthy. Unbeknownst to me, several years after my burial, a large stele appeared before my tomb, with a dragon-like arch above the inscription in seal script, bearing my rank and name. The text on the stele, however, described things I did not know, and even the parts that seemed to have some basis were exaggerated. My life was simple and honest, and I felt uneasy about this. Moreover, passersby often read the stele, leaving behind criticism, and spirits gathered to watch, adding more mockery. Unable to bear the constant chatter, I took refuge here, only visiting the grave during festivals to see my descendants."

The scholars gently consoled each other, saying: "Benevolent persons and filial sons, without this they cannot honor their parents. Even Cai Yong, a renowned scholar, felt ashamed for writing a eulogy for a man of low social status, and Han Yu, a prominent Tang dynasty scholar, also wrote flattering eulogies for the dead. There are many such examples in ancient times. Why should you be so concerned?" A certain scholar replied sternly: "The fairness of right and wrong is clear to everyone's heart. Even if one can deceive others, one will feel ashamed when questioning oneself. Especially since public opinion is already established, what benefit is there in lying? Honoring one's parents should be through public recognition, why use false words to invite criticism? It is not surprising that later generations see things this way." He brushed off his robe and stood up, leaving in a dazed and confused state. I believe this is a parable. His father-in-law, Tian Baiyan, said: "This event may not have actually happened, but this argument is worth keeping."

An Elderly Scholar from Jiaohe: Liu Junzhuo Liu Junzhuo, an elderly scholar from Jiaohe, lived in Wen Family Temple and taught in Cui Village. One day, deeply intoxicated late at night, he suddenly returned home. It was after a period of heavy rain, and both rivers in the area had suddenly risen. He had forgotten this, and as he walked to the riverbank, he suddenly felt the urge to bathe but was slightly afraid of the depth of the waves. Suddenly, a person appeared and said, "There is a place nearby where you can bathe. Let me guide you there." When they arrived, they found a flat stone resembling a fishing pier, and they washed together. Liu Junzhuo's intoxication slightly subsided, and he sighed, "This is less than ten miles from home, but the water obstructs the path, making it winding and difficult. I would need to walk an additional four or five miles." The person said, "There is another place where you can cross. Let me guide you again." They took off their clothes and crossed directly. As they neared home, the person hurriedly bade farewell and left. Liu Junzhuo knocked on the door and entered the room, startling his family. "How could you return home when the path was blocked?" they asked. Liu Junzhuo recalled the events but did not know how they had happened. He speculated that the person might be a certain Mr. He from Gaochuan or a certain Mr. Zhao from Liubuzhou (a village, the meaning of which is not clear). Later, he sent his son to thank the two families, but they all denied the incident. They searched for the flat stone in the river but found no trace of it. He finally realized he had encountered a ghost. Ghosts often torment drunkards, but this ghost was unique in guiding the drunkard. Perhaps Liu Junzhuo, a lifelong model of propriety and virtue, embodying the qualities of an ancient gentleman, was saved by a deity when he drunkenly crossed the turbulent waves, which would surely have been dangerous.

The Slave Boy Dong Zhu said: In Jinghe Town, there was a man named Jia. His brother had died, and his widowed sister-in-law was staying at her mother's house. Because of the busy farming season, he went with his wife to invite her back to help with the midday meal. They stopped at a dilapidated temple halfway there. The man made his wife guard the temple gate while he went inside to tease his sister-in-law. She angrily scolded him, but he eventually committed a violent act. The sister-in-law angrily refused and called for help, but there were no people nearby who could hear her cries. The wife went in to try to stop him, but he did not listen. Just then, a woman carrying food tripped on the road and broke her food container. Five or six laborers returned to eat, happened to pass by, heard the cries, and rushed to see what was happening. They explained the situation. The crowd became angry and allowed the sister-in-law to leave first. They took turns holding the man down, stripped the wife, and took turns raping her. They frequently shouted: "You raped your sister-in-law, and we are witnesses, so you should die. We raped your wife, and your sister-in-law will never testify against us. You can sue us, but we are leaving to have lunch." The man knelt down and begged the crowd to keep the matter secret. This is what is called using public means for private ends. This is similar to the case of Yang Sheng mentioned earlier, which was also unreasonable, but it also satisfied people's desires. Later, everyone in the village knew, but no one was willing to report it. One reason is that the laborers were all wandering people; they worked for a day, got paid, and then dispersed, making it impossible to know who they were. Another reason is that people hated the man. Everyone said: "The woman carrying food tripped neither too early nor too late. Isn't this as if someone had arranged it?"

Hanged ghosts and drowned ghosts all seek to take someone else's place; this phenomenon is described in various stories. However, those who die by suffocation or strangulation, as well as those who die from burning or being crushed, are not known to seek to take someone else's place. Why is this so? The Luohan Peak in Rehe, which resembles a seated monk, is a popular destination for visitors. Recently, one person fell to their death from the cliff, and shortly after, people in the city were seen running up to the peak's summit without reason, only to fall and die. Everyone said that this was because the ghosts were seeking to take someone's place, but inviting monks to perform rituals of repentance had no effect. Only when the officials passed by and the patrol guards arrived did the disturbances cease. Ghosts who take their own lives seek to take someone else's place because they valued their lives lightly, while those who die accidentally or are led by spirits do not. Why, then, must they take turns replacing each other? I believe that this may be due to divine punishment or the mischief of mountain spirits, who seek offerings or sacrifices. It is not appropriate to generalize all such incidents as cases of ghosts seeking to take someone else's place.

My hometown produces jujubes, which are transported north by cart to supply the capital and sold southward along the canals to various provinces. The locals often consider it a constant occupation. When jujubes are not yet ripe, they fear fog the most; if fog affects them, they become thin and wrinkled, leaving only the skin and pit. Whenever fog starts, sometimes burning piled-up firewood and grass upwind, the thick smoke disperses the fog, or shooting with bird guns to counter it, it disperses even faster. Because when yang energy is strong, yin gloom dissipates. All supernatural beings fear firearms. Mr. Songtao said that in Shanxi and Shaanxi, whenever yellow clouds suddenly appear in the mountains, there will be wind and hail damaging crops. Using large cannons to counter it, there were fallen toads as big as carriage wheels. When I was the supervisor of education in Fujian, mountain spirits sometimes walk on rooftops at night, making a clattering sound. When the gatehouse cannons are fired, they stagger and flee, becoming silent in an instant. Ghosts also fear firearms. When I was in Urumqi, I once shot a fierce ghost with a firearm, and it could not reassemble into a form. The details are recorded in *Panyang Xiangya Lu*. Because ghosts and spirits are all yin beings.

Dong Qiuyuan recounted that in Dongchang, a scholar was walking at night in the suburbs when he suddenly saw a grand mansion. He privately thought, "This must be the tomb of some family; how could there be such a residence here? It must be a fox spirit's transformation!" Having long heard of the tales of "Qingfeng" and "Shuixian" from *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, he hoped to encounter something extraordinary. He hesitated and did not move forward. Soon, carriages and horses approached from the west, their attire very splendid. A middle-aged woman lifted the curtain and pointed to the scholar, saying, "This young man is quite excellent; you may invite him in." The scholar looked behind the carriage and saw a young girl of extraordinary beauty, like a celestial being. He was overjoyed beyond his expectations. After entering the mansion, two maids came out to welcome him. Having determined that they were fox spirits, he did not inquire about their family or lineage but followed them in. He did not see the owner emerge, but the preparations were lavish, and the food and drink were abundant. The scholar waited for the wedding ceremony, his heart restless like a flag hanging in the wind. By evening, the sound of flutes and drums filled the air. An elderly man pulled back the curtain and bowed, saying, "The new groom has arrived at the door. As a scholar, you must be familiar with wedding rituals.
