We humbly ask you to serve as a witness, bringing honor to the three families involved."

The man was greatly disappointed. However, since he had not discussed marriage with her, there was nothing more to be said. Moreover, having dined heartily on their food and drink, it was difficult to leave immediately, so he hastily performed the wedding rites and departed without saying goodbye. His family, worried about his disappearance, searched for him for an entire day and night. When he finally returned, he angrily recounted his experiences, and all who heard him could not help but laugh and say: "It was not the fox playing a trick on you, but you yourself who played the trick." I then related the story of a man named Li Erhun, who, poor to the point of barely surviving, went to the capital to seek a livelihood. On the road, he met a young woman riding a donkey. Taking the opportunity to speak with her, he engaged her in mild teasing, but the young woman neither answered nor became angry. The next day, they met again, and the woman tossed him a handkerchief before whipping the donkey and riding off, glancing back as she said, "I shall stay overnight in Gu’an." Li opened the handkerchief and found several silver hairpins and earrings. Just as his funds had run out, he took them to the pawnshop, only to discover that they were exactly the items the pawnshop had lost the previous night. He was severely tortured and, in the end, falsely confessed to being a thief. This was indeed a true case of a fox playing a trick. Qiu Yuan remarked: "If you had not flirted with the young woman, how could you have ended up in this situation? It can still be called self-play."

Putaian Li Sheng Yuchong said: There was a man named Chen Zhigang, whose wife died, leaving behind two sons and one daughter. A year later, Zhigang also died. Several acres of land and a few rooms of house were all taken by his elder brother and his wife, who claimed to raise their children but actually mistreated them. Soon after, there were ghostly cries heard every night behind the house. Neighbors grew increasingly uneasy and realized that the ghost was Zhigang's spirit. They climbed onto the roof and shouted: "Why don't you torment your brother? Crying won't help." The ghost retreated several zhang away and sobbed in reply: "Brothers are closest of kin; I cannot bring myself to torment him. Below the father, the elder brother is the most respected. By ritual, I dare not torment him. I am only begging for mercy." The elder brother, moved by the ghost's words, cursed his wife: "You have made me less than human!" He also climbed onto the roof and shouted: "It's not me, it's my wife." The ghost sobbed again: "Your wife is your wife; you cannot torment her, so how could I?" The wife felt ashamed and dared not leave the house. From then on, she treated the children well, and the ghost no longer cried out. If all those who have suffered the loss of a sibling were like this, would ghosts still have disputes within the family?

卫媪是虞惇的堂弟的乳母，她的丈夫嗜酒如命，常常沉醉其中。有一晚，他锁上门独自外出，没有人知道他去了哪里。有人说在邻居家的花园井边发现了他的鞋子，一看果然是他的。人们查看后发现，尸体也在那里。大家认为墙并不很高，一个醉汉怎么可能翻过去呢？而且如果他跳井，为什么还要脱鞋呢？所有人都感到非常困惑不解。询问看守花园的人，他说那天他去卖菜了，没有回来，只有妇人带着幼子在那里过夜。妇人说，那天晚上她听到墙外有两个人在邀请客人，接着又听到有人被拉扯着要留下，然后传来一声巨响，像是有人从墙上跳下来，声音在墙内，接着又听到邀请客人进屋的声音，声音在井边，不久又听到催促客人脱鞋上床的声音，又是一声巨响，之后就安静下来，没有其他声音了。这个地方本来就有许多鬼怪，人们对此并不在意。没想到这个人竟然掉进了井里，难道是溺水的鬼魂在寻求替代者吗？于是他们填埋了那口井，之后也没有再发生什么。

My Uncle, the Scholar of the Elm Pavilion, once told me that he saw a woman in a whirlwind, spreading her sleeves and moving swiftly like a bird, and in an instant, she was already several miles away. He also once saw a creature jumping and leaping under a large locust tree, neither a dog nor a sheep, with brown fur, and when approached, it disappeared, leaving him unsure of what it was. I replied: "Uncle has devoted his life to studying the classics, and does not pay much attention to historical and literary works, but these two creatures are both recorded in ancient texts. The woman is a celestial demon, as recorded in the *Boyi Zhuan*, where it is said that during the Tang dynasty, Xue Cong saw an old monk in the Buddhist temple of Wei Prefecture, who spoke of seeing a celestial being being pursued in the Gobi Desert. The brown creature is a tree spirit, as recorded in the *Shiji* (Records of the Grand Historian), where it is mentioned that in the 27th year of the reign of King Zhao of Qin, a large oak tree was felled in the southern mountains, and a great bull was found. A note states: 'There is now a shrine to the fierce bull on the old road of Wudu, depicting a large ox with a tree growing from its body, and a bull emerging from the water, which was later seen in the River Feng.' The *Lieyi Zhuan* (Book of Strange Things) records that during the reign of King Wen of Qin, an oak tree transformed into a bull, and when the riders struck it, they could not overcome it, some falling to the ground, their hair loose, and the bull, fearing them, dove into the water, so the Qin people established the *Mao Tou Ji* (Hairy Head Cavalry) because of this. In the *Ku Shu Fu* (Ode to the Withered Tree) by Yu Xin, it is said: 'The white deer and the cypress, the blue ox and the cypress.' In the *Ji Dao Wen* (Ode to the Banner) by Liu Zongyuan, it is said: 'There was a great bull in Feng, which transformed into a giant oak, and the Qin people relied on the spirit, thus establishing the *Mao Tou Ji*.' This is precisely the event being referred to."

Wang De Pu recounted that there was a county official who rested in a pine forest at night and heard weeping. The official, known for his courage, went to investigate and found two people sitting on a stone table, whispering to each other as if they were a couple saying goodbye. Suspecting they were eloping, he questioned them about their situation. The man stood up and replied, "Do not come closer; I am a ghost. This woman was my beloved concubine, who unfortunately died young. Though she was buried elsewhere, her spirit often lingers here. Now she has been assigned to the cycle of rebirth, and from this point on, we will be separated forever, through the vast and endless eternity. That is why we grieve together." He asked, "If you were married in life, each had a spouse, how can you be together again after death?" The man replied, "Only chaste widows who remain loyal to their deceased husbands have their husbands temporarily stay in the underworld, waiting to be reborn together in the human world after death, to continue their previous relationship and make up for the suffering of their entire life. For others, their rebirth is determined by their karma, some are ready, others are not, and they cannot be equalized. You should leave now; our time is extremely precious, and we cannot talk about these matters of the afterlife with you." He opened his mouth and exhaled, causing leaves to fly chaotically. The official, startled, ran away. Later, when he passed by the same place again, he realized it was the tomb of a certain family. Wang De Pu compiled the "Autumn Lantern Collection of Stories" for Mr. Ningzhai, but this event was omitted. Could it be that Wang De Pu overlooked mentioning it, or did Mr. Ningzhai forget?

My maternal grandmother, Lady Cao, once told my mother: In Cangzhou, there was a woman from a bureaucratic family who was not respected by her husband and became depressed, her mental state deteriorating. Their marital harmony worsened, symbolized by the discord of the qin and se (a metaphor for marriage). Then, a virtuous nun came and inquired about the causes and effects of the situation. The nun said, "I am not a judge of the underworld, so I cannot examine the records of spouses. Nor am I a Buddha or Bodhisattva, so I cannot see three lifetimes." However, she continued, "The principle of cause and effect, I do understand." She asked, "Is it that your couple is bound by grudges? This is determined by fate, not by human will." Yet, she added, "Although fate is powerful, human effort can also overcome it." Therefore, the teachings of the Buddha allow for repentance. She advised, "Just eliminate your arrogance, suppress your pride, accept adversity calmly, resolve conflicts through emotion rather than logic, fulfill your duties within the household, serve your in-laws with filial piety, maintain harmony with your sisters-in-law, treat concubines with kindness, do your best within your control, and do not worry about what is beyond your reach. Perhaps this can improve the situation." She concluded, "Merely asking about past causes without taking action is futile." The woman followed her advice, and indeed, their relationship returned to harmony as before. My mother often recounted this story to the other women, saying, "The nun's words are truly a magical spell to resolve grievances in the domestic sphere. If one has faith and practices them, there is no doubt they will be effective. If they are not effective, it is likely due to insufficient practice."

Cai Taishou Bi Chang claimed to be the judge of the underworld, and some questioned this claim. However, Zhu Zhujun's father, whom Tang people referred to as "Xian De" (deceased father), as recorded in *Bei Meng Suo Yan*, had foreknowledge of his death date, and Zhu Zhujun's mother also knew her own death date in advance, with both dates being accurate. This raises the question of how else to explain such precise predictions? Zhu Shijun, the Military Governor, spoke at length about other matters, and Zhu Shijun was not one to speak falsely. Gu Demao, the Langzhong, also claimed to be the judge of the underworld, but later admitted to leaking secrets of the underworld and was demoted to the position of village god, a claim that could not be verified. I have heard his discussion of the laws of the underworld, which is already recorded in *Luan Yang Xiao Xia Lu*. His theory on the survival and dissolution of spirits is also quite reasonable. Generally, he posits that the residual energy of a person becomes a ghost, which gradually dissipates over time. However, there are three types of spirits that do not dissipate: those of loyal, filial, chaste, and righteous individuals, whose virtue preserves their spirit; those of brave generals and strong soldiers, whose strength preserves their spirit; and those of scholars with great knowledge and talent, whose intellect preserves their spirit. There are also three types of spirits that do not immediately dissipate: those of wronged souls and vengeful spirits, whose pain and anger keep their energy concentrated; those of the wealthy and powerful, whose vitality keeps their energy strong; and those deeply involved in family ties, whose emotional attachment keeps their energy focused. As for cruel and vicious individuals, their negative qualities also prevent their energy from immediately dissipating, but most fall into the hellish realm of "niraya" and are not included in this category. He speaks with certainty, perhaps there is some basis for his claims.

During the summer of the Yongzheng era in the year of the Wu Shen (1738), a powerful whirlwind swept from north to south over Cui Village, its force likened to the rising tide. The parapets of my family’s watchtower were partially torn off—such structures, common in rural areas, were built to deter thieves and topped with defensive walls. At the home of my paternal uncle, Cànchéng, two vases of flowers and a large jar of water were rolled up and placed on the roof, remaining in their original positions, not at all tilted or displaced. Meanwhile, a wind stove with a copper kettle in front of the steps, with the charcoal fire still burning brightly, remained undisturbed, the reason for which remained unknown. The next day, when I inquired of the villages to the north, they all reported not having seen the whirlwind. However, as one moved several miles beyond the village, the wind gradually rose into the clouds, appearing yellow in color and emitting a fishy odor when smelled. Perhaps, it was close to the Eastern Sea, not more than a hundred miles away, where sea deities were moving about, and aquatic monsters were flying and rising, perhaps as a playful or mischievous act.

During the intercalary third month of the year Jia Chen, my cousin Yu Dun, who was serving as the Confucian tutor in the city of Man, was invited by his colleague Dai Jun to tour Baoyang Mountain. Dai brought along two students named Peng and Liu, who went ahead of the group. Yu Dun accompanied his brother Ruqiao, his son Shujing, and two other students named Jin and Liu, who proceeded from the back of the mountain to view the scenic spots of Bull Horn Cave and Immortal's Chamber. As they ascended the base of the mountain, they saw a person standing on a rock at a distance, whom they assumed Dai had sent to meet them. They were still about a li away when they hurried to meet this figure. As they got closer, the figure appeared to grow smaller, and upon reaching the spot, they found a piece of white stone leaning against the rock, standing upright. The stone was about one to one and a half feet tall and four to five inches wide, and it bore no resemblance to a human form, yet it appeared human-like when viewed from a distance. How strange! Generally, objects appear smaller when viewed from afar, as the Europeans call it "parallax." This stone, however, appeared larger when viewed from a distance and smaller when viewed up close, which is even more extraordinary. After descending the mountain for about a li, they looked back and saw the stone still as it had been at first. The group believed the stone had some sort of spirit and decided to go back up the mountain to take it with them. Peng and Shujing went first but could not find the stone. Then Ruqiao and the two Liu students went together, and the path remained the same, with everything appearing as it had before, but the stone could no longer be seen. It seems that in the deep ravine of Man Valley, where the spirits reside, such occasional manifestations often occur. As for the Immortal's Chamber on this mountain, it is located on a sheer cliff that is inaccessible to people. The locals often see people coming and going at the entrance of the cave, and these must surely be practitioners of alchemy who have transformed into immortals.

Shen Zhan Cangding said that at Liu Zhi Temple, there were two students who were taking the imperial examinations. One night, while traveling, they lost their way and saw a dilapidated house. They decided to seek temporary shelter for the night. The courtyard was partially ruined and had no doors or windows. They planned to sit in the western room, but heard words from behind the tree: "Both of us are scholars, and I dare not refuse. The western room is where a young girl lives, please do not enter. The eastern room is where an old man teaches his disciples, you can sit there." They knew it was either a ghost or a fox spirit, but were too exhausted to go further. They temporarily bowed to the tree and sat down facing each other. Suddenly, they remembered that they should have asked for directions, so they stood up again to speak, but there was no response. In the dark, they felt something touch their hands. They touched it and found that each of them had half a melon by their side. They thanked them, but there was no response. At dawn, when they were about to leave, they heard words from behind the tree again: "Go two miles to the east, and you will find the main road. One word to offer: the concept of 'mutual body' in the *I Ching* cannot be discarded." They did not understand what was meant, and asked again, but there was no response. When they took the examination, the question was indeed about the concept of 'mutual body.' In the examination hall, everyone used the interpretations of Cheng and Zhu, but only these two students answered according to the advice given, and both ranked at the top.

In the year of Jiazi during the Qianlong era, I was in Hejian taking the imperial examination. A fellow student covered his head with a handkerchief, saying he had fallen from a donkey and injured his forehead. Later, a fellow traveler who knew about the incident said: "He encountered a beautiful woman dressed up alone under an official willow tree on the road. Suddenly, he stopped his horse to ask for directions. The woman said, 'This is the main north-south postal road, with carriages and horses coming and going. How could there be any danger of getting lost? You are just deceiving me because I am alone.' Suddenly, a flying tile struck him, causing him to bleed and break his face. The woman then went straight into the millet field, and it is unknown whether she was a person, a fox, or a ghost. However, since no hand was seen raising the tile, it is suspected that she was not a person. Ghosts should not appear during the day, so it is suspected that she was a fox. Gao Meicun said: 'This does not need to be deeply investigated. Whether it is a person, a fox, or a ghost, it should be struck in the end.' In the autumn of Dingmao, I heard that a son of a high-ranking official passed by the eastern cross street in the evening and was lured into a room by a prostitute. Suddenly, the woman's husband returned home at midnight, forcing him to take off all his clothes and shoes, leaving him naked without a thread of clothing, and carrying him to place him among the graves outside the gate. The son of the high-ranking official had no choice but to shout and claim to have met a ghost. Someone informed his family, and they welcomed him back. At that time, Yao An Gong was serving as the Minister of Revenue. Upon hearing this, he laughed and said: 'Now I know that ghosts can steal.' Both stories serve as warnings to those who are frivolous and disrespectful.

Chai Youlun, the Thousand Total commander of Urumqi, recounted that during the campaign against Ho Jie-jian, he led his soldiers to search the mountains and ventured into the deep valley of Zhutusi. There, they encountered the Ma Ha Qin, and he shot one of them. The wounded soldier carried the arrow and fled. The remaining seven or eight also scattered in all directions. Chai and his men took their horse and tent, and found a Hui woman tied to a tree. Her left arm and thigh had been eaten in small pieces until the bones were visible, and she made sounds like insects or birds. When she saw Chai, she repeatedly extended her neck and made a kneeling and scraping gesture, indicating her desire for a quick death. Chai understood her plea and drew his sword, piercing her heart. She stared with wide eyes, cried out, and then died. Later, Chai passed through the same area again, but the water had risen rapidly, and he dared not cross. He rested for a while, waiting for the water to recede. Suddenly, a whirlwind began to move back and forth in front of his horse, moving and stopping suddenly, as if guiding him. Chai realized it was the ghost of the Hui woman. He rode after the whirlwind and eventually found a shallow place to cross. Ji Lianfu recounted that in Taixing, there was a man named Jia who had passed the provincial examination and was entitled to receive a stipend. Despite this, he had a secret interest in talismans, exorcisms, and magical incantations. He sought out teachers and friends and eventually mastered the Five Thunder Techniques. Later, he fell seriously ill and saw ghosts coming to seize him. He raised his hand to make a gesture, and the ghosts could not approach. Soon after, his family heard the sound of metal or iron on the roof. Strange, fierce, and overwhelming ghosts entered, and they all fled in fear. They heard what sounded like a struggle from afar, and it lasted all night. By morning, they found Jia lying dead under the bed, having dug a deep hole with his hands. They did not know what had caused his death. Life and death are determined by fate, and when one's fate has run its course, using minor skills to compete with others is a sign of not understanding one's destiny.

Lian Fu further says that when Governor Zhong Guangyu was in office in Jiangning, he had two clerks who were also cousins. One was in charge of registration, and the other was in charge of dispatch. They always slept in the same room on the same bed. One night, one fell asleep first, while the other was still holding a candle. Suddenly, he saw a red-clad woman sitting beside the table. Terrified, he called out to wake the other. Rubbing his eyes in shock, he saw that it was not a woman but a strange-looking ghost. It rushed forward to fight, and both men fell unconscious. The next day, people found the door locked and broke it open. They saw that the one who had seen it first was dead, and the other was barely alive. They revived him with medicine, and he then described what happened that night. It is possible for ghosts to disturb people without reason, but when a ghost appears to demand a life, there is always a reason. The clerks in the government office are not officials, but they wield the power of officials. Their words and actions can determine life and death. It is easy to do good, and it is easy to do evil. This must be a result of accumulated grievances and retribution, but we do not know what specific event caused it. Military official Ru Daye of Urumqi says that there were Hui people from Gulang who were gambling in the temple. The temple monk was weak and helpless and could not stop them. One night, as they were drinking and gambling, one of them stretched out his thumb and shouted, "One!" Suddenly, a large fist, like a five-drum basket, appeared through the door. The fingers were spread wide, and a loud voice shouted, "Six!" With one slap of the hand, the candle went out, and the table was shattered. More than ten people were all shocked and fell unconscious. By dawn, they gradually came to, and since then, they have not dared to return. The Buddha has no heart of calculation towards all beings. Could this be the manifestation of the guardian deities?

Zhu Shenghuan from Suzhou, who passed the jinshi examination in the second place in the Shuntian prefecture in the year Renwu, was selected by me as an assistant examiner. One day, I gathered at Reading the Subtle Hut, and during the wine, we shared strange tales. Zhu mentioned that in the past, while on a boat, he saw a steersman with a medicinal patch on his forehead, about an inch long and twice as wide, claiming to have a wound and needing to avoid the wind for several days. A poleman whispered to a guest, saying, "This is a great oddity; the claim of having a wound is false. He once served as the head of a society that offered sacrifices to the Water God and customarily held incense. The night before, he violated purity, and while kneeling to offer prayers, the wind blew the ashes from the incense burner onto his face, causing him to tremble with fear and nearly fail to complete the ritual. Afterward, when he tried to clean himself, a black ink drawing of a secret game scene appeared on his forehead, vivid and lifelike, resembling his own couple. It could not be washed off and became even clearer, so he covered it with a medicinal patch. The others did not fully believe him, but since he had said so, they could not help but watch his forehead closely. The steersman noticed this and said, 'The child is talking too much again.' He sighed deeply and that was all. Thus, the story seems not to be false. It is a pity that it is not convenient to reveal it. My wet nurse, Madam Li, also said that in the past, while ascending Mount Tai, she saw prostitutes and their lovers all offering incense. They met at an inn, and during a brief moment, they kissed, and their lips stuck together, unable to be separated. When they tried to pull apart, it caused excruciating pain, and only after the crowd repented did it open. Some say that the temple priest bribed the prostitutes to make this appearance in order to inspire people's faith. This is also possible.

In Xian County, there was a clerk named Wang Jin. When he first took up his post, he accepted a bribe and was about to exonerate a murderer. As he dipped his brush to draft the document, the paper suddenly flew up and stuck to the ceiling beam, spinning and not descending. From then on, he dared not pervert the law for money. He often used this incident as a warning to his colleagues, occasionally not hiding it. Later, he lived a life of comfort and died a natural death in old age. Another clerk, however, often received bribes to manipulate legal documents and also lived a life without misfortune. Nevertheless, his three daughters all became prostitutes, and his second daughter was punished with a beating. The guard always warned his subordinates, saying, "This is the daughter of a certain master"—the local custom is to call officials "masters"—and she should be treated leniently. After the daughter received her beating, she said to the madam, "If my father had not been an official, I would not have been in this situation today." Alas! Who knows if his father had not been an official, he would not have been beaten today.

In Jiaohe, there were two courtesan sisters who were seduced by a fox spirit, causing them to fall gravely ill and near death. Their family invited a Taoist priest to exorcise the fox, but the fox refused to be captured. Angered, the priest quickly set up an altar to summon the Thunder Department. The fox then transformed into a scholar and appeared before the priest, saying, "Master, do not take it out on us." The fox argued, "To extract essence and kill, truly violates heavenly law, but also consider what these two women are like. They adorn their beauty, bewitching young men, destroying countless families, ruining countless careers, and disrupting countless marriages. Their crimes deserve death. If they extract the essence of others, I extract their essence; if they cause illness, I cause illness; if they take lives, I take lives. All of this is a case of 'trapping someone in their own scheme,' as it should be according to the way of heaven. Why should you favor them unfairly? Moreover, your exorcism is based on the belief that human life is precious, but these women, though appearing human, have the heart of beasts. They are cunning and deceitful, changing their ways like the weather, as the saying goes, 'a face of a human but a heart of a beast.' Since they are beasts, treat them as such. To kill a beast with a beast is a natural course of events. In the deep mountains and vast wilderness, those who prey on each other are countless as the Ganges. Should you involve the Thunder Department for each and every one of them? The priest then gave up and left. Some say the priest could not control the fox and fabricated this story. Yet, the fox's words were profoundly clear and explicit.

程鱼门说，朱某在淮河附近与一位名妓亲近，直到花光了所有钱财后被赶了出来。有一天，一位西方商人来访这位名妓，他的车马非常奢华，挥金如土。名妓因害怕他会离开，便把其他客人全部打发走，尽力讨好他，每天赠送给他大量的金银珠宝，数不胜数。过了两个月多一点，这位商人说暂时要去扬州，然后就再也没有回来。询问他人，也没有人知道他的下落。名妓的财富已经很丰厚，打算离开青楼成为良家妇女。检查她收到的礼物，发现已经一无所有，连朱某送的礼物也不见了。只留下两百多两银子，刚好够两个月的酒食费用。全家人都感到迷茫和困惑，好像刚从梦中醒来。有人说：听说朱某有一个狐仙朋友，这可能是他代为报复的结果。

Fish Gate once recounted that a wandering scholar, whose name is not specified, took a concubine in Guangling. She was skilled in literature and writing, and they were very much in harmony with each other, often reciting poetry together in their chambers. One night, when he returned home, the servants and maids were already asleep, and the room was dark with no lights. Upon entering, he found it silent and empty, except for a note on the table which read: "I am originally a fox spirit, living in seclusion in the mountains and forests. Because I have a debt to repay from the past, I have been with you for half a year. Now that our karmic connection has ended, I cannot stay any longer. I had planned to wait for you here to express my eternal farewell, but I fear that our mutual sorrow and affection would make it even harder to bear. Therefore, I endure the pain and continue on my way, not daring to face you again. Facing the wind, I turn back, my heart full of sorrow and knots. Perhaps this single thought, inscribed on the Three Lives Stone, will plant the seeds of future fate, though it may be uncertain. Please take care of yourself, and do not let the loss of one woman damage your clear mind. Then, even though I am gone, my heart will find some solace." The scholar, upon reading the note, was deeply moved and showed it to his friends, who all sighed with admiration. They recalled that such stories were recorded in ancient texts, and thus they did not doubt its authenticity. A month later, the concubine and her lover traveled north, and their boat was stolen. They reported the theft to the authorities and awaited capture, being detained in the Huai region for several months before the truth came to light. It turned out that her mother had sold her again, and she falsely claimed to be a fox spirit to escape. Zhou Shuchang commented: "This is indeed a true fox spirit; why speak of deception? I fear that among the many strange tales recorded in various books, there may be cases like this where one first meets a celestial maiden and then leaves after a long time."

During my time in the Hanlin Academy, I served as a reader for Mr. Suo Erxun, and we fasted and abstained together in the hall of the waiting officials. The hall once had a plaque written by Mr. He Yimen, which was a tablet of the old residence of Mount Heng, as well as a couplet of poetry. Now the couplet still exists, but the plaque has been lost for a long time. Mr. Suo recounted that during his campaign against Ho Jizhan, he was summoned by edict from a high-ranking official. On the way, they encountered heavy snow, and the carriage and entourage could not proceed. They were accompanied only by a single tent, which they used as a temporary resting place. Unfortunately, they lacked pillows, so they found three or four dead heads and used them as pillows. At night, the heads began to wriggle and move, but they stopped when scolded. I explained that this was not due to ghosts, nor was it because of the scolding that they stopped. When the heads were decapitated, their life force was not completely gone, bound by the severe cold and concealed within. With the warmth of human breath, the ice melted, and the life force could escape outward, causing them to move on their own. Once they moved, the life force dispersed, and they did not move again. Any object whose life force is not completely gone will move when heated with fire, and this is the principle behind it. Mr. Suo said that on ancient battlefields, one never heard of encountering ghosts. He disliked it, thinking his fate was declining. Today, I finally resolved this doubt.

Cui Village is abundant with jujubes, and they often grow into forests. It is commonly called the Jujube Row. When I was young, I heard that several women went out to pick vegetables and passed under the trees. There was a child sitting at the top of a tree, picking the ripe red jujubes and throwing them to the ground. The others rushed to pick them up. The child shouted urgently: "I myself am delighted by the charm of Second Sister Zhou, and I picked these to give her to eat. You black devils, how dare you take them?" The women were angry and scolded him. Second Sister Zhou, angry at his frivolity, also scolded him and picked up a stone to hit him. The child leaped over another branch, like a flying bird darting through the forest. Suddenly, she realized there was no such child in the village. It must be a demon. My grandfather said: "Thanks to Second Sister Zhou's scolding and hitting, otherwise, he would have been bewitched by her." All demons and spirits that bewitch people are self-invited. Su Shi, in his "Fan Zeng Lun," said: "Things must first rot before worms can breed in them."

A candidate for an official position was drinking with friends on a cross street at night, strolling under the moonlight and returning home. His residence was at the Pearl Market Gate, and he took a shortcut through the Fragrance Factory. A small servant carried a lantern in the middle of the road, which suddenly went out when he stumbled. Seeing a house with lights still on, he went to ask for a light. A woman answered the door and invited him in for tea. He knew it was a brothel, but he went in to seek pleasure. However, the woman was shy and demure, her expression dejected and dispirited. When he tried to leave, she held his sleeve firmly to prevent him from going. He tried to flirt with her, and she responded gracefully. Just then, he happened to have some money with him and gave it to her. The woman refused to accept it, but instead pleaded, "If you remember our affection this night, there is a servant named [name] living at [place]. He has been idle for a long time, his wife has died, and his children are young, so they cannot avoid hunger and cold. If you would take him along when you take up your post, I will be grateful to you in the afterlife." The candidate playfully asked if she could accompany him, and she tearfully replied, "I am not a human being, I am [his name]'s wife. He cannot provide for his children, so I have come to seek help despite my shame." The candidate left in a startled manner and looked back to see a new tomb. Later, moved by her feelings, he finally took this person and their children away. He sought a servant, and even ghosts would offer their pillows. The servant had much wealth, but it is unknown where the wealth came from. It is clear that he corrupted officials and harmed the people.

A calf or a young horse might be born with a unicorn's horn, but when a dragon is involved, it is not the true unicorn. There have also been instances where women sleeping outdoors have been involved in such unions. Once, a tenant farmer from the maternal uncle's family, nearly sixty years old, was walking alone in the rain when a storm with thunder and lightning struck. A dragon's claw pressed down on his hat, and he thought he was about to be punished by heaven, so he became terrified and fell to the ground. He realized the dragon was tearing apart his foreskin, thinking it was going to strip him naked before punishing him. Unexpectedly, the dragon twisted his back and lay on the ground, having sexual intercourse with him. If he tried to turn away or shrink back, the dragon would roar angrily and gnash its teeth at his head, and fearing to be eaten, he lay still and dared not move. After a short time, a thunderclap sounded and the dragon left. Groaning on the field, he was covered in the dragon's saliva. Fortunately, his son came with a raincoat to meet him, and carried him back home. Initially, he tried to hide it, but later, his injuries became severe, and he sought medical help. Finally, he told the truth. During the time of weeding crops, there were many women bringing food to the fields, and he had sexual relations with a man. There were also many young shepherds, and he had sexual relations with an old man. This is also something that cannot be understood.

Wang Fanghu recounted that Liu, a man from Mengyin, once stayed at the house of his relative. He happened to mention that there was a monster in the household, appearing and disappearing irregularly, and no one knew where it hid. However, whenever encountered in the dark, it would knock people down, and its body felt as hard as iron and stone. Liu, who enjoyed hunting, always carried a flintlock musket with him, saying, "If so, I should bring this to protect myself." The study room consisted of three bays, and he slept in the eastern room. One evening, as he sat alone by the lamp, he saw an object in the western room standing near the door. It had five senses and four limbs, all resembling those of a human, but its eyes were about two inches below its eyebrows, and its mouth was only a few inches below its nose. None of its parts resembled those of a human. Liu aimed his musket at it, and it immediately retreated and avoided him. Soon, it covered a door with its hand and peered out half of its face, making a show of wanting to come out but not actually doing so. As soon as Liu aimed his musket, it hid again, seeming to fear that if it came out, people would attack it from behind.

Liu, fearing that the monster would attack from behind, dared not go out first. After several rounds, the monster suddenly revealed its entire face, waving its hand and sticking out its tongue at Liu, then suddenly fired a gun. The lead bullet hit the door, and the monster escaped through the smoke. It seems that the monster lured someone to fire a gun, making it miss, not giving time to fire again, and then escaped. When two enemies face each other, the one who acts first loses; is this what it means? If one holds back and does not fire, until dawn, this monster, unable to pass through walls or windows, will have to come out through the door, and will definitely be hit. If it does not come out, it cannot help but reveal its true form. Thus, it was known that the monster fears guns. Later, Liu lay in ambush with a gun by the window lattice, waiting to attack it. The monster thudded to the ground, like the sound of tiles falling and breaking. Upon looking, it turned out to be a piece of broken jar. Nearby children, along a place without a window, playfully drew a human face. The brushwork was crude, drawn arbitrarily, just as Liu had seen.

A wealthy man's son fell gravely ill, died, and then revived, telling his family, "My soul has reached the underworld. I once donated money to save two lives, and I also once forcibly took a woman. Now, those I saved have filed a petition in the underworld on my behalf, and the father of the woman I took has also filed a complaint. The case is still undecided, so I will return. Two days later, he died again and revived, saying, "I cannot be saved. The underworld official says that taking a woman's life is a great evil, and saving a life is a great good, and they can offset each other. The underworld king says that saving a life and then taking another's (in this case, the woman's) creates a moral imbalance, but it can be accepted. However, the woman whose life I took is this person's daughter, and the life I saved is that person's. How can the virtue of saving a life repay the grudge of taking a woman's life? Good deeds are inherently significant and cannot be completely erased. It is better for the underworld not to impose punishment or reward, but to let the good and bad deeds be repaid in the next life, with kindness repaying kindness and grudges repaying grudges. After he finished speaking, he died again. European literature does not accept the Buddhist concept of reincarnation but instead adopts the idea of heaven and hell, which also holds that good and evil deeds do not offset each other, thus cutting off the path for evil people to do good. Generally, minor good and evil deeds can offset each other, but major good and evil deeds cannot. Cao Cao redeemed Cai Wenji, which cannot be denied as a righteous act, but is it enough to offset the crime of usurpation and regicide? Cao Cao did not actually usurp the throne, but he compared himself to King Wen of Zhou, indicating his ambition to do so. He was only afraid of public opinion. In the next life, people may not meet again, and events may not align, so karmic connections may only be fulfilled in several lifetimes."

Cun Village Granary —— named Dongbai Village, a nickname used by locals, who call it "Factory." There used to be a fox in the granary. When my family had not yet divided our belongings, Yao'an Gong studied in this village under Mr. Wang De'an. The servants often entered the granary at night and were frequently hit by flying tiles, though they never saw the fox's form. Only Mr. Wang was able to find coolness there and was not disturbed. However, he sometimes saw men and women walking around, and the wooden beds and bamboo pillows were always spotless, as if they had been constantly cleaned. One day, in the darkness, he saw a man walking along the wall, who appeared to be an old man. He called out and asked, "I have heard that foxes do not approach virtuous people. Am I not virtuous?" The old man bowed and replied, "Foxes that cause mischief and harm do not dare to approach virtuous people, but those that are educated and know propriety enjoy the company of virtuous people. You, sir, are a gentleman, so even young women and children do not avoid you, showing that you have no evil intentions. Why do you doubt yourself?" Mr. Wang said, "Even so, the ways of the living and the spirits are different, and it is ultimately not suitable for us to meet. If we must interact, please do not reveal your form, can we?" The old man bowed deeply and said, "Yes." From then on, he was never seen again.

Shen Ruizhang lived in a high temple to study, and one summer night he slept under the eaves of the Wen Chang Pavilion. After people had quieted down, he heard words spoken on the pavilion: "We also have no place to spend money, but you have accumulated much wealth, why?" One spirit replied, "I want to use this wealth to cast a bronze Buddha, to send to the West Mountain Tanzhe Temple for veneration, hoping to rely on the Buddha's protection and quickly be freed from my form." Another spirit made a spitting sound and said, "A great mistake indeed! Alms must be given with one's own wealth. The Buddha would not ask where the wealth came from, would accept gold stolen by you?" Then silence fell again. "Good indeed," he thought. "When wild foxes and donors gather in large numbers, if they hear these words, it should be like the sound of thunder."

Ruizhang further said, once accompanied by several friends, he traveled to Xishan. Upon reaching the depths of the forested mountains, the weather was warm and sunny, the springs and rocks were clear and spacious, mixed trees had new green leaves, and wildflowers were half-bloomed. While gazing and appreciating the scenery, he heard the sound of reading at the treetops. Looking up, he saw no one, so he bowed and called out from a distance, saying: "You are surely an immortal companion. We share the same scholarly pursuit, may I ask for a brief conversation?" The recitation suddenly stopped, and soon the clear sound was heard again on the other side of the stream. Some wanted to find the path to follow, but Ruizhang said: "People from outside the world, taking advantage of this fine time, still study classical texts. We, who are in the school, are here drinking wine and watching the girls. It is fitting that we are considered vulgar and ignored. Why go through all this trouble?" The group then stopped. In Cangzhou, there was a wandering nun, the same nun who previously explained the cause and effect to a certain lady. She does not allow women to come to her temple but is willing to go to people's homes. Even if it is a small household providing coarse food, she still goes willingly. She does not encourage women to make offerings but only encourages them to have good intentions and do good deeds. In the household of my maternal grandfather, Zhang Gong of Xuefeng, there was a servant woman named Fan. She gave a piece of cloth, and the nun bowed her hands and thanked her. She placed it on the table, and after a moment, she lifted it and gave it back to the woman, saying: "Your generosity has been seen by the Buddha. Since you have given me this, the cloth is now mine. It is already September, and I recently saw your aunt still wearing a single-layered robe. I respectfully present it to you, to make a cloak for your aunt, would that be acceptable?" The servant woman was embarrassed and said nothing, only having sweat on her face. Yao'an Gong said: "This nun deeply understands the heart of the Buddha. Unfortunately, many of her stories are passed down in the inner chambers, but no one can mention her name."

The wet nurse of my late mother, Liao Old Woman, recounted that on the twenty-eighth day of the fourth month, there was a social gathering in Cangzhou, where countless women were offering incense. At dusk, a young man saw an ox-drawn carriage heading eastward, carrying two beautiful women who did not look like they were dressed in a rustic manner. He suspected they might be members of a wealthy family, yet it was unusual for them to travel without a maid or servant and to sit in an uncovered carriage. While pondering this, one of the women dropped a red handkerchief on the ground, which appeared to contain hundreds of coins. Neither the women nor the driver paid any attention to it. The young man, being simple and honest, feared that picking up the handkerchief might bring him trouble, so he did not dare to do so. Upon returning home, he told his mother about the incident, who scolded him for being foolish. Half a year later, a youth from a neighboring village was bewitched by two fox spirits and died of consumption. Someone who knew the whole story said, "It was because he picked up the handkerchief and returned it, leading to a flirtatious encounter." Upon hearing this, my mother suddenly realized and remarked, "I now understand that being foolish is not foolishness, and not being foolish is foolishness."

A man took a servant's daughter as a concubine, but the servant did not wish it, yet had no choice. The man was originally registered under the Eight Banners system and also had a master. The concubine later gave birth to a daughter, who was fourteen or fifteen years old. The master heard of her beauty and also took her as a concubine, though he did not wish it either. He sighed and said, "If this daughter had not been born, this situation would not have occurred." His wife replied, "If you had not taken that servant's daughter as a concubine, this daughter would not have been born." He then felt a sudden sense of disappointment. Another woman among his relatives or close acquaintances daily plotted against her sister-in-law, making her suffer harsh criticism to the point of despair. When she got married, she was also plotted against by her younger sister-in-law and suffered daily criticism similar to what her sister-in-law endured. She returned home and cried in front of her sister-in-law, saying, "Now I understand how difficult it is to be a woman." She added, "The way of heaven is good at returning things to their rightful place; is this not true?" A young man liked to spy on women, trying every trick to secretly observe through small gaps in windows and curtains. One day, he got drunk and fell asleep, and someone playfully applied a medicinal paste to his eyes. Upon waking up, he felt swelling and pain that he could not bear, quickly peeling off the paste to find that his eyebrows and eyelashes were all pulled out. The paste was actually an aphrodisiac he had stored, with a nature that was extremely harsh and intense, affecting his eyes and eventually causing him to become blind. Another person liked to stir up conflicts, going around causing trouble and turning close relationships into enmity. One night, he drank cold tea to quench his thirst, and a scorpion fell into the tea, suddenly stinging his tongue and causing an ulcer. Though the sting did not kill him, his tongue became short and crooked, making it difficult for him to speak fluently. These things happened as if by fate, not by chance.

My late teacher, Mr. Chen Wenqin, said that there was a fellow townsman who did not wish to be known by his name during his lifetime, nor did he have any great vices, but his sole intention was to seek all benefits for himself and all harms for others. One year, he traveled north on the imperial carriage with several friends, staying at an inn. It suddenly rained heavily, and the house was leaking everywhere. When they first noticed the leak, only a few feet of the north wall were not wet. This person suddenly claimed to have caught a cold and lay on the bed under a blanket to sweat. Everyone knew he was faking illness, but there were no words to move him. The rain became even heavier, and everyone sat inside the house as if they were sleeping outdoors, while this person alone slept soundly. Soon the north wall collapsed, and those who had not fallen asleep rushed out quickly. This person was exactly under the collapse, with his forehead broken and blood flowing, and one foot and one arm broken. He was eventually carried back home. This is enough to warn those with ulterior motives. I recall my servant Yu Lu, who was very cunning, and accompanied me to Urumqi. One day, we set out early, and the sky was overcast with rain. Thinking it would rain, he put all his clothes in the carriage and covered them with mine. After traveling for more than ten miles, the sky cleared up, but the carriage got stuck in the mud, and water seeped in from below, soaking everything. The situation was similar to the previous one. Truly, those who are overly clever are what the universe abhors.

Shen Shusun, a native of Wuxian, was the granddaughter of Censor Zhiguang. Her father and elder brothers died early, and she was raised by her grandmother, who was the sister of Scholar Yang Wenshu, named Fen, with the courtesy name Yaoji. She was skilled in poetry and prose, especially adept at painting flowers and plants, and thus Shen Shusun also practiced calligraphy and painting, excelling in color application. When young, she was betrothed to my nephew Rubei but died before marrying. When she fell gravely ill, my late mother went to see her. Mrs. Shen cried out and said, "Zhao Sun—her nickname—your grandmother has come, you can recognize her now." She was already delirious, opened her eyes alone, tears welled up in her eyes, raised her hand to grasp my late mother's bracelet, removed it, and gave it to her. My late mother put it on her arm, and she smiled and passed away. I then understood her intention, wanting to be buried with Jì family items. When she first fell ill, knowing she would not recover, she drew a scroll, sealed it very tightly, and always kept it by her pillow. Asked her but she did not respond. By then I also understood that she left it for my late mother. Opened it, it was a painting of rain-soaked orchids, with the inscription: "Sitting alone, I painted the secluded orchid, the picture completed, I look at it myself. Pity the orchid in the empty valley, the wind and rain cannot withstand the cold." It seems there were unspeakable matters in her family, which delayed her wedding, and this was the reason. My late mother was very sad, wanting to buy land to bury her. Yao'an Gong said it was against etiquette, so he stopped her. Later, her coffin was transported back on a canal boat. My late mother still dreamt of her crying and bowing in her sleep.

Wang Xihou once recounted that during a journey with guest laborers for four nights through Huai Town West, he grew weary and rested briefly. While resting, he heard a ghost call out from afar, saying, "In the village, there is a festival in honor of the gods, with abundant wine and food. Let us all go and partake." The other ghosts replied, "The gods' feast is not meant for us; do not act rashly." The caller said, "This family is plagued by sibling rivalry and disputes between uncle and nephew. The air of discord fills their home, and the signs of misfortune are already present. The gods will not partake. You should go quickly, lest others go before you." Wang Xihou, who was always brave, stood to watch where the ghosts went. As the ghosts approached, the horses tied to the trees neighed in fright. All he could see was black mist swirling around, and the ghosts went on another path, their destination unknown. He reflected, "Blessings are based on virtue and cannot be sought through prayer; disasters are caused by evil deeds and cannot be averted through rituals. If one can do good, even without worshipping the gods, they will be helped. But to disrupt reason and order, and offer sacrifices in hopes of divine protection—would the gods accept bribes?"

Liang Huatang recounted the story of Liao Taixue, who, deeply melancholic and ill at ease, sought solace in a villa during the summer. The window of his villa overlooked a clear stream, opening to face the moon. One night, he heard the sound of lamentation and suffering from the other side of the stream. Upon closer inspection, it appeared as if a woman was tied down and being beaten. He was shocked and in unbearable pain, but the cliffs were steep and the water deep, with no way to cross. He questioned her, "You were buried in a certain mountain, how did you end up here?" The woman wept and said, "Before death, I relied on favor and committed many wrongdoings. After death, I was demoted and assigned here, like military exile in the human world. The village god is cruel and poisonous, often beating me with whips and rods. Only through a grand ritual of fire can one be freed." After speaking, she was dragged away by a host of spirits.

Liao, deeply in love, did not refuse her request, and thus invited monks to offer food, hoping to rescue her from the depths of suffering. After more than a month, the sounds were as before. When he rushed to see, the number of ghosts had increased. The lady was stripped naked and bound, even more humiliated and pitiable. Seeing him, she cried out in sorrow, "The previous rituals were not fully completed, and when I appealed to the gods for release, it was rejected and not carried out. The village god, seeing that the prayers were ineffective, increased the torment. Only a seven-day and seven-night water and land assembly can resolve this calamity." Liao suddenly realized, "If the village god is not present, who would be overseeing this punishment? If the village god is present, would the ghosts dare to speak out against his evil? Moreover, the village god has a temple. Why would he come here? Perhaps it is a cunning ghost in disguise, deceiving to request scriptures and repentance?" The lady saw him in deep thought and called out again, "I am indeed [someone]. Do not doubt me too much." Liao said, "This is clearly false," and questioned, "You have a red mole. Can you point out where it is located? Then I will believe you." The ghost dared not answer. After a short while, they gradually dispersed. From then on, they ceased. This can teach us about the cunning nature of human affairs, even ghosts are like this. It can also teach us that emotions can lead to complications, and things will inevitably exploit weaknesses. Liao claimed that a kitchen maid had died and was buried under this mountain, surely she knew of his affection and instructed the ghosts to do this. It can also teach us that sudden external threats necessarily have an internal spy.

Huatang said again: A scholar from Guangdong Province, on his way to the capital, passed through Bai Guo River. He stayed at an inn for lunch and saw a mule-drawn carriage carrying women, parked in the opposite house. After finishing his meal, he left first, but then entered by chance and saw a new poem inscribed on the wall: "Willows sway over the return stream, their greenery for whom? Poor catkins drift with the wind, like me, adrift. They randomly dot my silk robe, bidding farewell at the long pavilion. I urge you, it's better to settle in the mud, don't turn into water lilies." The scholar said: "This is the speech of a courtesan, expressing weariness with the mundane life." He followed her daily, even to the capital, where he sent a small servant to mark her place of disembarkation. Later, he searched for her and eventually took her as a concubine. They did not plan to meet but happened to meet by chance, using a small lyric as a red leaf, a true case of predestined encounter.

In the household of Uncle Chen, Master De Yin, there was a maid who hated the cat for stealing food. Whenever she saw it, she would beat it. The cat, upon hearing the sound of the plate and the maid's laughter, would immediately flee. One day, Madam Guo, the respected Lady of the household, sent the maid to guard the house. She closed the door and took a short nap. When she awoke, she found several pears missing from the plate. There was no one else nearby, and neither the cat nor the dog had any reason to eat pears. Unable to prove her innocence, she was severely beaten. By evening, the pears were suddenly found in the stove, which greatly surprised her. Upon inspection, there were clear marks of the cat's claws and teeth. She then realized that the cat had taken the pears, and so she too was being punished for stealing food. Even a wasp or scorpion has poison—how true this is! The maid, filled with anger, wanted to beat the cat again. Madam Guo said, "There is no way I would allow you to kill the cat." If the cat were killed, fear of vengeance might lead to strange and unforeseen events. From that day on, this maid no longer beat the cat, and the cat, upon seeing her, no longer fled.

Geng Shouyu from Tongcheng says that a scholar was traveling in Songshan, searching for ancient inscriptions, and did not realize it was late afternoon. It was midsummer, and he lay down on the grass under a pine tree. In the middle of the night, the dew fell, and the cold seeped into his clothes. He shivered and woke up, lying down to watch the moon. He saw several people coming from a small path, spreading out mats on a hillside, and sitting in a circle, drinking wine. He realized they were not ordinary people and was too afraid to move, so he listened quietly to what they said. One of them said: "The two of you are nearing the end of your terms of exile and will soon be reborn. You will soon see the light of day again. Have you learned where you will be reborn?" The two individuals seated above replied: "We still do not know." Soon after, they all stood up and said: "The land god has arrived." Soon, an old man came, leaning on a cane, and bowed to the two individuals, saying: "I recently received a document from the underworld, bringing good news. The two of you were good friends in a past life and will be married in the next life." He pointed to the right person and said, "You are the official." He pointed to the left person and said, "You are the lady." The right person smiled back, while the left person remained silent.

The Land Deity said, "Why are you so sad? Could Yama, the King of Hell, have made a mistake in his records?" This gentleman is upright and honest, but his uprightness makes him domineering, and his honesty prevents him from accommodating others' feelings. Throughout his life, he achieved much but also caused much harm, so he was submerged in a lower state for nearly two centuries before finally being released. However, when we consider the faults of a true gentleman, he still managed to become a high-ranking official. He was inherently a noble person, unwilling to bring harm or benefit to others, yet by neglecting to address every issue, he caused endless problems, leading to his fall into the realm of ghosts for two centuries and his reincarnation as a woman. Because he was profound yet not treacherous, gentle yet not sycophantic, he did not lose his wealth and status. Moreover, since this gentleman often offended others, but he always maintained a harmonious relationship with them, such karma was inevitable, and divine principles are clear. Why are you so sad?" The crowd laughed and said, "He is not sad; he is simply a newlywed woman, who cannot help but feel shy and bashful." There was wine and food, and they invited the Land Deity to officiate, asking if they could first perform the wedding ritual of sharing a wine cup. The atmosphere was noisy and lively, making it impossible to distinguish individual voices. Suddenly, the rooster crowed in the morning, and everyone hurriedly dispersed. It is unknown who this person was from the past.

According to Li Yingsheng, 甲 and 乙 were neighbors who had been close friends for generations. When they were children, they played together, and as they grew older, they studied together, becoming as close as brothers. The men and women of both families would visit each other frequently, even though they lived on opposite sides of a wall, it was like they lived in the same house. One day, someone spread false accusations about 甲's wife, saying she had an affair with her cousin. 甲 investigated but found no evidence, yet he remained suspicious. He secretly confided in 乙 and asked him to investigate on his behalf. 乙 was naturally cautious and careful, so he declined, saying he couldn't do it. 甲 privately thought that by declining without investigating, this meant 乙 knew about it but refused to investigate. Thereafter, 甲 stopped asking, but he did not explicitly state his suspicions. However, because of this, 甲 stopped responding to his wife, leaving her unable to clear her name. She eventually died of depression, and after her death, her spirit attached itself to 乙. She said to him, "Nothing is closer than husband and wife. Yet, the husband secretly asked you to investigate, which shows how much he trusted you."

If you were to vigorously defend my innocence, A would certainly believe and release me. Perhaps A would pretend to investigate and later inform me that there is no evidence, and A would still believe and release me. You would then fear that if the investigation reveals the truth, if you do not report it, you would betray A; if you do report it, you will bear the blame. Thus, you would stand aside, indifferent and self-preserve, causing me to carry resentment to the netherworld. It is like killing someone without using weapons. Today I am taking you to the King of Hell; you should go and be judged. Eventually, you suffered from epilepsy for several days and died. A also said: "The reason for needing friends is for mutual support in times of need. If this matter can deceive me, how could it not deceive others? Those who are distant from me might be deceived, but how could it deceive you? I entrusted you with my innermost thoughts. If there is nothing, you should say so. You should directly tell me not to let idle words come between husband and wife. If there is something, you should secretly inform me, so that I can handle it properly, and not let it tarnish the reputation of our descendants." You treated me as a stranger, by shirking responsibility and causing doubts. What value is there in having such a friend? So I also severed ties with you. And when you died, I did not even mourn for you. Did B truly want to kill me? Too worldly, thus too clever in avoiding trouble. Yet fearing small grievances, leading to great grievances. Fearing one person's resentment, leading to resentment from two people. Ultimately killing me and paying with their own life. Where is the cleverness in that? Therefore, only extremely wise people can act as if they are very naive.

Mr. Dou Donggao, a senior scholar, recounted that during his tenure as the educational official in Zhejiang, a small child in his office would constantly come and go, serving as an attendant. He was assumed to be the son of a laborer, and no one found it strange. Later, when he was sent to move something, he replied that he could not do so. This was unusual, and upon inquiry, he finally confessed that he had once been the servant of a previous educational official, who had died, and his soul had remained in this place. It is said that he has form but no substance, so he is able to convey words, but not to lift objects. This explanation seems reasonable. Yet if ghosts can do things no different from living people, as recorded in ancient texts, what explanation can there be for that? Tengger was once the site of Jinman County during the Tang dynasty, and there still remains a broken stele. Jimsa is home to the ruins of the Tang Northern Court Protectorate, which was built by General Li Jing, known as Li Weigong. The city wall was forty li in circumference, constructed entirely of sun-dried mud bricks. Each brick was one chi thick, one chi five or six cun wide, and two chi seven or eight cun long. The old tiles were over a chi wide and one chi five or six cun long. Within the city, a temple had completely collapsed, and a stone Buddha statue, from the waist down, had sunk into the earth, yet it still stood seven or eight chi tall. There was also one iron bell, towering four chi above the head, with inscriptions on it. However, the inscriptions were faded and indistinct, with not a single character legible. Only by scraping the edges of the characters and examining their strokes could one discern that they might be written in the Ba Fen script. The entire city was covered in black coal, and one had to dig one or two chi into the ground before reaching the soil. The Oirats said that this city was once captured by a fire attack, and the four cannon emplacements around it were built during the siege. As for which dynasty or which person was responsible, they could not say. It was certainly before the time of the Zunghars.

On a hill southeast of the main city, there stands a small fort that complements the larger one, according to the Oirats, who found it difficult to capture due to its strategic position and had to resort to artillery. In the winter of the year Gengyin, the Urumqi military garrison decided to establish a rear camp. My colleague and I, named Qìng and serving as the grain supervisor in Dihua, later appointed as the provincial governor of Hubei, were tasked with planning the location for the garrison. The area was surrounded by countless mountains, and after several days of deliberation, we still could not reach a decision. I told my colleague, "Li Weigong, known for his expertise in military strategy, would have chosen this very spot. The fort he built would certainly be in a critical position. Should we follow his example?" My colleague agreed, and the plan was finalized. This is now known as the Ancient City Fort. Originally named Broken City, it was renamed by the Grand Secretary Wen. At first glance, the fort appears isolated and vulnerable, but within the mountains, there are countless paths and trails, and any passage must go through this fort, demonstrating the wisdom of our ancestors. When the scholar Chu Yunxin compiled the *Western Regions Atlas*, he visited historical sites but forgot to mention this fort, which I now add to the record. In a cave in Kashgar, there are murals of people and horses on the flat walls, which the local people believe were painted during the Han dynasty. They are well-protected and cared for, so despite the passage of time, they can still be recognized. Han dynasty paintings, such as those found in the Wu Liang Shrine, are only known from carved versions, but these murals are the oldest surviving examples. Later, soldiers lit fires to keep warm, and the smoke and soot caused the murals to become completely blurred and erased. It is regrettable that when the army first arrived, there was no artist to quickly sketch or copy them, preserving them on paper.

The Second Son Ru Chuan's wife, Zhao, was of an extremely gentle disposition and served her in-laws with utmost filial piety. Madame Ma praised her for having perfect beauty, speech, and virtue, and it was not a biased statement. Unfortunately, she died young at the age of thirty-three, and I still mourn her to this day. Later, when Ru Chuan served as an official in Hubei, he bought a concubine whose appearance and demeanor were exactly like those of the wife. Everyone was greatly surprised upon seeing her, and everyone in the office and those who had seen the wife were also greatly surprised. Considering the time of her birth, the wife had not yet passed away; how could they be so similar? Plus, she was also the concubine of the same husband, which is even more unusual. However, this concubine died tragically after only a few months. Why did the Creator create such an illusion, allowing people to see her twice? Yao Biēfēng from Tongcheng was skilled in composing poetry, and his calligraphy imitated Zhao Mengfu, with a very close resemblance. He blackened the paper, and appraisers could not tell the difference. He was close friends with my maternal grandfather, Zhang Xuefeng, and often stayed at Zhang's house, sometimes for a couple of months. Later, I heard that he drowned while watching the tide, and my maternal grandfather was very sad and regretful. I often saw his handwriting when I was young, but unfortunately, I was too young to pay attention, and I have forgotten his name. My maternal uncle, Zhang Ziheng, was related to my paternal grandmother and my mother as aunt and niece. All of my grandmother's brothers, only Grandfather Xuefeng was called maternal grandfather. Relatives by marriage are called according to their closeness. I called all of them uncle, as a sign of respect.

A Man Named Biefeng Once Wrote a Book Biefeng once wrote a book while residing in a small garden to the west of his home. One moonlit night, he saw a shadow of a woman on the window. When he went out to look, there was nothing there. Looking around the garden, it seemed as if there were green skirts and red sleeves hidden among the trees, stones, flowers, and bamboo. When he approached from the east, she was in the west; when he approached from the south, she was in the north. He walked around for half the night but could not catch a glimpse of her. Tired, he rested, and then heard a voice outside the window saying, "If you write one volume of the Diamond Sutra, I will come and bow and thank you." "Not more than seven thousand characters, will you agree to do it?" Mr. Biefeng, known for his kindness, asked urgently, "Who are you?" But there was no response. The next day, he just happened to have a plain book of Xuan paper. He gave up all other writing tools and focused entirely on writing the sutra. After finishing, he burned incense and placed it on the table, hoping she would come to take it. At night, it was already lost. By evening, he wandered and looked with longing, and indeed saw a woman slowly coming from outside the flowers. She knelt and bowed to the ground. Mr. Biefeng just raised his hand to lead her, but she stood up straight, looking upward with both eyes, with blood dripping between her chest and abdomen. It turned out to be a ghost who had committed suicide by cutting her throat. Suddenly, he fell down in shock. The servant heard the sound, carried a candle, and arrived, but there was nothing to see. He stamped his feet in regret, blaming the ghost for deceiving him. Mr. Xuefeng said, "The ghost said to bow and thank, and she has already bowed and thanked. The ghost did not deceive you; you generated false thoughts yourself. What blame is there on the ghost?"

Mr. Nanming, the scholar of Confucian classics, said: The joys and sorrows of life are all endless realms, and the joys and sorrows of the heart also have no fixed measure. Once having experienced the utmost joy, even a slight discomfort feels like suffering; once having experienced the utmost suffering, even a slight relief feels like joy. I once set up a school in Kangning Village. The rooms were so low that one could hardly raise their head. The doors had no curtains, and the beds had no mosquito nets. The courtyard had no trees, and it was hot and stifling due to prolonged drought. It was like sitting in a steaming pot. Taking off clothes to rest at noon, flies buzzed around, preventing sleep. Frustration was almost unbearable. I thought this was a hell of raging fire. After a while, exhausted, I fell asleep. Dreaming of sailing in the sea, suddenly a hurricane arose, and the sky and sun became dark. The masts broke and the sails were torn, my heart and courage shattered. In an instant, we sank. Suddenly, it seemed as if someone pulled me out and threw me onto the shore. Immediately, someone tied me up with ropes and locked me in a cellar. It was so dark that I couldn't see anything, and my breathing was also obstructed. The terror and distress were indescribable. Soon, I heard a call by my ear, and suddenly opened my eyes. And I found myself lying on a three-legged wooden bed, feeling comfortable and my mind clear. As if I were living in the fairyland of Penglai and Fangzhang. That night, under the bright moon, I walked with my disciples along the riverbank and sat under a willow tree. Explaining this meaning, I faintly heard a sigh from the grass, saying: "These words are true. Our kind, though submerged in water, is still better than those in hell."

In the household of Zhou Lu Ma, my father-in-law, there was an old servant named Men Shirong. He recounted that he had once crossed the Goupan River at Wuqiao Bridge. By that time, it was already evening, and the accumulated rain had caused a sudden flood, making the ground wet and muddy in all directions. He did not know where it was safe to cross. He saw two men riding horses ahead, taking winding paths and always finding shallow spots, as if they were familiar with the terrain. He followed them, and as they approached the riverbank, one of the riders suddenly stopped his horse. When Men Shirong arrived, the rider spoke softly and said, "If you wish to cross the river, go left and around for about half a li. On the other side, there is a dry tree that can be used to cross. I guided this person here, and he will do everything necessary. Do not go with them, or you will fail." Suspecting they might be bandits, Men Shirong was frightened and turned back, taking a different path as indicated. He constantly looked back and saw one of the riders go ahead on horseback. The second rider followed and reached the middle of the river, where he suddenly disappeared, both rider and horse vanishing. The first rider also transformed into a whirlwind and left. Only then did Men Shirong realize they were revenge ghosts.

Tian Zhang, while serving as an official in Liangzhou, brought back a piece of the "Wannian Pine," which is warm in nature and invigorating blood circulation. When boiled, it turns a color like amber. It is effective for various female ailments such as blood deficiency and menstrual blockage. Relatives and friends passed it around, each asking for some, and eventually, it was all used up. Later, I saw the tree itself, which is actually the bark of an ancient pine tree, not a different species. The owner boils it as a substitute for tea, and it has a faint aroma. The largest one has its root in a deep ravine thousands of feet deep, with branches and trunk that rise straight up to the mountain ridge, still reaching twenty to thirty zhang in height. The bark is over two chi thick. My servant Wu Yubao once took a piece of it to make a bed. Bamboo or banana plants from Fujian and Guangdong have leaves that can accommodate one or two people lying down. If one more piece were used as a mat, it would also be a rare sight. I once saw a family that installed doors and windows in the hollow of the tree, using a ladder to go up and down. Entering it, it was like a house. I went up with Hu Yan, the governor of Huazhou, whose name is Hua Guo, from Chang'an, a jinshi in the year of Yiwèi (1825), and formerly the governor of Huazhou. Hu Yan said, "This family lives in a tree, combining the cave and the house." The area north of the Tianshan Mountains, where the Uyghur and Turkic peoples lived, did not need timber for construction, so the axe never touched them. It is likely that this is truly an ancient relic from the time of Pangu. The name "Wannian" (ten thousand years) is not an empty title.

Mr. Tian Baiyan said: The famous courtesan Yue Bin often visited the home of Yu Yang in the mountains, much like Su Dongpo's relationship with Qin Qiao. Su Dou Nan then recounted that when he was young, he met a courtesan in Shandong who claimed to be Yue Bin's granddaughter. She still had a fan given by Yu Yang, and I asked to see it. On the fan was painted a grass pavilion by the water, leaning against two willows. It was inscribed with "Dongchong wrote in the third month of Gengyin," but it is unknown who he is. On the left side, there was a poem written in running script, which said: "Misty threads blend with the green water, and slender waists compete in grace. At the wine table, I ask the old man of Xiangshan, how many stars has the willow constellation newly joined?" No name was signed, and a small seal was already blurred. Su Dou Nan thought it was a senior figure, who occasionally expressed idle sentiments, so he did not sign his name. I believe the poetic style is graceful and belongs to the Xin Cheng school. However, Yu Yang died in the summer of Xinmao, and Gengyin was the year before. At that time, he should not have had an old friend. The old man of Xiangshan must refer to whom? If it refers to himself, it should not say "I ask." Moreover, the words are light and playful, and do not resemble the style of an elderly writer. Perhaps it was in the Vimalakirti's room, where a celestial maiden left behind scattered flowers. Another young person may have written it on the fan, using this to tease her. The courtesan may have claimed to be a descendant of a famous person, but did not understand the meaning of the text, so she mistakenly identified herself as Yan Biao.

Wang Jingguang recounted that during the provincial examination in the year of Renwu, he and several friends rented a small house to study. In the room where Wang resided, the lamp light suddenly turned dark and blue in the middle of the night. After trimming and relighting it, he saw a head emerging from the ground, blowing on the lamp. He banged the table and scolded it, and it quickly retreated back in. After a short while, it emerged again, and upon being scolded, it retreated once more. This happened seven or eight times, almost until the fourth drum beat. Unable to bear the disturbance, and always priding himself on his courage, he did not want to call out to his fellow lodgers. Instead, he sat quietly to observe the change, only to find the entity opening its eyes and glaring angrily, never emerging from the ground. Realizing it could not do anything, he extinguished the light and finally slept. He did not know when it left, but from then on, he never saw it again. Wu Huishu remarked: "It must have been a wronged spirit wanting to make a complaint; I regret not asking once." I say that if it were indeed a wronged spirit, it should weep rather than glare angrily.

East of Lüliu Street, where the Powder House is located, there were many ancient burial grounds that residents gradually expanded upon. As they leveled the land and built houses, it was inevitable that some of the remains would be within the houses. The warmth and vitality of living people disturbed the spirits, making them restless, and thus strange phenomena appeared to drive them away. Initially, clapping hands and shouting showed no fear, so the spirits dared not show themselves. However, if they were seen and immediately shouted at, it indicated that there were still spirits present, and thus they would not completely leave. When the lights were extinguished and one went to sleep, the matter was entirely set aside, and the spirits knew they could not move the person, so they ceased their futile attempts to frighten. Su Dongpo wrote an essay about Cao Cao, which illustrates this very principle. As a child, I heard the notorious bandit Li Jinliang say: "When you arrive at someone's home at night, if you hear a cough in response to your presence, it shows fear, and you can attack. If they open the door and wait for you, it shows they are pretending to be brave, and you can still attack.
