If there is complete silence and no sound, it means you face a formidable opponent, and attacking them will likely result in failure seven or eight times out of ten. You should gauge your strength and act accordingly, which is also this principle."

The *Liezi* speaks of the dream of the banana and the deer, a truth that only Huangdi and Confucius could understand. Indeed, these words are profound. I was in the Western Regions, following a high-ranking official, Ba Gong, to inspect a military outpost. Ba Gong returned first, and I stayed behind due to unfinished business, sharing a room with the vice-general, Liang Jun. At the second watch of the night, an urgent dispatch arrived, and all the soldiers were sent out. I woke Liang from his sleep, instructing him to deliver it, promising to meet him halfway. Liang went about ten miles and, upon meeting the soldiers, returned immediately, resuming his deep sleep. The next day, he told me: "I dreamed I was sent to deliver an imperial decree, fearing I would miss the deadline, I whipped my horse into a mad gallop. Today, my thighs still ache, it's truly a remarkable event." To see what is real as a dream, even the servants and slaves smile.

I once composed a poem in Urumqi: "With a smile, I flicked the reins and the horse flew like the wind; I departed in a dream and returned in a dream, leaving no trace of my journey (as Su Dongpo wrote: 'Things are like spring dreams, leaving no trace'). Why question the truth of the banana and deer?" This is a record of that experience. There was also a case where a dream was mistaken for reality. My cousin, Zichen, told me about a person from Jinghai. After he went to bed, his wife was spinning in another house at night. Suddenly, he dreamed that his wife was being kidnapped by several people. He woke up in a panic, not realizing it was a dream, and immediately took a spear and went out to chase them. He ran more than ten miles and indeed saw several people in the wilderness carrying a woman, intending to commit a violent act. The woman's cries were deafening, and he was filled with anger and determination, fighting fiercely. The men were all injured and fled. He approached to console her, only to find out that she was a woman from a nearby village who had been abducted by bandits. They had known each other before, so he sent her back to her home. Confused and disoriented, he returned home, where his wife's spinning was not yet finished, and a lamp was still glowing faintly. This incident, it seems, was perhaps caused by the will of the spirits, and it cannot be simply dismissed as a dream.

Huang Junsheng of Jiahe reports that for those with broken bones, the use of the Kaiyuan Tongbao coin is recommended. This coin was cast at the beginning of the Tang Dynasty, with the inscription on its side written by Ouyang Xun. It has a slight crescent shape, which is a trace left by Empress Wende when she submitted a wax model, and thus was not altered. The characters on the coin should be read in a circular manner; the common misreading as "Kaiyuan Tongbao," attributed to Emperor Xuanzong, is a significant error. The coin should be burned and quenched in vinegar, then ground into a powder and consumed with wine. The copper powder will then form a ring around the broken bone, effectively binding the fracture. This treatment was tested on a chicken with a broken leg, which healed as if nothing had happened. After cooking the chicken and examining its bones, the copper ring was still intact, a phenomenon that defies explanation. How can the copper powder, which only enters the intestines, penetrate through membranes to reach the bones? Obtaining this coin can be difficult in a hurry. Later, I came across a reference in Zhang Zong's *Chao Ye Qian Zai* which states that a man from Dingzhou named Cui Wu broke his leg after falling off a horse. A doctor prescribed the copper powder mixed with wine, and he recovered. After his death, more than ten years later, during a reburial, the bones of his leg were examined, and the copper powder was still present. Thus, this ancient remedy only mentions copper powder, not specifically the Kaiyuan Tongbao coin.

Gathering gamblers, anciently referred to as "pocket families," as recorded in Li Zao's *Guzhigu Bu*, this practice has existed since the Tang dynasty. As for keeping courtesans and dividing the expenses for nocturnal entertainment, this was not a practice before the Ming dynasty. Households had their own courtesans, and government officials had theirs. After the official music and dance bureau was abolished, this custom became prevalent, becoming a source of profit for the wealthy and cunning, and a trap for the foolish and greedy. Although the law explicitly prohibits it, it cannot be eradicated. However, profit is always accompanied by danger, and greed ultimately harms oneself. I have seen those who engage in this business, with beautiful and alluring women close at home, and thus cannot prevent their descendants from all becoming like the carefree and drunken Ruan Ji. Both of his sons were corrupted by lust, spreading to the entire family, with lingering illnesses, leading to the extinction of the family line. The ghosts of the Ifao clan, ultimately starving to death.

A scholar named Li Mingru from Lin Qing said that a butcher from his village bought a cow. The cow knew it was destined for slaughter and refused to move forward when tied up. When whipped, it would dart sideways, exhausting its strength until it was forced to move forward. As the cow passed by a money shop, it suddenly knelt in front of the door, tears streaming down. The shop owner felt pity for it and asked about its price, learning it was eight thousand. He requested to buy it for the full amount, but the butcher hated its ferocity and strongly refused to sell, even offering additional interest, which was still refused. The butcher said, "This cow is so hateful; I must kill it with pleasure, even if it costs ten thousand coins." The cow heard these words, suddenly stood up on its own, and followed the butcher away. The butcher cooked its meat in a pot, then went to sleep. At dawn, he woke up and opened the pot, but his wife and children were surprised he didn't return. Suspecting something was wrong, they went to look and found that he had thrown himself into the pot, above the waist, tied up with the cow. All living beings fear death. Instead of feeling compassion for its fear, the butcher was angry at its fear. The cow's resentment and hatred were several times greater than usual. The fierce spirit of the cow, seeking revenge, would not delay. Indeed. My late uncle, Yi Nan Gong, once saw a butcher named Xu Xue leading a cow. The cow saw my uncle and knelt, not standing up. My uncle bought it and gave it to tenant farmer Zhang Cun, who raised it for several years. It plowed and pulled the plow, working twice as hard as other cows. Therefore, in matters of gratitude and resentment, even animals behave this way; how much more so should people reflect deeply?

A and B lived opposite each other across a wall, both being descendants of officials. Their wives were both renowned for their beauty, and the two men were as close as brothers, while their wives were also as close as sisters. Soon after, B died, and A's wife also passed away. A then tried every means to marry B's wife, which was widely criticized by scholars. On the day of the betrothal, there was a sound like drumming in the hall. On the night of the unveiling of the bride's face, the wind blew out the candle twice, and people knew it was the spirit of B. One day, on the anniversary of A's wife's death, A hung a portrait to worship, and suddenly a shadow of a person appeared beside the portrait, standing next to the woman, with his left hand resting on her shoulder from behind and his right hand playfully touching her cheek. The portrait also turned its gaze sideways, with a slight blush appearing. Upon close inspection, it was clearly like B. It seemed to be painted with light ink, with no trace of brush strokes, and it seemed to be faintly visible through the paper, yet the eyebrows, eyes, and clothing details were all clearly visible. Knowing it was a ghostly presence, A quickly tore it up and burned it. But it had already been seen by many and widely spread. Strange indeed, could the underworld be punishing his thin morality, making him pay for his actions in the afterlife, showing this transformation as a warning to those who betray their friends?

The Fourth Volume of *Huaixi Zazhi* from *Yuewei Caotang Biji* The instructor Lin Qingbiao said that in the past, while staying as a guest in Chong'an, he heard a tale of a scholar who lived near the foot of Wuyi Mountain. The scholar learned from tea pickers that on certain nights of the month, a rock would be filled with the sounds of singing and playing musical instruments, and when looked at from afar, they appeared to be celestial maidens. The scholar, being light-hearted and carefree, borrowed a mountain cottage to stay in. Whenever the moon rose, he would go there, but for several nights, he found nothing. The mountain cottage owner also spoke of this matter, saying that it only happened on the full moon, perhaps once or twice a year, and was not a common occurrence. The scholar pretended to be practicing quietness and waited for more than ten days. One night, he heard a faint sound as if there was something, so he stealthily followed the sound and hurriedly went to a hidden spot among the bushes. Indeed, he saw several celestial maidens who were all extraordinary. One maiden was about to play her flute when she suddenly saw a shadow and pointed her flute at it. The scholar immediately became stiff as if bound, but his ears and eyes could still see and hear. Soon, a clear sound penetrated the clouds, and a melodious voice stirred the soul. He could not help but praise himself, saying, "Although I am under restraint, the beautiful music and charming demeanor have already been enjoyed."

The words were not yet finished when suddenly a handkerchief flew and covered his head. He then fell into a dreamlike state, neither hearing nor seeing, half-asleep and half-awake, confused for a few moments, gradually seeming to revive. The women scolded the maids and dragged him out, shouting, "You foolish boy, you are out of line! Are you peeping at the heavenly flowers?" They ordered the breaking of a tall bamboo to carry out a beating. The scholar painfully explained himself, saying he was fond of music and hoped to eavesdrop on the celestial music, like Li Bai standing by the palace wall, really not having any other intentions, hoping to see the colorful phoenix's tent. One woman smiled slightly and said, "I pity your sincerity. There is a little maid who can play the transverse flute. I will give her to you as a gift." The scholar prostrated himself and thanked her, but when he raised his head, she had already vanished. Looking back at the maid, she had a broad forehead, large eyes, short hair, and a disheveled appearance, with a prominent waist and abdomen, breathing heavily. He was shocked and annoyed, trying to avoid and run away, but the maid insisted on leading him into intimacy, holding him tightly and wouldn't let go. He angrily struck her, and she fell to the ground, transforming into a pig and squealing away. The music at the rock ceased from then on. Considering this maid, she is probably an evil spirit rather than a fairy. Some say, "Perhaps the fairy transformed into a pig to play a trick on him." If that is the case.

Liu Xiefu said, there was a scholar, aged sixteen or seventeen, who was intelligent, handsome, and graceful, seemingly of the upper class, and greatly hoped for success. One day, he suddenly fell into delirious ramblings, as if he had seen ghosts and spirits. When he woke up, he recounted his experience: "I was watching plays in the society of Jingcheng, and without realizing it was late at night, I continued on my way home. On the way, I passed by a house and asked for a drink. There was only a young woman there, who gave me water and invited me to sit down. She told me that her husband was out on official business and would return the next day. She flashed her eyes and sent glances, as if wanting to engage with me. I loved her gentle beauty and we engaged in intimate relations. When I left, she cried and asked me not to come back again. She gave me two bracelets as a gift. The next day, upon seeing them, they were covered with greenish-blue patina, slightly silver-colored, and seemed to be from the ground for many years. I knew in my heart it was a ghost, but I could not forget the memory. Yesterday, I went back to that place, wandered and looked around, and suddenly there was a man with a black face and long beard, who hit my cheek with his hand. I stumbled and ran back home, and he also followed me there. From then on, I saw him frequently, and he scolded me fiercely. I would suddenly fall asleep and wake up, and did not know anything else. My parents went to the grave to set up an offering and buried the bracelets. Soon, his son angrily called out, 'My wife lost the bracelets, suspected there was another reason, but could not find the culprit's name. Only suspended and whipped five hundred times, sold to a distant place. Now I see you stealing them, now I know it was you who lured her. What kind of thing is this, can it be compensated with food, drink, and money?' He had epilepsy for over a month and eventually did not recover. Thus, even breaking through walls, even in the underworld, there are still calamities."

Li Yun recounted that in Dongguang there was a fox hunter who, whenever he went hunting, carried fire and nets, moving back and forth between villages and tombs. One night, while lying in wait, he saw a man wearing a square headpiece and a loose robe emerging from the top of a tomb. The man let out a long, ghostly howl—*lingling* (a sound like *xū*), as noted in the *Shuowen Jiezi*—and at once a group of foxes gathered from all directions, surrounding the dense undergrowth, howling menacingly and crying out in unison to capture this evil man and cook him into dried meat. The fox hunter had no way to escape and so climbed up a tall tree. The man in the square headpiece then directed the foxes, commanding them to saw the tree down. Immediately, the sound of sawing echoed loudly. The fox hunter, in desperation, bowed and cried out, saying, "If you would be so kind as to release me, I swear I will never set foot here again." The foxes did not respond, and the sound of the sawing grew even louder. He cried out three times in this way, and then the man in the square headpiece said, "If that is indeed your intention, then take an oath." After the oath was made, both the ghost and the fox vanished completely. This ghost and this fox may both be said to have handled the matter skillfully.

Continuing harassment, the situation forces one to take desperate measures, fight a decisive battle, using the combined strength of a ragtag group, originally it was not difficult to kill one person. But killing one person is easy, but killing one person and thereby provoking the anger of many, they will not stop until they destroy the nests and plow the holes. To merely make them fear and then let them go, temporarily seek peace, thereby future troubles will cease. Those with power do not exhaust their strength, thereby maintaining their authority; those who subdue others make it easy for them to submit, thereby achieving submission. In the Battle of Zhaoling, not holding them accountable for usurping the title of king, but holding them accountable for failing to provide tribute, making it easy for them to submit. When Qu Wan came to form an alliance and then withdrew the troops, not exhausting their strength, thereby maintaining their authority. Scholars who interpret the *Spring and Autumn Annals* debate whether Duke Huan of Qi achieved a small victory, the natural fortifications of Fangcheng and the Han River, who knows if one battle could be won. If one battle is not won, can anything else be done in the world. The Battle of Fuli in Huai West, I have verified this in historical records.

My cousin, Ji Xian, once stayed at a friend's house inside the Guangning Gate. One night, there was a fierce storm with heavy rain and strong winds. A bolt of lightning passed through the roof ridge—near the ridge of the roof, which is called "wushan" due to its resemblance to a mountain. This term is often used in poetry, as seen in the works of Fan Chengda. The lightning flashed like a sudden burst of light, causing the walls and beams to shake violently. The next day, they found two small holes in the east and west walls, about the size of a coin, as if the thunder god had driven the lightning through the wall to chase away evil spirits. Lightning that strikes people typically comes from the sky, while lightning that strikes strange phenomena often flies horizontally, as if it is pursuing and escaping from these phenomena. Ordinary lightning, on the other hand, is caused by the accumulation of earth's energy, which rises and manifests as thunderstorms. I once saw lightning emerging from the ground in Funing while crossing a mountain. It appeared like smoke rising into the sky, reaching halfway up before exploding with a loud sound, similar to the firing of cannons or guns. However, such phenomena are usually observed in uninhabited areas and not in populated ones. Some say that the benevolent heart of heaven does not want to harm those who are touched by lightning, but this is not necessarily true. Humans are the center of the three components of the universe (heaven, earth, and humanity). When people gather in large numbers, the energy of heaven and earth flows freely, preventing the accumulation of energy that causes thunderstorms. Therefore, it is rare to see lightning in populated areas. In contrast, in the harsh, cold regions beyond the frontier, as people engage in farming and pastoral activities, the land gradually becomes more habitable, and the energy of the earth begins to rise, leading to the occurrence of thunderstorms.

Wang Yuefang said, "There is a knife in my family, a relic of the former Grand Judge. Whenever there is a theft alert at night, it makes a loud, explosive sound and protrudes from its scabbard by one or two inches. Thunder chases evil spirits through the house, and the knife falls to the ground; from then on, it does not make any more sounds. It is commonly believed that swords and knives that have been stained with human blood will always make a sound when there is an alert, but this is not entirely true. Only those that have killed many people will do so. Each time it kills one person, there are two marks on the blade, which cannot be removed even after polishing. When I was young, at the home of Yangwei General Ha Gongyuan from Hejian, I saw one of his swords that also made a sound at night. Upon testing, it was indeed the case. Some say that the reason the knife makes a sound is because a ghost possesses it, but this is not true. Swords used in battle often kill hundreds of people, so how could there be hundreds of ghosts guarding one sword? After absorbing much blood, it has a strong spiritual essence, attracting evil spirits. When evil spirits are present, the knife reacts and makes a sound on its own, much like how a musical instrument responds when played. When struck by the intense heat of lightning, all evil spirits are destroyed, and the knife returns to being an ordinary piece of iron. It is not that the thunder gods specifically target this object."

I have often lamented the destruction of Han dynasty paintings in the Western Regions by smoke and coal, and I slightly doubted how one or two thousand years of inscriptions could still exist. My cousin Yu Dun said: "Red and black ink on stone, if it is not exposed to rain and wind, and if moss does not grow, then it can be preserved for a long time." There is a village called Shensheng in the area where Yizhou and Mancheng are adjacent. The Great River comes from the north and then turns southeast, with two peaks facing each other across the river. It is said that these peaks were formed by falling stars, hence the village's name. The peaks are wide at the top and narrow at the bottom, like clouds emerging from the ground, steep and dangerous with no path. Those who are fond of climbing tread on its holes and crevices, reaching the waist of the mountain, where there are many inscriptions by former people. The oldest ones include those from the Northern Wei and Five Dynasties periods, all of which are still clearly recognizable. Therefore, it is not surprising that Han dynasty paintings in the cave are still preserved today. Unfortunately, Yu Dun did not have time to record all the names. Yizhou and Mancheng are nearby, and one should visit the locals to inquire about them. Yu Dun also said that north of the fallen star stone, there is a fishing weir. The local people have long enjoyed its benefits, offering special sacrifices to the weir god every year. Someone once taught them a method to poison fish using *wán huā* (a type of herb) in the upper stream. Fish and shrimp in the lower stream would die and float up, yielding ten times more than nets. It was found to be very effective. They formed a cluster of firewood in the upper stream and applied this method daily. One day at noon, black clouds suddenly surged out from the dragon pond, with violent wind and sudden rain, lightning and fire blazing, burning their huts to ashes. The people were afraid and stopped. Farming and fishing methods, originating from Paoxi, still require the principle of not overfishing, with benevolent governance present. Cutting off the flow and fishing is still disapproved by sages, how much more so to be cruel and wasteful, gathering the clan and killing them. It is appropriate to anger the gods.

Zhou Shuchang said: Once I traveled to Quehua, where I stayed overnight in a peasant's house. Outside the window, old trees densely shaded the area, reaching directly to the top of the hill. The host said that sometimes ghostly voices could be heard, but it was impossible to understand what they were saying. That night was moonless, and indeed, I faintly heard them, though not very clearly. Fearing that they would be scared away, I quietly opened the window and crept out, crawling through the grass. Gradually approaching, I eavesdropped, and they were discussing the writings of Han Yu, Liu Zongyuan, Ouyang Xiu, and Su Shi, each pointing out the excellent parts. One person said: "Such writing is the middle tone, why must the Front and Back Seven Sons reject or not count it, and insist on speaking of the Qin and Han periods, thus sparking disputes over schools or factions." Another person said: "The gradual change from substance to style originally not one path. The literary style of the late Song period was vulgar, and the literary style of the late Yuan period was overly ornate. Therefore, the gentlemen of the Song Jielin period strived to emulate Han Yu and Ouyang Xiu, saving it with majestic and dignified style. After the Three Yangs, it became the style of the imperial court, gradually becoming superficial and empty. Therefore, the gentlemen of Li Kongtong again strived to emulate the Qin and Han periods, saving it with wonderful, grand, and splendid style."

After the reigns of Emperor Longqing and Emperor Wanli, literature degenerated into false forms, leading to the Changsha school turning against it. Generally, those who could stand out and form their own schools had solid foundations initially, which allowed their legacies to be passed on. However, each school also had its own flaws, leading to mutual criticism. Yet, despite the different writing styles of Dong Jiguo and Sima Xiangru, they did not attack each other during their lifetimes. Similarly, Li Bai, Du Fu, Wang Wei, and Meng Haoran had distinct poetic styles but also did not criticize each other. They had gained deeply in their craft. Later scholars, however, praised what was sweet and criticized what was bitter, accepting red but rejecting white, showing a shallow understanding. As I was speaking, I suddenly coughed, and the conversation fell silent, leaving me regretful that I did not hear the rest of his explanation. I said: "This is similar to the story recorded by Li Ciwan about Yishan, both using impartial opinions and entrusting them to the words of ghosts and spirits, making no need for a punchline." Shuchang said with a slight anger: "I have no virtue at all, but I cannot speak falsely. If you do not believe me, I也不敢坚持争论."

Dong Qujiang said, there was a scholar who often engaged in scholarly pursuits and was outwardly respectful and cautious, without any apparent faults. However, he was too proud and haughty, often blaming others with unreasonable arguments. A friend of his completed the mourning period in May and intended to take a concubine in July. This scholar wrote a letter to him, saying: "Before the full mourning period of three years, you took a concubine. I know that you had long harbored the intention to do so. According to the *Spring and Autumn Annals*, even though Duke Wen of Lu married during the mourning period, it was still considered a violation of mourning rites. As a friend, it is my duty to correct your mistake, and I dare not remain silent. What should I say to you?" His arguments were generally of this nature. One day, his wife returned home for a visit and was supposed to return on a certain day, but she came back a day early. He was surprised and asked her why. She replied, "I mistakenly thought the month was shorter, so I did not think it was a big deal." The next day, another woman arrived, and he was greatly shocked. When he searched for his wife, she had disappeared. From that day on, he gradually became emaciated and eventually fell ill with tuberculosis.

A fox spirit, in the guise of a woman, drained his vital essence, and the amount drained in one night was already considerable. The former concubine, upon hearing of this, also replied in a letter saying: "The act of husband and wife being intimate cannot be called improper, and the fox spirit assuming a form is also not something one could have anticipated. However, draining a great deal of true essence in one night would not have happened without unrestrained indulgence. Is there still something lacking in moderation and propriety in our intimate relations? Moreover, evil cannot overcome virtue, as an ancient teaching. The Neo-Confucian scholars Zhou, Zhang, Cheng, and Zhu were never known to have encountered a fox spirit, yet this spirit openly violated the respect due to a teacher. Is there still something lacking in the virtue of the teacher? The teacher is a virtuous person, and holding a virtuous person to high standards is the way of the *Spring and Autumn Annals*. The duty of friends to correct each other's faults, I dare not fail to inform you. What do you have to teach me?" The person who received the letter strongly argued that there was no such thing, and it was just fabricated by villagers. Mr. Song Qingyuan, upon hearing this, said: "This is like using your own spear to pierce your own shield."

Governor Yuan Yugu, whose given name was Shou Dong, was from Changshan and held the position of Governor-General of Zhili. He was posthumously given the title of Qing Que. When we were young, we were classmates and also related by marriage. He once said that when he was three or four years old, he could still vaguely remember his past life, but by the time he was five or six, his memories became hazy and unclear. Now, he only remembers being a juren (a scholar who passed the provincial examination), living not far from Changshan, but he has completely forgotten his name, place of origin, family background, and other details. When I was four or five years old, I could see things at night just as clearly as during the day. However, after I turned seven or eight, my vision gradually became dim, and by the time I was ten, I could no longer see anything at all. Sometimes, in the middle of the night, I would wake up and, by chance, be able to see again for a moment, but it would quickly return to normal. Since I was sixteen or seventeen, until now, I might see something once every one or two years, like a flash of lightning or a spark, disappearing in the blink of an eye. This is because as desires increase, the clarity of the spirit diminishes.

A Farmer from Jingzhou and a Spirit's Dilemma Li Xiyi of Jingzhou recounts that one of his family's tenant farmers was the boldest. He cultivated a small plot of land near a cluster of graves, where he always guarded the crops himself during the ripening season, sleeping alone in a thatched hut. Even when he occasionally heard strange sounds, he remained calm and fearless. One night, he heard the noisy chatter of ghosts, as if they were quarreling. He went outside to see and found two ghosts fighting on the grave mound, with a female ghost standing blankly nearby. He called out to ask what was happening. One ghost said, "Your arrival is most welcome. We have a matter we ask you to judge fairly. Is there anyone in the world who would be with their original husband while courting their fiancée?" The other ghost spoke similarly. The farmer called out to the female ghost, "Who exactly did you agree to marry?" The female ghost blushed for a long time before saying, "I was originally a courtesan. In the custom of courtesans, those who pay more are secretly arranged to marry. Now in the afterlife, I still practice the old ways, but I cannot remember all the names. I dare not say who has an agreement, nor dare say who does not." The farmer laughed and spat, "Where did you find these two fools?" When he looked up, the three ghosts had all disappeared. As a child, he heard his great-granduncle, Mr. Chen, whose courtesy name was Ying Sun—after many years, his titles and names have been forgotten. He was the younger brother of Duke De, the jiazi year's jinshi (imperial scholar), and the ancestor of Xiu Ting, the magistrate of Xianju. He spoke about a matter related to marriage: among his relatives, there was someone who remarried after the death of their husband. The spirit attached itself to an ill maid and spoke, "I asked you before, and you said you would not remarry. How could you betray me now?" The woman, however, was not afraid and calmly replied, "Is there anyone in the world who, while their husband is still alive, would say they will definitely remarry?" The spirit's question was previously muddled, so how can I be blamed for answering like this? These two stories can illuminate each other.

A scholar engaged in philosophical debate argued that ghosts do not exist. The crowd challenged him, saying: "Now that it is the height of summer, can you sleep alone in a graveyard for one night to cool off?" The scholar resolutely went, and indeed saw nothing, returning feeling even more self-satisfied, saying: "How could Zhu Xi deceive me?" I replied: "Traveling a long distance with heavy baggage without encountering bandits on the road does not mean there are no bandits on the road; hunting all day without meeting any wild animals in the wild does not mean there are no wild animals in the wild. Concluding that there are no ghosts in one place and therefore no ghosts in the world; concluding that there are no ghosts in one night and therefore no ghosts in all time, this is taking one example and discarding a hundred. Moreover, the argument against the existence of ghosts was first made by Ruan Zhan, not Zhu Xi. Zhu Xi merely considered the movement of the soul and spirit as a natural phenomenon, and all supernatural phenomena as not natural, but he did not explicitly deny their existence. Therefore, Jin Quwei's record states: The Cheng brothers initially did not say there are no ghosts and spirits, but they did not accept the ghosts and spirits as understood by the common people today."

Yang Daofu recorded: Rain, wind, dew, and thunder; the sun, moon, day, and night — these are the traces of ghosts and spirits. These are the spirits of fairness and righteousness that appear in daylight. But as for those that howl from the beams or touch the chest, these are the unjust, evil, and obscure spirits that are sometimes present, sometimes absent; sometimes coming, sometimes going; sometimes gathering, sometimes scattering. There are also those spirits that respond to prayer and grant requests — these too are called ghosts and spirits, and they are governed by the same principle. Bao Yang recorded: The principles of the birth and death of ghosts and spirits are certainly not as the Buddhist tradition claims, nor as the common people see them. Yet there are also cases that are clearly visible, yet cannot be explained by reason — and there is no need to concern oneself with them. He also said: Nanxuan merely stubbornly disbelieves, but things like the spirits of the Yudi (the cauldron of Yu) and the chimei wangliang (supernatural beings) are indeed real. Deep mountains and vast lakes are where they dwell; if people go there and occupy them, would it not bring calamity? In Yuzhang, there was a Daoist practitioner named Liu Dao Ren, who lived on the summit of a mountain and built a hermitage. One day, a number of lizards entered the耒 (a farming tool), and drank all the water in the hermitage. Soon after, hail began to pile up outside the hermitage, and the next day, hail fell in the valley below. There was also an Uncle Liu, a man of great integrity who did not speak falsely. He said that as he passed a mountain pass, he heard a sound in the forest by the stream — it was countless lizards, each carrying an object like crystal, moving away. Before he had gone a few miles, hail began to fall.

This principle remains unclear. Once, in a certain county, there was a large Buddha made of clay; one region revered and believed in it. Later, a disrespectful scion of a family severed its head, and the people gathered to mourn it. Then, relics emerged from the neck of the Buddha made of clay and wood. How could clay and wood contain such things? It was merely the result of the people's hearts. Wu Bidai recorded: "Discussing the case of seeing a ghost in the household of Xue Shilong, it was said that those who believe in ghosts and spirits all claim they are real and exist in the world. Those who disbelieve them firmly say there are no ghosts. Yet there are also those who truly see them. Zheng Jingwang therefore took Xue's sighting as real, not knowing that this was merely a phenomenon like a rainbow." Asked whether a rainbow is only air, or if it has form and substance, the reply was: "If it can drink water, it must also have intestines, but as soon as it disperses, it is gone. The thunder gods are of this same kind." Lin Ci recorded: "There are many people in the world who see ghosts, but we do not know whether they exist or not." "Many people in the world have seen them, so how can one say they do not exist? But it is not a correct principle."

Bao You became a ghost, and according to Yi Chuan, there is a different principle involved. This is because his vital energy had not fully dissipated, but he died prematurely, leaving his soul and spirit with no place to return, and thus he remained as such. Once, someone was traveling at night along the Huai River and saw countless shapes, resembling humans but not humans, appearing and disappearing between two bodies of water. This person knew they were ghosts, and despite this, had no choice but to pass through. When he inquired about this place, it turned out to be an ancient battlefield. Those who died there did not meet a natural end, harboring resentment and hatred, and thus their spirits did not disperse. Someone in the audience mentioned that in the countryside, there was a man named Li San who, after dying, became a ghost. Whenever there were local festivals or religious ceremonies, a portion of the offerings was always set aside for him. Later, someone set off firecrackers and burned the tree he relied on, and since then, he has disappeared. It was said: "He died unjustly, his energy had not dissipated, and was scattered by the firecrackers." Shen Xian recorded that when a person dies without accepting their fate, their energy does not disperse after death, and they become ghosts or demons, such as those who die violently or monks and Taoists who do not disperse after death. They focus on nurturing their spirit, which is why it remains concentrated. Wan Renjie recorded that when a person dies and their energy disperses, leaving no trace, this is the normal course of things. Those who reincarnate are those who accidentally gathered their energy and did not disperse, and they are reborn by coinciding with the vital energy.

**Leaf Hesun's Record:** In the case of a woman killing her husband in Tan Prefecture, after she secretly buried him, he caused disturbances. When the matter was discovered, it did not cause disturbances at that time. From this, we can see that in the realm of criminal justice, if such cases are not punished with a verdict, the wrongs of the deceased will not be resolved. **Li Zhuangzu's Record:** Someone asked about gods who are worshipped and fed in temples, lasting for several centuries, what is the reason for this? It was said: Over time, they also disperse. When I was governor of Nankang, during a long drought, I could not avoid praying to the gods everywhere. Suddenly, I came to a temple, and there were only three dilapidated rooms, in a very disordered state. The people there said that three or five decades ago, their spirits responded immediately. When someone came, the deity in the curtain would speak to them. Their spirit was like that then, and it is like this now, which can also be seen.

Leaf Hesun recorded: "Discussing matters concerning ghosts and deities, it is said that at the temple of the Second Son of Li Bing in Guankou, Sichuan, this temple was established by Li Bing after he opened the Li River levee. Now, many miraculous phenomena are observed, which are believed to be the manifestation of his second son. Initially, he was enfeoffed as a king. Later, during the reign of Emperor Huizong, who was fond of Daoism, he was enfeoffed as a True Lord. Zhang Wei, a military leader, prayed at the temple. That night, he dreamed that the god spoke: 'I was previously enfeoffed as a king and received offerings of meat, which allowed me to exercise power and bestow blessings. Now I am called a True Lord, although respected, people offer vegetarian sacrifices to me, without the nourishment of meat offerings, so I have no power or blessing. Now I must be re-enfeoffed as a king, then I will have power and blessings.' Thus, Zhang Wei requested the restoration of his title. It is unknown whether Zhang Wei had this dream or if it was a pretext for using military force. The Zitong deity is extremely powerful. These two deities seem to dominate the two provinces. Generally, deities that receive offerings of living things are made powerful by the vitality of these offerings. Ancient people sacrificed to bronze vessels and tortoise shells for this reason. Han Qing said that Li Tong said: 'Someone shot a tiger and saw several people following the tiger, who were people killed by the tiger. Their life force had not yet dissipated, so they formed this shape.'"

Huang Yigang recorded: When discussing the matter of invoking the spirit of Zigu to compose poetry, he said: There are cases where the spirit appears in its true form, and a young girl from the family sees it, not knowing what it is. For example, in Quzhou, there is a person who serves a deity. They only open the list of matters written on paper and seal it in front of the shrine. After a short while, when the paper is opened, there are answers already written on it. It is unknown how this works. All these sayings are recorded in detail in Li Jingde's Compilation of Sayings. Why does the master slander Zhu Xi? This old man read the book for a long time and sighed, saying: Does Zhu Xi still have this book? He left with a feeling of pity. However, I still have some doubts about Zhu Xi's main idea, which is that people are born by inheriting the qi of heaven and earth, and when they die, their qi returns to heaven and earth. As recorded by Ye Hesun, it is like a fish in water, where the water outside is the same as the water inside, and the water inside a mandarin fish is the same as the water inside a carp. The principle is profound. But there is no way to avoid the principle of sacrifice, which is established by the sage and recorded in the classics. Thus, it cannot be avoided to say that descendants are connected by the same qi, they gather again and receive sacrifice, and after the sacrifice is completed, they disperse into the void.

After this qi disperses and returns, does it mix with the primordial qi to become one, or is it mixed within the primordial qi? If it mixes to become one, it is like many rivers flowing into the sea, becoming one body of water, and cannot make the Yangtze, Huai, Yellow, and Milky Way rivers gather separately again; like the five flavors blending into a single taste, it cannot make ginger, salt, vinegar, and soy sauce gather separately again. How could one extract specific qi from such a mixture to communicate with descendants? If it is mixed within the primordial qi, it is like dust flying apart, unknown how it divides into countless places, or like threads flying randomly, unknown how far apart they are. When descendants offer sacrifices, it reunites in small, scattered parts, which seems far from reason. Even if it can be gathered, if this qi is without consciousness, how could it respond to offerings or enjoy them? If this qi has consciousness, where does its consciousness originate? It must have a mind. Where does the mind attach? It must have a body. If it already has a body, then it is still a ghost. Before it gathers, these countless tiny particles, each with its own consciousness, are not just one ghost. Buddhist ghosts are hidden underground, while Confucian ghosts rotate in the air; Buddhist ghosts are always present, while Confucian ghosts are temporarily assembled. How could one surpass the other? This is truly beyond the knowledge of the unlearned.

A certain captain of the Thousand in Urumqi was afflicted with a cold illness. A Taoist priest came to his door seeking to treat him, claiming they had a past-life connection and had come specifically to save him. At the time, a skilled healer named Gao, who was an exile's wife, saw the prescription and was shocked, saying, "If one takes cinnamon twig, it will lead to death in a case of excessive yang. The medicine is contrary to the illness; how can it be tried lightly?" She strongly opposed it. The Taoist priest sighed and said, "It is fate." He put on his clothes and left. However, Gao's wife used the *Chengqi Decoction* and the man was cured, leading them to consider the Taoist priest mistaken. After I returned home, I happened to read the official gazette and suddenly saw that the man had been executed for embezzling military grain. I then realized that the Taoist priest was not an ordinary man and had intended to kill him with the wrong medicine, thus preserving his reputation. This is similar to an old account of a clerk in the Ministry of War. Is it not that misfortune is self-created, beyond the reach of human intelligence to reverse?

Yao An Gong said, "Strange devices and miraculous traces in a household are ultimately not good things." He then mentioned that in the home of Mr. Mou Rong, a fellow examinee born in the year of the White Rabbit (癸巳), there was a stone inkstone naturally shaped like a goose egg, with a deep purple color. It had one eye shaped like a magpie's, as big as a bean, protruding from the center of the ink pool. The eye had spiral patterns clearly defined, and the pupils were bright and spirited. When touched, it felt smooth and did not stick to the hand; when struck, it was as hard as gold or iron. When breathed on, water appeared like dewdrops, and when ink was applied, it made no sound. After a few grindings, it produced thick ink. There were no inscriptions or marks, as if they loved its natural form and did not want to carve it. The box was also made of purple sandalwood, carved with roots that moved smoothly, and wrapped without any gaps, making no sound when shaken. On the back, there were three characters "紫桃轩," as small as beans, indicating that it was the property of Li Taifu Rihua. Li Taifu had a collection of writings called "Zitaoxuan Zazhu," and among all the Song dynasty inkstones he had seen in his life, this was the best. Later, he cherished this inkstone so much that he offended his superiors, nearly facing unforeseen danger, and in a fit of anger, he smashed it. When disaster was about to strike, at night he heard the inkstone as if it were groaning.

When I was in Ürümqi, the city defense commander, Officer Zhu, presented me with some new mushrooms. Officer Xu, the garrison commander, then said that when he was not yet in a high position, he once saw a seller of new mushrooms who wanted to buy them. An elderly man was nearby and rebuked the seller, saying, "He still has several more official posts ahead of him; how dare you?" The seller left hesitantly. This elderly man was a stranger to me, and soon afterward, I did not know where he went. The next day, I heard that there was a death from eating mushrooms in the neighborhood. I suspected that the elderly man might have been a local deity, and the seller was never seen again, leading me to suspect that it was a ghost seeking a substitute. The *Lüshi Chunqiu* states that the most delicious flavors come from mushrooms from Yue and Luo, which are originally not poisonous, but their poison is due to the presence of snakes and vipers. Those who consume them may laugh uncontrollably. Chen Yuren's *Mushroom Spectrum* describes a method of using water, bitter tea, and alum to detoxify. Both the *Bamboo Annals* by Zhang Hua and the *Compendium of Famous Physicians* by Tao Hongjing describe the use of earth water to detoxify. This is why earth water is effective. To prepare earth water, mix yellow clay with water, let it settle, and drink it.

A guest of a prominent household, who often stayed in a separate building with three rooms on the side of the main hall, began to dream every night of naked men and women chasing each other, with cosmetics and perfumes scattered around, surrounded by various indecent scenes. At first, he found it pleasurable, but after a while, the dream occurred every night, leading him to suspect it was a mental illness. However, when he moved to another room, he did not have the dream. He then suspected it was a ghost, but there was no sign of any supernatural activity before he went to sleep, and he saw nothing even when he held a candle until dawn. In the dream, the people were also engaging in intimate play, as if they did not notice anyone else around, which made it seem unlikely to be a ghost. He could not figure out the cause. One day, he suddenly realized that the bookshelf in the room contained several carved ivory and stone figures depicting lewd scenes, as well as numerous erotic books and scrolls. He concluded that these objects must be causing the haunting and secretly informed the owner, who burned all of them. Someone who knew about the incident said: "These objects cannot cause a haunting. This is the place where the owner gathers singers and selects courtesans. The spirits of lust are attracted by the energy here, and this man is also a frequent patron of brothels, so his spirit is drawn to such dreams. Just as decayed water breeds small creatures, and sour wine attracts flies, this is a natural phenomenon. There are many such objects sold in the market, but they do not all cause hauntings. Many people have stayed in this room, but not all of them had the dream. You should consider the root cause. Burning these objects will not help. Perhaps the family is declining? Within ten years, the house changed owners."

Venerable Sir Su Zhai, once the magistrate of Xian County, was a capable official. During his tenure as the governor of Taiping Prefecture, there was a case in doubt, and he changed his clothes to conduct a secret investigation. He chanced to rest in a small temple, where an elderly monk, over eighty years old, saw him and clasped his hands, standing respectfully. The monk called his disciple to prepare tea, and the disciple responded from afar: "The governor is about to arrive. Please lead the guest to sit temporarily in another room." The monk replied: "The governor has already arrived. Please come quickly." The official was greatly startled and asked, "How do you know I have come?" The monk answered, "You are the master of the entire prefecture. Every move you make is known throughout the country, how could it be only the old monk?" The official then asked, "How do you recognize me?" The monk said, "The governor cannot recognize every person in the prefecture, but every person in the prefecture recognizes the governor." The official asked, "How do you know what business I am on?" The monk replied, "The matter of a certain case has been spread by the parties involved for a long time. They all pretend not to recognize you." The official was disheartened and asked, "Why do you not pretend not to recognize me?" The monk fell to the ground and prostrated himself, saying, "Forgive me, forgive me, I wanted you to ask this question." The official, though no less capable than Gong and Huang, had a slight dissatisfaction among the people due to his fondness for investigations. This not only allowed cunning and corrupt officials to plan schemes of deception but also, among the common people of the countryside, who does not have relatives or friends, who does not have grievances or grudges? If you investigate the party of A, then A will seem righteous and B will seem wrong; if you investigate the party of B, then A will seem wrong and B will seem righteous; if you investigate those with whom you have a grudge, then those with whom you have a grudge will surely seem wrong.

Inquiring of those who have received favors, those who have received favors will be straightforward. As for women and children, their information may not be true; sick old women and feeble old men, whose speech is confused, can such information be relied upon as a verdict? Even if the official personally inquires, it is still like this; to rely again on others' ears and eyes, is there any hope? Moreover, the harm caused by inquiring is not limited to hearing cases; the benefits and drawbacks in the common people's lives, inquiring can also be harmful, especially concerning river channels and embankments. Common people each protect their own families; if water is beneficial, they block it to enrich themselves; if water is a hazard, they divert it to neighboring countries, this is their winning strategy. Who would consider the overall layout of the terrain, to plan for eternal calm? The old monk is an outsider to the world, should not interfere in worldly matters, let alone matters of the government. Buddhist compassion, to sacrifice oneself to help others; if it benefits things, should certainly speak boldly even at the cost of one's life. Only you should consider this. The official deeply thought about his words, finally returned without inquiring. The next day, he sent a servant to deliver money and rice, returned to report to the official, saying: "After the official returned, the monk told his disciples: 'My heart's task is complete, I passed away peacefully.'" This matter was mentioned by Yang Jun of Wenchuan. Yao An said: "In all cases of criminal matters, one should investigate with an open mind, truth and falsehood will become clear; trusting others or oneself is not the right approach. The problem with trusting others, the monk's words are correct; the problem with trusting oneself, there are also many things that cannot be fully described. How can we find another old monk, to also speak the Dharma?"

Uncle Jian Ting Zhang said that when he was reading at the Wild Cloud Pavilion, a group of classmates gathered at Tong's garden to perform the traditional purification ritual of *xu*. Accidentally, they used a divination method to summon a spirit, and collectively asked for its name. The divining board wrote: "Accidentally carrying a female companion, walking leisurely, Why bother asking a poet for their name? Do you remember the night of the bright moon on the Jade Terrace, Someone scolded and called Xu Feiqiong." They asked again for poetry to descend from the altar, and the divining board wrote: "Three-sided silk windows facing the water, Tong's garden is still the old pavilion, The east wind blows green grass on the pond, I have returned to the human world again." The crowd secretly discussed the sad and gentle poetry, fearing it might be the soul of a talented woman. But there are no such women nearby, perhaps it is a fairy who has refined her form and worshipped the moon. The crowd was confused and agitated, some stood thinking deeply, others lightly teased and communicated. Suddenly, the divining board wrote in large letters: "An old man withered, his hair full of white snow, Applying powder and perfume, watching the young, Accidentally sending the young men into foolish dreams, Pitifully, they truly worshipped the small fairy." Then it wrote the character for "laugh" in large letters and left. This is unknown which generation's poetic spirit, engaged in such playful behavior, it seems to be a light and frivolous intention, there was something to summon it.

Mr. Hu Hou'an recounted that there was a scholar who befriended a fox woman. Upon their first meeting, she gave him a gourd about two inches long and instructed him to wear it on his belt, while she herself entered it. Whenever he wished to meet her, he would pull out the stopper, and she would then emerge in a tender and affectionate manner; when she left, she would return to the gourd and plug it up again. One day, while he was walking in the market, the gourd was cut off by a thief. From then on, they never met again, and he always felt deeply disappointed. Once, he went for a walk outside the city to relieve his inner frustration and heard someone calling out from among the thick foliage. The voice was that of the fox woman. He went over to talk to her, but she hid and refused to come out, saying, "I have already transformed and can no longer meet you." He asked her in surprise why, and she wept and explained, "Collecting essence to refine one's form is the usual practice for foxes. Recently, an unknown Taoist from somewhere has been searching for us to provide for his collection of essence. If caught, we are bound by a divine incantation and become stiff like wooden dolls, at his mercy. If one has slightly stronger spiritual power and cannot expel it, then we are steamed and made into dried meat. Our flesh and blood are consumed, and our vital energy is also collected."

I entered the gourd to avoid this trouble, but unexpectedly, I was still sought out and forcibly taken back home. I fear punishment as severe as boiling in oil, so I have already offered her the elixir, fortunately sparing my life. However, after losing the elixir, I returned to my beastly form. From now on, I must refine my form for another two or three hundred years before I can transform again. Until the heavens grow old and the earth decays, we will not meet again. Grateful for your past kindness, I call upon you for one final parting word. Strive to live well, and do not miss me again. The man was filled with anger and said, "Why not appeal to the gods?" She replied, "Many have appealed, but the gods believe that what comes in by wrongdoing will go out by wrongdoing, it is a self-inflicted fault. They do not intervene in such matters." Thus, using all kinds of cunning methods, it only ends in self-destruction. From now on, I will focus on breathing exercises and will no longer practice this art. This event occurred during the Qianlong era, in the years Ding Si and Wu Wu. Mr. Hou'an once personally witnessed this person. A few years later, it was heard that a Taoist was struck by lightning in Shandong. Perhaps this Taoist, who had committed excessive sins of lust and killing, was struck down by heavenly retribution. While the mantis is catching the cicada, the oriole is lurking behind, and there is someone with a catapult lurking behind. This is what it means. My younger brother's house, Dongbai, is located behind the well in the village to the west. Before it was a house, it was surrounded by a wall, and a ring of earthen houses was built around it. There were several rooms, and there was always knocking on the door at night. Even though there was no other reason, the inhabitants were always restless. One day, the wall beside the door collapsed, and a wooden figure appeared, with its hands raised as if knocking on the door, and there were talismans on it. It was then known that the craftsman had wronged the master, and this was a form of haunting. Therefore, one should not lightly associate with or provoke small people.

Mr. He Zishan recounted that during the early years of the Yongzheng reign, a Taoist adept in talismans and spells once ventured deep into the Western Hills. He was captivated by the forest and springs and intended to build a hermitage to practice quietude. However, the locals warned that the area was a den of ghosts and spirits, and that cutting wood and gathering firewood required a group, as even wolves and tigers could not live there. Despite this, the Taoist did not heed their advice. Soon after, ghosts and spirits began to appear, stealing the materials for his house, haunting the craftsmen, destroying his belongings, and contaminating his food and drink. It was like walking through a thicket, encountering obstacles at every step; like wild fires breaking out, with leaves flying chaotically in the wind, and with a thousand hands and eyes unable to keep up. The Taoist became enraged and set up an altar to summon the thunder generals. When the gods descended, the spirits had already fled. A thorough search of the empty mountain yielded nothing. When the gods left, the spirits gathered again after several days, and this pattern repeated several times. The gods were angered by his impiety and no longer responded. Desperate, the Taoist formed a seal with one hand and held a sword with the other, fighting alone. Ultimately, he was defeated by the spirits, pulling out his beard and disfiguring his face, and was left naked and hanging upside down. He was rescued by a woodcutter and fled in a disheveled state. The Taoist probably relied too much on his skills. When power is at stake, even a sage cannot go against it; when a faction has already formed, even an emperor cannot break it. If it persists, it becomes difficult to change; if it grows large, it cannot be defeated. Therefore, during the Tang dynasty, the rivalry between the Niu and Li factions was more difficult than dealing with the regional warlords in Hebei. The Taoist misunderstood the balance of numbers and the dynamics of the conflict, did not measure his strength, and confronted the enemy, making his defeat inevitable.

The schemes of petty people are ever-changing, and they always seize opportunities to display their cunning. In my youth, I once heard that villagers, during the night, heard the sound of footsteps and suspected a thief. They took torches and searched, but found no trace of anyone. Concluding it was a spirit, they no longer investigated. Soon after, the thieves learned of this, and one night they came. The family still believed it was a spirit, so they lay undisturbed and paid no heed, allowing the thieves to satisfy their desires and leave. This was still using the situation to their advantage. In a certain county, there was a magistrate who was deeply engaged in scholarly pursuits and hated monks as enemies. One day, a monk came to report that he had been robbed. The magistrate rebuked him in court, saying: "If your Buddha has no power, why do you receive offerings in the temple? If your Buddha does have power, why cannot he show retribution to the thief, and instead trouble the officials?" He then waved him off and sent him away. He told others: "If all magistrates used this method, monks would not need to be purged—they would disperse on their own." But the monk was indeed very cunning. He publicly performed repentance and prayers with his disciples, while secretly bribing beggars to carry clothes and kneel outside the gate, appearing as if they were fools. All said, "The Buddha has a sacred altar, and offerings are increasing." This was using the very thing that harmed him to help him. Human nature is like this. Yet, to stubbornly adhere to one principle and argue with them—how could there be any hope?

Zhang and Qiu were childhood classmates, both of them looking good. Qiu got into a lawsuit, and Zhang accepted a bribe, learning about Qiu's secret plans and revealing them to Qiu's enemy, causing Qiu to suffer great humiliation and hatred Zhang to the core. However, the matter was secret with no evidence, so externally they did not sever ties. Soon after Zhang died, Qiu did everything possible to marry his wife. Everything was done according to ritual, but in family conversations, they still called her "Zhang's sister-in-law." The wife was simple and willing, thinking it was out of affection and playfulness, and she didn't mind. One day, while eating with his wife, Qiu suddenly jumped up and called his own name, saying, "Qiu, why are you so extreme? I truly betrayed him. My wife will return to you, it will be enough compensation. You will still call her sister-in-law, why?" The wife's remarriage is a common thing, and marrying a remarried woman is also a common thing. Since I am dead, I cannot prevent her from remarrying, nor can I prevent you from marrying her. I have already lost the friendship, and I cannot blame you for marrying a friend's wife. Now if you do not consider her a wife and still call her by my surname as a sister-in-law, it means you are not marrying my wife, but committing adultery with her. The one who commits adultery with my wife, I can kill. He died in a frenzy a few days later. To repay resentment with justice, the sage does not prohibit it. Zhang was indeed a common trait of a small person, not an unshared enemy. Calculating the marriage of his wife, the repayment was already excessive, and yet he treated her as a woman who relies on the door, tarnishing his family's reputation. This is an extreme within an extreme. How can one blame him for being furious and vengeful?

A wicked youth fell ill with a cold, and in his delirium, his soul had already left his body. He wandered aimlessly, feeling lost and alone, until he saw people passing by and followed them. Unaware, he found himself in the underworld, where he encountered a bureaucrat who was an acquaintance from his past life. The bureaucrat checked his records for a long time, then frowned and said, "You have often disobeyed your parents, and according to the law, you should be sent to the boiling oil hell. However, since your lifespan has not yet ended, you may return for now. When your lifespan ends, you will return to receive your punishment." The youth was filled with terror and knelt, begging for release. The bureaucrat shook his head and said, "This sin is too grave; even I cannot release you. Even the Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama, would be powerless to help." The youth wept uncontrollably, pleading continuously. The bureaucrat thought for a moment and said, "Do you know of a story? A Zen master once asked, 'Who can untie the bell on a tiger's neck?' No one could answer. A novice monk replied, 'Why not let the person who tied it untie it?' If you have offended your parents, return to them and seek their forgiveness. Perhaps you may be spared." The youth worried that his sins were too deep to be forgiven in a short time. The bureaucrat smiled and said, "There is another story. Have you heard of the butcher who, after putting down his knife, became a Buddha immediately?" He sent a ghost to return the youth to his body. Suddenly, the youth recovered. From then on, he cleansed his heart and thoughts, and his parents came to love and pity him. He lived to be over seventy years old. Though we do not know if he was truly spared from hell, his long life suggests that he may have been forgiven.

许文木说，老僧澄止有高深的修行，临终前对他的弟子说：“我持戒精进，自认为达到了四禅天人的境界，但世尊因为我平生的议论，喜欢尊奉佛教而贬斥儒家而生气。我的修行还未完全转化，恐怕还是要堕入轮回。弟子问道：“尊奉世尊，世尊反而生气吗？”澄止回答说：“这就是世尊之所以成为世尊的原因。如果只偏袒同党而攻击异己，抬高自己而贬低他人，怎么能成为世尊呢？我现在才明白，你的看法还是偏颇的。”接着，许文木回忆起杨槐庭的话：在乙丑年上公车时，与几位同年一起出行，恰巧有一位僧人同宿旅店，偶然闲聊时，一位同年对澄止说：“你怎么能与异端邪说的人交谈？”澄止不平地说：“佛教确实与儒家不同，但两者各有其境界，如果真是孔子，可以驳斥佛教，但颜回、曾子以下的人做不到；如果真是颜回、曾子，可以驳斥菩萨，但郑玄、贾谊以下的人做不到；如果真是郑玄、贾谊，可以驳斥阿罗汉，但程颐、朱熹以下的人做不到；如果真是程颐、朱熹，可以驳斥各派的祖师，但那些依附草木、自称讲学的人做不到。为什么呢？因为他们的境界不同。先生如果要驳斥佛教，难道不是把自己抬得太高了吗？”同年生气又带着笑意地说：“正因为各有境界，所以我们儒家可以驳斥你们这些僧人。”几乎就要吵起来而散去。我认为，各自从自己的教义出发，就像在家一样，自三王以来，儒家和道家已经持世很久了，即使再有圣人，也无法改变，就像主人一样。

Buddhism came from the Western Regions, and its teachings of emptiness and purity can make those who rush about cease their pursuits and those who are troubled find relief. Its doctrine of cause and effect is also sufficient to warn the ignorant and foolish, making them turn their hearts toward goodness, and it is not without benefit to the world. Therefore, its teachings have spread in China, like a guest with skills. However, the guest does not refine his own skills but instead seeks to change the master's household affairs, making the master step back and receive instruction. This is the fault of Buddhism. Considering the various branches, it is like farming; Confucianism is like the farmer, while Buddhism has lost its original purpose, not considering good and evil as causes of punishment and reward, but instead focusing on giving and not giving as causes of punishment and reward. Thus, it deceives the masses and wastes resources, often leading to the trespassing of boundaries and the stealing of crops.

Confucian scholars abandon their plows and tillers, neglecting their fields, and hastily wield sticks and carry spears, daily seeking to fight those who invade, trespass, and steal. Even if they win in battle, they do not know the state of their own fields. Is this not a folly of Confucianism? Since the reign of Emperor Ming of Han, Buddhism has spread for two thousand years; even if Yao, Shun, Zhou, and Confucius were to be reborn, they could not drive it away. Confucian scholars, with their emphasis on father-son, ruler-minister, military, penal, ritual, and musical systems, cannot govern without them. Even if the Buddha were to be reborn, he could not implement his teachings in China. Originally, there could have been no conflict, but the monks could not resist their greed, foolishly hoping that Confucianism would decline and Buddhism would flourish, expecting that those who return to Buddhism will increase their offerings. Scholars cannot resist their own desires, and if their works do not include several anti-Buddhist points, it is not enough to show their dedication to defending the Way. Thus, the sayings of both sides are like bubbles in water, appearing and disappearing, disappearing and reappearing, and they continue to insult and attack each other without end. However, even after a thousand years of contention, both still coexist as before. Even if they do not contend, after a thousand years, they still coexist as before. Each should focus on their own profession.

Chen Rui'an said, it is said that all the hills outside the city of Xian County are Han tombs. A farmer accidentally plowed over one of these tombs and, upon returning home, fell ill with fever and delirium. He was blamed for desecrating the tomb. By chance, Chen Rui'an happened to be there and asked him who he was. The farmer replied, "I am a person from the Han dynasty, and I am from Xian County, so the tomb is here. Why ask further?" Chen Rui'an then asked, "Was this place called Xian County during the Han dynasty?" The farmer replied, "Yes." Chen Rui'an continued, "This area was part of the fiefdom of Hejian during the Han dynasty, and the county was called Yuce. It was first renamed Xianzhou during the Jin dynasty and finally renamed Xian County during the Ming dynasty. How could it have been called Xian County during the Han dynasty?" The ghost did not speak, and after asking again, the farmer regained consciousness. It seems that the ghost relied on the traditional belief that the hills were Han tombs to seek sustenance, but it ultimately failed due to its own misjudgment.

Ma Qiren said: There was a man named Gengang, who was both brave and fierce. While traveling through the mountains, he encountered a tiger and fought it with a single staff; the tiger ultimately fled, and Gengang believed himself to be in the same league as Zhong Huang and Ci Fei, legendary warriors of old. One day, he heard that behind a certain temple there were many ghosts that often tormented drunkards. Enraged, he went to drive them away, and a few curious people followed him. When they arrived, it was nearly dusk, so they drank freely until nightfall and sat on the back wall, waiting for the ghosts to come. After the second drum of the night, they faintly heard a howling sound. Gengang then shouted, “Gengang is here!” Suddenly, countless human figures rushed toward him, all laughing mockingly, saying, “Is it you? It’s easy, after all.” Gengang, angry, leaped down, and the figures scattered like birds and beasts. He called out their names and scolded them. If he chased them east, they were west; if he chased them west, they were east—this one disappearing, that one appearing. In an instant, they changed positions a thousand times. Gengang spun around like a wind wheel, but in the end, he saw not a single ghost. Exhausted and wanting to return, the ghosts laughed at him and provoked him, gradually leading him further and further away. Suddenly, a strange ghost stood in his path, with teeth like saws and eyes like lightning, stretching its claws to attack. Gengang quickly raised his fist and struck, and suddenly the ghost fell over. His finger was broken, his palm shattered. It turned out he had mistakenly struck a tombstone. The group of ghosts shouted together: “Brave indeed!” Then, suddenly, they all vanished. The people watching from the walls heard Gengang crying in pain and together held torches and carried him back. He lay for several days before he could rise. His right hand became useless. From then on, his fierce spirit was completely gone; he even let spittle dry on his face. One who could fight a roaring tiger yet could not avoid being trapped by ghosts—tigers fight with strength, while ghosts fight with wit. Using limited strength to overcome infinite transformations—was this not the act of a fool in the world? Yet after one punishment, he was immediately warned, resolutely returned, and though he may be called a great wise person, it is also acceptable.

Zhang Guiyan returned from Yangzhou, carrying a zither and inkstone as a gift. The objects were worn and faded, with an ancient, dark luster. On the right side, near the bottom, were engraved two seal script characters "Xiya," indicating they were relics from Huilutang. In the center, a poem in running script read: "If we judge by literary talent, you originally surpassed Xie and Liu; a master of the brush in the Jade Hall, recalling the elegance of the past." The signature was "Zhi Sheng," the courtesy name of Gao Yangsun, the Chancellor. On the left side, a poem in small seal script read: "Grass green on the Xiang River, the cuckoo calls; the annals of Chaling have subtle words. This inkstone, as it has been passed down, is still cherished by people, and should be a five-character poem from Gaoyang." The signature was "Bu Diao," the courtesy name of Cui Hua from Taicang. Cui Hua was a disciple of Yuyang Mountain. Yuyang's couplets on poetry read: "The brook is greener than on the day I crossed it; the peach blossoms are as red as they were last year. Who in Jiangnan understands the heartbreak? Only Cui's seven-character poem." This refers to him.

The two poems are not included in the collection; perhaps they were omitted because they criticized or rebuked predecessors, slightly involving direct accusation, and were deleted during the compilation. Later, they were presented to Grand Marshal Danian, and Liu Shianan, the Censor-in-Chief, suspected they were fake. However, it is true that ancient people often had poems outside their collections, and their authenticity remains uncertain. Another gentleman, Yang Wenjiang, whose courtesy name was Keming, was the great-grandson of Yang Zhonglie, the loyal minister. He was appointed as a Langzhong of the Ministry of Revenue through the imperial examination for talented individuals and worked with my late grandfather, Yao An. He gifted a small stone to Grandfather Yao, which had an inscription on the back: "Since crossing the Liao River, I have carried you as my companion, drafting military documents, often in the middle of the night, my heart, only you can see." The inscription is titled "Zhi Gang," and it is said to be a stone from the military camp of Xiong Tingbi, obtained from his close relatives' home. Another small stone was also kept in the family, with the words "Baigu" carved on the left side by hand, likely made by Sun Chuanjing himself. The two stones are similar in size, and Grandfather Yao, considering them both famous ministers from previous dynasties, put them together in one box. Later, they were kept by my eldest son, Rujie, who died young, and the two stones were stolen and sold by maidservants, and now they cannot be found.

When I was seventeen, I returned from the capital and took the examination for the status of a child scholar. I stayed at the residence of Sun, a clerk, whose family referred to him as "Xun Shi," a local pronunciation. The rooms were newly built, but there was a wooden peg made from peach wood nailed into the bottom of a dirt pit, which caused significant inconvenience. I called the owner to remove it. The owner was quite earnest and shook his head, saying, "It cannot be removed; if it is, strange occurrences will happen." When I pressed him for the reason, he explained, "I bought this plot of land and built this inn. Guests often see a woman standing before the kang at night, neither speaking nor moving. There is no other harm, but those with courage would reach out to touch her, and there was nothing there to feel. A Taoist priest chanted a spell over the peach peg and nailed it in place, and she no longer appeared." I said, "There must be an ancient tomb below; with a person above, the spirits are unsettled. Why not dig up the bones and rebury them in a proper coffin?" The owner replied, "Yes, but I don't know if they were actually moved."

In the spring of the year Gui Si, I requested leave to convalesce in Beicang. My mother-in-law's family, the Zhao clan, asked me to preside over the naming ceremony. My late father-in-law, the Yao'an Gong, ordered me to go. Upon returning, I stayed at Yang Village, where it was already late at night. I went to bed first, while the servants were still busy preparing the horses. Suddenly, a woman in colorful clothing entered by lifting the curtain, but as soon as she appeared, she immediately left. I suspected she was a prostitute who had come to the event and called the servants to send her away. They all said the outer door was already closed and no one was there. The host said, "Four days ago, a woman from a high-ranking official's family stayed here and died. Yesterday, the coffin was moved away. Could this be the return of her soul?" I reported this to my late father-in-law, who said, "When I was a child, I studied at my maternal uncle Chen's house and once witnessed a servant woman experiencing the return of her soul at night. The moonlight was as bright as daylight, and I sat alone outside the room, wanting to see what the return of the soul looked like, but I saw nothing." He then said, "Why did you see it? Then you have seen less than I did." I deeply regret this lesson to this day.

River pufferfish are most abundant in Tianjin, and the locals eat them as if they were garden vegetables, yet there are still deaths, not because every household is skilled in cooking them. Aunt's husband, Mr. Ti Yuan Niu, said that there was a man who loved river pufferfish, and he eventually died from poisoning. After his death, he appeared in a dream to his wife and children, saying, "Why is there no river pufferfish in my offerings?" This is truly dying without regret. Mr. Yao An also said that in the village, there was a man who was roughly well-fed and warm, but later lost his family due to gambling. On his deathbed, he told his son, "You must place the gambling tools in the coffin. If there is no ghost, it will be just like a pile of white bones. What harm is there to this? If there is a ghost, in the wilderness and overgrown grass, what else can they do for amusement?" When the coffin was closed, everyone said, "Bury him according to ritual; we cannot follow this strange command." His son replied, "Don't you say that treating the dead is like treating the living? If I couldn't advise him when he was alive, should I go against his wishes now that he's dead?" He added, "I don't study Confucianism, so you gentlemen shouldn't interfere in family matters." They ultimately followed his command. Mr. Yao An said, "It is not proper, but it also shows an unending heart of filial piety." He added, "I dislike those who follow all ancient rituals, but have a cold-hearted attitude towards their parents."

A male servant, engaged in needlework, was not sold into servitude by his parents, unlike his siblings, and thus lived separately outside. His wife, in her twenties, was bewitched by a fox and died of consumption after more than a year. Initially, she did not speak openly about the encounter, but when her condition worsened, she revealed that the fox first appeared to her in the form of a woman, claiming to be a new neighbor. They conversed, and the interaction gradually became playful, then increasingly insistent. The fox suddenly embraced her, causing her to fall into a daze as if in a nightmare. From then on, the fox came to her every night, always changing form—sometimes male, sometimes female, sometimes old, sometimes young, sometimes ugly, sometimes beautiful, sometimes a monk, sometimes a Taoist, sometimes a ghost, sometimes a deity, sometimes dressed in modern attire, sometimes in ancient attire. Over the course of a year, there was no repetition in the forms. When the fox arrived, her limbs would become relaxed and limp, her mouth would close, and she would be unable to speak, though her mind remained clear. The fox did not speak a word, and it was unclear whether it was one fox transforming into different forms or multiple foxes taking turns. What was especially strange was that one day, her younger sister-in-law happened to enter her room and suddenly encountered the fox emerging. The fox leaped away immediately.
