The sister-in-law saw a man in a scholar's attire with a neat white beard, while the wife saw a dull, dirty figure resembling a coal seller. Both women saw the fox in different forms at the same time, an event even more unbelievable.

A Report by Master Ru Ai Master Ru Ai said: "He is my distant cousin by marriage, but in childhood he taught me the basics, so I have always treated him with the respect due to a teacher. There was a man from Jiaohe whose fields were near a tomb, far from his home, so he built a house nearby. At night, he often heard ghostly voices, and over time, he became accustomed to them and no longer found it strange. One evening, he heard a call from the tomb: 'How could you be in such a sorry state?' A man replied: 'Just now on the road I met a woman carrying a child. I saw that her face had a withering aura, indicating that her death was imminent, and I did not avoid her. I did not expect the woman to suddenly sneeze. Her aura struck me, like a large pestle pounding, and I was injured and fell to the ground, recovering for a long time before returning home. Now my chest still feels painful.' This person silently remembered the words. The next day, the farmers gathered and fully described the strange event. They then asked who the woman from yesterday evening was, which led to meeting a ghost on the way. One named Song said: 'My daughter returned home with my son last night, and there was no encounter with a ghost.' The crowd thought it was a lie. Several days later, Song's daughter was seized by a violent man, defended herself with a knife, and died maintaining her integrity. Thus it was known that the spirit of chastity and bravery, even in its waning, was still so strong. Ghosts and demons fear upright people, perhaps because of this."

Zhang Wanzhi, the Attendant, says: There was a man who conducted business outside and entrusted his household affairs to a fox. The fox guarded against fire, candles, thieves, and bandits, and if any children or maidservants committed mischief, they were all caught without fail. Household affairs were in order, even better than when the merchant was at home. Only his wife and the neighbor were present, and the fox seemed unaware of their presence. After two years, the merchant returned and highly praised the fox. After a while, he heard a little about the neighbor's affairs and blamed the fox severely. The fox replied, "This is decided by the gods, how dare we disobey?" The merchant, however, was not convinced and said, "Ghosts and spirits punish immorality, how can they lead to immorality?" The fox explained, "There is a reason for this. In a past life, the neighbor was a wealthy person, and you were his accountant. Because of his trust, you embezzled a lot of money. In the afterlife, it was decided that his wife would pay off the debt. One night's stay with a prostitute is equivalent to five stars of gold. Now only about seventy gold coins are left. Once the debt is paid off, it will be over. Why are you so impatient? If you don't believe, try to pay off the debt yourself and see what happens." The merchant then went to the neighbor's house and said, "I heard that you are very poor. This time I have made a good profit, so I humbly offer eighty gold coins to help you." The neighbor felt grateful and ashamed, and from then on, he broke off relations with his wife. At the end of the year, he sent exquisite gifts to show his gratitude. Calculating the value, it exactly matched seventy gold coins. The amount he gained showed that the debt from past lives cannot be increased or decreased. This is also something to be feared.

My cousin Zhu Ding said that there was a widow from a farming family who, after losing her husband, vowed never to remarry and dedicated herself to caring for her mother-in-law and raising her child for many years. One day, a young man dressed in splendid attire peered through a gap in the wall, and she thought he was a passerby who had mistakenly entered her home. She scolded him away. The next day, he returned. Realizing that there was no such young man in the nearby village and that no local person wore such fine clothing, she suspected that he was a spirit. She tried to chase him away with a stick, but he began to throw bricks and stones, damaging household items. From then on, he came every day, climbing the wall to express his affection. With no other options, she cried out for help at the temple of the local deity, but nothing worked. After seven or eight days, during the day, the sky darkened, and lightning struck and split open an ancient tomb to the south of the village, and the spirit was finally defeated. It is unknown whether it was a fox or a ghost. Using his charms to seduce people, he had already violated the laws of heaven, and especially seducing a woman who had taken a vow of chastity, his punishment was certainly just. However, it took a long time for the divine retribution to come, which seems to indicate that the same principle applies to both heaven and humans. Matters of life and death also require formal petitions before punishment can be carried out, and it took time for the local deity to report this to higher authorities. Yet, a cry from a common woman could reach the heavens so quickly, which also demonstrates the connection between filial piety and fraternal respect and the divine.

In the coastal region of Cangzhou, there are areas where salt is produced by boiling seawater, known as "zao pao." These areas stretch for hundreds of miles and are saline and unsuitable for farming. The land is covered with tall, overgrown grass, resembling the frontier regions, and thus, wolves often make their dens there. Trappers dig pits in the ground, several feet deep and three to four feet wide, cover them with wooden boards, and carve circular holes large enough for a bowl, resembling a collar. The trapper crouches in the pit, brings along a puppy or a piglet, and strikes it to make it bark. Wolves, hearing the sound, approach and reach into the hole to grab the bait. The trapper then grabs the wolf's foot, stands up, and carries it away. The wolf, separated by a wooden board, cannot use its claws or teeth effectively. However, if the wolves hunt in packs, they can still attack. When they see humans, they bark and gather, as if giving commands. This makes them a significant threat to travelers. A wealthy household once found two young wolves, raised them alongside their dogs, and they coexisted peacefully. As the wolves grew older, they became quite tame, and the household forgot they were wolves. One day, while the owner was sleeping in the hall, he heard the dogs growling. He woke up, looked around, and found no one. He returned to bed, and the dogs growled again. He pretended to sleep, and the two wolves waited for him to fall asleep, planning to bite his throat. The dogs prevented them from attacking. The wolves were then killed, and their skins were taken. This story was told by the author's cousin, Yu Dun. The proverb "a wolf in sheep's clothing" is indeed true. However, the danger of wolves lies not just in their ambition but in their deceptive behavior. They appear friendly but harbor hidden intentions, which is more dangerous than mere ambition. The real danger is the person who chose to raise wolves, bringing harm upon themselves.

A peasant woman from a village was very chaste and quiet. One day, while bringing food to the fields, she met a scholar in the wild. The scholar asked for water from her bottle, but she did not respond. He then threw a piece of gold into her sleeve and scolded her. The scholar fled in fear and panic. Later, she told her husband to search for the person, but there was no one like him, so she suspected it was a demon. A few days later, her husband went out and was blocked by rain, unable to return home. The demon then transformed into the shape of her husband and returned home in the rain. They quickly put out the light and suddenly engaged in intimate play. Suddenly, lightning flashed through the window, revealing the book scholar. The woman was very angry and clawed his face. The demon just leaped out the window, making a sound like a monkey's call, and no one knew where it went. The next morning, her husband returned, and outside the door was a dead monkey, its brain split open as if it had been struck by a blade. It seems that when demons seduce people, they do so because the person is sexually aroused. If the person has no such intention and the demon surprises them, causing them to lose their virtue, then the demon's crime is equivalent to rape. According to divine justice, the demon must be punished. This case is more quickly punished than the one mentioned by Zhudeng. Perhaps the local deity is too weak to make an immediate judgment, so the demon was immediately struck down by a heavenly spirit. Alternatively, because the demon was not yet fully formed, it could be punished without further ado.

Zou Daofeng, a fellow examinee, once said that there was a man named Han who, in the summer of the Ding Mao year, was studying in the mountains. His window faced a sheer cliff, and below the cliff was a deep ravine, so steep that although the two sides were close, they were visible but unattainable. On moonlit nights, he often saw a human figure across the ravine. Although he knew it was a ghost, he reasoned it could not cross, and so was not greatly afraid. Over time, he became accustomed to it and tried calling out to it and speaking with it; the ghost also responded. It said it was a ghost that had fallen into the ravine, waiting for a replacement. Han playfully poured the remaining wine through the window into the ravine, and the ghost descended to drink it, expressing great gratitude. From then on, they became conversational companions. In the spare time spent reciting and studying, this greatly alleviated his loneliness. One day, he asked the ghost, “People say that ghosts have foreknowledge. This year I am taking the examination — do you know whether I will succeed or fail?” The ghost replied, “The gods do not check records, and so cannot have foreknowledge, let alone ghosts! Ghosts can only judge a person's annual fortune by the waxing and waning of their yang energy, and a person’s moral character by the brightness or dimness of their spiritual light. As for one’s fate and destiny, only ghosts who serve the underworld officials may know it by secretly listening or watching; city ghosts may hear it through repeated transmission, but mountain and wilderness ghosts cannot. Even in the city, only clever ghosts can learn it — dull ones cannot. For example, if you sit quietly in this mountain, you would not even know the affairs of the local government, how much more the secrets of the imperial court?” One night, he heard someone across the ravine call out: “I bring you good news.” It seemed that the city god had just been patrolling the mountain and speaking with the village god, saying that this year’s top scorer in the examination was Han. The man secretly rejoiced. When the results were posted, the top scorer was Han ZuoLin. The ghost had only heard his surname. Han sighed deeply and said, “The people of the village spreading news of official affairs — is it really like this?”

The Editor of Wang Shiting said, "There was a young man named Cui who was sentenced to military service in Guangdong. Fearing that bringing his family along could lead to unforeseen troubles, he left his wife and concubines behind and traveled alone. Upon arriving at his posting, he was in deep poverty and depression, especially unable to find solace or comfort. Recalling the image of a young wife ascending a tower, only increased his sorrow and distress. One day, he accidentally met an old man who said his surname was Dong and his courtesy name was Wu Nian. Their conversation was quite harmonious, and the old man pitied his plight, inviting him to be a tutor for his son. They also got along very well. One evening, the host and guest drank together. The tower was high and the moon was full, and suddenly, feelings of separation stirred within him. Holding wine and leaning on the railing, he completely forgot the toasts and responses. The old man smiled and said, 'Do you have feelings for a woman with cloud-like hair and jade-like arms? Because of our friendship, I have already arranged it, but whether it will happen is uncertain, so I didn't tell you beforehand. In a month or two, you will hear something.'"

After half a year, the old man suddenly instructed the servants to sweep and tidy a separate room, his intention being very urgent. Soon after, three small sedan chairs arrived, and the wives, concubines, and one maid lifted the curtains and came out. Surprised and curious, the young man asked, and they replied: "We received your letter and came to fetch you. We were told to accompany the family of a certain official, and we could not wait long, so we came in haste. We have entrusted your family affairs to several brothers of several houses, and we will sell gold every year and send it to you." When he asked where the maid came from, she said: "She is a concubine of a certain official, the wife could not accommodate her, so she was sold at a low price on the boat." The young man was deeply grateful and bowed to the old man, until tears fell. From then on, they reunited and formed a family, no longer dreaming of the old home. After several months, the old man told the young man: "This maid was encountered on the way, we accompanied through hardships, it seems there is a connection, it seems we should share the same pillow and comb, not leaving anyone alone in the corner." After several more years, upon amnesty, the young man returned, overjoyed and unable to sleep, but the wives, concubines, and maid were all sad with the look of separation.

The guest comforted them, saying, "Do you think of the master's kindness? If you do not die, there will be a day when you will repay him." They all remained silent, only hastening to prepare the guest's belongings. On the eve of departure, the old man prepared wine for a farewell feast and called out the three daughters, saying, "Today, the matter must be clearly stated." With a bow, he said to the guest, "I am a local immortal. In a past life, we were colleagues. After death, you did everything to bring back my wife and children. I have always been deeply grateful. Now that you are separated from your beloved, it is fitting for me to take care of you. But the mountains and rivers are vast, and the two weak women, how could they come? So I summoned the flower demon to your home half a year ago. She observed the appearance and speech of your family, mimicking them perfectly, and learned about the old affairs of your household, so that you would have no doubt. They are actually three sisters, so I added an extra maid. They are all illusions, do not think about them again. When you return home and see the old people, it will still be the same as here."

The narrator requested to accompany the three daughters back home, and the old man said, "Ghosts and spirits each have their own territories; they can temporarily leave but cannot stay long." The three daughters held hands to say goodbye, their tears wetting their clothes. In an instant, they all disappeared. When the narrator boarded the boat, he saw them standing on the shore, but they did not come closer. After returning, the narrator's wife and children explained that the family was struggling, but thanks to the annual gold sent by the narrator, they managed to survive, which was likely due to the old man's help. If all parting people could meet this old man, there would be no more sorrow like the separation of the Cowherd and the Weaver Girl. The clerk said, "Indeed, but there are local immortals in Guangdong, and there must be such beings elsewhere. The immortal Dong has this ability, and other immortals must also have it. The reason no one meets them again is that in past lives, they have not received their help, and thus they do not exert themselves to find them."

A guest stayed at an inn in Bozhen and spent the night with a courtesan, giving her gold. The courtesan carefully examined the gold, holding it up to the light, and smiled, saying, "Isn't this a piece of paper money?" The guest was surprised and asked why she thought that. She explained that a few days ago, a grain ship was performing a play to worship the gods, and she had gone to watch it, returning late at night. There, she met a young man who gave her gold, and they had a tryst in a thatched hut by the riverbank. When she returned home and felt the gold, she found it too light. She took it out and discovered it was a piece of paper money. It seems she met a ghost. She then mentioned that in a nearby courtesan's house, a guest had given her very generous clothes and ornaments. After he left, they all became her belongings, and the lock remained unopened. She suspected that a fox had tricked her. The guest jokingly said, "The way of heaven is retributive." Another blind man named Liu Junrui said that in Qing County, there was someone who befriended a fox. They often drank together, and the fox was very sociable. Suddenly, the fox disappeared for a long time. One day, he passed through a dense thicket and heard a groaning sound. He looked and saw that it was the fox. He asked what had happened to make it so miserable. The fox, feeling ashamed and dejected for a long time, said, "Recently, I saw a young courtesan who was quite healthy and vigorous. I transformed my shape to spend the night with her, hoping to absorb her essence. I did not expect that the courtesan had a foul sore. After absorbing her essence, the poison seeped into my vital organ, mixing with the essence I had collected over my lifetime, like oil mixed into dough, no longer separable. It then ruptured and spread, reaching my face. Ashamed to be seen by my old friends, I had been avoiding them for a long time." This is another instance where the fox was defeated by a courtesan. The interplay of tricks and schemes, where gains and losses are intertwined, confused and entangled, how will it end?

御史李千之说，某位公子容貌俊美，有如卫玠般的璧人美誉。在雍正末年，他在丰宜门内参加秋季的科举考试，租了一间僧舍度过夏天。他用一间房设榻休息，另一间房用来读书。每天早晨起床后，书房里的桌椅、笔墨等物品都擦拭得一尘不染，甚至瓶中的花和砚池里的水也都整理得井井有条，这绝非粗心之人所能做到。他忽然想到北方多有狐女，或许可以通过她们来表达情感，也未可知。这个想法让他觉得颇为有趣。于是，他盘中稍稍放置了一些果饵，都是上等的美食，虽然不敢轻易食用，但还是增加了几分期待，希望美人能带来美好的机遇。一天夜晚，月光皎洁，他悄悄走到北窗外面，透过纸上的小洞窥视，希望能见到美丽的身影。半夜时分，听到室内器具有声，果然有一人在室内忙碌。仔细一看，竟是一位须髯浓密的伟丈夫。他吓得转身就跑，第二天便搬离了那间僧舍。搬走后不久，天花板上似乎传来了一声叹息。

Teacher Kang, a monk from the town of Dulin — in the northern custom, monks are often addressed by their family names, so his full name is not passed down. He was skilled in treating wounds and ulcers, and I saw him when I was young. He told me that in his hometown, a young female servant died in her prime, and her spirit did not dissipate. She occasionally caused disturbances, but she did not manifest herself or make any noise, nor did she attach herself to someone to speak through them or cause illness. Only occasionally did she interact with young men in their dreams. When they became slightly sickly, she would charm other young men, but she did not go as far as to kill them. So she caused disturbances but was not considered a disturbance. Even those who were once troubled by her also experienced hazy dreams and could not firmly grasp the reality. In this manner for several decades, she was not feared by people, nor was she punished by people. Truly a cunning ghost! One could say she was good at concealing her abilities, good at evading into nothingness, good at leaving something incomplete, and good at grasping the essence of Laozi. But eventually, someone knew about her, and someone spread the word. And thus, cunning and trickery will eventually fail.

It is said during the Kangxi era, there was a fire at the melon seed shop — located to the south and slightly east of Zhengyang Gate. There was a young man who was ill with consumption and could not escape, and the house burned down with him. After the fire was extinguished and the ruins were dug up, his body was charred, and a fox was found dead beside him. It was known that his illness was caused by a fox's charm. However, it is unclear why the fox also died. Some say the fox had deep feelings and could not leave him, nor could it abandon him. Others say the fox charmed the person to death and was struck by the gods. None of these explanations are correct. Fox ghosts can transform, and ghosts can pass through walls and buildings. Luo Liangfeng says so. Ghosts have form but no substance, they are purely energy; energy can reach everywhere, so nothing can obstruct it. Foxes can become large or small, like dragons, but they have form and substance, which can change to become small, but not to disappear. Therefore, they can escape through gaps, but cannot escape if there are no gaps. Even the most spiritual fox must enter and exit through doors and windows. During the time this young man was still alive, the fox still came to charm him. Suddenly, when the fire broke out, the doors and windows were all aflame, so they both became ashes.

A disciple named Xu Tongpan Jingru reported that in his hometown, there was a wealthy household with a maid who was highly favored by her master. The maid also showed deep affection towards her master and vowed never to remarry. The principal wife was jealous of her but could do nothing about it. When the wealthy household was away on business, the principal wife secretly summoned a female matchmaker to sell the maid to someone else. Upon the wealthy household's return, she would report that the maid had secretly escaped. The family, knowing the master's return, anticipated changes and pretended to buy the maid from the matchmaker, hiding her in a nunnery. When the maid arrived at the matchmaker's house, she stared straight ahead without speaking. She stood when lifted, walked when supported, and lay down when pressed, otherwise remaining like a puppet, motionless all day. She ate and drank when offered, but did not seek food or drink otherwise. The same behavior continued when she reached the nunnery. The doctor thought it was due to anger, resentment, and phlegm confusion, but the treatment was ineffective. Even in the nunnery, she remained in this state, neither dead nor alive, for over a month.

A wealthy person returned home, and indeed, she fought with her wife using swords, slaughtering a sheep, shedding blood to inform the gods, and swearing not to live together. The family thought it could not be hidden, so they told the truth, and hurriedly went to the nunnery to bring her back. She remained as痴 (foolish or dazed) as before, but when the wealthy person whispered her name in her ear, she suddenly woke up like from a dream. She said she first arrived at the woman's family, thinking this was only the master's mother's intention and that the master would surely not abandon her, so she ran back on her own. She worried about being seen by the master's mother and always hid in secret places to wait for the master's arrival. Now, hearing the master calling, she happily came out. She then recounted that on certain days at home, she saw certain people and did certain things, all clearly and accurately. Thus, she realized that her body had left but her soul had returned. From this, she inferred that the story of a soul-parting beauty is no more than this. Only novelists embellish the story to make it a good tale. As for saying that after the soul returns, the clothes are all layered, this is especially absurd. The clothes are her true form, and in a moment, the collar and belt are not untied; how could they be layered in? Why not say the clothes are like shed skins, which is still somewhat close to reason?

A Farmer Named Bùmǎn Initially, he was named Bùmǎn for the meaning of "not being self-satisfied," suggesting that his name had ancient connotations. Later, it was discovered that the name was actually derived from the homophone "tāotiè" (饕餮), a mythical creature known for its insatiable appetite, and "tiántián" (田填), which also sounds similar in Chinese. One night, while walking and losing his way, he mistakenly passed through a burial ground and stepped on a skull. The skull spoke, saying, "Do not damage my face, and I will harm you." Bùmǎn, who was stupid and fierce, shouted, "Who sent you to block the path?" The skull replied, "People moved me here, not that I wanted to block the path." Bùmǎn shouted again, "Why don't you harm those who moved you?" The skull responded, "They are currently in a period of prosperity, so there's nothing to be done." Bùmǎn laughed and became angry, saying, "Am I in decline? To fear the strong and bully the weak, what kind of logic is that?" The skull wept and said, "Your qi (vital energy) is also strong, so I dare not harm you, I just used empty words to scare you." The skull continued, "To fear the strong and bully the weak, this is human nature, why do you blame the ghost?" Moved by pity, Bùmǎn moved the skull into a hole in the ground, which was his kindness. He rushed past it, only hearing a wailing sound behind him, and nothing else unusual happened. I think Bùmǎn lacks compassion, but when he encountered a rude person and used grand words to provoke him, the ghost also had some fault.

Jiang Tao-sheng, the editor-in-charge, recounted that a scholar traveling north moored his boat between North Warehouse and Yangliuqing — North Warehouse is twenty li from Tianjin, and Yangliuqing is forty li from Tianjin. It was already dusk, and all around was vast and desolate, a bit away from the houses. A small girl stood alone by a tree, extremely beautiful and charming, but her clothes were elegant and clean, and her demeanor did not resemble that of a girl from a wealthy family. The scholar, being lighthearted and flirtatious, stepped ashore to speak with her. She spoke with a southern accent and claimed to be stranded there, saying that someone had promised to take her back, but they had not arrived yet. As they grew more familiar with each other, he flirted with subtle words and gave her a Han dynasty jade pendant from his fan as a gift. She blushed and replied, "You understand, and I cannot hide it, but I value the feelings of my old friend and cannot bear to leave and play the pipa." She placed the pendant and left. The scholar's desire was not yet satisfied and wanted to spy on where she stayed. He followed her silently, but after a few dozen steps, she suddenly disappeared. Only a small grave in the thick vegetation remained, and he realized she was a ghost. It is a great virtue for a woman to serve her husband; to be loyal to one person is to be chaste, while wild unions are considered promiscuous. Men who hold quilts have already lost their virtue, yet still claim to be loyal to one. Isn't it like not considering the root and focusing on the end? But compared to being unfaithful, it is still better in the end.

The respected teacher Chen Baiya once spoke of his mentor, Mr. X, whose name and surname are forgotten, but who seems to have been surnamed Zhou. He was deeply devoted to the teachings of Lu Jiuyuan and Zhu Xi, but did not seek fame through teaching, so he ended his life in poverty and old age, silent and obscure. Yet his inner conduct was pure and refined, and he was a pure and ancient gentleman. Once, he lived in a few empty rooms for rent. One night, he heard a voice from outside the window say, "There is something to report. I fear you will be frightened. What should we do?" The master replied, "Just enter without hindrance." Upon entering, he saw a person with a head on the neck, held by both hands. The head was not covered, and the body was wearing a robe, with blood staining half of it. The master bowed and sat down, and the person was as courteous and respectful as a ritual.

The scholar asked what was being said, and the ghost replied: "Unfortunate as I am, I was killed by bandits at the end of the Ming Dynasty, and my soul remains trapped in this house. Those who have lived here before did not wish to cause harm, but the negative energy and positive energy clashed, causing many people to be frightened, and I too felt uneasy. Now I have a plan: there is a house next door that can accommodate your family. If I go there and create many strange phenomena, they will surely leave. Anyone who comes to live there will be disturbed as before, and the house will definitely be abandoned and become a ruin. You can sell it at a low price and move to that place, while I will continue to live peacefully here. Would this not be a win-win situation?" The scholar said: "I have never done anything manipulative in my life, and how much less so, using ghosts to harm people. Morally, I cannot do it. I read in this room to find some peace. Since you are here, you can use it to store miscellaneous items and lock it up every day, can't you?" The ghost apologized with shame: "I only saw the *Xingli* (Principles of Nature) on your desk, so I dared to suggest this plan. I did not know you were truly a scholar of the Way. I spoke out of turn. Now that I am tolerated, I can stay under your roof." After living there for four years, there was no other disturbance. It was the righteous energy that subdued him.

Objects that closely resemble human forms often gain the ability to transform over time. My cousin Zhonghan recounted that while he was serving as an official in Jingde, one of his fellow officials enjoyed staging plays and commissioned a craftsman to create a woman. This woman was of the same height as a person, with a body shape and even the subtlest details perfectly resembling a human. Her hands, feet, eyes, and tongue were all equipped with joints and levers, allowing them to bend, stretch, and move. Her clothing, skirts, hairpins, and earrings could be changed according to the season, and the craftsmanship was so exquisite that it surpassed even the legendary skill of Yan Shi. She could stand upright beside a bookshelf or desk, or sit on a bed or stool, providing amusement and laughter. One night, a child servant heard a rattling sound in the study. By then, the room had already been locked, and the child poked a hole in the paper to peek through. The moonlight was shining through the window, and it turned out that this puppet was moving around on its own. The child quickly informed the master, who personally verified and found it to be true. When the puppet was burned, it emitted a sound like crying or moaning. Another story was told by my great-grandmother about Uncle Zhang, who lived in Diezhuang. His house had several empty rooms used for storing miscellaneous items. Sometimes at night, female servants saw a woman in the courtyard. She had a beautiful appearance, but her beard was long and sharp like a spear, and her cheeks were also bristly like hedgehog spines. She carried four or five children playing with her. The children were either lame or blind, or had broken heads or faces, or lacked ears or noses. Whenever people came, she suddenly disappeared, and no one knew what kind of spirit or demon she was. She did not harm people and did not go outside. Some said it was due to dizziness, others said it was nonsense, but they did not pay much attention. Later, when the room was inspected, a bed of broken clay figurines from Huqiu was found, which looked like the woman they had seen. The beard of the woman was likely drawn by children playing with ink and brushes.

Fang Kuidian of Jingzhou once recounted that in his youth he suffered from restlessness of the heart qi. When he engaged in even slight labor, it felt as if his heart was trembling or shivering. He took remedies such as jujube seeds and Polygala, but the condition would sometimes flare up and sometimes subside, without being very effective. One time, he happened to visit a friend’s home where spirit-writing was being conducted. It was said to be the Pure Yang True Person, so he bowed and respectfully asked for a remedy. The spirit-writing responded: “This condition manifests in the heart, but its origin lies in the spleen. It is because the spleen is weak that the child (the heart) feeds on the mother’s (the spleen’s) qi.” It suggested taking fried Bai Zhu regularly. He tried this and found it indeed effective. Fang Kuidian also mentioned that once he asked the spirit-writer about his prospects in the imperial examinations. The spirit-writing replied: “In the exam hall, if the brush is full and the ink is rich, and the writing is full of flavor, then you will pass. Why ask in advance?” Later, in the year Qianlong Bingshen (1776), he was admitted as a jinshi. The examiner in charge of his section examined the exam records and saw that the comments he had written on the paper were exactly these eight characters. If then the degree was predestined, was the commentary also predestined?

Gao Meicun said, "There were two villagers traveling together. One of them happened to stop and kick up a tile. Underneath the tile, there was a jar, and on the tile was engraved a single character, which was the surname of the other villager. Fearing that he would be seen, he made an excuse to return and hid in the dense foliage. When he saw that the other villager had gone far away, he went to secretly take the jar. Inside the jar, he found that it was filled with water. He could not bear the anger and drank it all up. By then, it was already dusk, and he had no place to stay. Remembering that the other villager's home was nearby, he went straight there to ask for shelter. In the middle of the night, he suddenly fell ill with what seemed like cholera, vomiting and diarrhea, soiling the bed and the floor. He was ashamed and could not bear to stay, so he fled during the night. At dawn, the other villager looked inside the jar and found that it was filled with refined silver, which had melted and poured onto the ground in sheets. However, I say that this story is only for amusement and may not be true. But Gao Meicun firmly insists that it is not a lie. Therefore, things have their own owners, and it is not by human effort that one can obtain them. This is clearly and certainly true."

Meicun also recounted that there was a man named Jiang Ting who made a living by trading cloth. He always carried a spotted dog with him. One day, while traveling alone, he met an old man on the road who called him to stop. Jiang Ting asked, "Why do you call me if we do not know each other?" The old man immediately knelt and said, "I am a fox. In a past life, I wronged you, and three days from now, your spotted dog will sever my throat. My fate is already determined, and I dare not escape death. However, I secretly think that over a hundred years have passed, and you have been reborn as a human, while I have fallen into the form of a fox. If I must kill a fox to settle the score, what good would that do you? Moreover, you no longer remember the incident of being killed, and even if you accidentally kill a fox, it would not bring you any satisfaction. Would you be willing to accept my daughter as redemption?" Jiang Ting replied, "I dare not bring a fox into my home, nor do I wish to take advantage of your danger to seize a human woman. I will forgive you, but how can I prevent the dog from eventually attacking you?" The old man said, "You just write a prescription by hand, stating that 'someone in a past life was wronged, and I voluntarily seek to have this debt erased.' I will present this to the gods, and then the dog will not attack. Karmic debtors must resolve their debts themselves, and the gods will not violate this." Just then, the old man had a notebook, paper, and ink, and he immediately wrote the prescription and gave it to Jiang Ting. The old man was overjoyed and left. After seven or eight years, Jiang Ting was trading cloth and crossing the Yangtze River when he suddenly encountered a violent storm. The sails could not be lowered, and the boat was about to capsize. He saw a person climb directly to the top of the mast and cut the ropes, causing the sails to fall. It looked like the old man, but he vanished in an instant. Everyone said, "This fox can repay a favor." I said, "This fox has no way to save himself, so how could he save someone thousands of miles away? It must be that the gods extended his lifespan out of compassion and sent this fox."

Zhou Taiyu said, there was a man named Liu Zhe who first had intimate relations with a fox woman, and thus took her as his second wife. She behaved just like a normal person, was filial to her in-laws, and harmonious with her sisters-in-law, treating the children from his previous marriage as if they were her own, which is especially difficult for people to do. She grew old and died, and her body did not change into a fox shape. Some say she was originally a runaway woman, and the truth was concealed, pretending she was a fox. Others say she was actually a fox, transformed into human form, but had not attained immortality, so she aged and died, having already shed her original form, hence her body was like a human when she died. I say: none of these explanations are correct; it was her heart that sustained her. The form of a person can be transformed by the heart. The Empress Xi became a python, and the Prefect Feng became a tiger, because their hearts were already those of pythons and tigers, so their forms were also those of pythons and tigers. According to old legends, the fox was originally a promiscuous woman named Azi, if a person has a fox heart, then a person can become a fox, if a fox has a human heart, then a fox can also become a human. Those who wear black robes and yellow hats may sit and transform without falling; loyal ministers and chaste women may have bodies that do not decay, all of these are sustained by their spirit.

This fox, upon death, does not change its form — thus, its kind is indeed a true mate! Taiyu said: Indeed so. It is said that when Liu first took in a fox, he could not help but feel doubt and fear. The fox said: "The woman merely desires a proper household. If a fox can be a proper wife, how is it different from a human? Moreover, people only know to fear foxes, yet do not realize that they often become companions to foxes themselves. That woman's behavior is without restraint, causing illness and shortening her life — how is this different from a fox's act of siphoning vitality? That woman's trespassing through walls and secret meetings in hidden places, seeking secret pleasures — how is this different from a fox's promiscuity? That woman's long tongue sowing discord, causing strife within the family — how is this different from a fox's seductive deception? That woman's secret theft of property, giving it privately to those she favors — how is this different from a fox's theft? That woman's loud scolding and domineering behavior, causing unrest among all six relatives — how is this different from a fox's haunting disturbance? Why, then, do you not fear *her*, yet fear *me*?" This fox's ambition is to surpass humans. Thus, it is fitting that it begins among humans and ends among humans. As for the many kinds of foxes spoken of, they are part of the six realms of rebirth, created solely by the mind. One must fear that when the eyes close at death, one may inevitably fall into that realm.

In ancient times, the system of hereditary stipends and official positions meant that the head of the clan must establish a successor, while the collateral branches did not perform ancestral rites, hence there was no explicit requirement in ritual texts for collateral branches to establish successors. Meng Pi is not known to have a successor, and neither is there any record of Confucius establishing one for him, which was not because he was the eldest son. Establishing successors for collateral branches is to ensure that widows can maintain their resolve and not be left without someone to offer sacrifices for them. It is like a scholar who originally had no one to compose an epitaph for him, but Xuan Benfu was the first to do so because he died in his duty. A young boy should normally be considered a child who died young, but Wang Qi was not because he defended the state. Rituals arise from righteousness and thus cannot be abandoned. All collateral branches without successors have become a precedent that cannot be abandoned, and family difficulties often arise from this. Dong Qujiang said that in Dongchang, there were three brothers; the second brother died first and left no successor. The eldest brother wanted his son to succeed, and the youngest brother also wanted his son to succeed. The eldest brother said, "The younger brother should yield to the elder brother."

The younger brother said: "The elder brother's son is young while his own son is grown, and the younger brother should yield to the elder brother." They litigated for a year, and in the end, the elder brother won. The younger brother became very angry, and his accumulated resentment turned into illness. When he was gravely ill, he told his son, "I will seek justice in the underworld." Soon after, he fell into a daze and remained unconscious for half a day before regaining consciousness. He said, "Is it not just the officials of the sun (worldly) who are perverse, but the officials of the underworld are even more so." His soul then wandered in the underworld, where he presented this case. An underworld official questioned him, saying, "Are you saying your elder brother has no heirs? Your elder brother already has heirs. You are only fighting over property." He used the metaphor of seeing an animal in the wild, where two people chase it; the faster one gets it first. Why do you litigate? They did not handle it at all. The dispute over succession was originally about property, but he angrily spoke to me about ancestral worship. How can he not understand things to this extent? Place many papers and pens in my coffin; I will appeal to the Heavenly Emperor. Is this truly someone who does not understand until death?" Qujiang said, "I still appreciate that he did not hide his true feelings."

During the year of Geng Mao, when I was serving as an examiner in Shanxi, Tao Xudong, as the magistrate of LePing, served as a co-examiner. Before the examination papers were submitted, we casually talked about matters of immortals and ghosts. Tao Xudong recounted a story: a friend of his once traveled to Nan Yue, reaching a deep and secluded part of the mountains and valleys, where he saw a woman sitting on a stone beside a flower. Having heard much about the stories of Zhi Qiong and Lan Xiang, he rushed to approach her. The woman covered her face with a silk fan and said, "You and I have no karmic connection, so we should not get too close." He replied, "Karmic connections arise from causes. Cannot we plant this cause here?" The woman said, "Causes must be predestined, and karmic connections require mutual agreement. Not just because one wants to plant it can it be planted." She then disappeared mysteriously, and one suspects it was a fairy. I believe the karmic connections of desire, as described by this woman, are correct. However, for the karmic connections of grudges and grievances, one person can plant them without the other's consent, and this must be considered separately. In Datong, Song Zhongshu said that once at home, he played with a medium board. The medium board moved, and he asked for the name of the deity. It was written: "I originally lived in the deep mountains, traveling among the white clouds. The wind suddenly became brisk, and the clouds moved like flowing water. I happened to follow along, drifting here and there. A quiet thatched cottage in a remote village, even a brief rest is delightful. Do not ask my name; I have forgotten it long ago. And ask how far this door is from the mountain." After writing, the medium board stopped moving, and perhaps this was a true deity.

The Battle of Hehe Hutongnoer saw soldiers who perished among the barbarians. In the year Yi Hai, the Ili region was pacified, and these soldiers, upon seeing the flags of the great army, surrendered in exchange for their lives and were resettled in Urumqi, where they were called "Little Li Ling." These soldiers did not know who Li Ling was and responded indifferently. Over time, they eventually forgot their original names. During the years Ji Chou and Geng Yin, I was in Urumqi and still encountered these people, who were now elderly. They recounted that in the Dzungar region, they had been sold to several masters, all of whom were shepherds. As the great army approached, in the eighth month of the previous year, they camped in a valley at night and saw a firelight in the sandy desert. The various tribes of the Western Regions often raided each other, and they suspected it was a band of robbers. They climbed a hill to look and saw a giant, about ten feet tall, dressed in elegant and proper attire, with attendants holding torches in front. There were about seventy to eighty of them, who then lined up and the giant stood upright and bowed toward the east with great reverence. They knew it was a mountain spirit. At that time, the Dzungar rebellion had just begun to subside, and they had heard of Amursana's request for military aid from the Qing. They secretly thought that perhaps this place would soon become part of the empire, so the spirits were already facing east in anticipation? Later, it turned out to be true. At the time, they did not know that the eighth month was the emperor's birthday, but after returning to the right path, they realized that the resounding imperial voice was a distant celebration of the emperor's long life.

Li Xuan, the military officer from Gansu, was skilled in the art of observing plum blossoms (觀梅) associated with Master Kangjie (康節). His predictions often came true. When he was stationed in the military camp during the pacification of the Western Regions, he accompanied Grand Secretary Wen Zhengming. One day, a soldier accidentally set fire to the dry grass in front of the camp, burning an area about a zhang wide. Wen Zhengming asked Li Xuan to interpret the omen. Li Xuan said, "There is no other reason; you will have a secret imperial edict within a few days." The fire, once it reached the dry grass, spread the fastest, symbolizing the urgency of the dispatch; the smoke rising upward symbolized the transmission of information to higher authorities, indicating a secret imperial edict. All secret imperial edicts are accompanied by the burning of grass. Wen Zhengming said, "I do not have anything that requires a secret imperial edict." Li Xuan replied, "The fire was set unintentionally, not premeditated." And indeed, within a few days, Wen Zhengming received a secret imperial edict. When it came to predicting a person's entire life, Li Xuan would pick up an object at random, or someone else would pick up the same object, but the interpretation would differ. In Beijing, a scholar picked up a smoke pipe and said, "It stores fire and its smoke, which breathes through the inside. You are not an official in a minor or insignificant department, but your position is not very prominent, and it still depends on others to promote you." When asked how long he would hold his position, Li Xuan said, "Do not be surprised by my direct words; the fire is not large, and once it is extinguished, it becomes ashes, meaning the heat will not last long." When asked about his lifespan, Li Xuan shook his head and said, "Copper vessels can last a long time, but I have never seen a smoke pipe that lasts for a hundred years." The scholar left in anger. After a year, everything turned out exactly as Li Xuan had predicted. Later, another official joined the gathering and also picked up the same smoke pipe. Li Xuan observed and said, "The fire in the smoke pipe has already gone out, so you must be an official in a minor or insignificant department. However, placing it on the bed indicates that you have experienced a pause, but picking it up again means that you will encounter promotion once more. In the future, there will still be times of success, but the nature of that success will be similar to what you have now." Indeed, as Li Xuan had predicted, the official's career saw a resurgence.

Wu Huishu brought a small hanging scroll, the paper color suggesting it was an artifact from over a century ago. He said he obtained it from the market at Changchun Temple. The brushwork was sparse and rough, half of it brushed with light ink to depict mist and haze, and the other half depicting water ripples. In the center, there was only a small boat, with one woman sitting under the awning and another woman rowing the boat. In the upper right corner, written in dense ink, was a poem: "Sand plovers live together in the misty clouds, I do not remember how many times the lotus fragrance has changed. It is oddly enough that Mazu has done too many things, yet she still knows the vicissitudes of the world." The inscription read, "The person in the painting painted and inscribed it herself." There was no date or seal. Some believed it was a divine brush, but the handwriting of a female deity, how could it be obtained by a human? Others thought it was a wandering woman, yet it should not be such a worldly foreign language. It is suspected to be a female Taoist nun from the late Ming dynasty, who hid from war in a fishing village or crab house and painted this picture herself. There are no old inscriptions or comments, making it difficult to confirm. Wu Huishu asked me to inscribe it, but I could not find a way to write about it. I put it aside for several days and returned it. Wu Huishu died in Sichuan, and it is unknown whether this painting still exists today.

Uncle An from Shizhai said that Old Cheng, a man from the village, had a daughter who was graceful and beautiful. One day, while buying cosmetics at the gate, she was courted by a young man from the neighborhood. She wept and told her parents, but they were afraid of his violence and did not dare to confront him. However, she was filled with anger and resentment that could not be dispelled, and she often felt depressed. Therefore, she befriended a fox, and they would drink together whenever the fox came. One day, the fox was surprised by her gloomy demeanor and asked her the reason, to which she confessed the truth. The fox silently left, and later that young man returned to her door, saw her smiling by the gate, and gradually engaged in soft words with her, leading to a tryst in a small garden or empty house. At the time of parting, she wept and could not bear to leave, and they agreed to elope. The young man then came to her door at night and led her away, to prevent Old Cheng from pursuing and demanding. He threatened her with a knife, saying, "If you dare to reveal it, you will die." After several days, there was no news, and Old Cheng realized that he was hiding the matter, feeling very pleased and indulging in excessive intimacy without restraint. Later, the daughter gradually revealed signs of being a demon, and it was known that she was a fox spirit. Despite this, they were very fond of each other, and he could not get rid of her. After a year, she fell ill with tuberculosis, and only her last breath remained. The fox spirit then left, and despite all efforts to treat her, she miraculously survived, but their wealth had been depleted. The couple had to live outdoors, and Old Cheng was weak and unable to work, living off the money from the trysts. He was no longer the same fierce man as before. Old Cheng did not know the reason and told the fox about it. The fox said, "It was I who sent a clever maid to play with him, and she must have taken your daughter's form, for otherwise, it would not have been enough to lure him. I had to make him think it was one of us to prevent ruining your daughter's reputation. Since she was in danger and I spared her, her crime did not warrant death, and I punished her sufficiently. Do not be unhappy anymore." Is this fox the equivalent of Zhu Jia and Guo Jie? While not being excessively harsh, this is something even Zhu Jia and Guo Jie could not do.

According to Cun Sun Shubao, in the winter of Xin Hai, he visited Ge Xianlian Zhongfang with his cousin Dao Yuan. On the table, they found dozens of new poems, among which were two quatrains: A good match in hand, yet the matter is again thwarted, The spring breeze vainly locks the double doors. Indeed, there is a dragon-rider husband in the world, At midnight, he suddenly flew through the wall. Not only do the beautiful women compete with Yin and Xing, Even the immortals envy their grace. Please look at the scratching claws of the Fairy Magu, The transformation is clearly that of Jilin. They did not understand what these lines meant and asked Ge Xianlian about the story behind the poem. Ge Xianlian said: "Yesterday, I met Zhang Junfu from Cangzhou, who told me about a certain man from Nanpi who was over twenty years old and had not yet married. Suddenly, two beautiful women came to him at night. When asked where they came from, they said they were foxes, destined by fate to be his wife and husband. Though they could not bring him fortune, they would not bring him misfortune. The man was captivated by their beauty and decided not to marry another. When someone advised him against it, he replied: 'The foxes have treated me well, and after living together for a long time, I have no illness. They are not bewitching me, and they even said they would give me a son, which would not harm my lineage. I truly cannot bear to betray their trust.'"

A Powerful Clan Marries Off a Daughter A powerful clan from the imperial consort's family arranged a marriage for their daughter. When a man named Jia heard of her exceptional beauty, he abruptly broke his previous engagement. At the moment the candles were lit in the bridal chamber, a sudden and powerful noise, like wind and thunder, shook the eaves and roof. With one hand, a large figure broke through the window and entered, as big as a winnowing basket, and seized Jia and took him away. The next day, Jia searched everywhere but found no trace of himself. After seven or eight days, a few children reported that a certain shrine had a sound like a cow's breath—this was a northern custom, where any shrine without a priest or caretaker, fearing that wandering beggars might rest there, often left a small hole in the door and placed an incense burner there. Peering through the hole, it seemed as if a naked person was lying down, but it was unclear who it was. Opening the door to look inside, Jia found himself there, already in a confused and unconscious state. Various treatments were administered, and he barely survived. Since then, the fox girl did not appear again, and Jia's family, fearing the fox girl's retribution, eventually divorced him. These two poems record this event. The fox has already attained spiritual power, and its actions differ from those of humans. Although Jia had married, what harm could it have caused? Yet, in its sudden and swift movements, it displayed its evil force, almost killing him. The fox can indeed be described as jealous and fierce. However, since there was no previous agreement, the fault lies with the fox. Having been careless at the beginning and made an agreement, and then not ending well by breaking it, it provoked itself to cause mischief, and it has its own reasons. Therefore, it cannot be blamed for the fox.

Bridges in the north are equipped with railings, only to prevent falling. In Fujian, where rain is frequent, all bridges are covered with houses to shelter pedestrians. Qiu Er Tian said that once, a person encountered rain at night and rushed to sit in one of these bridge houses. There was an official carrying documents, escorting several people who sought shelter under the house. The sound of their shackles clinking could be heard, indicating they were officials recording prisoners. Fearing the situation, the person dared not approach but shrank into a corner. One of the prisoners cried incessantly, and the official shouted, "Now knowing fear, how much better it would have been not to do it back then?" The prisoner wept and said, "I was misled by my teacher. My teacher taught daily, dismissing all teachings about the retribution of gods and spirits as the false words of Buddhism. I believed his words, secretly thinking that cunning could be deep, mending could be skillful, and thus various things could be done as desired, avoiding exposure for life. After a hundred years, the soul returns to the void, in darkness and silence, neither praise nor blame is heard. What fear prevents me from indulging my desires? Not realizing that hell is not a lie, and there is indeed a King of the Underworld, only then realizing he was sold by him, thus regretting and grieving for himself."

A prisoner said, "Your downfall is due to believing in Confucianism, while I was misled by believing in Buddhism. According to Buddhist teachings, even if one commits evil deeds, merit can eliminate them. Even if one falls into hell, chanting scriptures can save them. I thought that before death, burning incense and making offerings, and after death, inviting monks to chant, were all within my capabilities. With the protection of Buddhist teachings, I could do anything and it would not be punished by the underworld. I did not realize that what is meant by sin and merit is based on the goodness or evil of one's actions, not the amount of money spent. Money is wasted, and the consequences of cooking and pounding cannot be avoided. If it were not for relying on Buddhism, how could I dare to indulge so much?" After speaking, he wept bitterly, and the other prisoners also cried. They then realized that he was not a human being. The Six Classics are all present, and it is not said that there are no ghosts or gods. The Three Baskets of teachings are not for the purpose of collecting money. Confucian scholars seek fame, and Buddhist monks seek profit. The corruption of both has reached such an extreme. Buddhism is a foreign religion, and the monks use it to make a living. This is not something to blame them for, but why must Confucian scholars do this as well?

Nai Ao, a woman from Wucheng, was widowed before the age of thirty. Her in-laws wanted to remarry her, but she swore by her death to remain faithful to her deceased husband. Enraged, her in-laws expelled her from their home and forced her to fend for herself. She endured a life of hardship and displacement, raising two sons and a daughter. All three of her children married but were deemed unworthy, leaving her alone and without support. Her only remaining relative, a granddaughter, became a nun, and Nai Ao lived in a Buddhist temple, barely surviving. She is now seventy-eight years old. She is the epitome of a young woman who vowed to maintain her chastity until her old age. I pity her virtue and sometimes provide her with assistance. My wife, Lady Ma, once casually remarked to me: "As the Grand Master of Ceremonies, you oversee the national recognition and honors for chaste and loyal women. Yet, this old woman has slipped through your fingers. Why is that?" I replied: "The state's regulations are strict, and the process for recognizing chaste and loyal women is formal. They must be nominated by schools in the prefectures and counties, which then submit their names to the central government. Only then can a formal petition be made to the Ministry of Rites, which must deliberate and decide based on public opinion. The Ministry of Rites is responsible for verifying and deciding on such matters, but it cannot independently seek out and recognize such women to prevent favoritism and abuse. It is like the examiners who hold the power to judge the examination papers but cannot discover and admit talented individuals who have not yet been tested. This old woman has been away from her hometown for a long time, and there is no one to nominate her. In the vast city of Beijing, who would know that among the many displaced people, there is this lonely widow? She is a pearl lost in the sea, and this is the reason why. Could I have done something and failed to do it? Thinking of the hidden virtues of the past, they are often recorded in popular literature to bring out their hidden glory. Therefore, I have summarized the main points and included them in my miscellaneous notes. Although the book originally records strange events, it不免为例不纯, but in terms of promoting and upholding moral and social norms, it is consistent in its purpose."

The Record of Unfounded Tales, Volume Fifteen, Part One I have a strong inclination towards solitude, yet I cannot be at ease with myself. Books, scrolls, brushes, and inkstones have been with me since my youth, and I have never been away from them for more than ten days. Before the age of thirty, I studied textual criticism, and where I sat, classical texts surrounded me like fish surrounded by otters; after thirty, I competed with the world through writing, refining and polishing my prose, often thinking all night; after fifty, I led the editing of secret books and returned to studying textual criticism. Now that I am old, I no longer have the same enthusiasm as before, but I occasionally pick up paper and ink to record old news, merely to pass the time. Thus, I have completed the "Summer Record of Luan Yang" and other three books, and now have this collection. Reflecting on past authors, such as Wang Chong and Ying Zhongyuan, who cited classics and ancient texts, were broad and eloquent; Tao Yuanming, Liu Jingshu, and Liu Yiqing, who spoke briefly and naturally, were profound and far-reaching. I truly dare not rashly compare myself to these scholars. However, the main purpose is to conform to the moral and social norms. If one harbors grievances and reverses right and wrong, as Hui Tai and Chen Shansheng did, then I am confident that I have not done so. Recently, Sheng Zisongyun wanted to engrave this work, so I wrote a few lines at the beginning, mainly obtained from hearsay. Thus, adopting a phrase from Zhuangzi, it is named "Cuwang Tingzhi." On the twenty-fifth day of the seventh month, in the year Gengchen of the Qianlong era, the chess-playing hermit inscribed it himself.

A servant of Magistrate Feng Jingshan suddenly went mad, beating himself and speaking in delirious words: "Though I have lived a difficult life and died, I was still of the gentry. What kind of lowly person are you, so arrogant as to not yield the road? I punish you now to make you understand." Feng Jingshan went to see him and said, "Are you appearing in broad daylight? The realms of the living and the dead are different; it may not be appropriate according to reason. Are you invisible? Then you can see these people, but they cannot see you, so how can you avoid them?" The servant suddenly fell into a deep sleep and woke up after a short while, returning to normal. One of his students, Geng Shouyu from Tongcheng, was introverted and self-disciplined but enjoyed arguing about proper etiquette. I once discussed this matter with him, saying, "Scholars often act arrogantly to gain respect, calling it self-respect. But true self-respect comes from one's own actions and moral integrity. If one's morality is not ashamed before the sages, even if a king or nobleman sweeps the ground for you, it cannot bring honor; even if you are a low-ranking official or a laborer, it cannot bring disgrace. What is truly valuable lies within oneself, so external matters are not worth considering."

If one must regard external status as important or unimportant, it means waiting for others to respect you in order to be honored, and if they do not respect you, you are disgraced. Servants, laborers, and slaves can all control your honor and disgrace; is this not a sign of seeing oneself as too insignificant? Shou Yu said: "You were born into wealth and privilege, so you hold such views." Poor scholars should not be arrogant because of their poverty, for otherwise they lose their dignity and are further despised by others. I say: This is the view of Tian Zifang, which Zhu Xi has already refuted. The arrogance does not need to be debated; even if we consider his argument, it still holds that morality is inherently important and should not be compromised by poverty or low social status. It is not that one has no morality, but rather that poverty and low social status can make one arrogant. If we accept your argument, then beggars are even poorer than you, slaves are even lower in social status than you, and they all rise up to be arrogant toward you. Can one say that you have established a good character? My teacher Chen Baiya once personally inscribed a couplet on his study: "If one can be content with what they have, their heart will always be satisfied; when one has no desires, their character naturally rises." This is truly a fundamental and insightful statement, and these seven characters can endure for a thousand years.

Gong Jisheng said that in the year Qianlong's Jiwu (1779), while he was staying at Lingyou Palace in Beijing, he met a Taoist priest with whom he would often share cups of wine. One day, they went to watch a play together, and the Taoist priest invited him along, which he happily accepted. As dusk fell, they returned, and the Taoist priest bowed and said, "I thank you all for your kind intentions, but I have no way to repay you. How about watching a puppet show tonight?" At night, they entered the Taoist priest's living room, which had only a large square table. Near the edges, there were some wine and fruit, and in the center was a chessboard. The Taoist priest called a boy to close the outer door and asked the guests to sit around the table. After they had drunk a few rounds of wine, the Taoist priest clapped his ruler once, and several small figures, about eight or nine inches tall, appeared on the chessboard. They began to perform a play, making sounds like those of children aged four or five. The men and women were dressed up, and the intonation and plot were identical to those in the theater. One act ended—legend has it that a "one act" is called a "one fold," an old term first seen in Wu Renshen's *Dictionary Supplement*, which states it should be read as "one fold." This usage has been followed for a long time and cannot be abandoned, so I also follow the common practice in writing it—and suddenly disappeared. Then, a few more figures appeared and performed another act.

The audience was both amazed and delighted, drinking merrily until midnight. The Taoist priest then ordered the child to place several hundred chicken eggs and a few jars of white wine on a table outside the door. Suddenly, the music stopped, and all that could be heard were the sounds of eating and drinking. When they asked how he had done it, the Taoist priest replied: "Anyone who has mastered the Five Thunder Spell can command fox spirits. These foxes can change their size, so I sent them to perform this play for a night of entertainment. However, they can only be used for service; if you command them to steal things, harm people, or summon fox women for immoral purposes, divine retribution will come immediately." The crowd, having seen something they had never seen before, begged to see it again the next night. The Taoist priest agreed, but when they arrived the next evening at his residence, he had already left with his child early in the morning.

The diviner Dong Xijian once said that he had seen two people playing chess. One of them pre-marked a chess diagram, noting moves such as "black nine three, white six five," and sealed it in a bamboo box. After the game was finished, he opened the box to check, and not one move was wrong. Yet, no one knew what method he used. According to the records of predestined events, during the Kaiyuan period, there was a man named Wang Sheng from Xuanping Lane who divined for Li Kui's advancement. He gave Li Kui a sealed envelope containing dozens of pages, instructing him to open it on the day he was appointed as Left Censor. Later, Li Kui was recommended by Li Qiu and ordered by the prime minister to write essays. The topics were: an essay on the purple silk pouch for holding dew, a reply to a letter from Tibet, and a memorial on behalf of Nan Yue presenting a white peacock. Li Kui completed all three essays from noon to evening, with eight strokes crossed out and two lines added on the side. The next day, he was appointed as Left Censor. A few days later, he opened Wang Sheng's sealed envelope and found that all three essays were inside, with the same annotations. This shows that such a method has existed since ancient times, and this man just happened to have learned it. The act of holding a pen and thinking, placing pieces on the chessboard, even those involved in the game cannot predict or control in advance, yet the diviner could know it in advance. It is a matter that we do ourselves, yet still cannot escape fate. Those who seek by clever means or force, who strive daily with their hearts, is this something that cannot be stopped?

Exiled convict Gang Chaorong from Ürümqi reported that two men traveled to Tibet for trade, each riding a donkey. While traveling through the mountains, they lost their way and could not distinguish east from west. Suddenly, more than ten people leaped down from a cliff. They suspected these were bandits—known as "jiaba" in the western regions, similar to the "Mahaqin" of the Oirat Mongols. As the figures approached, they were seen to be about seven or eight feet tall, covered in fur that was either yellow or green, with faces that were partly human and partly not. Their speech was a jumble of sounds, incomprehensible, leading them to believe they were encountering some sort of supernatural beings. Thinking they were doomed, they trembled and lay prostrate on the ground. The more than ten people then laughed at each other, showing no signs of attacking or devouring them. Instead, they carried the men under their arms and led their donkeys away. They brought the men to a mountain hollow, where they set them down on the ground. One of the donkeys was pushed into a pit, while the other was killed with a knife, roasted over a fire, and then eaten while sitting in a circle. The two men were also seated, and each was given some meat in front of them. Observing that there seemed to be no ill intent, and being hungry and exhausted, they ate the meat. After they had eaten, all of the more than ten people rubbed their bellies and let out cries that sounded like the neighing of horses. Two of them, each carrying one of the men, flew over several steep mountain ranges as swiftly as monkeys and birds, and delivered them to the side of an official road. Each man was given a stone, and they vanished instantly. The stones were as large as melons and were all turquoise. They were taken back and sold, fetching a price many times what they had lost. This incident occurred between the years of Yiyou and Bingxu, and Chaorong once saw one of the men, who spoke about the event in great detail. It is unclear whether these beings were mountain spirits or forest ghosts, but judging by their actions, they did not seem to be malevolent. Perhaps there are such wild people living in the secluded and vast valleys, who have never interacted with the outside world since ancient times.

Zhangzhou is known for producing crystal, said to come in all five colors, though red crystals have never been seen, making purple the most prized. There is also something called golden crystal, which is distinctly different from yellow crystal and the hardest to obtain. If it is occasionally obtained, it is also as large as a long bean or as small as a melon seed. Only the family of the Duke of Haicheng possesses a three-legged toad, which can be used as a fan pendant. It looks like molten fine gold, transparent and clear, making it a rare treasure. Yang Jingxu, when he was serving as the governor of Ting, Zhang, and Long, once told me about it. However, it is also said so, and I have not seen it with my own eyes; I will record it to broaden the knowledge of strange things.

Mr. Chen Lai Zhang, my relative by marriage, once acquired an ancient inkstone with a phoenix engraved in the clouds, which was inscribed by Liang Yüanfeng, a high-ranking official. The inscription reads: "Its song is clear and resonant, it soars through the clouds, a sign of auspiciousness from the Tui clan, its song brings prosperity, it travels the four directions, spreading virtue and light." This was in the third month of the leap year in the year 1673. In 1680, the inkstone was stolen. In 1697, Mr. Chen's second son heard about the theft and managed to recover the inkstone. In June 1703, he asked me to inscribe it again, and I complied. The new inscription reads: "Lost and then found again, like a precious jade and a great bow, who could have made this happen? An old thing happens to be found. Like a powerful phoenix soaring through the clouds, it swiftly disappears into the distant sky, but when it returns, it will surely rest on the phoenix tree. Descendants of noble families often scatter and discard the personal belongings and legacies of their ancestors. I once saw a matchmaker carrying several jade pendants for sale, claiming they were from the household of a certain official. The outer wrapping was made of old paper, which turned out to be four pages of a Northern Song dynasty printing of the *Gongyang Commentary*. This made me feel disappointed for a long time. I heard that an item lost by my ancestors was bought back after eight years, and then asked someone to inscribe it to ensure its transmission. The way people think and act is vastly different."

In the village of Dongjiazhuang, there was a tenant farmer named Ding Jin. He had a son named Erniu and a daughter who took in Cao Ning as her husband, who helped with the work and got along very well. Erniu had a son named Sanbao, and his sister also had a daughter, who stayed with her mother's family and was named Si Bao. They were born in the same year and month, just a few days apart. The sisters-in-law carried each other, fed each other, and were married even before they left the cradle. Sanbao and Si Bao loved each other very much. As they grew older, they were never more than a step apart. The family did not know how to distinguish between proper and improper conduct, and when the children played, they would often point and say, "This is your husband, this is your wife." Although the children did not understand what the words meant, they became familiar with them. By the age of seven or eight, they began to understand things, but they still slept and woke up with Erniu's mother, without any concern for propriety. During the years from the Xinchou year of Kangxi to the Guimao year of Yongzheng, there were frequent poor harvests, and both Ding Jin and his wife died. Cao Ning first wandered to Beijing, where he was poor and could not support himself, so he pawned Si Bao to the home of a man named Chen, who was a doctor. No one knew his name, only that he was from Jiangnan. Erniu followed later, and the doctor needed a young servant, so he also pawned Sanbao to his household, but warned them not to say that Sanbao and Si Bao were husband and wife. The doctor's household rules were strict, and he often beat Si Bao, causing Sanbao to cry secretly, and when he beat Sanbao, Si Bao would do the same. The doctor suspected them, and then pawned Si Bao to the Zheng family, possibly the one known as the "Mink Skin Zheng," and expelled Sanbao. Sanbao returned to the old matchmaker and was taken in by another family as a young servant.

After a long time, I gradually learned the whereabouts of the Four Treasures. Then, through connections, I entered the Zheng household. A few days later, I saw the Four Treasures holding each other and crying bitterly; they were around thirteen or fourteen years old at the time. Zheng was surprised and then lied, saying they were siblings meeting. Zheng, thinking their names and birth order were linked, did not suspect anything. However, they were separated, and they could only communicate through eye contact when they met. In a year of good harvest, the two officials named Niu traveled to the capital to redeem their children. They made persistent efforts to find the Zheng family, and Zheng finally learned that they were originally a married couple. Zheng felt deep compassion and sympathy for them and wanted to help them get married through the ancient ritual while they continued to serve in their current roles.

The tutor, Yan Mo, was a scholar who taught, but he was unaware of the changes in customs and laws over time. He openly and strongly opposed the situation, saying: "Marriage between close relatives by marriage is prohibited by wedding rites and is also forbidden by law. Violating this prohibition will incur divine punishment. Although the host's intention may be good, we scholars who read books should take the moral education of society as our responsibility. To see violations of reason and disruption of proper social relationships and not to oppose them is to assist in doing evil, which is not the conduct of a gentleman." He argued strongly for or against the issue. Zheng was gentle and weak-willed, and Niu Cao were simple-minded and unsophisticated. When they heard that violating the law would result in severe punishment, they were all intimidated and stopped. Later, Si Bao was sold as a concubine, and she fell ill and died within a few months. San Bao went mad and ran away, and no one knew what became of her. Some said: "Although Si Bao was forced to leave, she mutilated herself and wept, and in fact, she did not share a bedroom with the man who bought her. It is a pity that we do not know the details. If that is the case, then these two will meet again in heaven and on earth, and they will surely not close their eyes to each other." However, Yan Mo committed this evil deed, and it is unclear what his intentions were or what the ultimate outcome would be. Nevertheless, the divine principles are clear, and there will be no good retribution for him. Others said: "This is not due to clinging to ancient practices, nor is it for the sake of reputation. It is likely that he coveted Si Bao and wanted to serve himself with her. If that is the case, then the setting of hell is precisely for such a person."

During the Qianlong era, in the year 1778, the Grand Canal had low water levels, and the grain ships were so closely packed that they could not move forward. To appease the deities, a performance of plays and rituals was held. All the transport officials were present, and they were in the middle of performing the act of *Jing Chai Ji* where a character throws herself into the river. Suddenly, the actor playing Qian Yulan knelt for a long time, wailing loudly, with tears flowing continuously as she mumbled incessantly in a Min dialect, her voice indistinct and unintelligible. It was known that a ghost had possessed her, and the officials asked the reason for this. The possessed actor could not understand their questions, and when given paper and a pen, she shook her head as if to say she didn't know how to read. She only pointed to the sky and ground, bowed her head, and cried bitterly. There was nothing to be done, so they supported her onto the shore, where she still sobbed and jumped around until the people dispersed and it stopped.
