After a while, she gradually came to her senses and said she had suddenly seen a woman holding her head and emerging from the water. She was frightened to the point of losing her soul, dazed and drunk-like, and did not know anything that happened afterward. This must be the soul of a person trapped in the water, seeing all the officials gathered, so she came out to cry for justice. However, no one saw her form, and the language was not understood. They sent good swimmers to search for the body, but there was no trace. The boatmen had no recently lost women, and there was nothing to investigate further. They prepared a document with multiple seals and burned it at the City God Temple. After four or five days, a waterman committed suicide without reason, perhaps this was the man who killed the woman, was this a punishment by the gods?

Zhen Taishou Shen, a cautious and careful magistrate, once mentioned that several friends of his had discussed Fujian poetry and expressed great dissatisfaction with Lin Ziyu. One night, as he was going to bed, he heard the sound of a brush and inkstone making a clicking noise, which he initially thought was a mouse. The next day, he found two lines of writing on the desk: "Like a proclamation in the rain over an ancient pond at dusk, the star of ritual opens a cold hall." It seemed that the likes of Qian Lang and others had not touched upon these themes, and could everything be considered a copy of Tang dynasty models of Jin dynasty calligraphy? At the time, several people were sleeping in the same room, and their handwriting did not match. Among the people in the room, no one else could write such words, leading Zhen to realize that scholars' competition for fame continued even after death, and the story of Zheng Kangcheng becoming a ghost was likely not unfounded. Huang Xiaohua said that in the western city, there was a spirit-medium who wrote poetry on a table. The poem read: "The west wind rustles, and the leaves of trees fly; heart-wrenching flowers fall, and geese arrive rarely. A Wu woman sits alone in her cold, secluded room at dusk, still wearing a delicate white silk garment." No one understood the meaning of the poem, so the spirit-medium wrote again: "Recently, I passed by a certain house and saw a young concubine who was locked in an empty room, abandoned and separated from her lover. Her fate was predetermined, but her hunger and coldness are pitiful and touch the heart. Thus, I wrote this poem with compassion. I respectfully advise all of you: if you lack the ability to tame lions and elephants, do not lightly entertain this thought; it is also a meritorious deed. When asked for the spirit's name, it was written: 'Wuchun.' When asked again, there was no response. According to historical records, Li Wuchen was a famous courtesan from the late Ming dynasty, a native of Xiangfu. She drowned when the city of Kaifeng fell. She had a collection of poetry that was particularly elegant and striking. Her poem lamenting Wang Lienu read: 'I feel ashamed that I have tears, but dare not say that there is no one in the world.' Her words were well-phrased and especially praised by other authors.

Leftover sheaves and scattered grains, the benefit of widows, this practice is traced back to the Zhou Dynasty's classical texts. In the countryside, when the wheat is ripe, women and children gather in groups of dozens, following the reapers, collecting the remnants, which is called "picking up wheat." The farmers have made this a custom, and they no longer look back, just as it was in ancient times. Human feelings are becoming thinner, and people pursue profit like a flock of wild ducks; the remnants are not enough to satisfy, so there is much theft and plunder, and the original intention of the practice is gradually lost. Therefore, in April and May, women can be seen sleeping outdoors in the fields. A few people were traveling east of Jinghai after sunset, seeking the cool of the night, and they saw a place with lights in the distance. They went there to ask for a drink, and upon arriving, they found the courtyard splendid, with servants dressed in fine clothes, the hall lit with lamps and music playing, as if a banquet was being held. From afar, they saw three distinguished men seated on couches, just as they were serving wine and skewers. The group expressed their intention to seek shelter, and the gatekeeper relayed their request. The host nodded in agreement, and soon called them back, as if he was whispering something in their ear. The gatekeeper came out and led an elderly woman to whisper: "This place is a bit far from the city, and we cannot bring courtesans in a hurry. The master wants to choose three of the women who came with us, who are well-mannered, to serve wine and recommend sleep, and each will be given a hundred gold coins, while the others will also receive rewards." The woman conveyed the message, and the rewards should be doubled.

The elderly woman secretly informed the crowd, who, seeing an opportunity to profit, persuaded a young woman to comply with their request. They then led three men into the house, where the women bathed, adorned themselves, and changed into new clothes to serve as guests. All the women were placed in separate rooms, and there was an abundance of food and drink. By midnight, the three wealthy men each took one woman to a separate courtyard, and the entire household extinguished the candles and went to sleep. The women, weary from their journey, also slept deeply, unaware of what transpired. When they awoke late in the morning, they found the household empty, with only wild grass rustling in the wind stretching endlessly as far as the eye could see. They searched for the three women, who were all found naked in the grass, their new clothes nowhere to be seen, only their old garments thrown more than ten steps away, fortunately still intact. They noticed that the gold given to them was all fake paper ingots, leading them to suspect it was the work of a ghost. However, the food and drink were all real, causing them to doubt if it was the work of a fox spirit. Perhaps the place being near the sea, it was the work of sea monsters? Greed led to their deception, and they only gained a full meal. They thought of each other in a state of bewilderment, recalling that night, which was very much like a dream. My late brother Qinghu said: "Dance costumes and singing fans, various postures and manners, the prosperity of a moment, always follows the flowing water. On the day when the society of swans disperses, looking back vaguely, all past events are empty. It is the same as waking from a dream with the three women naked in the grass. How can it be only a mirage, a fleeting illusion?"

The Commander of Urumqi, Officer De Jun Leng'e, reported that during his time in Gansu, he witnessed a mutual accusation between two individuals in Zhangye. One accused the other of fabricating false accusations and slandering, while the other claimed to have concrete evidence. Upon investigating the matter, it was revealed that the two were cousins. The first individual had taken his wife out of the frontier, and the second had accompanied him. They traveled east of Gansu for dozens of miles and, at night, lost their way. They encountered a person who appeared to be a servant from a wealthy family. The servant said, "This is a remote path with few people. My master is not far from here. It would be better to seek shelter for the night and tomorrow, he will point the way to the main road." Following the servant's advice, they traveled for three or four miles and indeed found a small fort. The servant entered and stayed for a long time before coming out and gesturing, "The official calls you in." They entered through several doors and saw a person sitting in the main hall, who asked for their names and origins. The official said, "It is late at night and there is no dinner. You may only stay for the night. There is a small house by the door that can accommodate two people. The woman may sleep with the elderly woman and maid." After they went to sleep, they faintly heard a woman's call. In the dark, they went out to look, but could not find the door, and the call had also ceased. They mistakenly thought it was just their ears ringing. When they woke up, they found themselves in the open field. They hurriedly searched for the woman and found her half a mile away under a tree, naked and bound with her hands behind her back, with disheveled hair and a fallen hairpin. Her clothes were hanging on a high branch. She said that a maid had led her there with a lamp. There were several elegant houses, with several maids and elderly women present, and soon the master arrived, forcing them to sit together.

She refused to comply, and the maids and female servants together held her, undressing and tying her arms, placing her on the bed. She shouted loudly, but no one responded, and thus, she was defiled. As dawn approached, the master placed two objects by her neck, and the house suddenly disappeared, leaving her lying on sand and stones. Looking at the objects by her neck, she saw they were two ingots of silver, each inscribed with a weight of fifty taels. The reign title was Chongzhen, and the county name was Yuci. The earth had eroded them, turning them dark and dull, and they were truly cast more than a hundred years ago. A warned B not to speak, agreeing to divide them equally, but later, he broke the agreement. B angrily accused and disputed, and thus, the matter was revealed. Despite the couple's firm denial, when questioned about the origin of the silver, they claimed to have found it. When further questioned about the woman's bound injuries, they claimed it was scratched, and their words were evasive. It was suspected that B's words might not be false. The magistrate laughed and dismissed A, saying, "According to the law, found property should be turned over to the authorities, but out of consideration for your poverty, you may take it." He then glared at B, saying, "If what you say is false, then you are also considered a finder, and you must also turn it over to the authorities, and you will have no share. If what you say is true, then this is a gift from a ghost to reward A's wife, and you will have no share. If you say more, you will be beaten." He then ordered them to be driven out, dealing with them by ignoring them, which can be considered good. This is similar to the case of the woman who picked up wheat, where one used clever persuasion and the lure of profit to sway her heart, while the other used force and the promise of profit to quell his anger. In both cases, they gauged human nature and catered to their preferences, employing tactics that were roughly equal.

In the past, the sturgeon fish from Shenyang was valued as highly as gold, and it is still valued today. Swans were also once highly prized, but they are no longer so. In the Liao dynasty, the yellow rat, also known as the "Pilingbang," was highly valued, and in the Ming dynasty, people still held it in high regard, but it is no longer valued today. In the Ming dynasty, the bear and deer were highly valued, with the deer likely being raised in pens, and it is still valued today, but the bear is unknown, and even the wealthiest families have never seen it. The value of things varies according to the preferences of different times, and there is no fixed standard. When I was young, ginseng, coral, and lapis lazuli were not expensive, but they have become increasingly so over time. On the other hand, turquoise, green jade, and rhinoceros horn were once very expensive, but they have become less so. In Yunnan, the jade was not considered true jade at the time, but was merely called jade, and now it is regarded as a precious treasure, with its price far exceeding that of true jade. The gray mink was once valued for its white fur, but now it is valued for its black fur. The sable was once prized for its long fur, hence the term "feng mao" (plentiful fur), but now it is valued for its short fur. The silver mink was once slightly more expensive than the gray mink, but it was far less valuable than the heavenly horse, but now it is valued almost as highly as the sable. Coral was once prized for its bright red color like pomegranate flowers, but now it is valued for its pale red color like cherries. There are even those who value white shells like the chahui as the most precious. Over a span of fifty or sixty years, prices have changed so much, and how much more so over several centuries? Confucian scholars reading the *Rites of Zhou* and correcting the text secretly doubt it, because they do not understand the differences in customs over time.

Among the eight delicacies, bear paws and deer tails are the most common; camel humps come from beyond the frontier and are already rare. This is the single-humped wild camel, not the double-humped camel. The lips of the orangutan are only heard of by name. In the year 1765 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, Min, the military governor with the courtesy name Shao Yi, presented me with two pieces, packed in a brocade box, indicating a high level of care and respect. From the forehead to the chin, they were completely skinned and preserved, with the mouth, nose, eyes, and eyebrows all clearly visible, like a stage mask, not just the lips. The cook could not prepare them, and they were passed on to another friend, whose cook also did not recognize them. It was given to someone else again, and it is unknown who it ended up with, and no one has ever known how to cook it.

Mr. Li Yodan said that Dongguang Bi Gong, whose name I occasionally forget, was a Tongpan (deputy governor) in Guizhou when he was transporting supplies and encountered bandits, dying in a bloody battle on the battlefield. He once received an order to survey the boundaries of the Miao villages, and the local official held a grand banquet to welcome him. Each guest and host placed a porcelain lid cup in front of them. The local official held it up to look at it, and there was a creature inside like a scorpion, moving slowly and rotating. The translator said: This is a scorpion orchid; it lives when it blooms, and dies when it withers. It only eats the orchid's petals, which are very hard to find. Now that it's orchid season, they searched rocks and caves and found two of them, so they must present them alive to show utmost respect. Then they sprinkled a little salt into the cup and covered it with the lid. After a short while, they opened it to see that it had turned into water, clearly green and transparent, like jade. The orchid scent filled the nose, used as a substitute for vinegar, the fragrance permeated the mouth, and half a day later, the taste still lingered. It's a pity I didn't ask what its name was. Among the fruits of the Western Regions, grapes are most abundant in Turpan, and melons are most abundant in Hami. In the capital, green grapes are valued for their color, but in fact, green grapes are slightly ripe and not very sweet. As they ripen further, they turn yellow, then red, and fully ripe they turn purple and are very sweet. Prince Fu Songyan, whose name is Fu Zengge, a son-in-law of the Yifu Mansion, told me when he was stationed in Biezhuan. The tribute melons are only about six-tenths ripe. During the journey, they were packed and sealed, and the melon's aroma was naturally heated. By the time they reached the capital, they were about eight-tenths ripe. If they were already eight or nine-tenths ripe when packed, they would steam and rot.

I once asked King Su Laiman of Hami—son of Emin Hoja—a garden keeper in the capital city, about melon seeds. He said that for the first year, both the form and flavor of the melons are preserved; in the second year, the flavor has changed, but only the form is roughly similar; in the third year, both form and flavor have completely changed. Is it not because the local climate is different? He explained that the soil in this place is warm, the spring water is sweet, and there is no rain, which makes the melons flavorful. When planted in the interior, it should naturally decrease, but the method of nurturing is also incorrect. If the seeds from this year are planted next year, even in this place, the flavor is not good, because the qi (vital energy) is thin. The method should be to use ash to nurture the seeds, storing them in a dry and damp-free empty warehouse. They can be used after three to five years, and the longer the years, the better, because the qi (vital energy) is full. If nurtured for fourteen to fifteen years, only the king's garden has it; the common people cannot wait, nor can they store it for a long time without it deteriorating. His words seem to be close to reason, but the method of nurturing with ash must have rules and also have what is suitable and unsuitable. Perhaps if China were to do it according to their own ideas, it might not be able to achieve what was said.

Qiu Chaoran, the compiler, recounts that when Yang Qinque Gong was young, he would often visit the village school. There was a woman in green clothing who would occasionally peep through a gap in the wall to watch him. Sometimes, if he happened to dodge inside, she would always glance back with a smile, as if they had agreed with a look. Yang Qinque Gong, however, never glanced sideways at her. One day, she picked up a stone and threw it at him, saying, "Such beautiful skin, yet containing foolish bones." Yang Qinque Gong bowed and replied, "Digging holes and leaning on walls, I truly do not understand. How about finding someone who is not foolish?" The woman suddenly stared straight at him and said, "How can you seek revenge from me? Just wait until the next life." She let her hair down, stuck out her tongue, and left. From then on, she was never seen again. This shows that one's heart is upright; even an angry ghost has no way to harm him. It also shows that a great statesman, at a young age, had already established himself in this way. Mr. Wang Zhongying of Hejian, Mr. Li Wenzheng of Anxi, changed his courtesy name to Zhongtui, but the original name had been in use for a long time, and nobody called him by the new name. His given name was Zhirui, and he was a high-ranking disciple of Mr. Li Wenzheng. He was deeply knowledgeable in Confucian classics, and his conduct was upright, making him a true ancient gentleman. During the years of Yi Mao and Bing Chen, I followed Mr. Yao'an in the capital, but Mr. Wang was still a teacher at the Imperial College, and I did not get to meet him once. I am still disappointed. It is said that Mr. Wang once happened to be in the empty courtyard behind a residence at night, where he pulled up the radishes he had planted to drink. He seemed to see a human shadow, suspecting it to be a thief, but it suddenly disappeared. Knowing it was a ghost, he sternly rebuked it with the principle that the living and the dead are on different paths.

A voice was heard from among the dense bamboo, saying: "Master, you are profoundly knowledgeable in the *Yi Jing*. The alternation of yin and yang is the way of heaven. People come out during the day, while ghosts come out at night; this is the distinction between the visible and the invisible. People live in places where there are no ghosts, and ghosts live in places where there are no people; this is simply different paths. Therefore, there is nowhere in the world without people, nor is there anywhere without ghosts, but they do not interfere with each other, so they can coexist. If a ghost were to enter your room during the day, it would be right for you to rebuke it. But now it is late at night, and the place is empty. Entering during the time when ghosts come out and into the place where they dwell, neither holding a candle nor making noise, unexpectedly and unprepared for, suddenly meeting, this is you offending the ghost, not the ghost offending you. Showing respect and avoiding seems sufficient, why do you blame so deeply?" The master laughed and said: "Your words are correct, but let us set this aside for now." He then picked up a radish and returned. Later, he told his disciples about this. The disciples said: "Since the ghost can speak, and the master is not afraid, why not ask for its name, temporarily flatter it, and question the sayings of the underworld, whether they are true or false, or perhaps it is one method of investigating things." The master replied: "This is another case of familiarity between humans and ghosts; how can one speak of the different paths of the visible and the invisible?"

郑慎人说，从前与几位朋友前往九鲤湖，住在仙游山的一户人家，夜晚凉爽未睡，出门散步赏月，忽然一阵轻风清凉地穿过树林，树叶沙沙作响，栖息的鸟儿惊飞起来。感觉到各种花香沁人心脾，出林后沿着溪流而去。水鸟也发出杂乱的叫声，似乎看到了什么，但凝视之下却什么也没看见。心里知道这是仙灵往来。第二天，查看林内，微雨初晴，绿苔如毯，每一步都留下弓形的脚印，还有赤脚的痕迹，但总没有超过三寸的。溪边的泥迹也是如此。数了数大约有二十多人，大家指指点点，徘徊不前，一起感叹奇异，不知道是什么神女。郑慎人写了四首诗记录这件事，忘记了留下手稿，无法回忆起来。 郑慎人又说，一天庭院里的花盛开，听到女仆和老妇惊慌地呼唤，推开窗户看去，她们都指着桂树的顶端，原来是一只像手掌大小的蝴蝶，背上坐着一个穿着红衣的女子，大约拇指大小，翩翩起舞，一会儿就飞过墙去了。邻家的孩童又惊慌地呼唤起来。这不知道是什么怪事，大概就是所谓的花月之妖吧？说起这件事时，在刘景南的家中，景南说：怎么知道这不是闺房里的游戏，用通草花中的人物绑在蝴蝶背上放飞呢？这也是一个说法。郑慎人说：我确实看到小人在蝴蝶背上，有骑马驾驭的姿态，俯仰顾盼，意态生动，完全不像木偶。这又是一个未知的谜团。

Uncle An, the upright one, related that in the past, while serving as an official in Rui Prefecture under Mr. Liu Bosi from Gaoyang, he heard that at a shrine to the earth deity in the west of the city, a clay ghost suddenly fell to the ground, followed by another ghost with a green face and black hair, whose clothing and appearance were identical to the clay ghost, pressing down on top of it. Upon closer inspection, it was revealed that this was a young man from the village who had pretended to be a ghost, having already broken his spine and died. Everyone was shocked and amazed, unable to understand the reason. After some time, someone who knew about the incident said: "A certain young woman from the neighborhood, who was young and beautiful, had teased the young man, and he scolded her. That day, she went to her mother's house and expected to return home at night, passing in front of the shrine. The shrine was a bit far from people, so he pretended to be a ghost, hiding behind the statue, waiting for her to arrive and suddenly covering her, intending to take advantage of her fear and fainting to fulfill his desire. He did not expect the god's retribution. It is said that his sister-in-law was involved in the plot, initially not daring to tell anyone, but after the matter was settled, she gradually revealed it." Uncle An also mentioned that there was a case where a young man and a young woman met in front of the Confucian temple in Hejian, engaging in playful banter without any restraint. Suddenly, a tile flew and struck the woman's head, breaking it, with no one knowing where it came from. The virtue and morality of sages are equal to heaven and earth. How can it be like the teachings of Buddhism and Daoism, which must rely on supernatural phenomena to be believed and must wait for divine protection to be respected? However, gods and ghosts punishing wrongdoing is reasonable. To say that Zhu Jin becoming the top scholar was due to his previous life of repairing the Confucian temple, it is to regard sages as too small. To say that the high walls of the Confucian temple still have no divine protection, this is again the impracticality of Confucian scholars.

Three towers—Mongolian name Gurbansubul. The Han and Tang dynasties' Yingzhou Liucheng, and the Liao dynasty's Xingzhongfu, are now part of the Karakorum Right Wing. A Jin dynasty inspector reported—Qiu Wendada's nephew-in-law, whose name I accidentally forgot. A woodcutter met a tiger while walking in the mountains and fled into a cave in the rock. The tiger followed him in. The cave was naturally hollow and winding, and the woodcutter turned around to retreat inward, gradually becoming too narrow for the tiger. The tiger was determined to attack the woodcutter and struggled to force its way in. The woodcutter was in a desperate situation and saw a small hole nearby, just enough to fit his body. He then crawled in like a snake. Unexpectantly, after a few winding steps, he suddenly saw the light of the sky and ended up emerging outside the cave. Then he used all his strength to move several stones, blocking the tiger's retreat path. He burned the tiger by gathering firewood at both holes. The tiger was scorched by the smoke, roaring, shaking the rocks and valleys, and died within an instant. This incident also serves as a warning against stopping when one should continue.

The Jin patrol inspector again said that there is a Taihu stone in his office, which rises above the eaves, with cracked and mottled surfaces, intricate holes and cavities, and when viewed, it seems to be flying and moving. It is said to be an old object from the Liao and Jin dynasties. Upon examination, the Jin dynasty once dismantled the strange stones of Genyue and transported them northward. This is probably what is meant by the myriad forms of the strange peaks called Qingyun. However, the Jin dynasty made Dading Prefecture the capital (Beijing), which is now the city of Dading. The Liao dynasty's Xingzhong Prefecture was demoted to a state (by the Jin dynasty), and it should not have been placed in the state capital. This is another doubt that cannot be clarified. It is also said that the stones of Tu'er Mountain in the capital were all remnants of Genyue, and I still saw them when I was young. My house on Huofang Bridge was the former residence of the Duke of Weixin, and in the eastern part of the hall, there was a stone that was about seven or eight feet tall. It is said to have been granted when the house was first built during the reign of Emperor Yongzheng, and it was also moved from Tu'er Mountain. Among all the Taihu stones in the southern city, this one is the best. I also called myself the Old Man of the Lonely Stone, because of this.

The oldest flowers and trees in the capital city are first the Wisteria flowers at the Guji Temple belonging to the Li family, and second the green Paulownia tree in my family's courtyard, both of which are several hundred years old. The Paulownia tree has a horizontal diameter of one foot and five inches, standing tall and majestic. In summer, the courtyard is filled with a lush green color, but unfortunately, there is a hole caused by insects, and rainwater has seeped inside, causing it to rot from the inside to the roots, and ultimately wither. The Li family's residence was later sold to Gao Taishou Zhaohuang, who then resold it to Cheng Zhushi Zhenjia. The Wisteria is still there, supported by materials used for beams and pillars, and its shade covers one courtyard, while its tendrils spread out, covering another courtyard to the west. When it blooms, it looks like purple clouds hanging on the ground, and its fragrance clings to one's clothes. During the days of Muta Tang, who was a scholar-official named Yuanlong, passed the provincial examination in the year Gengwu, and was the brother-in-law of Zhu Shijun, and studied under the same master, Dong Wenke, he would sometimes host guests himself, or his friends would borrow his place to host guests, and there was almost no night without drinking and poetry. It has been over forty years since then. When I returned to these places I had visited before, they were no longer owned by the original owners, and I felt a deep sadness akin to that of hearing a neighbor's flute. Ni Suichou, my senior, once wrote a couplet for it: "A courtyard surrounded by fragrant grass and new green, ten mu of Wisteria flowers, fallen and fragrant with age." The calligraphy is exquisite, like a thirsty horse and a fierce lion, but I don't know where it is now.

Mr. Chen Gushan, a respected elder, moved to a new residence. While moving furniture, he first placed more than ten boxes of books in the courtyard. It seemed as if there was a small voice from behind a tree saying, "More than thirty years, I haven't seen this thing here." When he looked, it was as quiet as a tomb. Someone suggested it must be a fox, and Mr. Chen Gushan shook his head, saying, "Understanding this saying, even a fox is very good." My ancestor, Guanglu Gong, established a pawnshop in Cui Village during the reign of Emperor Kangxi. The person in charge was Shen Yubai. Once, a person deposited two boxes of wooden puppets, which were about a foot tall and made with great craftsmanship. The loan period passed without repayment, and the puppets could not be resold, so they were discarded and kept in a waste room for a long time. One night when the moon was bright, Shen Yubai saw the wooden puppets dancing in the courtyard, performing a play. Listening to them, they sounded like they were singing a song. Shen Yubai was brave and shouted at them fiercely, causing them to scatter immediately. The next day, they burned them, and there was no other unusual event. It is said that objects that have been around for a long time can become spirits, and burning them causes their essence to dissipate, so they cannot gather again. If something is possessed, it can also become a spirit, but burning it causes it to lose its attachment and cannot be effective, which is simply the natural order of things.

A magistrate in Xian County treated his clerks and servants with kindness. After his death, his relatives were still in the office, but not a single clerk or servant visited them. The relatives forcibly called several servants to come, and they faced each other ferociously, as if it were the past. The magistrate's wife was indignant and wept bitterly in front of the coffin. Tired, she took a nap and vaguely saw the magistrate say, "These people are unvirtuous; this is their nature. I hoped they would be grateful, but I was already greatly mistaken. You blame them for betraying kindness, but isn't that also mistaken?" Suddenly, she awoke and then no longer had any grievances. At the end of the reign of Emperor Kangxi, in Zhanggeqiao, a place in Hengjian County, there was a man named Liu Heng (pronounced "heng"). He was called "heng" because of his strength and fierceness, not because it was his real name. He lived by the riverbank. One day, the river suddenly overflowed, and small boats carrying heavy loads often sank. He happened to see a woman in the middle of the river, holding a broken oar and floating up and down in the waves, calling for help. No one dared to rescue her, but Liu Heng alone said resolutely, "Are you men? How can there be those who see death and do not save?" He rowed a small boat, chasing after her for three or four miles, nearly capsizing several times, and finally rescued her. The next day, she gave birth to a son. After a month, Liu Heng suddenly fell ill. He immediately ordered his wife and children to prepare for his funeral, although he was still able to walk and stand at the time. Everyone was surprised by this. Liu Heng sighed and said, "I will not recover."

On the night I rescued someone from drowning, I had a dream where I was led to an official place by officials and soldiers. The official held a register and showed it to me, saying: "You have accumulated many evil deeds throughout your life. You were supposed to die on a certain day this year, and after five lifetimes as a pig, you would suffer the punishment of slaughter and cutting. Fortunately, you saved two lives in one day, performing a great hidden good deed. According to the laws of the underworld, this should extend your lifespan by two decades. However, your lifespan has now been canceled to offset your karma, and you will still die on the originally recorded date. The reason for summoning you is that the deadline is approaching, and I fear that people may be ignorant and mistakenly believe that such good deeds can shorten one's life. Therefore, I am calling you to provide proof so that they may understand the truth. Your karmic balance for this life is now settled, and you should strive hard in your next life." Upon waking, I felt a deep sense of dread and did not share this dream with anyone. Now, as the appointed time has arrived and I am indeed ill, I wonder if I can still survive? Soon after, everything unfolded as he had said. This shows that the divine principles are clear and precise, with no deviation in the slightest. The calculations of rewards and punishments span multiple lifetimes, so do not dismiss them as mere coincidence or claim that the ways of heaven are devoid of knowledge.

Zheng Suchuan said, there was a man who arranged a secret meeting with his neighbor's wife and resented his wife being at home. He had a long-standing debt of several thousand coins to his wife's family and sent his wife to return the money. She happily went. Unexpectedly, the neighbor's wife missed the appointed time, and his wife was robbed on the way by violent criminals, who took away all her clothes and jewelry and tied her up in a dense patch of sorghum. These were temporary workers or migrants, and there was no way to pursue them. The man was left helpless and sighed in despair, saying nothing more. People also did not know about the neighbor's wife's involvement. Several years later, there was a son of a village old woman who seduced other women, and the old woman discovered it. She repeatedly warned him, citing this matter to illustrate cause and effect, and people then gradually knew. It is said that this person communicated with the neighbor's wife, and actually, this old woman mediated the communication, so she knew it accurately. Only the name of the neighbor's wife was mentioned, but the old woman always would not reveal it, fortunately, it was not ruined.

Feng Seng Hui Zhen said, there was a monk from Zhejiang who set his heart on spiritual progress, making vows that were both arduous and firm, never allowing his side to touch the bed. One night, a beautiful woman peered through the window, and he knew that a demon had arrived, yet he acted as if he did not see her. The demon bewitched him in countless ways, but could not approach his meditation mat. Night after night, she would come, yet still could not make him entertain a single thought. When the demon's skills ran out, she spoke from afar: "Your determination is so strong, I should indeed cut off all delusions." Although, you are a being from the Trāyastriṃśa Heaven, knowing that approaching me would surely lead you astray, hence you fear me like a tiger or a wolf. Even if you strive to reach the Non-Existence Realm, it would still be nothing more than soft flesh touching your body, like holding ice and snow, or seeing a charming form as if seeing dust, unable to escape the realm of form. If your mind reaches the Fourth Dhyana Heaven, then flowers will naturally reflect in mirrors, mirrors will not know the flowers, the moon will naturally reflect in water, water will not know the moon, thus you will have transcended the realm of form. If you reach the realm of the bodhisattvas, then there will be no flowers, no mirrors, no moon, no water, no form, no appearance, no separation, no attachment, it will be a state of spontaneous and wondrous supernatural power. If you dare to let me come close, and yet remain in pure emptiness, unsoiled, then Mādhyamikā will wholeheartedly seek refuge, and will no longer disturb Ananda. The monk, confident in his spiritual strength, calmly agreed, and as he embraced and caressed her, he destroyed his moral body, filled with deep regret and loss of purpose, ending in dejection. Indeed, grinding does not make it thin, dyeing does not make it black, only sages can achieve this, the great virtuous cannot. This monk, swayed by a sudden impulse, opened the door to thieves, those who rely on their own strength to do what others dare not, ultimately leading to their downfall and disintegration, all because of this monk.

When Master De Shunzi performed the divination ritual, the spirit descended upon the medium and did not compose poetry. Instead, it wrote its own name as Liu Zhongfu. The audience did not know who this was. A top-level chess player was present and said, "This is a renowned chess player from the Southern Song Dynasty, known for his four treatises on chess." Despite this, they insisted on a game of chess. The spirit responded, "If we play, I will surely lose." Despite the insistence, the spirit agreed to the game and indeed lost by half a point. The audience remarked, "The great spirit is humble, perhaps to encourage the younger generation?" The spirit responded, "Not so. People of today are inferior to those of the past in every way, except in the calculation of celestial movements and chess, where we surpass them." Some argued that it was because the ancients had already achieved a certain level, and through further refinement, they surpassed their predecessors. This applies to the calculation of celestial movements, not to chess. As the cultural climate diminishes and people become more cunning, their strategies of competition and attack become increasingly intense, leading to endless changes and variations, using cunning and surprising tactics, leaving no room for retreat.

The ancients were often unwilling to do what the later generations often did; the ancients dared not take risks where the later generations often did; the ancients were reluctant to propose strategies where the later generations often did. Therefore, all worldly affairs and strategies surpass those of the ancients. Chess is also one such strategy. Hence, the top players of the Song and Yuan dynasties had fallen behind by one move by the Ming dynasty, and now they have fallen behind by one and a half moves. However, the maximum loss for ancient masters was one move, whereas modern masters might lose by two or three moves. This is the distinction between solid and hollow strategies. Is there no constant winning method in chess? Another judgment states: there is no constant winning method, but there is a method that ensures one is never at fault. If one does not play, one is always faultless. Due to innate wisdom, I have become a spirit or immortal, detached from worldly affairs, free from the desire for fame, playing along with the world's games, finding no significance in winning or losing. If you are involved in the game, be cautious in your pursuit of gains and losses. Those present who have experienced the world often sigh deeply.

Ji Canzhou told a story about a fox that had lived in a certain scholar's library for several decades. The fox was known for organizing the scrolls, driving away insects and mice, and was so skilled that even the best book collectors could not match its abilities. The fox could speak with humans but never revealed its true form. When guests gathered for feasts, the fox would sometimes take an empty seat and engage in conversation. Its speech was calm and refined, with subtle and accurate insights, often impressing those present. One day, during a drinking game, the person responsible for managing the game asked each participant to confess what they feared. The rules were that anyone who gave an unreasonable answer or admitted to fearing something that wasn't unique to them would be penalized. The guests mentioned various fears: some feared scholars, others feared celebrities, some feared the wealthy, others feared powerful officials, some feared flatterers, others feared those who were overly modest, and still others feared those who were overly cautious and hesitant to speak. When it was the fox's turn, it said, "I fear foxes." The guests burst into laughter, saying, "It's understandable for humans to fear foxes, but you're of the same kind, so what do you fear? Please drink a large cup of wine." The fox smiled and said, "In this world, only those of the same kind are to be feared."

The people of Ou-Yue do not compete with the Xidi for land, and the people of Jiang-Hai do not compete with vehicles and horses for roads. They are of different kinds. Those who compete for property are always sons of the same father, those who compete for favor are always wives of the same husband, those who compete for power are always officials in the same office, and those who compete for profit are always merchants in the same market. When they are close in status, they hinder each other; when they hinder each other, they clash. Moreover, those who hunt pheasants use pheasants as decoys, not chickens or ducks, and those who hunt deer use deer as bait, not sheep or pigs. All those who engage in counterintelligence and internal support must be of the same kind. If they are not of the same kind, they cannot appeal to their preferences and enter, nor can they wait for an opportunity to strike. From this perspective, how can foxes not fear other foxes? Those who have experienced difficulties in life often praise the truth in this saying. A single guest poured a drink before the fox and said, "Your words are indeed true, but this is something feared by all under heaven, not just you, so you should still drink a large cup." And they all laughed and dispersed. I think the penalty for the fox should be reduced by half, because when they hinder and clash, it is known throughout the world. But to hide in one's side and become a great danger to one's heart and abdomen, to rely on a close relationship like water and milk, yet conceal deep and cunning plans, then there are many who do not know.

An Elderly Scholar Named Zhou Maoguan, Who Spoke with a Southern Accent, Whose Origin Remains Unknown, Long Trapped in the Arena of Fame, Wandering and in a State of Distress, Often Visited the Homes of Zhou Xiqing and He Huafeng. He Huafeng Also Bore the Surname Zhou, Perhaps Belonging to the Same Clan as the Two Gentlemen? In the Early Years of the Qianlong Era, I Still Had the Opportunity to Meet Him. He Was Awkward, Rigid, and Slow-Witted, Truly an Ancient Gentleman. Whenever He Took the Examinations, He Was Either Disqualified for Minor Errors in Brushstrokes or Eliminated for a Single Miswritten Character, and He Had Also Been Unjustly Obstructed on Several Occasions, Such as When the Topic Involved Writing the Character "曰," Which Was Slightly Narrowed and Thus Mistakenly Read as "日," or When the Character "己" Was Written with a Slightly Pointed Final Stroke and Mistakenly Read as "已." These Incidents Filled Him with Deep Resentment and Unease. One Day, He Burned a Petition at the Wenchang Shrine, Complaining of a Life Free from Grave Sins Yet Unjustly Hindered. Several Days Later, He Dreamed of a Red-Clothed Official Leading Him to a Hall, Where a Divine Figure Sat Behind a Desk and Said, "Your Path to Fame and Fortune Has Been Bumpy. You Have Rashly Offended the Enlightened Deities, Carrying Only Grievances Without Understanding the Law of Cause and Effect. In Your Past Life, You Were a Low-Ranking Official in the Ministry, Cunning and Adept at Manipulating Documents. Therefore, You Are Punished in This Life to Be a Bookish Fool, Utterly Incomprehensible. Because You Enjoyed Nitpicking Documents, Even When You Knew They Were Correct, You Used Clever Words to Twist and Manipulate, Coercing for Financial Gain. Hence, You Are Punished in This Life to Be Constantly Disqualified for Errors in Brushwork."

He then pointed to the register and said, "You have the character '曰' attached, which refers to this official whose former life was that of the wife of Yindebu, a garrison commander in Fujian, and she was an old chaste widow. Because in the consultative document, '音' was written as '殷,' the translation harmonized the sound, and there was no fixed character. You repeatedly questioned and disputed, coming and going multiple times, causing the poor and widowed woman to receive insufficient funds for the construction of a memorial arch, which was not enough for travel expenses. You have the character '已' attached, which refers to this official whose former life was as a county magistrate, and he served for three years and one month. You did not get what you demanded, so you changed the text from three characters to five, and one character to ten, and then you shifted the calculation to five years and ten months, which should have resulted in a separate case of punishment. By the time it was clarified, it was due to the original text's error, and it had already been delayed for more than a year."

The karmic bonds of past actions have brought us together in this life. What grievance can you complain about? All other circumstances are the result of past causes, and I cannot fully explain or reveal them to you. You should accept your fate and stop complaining incessantly. If you do not believe this, then both Buddhist monks and Taoist priests will oppose you, and you will come to a clear realization. After speaking these words, I dismissed you, and you suddenly woke up, but you did not understand the reference to Buddhist monks and Taoist priests. At the time, you were staying in a Buddhist temple, so you moved away to avoid potential conflict. During the provincial examination in the year of Yin Mao, you were initially ranked thirteenth. In the second part of the exam, in the section on the veneration of parents by monks and Taoist priests, you used the phrase "long bow to parents," which referred to Fu Yi's criticism of the practice of monks and Taoist priests showing respect to their parents. The examiners considered this a flaw and disqualified you. Only then did you realize that the divine words were not false. This story was told to you by Chen Mo, a former official from Zaoqiang who served as a manufacturing supervisor, and he recounted it to your mentor. After that, your fate is unknown, but you likely died in a humble or unfortunate manner.

虞倚帆待诏说，有一位选人张某，带着一位妻子和一位婢女来到京师，租住在海丰寺街，一年多后妻子病逝，又过了一年多婢女也突然去世。正当他们准备安葬时，妻子忽然像有呼吸，随后眼睛开始转动，已经苏醒过来。她呼唤选人，握住他的手哭泣说：“分别一年多，没想到又相见了。”选人惊讶不已，她接着说：“你不要怀疑这是胡话，我是你的妻子，借用了婢女的尸体重生了。”这位婢女虽然侍奉你，但总是郁郁寡欢，不愿居于我之下，于是她去咨询了一位女巫，用法术折磨我。我因此生病去世，魂魄被术者收在瓶中，用符咒镇压，埋在尼庵的墙下。那里狭窄昏暗，痛苦难以言表。后来尼庵的墙倒塌了，工人挖地重修时，砸破了瓶子，我得以脱身，如今茫然不知所措，不知该去哪里。

A temple guardian deity directed me to the city god, and all those who practiced casting spells were said to have malevolent deities as their protectors. The case was obstructed and delayed, preventing it from being resolved, until it reached the Eastern Hell. There, the practitioners of magic were captured and interrogated, and their confessions were obtained. The maid was then detained and sent to hell. Since my life was not yet over, but my body had already decayed, the judge ordered the use of the maid's body to bring me back to life. The entire family was both sad and happy, and they continued to treat me as the mistress of the household. The nun who was accused of casting spells claimed that someone had chosen the maid as a wife, and thus she faked her death for a short time, fabricating such words, without caring about the severe punishment she might face. She was eager to accuse or sue someone. However, there was no concrete evidence, and people were afraid of being accused of superstition, so they remained silent. Yet, Yi Fan privately questioned the servants, who told him that after I was brought back to life, I recounted past events without any discrepancies. My voice and manner of walking were exactly the same as before. Moreover, the maid was not good at embroidery, but I was skilled, and I finished half of an unfinished pair of shoes, which was clearly done by me. This event occurred at the end of Emperor Yongzheng's reign.

Fan Hengzhou, a distinguished scholar from Shanyin, who passed the imperial examination in the year of the Horse (甲戌) and served as the governor of Liuzhou Prefecture, had a niece who died by suicide to preserve her chastity before marriage. She swallowed a gold ring but did not die, and eventually threw herself into the river. The daughter of a former governor from Jiaxiang, a descendant of Zengzi, occasionally forgot her name. She died by suicide to save her mother and was burned alive. The details of her story were well-known at the time. Forty years have passed, and we cannot recount all the details of their stories. Extraordinary events are easy to remember, while ordinary actions are easy to forget, which is a natural course of things. By recording names, we hope to preserve their virtues. The *Analects of Confucius* records 72 disciples, and it is not necessary to record every detail of each disciple's life. Hengzhou mentioned a man from his hometown who was very simple and virtuous, never engaging in reckless behavior. One day, while napping during the day, he dreamed of several officials holding a warrant to take him away. They arrived at a public office where the King of the Underworld was sitting on the dais. He was interrogated for plotting to kill a certain person, Person B. When Person B arrived, he also insisted strongly on his innocence. It turned out that Person B had come back from collecting a debt, and it was still dark when he left early to take advantage of the coolness. He encountered several people who saw his valuable possessions and collectively killed him, taking the money and abandoning his body by the riverbank. The man happened to be rowing a small boat when he saw the body and was greatly startled. Upon closer inspection, he recognized it as Person B, who was still slightly alive. He then instructed the neighbors to carry the body onto the boat and take it back home. Person B, on the verge of death, suddenly regained consciousness and opened his eyes to see the man. He thought that the group had taken the money and left the body in the river. Therefore, when his soul reached the underworld, he accused the man alone.

The King of the Underworld was checking the records and found that the thief was listed as someone else, not person A. Person B, having seen the situation, insisted on the matter, and the official of the Underworld, relying on the records being correct, also argued with person B. The King of the Underworld said, "The records are correct, this is considered the norm. But how can one know that something that has been correct for thousands of years will not have a single error?" He then said, "It is better to verify my judgment with others, and the official's statement is not as reliable as the prisoner's testimony." Therefore, person A was detained. Person A provided a detailed account of the delivery, which was verified by the mirror of karma, and person B finally understood. Initially, person A was puzzled by his detention, but the King of the Underworld explained the reason, and person A also understood. The case against person B was then resolved, and person A was released. This is why the King of the Underworld is the King of the Underworld, demonstrating his wisdom and fairness.

Confucius did not go to extremes for himself, not only to prevent the overcorrection of a wrong, but because the sage considers the long-term. Laozi said: "If the people do not fear death, how can you frighten them with death?" The people have never failed to fear death; until they know they will die, they do not fear; once they no longer fear death, nothing is beyond their reach. When I was young, I heard of a wealthy family that was robbed. A bounty was posted for their capture, and after more than half a year, all the robbers were caught and confessed. However, the wealthy family harbored great hatred for the robbers and bribed the prison guards with a large sum of money, subjecting the robbers to extreme torment. They were tied up so tightly that their feet could not touch the ground, their shoulders could not reach the bed, and they were not allowed to go to the toilet. Maggots wriggled in their pants, nibbling at their thighs and buttocks. The only thing left was to keep them alive without food or water, so they would not die quickly. The robbers harbored great hatred for the wealthy family and privately calculated that if they committed armed robbery and gained wealth, the law would not distinguish between the ringleader and the followers in terms of punishment. Similarly, in cases of gang rape, the law also did not distinguish between the ringleader and the followers in terms of punishment.

Two crimes are judged under one category, both are sentenced to death by decapitation, and there is no justification for adding the more severe punishment of dismemberment. During the court hearing, the defendant confessed to defiling the women of the family. Although the officials did not record this confession, the crowd firmly insisted, and the public widely knew about it, making it impossible to suppress this statement. Those who do not favor the powerful families then associated and connected the case, saying that the thief had already been sentenced to death, which was sufficient to cover the crime, but they did not hesitate to spend a lot of money and torment them with every means. The deep-seated resentment stems from this. Rumors abound, and there is no way to refute these doubts, thus greatly tarnishing the family name. It is too late to regret. Robbers are executed together, and they cannot blame the owner; even if they are tortured, interrogated, shackled, and imprisoned, they cannot blame the owner. These are punishments they should receive according to the law. However, when they are tormented beyond the law, their resolve is not satisfied. Throwing a stone at a stone, if the force is too great, it will inevitably rebound. Seeking a momentary pleasure, they suffer a stain for a hundred generations, is this not due to going too far? Thus, the wise sages have far-sighted concerns.

Huo Yangzhong said, during the early years of the Yongzheng reign, there was a farming family in Dongguang who had a modest but sufficient level of wealth. One night, robbers entered the house but did not search for valuables; instead, they took the woman from her bed and led her into the back garden, where they tied her up upside down to a bent-neck old tree. Their intention was not to rob, but to commit another act. The woman cried and cursed. A hired worker named Gao Dou, who was sleeping in the garden, heard the commotion and sprang up, drawing a weapon and fighting the robbers, who were all defeated. The woman escaped, but she was angry and wept, refusing to speak or eat. Her parents tried to comfort her, but she remained inconsolable. After persistent questioning, she finally said: "My body is exposed, can I let Gao Dou see me like this?" Her parents understood her meaning and eventually married her to Gao Dou. This incident is similar to the story of Zhong Jian from Chu. However, Gao Dou did not initially intend to marry her; he did so only because he had cared for her father when he was ill, provided medicine, buried him after his death, and even offered her mother a place to cook and live. Thus, he was moved to act with such dedication. The collection *Heliang Yulu* by Luo Daqing contains a poem about Zhu Hai, which says: "High talk about the affairs of the sage rulers of the Tang and Yu dynasties, betraying one's lord and selling one's friends is beyond words, do not laugh at the simplicity of the golden hammer, but it is the butcher and the wine seller who understand gratitude." How profound are these words!

Li Bai's poem says: "Wandering, reflected in a singing fan, like the moon seen through clouds; meeting without intimacy, it's better not to meet at all." This speaks of the nature of casual relationships. In households where couples are separated or estranged, yet see each other every day, it is unclear what the cause or effect of this situation is. Guo Shizhou recounts that there was a man named Li from the Central Plains who married his wife for about ten days when his mother fell ill. The couple took turns caring for the sick mother, staying up for several months without changing clothes. After the mother's death, Li strictly adhered to mourning rituals and did not spend the night in his wife's room for three years. Later, he became very poor and moved to his wife's family home. The wife's family home was modestly comfortable with limited space, and a room was cleaned for Li to stay. Within a month, the wife's aunt's younger brother moved away for work, and her mother came to stay with the sister. There was no room for the mother, so she shared a room with the daughter, while Li stayed in the study with a separate bed, only eating at the same table in the morning and evening. After two years, Li went to the capital to seek advancement. The wife's father also moved his family to Jiangxi for work. Later, Li received news that his wife had died. Li became depressed and despondent, feeling lost and unable to support himself.

Still attaching oneself to a boat and heading south to seek one's father-in-law, the father-in-law had already changed his master and followed to another place. With nowhere to settle or rely on, Li temporarily sold his calligraphy to make a living. One day, in the market, he met a tall and imposing man who took a look at his writing and said, "Your calligraphy is excellent; can you earn thirty to forty gold pieces in a year as a secretary or scribe?" Li was overjoyed beyond expectation and immediately boarded the boat. The journey was hazy and indistinct, with no clear destination. Upon reaching the house, the provisions and arrangements were also very grand. However, upon observing the master's writing materials, it became clear that the master was a bandit. Unable to do anything about it, Li temporarily relied on the bandit, worried about future troubles, and therefore falsely changed his hometown and name. The master was generous and extravagant, with entertainers all around, and was not very careful about guests. Every time music was played, Li must be summoned. He occasionally saw a woman who extremely resembled his wife, suspecting her to be a ghostly woman, and she also frequently looked at Li, seeming to have met before, but neither dared to speak a word to each other. It turned out that the father-in-law was traveling by river and just happened to be robbed by this bandit, who saw the woman had a beautiful appearance and took her away. The father-in-law considered this a great shame and quickly bought a cheap coffin, deceptively saying the daughter was injured and died, falsely mourning and preparing the body, and carried her back. The woman, fearing death, lost her virtue and became the bandit's concubine, hence they met again here.

However Li Sheng believed his wife was dead, and his wife did not know that Li Sheng had changed his name, so she suspected him of looking similar to her husband and thus they lost each other. Generally, they would meet every few days, and after becoming accustomed to seeing each other, they no longer recognized one another. This continued for six or seven years. One day, the master called Li Sheng and said: "My affairs are about to fail. You, as a scholar, do not need to be involved in this difficulty. Here is fifty taels of gold; you can carry it with you and hide it among the reeds in a certain place. Wait until the troops withdraw, then quickly find a fishing boat to return. The people of this place all know you, so you do not need to worry that they will not send you." After speaking, he waved his hand, making Li Sheng go quickly to hide. Not long after, he heard the noisy sound of fighting, and shortly thereafter, he heard an announcement: "The thieves have all departed in their boats, and they have also taken a list of their gold, silk, and women." By then, it was dusk, and in the firelight, he saw that the various entertainers were all shaved-headed and naked, bound behind their necks with ropes, and driven along with whips and sticks. This lady was also among them, trembling with fear, which made one feel pity. The next day, there was no one left on the island. He stood foolishly by the water for a long time, when suddenly a person in a small boat called out: "Mr. Someone? The king is still well, and he will send you back."

One day, he arrived at the shore, fearing discovery, and carried gold to return north. Upon arrival, his father-in-law had already returned home, and he stayed with his family. Selling what he had brought, he gradually became wealthy. Reflecting on the couple who were deeply in love and had been married for ten years, yet never shared a bed for even one month, he felt that now that his resources were somewhat sufficient, he could not bear to bury her with a simple coffin. He planned to replace it with better wood and also wished to see her remains, a long-held emotion. His father-in-law tried to dissuade him but could not stop him. Eventually, he ran out of words and revealed the truth, hurrying and traveling day and night to Yuzhang, hoping to reunite like the mirror of Lechang. However, the captured musicians had been distributed for a long time, and he did not know where they had been scattered. Whenever he recalled the six or seven years of separation, it felt like thousands of miles away, leaving him feeling lost and confused. He also remembered the state of being bound and flogged, wondering what further hardships awaited, which left him heartbroken. From then on, he never married again, and later heard that he became a monk. The elder Mr. Ge Jiezhou said, "This story could be made into a legend, but it lacks a conclusion, comparable to *The Peony Pavilion*. Though the end is not seen, the peaks on the riverbank are green, deep and lingering emotions, in the endless misty waves, it still adds to one's sorrow."

Jin Keteng said: This is Jin Jilian from Zhejiang Province, named Jia Yan, who shares the same surname and title as Jin Dasi, but they are two separate individuals. There was a gentleman named Zhao, who held the position of a high-ranking official overseeing local administration. In his later years, he lived at home and acquired a maid named Zitao, whom he favored above all others. No other concubines were allowed to spend the night with him, and Zitao was always graceful and attentive in her service. Whenever Zhao called for her, she was always by his side, never failing him once. Zhao, being perceptive, suspected something unusual and questioned her closely while they lay in bed together. Zitao admitted that she was a fox spirit, but she explained that she had a predestined connection to serve him and would not harm him. Zhao loved her dearly and did not say anything more about it. One day, Zhao had a garden pavilion, and when he stood between two rooms and called for Zitao, two Zitaos appeared in each room, causing him great alarm. Zitao apologized, saying, "I have divided myself into multiple forms." She continued, "I happened to be walking in the countryside one spring day with a cane when I met a Taoist priest. We conversed, and he was very wise and reasonable. We got along well, and he asked me where I came from. I told him that I came for you. You are a demigod who was exiled to this world, and your term is now up. You should return to the Three Islands, but your elixir of immortality has been stolen by a fox, and you cannot return. If you do not treat this matter, your lifespan will be shortened. I am an old companion of yours, so I came to see you." Zhao understood Zitao's story and invited the Taoist priest to return with him. The priest sat cross-legged in the hall, picked up a brush, and wrote a talisman. He then let out a long, melodious shout. Inside the mansion, there was chaos and commotion. Dozens of Zitaos appeared, all with identical appearances, clothing, and decorations. They knelt in the courtyard, filling it entirely. The Taoist priest called out for the genuine Zitao to come forward. The others looked at each other and said, "There is no genuine one." The priest then called out for the first Zitao to appear. One woman knelt and said, "I am the maid."

The Taoist monk shouted, "You have already stolen Zhao Gong's essence, and now you are calling your friends and leading others to ruin his path, why is that?" The woman replied, "There are two reasons. Zhao Gong's previous life, he refined essence for four or five hundred years, making his fundamental gateway firm. Only by taking turns and alternating can one achieve this. However, Zhao Gong is not an ordinary person; seeing many beautiful things advancing, he will surely feel it as a delusion and will definitely not accept it. Therefore, they have always been in one form together, hiding their tracks. Now the matter has been revealed, and I wish to disperse." The Taoist monk waved his hand and ordered them to leave. Looking at Zhao Gong, he sighed and said, "Petty people flatter and gather, but the gentleman does not accept it. A petty person waits for the gentleman's weakness, catering to what he values, and many petty people follow and secretly assist him, then the gentleman will not realize it."

In the Guai hexagram of the *I Ching*, the first line (初六) marks the beginning of yin energy. The image is of something tied to a metal stopper (金柅), which serves to halt a carriage, indicating that one should stop. If not stopped, it is like the initial step on frost, which gradually becomes solid ice; over time, it leads to the fifth line of the Po hexagram (剝卦六五). Is this what today's situation signifies? However, if there is no opening, even a small person cannot take advantage; if there is nothing desirable, even a small person cannot throw themselves in. A dike worth a thousand gold pieces can be destroyed by ant holes; this is because there is a crack. You initially mistakenly entered a side door, intending to learn the methods of Rong Cheng. Later, indulging in beautiful and alluring things, you lost your original intention. As desires deepened, evil spirits took advantage and gathered around you. The cause arises from oneself; what fault does it have with others? From beginning to end, it is indeed its nature. To drive them away without blame, it is because of this. I arrived a bit late, and it is of no benefit to public affairs. However, from now on, if one calms the mind and remains quiet, one can still become a ninety-year-old man. Take care repeatedly. He left suddenly, and Zhao Gong later lived to be over eighty years old.

People living by the Wei River say that when the river is about to break, the water in the middle must rise up, higher than the banks, but they do not know exactly where it will happen. When the rod-shaped fish gather in one place, the spot where they gather will break within one or two days. Passed down by the elders, this has been verified without fail. The rod-shaped fish are named after their shape; they are not known to be found elsewhere, and they have not been caught by nets or hooks. When the river swells, they come in large numbers, and those guarding the embankment see them bumping their heads against the bank, like thousands of hammers striking simultaneously, and the breach will occur in an instant. Is this not fate? However, the great flood during the reign of Emperor Yao was the will of heaven, and the divine Yu followed the course of events, which is human action. Only the sage can know the will of heaven, and only the sage does not blame heaven. Prepare in advance, and take remedial action afterward. Even if they cannot completely eliminate the disaster, they will still be able to mitigate the damage.

Jiang Xinyu said, there was a guest who went on a lake tour with someone, and upon arrival, they saw a decorated boat with flutes and drums, and a woman in a red skirt serving wine. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be his wife, who was two thousand miles away from home, and he did not know how she ended up there. Afraid of being disgraced, he kept silent. The wife, however, acted as if she did not recognize him, showing no signs of fear or shame. She played music and passed wine, remaining calm and composed, though her voice did not sound the same. Additionally, the wife had a habit of covering her mouth when she laughed, which this courtesan did not do, nor did her appearance match in this regard. However, the red mole on her right wrist, like a grain of millet, was exactly the same. The guest was greatly perplexed and could not understand the situation, so he hastily ended the banquet and began to prepare for his return. Soon after, he received a letter from home, informing him that his wife had died half a year ago. He suspected it was a ghost and did not delve deeper into the matter. Those close to him noticed her unusual demeanor and repeatedly questioned her in private, finally understanding the reason. They all believed that their appearances coincidentally matched. Later, they heard that a traveling scholar was moving between Wu and Yue, not engaging in flattery or socializing, nor in business or trade. He only brought several concubines and lived in seclusion, occasionally sending one or two out, entrusting a matchmaker to sell them.

People believed that those who trafficked in women had nothing to do with human affairs, and thus no one questioned them. One day, however, a man was in great haste, urgently hired a boat, and set out for Tianmu Mountain, seeking a virtuous monk to conduct a Buddhist ritual. The monk found the man's written petition obscure and incoherent, and could not discern what the matter was. Moreover, the text was based on a Buddhist scripture, and the man claimed that he sought the Buddha's protection, relying on the mercy of the compassionate cloud, hoping that the punishment from the Thunder Department might be lenient. Suspecting there was some hidden reason, the monk returned the offering and sent him away. When the man reached the middle of his journey, he was indeed struck down by lightning. Later, his attendants discreetly revealed the truth, saying: "This man had received strange techniques from a red-robed foreign monk, who could recite mantras to capture the bodies of newly deceased women. He also captured fox spirits and lustful ghosts, attaching them to the bodies to bring them to life, and used them as his attendants. When a new body became available, he would sell the old one to others, making enormous profits." Because he had dreamed of a deity accusing him of having accumulated enough evil deeds to warrant heavenly punishment, he repented in hopes of being spared, but in the end, he could not escape his fate. It is suspected that the wife of this traveler had been captured by this very man. Minister Liu of the Board of Colonial Affairs also remarked that lamas of the Red Sect possess the power to summon and capture women, and for this reason, the Yellow Sect denounced them as demonic.

My maternal uncle, Wang Yueqian, told me about a wealthy man from a nearby village who bought a concubine. After two months, she escaped. Her father, in turn, accused him of killing her out of jealousy and burning her body, and filed a lawsuit. Coincidentally, the county official was in the capital waiting for a position, and the escaped concubine filed her own lawsuit, which was similar in nature. This touched upon the official's old grievances, and after a thorough investigation, false accusations were uncovered. The plan to frame the wealthy man failed, but he stubbornly refused to admit to selling her again, as there was no concrete evidence of enticement and escape, making it difficult to investigate further. The concubine was finally without a trace. The wealthy man's younger brother-in-law lived in a different county. When the wife returned to her parents' home, she heard that her brother-in-law had taken a new concubine and wanted to see her. The concubine shut the door and refused to come out, but her brother-in-law dragged her out himself. As soon as she saw him, she threw herself to the ground and bowed her head, confessing to being guilty of a capital crime. She was indeed the lost concubine. The brother-in-law, unwilling to accept her as the former concubine of the wealthy man, and the wealthy man, unwilling to accept her as someone who had served his brother-in-law, both refused to take her. She was beaten a hundred times and given to an old servant, ultimately ending her days as a kitchen maid. Rich families engaging in legal disputes, especially those involving private matters, cannot be resolved quickly. In this case, the county official happened to be in a position to handle the matter, and the woman, who had been sold multiple times, was deeply hidden in her chambers, making it hard to find her. Yet, she was found by her brother-in-law. Various cunning schemes were employed, which could be considered ingenious, but how could one know that the creator is even more ingenious?

A Song Dynasty poet wrote a verse about crabs: "Even in clear water, their claws cannot escape being black; in autumn, their backs cannot avoid turning red." This verse uses the physical characteristics of crabs to metaphorically criticize the greed of Zhu Min, suggesting that greed will inevitably lead to downfall. However, other creatures, when caught, are merely offered to the kitchen and face a quick death. Only crabs are thrown alive into cooking pots and slowly subjected to steaming and boiling. From the initial boiling to being cooked, even the fastest process takes several moments, and their suffering is such that they cannot even die. It is said that when Zhao Gong Hongxie served as the governor of Zhili, the province had not yet appointed a governor-general. One night, he dreamed that dozens of household servants, including young male servants, male servants, female maids, and female servants, who had already died, were kneeling in a circle at the foot of the stairs, all knocking their foreheads and begging for their lives. They said, "We, your servants, have lived under your kindness of being fed and cared for, but we have formed cliques and obscured your vision. Over time, our actions have branched out and entangled, growing strong at the roots, forming an unbreakable situation. Even if there is slight exposure, we can skillfully resolve it with a single voice, making you know in your heart but unable to do anything about it."

For a long time, they secretly hindered and obstructed each other, failing to meet the expectations of the people, and thus could not accomplish anything. Because of this sin, they fell into the realm of aquatic creatures, suffering the punishment of boiling in oil from generation to generation. The next day, the host served crab as a meal, which was the reincarnation of the servants, and they begged for forgiveness. The Master Zhao, being kind and benevolent, informed the chef about the dream, ordered the crabs to be thrown into the water, and performed rituals of repentance and did good deeds. At that time, the crabs were fat and delicious, and those served at the residence were especially chosen for their richness and quality. The servants all secretly laughed and said, "The old man is cunning, is he making up this story to scare us?" We are not fooled by you, they finally cooked the crabs like the cook, and claimed that they had been released, and embezzled the money for good deeds, and claimed that the Buddhist rituals were completed. The Master Zhao ultimately did not know about it. These people's mischief is their usual behavior, also, these dozens of servants and maids, leaving these ingrained habits, they only harm themselves. To trap someone in their own scheme, this is what it means. A governor was executed for his greed and tyranny. After his death, the people of the state spread rumors about his various punishments in the afterlife, which were too numerous to record. I believe that this is due to the resentment and poison not yet being resolved, and the creation of false rumors. My elder brother Qinghu said, "Heaven and Earth are impartial, and their hearing and sight are in the hands of the people. If the people speak like this, it is also a cause for concern."

The Military Academy in Urumqi's Wang Fu said: In the past, while stationed in Xining, he and several comrades went into the mountains to hunt. They saw a foreign woman walking alone in the middle of the mountain, with four wolves following behind her. They thought the wolves were about to attack, but the woman did not notice. They all shouted and made noise, but the woman seemed not to hear them. One of them drew his bow fully and shot at the wolves, but accidentally hit the woman, causing her to fall and tumble down the mountain. When they looked, they realized she was also a wolf, and the four wolves had already fled. It seems that a demon or evil animal had transformed into a human to lure and devour people, and unfortunately, it was killed. Perhaps she had committed many evil deeds and was thus punished. Fox spirits can transform and deceive, but it is unclear how they perceive themselves and each other. Ghosts, being the residual energy of a person, are not more intelligent than humans. Humans cannot transform nothing into something, small into large, or ugly into beautiful, yet ghosts can turn their coffins into palaces and their graves into courtyards, allowing people to enter and stay. Even ghosts who died a bad death and have many evil traits can transform into beautiful beings. Is there someone teaching them these transformations? This is even more difficult to understand than the transformations of fox spirits. I remember on a journey in Liangzhou, the driver pointed to a mountain pass and said: "In the past, we camped with dozens of carriages in these mountains. Under the bright moonlight, we saw what appeared to be a village in the middle of the mountain, with earthen walls surrounding it, and every corner could be counted. The next day, when we passed by, we found only a few graves." Even in uninhabited places, such illusions can appear. Could the sage know about these situations?

The wet nurse from Cangzhou, my wet nurse, had a son named Zhu'er. He recounted that when he went to the sea to let his livestock graze, the coastal area was vast and open, with dense grass growing everywhere. The locals would drive their cattle and horses there to graze, which they called "letting them graze." One night, a salt worker was just about to sleep—these were the people who made salt by the sea, known as salt workers. He heard a faint, rustling sound inside the house. At that moment, the moonlight shone through the window, and he carefully looked around but saw no one, assuming it was probably caused by small creatures like insects or rodents. Soon, he heard voices coming from far away, growing louder. Someone was calling out continuously, saying, "She has run into this house!" In a state of doubt and surprise, the person had already reached the window and knocked, asking, "Are you here?" The woman inside responded with tears, saying she was there. Then, the caller asked, "Are you staying here?" She replied with tears, "Yes, I am staying." The caller then asked, "Are you sleeping in the same bed with someone else, or are you sleeping separately?" She cried for a long time before answering, "I am not sleeping in the same bed, so who would want to keep me?" The person outside stamped their feet and said, "It's ruined!" Suddenly, a woman burst out laughing and said, "I guessed she had run to another place, and people will definitely not spare you. I didn't think it would be so, but now what? Do you still have the face to take her back?" After that, only the faint sound of footsteps could be heard, and there was no further conversation. Then, the woman laughed again and said, "This still doesn't resolve what you are, does it?"
