Now I know this is your doing, and you let me die while this man saved me. How could I live with myself?" The speaker then said, "I will come with my sisters to punish you." The fox girl was startled and tried to escape, but several women burst in and struck her down, killing her instantly. The man, deeply infatuated, felt deep sorrow and anger, and blamed the fox girl for being unvirtuous and taking away his love. The fox girl repeatedly explained herself, but he did not listen. She then drew a knife and leaped up, intending to avenge the other fox girl. Crying bitterly, she climbed over the wall and left. The man, after this incident, still resented it. This is what is meant by being loyal and yet slandered, trustworthy and yet doubted.

It is said by the elder from Dongqujiang that there was a scholar who had an eccentric nature and liked to impose strict rituals on his students. The students suffered from this, but the scholar had a reputation for being upright and proper, so they could not criticize him for his faults. Behind his schoolhouse was a small garden. One evening, as he strolled under the moonlight, he saw a faint shadow in the flowers. It had just stopped raining, and the earthen wall was slightly damaged. He suspected it was someone from the neighborhood stealing vegetables and approached to question them. It turned out to be a beautiful woman hiding behind a tree, who knelt and replied: "I am a fox girl. Afraid of just and upright people, I dare not approach, so I came at night to pick flowers. I did not expect to be seen by you. Please show leniency." Her words were gentle and soft, and in her glances, a hundred charms came to life. The scholar was bewitched by her, engaged in conversation with her, and she responded in a graceful and yielding manner. She also said that she could become invisible and leave no trace. Even if someone was nearby, they would not see her, and she would not come to the knowledge of the students. They then became intimate and affectionate. As dawn was approaching, the scholar urged her to leave, saying, "There is noise outside. I can leave through the crack in the window. You need not worry." Soon the morning sun filled the window, and the students gathered in large numbers. The woman still lay down with the curtain hanging. The scholar's heart was restless, but he still hoped that no one would see. Suddenly, someone said that an old woman came to fetch the girl. The woman put on her clothes and went out directly, sat on the mat, and combed her hair, then bowed and said with gratitude, "I did not bring makeup tools, so I will go back to wash and comb my hair. I will come again on a leisure day to ask for the brocade from last night." It turned out to be a new courtesan who had just arrived in the neighborhood, and the students had bribed her to do this. The scholar was greatly discouraged, and after the students finished their lessons and returned for breakfast, he had already carried his clothes and fled. Those who appear to have more externally must have less internally. Is this not true?

Qujiang added that in Jinan there lived a noble young man whose concubine and wife had died one after the other. One day, he sat alone in a lotus pavilion, half-asleep yet not fully awake, when he suddenly saw the ghost of his deceased concubine, the one he had always loved and pitied. Though he was not afraid, he asked her how she had managed to return. She replied, "Ghosts have their own territories, and the local earth deity forbids unauthorized entry. But today and tomorrow, during the time when Lady [his wife] is reciting sutras, they are holding continuous *yan Kou* rituals, so I have come to receive the spiritual food." He then asked, "Has Lady [his wife] come?" She answered, "Lady’s case in the afterlife is not yet concluded—how could she come herself?" He then asked, "What benefit is there in offering uncovered food to the dead through the *yan Kou* ritual?" She said, "Heaven is filled with compassion, and the Buddha is full of mercy. Whether one feeds the living or the dead, the Buddha and Heaven are pleased. This is done to provide spiritual merit for the deceased, not for her to come and eat herself."

A Question About the Afterlife One asked, "What is the state of the afterlife like?" The response was: "Falling into a female body is my past karma, and filling the lower ranks is your past affinity. Both karma and affinity are fulfilled, so I quietly await rebirth. There is also no great suffering or joy, but there is a lack of a small maid for service. Can you burn a clay figure for me?" Awakening in a daze, one temporarily believed that it existed, so a clay figure was made and burned. The next night, one saw a dream, and a small maid followed. As for binding straw and bamboo, cutting paper and tearing silk, creating a form artificially, why are they also spiritual? Because the essence and energy coalesce, and all things take form. Forms are not empty, holding energy and containing essence. Even if they decay over time, they still transform into something, mushrooms and fungi grow. Therefore, the essence and energy of people that have not dispersed become ghosts, and the essence and energy of cloth and silk, the clothes of ghosts, are also alive. Regarding objects, once they have their substance, their essence and energy coalesce. Using substance as a model, the form is completed. Fire burns the dross but not the essence. Therefore, the body becomes ashes, but the spirit gathers in the netherworld, like a person's death, the soul descends and the spirit ascends. In the Xia dynasty, burial objects were made; the Shang and Zhou dynasties followed suit. Thus, sages understand the nature of ghosts and spirits. As for the golden wash and the spring branches, the fine tomb has not yet been built. The funeral hall is desolate, and one walks alone at night, thrown into the flames, a faint sound is heard. This is the decayed energy calling forth, the demon arising from a person, or perhaps something else possessing it. (There was a woman named Fan in Dongguang who saw this.)

Zhu Ziyin, the transport commissioner, recounted that when he was serving as the magistrate of Xuyong, returning from Chengdu to his office, he accidentally encountered a dense forest and paused to rest briefly. From afar, he saw what seemed to be a house atop thousands of peaks, standing upright like a thousand-foot cliff, a place where people could not reach. Just then, he happened to be carrying a Western telescope and tried to look through it. Through the telescope, he saw a thatched cottage with three rooms, facing the sun with open doors. There was an old man leaning against a pine tree standing, and a young girl sitting under the eaves, holding something in her hand, it seemed she was bending down to sew and mend. The pillars of the house seemed to have a couplet, but the view became unclear. Soon, the clouds became thick and dense, and it was no longer visible. Later, passing through the place again, the forest and mountain foot remained the same. Trying to look through the telescope again, it was just an empty mountain. Perhaps the dwelling of the immortals was accidentally seen by humans and thus moved elsewhere.

Pan Nantian's paintings are imbued with a free and unrestrained spirit, yet his personality is aloof and unyielding, often drinking heavily and cursing others, making him out of step with his time. Once, he painted a horizontal scroll of plum blossoms for me, and I wrote a poem in response: "The shadows of bamboo or plants are slanted near water, as if they had been in the home of a hermit on Solitary Mountain. Only the twisted branches, strong like iron, seem to suggest that even the most elegant and refined must yield to the beauty of peach blossoms." This was a playful remark. Later, while serving in the military in the border regions, my concubines found the plum blossom painting too dull and replaced it with a peach blossom painting. Thus, even minor details seem predestined. Wang Enpu of Qing County was a descendant of his grandmother Zhang's wet nurse. One day, returning home from Xingji at night, the moonlight was as bright as daylight. He saw several people drinking and eating disorderly under a large tree. A young man invited him to join them, and an older man scolded the young man, saying, "We have never met before, don't play tricks." Then, speaking seriously to Wang Enpu, he said, "You should leave quickly, we are not human, and it would be harmful to you." Wang Enpu was greatly frightened, running in a disheveled and desperate manner, and barely had the strength to make it home. Later, at a funeral at a relative's house, he suddenly saw the same old man, and was so shocked that he almost fainted, calling out "ghost, ghost." The old man laughed and lifted him up, saying, "I indulge in alcohol, always not enough. During the moonlit night, I was invited by neighbors. The wine was almost gone, you happened to arrive, fearing that adding another guest would not satisfy our empty stomachs, so I lied to send you away. Did you really think it was true?" The room was full of guests, and none could contain their laughter.

A guest from the middle encountered this event and often told it to others. Once at night, he passed by an abandoned temple and saw several people drinking noisily. He was also invited to sit down, but he felt the taste of the wine was unusual, and his heart was filled with doubt and surprise. He was then pushed by a group of ghosts into a deep swamp and transformed into phosphorescent lights that dispersed. As the east was gradually turning white, a farmer rescued him and he came out. Due to this, his courage was shattered, and he then doubted that what Master Enpu saw were real ghosts. Later, he met this old man on the road and was finally too afraid to talk to him. This was said by my cousin Zhang Zixiu. Master Dai Jun Enzhao said: "There really was such an event, but the transmission was greatly reversed. It was that this guest first met the ghost, and Master Enpu heard about it. Once at night, he passed by a certain village, met an old friend whom he had not seen for many years, and invited him to drink together. He doubted that he was already dead and fled in panic. Later, they met at an in-law's house, and he was greatly criticized." It is not yet clear which of the two accounts is true. However, from Zhang's account, it is known that one cannot conclude that everything is true just because one has experienced something once, leading to mistaken belief. From Dai's account, it is known that one also cannot conclude that everything is true just because one has experienced something once, but rather be defeated by excessive suspicion.

Li Qiuyi said that an elderly scholar's household had a fox living in an empty granary, which had not caused any harm for over thirty years. The fox always conversed with people, was fairly knowledgeable about books, and would also come out if invited to drink, but one could not see its form. After the old scholar died, his son, also a student, interacted with the fox as his father did, but the fox did not respond much and gradually became more disruptive. The son set up a school in his home and also did legal documents for others. All the essays he graded were not lost, but whenever he drafted a legal document, it would be immediately torn up, or the pen would be pulled from his hand. The income from teaching was not lost by a single coin, but the money earned from legal work, even when locked securely, was stolen. Students coming and going saw nothing unusual, but when litigants arrived, they would be attacked with tiles and stones, causing bleeding, or the fox would make human sounds from the eaves, revealing their secret plans to the crowd.

He was greatly troubled by it and invited a Taoist priest to investigate and exorcise the problem. The priest climbed the altar and summoned the generals, bringing the fox before him. The fox spoke eloquently and clearly, saying: "His father did not look at me as an outsider; our relationship was very close. I did not consider myself an outsider, treating his father like a brother. Now his son has brought disgrace to the family, committing various evil deeds, and will not stop until he dies. I could not bear to watch idly, so I troubled him to change his ways. All the gold he stole was buried in his father's grave, waiting for his downfall to provide for his wife and children. I truly have no ulterior motives. I did not expect the Taoist priest to blame me; life and death are at your command." The priest got up abruptly, bowed three times, and shook hands, saying: "If my deceased friend had such a son, I could not bear it. If I cannot, I fear that among a thousand people, only one or two can. Is this action from you all?" Without saying goodbye to the host, he sighed deeply and left straightaway. The son was ashamed to the point of being unable to bear it, swore to abandon this trade, and ultimately lived to a natural end.

During the reign of Emperor Qianlong in the years 1766-1767, Mr. Chang Tai, an official of the Ministry of Finance, had a servant woman in her early twenties who suffered from a stroke and became dizzy, her breathing weak and barely perceptible. She died at night, and the next day preparations for her coffin and burial were made. Suddenly, her limbs began to move, and she gradually regained the ability to bend and stretch. Soon after, she sat up and asked, "Where am I?" The people thought she was still delirious, but as she looked around the room, it seemed as if she had come to her senses. She sighed several times and remained silent, from which point her illness suddenly healed. However, upon observing her voice and gait, they all seemed like those of a man, and she also could not comb her hair. When she saw her husband, she seemed not to recognize him. Feeling something was strange, they asked in detail about the reason, and she then said she was originally a man. She had died a few days ago, and her soul went to the underworld, where the judge checked but found some accounts not settled. She was demoted to a female body and ordered to be reborn in this woman's body. She felt suddenly as if falling asleep, then suddenly as if waking from a dream, and found herself lying on a wooden bed. When asked for her name and hometown, she firmly refused to say, saying only, "Why should I be humiliated again for past lives?" So they did not pursue further. At first, she refused to sleep with the servant, but later had no excuse to refuse, so reluctantly complied. However, every time the pillow was offered, she would weep until dawn. Sometimes they heard her muttering to herself, saying, "I studied for twenty years and served as an official for over thirty years; how can I endure shame and be humiliated by a servant?" Her husband also heard her mumbling, saying, "Accumulating wealth only for the pleasure of my children, what is the point of having more?" When called awake to ask, she said nothing, knowing she deeply concealed it, so let it go. Mr. Chang disliked talking about supernatural matters and forbade the family from spreading it, so the story was not widely known. But many still knew about it. After more than three years, she finally died of depression, and no one knew who she was.

A Respectful Teacher Named Qiu Wendagong Said A Respectful Teacher named Qiu Wendagong said that there was a young man named Guo who was straightforward and had a proud disposition. By chance during a Mid-Autumn Festival gathering, Guo and his friends discussed ghosts and spirits. Guo claimed not to be afraid. The others invited him to stay in a certain haunted house to test his claim. Guo readily went with a sword. The house had about several dozen rooms, with autumn grass covering the courtyard, and it was desolate and overgrown. Guo locked the door and sat alone, experiencing silence and no sights or sounds. After the fourth watch of the night, someone stood in front of the door. Guo, ready to draw his sword and stand up, saw the person wave his sleeve lightly. Guo felt his mouth closed and his body stiffened, as if he were in a nightmare, but his mind and eyes remained clear. The person bowed deeply and spoke, saying, "You are indeed a bold man. You came here because you were provoked by others."

A competitive spirit is natural, and I do not blame you for it. Having been so kind as to visit me, I should have done something to entertain as a host. However, today is a festival, and my family has gone out to enjoy the moon. According to etiquette, there is a distinction between inside and outside, and it is really not desirable for you to see them. You also have nowhere to go at such a late hour. So, I have devised a plan to entertain you. I will ask you to enter the lotus vase; please do not be angry. I will provide wine and food to alleviate your boredom, and I hope you will not reject this offer. Several people then carried Guo and placed him in a large lotus vase, covering it with a square table and pressing it down with a large stone. Soon, laughter and conversation from the other side of the vase could be heard, with about several dozen men and women calling for wine and serving hot dishes, each person identifiable. Suddenly, the aroma of wine reached his nose. In the dark, he felt around and found a wine pot, a cup, and a small plate with four items, with two ivory chopsticks lying across. Temporarily, he drank and ate. Then, several children sang love songs around the vase. Someone tapped on the vase and said, "The host ordered to entertain the guest," and the soft and pleasant music was enjoyable to listen to. After a long while, someone else tapped on the vase and said, "Guo, please do not blame us. Everyone is drunk and unable to lift the large stone. Please bear with it for now. Your esteemed friend is coming soon." After speaking, it became silent. The next day, seeing the door not opening, they suspected something was wrong and climbed over the wall to enter. Guo heard people and cried loudly inside the vase. The crowd exerted all their strength to move the stone, and he suddenly emerged. Describing what he saw and heard, no one could help but laugh.

The utensils in the jar appeared to be his own belongings, so he returned home to inquire. Upon investigation, he discovered that the family swallows and the wine and food had vanished last night, and they were just then cursing and searching wildly. This demon could indeed be called cunning and mischievous. However, hearing this made one laugh rather than angry. When he came out of the jar, even Guo Sheng himself was silent. Truly, a prankster! I, Rongruo, said: This is still just a playful act. In the past, while traveling in the Qin and Long regions, I heard of a young man who followed a private teacher to study in a mountain temple. It was said that there was a demon in the temple tower, which occasionally appeared to charm people. He secretly thought that the fox girl must be extremely beautiful, so every night he would go to the outside of the tower and pray with lewd words, hoping to meet her. One night, as he wandered under a tree, he saw a little maid waving her hand. He knew that the fox girl had arrived and jumped up to meet her.

A young maid whispered, "You are a discerning person, so I won’t bother to explain further. The lady greatly appreciates you, but what kind of thing is this? To openly wish for it! The master is very angry with you, because you are a noble person, he dares not harm you, but he only strictly restrains the lady. Tonight, fortunately, he is out, and the lady has sent me to secretly invite you, so you should go quickly." The young man followed her. He felt that the deep inner chamber was not the usual path of the temple. He arrived at a room, the red door was half open, although there was no light, he could faintly see the bed and curtains. The maid said, "The lady feels shy at first meeting, she has already lain down inside the curtain. You just undress and climb onto the bed, do not speak a word, for fear that other maids might hear." She finished speaking and left immediately. The young man was overjoyed and hastily lifted the blanket, embracing her and kissing her. Suddenly, the person woke up and screamed. She stood back and looked in shock. Then, all the rooms and buildings disappeared. It turned out that the tutor was sleeping under the eaves to cool off. The tutor was angry and severely punished him. He had no choice but to reveal the truth and was ultimately expelled. This is truly a bad prank. Wen Da Gong said, "Guo Xun relies on his pride, therefore, he is only mocked by ghosts. This person harbors evil intentions, therefore, he is ultimately ensnared by ghosts. Both students get what they deserve. Is it the ghosts that are good or evil?"

In the village of Li, there lived a farmer's wife who, every morning and evening, went out to deliver food to her husband working in the fields. Whenever she went, she would see a woman following her on both sides; when she asked those who walked with her, they could not see her. She felt great fear. Later, the woman gradually followed her home, but always remained in the courtyard, sometimes lingering in the corner of a wall, never entering the bedroom. When the woman looked closely at her, the spirit would retreat; when the woman turned back, the spirit would follow again. Realizing this was a vengeful spirit, she asked it from a distance. The woman replied: "In a former life, you and I were both concubines in noble households. You envied my favor, falsely accused me of theft and adultery, and caused me to be secretly buried alive. Now I have come to seek retribution. But now, in this life, you are filial to your mother-in-law and constantly protected by benevolent spirits. I cannot approach you, so I follow you every day. Considering the situation, there is no way for me to take revenge. If you were to hold a Buddhist ritual to save me, I would be reborn, and thus our grudge would be resolved." The woman excused herself, saying she was too poor. The spirit replied: "Your poverty is not false. If you can sincerely recite the Buddha’s name ten thousand times, you can also save me." The woman asked, "How can reciting the Buddha’s name save a spirit?" The spirit replied: "Ordinary people reciting the Buddha’s name are not heard by the Buddha. But if you recite with sincerity, as though directly before the Buddha, then you will discipline your own mind. If a loyal minister or filial son sincerely moves the divine, then a single recitation of the Buddha’s name will be heard throughout the three realms. Thus, its power is equal to that of a scripture recitation. You are a filial woman; I know it will surely work." The woman followed the spirit’s advice and began to recite with sincerity. Every time she recited one name, the woman bowed once. When she completed ten thousand recitations, the spirit was no longer seen. This story has been told by the elders, knowing that steadfast devotion to one’s parents surpasses faith and reverence for the Buddha.

It is said that in the village of Wa Dong, there was a man named Liu whose mother loved her younger son dearly. Liu loved his younger brother even more than his mother did. The younger brother suffered from a chronic illness, and the mother was so worried that she neglected sleep and food. Liu took care of the treatment, to the extent of selling his own child to provide for medical expenses. Once, he told his wife, "If the younger brother is not saved, then the mother will be worried. Rather than me dying." His wife was moved by this and even sold her red robe, without any complaints. When the younger brother's illness became severe, Liu and his wife wept and guarded him day and night. One night, a beggar stayed in a shrine to the earth god and heard the ghost say, "The divine light is too bright, and suddenly cannot enter. There is a violation of the underworld's limit, what to do?" The earth god replied, "The military strategy of feinting east and attacking west, do you know it?" The next day, Liu's mother suddenly fell ill at the stove. Liu and his wife rushed to see her, and while the mother recovered, the younger brother had already died. It seems that the ghost took advantage of the situation. Later, both husband and wife lived to be over eighty years old before they died. The daughter of Liu Qi, a servant, married in the village of Wa Dong. She heard it from the old people, saying, "Aside from serving his mother, Liu was stupid like a cow in other matters. Someone told him that someone was disrespectful to his mother. Liu shook his head and said, 'How could there be such a person in the world, how could there be such a thing, you must not fabricate.' His foolishness was often like this. It was passed down as a joke, but it was not known that it was his innate sincerity, just that fulfilling filial piety was natural to him. So there was this doubt." A poem about Wang Yan Zhang's tomb in the Yuan dynasty said, "Who would believe there is Feng Dao in the human world," which means the same thing.

When Jing Shao, the Sima Jiez, served as an official in the Hanlin Academy, he stayed overnight in the Qingmi Hall — this name was adopted since the Qianlong era, when the emperor inscribed the title "Ji Xian Qingmi" on the hall, and it has been used ever since, though the hall itself does not actually exist. After a long rain had cleared, the moon had not yet risen, and he sat alone under the eaves, hearing voices from the Yingzhou Pavilion say: "Today, from the tower, we saw the Western Hills, and understood Du Ziwēi's lively line about the state of the mountains after the rain; it is truly a stroke of divine inspiration." One person said: "This line is good because of the lively word, and even better because the word 'state' highlights the liveliness. If it were 'mountain color' or 'mountain green,' the vivid imagery would all be reduced." Jing suspected that Bo Xizhi and others had not yet gone to sleep, cooling off by the pond, and called out to them but received no response. He pushed open the door and looked inside, finding no trace of anyone. The next day, he told Xizhi about this, and Xizhi laughed and said: "The ghosts of the Hanlin Academy, therefore, would say such things."

Buddhist monks can take over a body, and Taoist practitioners can change their form. Those who take over a body rely on a pregnant woman to be reborn, while those who change their form have declined physical strength and have not yet completed the great elixir. They then borrow a strong and robust body to exchange with theirs. Foxes can also do this. My clan brother Cichen said: There was a man named Zhang Zhongshen who was friends with a fox. He once asked the fox about his methods of cultivating the Dao. The fox said that initially, he practiced illusionary transformation, and as the Dao became deeper, he practiced transformation by shedding his form. After shedding his form, he could change his form. Ordinary people who were dull suddenly became clever, and those who were clever suddenly became crazy. Unlike those who did not initially learn Daoism, they suddenly started enjoying taking elixirs and practicing qigong. People found it strange that their characters changed. They did not know that all their spirits had left, and the fox had attached itself to their bodies and was living through them. However, once they changed into a human form, they then belonged to the human path and could no longer transform and fly. From this point, if they progressed diligently, it was the same as humans practicing Daoism. Achieving enlightenment was easier, but if they became entangled in sensory pleasures and material wealth, desires and attachments, then it was the same as humans being deluded and drowning in desires. Falling into the cycle of rebirth was also easy. Therefore, without strong Daoist power, they dare not lightly engage with worldly affairs, fearing that they would gradually be permeated without realizing it. What he said seems reasonable, but then, the danger of human desires is truly terrifying.

Zhu Jieru said, once he fainted due to heatstroke, and suddenly found himself in a vast wilderness. A refreshing breeze blew, making him feel very refreshed and comfortable. However, when he looked around, there was no path to follow, and he did not know where to go. He saw several dozen people walking ahead and decided to follow them. They arrived at a government office, and he also decided to follow them inside. He saw a grand and spacious hall with long corridors on both sides, and officials and servants were running around as if a high-ranking official were about to take his seat. A clerk suddenly grabbed his hand and asked, "Why have you come here?" Looking at him, it turned out to be his deceased friend Zhang Hengzhao. Realizing he was in the underworld, he explained his lost way situation. Zhang said, "Living souls sometimes mistakenly arrive here. The king will not punish you, but there will still be many questions. It is better to sit in my corridor for now. I will wait until the session ends and then send you back. I also want to ask about family matters." After sitting down for a short while, the king had already taken his seat. Zhu peeped through a window crack and saw the dozens of people who came with him being questioned in court one by one. The conversation was not very clear, but only one person stood up and argued, seeming不服罪 (not guilty). The king raised his sleeve and waved it, and a large round mirror suddenly appeared on the left side of the hall, about a meter in diameter. In the mirror, a woman tied up and being whipped appeared, then suddenly like a flash of lightning, another woman crying and lying down appeared. The person bowed his head and said, "I submit."

He was immediately dragged away. After a long time, the court session was dismissed, and Zhang asked about the recent news of his descendants. Zhu briefly mentioned a few things, and Zhang waved his hand and said, "Do not speak any further, for it only confuses the mind." Then he asked, "Was what you just saw the Mirror of Karma?" Zhu replied, "Yes, it was." Zhang then asked, "The shadow must resemble the form, but now there is no form yet a shadow appears—why is that?" Zhu answered, "A human mirror reflects the form, but a divine mirror reflects the heart. When a person does something, the heart knows it. Once the heart knows it, it means the heart has this matter, and if the heart has this matter, it means the heart has the image of this matter. Therefore, when it is reflected, all is revealed. If one does something unintentionally, they do not know it themselves, and thus the reflection cannot see it; if the heart does not have this matter, then there is no such image." The underworld judges cases solely based on whether there is intention or not to distinguish between good and evil. You should understand this. Zhang then asked, "How can the divine mirror reflect the heart?" Zhu replied, "The heart is not visible, but it is manifested through things. The body and soul have separated, but what remains is the spiritual essence. The spiritual awareness is not extinguished, like a lamp flickering, with outer light unobstructed and inner light clear and bright. The inner and outer are clear and bright, so even the smallest things must be revealed." After speaking, he was suddenly dragged away, and he felt his body suddenly rise and fall, like a withered leaf being blown by the wind. He suddenly woke up, and he was already lying on his bed. This event occurred in the seventh month of the year Jiazi. It was strange that he arrived late for the provincial examination, so he explained it in detail.

The Chaste Widow of Dongguang, a relative of my wife, became a widow before the age of twenty, without parents-in-law or siblings, and also without children. She endured great hardship, living in a dilapidated hut, supporting herself through washing, sewing, and mending. When she was so poor that she had to sell her pot to buy grain, she even picked up broken earthenware pots to replace it. She lived to be over eighty before passing away. I once wrote a preface to the Ma family genealogy, but I have long since forgotten the names of her husband and the clan name of her mother. It is said that when she was eleven or twelve, she accompanied her mother to her mother's family, and there were foxes that, at night, threw tiles and stones at her window. She heard a stern voice from the roof saying, "There is a noble person here; you must not die." Yet, she ended her life as a common woman. Perhaps this is what Mencius called the "heavenly rank"? My former teacher, Mr. Li Youdan, who was from the same village, once wrote a poem about her, saying: "In his early years, he sang of the yellow crane, enduring hardships for forty years, holding a chaste heart like iron, preserving her chastity until her hair turned silver, with a generous spirit, she aimed for immortality, withered and remained alone, enduring many hardships, this thought remained unblemished."

A Chaste Widow's Resilience When the widow first became widowed, she still had a few acres of land, but those who wanted to force her to remarry gradually took everything from her. Her actions were so powerful that they shook the heavens and thundered the winds and rains, and she invoked or relied on ghosts and gods. (One year, continuous rain lasted for a week, and new houses of her neighbors all collapsed, but the widow's dilapidated house, with its leaning pillars, remained unharmed.) Heaven originally always protects the good, yet people still do not pity the poor. She felt that her friends and relatives were few, and she was ashamed to ask for help frequently. Her family lived in a house with nothing but four walls, and they survived on nine meals over three weeks. She starved to the point where her stomach churned in vain, and her hands were roughened from sewing. All that was available to her was the sweeping of leaves, and there was no pot to collect dust. Her face was as dark as a crane, and her clothes hung half like crows. The door was barely covered with a broken mat (the door was so broken that it could not be repaired, and a broken mat had been used as a door for over ten years). Lying on grass was like lying on a luxurious carpet. She only willingly endured hunger and cold, and even talking about hardships was seen as a burden. Thieves mocked the starving (one night, a thief passed over the widow's house, and the widow called out to ask, and the thief laughed and said: "How could I enter the house of a starving ghost?"). Female companions laughed at the foolish (there was a poor woman in the same alley who remarried a wealthy man, and when she returned home in fine clothes, she passed by the widow and said: "See what I am enjoying; aren't you a great fool?"). Her heart remained unchanged through life and death, and the principles of existence and non-existence were the same. Relying on sparrows and swallows in a noisy environment, her strength and resilience came from the pine and bamboo. I admire her virtue and have lived with her for many years in the same neighborhood. I do not hesitate to compose clumsy songs, to express genuine emotions. Public opinion is preserved in the village school, and court evaluation awaits the historian. At some point, she may receive a purple edict, shining brightly along the banks of the nine rivers. This was written by the teacher in 1762 when he was in charge of my family. Therefore, it only mentions forty years of hardship. The poetic style is extremely similar to that of Xiangshan. I respectfully record it here, one to demonstrate the virtue of the chaste widow, and one to preserve the writings of the former teacher. Later, the father-in-law Zhou Lu Ma saw this poem and then cut out 300 acres of fertile land to establish an heir for the chaste widow and also to request a banner of honor. Perhaps it is the power of admonition and guidance.

During my service in the Western Regions, I was so busy drafting official documents and military proclamations that I had no time for poetry, and thus stopped reciting it. Occasionally, I would get a line or two, but they would be forgotten immediately after the moment passed. The 160 miscellaneous poems from Urumqi were all composed by recalling them on the way back, not written on the spot. One day, I wrote a quatrain for a subordinate officer named Mao, recounting my life and lamenting the contrast between past and present. The poem reads: "Ambition wanes with age, / Drunk, I lie on an ancient battlefield, / In the middle of the night, I wake to play the iron flute, / The moon fills the sky, and frost covers the forest." Mao did not understand poetry, and I no longer kept the manuscript. Later, a fellow scholar named Yang Fenyuan visited, and we casually talked about it. I don't know on which day Yang climbed the Guandi Temple tower in the northern part of the city and playfully wrote the poem on the wall without signing his name. A Taoist monk happened to pass by and attributed the writing to the hand of a deity. Both I and Yang were afraid of people asking for poems or calligraphy, so we never spoke of it. People also slightly knew that I could write poetry but not calligraphy, and that Yang could write calligraphy but not poetry, so they did not suspect us. The poem nearly became a painting. When I returned to the capital in 1851 for a farewell ceremony, I finally revealed it to the crowd, and I felt a sudden sense of loss. In the Southern Song Dynasty, a person from Fujian named Lin Wai inscribed a poem at West Lake, which was mistakenly attributed to the hand of a deity. In the Yuan Dynasty, a poem by Wang Huanghua inscribed in Shanxi was later copied in Yunnan and also mistakenly attributed to the hand of a deity. Thus, the various books that call such poems divine are numerous in this category.

Mr. Tu Yu-zhai once recounted that there was a candidate for an official position who was touring the Fishing Terrace. At the time, there was a social gathering at the Western Peak, and many women were present. As dusk approached, the number of carriages and horses gradually decreased. A woman came gracefully, holding a child in her left arm and a rattle drum in her right. Upon seeing the candidate, she shook the rattle drum once. The candidate smiled, and the woman also smiled. The candidate, known for his cunning, guessed that the woman's attire resembled that of a noble family, but she was carrying a child alone, which seemed like a village woman. Her movements were strange, and he suspected her to be a fox spirit. He followed her and engaged in a long conversation. The woman subtly revealed her young widowhood. The candidate smiled and said to her, "Don't talk much; I know you, and I am not afraid of you. However, I am poor. I have heard that you can bring wealth. If you can support me, I will follow you." The woman also smiled and said, "Then we will go together." They arrived at her house, which was not very grand but was quite elegant and clean. She also had parents, aunts, and sisters. They understood each other without talking about family backgrounds and only exchanged gifts and were friendly. When the wine was finished, they went to bed, and their intimacy was to the utmost. The next day, they entered the city, and he took a small servant and a blanket with him. They were quite comfortable. However, the woman was wanton without limit, running around almost exhausted. She gradually made the bed, served in washing and grooming, arranged clothes, took care of cleaning, and even tended to the stove and tea bowl.

After a long time, the woman's mother-in-law and sisters began to tease and command her like a servant, the man was captivated by her beauty and tempted by her wealth, unable to refuse them. One day, they made him clean the toilet, but the man refused. The woman became angry and said, "Everything follows your wishes, is this not following mine?" The other women also joined in mocking and scolding him, and thus they gradually became at odds. Soon, she would go out at night and not return, claiming to stay overnight with relatives. Sometimes guests would come, and they were all said to be cousins. They would laugh, joke, and drink together, or play the pipa and sing, while forbidding the man from coming near. The man became furious, and the woman also became angry, and she laughed and said, "If not like this, where would the gold and silk come from? If I were to refuse guests, it would be easy, but we have thirty people in our family, can you provide for us?" The man knew he could not stay, so he took a small servant to the capital and rented a house. The next day when he returned, the place was desolate with wild grass and smoke, and no one lived there anymore, and his clothes were unknown where they went. The man originally brought hundreds of gold, was good at managing wealth, and his clothes were quite tattered, but suddenly he was dressed in fine and elegant clothes, and everyone was surprised. He explained the situation of a son-in-law, and people did not doubt him. Soon, he was tattered again, reluctant to speak about it himself, and later the small servant secretly revealed the matter, and people then knew it. Cao Mutang, the Chief Censor, said, "This demon secretly fled, still had some human reason, but I have seen things even worse than this."

Wuqiang Zhang Gong, with a good reputation, was a juren (provincial-level imperial examination pass) in the year Dingyou of the Kangxi era and was Liu Jingnan's father-in-law. He recounted that a certain official married a concubine, with a rather light dowry. It was said that her mother loved her daughter very much and that she should be at the residence on the fifteenth day of each month, returning to her parents' home on the fifteenth. Pleased by her beauty and the low cost, he finally complied with it. Later, another official also married a concubine, agreeing to similar terms. When the first official initially refused, he cited this other official as an example. After inquiring and finding it to be true, he also complied. The two men were actually the same year's juren. One day, they talked about it, and the first official suddenly realized: "Your daughter Ah Qiao, does she return to her parents' home in the first half of the month or the second half?" The other replied, "The second half." The first official then understood and suddenly brought her into the inner room to see her, and indeed, it was the same person. Because when she was first sold, they had already reserved the possibility of reselling her. Zhang Gong is a sincere and honest gentleman, and he must have no false words. However, families in the capital who sell women, even if they change in countless ways, will still deceive with their own methods, so their scheme did not fail immediately. If she returns to her parents' home on a fixed date every month, it is not reasonable or logical, and frequent travel between the two families—how could people not know? This is surely a path to failure. Cunning people would never do this. Perhaps the hearsay was inaccurate, and did Zhang Gong mishear it? Even when walking on the purple path to admire flowers, one often gets lost. The creation of this story, indeed, is not without reason.

Zhu Qinglei recounted that Li Hualu, while in the capital, purchased a concubine for five hundred taels of silver. On a trip to Tianjin for another matter, he returned to the capital and encountered a friend on the road. He got out of the carriage to greet him, only to see his concubine and two matchmakers passing by in a carriage. He was greatly startled, and the concubine seemed not to recognize him. He feared a mistaken recognition, thinking she was wearing the embroidered robe he had recently made for her, which added to his suspicion. They parted hastily, and upon returning home, he found the concubine still there. As soon as he saw her, he asked if she had arrived earlier, and the matchmakers asked where she was to be married. The concubine was in a state of panic and unable to respond. Li Hualu became angry and sent a servant boy to call her parents to take her back. The parents arrived in a fluster, and her sister, hearing that something had happened to her sister, came along as well. Upon entering the house, they found the concubine was indeed the same girl they had seen in the carriage, and she was still wearing the borrowed embroidered robe. It turned out that she was a year younger than her sister and looked somewhat similar. Li Hualu, jumping and roaring like a tiger, realized what had happened and became silent. The parents questioned why he had called them, and he explained the mistaken recognition, deeply admitting his fault. The parents then explained that they were about to sell their second daughter and had borrowed the embroidered robe to follow the matchmaker on the journey. They asked for the price, and he said, "Three hundred taels, and it is not agreed upon." Li Hualu smiled, quickly opened a chest and took out five hundred taels, placing them on the table and saying, "Is it acceptable if it is the same price as her sister?" They reached an agreement in an instant, and he decided to keep her and not send her back. That night, they shared the same bed. Their meeting was by chance, and this can also be called a delightful tale.

Liu Dongtang said that there was a certain wild scholar who had a perverse and deluded nature, criticizing both ancient and modern times, and positioning himself as superior. Anyone who pointed out even a single flaw in his poetry or prose harbored deep resentment towards him, sometimes even leading to physical altercations. During the annual examination in Hejian, several dozen people were staying in the same lodging, some of whom were acquainted, while others were not. On a summer night, they were scattered in the courtyard, cooling off, when the wild scholar began to speak freely and loftily. Everyone feared his words and remained silent, not responding. Only one person sitting behind a tree argued back with words, repeatedly finding and exploiting his weaknesses until the wild scholar had no logical basis and ran out of words. Enraged, the wild scholar asked, "Who are you?" The person responded in the dark, "I am Jiao Wangxiang, an old scholar in Hejian." Shocked, the wild scholar asked, "Are you not going to die soon?" Jiao Wangxiang laughed and replied, "If I don't die, how dare I touch the tiger's whiskers?" The wild scholar jumped and shouted, searching around the wall, but only heard laughter, sometimes in the tree, sometimes at the eaves.

Wang Hongxu said, when the embankment was being built in Maozhou, there was a young woman carrying a bundle, struggling as she walked along the embankment, resting under a willow tree. At that time, dozens of workers were also resting under the trees. The young woman said she was returning from her mother's home, and her younger brother was riding a donkey to send her. The donkey got startled and fell to the ground, and her brother entered a millet field. From morning until noon, he had not returned, so she unwillingly continued along the embankment herself. Her home was northwest of this place, about four or five miles away. She asked, "Who can carry my bundle and accompany me? I will pay you a hundred coins." A young man secretly thought this was an opportunity to flirt, or at least he would still get paid, so he followed her. They chatted and flirted along the way, but she did not respond much, nor did she reject much. After traveling three or four miles, suddenly, seven or eight people blocked the road and said, "What kind of crazy person are you, daring to lust after our family's woman?" They all tied him up and beat him. They all said that taking him to the authorities would only lead to litigation, and it would be better to bury him. The young woman then described his flirtatious remarks, making it even more difficult for him to defend himself. He only repeatedly begged for mercy. One of them said, "Let's forgive you this time, but you must be punished by digging open this ditch and draining all the accumulated water." They gave him a spade, sat and watched, urging him. He dug until midnight, and the waterway was finally opened. The others also disappeared. Looking around all sides, reeds grew abundantly, and there were no villages in sight. They suspected that a fox den was flooded, and they lured this person to dig it out.

"Records of the Invisible Pavilion" Volume 17, "Casually Listening to It" Chapter 3 My cousin Zhuting recounted that in Wen'an, there was a laborer who worked outside the ancient pass of Gubeikou. He had been without news for a long time. His parents, during a year of famine, also went to seek food outside and were searching for their son, but they too had no news for a long time. Later, someone saw them near Mount Tai and said that they had once traveled to the northeast of Miyun, where it was already dusk. The sky was filled with wind and clouds, and they saw lights in the valley from afar. They went there to seek shelter, and upon arrival, they found a few earthen houses surrounded by sorghum fences. An old woman answered the door, and when asked about their hometown, she was informed and then sent to ask about their names, ages, and whether they had a son who had gone out, what his name was, and how old he was. They answered truthfully. Suddenly, a woman dressed herself and came out, inviting them to sit in the upper seat. She bowed and stood beside them, urging the old woman to have the maids prepare wine and dishes. Her demeanor was very close and affectionate. They could not understand the reason for this, so they got up and firmly questioned her. She then fell to the ground and said, "I dare not deceive my father-in-law and mother-in-law. I am a fox spirit. I once married your son, and we were in love, but I did not have the intention to flatter him. I did not expect him to love me too much, and he eventually died of tuberculosis. I always feel guilty and regretful, so I have vowed not to marry anyone else and have lived by his grave. Now, I did not intend to meet my father-in-law and mother-in-law, but please do not go elsewhere. I can still take care of them."

At first, she was greatly frightened, but soon she saw that her intentions were sincere. They embraced each other, weeping, and decided to live together. The fox woman served without fail, and her service surpassed that of a human son. For six or seven years, the fox woman suddenly sent an old woman to buy a coffin and prepare spades and baskets. Curious, they asked why. She replied joyfully: "My parents-in-law should celebrate me. I serve my parents-in-law, remembering the deceased, just to express a small part of my heart. Unexpectedly, this moved the local deities, who informed the Lord of Mount Tai. The Lord of Mount Tai, moved by her sincerity, allowed her to achieve enlightenment without waiting for the elixir to be completed. Now, I am burying my remains together with my husband, symbolizing our unity in death." She led them to a side room, where indeed a black fox lay on the bed. Its fur shone like lacquer, and when lifted, it was as light as a leaf. When struck, it made a metallic sound, confirming her true immortality. After the burial, she announced: "I now serve the Goddess of Mount Tai as a female official and will go to Mount Tai. Please come with me." Thus, they traveled there together, renting a house and living among the locals. The fox woman only refused to let people see her true form, but her service remained unchanged. Later, her ultimate fate remained unknown. This story is somewhat similar to the previous one about the fox woman. However, in the earlier case, she acted with a purpose and thus narrowly escaped punishment. In this case, she acted without any ulterior motive, and thus she was able to achieve enlightenment. There are no disloyal or unfilial immortals in heaven; this statement is surely true.

Zhudting further said, there was someone who stayed overnight in the corridor of the Temple of the City God and heard a ghost speaking in the hall: "Issued a warrant to arrest a certain woman. The woman loved her sick mother-in-law and refused to die, repeatedly and firmly questioning, the spirit did not leave the house, and could not be taken away, what shall we do?" The City God replied, "Stupid loyalty and filial piety, often do not consider success or failure, and struggle against fate, those who suffer alone are indeed not few. The utmost sincerity and faithfulness, that which cannot be taken away by ghosts and gods, can save a few, sometimes it happens. Resisting fiercely with a strong spirit, the situation is vastly different, this should be appealed to the Emperor of Mount Hua for instructions, do not hastily send a fierce ghost." After the words ended, it became silent, and it is unknown whether they were eventually able to arrest her, but it is enough to know that man can conquer nature, and there is indeed such a principle. Consealangzhong Demao, known in the world as one who judges cases in the underworld, once said he overturned a case and felt quite proud, but he dared not reveal his name. The case involved a woman who was expelled by her mother-in-law due to the slander of her younger sister-in-law, which was not her fault. The mother-in-law was stubborn, and in a sudden judgment, there seemed to be no way to reverse the situation, and there was no one from her family to support her. Thus, she entered a nunnery, waiting for her mother-in-law's mind to change. Her husband pitied her and occasionally visited her, and he could not remain indifferent. There was an abandoned garden beside the nunnery, and they would secretly meet by breaking through the wall at night, coming and going for over a year until they were discovered by the nun's teacher. The teacher strictly adhered to the precepts and considered it to defile the Buddhist land, rebuking the husband not to come, and if he came, she would be expelled. The husband thus ceased to appear, and the woman eventually died in sorrow. The official of the underworld said that having entered the monastic life, she should follow Buddhist law, but she indulged in lust and violated the precepts, and should be judged according to monastic rules, and was sentenced to hell.

Con rebutted: A nun who violates the precept against sexual misconduct indeed deserves a clear punishment. However, if she initially took refuge in the Three Jewels but later violated her vows, then punishing her according to monastic rules would be irrefutable. But this woman was guiltless in her separation, hoping to mend a broken relationship, and the bond between them was not severed; her resolve remained steadfast. She was merely alone and destitute, seeking refuge in a remote temple. As for her becoming a nun, one could only say that she mutilated her appearance, not that she followed the law; as for her residing in the temple, one could only say that she borrowed a bed, not that she practiced meditation. If one were to consider her wandering life as a sinful deed, then what crime would be added to the story of the celestial maiden taking a man as her husband? As for her feelings for her former husband and their secret meetings, her actions seemed to resemble the gift of a peony, and the situation was akin to the gathering of withered grass. Though they were once intimate, the principle of losing chastity was different. The laws of the living world prescribe only corporal punishment for premarital relations, and even then, redemption is possible; in this case, the violation of propriety might be seen as less severe, especially since she had already died of depression. Even if there were minor faults, they would suffice to cover her sins. Therefore, her punishment should be lenient, and she should be directly sent to the wheel of rebirth, balancing reason and emotion, which seems to be a fair compromise. The matter was presented to the king of the underworld, who ultimately agreed with the argument. Whether this statement is true or false cannot be verified, but according to the reasoning presented, it is indeed a fair judgment. Moreover, when Con was on his deathbed, he claimed that he was demoted to a village deity for revealing too many secrets of the afterlife. Let us provisionally accept this claim, as it serves as a warning to those who are overly critical or judgmental.

Kurkala Wusu—Kurkala, translated as "black." Wusu, translated as "water." The military officer Li Yin once accompanied the military commander Liu De in the mountains, where he saw an old pine tree pierced by an arrow, the cause of which was unknown. In the evening, they stayed at a posthouse, and Li Yin then recounted that he had passed through this place before and had seen a rider galloping from afar, suspecting it to be a *Mahachin* (bandit), he hid in the deep grass to wait. As it approached, he saw a being that was neither human nor animal, riding a wild horse. Recognizing it as a strange creature, he shot an arrow and hit it. It resonated like a bell, transformed into black smoke and disappeared, and the wild horse also fled in fright. Now, this arrow is still in the tree, indicating that it was a *Mu Yao* (wooden demon). When asked why he had not spoken of this earlier, he said: "When I shot, it had not seen me. Since it has a spirit, I feared it might hear and seek revenge, so I chose to remain silent." His caution was typical of his cautious nature.

One day, in Tacheng, a fugitive criminal named Mandar was captured and brought before the official Yin, who was ordered to meet and release him. Mandar's hands were bound with an iron ring, and his feet were chained to the horse's belly. At that time, he was on the brink of death, barely alive, and when given food, he could hardly swallow it. Whenever he was on the horse, he would often want to fall off, but thanks to being chained to the horse, he did not fall. They were only concerned about his death, not about his escape. When they reached the Gobi Desert, where two horses were side by side, Mandar made another attempt to fall off. The official Yin reached out to steady him, but Mandar suddenly stood up straight and struck the official with the iron ring, causing him to fall off the horse. Mandar then turned the reins and galloped into the Gobi Desert. The Gobi Desert stretches northeast to Kobdo, covering hundreds of miles, a place where no human has ever been, and nobody could catch up to him. It was then realized that his illness was feigned. The deputy general Yue Ji was severely punished for this, and the official Yin was also sentenced to wear a long wooden collar. Later, in Ili, Mandar was captured again. He was a member of the Oirat tribe that had come to surrender and received the thickest reward. Mandar came out for the bait and was therefore captured. When asked why he dared to come again, he said, "My crime is so severe, I assumed they would not expect me to come; I came with the crowd, and they would not suspect me among them." His plan was indeed clever, but he did not anticipate that they would recognize the arrow scar on his head. Despite the official's cleverness, he was ultimately deceived by the trick; despite the fugitive's cunning, he was ultimately defeated by deceit. They were constantly battling each other mentally, and truly, it is hard to say where this ends. Even the wisest will eventually meet their match, and there is no one who never makes a mistake after much thought. This is a certain truth.

Li Shangyin's poem: "Hearing only the ghostly lament of the *Ziye* song," which uses the *Ziye* song from the Jin Dynasty. Li He's poem: "Ghosts singing *Bao* family poems in autumn graves," which transforms Bao Zhao's *Hao Li Xing* into a fanciful song. Indeed, there are often such events in the world. Tian Xiangqin recounts: Once, while reading in a secluded house, on a night when the wind was calm and the moon was bright, he heard someone singing *Kunqu* opera. The voice was clear and resonant, heart-wrenching and soul-stirring. Carefully examining it, he realized it was the *Jiao Hua* scene from *The Peony Pavilion*. He forgot his surroundings and listened quietly until the end, then suddenly realized that outside the wall were only broken waters and desolate dikes, rarely visited by people. Where did this song come from? Opening the door to look, he saw only the rustling of reeds and wild rice.

Xiang Wan further said, there was an old scholar who taught disciples in a wild temple, where many graves lay outside. At night, they sometimes saw ghostly forms or heard ghostly voices. The old scholar, being brave, was not afraid, and his servants, having become accustomed, were also not afraid. One night, through the wall, he heard a voice say, "I have been your neighbor for a long time, knowing that you are not surprised. I have heard your recitations; there should be a poem by Wen Tingyun on your desk. I ask you to copy one of his *Damozhi Qu* and burn it." The voice then whispered, "The last line, 'Yecheng wind and rain connect the grass to the sky,' I ask you to write 'connect' as '粘' instead of '连,' which would be very moving. Just now, we were arguing over this one word, betting small drinks and food." The old scholar happened to have Wen's collection, so he threw it over the wall. After about an hour, suddenly leaves flew wildly, a violent whirlwind swept up mud and sand, splattering the windows like a sudden downpour. The old scholar laughed and scolded, saying, "You lot don't be so petty. I've thought this through carefully; when two people compete, one will lose, and the loser will complain, which is just how things work. But if I change the word to cause complaints, my words are wrong; if I use the original word to cause complaints, my words are right. Listen to your tricks, I don't feel ashamed." After he finished speaking, the wind stopped. Zhu Heting said, "It's a ghost who reads books, so even though it's proud and seeks victory, it can be reasoned with." But if the old scholar hadn't given out this collection, wouldn't it have been better for both sides? Wang Guyuan said, "You're talking about worldly law; if the old scholar understands worldly law, he wouldn't be an old scholar anymore."

The old woman who manages the kitchen said: She saw the head of a drunken Zhong Kui. There was a woodcutter who was cutting wood on a hillside, tired and taking a short rest, when he saw a person carrying several sets of clothes and discarding them along the way. He was puzzled by the reason and observed closely, seeing that the person moved through dangerous obstacles as if they were smooth paths, moving very quickly, beyond human capability, with a pale and ghostly appearance unlike that of a normal human. Suspecting it to be a demon or ghost, he climbed a high tree to look down on it, but the person was already gone. Following the path of discarded clothes, he turned and came to a valley where a tiger was lying in wait. He realized the person was a ghost and the clothes were from those the tiger had eaten. He quickly abandoned his wood and fled down the hill. The next day, he heard that someone from a village died there at the hands of the tiger. The path was not one that people must necessarily take, so he knew the ghost lured the person there with the clothes. Nothing is more intelligent than humans, yet humans are often lured by bait; now, the object lures humans with bait. Is it not that human intelligence is obscured by greed, making their wisdom fall below that of animals? However, once this matter became known, hunters followed the trail of the discarded clothes to find the tiger's lair, combined their firearms to shoot, and killed three of them. Thus, the tiger was defeated by its own intelligence. Endless cycles of dependence and deceit, how can such schemes ever end? Some say: The tiger is extremely fierce but also extremely foolish, its cunning and calculation far from reaching this level. It is said that the ghost serves the tiger and can only be reborn after finding a replacement, likely luring people to replace them, thereby leading them to hunt and kill the tiger to avenge themselves. The ghost is a human who has transformed, and considering human affairs, such things do indeed exist. It is a pity that the tiger knows the ghost helps it but does not realize that the ghost is also harming it.

Liang Huotang reported that there was a wealthy merchant from Guangdong who enjoyed learning Daoist practices. He recruited dozens of Daoist practitioners, who all claimed that immortality could be achieved effortlessly, and the cost was immeasurable. However, they also had minor successes from time to time, which made the merchant believe in them even more firmly. One day, a Daoist practitioner visited him. Although he wore tattered clothes and a worn-out hat, he exuded a noble presence, like a solitary crane and a lone pine tree. When the merchant spoke with him, his words were profound and far-reaching, often surprising the merchant. When the Daoist practitioner demonstrated his methods, he could summon and control spirits, call forth wind and rain as if holding a bond, and produce delicacies such as pine nuts, freshwater fish, mushrooms, and exotic fruits like oranges from Wu and litchis from Min, as if they were within easy reach. He could also command celestial maidens playing musical instruments and jade maidens dancing and singing, as if they were mere servants. When the Daoist practitioner held his talisman, he could travel to the mythical islands and lands of the Daoist cosmology in a dreamlike state. He could turn tiles and stones into gold, and the gold remained unchanged through countless refiners. The merchant was greatly impressed and humbled, and the other Daoist practitioners, seeing the Daoist's power, also bowed deeply and called him a sage teacher, all wishing to become his disciples and seek the Dao. The Daoist said, "Then we shall choose a day to set up an altar, and I will teach you one by one."

At the appointed time, the Daoist ascended the seat, and the crowd bowed in reverence. The Daoist asked, "What do you seek?" They replied, "We seek immortality." The Daoist then inquired, "How can I help you in seeking immortality?" They responded, "Given such marvels, how could you not be a true immortal?" The Daoist laughed heartily for a long time before saying, "This is merely a technique, not the Dao. The Dao is ineffable and natural, one with the primordial qi; how could there be such things as these?" The Daoist continued, "The three teachings have been lost for a long time. The essence of Confucianism is to understand the principles and apply them practically. Memorizing texts and discussing the heavens and human nature are not the way; the essence of Buddhism is no birth, no death. Giving alms and making offerings are not the way, nor are sharp-witted sayings and dialogues; the essence of Daoism is purity, emptiness, and non-action. Ritual texts, spells, and talismans are not the way, nor are alchemical practices and elixirs. All that you see is merely the result of such practices, far removed from alchemical elixirs. How much more so for immortality?" However, without concrete evidence, to hastily reject it as false, you will surely say that you praise what they can do and criticize what they cannot, which is just empty talk.

I will show you various things that can be done, and tell you various things that cannot be done, perhaps you will realize and return to the right path! Confucianism and Buddhism are filled with deception and each has its own way, so there is no need to argue with them. I am deeply troubled by the increasing deception in Daoism, so because you are fond of the Dao, I will briefly correct it for you. Pointing to various Daoist practitioners, I say: Your fasting is just taking a pill to abstain from eating; your foresight is just a peach wood doll; your alchemy is just a medicine in the bedroom; your ability to turn base metals into gold is just a silver reduction method; your ability to enter the underworld is just a jasmine root; your ability to summon immortals is just capturing souls; your ability to bring back the dead is just using fox spirits; your ability to move objects is just a five-ghost trick; your ability to ward off weapons is just an iron shirt; your ability to leap high is just a deer leap. Those who call themselves Daoists are all charlatans. If you do not disband quickly, the thunder gods will come.

Shaking off his clothes and about to rise, the crowd held onto his garments and bowed their heads, saying: "The common people are deeply engrossed, they have already acknowledged their wrongdoing, fortunate to encounter the divine presence, this is also a predestined connection. How can we not experience liberation once?" The Taoist priest sat back down and addressed the merchant from Guangdong: "Have you ever heard of someone waving their hand and ascending to heaven amidst the sounds of music and splendor?" Then he looked at the various alchemists and said: "Have you ever heard of someone transforming into a spirit after showing off magical tricks for profit?" Those who practice the Dao must reject all worldly attachments and maintain a single-minded focus. They must make their hearts completely still as if dead, and only then can they avoid death; they must make their breath continuously flow without interruption, and only then can it be sustained indefinitely. Yet, this is not just about sitting idly. Deities have divine bones and divine connections. Bone cannot be exchanged by medicine, and fate cannot be forged by affection. One must accumulate merit and virtue, and only then can one be listed in the divine registry. Divine bone is born, and when divine bone is formed, the true spirit naturally communicates, and the divine connection is fulfilled. This lies in your own cultivation. How can the deities have a method to guide others? He then fetched paper and wrote down sixteen characters: "Internally cut off worldly connections, externally accumulate hidden virtue, nothing strange or unusual, this is the true secret." Throwing the brush on the table, the sound was like thunder, and he was already gone.

Uncle Wang Hongsheng's family had a fox living in their granary, which did not cause much trouble. However, when little children played near the granary, they were often hit by tiles. One day, a small fox was caught in the kitchen, and the family wanted to kill it to vent their anger. Uncle Wang Hongsheng said, "This is a provocation. When humans fight with spirits or monsters, can they possibly win?" He then led the fox to the bed and fed it with fruits and snacks, personally escorting it to the granary exit. From then on, the children no longer hit the fox when they played near the granary. This was a case of subduing the enemy without fighting.

Another uncle, Mr. An, lived in Liufu Village, east of the county. One night, two dogs at his neighbor's house barked very urgently. The neighbor's wife went out to see and found no one. She only heard a voice from the roof saying, "Your dog is too fierce. I dare not come down. There is a runaway maid hiding inside your kitchen stove. Please use smoke to drive her out, and she will come out on her own." The woman was greatly frightened. She went inside to look inside the stove and indeed heard a faint crying sound. She asked what it was and why it was there. The voice from inside the stove said softly, "My name is Lüyun. I am a maid from a fox family. I could not bear the beating, so I fled and hid here. Hoping to delay my death for a moment, please pity me." The woman, who had been fasting and worshipping Buddha, felt very compassionate. She spoke to the roof, "He is too afraid to come out, and I really do not want to use fire. If she has not committed a serious crime, please let the fairy family go." In the local custom, foxes are called fairy families. The voice from the roof replied, "I just bought her for two thousand coins, how can I let her go?" The woman said, "Will two thousand coins redeem her?" After a long time, the voice replied, "That might be possible." The woman threw the money onto the roof. Then no more sound was heard. She knocked on the stove and called out, "Lüyun, you can come out. I have redeemed you. Your master has gone." The voice from inside the stove replied, "I am grateful for saving my life. I will now serve you." The woman said, "People cannot keep a fox maid. You should go on your own. You might frighten the children. Also, be careful not to show your true form." Indeed, something black darted away quickly. Afterwards, every New Year's Day, she would hear a voice calling from outside the window, "Lüyun, bowing her head."

The Mongols used sheep bones for divination, burning them and observing the cracks (坼兆) that formed, a practice similar to the chicken bone divination of the Miao people (蛮峒). During the time when the scholar Ho was stationed at the military camp of Kuisutu, an elderly woman who understood this method was asked to predict the return date. After a long gaze, she said: "The horse has not been saddled, and the person has not been crowned; thus, the journey will not take place. However, the saddle and the crown are now prepared, and there are signs that the journey will proceed." Several months later, another divination was performed, and upon a single glance, the woman bowed and said: "The horse is now saddled, and the person is crowned; will you not return soon?" Indeed, he was soon granted leave to return. Another high-ranking official, Wen, recounted that during a previous campaign against Ush, over ten people from the Uighur region were captured and imprisoned in a secret underground location. One day, they pointed to their mouths and expressed hunger, and apricots were given to them. After sharing and finishing the apricots, an elderly captive held the pits, whispered a prayer, and threw them onto the ground. Observing the arrangement of the pits, he suddenly cried out in grief, and the others, upon seeing this, also began to weep. Shortly after, a decree arrived ordering their execution. It was suspected that this method was similar to the fire and coin divination (火珠林钱卜), which, while different from the use of yarrow sticks and turtle shells (蓍龟), both rely on human spirit and the belief in supernatural forces.

In the autumn of the year 1663 during the reign of Emperor Kangxi, a tenant farmer named Zhou Jia from Songcun Factory could not bear his wife's beatings. One night, he waited until his wife was asleep, then fled and hid in a dilapidated temple, planning to seek pity from his neighbors at dawn. His wife discovered his absence and tracked him to the temple. She stood before the god statue, enumerating his sins and commanding him to submit to her punishment. The temple was known to be haunted by foxes. As she began to whip him, just a dozen times, he cried out in pain, and the foxes suddenly emerged, crying, "How unfair this is!" They seized Zhou Jia and placed him in a corner of the temple. They stripped his wife naked and used her own whip to beat her until she bled. Just as they were about to release her, another group of foxes, led by a female fox, emerged, saying, "Men only protect other men. How can he die for hiding a woman from his wife?" They seized Zhou Jia's wife and placed her in a corner, while the foxes fought to save him. The struggle continued for a long time. The guards, suspecting a robbery, shouted and fired their guns to provide support. The foxes then scattered. The woman was exhausted, and Zhou Jia, limping and staggering, carried her home. Mr. Wang De'an, who was teaching in the area at the time, saw the woman on the road, still muttering curses. Mr. Wang once remarked, "How delightful these foxes! When ritual is lost, it must be sought in the wild. The female fox did not want to harm her own kind, yet she held a different principle, wielding the sword of her own household. When factions form, cliques arise; when cliques are strong, public opinion becomes confused, chaotic and confused, right and wrong arise in great numbers. They have been grinding against each other for a long time."

Mr. Zhang Qianxi's household, one night, found a maid missing. They assumed she had fled, but the next day, she was discovered drunk, lying under a pile of firewood in the courtyard. The empty room was locked, and it was unknown how she had entered. She washed her hair and soaked her face, and only woke up at noon. She recounted that the previous night, she had heard laughter and merriment from the back courtyard. Familiar with fox spirits, she was accustomed and not afraid. She had been secretly peeping through the door crack and saw wine and food laid out, with several young men gathering to drink. Soon, she was discovered, and she suddenly jumped up and dragged her over the wall. In a daze, as if asleep or dreaming, she was unable to speak. She was then forced to sit down, with fine old wine and harsh penalties. Eventually, she became deeply intoxicated, did not remember how long she had slept, and also did not know when she had left.

Mr. Xuan'er, known for his natural uprightness and integrity, went himself to reprimand him, saying: "We have lived together for many years, and aside from daily fetching firewood, there has been no wrongdoing. Why, then, have you suddenly violated propriety by treating a virtuous servant girl as a prostitute to entertain with wine? The youths are reckless and dissolute, yet where are the father and elder brothers? Should not the head of the household feel ashamed?" At midnight, a voice spoke from outside the window, saying: "The youths have been indulging in licentiousness and have already been whipped. However, among them, there is one who has sought mercy even slightly — this maid first reached her hand into the room, and with a joking word, asked for meat, not forced by coercion. Moreover, she has spent her time in moonlight and among flowers, exchanging orchids and peonies, having been with many men, and breaking many vows over the years. Hence, the youths dared to exchange intimate words with her. Otherwise, why have other maids, who are no less beautiful, never been dared to approach?" "The failure to guard against this," the voice continued, "means that the servant and I seem to share the blame equally. Please look upon this with leniency." Mr. Xuan'er replied, "If you have already whipped the youths, then I shall also severely whip this maid." The fox spirit laughed and said, "She has passed the age of coming of age, yet you have not chosen a spouse for her. Her repressed emotions have burst forth uncontrollably — can the blame be placed solely on this maid?" Mr. Xuan'er fell silent. The next day, he summoned a matchmaker and arranged for all the older maids to be married.

County Magistrate Tian Jin of Qiú reported that there was a Western merchant named Du Kui, whose origin was unknown, but his speech resembled that of someone from the regions of Ze and Lu. He was firm and courageous, unafraid of ghosts and spirits. Whenever he traveled, he would sleep alone in empty houses and ruined temples, and saw or heard nothing.
