Once, while traveling near the foot of Liupanshan Mountain, the sun had already set and it was dark, so he took refuge in a ruined fortress or broken-down house. Wild smoke curled through the grass, and there was no sign of people in any direction. He estimated that there could certainly be no bandits or robbers, so he unpacked his belongings, tethered his horse, picked up dry branches to strike fire and keep warm, and finally spread out his quilt and lay down to sleep. As he was about to fall asleep, he heard a crying sound. He listened carefully and it seemed to be coming from behind the house, as if from underground. At that time, the fire was still burning, and the room was as bright as daylight. Therefore, he lay on his side and held the sword in readiness. Soon the sound came closer, and it was now wailing continuously in the dark outside the window, but it never revealed its form. Du shouted and asked, “I have never seen creatures like you before. What kind of ghost or spirit are you? Please come out and speak.” Someone answered from the darkness, “I am a woman, naked with not a thread of clothing, and I am ashamed to be seen. If you do not reject me, allow me to enter under the covers; then there is something that will conceal my form, and we can converse.” Du realized that she wanted to seduce him, but he was not afraid. He smiled slightly and said, “If you wish to enter, then enter.” A cold wind blew, and suddenly a beautiful woman was lying beside him in bed. With a shy and bashful face, she covered her face and wept, saying, “As soon as we spoke, you immediately leaned close to me. Though I am a promiscuous woman, how could I go so far? It is due to a grievous situation that I am compelled to confess. Though it may seem hasty, do not be surprised that I have run away in a romantic frenzy.”

This former stronghold was once the lair of bandits. I happened to be walking alone and was robbed by them, stripped of all my clothes and jewelry, and tied up, thrown into a ravine. In summer, I was immersed in cold springs; in winter, I was buried under accumulated snow. Deeply dark and frozen, the endless hardships were hard to describe. Later, the wicked gang was punished, and the place was abandoned, becoming a wilderness. There was no one to tell, and I endured the pain until now. Fortunately, I heard a rare and welcome sound in an empty valley and met a gentleman. Such an opportunity is rare, and it is a once-in-a-thousand-years chance. Therefore, I endured the shame and sought help, not hesitating to offer myself. I asked for a small reward in exchange for one night of love, hoping to move my bones to a plain. Perhaps the ground would be slightly warmer, and I could find peace for my soul. If more Buddhist rituals were performed, I could be liberated and reborn. Then, the grace of rebirth would be a debt I would swear to repay by serving as a concubine in future lives. After finishing speaking, I wiped away my tears and relaxed my body into his embrace. Du sighed and said, "I thought you were a demon. How could you have such deep-seated suffering? Although I am fond of women, taking advantage of someone in dire straits and using coercion to seek pleasure is not something a true man would do. You are afraid of the cold; it would not hurt to come to me for warmth. If you talk about a secret rendezvous, it would be better to leave directly." The woman knelt at the pillow and bowed her head, saying nothing more. Du embraced her, and she quietly accepted the embrace. By dawn, she had disappeared. He stayed for several days, arranging for her burial and Buddhist rituals. After several years, he returned home. There was a young girl from a neighboring family who saw Du and followed him affectionately. She grew old without children and asked to become a concubine. Her parents refused, but the girl asked to follow him and eventually had a son. Those who knew the story all suspected it was the reincarnation of this ghost.

The *Song Book of Auspicious Signs* states: "The coral hook appears when the ruler is respectful and trustworthy, but it does not describe its form." It is said to be a natural treasure. Du Fu's poem says: "The official with flowing robes, his writing adorned with a coral hook," which seems to refer to this. Xiao Quan's poem says: "The pearl curtain is half raised by a coral hook," indicating that the hook is made of coral. I have seen a coral hook owned by the former university scholar Yang Gong. It is about four inches long and about one and six-tenths of an inch in circumference. The hook is shaped like a hanging branch, with the attached branches cut off and a dragon's head formed. The silk ring is made from a horizontal outgrowth, carved into a fungus, with a naturally curved stem, clear veins, and no trace of chiseling. The color is pure cherry red, almost unparalleled. The ring of the hook is made from intertwined branches, with the outer branches removed and the surrounding parts retained, also seeming to be naturally formed. However, coral with intertwined branches is common, and such hooks are not unusual. It was said to have been obtained for 1400 gold pieces from a foreign ship. This was during the period of 1722-1723 AD, when coral was relatively easy to obtain and not very expensive.

Once again, when I was in Ürümqi, I saw the late Grand Secretary Wen, who had a piece of jade, the size of a palm, which could be used as a wrist support. The jade was of pure white and lustrous quality, with four red spots on the surface, all as large as the top of a finger. These spots were vivid and fresh like flower petals, not dyed with blood, not treated with oil, not heated by amber, deeply embedded in the texture, and the color spread outward in a halo, gradually fading away until it disappeared. This is a natural formation. The Grand Secretary always carried it with him. During the Battle of Muguomu, he managed the chariot and restrained the horse, dying bravely. This object must have been lost in the wilderness.

Once I saw a merchant holding a jade hairpin, more than five inches long, round like the tube of a painting brush, with the upper half pure white and the lower half clear and transparent like amber, something I had never seen before. Someone offered nine hundred gold pieces for it, but the merchant firmly refused to sell it, and I eventually doubted that it was the result of alchemy. Fifty years ago, I saw a jade crab belonging to Lord Dong Wenke. It was not very large in size, but it was pure white without any flaws. When viewed alone, it was still jade, but when compared to other white jade, it was either slightly blue, yellow, or reddish-brown, with none being perfectly white. This is how I came to know its value. Recently, I talked about it with Sir Zhe Lin, the Agricultural Commissioner, and he said: "When he was alive, he happened to be in a shortage and resold it for six hundred gold pieces." In Yidu, there was a scholar whose talent burst forth, making him quite outstanding. One day, during the cool late afternoon, he went for a walk and exchanged glances with a village girl, agreeing to meet. He secretly sent a servant woman to convey a message, arranging for her to slightly open the back door on a certain evening. The scholar hid his tracks and shadow, feeling his way along the wall in the dark, when suddenly a flash of light, bright as moonlight, revealed a fierce ghost standing in front of the door. He hurriedly ran back, almost losing his soul. The next day, he went to the school, where the school teacher suddenly sat upright and spoke loudly: "I have worked hard to accumulate a small virtue, and there should be a grandson who passes the imperial examination. Why did you climb over the wall and dig a hole, self-destructing your success? Fortunately, I changed my form to block you, so you did not lose your position, but you did come last in two attempts. You receive teaching fees from others, teaching others' children, why are you so unrestrained? He slapped his own cheeks more than ten times, faintly falling to the ground.

During the reign of Emperor Fangguan, a servant in the house also beat her own cheek and said, "You are a servant in my family for three generations; how can you be fickle like someone who serves Qin one day and Chu the next?" The young master had acted rashly; he should have been advised and warned. If he did not listen, he should have reported to the master, but instead, he flattered in hopes of praise, almost ruining his whole life. Isn't this disloyal? If you do not repent again later, I will take away your spirit. After speaking, she also fainted. After a long time, she regained consciousness. The disciple Li Nanjian once saw it with his own eyes. The accumulation of the ancestors is so difficult, while the decay of descendants is so easy. The ancestors' concern for their descendants is so great, even after death they do not forget. Can people not deeply ponder this? However, Li Nanjian said, this person did not pass the imperial examinations throughout his life and died with a chin full of grit. Perhaps he was lost and did not return, and his ancestors could do nothing about it. Or perhaps he attached himself to a private tutor, attached himself to a servant, but not to his grandson, nor to his son. There was still a lingering affection, so he never truly learned a lesson.

Fox spirits are what people fear. In the village, there was a man named Luo who read novels and miscellaneous records, and he had heard much of the beauty of fox maidens, regretting that he had not met one. Near the outskirts, there was an ancient tomb, and people said that foxes lived there, and sometimes humans were intimate with them. He pointed out their den, prepared gifts, animals, and wine, and sent a letter asking for marriage. He also mentioned that perhaps a beautiful young lady in her boudoir, who had already married a nobleman, or perhaps despised as a useless person, unable to be a worthy husband, then he asked for a beautiful maid to serve as a concubine. Feeling grateful, he bowed twice and placed the letter before returning. For several days, there was silence. One night, while sitting alone and deep in thought, suddenly a beautiful woman appeared from the light, smiling and saying, "The master appreciates your sincere intention, and on this auspicious day, sends the young maid Sanxiao to serve you; please accept her." She bowed according to the proper etiquette. With a fixed gaze standing to the side, her charm and allure were abundant, and Luo felt greatly relieved and they consummated their relationship that night, thinking it was no less than a fairy from the imperial palace. The maid is good at hiding herself, and people cannot see her, even when traveling far and staying elsewhere, she follows him. It more than fulfilled his wishes, but she has a gluttonous nature, and food in the house is often stolen by her. When food is insufficient, she steals clothes and utensils, sells them for money to buy more food, and it is unclear who is managing this. It seems she has companions who come with her. Because of this, he scolded her slightly, but her charming demeanor and gentle feelings captivated him, and a slight glance from her lowered eyes made him forget his anger. She is also extremely promiscuous and bewitching in countless ways, and she does not stop day or night.

A severe shortage prevailed, leading to the ruin of the family and the weakening of the individual. Over time, they became exhausted from constant efforts, with complaints and curses often heard, gradually starting to create disputes, and eventually forming enmity. They called friends and attracted like-minded people, causing evil spirits and supernatural disturbances to flourish, making daily existence unbearable. The True Person of the Zhengyi sect was summoned to investigate and punish the situation. The maid appeared and defended herself by saying: Originally, it was due to a request, not an act of elopement. Subsequently, she followed the master's command, not out of casual union. The written records are all present, showing that it was not done without reason to become a demon. As for stealing and licentiousness, these are the nature of the fox, having been so throughout ancient times. They are not unaware of it. Since they are obsessed with beauty, giving up humans for foxes, yet holding the fox to human standards, is this not absurd? Even if we consider human standards, those seeking pleasure from sound and color cannot be stingy with the cost of keeping and raising. Once they are filled as concubines, they should depend on their master for sustenance. If what is provided is insufficient, they cannot help but take things privately.

Within the family, such situations are common. Compared to stealing from others, domestic theft is still somewhat different. As for intimate relations within the private quarters, anything is possible. Sages establish rituals, but they cannot set limits for all situations. Emperors establish laws, but they cannot create rules for all situations. For the legitimate wife, it is still a matter of normalcy; for concubines, it is their proper duty. To record such actions as a crime, yet be unwilling to do so, is a matter of moral complexity. The real person said, "To gather a crowd and cause disturbance, what logic is there in that?" He replied, "To marry off one's daughter with the intention of seeking something, but not being satisfied with what is desired, leading to collective disorder, such situations are countless. Yet no one is punished for such actions; instead, a fox is blamed." The real person thought for a long time and looked at Luo Sheng, smiling and saying, "What you call seeking virtue and getting virtue, what more is there to complain about?" The old man, now old and feeble, cannot command spirits or intervene in family affairs. Later, Luo Sheng's family was extremely poor, and he eventually died of consumption.

According to my cousin Xiu Shan, the servant Wu Shi Jun once fought with another man and was defeated. In anger, he sought to commit suicide and wished to find a secluded place outside the village. As soon as he stepped beyond the gate, two ghosts approached him. One said throwing oneself into a well would be good, and the other said hanging oneself would be even better. He was pulled and restrained from all sides, unsure of where to go. Soon, an old acquaintance named Ding Wenkui arrived from the north. He struck the two ghosts with his fist, causing them to flee, and then escorted Shi Jun back home. Shi Jun awoke as if from a dream, and his desire to commit suicide suddenly faded. Wenkui had also previously died by hanging, for the two of them had once served together in the household of my uncle Li Fu. After Wenkui’s death, his wife and child fell ill and remained bedridden, and Shi Jun once helped them with five hundred coins. Thus, Wenkui returned in this way to repay the debt. This is an event from my family in recent years, and it is somewhat similar to the story recorded in *Xin Qi Xie* about a needleworker meeting a ghost. It is indeed a verifiable and solid occurrence. And Wenkui came to take his place in order to repay a debt of gratitude, and then departed — this is especially sufficient to move those who are shallow and worldly.

Mr. Zhou Jingyuan once said that there was a family from a wealthy household who were on a journey by boat, mooring late in the great river. Soon after, a large ship arrived to moor alongside them. It bore lanterns at its entrance and banners on its masts, and was also an official vessel. As the sun was about to set, more than twenty men from the ship leapt aboard, brandishing their weapons, and drove all the women out of the cabin. A well-dressed woman, from inside the window, pointed to a small servant girl and said, "This is the one." The bandits, upon hearing this, dragged the girl away. One of the bandits shouted loudly, saying, "I am the father of your servant girl. Your daughter was cruel to my daughter, beating and scalding her with whips and branding irons, and no one cared. Fortunately, she escaped and met me. You failed to capture her, and she carried her grudge deep in her bones. Now I come to take revenge." After speaking, he raised the sail and set off downstream. In a moment, the ship vanished from sight. Searches found no trace of them, and the woman ultimately did not know what had become of her. Yet the situation is easily imagined. When poverty forces a family to sell their daughter, how could they do anything else, yet they do not consider that she might become a bandit? When a servant girl suffers cruel treatment, how could she possibly avenge herself, yet her father is not considered capable of becoming a bandit? Is this not what is meant by the saying, "Even a scorpion or wasp has poison"? Again, Mr. Li Shou said that there was a palace maid who was cruel. Once, for a minor offense, she was locked in an empty room, where she froze and starved to death.

Yet without any visible wounds, his father's lawsuit did not receive justice, and instead, he was beaten. Grievance and anger could not be relieved, so at night, he climbed over the wall to enter and killed his mother and daughter with his own hands. After being pursued for many years, he eventually escaped, showing that even if one is not a thief, one can still take revenge. It is also said that a house in the capital city caught fire, and the husband, wife, and children were all burned. This was also the result of the resentment and hatred of the maids, but there was no clear evidence, so there was nothing to pursue. This shows that even without a father, one can still take revenge. I have relatives and acquaintances who whipped the maids and concubines, laughing and joking like a child's game, with occasional deaths. One night, a black mist like a cartwheel fell from the eaves, spinning like the wind, making a chirping sound, and went straight into the inner room and disappeared. The next day, a boil appeared on the neck like a millet seed, gradually spreading to all sides, and the head was severed as if cut off, showing that even if humans cannot take revenge, ghosts can. Who loves their children more than I do? Those who are strong bear their grievances and pain, their pent-up anger cannot be expressed, and once released, it inevitably flows out; those who are weak suffer cruel treatment, carrying their hatred to the netherworld, their sorrow affecting the spirits, how can there be no divine justice? If there is no human punishment, there must be divine retribution, which is also a natural law.

Common belief holds that all ancient jade was carved with the *Kunwu* knife, but this is not entirely true. In the *Dian Lun* (典论) by Emperor Wen of Wei, it is already stated that he did not believe in the existence of the *Kunwu* knife, indicating that such a tool had already disappeared by the Han Dynasty. Li Shangyin's poetry mentions that jade was cut by sand in the desert, suggesting that by the Tang Dynasty, sand was used for carving. Today, the skill of jade carving is most advanced in *Hundustan* (modern India), the same place referred to as India in Buddhist texts and *Shan Du* in the *Han Shu* (汉书). Those skilled in this craft are said to be descendants of jade workers from the time of Emperor Wu of Han, and thus, their carvings often incorporate Chinese flowers and plants, which are not native to the West, following the old tradition. It is also said that there is a special medicine that can soften jade, allowing for intricate and flexible carving. I once saw Ma Xiaocai Xinga, who bought a branch of plum blossoms from the Western Regions. The twisted trunk was so flexible that it could be placed in a vase, and when opened, it formed a box with a lid and a base, even the thin branches and broken petals were hollow. I also saw a bowl with an inner and outer layer that could rotate but not be removed, with a gap between the layers as narrow as a single hair. It made no sound when shaken, and it was impossible for a knife to fit, nor could a knife bend three times and reach the bottom of the bowl. It is suspected that there is another medicine that can bond without leaving a trace, not just soften. In earlier times, seeing such craftsmanship was rare and considered supernatural. Now, with increased trade, such items are common and no longer considered extraordinary.

A Woman from Fujian A woman from Fujian died before marriage and was already buried. After more than a year had passed, a relative saw her in another county. At first, he suspected she looked similar, but her voice, body, and demeanor were not similar to this extent. Catching her by surprise, he called her pet name from behind. The woman suddenly turned around, and he knew it was no mistake. He then suspected she was a ghost and returned to inform her parents. They opened the tomb to inspect it and found it empty. Together, they investigated the matter. At first, she pretended not to recognize them, but the parents pointed out the scars and moles on her chest and ribs. They called in a neighbor’s wife to examine her closely, and she finally confessed. When they searched for her husband, he had already fled. It is said that in Fujian, the root of the jasmine flower, ground into juice with wine, when drunk in a quantity of one inch, can make a person appear dead for a day. If consumed in six inches, one can still revive; at seven inches, it becomes true death. The woman already had a husband, but she secretly engaged in intimacy with a neighbor’s son. Therefore, she ground this root to fake her death, waiting for her burial and then to dig up her body and flee together. The husband’s family reported the matter to the authorities and captured the neighbor’s son, whose testimony matched the woman’s. At that time, Wu Lintang was the magistrate of Min County and personally investigated the case. He considered citing the law on opening a coffin to see the body, but the person was not actually dead, so the case differed from theft or financial gain. He also considered citing the law on drugging children, but the woman was originally a co-conspirator, so the situation differed from abduction or selling. Since there was no direct legal provision to apply, he judged the case according to the original law on adultery and abduction. How ever-changing and strange human nature can be!

People of the Tang and Song dynasties highly valued inlaid rhinoceros horn. As mentioned, various figures, extremely intricate and ingenious in form, were depicted on these artifacts. The inlaid rhinoceros horn of Empress Wu of Tang was shaped like two dragons facing each other, and the inlaid rhinoceros horn of Emperor Xiaozong of Song was shaped like the Southern Pole Old Man leaning on a staff. There are multiple records of this in various books, so it is not a false claim. Only two colors, black and white, are known, and no one has heard of any that resemble human figures. Why is this the case? Marble often resembles paintings. I once saw at the home of Liang Shaosi, the Iron Pillar, an inlaid screen depicting an eagle perched on a crooked branch of an old tree. Its beak, claws, wings, and tail are all extremely similar. It looks sideways and glances, as if it is about to swoop down, and its spirit is also very lively. Zhu Yunshi Ziyi once gave a marble paperweight to my deceased son Ruiji. It was about two inches long, one inch wide, and five to six fen thick. One side shows a cliff face opposite each other, with two people in a boat flowing downstream. The other side shows two tilted pine trees, with clear needle-like branches, and water patterns below. One moon is on the top of the pine tree, and one is in the water. It looks like two small ink paintings. There are inscriptions on it, one titled "Light Boat Leaving the Gorge," and the other "Pine Creek Reflecting the Moon." On the left side, it is inscribed "Ten Peaks Mountain Man," and the characters are all written in eight-part script. It was the property of Wang Yin of the Ming dynasty. My son Ruiji gave it to me, but I did not pay much attention to such objects, and later they were taken away, like fleeting clouds and mists. I occasionally remembered it and thus recorded it together.

I previously attempted to provide a translation for you, but it seems there was an issue with the delivery. Let me now provide the final translation of the source text: I once possessed eight paintings from the Northern Song imperial garden, none of which bore the names of the artists. The silk was as fine as cloth, and the brushwork and inkwork were solid and meticulously detailed. There was a profound and dignified aura, leading me to suspect they were genuine. The paintings depicted various stories, but three of them remain unidentifiable. One painting showed a scene below with hidden military equipment, and above, a moon partially obscured by the top of a tree, with a woman whose flowing garments seemed to dance as if she were gliding through the air like a bird. Another painting depicted a vast wilderness, where a messenger stood with a decree, and a man in tattered clothes approached from the right, with two children bowing to him on the left, while the man reached out to help them. The messenger seemed unaware of the three people, and the three people also seemed unaware of the messenger. A third painting showed a very grand and spacious hall, with five large jars of wine arranged on the steps. On the left side, several beautiful women were elegantly dressed and adorned, resembling noblewomen, while on the right side, elderly women and maids carried and held young children, all standing respectfully. In the center, one person in regular attire sat on a couch, holding a wine jar and drilling it. The first painting was identified as the story of Hong Xian, but the identities of the other two paintings remain unknown. I have recorded these details here, awaiting the examination of learned scholars.

### Zhang Shilin, Mr. Zhang Shilin 张石邻先生，姚安公同年老友也，性伉直，每面折人过，然慷慨尚义，视朋友之事如己事，劳与怨皆不避也。尝梦其亡友某公，盛气相诘曰：君两为县令，凡故人子孙零替者无不收恤，独我子数千里相投，视如陌路，何也？先生梦中怒且笑曰：君忘之欤？夫所谓朋友，岂势利相攀援，酒食相征逐哉！为缓急可恃，而休戚相关也。我视君如弟兄，吾家奴结党以蠹我，其势蟠固，我无可如何，我尝密托君察某某，君目睹其奸状而恐招嫌怨，讳不肯言，及某某贯盈自败，君又博忠厚之名，百端为之解脱。我事之偾不偾，我财之给不给，君皆弗问，第求若辈感激称长者而已。是非厚其所薄，薄其所厚乎？君先陌路视我，而怪我视君如陌路，君忘之欤？其人瑟缩而去。此五十年前事也。 Mr. Zhang Shilin was an old friend of Yao An Gong, with whom he had been a contemporary. He was frank and upright in nature, often openly correcting others' faults, yet he was generous and valued righteousness, regarding his friends' affairs as his own, avoiding neither labor nor resentment. Once, he dreamed of a deceased friend, who angrily questioned him: "You have served as a county magistrate twice. For all the descendants of your old friends who have fallen on hard times, you have rescued and comforted them, but my son traveled thousands of miles to seek your help, yet you treated him as a stranger. Why is that?" In his dream, Mr. Zhang Shilin responded with both anger and laughter: "Have you forgotten? What is meant by friends? Is it to seek each other out for power and profit, or to gather for feasts and banquets? A true friend is someone one can rely on in times of need, someone who shares joys and sorrows. I treated you as a brother. My household servants conspired to harm me, and their influence was deeply entrenched; I could do nothing about it. I once secretly asked you to investigate someone, and you witnessed their treachery but feared to speak up for fear of resentment. Later, when they were exposed and fell, you gained a reputation for being loyal and kind, finding ways to excuse them. You did not ask about my affairs or my finances, only seeking gratitude and praise from them. Is this not valuing lightly what should be valued and valuing heavily what should be valued lightly? You treated me as a stranger first, and now you wonder why I treated you as a stranger. Have you forgotten?" The person shrank back and left. This was an event from fifty years ago.

Generally, the habits of scholars and officials tend to regard not talking about others' faults as a mark of a gentleman, without considering the closeness or distance of the relationship, or the benefits or harms involved. I often see Hu Mutting being exploited by his servants to the point where he cannot provide for his clothing and food. The same year's scholar-official Zhu Zhujun, bravely drove them away, and Hu Mutting's livelihood was somewhat revived. I have also seen that after Chen Yuzhai's death, his widowed wife and orphaned children were bullied by her son-in-law. The same year's official Cao Mutang also bravely gathered old friends and drove them away, and his son was thus able to survive. At that time, public opinion was that those who praised ancient virtues were few and far between, while those who called it meddling were about eight or nine out of ten. I have also seen that Cui Zongxian Yingjie married his granddaughter, renting a decorated sedan chair for the wedding. His household slaves conspired together, and without three hundred taels of silver, it could not be obtained. All voices were in unison, and by the day or two before the event, the price doubled. Cui Gong was filled with anger and sought friends to rent on his behalf, but friends all avoided resentment and did not respond. Some even said that the decorated sedan chair had no fixed price, and the cost varied according to the person's wealth, and it was not something others could rent on their behalf, using cleverness to mediate. Unable to do otherwise, he used his own sedan chair, decorated with two hundred pieces of silk. At that time, public opinion was that those who stood by and watched without intervening were few and far between, while those who understood the lower people's feelings were about eight or nine out of ten. This is right, that is wrong; a matter of perspective. How can one judge between them?

Zhu Qinglei once said, "I once visited the Jiashan Temple and saw several people entering in groups. Everyone else knelt and bowed, but one person alone performed a long bow. Someone asked him why he did so, and he replied, 'Yang Gong is a junior official, and I am also a junior official. We are of equal rank, so there is no need for court ceremony.' Another person said, 'Yang Gong is a loyal minister,' to which he angrily responded, 'Am I a traitorous minister?' Yu Dayu then recounted that Nie Songyan once rode a donkey and encountered a stone mason who was angry because he did not yield the road. The stone mason said, 'Stone workers meet stone workers—Songyan, a disciple of Zhang Maojun from Anqiu, who was famous for his seal carving—how could I let you pass?' I also mentioned a teacher from Jiaohé who argued with Zhang Qinglan about their essays, and the teacher angrily said, 'I and you enrolled in the same year, and we are both here today without passing the examinations. Where do you surpass me?' These three stories are similar. Even the best debaters cannot do anything about it. Tian Baiyan said, 'The vastness of heaven and earth, nothing is impossible. When encountering such people, one should treat them with non-treatment, which causes no harm. But if one insists on making them understand, it only makes the situation more complicated. I once saw two students staying in the same Buddhist temple, one cursing Ziyang and the other cursing Xiangshan. They shouted and cursed until midnight. A monk tried to mediate, but they accused him of supporting heterodoxies and harmed the orthodox, and together they fought with the monk. The next day, the three of them went to the court with broken foreheads. Is it not true that there is no inherent trouble in the world; it is the mediocre who create it?'

In the district of Changping, there lived an elderly woman who kept a vast number of chickens, selling only their eggs and refusing to sell the chickens even if the price was ten times higher. Her residence was nestled at the foot of a mountain, and over time, the chicken population grew so large that they could be measured by the grain. Just before dawn, the chickens would crow loudly and simultaneously, like a call and response. One day, as the wheat was being cut and laid out to dry outside, a flock of chickens suddenly gathered in the hundreds, surrounding and pecking at the food. The elderly woman tried to drive them away with a stick, but they wouldn’t move. She called out to men and women to help, and they beat the chickens with their hands, but the chickens scattered in all directions, and nothing could be done. Just as the commotion was happening, the five-room house collapsed with a loud noise, and the chickens then all flew into the mountains in fright. This event is similar to the story of Li Jia’s mouse in the *Xuan Shi*, which repaid a favor. Cranes know midnight, and chickens know dawn, their spirits moving in response to the energy around them, but it is not that they know time themselves. Therefore, Master Shao said: birds and animals sense the energy first. As for the fate of all things coming and going, it is certainly not something birds and animals know beforehand. How did the chickens gather in a group and save their master from danger? This must be something they rely on.

My cousin Ru Kui told me that A and B both made a living by catching foxes. Their homes were about ten miles apart. One day, they discovered a grave with fox tracks and decided to go there together, planning to meet at a certain place after sunset. When B arrived, A was already there. They went to the side of the grave and examined the den, which could hold a person. A instructed B to hide inside the den while he concealed himself in the dense vegetation near the grave, waiting for the fox to return to its den. A would block its exit, and B would catch and restrain it from inside. B sat in the dark until midnight, but there was no sound. He wanted to come out and discuss their next move with A, but called out for a long time without receiving a response. He tried to go out and look for A, but found that two tombstones were lying across the entrance of the den, with only a narrow slit of light, about an inch wide. The stones were too heavy to lift, and then B realized he had been betrayed by A. The next day, he heard the sound of a herdsman calling out to his cattle. B shouted as loudly as he could, and the herdsman finally heard him. He reported this to his family, who came to see him. They called in people to move the stones, and B had been imprisoned for a day and night. B suspected that A had attempted to kill him and led his family to confront A, intending to report the incident to the authorities.

Until halfway, they saw Jia stripped and bound with his hands behind his back to a willow tree, surrounded by people who spat at and cursed him, some whipping and beating him. When Jia was going to meet someone, he met a woman on the road who teased him, and they secretly had intimate relations in a millet thicket. It was a hot summer day, and each undressed and placed their clothes on the ground. Just as they were about to let go, the woman jumped up, grabbed her clothes, and ran away, no one knowing where she went. Fortunately, no one saw them, and Jia returned in a disheveled state. Before reaching home, he met a group of armed robbers who saw him and shouted, "The slave is here!" The robbers, who were a few young women from the neighboring house sleeping in the courtyard, suddenly saw Jia undressing and lying down. Startled, they called everyone to wake up. He had already thrown off his clothes and fled over the wall. Just as the villagers were pursuing him, Jia had no way to clear his name and could only cry out to heaven. Yi recounted the events of yesterday, and they then realized that they had all been tricked by a fox. However, they waited for its den and ambushed it, as this was a grudge for killing. To repay the grudge with a game, they sealed it so it could not escape and left a gap so it would not die, stripped its clothes so it was bound and could not defend itself, and if anyone noticed, it would flee. Even if its crime did not warrant death, it can still be said to have left room for leniency.

In the world, there are extremely minor matters that even Gao Yao could not resolve. A student named Zhe Sheng Yu Lan, a capable official, was once serving in Anding when two families were embroiled in a dispute over a burial hill, a lawsuit that had lasted for forty to fifty years and spanned two generations. The land was vast but not exceeding one mu in size, with two graves in the middle, each family claiming it as their ancestral tomb. If one asked local witnesses, they were in the midst of countless mountains, and one had to carry food and water to reach them, with no residents around. If one asked about deeds, they all said that the documents were destroyed during the Ming dynasty's wars and no longer existed. If one asked about land tax receipts, both parties were present. Their statements were all the same: "This land is not suitable for farming, there is no profit to be gained, but there is a land tax quota. That is why we continue to appeal endlessly—simply because of our ancestors' graves, we do not want others to take them." They also said, "If it were not the remains of our ancestors, who would litigate for decades and acknowledge others as their ancestors?"

Some suspect that it is a plot to seize auspicious burial grounds, but the people involved all say: "The Qin and Long regions have never discussed this matter before; we truly have no such intention, and we do not doubt that the other party has such an intention. Moreover, the surrounding area is entirely rocky, making it impossible to accommodate another coffin. If they were to obtain the land and then dig up and re-bury the remains, it would be tantamount to giving the opportunity to those who cannot have it. Who would dare to do such a thing?" There is no way to convince them, nor is there a way to divide it equally or resolve it by involving the authorities; it cannot be decided either. Generally, every time they worship, they fight, and every fight leads to a lawsuit. The officials only deal with the fighting, not asking about the cause. Later, Cai Xizhai became the governor of Gansu Province and heard about it, saying: "This is a dispute over worship, not over property. Why not reason with them by saying: 'You claim it as your ancestral tomb; you should be allowed to worship. Those who come to dispute the right to worship already wish to consider your ancestors as their own; it does no harm to your ancestors, nor to you. Allowing them to offer sacrifices is also very good; why reject them?' It is also a temporary solution out of necessity, but it is unknown whether they followed this advice."

Hu Muting said that in his village there was a wealthy man who indulged himself lavishly, shut himself off from outside affairs, and few people ever saw his face. Though unskilled in managing his wealth, his fortune never diminished; though unskilled in self-care, he never fell ill; and whenever misfortune came his way, it was somehow resolved unexpectedly. Once, a maid of his committed suicide by hanging. The village official was delighted, publicized the incident, and reported it to the authorities. The official was pleased and came the very same day. When the body was laid out for examination, suddenly the maid's hands and feet began to move slightly, causing everyone present to be shocked. Soon after, she yawned and stretched, then rolled over, then sat up — and thus she revived. The official still intended to fabricate charges of sexual impropriety and falsely accuse her of attempted suicide, so he gently questioned her. The maid knelt and said, "The mistress's concubines are like immortals — could they have any affection for me? If they had, I would have been too happy to consider suicide. How could I have harmed myself? In truth, I heard that my father was beaten to death by the official for some unknown reason. My grief was unbearable, and I was filled with anger and rage, seeking death. There is no other reason for it."

The official then greatly became disheartened and left, and others often similarly. The villagers all said he was a foolish creature, yet he had this fortune, the reason cannot be understood. Once, through divination, they called upon spirits to inquire about this, and the divination response said: "You gentlemen are mistaken; his fortune is precisely because of his foolishness. This old man in his past life was a village elder. He was simple and dull, without any calculating mind; carefree and indifferent to gains and losses; detached and indifferent to likes and dislikes; open and impartial, without any favoritism. When others insulted him, he had no competitive spirit; when others deceived him, he had no scheming mind; when others slandered him, he had no anger; when others harmed him, he had no desire for revenge. Even if he died in poverty, he had no great merit, but it was precisely this heart that was blessed by the gods, and he received his reward in this life. His foolishness without knowledge is precisely his different nature that exists, not yet forgetting his past good roots. You gentlemen think it is doubtful, is it not a mistake?" At that time, those present were half-believers and half-doubters, and I secretly find this saying interesting. I said: "This is the self-written biography and praise of the master, entrusted to these people. But the principle is indeed there."

Liu Yaozhi, the Secretary, spoke of Liu Sheng, whose name is Yin. This was mentioned during a conversation at Liu Jingnan's home, where it was noted that the northern and southern dialects differ, though it is uncertain if this refers to this Yin. Liu Sheng's family was extremely poor. His father had made a promise of marriage with a friend early on, which was considered binding without the need for matchmakers, marriage contracts, betrothal gifts, or formal wedding arrangements. Both the parents were aware of this arrangement. After Liu Sheng's father passed away, his friend also died. Liu Sheng, being young and inexperienced, became even more impoverished, to the point of relying on a temple for food. The friend's wife plotted to break off the engagement. Liu Sheng could do nothing about it, and the girl eventually died of depression. Liu Sheng learned of this and only mourned deeply. That night, sitting alone under the lamp, he felt uneasy and restless. Suddenly, he heard sobbing outside. He asked who it was, but there was no response, and the sobbing continued. He firmly asked again, and heard a faint response that seemed like "I." Liu Sheng suddenly realized, "Is this the son? I understand now. The matter has reached this point. We can meet in the next life." After saying this, the sobbing ceased. Later, Liu Sheng also died young. Unfortunately, no one took good care of him, and they could not arrange a proper burial on Mount Hua. The story was told, and everyone sighed deeply, forgetting to ask about Liu Sheng's hometown and birthplace. Liu Yaozhi's home is in Suzhou, so perhaps Liu Sheng was from the same region. The "Song of Eternal Sorrow" says, "The sky and earth may have an end, but this sorrow is endless." This is what the story illustrates. Although the engagement was broken without a trace, it cannot be called chaste. And he died of illness, so it cannot be called heroic. Yet his spirit was both chaste and heroic.

In the region of Hejian, there was a wandering monk who sold medicine in the marketplace. He placed a copper Buddha on a table, with a tray containing medicinal pills, and the Buddha appeared to reach out as if to take something. Those who wished to buy the medicine would first pray to the Buddha, then hold the tray and present it to the monk. For those whose illnesses could be cured, the pill would jump into the Buddha's hand. For those whose illnesses were difficult to cure, the pill would not jump. The entire country believed in this practice. Later, someone in the temple where the monk stayed saw him closing the door and grinding iron filings, and realized that the pills in the tray must be half with iron filings and half without. The Buddha's hand was made of lodestone, and gold was plated on the outside. When tested, it was confirmed to be true, and his trick was exposed. There was a scholar who secretly wrote a lawsuit document and was accused by someone. When he went to the authorities, he remained confident and unconcerned, speaking confidently and forcefully. He took the annotated *Xingli Dachuan* (Great Book of Nature and Principle) for verification, and the handwriting matched. He then bowed his head in submission and confessed his guilt. The governor Xu Gong, a learned and well-rounded scholar, heard about it and laughed, saying: "I have always believed in Buddhism but not in monks, and believed in sages and virtuous people but not in Daoist scholars. Today, I see that this is clearly not mistaken."

Elm Willow Pavilion Elder once had a paternal uncle who was reading in a mountain temple one summer night. By midnight, his disciples were all asleep, but he continued to read by candlelight, dozing off from exhaustion. Suddenly, someone knocked on the window and spoke: "May I respectfully ask, which path should I take to get to a certain village?" He was surprised and asked who it was. The voice replied, "I am a ghost. The winding paths in the valley are confusing, and I have lost my way. Ghosts are rare in the empty mountains. Even if I encounter a few mischievous and lowly ones, I would not dare to speak to them. Even if I were to ask them, they might not tell me. Though separated by the realms of the living and the dead, we are of the same kind, so I came to you because I heard the sound of books." He explained the way, and the ghost thanked him and left. Later, he told this story to Elm Willow Pavilion Elder, who sighed and said, "I now know that being aloof and hard to get along with, even as a ghost, would be difficult." Li Qiuyan and Jin Gucun once sat in the Li Xia Pavilion in Jinan on a clear autumn night after a light rain. The first sliver of the moon was just rising. Li Qiuyan said, "Wei Zhuang's line 'the clouds part and the moon shines forth' has a natural and elegant quality. Compared to Zhang Xian's line 'the moon breaks through the clouds and the flowers play with the shadows,' it feels much more effortless." Jin Gucun had not yet responded when someone in the dark said, "Not only is the effort different, but the meaning is also quite different. One is poetic language, the other is literary language, and the style is also quite different. For example, the line 'fine rain moistens the flowing light' from the *Huajian Ji* is a wonderful line in literature, but in poetry, it would be considered effeminate." Startled, they looked around, but there was no one.

Fa Nanshu from Jiaozhou once ascended to the viewpoint of the Sun with a friend. There was already a Taoist sitting on a rock, arrogant and not paying respect, and the two men also did not speak to him. Soon, the red dawn was about to emerge, the sea and sky were dazzling, with thousands of forms and appearances, none of which could be grasped. Nanshu recited a Yuan poem saying, "Throughout history, Qizhou appears as nine points of smoke, at the fifth watch of the night, the sun rises over the sea, isn't this true!" The Taoist suddenly smiled and said, "Li He used it in his Dream of Heaven poem, so it is a strange phrase, using it for Mount Tai, isn't this too much of a stretch?" Nanshu looked back, and the Taoist immediately stopped speaking. Soon, the sun rose, and Nanshu told his friend, "The sun is true fire, so it does not get wet when it enters the water." The Taoist smiled again and said, "Do you say the sun rises from the sea?" This is because one does not know the shape of the sky, so one does not know the shape of the Earth, and not knowing the shape of the Earth, one does not know the shape of the water. The sky is elliptical like a chicken egg, the Earth is spherical like a ball, and water flows along the Earth, like the wrinkles on a walnut. The elliptical shape is far east-west and close north-south, with nine layers in total, the highest layer is called the Primordial Movement, the outer layer of the primordial energy, with no form to be seen; the next layer is the stars, high and immeasurable; the next seven layers, the sun, moon, and five planets each occupy one layer, rotating with the atmosphere, about two million miles away from the Earth, regardless of the sea. The spherical Earth has no fixed top, wherever you stand is the top, the Earth has no fixed level, wherever the eye sees, it appears flat, in the vast, open plains, where the sky and earth meet in all directions, its circle is perfectly round, the center is high and the four sides are low, which is the proof. This is the horizon, outside the circle, what the eye cannot see, is below the horizon.

In the midst of lakes and seas, where the sky and water merge in all directions, the surface is also perfectly circular, conforming to the natural shape of the terrain, with the center being higher than the surrounding areas. This is further evidence of the water's adaptation to the topography. However, the waters of rivers and streams are narrow and shallow, confined between two banks and flowing through the land. Therefore, the sun only begins to receive its light when it rises above the ground. Only the vast and deep sea, which adheres closely to the earth's surface, has no obstructions, allowing the central elevated area to appear like half of a crystal ball. Before the sun reaches the horizon, its reflection shoots upward, initially appearing as a thin line; as the sun approaches the horizon, its slanted shadow crosses the sky, visible before dawn. What we see now is the reflection of the sun, not its actual shape, which is the reflection of the sun in the sky being reflected again by the water, not the sun's reflection in the sea rising from the water. When the sun rises to the horizon, its shadow slants and falls into the sea, and it can no longer be seen.

Ritualists have likely seen this scene, therefore they believed that the sky encloses water, and water floats the earth, with the sun rising and setting in the water, yet they did not know that the sun naturally adheres to the sky, and water naturally adheres to the earth; Buddhists have not seen this scene, thus they consider the four sides of Mount Sumeru as the four continents, with the sun circling this mountain, the south being day while the north is night, the east being evening while the west is morning, the sun constantly rotating, moving parallel and never entering the earth, proven by what we see today, its error is even more obvious and needs no further debate. Nanshu is amazed by his extensive and eloquent arguments, wanting to discuss further. The Taoist laughed and said: Let me finish my explanation, you do not know the vast circle of nine thousand miles, gradually extending and gradually turning, gradually extending and turning, thus completing the circle. You must believe that people can stand upright but not upside down, you take up Yang Guangxian's arguments and stubbornly challenge them. An old man is lazy, I cannot accompany you to Daling Mountain to see the Southern Dipper—Daling Mountain is in the country of Yalu, opposite China in latitude, at that location, the southern pole is 35 degrees above the horizon, and the northern pole is 35 degrees below the horizon—I will not do it. He shook off his robe and left directly, finally, one cannot guess who he is.

The Grand Secretary Wen Gong said that during the campaign against the Uyghurs, there was a brave cavalry officer who had multiple stab wounds in his abdomen. The doctor could not suture them. Coincidentally, several Uyghur women were captured. The doctor said, "I have found a solution." He chose a young, strong, and fair-skinned woman, cut open her abdomen, and used her skin to cover the officer's wound. He then bound the wound with cloth strips, and the officer eventually recovered without harm. After the wound healed, it became one, and the pain and itching were also the same. Wen Gong said that such an illness does not occur in battle, nor does such a remedy. Indeed. However, rebels and traitors should be punished by law. If not skinning, then decapitation. This was done to save loyal and righteous people, which is fundamentally different from killing, to save lives instead.

Zhou Huayuan said, "There were two scholars who wandered through Huangshan Mountain, lingering over pines and stones, forgetting to return as dusk fell. The night was dim and vast, the grass was thick and the moss slippery. They sat together at the foot of a sheer cliff, looking up at the precipitous wall. The paths of monkeys and birds seemed to end, and in the middle of the wall, a piece of stone leaned slightly, like a cloud emerging from a mountain. A crescent moon rose faintly, and they saw two people sitting on it. Knowing that they were either immortals or ghosts, they held their breath and listened quietly." The person on the right said, "Just now we visited Yuelu Mountain. What did this old man say?" The person on the left replied, "When we left, we were just gathering to discuss *Western Inscription*, and now we are discussing *Great Learning: Expounded*." The *Western Inscription* discusses the unity of all things, and the principle is originally like this. But is it enough to merely know this principle in the mind, and thereby save the world with the Way? Parents' love for their children can be said to be deep love. If their children are sick, why can't they heal them? If their children are in trouble, why can't they save them? It is simply a matter of lacking skill. A person's own body, there is no one who does not deeply love themselves. If one is sick, why can't they heal themselves? If one is in trouble, why can't they save themselves? It is also simply a matter of lacking skill.

Nowadays, we do not discuss the governance of the state (*tǐ guó*) and the management of the land (*jīng yě*), nor the methods of guarding against disasters and managing crises, but instead claim that our benevolent heart is the same as the heavens and earth in giving rise to all things. If this heart, by raising all things, can indeed bring life into being, is that truly the case? I do not know. As for the categories of *The Great Learning*, from investigating things (*gé zhì*) up to governing the state and pacifying the world (*zhì píng*), each step follows the previous one, and each step has its own power and contribution. For example, just as soil produces sprouts, sprouts become stalks, stalks become grain, grain becomes rice, and rice becomes food — these stages are sequentially connected. Yet, if the soil is not cultivated, sprouts will not grow; if the sprouts are not irrigated, stalks will not be obtained; if the stalks are not harvested, grain will not be gained; if the grain is not pounded, rice will not be produced; if the rice is not cooked, food will not be made. Thus, each stage also has its own power and contribution. Zhu Xi of Xishan composed *The Elaboration of the Great Learning* (*Dàxué Yǎnyì*), listing categories only up to managing the family (*qí jiā*), and claimed that governing the state and pacifying the world could be directly carried out and put into practice. But do we know for sure that during the time of Emperor Shun, his blind father Gushou truly accepted the teachings, and as a result, the flood was immediately calmed and the Sanmiao people were immediately subdued? Or is there still a method of governance to be considered? Again, do we know for sure that during the time of King Wen of Zhou, the virtuous influence of Tai Si truly transformed the Jiang and Han rivers and caused Chonghou to submit? Or is there another political doctrine preserved? Now, all of this is being set aside, and the root is traced back only to managing the family — is this not like trying to cook soil into rice? I do not know this either.

The person on the left said, "Are the methods of governing in peace proposed by Qiongshan complete?" The person on the right replied, "Zhen is overly fixated on the fundamentals, while Qiu is overly concerned with the details. Neither considers the changing circumstances of the past and present nor understands the differing conditions of the north and south. They list many methods in a trivial and overly detailed manner, and propose to implement each one, which would disorder the world. Even in their proposal about maritime transport, they list the losses over the years due to shipwrecks and claim that the savings from reduced transportation costs would be sufficient to offset these losses. But how many lives are lost in one shipwreck? How many hundreds of lives are lost in dozens of shipwrecks? How can these losses be offset? It is just idle talk." The person on the left said, "That is true. The feudal and well-field systems proposed by various scholars are the great laws of the ancient kings and have a practical demonstration of peace. How are they?" The person on the right replied, "The feudal and well-field systems definitely cannot be implemented, and many criticize them. However, those who uphold these views have a different intention, and those who criticize them have not grasped the main point. The fact that the feudal and well-field systems cannot be implemented is known even to those who criticize them, and scholars themselves know this. Yet they still uphold these views because their intention is to use a thing that definitely cannot be done to hide their true intentions."

To speak of principles, of qi, of nature, of the mind — all are vague and intangible, unable to be verified. Who could examine what form they took before the heavens and earth were divided, or what condition they assumed in the obscure and ambiguous state? As for actual matters, there is evidence. If tested and found ineffective, all can see their shortcomings. Therefore, one must hold a doctrine that is not feasible, making it so that people cannot try it, will not try it, and dare not try it. Only then can one proclaim to the people: "The law I have transmitted from the ancient kings — my method can bring peace to all ages — but what is to be done, when no one employs it?" If no one can question or investigate it further, others will follow suit and praise: "The teacher is a talent fit to assist the ruler — alas, it is a pity that his talents were not fully used." To carve a mother monkey on the tip of a thorn, and yet require three months of fasting and purification merely to see it — this is precisely the method in question. But in that case, there still is a thorn and a mother monkey, so people can at least seek to carve them. Here, however, the claim is entirely based on empty words — there is nothing to carve or seek at all. The most skillful thing in the world is none other than this. Those who criticize it do so with narrow-mindedness and impracticality — how could they understand its intent? For a long time, they sighed together, then suddenly let out a long sigh and departed. Two scholars secretly recorded their words, and the story was widely recounted. A scholar who heard of it said: "Learning is only for the purpose of understanding the Way. The Way, so-called, is nothing but heaven, nature, and the mind. Loyalty, filial piety, integrity, and righteousness are still matters of lesser importance; rituals, music, punishment, and governance are even more secondary than that." Those who speak thus are surely followers of the Yongjia school.

Dong Xiangwan performed divination in his guesthouse and invited me, but I did not attend. It is said that he composed two poems: In this place, spring mountains grow medicinal herbs, I idly follow butterflies across the stream bridge, In the forest, I borrowed a woodcutter boy’s axe, And carved myself a gourd-shaped vessel from the root of a Chinese scholar tree. Overhanging rocks hang with thousand-year vines, Monkeys and apes climb but cannot reach, I remember the broom made of palm leaves that I carried with me, Left behind last year at the highest level. Although the atmosphere is slightly narrow, It is also neatly and gracefully arranged. In the Spring and Autumn Annals, there is the principle of judging by the original intent, and there is the principle of condemning the heart. In Qing County, there was a person sentenced to death. The county magistrate liked to have favorites. His son, aged 14 or 15, was quite beautiful and delicate. Taking advantage of his trip to the provincial capital to stay in an inn, the magistrate invited him on the way, pretending to be delivering legal documents and offering himself. The case was ultimately resolved. He was actually a young male lover, but people did not regard him as a young male lover as something lowly; they judged by his original intent. In the village, there was a young woman who indulged in intimate relations with her husband without restraint. Her husband died of consumption. The mother-in-law observed her nature as wild and unrestrained, always supervised herself. Sleep and meals must be together, going out and coming in must be together. For five or six years, they never took a step apart, and ultimately ended depressed. She was actually a chaste widow, but people did not regard her as a chaste widow; they condemned her by her heart. I think this young man is similar to the case of Guo Liu, only lacking a death — details are in *Pulianyang Xiaxia Lu*. This woman’s heart is unknown, but her body remains untainted. The poem *Da Che* says, “I fear you will not run, I fear you will not dare.” Above, there is still the rule of law, and below, there is still the observance of rites and laws. A gentleman acts with benevolence toward others, and after the coffin is closed, one should still regard her as a chaste widow.

A woodpecker is said to be able to perform a ritual akin to the legendary Yu the Great's steps to ward off evil, and it turns out to be true. A servant named Li Fu, who had a stubborn and bad nature, once climbed to the top of a tall tree, blocked its nest entrance with a small stake, and flattened the surface around it. He then hid in the grass to wait for it. When the woodpecker returned, it gracefully descended the tree, drew a pattern in the sand with its beak that resembled a talisman, finished drawing, and wiped it with its wing. The stake at the nest entrance was suddenly pulled out with a sharp sound, like a dart. How can this be understood? While I was at the publishing house destroying superstitious books, I came across a talisman described in the *Wanfa Guizong* (Collection of All Methods) that matched this pattern. Its design was a complex interweaving of horizontal and vertical lines, roughly resembling two empty characters of the small seal script placed side by side. I do not know how the woodpecker obtained this talisman, nor do I know whether such beliefs are true or false.

Fu once ventured out on a moonless night, leaving the village and entering the dense graves in the south, making a wailing sound like a ghost to frighten passersby. Soon, phosphorescent lights appeared everywhere, all coming wailing towards him, and Fu fled in a panic. This is calling things of the same kind, so children of the family should be cautious about whom they associate with. During the 1892 Beijing provincial examination, I was co-examining with the respected scholar Li Yanbin from Anxi. We casually discussed tigers, and Li Yanbin said: In a village, there was someone who went into the mountains to cut wood. He saw a beautiful woman walking across a stream, dressed in fine clothes, not like a peasant's attire. He knew in his heart it was a demon, so he hid in the dense undergrowth, watching where it was going. Just as a deer led a young deer down to the stream to drink, the woman saw them, suddenly fell to the ground and transformed into a tiger. Her clothes fell to the ground like a cicada's shell. She immediately seized the two deer and ate them. In a moment, she transformed back into a beautiful woman, tidied up her clothes, and slowly walked along the mountain. Looking at her reflection in the stream, she exuded charm and seduction, almost forgetting that she had once been a tiger. The respected scholar Qin Jianquan commented: Her charming and bewitching nature, though she does not change into a tiger's form, her nature of hunting and devouring is the same. When she revealed her true nature, they were suddenly surprised. What is this woodcutter, seeing something strange and being surprised?

The Grand Secretary Wu, while stationed in Urumqi, was quite fond of composing poetry, but he had not seen any of his drafts. He only came across a poem inscribed on the wall of a post station, which read: "Looking far, I see the lone city, the vast expanse revealing the four suburbs. The setting sun casts its light on the tops of tall trees, while leftover snow lies in disarray among the mountains. The herded horses neigh as they return to their stables, the singing birds weary from their calls and return to their nests. The Qin soldiers truly endure the cold, still sounding their arrows at dusk." This poem has a distinctive style reminiscent of the Middle Tang period. According to Shouzhou tenant farmer Shao Renwo, there was a woman from the Li family who was returning to her maternal home. As dusk approached and a fierce storm erupted, she took shelter in an abandoned temple. By nightfall, the rain had slightly subsided, but it was already too dark for her to continue her journey. Just then, several short-term laborers—commonly referred to as day laborers, who work for others by the day, planting and harvesting crops, and whose movements are unpredictable—entered the temple carrying their hoes. Fearing they might encounter violence, she fled again, this time into a dilapidated house behind the temple. In the darkness, the laborers saw her shadow and called out to each other, following her trail. Desperate and with no other options, the woman began to wail like a ghost. Soon, both inside and outside the wall, there were eerie wails that seemed to echo back and forth. The laborers, frightened, turned and fled. By midnight, the rain had stopped, and she managed to slip away unnoticed. This story is somewhat similar to the tale of Li Fu, but here, one instance was a mere coincidence of pursuit, while the other seemed like a rescue. It could be said that her upright and sincere heart moved the spirits, and such an outcome might not be entirely impossible.

I say again with kindness, there was a bandit who robbed a wealthy household. He attacked the main gate, nearly breaking it down. His accomplices held torches and showed their weapons, threatening the household members, saying, "Whoever dares to shout will die. Even if there is a strong wind, no one would hear the shouts. What good would it do to die?" All the household members remained silent. One kitchen servant, aged fifteen or sixteen, was sleeping in the kitchen. She secretly carried a fire starter, moved stealthily on the ground like a snake in the dark, and reached the back courtyard. Taking advantage of the wind, she started a fire, burning the accumulated firewood. The smoke and flames lit up the sky, and the entire village woke up in alarm. Nearby villages within several miles also came to help. When the crowd gathered, the firelight was as bright as daylight. The bandits were unable to escape the fight and were captured together. The homeowner deeply appreciated the servant and wanted to keep her as a daughter-in-law. His son also agreed, saying, "With such intelligence and strategy, she must be capable of managing a household. What harm is there in being a kitchen servant?" The homeowner was very happy and quickly prepared clothing and ornaments. The ceremony took place that very night. He said, "If we delay, we will have to discuss hierarchy, class, and the right and wrong of the situation, which could lead to complications." Indeed, she was a remarkable woman.

Master Bian Qiuya said, one night, a scholar visited his study and suddenly saw a human head on the desk, greatly alarmed and thinking it a sign of impending misfortune. There was a Taoist priest in the village who could use talismans and was often involved in funeral arrangements, so he was urgently summoned to interpret the omen. The priest was also shocked and said, "This is extremely ominous, but it can be averted. The offering for the ritual will not exceed a hundred gold pieces." As they were discussing, someone outside the window spoke, saying, "Alas, I was unfortunate to die by law and thus have no head, making it impossible for my spirit to be reborn. Therefore, I always carry my head with me like a burdensome tumor. Just now, I saw your smooth and clean desk and placed my head there. When you suddenly arrived, I was in such a hurry that I forgot to take it, which caused this commotion. This is due to my carelessness and has nothing to do with your fate. Do not believe the words of the fortune-teller." The priest left dejectedly.

A household of officials was troubled by fox spirits. They invited a magician to exorcise them, but the method failed, and instead, the foxes harassed them. The officials went to seek help from the magician's teacher, who again asked for talismans to be brought. Just as he was about to ascend the altar and summon spirits, he heard sounds of moving furniture upstairs, followed by echoing sounds, and the foxes left in a noisy and orderly manner. The magician looked around with a satisfied expression, and the officials deeply thanked him. Suddenly, they looked up and saw a note on the wall, which read: "Your declining fortune is about to come, so we could trouble you. Yesterday, you donated nine hundred coins to build a baby welfare hall. Your virtue has touched the gods, and further increased your blessings, so we have left as a family. The magician performed his ritual just at that time, claiming it as a success, but he deeply feels ashamed. He secretly offers you wine and food to slightly cover his embarrassment, perhaps it might be possible. If there is any reward or gift, it would be too fortunate for me." The characters were about an inch in size, and the ink was still wet. The magician felt ashamed and dejected, and finally remained silent and dared not speak. In a letter written by Emperor Liang Jianwen to Prince Xiangdong, he quoted a proverb: "If mountains and rivers could speak, the geomancers would have no food to eat; if internal organs could speak, the physicians would turn pale." These two things can be said to be cases where ghosts and spirits can speak. The magician should know this.

According to Zhu Daojiang, there was a wife whose mourning period had ended, but she suddenly became a ritualist, expressing great sorrow and distress, even more so than during the initial mourning. When asked, she did not initially mention her relatives, but when someone privately inquired, she tearfully said: "My wife and I spent half our lives together, and I did not initially find any obvious faults. Recently, I suddenly dreamed of reaching the underworld, where I saw hundreds of women, locked with silver chains and driven with cudgels, entering a large official office. Soon, I heard heart-wrenching cries, shaking my soul and spirit. Then, one by one, they were led out, covered in blood, crawling on their knees, like dragging sheep and pigs. One person among them waved to me, and upon seeing, it was my deceased wife. I was shocked and asked what crime brought her here. She replied: 'Because of the many things she had double intentions with you, I initially thought it was a normal part of family life, but unaware that the laws of the underworld are extremely strict, it is the same as deceiving one's father or king, hence, she fell into such a state.' I asked what these double intentions were, and she said: 'Nothing more than privately favoring children within the family, maids and servants among slaves, relatives among friends and acquaintances, all of which you were unaware.'"

Human relationships are marked by cunning and deceit, none more so than in the capital. I once bought sixteen pieces of Luoxiaohua ink, which came in an old, tattered lacquer box, truly an antique. Upon testing it, I found it to be kneaded clay dyed black, with a white frost on its surface, formed in damp places. During the Ding Mao provincial examination, I bought a candle at my temporary lodging, but it failed to light, as it was made of clay and coated with mutton fat. One evening, I heard someone selling roasted duck; my cousin Wan Zhou bought one, and we ate all the meat, leaving the entire skeleton intact. The duck's body was filled with clay and covered with paper, dyed to look like roasted and exploded, and coated with oil, with only the palms, head, and neck being real. My servant Zhao Ping bought a pair of leather boots for two thousand coins and was very pleased. One day, it rained heavily, and he wore them out, coming back barefoot. The boots' legs were made of black, oily Korean paper, crumpled to look like wrinkles, and the soles were glued with old cotton wadding and lined with cloth. There are many such deceptions. However, these are still minor matters. There was a person who had passed the imperial examinations and saw a beautiful young woman living next door. He asked about her, and learned that her husband was traveling for work, leaving their family in the capital and living with her mother. After several months, suddenly white paper was pasted on the door, and the whole family cried, as the news of her husband's death had arrived. They set up a place for mourning and offering sacrifices, recited scriptures to pray for the deceased, and there were also many people who came to pay their respects.

Afterward, she gradually sold her clothes, claiming she lacked food and was planning to marry. A suitor married into her family, but after several months, her husband unexpectedly returned alive, and it was then known that the bad news was false. The husband was very angry and planned to sue the authorities. The mother and daughter pleaded, and they then kept all her belongings, driving the suitor out. After half a year, the suitor was at the office of the city inspector, where he saw the woman in court. The one who returned first was the woman's lover, and they had conspired to take the suitor's wealth. Later, when the husband truly returned, the plot failed. Isn't the skill of the Liqi becoming more and more strange? In the western city, there was another house, about forty to fifty rooms, rented for more than twenty gold coins per month. A person lived there for more than half a year, always paying the rent in advance. Because he didn't check for one day, he suddenly closed the door and left without telling the owner. The owner went to see, and found scattered tiles and rubble, with not a single beam remaining, only the front and back street-facing houses remaining. Because the house had a front and back door, the occupant set up a wood shop at the back door, selling house materials, and secretly dismantled the beams, columns, doors, and windows inside the house, selling them mixed in different alleys, so people could not notice. Stacked floors and connected roofs were transported without trace, especially skilled. However, these five or six things, some for cheap prices, some for convenience, because they were greedy and took the bait, the blame is not entirely on the person. Mr. Qian Wenmin said: interacting with people in the capital, being very cautious, not falling into traps is already fortunate. Seeing a little benefit, will surely hide tricks, supernatural villains and huge pests, a hundred strange things and a thousand oddities, how could there be benefits for us. Indeed, these are wise words.

Wang Qingshi said, there was a younger brother who plotted to seize his elder brother's property. He invited a lawyer to a private room, where they lit lamps and planned. The lawyer set up traps and schemes, all thoroughly thought out, including counterintelligence and inside help tactics. Once the plan was set, the lawyer said, "Your elder brother, though fierce as a tiger or leopard, will also find it hard to escape the iron net. But how will you reward me?" The younger brother replied gratefully, "You and I are close friends, as close as brothers. How could I forget such great kindness?" At that moment, two men were sitting opposite each other at a table. Suddenly, someone leaped out from under the table, danced around the room on one foot, with eyes like torches and long hair hanging down like a fishing cape. Pointing at the lawyer, he said, "Consider carefully, this man regards you as a brother, but you might be in danger." Laughing and dancing, he leaped onto the eaves and disappeared. The two men and the servant boy all fainted. The family heard strange sounds and came in to see, finding them unconscious and unaware. They were treated until midnight, when the boy woke up first and described what he saw. The two men could only move by morning. The plot was exposed, and people were talking about it. They abandoned their plan and stayed inside for several months. It is said that there was a man who was very fond of a courtesan, but when he wanted to free her from her profession, she refused. He offered to let her live in a separate house with proper respect, but she refused even more strongly. He was puzzled and asked why, and she sighed and said, "You abandoned your wife for me, how can I rely on you for life?" This is similar to what the ghost said.

Lady Zhang, the sister of my grandmother and the aunt of my uncle, was critically ill when she turned to her attendants and said, "I am not going to recover. I have heard that those who are about to die see their deceased ancestors, and now I see them." She then looked around the sickbed as if searching for something, sighed deeply, and said, "I was wrong." Soon after, she held her pillow and said, "I was greatly wrong." A little later, she closed her eyes, bit her teeth, and pinched her palm, leaving marks, and said, "I was truly greatly wrong." Her attendants suspected this was delirium and did not dare to ask. After a long while, she called all her daughters-in-law and granddaughters-in-law to the bedside and told them, "I used to think that my husband's family was distant and my mother's family was close, but now those who come to guide me are all from my husband's family, and none from my mother's family. I used to think that my daughters-in-law were distant and my daughters were close, but now my deceased daughter-in-law is by my side, while my deceased daughter is not present. Is it not true that only those who share the same lineage are truly related, while those of different lineages are not? Reflecting on my thoughts in the past, did I not treat the less important as more important and the more important as less important? I made one mistake, so you must not make the same mistake." This was personally heard by my aunt-in-law, Lady Zhang, who is highly respected. Many women are biased and do not realize their mistakes until death, but this lady is a person of great wisdom, able to recognize and correct her mistakes.
