Confucius once said, there are five forms of remonstrance, and I follow the indirect form. This is how the sage thoroughly understands human nature. Among her family, there was a woman who was childless and secretly jealous of her stepchildren. Her nephew and son-in-law also spread rumors and created trouble, and their private factions were tightly bound, making it nearly impossible to reason with them. The woman had an elderly wet nurse who was over eighty years old. Hearing this, she crawled in to pay respects, bowed once, and immediately burst into tears, saying, "I, the old servant, have not eaten for three days." The woman asked why she did not rely on her nephew, and the wet nurse replied, "My nephew lured all my money away. Now they treat me as if I were a stranger, and I cannot even get a bowl of rice." The woman then asked why she did not rely on her daughter or son-in-law, and the wet nurse said, "My son-in-law lured all my money away just like my nephew. After I had no more money, they abandoned me just like my nephew. Even my daughter could do nothing about it."

When asked why close relatives had betrayed her and why she had not sued them, she replied: "I did sue them, but the official thought I had already married, making me a different surname to my own clan. Since she has already married, she is also a different surname to me. The act of adopting is an extraordinary situation, and if they do not adopt, there is no legal fault, so I cannot be justified." When asked what she would do in the future, she said: "My deceased husband used to follow a certain official outside. He married a wife and had a son, who is now grown up. When I sued my nephew and son-in-law, the official thought that since he has this son, he should support his father's legitimate wife. If he does not support her, he will be severely punished by law. The official has already issued a document to summon, but I don't know when it will arrive." The woman was suddenly lost and disoriented, and from then on, her actions gradually changed. This was a case where relatives and clan members, exhausted from arguing, could not prevail, but this old woman changed their minds with a few words, speaking from personal experience. Those who speak it are not guilty, and those who hear it are warned. The strategy of Lord Chudongjun with Queen Dowager Zhao, it seems, was used in this case. Reading the Micro Garden, Volume 18, Chapter 4, "Listen Carefully."
During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

Dai Dongyuan said that there was a fox living in an empty house of a family, communicating with the owner through speech, exchanging gifts, and sometimes borrowing and lending items, coexisting peacefully like neighbors. One day, the fox told the owner: "Your other courtyard has an empty house where a hanged ghost has been for many years. Recently, you demolished this house, leaving the ghost without a place to dwell. It has come to fight with me for the house, often appearing in a vile form, frightening the children, which is already disgusting enough. Moreover, it causes illness with chills and fever, making it even more unbearable." The fox observed that a Taoist priest could exorcise ghosts and advised the owner to seek him out to eliminate this harm. The owner indeed found a talisman and burned it in the courtyard. Soon, a violent wind arose, and the sound was thunderous like a clap of thunder. While still in shock, they heard the tiles rattling and clattering as if dozens of people were running and trampling. From the roof, a voice cried out: "My plan has gone terribly wrong; I regret it too late. Now, a divine messenger will descend to strike, binding the ghost and driving me away as well. I am leaving you now." This shows that when one acts out of anger and seeks immediate satisfaction, both parties often suffer damage. From this fox, we can learn a valuable lesson. Another cousin of the Lu family said that someone suffered from a fox spirit, inviting a sorcerer to perform exorcisms. After the fox was driven away, the sorcerer demanded endless favors, sometimes sending wooden figures and paper tigers to disturb the family. They bribed him to stop temporarily, but after ten days, the disturbances resumed, and the spirit was even more troublesome than the fox. They had to take their family to the capital to avoid it, and only then were they free from the trouble. Being keen on winning and relying on untrustworthy individuals often leads to retaliation, and this is another sign of such consequences.

Hai Qiyun, the military officer in Urumqi, reported that during the campaign against Wushi, after the battle, returning to the camp, he saw a person's head peeking out from a tree branch near a cliff. Suspecting it to be a spy, he thrust the spear at him. In the military, the spear is called "miaozi," which is a phonetic variant. The spear struck the stone, and sparks flew, breaking the arm and nearly damaging it. He suspected it was due to dizziness, but there were bloodstains on the spear and the ground. Not knowing what kind of monster it was, he believed this must be a mountain spirit. Deep mountains and vast marshes, where nothing is beyond their capacity to produce. The *Bai Ze* book, though often embellished, likely has some truth to it. There is also a report of a wandering soldier who saw a black object sitting on the rock, suspected it was a bear, pulled the bow fully and shot it, hitting three times. But the object remained calm as if it didn't know. Terrified, he rushed back and called his companions to bring a gun, but it had already gone. He believed this is also a mountain spirit.

In the Changshan Valley, the extra carriage bearer Liu Fu said: "When a high-ranking official travels out of the capital, they are carried in a sedan chair by eight people, and four more are added, making a total of twelve. This is called 'extra' work. Chang Jie, whose last name is unknown, is a young woman from Shandong who is a migrant. Her parents have gone to Chifeng to find food. She rents land to farm. One day, while she was in the mountains gathering firewood, she encountered a storm and took shelter under a rock. When the rain stopped, it was already dark, and she was afraid of tigers and did not dare to move. She hid in the grass and saw two torches in the distance, which she thought were the eyes of a tiger. When she approached, she found several officials sitting on a rock. They ordered her to come out and called for her to kneel. Chang Jie thought they were mountain gods and knelt down. The official said: 'Your past sins should be your food. Now that you are captured, I will eat you. Quickly undress and lie down on the rock, without leaving a single thread, so that it does not get caught in my teeth.' Chang Jie realized they were not gods but tigers and trembled with fear, pleading for mercy. The official said: 'Your appearance is still acceptable. If you are willing to serve me in bed, I will spare you. I will visit your home in the future and bring you good fortune.' Chang Jie angrily stood up and said: 'How could a divine being say such things? They must be evil spirits!' She said, 'If you want to eat me, go ahead, but I am a virtuous woman and cannot do such a thing.' She picked up stones and threw them, causing the officials to flee. This was not because she had the strength to defeat them, but because her courage and integrity were enough to overcome them. Her steadfast and valiant heart was enough to lead her courage. Therefore, it is said that her courage is great and strong."

Zhang Taishou Menggu recounted that in the areas of De and Jing, there was a wealthy household that always stored grain but not gold, to prevent theft. During the reigns of Emperors Kangxi and Yongzheng, there were frequent years of poor harvest, and the price of rice soared. The wealthy man closed his granary and refused to sell even a single measure of grain, hoping the price would rise further. The villagers were distressed by this and could do nothing about it. A courtesan known as "Jade-faced Fox" said: "This is easy to deal with; just prepare the money and wait." She then went to his home and said: "I am a money tree for my madam, but she treats me cruelly. Yesterday we quarreled, and she agreed to let me redeem myself with a thousand gold pieces. I am also tired of the world of prostitution and wish to entrust my life to a kind and honest gentleman. There is no one like you, and if you can donate a thousand gold pieces, I will serve you for life." Hearing that he did not like accumulating gold, she added, "Even if you give me two thousand strings of cash, it would be enough. A timber merchant heard about this and has already returned to Tianjin to gather funds. He should arrive in about half a month. I do not wish to follow this ordinary man. If you can decide within ten days, you will do me a great favor." Zhang Taishou was indeed captivated by this courtesan, and upon hearing her, he was both surprised and delighted. He quickly sold his grain at a low price. Once the granary was opened, buyers flocked in, and it could not be closed again, leaving him with nothing left. The price of rice then greatly decreased. On the day the grain was gone, the courtesan sent a message to the wealthy man saying: "My madam has raised me for a long time. We quarreled out of anger, leading to this agreement. Now I regret it and want to stay, but it is not right to break my word. What I said can be postponed for another day." The wealthy man had originally made a private agreement with her, with no matchmaker or witnesses, and no money had been given as a dowry, so there was nothing he could do. This story was also mentioned by Li Luxuan, so it is likely not false. It is said that this courtesan was only sixteen or seventeen years old and was suddenly able to accomplish this, truly a female hero.

A man named Ding Yao Yuan said that there was a virtuous scholar who, at the age of forty, had no children and so bought a concubine who was very bright and clever. The primary wife could not bear to share her husband, and they quarreled day and night. The next year, when the concubine gave birth to a son, the primary wife became even more intolerant and sold the concubine to a distant place. The virtuous scholar was deeply distressed as if he had lost something precious. One night, while he was alone in his study and not yet asleep, the concubine suddenly entered by pulling aside the curtain. He was startled and asked her how she came to be there. She replied, "I have escaped back." The virtuous scholar pondered and said, "If you have escaped, they might come to capture you. Would a jealous wife hide you? Besides, since things have come to this, where could you possibly go?" The concubine smiled and said, "I am not deceiving you. I am actually a fox spirit. Before, I came in human form, and as a human, I had to endure the rules and could not avoid being scolded. Now that I come as a fox, I can change and move without a trace, and how could they possibly know?" Because of this, she was as affectionate as before, but after a long time, the secret was gradually revealed to the children and servants. The primary wife was greatly enraged and offered a large sum of money to hire a magician to arrest and punish the concubine. One magician issued a decree to capture the concubine, but she refused to admit guilt and boldly argued with the magician, saying, "If my husband took a concubine because he had no son, then taking her was reasonable. But if he sent her away after having a son, then he was unfaithful. I was sent away without cause, and the fault is not mine." The magician said, "Since you have been sent away, how can you return privately?" The concubine replied, "A divorced mother who has not remarried and has not yet given up her son is still connected to her husband. A divorced woman who has not remarried is still connected to her husband. Besides, the one who sold me was a jealous wife, not my husband. If my husband still accepts me, then I have not been divorced. How could I not be able to return?"

The Taoist practitioner angrily said, "You are originally of the animal kind, how dare you claim human rights to argue?" The woman replied, "A person with a beastly heart is subject to both Yin and Yang laws, while an animal with a human heart is considered a crime. On what legal basis does the priest act?" The practitioner became even angrier and said, "I hold the Five Thunder Method, I know how to eliminate demons, but I do not know anything else." The woman laughed and said, "Demons are also one of the creatures of heaven and earth. If they are not guilty, heaven and earth do not fail to nurture them together. If the Supreme Deity does not punish them, why does the priest wish to exterminate them all?" The practitioner slammed the table and said, "Seducing men, is that not your crime?" The woman said, "I accepted him through proper etiquette, so it cannot be called seduction. If it were seduction, then absorbing essence and breath would have left this life long and dry. Now I have been at home for two years, and upon returning, I have been there for another five or six years, and I am healthy and strong without any harm. Where is the so-called seduction?" The priest said, "The jealous woman gave me much gold, and I fabricated evidence to satisfy my greed. How could I submit to such corruption?" During the exchange of questions and answers, the Taoist practitioner looked at the summoned divine generals, who had already disappeared, leaving him helpless. With a frown, he said, "I will not argue with you now, but tomorrow I will summon the Thunder Department." The next day, if the altar is hastily set up again, I will flee at night. Because the method he holds is correct, but it is used for bribery, therefore, the demons do not fear, and even the divine generals are dissatisfied. It is said that when Mr. Liu Niantai served as the Grand Censor, he wrote a couplet for the Censorate: "Without desire, the heart should be like water; with words, one should feel as cold as frost." This can be called knowing the root.

A Story Told by Mo Xueya There was a man in a village who fell ill with an epidemic. He lay exhausted on a straw mat, and suddenly, his soul left his body and went outside. He felt an immediate relief from the heat and discomfort, and his mind was strangely at ease. The paths were unfamiliar to him, and he wandered aimlessly. By chance, he met an old friend, and they greeted each other with a mix of sorrow and joy. The man remembered that his friend had died and suddenly realized, "Am I entering the afterlife?" His friend replied, "You are not yet due to die; this is just your soul traveling here. This place is not one that people can reach. Why don't we explore together to broaden our horizons?" So, he followed his friend. They passed through various towns and villages, which did not differ much from the human world. People were bustling about their daily affairs, each engaged in their own pursuits. The villagers watched the man as he passed by, but no one spoke to him. The man asked, "I've heard of hell. Can we see it?" His friend said, "Hell is like a prison, and only a judge of the afterlife can open it, and only a clerk of the afterlife can guide you there. I cannot take you there." There were a few strange ghosts near hell, and the man could go and see them. So, they changed their path and walked for about half a li. They arrived at a desolate place, like an ancient tomb. They saw a ghost who looked like a person but had no mouth below the nose. The man asked why this was so, and the ghost replied, "When I was alive, I was skilled at responding to people, using flattering words to please the world and delight others. Therefore, I received this punishment, making me unable to speak. If I encounter boiling water or hot soup, I must drink it through my nose." They then saw another ghost whose buttocks were raised upward, his head bent downward, his face pressed against his abdomen, and he walked with his hands supporting him.

"Why is this so?" it was said: "This person, in life, was arrogantly overbearing, so he received this retribution, causing him to be unable to look up with arrogance." Another ghost was seen, with a split from chest to abdomen, several inches wide, and his five organs and six viscera were completely empty. Asked why, it was said: "This person, in life, had a deep and concealed heart, which people could not fathom, so he received this retribution, making him transparent." Another ghost was seen, with feet two feet long, fingers as large as hammers, heels as large as wine measures, and a weight like a thousand-dan boat, moving only an inch in half a moment. Asked why, it was said: "This person, in life, had quick wit and agile feet, always taking the lead in all affairs, so he received this retribution, causing him to be unable to walk." Another ghost was seen, with ears dragging on the ground, like dragging two wings, and in a state of confusion with no openings. Asked why, it was said: "This person, in life, was suspicious and distrustful, liking to hear gossip, so he received this retribution, causing him to be unable to hear." All these are according to the depth of their evil deeds, waiting until the time of retribution is complete, before they enter the cycle of rebirth. Their punishment is one degree less severe than hell, like the punishment of exile in the laws of the Yang Dynasty. Suddenly, a variety of chariots and horsemen were seen, and a judge of the underworld passed by, seeing a local person and exclaimed in surprise: "This is a living soul, mistakenly wandering here, fearing that he might get lost and not be able to return. Who knows his home? Can guide him to leave." The friend knelt and said that he was an old acquaintance, and the official immediately ordered him to be sent back. As he approached the door, he sweated heavily and woke up, and from then on, he recovered from his illness. Xueya has a naturally cheerful and open disposition, with an open heart with no lingering thoughts, and he plays and jokes with friends, often generating brilliant and witty arguments. This must be his allegory, and it may not necessarily be true. However, Zhuangzi and Liezi, half of which are allegories, have morals sufficient to encourage and deter, so there is no need to be rigidly literal like marking a boat to find a lost sword.

陈半江说，有个书生在一个月圆之夜遇到了一位女子，她容貌颇为美丽。书生用含蓄的话语挑逗她，女子欣然回应。她说自己家就在附近，但不肯透露姓名。又说她的丈夫经常几天才外出一次，家里有后窗可以打开，墙上有缺口可以攀爬，只要有机会就会来，但不能预先约定时间。就这样过了五六年，两人感情甚笃。一年，书生即将远行，女子在夜晚前来告别。书生说会随缘安排，以后再无相见之期，心中充满凄楚与眷恋，哽咽得几乎说不出话来。女子忽然嬉笑着说：“你如此痴情，必定会因相思而生病，这并非我最初来与你亲近的本意。实话告诉你，我是个等待替代的鬼魂。凡是与鬼亲近的人，没有不生病甚至死亡的，因为阴气会耗尽阳气。只是我怜惜你年轻俊秀，不忍心让你像美玉折断、兰花凋零一样死去，所以必定要等七八天后，待你的阳气恢复，才会再来。阴阳交替，所以你才能无恙。如果遇到其他鬼魂，她们会纵情放荡，不出半年，就会在枯鱼之肆找到你。我们鬼魂很多，但像我这样的人却很少，你务必小心。感念你的恩义，这就是我报答你的方式。”说完，她散发吐舌，现出鬼魂的形态，长啸一声离去。书生吓得几乎魂飞魄散，从此即使遇到再美的女子，也不再侧目。

After some matter brought him to the village, he visited her house, but she also refused to see him. Realizing that they had severed ties, he felt great resentment. There was a case where someone brought a courtesan to entice others into gambling, and the official sentenced the courtesan to be sent back to her original hometown. The official saw her and recognized her as a village woman. He spoke to her, and she complained about being strictly controlled by her husband, feeling ashamed and regretful, and deeply missing him. She said that she was fortunate to meet him again and begged him to remember their past few days of happiness, asking for leniency and avoiding punishment. The official was moved by her words and reported to the higher authorities, saying that the courtesan's records indicated she was from her mother's hometown and was actually the wife of a certain village man in the county. He suggested investigating the man. It turned out that the official had hoped to persuade the authorities to sell the woman, so he could buy her himself. He then ordered the arrest of the village man, and the village man brought his wife, who turned out to be a different person. When asked if the information was true, the villagers all confirmed it. When asked why the official had accused the village man, the official could not provide an answer. He only said, "It was a rumor." When asked who had spread the rumor, he remained silent.

The official called a prostitute to ask her, and she said that the magistrate had initially intended to defile the wife of a peasant. The wife thought that if she complied, she would lose her virtue; if she refused, her husband would die. Seeing the prostitute newly arrived, she removed her hairpins and jewelry to bribe her into impersonating her, thus befriending and becoming acquainted with the magistrate. Now facing punishment, she coincidentally met the prostitute, who continued to deceive by pretending to be the wife, hoping to escape the beating. She did not anticipate that there was another scheme, resulting in mutual failure. The official re-examined the peasant, who indeed was falsely accused. Considering that the plan was devised to save life, and it was the wife who came up with it, the official chose not to pursue it, and severely punished this magistrate. The magistrate, a great corrupt official, was ensnared by a village woman, treated like a child. The foolish are often defeated by the wise, and extremes always revert. Often, there are those who, beyond what was prepared, suddenly emerge with greater wisdom and prevail. There is no going without returning, it is the way of heaven. If the wise never fail, then the world would only have the wise, and the foolish would be extinguished.

Ghosts torment people to death, but the reason is unknown. Ni Yujiang said: I have heard it from Shi Liangsheng. They consume the living souls of the people. Ghosts are residual qi, gradually dissipating and diminishing until there is none. By gaining the qi of living souls to replenish themselves, they can prolong their existence. Therefore, female ghosts always want to flirt with people, capturing their essence. Male ghosts cannot capture human essence, so they kill people and suck their vital energy. Both are similar to foxes practicing alchemy. Recalling what Liu Tingsheng said, during the year of Gengzi in the reign of Emperor Kangxi, there were five scholars who encountered a rainy night and took shelter in a dilapidated temple. Four of them had already fallen asleep, while one was not yet fully asleep. They felt a chilly wind, and several dark shadows entered through the window. The shadows exhaled breath towards the four people, causing them to fall into a state of dream-induced paralysis. They then exhaled breath towards the one who was not fully asleep. Although his mind was clear, he gradually became confused. He felt as if he was being dragged, and when he slightly woke up, he had already moved from his original place, as if he was being bound. He wanted to call out but could not make a sound. He saw the four people lying on the ground, and the ghosts collectively lifted one of them and consumed him. In a short while, they had eaten him completely, and then they proceeded to eat two more people. When it came to the fourth person, suddenly an old man entered from outside and sternly rebuked: "Wild ghosts, do not act rashly! These two people have auspicious appearances and should not be harmed." The ghosts were startled and scattered, and the two people suddenly woke up. They described the same things they had seen. Later, one of them became a teacher, and the other became a mentor. Mr. Bao Jingting heard this and laughed, saying: "All my life, I considered this position lowly, unaware that I was valued by ghosts and gods." Seeing what he said, it seems that Mr. Liangsheng's theory is not empty.

李庆子说，朱生立园，辛酉年北上参加顺天考试，傍晚经过羊留之北，因为绕开泥泞，结果绕远了迷了路，没有旅店可以投宿，远远地看见林外有户人家，试着前去投宿，到了那里只见土墙瓦房，大约有六七间，一个小孩出来应门，朱生详细说明了请求住宿的意思。一位老翁衣着朴素文雅，邀请他进去，在旁边的房子里安顿下来，叫人拿灯来，灯很暗，几乎看不见光，老翁说：“今年油不好，真的很让人郁闷，不过也没有办法。”又说：“夜深了不能准备丰盛的菜肴，就喝点村酒，不要觉得简陋。”态度非常热情，朱生问家里有什么人，老翁回答：“孤苦伶仃，只有老伴和几个仆人一起住。”问朱生要去哪里，朱生告诉他要北上，老翁说：“有一封信和一些小东西要寄到京城，这偏僻的路上没有邮局，今天遇到你真是太幸运了。”朱生问四周没有邻居，独自居住不害怕吗，老翁说：“有几亩薄田，让仆人们耕种，因此在这里定居，虽然贫穷没有积蓄，但不怕贼。”朱生说：“是说荒野里多鬼怪吧。”老翁说：“鬼怪我还没见过，如果你害怕，我可以陪你坐到天亮，可以吗？”

He borrowed red paper and ink, and wrote a letter, sealing various items inside the envelope, wrapping it with old cloth, and stitching it tightly on the outside. He handed it to Zhu and said, "The address is written on the envelope. You will know when you arrive in the capital and open it." They parted at dawn, and again earnestly warned not to lose the trust items, finally parting with great affection. When Zhu arrived in the capital, he opened the cloth wrapping and found the envelope labeled with the words "Letter to Mr. Zhu Liyuan." The items were a pair of gold hairpins and silver bracelets. The letter said that the sender was old and had no children, and had been mistakenly influenced by his wife's words, taking his son-in-law as his heir. Even his grandson was only occasionally offered sacrifices, and later they were treated as strangers. The offerings of paper money and wheat porridge had long been absent, and his lonely grave three feet high had also collapsed. He endured pain in the netherworld, with a hundred regrets that could not be undone. He carefully enclosed in the coffin with these meager items, asking Zhu to sell them. On the return journey, he instructed Zhu to use the money obtained to repair the neglected tomb and slightly dredge the waterway south of the tomb, so that excessive rain would not flood the tomb. If Zhu agreed to his request, he would repay him like Du Hui tying grass, knowing that Zhu feared ghosts, he would bow in secret and not appear, and advised Zhu not to give rise to doubts. The deceased Yang Ning bowed respectfully. Zhu was terrified and drenched in sweat, only then realizing he had encountered a ghost. Because of the words in the letter about the return journey, he knew it would not sell, and indeed it turned out that way. He returned to Yangliu, using the money from selling the hairpins and bracelets to send a servant to repair the tomb, and ultimately dared not go there again.

In questioning the teacher, I was often met with rebuke. After becoming a ghost, I came to realize that all gods fulfill their duties, much like officials in the world, and are not mere fleeting illusions. I regret that I cannot further question the teacher with the knowledge I have gained. However, at that time, those who held positions of authority had long been ghosts and should know it themselves; there is no need to question them further. Generally speaking, sages have not espoused the belief in the non-existence of ghosts. The great scholars feared flattery and disrespect, so they forcefully created this statement. Yet, while it is possible to prohibit indulgence, abolishing wine and liquor is not possible. Similarly, while it is possible to prohibit promiscuity, abolishing marriage is not possible. While it is possible to prohibit greed, abolishing wealth is not possible. While it is possible to prohibit conflict, abolishing weapons is not possible. Therefore, with the fame of an era and the support of countless factions, they can make people silent and unable to speak, but ultimately cannot satisfy their hearts, for this reason.

Those who transmit the teachings, though they know in their hearts that it is not so, yet if they do not hold this view, they cannot be called the study of profound principles, nor do they agree with it against their conscience. They say: Reason must be such, they claim. The ruler does not understand the intention of earlier scholars to correct excesses, which arose from mutual provocation and was not their true intention; the heretical views of later scholars, suppressed by fear, were also not their true intention. Ultimately, the ruler believes that Confucian scholars truly deny the existence of ghosts and deities. A thorough questioning reveals that the ruler has been deceived for a long time. The people in the netherworld do not wish to remain in contact with the living for long, and the ruler should not stay too long with ghosts. The words end here, and the rest can be inferred. Departing with a long, mournful cry. This text argues that Confucian scholars know there are ghosts but claim there are none, and similarly, the two ghosts from Mount Huang know the well-field system and feudalism are impractical but claim they are feasible, both of which are insightful analyses of the root causes. Simply regarding these views as impractical is still being in a state of confusion.

Wang Hou Shi, the Magistrate, reported that there was a person performing divination at West Lake, and the poem written was as follows: "At the place where she was buried, the grass grows tall, only the night moon at Xiling knows. Poets come to mourn the past, the spirit of the deceased is deeply saddened by the inscriptions. Through many changes, the lake remains green, the dream of the past still lingers in doubt. Who would believe that the spirit of the flower-scattering girl from Mount Ling is now reflected in the light of Buddha against the glass-like surface of the lake?" Everyone recognized this as Su Xiao Xiao. Someone asked: "Since the fairy maiden lived in the Southern Qi dynasty, why can she also write seven-character regulated verse?" The divination board replied: "Through the years, the same principles apply in both the visible and invisible worlds, the spirit remains clear and aware, adapting to the world. Confucius only recognized ancient script, why is the prayer written in clerical script? The Buddha did not understand Chinese, why is the text written in parallel prose? This shows that people from a thousand years ago still have their consciousness today, and they can understand modern language and literature. Jiang Wen Tong and Xie Yuan Hui could write an eight-line regulated verse about a concubine being exchanged for a horse (as recorded in Zuan Yi Ji), Shen Xiwen could write a five-character regulated verse about the ancient capital of Jinling. If there were such things in ancient times, why doubt them now?" Someone then asked if she could still write in the Yongming style, and the board wrote four poems: "You come but cannot come, I leave but cannot leave, the stone wind is annoying, it stops people from crossing the river overnight. Where did you come from? Today there is a great storm, my clothes are soaked, all the hardship is because of you. I finish dressing in my butterfly skirt, row the small boat for you, turning along the embankment, the green waves reflect our shadows. Do not dock at the lotus bank, dock at the willow bank instead, someone walks outside the flowers, but no one can be seen in the deep willows." These are the songs of the night. Although these poems are attributed to a talented ghost, they can still be considered clever and eloquent.

My Cousin An Yi recounts that during the autumn harvest in the town of Hecheng, a young woman was seen carrying her child along the field paths. Suddenly, she lost her footing and fell to the ground, lying motionless and unable to get up. The workers, seeing this, suspected there was a reason and rushed over to check, only to find her dead. The child, too, had struck a corner of a tile and died with a crushed skull. The workers reported this in alarm to the landowner, who in turn informed the village headman. Upon identifying and verifying the bodies, it was discovered that no one in the surrounding tens of miles recognized the woman. Moreover, her clothing and ornaments were elegant and clean, and the child wore silver bracelets and a red silk shirt, indicating they did not belong to a poor household. This left the investigators greatly confused and unable to understand what had happened. They covered the bodies with reed mats and took turns guarding the scene, then urgently reported the incident to the authorities. The town of Hecheng is close to the county seat, and the official arrived the next day at dusk. Upon lifting the mats to inspect, they found a bundle of dry straw in the middle, but the two bodies were no longer there. The bricks pressing down on the mats had not moved, and the guards had not left for a moment. The official became furious and detained the landowner and the guards, interrogating them in many ways. However, there was no sign of murder or disposal of bodies. The case became complicated and entangled, lasting over a year before it was marked as a doubtful case and reported to higher authorities. The higher authorities, finding the case vague and unclear, went back and forth, questioning and refuting, and after another year, they decided to temporarily wait and investigate further. By then, the family had completely disappeared. This incident occurred during the years 1683 and 1684 in the reign of Emperor Kangxi.

According to tradition, in the area between graves and tombs to the south of the village, there was a black fox that worshiped the moon every night, and many people saw it. One of the villagers, who enjoyed hunting, secretly went to ambush the fox, shot an arrow from a bow and hit its thigh, causing it to scream loudly and transform into a fireball that flew westward. They searched its den and found two small foxes, tied them up and returned, but they soon escaped. After a month, the incident occurred, and it was suspected that the fox had transformed to seek revenge. However, the strange and unverifiable nature of the event made people dare not report it, and officials也不敢记录在案. Consequently, the case was considered as concealing a corpse, leading to considerable disturbance. There is also a story about a beggar woman in a certain village to the west of the city. She was abused by her grandmother and hanged herself in the temple of the earth god. Her body was covered with a screen and waiting to be inspected, with people taking turns to watch over it. When officials arrived, both the body and the watchers disappeared. The case was investigated as thoroughly as in Hecheng, and after seven or eight years, the body was found in Anping, a county under the jurisdiction of Shen Prefecture. It is said that the woman was quite fair-skinned, and when a young man was on guard duty, he stripped her lower garment and had intercourse with her corpse. The corpse regained its vitality due to the human touch and escaped with him. This incident occurred during the late reign of Emperor Kangxi. Some suspect that the events in Hecheng might be similar, but this is uncertain. If they were the same event, then the rumor might be wrong.

龚肖夫说，有个人四十多岁还没有儿子，妻子凶悍嫉妒，万万没有纳妾的道理，常常郁郁寡欢，身体不适。有一次他偶然来到一座道观，有个道士招呼他说：“您的面色凝滞，似乎有重大忧愁，道家以济物为念，您是否可以告诉我实情，或许我可以效劳。”他觉得这话奇怪，就把自己的情况告诉了道士。道士说：“我确实听说过，只是问问您罢了。您为我制作十来套鬼卒的服装，我会有所报答，如果您做不到，也可以请伶官来帮忙。”他心里更加觉得奇怪，但估计道士不会无故欺骗，应该有原因，于是姑且试试他的做法。当天晚上，他的妻子做了一个恶梦，叫不醒，而且呻吟号叫得很惨，第二天双腿都变得青黑，问她却秘而不言，只是叹息而已。三天后又出现了同样的情况，从此每隔三天就会发生一次，半个月后，她突然派奴仆去叫媒婆，说要买妾，大家都不相信，他的丈夫也担心会有后患，非常犹豫。后来，他的妻子昏睡了好几天，醒来后催促买妾更加急切，把钱放在桌上，与仆人们约定，如果三天内找不到合适的，就要重重责罚，如果找到的不合适，也要重重责罚。看她的样子似乎不是在说谎，于是找了两个女子来应验，并把她们留下。当天晚上，她就整理好床铺，催促丈夫进房。

The whole family was startled and astonished, unable to understand its meaning, and the man was also confused as if in a dream. Later, when he saw the Taoist priest again, he learned that the priest had a skill to capture souls. At night, he made the monks in the temple dress up as ghosts, while the Taoist priest, with his star-crowned hat and feathered robe, sat on the upper hall, burning talismans to capture the woman's soul. The priest said that her ancestors and in-laws had accused her of filial impiety for cutting off ancestral sacrifices, and had prepared a document to sue her in the underworld. They were to be punished with a hundred lashes using a peach wood stick and then sent back, with a deadline to take a concubine. The woman initially thought it was a nightmare and was unwilling, but every three days she was captured again, as if it were a regular summons. If she remained dazed and confused for several days, her soul would be hung upside down, and vinegar would be poured into her nose, with a warning that if she did not find a good woman within three days, she would be sent to the underworld. The skill of capturing souls, though a minor technique, is not a proper method. However, there is no such thing as good or bad law; it depends on how it is used. Just like a spear, if used for killing and looting, it is a bandit's tool; if used for conquest, it is a king's army. The same is true of skills, regardless of their size; it depends on how they are used. For example, a medicine that prevents chapped hands can be used to protect against frostbite, but it can also be used to decisively defeat a Yue army. Is the Taoist priest skilled in using his technique? As for a noisy, stubborn, and fierce woman, reason and law cannot explain or control her, but the Taoist priest can use his technique to subdue her. Even Yao led a sheep, and Shun followed to whip it; the sheep did not move, but a shepherd boy drove it, and it moved in a group. Everything has its own controller, and every drug has something it fears. The divine way sets up teaching to tame the stubborn and strong in the world. The intention of the sage is profound. How can a scholar of mere words understand it?

A Tale Told by Zhu Heting There was a wealthy student at the Imperial Academy named Zhu Heting, who had millions in assets. His wife gave birth to a son who died, and he remarried a woman of great beauty, which bewitched him. He claimed that he needed help managing household affairs and brought his mother to live with him. His mother then brought two of her sisters, and within a year, his brother and two younger brothers also moved in with their families. Over time, the household staff, including servants and maids, became dominated by his wife's relatives. The student and his son found themselves isolated, as if they were guests in their own home. Eventually, they were even excluded from managing the household finances and daily expenses. They were treated with disdain, receiving only leftovers and cold food. Unable to bear this treatment any longer, Zhu Heting attempted to reclaim his rights, but his wife's brothers and family members shouted and scolded him from outside and inside the house. Once, he was surrounded and beaten by the crowd, losing his hair and bruising his face. When he called for help, no one came to his aid. His son, in a fit of rage, ran to him and slapped him to the ground. Zhu Heting, in despair, only knelt and begged for a slow death. Overwhelmed with anger, Zhu Heting went to the back garden to hang himself. Suddenly, an elderly man stopped him and said, "Do not do this. The situation in your household has long been a source of anger for both gods and people. I have lived in your home for a long time and have been particularly upset by the injustice. You should burn a petition at the earth god's shrine, asking the fox in the back garden to drive away the evil influence. The earth god will surely grant your request." Zhu Heting followed the advice and burned the petition at the earth god's shrine, asking the fox to drive away the evil. The earth god granted his request, and the situation in his household began to improve.

That night, the tiles of the house indeed made chaotic sounds, the windows and doors trembled, and the wife's relatives were all struck by bricks and stones, their foreheads broken and they bled. Soon after, the wife's relatives and women were all bewitched by foxes, even their mother was not spared. During the day, they went mad and walked naked, speaking vulgar words and engaging in indecent actions, doing everything. At night, dozens of foxes would gather in each room, taking turns teasing and playing, causing them to suffer from the wounds, and their cries for mercy could be heard. The food in the kitchen was all taken and placed before the father and son of Taixue, and the wife's relatives' food was all mixed with filthy things. Knowing they could not stay, they all fled back. Taixue then gradually gathered his old servants and resumed household affairs, finally being able to survive on his own. The wife's relatives' covetousness did not cease, and they always came to visit; as soon as they entered the door, they were struck. If they privately brought something, by the time they returned home, their bags were already empty. If his wife privately gave something, it was the same. Thus, they finally stopped coming. However, when calculating the loss of property, it was already severe. Without the foxes' help, the father and son of Taixue would have starved to death. This is something even the closest friends could not do for them, but these foxes did it with all their efforts. Is it that the foxes are indeed superior to humans? People, being deeply involved in worldly affairs, avoid suspicion and fear resentment, seek ease and avoid difficulty, and sit by and watch without helping. The foxes, not being familiar with worldly affairs, are not skilled at gaining the reputation of being loyal and kind elders. When it is the right thing to do, they rise up with determination. Even if it is a fox, to hold its whip, that is what I admire.

A blind man named Liu Junrui reported that another blind man in his thirties frequently traveled near the Wei River. Whenever he encountered people who had docked their boats, he would always ask, "Is there a Yin Tong here?" He would further explain, "The Yin of the Xia and Yin dynasties, and the Tong tree." Sometimes, in his dreams, he would mutter these two words. When asked for his name, it would change every ten days, but no one deeply questioned him. This went on for over ten years, and many people recognized him. Whenever someone encountered him asking, they would call out, "There is no Yin Tong here, look elsewhere." One day, a grain ship docked on the riverbank, and the blind man asked as usual. A man stood up and went ashore, saying, "Are you?" The Yin Tong is here, what can you do?

A blind man roared like a fierce tiger, grabbing the man's neck and biting his nose, causing blood to flow everywhere. Despite the efforts of the crowd to separate them, his grip was unbreakable, and they eventually fell into the river together, drowning in the current. Later, the body was found in front of the Temple of the Goddess Mazu, where bodies washed ashore from the river. The victim was beaten with a plank until his left ribs were broken, but the blind man never let go. His fingers dug into the victim's shoulders and back, penetrating more than an inch. He bit the victim's cheeks and jaws until almost all the flesh was gone, yet he never knew what wrong had been done to him, and it was suspected that he was avenging his parents' wrongs. How could a blind man track someone with eyesight? How could a weak man defeat a strong and ruthless one? It was as difficult as Wu Zixu's revenge against the state of Chu. Yet, after ten years of unwavering determination, he finally caught and ate the man's flesh. Could this not be attributed to the utmost sincerity and determination, even defying heaven and earth? Emperor Gaozong of Song's indulgence in pleasure cannot be excused by his weakness.

Wang Kunxia wrote a volume of travel notes on Yandang Mountain. Zhu Daojiang wrote a hanging scroll for me, quoting one section from it, which says: On the evening of the 17th of the fourth month, leaving through the Small Stone Gate and reaching Beiguan, I enjoyed the scenery so much that I forgot to return. Sitting under a tree waiting for the moon to rise, feeling tired, I wanted to take a light nap. A mountain breeze blew on my clothes, and I suddenly woke up with a start. Hearing faintly a voice saying, "The night air is clear, making it especially secluded and beautiful. It surpasses the beauty of gold and blue landscapes in painted scrolls." Thinking that fellow travelers had arrived at night, soon after, it said, "The inscription on an ancient zither says, 'The mountains are vast and the waters deep, with all sounds echoing softly. There are no traces of ancient people, only towering rocks.' Truly a wonderful depiction of a scene that is hard to describe. Once asked Hong Guxi to paint this scene, but he ultimately could not put brush to paper. Secretly wondering who this person might be, and then seeing Jing Hao. Getting up to listen, it then said, 'Recently, Dongpo painted half a wall with bamboo. Branches and leaves spreading like spring clouds emerging from a mountain. Sparse and dense, with a natural appearance. Without the appearance of branches spreading in anger. Recently seeing his poem about West Tianmu, like a clear autumn river, with misty waters stretching far. An old crane calling out, with a clear breeze carrying it away. Also dissipating the bold and unrestrained spirit. Because a talented scholar's pen strives to exhaust all ingenuity. The brush of a flying immortal, marvelously natural. The realms are therefore different.' Knowing it was a fairy, standing up and looking up, suddenly hearing a rustling sound. Mountain flowers falling in disorder. Two birds flying into the clouds. His poem includes the lines 'Wearing clogs, I laugh at Xie Kangle, transforming into a crane, I see Xu Zuoqing.' That is how this event is recorded.

Liu Nishan’s family lost a gold bracelet, and he harshly questioned the young female servant, who fully confessed that she had sold it to a drum-beater. In the capital city, there were many idle and lawless wanderers; women remained at home by the door, while their husbands avoided the house during the day, carrying two bamboo baskets and beating a small drum with a short handle to announce their presence, buying miscellaneous goods from door to door—this was known as "drum-beating." All young female servants and children often took items they had stolen, and the drum-beaters would buy them at low prices. Though they themselves were not thieves, they were the wings of thieves. However, the stolen goods were small and of little value, and their movements were secretive, making them impossible to trace. Thus, the law of the realm could not prohibit such activity. Liu Nishan then questioned the drum-beater again about the appearance of his clothes, but failed to find him. He continued to interrogate harshly, when suddenly a slight cough came from above the ceiling, saying: "I have lived in your household for forty years, never revealing my form or voice, so you did not know I was here. But now I can no longer bear it." The voice asked, "Is this bracelet not the one your lady placed in the lacquered box while checking her belongings?" Following this suggestion, they searched and indeed found it without error. Yet by this time, the young female servant had been beaten until she had no skin left intact. Liu Nishan was filled with lifelong remorse, and he often recounted the incident, saying: "Such things are inevitable from time to time, but how could there be such a fox everywhere?" Because of this, throughout his more than twenty years in official service, he never resorted to torture in judicial inquiries.

A Statement by Xiao Shan Once, in Jingzhou, Xiao Shan encountered someone performing the fújī divination ritual. When they attempted to summon a deity, it did not appear. After burning another talisman, the medium shook violently for a long time, and then wrote a poem: A life as fragile as a leaf, A fragmented soul like a dandelion seed, A white silk robe three feet long, A single red branch where flowers have withered. Though the promise of union was long, The misty path remains blocked. In autumn graves, ghosts sing in vain, Leaving regrets in the eastern part of the Song dynasty. Knowing this was the spirit of someone who had hanged themselves, Xiao Shan tentatively asked for its name, and the spirit wrote: I originally come from Wu Gate, My family lived in exile in Chu Marsh. By chance, our karmic connections came together, And I conveyed words gracefully. How could it be that our good dream was not completed, That I died in a panic? According to the rituals of sages and virtuous people, Gentlemen should criticize me; But understanding the affection of lovers, Talented people may pity me. I briefly express my grief and resentment, Do not ask for my name. This talent is no less than that of Li Qingzhao, And my self-assessment as a child of sages and virtuous people is also true.
The *Xin Qi H

I have heard from the monk Mingyu of Mount Wutai that the critique of Buddhism is profound in the Song scholars but superficial in Han Yu, refined in the Song scholars but coarse in Han Yu. However, the Buddhist monks fear Han Yu but not the Song scholars, and resent Han Yu but not the Song scholars. The Buddha that Han Yu critiques is the one of alms and offerings, spoken to the common people; the Buddha that the Song scholars critique is the one of enlightened mind and nature, spoken to the literati. There are fewer literati than common people in the world, and the means by which monks sustain themselves rely more on the common people than on the literati. If Han Yu's critique prevails, then there would be no offerings, no monasteries, and even if there are great wise teachers, could they lead countless followers to preach with empty stomachs and exposed to the elements? It is like a military commander who first cuts off the enemy's supply lines, causing them to collapse without a fight. Therefore, they fear Han Yu greatly and resent Han Yu greatly; if the Song scholars' critique prevails, then your Confucian principles are like this, your Confucian methods are like this, you do not need to follow me, my Buddhist principles are like this, my Buddhist methods are like this, I also do not need to follow you, each respects what they hear, each acts according to what they know, they support each other, causing no harm. Therefore, they do not fear the Song scholars, nor do they greatly resent them. Thus, scholars before the Tang dynasty had every word with practical use, while scholars after the Tang dynasty had everything as empty talk. The critique of Buddhism by scholars does not add or subtract anything from Buddhism, it is just noise. If recorded as a contribution, it is indeed a partisan view, if recorded as a fault, it is also a matter of valuing Han Yu too highly.

The Servant Wang Fa, returning from a night hunt under the bright moonlight, saw a woman being held by two others, each holding one of her arms, and dragged back and forth, yet there was complete silence. Suspecting that they might be stripping her of her clothes in the darkness, he fired a shot into the air, causing the two people to scatter in panic. One of them returned and suddenly disappeared. Only then did he realize they were ghosts. By the time he reached the village entrance, he saw a household lit up, and people were talking loudly, saying the newly married woman had hanged herself but revived. The woman explained that the grandmother had ordered dinner to be prepared, and the dog had taken away two or three cakes. The grandmother suspected her of stealing food and beat her, leaving her feeling wronged and standing dazed under a tree. Soon another woman came and advised her to die due to her suffering. She hesitated but then another woman encouraged her, and she became confused and disoriented, eventually hanging herself with the help of the two women. She experienced suffocating pain and was almost unable to describe it, gradually falling asleep and not realizing she had left the house. One woman said she would take responsibility for the first advice, and another said she would take responsibility for the final encouragement. As they were arguing, a thunderclap sounded, and the light illuminated everything. The two women ran away, and the servant returned home. After that, whenever the servant returned at night, he would hear distant cries and curses, saying they had ruined their plan and swore to kill each other. The servant was not afraid. One night, he heard the cries again and shouted, "You killed someone, I saved someone. If you tell the gods, I am in the right. If you dare to kill, kill me. Why threaten me in vain?" From then on, the events stopped. Therefore, saving someone from death also attracts the resentment of those who want to kill. It is better to stand by and do nothing. This servant can also be called slightly unusual.

Mr. Song Qingyuan said that when Mr. Wang Tanzhai was studying in the magistrate's office, a friend recounted a dream in which he traveled to the underworld. There, he saw dozens of people in official robes entering one after another. The king of the underworld interrogated them for a long time, and then they left one after another, each with a look of regret and anger. He happened to see an official who seemed familiar but whose name he couldn't remember. He tried to bow to him, and the official responded. He then asked, "Who are these people, and why do they look like this?" The official smiled and said, "You also work in the magistrate's office, so surely you must have at least one old acquaintance there, right?" The narrator replied, "I have only served twice in the magistrate's office and have never entered the government office." The official said, "Then I truly don't know. These are the so-called 'Four Saviors' of Mr. Four." The narrator asked, "What does 'Four Saviors' mean?" The official replied, "Those who assist in the magistrate's office have a traditional saying: 'Save the living, not the dead; save officials, not the common people; save the important, not the minor; save the old, not the new.'"

Saving the living rather than the dead, the dead are already dead, and there is no way to save them. The living still have a chance, but if they are killed to compensate for the dead, it results in one more death. Therefore, it is better to yield or make concessions to save them, and whether the dead harbor a grudge is not considered. Saving officials rather than common people, in cases appealed to higher authorities, if the injustice is redressed, the official's fate is unpredictable. If the injustice is not redressed, the official will be punished, but it will not be severe, only military demotion. Saving higher-ranking officials rather than lower-ranking ones, if the crime is attributed to the higher-ranking official, those with greater power and position receive heavier punishment, and many others will be implicated. If the crime is attributed to the lower-ranking official, those with lesser responsibility receive lighter punishment, and it is easier to conclude the case. Saving old officials rather than new ones, the old official has left office, and there are unresolved matters. If they are detained, they may not be able to pay. The new official has just arrived, and there are matters to be passed on. Forcing them to stay can still be managed, but whether the new official can handle the situation is not considered.

These are all virtuous acts performed with the heart of a noble person, not done to seek personal gain or to manipulate documents, nor are they driven by private grudges or revenge. However, human nature is complex and events are ever-changing, so one cannot judge everything by a single principle. If one rigidly adheres to this example, one may overcorrect and lose balance, focusing on one thing at the expense of another, turning what was meant to benefit into harm, and what was meant to resolve into further trouble. Such unintended consequences often occur. If divine judgment brings misfortune in this way, one might ask what the outcome will be? It is said: "Plant melons and you will reap melons; plant beans and you will reap beans." Past actions and karmic ties will eventually come to fruition, and in future lives, one may simply encounter the Four Saviors, who will be listed among the Four Who Cannot Be Saved. In the blink of an eye, one may suddenly awaken, unable to understand the reason for entering such a dream. Could it be that a divine or supernatural force is guiding or warning one?

In the year 1763 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, there was a plague in the capital during the spring and summer. Treating it with Zhang Jingyue's method, most people died, and treating it with Wu Yike's method was also not very effective. However, a physician from Tongcheng treated a lady with a heavy dose of gypsum, which was surprising but effective. Those who followed this method saved countless lives, with some doses reaching eight taels and one person taking up to four pounds. Even Liu Zhen's *Yuan Bing Shi* and Zhang Yuansu's *Rumen Shiqin*, which primarily use cold and cooling treatments, would not dare to use such heavy doses. This was truly unprecedented in history. The use of gypsum was most prevalent in the works of Ming dynasty physician Miuyongzhong (Miuyongzhong), who lived during the Tianchong period and was contemporary with Zhang Jingyue, but their methods differed. Miuyongzhong's method was not the middle way, and Wang Maohong's *Baitianji* contains a treatise on gypsum that strongly argues against its use, questioning why it was so effective. This might be due to the specific conditions of the year, and it should not be taken as a fixed rule.

From the respected elder Jun Zhang Gong, I heard that a relative of my mother's side, a certain Mr. Zhi, was cooling off on a moonlit night outside the village when he met a man who seemed to be a scholar. The man bowed deeply and said, "I, unfortunately, have been punished by the village deity, and my prayers have been to no avail. In the village, only you worship the village deity most abundantly, yet for decades, you have made no requests or petitions. The village deity greatly appreciates and values you. If you pray for me, the deity will surely listen." Mr. Zhi replied, "Who are you?" The man answered, "I was once a scholar, and I knew your ancestor. I have been dead for over thirty years. Yesterday, I happened to ask for food from a certain house, and they complained." Mr. Zhi said, "If you do not pray for your own affairs, why pray for others'? If you do not pray for human affairs, why pray for ghostly matters? I am unable to serve you, sir, so please rest." The man left with a wave of his arm, saying, "I am a self-reliant man; you are not worth my trouble." Mr. Zhi explained, "Food and wine must be abundant, showing respect to the spirits. Making no requests, distancing oneself from the spirits. Respecting the spirits but keeping a distance — this is the way of the people. Compared to the flattery and disrespect of the common people and the arrogance and contempt of the scholars, this is the middle way." When I heard this story, I was just eight or nine years old. This relative of my mother's side had forgotten his name. At that time, the village customs were pure and sincere, and it was only the homes of those who were upright and honest that would intermarry. Those who acted in this manner, many could not guess who they were. The high mountains we look up to, the bright paths we follow. In the span of seventy years, can we not look back with admiration?

The Yellow Leaf Hermit, Pan Ban, once sat beside a nobleman in seclusion and repeatedly called the nobleman "elder brother." The nobleman, both angry and amused, said, "I am now over seventy years old." At that time, Pan Ban was already drunk and raised his head to say, "Your age in the former dynasty should be compared with those of the former dynasty, not intruding into this dynasty. If we consider the age of this dynasty, I was born in September of the second year of the Shunzhi era, while you entered the Qing dynasty in May of the first year of the Shunzhi era, with a difference of only a few months. A Tang poem says, 'I am one year older than my elder brother.' Calling someone 'elder brother' is an ancient custom; why do you blame me so harshly?" Everyone present was startled. Some said that Pan Ban was a madman, and his words were too hurtful to the virtue of loyalty and kindness, which is why he had a difficult life. However, one cannot say that his words were entirely without reason. When I compiled the *Four Treasuries Catalogue* for the Ming dynasty collection, I placed eight individuals, including Lian Zining and Gong Xu, who died at the Jin川 Gate, before Jie Jin and Hu Guang, and added a note: "Carefully examined, the eight individuals from Lian Zining onwards were all former officials of Emperor Hui. Examining their years of service, some were later than Jie Jin and others. However, one was loyal to his former lord, while the other sought favor with the new ruler. Eagles and phoenixes are of different kinds and cannot be placed together. Therefore, they were separately compiled, each in their respective categories. As for Gong Xu, who died in the year Xinchou of the Chenghua era, he was even further behind Jie Jin and others. Now, he has also been moved ahead, to clarify the right and wrong of the teachings of morality, so that throughout history, the glory of high-ranking officials cannot be compared with that of an old soldier carrying a halberd, in terms of the order of their names. The grave may be easily forgotten, but the green bamboo scrolls cannot be defamed. How can Pan Ban's words be dismissed as frivolous?"

Zeng Yinghua said, there were several scholars traveling to take the provincial exam during the long, hot summer. Taking advantage of the moonlit night, they grew tired and stopped before an abandoned temple, resting on the steps. Some slept, while others woke up. One scholar heard voices behind the temple and suspected they were people guarding melons and dates, or perhaps thieves. He held his breath and listened carefully. One voice asked, "Sir, where are you coming from?" Another replied, "Recently, I had a dispute with a neighboring tomb over the boundary and sued the village deity. Sir, you are an old hand in the law, please estimate the outcome." One laughed and said, "Sir, are you truly a bookworm?" The outcome of disputes is not fixed. This can make the later litigant win, questioning the earlier litigant: "He did not sue, but you did, so you started the conflict and invaded him." This can make the earlier litigant win, questioning the later litigant: "He sued, but you did not, so you started the conflict and knew you were in the wrong." This can make the later arrival win, questioning the earlier arrival: "You took it before he arrived, so you took it early." This can make the earlier arrival win, questioning the later arrival: "The boundary has been established for a long time, but you suddenly overturned the old arrangement, so you caused trouble without reason." This can make the rich win, questioning the poor: "You are poor and unscrupulous, wanting to make others fear lawsuits and bribe you." This can make the poor win, questioning the rich: "You are rich but unkind, and you do not stop expanding, wanting to use your wealth and power to oppress the weak." This can make the strong win, questioning the weak: "People tend to suppress the strong and support the weak, so you want to make a superficial complaint to attract attention." This can make the weak win, questioning the strong: "In the world, the strong oppress the weak, not the weak oppress the strong. He is not truly wrong, so he dares not take the risk to face your sharp edge." This can make both win, saying: "With no evidence or proof, how can the dispute be resolved? Splitting it in half to end the dispute is also acceptable." This can make both lose, saying: "People have fields and paths, but spirits do not have boundaries. Outside a single coffin, everything belongs to people, not to you, so giving it as idle land is also acceptable." With all these outcomes, there is no fixed result. One asked, "Then, what should be done in the end?" One replied, "These ten arguments each have a point to stand on, and each can be explained, leading to endless debates that never end." The village deity's decision is unknown, but as for the officials and ghosts in the underworld, they will forever possess two beautiful estates. After speaking, they became silent. Truly, these are the words of an old hand in the law.

Snakes can avenge wrongs, as recorded in ancient texts, while other venomous creatures cannot. However, I have heard from the old saying that whenever encountering venomous creatures, if one has no intention to harm them, they will not be bitten. If one sees them and immediately harms them, there will surely come a day when one suffers their venom. This has been tested and found to be true. It is not that the creatures know to avenge, but rather it is a matter of the interaction of their energies. Dogs bark at those who kill dogs, not because they recognize the person, but because they sense the energy. There are also those who eat venomous insects alive, claiming it can increase their strength. Venomous insects can kill people, but when stored entirely in the stomach, they cause no harm. What is the reasoning behind this? A rascal youth from Cui Village practiced this skill. I once saw him grasp a red-banded snake, sever its head, and bite it alive, as if savoring the taste. Perhaps his fierce and ruthless energy is enough to overcome it. Why does one need to increase strength? Even if strength is increased, there are many prescriptions, so why must it be this way? Jia Gonglin said that there was a merchant who traded in Fan Village and befriended a fox. The fox often invited him to its dwelling, where the house was just like a human home, but once he left and looked back, it would disappear. One evening, while drinking at the fox's home, the fox's wife served wine, and she was very beautiful. After the man became drunk and his heart was stirred, he playfully grabbed her wrist. The wife looked at the fox, who glanced aside and laughed, saying, "Are you trying to be Teng Ping?" She was not particularly angry and continued to joke as usual. After the man returned home, one day a guest arrived on a donkey, bringing his wife, saying they had received urgent news that the man had suddenly suffered a stroke, so they borrowed the donkey to arrive hastily and连夜, causing the man to be greatly startled, thinking his companions were playing a trick on him.

The inn had no space to accommodate his family, so he hired a guest to escort his wife home. The hired guest had already left, and it was not a day's journey from home. It was just past noon when he took control of the donkey to escort his wife. On the way, he encountered a young man who brushed past his wife. The young man touched his wife's foot, and she angrily cursed him. The young man only smiled and apologized, but the words were somewhat disrespectful. This person became angry and fought with the young man, causing the donkey to panic and run off into a side path. The sorghum was just beginning to grow, and soon, he could no longer see it. He left the young man to chase after his wife, following the hoof prints for a couple of miles. The donkey got stuck in the mud, and the wife was nowhere to be found. The fields stretched out in rows, with no signs of people around. He rode all night, wandering until dawn, then rode the donkey back home to discuss and search for his wife again. Not even a few miles away, he heard a loud cry from the side of the road, saying, "The thief has been caught!" It turned out that a donkey from a neighboring village had been stolen last night, and they were searching everywhere for it. The crowd seized and bound him, and he was severely beaten. Fortunately, he was known by someone who recognized him, and after much explanation, he was finally released. He returned home in frustration, finding the spinning wheel still turning. His wife was drawing thread, and when he asked about what had happened yesterday, she was completely unaware. He then realized that his wife, the hired guest, and the young man were all illusions created by foxes. Only the donkey was real. The fox's revenge was indeed evil, but the cause of this was initiated by this person himself.

In the spring of the year of the pig, a group of dozens of woodcutters from Luanyang spent the night in a mountain hollow. They saw several deer wandering around on the slope across the ravine, and two people going back and forth under the trees, weeping together. They were all surprised that people had entered the deer's group, and the deer did not flee. They suspected they were immortals or ghosts. Despite the high cliff and swift water, which made human paths impassable, the moon was as bright as daylight, and everything was clearly visible. One person could barely recognize someone who resembled a former woodcutter. Suddenly, a mountain wind arose, causing the leaves to rustle. A tiger leapt out of the forest and killed two deer. They realized that what they had seen were the living souls of the deer. A line from a poem by Su Shi (Su Dongpo) came to mind: "Before death, the spirit weeps," and this was indeed the case. They heard that the woodcutter was not particularly evil but was cunning and always sought advantage. Plotting and scheming are frowned upon by Taoists, and there is a reason for it. During a military campaign against the Uighurs, one day, the soldiers were attacking a city fiercely. One soldier was fighting with great effort when a sudden arrow came from the side, and he did not see it. Another soldier saw the arrow and quickly raised his knife to block it, but ended up being killed instead. The first soldier was moved and mourned him. That night, he dreamed of the deceased soldier, who said: "In our past lives, we were officials together. I always shirked the hard work while taking credit for the good deeds. Because of this, the underworld has decreed that I should die in this life in place of you. From now on, there will be no more grudges between us. I will receive my own reward, and you do not need to offer me any sacrifice." This story is similar to that of the woodcutter, whose plotting led to a severe punishment, while the soldier's lesser cunning resulted in a lighter penalty. Thus, true cleverness is actually the opposite of being overly cunning.

My disciple Hao Ai, from Meng County, was the student I selected during the imperial examination in the year Jia Mao (1589). He became a Jinshi (advanced scholar) and was appointed as the magistrate of Jinxian. He wore simple clothes and ate plain food, treating the people's affairs as if they were his own family matters. He kept a record of the warehouse's inflows and outflows every month, prepared in advance for his return journey's boat and vehicle expenses, and locked them in a chest. Even in urgent situations, he did not use even a small amount of money. His bags and boxes were packed in his room, as if he were preparing to travel, showing that there was no day without planning to leave office. People saw that he could resign every day, but could do nothing about it. Later, after becoming ill, he requested to return home, without a single coin, and ended his life teaching at home. It is said that when he was young, during the Spring Festival, he saw crowds of visitors like a woven fabric. He encountered an old woman with two daughters dressed in rural attire, with natural grace. Walking alongside them, he never glanced sideways. Suddenly, he saw the old woman and her two daughters stepping on broken stones, walking across a dangerous ravine, and standing like cranes under a tree. He found it strange that they did not take the usual path, as if they were avoiding something. He turned and looked at them intently. The old woman calmly approached and spoke: "The festive season is warm and bright; leading her children to enjoy the spring, each seeking a spouse, because a virtuous person dares not approach. I also ask you not to approach my children, so as not to make them uneasy." Aio realized they were fox spirits and waved his arm and left. Thus, the spirits of flowers and the moon, summoned by human hearts, are clear.

A Man Named Mulan, Who Cut Down Public Trees Mulan, who cut down public trees, saw several tigers on the opposite mountain from afar. The cliffs were steep and sheer, and one could not reach them without detouring several miles. People did not fear tigers, and tigers did not fear people either. Soon, he saw another group of tree cutters pass through the tiger area. The crowd stood still in fear, yet people acted as if they did not see the tigers, and tigers acted as if they did not see people. Several days later, they met and talked about it. The other group said: "That day, we also saw the group from afar, and it seemed we heard the noise. But what we saw were just several large rocks, not a single tiger." This must be because their fate was not to be eaten by the tigers. But how can fate turn tigers into rocks? There must be a controller of fate. The controller of fate is empty and inscrutable, dark and unknowable. How can such an entity turn tigers into rocks? It must be heaven and ghosts. Heaven and ghosts can control fate, but they are called the principles of heaven and the good abilities of the two forces. Then the principles and forces are mixed, sometimes contracting and sometimes expanding, and by chance, when this person encounters a tiger, it becomes a rock. I cannot understand it.

Gao Guan Ying of Jingzhou was born after dreaming of Gao Jiangcun, hence his nickname "Ming Shi Qi" (famous scholar). Diligent in learning and capable of writing, he always ranked first in minor exams but failed in provincial exams, ultimately ending up in a difficult and unsuccessful life. When he was in his twenties, an astrologer analyzed his fate, stating that the celestial officials and stars associated with literature and fortune were all gathered in one palace, indicating that according to the rules, he should have been selected as the top scholar and entered the Hanlin Academy. However, that year he only managed to get a stipend. Considering his entire life's experiences, there was no other time when he could achieve success with the stipend. Because his fate was inherently weak, even with the most prosperous destiny, what he achieved was still limited to this. Tian Baiyan said: Based on Zhang Wenhong's eight characters, the astrologer calculated his entire career and compared the positions of the stars, concluding that his promotion was only equivalent to a scholar's robe. This can be compared with Gao Guan Ying's fate, and astrologers should use this information to understand, not relying solely on the positions of the stars to hastily determine good or bad fortune. Once, I saw an astrologer who said that all soldiers who died in battle, when calculating the time of their deaths, must have had extremely prosperous destinies. Because they died for their country at a certain time, left a name for a thousand years, brought fragrance for a hundred generations, and glory to their descendants, what they gained exceeded even kings, princes, generals, and prime ministers. The theory is extremely strange, but there is indeed profound truth. This is beyond the usual rules and not within the framework of Li Xuzhong and others.

冠瀛 long confined in the arena of fame, feeling particularly depressed, once told me and Xueya: I heard of an old house where anyone who stays overnight experiences nightmares, whether caused by a ghost or a fox spirit, it is unclear. I, being brave and strong, wanted to find out what was causing the haunting, so I slept in the house. After midnight, a black shadow appeared and disappeared quickly, moving forward and backward. When I turned over, the shadow crouched still, knowing it feared people, I pretended to sleep to wait for it. Gradually, I made snoring sounds, and suddenly felt something climbing from my feet to my chest and abdomen, causing me to feel drowsy. I quickly raised my right hand to grab it, seizing its tail, and then used my left hand to choke its neck. It let out a loud cry and spoke in human language, asking for release. I quickly called for a light and saw that it was a black fox. The crowd held it down, cut through its thigh with a blade, threaded a rope through the wound, and tied it to my left arm. Assessing that it could not transform, I held a knife and asked it about its intentions for causing the haunting. The fox spirit mournfully cried and said: All fox spirits that are spiritually adept seek immortality through cultivation. The highest level involves breath control and spirit refinement, discussing the principles of the water and fire elements and the mythical creatures of the dragon and tiger, absorbing cosmic energy, and using it to form an internal elixir, shedding one's physical form, and achieving immortality. This requires divine instruction and innate talent, which I do not possess; the next level involves the practice of seduction and manipulation, capturing energy for personal benefit, which can also lead to the formation of an elixir, but if too little energy is captured, the path cannot be completed, and if too much is captured, it harms others and benefits oneself, avoiding divine retribution but inevitably facing heavenly punishment. Such practices, I dare not engage in.

Therefore, using the skill of plagiarism as a strategy for hunting, taking advantage of someone's deep sleep, breathing in their remaining breath, like bees collecting pollen without harming the flower, gradually accumulating and merging into one, it can also prevent the original spirit from dissipating, and over time, achieve enlightenment, which is exactly what we are. Although the method is shallow and the technique is sparse, the accumulation of effort is still arduous. If not released, then a hundred years of energy will be wasted, only the virtuous person will feel compassion and forgive. The author feels moved by the heartfelt words and decides to let them go. This matter occurred at the end of the Yongzheng era and has been passed down for a long time. I therefore reflect on those who excel in the imperial examinations, their great talents and profound learning, I cannot match; those who take risks and seek侥幸, I也不敢; those who plagiarize and hunt, I can do it, but I am unwilling to. My path is exhausted. Both of you have already achieved success in the imperial examinations, what can you teach me? Xueya jokingly said: to regard you as the successor of Jiangcun, just as Xiangshan was to Bai Lao. Only this one thought, perhaps the body and spirit are different. This illness is so deep, we truly have no remedy to help. Laughing together and ending it. Guan Ying likes to create new and difficult words, his writing is hard and abstract, often causing him to fail in the examinations, mostly due to this. Therefore, Xueya uses it as a joke. In his collection, there is a couplet that reads: 'Alone, the shadow at the bottom of the pond, counting breaths by the tree,' with a note below it that says: 'Two lines took three years to write, tears flow when recited, if no one appreciates, return to sleep in the old mountain in autumn.' Throughout history, eccentric people, their thoughts are roughly similar.

The soldiers stationed at Jiumusa Tai reported that once while chasing pheasants into a deep mountain, they saw someone standing on a cliff. Crossing a ravine to take a look, they found the person about forty to fifty feet from the ground.
