The person was wearing purple felt, with a face and hands and feet all black, and had fur about an inch long. There was also a very beautiful woman dressed in Mongolian attire, only barefoot, no boots, and her clothing was green felt. They were sitting opposite each other roasting meat, with several black-furred people serving, all like small children, not wearing a stitch of clothing, and laughing and joking at the sight of people. Their language was not Mongolian, not Oirat, not Hui, not Tibetan, and they were chirping like birds, impossible to understand. Their appearance seemed not to be that of a monster, so they knelt and bowed to them. Suddenly, they threw something down the cliff, which turned out to be half a forearm of cooked wild donkey meat. The soldiers then bowed and thanked them, but they all waved their hands. The soldiers took the meat and returned, enough for three or four days of food. Again with horse herders, they went to search, but did not see them again. Perhaps they were mountain gods?

Common sayings suggest that when a rainbow appears, the rain stops, which is actually a misstatement. In reality, it is when the rain stops that the rainbow appears. When clouds break and the sun emerges, the reflected sunlight shines upon the opposite clouds. The Earth is spherical, with the sky above it like a hat. When viewed from the top, one looks up; when viewed from the sides, one looks sideways. Therefore, it appears as a single line, and its shape follows the downward curve. The two sides bend like a bow. When viewed from the side, those that are slanted towards the eye appear closer, while those that are level with the eye appear farther. Gradually becoming farther away, all layers of clouds are seen at their edges, layered into multiple layers of red and green colors. It is not a real object like a belt spanning half the sky. Its ability to descend into ravines and drink water, or its head resembling a donkey, as mentioned in Zhu Xi's collected sayings. There are also those who can flirt with women, which is likely a different kind of demon. Its appearance is like a rainbow, or it is a different kind of demon that transforms into the shape of a rainbow. According to Mr. Ru'ai, he once personally saw a fly fly into a person's ear and cause mischief, able to speak human language, but only the sick person could hear it. Some say that flies are so stupid, how could they become demons? Perhaps the demon transformed into the shape of a fly. This statement is close to the truth. The green-robed child announcing amnesty, the household guest reciting poetry, all are fabrications in novels, not worth relying on.

The Dust-Repelling Pearl My father-in-law, Ma Gong Zhoulu, once encountered the dust-repelling pearl, and it is indeed a real thing, though unfortunately, its form has not been seen. Initially, at Longfusi Temple, a vendor selling various jewels spread a mat on the ground, commonly called setting up a stall, and arranged various small boxes on top of it. Even in strong winds and dust storms, there was no dust. Someone jokingly said that the bag contained a dust-repelling pearl, but the person, being simple-minded, merely laughed and did not believe it. Like this for half a year, one day, he stomped his foot and shouted, "I truly sold a priceless treasure by mistake!" Because on that day, dust suddenly gathered, and he then realized that it was indeed the pearl that repelled the dust. According to medical books, there is a method of taking sound beans. Sound beans are the seeds of the Chinese parasol tree that explode at night. One tree only produces one, and it cannot be identified. The method is to cover the tree with a silk net when the flowers first bloom to prevent birds from eating them. After the fruits ripen, they are stored in many cloth bags. At night, use them as pillows and discard those that make no sound. Like this, pass the pillows, and there will definitely be one bag that makes an explosion sound. Take this bag and divide it into many small bags, and use them as pillows and divide those that make the first sound. Like two pillows, gradually dividing until only two remain. Divide them again, and the sound bean is obtained. The number of pearls this person sold must be few. If using this method to test, it would be obtained in no time. Why would it be lost by a mere chance? But he carelessly did not understand and ultimately discarded it lightly. It must be because his destiny was originally thin.

In the year 1774 during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, the city of Jinan experienced frequent fires. At the end of April, another fire broke out in the western cross street inside the south gate, spreading from east to west. The narrow alley was filled with fierce flames, and the wind was strong. A man named Zhang lived in a thatched house with three bays on the north side of the road. Before the fire could spread, he could have taken his wife and children out, but because his mother's coffin was there, he had to plan how to move and avoid the danger. It was impossible to leave. His wife, children, and himself, four people in total, embraced the coffin and cried bitterly, swearing to die with it. At that time, a military officer under the garrison, a sub-general, was supervising the army to put out the fire. He faintly heard the cries and ordered his subordinates to climb up the houses in the back alley to search for the source of the cries. They reached Zhang's house and lowered a rope to pull them out. Zhang's wife and children both cried out: "The coffin of our mother is here, how can we abandon it?" His children also cried out: "If our parents are willing to die with their mother, shouldn't I also die with them?" They also refused to climb up. Soon the fire reached them, and the soldiers jumped over the houses to escape, only escaping with their lives. They thought that the entire family had been burned to ashes and looked afar, sighing in sorrow.

The fire was extinguished, and upon inspecting the house, it stood firmly and alone. It was because a sudden strong wind had arisen, causing the fire to turn northward and circle around the back of the house, burning down a neighbor's pawnshop before turning westward again. Without divine protection, how could this have happened? This event occurred in July of the year癸丑 (1803), and Mr. Zhang Qingyuan, the headmaster of Dezhou, recorded it and sent it to me. It is similar to the story of a widow in my *Pulian Summer Record*, but the husband, wife, and children were united in their wishes, making it especially difficult. As the saying goes, "When two people are united in heart, their power can sever metal"; how much more so for six people? When the servant girl called out, thunder descended from the sky; how much more so for six people who were all pure and filial? The utmost sincerity moved the three spirits of heaven, earth, and man, and even if fate had its course, it could not help but be altered. "Human effort can overcome fate"—this is one such example. Though the story is extraordinary, it can also be seen as a matter of common sense. I did not know Mr. Zhang, but he sent it through various difficulties to ensure it would be recorded, showing his intentions clearly. Therefore, I corrected the text and included it in this collection.

Lü Taichang Huanhui said, in the capital city, there was a household with a coffin that encountered a fire, with no way to escape, and no one willing to help carry it. The entire family, men and women, used shovels, picks, knives, and chisels to dig a pit inside the house and place the coffin in it. They covered it with earth, and just as the pit was covered, the fire reached it. Although the house was burned, the coffin in the pit remained unharmed, because fire rises upward. This is also an urgent and clever response to an emergency, and it is appended because of the story of Zhang the filial son. In Jiahe Bay Town, there was a man named Wang who was skilled in martial arts, known as Wang Fei Ji. "Ji" is commonly written as "leg," but this has been the case for a long time, though it is not the correct character. One night, he happened to pass by a graveyard and saw several children playing in the road, about four or five years old. He shouted at them to move aside, but they paid no heed. He angrily slapped one of them, and the children all shouted and cursed back. Wang became even angrier and kicked them with his foot. The children rushed forward, each holding bricks and tiles to hit his shins. They were quick like monkeys, and he could not catch them. If he blocked the left, they came from the right; if he blocked the front, they came from the back. They circled around and supported each other, ultimately causing him to fall and be injured. His head and eyes were also injured, and he repeatedly got up and fell until midnight. The next day, his family found him and brought him home, and his feet were blue and purple. He lay in bed for half a month before he could get up. The children were actually foxes. With Wang's strength, he could normally defeat dozens of strong men and still move freely, but when he met these small spirits, he was completely defeated. The *Huainanzi* quotes Yao's admonition: "Be trembling and fearful, day by day more cautious. No one stumbles on a mountain, but they stumble on a small mound." The *Zuo Zhuan* says: "Even a scorpion and a wasp have poison; indeed they do."

De Tonglun said, "There was a person from Fu Cheng who went out and had no news for several years. One day, he returned in a hurry at night and said, 'I was destitute and homeless, and mistakenly fell into a group of bandits. The number of robberies and murders is not one. Now that the matter has failed, I luckily escaped. However, I heard that those who were captured have already provided my name and address. It is already calculated to send out urgent dispatches to apprehend my relatives. You should plan for yourselves; dying together will be of no benefit.' He shed tears and left, without another word. The entire family was terrified and scattered like stars in one night. The entire residence was eventually abandoned and turned into ruins. People also do not understand the reason for it. Several years later, this person returned to his old home to inquire about his parents, wife, and children, where they moved to. Neighbors told him that they had been hiding for a long time. Also completely at a loss and unable to guess the cause, he gradually traced them and learned that his wife was working as a servant in De Tonglun's household. Knocking on the door to visit, he then knew the reason. However, he was actually not involved in any banditry, nor did he actually return at night. De Tonglun checked the official documents, and there were no records of any arrest. After a long time, he recalled his time farming in Badao, the former land of Han's Youbeiping, where he built a house on a hillside. Behind the hill, there were foxes that sometimes stole things and sometimes howled at night, disturbing people's sleep. He gathered people to break into their dens and smoked them out with fire, and the foxes all left. He suspected that it might be a spirit seeking revenge."

A servant named Shi Jinwen once traveled to Lunzhou to seek a doctor. In the hot summer, he did not bring a sleeping mat and rode a horse. Upon reaching the west side of Zhangjia Gou, he suddenly fell ill and tied his horse to a tree, leaning against it to rest. Gradually, he fell into a drowsy sleep. In his dream, he arrived at a place with a few simple houses. An old man and an old woman sat outside, saw Jinwen, and invited him to sit. They asked for his name, and he replied that his name was Li, the sixth in line. He mentioned that he used to live in Cui Village for two years and had a relationship with his father, Shi Chengde. Jinwen also recalled meeting him when he was young and wondered if he had grown up to be like this. He felt a sense of gratitude and remembrance, and his heart was quite sad. The old woman then asked if Wu Kui, whose nickname was Wu Kui, was well—Wu Kui was Shi Jincan's nickname—and if San Hei was still with him—San Hei, a man surnamed Li, was Jinwen's half-brother who came with his stepmother. The old man also spoke about the year's floods, saying that along a certain road to a certain place, the water was deep but the sand bottom did not sink, and along another road to another place, the water was shallow but it was all red clay mud, sticky to the horse's hooves and hard to walk through. It was about to rain, and the sun had already passed noon, so he should go quickly; they would not keep him sitting there. Suddenly, Jinwen woke up and saw a solitary grave about four or five meters away. He wondered if it was where Li Six was buried. Following the directions given, he arrived at Chang's Brick River in the evening and indeed met with rain. He returned and told his stepmother, who said, "He used to sell melons and fruits in Cui Village and often hung out with your father in a drunken state." She added, "He has passed away and gone to the underworld, but he still cared about the son of an old friend. He was also a person with some awareness."

A servant named Fu Xian enjoys reading, has some knowledge of literature, and also has some knowledge of medicine. He has a slow and deliberate personality, and looks like a lazy old scholar. One day, he walks gracefully in the market, and every time he meets someone, he asks if they have seen Brother Wei San—Brother Wei San is the third in line among his siblings. If someone points to where he is, Fu Xian then walks gracefully towards him. When they meet, he breathes heavily for a long time. Wei San asks what the purpose of the meeting is. Fu Xian says: "Just happened to be at the well of bitter water, where I met Sister-in-law Wei San working on embroidery under a tree, tired and taking a nap. A child was playing near the well, only three or five feet away. It seems concerning. Men and women have their distinctions, and it was inconvenient to call Sister-in-law Wei San to wake up. So I went to find Brother Wei San." Wei San is greatly startled, runs towards her, and finds that the woman had already fallen into the well, crying for her child. Indeed, servants reading books can be called a good thing. However, reading books is to understand principles, and understanding principles to apply them. Eating without digesting to the point of confusion and absurdity causes endless harm. Why value such scholars?

In Wuqiang, there was a prominent family. One night, there was a robbery. The people rose up to chase and pursue the thief, but he escaped. The people united and pursued relentlessly. The thief fled to the ancestral tomb among the cypress trees. The forest was deep and the moon was dark; people dared not enter, and the thief also dared not come out. At the moment of confrontation, a whirlwind arose within the trees, and sand and gravel flew chaotically. People all squinted and could not see each other. The thief took advantage of the moment to break through and escape. The people were greatly surprised and wondered, "How could the ancestors help the thief?" The owner dreamed of his ancestor at night and said, "The thief who robbed money could not be left unpursued. If the authorities caught him, he would be punished according to the law. The thief could not blame the owner. If the thief did not get the money, it would be better not to pursue him. If they caught the thief and fought, they might hurt people. Wouldn't the loss be great? If the people's strength was enough to kill the thief, and the thief was killed, they would have to report it to the authorities. The authorities might not understand and charge them with unlawful killing. Wouldn't the loss be even greater? And our group is loosely formed, while the thieves are all close associates. The thieves could wait for us every night, but we cannot prepare for the thieves every night. If we become enemies, the hidden worries will be great. Shouldn't we think deeply and carefully? The whirlwind was what I used to resolve this problem. Why are you blaming me?" The owner woke up and sighed, "I now realize that the wisdom and foresight of the elderly surpass the impetuousness of the young."

The City Commander of Cangzhou, Officer Yong Gongning, was on friendly terms with his uncle, Zhang Gong Mengzheng. I was young and living with my maternal relatives when I heard him recount an incident to his uncle: "A soldier's daughter named Ping Jie, aged eighteen or nineteen, was not yet married. One day, while buying cosmetics outside the door, a young man flirted with her, causing her to angrily scold and enter the house. Her parents went out to see what was happening, but there was no sign of the young man, and neither neighbors nor anyone else had seen him. That night, as she closed the door and went to sleep, the young man appeared by the light of the lamp. Knowing it was a demon, Ping Jie neither screamed nor spoke to him, but held a sharp scissors and pretended to sleep, waiting for him. The young man dared not approach, only standing by the bed and trying to persuade her in many ways. Ping Jie acted as if she did not hear or see anything. The young man suddenly left, and after a short while, he came back, holding dozens of gold and pearl hairpins and earrings, worth about a thousand gold coins, and placed them on the bed. Ping Jie still acted as if she did not hear or see anything. The young man left again, and the items were not taken, as dawn was approaching. The young man then said: 'I have watched you all night, and you did not even look at them once. A person cannot be moved by profit. What one's will does not approve of, even ghosts and gods cannot argue, how much more so for us? I misunderstood your private wish, wrongly thought you were using your parents as an excuse, hence my actions. Do not be angry.' He gathered up the items and left on his own. Because the girl's family was always poor, her mother was old and sick, and her father's allowance was not enough to support the family. She once privately wished before the Buddha, hoping to find a husband soon to support her parents, which was secretly heard by the demon. Thus, a single word spoken, a single thought conceived, all are subject to scrutiny in the shadows. In front of eyes and ears, can one deceive or pretend?"

In the town of Yaojing, there was a man who was fond of gambling. His poverty was so severe that he had no cooking pot. On a cold night, he and his wife cried to each other, regretting their past actions. The husband said, "If only we had three or five thousand coins at this time, we could carry and sell goods to provide for our daily needs, and even if we died, we would not have to enter the door of a moneylender." But he wondered where they could obtain such money. Suddenly, he heard someone knocking on the window and saying, "If you truly regret, this is also easily obtained, and even more than this is also easily obtained, but I fear your old tricks might resurface." Thinking that respected elders in the same courtyard showed compassion and helped them, they then cried and took an oath, their words very firm and difficult. They opened the door and looked out, but the bright moon was like day, and there was no one. They were confused and could not fathom why. The next evening, they heard the same voice again, saying, "The money has already been returned completely; you can take it yourselves." Holding a torch, they got up and looked inside, and there were hundreds of coins, piled up in the house, exactly matching what they owed.

The husband and wife were wildly overjoyed, believing it to be a dream. When they pinched each other's wrists, they all felt pain, knowing that it was indeed real—according to folk belief, those who doubt they are dreaming can pinch their wrists; if they feel pain, it is real, but if not, it is a dream. They believed it was the help of ghosts and spirits, and they bought sacrificial liquor and meat to thank them. On the road, they met an old gambler who said, "Has your skill improved? Has your luck turned? Why did you pay off the debts you owed over the past few years in just one day?" The man had no idea how to respond, and could only nod in agreement. As soon as they returned home, they set up the sacrifice, but then heard words from the eaves: "Do not make a false sacrifice, for it may summon evil spirits. The one who played for you yesterday was me. I live near your father's grave. Your father was angry with you for your wandering ways and cries and wails every night. I could not bear to hear it, so I transformed into your form to go and retrieve the money for you." Your father has a message for you: this can happen once but not again. After the words were spoken, there was silence. From then on, this man reformed his behavior and lived comfortably until the end. Alas, the worthless son believed he could do whatever he wanted. Perhaps he should also think of those below the Yellow Springs, those who cry and wail every night.

Li Xiusheng recounted that there was a wealthy household in Shanxi with an elderly man who had only one son. The son was suffering from a wasting disease, and his wife was also ill with the same condition, both seemingly beyond recovery. The parents were greatly distressed by this situation. The son's wife passed away first, and then the father urged the son to take a concubine. The mother was startled and exclaimed, "Is this not hastening his death?" The father replied, "I knew he would not recover, but before this son was born, I had prayed for descendants at Lingyin Temple. In a dream, a bodhisattva told me, 'You originally had no descendants. By donating gold to help with famine and saving a thousand lives, I specially grant you a grandson to accompany you in old age.' If we do not take a concubine while he is still alive, where will the grandson come from?" They proceeded with the arrangement, and within three or four months, the son died, leaving behind a pregnant concubine. Indeed, she gave birth to a son, thus prolonging the family lineage. Huang Tingjian's poem says, "If one can share the harvest with the poor, surely a bright moon will emerge from a pearl shell." Indeed, this is not false.

Wang Sihe of Baodi is a relative by marriage of mine. Once, he showed me a written account of the filial son Ai, saying: Ai Zichen is from Ailin Village in Ninghe. His father, Ai Wenzhong, earned a living as a carpenter. One day, he accidentally knocked someone down in a fight and mistakenly thought the person was dead, becoming afraid and fleeing. Even his wife did not know where he went; there were only vague rumors suggesting he might have left through Shanhaiguan. At that time, his wife was pregnant for two months, and she then gave birth to Ai Zichen. Ai Wenzhong did not know he already had a son. Ai Zichen was raised by his mother when he was young and also did not know he had a father. Until he began to understand things, he then asked his mother about his father's whereabouts. His mother cried and told him the reason, and Ai Zichen became confused and disoriented as if something were lost. He constantly asked about his father's age, appearance, and other details, as well as the names of his ancestors and the surnames, names, and hometowns of his relatives. No one could understand his intentions, so they told him one by one. By the time he grew up, someone offered to marry his daughter to him, but Ai Zichen firmly declined and said: How can there be a son living comfortably at home while his father is wandering in exile? Then it became known that he had the intention of finding his father, but because his widowed mother was still at home, he did not want to leave her. However, Ai Wenzhong had been out of contact for a long time, and Ai Zichen had not left his hometown. The world is vast, how could one trace his footsteps? No one believed that he would actually go, and Ai Zichen had never discussed this matter before. He worked hard to support his mother. After twenty years, his mother died of illness. After completing the funeral arrangements, he then packed his belongings and set out for Liaodong. Some discouraged him, saying that his father's survival was uncertain. Ai Zichen wept and said: If we meet, we will return together; if he is dead, I will carry his bones back. If we do not meet, I would rather die on the road than return alive. Everyone shed tears and saw him off.

After truly leaving the pass, the son thought about his father who had fled to avoid punishment and gone into hiding. He must have concealed himself in remote and secluded places, such as deep mountains and remote valleys, dangerous and hidden locations, and searched for him everywhere. After a long time, when his resources and funds were exhausted, he resorted to begging for food to survive. This went on for twenty years, during which he never harbored any regrets. One day, in the mountains of Ma Family City, he met an elderly man who felt sorry for his poverty and hunger. He called out to him and spoke with him, learning the reason behind his plight, and was moved to tears. He brought the elderly man home and treated him to wine and food. Soon, a carpenter entered with tools. When he calculated that his age was the same as the father's, the son was touched and carefully examined his appearance, finding it somewhat similar to what his mother had described. He then tugged at the skirt and wept, fully recounting the year and month of his father's departure, and also detailing the family history and relatives, hoping that it might be true. This person was both startled and sad, as if wanting to recognize him, but he doubted that he had a son at home. The son then told him the entire story, and they embraced and wept together.

Wen Zhong, after evading and fleeing repeatedly, eventually reached this place, having passed over forty years. He then changed his name to Wang Youyi, and thus could not be found. Until now, they happened to meet. The old father was moved by his filial piety and planned for his return. However, having been wandering for a long time, Wen Zhong had many debts and was unable to move forward. Zi Cheng hurriedly ran back, mortgaged his land and house, borrowed from relatives and friends, and obtained a hundred gold coins to go again. He finally escorted Wen Zhong back. After returning, Wen Zhong lived for seven more years before passing away. After obtaining his father, Zi Cheng married and now has four sons, all of whom are diligent, frugal, and capable of managing their lives. In the past, Wen An Wang Yuan searched for his relatives thousands of miles away, and his descendants are still a prominent family. Zi Cheng's story is similar, and is heaven about to prosper his family? Zi Cheng farms the remaining land, and his residence is only two li from my other property. I respect his character and therefore asked for details, and recorded the main points as above. To let scholars and officials know that such a person exists in the fields. It was two days after the Double Ninth Festival in the year GuiChou. Zi Cheng sought his father for many years, and they met by chance, similar to the story of Song Dynasty's Zhu Shouchang searching for his mother, both seemingly aided by divine intervention, not something human effort could achieve. However, due to the utmost sincerity, it moved both the living and the dead, and even calling it human effort is acceptable.

Citing ancient meanings should be based on the classics, while other miscellaneous sayings should be considered and balanced, but not all can be taken as definitive conclusions. The *Book of Han's Chapter on the Five Elements* lists the birth of three sons in one birth among human ailments, interpreting it as a sign of the mother's excessive qi, and thus as an omen of calamity. However, in the Zhou dynasty, eight scholars were born with four breasts, and they were regarded as sages, not as strange or ominous. Why is this the case? The intermingling of heaven and earth gives rise to the refinement of all things; it is not the earth alone that can bring forth life. Similarly, the union of male and female qi brings forth all living things; it is not solely the woman who can do so. If the birth of three sons in one birth were due to the woman alone, it could be seen as a human ailment; but since the father is involved, it is clear that the father's qi is also strong. Why, then, is it solely attributed to the excess of yin over yang? Following this logic, one might also say that the bountiful grain filling a cart and the different fields with the same ear of grain, as described in historical records, are signs of excessive earth qi. Generally speaking, the *Great Plan of the Five Elements* often contains forced interpretations, and this particular case is especially difficult to understand. It should not be elevated to the status of a sacred text simply because it was transmitted by Fu Sheng. According to state regulations, the birth of three sons in one birth is rewarded, effectively sweeping away the biased and narrow views of scholars, truly establishing a definitive judgment for all time. In my revision of the *Continued Comprehensive Examination of Literature and History*, I changed the examples of Ma Duanlin in the section on auspicious and inauspicious signs, removing this category in accordance with official decree. In July of the year of the Pig, I completed this book, and just then, the officials presented a draft for rewarding the birth of three sons in one birth for my signature. I happened to discuss this with them, and thus added this note at the end of the book.

As the setting sun approaches, my spirit and strength diminish daily, and I no longer have the will to write books. Instead, I occasionally jot down miscellaneous notes to pass the time. The *Puan Yang Xiao Xia Lu* and three other works, which were all efforts to fill the days with writing, are now behind me. In recent years, I have grown even lazier in this regard. Sometimes, I hear strange tales and hastily jot them down on a slip of paper; other times, I suddenly recall old events and intend to add them to the previous volumes, but often neglect to do so, like fleeting clouds and smoke. Thus, it has been a long time since I have completed a book. This year in May, I accompanied the emperor to Puan Yang. In my spare time after returning from duty, with the long days providing ample leisure, I compiled these notes into a book, naming it *Puan Yang Xu Lu*. After completing the transcription, I wrote a few lines to record the origin of this work. As for the purpose of writing, it is fully explained in the prefaces of the first four volumes, so I will not elaborate further. Written by the Daoist who watches chess on the seventh day after the Qixi Festival in the year of Wuwu during the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, in the office of the Ministry of Rites. I was seventy-five years old at the time.

During the fifth month of the Wuwu cycle in the year of Jiaqing, I accompanied the emperor on a journey to Luanyang. Before we departed, the elder Zhao Lüquan informed me that there was a blind fortune-teller named Hao Sheng, who resided with the family of the high-ranking official Peng Yunmei. Hao Sheng traveled among the literati, using bone divination, and his predictions were often strange and perilous. He accurately divined that Hu Zhijiu Changling was a fourth-rank official but did not recognize him as the top scholar (zhuangyuan). Among the wandering fortune-tellers, Hao Sheng's skill was somewhat proficient. He claimed to be from Hejian, but when I inquired about his hometown, no one knew of it, suggesting he had been away for a long time. Hao Sheng also mentioned that his teacher was a monk, whose skills were even more advanced. With just a few words, the monk could discern one's official rank and salary. He lived in seclusion in the mountains, intending never to leave, and his abilities were so extraordinary that I found them hard to believe. According to the *Zuo Zhuan* and the *Book of Han*, the practice of physiognomy has a long history. However, the methods of pulse diagnosis and bone divination were not known in ancient times. Pulse diagnosis first emerged during the Northern Song Dynasty, and its origins and transmission are often fragmented and forced, clearly relying on other sources.

I have already provided the final translation in the previous response. Here it is again for your reference: In my *General Catalogue of the Four Treasuries*, I have already discussed this in detail. Even bone divination cannot clearly explain its origin. According to *The Annals of the Three Kingdoms*, cited in volume 136 of *A General Record of the Taiping Era*, during the reign of Emperor Gao of Northern Qi, he went hunting with Liu Gui and Jia Zhi, and encountered a blind old woman who touched everyone, saying they were all wealthy and noble. When she touched Emperor Gao, she said that all their fortunes came from him, suggesting that this practice already existed in the Northern and Southern Dynasties. *Record of Fates and Fortunes* mentions that in the fourteenth year of Tianbao (755 CE), a blind man named Ma Sheng from Chenyang County could predict the official rank and wealth of Zhao Ziqin by feeling his skull. *Record of Stories and Anecdotes by Liu Gong* states that at the end of the Zhenyuan period, there was a bone diviner named "Mountain Man" who was blind in both eyes. When people sought his divination, he would touch them with his hands and always know their rank and status. *Record of Conversations and Anecdotes* records that during the Kai Cheng period, there was a man named Long Fuben who was blind but skilled in listening to voices and divining through bones. Thus, this practice became widespread during the Tang dynasty. Since it has been passed down for so long, it must have some origin, and even a partial understanding can sometimes hit the mark. It is slightly more grounded than the *Tai Su Mai* (a form of pulse diagnosis). The grandson of the Duke of Chengmou, who was the son of the Duke of Wencheng and inherited the title, said: East of the Lantern Market, there is a Temple of the Second Lord. The temple faces west, and when the morning sun rises, it often emits golden light into the room, as if reflecting back. The neighboring houses do not experience this, and the reason is unclear. Some say that the temple's foundation is aligned east-west with the Hall of Central Harmony. The fire pearls on the palace roof—anciently, the golden-topped palaces were called fire pearls. Cui Shu of the Tang dynasty wrote a poem about the fire pearl in the Hall of Supreme Harmony. Perhaps this is the case.

Grandfather casually asked me about Xingtian's weapons, and I cited the *Classic of Mountains and Seas* in response. Grandfather said: "Do not say that ancient records are absurd; they are truly real. In the past, the Khalkha prince Dalma Dada, once hunting in the deep mountains of the northern desert, encountered a deer carrying an arrow and running away. He drew his bow and killed it. Just as he was about to collect it, a rider galloped up. The person on the saddle had a body but no head, with eyes on his nipples and a mouth on his navel. His speech, chattering from his navel, could not be understood, but by observing his hand gestures, it seemed he was saying that the deer he shot should not be taken away. The followers were all shocked and disordered, but the prince, who was usually brave, also indicated with gestures that he did not shoot it down and that this arrow was obtained. They should split and divide it equally. The person understood, nodded in agreement, and finally took half the deer and left. We do not know what tribe he is from or where he lives, but according to his appearance, isn't he a descendant of Xingtian? The vastness of heaven and earth contains everything, and Confucian scholars are limited by their own knowledge. According to history, the *Classic of Mountains and Seas* and the *Chronicle of Yu* are said to contain all monsters, and I do not dare to believe them. The book was originally written before the Han dynasty. The *Liezi* says that Emperor Yu saw it, Bo Yi knew and named it, and Yi Jian heard and recorded it. His words must have a basis, but later people added to it and distorted it, so it is often too far-fetched and mixed with place names from the Qin and Han dynasties. It can be viewed separately. It must be said that the *Shanhaijing* was originally based on *Tianwen*, but it should not cite the *Shanhaijing* to annotate *Tianwen*; that is too much."

Hu Zhongcheng Tai Chu and Luo Shanren Liangfeng, both able to see ghosts. Heng Ge Xue Lantai also has this ability, though not always consistently. In the fifth month of the Wuwu year, they happened to discuss this in the study at the Summer Palace. Lantai described that the shape of ghosts is still like that of a person, but their eyes look straight ahead, and their clothing appears as if pieces are hanging on their body, tied and hanging down, slightly different from humans; their substance is like smoke and fog, when looked at, vaguely like a human shadow, when viewed from the side, the whole body is visible, when viewed directly, it seems half of the body is inside the wall, half protruding, their color is either black or gray, they always stay about ten to twenty feet away, not daring to approach closely. If suddenly unable to avoid, they may shrink back and hide in a corner of the wall, or hide in a well or ditch, emerging slowly after the person has passed. It is not surprising to encounter them when the lights are dim, the moon is dark, and the sun sets with overcast clouds. What he said is roughly similar to what Hu and Luo said, but the description of the shape is more detailed. Knowing the principles of the visible and the invisible, it is no more than this. The reason some are black and others are gray is that ghosts are remnants of the qi (vital energy) of the living, gradually dissipating over time until they disappear. Therefore, the *Zuo Zhuan* states that new ghosts are large, while old ghosts are small, probably because the qi has varying thickness, thus the colors vary in intensity.

The LanTai reported again, once on a clear daytime, looking up and seeing a dragon moving from west to east. Its head and horns were roughly the same as those in the drawings, but its four legs were spread out, shaking like a boat rowing with four oars. The tail was flat and broad, gradually becoming thinner at the end, and it was between the likeness of a snake and a fish. The underside was pure white like a ribbon. Usually, dragons are seen in rainy weather, sometimes only the head, tail, scales, and claws are visible. There has never been a time when the sky was completely clear, without wind, rain, lightning, or thunder, and seen with such clarity. Recording it is also sufficient to contribute to the study of natural phenomena.

Master Zhao Lüquan once said that Mr. Sun Xuchuan, before he passed the imperial examinations, served as a tutor in a certain family. The master’s mother had just fallen critically ill, and the houseboy had prepared dinner, but due to other matters had not yet eaten, and was ordered to place it on a table in another room. Suddenly, he saw a white-robed figure enter the room. He was in a state of confusion and shock when another black-robed, short figure hesitantly entered. The gentleman entered the room to investigate and found the two figures eating heartily. He shouted at them sternly, and the white-robed one fled. The black-robed one, blocked by the gentleman at the door, hid in the corner of the wall. The gentleman then sat outside the door to observe the situation. Soon, the master staggered out and said: "Earlier, the patient spoke in the language of the dead, claiming that messengers of the Underworld had come with a summons to take him away. One of them was being held back by the gentleman and could not escape, fearing that they would miss the time limit and cause the deceased to suffer great punishment. I am uncertain whether this is true or false, so I came out to investigate." The gentleman then moved to sit elsewhere and faintly saw the black-robed short figure flee in disarray, while inside the inner chamber, the sound of weeping was like boiling water. Mr. Sun was a sincere and virtuous gentleman, and never spoke a falsehood in his entire life. Therefore, this event must have truly occurred. Indeed, the laws of the Underworld are extremely strict, and the gods’ hearing is extremely sharp. Yet even the soul-capturing officials of the afterworld are not without fault, for they cannot help but seize the food and drink of the sick family. If such is the case, then how could the officials and attendants of this world fail to be rigorously examined?

A disciple named Yi Bibo Bingshou said: There was a scholar who traveled to the capital to take the imperial examination and stayed at an inn by the Western Riverbank. On the wall hung a scroll depicting a courtesan, whose graceful and alluring appearance seemed almost alive. Whenever he sat alone, he would gaze at it in deep thought, so absorbed that he sometimes failed to notice when guests arrived. One night, she suddenly appeared from the painting, descending gracefully as if she were a real woman. Though the scholar knew she was a spirit, his thoughts had long been fixed upon her, and he could no longer restrain himself. Thus, they conversed and laughed together in tender and harmonious fashion. After failing the examination, he returned south, and unexpectedly bought the painting and took it home. When he hung it in his study, there was no further sign of supernatural activity. Yet the voice of "Zhenzhen" did not cease. Three or four months later, she suddenly appeared again, speaking of old matters but not elaborating much. He had no time to question her, but only shared sorrow and joy with her. From then on, they became intimately familiar, and he soon fell ill with a wasting disease. His father summoned a Taoist priest from Mount Mao to perform an exorcism. The priest examined the wall closely and said, "This painting has no demonic energy; the one causing the disturbance is not this one." He set up a ritual circle and performed the ceremony. The next day, a fox was found dead beneath the circle. The priest realized that the scholar had already harbored impure desires, and thus had summoned a demon with his own impurity, allowing the fox to take advantage. The fox he had met in the capital was likely another one altogether.

The decision of right and wrong in the world is based solely on propriety and law, yet there are those who, though they defy propriety and must be restrained by law, steadfastly follow their own path. In a family related by blood or marriage, there was a servant girl named Liu Qing. When she was seven or eight years old, the master arranged for her to marry a young male servant named Yi Shou, and when she reached sixteen or seventeen, they were to be married. One day, Yi Shou suddenly fled due to gambling debts, and after a long time without any news, the master intended to marry her off to another servant, but she refused even unto death. The servant girl was quite beautiful, and the master took the opportunity to seduce her, offering her the position of a concubine, but she also refused even unto death. The master then sent an old woman to persuade her, saying: "Since you refuse to betray Yi Shou, and temporarily follow the master, we will search for Yi Shou in many ways and still marry you to him. If you do not agree, you will be sold to a distant place, and there will be no more chances to see Yi Shou."

The maid wept in secret for several days, and eventually prostrated herself at the master's feet, offering her body. She only urged the master to find Yi Shou frequently. After four or five years, Yi Shou returned to the master, and they were married according to tradition. After the wedding, she continued to serve as usual, but she no longer spoke to the master. When the master tried to approach her, she would avoid him. Even when he beat her and bribed Yi Shou to force her, she still refused to comply. There was nothing he could do, so he sent her away kindly. As she left, she placed a small box before the master's wife, bowed, and then departed. Inside the box were all the gifts the master had given her over the years, none of which were missing. Later, Yi Shou traveled to sell goods, and the maid supported herself by sewing and mending, living a frugal life but never regretting her decision. In 1855, I was at home, and Yi Shou still came to sell some porcelain and pottery, his head now white. When I asked about his wife, he said she had been dead for a long time. Strange indeed, this maid was neither chaste nor lewd, both chaste and lewd, and it is difficult to categorize her. I record this story in hopes that scholars will provide a definitive judgment.

Wu Maolin, a guest in the household of Yao An Gong, saw two boys cursing each other and then recounted an incident: In Jiaochang, a man once met an old man on the road. The mud was slippery, and the old man lost his footing, nearly knocking the man over. The man, who was arrogant and domineering, then insulted the old man's mother. The old man, angry, wanted to fight, but he suddenly lowered his head and deeply thought. He bowed and apologized, and inquired about the man's name, surname, and address before parting ways at a fork in the road. When the man returned home, his mother closed the room door during the day, called out to her but got no response, and her breathing sounds were quite unusual. The man, worried, peered through a hole in the window and saw his mother completely naked, dazed and drunk-like, with a man having intercourse with her. Upon close inspection, it was the old man he had met. Furious and shouting, the man wanted to enter and capture him, but the doors and windows were all strong and unbreakable. He quickly took out a bird gun and fired from outside the window. The old man screamed and fell down, revealing himself to be an old fox. Neighbors gathered to watch, and no one failed to be shocked and laugh. The man had insulted the fox's mother, it was just empty words, but ultimately, this fox actually paid for it, which can serve as a warning for those who are good at cursing. This fox, in seeking to satisfy a momentary anger, ended up losing his life, and this also serves as a warning for those who seek revenge for even small grievances.

A statement by the noble and valiant Cheng Mou: In front of Changchun Garden, there is a small stream. At night, the eunuchs often said that during dark nights, they would see a bright star suspended in the sky. They were all surprised and searched for it, eventually seeing the light coming from the stream. Recognizing it as a sign of treasure, they planned to retrieve it and found a clam with a diameter of about four to five inches. Upon opening it, they discovered two pearls joined together, one larger and one slightly smaller, both shaped like a gourd. They dared not keep them for themselves and presented them to the emperor. The emperor has used these pearls as the top of his imperial crown to this day. This event occurred at the beginning of the Qianlong era. It is unlikely for a small stream to produce large clams, and it is unheard of for pearls to be joined together. This must be a sign of divine favor for the emperor, granting him a long life of ninety years and continued health, which is not a coincidence.

The lotus flowers typically bloom in summer, but those in the Summer Palace bloom in autumn, being a month or more later than those within the Great Wall. Although they bloom late, they also wither late, and by the beginning of September, their green umbrellas and red robes still stand out clearly. In the garden, they are often paired with chrysanthemums in vases, and this combination is frequently seen in imperial poetry. The reason for this delay is that the northern region is cold, and spring arrives later, causing the flowers to bloom later as well. However, it is puzzling why they wither late despite the early cold in autumn. This year, upon reading the annotations of the imperial poetry, I learned that the ponds in the Summer Palace collect the three sources of Wu Lie water and are further fed by hot springs, which provide internal warmth, allowing the flowers to withstand the cold. Mr. Dai Sui Tang, whose courtesy name was Henry, was a fellow scholar of the year Gui Si with the Yaoan Official. After serving as the magistrate of Qihé, he returned home and once stayed in my household. He spoke of his ancestors, who were originally from Zhejiang, and were known for their clever and meticulous minds, often competing with Westerners. While working at the Imperial Astronomical Bureau, he had a conflict with Ferdinand Verbiest, a Westerner who served as the director of the bureau. As a result, Mr. Dai moved to Tieling, and thus he is a native of Tieling. When he was young, he saw his ancestors create a bird-cannon, which resembled a pipa. All the gunpowder and lead bullets were stored in the barrel, and it was operated by a mechanism with two parts that fit together like male and female. When one part was pulled, the gunpowder and lead bullets would fall into the barrel, and the second part would move along with it, igniting the gunpowder with a stone and firing the cannon. It could fire twenty-eight times before the gunpowder and lead bullets ran out, requiring a reload. He intended to offer this invention to the military, but in a dream, a person scolded him, saying, "The Lord loves all living beings. If you offer this device, it will spread among people, and your descendants will have no survivors." Fearing this, he did not offer the invention.

When speaking of this matter, the narrator turned to his nephew, Bing Ying—a Jinshi (imperial scholar) of the Qianlong era's Yi Chou year, who served as the magistrate of GaoTai in Gansu—and asked, "Is it still in your house? Could you bring it here for me to see?" His nephew replied, "When I was studying at the Ministry of Revenue, my fifth brother's son pawned it for money, and it has since been lost beyond trace. It may have been lost in reality, or perhaps it was kept out of love and care, so it is uncertain. Nevertheless, this device is also quite extraordinary. The valiant and wise official, Cheng Mou, mentioned that during the campaign against Wushi, Wencheng Gong and Yiyong Gong set up camps in an interlocking formation, about a li away from the enemy camp. Whenever they moved back and forth, lead bullets would fall around their horses, fortunately without hitting them. Judging by the range of the musket, it could not have reached more than thirty steps, so it must not have been the cause. We suspected there might be an ambush in the ditch, but our search found nothing. We could not understand the reason. After defeating the enemy, we captured prisoners and questioned them, and then we learned that their national treasure included two guns, both of which had a range of over a li. We searched and found them, and tests confirmed their power. We each took one. Yiyong Gong died in battle while fighting in Burma, and the whereabouts of his gun are unknown. The gun obtained by Wencheng Gong is still kept at home, but no one knows how it was made."

During the Song Dynasty, there was the Shenbi Bow, which was actually a powerful crossbow. It was placed on the ground and operated by stepping on its trigger mechanism, capable of penetrating armor at a distance of 300 steps (approximately 150 meters). It was also known as the "Crushing Enemy Bow." In the literary examination of Hong Rong, there was an inscription on the "Crushing Enemy Bow." The Song forces, resisting the Jin, relied heavily on this as a powerful weapon. Military regulations prohibited the loss of any single crossbow. If it could not be carried away after a defeat, it was destroyed to prevent the enemy from obtaining and replicating its mechanism. When the Yuan Dynasty, under Emperor Kublai Khan, conquered the Song, they acquired the design and used it to achieve victory. By the Ming Dynasty, the knowledge of its construction was lost. However, the Yongle Encyclopedia still contained complete illustrations and descriptions of the crossbow. Yet, its components were depicted separately, with only measurements of length, width, and depth, and descriptions of their shapes, without a single complete diagram. I and Mr. Nianqiao, the Attendant, spent several days trying to piece together the diagrams, but found no clear solution. I considered having Western experts study the diagrams, but my mentor, Mr. Liu Wenzheng, said: "Westerners are deeply secretive, even about mathematical methods that originated in China and spread to the West. They call such methods 'Eastern Techniques.' Now that they are learning mathematics from us, they are even more secretive and unwilling to share all their knowledge. Given that this crossbow is a well-known powerful weapon, how can we be sure they won't secretly take it and claim they don't understand it? The Yongle Encyclopedia is stored in the imperial library, and there might be scholars who can solve this problem without needing to consult Western experts. Why seek help from foreigners?" Thus, I and Mr. Nianqiao decided to stop pursuing the matter. This reflects the wisdom and foresight of the elder statesman, seeing far ahead and understanding the broader perspective.

The Master of Spring Light, Prince Chun Hui, says: In the Temple of the Immortal Lady Bi Xia in Rehe — commonly known as the Temple of the Goddess — two temples have sculptures depicting the transformations of hell. In the western wing of one temple, there is a ghost soldier that appears grim and terrifying, a local ghost as commonly referred to. Some people have seen it buying miscellaneous items, such as firewood and charcoal, which it often piles up inside the temple. When asked, the locals confirm this, though it does not harm people and people eventually become accustomed to it and forget about it. Some say that ghosts do not cook, so why would they need these items? The *Zuo Zhuan* says: Stones cannot speak, but things may rely on them; could they be other spirits or monsters? It is feared that it may become a problem over time, and it should be dealt with early. I say that the vastness of heaven and earth, all things are born from one breath; deep mountains and vast lakes have everything. The deep gorges and great ravines of Rehe are close to residential areas, and people naturally get close to it, and it gets close to people, which is reasonable for it to exist. Or perhaps it is a spirit of plants, following its inherent nature, or a fox, returning to its original home, taking on a form through transformation, adorning the clay figures, which is also reasonable for it to exist. Ultimately, they are all nurtured by the Creator. The sages cast demons and monsters into the Yu Ding, and the *Ting Shi* and *Fang Xiang* are listed in the *Rites of Zhou*, removing those that harm the people. They did not completely eliminate all strange beings; since it does not cause harm, it can be left to come and go. A sea guest playing with seagulls, suddenly flying away without landing, as soon as one has ulterior motives, the other responds in kind, or it may become entangled and troubled.

Tchen He-ling of Wanping, whose name was Yong-nian, was originally a wealthy landowner but later experienced a decline in his fortunes. His younger brother, Yong-tai, died first, and his widow requested to divide the property, to which he reluctantly agreed. The widow then said, "My husband was a capable man who could manage affairs, but I am a widow with young children, so I ask for two-thirds of the property." The relatives all said it was not possible. He-ling replied, "The widow's request is reasonable, and I should comply. Moreover, as a widow and her children, she cannot collect the debts owed to her, so she wants to claim two-thirds of the wealth. In return, she will settle the promissory notes that have been outstanding for years, including the interest, which amounts to one-third of the property. I will also agree to this." Later, when the promissory notes were demanded, they could not be collected, and He-ling became very poor. This was the year 1776 during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor. The Tchen family had no one who had passed the imperial examinations before, but that year, He-ling's son, San-li, unexpectedly passed the provincial examination. On the day the results were announced, my contemporary and friend Li Bu-yu, who lived nearby, heard the news and sighed deeply, saying, "The moral order of the universe will not disappoint a person."

Zhang Fuchacha of Nanpi, whose name is Jingyun and who is the author of *Qiu Ping Xin Yu*, had a son who died young. His wife chose to commit suicide by hanging to follow him. The place where she hung herself was on the wall, and there appeared a small portrait of the deceased son, about a foot tall, with lifelike eyebrows and eyes. The traces of the portrait were neither a painting nor ink, but resembled both. The wife was not skilled in painting, and no one else could recreate the portrait. Moreover, the bedroom was inaccessible to others. At the time, relatives and friends gathered, all unable to understand how the portrait came to be. The Zhang and Ji families were related by marriage; dozens of Ji women had married into the Zhang family, and dozens of Zhang women had married into the Ji family. Everyone who saw the portrait was amazed and considered it strange. I believe this was not strange, but rather a manifestation of the extreme devotion and sincerity of this heroic woman. It is said that when the spirit focuses, energy gathers; when energy gathers, the spirit becomes concentrated; when the spirit and energy combine, an image is formed; and when the image manifests, traces are left behind. The living person's spirit and energy influenced the dead person's spirit and energy, and the two combined to form this image. Therefore, it is said that images arise from the mind, and that extreme sincerity can even move metal and stone. Zhang Fuchacha recorded these events and collected poems and praises from scholars and officials. I planned to write a poem about this, but the concept is profound, and my writing skills are insufficient to fully explain it. I revised the poem multiple times but was never fully satisfied. To this day, I still feel troubled by the event. I have recorded it here to show the connection between the living and the dead, and I will complete the poem at a later time.

Immortals who consume elixirs are mentioned in various books, and one may occasionally encounter such a person. However, if one does not follow the correct method, it can instead cause harm. Mr. Dai Suotang said that he once saw a man who had been consuming pine resin for over a decade. His skin and flesh were full and robust, and his spirit was strong and solid. He believed he had benefited greatly from it. However, after a long time, he began to feel a slight discomfort in his abdomen. After even longer, he developed a condition of dryness and hardening. Treating it with sesame seeds or similar remedies was ineffective, and using strong purgatives like nitre and sulfur also failed, resulting in excrement so thin that it could barely pass through a thread. He then realized that the resin had stuck to his intestines, gradually accumulating and hardening, making the passageway narrower and narrower, until it reached this state. There was no medicine that could cure it, and he eventually became exhausted and died. He also saw another person who had been consuming sulfur, whose skin split open like flaying, and placing it on ice slightly reduced the pain. Ancient poems say that seeking immortality through medicine often leads to being misled by the medicine. How could this not be true?

Beyond the Great Wall, surrounded by countless mountains, yet they are all gentle slopes like hills and mounds. But eastward to Wangjiaying, they become rugged and majestic, their textures and creases all containing the meaning of paintings. This is because heaven and earth have bestowed their divine essence upon this place. There is a Luohan Peak, which resembles a monk seated in meditation, its head, chest, abdomen, arms, and elbows clearly visible; there is a Bell Hammer Peak, which is the same as the Wuli River mentioned in the *Shui Jing Zhu*, beside it is a solitary rock that seems to rise like a cloud, it is wide at the top and sharp at the bottom, standing as if carved. When I was compiling the *Rehe Zhi*, I once climbed a ladder and used a rope to reach its base, and found it to be formed by countless stone eggs and fine sand, remaining unshaken since ancient times, and no one knows why. There are Twin Towers Peak, standing tall and upright, from afar they look like two pagodas rising from the ground, there is no path to climb, but at night one might hear the sounds of bells, chimes, and chanting, and during the day, there are occasional clouds passing by.

In the year 1760 (Qianlong庚戌), the emperor ordered officials to construct wooden ladders and sent someone to climb and inspect the peaks. One peak had a circumference of 160 steps, with a small house on top containing a table and an incense burner. Inside the house was a stone tablet inscribed with the words "Wang Xiansheng," likely the name of a Taoist immortal. Another peak had a circumference of 62 steps, with two beds of leeks planted on it, neatly arranged furrows, and a garden-like appearance. These features certainly could not have been the result of human effort; it cannot be called anything else but a place of divine presence. Even within sight, the mysterious and unexplainable nature of the peaks remains. Scholars who hold their own opinions often dismiss such phenomena as impossible according to reason, but isn't this absurd? Near the Twin Towers Peak, there is a Guandi Temple, and the abbot, Wuzhen, recounted that in 1772 (Qianlong壬寅), during a night of heavy thunder and rain, a stone Buddha statue fell from the Twin Towers Peak. However, it was merely a rough piece of stone with one side resembling a Buddha. This event occurred eight years before 1760, in 1752. Perhaps the stone Buddha fell due to the peak's spiritual power, drawing attention to Buddhism. Doubt is passed on in doubt, and such accounts are recorded alongside other uncertain phenomena.

The Words of Cai Fang, a Contemporary Cai Fang, a contemporary of mine, once recounted that he and his friends traveled to the Western Mountains. They reached a deep, secluded area where they found a narrow path. They followed it up, and found a place entirely silent and uninhabited, with only a few dilapidated houses, overgrown with moss and grass. On the wall, they saw a large, bold character "我" (I/me), written with forceful and vigorous strokes. They entered to examine it further and found more writing behind it. Upon careful inspection, they discovered two poems. The first poem read: "Strolling by the stream, I met a neighbor, Inviting me to taste the tender fiddleheads. We held hands and eagerly discussed the Southern Crossing, Unaware that we had broken a branch with flowers." The second poem read: "Drunk and lying asleep in front of an old pine tree, Dew falling on the empty mountain at night, silent and still. A wild deer has truly become familiar with us over the years, It also comes to share my green moss bed." The poems did not include dates, names, or other identifying information. The flavor of the words and their meaning suggested that they were written by a remnant of a former dynasty. Some thought they were written by a fairy, but that is not the case. My cousin An Zhongkuan once accompanied a timber merchant through the ancient pass of the Northern Frontier. He went to visit a friend at the Three Pagodas, also known as the "Three Towers," which was the ancient region of Yingzhou during the Tang dynasty and the administrative region of Xingzhongfu during the Liao dynasty. The host told him that the mountain dwellers once caught a deer. As they were tying it up by the stream to butcher it, the ropes suddenly snapped one by one, and the deer leaped away. From afar, they saw a person wearing a bamboo hat on the opposite mountain, as if pointing and drawing. They suspected that the person was using some sort of magic to restrain the deer. The mountain was steep and had no signs of human presence in ancient times. Perhaps it was a fairy?

Mr. He Lian'an (何励庵), whose given name is Xiu (琇), was a scholar who passed the imperial examinations in 1733 (雍正癸丑) and served as a low-ranking official in the Imperial Clan Court (宗人府). His career was fraught with difficulties and hardships, and he ultimately died in poverty and illness. He authored *Qiaoxiang Xiaoji* (樵香小记), a work that contains many verifications and explanations of doubts in classical texts, which is included in the *Siku Quanshu* (四库全书). He had a particular fondness for the poetry style of Lu You (陆放翁). One day, he wrote a poem that went: "Cold and desolate is the office, where work ends early, The leisurely official's life is like that of a hermit. Occasionally visiting old friends for chess games, There is little money left for gambling. Light green wine is good for summer consumption, The last spring flowers have arrived. Frequently looking in the mirror, seeing my hair as white as snow, I cherish the fleeting time even more." This poem was written on a fan, which was seen by a respected figure named Yao'an Gong (姚安公). He pondered over it and said, "How much sorrow and despair does this poem convey, it seems his spirit has already waned." Indeed, Mr. He passed away that same summer and autumn. The saying "poetic omen" (诗谶) suggests that there may be a deeper meaning or prophecy in the poem, reflecting the poet's state of mind and the circumstances of his life.

Mr. Zhao Lüquan, the elder, says that Lu City, built by Wu's Lu Meng, has two local god temples on both sides of the river. One is Tang Dynasty's Prince of Fenyang, Guo Ziyi, which is already inexplicable; the other is Yan Liang, a general under Yuan Shao, and even less can one understand where he comes from. The locals' prayers are quite effective. Within the jurisdiction of fifteen li, no Guandi temple may be erected; if erected, it will bring disaster. There was a county magistrate who did not believe, and during the Yan Temple festival, he went to see it personally. He ordered the actors to perform a play about the Three Kingdoms. Suddenly, a strong wind arose, lifting the thatched roof into the air, tossing it about, and then dropping it. Some actors died, and within the fifteen li, a severe epidemic broke out, causing widespread death among people and livestock, and the magistrate also fell gravely ill. I say that when two armies oppose each other, each fights for its own side, and victory and defeat cannot coexist. This is killing out of public justice, not out of personal grudge. Among those who fail due to unexpected circumstances despite their wisdom and bravery, their fate is determined by heaven, and they cannot be blamed. Those who lose because of their inferior abilities compared to their opponents, the fault lies with themselves, and they cannot be blamed either.

Zhang Suichang's vengeful spirit killed the rebels, fighting for the safety of the state by defending one prefecture. This was for the sake of the state and the ruler, not for personal gain. If those who died in battle after the success of a campaign were all to hold grudges, then all ancient famous generals would be struck down by spirits. Is this reasonable? Moreover, Yan Liang has been killed for a long time, over a thousand years, and there has been no sign of his spirit. Why should he suddenly become a god today and seek revenge today? Judging by the principles of heaven, it is certainly not so. This is likely due to the temple priests and sorcerers fabricating strange stories, and mountain and water spirits taking advantage of the people's confusion. Liu Jingshu's *Yi Yuan* says: In Danyang County, there is a temple dedicated to Yuan Shuang, the fourth son of Yuan Zhen. Yuan Zhen was killed by Huán Xuānwǔ, and Yuan Shuang disappeared. During the Taoyuan period, he appeared in Danyang and requested the establishment of a temple, but it was not immediately built. There were many tiger disasters, and the families affected dreamed of Yuan Shuang arriving, urging the construction urgently. The people built a祠 (temple), and the fierce tigers ceased their attacks. They would always perform rituals and pray at the祠 on the last day of the second month, and it would always be windy and rainy on that day. In the fifth year of Yuanjia, after the offerings were made, a villager named Qiu Du saw something in the courtyard of the temple, with a human face and a toad body, wearing a straw hat, with seven holes neatly arranged and smelling of alcohol. It is unknown whether this was the spirit of Yuan Shuang or a possessed creature. I believe that if it comes with wind and rain, it must be a water spirit. However, such things have existed since ancient times.

Gong Zizheng, the uncle of Zhang Gong, said in his dream — he was also known as Shangwen, and his given name was Jingshuo. In Wujia Village in Cangzhou, there was a small temple to the east, which had been abandoned for many years without a monk. It had always been a place for travelers to rest. There was a man named Yuezuoren who often met a person at the temple, whom he invited to sit and talk. They became quite compatible, and gradually, they went to the market together to drink. Their relationship became even more intimate. One day, he asked about his hometown and residence, and the man, feeling ashamed, replied, "I have been close friends with you and dare not deceive you. In fact, I am an old fox spirit from this temple." Yuezuoren did not fear him and continued to visit as before. One night, they met again, and the fox spirit handed him a bird gun and said, "I have been intimate with a woman, and my younger brother has also been intimate with her, which is like stealing my wife. I cannot stop him, and if I hit him, my strength is not enough to prevail. I am furious and cannot bear it any longer. Tonight, I will wait for him at a crossroads and fight to the death. I have heard that you are skilled with a gun. When we fight, please shoot him, and I will be forever grateful. The moon is bright as if it were daytime, and you will easily recognize me." Yuezuoren agreed, and he hid in the grass at the place the fox spirit pointed to. Later, he thought to himself, "His younger brother is indeed disrespectful and deserves to die. However, the woman he is intimate with already has a husband, so she is not my wife. Between family members, it is best to handle things properly. If I kill him, am I not being too cruel? Even his brothers are like this, and if I am involved in such matters, I fear they might turn on me too." So, he took advantage of the moment when they were entangled and fired one shot, killing both of them. The poem *Tangdi* says, "Brothers may quarrel within the walls, but they unite against external threats. When family members fight, there is no end but mutual harm." Uncle Gong Zizheng often used this story to warn his nephew. It is said that this man returned with two foxes, and he had witnessed it himself.

The Cook Yang the Old Woman said, in her hometown there was a man named Mr. Yi who, on the verge of death, gave instructions to his wife: "I have no remaining wealth; after my death, you and our child will certainly suffer from cold and hunger. We are a family line that has only one male descendant for four generations, leaving this young son. Now I make an agreement with you: regardless of who, if they can take care of my orphaned child, then marry them. There is no need to observe mourning periods or daily meals, and once the obligation is fulfilled, go ahead." After giving these instructions, he closed his eyes and said nothing more, only groaning until he died. After half a day, he then died. There was another man named Mr. Yi who heard that she was beautiful and sent a matchmaker to ask according to the agreement. Although the wife agreed to marry, because she could still support herself, she could not bear to go through with it. After several months, when she could no longer afford to cook, the marriage was completed. On the night of the wedding ceremony, after extinguishing the candles and going to bed, she suddenly heard a sighing sound from outside the window. She knew it was the spirit of her deceased husband. Speaking through the window, weeping, she said, "You left a last message; I did not marry on my own. The events of tonight were inevitable given the circumstances. Why are you causing trouble?" The spirit also wept and said, "I came to see the child, not to cause trouble for you. Because I heard you crying and undressing, thinking about your poverty, that led you to this situation. My heart and spleen were moved, and unconsciously, I sighed." Mr. Yi was very frightened and quickly put on his clothes and said, "From now on, if I do not treat you like a son, may the sun bear witness." The spirit's words then fell silent. Later, Mr. Yi became obsessed with his beautiful wife and never left the house. But the wife always seemed lost and disoriented. Mr. Yi loved the child even more to please her, and then she began to smile and talk. After seven or eight years, Mr. Yi died of illness, having no children and also having no other relatives. The wife took control of his wealth and hired a teacher to educate the child, who eventually passed the local examination.

She remarried and bore two grandsons. When she was in her forties, she suddenly had a dream in which her deceased husband said to her: "I have followed you here, never leaving this place. Because my son has everything he needs, though you may daily be close to him, you still think of me constantly. In the quiet moments of lamp-light and moonlight, you shed tears in private, and I have seen all of this. Therefore, I do not wish to reveal any signs of my presence, nor alarm you and your child. Now that he has passed on to another life, your life is also coming to an end. The remaining feelings are not yet severed, and you should follow me back." A few days later, she indeed fell ill. She told her son about the dream and refused to take medicine, gradually declining and eventually dying. Her son carried her coffin and buried her together with her deceased husband, in accordance with her wishes. Cheng Zi said, "Starving to death is a minor matter, but losing one's moral integrity is a grave matter." This is truly a timeless principle. However, it is only speaking from the perspective of an individual. This woman endured shame for herself to prolong the ancestral worship, preserving the greater good, and it seems that it should be considered separately.

The elderly woman could provide her name, surname, and place of residence, and she returned the heirloom jade to Zhao, but in the end, it was not perfect, so I concealed and did not record it. I pitied her fate and grieved for her aspirations, covering up for the virtuous. In my hometown, there was a woman who remarried after her first husband's death, and her cousin lived nearby. After her remarriage, she would visit her mother-in-law with formal respect and inquire about her health and well-being every few days, and sometimes provide financial support. Her mother-in-law depended on her for survival. After her mother-in-law's death, she spent money on burial arrangements and sent someone annually to offer sacrifices at her grave. In the capital, there was another woman who was young and widowed. Although she was quite beautiful, she was not skilled in needlework and cooking. She consulted her father-in-law and mother-in-law, falsely claiming to be their daughter, and sold herself as a concubine to a wealthy family, ultimately supporting her in-laws for life. These were all women who had lost their virtue, and originally, they were not worthy of praise. However, they did not forget their old favors, and this was enough to encourage a thin custom. The virtuous person acts kindly toward others and should not overlook their small virtues. Scholars who hold strict views, however, make it impossible for those who have momentarily faltered to redeem themselves, leading them to willingly abandon themselves, which is not the way to teach people to correct their mistakes.

Huideng the monk said, there was a scholar who rented a small hermitage outside Fengyi Gate to spend the summer, the place being very secluded. One day, he obtained a secret manuscript to study and copied it by hand under the lamp. He heard someone outside the window as if rustling and asked who it was. The voice replied, "I am a lonely spirit, trapped here, not hearing the sound of books for over a hundred years. Listening to your recitation for several days has stirred my old feelings, and I wish to have a conversation with you to relieve my pent-up emotions. We are of the same kind, please do not be alarmed." After finishing speaking, the spirit pulled aside the curtain and entered directly, behaving in a gentle and refined manner, very much in the style of a scholar. The scholar was frightened and called for a temple monk. When the monk arrived, the ghost was not afraid, pointing to a chair and saying, "Please sit down, I have known you before. You are simple and unworldly, without the airs of the monastery or the marketplace, we can talk." The monk and the scholar both felt awkward and could not answer. The ghost then took the copied book, only read a few lines, and suddenly threw it on the ground, vanishing suddenly.

Yang Yuting reported that in the deep mountains of Laizhou, there was a boy herding sheep who daily lost one or two of them. The owner severely beat him for this, and the boy paid close attention to investigate the matter. It turned out that two large snakes were emerging from a mountain crevice, sucking and swallowing the sheep. The snakes were as big as jars, and no one dared to approach them. The boy was very angry and consulted his father. The father set up a plow blade in the mountain crevice, and indeed, one snake split its belly and died. Fearing that its mate might take revenge, they dared not graze there again. They sometimes secretly watched, but found no signs of the snakes, thinking they had moved elsewhere. Half a year later, greedy for the better grass and water of that place, they drove the sheep to graze again. They had not grazed for three days when the boy was swallowed by the snake. It seems the snake had hidden and not emerged to lure the boy to come. The boy's father had a plan; he acted as if not searching but secretly prayed to the military officer. He hid a cannon in the deep grass and sometimes secretly went to observe. After more than two months, he saw traces of winding on the stone, so he carried fire at night and lay in ambush beside it. The snake indeed descended to drink in the stream, making a rustling sound. He then fired one shot, and the snake was minced.
