After returning home, the father suddenly went mad and beat himself, saying, "You planned to kill my husband, I planned to kill your son, it is fitting. I have hidden deeply and not emerged, you again use all means to kill me, then I am wrongfully dead. Now I will not spare you." After several days, he died. There is a folk saying that says, "If the contest of strength is not resolved, both must fall to the ground. If the contest of drinking is not resolved, both must become drunk." This saying though small, can be used to illustrate great things.

Meng Lüzhou's self-recorded account of his inspection of Taiwan states: In the year Dingyou of the Qianlong reign, I happened to engage in a divination session with a friend. The divination spirit bestowed upon me a poem that read: "Sailing ten thousand miles across the vast ocean, amidst wind and rain the fish and dragons gather with a hundred spirits. The sea mist clings to the sky, obscuring the islands; the sound of the tide crashes against the shore like the rumble of thunder. The waves of the great sea do not hinder the Three Divine Islands; the inhabitants of the underwater palace eagerly watch the two emissary stars. Remember the place where the white clouds are hazy, where someone is gazing at the green mountains of Shu." At that time, I was about to undertake the mission of inspecting Taiwan. I suspected that I would be sent on this journey within a few days, and indeed, the order was issued. In June, I set out, and by August, I had arrived in Xiamen to cross the sea. I remained there for half a year before returning. On my return, the wind was favorable, and I landed on shore in just one night. On my departure, however, the journey was fraught with danger and lasted seventeen days. When I first left Xiamen, thunder and rain came together, and the clouds and mist were thick and dark. We sailed blindly, not knowing where we were heading. Suddenly, a foul wind struck our nostrils, and the sailors said, "This is the Black Water Ocean." The water here is several dozen zhang deeper than the sea, spanning dozens of li in width, and its length is unknown. It is dark and deep, like ink splashed on paper. The crew signaled to us to remain silent, saying that below us lies the Dragon Palace, the first and most dangerous place. Once we pass this point, we will be safe.

When we reached White Water Bay, we encountered a giant fish with its fins flaring, approaching us. It raised its head like a towering peak blocking the sun. Every time it swayed, the waves surged as high as mountains, and the sound was like thunder. It took several moments for the fish to pass. Estimating its length, it was likely hundreds of miles long. The boatmen said the fish came to welcome an angel, which might be true. Soon after, a hurricane arose, nearly capsizing our boat. Suddenly, dozens of small birds circled around the mast. The boatmen rejoiced, saying the birds came to save us. The wind suddenly stopped, and we were able to anchor in Penghu. With the sage ruler in power, all the gods fulfill their duties—it's not false. Reflecting on what we experienced, it matched the words of poetry. Is it not that ghosts and spirits can foretell the future? At that time, my father was still alive and heard about my sea voyage. He ordered my brother to come to Chikan to see me, and we both climbed the Seaview Tower. Even the last two lines coincided, further believing that everything is predestined, not something that can be achieved by human effort. In the autumn of Wuwu, I followed the court to Luanyang and talked about it with Zongbo Xiaolan. Zongbo was writing the sequel to Luanyang Records and recorded the gist for him. It might also provide material for conversation. All of the above is the preface written by Lüzhou. Examining Tang Dynasty Zhong Lu's *Dingming Lu*, the main purpose is to warn against impatience and ambition, and not to seek what is not deserved. This spirit medium predicted the future, and all its words came true. It can make people realize that there is no need to be alarmed by matters unrelated to fortune or misfortune. And the random gathering and dispersal of things are not accidental. It also helps to eliminate the machinery of seeking and avoiding.

A Man from Gao Mi, named Yu Yan, lived in a prominent household in Shandong. For no apparent reason, the granary at home burned by itself, and they initially thought it was an accidental fire. Soon, strange occurrences began to happen, greatly disturbing the entire family. One day, there was a loud bang in the main hall, and all the displayed and played-with objects were broken. The master of the house, known for his strong and resolute nature, shouted angrily, "Under the bright sky and clear daylight, what kind of evil spirit dares to cause such disturbances? I will report you to the gods!" Suddenly, a clear voice responded from above the beam: "You enjoy hunting and have killed many of my descendants. Holding onto your flesh and bones, I have been waiting for an opportunity at your home for eight years. Your ancestors' grace is abundant, and your fortune is not yet exhausted. The gods of madness, the stove god, and the gatekeeper prevent me from acting, so I can do nothing about it. Now, your family is divided by internal strife; your brothers fight over wives and concubines, each forming factions, looking like enemies. Signs of decline are visible, and the evil qi responds to it. The gods no longer accept your sacrifices, and evil spirits have already entered your home. So, I can satisfy my desire. Are you still confused?"

The voice was intense and harsh, and everyone in the household heard it. The master was startled and began to reflect, placing his hand on his chest and sighing deeply. He said, "Evil cannot overcome virtue, an ancient saying. If virtue is not cultivated, why blame the evil?" He then called out to his younger brother and concubines, saying, "Calamity is not far off, but it has not yet arrived. If we can jointly resolve our old grievances, each stop pursuing their own faction, and suddenly and completely change our behavior, we can still be saved. Today's matter should start with me. Listen to me, this is the will of our ancestors and the happiness of our descendants. If you do not listen to me, I will cut my hair and go into the mountains." He repeatedly explained and advised, taking responsibility and blaming himself. Tears streamed down and wet his sleeves, and the hearts of the people were moved. They fell to their knees and cried. Immediately, over ten sycophantic servants were expelled, and all matters of mutual strife were immediately resolved. They took a pig to the pen, cut its blood, and swore an oath to the gods, saying, "Those who harbor divided loyalties shall be like this pig." As they were exchanging apologies, someone on the beam above stamped their foot and said, "I took revenge and inadvertently revealed myself; this is my fault." They expressed surprise and left. This event took place during the Qianlong era, around the eighth or ninth year.

The concubine Ming Qian, who had a rough understanding of literary meaning, was also able to craft elegant sayings with common words. Once, on a bright summer night, the moonlight illuminated the room, and the window was filled with blooming peach blossoms. The shadow of the flowers fell onto her pillow, inspiring her to compose a poem about the flower shadows, which went: "Jiàng táo yìng yuè shù zhī xié, yǐng luò chuāng shā tòu zhàng shā, sān chù pó suō huā yī yàng, zhǐ lián liǎng chù shì kōng huā." She felt quite pleased with herself, but the following year, she died of illness. Her maid Yu Tai had served me for more than two years, and at just eighteen, she too died young, leaving behind the empty flowers. The two empty flowers thus became a poetic omen. The writer was unaware that it was driven by the natural flow of qi. One cook had followed me for several years. This year, he accompanied me to Luan Yang, but suddenly left without reason, packing his belongings and renting a place to live in a nearby alley. He took advantage of me being unable to cook and charged a high price. Everyone felt unjustly treated, and I could not help but feel angry. Suddenly, I remembered Liu Jingnan from Wuqiang, who was an official in the Secretariat and was extremely poor and distressed. A servant of his was arrogant and wanted to leave, and Liu Jingnan sent him a poem saying: "Jī hán pò rǔ gè móu shēng, sòng rǔ yī yī shàng yǒu qíng, liú qǔ tā nián xiāng jiàn dì, lín jiē wéi tàn liǎng sān shēng." Sincere and kind words, overflowing in expression, I repeatedly recited it, and felt the narrow and urgent feelings dissipate.

*The Reading the Subtle Hut Notes, Volume Twenty, Luan Yang Continued Record Two* There was a clerk in the imperial city who, after being promoted to the position of "Jingli" (a minor official), had been waiting for an opportunity for a long time but had not been assigned any duties. He was in a state of great distress. A superior official, feeling sorry for him, temporarily appointed him as a "Dian Shi" (a low-ranking official). The clerk then began to act with great arrogance and power, using his newfound influence to bully his colleagues. As a result, he was eventually dismissed from his position for other reasons. Scholar Shao Er Yun once mentioned this incident and added that in his hometown, there was once a man who was reading at night when he heard a sound coming from the window. Upon closer inspection, he saw that a piece of paper had been torn, and two small hands were pushing it apart. The hands were about the size of sesame seeds, and then a small person leaped into the room. The small person was dressed in colorful clothes and red shoes, with two braids and beautiful features, standing only about two inches tall. The small person grabbed a brush from the desk and began to spin and dance on the inkstone, dragging ink and staining the books and scrolls. The man was initially very startled, but after watching for a while, he realized that the small person had no other tricks. He then raised his hand and tried to catch the small person. The small person let out a cry and was caught in his hand. The sound was like that of a small insect or bird, and it seemed to beg for mercy. The man was furious and burned the small person alive in the lamp. The room then filled with the smell of dead willow wood, and there were no further strange occurrences. The small person, having just achieved a state of refinement, had no magical abilities, yet it acted without restraint and mocked others, inviting disaster. Could this be the same as the clerk's behavior? I do not know if this story is based on a real event or if Shao Er Yun fabricated it, but hearing it is still a lesson to be learned.

The Garrison Commander Liu Deyan of Changji, during his campaign against the Uyghur region in the past, received an urgent dispatch and set out along the Zhurtsus Road. On a gloomy and overcast day, he lost his way, and more than ten of his cavalry companions became disoriented. Their provisions were nearly exhausted, and there was no water source. They temporarily sat at the base of a tree, hoping for clear skies to help them distinguish north from south. They saw several human and horse bones at the foot of a cliff, though eroded by wind and snow, their clothing and weapons were all rotted. Upon examining their forms, it seemed they were our soldiers. Liu Deyan addressed them and sighed, saying, "If the weather does not clear in another two days, we will be companions here with you." Soon, a whirlwind arose outside the forest, coming and going suddenly, as if inviting them. He tried riding his horse to follow it, and the wind then led the way; when he tried to rest briefly, the wind also did not move. He clearly knew it was the spirit of these bones guiding him. Following it, they traveled back about thirty to forty miles and crossed two ridges before finally finding the old path. The wind then quietly stopped. The group cried and bowed to the spirits before leaving. Alas, after death, their bodies were given up, but their spirits still served the country, remaining present while their names and status remained obscure. This is also a pitiful matter indeed. As for immortals, some say they do not exist, while others claim to have encountered them. Some say there are immortals, but they are not always encountered. Since the time of Liu Xiang, Ge Hong, and Tao Hongjing, there have been more than a hundred books recording immortals, mentioning more than a thousand names and titles. However, later generations no longer mention them. Immortals encountered in later times have their own immortals of later times. How can one preserve vital energy, even if it is prolonged, and ultimately not change? Immortals who practice purity and tranquility, and alchemists who perform magical transformations, originally each have their own paths. As recorded in various books, all who perform magical transformations are called immortals, which is distinctly without distinction.

There was a woman named Wang Ao, a native of Fangshan, whose home was in the deep mountains. She once told my grandmother, Lady Zhang Tai, that there was a Taoist monk in the mountains, aged about sixty or seventy, who lived in a small hermitage. He gathered mountain fruits for food, drank water scooped from springs, and day and night struck a wooden fish and recited scriptures. He never once went to a human household, and when people visited his hermitage to speak with him, he did not respond much, nor did he accept gifts. Wang Ao's nephew, who lived outside, returned one night to visit his mother and passed by the hermitage. The Taoist monk was greatly startled and said, "At night, tigers come out. How can you be traveling? I must accompany you." He then loudly struck the wooden fish in front, leading the way. Not half a mile away, a tiger suddenly appeared. The monk blocked it with his body, and the tiger left on its own. The monk did not say goodbye and left on his own. Later, he suddenly disappeared. This might be like a fairy, she said. My uncle, Master Mei'an, once said that he saw someone make a child climb a three-story open-air pavilion. In the north, those covered with tiles are called dark pavilions, and the upper level is shaped like a battlement, prepared for defense against bandits, called a bright pavilion. With a hand, he called to it, and it gracefully descended without any damage. Then he threw a copper bowl into the stream, calling to it, and it slowly floated out. These are all the techniques of Taoist magicians, not the realm of immortals.

My maternal uncle, Mr. Zhang Jianting, recounted an incident that occurred in a village called Zuanhe. A farmer in the village was tending several cows in the fields when they suddenly died one after another. A Taoist priest passed by and said, "These are not true deaths; they have been captured by evil spirits. Quickly give them my medicine to prevent their organs from deteriorating. I will exorcise them for you and summon their spirits." The farmer then invited the Taoist priest to his home, where he performed a ritual dance called "Yubu" for about half a minute. To everyone's amazement, the cows suddenly stood up and revived. The priest stayed for a meal but left without accepting any payment, saying that he did not seek financial gain and that he had come to help the villagers, not to deceive them. Someone who knew the truth later revealed that the Taoist priest had actually placed poisonous herbs in the grass and then used medicine to counteract the poison. After this revelation spread, the Taoist priest never returned. This shows that even among Taoist practitioners, there are true and false ones, and not all are genuine immortals or deities.

Li Nanjian told of a young man from a neighboring county, the son of an old family. He was arrogant and engaged in homosexual activities. One day, he returned from a drink at a relative's house, which was a bit far from the city. The sky was overcast, and it was dark on the road. He thought he wouldn't make it back in time, and light snow was falling softly. Just as he was hesitating, he saw a light about ten or so steps ahead. He sent a servant to check, and it turned out to be a few thatched cottages with no other inhabitants. Inside, there was only a young boy and an elderly woman. They asked if there was a place to stay, and the woman said, "My grandson and I live here. We have two empty rooms, though they are low and narrow. If you don't mind, you can stay for the night." So, he had the servant tie the horses to a tree and invited the young man inside to sit. The woman said she was old and sick and needed to sleep early, so she instructed the boy to entertain the guest. The boy was about fourteen or fifteen years old, dressed in tattered clothes, but he had beautiful features. The young man tried to chat with him, but the boy was not very responsive, only blowing fire and brewing tea.

A playful conversation ensued, and she seemed to understand his intentions, albeit barely. Suddenly, she seized the moment to whisper softly, "This place is close to the grandmother's quarters. When the snow clears, I will come in person to the official's home to request a reward." The young man was overjoyed and relieved. He opened the embroidered bag and gave her a jade pendant. She received it with modesty, and they spoke softly for a long time. She then closed the door and departed with a lamp. The young man and his servant leaned against the wall, feeling weary, and soon fell into an unconscious sleep. When he woke up, the house was no longer visible. He found himself sitting under the cypress trees near a tomb. His fur coat and sable hat, along with his clothes adorned with jade buttons, had all been stripped away, leaving him completely naked. He was exposed in the snow, and the cold was unbearable. The two horses were also missing. Fortunately, the servant's clothes remained on. The young man took off his old coat to cover his upper body and stumbled back, claiming to have met a thief. Soon, the two horses recognized the way and returned on their own, but their tails and manes had already been cut off. The clothes and hat were retrieved from the latrine, along with a mess of filth. It was clearly not a case of theft, and there was no way to explain it. The servant then fully revealed what had happened. The young man realized that his frivolous behavior had invited ridicule and that he had been tricked by the fox.

In the year of the Earth Rat (戊子), the Changji Rebellion erupted without prior warning. The local military commander held a feast for the exiles on the night of August 15th, serving wine on a hillside where men and women sat together. After becoming drunk, the commander forced the wives of the exiles to sing, which quickly sparked a violent uprising. The rebels killed the commander, looted the military armory, and took control of the city. Early on the sixteenth, news of the rebellion reached Urumqi. Grand Secretary Wen hurriedly gathered troops, but at the time, the stationed troops were scattered across various outposts, leaving only 147 men in the city. Yet, they were all battle-hardened veterans who looked at the rebels as nothing. Wen led them directly to the Red Mountain Pass, where the garrison commander Liu De stopped his horse and said, "It is ninety li to Changji; I can ride there in one day. They are rested while we are fatigued, they are defending while we are attacking from above. This cannot be done by just over a hundred men. And the way to Changji is all flat land. The Manas River is somewhat wide, but it can be crossed at any point by horse, with no defensible terrain. The only defensible point is this narrow pass. The rebels will not stay in the city; they will immediately come. You should station your troops here, using the steep cliffs for cover. The rebels won't know how many troops you have. Wait for them to arrive and attack from a defensible position. This turns defense into offense, fatigue into rest, and the rebels can be defeated."

Wang Anshi followed the plan, and when the enemy approached, Liu De held a red flag in his left hand and a sharp blade in his right. He ordered the troops: "Observe the dust and smoke. Though there may be fewer than a thousand, they are desperate men who will fight to the death. They are not easily to be withstood. Fortunately, their horses are untrained and inexperienced, and when wounded, they will surely retreat. Each of you should hold your guns and kneel on one knee. Wait and strike the horses when they pass. When the horses scatter, the enemy will become disorganized." He then commanded: "If you shoot at their shadows, your guns will not reach them, and the gunpowder will be wasted. The enemy will then have an advantage. Do not fire until I raise my flag. Anyone who fires first will be killed with my own hand." Soon, the enemy's guns roared, shaking the ground. Liu De said: "These are all empty shots; they are of no use." When one of the enemy's lead bullets struck a man in the front ranks, Liu De said: "Their guns have reached us; ours will surely reach them." He raised the flag and waved it, and the soldiers fired their guns together. The enemy's horses indeed ran wildly, colliding with each other. The soldiers charged, and the enemy was annihilated. Wang Anshi sighed: "Liu De looks like a village elder, but he is so calm and composed in battle. The commanders and provincial officers are merely good at formalities and ceremonial walking. Therefore, Liu De was credited with the first victory. However, the victory report did not detail the intricacies of the battle. I have recorded it in full so that his achievements are not forgotten."

From Urumqi to Changji, the southern boundary is the Tianshan Mountains, with no path to ascend, and the northern boundary is the reed lake, extending to the horizon, with deep mud reaching up to a person's chest, making anyone who enters sink completely. When the enemy was defeated, they did not retreat westward to Changji but instead moved north and south, entering a desolate area, acting out of panic and confusion. After capturing prisoners and questioning them, they said: "At the moment of panic and retreat, we originally intended to flee westward, but suddenly saw Guan Yu on a horse in the clouds, blocking our escape route. Therefore, we had no choice but to move sideways, hoping to hide and escape." The divine power of the gods extends even two hundred thousand miles away, and the blessings of the nation could bring divine assistance from two hundred thousand miles away. Small-scale disturbances and minor rebellions are insignificant.

Before the chaos in Changji, the Deputy Magistrate Herxi was summoned on official business to Urumqi to inspect the warehouse. Upon hearing of the city's fall, he was so enraged that he did not wish to live. He sought instructions from General Wen, saying: "The sudden rebellion of the military officials was not necessarily of their own accord. I wish to ride alone to meet the rebels in the middle of the road, and explain the benefits and harms to them. If they bind and surrender the ringleaders, we can avoid the trouble of military action. If they are surrounded by monsters and refuse to return to the right path, I will personally kill their leader and refuse to live alongside them." General Wen could not dissuade him, and so he finally rode off hastily, entering the midst of the rebels. He repeatedly tried to reason with them using great principles. The rebels all said: "You are a good official, this has nothing to do with you." The matter had already reached this point, and the situation was irreversible. They then forced him to the side of the road and left him there. Realizing that the matter was hopeless, he drew his sword and fiercely killed several rebels, dying in combat. At the time, public opinion lamented his death, saying: "The military officials were not under his jurisdiction, and the displaced people were not under his administration; it cannot be said that he was negligent. The conflict arose suddenly, and it was not premeditated treason; it cannot be said that he was negligent in his oversight. He was summoned elsewhere on official business, and he was not present in his jurisdiction; it cannot be said that his defense was weak, or that he abandoned the city and fled. The military supplies warehouse was looted, and it was under the control of the military officers; it cannot be said that there was a failure in defense. By law and reason, he could have avoided death, but he ultimately chose to die with the city, willingly sacrificing himself. If one follows this spirit, even a defense like that of Changshan and Suixian would be justified. Therefore, when his coffin returned, everyone mourned and paid respects. As for the remains of the military officials—those who were tortured with iron instruments, from the feet, inch by inch, to the top of the head, after the chaos subsided, their remains were finally collected. No one burned a single piece of paper money for them."

Zhu Qinglei said that he once saw a long scroll with characters as large as cups, which were remarkably similar to the handwriting of Zhang Ershui. The scroll was titled "Records of Dreams" with ten poems, but it was damaged by insects and had broken down, with only two poems remaining intact and readable. The first poem read: Dreaming to the peak of Penglai, The jade towers stand on the green mountains. The waves float halfway up the sky, The sun rises in the midst of the sea. Far off, the immortal officials stand, More so, the wild old men are at ease. The cloud sails rise thirty zhang high, Highly hoisted, they return to the west. The second poem read: The lush long-life trees, Layer upon layer of ancient moss. The empty mountains remain uncarved, The primordial qi still embryo. Where is the spiritual realm? How many times have I dreamed and traveled? Most pitiable are the fish and birds, They meet without being startled. The date, name, and other identifying information were all missing or damaged, so it is unknown who wrote these poems. Zhu Qinglei once wrote a poem on a fan for Li Yudian and added a preface. Some said that these poems might have been written by Zhu Qinglei himself, but the "砶之古人" phrase is unclear. However, Zhu Qinglei's usual poetic style is graceful and refined, similar to Qin Shaoyou's "Xiaoshi" style, which is not close to the style of these two poems. Others said that both the poems and the handwriting resemble those of Zhang Donghai. Zhang Donghai's collection, which the author once saw, does not remember if these two poems are included, and further research is needed. Zhu Qinglei's preface mentioned that the last four lines of the first poem have not been previously mentioned. However, Han Yu's poem, "I can navigate the complexities of the world, how can I seek immortality from others," conveys the same meaning, and the idea is borrowed without leaving a trace.

A wealthy man from the Hui region had a son who was corpulent and walked with a staggering gait. He was unkempt, with a face always covered in dirt and grease. Despite this, he enjoyed wandering through the narrow alleys and would always stare at women. One day, while walking alone, he encountered a young woman of exceptional beauty. It had just rained, and the ground was muddy. He suddenly approached her and said, "The road is so slippery, would you mind helping me?" The young woman sternly replied, "You must not be so foolish. I am a fox spirit, and all my life I have only practiced moon worship and self-cultivation. I never engage in seductive or manipulative behavior. What kind of person are you to act like this? You will bring misfortune upon yourself." She then scooped up some sand and sprinkled it on his face, causing him to recoil in shock. Suddenly, he fell into a ditch and struggled to climb out, but the young woman had already disappeared. From then on, he was always anxious, fearing that she would bring him harm, but he remained unharmed. Several days later, a friend invited him to a banquet, where a newly introduced courtesan served the wine. Upon closer inspection, he realized it was the same young woman he had met before. He was filled with doubt and confusion, not knowing what to do. He forced himself to ask her, "Did you go to the east village after the rain on that day?" The courtesan casually replied, "My sister went to the east village to visit our aunt that day, and I did not go. We look very similar, did you meet her?" Her words were so vague that he could not decide whether she was a ghost or a human, or even if she was the same person. He then made an excuse and left the gathering.

After he left, the courtesan recounted the matter saying: She actually detested his ugly appearance and his violent and domineering behavior. She pretended with false words, hoping to avoid or escape. Fortunately, he fell down by himself, and she then hid behind the pile of straw in the threshing floor. She did not expect him to believe it was true. No one at the gathering did not laugh heartily. One guest said: Since she has entered the brothel, how could she choose her clients? He is one who can buy laughter with a thousand gold pieces. Why don't you take her there? They then went together, fully describing the courtesan's parents and her husband's name and surname, and her doubts were then resolved. The courtesan's sisters, known as the Great Yang and the Second Yang, were the subject of many famous scholars' poems in the style of "Willow Branches," all of which used their surnames as a metaphor. The courtesan then thanked him, saying that she had known him since childhood, and that yesterday, she was glad to see his pity, so she replied with jesting words. Who would have thought it would lead to such rudeness. She deeply felt sorry and dared to offer her bed to make amends. Her words were refined and graceful, and her demeanor was full of charm. She then greatly captivated him, and they lingered for several nights. They summoned her husband, agreeing to pay a monthly fee for the night. They were intimate for many years, but she ultimately died of diabetes. My elder brother Qinghu said: If a fox transforms into a human, then one would fear it, fearing death; if a human transforms into a fox, then one would not only not fear it, but also not fear death. Is this still being able to fulfill its kind? It will soon bring disaster to you, he had already said so. This man, who died at the hands of the courtesan, can still be said to have died at the hands of the fox.

De Guo Dachun, De Guo Shuanggui, and De Guo Sanhuai were brothers. Sanhuai repeatedly insulted his elder brother and even went to the county office to sue him. After returning and resting at a temple, he saw the temple hall filled with monks in their black robes, all competing in their chanting. Although the temple master was dressed in auspicious attire, his appearance was gloomy and dispirited. When he made sincere appeals, tears followed his voice. Sanhuai stood dazedly for a long time, then suddenly went mad, stamping his feet and beating his chest, shouting, "How could other people's brothers be like this?" He repeated this phrase without stopping. They led him home, where he neither slept nor ate, still stamping his feet and beating his chest, reciting this phrase for several days without stopping. Dachun and Shuanggui, who had previously lived separately, both came upon hearing the news and held his hand, crying, "How could it come to this?" Sanhuai stood dazedly for a long time again, then suddenly embraced his two brothers, saying, "Was it like this?" He cried loudly several times and leaped once before dying.

All say that the deity struck him down, but this is not true. Sanhuai felt ashamed and blamed himself, which is what the sages call correcting one's mistakes and what the Buddhists call repentance. If one fully commits to this resolve, even Tian, Jing, Jiang, and Bei could achieve it. The deity has approved it; how could it strike it down? He cried out in grief and instantly died, moved from within by his innate goodness aroused, realizing he could not stand in the world, so he closed his eyes and did not look again, hiding his shadow in the underworld. Was it the deity who stripped his soul? It is a pity that he knew his mistake but did not correct it, his inherent nature took over, and once gone, there was no turning back. Without learning to assist him, without wise teachers and helpful friends to guide him, without virtuous spouse and children to support him, he could not have a bad start and a good end, nor could he redeem himself later. This is his misfortune. Once upon a time, a sister of the family Tian bought a young servant girl, a woman from a family of entertainers. When she heard someone criticize a neighbor woman for being promiscuous, she was startled and said in surprise: Is this something to do? I thought it should be like this. Later, she married into a farming family and remained chaste for life. Thus, Sanhuai acted against reason because he did not know, so children and grandchildren should first be taught to know propriety. The Korean envoy Zheng Sixian gave me a pair of chess sets, both naturally smooth and round, not made by hand. The black ones are beach stones that have been polished by the waves over the years, and the white ones are shells of marine mollusks also polished by the sea. Neither are rare, but they were carefully selected for their uniform thickness, proper shape, and even color, accumulated over time through comparison and exchange, not the result of a single day or night's effort. Placed in the study, they were quite elegant items for leisure. Later, they were taken by Fan Dashi Nong. After Dashi Nong's death, his family fell into destitution, and now it is unknown where they are.

In the ocean, there are three islands and ten continents, and on Mount Kunlun, there are five cities and twelve towers. These mythical places have been used by writers of poetry and prose. Korea, Ryukyu, and Japan, among other countries, can read Chinese books. I have seen the geographical records and maps of Japan's five capitals and its mountains and rivers, which show a vast territory extending for thousands of miles, but there are no such things as fairy mountains or enchanted lands. I have also spoken with envoys from Korea and Ryukyu, and they all said that in the East beyond Japan, there are dozens of small and large countries, and countless small and large islands, many of which Chinese people cannot reach. Despite the long voyages of ships traveling thousands of miles, no one has ever reported such tales. Only the island of Ryukyu, known as Luer, seems to be separated by the vast sea of chaos. However, occasionally, when the tides are calm, the boats from Ryukyu may return, but they do not report the existence of silver palaces that can be seen but not reached. Therefore, are the three islands and ten continents merely fabrications? Both the *Er Ya* and the *Records of the Grand Historian* state that the Yellow River originates from Mount Kunlun. Upon examining the sources of the Yellow River, there are two: one from Hotan and one from the Pamir Mountains. Some say that the Pamir Mountains are the main source, with the waters of Hotan flowing into it, while others say that Hotan is the main source, with the waters of the Pamir Mountains flowing into it. Once the two streams merge, it is difficult to determine which is the main stream and which is the tributary.

However, the Kunlun Mountains and the region of Hetian are within the current territory, and for over forty years, military outposts and settlements have been established, even reaching remote and rugged areas where agriculture and livestock are now practiced. Regardless of which of the two mountain ranges is the true source of the river, one of them must be the source of the Kunlun Mountains. Indeed, the mythical places and plants such as the Jade Pool, the Hanging Garden, the Pearl Tree, and the Ginseng Field have not been seen or heard of. Thus, the Five Cities and Twelve Towers are not also absurd? Moreover, the Lingjiu Mountain is now in Badakhshan, where the relics of the Buddha and bodhisattvas are preserved, and the inscriptions in Sanskrit match the scriptures, with over six hundred stone chambers, known as the Great Sound Temple. The Uyghur nomads live there, and our troops pursued and defeated Polomudou and Huo Jizhan, reaching that place, but what we saw was no different. The grandeur and solemnity seem to be embellished words. It is said that the Uyghur homeland is a city made of copper; the western Uyghurs claim it is to the east of their land by ten thousand miles, while the eastern Uyghurs claim it is to the west of their land by ten thousand miles. They both bow to each other from afar, but no one has ever reached that place. Therefore, it is likely that the wonders and strange phenomena recorded in Ferdinand Verbiest's *Kunyu Wanguo Quantu* (Complete Geographical Map of the World) are of this nature. Zhou Shuchang, a scholar, said: "Only those with a karmic connection to Buddhism can see the Buddha realm, and only those with a karmic connection to Daoism can see the immortal realm. One cannot judge the existence or non-existence of such things with ordinary senses." I once saw a Daoist who returned from a journey to the Kunlun Mountains, and his account did not differ from the old records. Thus, I do not know the truth.

Pai Jishi, a provincial examiner, had a servant who was a trusted attendant in the capital. This servant was cunning and skilled in conversation, and Pai Jishi greatly favored him. One day, both of his young sons died suddenly, and his wife also hanged herself at home. The cause of these events was unknown. They were simply buried. An elderly woman in the household privately told others: "She had a secret affair, intending to poison her husband and then take her children to remarry. She secretly purchased arsenic and made cakes as a cover. When her husband returned, she did not expect her two sons to eat the cakes, and they died as a result. The wife, filled with regret and unable to find resolution, also took her own life. However, the old woman, while eavesdropping in the dark of night from outside the window, only heard roughly the secret plans, could not identify who she met, and had no means to investigate further."

The servant soon fell ill and died. After his death, his peers secretly remarked, "The master only trusted him, and he deceived the master in a hundred ways. Aside from other matters, take for example the incident yesterday at the fourth watch when he was supposed to attend court at the Summer Palace. He deliberately let the driving donkey escape, and when the driver pursued it, he did not return. Time was short, and knocking on the door to borrow a carriage would not have been in time. In a hurry, they were asked to hire a carriage, but they said, 'Rain and wind are coming; no one will go for five thousand coins.' The master had no choice but to reluctantly comply. Wasn't this too much? Perhaps strange misfortune came from this?" Ji Shi, upon hearing this, said, "It is too late for him to die; I mistakenly thought he was a capable person." Mr. Yang Huating, the elder, recounted that in his hometown, there was a person who had completed his official career and returned to his hometown. He closed the door and rested, not involved in external affairs, and quite enjoyed the pleasures of seclusion. However, he was worried about having no descendants. Late in life, he got a son whom he treasured very much. The son suffered from a very serious smallpox, and they heard that Mount Laoshan had a Taoist priest who could foresee the future. They went to consult him. The priest smiled and said, "Your virtuous son still has many things to finish; how can he die so soon? If he meets a good doctor and recovers." Later, the son indulged in pleasure and became arrogant, eventually ruining his family. He wandered and lived by begging, and the ghost of Ifu became hungry. The townspeople commented, "This old man neither had blame nor praise; he should not suddenly have this son. Only a poor scholar, serving as an official for no more than ten years, yet his official's purse exceeded tens of thousands. Is there an unknown factor in the way to wealth?"

Hua Ting once said: There was a man who learned the methods of Mount Mao and was skilled in exorcising ghosts and spirits, often achieving remarkable results. One day, a family was troubled by a fox spirit and invited him to come and drive it away. He prepared his ritual tools and was about to perform the exorcism when an old man he knew came to him and said: "I have been friends with a fox spirit, and it is in a difficult situation. I ask you to intervene on its behalf. The fox spirit has not offended you, and you have no grudge against it; you only receive a gift from it, so you handle the matter accordingly. The fox spirit promised to give 24 gold pieces after the matter was settled, and now I wish to pay ten times that amount to you. Can you stop the exorcism?" He then placed the gold on the table. The man was greedy and accepted it immediately. The next day, he thanked and dismissed the person who had invited him, saying: "My method can only deal with ordinary fox spirits. Yesterday, I summoned to check, and the spirit troubling your family is a celestial fox, which is beyond my control." After receiving the money, he was very pleased and thought: "Since the fox has a lot of money, I can obtain it through magic." He then summoned all the foxes in the surrounding areas, threatening them with thunder, axes, and fire, and made them pay bribes. The demands became frequent, and the foxes could not stand the disturbance. They then conspired to steal his talismans and seals. He was possessed by the foxes, went mad and screamed, and threw himself into the river. The foxes then took the money away, not a single coin remaining. People thought he was like Fei Changfang and Ming Chongyan, famous exorcists. Later, his disciples secretly revealed it, and they then knew the reason for his downfall. Holding talismans and seals, commanding spirits to drive away evil, this power is equal to that of officials. Accepting bribes and indulging in corruption is already wrong, and using various methods to satisfy his greed, how can one escape judgment by the will of heaven and the divine? Without the foxes killing him, divine punishment would have eventually not escaped him.

The heavens are high and the earth is far, the gods and spirits are obscure, as if unrelated to humans, yet sometimes their response is like an echo, exhausting human intelligence and effort, beyond what one can contend with. In the upper bank of the river in Cangzhou, there was a girl from a certain Mr. Jia, engaged to marry the son of a certain Mr. Yi. Both families were moderately well-off, and the wedding was scheduled to take place within one or two years. An astrologer passed by Mr. Jia's house, stayed overnight due to rain, and was asked to predict the girl's fate. The astrologer thought deeply for a long time and said, "I did not bring my calculation book, so I cannot predict this fate." Realizing there was something unusual, he was pressed for details and finally said, "According to this birth chart, it is a concubine's fate. Your family seems not to have reached this level, and I heard that she is already engaged to be married. Moreover, her birth chart shows no conflicting elements, and it is certain she will not remarry, which is why I am even more doubtful." A cunning person heard about this and wanted to use it for profit. He told Mr. Jia, "How much wealth does your family have? If you marry off your daughter, it will certainly cost a lot, and you will not be able to afford it. Since her fate is like this, why not first falsely claim the girl is ill, then falsely claim she has died, buy an empty coffin and bury her quickly, and take her away to the capital at night, change her name and sell her as a concubine in a noble household? Then you can easily obtain a lot of money." Mr. Jia followed his advice. There was a high-ranking official who was going to marry off his daughter and was seeking a beautiful concubine. He bought the girl for 200 gold coins. After more than a month, he sent her off by boat, traveling south to the Tianfei Lock. The entire family was drowned in the river, but only the girl of Mr. Jia was rescued and survived. No one dared to adopt a young girl, so she was reported to the authorities.

The officials inquired about the origins of the matter. The girl had only been in that household for a short time and knew only the owner's surname, unable to name his official rank or hometown. She spoke with certainty about her parents' names and address. The officials then issued a document to Cangzhou, and the matter was resolved. At that time, Mr. Yi's son had already married his cousin, and there was no reason to change the marriage. Upon hearing that Mr. Jia had gained a large sum of money, Mr. Yi's son became furious and wanted to sue. Mr. Jia, in a difficult situation, wished to still marry the daughter to his son. Upon hearing this, Mr. Yi's son's family also wanted to sue, leading to a chaotic and complicated situation that was about to become a major case. The two families were old acquaintances and relatives, and many people mediated. They made Mr. Jia pay money to bring the girl back, and as the secondary wife of Mr. Yi's son, the difficulty was resolved. After returning home, Mr. Yi's son had already gone to fetch the girl. Mr. Yi brought the girl to his home in a carriage and saw his mother-in-law pleading that it was not her intention. The mother-in-law said, "If it was not your intention, why didn't you say you were already married when you were sold?" The girl had no answer and was led to bow to the primary wife. The girl hesitated slightly, and the mother-in-law said, "When you were sold as a concubine, didn't you bow?" The girl had no answer again and bowed according to the proper etiquette. The mother-in-law treated her like a slave for life. This was during the late reign of Emperor Yongzheng. When my great-grandmother, Lady Zhang, was staying at Shuiming Pavilion, she knew this matter very well. She once told the maidservant, "Her father just wanted a lot of money, and the girl just wanted wealth and status, so they hatched this plan. Who knows it wasn't not only useless, but also lost what she originally had. You all should see this and eliminate all your delusions."

My fourth uncle's wife, Lady Li, had a maid named Wenluan, whom she loved dearly. When I sent a letter looking for a maid, Lady Li, who favored me among her nephews, planned to give Wenluan to me as a gift. She privately asked Wenluan, who did not refuse. Lady Li made clothes and jewelry for her and had already prepared for her to come by carriage. However, someone who was jealous persuaded her father to make many demands, and the matter was ultimately thwarted. Wenluan eventually fell ill from depression and died. I did not know about this; years later, I gradually heard about it, and it was like a wild goose flying across the sky, its shadow sinking into the autumn water. This year in May, I was about to accompany the imperial entourage on a journey. After arranging matters and feeling a little tired, I sat down and dozed off. Suddenly, I dreamed of a woman who came gracefully. I did not recognize her and startledly asked who she was. She stood still in silence, and I suddenly woke up, unable to understand the reason. When the family gathered for a meal, I mentioned it casually. My third son's wife, my niece, was young and had played with Wenluan at my mother's house. She also well knew Wenluan's grievance. She was startled and said, "Is it Wenluan?" She then described her appearance and body in detail, which matched what I had seen in the dream. Was it her or not? Why, after twenty years, had she suddenly entered my dream without any reason? I asked about her burial place and planned to erect a stone tablet in the future. They all said that the mounds were now flat, having been buried for a long time in overgrown weeds, and could not be recognized. I will record this here to console her in the underworld. I remember in September of the year Xinmao during the Qianlong era, I composed a poem about the autumn begonia: Wilted and delicate, the flowers are extremely pitiful, In the courtyard under the setting sun, late autumn. The poet, old and diminished in romantic feelings, Still faces the fading red with a sigh of regret. It seems to reflect this person indeed.

Mr. Jingting, a member of the imperial family and the fifth-generation descendant of Prince Ying, compiled the *Four Pine Hall Collection* in five volumes. Among these works is the *Record of the Simple Magpie Pavilion*, which states: "Magpies nest while crows live in them, implying that magpies are clever while crows are simple. The magpies in the small garden have companions in the tens and hundreds, and they only nest in the forest. Observing their behavior, it is not because they dislike living in nests, nor because they fear being displaced by crows. It is because of their simplicity; they consider crows even more so, and are likely not good at building nests. Therefore, in rain, snow, frost, and sleet, their feathers rattle, but at dawn, they gather again to chirp in the treetops, their voices pleasant, as if they do not find it difficult to live in the open. They do not fly very high or travel far, only drinking and feeding within the garden, sometimes entering the master's hall. When the master discards leftover food, they come and place their beaks. When the master's guests arrive, they do not fly away, as if viewing both guests and the master as free from cunning. In the first winter of Xinchou, a pavilion was built to the north of the hall, surrounded by frozen forests, and the magpies circled and nested there, hence it was named the Simple Magpie Pavilion. Crows are simple as they should be, but why are magpies simple? Yet, without simplicity, they would not be the magpies of my garden. This record uses magpies to convey meaning, and the events are close at hand, so they are not fabricated; this is also a strange tale. My brother, Cangchang Shilang Yi Gong, had the collection of my works carved, and I proofread and edited it, thus selecting and recording this to serve as a topic for conversation."

A physician named Yin Zan'an, who specialized in treating skin diseases, returned from a patient's home in Shen Zhou. The host sent an attendant named Yang to accompany him. Yang was inherently violent and fierce, and everyone called him the "horizontal tiger." He sought trouble along the way and there was no day he did not quarrel with someone. One day, at dusk, he arrived at a village where all the inns were full, so he went to a temple. The monk said, "There is only a vacant room behind the Buddha hall, but there is something that haunts it, so I dare not deceive you." Yang angrily said, "What creature dares to haunt Yang the horizontal tiger? I am just looking for it!" He urged the monk to sweep the bed, and they slept together. Zan'an was timid, so he slept close to the wall, while Yang lay outside, waiting with a lit candle. After the hour of man, a sound like "wo-wo" came from outside. It was a beautiful woman, who gradually approached the bed. Yang suddenly rose to embrace her, kissing her and playing with her. The woman suddenly appeared as a hanged ghost, with a terrifying appearance. Zan'an trembled, and his teeth chattered. Yang calmly laughed and said, "Though your appearance is repulsive, your lower body should not be different from a human, and let's have some fun!" He held her back with his left hand and quickly pulled down her pants with his right hand, intending to place her on the bed. The ghost screamed and fled. Yang chased and called after her, but she did not return. He then slept peacefully until dawn, and as he was leaving, he told the monk, "This room is very good, I will come back on a certain day and stay again, do not let other guests stay here!" Zan'an once told Wang You San of Cangzhou, "The world has people who forcibly have sex with hanged ghosts, the name 'horizontal tiger' is definitely not empty of meaning."

The examination hall is for the state to select talents, not for the examiners to take disciples. Later, due to the fixed number of positions in each room, the exam papers were divided among the examiners. The quality of the papers was not fixed, and thus there was a practice of reallocating the papers. During the imperial examination of 1733 (雍正癸丑会试), Mr. Yang, a respected examiner, had seven out of ten papers reallocated to his room. Mr. Yang did not take it to heart and said, "The papers in other rooms are indeed better than mine; I dare not harbor prejudices and make black and white inverted." This I heard from my mentor, Mr. Jie Yayuan, who was the one who reallocated the papers to Mr. Yang's room. During the imperial examination of 1772 (乾隆壬戌会试), Mr. Zhu Xiangqi, a respected examiner, refused to participate in the reallocation, and only seven papers were selected in his room, which the chief examiner also accepted. It is said that Mr. Jingru, a respected examiner, was the top candidate in his room but ended up as the twentieth-ranked candidate, while Mr. Wang Mingxi did not even get selected as the top candidate. Mr. Ren DiaoTai, a respected examiner, had two top candidates in his room. During the imperial examination of 1778 (戊辰会试), Mr. Zhu Shijun, a respected examiner, was the top candidate in Mr. Tang Yaowang's room, but he was actually reallocated from Mr. Jin Yushu's room, making Mr. Jin Yushu also have two top candidates in his room. At the time, there were many different opinions, and the collected exam papers published, which I have all seen personally.

In the year of the Fire Dragon, during the imperial examination, the senior scholar Qian Tuoshi drew peonies with blue ink and gave them to all his colleagues, thus they took turns inscribing and composing verses. At that time, the official Gu Qingsha selected the most number of scrolls for advancement, while Zhu Shi Jun selected the most scrolls for elimination. I inscribed on Gu's painting, saying: "To deeply irrigate with spring water and carefully cultivate the sand, to nurture the most splendid flower of human society, like the bright sun in the third or fourth month, the lingering fragrance carried by the wind to the neighboring houses." The senior scholar Bian Qiu Yan responded with a verse: "A fine rain cleanses the dust and sand, the entire spring beauty belongs to the flowers in the imperial garden, these are the flowers planted by the side of the Sinking Fragrance Pavilion, do not let them be moved to the homes of common people." I also inscribed on Shi Jun's painting, saying: "To beg for a few stems of flowers from the heavenly garden, vividly red and purple, yet unnamed, why ask whose garden they are from, for upon receiving them, they already possess a connection."

Stone Master composed in response: "Spring wind and spring rain leave behind withered stems; How often has a nation been turned upside down by her, yet no one ever asked her name? His heart is like that of the elderly layman Vimalakirti, who remains detached as celestial flowers fall without attaching to anything." Zhang Jinghe, the senior, followed with his own composition: "Ink is ground in blue mud from the inkstone, deep blue writes out the flowers of Luoyang. Why not dye it with rouge? I would ask the painter about the karmic connections. Black as the flower petals, green as the stem; the *Ou谱* knows its ranking. But I wonder why the jade plate holding the dew is cold; the Fragrant Hill Recluse is too emotionally involved." These were all long-time close friends, free and unconstrained, who took each other's teasing and jesting as amusement, having no preconceived notions about each other. Jiang Wenke, at the time, was the president, and upon seeing this, he said: "The gentlemen are unrestrained and elegant, truly a fine tale. Yet, ancient people often developed animosity from playful jokes. It would be better not to have this, to preserve the integrity of friendship." All deeply admired his words, for this shows the wisdom of the experienced. This is recorded to remember the mistakes of youth, and later generations of talented people should not follow this example.

In the imperial examination hall, after the list of successful candidates has been posted, it is rolled up and placed horizontally on the table. The chief examiner and the chief examiner-in-chief, dressed in formal court attire, perform nine bows, and then carry the list out. The court officials call this act "bowing to the list." This is a mistake. From the perspective of official duties, all the names on the list are candidates, so why would the examiners bow to the candidates? From the perspective of personal relationships, all the names on the list are students, so why would the chief examiner bow to his students? Some try to justify this with the text about the seven days of bowing and receiving the census, which is a stretch. On the day the list is posted, the list of successful candidates should be presented immediately. The list cannot be written first; one must open the examination papers, announce one name, fill in the list, and then provide a slip of paper to write down the name. Now the slip of paper is still called a "recording slip." One must bow and send it, just like the ritual of bowing to a memorial. The list is not posted, and the recording slips are not issued. The recording slips are not completed, and the list is not posted. Therefore, the recording slips and the list must be placed together on the table before the bowing. The list is large, and the recording slips are small. Under the bright light, people see the list but not the recording slips. So it is mistakenly believed to be bowing to the list. Later, sometimes the recording is not finished, and dawn is approaching. Or the examiners are in a hurry to return, and they order the bowing to proceed first. So there are times when the recording slips are not placed on the table during the bowing. Over time, this becomes seen as a natural thing. The court officials may bow without the recording slips, so they no longer place them on the table. And because the recording slips are not placed, they can be delayed and sent later. So after the list is written, there are no recording slips to place, and the bowing has quietly shifted to the list. I once asked my former teacher, Mr. Aw Wenqin, and he recounted what Mr. Li Wenzheng said. Mr. Li Wenzheng was the chief examiner of Mr. Aw in the year of Gengchou.

The Hall of the Hanlin Academy does not open its central door, as it is said that doing so would bring misfortune to the president. In the year of Gui-Si, the Siku Quanshu (Complete Library in Four Sections) was opened, and Prince Zhi oversaw the event. After the door was opened, both President Liu Wenzheng Gong and Prince Jueluo Fong Gong died shortly thereafter. Additionally, there is a belief that the soil on the sand embankment in front of the gate forms into balls, and if these balls are accidentally broken, it will harm the Hanlin scholars. In the year of Gui-Wei, rainwater washed away one of these balls, and a child broke it, leading to the death of the elder Wu Yunyan. Furthermore, in the southwest corner of Yuanxin Pavilion, Hanlin scholars with parents should not sit, as doing so would bring misfortune. Lu Ershan, a scholar-official, refused to believe this superstition and later experienced the death of his father, fulfilling the prediction. The left side gate has been closed for a long time, and if it is opened, the person in charge will face punishment. No one dares to test this, leaving its truth unknown. Other departments also have their own taboos. For example, the screen door of the Li Department's corridor used to have no bridge, but now it has a wooden bridge that allows officials to drive their carriages directly through, avoiding the need to go around. Qian Tuoshi, an elder, ignored this practice, and shortly after, there was an incident involving the lamp pole at the Temple of Heaven, reinforcing the belief in the importance of following such practices. Such events often occur, suggesting that there must be some underlying reason, though it is not clear what that reason is.

It is said that in the Baoshanting of the Hanlin Academy, there was a fox girl named Second Miss, though her appearance was never seen. Only when the scholar Zhujinyin was staying in his study room did he dream of a beautiful woman taking him along, leaping over walls as if stepping on clouds and mist. They arrived at the foot of the city wall near the Goryeo Pavilion, where they met an old man who exclaimed in surprise, "This is Scholar Chu, Second Miss, why are you so hasty? Quickly send him back." He then suddenly woke up. Zhujinyin spoke of this himself in the Qingzhen Hall. Cunning and deceitful people sometimes fail. Wealthy and arrogant individuals also sometimes fail. Using cunning and deceit with wealth, and using wealth to aid in deceit, these things cannot be fully investigated. Jingzhou's Li Luyuan said that in the region between Yan and Qi, there was a wealthy household that lost a spouse. The man saw a villager's new wife and found her attractive. Secretly, he sent an old woman to rent a house next door. With all sorts of schemes and lavish bribes, she persuaded the in-laws to expel her as ungrateful, agreeing not to let her son know. Another old woman, who had previously been in contact with the wife's family, was sent with lavish bribes to persuade her parents. They pretended to send her back, and the in-laws also pretended to regret it, keeping her for a meal and then calling her into the room. Soon, they started arguing and still quarreled, sending her back home without letting her know. Thus, the bought and sold divorce and the collusion with the mother-in-law's family left no traces. Later, the two old women pretended to be matchmakers, arranging a marriage with the two families. The wealthy household refused due to fear of being ungrateful, and the wife's family refused due to the difference in wealth. Thus, the plan to take her was also untraceable. After a long time, friends and relatives arranged the marriage again, and they still agreed to the match.

His wife, though poor, was of noble lineage. Pressured by his parents, he abandoned her without cause, which left him deeply depressed and ill. Yet, he still hoped for a reunion, like two broken mirrors mended. Upon hearing that she was to be remarried, he became so enraged and despondent that he died, and his spirit haunted the wealthy household. On the wedding night, he appeared in the lamplight, causing chaos and preventing them from sharing the same bed, such disturbances lasting for several nights. When the wedding was rescheduled for daytime, the wife lamented, "How can I be with my former husband and my new husband in such a manner?" She also questioned, "How can a newlywed wife of three days be confined indoors during the day in such a way?" She wept bitterly and refused to comply, and there was nothing that could be done. They then invited a magician to exorcise the spirit. The magician ascended the altar, burned talismans, and shouted commands, as if seeing something, and quickly left, saying, "I can drive away evil spirits, but I cannot drive away a wronged spirit." Inviting a monk to perform a confession also proved ineffective. Suddenly remembering that the man had always been filial, they decided to divorce her without hindrance. They bribed the husband's in-laws to persuade him to send away his son. Despite their pain, the in-laws were enticed by the money and came to scold the wife. The spirit cried, "My parents have expelled me; there is no reason for me to stay. I will sue in the afterlife." From then on, the disturbances ceased. Not half a year later, the wealthy man died, perhaps because he had won his case in the afterlife. This action, even if it had been undertaken by Deng Sizheng or Bao Zheng, who were known for their investigative skills, would not have been able to resolve it. He relied on his wealth, believing it could even drive away ghosts, and his schemes were indeed clever, but in the end, he could not escape the judgment of the underworld. It is said that the expenses amounted to no less than several thousand gold pieces, bringing little joy, and instead leading to his demise. Though one might call it the height of foolishness, where is the cleverness in all this?

In the capital, there is a temple dedicated to Zhang Xianggong, but its origins are unknown, and it is unclear who Zhang Xianggong was. Some locals believe it to be a temple for a river god. However, a river god should be located between the Gu water and Yin county, not in the capital. Additionally, there is another temple of Zhang Xianggong in Miyun, which is a mountainous area, not a river region. Is it not further from the river? Such gossip from the back alleys is far from being credible. I believe that the Tang dynasty figures Zhang Shoulu and Zhang Zhongwu both once governed the Pinglu region. Examining Gao Shi's preface to "Yan Ge Xing," this poem was actually written for Zhang Shoulu. One line says, "Half of the soldiers die on the battlefield, while the beautiful women still dance and sing under the tent." Another line says, "You don't see the hardships of the border wars; even now, people still remember General Li." There are subtle criticisms of Zhang Shoulu. Zhang Zhongwu, on the other hand, defeated the Xikou and had the merit of defense and protection. His proclamation is still recorded in "Wen Yuan Ying Hua." Reasoning from logic, perhaps scholars built a temple to worship Zhang Zhongwu, though it is uncertain. There are no books in my traveling bag to check, so I will examine this further after returning with the imperial entourage.

*Notes on the Title:* "Yuewei Grass Hall Notes, Volume 21: Continuation of the滦yang Records, Part 3" (“阅微草堂笔记卷二十一滦阳续录三”) The doctrine of reincarnation is clearly and certainly true. The uncle of Heng Lantai, when only a few years old, claimed that his previous life had been a monk at Wanshou Temple in the western part of the city. He had never been there before, yet he took up a brush and roughly sketched the temple’s hallways, gates, and pathways, its solemn arrangements, and the rows of trees and flowers. When they went to verify it, everything matched exactly. Yet throughout his life, he never wished to go to this temple, though the reason remains unknown. This is indeed a case of reincarnation. What Zhu Xi said—that although reincarnation exists, it is when the vital energy has not yet been exhausted and accidentally coincides with another living being—is also a real phenomenon. The son of my tenant farmer, Shang Long, from Cui Zhuang, had just died and was immediately born into the neighboring family. Before the month had passed, he could already speak. On New Year’s Day, his parents went out briefly, leaving the child alone in his swaddling clothes. A villager knocked on the door, saying he had come to greet the New Year. The child recognized his voice and immediately replied, “Is that Uncle So-and-so?” The parents came out, and since the door was not locked, the visitor asked, “May I come in and rest for a moment?” Those who heard this were startled and laughed, but the child soon died young. What Zhu Xi said must be referring to cases like this. The principles of the world are infinite, and the events of the world are also infinite. One cannot base an argument solely on what one has seen and hold fast to a single perspective.

Li Qiuyan from Dezhou recounted that he once traveled with several friends to take the autumn imperial examination in Jinan and stayed at an inn. The inn was rather dilapidated and shabby, but there was a nearby courtyard with two rooms that were somewhat clean, which were locked. He wondered why the owner did not offer these rooms to guests, thinking perhaps they were waiting for rich and noble guests to stay. The owner explained that the room had a ghost or spirit, it was unknown whether it was a fox spirit or a ghost, and it had been empty for a long time, so it was somewhat clean, not because he was choosing guests. One friend insisted on opening it, spread out the bedding, and slept alone. Just before sleeping, he loudly said, "Are you a male ghost? I will wrestle with you. Are you a female ghost? Come to my bed. Don't hide and come out." He closed the door and extinguished the candle, and there was no other unusual occurrence. After midnight, he heard a small voice from outside the window saying, "The one who will come to my bed has arrived." Just as he was about to get up to look, suddenly a large object pressed on his body, heavy as a millstone, almost unbearable. He felt long hair and heard breathing like a bull's roar. This friend is usually very strong, so he then grabbed and fought. This thing was also very strong, dragging and pulling him up and down, rolling around the room several times. The other friends heard the noise and came to see, but the door was closed and they could not enter, they could only hear the loud noises.

About two or three quarters of an hour later, the demon was struck in a vulnerable spot and fled suddenly. The friend opened the door and came out, seeing a group of people gathered around, gesturing wildly and describing the recent events, feeling very satisfied. Just as the third drum stroke was sounding, everyone returned to bed. The friend was half-asleep when he heard a small voice from outside the window say, "The pillow-recommender has truly come. I wanted to approach you a moment ago, but my elder brother was eager to try his strength first, thus I was abrupt. Now he is ashamed and discouraged and dares not come out. I respectfully come to fulfill the promise." After finishing speaking, she had already come to the bed, reached out to touch her face, with fingers delicate as spring bamboo shoots and skin smooth and lustrous as jade fat, the scent of powder pervading the heart. Knowing her intentions were not good, he loved her softness and charm, and decided to sleep together to see what would happen. He then led her into the quilt, and they were extremely intimate and affectionate. When reaching the peak of pleasure, he suddenly felt the woman's abdomen inhale sharply, and immediately the mind and spirit became dazed, all the pulses boiled up, and he was dazed and unconscious. By morning, the door was not opened, and calling did not elicit a response. Urgently, the owner broke the window to enter, poured water on him and sprayed it, and he woke up, already weak and sickly. He was sent back home, treated with medicine for half a year, and could only walk with a cane. From then on, all his heroic spirit was gone, and he no longer had any lofty aspirations. Though he could defeat strong and violent opponents, he could not resist the seductive. Ouyang Xiu said, "Disasters often arise from trivial matters, and wisdom and courage are often trapped by indulgence." Isn't that so?

My family's water tower, the Shuiming Tower, and my maternal grandfather's Zhang family's Du Fan Tower, both overlook the Wei River. One day, the Master Zhengyi arrived by boat and moored it below the Du Fan Tower. My grandmother and my mother, who were cousins, happened to be visiting home at the same time. They heard that the Master could command spirits and, together, they climbed the tower to look out through the window. They saw three people kneeling on the shore, as if they were making a complaint. Soon after, they saw the Master seemingly holding a brush and making a judgment. They surmised that this must be an affair involving evil spirits and sent a servant to investigate. The servant returned and reported: "The opposite bank is within the borders of Qing County. In Qing County, there are three village women who, while picking wheat, all collapsed in the fields. They were thought to be suffering from heatstroke and were carried back home. However, they have been speaking incoherently ever since, neither dying nor recovering. They are known to be possessed by evil spirits. They heard that the Celestial Master had arrived and came to make their complaint." The Master did not know what kind of spirit it was and wrote a talisman, stamped it, and instructed the servant to take it back and burn it at the place where the women had collapsed, saying that he would summon divine generals to investigate. A few days later, it was widely reported that the three women had been kidnapped by ghosts, but the Celestial Master managed to exorcise the spirits and restore them to life. After some time, the details were revealed: the souls of the three women had been captured by many ghosts and taken to an empty forest, where they were about to be subjected to indecent acts. One of the women, who was paralyzed, was the first to be defiled.

A woman initially resisted, but the ghost teased her, saying, "On a certain day and in a certain place, you secretly met someone in a sorghum thicket. We surrounded and laughed at you, but you didn't know. Are you suddenly pretending to be a chaste woman?" The woman was suddenly caught and had no way to defend herself, so she was also defiled. More than ten ghosts then abused her in turn, leaving her in a mess, exhausted, and almost unable to bear it. Next, they dragged another woman, who angrily cursed and said, "I never did anything disgraceful. I was forced by you all. How dare you evil ghosts do this?" She raised her hand and slapped the ghost's cheek, causing the ghost to run and fall several steps away. The other ghosts were also startled and looked at each other, saying, "This woman has a righteous spirit that cannot be approached. We mistakenly took her." Then, they carried the two women into the deep forest and abandoned this woman on the field embankment, speaking from afar, "Do not blame each other. In a while, the aunt will send you back." She hesitated and looked for a way when suddenly a god holding a halberd descended from the sky, directly entering the forest. Immediately, cries of begging for mercy were heard, and in a moment, silence. The god carried the two women out and said, "All the ghosts are already killed. You all follow me back." She felt dazed like a dream, already revived. When she went to ask the two women, they were all moaning and could not stand up. One of them originally leaned against the market, just sighing, while the other thought this woman must reveal her words, moving home and leaving several days later. I once wondered how a woman so fierce could be captured by ghosts. My elder brother Qinghu said, "This woman was originally an ordinary woman. Without encountering hardship, there was no way to see her fierceness. When she saw the two women being humiliated, her sense of justice was stirred, and her fierce spirit suddenly emerged. Her righteous and upright spirit made the ghosts have to avoid her. Therefore, they initially made a mistake and finally dared not to touch her. What is there to doubt?"

Liu Shutai said that in his village there were people practicing Daoist exercises to seek immortality. They sat and cultivated qi, which caused their hands and feet to become stiff and contracted. However, they continued to practice without stopping. There were those who heard about it and were pleased by it, treating them as teachers and following them daily to learn the methods. After a long time, these followers also experienced stiff and contracted hands and feet. Their wives and children were concerned about their idleness and the resulting depression, so they each made a chair and carried them into a room where they could sit and discuss the secrets of alchemy. The two would sit close together and talk, regardless of cold or heat. They always believed that the mysteries of immortality were profound, known only to them and no one else. When people secretly laughed at them, the two sighed and said, "The short-lived mushroom does not know the phases of the moon, and the cicada does not know the seasons. Indeed, this is true." Immortality is not judged by physical form, they said, and they never regretted it. They still instructed their descendants to secretly preserve their books, waiting for someone with the right karmic connection five hundred years later. Some said, "These are people of virtue, pretending to be disabled to hide themselves." I have read some miscellaneous books, but I have never read the alchemical texts. Is this true or not? It is not for outsiders to know. An Gong Jieran said that in Shuzhou there was a poor man who sold his wife. The payment had been received, but his wife escaped. The seller was about to sue, and the man said, "Selling and buying a wife, both are equally guilty. The money will go to the government, what benefit do you gain? Now if you substitute your sister, you lose a second wife and gain an unmarried girl. What is there to not be beneficial?" The seller agreed. Some said the wife escaped to preserve her chastity, others said he wanted to sell his sister but feared public opinion, so he did it out of necessity. Later, his wife returned and escaped again, and everyone said it was fate.

Cheng Yumen, the scholar-official, said: There was a scholar who became intimate with a fox girl. When they first met, she did not hide her feelings and said: "I am not here to harm you through alchemy or sexual practices, nor do I wish to claim a predestined fate. I am simply attracted to your beauty and grace and cannot hold myself back. However, once I saw you, I became deeply attached and could not leave. Perhaps it is a predestined fate?" She did not come frequently, saying: "I am afraid that you might become ill due to excessive indulgence in pleasure." When she saw him reading or writing, she would leave, saying: "I am afraid that it might interfere with your scholarly pursuits." In this way, their relationship lasted for nearly ten years, and their bond was like that of husband and wife. The scholar had been childless for a long time and once jokingly asked: "Can you give me a child?" She replied: "It is uncertain. The embryo is formed when two essences collide and come together. During the act of union, if the male essence arrives but the female essence does not, or if the female essence arrives but the male essence does not, conception cannot occur. If both essences arrive, but there is a difference in timing, the essence that arrives first dissipates and is not captured, and conception cannot occur. If neither arrives first nor last, and both essences arrive simultaneously, if the male essence arrives first and is enveloped by the female essence, a male child is formed; if the female essence arrives first and is enveloped by the male essence, a female child is formed. This is the natural principle of transformation, not something that can be controlled by human effort.
