Therefore, there are cases where conception occurs immediately, and there are cases where conception does not occur even after many attempts, hence it is uncertain."

What about twins? It is said that two strong energies meet and collide. If the collision is direct, it splits into two. If the collision is indirect, one side has more yang (male) energy and less yin (female) energy, and yang envelops yin. The other side has more yin energy and less yang energy, and yin envelops yang. Therefore, there are often two boys and two girls, but sometimes there is one boy and one girl. Why does sperm have to be released in a state of joy and ease? Young brides on their wedding night may be too shy or nervous, yet there are cases where they conceive on their first night. How does the female sperm reach? It is said that during the intimate moment of marriage, both hearts are delighted. It may initially be difficult, then become easier, or physically tired but mentally at ease. When emotions are harmonized, so is the release of sperm. Thus, it is also an occasional occurrence. Why must conception occur after the menstrual blood has fallen? It is said that sperm is like a grain seed, and blood is like fertile soil. Old blood loses its vitality, while new blood generates life. Only in the presence of new life-giving energy can the embryo be nourished. I once served a fairy goddess and heard secretly about the origins of life and transformation, so I have a rough understanding of it. What ordinary people know, sages may not know. This is what it means. Later, a scholar's beard grows suddenly after age thirty. The fox sighed and said, "These sudden growths are like needles. How can people bear them? Seeing them causes fear. Is it the end of one's destiny?" At first, it was thought to be a joke, but later, it never happened again. Fish Gate has a lot of facial hair. Ren Zitian told this story to playfully tease Fish Gate. Fish Gate had heard this story before and also laughed. Then he said, "This fox indeed has a great deal of eloquence. You did not explain it fully, so I will fully describe the theory as follows." Because it has a certain level of reason, I recorded it in memory.

The *Lüshi Chunqiu* recounts the tale of the ghost of Liqiu Village, who was adept at transforming into human form, and indeed such phenomena exist. While I was in Ürümqi, the military official Ba Haba told me: In Gansu, there was a wealthy man named Du翁, who lived in a remote wilderness where there were many fox and badger dens nearby. Disliking the continuous howling of the animals all night, he drove them away with smoke. Soon after, his family saw an old man sitting in the inner room, and another old man sitting outside the hall. Everywhere they went, there seemed to be another old man moving around, no fewer than ten or more, all looking, sounding, and dressed identically. They managed the household affairs with the same demeanor, causing great disturbance throughout the household. The wives and concubines all locked themselves indoors, and one concubine noted that the old man had a white bag on his waist, which could be used to distinguish him. However, upon inspection, there was none, as it had likely been stolen first. Someone advised them: At night, if he insists on entering the bedroom and is not admitted, he will return, and that is the husband. If he insists on entering no matter what, then it is the ghost. They all sighed and lamented, angrily scolding and shouting for an entire day and night, but could do nothing about it.

A certain courtesan, whom the old man favored, would stay at her house three or four times every ten days. Upon hearing of this, she went to his door and said, "The demon has allies. Whatever can be communicated verbally must be known first, and whatever can be verified by objects must be transformed. Why not bring him to my house? I am a former entertainer, so I have no regrets. Have a strong man stand by the bed with a large axe. I will undress and lie on the bed. In sequence, we will engage in intimate activities. During the process, we will turn, stretch, move quickly or slowly, advance or retreat, and caress and cuddle. Things that cannot be communicated by words or seen by the eyes, even the slightest differences, I will understand intuitively. Even the old man himself does not know, but the demon definitely cannot know. When I call out 'chop,' chop immediately. The demon will surely be defeated." Everyone followed her suggestion. One old man just opened the quilt and entered. The courtesan called out "chop," and the axe fell, revealing a fox, which died with its brain split open. Another old man stumbled slightly, and the courtesan called out "chop," and it was startled and fled. When it came to the third old man, the courtesan hugged him and said happily, "The real old man is here; I can kill the rest." With knives and sticks, they killed most of them, who were all foxes and badgers. Those who fled never returned. Wild animals calling at night, what does it have to do with human affairs? This old man must have cleaned out their den? The disturbance was self-inflicted. Since foxes and badgers can transform, why not come and plead with the old man to avoid being driven away? They quickly indulged in their demonic tricks, and their death was self-inflicted. Considering their intelligence, it seems they were all inferior to this courtesan.

Wu Qing Xu, a respected elder, recounted that in a house on a cross street, it was said there was a spirit, causing those who lived there to be often uneasy. The owner of the house was troubled by this and invited monks to perform Buddhist rituals. At night, when the monks were performing the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts, suddenly two female ghosts appeared under the lamp and bowed to the monks, saying, "You all drink alcohol and eat meat, and reciting scriptures and repenting are of little benefit. Even the ritual of feeding the hungry ghosts is just a waste of rice and grains without the power of the Dharma to transform them. The ghosts cannot benefit from it. Please tell the owner to invite someone of high moral character to perform the rituals, then they will be fortunate to be reborn." The monks were terrified and ashamed, unconsciously stumbled and fell from their seats, and did not complete the ritual, extinguishing the candles and leaving. Later, the respected Master Cheng Wen Gong lived there and invited different monks to recite sutras and chant, and the sounds then ceased. After Master Cheng Wen Gong passed away, the house now belongs to Li Nai Shi, an official from Cangzhou.

My cousin An Yi says that in a certain county, there was a man who had a close relationship with a fox spirit. He often used the resources from his wife's nighttime meetings to buy jewelry and cosmetics for the fox spirit. The fox spirit frequently visited their home, but only this man could see her; others could not. One day, the wife scolded her husband, saying, "Where did you get this money, and why are you spending it like this?" The fox spirit suddenly replied from the darkness, "Where did your money come from, and why do you blame me alone?" Those who heard this all collapsed in laughter. I think this is An Yi's allegory, but it also shows that only those without flaws can criticize others. There was a man who was a competitor to Shang Yang, but he did not wish to reveal his name, clan, or place of origin. He was an old scholar who brought his family to live in the capital. By nature, he was harsh and stingy. For every good person or good deed, he would always find some flaw or defect. This is why he earned this reputation. When Qian Duntang, the editor, passed away, his disciples arranged for his funeral and provided for his family, ensuring that everything was done properly. The man who was a competitor to Shang Yang said, "There is no one in the world as good as these disciples. They are trying to gain a reputation for ancient virtue, so that influential people will notice them and it will be easier for them to seek advancement."

A poor woman died on the road, kneeling to beg for money to buy a coffin. Her appearance was emaciated and withered, and her voice filled with sorrow. People competed to throw money at her. Someone, like Shang Yang, said, "This is pointing to a corpse to gather money. The corpse may not even be her mother. Others can be deceived, but I cannot be deceived." Passing by a monument honoring a chaste widow, he looked up and smiled slightly, saying, "That family is wealthy, with servants as numerous as clouds. How could they lack the likes of Qin Gong and Feng Zidu? This matter needs to be verified; I dare not hastily say it is false, nor dare I hastily say it is true." His lifelong opinions were all like this. People all feared and avoided him, no one dared to invite him to teach, and he eventually died in poverty and hardship. After his death, his wife and children wandered, their fate beyond description. Someone met a courtesan at a banquet, whose behavior still had the manner of a scholar, surprising that she was unlike a common courtesan. When asked, she was his daughter, and it is also pitiful. The late Yao An Gong said, "This old man's life had no major faults, but he insisted on being one step ahead of others, so he didn't realize it had reached this point. One should be warned."

In the ninth month of the Qianlong era, the year of Rénwǔ (1782), my disciple Wu Huishu invited a fengshui diviner. The spirit descended in my Green Intention Pavilion and wrote the following poem on the altar: "By the side of the Chénxiāng Pavilion, under the bright sun, he once wrote a hundred poems after drinking a dou of wine; eighteen-year-old beauties personally held the inkstone, and even now, his body carries the fragrance of the imperial incense burner. In the autumn wind, leaves fill the city of Jìmen, and he felt nostalgic for old travels five hundred years ago. He accidentally met the fairy of Penglai and went together to a wine shop." I asked, "Then is it the recluse of the Lotus?" The response was, "Yes." Suddenly, Zhao Chunjian asked, "The immortal wrote a hundred poems after drinking a dou of wine, but it doesn't seem to have happened at the Chénxiāng Pavilion; Yang Guifei fell at Ma嵬, aged thirty-eight, so she was not sixteen at the time; the immortal's lifetime travels did not reach Yúyáng, so why suddenly feel nostalgic for old travels? From the Tianbao era to now, it is not even five hundred years, so why did the immortal make a mistake?" The fengshui diviner only responded with the four words, "I am drunk and want to sleep," and when asked again, it did not move.

Generally speaking,乩仙 are often entrusted to spirits or ghosts, but they still have a real basis. Those who perform扶乩 are similar to people who can roughly understand poetry and have practiced the technique, so they must work together with another person to form characters; if one person changes, they cannot write. The poems they produce are all superficial and can be used anywhere, indicating that they are definitely not genuine communications from ancient people. One day, I was suddenly caught by Chunjian, and the state of distress was evident. Later, I happened to discuss this with Dai Dongyuan, who was surprised and said: "I once saw another person performing扶乩, and when Liu Zongyuan descended to the altar, it was also these two poems, but with slight changes—'满城' became '满林,' and '蓟门' became '大江.'" Knowing this, it is clear that wandering scholars have their own such draft manuscripts, which are passed down from one to another, and it is not worth deeply questioning them. Song Mengquan also said: "There was a person performing扶乩 who came to Dezhou, and the poem was completed instantly. Later, when checked, it was found to be sentences from a collection of village poetry and literary works."

During the reign of Tian Zhen, who was cultivating fields, he was leading troops and stationed at Balikun (also known as Bariqun, where the character "坤" is pronounced with a sound similar to blowing one's lips, which is a combination of sounds related to "库尔"). One day, soldiers digging a well found a mirror, finely crafted with inscriptions that were neither regular script (隶) nor clerical script (八分), but resembled those on the Jinglong bell. However, the inscriptions were mostly worn away due to soil erosion. Tian Zhen treasured the mirror greatly and often kept it with him. He died while serving in military camp in Guangxi. At that time, the mirror was given to the author's sister-in-law's husband, Tian Xianggu, and passed down to Tian Xianggu's grandson. The mirror suddenly disappeared, but later, a relative named Ge found it in the market and returned it to the Tian family. Last year, someone wanted to make a mirror screen and sent it to the capital city to ask the author to authenticate it. The author entrusted the authentication to Mr. Shupei, who examined the inscriptions and concluded that the mirror was from the Tang dynasty. The author then engraved the translation of the inscriptions on the base of the screen and wrote three poems on the back of the screen: Once it followed a caravan out of the Jade Gate, The inscriptions from the middle Tang period are still partially visible. After examining it several times, they are clearly visible. There might be traces of the hands of the former owner. Like a yellow crane, it has no way to return to its homeland, It remains in the desert battlefield. No one knows that after a thousand years of soil erosion, It now reflects the frost on a general's temples. Temporarily lost but returned to its original owner, Like a precious sword, it reunited with its owner. It would be better to clean off the pearl powder, Store it in a box to preserve its value. Tian Xianggu's grandson had his own inscriptions on the mirror, which were also engraved on the back of the screen, providing a detailed account of the mirror's history. "Night Lamp Following Records" mentions that during the Western campaign of General Yue Zhongqi, who was known as the "Wenxin Gong," one of his subordinates found an ancient mirror. General Yue could not find the mirror, and the subordinate suffered misfortune. This event occurred at the same time and place as the events involving Tian Zhen, and it is likely that this mirror was misidentified in the story.

A student named Qiu Renlong recounted that a government official, while traveling by boat and moored at a sandy riverbank, was visited at midnight by several thieves who entered with torches and exposed their weapons. Everyone was terrified and submitted. One thief pulled the official’s wife up and, kneeling on one knee, said, "I wish to ask for one thing from Madam. Madam, please do not be alarmed." He then immediately cut off one of her left ears and applied some medicine powder, saying, "Do not wash it for several days; it will naturally form a scab and heal." The thieves then left, shouting and laughing. She was so terrified that she almost lost her soul, but her wound did not bleed much, nor did it hurt terribly, and it soon healed. Was this an act of revenge? They did not kill or rape. Was this an act of theft? They did not take anything. Since they did not steal, kill, or rape, why did they cut off her ear? Having cut off her ear, they also gave her good medicine. Was this specifically to take her ear? What was the purpose of taking this ear? After much thought and consideration, I still cannot understand the reason. Indeed, there are truly events beyond reason in the world. Qiu said, "If I could catch these thieves, I would certainly understand their motives. Their motives must also be within reason, but they are certainly not the kind of reason I can understand." Therefore, when discussing matters of the world, can we rely on reason to determine their existence or non-existence? Heng Lantai said, "Perhaps these were members of a group that captures and mutilates people to make medicine. This seems to be the closest explanation."

Mr. Dong Tian Shi, a distinguished scholar of the late Ming dynasty, earned his living through painting and never took anything unjustly. He was an old friend of my great-great-grandfather, known as Houzai Gong, who often exchanged poems with him. The remaining works collected in the "Flower King Pavilion" anthology still allow us to glimpse his character. Some elders claimed that he had a fox spirit as a concubine, while others said he was reclusive and that he certainly did not. My great-uncle, Zan Yuan Gong, said: "He did have one, but there is another story." I heard from Mr. Dong Kongru: "Mr. Dong Tian Shi lived in an old house with two rooms, never married, and had no servants or maids. He personally handled all household chores. One morning, he saw that his clothes and shoes, which should have been worn, were neatly arranged and placed under his hand. Upon looking again, he found that the washing and rinsing tools were already set up. Mr. Dong Tian Shi thought: 'There must be something unusual. Is this demon trying to flatter me?' A small voice replied from outside the window: 'We do not dare to flatter you; we have a request of you. It is difficult for us to offer ourselves, so we prepared this to await your inquiry.' Mr. Dong Tian Shi, who had always been brave, ordered her to come in. She immediately knelt and bowed, revealing herself to be a beautiful, quiet, and virtuous woman. He asked her name, and she said it was Wen Yu. He then asked what she wanted, and she replied: 'Foxes fear five things: fierce and violent people, to avoid their fierce energy; magicians or sorcerers, to avoid their accusations and punishments; gods and spirits, to avoid their scrutiny; people who are fortunate, to avoid their good fortune; and people who are virtuous, to avoid their righteous energy.'

Yet violent and cruel people are not always present, and in the end, they bring about their own downfall. Sorcerers and spirits, I do not wrong them, and none can harm me. Those with good fortune who are in decline, I treat with indifference. Only those with virtue fear and respect me. If one can associate with a virtuous person, then one's family and clan regard it as an honor. Her character is clearly superior to that of her peers. Though he is poor and of low station, he does not take what is not right, nor does he act without propriety. If he were to allow me to approach him, then I would serve him as a concubine, tending to his needs—this would be a blessing in three lifetimes. If he does not accept me, then I will request a favor and inscribe a fine fan with a false name, writing: "On such and such a date, this fan was made for the concubine Wen Yu." In that case, I would still share in his lesser light. She immediately took out a fine fan and placed it on the table, moistened the ink and adjusted the colors, then stood respectfully waiting. Master Tian smiled and followed her. The woman took Master Tian's small seal and stamped it on the fan, saying, "This is the work of a concubine; I dare not trouble you with it." She bowed twice and departed. The next morning, as he awoke, he felt something beneath his feet, and upon looking, it was Wen Yu. He laughed and said, "Indeed, I dare not let my lowly body defile you, but unless we share a night together and I personally attend to the duties of a concubine, the title of 'concubine' would remain a mere pretense." She then held his clothes and shoes, waiting on him as he washed and dressed, and after bowing twice, said, "I shall now depart." In an instant, she vanished and never returned. Could it be that in the late Ming period, the value of scholars was highest, and even this fox-girl was influenced by the prevailing customs? Yet her character was open and bright, possessing the refined elegance of Lady Wang of the forest, which is why Master Tian did not reject her.

Grandfather Yao'an said: After the young men and women finish their studies, they should also be taught a rough understanding of family matters and worldly affairs, so that they can later manage the household and navigate the world. In the later years of the Ming dynasty, the respect for Daoist learning increased, and the importance of the imperial examination system grew. As a result, the cunning ones sat and talked about heart-mind learning to gain prestige and influence, while the simple ones stuck rigidly to their textbooks to seek official positions. Among those who read books, only one or two out of ten could understand things. During the reign of Emperor Chongzhen in the year 1632, Uncle Houzai brought the family to Hejian to avoid the local bandits from Mengcun village. After Uncle Houzai's death, when they heard that large forces were approaching Hejian, they planned to return to their hometown. As they were about to leave, a neighborly old man looked at the door god and sighed, saying, "If there were someone today like Wei Chi Jinde or Qin Qiong, it wouldn't have come to this."

Your two great-uncles, one named Jingxing and the other named Jingchen, were both famous scholars. They were just tying up their bundles of clothes and bedding when they heard something and began to debate, saying, "These are the images of Shentu and Yulu, not of Wei Chi Jingde and Qin Qiong." The old man did not accept their argument and checked the *Xiyu Ji* by Qiu Chuji as evidence. The two great-uncles considered the popular, less reputable literature insufficient as proof and went inside to fetch the *Shen Yi Jing* by Dongfang Shuo to argue against him. By then, it was late in the evening, and after searching for a while, they continued to debate for some time more. The city gate had already closed, so they could not leave. The next day, they were about to depart, but the large army had already surrounded the city. The city was captured, and thus the whole family met with disaster. Only your great-grandfather Guanglu Gong, your great-uncle Zhenfan Gong, and your uncle Yuntai Gong survived. Life and death were in the balance, a hair's breadth apart, yet they still debated the authenticity of ancient texts. Is this not because they only knew how to read books and did not concern themselves with external affairs? This statement by Yao'an Gong, I initially wrote various notes but dared not include them, because it involved your two great-uncles. Now, reconsidering, being a bookworm is not a bad thing; there have been many great scholars like this in ancient times. Therefore, I added this here.

Slave Liu Furing was skilled in making nets, snares, bows, and crossbows, and was proficient in all matters of hunting birds and beasts. When the household was divided, he was assigned to me, but his skills were not utilized, leaving him quite unhappy and discontented. In his eighties, he was still strong and could eat well, but he would occasionally carry a bird gun and walk in the fields. His gun never missed its target. One day, he saw two foxes lying on a ridge. He shot them twice, but missed, and the foxes were not alarmed. He realized they were spiritual beings and returned home with fear, and thereafter, nothing else happened. My maternal grandfather, Zhang Gong, at the Shuiming Tower, had a night watchman named Fan Yu. Fan Yu often heard sounds on the roof tiles and suspected thieves. He got up to look but saw nothing, then stealthily investigated and saw a black shadow pass over the roof. He set up a trap in the roof tiles and lay on his back to listen. In the middle of the night, he heard the trap go off and a woman's cry of pain. He climbed onto the roof to investigate and found a black fox with a broken leg dead. That night, he heard someone cursing from the roof: "Why did you kill my concubine, Fan Yu?" At that time, a neighbor's son was bewitched by a demon. Fan Yu privately thought it must be that fox and cursed back: "You let your concubine run off, not knowing shame, and you blame me. I am doing the neighbor's son a favor by killing you." Then there was silence. But from then on, he felt that every night someone was pouring lime into his eyes, and as soon as he closed his eyes, it happened again. He washed it off but it happened again, gradually causing swelling, pain, and ulcers, eventually leading to blindness. This was the fox's revenge. What he saw is far inferior to what Liu Furing saw. One is an experienced adult, and the other is a young man who enjoys adventures.

A student who became an official in Yunnan, originally from a poor family, went there with difficulty, bringing only his son and a servant. After a long wait in the capital city, he was eventually appointed to a county. Although the area was still fertile in Yunnan, it was far from the provincial capital, and his family lived in a remote village. Letters were not easy to send, and even when they arrived, they were often delayed. As a result, he almost lost contact with his wife and children, learning only of his appointment from the official gazette. Once, a cunning servant committed fraud, and he was beaten and dismissed. This servant harbored deep resentment and knew all the details of the family's affairs. He forged a letter from the servant, claiming that the master and his son had died one after the other, and their two coffins were now temporarily placed in a temple. He should borrow money to bring them back and described the master's last wishes and the management of the family affairs in great detail.

Initially appointed to Yunnan, his relatives and friends, considering his humble and taciturn nature, did not expect him to secure a position, and even if he did, it would likely be a poor one. Later, they heard he was appointed to the county, and gradually, they began to befriend him, with some even taking care of his family. At times, they would give gifts and visit, and his son might borrow money or goods, to which people would readily agree. Some even arranged marriages for their children, and when there were village feasts, his son would always be included. However, upon receiving a false letter, everyone was greatly disappointed. Some came to offer condolences, while others did not, and gradually, some began to demand repayment. On the roads, they met as if they didn't know each other, and all the servants and maids left. Within half a year, the door was as quiet as a trap for sparrows. Later, the official entrusted the imperial inspector to send 1,200 taels of gold, bringing his wife and children home. Then he realized the previous letter was false, and the whole family burst into laughter, as if in a dream. Relatives and friends gradually started to gather again, with some who avoided him and dared not see him. In his letter to his relatives, he wrote: The differences between being rich and poor, and between being poor and rich, have been experienced by many. But to suddenly die after being born, and then come back to life after half a year, with the events in between, those who can personally experience it are probably the first ones.

A student from Fu'an, Chen Fang, said that there was a man from Fujian who, while traveling through the mountains at night, suddenly lost his way. Fearing that he would become more lost and further away, he sat at the foot of a cliff waiting for dawn. Suddenly, he heard someone speaking. At that time, a crescent moon was just rising, and the shapes and colors were barely discernible. It seemed like twenty or thirty people were sitting on the cliff, and another ten or so were appearing and disappearing in the dense vegetation. Looking around, he saw that all around were ruined tombs. Knowing in his heart that they were ghostly beings, he lay down and dared not move. Soon, he heard them talking about the village god and the land deity coming. He secretly looked at them and saw that they were dressed in elegant attire, about thirty years old, quite like scholars, and not dressed in the stereotypical white beard and cloth robe of a theater actor. They first went to the top of the cliff, but he didn't know what they were doing. Then they went to the dense vegetation, and sighed to the ten or so ghosts, saying: "Why have you brought about your own untimely deaths? Causing the other ghosts to not consider you as equals, you are pitiable for hunger and cold. Now there is something to nourish you." So he scooped up some food and scattered it among the grass.

More than ten ghosts vied for something, some laughing while others wept. The local deity sighed deeply and said: "The customs of this region generally have an excessive focus on winning and losing, and the distinctions between gratitude and resentment are too clear. The weak ones, unable to match the strength, thought to harm themselves to burden others. They did not know that cases of suicide had no equivalent punishment under the law, and they only destroyed their own lives. The strong ones arrogantly thought that if each family killed one person, it would be sufficient to offset the harm, and they fought with weapons to vent their anger. They did not know that according to the law, killing two people would be offset separately with the living, not with the dead. The dead only knew it was too late to regret, and the living did not know and did it even more; is it not sad? The more than ten ghosts all cried. Soon, the bell of the distant temple rang, and all became silent at once. This person once told Mr. Chen, and Mr. Chen said: 'What the local deity said is not as good as what the magistrate said.' However, the divine way of teaching might turn around one or two cases, it is also possible."

In the winter of the third year of the Jiaqing era, I, serving as the Minister of War, went out through the Dadingmen gate to supervise archery practice. The camp commander used Shichahai, a former Ming dynasty temple, as our lodging. The halls, buildings, and pathways were completely different from what Liu Tong had described in his *Imperial Capital's Scenery and Objects*. It was no longer the case where monks and Buddha shared a single room as it was in the past. The temple monk lived in a small hut near the temple gate, while I resided in the rear hall of the temple. The rooms were also clean and tidy, but many were sealed. Upon inspection, I found that some rooms were sealed in the 31st year of the Qianlong era, indicating that the temple had been neglected and abandoned for a long time. I lived in the eastern corridor room, where the air was as cold as ice. I lit several stoves, but they did not warm up. Several lamps emitted a dim green light, and I knew it was not a good place. However, since I had already moved in, I stayed for one night and finally slept safely and without harm. The servants lived in the western corridor and were too afraid to sleep. They lit torches and sat under the corridor all night, and they were also fortunate to be without harm. They only heard soft voices from the sealed room, but when listening, the voices were not very clear.

Nine bearers entered a room and fell into a deep sleep. By dawn, one of them had already died. They were instructed to find another place to stay and moved to the True Martial Temple. A Taoist priest in the temple said: "I heard of an old monk from the Ten Temple Sea who once saw two ghosts meeting. One asked, 'Where are you from?' The other replied, 'My time for rebirth has not yet come, so I am just wandering around casually. Where are you from?' The first ghost said, 'I am a hanged soul seeking a replacement.' The other asked, 'How long have you been here?' The first ghost replied, 'For over ten years.' The second asked, 'Why can't you get a replacement?' The first ghost said, 'People see me and run away; there's nothing I can do about it.' The first ghost then said, 'Those who are good at attacking hide their intentions. The dagger is about to come out of the sleeve, but with a calm and pleasant expression, one can ensure success. You scare them with your strange appearance; why wouldn't they run away? Why not use fragrant cosmetics to charm them, hold a quilt and pillow to comfort them, and you will definitely succeed.' The old monk, who is usually strict and upright, scolded them sternly, and they suddenly disappeared into the ground. A few nights later, there was indeed someone who hanged themselves in the temple. This ghost can be called cunning and treacherous. However, the temple that is closed off seems to have many more ghosts, not just these one or two."

Master Wang Xiu Yuan of the Hanlin Academy said: There was an old monk who passed through a butchery and shed tears. Someone was surprised by this and asked him, saying: "Your story is long; I can remember two lifetimes' worth of events. In my first life, I was a butcher, and I died before I reached my thirties. My soul was seized and bound by several people, and I was taken before the underworld officials, who blamed me for the heavy karmic sin of killing. I was dragged to the Wheel of Rebirth to suffer a bad karmic consequence. I felt dazed and confused, as if drunk or in a dream, with only unbearable heat tormenting me. Suddenly, it seemed cool, and I found myself already in the pigpen. After weaning, I saw the food was unclean, and I knew it was filthy, yet the fire of hunger burned fiercely, and all my five internal organs felt as if they were burned to pieces. I had no choice but to eat it. Later, I gradually came to understand pig language, and would sometimes greet my fellow pigs. Many of them could remember their previous lives, but they could not speak to humans. Most of them knew they were to be slaughtered. Those who made moaning sounds were in sorrow; those whose eyelashes often bore traces of wetness were filled with self-pity. Their bodies were dull and heavy; in summer, they suffered greatly from the heat, and the only relief was to submerge themselves in mud water, though this was not often possible. Their fur was bristling and strong; in winter, they suffered greatly from the cold. Seeing dogs and sheep with soft, thick fur, they seemed like divine beasts.

When captured and seized, knowing that escape was impossible, one would temporarily leap and run to avoid, hoping to delay for a moment. After being caught, one would be kicked and stepped on the head and neck, with the hooves and elbows twisted and bent, the four legs bound deeply to the bone, the pain as if being cut by a knife. If carried on boats and carts, one would be stacked and pressed on each other, the ribs as if about to break, all veins blocked, the abdomen as if about to split. If carried on poles, it would be even more painful than the three wooden devices. Arriving at the slaughterhouse, one would be thrown to the ground, the heart and spleen all shaken to the point of breaking, or die on the same day, or be bound for several days, even more difficult to endure. Often seeing the knife and cutting board to the left, the boiling pot to the right, not knowing when it would happen to oneself, what kind of pain it would bring, one would constantly tremble uncontrollably. Also often looking at oneself, thinking about the future, not knowing if it would be torn apart and scattered, become someone else's soup, extremely sad and pitiful.

When being executed, the butcher pulled once, and immediately the condemned person was frightened, panicked, and confused, with all limbs becoming weak. They felt their heart oscillating left and right, as if their soul had flown out from the top of their head and then returned. Seeing the flashing blade of the knife, they could not look directly and only closed their eyes, waiting for the skinning and dismemberment. The butcher first thrust the blade into the throat, shaking and manipulating, causing blood to flow into a bowl. The suffering was beyond words, and they cried out loudly, unable to die. When the blood ran out, the heart was pierced, and they felt great pain, unable to make a sound. Gradually, they became dazed and confused, like being drunk or in a dream, like being reborn for the first time. After a long time, they gradually woke up and looked at themselves, seeing a human form again. The underworld officials believed that in past lives there were still good deeds, and they were still allowed to be reborn as a human, which is the current body. Recently, they saw this pig suffering from torture and thought back to when they themselves suffered from such torture. They also pitied this person holding the knife, who in the future would also suffer from such torture. These three thoughts intertwined, and therefore, tears flowed without knowing why. The butcher heard it and immediately threw the knife on the ground, finally changing profession to become a vegetable seller.

When Xiao Yuan was telling this story, Li Hui Chuan also cited two other cases and said: A butcher died, and in a neighboring village, a pig was born, about four or five li away from the butcher's house. This pig would always come to the butcher's house to lie down, could not be driven away, and even when the owner caught it, it would still come back. It could only be stopped by locking it up. It was suspected to be the afterlife incarnation of the butcher. Another butcher died, and after more than a year, his wife was about to remarry. She was dressed in her colorful wedding attire and boarding the boat when suddenly a pig rushed towards her, with fierce, glaring eyes, tearing her skirt and biting her leg. The crowd rushed to rescue her, and they all pushed the pig into the water. Only then could they start rowing the boat. The pig jumped out of the water and continued to chase along the shore. Fortunately, the wind was favorable, allowing them to raise the sails and leave. The pig then returned home, looking dejected. It was also suspected to be the afterlife incarnation of the butcher, angry at his wife for taking up with another man. This serves as additional evidence for the butcher's reincarnation as a pig. Another case was mentioned where a butcher died shortly after slaughtering a pig. Just as his wife was pregnant, she immediately gave birth to a girl. As soon as she was born, she made a pig-like squeal and squealed for three or four days before dying. This also proves that pigs can be reborn as humans. I believe this is what Zhu Xi referred to as 'life force not yet exhausted' or 'life force coincidentally coming together,' which is a different principle and not to be considered in terms of reincarnation.

When Mr. Wang Shouhe, the compiler, was a scholar, he dreamed that his maternal grandfather, Mr. Shi E, brought a person to his home and pointed to him, saying, "This is my contemporary, Ji Xiaolan, who will be your teacher in the future." He secretly remembered the person's attire and appearance. Later, in the year Jihou, he was selected as a biaoguan and took the imperial examination. During the examination, I was the chief examiner and ranked him highly. After he was appointed to an official position and came to visit me, he recounted the dream in detail. He also noted that the person's attire and appearance were identical to what he had seen in the dream, believing it to be a fulfillment of the dream. By the year Jicheng of Emperor Jiaqing during the imperial examination, I was the chief examiner, and his paper was sent to me to read first. All the papers recommended by the examiners were first sent to a chief examiner by the imperial censor, who then reviewed and passed them on for public evaluation. He was again selected. In the palace examination, he ranked second, and thus I knew that the dream had come true. As for the nature of dreams, their causes are difficult to understand. The *Shi Shuo Xin Yu* records that Wei Jie asked Le Yi about dreams, and Le Yi said they were thoughts or causes.

But he has not thoroughly understood the underlying reasons. In the summer of Wuwu year, accompanying the emperor to Luguo (Pulang), I discussed with my son Moqing using reason to explore. When there is a thought focused on something, focusing the mind creates an image, which is a dream created by consciousness, as in the case of Confucius dreaming of Duke of Zhou. When good or bad fortune is about to come, signs appear first, similar to seeing omens in divination tools and movements in the body, which is a dream influenced by the flow of qi (vital energy), as in the case of Confucius setting up offerings at two pillars. When the mind is confused and disordered, the spirit is dazed, and the heart has no fixed master, various illusions appear, like a sick person seeing ghosts, or a dizzy person seeing flowers, these are divergent manifestations of imagination. Or when good or bad fortune is not yet evident, and ghosts and gods foreknow, through images and subtle words, these are calls to the flow of qi. Though they change in mysterious ways, taking countless forms, the main points do not go beyond these. As for the theory of dream interpretation, it is mentioned in the *Rites of Zhou*, it is close to prayers and rituals, involving shamans, and it is somewhat doubted by those who criticize the *Rites of Zhou*.

In the *Xiao Ya* section of the *Book of Songs*, the text is also found, and it is clearly transmitted by ancient scriptures and records, though it cannot avoid much interpretation and embellishment. Yet, it is indeed a real practice. However, there are those who, despite deep thoughts and concentrated attention, never dream, and the mind sometimes cannot create dreams. Sudden misfortunes and unexpected blessings sometimes come suddenly without being known, and the flow of qi sometimes does not respond. Moreover, the people of the world are as numerous as the sands of the Ganges, so why do spirits and deities show dreams only to this person? The gains and losses in this person's life are not one and the same, so why show dreams only about this matter? If the matter is not to be revealed, why show it at all? Even if it is shown, why hide it in obscure symbols and confusing language? For example, the *Youyang Zazu* records a dream in which someone received a date, and it is said that the character for "date" (枣) resembles two "come" characters (来), and the repeated "come" suggests the image of the soul returning. Indeed, the person died.

The *Zhaoye Qianzai* (Records of the Court and the World) recounts a dream of Cui Ke, who saw himself seated and listening to a lecture while holding a mirror. The dream suggests that his position came from above, and the character "镜" (mirror) is interpreted as "金旁竟" (metal and end), symbolizing the end of something. Novels often describe dreams in such indirect and complex ways—do ghosts and spirits not tire of constantly creating riddles? For matters of great importance, they may be communicated through dreams, but even trivial matters are also conveyed this way. For example, the *Dunhuang Records* describe a dream where the dreamer sees himself sitting in a bucket and being beaten with two sticks. The interpretation is that the person in the bucket represents meat, the two sticks represent chopsticks, and the outcome is a satisfying meal—does this not seem too trivial? Generally, one should understand what can be understood and leave the rest unexplained. In the *Biography of Xie Xiao'e*, the spirits of her father and husband inform her that they were killed by robbers. They should have directly told her the names of the robbers, Shen Chun and Shen Lan, but instead, they cryptically described them as "a man walking in the fields for one day" (Shen Chun) and "a monkey in a cart near the east gate" (Shen Lan), requiring years to decipher. Is this not absurd? Such accounts often arise because the recorders wish to add mystery, and they may not be based on actual events. All dream interpretations in various texts can be viewed through this lens; the method is not the traditional or ancient way.

He Chunzai, a junior official, was the grandson of He Gonghuigong. He recounted that during his tenure as the Deputy Governor of Coastal Defense in Zhejiang, Gonghuigong once saw a Taoist priest kneeling and presenting an object to him while he was in his sedan chair. The experience was neither a dream nor reality, and he woke up feeling disoriented. When he looked around, the Taoist priest had disappeared, but the object remained in his hand. It was a black crystal seal. Upon examining the inscription, he found it engraved with the words "Qing Gong Taibao" (Grand Tutor of the Eastern Palace), which he could not understand. Later, he served as the Governor of Henan and died in office—there was a position of Governor of Hedong, but no Governor of Henan. At the time, Gonghuigong held the title of Governor of Henan with the additional rank of Governor, hence the designation. He was posthumously granted the title of Taibao of the Crown Prince, and only then did he realize that the seal was a divine prophecy. In the world of officialdom, promotions and demotions are frequent, but the posthumous honors bestowed upon one's death are the ultimate conclusion of one's life. The *Dingming Lu* (Record of Destiny) records that Li Jiu showed that he would become a Shizhong (Grand Secretary) but died as a Minister of War, and was posthumously granted the title of Shizhong. It also records that Zhang Shoulu showed that he would become the Governor of Liangzhou but died as the Prefect of Kuozhou, and was posthumously granted the title of Governor of Liangzhou. This shows that the gods inscribe one's fate, and posthumous grants are equivalent to actual appointments. Gonghuigong reached the rank of Governor, and the divine prophecy of his posthumous title was also a manifestation of this principle.

High Crown Yanyan said, there was a fox living in an empty house behind someone's home. It could not be seen, but it could speak face-to-face with people. The family was moderately well-off, and some thought it was helped by the fox. Some believed this story, so the man sought to make friends with the fox, and the fox also treated him kindly. One day, he wanted to set up a feast for the fox. The fox said it was old and greedy, so he prepared many drinks and dishes to welcome it. By dusk, several foxes had fallen drunk and revealed their true forms. It was then known that they had called their friends and relatives. This happened several times, and he became exhausted from providing for them. His clothes and valuables were all pawned. He then subtly hinted at his need for help. The fox laughed and said, "I only lack money to provide drinks and food. That is why I keep coming to you. If I had more money, I would drink and eat to my heart's content. Why would I take anything from you and be your friend?" Notes from the Reading of My Small Hut, Volume Twenty-Two, Continuation of the Records of Lanyang, Chapter Four Liu Xiangwan said, there was an old scholar staying at a relative's house. Soon, the master's son-in-law arrived. He was a good-for-nothing. They did not get along, and both did not want to live in the same room. So the old scholar was moved to another room. The son-in-law looked at him and laughed, but no one understood why. The room was also elegant and clean, and writing utensils and books were all present. The old scholar wrote a letter home by the light of the lamp. Suddenly, a woman stood by the lamp. She was not very beautiful, but her demeanor was quite refined and graceful. The old scholar knew she was a ghost, but he was not afraid. He pointed to the lamp and said, "Since you have come here, you cannot stand idle. You can trim the candle." The woman immediately extinguished the lamp and stood close and faced him. The old scholar was angry and quickly rubbed his hand on the inkstone. He slapped her face and smeared her with ink, saying, "Take this as a mark. Tomorrow, I will find your body, chop it up, and burn it."

The ghost suddenly made a sound and disappeared. The next day, the owner was informed, and the owner said: "There was once a servant who died in this room and often appeared at night to disturb people. Therefore, we only host guests during the day, and no one stays at night. Yesterday, there was no place to accommodate you, and considering your advanced age and great learning, the ghost would not have appeared. I did not expect it to still appear in its true form." The owner then realized why the son-in-law had laughed. This ghost often walked around the courtyard at night. Later, family members sometimes encountered it and would cover their faces and rush away. On another day, the narrator carefully observed it and found that its face was still covered in ink stains. The ghost has form but no substance; it is unknown how it can be stained, but it must still be a material object that has long ago become a spirit or demon, taking the form of a deceased servant. *The Miscellany of the West Regions* records: "During the reign of Guo Yuanzhen, while he was living in the mountains at night, he saw a face as large as a plate, glaring out from the lamplight. Guo Yuanzhen wrote with his brush on its cheek: 'Those who have been stationed for a long time grow old, and horses that have traveled long distances do not become fat.' The creature then vanished. Later, while following a woodcutter on a leisurely walk, he saw a large ear on a tree, about the size of several buckets, with the words he had written inscribed on it. This is also a proof of the matter."

In the Urumqi countryside, farmers often irrigate their fields with water and build houses near the fields, so they cannot live side by side in rows. Often, they construct a few small rooms themselves, with no neighbors around, much like a single-family village described by Du Fu in his poetry. Moreover, they have no corvée labor, and their land is not officially surveyed or taxed. By paying tax on thirty acres, they can farm hundreds of acres. Therefore, such isolated settlements are especially common in deep rocks and remote valleys. Once, a soldier from Yili went into the mountains to hunt and saw a house with tightly closed doors and gates. In the courtyard, there seemed to be over ten horses, all with saddles and bridles prepared. Suspecting that the Makhans had taken over, the soldiers shouted and surrounded the house. The Makhans, seeing the large number of soldiers, abandoned their pots and tents and broke out. The soldiers, fearing their fierce fighting, did not pursue them. Upon entering the house, they found bones scattered and no one there, except for a faint sound of weeping. Searching, they discovered a child about thirteen or fourteen years old, naked and hanging from the window frame. They untied him and asked him about what had happened. The child said that the Makhans had come four days ago. His father and brothers fought but were defeated, and the whole family was bound. Daily, the Makhans would take two people to the mountain stream to wash and drag them back, cutting them into pieces and roasting them to eat. Seven or eight men and women were all killed. Today, when they were about to leave, they washed him and finished, and were about to eat him. One person shook his hand to stop them. Although the translator did not understand the Oirat language, observing their gestures, it seemed they wanted to cut him into several pieces, each to carry on their horses as food. Fortunately, the soldiers arrived and they abandoned him, and now he has been saved.

She wept incessantly, moved by the child's loneliness and hardship. He was brought back to the camp and temporarily assigned to menial tasks. The child then mentioned that his family had buried valuables in a pit. The camp officer led them to dig it up and found a large amount of silver coins and clothing. Upon further questioning the child, it was learned that his father and elder brothers were all bandits. They would rob travelers on the post road near the mountains, attacking one or two carts traveling alone, with no one within ten miles to help. They would suddenly rise and kill the people, immediately load the bodies onto the cart and take them into the deep mountains. When the cart could no longer pass, they would gather and smash the bodies with a large axe, along with the bodies and wrapped bedding, throwing them into the sheer ravine. They would only use horses to carry the goods away. When the horses could no longer pass, they would throw the reins and bridles into the sheer ravine, let the horses go wherever they will, and carry the loot together along the bird's path back. The distance from the place of the robbery was hundreds of miles. After returning, they hid the treasure in a pit for one or two years, then sent someone to pretend to be a merchant, taking a detour to various places like Biezhuan and selling in the market. Thus, for many years, no one suspected that the Mahaqin would destroy their family. The child was young and thus exempt from collective punishment, but later, while herding horses, he fell off a cliff and died, leaving no surviving descendants. This matter was managed by me while I was in the military camp, and because the thief was already dead, it was put aside and not pursued. Reflecting on it now, this thief's tracks were mysterious, and it was not easy to catch suddenly. Then the Mahaqin came, to avenge the cruel murder. The Mahaqin have no limit to their appetite for human flesh, and they left one child, to show the cause of their calamity. There seems to be a divine principle at work here, it is not accidental. The thief's name has long been forgotten, only when the child fell off the cliff, the official report recorded the name as Qiu'er.

The wife of Liu Pochē, a tenant farmer, recounted that one morning, as she rose early and swept the courtyard while cooling off, she saw two people lying naked in a thatched hut behind the house. Startled, she called her husband, and it turned out to be the daughter of a neighbor and her monthly worker. They were both lying stiff and motionless, seemingly dead. Soon after, the neighbor arrived, realizing what had happened but not understanding how it had come to pass. They revived the couple with ginger soup, and they could no longer hide the truth. They confessed that they had long planned the meeting, but due to the cramped space and lack of privacy, they had taken advantage of a crack in the wall after the rain, when the sky was dark and overcast, knowing that Liu Pochē’s thatched hut was empty. Thus, they had met secretly on the grass. When they grew tired and rested, they were still clinging to each other, not yet rising. Suddenly, they heard a broken moon appear, shining brightly as daylight. Looking back into the hut, they saw seven or eight ghosts sitting there, pointing and mocking them. Startled and terrified, they lost their composure and only now awoke. The people around them found this strange. Liu Pochē’s wife said, “My house has never had ghosts. They must have wanted to watch a play and came along with us.” Her older brother from the same clan, Màoyuán, remarked, “Where is there no ghost? To watch a play is only that some people see it and others do not. This is not strange at all.”

Upon recalling the public office at Zouguan Pass in Fujian, commonly known as Shuikou, which was rebuilt by Chancellor Yang during his tenure overseeing Fujian and Zhejiang, I was on an inspection tour when he spoke to me: "When you arrive at the Shuikou public office, you will see something at night. Do not be afraid; it will not cause harm." I once stayed in this place, having already locked the door and gone to sleep. Because of the heat, I moved my bed closer to the window and looked through the gauze curtain at the sky's brightness or cloudiness. Even though it was dark at night, the six lamps hanging from the eaves had not yet burned out. I saw a black shadow in the courtyard, slightly resembling a human form, in front of the steps, either sitting, lying down, walking, or standing, and silent without a sound. At midnight, I looked again, and it was still there. Until the rooster crowed, it gradually receded into the ground. I tried to ask the post official, but none knew about it. I said, "You are an envoy and chancellor; you should have ghosts and spirits as your unseen attendants. How could I have that?" He replied, "Not so. Within the Xianxia Pass, this place is a crucial point of land and water transport, a place that must be contested by those who use military force. In the late Ming dynasty, the Prince of Tang, and in the early Qing dynasty, the Zheng and Geng families, battles and casualties, cannot be counted. These are the spirits of those who perished, taking advantage of the emptiness of the buildings to occupy them. When a high-ranking official arrives, they hide and leave." This also serves as evidence for the idea that there are ghosts everywhere.

The elderly servant Xi once said: "Of all things in the world, ghosts are the most foolish. When ghosts occupy a house, people rarely go there. Occasionally, a guest might stay, but only temporarily. What harm is there in letting them stay for a while? Why must they necessarily disturb them? If they encounter someone with a strong fortune or a robust personality, they often bring about their own downfall, and sometimes even face unforeseen dangers. Even if not, since no one lives there, the house will not be maintained, and over time it will collapse on its own. Where will you go then?" Another elderly servant Liu Wendou said: "This statement is indeed reasonable. But who can convey this to the ghosts to know? Are you not even more foolish than the ghosts?" Upon hearing this, the respected elder Yao An said: "Liu Wendou is exactly the one who does not suffer from foolishness." Xi, whose nickname was Jv'er, was the same age as Yao An. At eight years old, he became a companion to Yao An, and after several years, he could recite the Thousand Character Classic by heart, but when he opened a book, he did not recognize a single character. However, he was naturally loyal and straightforward, treating the master's affairs as his own, even if they were disliked or resented. At that time, the household relied on Xi externally and on Aunt Liao internally, so everything was well-organized. In the year of Gengchen during the Yongzheng era, I was eleven years old. On the first night of the first lunar month, I happened to buy some playthings. Xi informed Lady Zhang, the respected lady, saying: "Master Si today visited the lantern market and bought some miscellaneous items. Money is not something to be sparingly used, but Mr. will open his school tomorrow. Will he be preoccupied with play or with studying?" Lady Zhang nodded in agreement and said: "You are right." She then took the items and locked them in a chest. Although this was a trivial matter, it was indeed speaking what others find difficult to say. Now, I look around and feel a sense of loss and nostalgia.

My elder brother Qinghu's fourth son, Ru Lai, was young and graceful, and I loved him most of all. He was also quite fond of reading. After marrying and having children, he suddenly fell into madness, as if no one were caring for him—he would not comb his hair or wash his face. In summer, he would wear flaxen garments, and in winter, hemp clothing, unaware of his own condition. Yet he was not ill, seeming untouched by the cold or heat. When called to eat, he would eat; when not called, he would not seek food. Sometimes he would take bread from the market on his own and call children to eat with him, not asking for the price, and showing no concern for the leftovers. Occasionally, after one or two days of being missing, he would suddenly return on his own. One day, after a thorough search, no trace of him could be found. Some said they had seen a faint figure in the willow grove outside the village. When they rushed to look, he was already sitting upright and stiff. Whether he died from confusion or bewilderment, we cannot know. Or perhaps he had attained some understanding, using this madness as a means to blend into the world, and when his time was up, he vanished—this too is unknown. I recall when I returned from Fujian to my hometown, I saw him still kneeling and bowing in proper ritual. After bowing, he suddenly said: "Uncle, you have worked very hard." I replied: "It is unavoidable." He then suddenly said: "Uncle, don't you feel the hardship?" He silently withdrew. Later, reflecting on his words, it seemed as if he had some intention behind them, and so to this day, I have never been able to fully understand him.

Yao'an Gong said that when Mr. Sun Qishan from Lujiang was waiting to be selected for an official position, he was poor and lacked funds, so he traveled by hiring a donkey, which is called "short pan" in the north. One day, he reached the south gate of Hejian but could not hire a donkey. Suddenly, heavy rain came, and he took shelter under the eaves of a civilian's house. The owner saw him and angrily said, "You did not contribute money when the house was built, and you did not contribute labor when the ground was prepared. Why are you sitting here without permission?" He pushed Qishan into the rain. At that time, the position in Hejian was still vacant, and Qishan entered the capital. After a few months, he unexpectedly obtained the county. When he took office, the former homeowner recognized him and felt ashamed and regretful, planning to sell the house and move the family. Qishan heard about this and called him over, smiling and saying, "Why should I quarrel with you? Now that it has happened, it will not happen again. This is also a way to cultivate blessings through kindness and integrity." He then told a story: "In my hometown, there was someone who loved growing flowers. One night, he suddenly got up and saw several women standing under the flowers. They were not people he knew, so he knew they were fox spirits. He hastily threw a stone and said, 'How dare you peep at the flowers?' One woman laughed and replied, 'You enjoy the flowers during the day, and I wander at night. What do I have against you? I come here every night, and the flowers are not damaged by a single stem or leaf. What do I have against the flowers? Why are you so petty and stingy? I am not incapable of crushing your flowers, but I fear that people might say what we see is the same as yours, so I do not do it.' They then left gracefully, and there was no more trouble. Even fox spirits do not quarrel with you, and I am not as good as them? After that, the former homeowner could not find peace and moved his family, but no one knows where they went." Qishan sighed and said, "The heart of a small person truly believes that everyone in the world is small."

Shen Tiechan of Taiyuan was fond of using perfumed cases and ornate poetic forms to express feelings of unfulfilled aspirations. Once, after visiting a certain official and not being received, he composed a playful, untitled poem: White-painted walls enclose a painted pavilion, Through the window, I hear the delicate sound of the箜篌. No messenger can deliver messages through the green bird, In vain did the traveler halt his purple steed. The moon goddess must surely follow the moon rabbit, The star maiden may pause and await the oxherd. In the sparse branches of the willow, the carved window is near, Only regret that the pearl curtain does not hook. This poem possesses the refined style of Yuxi Sheng (Li Shangyin). Wang Jingguang remarked: It seems one should not suspect the Weaving Girl, thereby defaming the celestial spirits. I say: Alas, it is over. The Weaving Girl parts from the Cowherd, meeting once a year; between them, separated by a river, what things are not present? This is a poem by Yuan Weizhi. A sea traveler rides a raft into the purple haze, The star maiden stops weaving and meets him once. Only because she does not fear the jealousy of the oxherd, She gives you the stone from her loom. This is a poem by Li Yishan (Li Shangyin). Yuan Weizhi’s intent was toward Shuang Wen; Li Yishan’s intent was toward Linghu. Literary men play with ink and brush, using them as metaphors — originally, they had no connection to the Weaving Girl. Shen Tiechan’s words, too, are akin to the sentiments of Yuan and Li, and thus do not constitute a defamation of the celestial spirits. As for entirely fabricating fictitious words, making them seem like real events, naming specific times, places, and individuals — such is the story of Guo Han meeting the Weaving Girl, as recorded in *Lingguai Ji* (A Collection of Strange Tales). *Lingguai Ji* is now lost, but this passage appears in *Taiping Guangji*, volume 68. This is extremely absurd and baseless.

Writers and scholars often cite from a wide range of sources, but they cannot always verify each and every reference. However, it is important to be aware of the tendency to spread false and misleading information. This practice has its roots in the allegorical tales of Zhuangzi and Liezi, which were used to express philosophical ideas. During the Warring States period, various philosophers wrote extensively, and apocryphal texts and minor officials' records often contained unverified claims. Over time, these sources influenced each other, leading to a period of reckless and unrestrained dissemination of information. For example, Li Rong's *Unique Records* falsely claimed that Fuxi and his sister Nüwa were married, which is a grave distortion. Zhang Hua's *Records of Rare Things* went even further, falsely accusing Confucius of being a homosexual, which is particularly egregious. It is unlikely that Zhang Hua would have made such baseless claims; it is more probable that these were added by later editors. Such false claims are numerous and continue to circulate, causing great frustration. Additionally, there are instances where historical texts are misinterpreted or manipulated. For instance, the *Book of Han* mentions that Governor Wu Gong loved and favored Jia Yi. The *The Literary Mind and the Carving of Dragons*, a book on rhetoric and literary composition written by a Ming dynasty author (the name of the author is unknown) and published by Chen Mei, incorrectly classifies Jia Yi as a homosexual, labeling him as a "dragon boy." Similarly, the *Records of the Grand Historian* describes how Liu Bang's mother was in a marsh when his father, Tai Gong, saw a dragon above her. Chao Yidao's poem includes the line "kill the old man and share a cup of soup," suggesting that Liu Bang was born from a dragon and not his father. The *Zuo Zhuan* mentions the secret relationships between Chengfeng and Ji You, and Jing Ying and Xiang Zhong. The term "private affairs" refers to secretly forming alliances to support their sons. Later scholars, including Zhu Xi, criticized the use of the term "private" in this context, calling it a great evil. Such misinterpretations are numerous. Scholars should carefully examine the truth and falsehood of historical accounts, avoiding the temptation to show off their knowledge or seek novelty by hastily accepting unverified information as topics of conversation.

From Uncle Meian, I heard that there were two young men in our clan—these are the names I remember from when I was a child, and they were likely my uncles or cousins. They heard that there were fox tracks in a certain tomb, so at night they took a gun and went together, hiding in the grass to wait for the foxes. They slept with their backs leaning against each other, but when they woke up, their hair was intertwined into one, passing through and winding around each other. Suddenly, they could not be untangled, and they were pulling each other, unable to move or stand. Slightly moving, they both cried out in pain, and they were stuck together all night. Seeing a passerby, they called for help, and the passerby cut them apart with a dagger, and they returned in a state of disarray. Angry and wanting to go back to report, the elders said, "They have no form or sound, and cannot be overcome by force. Moreover, without reason to invade them, the logic is not right. The insult was self-invited, so why harbor resentment? Retaliation will only lead to greater failure." The two then stopped. This was a small torment to make them cautious, without deeply harming them, to provoke a certain revenge, and it can also be said that they were good at preserving themselves. However, even a small torment can provoke anger, and it is better to restrain oneself and not act, making sure there are no traces to be sought even better.

There is a stone box at the base of the steps of Taihe Gate, and no one knows its name or what it contains. An elder named De Bao, also known as Ji Zhai, who was a Jinshi during the reign of Emperor Qianlong and served as a reader in the Hanlin Academy, was distinguished from another person of the same name by being called "Da De Bao" (Big De Bao) and "Xiao De Bao" (Little De Bao). He mentioned that the grandfather of Tu Yu Zhai had inspected the contents of the stone box when he was in charge of palace construction. I asked Tu Yu Zhai about it, and he said: "Indeed, it seems to be rice and grain transformed over time, and only half of the box is filled, not completely. Upon careful examination, it appears to be the result of long-term transformation." I replied: "If the stone box on the left of the base of the steps contains good seeds, then this one likely contains the five grains, which makes more sense logically. Moreover, the treasure vase carried on the back of an elephant in the imperial procession also contains the five grains. Farming is considered a treasure, a tradition passed down from ancient times. The Book of Changes emphasizes food as the first of the eight policies. The significance of this custom is profound and far-reaching."

Within the inner city wall of Xuanwu Gate, there are five mounds, built with bricks, which the locals call the tombs of the Five Fire Gods. During the time when Emperor Chengzu was on a northern campaign, he used the five elements of fire (仁, 义, 礼, 智, 信) to create flying cannons, defeating the Yuan forces at Luan Cai Gou. Later, fearing that their skills were too advanced and might cause trouble, he killed and buried them there, setting up five poles beside the beautiful tower, and worshiped them annually to make the spirits have a place to return, so they do not become malevolent. Later, Emperor Chengzu was reborn as Emperor Chongzhen, and the five were reborn as Li Zicheng, Zhang Xianzhong, and other rebels, to take revenge. This is a story from Qi and Dong, often unreliable; not only is there no record in official histories, even in the numerous unofficial novels of the Ming dynasty, there is no mention. In the autumn of the year Wu Zi (1638), I met a Han army infantry officer named Dong, who said he heard from an old soldier in the capital garrison that this is the situation. The terrain of the capital, only Xuanwu Gate is the lowest; water from the streets and alleys, when it rains, all collects in the inner city. Every time there is a heavy rain at night, the guards get up, look at these mounds, and if the water reaches the top, they call for the gate to be opened to release the water. If the water covers the top, the door is blocked by the water and cannot be opened. Over time, people have gradually forgotten, so sometimes it causes problems. The five poles on the city wall are related to the signal cannon of the White Pagoda; if the signal cannon is heard, then during the day, a flag is raised, and at night, a lamp is hung. What does this have to do with the Five Fire Gods? This explanation seems reasonable, but it must have some basis.

In the imperial examination, when papers are redistributed, those who receive new papers often feel dissatisfied, which is also a natural human sentiment, but it ultimately depends on the quality of the paper itself. During the provincial examination in Renwu year in Shuntian, I served as a co-examiner, and at that time, there was no rule to avoid grading papers from my own province. I came across a well-written paper, whose prose was excellent but the poem was not up to par. Due to the recent change in the rules for testing poetry, allowing for leniency, I submitted the paper for recommendation to the chief examiner, Liang Wen Zhuang Gong, and it was already selected. However, during the final stage of compiling the list of successful candidates, Liang Gong criticized the paper for not revising this particular line, which affected the subsequent text—this line was part of the four-line segment titled "始吾于人也." He rejected the paper and replaced it with another reserve paper, which I reviewed first. The sixth line of the poem read: "素娥寒对影，顾兔夜眠香" — the title being "月中桂." I was already pleased with its elegance and refinement, and when I saw the seventh line: "倚树思吴质，吟诗忆许棠," I exclaimed: "吴刚字质," for Li He in his poem "李凭箜篌引" wrote: "吴质不眠倚桂树，露脚斜飞湿寒兔." This poem is not included in standard anthologies, and those who have not seen Li He's collection "昌谷集" would not know about it.

The poetry contest in Huazhou selected Xu Tang as the first-place winner for his poem about the moonlit osmanthus. Xu Tang's poem is no longer transmitted, and those who have not seen Wang Dingbao's *Zhiyan* or Ji Minfu's *Tangshi Jishi* would not know. How does the poem in the other scroll, which speaks of flowers blooming in the upper realm and a celestial youth holding an axe, compare to this poem? If it were not for your intervention, I would have willingly changed it. This is Zhu Ziyi. After the results were announced, it was already September, and he was poor, lacking a warm coat. Jiang Xinyu, who had always exchanged poems with him, lent him a coat, and he came to see me, presenting the poems he had written as a gift. During the year of Bingzi, when I was accompanying the emperor to the north of the Great Wall, the carriages and horses were congested. I took a rest in a hostel and saw a poem on the wall, half of which was worn away, but only a few lines were still legible. I loved the lines 'The water rises, and the human voices grow distant; the green mountains stretch to the hooves of the horses.' I thought that 'The road in the clouds winds around the color of Bashan, and the trees hide the sound of the Han River flowing,' was not an exaggeration. I regretted not knowing the author's name, but when I opened his scroll, this poem was there. Thus, I realized that our affinity had been present for six or seven years, and we sighed together for a long time. Zhu Ziyi treated me with the utmost courtesy, and after his death, his two sons continued his legacy, and I still felt a deep affection for them. The connection through writing and calligraphy was indeed not accidental. How could I ever consider the selection of rooms as a measure of closeness or distance? My poem on the boat in the Yangtze River says: 'The mountain colors are hazy and light as smoke, the uneven green reaches the edge of the great river, the setting sun and flowing water urge me to sit under the awning, and everywhere I go, I want to board a boat.' This is indeed derived from the line 'The green mountains stretch to the hooves of the horses.' I once told Zhu Ziyi: 'People say that blue comes from indigo, but today it is the indigo that comes from the blue.' Zhu Ziyi humbly declined, but his expression seemed to silently agree. This is also a fine story in the poetry world, and I will record it here.

My esteemed teacher, Mr. Jie Yeyuan, served as Vice Minister of Rites and accompanied on a southern tour, where he passed away on the road. The night before his death, a star fell in front of the boat. After his death, the capital did not yet know. His wife, Lady Shi, dreamt that he came riding a horse to the door, accompanied by a splendid entourage, but he stood still and did not enter. Instead, he sent someone to deliver a message: "Take good care of yourself at home, I am leaving." He hurriedly passed by, and in the dream, she thought he was still on the tour, suspecting there might be urgent orders, so he did not have time to enter. Upon waking, she was greatly shocked. When the news of his death arrived, it was the same night. He had often held the position of controlling literary matters, presiding over the metropolitan examinations four times, the provincial examinations four times, and numerous other examinations, almost too many to count. He once wrote a poem for the banquet of honor: "In the imperial garden, the new class of parrots gathers, (the phrase 'new class of parrots' is uncertain in origin, and I intended to ask him about it at the time but forgot to do so) the worn-out old crane rides in the carriage, on Longjin Bridge, the Golden List, four times I saw my students become top scholars." This was written in the year Ding Chou. He also gifted a couplet to Wen Xiang Gong: "The articles of the world follow the same path, half of the nobility and officials are like peaches and plums under the teacher's door." This can be considered the utmost honor for a scholar. However, a diviner predicted his fate, saying he would ultimately reach the rank of one-star general, perhaps to be sent out as a general? He laughed and said, "If what you say is true, then the general does not like martial affairs." Upon his death, the emperor was deeply grieved and specially posthumously awarded him the rank of General of the Army. Although he served in the Ministry of Rites, he also served as Deputy General of the Army. During his tour, he was in the order of Deputy General of the Army, thus advancing one rank in the military hierarchy. The diviner's prediction, it can be said, was indeed verified.

Mediums often impersonate ancient people, but sometimes they provide small verifications. Wen Tieshan, a former official — named Wen Min, a jinshi in the year *yichou*, who held the position of Shengjing Shilang — once encountered a medium. He asked about his lifespan, and the medium responded: "Sixty-two years." He believed this meant sixty-two years, but he passed away two years later, realizing that "two autumns" referred to two years. It seems that even spirits can sometimes predict the future. Another account tells of a provincial governor, a duke, who consulted a medium about his lifespan. The medium replied: "I do not know." When asked if there was anything the medium did not know, the response was: "Others may know, but not you. Life spans are determined by fate, and each person receives what they are allotted. If you are a high-ranking official with the power to give life or take it away, one good policy can bring blessings to thousands of people and extend your own life, while one bad policy can bring disaster and shorten your life. Even the gods of fate cannot predict the consequences of your actions, so how could I? Have you not heard that Su Fan's life was shortened by two years after he mistakenly killed two people, and that Lou Shide's life was shortened by ten years after he also mistakenly killed two people? Therefore, matters of life and destiny are something you should ask yourself, not me." This statement is clearly in line with reason, and it seems that the medium he encountered was indeed a true spirit.

Cousin Yu, the speaker of ten thousand words, once lived north of Zhang Ge Bridge. There, a person saw a black fox drunk and sleeping in a field house—a small hut used to guard grain and crops, commonly known as a field house. Initially, the person wanted to capture the fox, but then they thought that the fox could bring wealth, so they covered it with a cloth and sat guarding it.
