When the fox woke up, it stretched and contracted several times, transforming into a human form. The fox was very grateful for the protection and care, and so they became friends. The fox also occasionally gave gifts. One day, the person asked the fox: "Suppose someone hides in your home, can you hide them without revealing them?" The fox replied, "Yes, I can." The person then asked, "Can you attach yourself to a person and run wildly?" The fox replied, "I can do that too." The person then earnestly begged, "Our family is extremely poor, and the gifts you give are not enough to sustain us. I also feel ashamed for bothering you so often. Now, there is a wealthy person in the village who is very afraid of lawsuits. I recently heard that he is looking for a woman to cook for him. I want to send my wife to respond. After staying for several days, she will wait for an opportunity to escape and hide in your home, while I will claim that I have lost my wife and pretend to sue. My wife still has a decent appearance, so we can falsely accuse her of scandalous rumors to extort a large sum of money."

After obtaining the gold, the official with the warrant rushed to the villa of a certain person (甲). Then, he sent someone to find it, and thus received a lot of money. The fox did as said and indeed obtained a lot of money. After finding a wife and returning, the owner (某甲) did not dare to ask further because the fox was in his villa. However, the woman's madness did not heal. She always dressed up and at night seemed to play and laugh with someone, preventing her husband from approaching. The narrator rushed to ask the fox, who said there was no such reason. He tried to investigate but returned shortly and stamped his feet, saying: "It's ruined. It's the fox on the upper floor of 某甲's house, lured by your wife's beauty, taking advantage of my absence and entering. This fox is not something I can defeat; there's nothing I can do about it." This person persistently begged, and the fox said seriously: "For example, someone in your neighborhood who is as violent and fierce as a tiger. If he forcibly takes someone's wife, could you fight for her?" Later, her wife's madness and pain worsened daily, and she also revealed her husband's plot. Various treatments were ineffective, and she finally died of consumption. The people in the neighborhood all said: "This person is as cunning as a ghost, and combined with the fox's magic, should have no worries. But they did not expect that the fox would call another fox, like the mantis and the oriole waiting for each other." An ancient poem says: "Near profit, there is a hidden knife; greedy people harm themselves." Indeed.

A disciple named Wang Tingshao reported that in Xinzhou, there was a man who sold his wife due to extreme poverty. She was taken away and did not return for nearly two years. Suddenly, she returned on her own. She said that when they first took her, they led her to a house. Soon after, a Taoist priest arrived and took her into the mountains. She felt very suspicious and fearful, but since she had already been sold, there was nothing she could do. The Taoist priest told her to close her eyes, and she immediately heard the wind whistling in her ears. Then he told her to open her eyes, and she found herself on a high peak. The buildings were elegant and clean, and there were more than twenty women who came to greet her. She was told that this was a heavenly abode, and there was no suffering here. When asked what she would be doing, she was told that she would take turns serving the master ancestor during his sleep. There were mountains of gold and silver, and jewels and silk were abundant. Delicate dishes and precious fruits were available, and spirits could be summoned and commanded to appear instantly. Daily consumption and necessities were provided at the level of princes and dukes. The only discomfort was a small pain once a month, but it was not harmful either. She was shown the storage rooms, the kitchen, the living quarters for the women, and the master ancestor's quarters. He pointed to the highest point and said, "This is where the master ancestor worships the moon and the Big Dipper, and this is where he refines silver." There were servants, but no men at all. From then on, during the day, the Taoist priest would call her to serve him, and at night, the master ancestor would ascend the altar for worship, after which everyone would go to bed.

A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms After her menstrual period, the woman was stripped of her inner and outer garments, and a large rope made of red silk was tied to a tall tree, immobilizing her hands and feet so that she could not move even a little bit. A ball of cotton was placed in her mouth to prevent her from speaking, and her master, holding a golden tube that looked like chopsticks, examined her acupuncture points, piercing both her arms and legs, and sucking her blood, a very cruel and poisonous act. After sucking her blood, he sprinkled powdered medicine on the wounds, and she did not feel any pain immediately, forming a scab in a moment. The next day, the scab fell off as it was originally. The place was very high, and looking down, she saw clouds and rain below. Suddenly one day, a violent wind arose, dark clouds like ink pressing on the mountain top, lightning and thunder flashing, the situation extremely terrifying. The master was terrified and called more than twenty women, who surrounded him naked like a flesh screen. Firelight entered the room several times, all of them returning immediately after a quick pull. Suddenly, a dragon's claw as big as a basket grabbed the master away in the crowd, a thunderclap, the valleys shaking, heaven and earth becoming dark and gloomy. She felt dazed like sleeping, waking up from a dream and finding herself lying by the roadside. Asking local people, she knew that she was only hundreds of miles from home, then exchanging bracelets for old clothes to cover the body, begging for food and returning home. People in Xinzhou still saw this woman, with a withered appearance, soon suffering from consumption and dying. Because her essence and blood were completely drained by the道士. According to what he said, it was a炼丹术士, his technique being magical and mysterious like this, still not escaping from heavenly punishment. How much more so, then, for those who do not receive the transmission, merely being deceived by charlatans, and hoping to become immortals, is not a foolish endeavor?

A Scholar-Gentleman from Southern China, Wu, was a student of Zhu Shijun. He was a talented man who died young, and his wife vowed to sacrifice her life for him. She attempted to hang herself multiple times but could not die. Suddenly, under the light of the lamp, the form of Wu appeared and said, "Change into a different type of clothing, and then you will die." Following his advice, she indeed died. The villagers of Wu's hometown recorded the story and invited poets to write about it, and many poets contributed. I also wrote two poems on the subject, and Zhu Shijun composed an inscription for the tomb. Both the hardships of Wu and the bravery of the virtuous wife were deeply mourned and lamented in the inscription. However, this incident was not mentioned at all. Some suspected that the villagers exaggerated the story. I replied, "No, the different categories of literature each have their own style. Guo Pu's commentary on the *Shanhaijing* and *Mujizhuan* described the story of the Queen Mother of the West in great detail. In contrast, his commentary on the *Erya* regarding the western lands of darkness and chaos simply stated, 'the western lands of darkness and chaos,' without adding a single word. This is how commentary on classics should be done. Inscriptions on metal and stone complement historical texts but cannot be compared to unofficial or anecdotal records, nor to poetry or prose. Zhu Shijun is extensively read and knowledgeable, deeply understanding the different categories of writing. His decision not to mention this incident in the tomb inscription follows ancient literary conventions, not because it was false, but because it did not fit the genre. Due to old age, I have many forgettings. I remember the name of the scholar-gentleman as Chengfu, but I cannot recall the surname of the virtuous wife. I will briefly record the details here. Awaiting the return of the imperial entourage, I will then seek to record the details more fully."

The Elderly Servant Shi Xiang, once riding a horse at night, reached Zhang Bai. The surrounding area was desolate and open, and in the darkness, several people were throwing sand and mud. The horse was startled, neighed, and would not move forward. Shi Xiang knew they were ghosts and scolded them, saying, "I will not go among your graveyards, why are you troubling me?" The group of ghosts mocked him, saying, "We are just playing around, who is arguing with you about logic?" Shi Xiang, angry, said, "Since we are not arguing logic, it's a fight." He then dismounted and struck them with his whip. There was a prolonged commotion, but his strength was not enough to match theirs. The horse also jumped and restrained his arm, leaving him in a difficult situation. Suddenly, he saw a ghost rushing madly from a distance and shouted loudly, "This is my good friend, you all do not act rashly." The group of ghosts then dispersed. Shi Xiang mounted his horse and galloped back, also not having time to ask who the ghost was. The next day, he carried wine to the place of yesterday and offered it, asking for a response from the spirits, but they silently did not respond. Shi Xiang's friends were just servants, butchers, and vendors, but in the underworld, the feelings of old friends are like this.

A Student Named Wu Zhongqiao Once Wrote a Short Biography of the Goddess of Fulfillment, Using Allegorical Humor and Writing as a Form of Play. Later, He Became a Magistrate in Sichuan Province and Met His Death on the Road While Supervising the Transport of Gunpowder During the Jinchuan Campaign. His Poetry and Prose Are All Lost, but This Piece Was Accidentally Found Among Old Papers and Is Included Here. The Text Says: The Goddess of Fulfillment, Ruyuan, is a deity from the realm of water spirits. In the past, she was given to Ou Ming of Lu陵 by Pengze Qinghong Jun, a legendary figure associated with water deities. She is called Ruyuan because she can fulfill all requests. Water spirits exist everywhere, and whether one encounters her or not depends on one's fate. There were once four people who traveled together in search of the Dao, crossing rivers and seas. They encountered a dragon god who summoned them and said, "I see that you are all diligent in your pursuit. Today, I will each grant you a Ruyuan." Immediately, four women accompanied them. One of them could fulfill any request, and she was extremely pleased. However, after a few months, she fell ill and died. The woman said, "What one enjoys in this life is the result of one's past deeds. The merits you accumulated in previous lives have been exhausted in just a few months."

Please return and report your mission; this person indeed did not rise or act as expected. Another person's requests were always fulfilled, yet their desires were still not satisfied. During the winter month, they requested fresh litchis as large as a melon. The woman said, "While one's desires can be filled like a river or valley, they are insatiable, and this is beyond the power of the gods to fulfill." Another person's requests were sometimes fulfilled and sometimes not, and they blamed the woman for the unfulfilled requests. The woman said, "The power of the gods varies; I can fulfill some requests but not others. Just as the sun must set and the moon must wane, there must be times of scarcity. Any lack is actually a blessing for you. Haven't you seen those who have already passed away?" The person became cautious, and the woman stayed with them and did not leave. Another person achieved their desires without making any requests, and their desires were fulfilled by themselves. They felt uneasy and anxious. The woman said, "Your virtue and fortune are high; heaven and earth witness this, and spirits and deities protect you. You gain ten times more without making requests. You do not need me, as I only secretly assist you." On another day, they met and told each other about their experiences, feeling either happy or regretful. They said, "Alas, the deceased cannot hear us." This is a story crafted by Zhong Qiao. Such playful writing, done occasionally, can serve as a means of punishment and encouragement, and is not inappropriate. If such writing becomes lengthy and voluminous, it would no longer be suitable for a book.

Guo Shizhou said that a wealthy man from Henan, after a successful career in government service, returned to his hometown at the age of sixty and was as strong and healthy as a young adult. He always kept three or four young concubines. When they reached twenty years old, he would prepare trousseaus and marry them off. All remained as intact as unbroken jade, and those who married them secretly praised his virtue, while many were happy to sell their daughters to him. However, in his home, intimate relations in bed were the same as ordinary people. Some believed he collected red lead for medicinal use, others thought he was only interested in pleasure, and some thought he was too old to father children. No one knew the true reason. Later, a servant revealed the secret: he was actually engaging in sexual relations with the women. A close friend asked about the truth, and he did not hide it, saying: "My vitality is still strong, and I cannot completely abandon my desires. Having relations with women can still allow me to have children, but I fear it will be a burden after my death. I want to seek male beauty, but I fear the incident of Ai Seng, which would be a shame for my descendants. Therefore, I take this unconventional approach." This case is unique and unheard of in ancient times. What goes on in the inner chambers is beyond comprehension, but the bedroom matters need not be deeply discussed. Only the fact that year after year, the women are changed, allowing good families' daughters to gain the name of being remarried, seems to benefit them, yet without checking the marriage date, it does not harm their virginity, which also seems to be a favor. Such cases, it is difficult to determine right or wrong. The respected elder Go Jiezhou said: "It is not difficult to judge. He relies solely on his wealth, indulging in lust outside the law." In the past, Sin Er Dong, when robbing, would always leave behind warm clothes and bedding, and money to return home, considering it a virtue. This old man's favor is also like that.

In the neighborhood, there was a scholar who was agile and strong, and who also practiced martial arts and long-distance jumping techniques. He could gracefully leap up a height of two or three zhang, and gracefully leap over a width of two or three zhang. I still remember seeing him when I was young. I once asked to see his skills, and he had me stand in a hall. When I faced the front door, he stood outside the front door, facing me. When I turned to face the back door, he stood outside the back door, facing me. He did this several times. With one leap, he could fly over the roof ridge. Later, I passed through Dulin Town and met a friend who invited me to drink at a wine shop by the bridge. After drinking to our hearts' content, we stood on the riverbank together. My friend said, "Can you leap over this?" The scholar responded with a leap, crossing over. My friend called him back, and he responded again, leaping over. His feet barely touched the shore, but unaware that the shore was about to collapse, he stepped onto a steep section near the water that had cracks and patterns. The shore collapsed about two zhang, and he followed it down into the river, being carried away by the current. He had never learned to swim, but he could only jump up a few feet from the waves, able to go straight up but not to the side to get close to the shore. He continued to fall back into the water several times, and eventually drowned. The greatest danger in the world is having something to rely on. Those who rely on wealth ultimately fail because of it, those who rely on power ultimately fail because of it, those who rely on intelligence ultimately fail because of it, and those who rely on strength ultimately fail because of it. Those who have something to rely on dare to take risks, which is why he met his downfall. Lord Tian Songyan bought a walking stick from Laoshan in Punyang and inscribed a poem on it: "On moonlit nights and flower-filled mornings, it accompanies me on my journey. Even on flat roads, be cautious of falling. Dare to rely on you without worry, then take steps on uneven terrain." These are truly words of experience, valuable and worthy of wearing on one's person.

Resting by the well in the watery wilderness, there was an elderly nun known as Master Hui, whose nickname or title was unknown, and it was uncertain whether the word "Hui" referred to her. Nonetheless, she was called by that name. When I was young, I often saw her entering and leaving the home of my maternal grandfather, Zhang Gong. She adhered strictly to her precepts, abstaining even from sugar. She said, "Sugar is also made with pig fat." She did not wear fur, saying, "Sleeping on skin is as bad as eating meat." She did not wear silk or satin, saying, "One foot of silk is the life of a thousand silkworms." When offering tofu skin to the Buddha, she always made it herself, saying, "In the market, it is all trodden on by feet." When burning incense, she always struck a flint to get fire, saying, "The kitchen fire is not clean." She took a simple vegetarian meal, sufficient for her own needs, and did not actively seek donations. A servant woman from my maternal grandfather's household offered a piece of cloth as a donation. The nun recognized it upon seeing it and said, "Offering should be done with one's own wealth to truly be considered merit." The household had lost this piece of cloth and beat several young maidservants. "Would the Buddha accept such a thing?" the woman explained. "I thought there were dozens of pieces of cloth, so I did not check each one carefully. I took one by accident, not realizing that it would cause others to suffer punishment. We cursed each other daily, and I felt truly uneasy, so I offered it to seek forgiveness for my sin." The nun threw the cloth back and said, "Why not secretly return it to its original place? That way, people would know, and you would feel at ease." After the woman died several years later, her disciple revealed the story, and thus others came to know about it. During the years 1754 and 1755 of the Qianlong era, when she was already in her seventies or eighties, she suddenly visited our home, saying she was going to the Tanzhe Temple to worship the Buddha and to take vows as a novice nun. I mentioned the previous incident, and she shook her head, saying, "There was no such thing; the small mischievous nun is just talking nonsense." We all admired her loyalty and kindness.

On the verge of departure, he requested me to inscribe a plaque for the Buddha hall, and I commissioned Zhao Chunjian to write it on my behalf. Folding his hands, he said: "Whoever writes it, I will request the inscription of their name — do not make false statements before the Buddha. To change Zhao’s name, he took it away, and afterward he did not return." Recently, I inquired among people of Cangzhou, but no one now recognizes it. Another monk from the Temple of Tianqi in Jingcheng was the third disciple of the abbot Guocheng. Scholars respected him, and all called him "Third Master," so his name was lost. Guocheng’s disciples were mostly unworthy, scattering to beg alms throughout the land, but this monk did not fall from the tradition. He had no air of worldly sophistication found in attendants of great temples, nor did he carry the proud and noble air of a seated meditation master. His precepts were strict and arduous; even over a thousand miles, he would pack his belongings and walk, never riding in carriages or on horseback. My elder brother Qinghu once met him on the road and earnestly invited him to ride with him, but he finally refused. When officials came to the temple, they treated him with no extra courtesy.

When peasants and rustic elders come to the temple, they are treated with no less courtesy. Whether they give much, little, or nothing, they are treated with the same respect. After chanting and reciting, he only sits quietly in one room, entering his temple as if no one were there. His actions are simply as such. Yet the men and women of the village all say: "The Third Master's conduct is clear and lofty." When asked where his conduct is clear and lofty, they are at a loss to answer. The reason why he moves people's hearts is truly unknown. I once asked Yao An Gong, and he said: "According to what you have seen, is there anything not clear and lofty? If there is nothing not clear and lofty, then it is clear and lofty." Do you necessarily want to regard the flying and cup-crossing as good knowledge? This nun and monk are also solitary practitioners in their own way. The Third Master will not be long in nirvana, and his name will be known by someone. Wait until you see the village youths taking the exams, and send them back to inquire about him in the temple.

The vastness of the world, from the nine provinces, theft and corruption are everywhere and at all times, none of these are considered unusual. Even if a thief is somewhat different from other thieves, he is still fundamentally a thief; even if someone is somewhat different from others who commit adultery, they are still fundamentally adulterous—this is what makes them different. If people allow or condone theft and adultery, this is even more unusual. Conflicts or incidents arise suddenly and then quickly resolve themselves, like boiling water rising up and lightning striking down—this is not the most unusual thing of all? My maternal or paternal uncle, An Gong Wuzhang, said that there was a man who lost his wife at middle age, already having children, who then bought a married woman. Due to skillful control, the situation was manageable. However, when the woman's husband died, the private savings accumulated over time were all in the hands of this woman. Her son heard about the situation and sought the money, but there was no evidence to support the claim, and the woman did not admit to it.

The husband, having discovered where his wife had hidden the money, dug a hole in the wall at night and entered the room. Just as he opened the chest and was about to take the money out, the wife woke up and shouted loudly that there was a thief. The family members jumped up in alarm and entered with weapons. The husband's son, in a panic, came out through the hole and immediately attacked the intruder, causing him to fall down. The husband then immediately entered through the hole to search for any other thieves. Hearing breathing sounds under the bed, they shouted that there was still one thief, pulled him out together, and tied him up. When the light was brought, they examined carefully and found that the one with a broken forehead and unconscious was the husband's son, and the one under the bed was his former husband. After the son woke up, he and the wife each made their own statement. The son said that taking the father's money was not stealing, and the wife said that a wife returning to her former husband was not adultery. The son said that a former husband could be remarried but not secretly met, and the wife said that a father's money could be claimed but not stolen. They argued back and forth, neither giving in. The next day, the clan secretly discussed that litigation would be a loss for both sides and only tarnish the family's reputation. They secretly mediated to make sure all the money stayed with the son and allowed the wife to return to her former husband. The difficulty was resolved, but the situation had already become known. My late uncle, Master Yin Nan, said: "This matter happened by coincidence, which is fate; the reason for this matter is due to human actions. If they did not accept a woman who already has a husband, how could the son steal and how could the wife commit adultery? What they rely on is their ability to control during life, but they cannot control after death."

The *Records of Ghosts and Strange Events* from the *Yue Wei* Pavilion, Volume 23, Continuation of the *Luan Yang* Records, Section 5 Master Dai Dongyuan recounted that one of his clan ancestors once rented an abandoned house in a secluded alley, which had been empty for a long time. Some said it was haunted. The ancestor spoke sternly, "I am not afraid." At night, as expected, he saw a figure under the light, and the gloomy and miserable atmosphere pierced his flesh and bones. A huge ghost shouted angrily, "Are you truly not afraid?" The ancestor replied, "Yes." The ghost then displayed various evil appearances. After a long time, the ghost asked again, "Still not afraid?" The ancestor answered, "Yes." The ghost's mood became slightly calmer and said, "I do not have to definitely drive you away; I find your boastful words strange. Just say the word 'fear,' and I will leave immediately." The ancestor was angry and said, "I am not really afraid of you. How can I pretend to be afraid? You can do whatever you want." The ghost repeated this several times, but the ancestor never responded. The ghost sighed deeply and said, "I have lived here for more than thirty years and have never seen someone as stubborn as you. Such a foolish creature, how can I live with it?" Suddenly, the ghost disappeared. Someone blamed the ancestor, saying, "Fearing ghosts is a common sentiment; it is not dishonorable. Answering falsely with fear can calm things down and pacify people. Provoking each other will lead nowhere." The ancestor said, "Those with deep spiritual power can drive away evil spirits by being calm. I am not that person. By dominating with spirit, a strong spirit makes ghosts not bother. Slightly yielding, a weak spirit makes ghosts take advantage. He uses various ways to tempt, but fortunately, I did not fall into his tricks." Those who discussed thought his argument was correct.

Food, drink, men, and women—these are the great desires of life. Violating names and titles, desecrating moral norms, and corrupting customs are strictly prohibited by law. However, simple-minded boys and girls who have a particular fondness for each other, though not entirely in line with propriety, perhaps should not be treated with excessive severity. I heard from a young age about a certain official who, while serving in a government office, took pride in his integrity and uprightness. He once arranged for a young maid to be paired with a young male servant, not for a year now. They did not avoid each other when meeting. One day, the official happened to arrive and saw the two still smiling. Angered, he said, "This is a case of illicit sexual conduct. According to the law, those who commit adultery with an unmarried wife shall be flogged." He immediately called for the flogging. The crowd said that the young people were just playing and there was no actual misconduct. The maid's eyebrows and breasts could be examined. According to the law, if the act was planned but not carried out, it is only reduced by one degree. Reduction is acceptable, but exemption is not. Finally, both were flogged, and the injuries were almost fatal. The official considered himself to be following the family law of the Liu family of Hedong, and believed that his actions were not wrong.

Henceforth, he detested their lack of propriety, so he postponed their wedding date. As a result, during their time working together, they stumbled when they walked; when there was nothing to do, they hid when they saw shadows, stumbling forward and backward, unable to bear the day. Gradually, they became depressed and fell ill, and within half a year, they died one after the other. Their parents mourned them and requested a joint burial, but the official still angrily said, "Marriage of the deceased is against propriety. Have you not heard of this?" He did not listen. Later, when the official died, he mumbled incoherently, saying things that were not very clear, but "Not me, but it is not right" and "According to propriety, it is not right" were repeated many times, clearly and distinctly. Everyone suspected that he had seen something. Men and women do not know each other's names without a matchmaker, which is an ancient custom. The official arranged their marriage when they were still children, making them aware that they would be husband and wife in the future, living together day and night, and wanting them to be无情, it was impossible. Words spoken inside the inner chamber do not go out, and words spoken outside do not enter the inner chamber, which is an ancient custom. The official had few servants and maids, and he could not make them manage their own affairs, so he often personally taught and received them. Wanting them to not speak a single word was also impossible. The root is not right, hence the branches are not straight. This is the reason why the two violated propriety, and the owner contributed to it. He acted too hastily and dealt with the situation excessively, so could the hearts of the dead be content? The spirits of the wronged became malevolent, and even according to propriety, it could not be a valid excuse. Is this what scholars of learning would do?

People from Shanxi often engage in commerce outside their home province. At around ten years old, they start learning trade and wait until they accumulate wealth before arranging a marriage. After marrying, they continue to pursue profit and usually return home for a visit every two or three years, which is a common practice. Some face poor fortune or are bound by affairs, and may not return home for one or two decades. Some may run out of money and lose their clothes, feeling ashamed to return home and wandering aimlessly, with no communication with their families, which is often the case. There is a person named Li Jia who moves around and becomes the adopted son of a local man named Jin Yi. He takes on the family name of Jin, and his family does not know his whereabouts, leading to the report that he is dead. Soon after, his parents both die, leaving his wife with no place to go. She relies on the mother's family and the maternal uncle's family for food. His maternal uncle originally lived in a neighboring county, took his family along, and pursued a tenth of the profits from business, trading by ship in the north and south, and had no fixed residence each year.

Jia had not received a family letter for a long time and also thought he was dead. Jin Yi plotted to marry Jia. Coincidentally, the woman's maternal uncle was a traveling soldier, and her family was displaced and lived in Tianjin. Thinking that a young widow was not a long-term plan, they also planned to marry her to a man from Shanxi, hoping she could still return to her hometown in the future. Fearing that people would look down on her for having no maternal family, they falsely claimed her as Jin Yi's own daughter. Various people acted as matchmakers, and thus the matter was completed. On the night of the wedding ceremony, having been separated for eight years, both doubted but dared not ask. In private conversation at midnight, they only then understood clearly. Jia was angry that she had not obtained solid evidence and hastily married, while cursing and beating her. The whole family was startled awake, and Jin Yi called out through the window, saying: "Do you have solid evidence that your wife is dead when you remarried? Moreover, having been displaced and waiting for you for eight years before marrying, one can also understand that it was not done out of choice." Jia had nothing to say, and they became husband and wife as before. The broken mirror was reunited, and there have been such things in ancient times. As for remarrying and still being with the original spouse, and a woman remarrying without losing her virtue, since the records began, I have never heard of such a thing. Aunt's husband, Wei Gong Keting, once saw it with his own eyes.

Cangzhou Wine, which Mr. Ruan Ting called Magu Wine, is not actually referred to by this name by the locals. It has been famous for a long time, but there are differing opinions among critics. Those traveling by boat all buy it on the shore, where the village wine in the shops is thin and poor, not worthy to stain the cups. The locals guard against insatiable demands and warn each other not to provide real wine to officials, even if beaten, they would not give it up. Even if the price is ten times higher, they would not give it up. Even the governor of Baoyang could not get even a drop, so it can be imagined how rare it is. The wine cannot be brewed by common people; it must be brewed by old families and clans, passed down through generations, to achieve the right balance of water and fire. Although the water is taken from the Wei River, the yellow stream cannot be used for brewing wine. It must be taken from under the South River Pavilion, using the method of taking the central spring from Mount Jin, by sinking a tin pot to the bottom of the river to get the clear spring that rises from the ground, only then does it have the ethereal quality. It is sensitive to cold, heat, moisture, and steam; if these conditions are violated, the flavor will be ruined. The new wine is not very good; it must be stored for more than ten years to become a top-grade wine. One jar can be worth four or five gold pieces, but it is often given as gifts, and they are ashamed to sell it. Moreover, large families like Dai, Lu, Liu, Wang, Zhang, and Wei have mostly declined, and those who brew it are also few, therefore it is especially rare. Sometimes it is transported to other places, whether carried by shoulder, cart, or boat, if it is shaken, the flavor will change.

After the wine arrives, it must be left in a quiet place for half a month to regain its flavor. When taking it to pour into a pot, one should use a ladle to scoop it evenly. If stirred or moved around several times, the flavor will also change, and it will return after being left to rest for several more days. Mr. Yao An once said: "There are a hundred taboos and ten thousand kinds of labor involved in drinking Cang wine; one can only get a sip under the flowers and moon, and the effort does not compensate for the trouble. It is better to send a small servant to buy it casually, and one can be pleasantly self-satisfied instead." This is why. The method to test authenticity is as follows: those brewed with water from Nan Chuan Lou, even if one gets very drunk, the chest does not feel bad; the next day, even if one gets drunk, one does not suffer from hangover; one just feels the limbs are comfortable and can sleep soundly. Those brewed with water from the Wei River are not like that. The method to test new and old is as follows: those stored for two years can be warmed once more; those stored for ten years can be warmed ten times without change; on the eleventh warming, the flavor changes. Those stored for one year change on the second warming; those stored for two years change on the third warming. Not even a tiny bit can be faked, and no one knows why. Mr. Dong Qujiang's uncle, named Si Ren, loved drinking the most. When he was governor of Cangzhou, he knew that good wine should not be given to officials, and he tried every means to persuade people, but they finally refused to break the prohibition. After resigning, he returned to Cangzhou and stayed at Mr. Li Ruidian's home. He then poured out all his family's wine and said to Ruidian: "I deeply regret not resigning earlier." This was just a joke at the time, but it also shows that good Cang wine is hard to come by.

My esteemed teacher, Mr. Li Yodan, said that there was a man from Dongguang named Zhao, whom my teacher once mentioned by his courtesy name, though I cannot recall it now. He seemed to be a respected figure among my teacher's peers. Once, while passing through Qingfeng Inn, he hired a young courtesan to entertain him with wine. During their conversation, he casually mentioned staying in a certain year, when he had invited a beautiful woman to stay for two nights. Calculating her age, she was not yet forty. He then mentioned her given name, and the courtesan was startled and said, "That is my aunt! She is still alive." The next day, they went to her home together, and she recognized them as old acquaintances. As they greeted each other, her grandmother heard that a guest had appeared and was even more startled, saying, "Is that Zhao from Dongguang? We haven't seen each other for over thirty years. Though her hair is almost white, her appearance and voice are still somewhat recognizable. Aren't you called [the name]?" When asked, she also remembered that he was a young man she had once known. Three generations gathered in one room, with no taboos or inhibitions. They passed cups and talked about old times, feeling dazed as if in a dream. They stayed at her home for two more nights before parting. Before leaving, he mentioned that her ancestral hometown was Dongguang, and that since her grandfather moved there, they were now in the fourth generation. He did not know if the ancestral tomb still existed. He then mentioned his grandfather's name and asked to inquire about it. After Zhao returned home, he casually asked the elderly of the village, and one of them was stunned for a long time, saying, "I only now begin to believe in the way of heaven." That man was a guest in your family. Your great-grandfather was involved in a lawsuit, and that man accepted money from the enemy, secretly acting as a double agent. The lawsuit was not resolved, and after a long time, the matter was exposed. Feeling ashamed, he fled with his family, thinking he was far away in the ends of the earth. Unexpectantly, he met you, causing three generations of women to repay his karmic debt. Alas, how terrible!

The Mr. Dan again said: There was a person named An Sheng, who was quite intelligent. Suddenly, he was sucked up to the ceiling by a group of fox spirits, who played bamboo flutes and tuned silk strings, served food, and encouraged drinking, engaging in extremely intimate, flirtatious, and wanton behavior. Listening through the paper, it was very clear, but the ceiling initially had no tiny gaps, so it was puzzling how they could enter. After the banquet ended, they threw him down from the air, injuring his head and face, sometimes to the point of breaking bones and bleeding. After being treated and slightly recovering, they sucked him up again as before. When the ceiling was destroyed, they sucked him up to the roof, and they threw him down as before. However, he did not complain about the suffering. His father bought a talisman and hung it on the wall. When An Sheng saw it, he trembled and crouched on the ground, and the fox spirits also died out. The father asked what An Sheng saw on the talisman, and he said he initially did not see the talisman, but only saw fierce soldiers with spears and armor, shining brightly. Did the fox spirits consider this an enemy? They should not have had intimate and affectionate joy. Should they have been considered flattering? They should not have had cruel and violent actions. Suddenly happy, suddenly angry, all cannot fathom their intentions. Some say it is an enemy, flattering them led to death without realization. But flattering them was enough to cause their death, why bother with an extra throw?

Li Hui-chuan said, there was a Mr. Yan, whose name and courtesy name were forgotten, who, as the provincial examination approached and the students had dispersed, was reading by lamplight at night. A young servant entered to deliver tea, suddenly lost his voice and fell to the ground, causing the bowl to shatter with a loud noise. Yan was startled and looked up to see a ghost with disheveled hair and glaring eyes standing before the lamp. Yan laughed and said, "How can there be ghosts in the world? You must be a cunning thief disguising yourself as a ghost, wanting me to run away." "I have nothing of value," he continued, "only a pillow and a mat; you can go elsewhere." The ghost still did not move. Yan became angry and said, "Still trying to deceive me?" He picked up a ruler and struck the ghost, which vanished suddenly. Yan looked around and found no trace of the ghost, pondering and saying, "Was there really a ghost?" He then added, "The soul ascends to heaven, the body descends to earth; this principle is very clear. There are no ghosts in the world; it must be a fox spirit." He continued to read by the lamp, reciting loudly without stopping. This man's stubbornness was extreme, yet even the ghost avoided him.

Indeed, a resolute spirit, unyielding despite repeated setbacks, is also sufficient to overcome adversity. I once heard of a Confucian scholar walking along a corridor at night when he suddenly saw a ghost. He called out to it and said, "Have you also been a human before? Why, once you became a ghost, are you now ignored by all?" "Is there not a time when it is deep into the night, dark and obscure, when one would not distinguish between inside and outside, and yet dare to enter the courtyard?" The ghost then disappeared. This was because his heart was not frightened, so his spirit was not confused or agitated, and thus the ghost could not harm him. Another account tells of Mr. Shen Nonggong from the old city, whose given name was Dingxun, and who was a fellow examinee of Yao'an Gong. One night, on his way home, he encountered rain, and the ground was covered in mud and water. He walked with a servant, supporting each other, but could not find his way. They passed by an abandoned temple, which was said to be haunted. Mr. Shen said, "No one is here to ask, so let us go into the temple and ask the ghosts ourselves." He went straight in and, walking around the temple corridors, called out, "Ghost brothers, ghost brothers, could you tell me how deep the water is ahead?" There was no response at all. Mr. Shen laughed and said, "I suppose the ghosts are all asleep; I will take a short nap as well." So he and his servant leaned against a pillar and slept until dawn. This was because his heart was open and unburdened, so he treated the whole situation as a game.

When General Awencheng pacified Ili, he captured a Ma Ha Qin in an empty mountain. He questioned him about how he managed to survive, and the Ma Ha Qin replied, "I hunted for food." When asked how he obtained such a large amount of gunpowder after being hidden for a long time, he answered, "Dung beetles are dried and ground into powder, mixed with deer blood, and dried again. This can also serve as a substitute for gunpowder, though it is slightly weaker than saltpeter and sulfur." Another Mongol prince said, "After loading gunpowder and lead bullets into a bird gun, if you take a dried dung beetle and insert it with a thin stick, the range can be extended by one or two dozen steps." This is a physical phenomenon that is hard to understand, but it has been tested and found to be effective. A physician named Yin Zanan said, "Mercury can corrode five metals. Gold turns white when it meets mercury, and lead turns into liquid when it meets mercury. For lead bullets used in battle that become lodged in bone or flesh, cutting them out causes extreme pain. However, if mercury is poured into the wound, the lead will turn into water and be expelled along with the mercury." It is unknown whether this is true, but it is reasonable to believe.

田 Bailyang said, there was a scholar who rented a room in a temple. On the wall hung a painting of a beautiful woman, whose eyes and eyebrows looked lifelike, and whose clothes fluttered as if in motion. The scholar asked the monk: "Do you not fear that this disturbs your meditation?" The monk replied: "This is a painting of a celestial maiden scattering flowers, by the artist Dufengmu. It has been in the temple for over a hundred years, and I have not had time to examine it closely." One evening, while focusing on the painting by the light of a lamp, he saw that the figure in the painting seemed to rise slightly, by an inch or two. The scholar said: "This is a Western-style perspective painting, so it appears to rise and fall; how could it be a work by Dufengmu?" Suddenly, a voice came from the painting: "I wish to descend, sir, do not be surprised." The scholar, who was usually stern and upright, shouted angrily: "What kind of demon or ghost dares to tempt me?" He quickly pulled the painting and intended to burn it with the lamp. The painting then wept and pleaded: "I am nearly complete in my process of refining my form. If I am handed over to the fire god, my physical form will disappear and my spirit will disperse, and all my previous efforts will be wasted. Please grant me mercy, and I will be eternally grateful."

The monk heard the disturbance and hurried to see the scholar, who told him the reason. The monk suddenly realized and said, "My disciple lived in this room, suffered from consumption and died, was it not because of you?" The demon did not respond. After a while, the monk said, "The Buddha's path is vast, what cannot it contain? The elder is compassionate, should save and liberate." The scholar angrily said, "You have killed one person, now you are being let go again, do not know how many more people you will kill. This is sparing one demon's life while destroying countless human lives. Small kindness is the thief of great kindness. Venerable one, do not be stingy." He then threw it into the furnace. Smoke and flames rose, the stench of blood filled the room, suspected that more than one monk was killed. Later at night, there were sometimes faint cries. The scholar said, "The demon's lingering energy has not dissipated, it may soon reassemble into a form." Only yang strength can break through yin evil, so he bought dozens of strings of firecrackers. In the capital, they are called fire whips. He summarized the fuse lines into one, when the sound was heard, suddenly detonated. Windows and doors all shook, from then on, it became quiet. Eliminate evil thoroughly, this scholar has done so.

A man had a friend who was a celestial fox, a being of great supernatural power. This fox could transport him thousands of miles away, allowing him to freely explore famous mountains and scenic spots. With a snap of its fingers, the fox could whisk him away and bring him back as if it were just a single room. The fox once said that it dared not go to places where sages and saints resided, nor to places where true spirits dwelled, but it would consult maps and records and go wherever its will desired. One day, this man asked the fox, "Can you carry me beyond the nine regions and place me in someone's inner chamber?" The fox asked why, and he replied, "I once visited a friend's home and attended a musical banquet in the back courtyard. His beloved concubine and I formed a connection through our gaze, though we never exchanged a word, our hearts understood each other. But the courtyard was deep and secluded, separated by a wide stream, and I could only long for her. Can you, in the quiet of a deep night, transport me to her inner chamber? My wish will surely be fulfilled." The fox pondered for a long time and said, "There is no reason why not. But if the owner is there, what should we do?" The man said, "I will check if she is staying with another concubine tonight, and then you can take me there." The fox later confirmed that the owner was indeed staying elsewhere and agreed to accompany the man. Without waiting for him to dress, the fox swiftly carried him in flight to a place and said, "Here we are." With a sudden disappearance, the man groped in the dark, hearing no human voices, only feeling that everything around him was scrolls and books, realizing that he had been transported to the owner's study.

Knowing he was being toyed with by the fox, he panicked and made a mistake, accidentally knocking over a table. The objects on the table fell onto the floor with a loud crashing sound, and the guard cried out that there was a thief. The servants rushed in, unlocked the door, and lit the candles, carrying weapons inside. They saw someone cowering behind the screen and all rushed forward to strike and knock him down, tying him up tightly with rope. By the light of the lamp, they recognized him and were both greatly shocked and surprised. This person was indeed cunning and clever, claiming that he had accidentally offended a friend of the fox and was brought here. The master, being well acquainted with him, clapped and teased, saying, "This fox was playing a prank, wanting me to beat you severely, but for now, let's spare the beating and just expel him." So he sent a servant to escort him back. One day, he spoke about it with his close friend, and cursed, saying, "The fox is indeed not a human, having been with me for over ten years, and sold me to this place." The close friend angrily said, "You have been with someone for over ten years, and used the fox's power to disrupt their private life. Who is not a human here?" The fox, though angry at your lack of righteousness, used a game to warn you, but still left you a way to resolve things yourself, showing great kindness and leniency. If it waited for you to be dressed in fine clothes, secretly took you and placed you under the master's bed, what excuse would you have to cover yourself? From this perspective, that fox is like a human, and you are a human who acts like a fox. Are you not reflecting on yourself? This person felt ashamed and left, and the fox no longer came. The close friend also cut off ties with him. Guo Tonglun had a connection with the close friend, so he got the details.

An elderly scholar named Liu Taiyu, whose courtesy name was Dingguang, made a living by teaching. A doctor from Zhejiang, who had brought his young son to live in the area, found that the two men got along well and decided to live as neighbors. The child was graceful and handsome, and the doctor regarded Liu Taiyu as his teacher. With no other relatives, the doctor entrusted his son to Liu Taiyu in a state of imminent death, and Liu Taiyu treated him as his own son. One winter night, they shared a bed, and there was a man named Yang Jia, whom Liu Taiyu had not treated politely, who fabricated slander, saying that Liu Taiyu was involved in inappropriate behavior with the doctor's son. Liu Taiyu was angry and resentful, and when he asked about the child, he learned that the child still had an uncle who was a clerk on a grain transport ship. Liu Taiyu then took the child to Cangzhou, where he rented a small house to live in. He saw the grain transport ships from Zhejiang and called out to them from a distance, asking if anyone knew of a certain Mr. So-and-so. After several days, he finally found the uncle and handed the child over to him. The uncle wept and said, "Last night, I dreamed that my brother told me that my nephew would return, so I have been sitting alone on the ship's bridge every day, looking out for him."

My brother also mentioned Yang's matter, saying that I was justified before the gods, but I did not understand what he meant. Taiyu also did not clearly state it, and returned home dejectedly. The pedantic scholar who was overly cautious always thought that this matter could not be clarified, and died due to accumulated worries causing illness. Under the lamp and moon, Yang always saw him glaring. Yang was naturally fierce and did not take it seriously, and after several years, he also died. His wife remarried, leaving behind a beautiful and elegant son, who was lured by a promiscuous official's son into being a boy lover, and paraded through the streets, causing those who saw it all to sigh. Taiyu was said to be from Sucheng, or Renqiu, or Gaoyang, but it is not known precisely, and he generally lived west of Hejian. Tracing his life, is he the kind of person who can be worshipped after death? This matter occurred during the middle years of the Kangxi era. Uncle Canchen, my third uncle, enjoyed talking about cause and effect and often used it as a warning, but eventually forgot about it. On the twelfth day of May in the year Wumu, while staying in a tent at Miyun, I woke up in the middle of the night, suddenly remembered, grieved that his name and surname were obscure, and after reaching Lanyang, I recorded the general outline as above.

<draft> Chang Sifu, a native of Zhenfan, was captured during the early years of the Kangxi reign for following a crowd in pillaging. He was sentenced to death, but his great-great-grandfather, Guangji Gong, who was then the commandant of Zhenfan, found his appearance extraordinary and petitioned Deputy General Han to spare him. Guangji Gong also provided him with a salary and took him as a personal attendant. After Guangji Gong retired from office and returned home, he sent Chang Sifu back to his family, but Chang Sifu stayed and did not return. His great-uncle, Zhongxiu Gong, once remarked: Chang Sifu was exceptionally agile and skillful. When young, Zhongxiu Gong saw him hanging upside down on the battlements of a watchtower with his feet, sweeping the snow from the bricks, clearing the area all around. Many notorious bandits can climb by placing their feet upward and hands downward, embracing the corner of a building. Near the battlements, bricks protrude three inches and are set around, making it impossible to climb because the feet cannot hang in mid-air. This is commonly called the "brick line." Holding a broom, he descended gracefully, like a bird landing, truly a strong and capable man. Later, Guangji Gong married him and he had children. It is said that he still has descendants today, who farm the land of the four estates. </draft>

Door couplets had already existed by the late Tang Dynasty. Xin Yinxun from Shu wrote a peach wood charm for Meng Chang: "New Year welcomes surplus blessings, joyful festival called Eternal Spring." But nowadays, writing them on red paper is different. My hometown friend Zhang Qingfeng, before New Year's Eve, wrote his own door couplet: "Three rooms leaning east and west, one person refined through repeated trials." Coincidentally, someone who had been through trials asked Peng Xinfu to write a door couplet, and Peng Xinfu humorously wrote these two lines for him. The two households faced each other, and everyone who saw them couldn't help but laugh. The two men were actually classmates in the imperial examination in 1891 and had a close relationship, but because of this, they ended up becoming estranged. All joking is without benefit, and this is one example. Another example is the elder Dong Qujiang, who enjoyed witty humor. In his hometown, there was a theatrical performance at a funeral, and he was asked to write a title on the stage. Dong Qujiang wrote "Those who mourn are greatly pleased," which became the subject of gossip in the whole county, causing the person involved to gnash his teeth in anger and almost be falsely accused. Later, Dong Qujiang regretted his actions and often used this as a warning to his friends.

Zhang Qiu Yuan recounted that there was a man named Zhang who, in his youth, served as an assistant in the local government offices. By middle age, he had sufficient means to live comfortably and chose to retire, spending his time cultivating flowers and bamboo for pleasure. One day, while he was away for several days, his wife suddenly passed away, and he was unable to bid her farewell. He often felt a deep sense of loss and longing. One night, under the lamp, she appeared before him, and they embraced in a mix of sorrow and joy. She said, "Since I was taken away, I had minor sins to atone for, which kept me bound until now. Now that they have been resolved, I can enter the cycle of rebirth. But since I still have several years to go, I feel grateful for your remembrance and have asked the officials of the underworld to allow me to visit you. It is also because our karmic ties are not yet finished." They spent time together as if they were living their lives as before. From then on, she would always come at the hour of the human and leave at the crowing of the rooster, showing increased affection and tenderness. However, she never mentioned household matters or asked about their children. She said, "The world is noisy and chaotic, and those in the underworld have escaped the sea of suffering; I do not wish to hear of it." One night, she arrived a few moments early and did not respond much when he spoke. She said, "Wait a little, you will understand soon." Soon after, another woman entered, pulling back the curtain, and she looked exactly like the first, except for her attire. The first woman was shocked and exclaimed, "This is a lustful ghost disguising itself to seduce; the gods will not tolerate you!"

After the secondary wife hurriedly left the house, this woman took Zhang's hand and wept, leaving Zhang bewildered and unsure of what to do. She said, "Hungry ghosts often assume names to beg for food, and lustful ghosts often take on forms to seduce. Many spiritual words are not genuine. This ghost was originally a prostitute from the western market, who took advantage of your thoughts and came through an opening to steal your yang energy. Just now, another ghost informed me, so I lodged a complaint with the local deity to have her expelled. She has surely been beaten by then. Where is she now?" she asked. "Originally, I had a karmic connection with you for another life. Because I outwardly observed propriety while serving my in-laws but inwardly resented them, when they were ill, I neither hoped for their death nor urgently sought their recovery. I was recorded by the divine and descended as your concubine. Furthermore, holding private grudges, I provoked you with words, leading to your brothers not being very close, and I descended again as a maidservant. I will be born after more than twenty years after the master. Now, I still drift among the ruins and graves." Zhang led her into the curtain and said, "The separation between the underworld and the living world fears punishment in the afterlife. In the next life, this wish will be completed." She cried several times and then disappeared. At that time, Zhang's parents had passed away, and only his brother lived separately. Zhang then visited his brother and fully recounted the matter, showing the same affection as before.

A widow, not yet twenty years old, had only one son, who was just three or four years old. Her home was as poor as it could be, with nothing but four bare walls, and she had few relatives. Thus, they decided to arrange her marriage. The woman was quite beautiful, and her distant relative, Mr. Jia, secretly sent an old woman to persuade her, saying: "I have no right to marry you according to propriety, but I think about you so much that it disrupts my sleep and meals. If you can pretend to remain loyal to your vows while secretly being intimate with me, I will provide you with a certain amount of money each month, enough to support you and your son." Although their homes were in different alleys, the back rooms were separated by only one wall, allowing them to come and go via a ladder, and no one could see them. The woman was persuaded by his words and began to go in and out like a concubine. People wondered how she could support herself, but there was no evidence to be seen, so they assumed she still had some savings. After a long time, Mr. Jia's servants revealed the affair. Since her son was young, she sent him to stay at an external school. By the time he was seventeen or eighteen, he also heard some of the gossip. He often wept and tried to advise her, but she did not listen. They engaged in intimate and mixed seating, deliberately allowing him to see and hear, hoping to stop him from speaking out.

A Man's Act of Filial Piety and Its Aftermath The man was greatly angered, and so he entered the home of someone named Jia during the day, stabbing him in the heart and the sword came out from the back. He did this because his attempt to borrow money had failed, and he had been insulted. His anger led to his killing, and he confessed to the authorities. The officials investigated and discovered the truth, but despite their best efforts to persuade him, he refused to reveal the real story. In the end, he was sentenced for intentional murder. The villagers pitied him. Well-wishers wanted to erect a stone tablet to mark his tomb and asked the respected elder Zhu Meiyi for a written tribute. One night before, Zhu Meiyi dreamed of this person, who appeared with a sad and dejected expression, bowing and standing respectfully. At this point, Zhu Meiyi suddenly woke up and said, "This should not be done." If we do not record the truth, then he is just a wicked person, how can we mark his tomb? If we record the truth, then we will highlight the name of the filial son, but just hurting the heart of the filial son. This is not for the purpose of comforting his spirit. So, he strongly opposed and canceled the matter. That night, he dreamed again of the person bowing and leaving. This person willingly sacrificed his life to avenge his father's grudge, and also did not expose his mother's fault to disgrace his father. He can be said to skillfully handle changes in human relationships. Someone said, "Cutting off his ancestral line, the ancestors will grieve. Why not wait until he has a child and do it then?" This is the excessive blame of scholars, not something I dare to hear.

The schemes of petty people often inadvertently benefit the virtuous. This statement may seem roundabout but is actually true. Li Yunju recounted that when his brother, Xianwei, was an official in Guangdong, he heard of a wandering scholar with an eccentric personality who had traveled over the mountains to visit old acquaintances and gained some benefits. Upon returning, besides his belongings of bedding, clothes, and shoes, he had two large boxes that required four people to carry, and no one knew what they contained. One day, at a place where they were changing boats, the two sides of the boat were connected by a thick rope, and they carried the boxes across. Suddenly, the four ropes all broke as if cut by a knife, and the boxes fell with a loud thud onto the deck, both breaking open. They stamped their feet in grief and regret, quickly opened the boxes to inspect their contents, and found one box filled with a new high-quality inkstone and another with Yingde stone. Inside the Yingde stone box was a bundle of silver, about sixty to seventy taels, wrapped in paper that had also torn. Just as they were picking it up to examine it, it slipped from their hands and fell into the water. They asked a fisherman to dive into the water to retrieve it, but only a small part was recovered. Just as they were feeling dejected, a fellow traveler on the boat suddenly congratulated them, saying: "Thieves have been following these two boxes for several days. Because there were people on the shore, they dared not act, so I was worried and didn't dare to say anything. Now that we see they are not valuable items, the thieves have already left." "You are truly a fortunate person, or perhaps you have been blessed by your good deeds and divine protection," the fellow traveler added. Another passenger on the boat whispered privately: "What good deeds has he done? He has just recently done a foolish thing. While he was in Guangdong, he once entrusted the innkeeper with 120 taels of silver to buy a concubine, claiming she was a newly married wife who, due to extreme poverty, had sold herself to survive. On the day she arrived, her parents-in-law and her husband came to see her off, all looking as weak and sick as beggars. As they were about to enter the room, they embraced each other, weeping and bidding farewell."

After parting, they still took a few steps, continuing to chat. The matchmaker forcibly dragged the woman inside. Her father, holding a baby no more than a few months old, bowed deeply to him and said, "This child has lost its mother's milk, and his life or death is uncertain. Please allow his mother to breastfeed him once, and we will delay matters until tomorrow." Suddenly, the man leaped up and said, "I thought the woman was being sent away. Now, seeing the pitiful situation, it deeply moves my heart. I will take your wife away, and the money need not be paid back." He reflected, "From ancient times to now, people are not far apart in their nature. How could I not do what Feng Jing's father did?" He then burned the contract in front of everyone. Unbeknownst to him, the owner had seen his sincerity and deceived him by pretending his own daughter was the woman. If he had accepted, there might have been another scheme. Everyone in the lodging knew, but he still hasn't realized it. Could the gods have recorded his good deed? Another guest said, "This is indeed a good deed. Although the matter seems foolish, his heart was truly driven by compassion. The gods observe and judge, but they also observe the heart. Today, he avoided disaster, and it was because of this matter."

The innkeeper, still unaware of the true situation. My esteemed teacher, the venerable Master Danyan, is the elder brother of Yunju. He said to Yunju: "I find the guest's argument to be correct." I also recall what Mr. Yao An said: During the Western Campaign of Tian Zhen, he sent Li Hu, the defender of Pinglu Road, along with a Thousand Commander, leading three hundred soldiers, to patrol the border. Suddenly, they encountered the Oirats coming via a secret route. The Thousand Commander told Li Hu: "The enemy's horses are strong; if we retreat, we will surely be caught up with. Please lead the front unit to block the mountain pass, and we two will lead the rear unit to assist you." The enemy did not know how many we were, so we could still defend. Li Hu thought this was right and led the troops to fight fiercely. However, the Thousand Commander had already fled first. It seems that he deceived Li Hu into fighting to delay time, and when Li Hu was defeated, the Thousand Commander had already fled far away. Li Hu then died in battle. Later, his son Xianjie was bestowed the same rank as his father. Although Li Hu was deceived and defeated, being deceived just completed his loyalty. Therefore, it is said: "The schemes of the wicked never fail to benefit the virtuous." This statement may seem roundabout but is indeed true.

Yun Ju also reported that there was a man who was the richest in the entire village, with thousands of shi of grain stored. When a bad harvest occurred, he closed his store and refused to sell. Suddenly one day, he gathered his servants and arranged measuring tools, wrote a red note by hand, and posted it at the door saying, "During a bad harvest when people are hungry, how can one person be the only one who is full? Now, I am planning to lend all the grain I have accumulated over the years to my neighbors, each getting one shi as a rule. On the same day, each should bring their bags and come to collect; if they wait, the grain will run out." The nearby residents heard the news and came in large numbers, and within a day, the grain was all gone. Some people asked to see the owner to express their gratitude, but the owner had disappeared. The officials hastily searched and found him in a long-sealed dilapidated house, deeply asleep. Upon waking, he stretched and yawned, and the crowd was surprised and helped him up. From his side, they found a note that read, "Accumulating wealth without sharing it is a source of resentment. Resentment gathers, and so does disaster. When a thousand families are hungry and one is full, robbery and looting are inevitable. Grateful for your old kindness, I am doing this to repay your virtue. Hope for forgiveness for acting independently, this is deeply prayed for." He did not understand what was being said, and upon inquiry, learned the whole story and sighed. At the time, the people were restless, and there was actually a plan to burn and loot. By doing this broad distribution, it turned disaster into good fortune. This shape-shifting demon can be said to love people with virtue. As for the old kindness, the reason is unknown. Some say that in his home garden, there was an old house where foxes lived for decades, and the house fell down before they moved away.

As a child, I heard my wet nurse Li say that there was a household adjacent to a Buddhist temple. Once, a small fox accidentally jumped down from the temple corridor and was caught by the children, who tied it up and beat it. The fox was frightened and remained still. When released, it would roam freely within the courtyard but would not go elsewhere. When offered food, it would eat; when not given any, it would not dare to steal. When hungry, it would simply wag its tail. When called, it seemed to understand human speech, and when directed, it seemed to understand human intentions. The entire family pitied it and constantly forbade the children from mistreating it. One day, the fox suddenly spoke in human language, saying, "My name is Xiao Xiang. I am a servant in the fox family on the bell tower. I accidentally played and made a mistake, and because your children are mischievous, I was punished for a month and subjected to their abuse. Now my punishment is over, and I must return, so I am bidding farewell. Why don’t I try to escape?" it asked. "My master has raised me for many years. How could I think of escaping?" it replied. After finishing its speech, the fox bowed its head and gracefully leaped over the wall to leave. At the same time, a small servant in my household had stolen something and fled. Upon hearing this, my wet nurse sighed and said, "This servant is not as good as this fox."

A junior official named Chen Yunting recounted that in the deep mountains of his hometown, there was an abandoned Buddhist temple, said to be inhabited by spirits. No one could restore it. A monk of high moral character and purity went directly to establish a hermitage there. In the first two nights, it seemed as if something was watching or lurking, but the monk did not hear or see anything, and the presence remained unseen and unheard. On the third or fourth night, a night demon pushed open the door and entered, appearing fierce and aggressive, spewing fire and smoke. Despite this, the monk remained calm and focused in meditation, and the demon attacked the monk's meditation cushion several times but never got close. By dawn, the demon departed with a long, loud cry. The next night, a beautiful woman arrived, bowed, and asked about the essential teachings of Buddhism. The monk did not respond. She then recited the Diamond Sutra clearly and distinctly, asking the monk after each section what it meant, but the monk still did not answer.

A woman suddenly began to spin and dance for a long time, shaking her sleeves as something rustled and fell to the ground. She asked, "How does this compare to scattering flowers?" Dancing and retreating at the same time, she disappeared in an instant. The ground was covered with children about an inch tall, creeping around in the thousands and tens of thousands. They struggled to climb onto shoulders and reach the top, crawling through the clothes. Some bit or scratched, like mosquitoes, flies, lice, and fleas gnawing; others picked at the ears and eyes, tearing at the mouth and nose, like the venomous sting of snakes and scorpions. Picking them up and throwing them to the ground, they burst with a sound, one splitting into dozens, adding more and more. Left to support, right to lean, unable to bear the strain, the narrator eventually collapsed on the meditation couch. After a long time, regaining consciousness, there was nothing left. The monk sighed and said, "This is a demon, not an illusion. Only the power of the Buddha can subdue demons, which is beyond my ability. A monk does not stay three nights under a tree, why should one be attached to this place?" At dawn, he packed his belongings and returned. The narrator said, "This monk made up this allegory, perhaps likening a virtuous person's frustration with a group of petty people. However, it also serves as a warning to those who lightly taste such things." Cloud Pavilion said, "I have no talents, but I have never told a lie. This monk passed by my house on his way back, with fine bloodstains on his face like tangled hair, which I actually saw with my own eyes."

The old servant Liu Ting declared: In the early years of Emperor Yongzheng's reign, the tenant farmer Zhang Huang built a round hut—commonly called a round hut, with the character "焦" being a phonetic variant, the two characters appearing in the annals of the Northern Qi dynasty—to guard his melons. At night, he often saw a person walking slowly and heavily, moving northwest. One night, he secretly followed to see where he was going and saw him reach a cluster of graves, where over ten female ghosts came out to greet and immediately engaged in intimate and playful behavior. He knew they were demonic beings, but they seemed dull and incapable. So he hid a firelock in the round hut and waited for them every night. One night, he saw him pass by again, fired the firelock suddenly, and the sound was loud as he fell to the ground. He held a torch and rushed to look, and it turned out to be an old man. The next day, he piled up wood and burned it to ashes, and there were no other strange occurrences.

At night, I dreamed of more than ten women bowing in a circle, saying: "This monster, whose origin is unknown, is as strong as a bear or tiger. All newly buried female ghosts, regardless of age, are forced into sexual acts. Those who resist are jumped upon several times by the entity, causing the grave to collapse and the coffin to break, leaving them with no place to rest. Thus, they dare not resist, but they harbor resentment for a long time. Now that it has been driven away, we come to thank you. Later, someone from Gaocun mentioned that at the tomb of Feng Dao in Shirenwa—Feng Dao, a person from Jingcheng, whose residence is still known as Xiangguozhuang, about two or three miles from Jingcheng, and whose tomb is now in Shirenwa—I saw remnants of stone animals and stone officials when I was young, and some still exist. The local gazetteer states that the location of the tomb is unknown, which is a mistake carried over from the old records. Suddenly, one of the stone figures disappeared, and I realized it was the entity. This entity, from the Five Dynasties to the present, began to form into a shape. The passage of time is not short, and it just began to transform, indulging in wickedness and lust, and ultimately brought about its own destruction by fire. This is similar to the account of a wooden puppet mentioned by Shao Er Yun. All serve as a warning to those with small minds who are easily filled with pride."

Uncle Zhang Gong of Diezhuang had a study room that was spacious and well-lit, surrounded by a veranda, with thirty to forty peony plants inside. During the flowering season, their fragrance wafted over the neighboring walls. One day, a guest named Mr. Min, accompanied by a servant, stayed in the study room. That night, after settling into bed, Mr. Min suddenly heard a woman’s voice outside, saying: "The young lady greets Mr. Min. Today the flowers are blooming, and the moon is beautiful. She invites a few companions to come and enjoy the view. There will be no harm to Mr. Min, so please do not open the door abruptly; this will show your great tolerance." Mr. Min was too startled to respond and remained silent. Soon after, he faintly heard the sound of footsteps and the rustling of clothing. Peering through the papered window, he saw no one. Listening closely, he occasionally heard soft whispers, but the words were indistinct. Unable to sleep, he tossed and turned all night. After the third drumbeat, he thought he heard footsteps again. Soon, dogs began barking in the neighboring courtyard, then in the nearby houses, and finally in the alley, one after another. The barking echoed towards the northeast, and Mr. Min wondered if they had left. Fearing that he might have angered them and invited misfortune, he dared not open the door. The next morning, he went out to look, but found no trace of the visitors. Only on the western veranda did he see faint imprints on the dust, which resembled the shape of a bow, though it was not clear. It turned out to be a fox girl. My grandfather, Snow Peak Gong, remarked: "To enjoy the flowers like this, why bother to ask the owner? It seems Mr. Min acted rashly and coarsely, fearing that he might accidentally run into them and ruin the mood."

In Cangzhou, there was a man named Dong Hua who, despite his efforts, failed to achieve success in his studies and thus drifted into a lowly position as a clerk in a market or shop. He was unable to perform well in his subordinate role and was subsequently pushed aside and excluded from his position. In order to make ends meet, he took up the low-status occupation of selling medicine and fortune-telling, which led to his extreme poverty, to the point where he had no place to stand. He lived with his mother and wife, who supported him through their sewing, weaving, and washing, but they still struggled to afford basic necessities. During a year of widespread famine, he found himself isolated and starving, with the dire situation threatening to lead to their collective demise. Hearing that a wealthy man in a neighboring village was buying a concubine, he consulted with his mother and devised a plan to sell his wife in order to survive. Initially, his wife refused, but Dong Hua argued that losing one's virtue was a grave matter, and that allowing his mother to starve to death would be even worse. Overcome with emotion, she reluctantly agreed, with the condition that if they managed to survive, she would request to remain his wife. Dong Hua promised to honor her request.

The woman was naturally beautiful, and the wealthy man greatly favored her. However, there were often tear marks on the pillow and bed, indicating her emotional distress during intimacy. The wealthy man asked her about this, and she answered resolutely: "My body belongs to you, and everything can be done according to your will. To the point of feeling and remembering old kindnesses, even if knives and saws are in front of me, I still cannot sever this thought." Just as the famine struck again, Hu and her mother both became starving corpses. The wealthy man feared a change in her emotional state and hid the truth from her. A neighbor woman accidentally revealed it to her. The woman did not cry at all but sat idly for a long time, then told her maidservants: "The reason I have endured this stain in silence is one to save my mother-in-law and husband's lives, and one because the master is already over seventy. I estimate that within a few years, he will be in his coffin. I am still young, and I calculate that his son will surely not keep me. I still hope that the broken moon will be reunited."
