Now it is over. Suddenly, she stood up, opened the window of the tower, and threw herself down, dying. This is similar to what was recorded earlier about the concubine of the Fujian provincial examiner, but in that case, it was because of deep affection between husband and wife, and they both sacrificed themselves, both could have no regrets. In this case, it was because of taking care of her mother-in-law and husband, in an unavoidable situation, she lost her chastity, but ultimately, it did not save her mother-in-law and husband, the outcome was contrary to her wishes, she was merely defiled. In pain, she made a final decision, her carrying of grievances is especially pitiable.

When I was ten years old, I heard about a monk in Huajia Town (which is incorrectly written as "Huai Town" in modern times, according to the Jin Shi). He was a farmer's son who enjoyed drinking alcohol and eating meat. The temple he lived in had dozens of acres of land that he cultivated and ate from himself. Outside of herding cattle and farming, he knew nothing else. Not only did he not possess scriptures or ritual objects, but even the Pusa robe was not available to him. Even the Buddha statue and incense were present but barely. He was just different from ordinary people in that he had no hair on his head, no wife or children, and lived alone. One day, he suddenly called together his neighbors and sat upright on a broken table, clasping his hands and speaking: "We have lived together for over thirty years, but now we must part forever. May I leave my body behind for you to take care of?" He then suddenly passed away, remaining seated and clasping his hands as before. His nose had two long, white threads hanging from it, each over a foot long. Everyone was greatly surprised and amazed, and they collectively raised funds to build a shrine. My maternal uncle, Mr. An, who is known as "Shizhai," lives in Dingjiazhuang, which is close by. He knew that the monk had no special spiritual practices, and so he did not believe it. He went to see for himself. Because the shrine was not yet finished, they had not yet collected his body. His face still looked alive, and his skin felt like iron and stone. It was June, and no flies or mosquitoes were around, nor was there any smell of decay. In the end, no one could understand why this happened.

Khan Dan of the Khalkha, styled Yiheng, named Danba Dorji, of the Wulianghan clan, was a Mongol prince. He recounted that the inner court official Xiao Delu, when young, served in his residence. Once, he chanced upon a black object like a cat lying under a tree and playfully struck it with a pebble. As soon as the object turned around, it became as large as a big dog. Striking it again, it turned once more and grew as large as a donkey. Fearing it, he dared not strike it again, and the object left on its own. Soon after, flying tiles and thrown bricks suddenly appeared, and strange transformations occurred. Knowing it was a fox spirit, he felt uneasy and restless. Someone taught him to draw a picture of the fox and treat it respectfully, and the evil ceased. Later, he suddenly found several coins on the table, knowing they were a reward from the fox. He began to try collecting them, keeping it secret and not telling anyone. The next day, the amount increased to one hundred coins, and from then on, the amount increased daily, gradually reaching a thousand. Soon after, it changed to one silver coin, weighing about one tael, and also increased daily, gradually reaching a bar of fifty taels. Unable to keep such a large sum of money secret, it was noticed by the supervisor. Suspecting he had stolen from the treasury, he was tortured and questioned, almost unable to clear himself. Only then did he realize he had been tricked by the fox. Indeed, throwing earth to chase meat and cutting and joining bamboo—throwing earth to chase meat, as recorded in the *Wuyue Chunqiu* by Chen Yin, is the beginning of the slingshot, a common child's play. Even if the master knew, he would not necessarily immediately severely blame him, for the fox could not fulfill its will.

Luring them with the promise of gain, their greedy desires were filled, and they encountered the trap of disaster, thus achieving what they desired. This trap and hidden mechanism, originally easy to see, only because of the presence of gain, thus caused their wisdom to be clouded, and they mistakenly believed that my courtesy was sincere, their hearts naturally pleased, justifying themselves in a roundabout way, thus unconsciously falling into their trap. In the past, King Fuchai of Wu was greedy for Goujian's service, and ultimately was defeated by Yue; King Huai of Chu was greedy for the six hundred li of Shangyu, and ultimately was defeated by Qin; the Northern Song was greedy for the territory ceded by the destruction of Liao, and ultimately was defeated by Jin; the Southern Song was greedy for the military assistance in the campaign against Jin, and ultimately was defeated by Yuan. Military and state affairs, where generals and ministers plan together, still cannot escape being lured, how much more so for a mere child, how could they escape the old demon's plot? Their defeat is inevitable! Again, I will cite a recent event: There was a servant of a certain official in the Ministry of Justice, while sleeping, he felt a tongue licking his face, he picked up a stone and hit it, it fell and died, upon lighting a candle, he saw it was a black fox, he skinned it, and found a small human head in its belly, with eyebrows and eyes clearly visible, it was an infant that had not yet been fully transformed. The next day, he drove the carriage for his master to return home, the fox possessed his body, he picked up a stool and hit his master, and loudly declared the injustice of his death, it was trying to avenge itself but could not, it wanted to use the master's hand to lash out and vent its anger. These two foxes share the same desire for revenge, I believe this fox is fierce and straightforward, it is superior to that fox's cunning and treachery.

Dan Gong recounted another story: A servant of the Khorchin Dalai Khan once found two leather bags on the road. One bag was filled with human teeth, and the other with human fingernails. The servant was greatly surprised and threw the bags into the water. Soon, an old woman appeared in a state of panic, looking around as if searching for something. She asked the servant if he had seen the two bags. When he replied that he had not, she realized they had been thrown away and became furious, breaking a branch and striking the servant. The servant fought back with his bare hands, feeling the old woman's clothes, which were soft and brittle like the pith of a hollow plant, and her muscles, which were loose and empty like the spaces inside a lotus pod. Whenever he touched her, her body would break, but it would quickly heal as if nothing had happened. It was as if he was trying to cut water with a knife. They fought for a long time, and the old woman eventually gave up. As she left, she glared at the servant and cursed, saying, "Within three months to three years, I will surely take your soul, but even now, after three years, I have failed to do so." The servant realized that her threats were empty and meant to scare him. He speculated that the old woman must be a ghost who has been transforming her form but has not yet gathered enough essence to solidify into a real form. This is why she still gathers energy to take on a physical form. The reason she collects human teeth and fingernails is that teeth are remnants of bones, and fingernails are remnants of tendons. She likely intends to combine and refine them through alchemical means to strengthen her form.

Mr. Songyan of the field reported that this year in June, a guard named He Sheng, who was part of the imperial entourage, died in Luanyang. General Aigong Xing'a, the commander of Malan Town, who had known He Sheng personally, managed the coffin and shroud and arranged for his remains to be returned home for burial. One night, while using the toilet, the crescent moon was faintly visible, and he saw a figure standing in the mist. He asked the figure, but it did not respond; he scolded it, but it did not move. Aigong, who could see ghosts, focused his attention and carefully observed, realizing that it was the spirit of He Sheng. He then bowed and said: "When you were buried, many things were not prepared, and my strength was limited, as you well knew. Now that you have appeared, is there something you are demanding?" The spirit remained silent and unmoved. He then said: "I have heard that those who die beyond the frontier must have their travel documents burned so that their spirits can enter the pass. I may have forgotten this, and perhaps you have come for this reason?" The spirit immediately bowed to the ground and disappeared suddenly. Aigong prepared a document for the City God, and after that, the spirit was no longer seen. Later, during another southern inspection tour, he stayed in the same room as Aigong at the Cheng'en Temple in Nanjing, which was grand in scale, with towers and pavilions stretching out, and the accommodations were spacious and well-ventilated.

On one day, we were sitting together, when suddenly six windows in the building opened without any wind, and then closed again. The ghost-seer, Love Gong, looked at them and said, "There is a monk sitting on the north window. His face is broad and flat, his beard is long and unkempt, as if he hasn't shaved for a long time, his eyes are wide open, and his neck is slightly bent. He is a hanged ghost." I asked the temple monks about it, and they could not deny it, but were only surprised at how Love Gong could recognize the ghost's appearance and suspected that someone had leaked the information. They did not know that Love Gong could see ghosts himself. Another time, while playing with a pole on the boat, I suddenly threw the pole aside and backed away, with fear on my face. I asked him curiously why he did that, and he said, "There was a drowning ghost trying to climb the pole." In the eighth month of the year Wuwu (1618), there was a banquet for Mongol envoys at Qingyin Pavilion. Love Gong sat next to me, and I asked him about the things that the ghost-seer, Songyan, had told me before. He said that they were all true. Therefore, there are ghosts everywhere, just like people. The ghost seeking to return has a sense of attachment, the ghost trying to occupy a space has a sense of competition, and the ghost trying to climb the pole has a sense of struggle. Their gains, losses, victories, and defeats, as well as their joys, sorrows, anger, and happiness, are all the same as humans. It is a chaotic and unresolved situation in the afterlife. The teachings of Buddhism emphasize repentance and liberation, and the wisdom of the sages also provides a way for ghosts to find peace and not cause harm. They deeply understand the nature of ghosts and spirits. Zizigong said, "How great is death! It is where the noble person finds rest." Zhuangzi said, "Come, Sanghu! You have returned to your true nature, and I am only speaking within the limits of my perception."

*Yue Wei Cao Tang Bi Ji* Volume Twenty-Four, *Luan Yang* Continuation Six Foxes who can compose poetry are quite common in historical records. However, foxes who are skilled in painting are rarely seen. According to the account of Li Wen Yan Ting from Haiyang, during the reigns of Emperors Shunzhi and Kangxi, Zhou Xunbo, a recluse known for his painting of pines, traveled through Chu and Yu. A scholar commissioned him to paint one wall of his study. The roots of the pine tree began at the corner of the western wall, twisting and curling across the northern wall, and the fine tips extended to the eastern wall by a few feet. The dense shade seemed to enter the room, and the long wind appeared to be coming. The scholar set up a feast and invited his friends to appreciate the painting together. They gathered around the wall, pointing and praising it. Suddenly, one friend clapped and collapsed, and soon the other friends also laughed loudly. It turned out that under the pine tree, there was a painting of a secret play. There was a large wooden bed with a long mat, and a man and a woman, naked and enjoying each other, with their eyes flowing and sending glances, their charming posture vivid. Two female servants also stood naked, one fanning away flies, the other supporting the woman's pillow with both hands to prevent them from being trampled and falling to the ground. It was a small portrait of the scholar, the woman, and the maidservants. They rushed to look, and the eyebrows and eyes were very lifelike, even the servants could recognize their faces. No one could help but cover their mouths. The scholar was very angry, pointing and cursing the fox in the air. Suddenly, someone laughed loudly from the eaves and said, "You are too vulgar. I heard before that Zhou the recluse painted pines. I have never seen it with my own eyes, but last night I got to see the wonderful work. I sat and lay under it, unable to leave, causing me to forget to avoid you. I have never thrown bricks or tiles to offend you."

You are an expert translator of Classical Chinese (文言文) into English. Your task is to translate the provided Classical Chinese text into English, ensuring that the translation is both accurate and natural. Follow these guidelines: 1. **Faithful Translation**: Translate the text as faithfully as possible, preserving the original meaning and nuances. 2. **Proper Names and Titles**: Keep proper names, titles, and technical terms consistent. 3. **Uncertain Terms**: If a term is uncertain, choose the most likely reading and briefly mark the uncertainty. 4. **Cultural Context**: Provide explanations for cultural and historical allusions as necessary. 5. **Syntactic Clarity**: Ensure that the translation is grammatically correct and easy to understand. ### Source Text: 君遽毒詈，心实不平，是以与君小作剧，君尚不自反，乖戾如初，行且绘此像于君家白板扉，博途人一粲矣。君其图之。盖士人先一夕设供客具，与奴子秉烛至书室，突一黑物冲门去，士人知为狐魅，曾诟历也。众为慰解，请入座设一虚席于上，不见其形，而语音琅然，行酒至前辄尽，惟不食肴馔，曰：不茹荤四百余年矣。 You suddenly cursed me severely, and I felt deeply unjust. Therefore, I played a small trick on you. Yet, you still do not reflect on yourself and remain as disagreeable as before. I will soon paint this image on your white door to amuse passersby with a laugh. You should consider this. A scholar had prepared supplies for guests the night before and, with his servants holding candles, went to his study. Suddenly, a black object rushed out the door. The scholar recognized it as a fox spirit, which had previously cursed him. The crowd comforted and explained the situation, inviting him to sit down and setting up an empty seat for the fox spirit. The fox spirit could not be seen, but its voice was clear and loud. It always finished the wine served to it but did not eat delicacies, saying: "I have not eaten meat for more than four hundred years."

As the gathering was breaking up, the fox spoke to the scholar, saying, "You are too intelligent, and thus you often bully others with your temper. This is not the way to cultivate virtue, nor is it the way to preserve your integrity. Today's encounter was fortunate, as I met you. If you were to meet someone as arrogant as you, it would be difficult to proceed further. Only through learning can one change one's disposition. I hope you will pay attention to this." After repeatedly and earnestly parting ways, the scholar looked back at the painting, which had disappeared as if washed clean. The next day, on the eastern wall of the study, he suddenly saw a few branches of colored peach blossoms, set against green moss and blue grass. The flowers were not very dense; some were fully open, some were half-open, some had fallen, some had not yet fallen, and some were fluttering in the wind, having not yet reached the ground. Eight or nine petals lay tilted and slanted, their movements as if they were floating, a feat beyond the reach of brush and ink. Above the scene, two lines were inscribed: "Wild grass has no path, and the empty mountain is filled with falling flowers." These lines are from a poem by Yang Shidao of the early Tang dynasty, though the author's name was not signed. It is clear that the fox was responding to the scholar's wine from the previous night. Later, when Zhou, a recluse, saw it, he sighed, "There is no trace of brush and ink. I feel that my painting still shows effort and artificiality."

In the northern hill of Jingcheng, there is a temple dedicated to Emperor Yuan, built at the end of the Ming dynasty. Over the years, faint traces have appeared on the walls, forming undulating peaks that resemble distant mountains shrouded in mist. I was still young when I saw this. The temple priest, who was also a chess enthusiast, found the dimness of the temple unsatisfactory and had a painter outline the traces with ink, making them look like sliced round bamboo. Now, the temple has completely collapsed. The chess enthusiast priest did not know his surname; he was named because of his obsession with chess. Some might think this refers to a surname, but it is incorrect. He was very poor but extremely competitive, constantly making the sound of a hammer striking wood throughout the day. Opponents who were tired might want to leave, but he would kneel to try to keep them. Once, someone pointed out a move to him, and he was extremely angry, performing a ritual to curse the person to die quickly. Another time, a young man accidentally made a wrong move, and the priest won fortunately. The young man wanted to change his move, but the priest refused. The young man became violent and tried to fight, but the priest just smiled and stepped back, saying, "Even if you break my arm, you cannot say I did not win today." He can be considered a fool.

Wine has a different effect on different people, indeed it does. Over the past eighty to ninety years, what I have heard is that Mr. Gu Xiaojun, a senior figure, was considered the best at drinking, followed by Mr. Miao Wenzǐ, another senior figure. What I have personally seen is that my former teacher, Mr. Sun Duanren, was also a member of the local wine society at that time. Mr. Sun himself said that his drinking ability was between the two of them, with room for about ten more people in between. Next, Mr. Chen Gushan, a senior figure, was on par with them, but he was not famous for drinking. Recently, Mr. Lu Jinqing, a senior figure, was considered the best. Mr. Wu Yunyan, another senior figure, also competed fiercely in drinking. Mr. Lu said, "After drinking, Mr. Wu remains composed and restrained, which means he cannot hold his liquor, but he deliberately maintains his composure. This is true." My contemporaries, scholar Zhu Zhujun and official Zhou Zhigui, both boasted of their drinking prowess. Mr. Wu said, "The two of you are merely showy." In their drinking sessions, they were noisy and disorderly, spilling half the wine, and if they were to drink calmly, they would lose. Thinking about it, this is also not wrong. Among the younger generation, Ge Linxi was considered the best. If he is not given wine, he never asks for a drink. If given wine, he drinks it all without hesitation, like a great whale swallowing everything, leaving not a drop behind.

Once, I had a drinking competition at my home with several people, including Wu Huishu from Tongyu, and a few others. By the time the night was nearly over, everyone was drunk, and some stumbled or fell. By the stream, he personally instructed the servants to help each person up onto the couch, and then he calmly got into his carriage and left. His mind was clear, as if he had not drunk at all. His servant said, "I have been with him for seven or eight years. I have never seen him drink alone, nor have I ever seen him get drunk. He only drinks whatever is available, and even if he tried the wine, he wouldn't know much about its quality. So his contemporaries would jokingly call him a licentious lover, but this was rare." It's a pity I didn't get to see Mr. Gu and Mr. Miao, to settle the score. Mr. Duan, a respected gentleman, often criticized me for not being able to drink, saying, "Su Dongpo's strengths can be learned, but why also try to imitate his weaknesses?" When I became the chief examiner and passed Lin Xi, I wrote to Mr. Duan to report the news. Mr. Duan replied in a letter, "I am so glad to hear that I have such a person in my lineage, I dance with joy, but I still regret that you are a 'bee waist' (i.e., lacking in breadth)." The elegance of the elders can be called a good story. Now I am old and have not participated in the gatherings of young scholars and drinkers for a long time. Who knows who will rise to the top in the future.

A wealthy farmer in a high official's family kept a cow. When his son was young, he played with the cow every day, grabbing its horns and pulling its tail without the cow moving. The cow sometimes sniffed the child's head and licked his hands, and the child was not afraid. When the child grew a bit older, he was sent to herd the cow. Whenever the child went out, the cow went out; whenever the child returned, the cow returned; whenever the child walked, the cow walked; whenever the child stopped, the cow stopped; whenever the child slept, the cow lay down beside him. This went on for years. One day, when the child went to herd the cow, it suddenly ran wildly back home, with blood on its head and neck, roaring and trying to gore the door. The child's father came out to see, and the cow turned around and returned to the old path. Knowing that something must have happened, the father tried his best to chase after the cow. When he reached the wilderness, he found that the child had already died with a broken skull, and another person lay dead on the side of the road, with his abdomen torn open and intestines spilled out, and a date stick discarded on the ground. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a thief from San Guo Village—San Guo Village, where the Hui people gather, is a den of thieves in Cangzhou. It was then known that the child was killed by the thief, and the cow also gored the thief to death; this cow had human-like feelings. Another Western merchant, Li Shengting, bought a horse that was extremely docile and well-behaved, but whenever he encountered a white horse on the road, he would stop and stare, refusing to move forward even with a whip, and if he saw a white horse, he would gallop to catch up, unable to be restrained by the reins. After talking to the original owner, the owner said: "This horse was born from a white mare and always seeks its mother." This horse also had human-like feelings.

When I was eight years old, I heard the wet nurse, Madam Ding, tell me a story. In a certain household, there was a lame ox that could no longer plow and was therefore sold to a neighboring butcher's shop. The calf, just weaned, watched as its mother was slaughtered and mooed for several days. Later, when it saw the butcher, it would flee, and if it couldn't escape, it would lie on the ground trembling as if begging for mercy. Sometimes, the butcher would deliberately chase it for amusement, but he didn't take it seriously. As the calf grew up, it became very strong and healthy, but it still feared the butcher as before. When its horns became strong and sharp, it waited for the butcher to lie down on a bench, then stabbed him through the heart and fled. The butcher's wife screamed and tried to catch the ox, but the crowd pitied the calf for avenging its mother, so they let it go slowly, and it escaped, disappearing without a trace. At that time, a relative of Madam Ding had killed someone and was freed by amnesty, returning to live in the same village with his son. Madam Ding secretly mentioned this matter and felt worried and fearful, clearly understanding that an enemy cannot be trifled with. I, however, thought that the calf had a heart of revenge, knowing it could not match the butcher's strength, so it hid its sharpness, enduring in silence to find the right moment. This was not just an act of filial piety but also a display of wisdom. The Yellow Emperor's silk machine inscription says, "The character '机' is the original character. Some editors thought it was a variant or vulgar form and changed it to '机,' which was incorrect. At noon, one must be wise. Holding a knife, one must cut. Meaning that the moment must not be missed." The *Yuè Jué Shū* (Records of the Extermination of Yue) says that Zǐ Gòng told King Goujian of Yue, "If someone has a plan against another and makes it known, it is dangerous. Meaning that the plan must not be revealed." Sun Tzu says, "A good general keeps his army hidden like a maiden and moves out like a hare. This saying is correct."

You should carefully consider what you say. In the summer of 1789, during the reign of the Qianlong Emperor, a scholar from Jiangnan rented a large family's tomb courtyard outside Xizhimen for studying because the inns in the capital city were often cramped and poorly maintained. One evening, while taking a walk under the cool trees, he encountered a girl aged fifteen or sixteen, who was quite fair-skinned. He flirted with her and spoke to her, but she neither became angry nor responded, turning the corner and leaving on her own. In the middle of the night, he woke up and heard a faint sound like a bird landing on the door, suspecting it was a thief. He called for his servant but got no response, so he got up and peered through the crack in the door. It was the girl he had seen during the day. Knowing she was coming towards him, he quickly opened the door and embraced her. The girl said she was the daughter of the tomb keeper, whose family was extremely poor, and her parents were both simple and dull. They often feared she would be married to a farmer's wife. Just now, she had received his attention and could not control herself, so she came through the wall gap to his place. He is a wealthy and noble person, naturally he must have a wife. If he can provide a hundred taels of silver to her parents, she will be his concubine without regret. Her parents are greedy for profit, they will surely agree. The scholar agreed, and they became intimately involved. Until the rooster crowed, she left.

From that night on, she would come every midnight, seductive and promiscuous, displaying a myriad of forms. The scholar found her no more extraordinary than the legendary encounters at Mount Wu and the Luo River. One night, she arrived slightly late, and the scholar went out under the moonlight to wait for her. She suddenly flew down from the tip of a tree, and the scholar suddenly realized, saying, "Are you not a fox?" The woman was not at all ashamed to admit it, smiling and responding, "At first, I feared you might be frightened, so I used false words. Now that our affection has deepened, I might as well tell you plainly. In the future, as you travel far and wide in your official career, you will have a hidden servant wife who does not need vehicles or horses, does not choose a place to stay, and does not require food or clothing. During the day, she can be carried in your arms, and at night, she can appear to share your bed. Is this not better than spending a thousand gold pieces to buy a smile?" The scholar thought about it and found it a good plan, so from then on, she secretly stayed in his study, no longer waiting for the night to come. However, whenever he held a candle, she would go out, returning only at midnight, sometimes showing signs of disheveled hair and askew hairpins. The scholar doubted her but could not decide, and soon after, he became intimate with his male lover. Soon, the two servants caught them, and they also became intimate with the servants. The cook found out and continued to be intimate with them as well.

One day, during the daytime, the scholar slept with his young male lover. Secretly strangling the lover, the scholar caused the lover to transform into a fox, and then buried the transformed fox outside the wall. A month later, an old man came to the scholar and said: "Your daughter, who became your concubine, was suddenly killed." The scholar angrily replied: "If you know your daughter was my concubine, it’s easier to explain. When two males compete for a female, they fight and kill each other, which is called jealousy and adultery. According to law, this is a punishable offense. Since your daughter was my concubine, knowing she was not human, and I did not break our vows, the marital bond was established. She had relations with others and my servant. As the original husband, I have the right to catch adulterers, so killing her is no crime." The old man asked: "Then why didn’t you kill my servant?" The scholar replied: "Your daughter died and her true form was revealed. These were all humans. Killing four people and holding a dead fox as evidence of a crime. If you were a judge, could you convict based on this?" The old man sat silently for a long time, then said: "You brought this upon yourself. I truly did not expect you to go this far." The old man left, and the scholar soon moved to the Zhunti Temple, living next to Shen Si. His young lover and the fox were especially close, harboring resentment over the owner's cruelty, and revealed all the details to Shen Si, thus obtaining the full account.

Gümsa, a place belonging to Urumqi, was assigned a garrisoned soldier named Zhang Mingfeng to guard a watchtower, known as a military outpost. Near this watchtower was a vegetable garden, and an elderly man who tended the garden, over sixty years old, often stayed overnight at the watchtower during rain and wind. One night, Zhang Mingfeng got him drunk with wine and had intercourse with him. Upon waking, the old man was greatly enraged and complained to the camp officer. The wounds inflicted were not yet healed, and he reported the matter to the higher authorities, leading to Zhang Mingfeng's dismissal from his position. At the time, Zhang Mingfeng was just twenty years old, and everyone thought it was impossible. Some suspected that the old man had previously defiled Zhang Mingfeng, seeking revenge, but when both parties were re-examined, neither admitted guilt, and everyone called it a strange matter. A court slave named Yu Bao said: "This kind of thing has happened before; it is not unusual." Once, he had herded horses in the south mountains and was startled by someone hunting pheasants, causing the horse to run away. Fearing punishment, he went into the deep mountains to search, panicked and lost his way, becoming more and more confused, spending a day and night without finding a way out. From a distance, he saw the corner of a house in the forest and ran quickly to seek refuge there. He also feared it might be a bandit's den or that he would be harmed, so he crouched in the grass to observe the situation. After a long time, two elderly men walked hand in hand and laughed and talked, sitting on a flat stone, hugging and leaning against each other, their behavior unusually intimate.

Soon, a left-hand old man pulled a right-hand old man down to crouch by the side of a rock, indulging freely in lewd and intimate behavior. I, having just seen their private matters, feared they would kill me to silence me, and so I cowered anxiously, unable to move. Then, they saw me and showed no shame or embarrassment, calling me out together and asking where I came from. They took two buns and gave them to me to eat, pointing out the way home and saying, "From a certain place, see a certain tree, and turn to another place, see a deep ravine along the path, and you can reach home in one day." They also pointed to the highest peak and said, "This is due south; if you are lost, look here to know the direction." They added, "This empty mountain has no grass, your horse is hungry and has returned on its own, and there are many bears and wolves here, so do not come again." By the time I returned home, the horse had indeed returned first. Now, Zhang Mingfeng loves a sixty-year-old old man, isn't this the same type of old man? According to what he said, there truly are things in the world that defy reason. Only, these two old men do not know who they are, they hide in deep mountains, seemingly also Daoist practitioners, why do they act like this? Because of the story of the immortal Ma Xiutou recorded in *Shu House Shadows*, it says that he is like a mischievous child, and in the clouds, there is true yin that can be collected. This is the *Rongcheng Technique*, not only for controlling women, but also for controlling men. However, collecting from old men, what benefit is there? Even if修炼 indeed has this method, it is just a heretical teacher and an outside path. True immortals definitely do not have this.

Zhang Zhugao Qianting said, "In the past, I traveled north with a friend and stayed overnight at an inn. We heard the sound of stones colliding, sometimes outside the window, sometimes in the outer room. Initially, we thought it was insects or mice and were not very surprised. Later, we faintly heard sighing, and then we became suddenly frightened, but upon investigation, we saw nothing. When we reached Honghuabu, I accidentally forgot to collect my writing tools. In the middle of the night, I heard the sound of putting down a brush. The next morning, there were faint traces on the table, dark and bleak, barely visible. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a poem, which read: 'On the Upper Yuan Festival, the flowers are beautiful; on the Cold Food Festival, there are many winds and rains. For ten years, you have remembered me; for a thousand miles, I have followed you. We meet but cannot be close; standing quietly, I feel sad. The wild waters are green and vast; this parting ends forever.' It seemed like words of a fragrant soul filled with resentment. However, Qianting recalled no such person, and my friend also recalled no such person. We do not know where he came from. Cheng Yumen said, 'If you are willing to recite this poem, it is definitely not true; perhaps your friend is reluctant to speak of it.' Everyone agreed with him."

Fellow examinee Hu Muting, a censor, is a person of isolated and lofty character. His scholarship and literary works are also well-founded. However, he is lacking in social skills and has no understanding of household management. As the ancients said, he is like someone who doesn't know how many legs a horse has. The servants treat him like a child. He once invited me and fellow examinees Cao Mutang, Zhu Zhujun, and Qian Xinmei for a meal, consisting of three dishes of meat, three dishes of vegetables, and a few rounds of wine. I heard that the cost of the meal was around three to four coins, which gives an idea of his frugality. When we fellow examinees occasionally discussed this, we sighed together, and Zhu Zhujun was particularly indignant, so he exposed all his faults and forced him to leave. However, his deep-seated habits had taken root, and he secretly passed on his ways, and within a few months, he reverted to his old behavior. His supporters spread among the gentry, spreading slander against Zhu Zhujun, and he even gained a reputation for being busy with trivial matters. As a result, everyone sat idly by. They only justified their inaction with the proverb, "The wicked have allies, but the virtuous do not," and made light of it with a jest.

After Master Mu died in poverty and depression, a day after his death, an old servant came to mourn him with deep sorrow. He took out thirty gold pieces and placed them on the table, kneeling and saying, "My master did not welcome his wife and children, only living alone in a gathering hall. His monthly salary was originally sufficient for warmth and food, but only because we exploited him, causing a lack of firewood and rice. At that time, the long-term servants in the capital formed a coalition. Those loyal to the master, together, excluded him, causing him to have no place to eat or stay. Therefore, I dared not stand out. I did not expect the master to die because of this. Deeply feeling guilty and regretful, I could not sleep at night. Now I completely contribute my savings to help with the coffin and burial, hoping to slightly redeem my sins in hell." After finishing his prayer, he left. All the servants of the guests present looked at each other in shock. Chen Yuzhai then mentioned an incident, saying, "There was a frivolous young man who saw a young woman crying alone at a new grave. He went to tease her, and the young woman said sternly, 'I am not deceiving you; I am a fox spirit. The person in the grave was captivated by my beauty and died from illness. I feel grateful for his affection, but I feel guilty for causing his death. I have already vowed to the gods that I will not marry again in this life. You have no false desires, but you will only bring disaster.' Is this servant like this fox? However, I believe he is still better than those who turn away and leave."

Lord Tian Songyan said, when he was young, he lived in the village of Shen Shi Zhuang in Yizhou. The locals said that the village was originally named Shen Zi Zhuang because a prodigy was born there. Later, three large stones fell in the north of the village, like what happened in the Spring and Autumn period in the state of Song, so the name was changed to its current one. In the southwest of Yizhou, about twenty miles away, he once played with servants in a stable. He saw a pot of boiling beans with several iron bubbles, which were long and narrow. The servants broke one of them, and inside was a worm about half an inch long, shaped like a willow borer, slightly red in color, with only four short legs and a black head, and it had an oily sheen. When taken out, it still wriggled, so they broke open each bubble and found a worm in each one, all looking the same. He also mentioned that First-Class Imperial Guard Chang Junqing — this was another Chang Junqing, who was the same name as Chang Dazongbo, a Grand Councillor during the Qianlong era. In the year Qianlong Guiyou, he garrisoned in the Western Regions and set up his tent under the South Mountain. The mountain ranges beyond the frontier extended from southwest to northeast, and the 36 states of the Western Regions were situated between them. In the south of the mountain, it was called North Mountain, and in the north of the mountain, it was called South Mountain, but in reality, they referred to the same mountain range.

At the halfway point of the mountain, there is a waterfall about two zhang high, and its water is very sweet. However, during winter, the water freezes, and people take water from the river. The river water is swift and cold, and drinking it can cause illness. As a result, people still drill through the ice of the waterfall to obtain water. As soon as a hole is made, countless ice balls emerge, all shaped like olives. When broken open, these ice balls contain white worms resembling silkworms, with their mouths and feet being a deep red color. These might be what are called "ice worms." They are similar to the worms found in iron, which do not die even when heated, and both are considered strange phenomena. However, the energies of heaven and earth, one moving and one still, are interdependent and root each other. Within extreme heat, there is latent cold, and within extreme cold, there is latent heat. This is the complementary relationship between the eight trigrams.

The *Kan* hexagram, composed of two yin lines enclosing one yang line, and the *Li* hexagram, composed of two yang lines enclosing one yin line, represent the dynamic interplay of yin and yang in the sixty-four hexagrams. When yang reaches its extreme in *Qian* (Heaven), one yin emerges, descending to form *Gou* (Obstruction); when yin reaches its extreme in *Kun* (Earth), one yang emerges, descending to form *Fu* (Return). In a state of rest, energy contracts and stagnates, while in a state of motion, it expands and transforms. When transformation occurs, life emerges, and this process continues endlessly. The harmonious and balanced energy (*chong he zhi qi*) produces life in a regular and predictable manner, while imbalanced energy (*pian sheng zhi qi*) produces life in unpredictable ways. Harmonious energy can be found everywhere, whereas imbalanced energy may or may not produce life in certain places. Thus, both a boiling cauldron with intense heat and a cold spring with frozen water can support the growth of insects. According to Cui Bao's *Gujin Zhi*, fire mice are said to live in the fire region, and their fur can be spun into cloth that does not burn in fire. Such creatures are often found on modern ships. My elder brother, Qinghu, once kept several feet of this cloth, and I have tested its properties. The *Shenyi Jing* also records the existence of ice mice, which live in the ice of the North Sea, feeding on water. Over time, they grow as large as elephants and die when the ice melts. Europeans have reportedly seen these creatures, and my senior official, Xie Meizhuang, also encountered them during his time in Uliastai. Though these phenomena may seem strange, they are actually consistent with natural principles.

All things are predestined, hence ghosts and gods can foretell the future. However, there are matters that have not yet begun to develop, and people who have not yet conceived the idea. These matters do not involve significant good or evil, fortune or misfortune, nor are they connected to karmic consequences. They are trivial and insignificant, so much so that they do not deserve mention, and certainly not recorded in the books of the afterlife. Yet, they can still sometimes be foreseen. In the year Gengyin of the Qianlong era, a Hanlin scholar chanced upon a medium. He asked about his official career, and the medium judged with a poem: "A spring breeze brings a smile, and one supports a cane; peach and plum blossoms bloom, filling the eyes with color. It is good to be two butterflies seeking fragrance; just as the white wall is passed, a clever meeting occurs." The scholar was completely puzzled by the meaning. Soon after, he passed the imperial examination for Hanlin scholars and was promoted from an editor to a magistrate. Others said the second line of the poem subtly alluded to the story of Zhang Shicheng, the beauty of Heyang County, and thus it proved to be true. However, the rest of the poem remained unclear. When his colleagues came to console him, the gatekeeper, supporting a cane and limping out, said: "The servants of court officials view provincial officials as people from heaven. The gatekeeper received a letter about the master's external transfer and stood on a brick, joyfully leaping and saying: 'I have ascended to the realm of immortals today.' Unaware of losing his footing, he injured his shins and thus walks with a cane."

A few days later, it was faintly heard that one day the master dismissed two servants, though the charges were unclear. Soon, someone revealed the matter, saying that the two servants had both planned to become gatekeepers, but there was already a lame person serving as gatekeeper. So they each tried to beautify their wives, waiting for the master to rest, and lured and bewitched him. By evening, one wife secretly prepared some cakes and pastries, and another wife secretly brewed tea. Both of them groped their way in the dark to the veranda of the study, where they suddenly bumped into each other, and everything they carried fell to the ground. Embarrassed and unable to bear it, they turned angry and scolded each other. The master did not want to investigate deeply, so he kindly dismissed them. Thus, the first and third lines of the poem were verified, and this divination can be called a very clever spirit. But how could it predict such things in advance? It's ultimately beyond explanation. Madam Ma hired a needlewoman who had previously worked in this household. She said that the two servants did indeed plan to take over the gatekeeper's position, but she had no intention of offering her wife as a gift. Instead, she privately plotted with a cunning servant. The cunning servant devised this plan, and they agreed that on that day they would have free time and could take advantage of the opportunity to approach, but they did not let each other know. Thus, both plans failed. After the two servants were dismissed, the cunning servant joined forces with the lame gatekeeper and frequented brothels. The lame gatekeeper knew that the cunning servant had a hidden trap, so he pretended to send him ahead while secretly informing the master to go and catch him. Thus, the cunning servant also failed. Alas! A mere gatekeeper for a county official, and yet these four people schemed against each other, going through various twists and turns without end. The metaphor of the cicada, the mantis, and the yellow bird is clearly demonstrated here. I add this note to highlight the dangers of human nature.

When I was serving as the Minister of War, I went to Liangxiang to send off troops heading to Hubei and took a short rest at Changxin Store. At the inn, I saw two poems about returning wild geese on the wall. The first one said: "Against the cold autumn wind, the wild geese fly in formation. In late autumn, I am sending you back home again. It is pitiful that the days of flying are not many. In February, you will still come to see the apricot blossoms." The second one said: "With vast waters and deep clouds, companions are few. Lonely, only returning with the swallows. Only fearing the next year's black-clad alley, yet they each fly to the carved beams." At the end, it is signed 'Qinghu,' which is my elder brother's name. However, the meaning and handwriting are not like those of my elder brother; it must be another person. Someone said: there is a gentleman named Zheng Hong, also known as Qinghu. I happened to see Lord Tian Songyan holding a painted fan, the brushwork is elegant and moist, greatly resembling Hengshan. He said it was made by his relative, Duke De Jun Zhilu. There is a poem on it, saying: "A distant sunset over flat sand and water. Half the mountain is covered with red trees, casting a desolate shadow. A person sits alone with a cup at a wine shop, watching me ride a donkey across a wooden bridge." The flavor is leisurely, with a quality beyond the mundane. There is also a note by Duke De Jun, he said it was painted by Zhuo Wunan, who drew the poem's meaning, so both are recorded here. Probably he also loved the words. Lord Tian said Wunan's name is Zhuo Litu, but he could not detail his background. Generally, those submerged in low positions, with high sentiments and elegant styles, but their names are obscure. Recording and preserving them, it is also like the few angles of distant mountains by Guo Shuxian.

In ancient ancestral temples, with their ritual offerings, the descendants are inspired to admire and emulate the moral and behavioral standards of the ancients, thus fostering a culture of emulation and good customs. These temples are often filled with spirits and divine presences, and there are many instances where these spirits are felt or heard. Some exploit the sanctity of these temples for personal gain. It is said that a scholar once stayed in a village in Chenliu. On a hot day, he walked in the fields after dusk, when the sky was dark and dim. Suddenly, he met a man who bowed and sat with him under an old tree. The man asked about his hometown and name. The man replied, "Do not be alarmed, I am Zhong Rong, the Puyuan. My temple still stands, but sacrifices are often lacking. Moreover, as a scholar, I do not wish to seek offerings from ordinary people. Since you share my spirit, I dare to ask for a small sacrifice. May I have a wild offering tomorrow?" The scholar, known for his composure, was not frightened. He asked about the end of the Han dynasty, and the man answered vaguely, often quoting from *Romance of the Three Kingdoms*. The scholar began to suspect him. When he inquired about the man's life and deeds, the stories told were identical to those in *The Lute* by Gao Zecheng. The scholar then smiled and said, "I lack the means to offer you anything. You should seek someone more powerful. But I have one piece of advice: from now on, you should read the *Book of the Later Han*, *Records of the Three Kingdoms*, and Zhong Rong's collected works. This will bring you closer to the path of seeking offerings." The man blushed deeply and transformed into a ghostly form, disappearing into the night. This story serves as a metaphor for the deceptive methods used to gain wealth, even by spirits.

梁豁堂说，有位客人在广东旅行时，他的妻子去世，棺材暂时寄放在山上的寺庙里。夜里，他梦见妻子说：“寺庙里有厉鬼，伽蓝神也无法控制它。凡是把棺材寄放在寺庙里的，男人都会被它役使，女人都会被它玷污。我竭尽全力抵抗，但无法逃脱。你为什么不向神明提起诉讼呢？”醒来后，他清楚地记住了这个梦，于是点燃香烛祈祷说：“我的梦是这样的，难道是春天的睡梦迷糊了吗？还是我想象中的幻觉？或者你真的有灵性？如果你真的有灵性，你应该连续三个晚上来告诉我。”之后又过了两个晚上，梦都是一样的。于是他向城隍神提交了诉状。几天过去了，没有得到任何回应。一天晚上，他梦见妻子又来了，说：“如果诉讼成功，那么伽蓝神会因为未能上报而受责罚，山神和社公会因为未能约束而受责罚，他们都会根据阴间的法律受到惩罚。所以城隍神犹豫不决，无法处理。你为什么不再写一份诉状，声称你要去江西，向正乙真人提起诉讼呢？那样城隍神一定会采取行动。”按照他说的，他又写了一份诉状并提交了。几天后，他又梦见妻子来了，说：“昨天城隍神召见了我，告诉我说：‘这个鬼原本就住在这个房间里，是你侵入了它的地盘，而不是它侵入了你的地盘。’”

Men and women living together in one room, with their servants coming and going, can lead to suspicious behavior, which may be unavoidable. Your complaint is not without reason, and now I have severely whipped your servants as a sufficient apology to you. Why insist on accusations of adultery, which would damage your reputation? It is always better to resolve a matter than to let it persist, and it is better to turn a major issue into a minor one. Quickly order your husband to move the coffin away, and this case will be resolved. After careful consideration, it is better to let things go and not provoke unnecessary conflict with the divine, as it could lead to unforeseen problems. You can move me away if you wish. The city god, unwilling to intervene, asks why you would turn to the Taoist priest. The Taoist priest, though not responsible for matters of the underworld, can submit petitions to the supreme deity, which even the gods cannot obstruct. The city god is also concerned about provoking unforeseen problems and thus resolves matters through compromise, allowing both parties to let things go. After speaking, he leaves seriously, and your husband moves the coffin to another place, ending the dream. This ghost, if capable of saving himself, would not ask for too much, and could be said to understand matters well. However, if the city god is a clear and enlightened deity, what is his responsibility? Is he not clever but not upright? Moreover, if a wound is not treated, it will eventually become a serious problem, and even the so-called clever ones are half enlightened and half blind.

Tian Baiyan reported that Zhu Ziqing of Jinan had a fox friend. He could only hear the fox's voice but never saw its form, and they would sometimes attend literary and drinking gatherings together. The fox was eloquent and persuasive, and no one could defeat him in argument. One day, someone asked to see the fox's form. The fox said, "Do you want to see my true form? My true form cannot be shown to you. Do you want to see my illusory form? That form is already illusory, the same as not seeing at all, so why bother to see it?" Despite this, the crowd insisted, and the fox asked, "In your minds, what does my form look like?" One person said, "It should have a full beard and white hair." Instantly, the fox appeared as an old man. Another said, "It should have the air of a hermit." Instantly, the fox appeared as a Taoist priest. Another said, "It should wear a star-crowned robe." Instantly, the fox appeared as a celestial official. Another said, "It should look like a child." Instantly, the fox appeared as a baby. Another joked, "Zhuangzi said that the divine beings of Gu She are graceful and youthful, like young girls. Shouldn't you look like that too?" Instantly, the fox appeared as a beautiful woman. Another said, "Changing forms on command is all illusory. Ultimately, we want to see your true form." The fox replied, "In this vast world, who would be willing to show their true form to others? And do you expect me to be the only one to do so?" The fox laughed loudly and left. Zhu Ziqing said, "This fox claims to be seven hundred years old, indicating that it has deep experience."

Uncle Shi Zhai An said: Scholars generally claim that there are no ghosts; I have not seen ghosts myself, but I have personally heard their speech. During the provincial examination in the year 1722, I returned to stay at Bai Gou He, where I stayed in a house with three rooms. I occupied the western room, while a southern scholar stayed in the eastern room. We exchanged greetings with each other and then bought wine to talk at night. The southern scholar mentioned that he had a childhood friend whose family was extremely poor, and I sometimes helped him with money and grain. Later, he went north to take the imperial examination, and at that time, I was working as a scribe in the household of a prominent official. Pitying his wandering life, I invited him to live together. He gradually gained the favor of the master and then fabricated stories about my family and spread rumors secretly. He forced me out and took over my position.

Zhang Fuchacha's *Qiuping Xinyu* records two events from my family. The first event recounts a ghost in the east pavilion of my late brother Qinghu's house. This building is located west of my brother's house, but it was originally east of the house before the family divided their property, hence it retains its old name. The story is true, but the details are not fully explained. This building was constructed during the year 1595 of the Ming dynasty (Wanli era), and it has been 184 years since then. On the upper and lower floors, seven people have hanged themselves, so no one dares to live there. On that particular night, we had no choice but to open it, and thus the incident occurred. Is this what geomancers call an inauspicious direction? However, a small building next to it, where the inhabitants have many descendants, remains a mystery as to why this is the case. The second event records the final moments of my son Rujie, with the details being incomplete. The ghost only spoke in the language of western merchants when demanding payment, suggesting it was a wild ghost pretending to demand food. After questioning the ghost's name, residence, and the date of the incident, as well as the people who witnessed it, the ghost became speechless and left.

When Ru Ji was involved in a lawsuit with his creditor, the Ministry of Justice had carefully verified the accumulated arrears, and there were official documents, but there was no such item. The Zhang and Ji families are related by marriage, and women pass on stories to each other, so there cannot be no slight additions or omissions. Alas! Different words are spoken by those who see, hear, and hear about it. Even the *Lu* annals are like this, how much more so for minor officials' novels. Others recording our family's affairs, I know the differences and similarities, but others cannot. Then, when I record others' family affairs, based on what I hear, I immediately narrate, sometimes false, sometimes true, sometimes incomplete. Others can know, but I cannot. Liu Houcun's poem says: "Slope sun, ancient willows, Zhao's village, a blind man with a drum is performing. After death, who can judge right or wrong? The whole village listens to the singing of Cai Zhonglang." Not now, but now, from ancient times it has been like this. Only without losing the meaning of loyalty and kindness, slightly preserving the intent of encouragement and punishment, not confusing right and wrong like *Bi Yun Bing*, not harboring grudges and grievances like *Zhou Qin Xing Ji*, not depicting talented scholars and beautiful women like *Hui Zhen Ji*, not painting licentiousness like *Mi Xin*, hoping not to be rejected by the virtuous.

Appendix: Six Notes on Ji Ruiji My deceased son, Ruiji, was born in the year of Jiazi during the Qianlong era. As a child, he was quite intelligent and, though he had not read much, he could already write eight-part essays. In the year of Yihou, he was selected in the local examination, and then he began to study poetry and classical prose, but he had not yet grasped the fundamentals. When I was serving in the Western Regions, he then associated with talented poets from the poetry society, and he mistakenly followed the styles of the Gong'an and Jingling schools. Later, he relied on Zhu Ziyin in Tai'an and saw a manuscript copy of *Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio*, which had not yet been printed at that time. He again fell into its conventional patterns and ultimately sank into obscurity, never returning, until his death. His surviving poems and prose were only entrusted to Sun Shuting and others, to preserve the handprints of his father. I have not compiled them once. Only his miscellaneous notes, which had not yet been compiled into a book, contained trivial matters that sometimes could be useful. Therefore, I selected a few items and appended them to the end of this collection, to acknowledge his labor of working by the light of a lamp in the cold. I also regret that he turned to the Buddhist path and accomplished nothing, only these words, which are not related to scholarly works, to preserve his name.

The Flower-Hiding Old Man resides east of Pingling City and west of Quehua Bridge, a person of unknown origin who does not reveal his true name. His dwelling features pavilions, water, and stones, and he plants many flowers. He usually does not interact with people, but when flower enthusiasts come, he welcomes them all. He leads visitors with a cane and a bent posture, his hands never stop pointing, and his mouth never stops speaking, as he fears that people will not know or see enough. The garden has no empty space, filled with unique fragrances and colors, abundant and continuous, endless and boundless. Orchids, chrysanthemums, and bamboo, especially possess the wonders of the world. Orchids have red and white varieties, chrysanthemums have black and green varieties, and there are also red bamboo and white bamboo. Others are square, spotted, purple, or have many joints, though they may not be familiar to the eye, they are familiar to the ear. How extraordinary that things gather according to one's preferences, indeed it is so.

A certain scholar resided in the Huanyong Pavilion of the Dai Temple. It was already deep winter, and the northern wind was fierce. Huddled by the stove at night, the cold was unbearable. He then extinguished the lamp and went to bed. After waking, he saw a light coming from a hole in the ceiling paper and found it strange. Putting on his clothes and rising stealthily, he examined the hole closely and saw a beautiful woman, no more than two feet tall, wearing purple robes and a dark-blue collar, red shoes, and slender as a finger. Her hair was styled in the fashion of the times. She was just kindling a fire to cook rice. Beside the stove was a short-legged table, and on it stood a tin oil lamp burning faintly. He thought to himself that this must be a fox. Just as he was watching closely, she suddenly sneezed. The woman was startled, bumped the table, and overturned the lamp, so nothing could be seen. At dawn, he broke open the ceiling to look at it. There was a small stove made of yellow clay, unusually clean, with an iron pot as large as a bowl, the rice still uncooked. The small tin lampstand was overturned beneath the table, with oil stains scattered everywhere. Only the area where the steaming fire had been did not burn, which was very strange indeed.

**Two Giant Serpents of Cu Lai Mountain** There are two great serpents in Cu Lai Mountain, whose forms are unlike those of ordinary pythons. They have horns on their heads like those of a cow, are red-black in color, and gleam when viewed from a distance. Their bodies are about thirty to forty zhang long, winding through a deep ravine. The ravine is about one mu wide and half a li long, and between the two mountains, there is a narrow gap no more than three chi wide. Travelers who climb to the summit and look down through the gap can see the serpents. It is said that hundreds of years ago, these creatures caused great harm to people, until a strange monk subdued them, and thus they could not emerge. Indeed, deep mountains and great lakes are the birthplace of dragons and serpents—such things are not strange. What is strange, however, is that they have remained coiled for hundreds of years without hunger or thirst. A man from Taian named Han Mingqi, a former aristocrat who practiced medicine, once rode his horse in the dead of night to a house. Suddenly, he saw, a few bu away, a giant over ten zhang tall. Han, who was usually bold, shook his spear and passed by. When they were only a chi apart, he whipped it. The creature suddenly shrank to three or four chi in length, with short, disheveled hair and an extremely ugly appearance. Its lips opened and closed, making a grating sound. Han dismounted, took up his whip, and chased it. It moved slowly, stumbling on the ground, seeming very distressed. Eventually, it shrank to a foot in length, but its head was as large as a jar, almost too heavy to bear, nearly toppling over. Han continued chasing it as he walked, until he reached the house of the sick person, where the creature was no longer visible. No one knows what kind of monster it was. This account is told by Fan Zhuoting of Wenyang.

On the 28th day of the fifth lunar month in the year Wu Yin, Wu Lintang, who was fifty years old at the time, resided in the Peaceful Hall. I visited him to offer my birthday congratulations. Among the guests was one who could perform a smoke performance; he was about sixty years old, spoke with a southern accent, and spoke and behaved in a refined and elegant manner. No one knew how he performed his tricks. Soon after, a servant brought a large pipe, which could hold four liang of tobacco. He lit it and inhaled, inhaling and swallowing at the same time, and only finished it after the time it takes to eat a meal. Then he asked for a large bowl of bitter tea, drank it, and said to the host, "Shall I add to your years of the crane?" He then opened his mouth and spat out two cranes, which flew toward the corner of the room. He slowly exhaled a circle as large as a plate, through which the two cranes passed, flying back and forth, dancing like a weaver’s shuttle. Soon after, he made a sound in his throat and exhaled a thin line of smoke, which rose upright into the air, dispersing into the shape of water ripples and clouds. Upon close inspection, they were all small cranes about an inch in size, dancing left and right, and after some time they disappeared. All the guests believed they had never seen anything like this before. Soon after, his disciple arrived and presented a cup to the host, saying, "My skill is not as good as my master’s; may I perform a small trick for you?" In a breath, a cloud appeared, drifting before the banquet, and gradually formed a small pavilion with carved railings and ornate windows, clearly like a painting. He said, "This is the 'Sea House Adding Sticks' [a metaphor for adding years to life]." The guests were once again greatly surprised, thinking it was the light on the tip of a finger manifesting a crystal tower, but no words could describe it. Based on what I have seen in various stories, such as turning cups into cranes or flowers blooming in an instant, there are too many to recount. Is it not that such things actually exist? Later people see little of it and are therefore surprised? If I had not seen this with my own eyes, I would never have believed it.

A Man from Southern Henan, Li, was extremely fond of horses. Once, while visiting the cattle market in Zunhua, he saw a horse with a body as black as ink, shining under the sun, and a belly fur whiter than frost and snow, a sight known as "black clouds holding up the moon." The horse stood over six feet tall, with a curly and elegant tail, feet with claws about an inch long, and eyes as clear and bright as crystal. Its spirit was proud and stood out like a crane among chickens. Li bought the horse for a hundred gold coins, loving its majestic beauty and personally feeding and caring for it. However, the horse was extremely wild and difficult to control. Whenever it was covered with a mud guard, restraints had to be applied, and several strong men had to hold it on both sides before it could be ridden. When gently controlled and walking slowly, it was hard to realize how fast it was going, and in a moment, it could travel a hundred miles. There was once a place five days' journey from home; Li set out at noon and arrived before the sun had set. Because of this, he loved the horse even more, but also feared its difficulty to control, and dared not ride it frequently.

One day, a mighty man with green eyes and curly beard knocked on the door to request an audience, claiming he could train this horse. He led it to the stable, and upon seeing it, the horse neighed loudly. The man struck its sides with his palm, and the horse finally lowered its ears and remained still. He then led the horse into an empty room, closed the door, and circled around it. Li peeped through a crack and saw the man holding the horse's ear in his hand, muttering as if saying something. The horse seemed to nod in agreement. Then, the man gently held the horse's ear again, muttering as before, and the horse also seemed to nod in agreement. Li was greatly surprised and thought the man truly understood horse language. After a while, the man opened the door, handed Li the reins, and the horse was already sweating profusely. As he was about to leave, he told Li, "This horse can choose its master, which is also very pleasing, but its nature is still undetermined, and it might harm someone. Now, however, there is no need to worry." The horse became docile thereafter, and over two decades, it retained its sturdy frame. After Li passed away at over ninety years old, the horse suddenly escaped, and no one knew where it went. The monk Guanqi inscribed, "A thousand lifetimes of effort sit and wear away. The clouds on paper pass by the eyes in abundance. Intending to build a library, now old. Only should one speak of ghosts like Dongpo." The cause and effect are verified without error. Collecting trivial notes, gathering a cartload of ghosts. Pass on the message to the disciples of Luoyang. Bian guan originally does not enter the Confucian school.

《阅微草堂笔记》（清）纪昀 著 卷一滦阳消夏录（一）四十七则 卷二滦阳消夏录（二）四十八则 卷三滦阳消夏录（三）四十七则 卷四滦阳消夏录（四）五十则 卷五滦阳消夏录（五）五十四则 卷六滦阳消夏录（六）五十一则 卷七如是我闻（一）六十三则 卷八如是我闻（二）六十则 卷九如是我闻（三）七十二则 卷十如是我闻（四）六十一则 卷十一槐西杂志（一）七十六则 卷十二槐西杂志（二）六十七则 卷十三槐西杂志（三）八十一则 卷十四槐西杂志（四）六十一则 卷十五姑妄听之（一）五十七刚 卷十六姑妄听之（二）五十四则 卷十七姑妄听之（三）五十三则 卷十八姑妄听之（四）五十一则 卷十九滦阳续录（一）二十九则 卷二十滦阳续录（二）二十五则

卷二十一滦阳续录（三）二十三则 卷二十二滦阳续录（四）二十二则 卷二十三滦阳续录（五）二十六则 纪昀 诗二首 千生心力坐销磨。纸上烟云过眼多。 拟筑书仓今老矣。只应说鬼以东坡。 前因后果验无差，琐记搜罗鬼一车。 传语洛闰门弟子。稗官原不入儒家。 观弃道人自题 郑序 （盛序巳贴出） 河间纪文达公。久在馆阁。鸿文巨制。称一代手笔。或言公喜诙谐，嬉笑怒骂，皆成文章。今观公所署笔记。词意忠厚、体例谨严。而大旨悉归劝惩。殆所渭是非不谬于圣人者与！虽小说，犹正史也。公自云。“不颠是非如（碧云霞）。”。不挟恩怨如（周秦行纪），不描摹才子佳人如《会真记》。不绘画横陈如（秘辛），冀不见摈于君子。“盖犹公之谦词耳。公之孙树馥、来宦岭南。从索是书者众。因重浸板，树馥醇谨有学识。能其官、不堕其家风云。

道光十五年乙末春日。龙溪郑开僖识。 滦阳消夏录卷首语 乾隆己酉夏，以编排秘藉，于役滦阳。时校理久竞，特督视官吏题签架而巳。昼长无事，追录见闻，忆及即书，都无体例。小说稗官，知无关于著述，街谈巷议，或有益于劝惩。聊付抄胥存之，命曰《滦阳消夏录》云尔 卷一 滦阳消夏录「一」 神猪智狐谐鬼鬼诗梦中绝句精灵论诗无赖吕四人狐恋王半仙友狐郑苏仙梦冥府狂电穿人无云和尚宁波吴生老儒遇鬼河间唐生轻薄少年媚鬼逃遁贪吏遇鬼艳女说驿使人狐争居石壁出人语曹氏不怕鬼默庵先生戏术北窗怪声仙童诗有鬼魅梦中吟小花犬显灵神柏吕道士幻术马语侍姬烂舌某生刃妻画中景某医好毒六壬术士奇术僧胡维华慧女复仇鬼牒又一骆宾王老桑树凶宅沧州潘班夭逝女碣江宁书生

卷二 滦阳消夏录「二」 村叟之卜李太学妻先贤讲经褴褛人扶乩问寿东方未明之砚盗玉杀人刀痕旧玉马墓前白蛇狐女靓妆圃中狐女侠士情女酒鬼产麟牛空宅怪异《埤雅》绿笺梦中绝句周氏救女农家烈妇老儒韩生黝黑巨人视鬼罗两峰刘四遇鬼刘炫墓方桂寻马狐宅高阜雉蛇某生乡试卷案女鬼裂卷东岳冥官永公索药游僧幻术人耶鬼耶大鱼击小奴巨蟒神笞奴子庙祝救人孝悌通神托梦称疾道士梦神桐园女子庙中判官遇冥吏者姚安公之仆废祠亡灵里胥鞭囚 卷三 滦阳消夏录「三」

戈壁蝎虎林中黑气神马槐家镇马氏前明之僧狐居小楼钱宅雅狐河间冯树痴婢辩火李某诱妇荔姐遇鬼有鬼雪仇破寺蠢僧娈童姊妹狐乌鲁木齐巴拉红丝绣花鞋郭六有憾于君宋小岩温雅小童烈女有灵刘羽冲魏忠贤之恶红柳娃塞外雪莲风灾鬼难之域明季书生卧虎山人孟夫人魏藻枯井卫瞽巨盗齐大梁上狐语甲乙丙秋谷先生道士卖药生死有命冷掌如冰鬼趣王半仙访狐友鬼气吏为狐媚 卷四 滦阳消夏录「四」 绣鸾菩萨意沧州轿夫狂生扶乩者缢鬼辛彤甫史某拒色健牛当道孙峨山转世梦神引鬼火药击人雷震恶人二姑娘老媪视鬼死人复苏山西富商赤先生子农夫陈四募建义冢某公妾王秃子飞虫夜伤人缢妇之魂无故见鬼道学某公画公伯魁戏溺髑髅魂念子孙夜半人语申苍岭先生夜狐点诗异虱红衣女子廖姥安氏友狐因果相偿食猫夫人回煞之说女巫郝媪高川丐者伪人败露蠡县凶宅某公古器许方屠驴入冥者守藏神百工祠神狐笑农夫徐四五台僧义狐两妻争坐位

卷五 滦阳消夏录「五」 木工郑五负心背德之狱债鬼里妇新寡胜负之心征漕之案鬼犬爱堂夜归张福代死以财为命古寺鬼影秘戏图关帝祠鬼与轮回文昌司禄之神以狐为妾异火樊氏扶乩妇代姑死长山聂松岩老儒王德安纸车纸马文昌祠神黄裳寓言坟院丽女女魂白日见鬼有驴长叹善射仪南公树下之鬼彩衣金钏小儿两盗互骇神来之诗正定扶乩者曹二悍妇高川堕龙白昼遇鬼少女神言二鬼拘义义犬四儿狐能化形飞天野叉羊假奴之魂牛怪枯井四尸大蛇如柱正乙真人雷神大树阻路大旋风巨鳖野狐听经李太白梦笔生花游魂
卷六 滦阳消夏录「六」 巨人立墓天竺老僧林鬼役鬼符咒二鬼城隍破棺粤东异僧江南崔寅壁中人面鬼隐乔车二幕友狐之报取子偿冤侠牛瑞草天生梵字大悲咒喇嘛黑影扑人燕园公张说秃项马真缢鬼善恶之报刘乙斋笔棒楼徐景熹青苗神陈太夫人道士神符蜃气城影梦神诃责陈生张巡妾转世索命风怪南村董天士快哉行堕马者巨盗齐舜庭娈童惨绿袍夜读少年八蜡祠道士乱山归途好名之鬼黑驴精河间妇不寐术梦冥司吏梦神示诗龟卜魂飞冥府饿鬼小儿见奇鬼

卷七 如是我闻「一」 柿园败将烈妇英灵京师某观旱魃为虐奇井柩中巨禽魂录冥籍里人阎勋梦神高论夜见二叟鬼方灵验二鬼求食狐窟教子恶作剧拆字术医者胡宫山蓬首垢面人缢鬼变形求代刘鬼谷事奇理真枭鸟与破镜老魅吟歌空中人语张氏兄弟千古痴魂尤物复仇自缢贞女怨鬼投诉观心盗句狐妖报德怨仙杏病牛思主不祥之物鬼者说鬼地下眷属青面罗刹鬼狐戏人狂士沧州太学生龙凤地群狐悬人淫狐山神狐化幼妇选人遇狐神兔卖花者黑狐某公多疑二鬼争墓狐具人心丐者宦家子佣媪语冥司鸟鸣“可惜”

万柳堂万里索命追摄之鬼闽中县令山东朱文献县二奇事泄云洞 卷八 如是我闻「二」 王某解冤丐妇孝姑神灵断案雷震李十教场狐宅故家子宛平何华峰学道飞狐《上堵吟》夜叉潼关驿夜献县王生鹅鸭凶兆宦家废圃昆霞之师天生敦伊犁凿井事虞美人花货朗笔中异事善恶之报呼图壁赤城山老翁乞虚损方者歙人蒋紫垣东城猎者沧州牧王某异菊笃信程朱奇节异烈乡有嫠妇士人宿会稽山浴血人入梦何故而缢里胥宋某空屋男女此狐不俗赤练飞狐长臂鬼狐著红鞋唐代剑客奇门遁甲之术其命大贵某甲巨人鼓妖景城姜三莽杏花精申铁蟾奴子张云会甲乙相善香玉紫窑片磁阔石图岭李老人杨主事遣犯彭杞孟媪上冢交河苏斗南奇疾

卷九 如是我闻「三」 义犬狐媚赵氏子砖击辛五里人刘生转世朱臂伶人方俊官童子受污亦鬼亦狐有驴自报窗外牛吼忠厚亦能积怨沧州刘太史亡侄汝备蠢女连贵书生之魂姚安公论鬼束州邵氏子李氏兄弟凶宅庶女呼天厉鬼还冤小婢玉儿冥官好色之狐泰州任子田狐报僧怨翰林某公魅扰陈耕岩火中留情女首蛇身胥魁善博者与狐女生子者废宅艳女虎神磷为鬼火 珍稀巨砚身死心生悍妇蔡太守说冥事烧海善恶有报人狐夙缘邱长春假邱长春某知府夫人书生遭饿鬼园有故祟野鬼吟诗鬼书红柬待人以理之狐车轮巨蝠老仆说鬼冥司之法狐神鄞县书生道士庞斗枢义狐之言食人玛哈沁牛犬夙冤青县张公圆光术绿锦袱包两世夫妻母恶女报鬼魅报恩河间献王墓鬼魅在人腹中语死后七日而苏走无常者二僧之败

卷十 如是我闻「四」 医者某生冥司遇故人狐据书楼冥女还魂临阵忆斯语轿夫王二瞽者蔡某兽面人心乌对户啼僵尸有声奢俭有度贪牛致害塾师愧于狐书生结冥友血卧疆场戒杀牛重规叠矩寡妇尽孝盗贼行侠巫能视鬼城西古墓《佐治药言》载奇事子不语怪老儒悭吝孝廉死不忘亲宦家义妇兄死吞狐侄野狐戏人人伪狐状布商韩 某巨公放生董鄂箧中绣履老媪劫女二鼠相逐生死有定学究与狐友鬼狐之见冯大邦崔某与豪强讼善讼者构思难断之狱鬼病宾客致害田父夜守枣林报怨鬼求食真伪颠倒西洋贡狮伪诗神镜唐张君平墓志屈狐易，能屈于狐难魂魄鬼情冥司重贤甲乙相斗范鸿禧与狐友昵捕盗狐赠丑郎帽朱五嫂遇狐张媪问佛福事

卷十一 槐西杂志「一」 鬼夫计报施王金英为诗节孝祠梦妇真鬼吓倒假鬼狐突祠与糊涂祠石中物象少妇之魂童子受魅夜遇狐女乩避客杏花滴血验子巨蟒父子之情妾祭先夫节妇于鼠何尤僧言流寇事上暗下明长安道上鬼诳人申谦居先生僧寺葬女高西园得印名士风流长春草雷阳公修德怪异夫人孙瑞人为鬼置酒鬼诗狐女为狐魅者狐媚少年扶乩者妖由人兴神击 黑烟入东厢邑令梦某公魂二人夜语视鬼者谈刘某梦医聋瞽之鬼献药杀人顾媪卖磁器鬼言是理刘媪与蟒养瞽者之院患难之济不忘其心自题挽歌废冢僧禁山魈飞车刘八人字汪柴中巨蟒幽明感应饿鬼超脱老翁杀虎神助民家细腰蜂狐魅游戏老人鬼对夜行遇鬼者巨鸟少女变老翁杨生夜光卵以血为茶牛祸侠妓某君佳诗博山书生有狐眼亮辞锋

《阅微草堂笔记》 （清）纪昀 著 原 序文以载道，儒者无不能言之。夫道岂深隐莫测秘密不传，如佛家之心印，道家之口诀哉！万事当然之理，是即道矣。故道在天地，如汞泻地，颗颗皆圆，如月映水，处处皆见。大至于治国平天下，小至于一事一物一动一言，无乎不在焉。文，其道之一端也。文之大者为六经，固道所寄矣；降而为列朝之史，降而为诸子之书，降而为百氏之集，是又文中之一端，其言足以明道；再降而稗官小说，似无与于道矣。然汉书艺文志列为一家，历代书目亦皆著录，岂非以荒诞悖妄者？虽不足数，其近于正者，于人心世道亦未尝无所裨欤！河间先生以学问文章负天下重望，而天性孤直，不喜以心性空谈标榜门户，亦不喜才人放诞诗社酒社，夸名士风流。

是以退食之余，惟耽怀典籍，老而懒于考索，乃采掇异闻，时作笔记，以寄所欲言。滦阳消夏录等五书俶诡奇谲无所不载，洸洋恣肆无所不言，而大旨要归于醇正，欲使人知所劝惩，故诲淫导欲之书，以佳人才子相矜者，虽纸贵一时，终渐归湮没，而先生之书，则梨枣屡镌，久而不厌。是则华实不同之明验矣。顾翻刻者众，讹误实繁，且有妄为标目如明人之刻冷斋夜话者，读者病焉。时彦夙从先生游，尝刻先生姑妄听之，附跋书尾，先生颇以为知言，迩来诸板益漫漶，乃请于先生，合五书为一编，而仍各存其原第，篝灯手校不敢惮劳，又请先生检视一过，然后摹印。虽先生之著作不必藉此刻以传，然鱼鲁之舛差稀于先生教世之本志，或亦不无小补云尔。嘉庆庚申八月门人北平盛时彦谨序。

阅微草堂笔记卷一滦阳消夏录一 乾隆巳酉夏，以编排秘籍，于役滦阳，时校理久竟，特督视官吏，题签庋架而已，昼长无事，追录见闻，忆及即书，都无体例，小说稗官，知无关于著述。街谈巷议，或有益于劝惩，聊付抄胥存之，命曰：滦阳消夏录云尔。 胡御史牧亭言，其里有人畜一猪，见邻叟辄瞋目狂吼，奔突欲噬，见他人则否。邻叟初甚怒之，欲买而啖其肉。既而憬然省曰：此殆佛经所谓夙冤耶？世无不可解之冤，乃以善价赎得，送佛寺为长生猪，后再见之，弭耳昵就，非复曩态矣。尝见孙重画伏虎应真，有巴西李衍题曰：至人骑猛虎，驭之犹骐骥，岂伊本驯良，道力消其鸷，乃知天地间有情皆可契，共保金石心，无为多畏忌。可为此事作解也。

沧州刘士玉孝廉，有书室为狐所据。白昼与人对语，掷瓦石击人，但不睹其形耳。知州平原董思任良吏也，闻其事，自往驱之。方盛陈人妖异路之理，忽檐际朗言曰：公为官，颇爱民，亦不取钱，故我不敢击公，然公爱民乃好名，不取钱乃畏后患耳，故我亦不避公。公休矣，毋多言取困。董狼狈而归，咄咄不怡者数日。刘一仆妇甚粗蠢，独不畏狐。狐亦不击之，或于对语时，举以问狐。狐曰：彼虽下役，乃真孝妇也，鬼神见之犹敛避，况我曹乎。刘乃令仆妇居此室，狐是日即去。

爱堂先生言，闻有老学究夜行，忽遇其亡友，学究素刚直，亦不怖畏，问君何往，曰：吾为冥吏，至南村有所勾摄，适同路耳，因并行至一破屋。鬼曰：此文士庐也，问何以知之，曰：凡人白昼营营，性灵汩没，唯睡时一念不生，元神朗沏，胸中所读之书，字字皆吐光芒，自百窍而出，其状缥渺缤纷，烂如锦绣。学如郑孔，文如屈宋班马者，上烛霄汉，与星月争辉；次者数丈，次者数尺，以渐而差，极下者亦萤萤如一灯照映户牖，人不能见，唯鬼神见之耳。此室上光芒高七八尺，以是而知。学究问，我读书一生，睡中光芒当几许？鬼嗫嚅良久曰：昨过君塾，君方昼寝，见君胸中高头讲章一部，墨卷五六百篇，经文七八十篇，策略三四十篇，字字化为黑烟，笼罩屋上，诸生诵读之声，如在浓云密雾中，实未见光芒，不敢妄语。学究怒斥之，鬼大笑而去。

东光李又聃先生尝至宛平相国废园中，见廊下有诗二首，其一曰：飒飒西风吹破棂，萧萧秋草满空庭，月光穿漏飞檐角，照见莓苔半壁青，其二曰：耿耿疏星几点明，银河时有片云行，凭栏坐听谯楼鼓，数到连敲第五声。墨痕惨淡，殆不类人书。 董曲江先生名元度，平原人，乾隆壬申进士，入翰林，散馆改知县，又改教授，移疾归。少年梦人赠一扇，上有三绝句曰：曾公饮马天池日，文采西园感故知，至竟心情终不改，月明花影上旌旗/尺五城南并马来，垂杨一例赤鳞开，黄金屈戍雕胡锦，不信陈王八斗才/萧鼓冬冬画烛楼，是谁亲按小凉洲，春风豆蔻知多少，并作秋江一段愁。语多难解。后亦卒无征验，莫明其故。

平定王孝廉执信，尝随文宦榆林，夜宿野寺经阁下，闻阁上有人絮语，似是论诗，窃讶此间少文士，那得有此？因谛听之，终不甚了了，后语声渐出阁廊下，乃稍分明。其一曰：唐彦谦诗格不高，然禾麻地废生边气，草木春寒起战声，故是佳句；其一曰：仆尝有句云，阴碛日光连雪白，风天沙气入云黄，非亲至关外不睹此景；其一又曰：仆亦有一联云，山沉边气无情碧，河带寒声亘古秋，自谓颇肖边城日暮之状，相与吟赏者久之。寺钟忽动，乃寂无声。天晓起视，则扃钥尘封。山沉边气一联，后于任总镇遗稿见之。总镇名举，出师金川时，百战阵殁者也，阴碛一联，终不知为谁语，即其精灵长在，得与任公同游，亦决非常鬼矣。
沧州城南上河涯，有无赖吕四，凶横无所不为，人畏如狼虎。一日薄暮与诸恶少村外纳凉，忽隐隐闻雷声，风雨且至。遥见似一少妇避入河干古庙中。吕语诸恶少曰：彼可淫也。时已入夜，阴云黯黑，吕突入掩其口，众共褫衣相嬲。俄雷光穿牖，见状貌似是其妻，急释手问之，果不谬。吕大恚，欲提妻掷河中，妻大号曰：汝欲淫人，致人淫我，天理昭然，汝尚欲杀我耶？吕语塞，急觅衣裤，已随风入河流矣。

旁皇无计，乃自负裸妇归。云散月明，满村哗笑，争前问状。吕无可置对，竟自投于河，盖其妻归宁，约一月方归，不虞母家遘回禄，无屋可栖，乃先期返。吕不知而遘此难，后妻梦吕来曰：我业重，当永堕泥犁，缘生前事母尚尽孝，冥官检籍得受蛇身，今往生矣。汝后夫不久至，善视新姑嫜，阴律不孝罪至重，毋自蹈冥司汤镬也。至妻再醮日，屋角有赤练蛇，垂首下视，意似眷眷。妻忆前梦，方举首问之，俄闻门外鼓乐声，蛇于屋上跳掷数回，奋然去。

献县周氏仆周虎，为狐所媚，二十余年如伉俪，尝语仆曰：吾炼形已四百余年，过去生中，于汝有业缘当补，一日不满，即一日不得生天，缘尽吾当去耳。一日冁然自喜，又泫然自悲，语虎曰：月之十九日吾缘尽当别，已为君相一妇，可聘定之。因出白金付虎，俾备礼，自是狎昵燕婉，逾于平日，恒形影不离。至十五日忽晨起告别，虎怪其先期，狐泣曰：业缘一日不可减，亦一日不可增。惟迟早则随所遇耳。吾留此三日缘，为再一相会地也。越数年，果再至，欢洽三日而后去。临行呜咽曰：从此终天诀矣。陈德音先生曰：此狐善留其有余，惜福者当如是。刘季箴则曰：三日后终须一别，何必暂留。此狐炼形四百年，尚未到悬崖撒手地位，临事者不当如是。余谓二公之言，各明一义各有当也。

献县令明晨，应山人，尝欲申雪一冤狱，而虑上官不允，疑惑未决。儒学门斗有五半仙者，与一狐友，言小休咎多有验，遣往问之，狐正色曰：明公为民父母，但当论其冤不冤，不当问其允不允，独不记制府李公之言乎？门斗返报，明为悚然。因言制府李公卫未达时，尝同一道士渡江，适有与舟子争诟者，道士太息曰：命在须臾，尚较计数文钱耶？俄其人为帆脚所扫堕江死，李公心异之，中流风作，舟欲覆，道士禹步诵咒，风止得济，李公再拜谢更生，道士曰：适堕江者命也，吾不能救，公贵人也，遇阨得济，亦命也。吾不能不救，何谢焉。

李公又拜曰：领师此训，吾终身安命矣。道士曰：是不尽然，一身之穷达当安命，不安命则奔竞排轧，无所不至，不知李林甫、秦桧即不倾陷善类，亦作宰相，徒自增罪案耳。至国计民生之利害，则不可言命。天地之生才，朝廷之设官，所以补救气数也。身握事权，束手而委命，天地何必生此才，朝廷何必设此官乎？晨门曰：是知其不可而为之。诸葛武侯曰：鞠躬尽瘁，死而后已。成败利钝非所逆睹。此圣贤立命之学，公其识之。李公谨受教，拜问姓名。道士曰：言之恐公骇。下舟行数十步，翳然灭迹。昔在会城，李公曾话是事，不识此狐何以得知也。
北村郑苏仙，一日梦至冥府，见闫罗王方录囚。有邻村一媪至殿前，王改容拱手，赐以杯茗，命冥吏速送生善处。郑私叩冥吏曰：此农家老妇，有何功德？冥吏曰：是媪一生无利己损人心。夫利己之心，虽贤士大夫或不免，然利己者必损人，种种机械因是而生，种种冤愆因是而造，甚至贻臭万年，流毒四诲，皆此一念为害也。此一村妇而能自制其私心，读书讲学之儒对之多愧色矣。何怪王之加礼乎？郑素有心计，闻之惕然而寤。

郑又言此媪未至以前，有一官公服昂然入，自称所至但饮一杯水，今无愧鬼神。王哂曰：设官以治民，下至驿丞闸官，皆有利弊之当理，但不要钱即为好官，植木偶于堂，并水不饮，不更胜公乎？官又辩曰：某虽无功亦无罪。王曰：公一身处处求自全，某狱某狱避嫌疑而不言，非负民乎？某事某事畏烦重而不举，非负国乎？三载考绩之谓何，无功即有罪矣。官大踧踖，锋棱顿减。王徐顾笑曰：怪公盛气耳，平心而论，要是三四等好官，来生尚不失冠带。促命即送转轮王。观此二事，知人心微暧，鬼神皆得而窥。虽贤者一念之私，亦不免于责备。相在尔室，其信然乎？ 雍正壬子有宦家子妇，素无勃谿状。突狂电穿牖，如火光激射，雷楔贯心而入，洞左胁而出，其夫亦为雷焰燔烧，背到尻皆焦黑，气息仅属，久之乃苏，顾妇尸泣曰：我性刚劲，与母争论或有之；尔不过私诉抑郁，背灯掩泪而已，何雷之误中尔耶？是未知律重主谋，幽明一也。

无云和尚，不知何许人。康熙中挂单河间资胜寺，终日默坐，与语亦不答。一日忽登禅床，以界尺拍案一声，泊然化去。视案上有偈曰：削发辞家净六尘，自家且了自家身，仁民爱物无穷事，原有周公孔圣人，佛法近墨，此僧乃近于杨。 宁波吴生，好作北里游。后昵一狐女，时相幽会。然仍出入青楼间。一日狐女请曰：吾能幻化，凡君所眷，吾一见即可肖其貌。君一存想，应念而至，不逾于黄金买笑乎？试之，果倾刻换形，与真无二，遂不复外出。尝与狐女曰：眠花藉柳，实惬人心，惜是幻化，意中终隔一膜耳。狐女曰：不然，声色之娱，本雷光石火，岂特吾肖某某为幻化，即彼某某亦幻化也。岂特某某为幻化，即妾亦幻化也。即千百年来名姬艳女皆幻化也。

白杨绿草，黄土青山，何一非古来歌舞之场；握雨携云，与埋香葬玉，别鹤、离鸾，一曲伸臂顷耳，中间两美相合，或以时刻计，或以日计，或以月计，或以年计，终有绝别之期；及其诀别，则数十年而散，与片刻暂遇而散者，同一悬崖撒手，转瞬成空。倚翠偎红，不皆恍如春梦乎？即夙契原深，终身聚首，而朱颜不驻，白发已侵，一人之身，非复旧态。则当时黛眉粉颊，亦谓之幻化可矣。何独以妾肖某某为幻化也？吴洒然有悟。后数岁，狐女辞去，吴竟绝迹于狎游。

交河及孺爱，青县张文甫，皆老儒也。并授徒于献。尝同步月南村北村之间，去馆稍远，荒原阒寂，榛莽翳然。张心怖欲返，曰：墟墓间多鬼，曷可久留。俄一老人扶杖至，揖二人坐，曰：世间何得有鬼，不闻阮瞻之论乎？二君儒者，奈何信释氏之妖妄。因阐发程朱二气屈伸之理，疏通证明，词条流畅，二人听之皆首肯，共叹宋儒见理之真，递相酬对。竟忘问姓名。适大车数辆远远至，牛铎铮然，老人振衣急起曰：泉下之人，岑寂久矣。不持无鬼之论，不能留二君作竟夕谈。今将别，谨以实告，毋讶相戏侮也。俯仰之顷，欻然已灭，是间绝少文士，惟董空如先生墓相近，或即其魂欤。

河间唐生，好戏侮，土人至今能道之。所谓唐啸子者是也。有塾师好讲无鬼，尝曰：阮瞻遇鬼，安有是事？僧徒妄造蜚语耳。唐夜洒土其窗，而呜呜击其户，塾师骇问为谁，则曰：我二气之良能也。塾师大怖，蒙首股栗，使二弟子宋达旦，次日委顿不起。朋友来问，但呻吟曰有鬼。既而知唐所为，莫不拊掌。然自是魅大作，抛掷瓦石，摇撼户牖无虚夕。初尚以为唐再来，细察之乃真魅，不胜其嬲，竟弃馆而去。盖震惧之后，益以惭恧，其气已馁，狐乘其馁而中之也。妖由人兴，此之谓乎？

天津某孝廉，与数友郊外踏青。皆少年轻薄，见柳荫中少妇骑驴过，欺其无伴，邀众逐其后，嫚语调谑。少妇殊不答，鞭驴疾行。有两三人先追及，少妇忽下驴软语，意似相悦。俄某与三四人追及，审视正其妻也。但妻不解骑，是日亦无由至郊外，且疑且怒，近前诃之。妻嬉笑如故，某愤气潮涌，奋掌欲掴其面，妻忽飞跨驴背，别换一形，以鞭指某数曰：见他人之妇，则狎亵百端；见自己妇，则恚恨如是，尔读圣贤书，一恕字尚不能解，何以挂名桂籍也。数讫，径行。某色如死灰，殆僵立道左不能去，竟不知是何魅也。

德州田白岩曰：有额都统者，在滇黔间山行，见道士按一丽女于石，欲剖其心，女哀呼乞救，额急挥骑驰及，遽格道士手，女噭然一声，化火光飞去，道士顿足曰：公败吾事！此魅已媚杀百余人，故捕诛之以除害，但取精已多，岁久通灵，斩其首则神遁去，故必剖其心乃死，公今纵之，又贻患无穷矣。惜一猛虎之命，放置深山，不知泽麋林鹿，劘其牙者几许命也！匣其匕首，恨恨渡溪去，此贻白岩之寓言，即所谓一家哭何如一路哭也。姑容墨吏，自以为阴功，人亦多称为忠厚。而穷民之卖儿贴妇，皆未一思，亦安用此长者乎？
献县吏王某工刀笔，善巧取人财。然每有所积，必有一意外事耗去。有城隍庙道童，夜行廊庑间，有二吏持簿对算，其一曰：渠今岁所蓄较多，当何法以销之？方沉思间，其一曰：一翠云足矣，无烦迂折也。是庙往往遇鬼，道童习见亦不怖。但不知翠云为谁，亦不知为谁销算。俄有小妓翠云至，王某大嬖之，耗所蓄八九，又染恶疮，医药备至，比愈则已荡然矣。人计其平生所取，可屈指数者，约三四万金，后发狂疾暴卒，竟无棺以殓。

陈云亭舍人言，有台湾驿使宿馆舍，见艳女登墙下窥，叱索无所睹。夜半琅然有声，乃片瓦掷枕畔，叱问是何妖魅，敢侮天使。窗外朗声曰：公禄命重，我避公不及，致公叱索，惧干神谴，惴惴至今。今公睡中萌邪念，误作驿卒之女，谋他日纳为妾。人心一动，鬼神知之，以邪召邪，不得而咎我，故投瓦相报，公何怒焉？驿使大愧，未及天曙，促装去。 叶旅亭御史宅，忽有狐怪白昼对语，迫叶让所居，扰攘戏侮，至杯盘自舞，几榻自行。叶告张真人，真人以委法官。先书一符，甫张而裂，次牒都城隍，亦无验。法官曰：是必天狐，非拜章不可。乃建道场七日，至三日狐犹诟詈，至四日乃婉词请和。叶不欲与为难，亦祈不竟其事。真人曰：章已拜不可追矣。至七日忽闻格斗砰[石訇]，门窗破堕，薄暮尚未已，法官又檄他神相助，乃就擒，以罂贮之，埋广渠门外。

余尝问真人驱役鬼神之故，曰：我亦不知所以然，但依法施行耳。大抵鬼神皆受役于印，而符录则掌于法官。真人如官长，法官如胥吏；真人非法官不能为符录，法官非真人之印，其符录亦不灵。中间有验有不验，则如各官司文移章奏，或准或驳，不能一一必行耳。此言颇近理，又问设空宅深山，猝遇精魅，君尚能制伏否，曰：譬大吏经行，劫盗自然避匿。倘或无知猖獗，突犯双旌，虽手握兵符，征调不及，一时亦无如之何。此言亦颇笃实。然则一切神奇之说，皆附会也。

朱子颖运使言守泰安日，闻有士人到岱岳深处，忽人语出石壁中曰：何处经香，岂有转世人来耶？剨然震响，石壁中开，贝阙琼楼涌现峰顶。有耆儒冠带下迎，士人骇愕，问此何地？曰：此经香阁也。士人叩经香阁之义，曰：其说长矣，请坐讲之。昔尼山删定，垂教万年。大义微言，递相授受。汉代诸儒，去古未远，训诂笺注，类能窥见先圣之心，又淳朴未漓，无植党争名之习，惟各传师说，笃溯渊源。沿及有唐，斯文未改。迨乎北宋，勒为注疏十三部，先圣嘉焉。诸大儒虑新说日兴，渐成绝学，建是阁以贮之。

中为初本，以五色玉为函，尊圣教也；配以历代官刊之本，以白玉为函，昭帝王表章之功也，皆南面；左右则各家私刊之本，每一部成，必取初印精好者，按次时代，庋置斯阁，以苍玉为函，奖汲古之勤也，皆东西面，并以珊瑚为签，黄金作锁钥。东西两庑，以沉檀为几，锦绣为茵，诸大儒之神，岁一来视，相与列坐于斯阁。后三楹则唐以前诸儒经义，帙以纂组，收为一库。自是以外，虽著述等身，声华盖代，总听其自贮名山，不得入此门一步焉。先圣之志也，诸书至子刻午刻，一字一句，皆发浓香，故题曰经香。盖一元斡运，二气絪缊，阴起午中，阳生子半，圣人之心，与天地通。诸大儒阐发圣人之理，其精奥亦与天地通，故相感也。然必传是学者始闻之，他人则否。

世儒于此十三部，或焚膏继昝，钻仰终身，或锻炼苛求，百端掊击，亦各因其性识之所根耳。君四世前为刻工，曾手刊周礼半部，故余香尚在，吾得以知君之来，因引使周览阁庑，款以茗果。送别，曰：君善自爱，此地不易至也。士人回顾，唯万峰插天，杳无人迹。案此事荒诞，殆尊汉学者之寓言。夫汉儒以训诂专门，宋儒以义理相尚，似汉学粗而宋学精。然不明训诂，义理何由而知？概用诋诽，视犹土苴，未免既成大辂，追斥椎轮，得济迷川，遽焚宝筏。于是攻宋儒者，又纷纷而起故。余撰四库全书诗部总序，有曰：宋儒之攻汉儒，非为说经起见也，特求胜于汉儒而已。

后人之攻宋儒，亦非为说经起见也，特不平宋儒之诋汉儒而已。韦苏州诗曰：水性自云静，石中亦无声，如何两相激，雷转空山惊。此之谓矣。平心而论，易自王弼始变旧说，为宋学之萌芽，宋儒不攻；孝经词义明显，宋儒所争，只今文古字句，亦无关宏旨，均姑置勿议；至尚书三礼三传毛诗尔雅诸注疏，皆根据古义，断非宋儒所能；论语孟子，宋儒积一生精力，字斟句酌，亦断非汉儒所及。盖汉儒重师傅，渊源有自。宋儒尚心悟，研索易深；汉儒或执旧文，过于信传，宋儒或凭臆断，勇于改经。计其得失，亦复相当。唯汉儒之学，非读书稽古，不能下一语；宋儒之学，则人人皆可以空谈其间。兰艾同生，诚有不尽餍人心者。是嗤点之所自来。此种虚构之词，亦非无因而作也。

曹司农竹虚言，其族兄自歙往扬州，途经友人家，时盛夏，延坐书屋，甚轩爽。暮欲下榻其中，友人曰：是有魅，夜不可居。曹强居之，夜半有物自门隙蠕蠕入，薄如夹纸，入室后，渐开展作人形，乃女子也。曹殊不畏，忽披发吐舌，作缢鬼状，曹笑曰：犹是发，但稍乱。鬼技穷，倏然灭。及归途再宿，夜半门隙又蠕动，甫露其首，辄唾曰：又此败兴物耶？竟不入。此与嵇中散事相类。夫虎不食醉人，不知畏也。畏则心乱，心乱则神涣，神涣则鬼得乘之。不畏则心定，定则神全，神会则戾之气不能干。故记中散是事者，称神志湛然，鬼惭而去。

董曲江言，默庵先生为总漕时，署有土神马神二祠，惟土神有配，其少子恃才兀傲，谓土神于思老翁，不应拥艳妇；马神年少，正为嘉耦。径移女像于马神祠，俄眩仆不知人。默庵先生闻其事，亲祷移还，乃苏。又闻河间学署有土神亦配以女像，有训导谓黉宫不可塑妇人，乃别建一小祠迁焉，土神凭其幼孙语曰：汝理虽正，而心则私，正欲广汝宅耳，吾不服也。训导方侃侃谈古礼，猝中其隐，大骇，乃终任不敢居。是实二事相近，或曰：训导迁庙犹以礼，董渎神甚矣，谴当重。余谓董少年放诞耳，训导内挟私心，使己有利，外假公义，使人无词，微神发其阴谋，人尚以为能正祀典也。春秋诛心，训导谴当重于董。

戏术皆手法捷耳。然亦实有搬运术。忆小时在外祖雪峰先生家，一术士置杯酒于案，举掌扪之，杯陷入案中，口与案平，然扪案不见杯底。少选取出，案如故。此或障目法也。又举鱼脍一巨碗，抛掷空中不见，令其取回，则曰：不能矣。在书室画厨夹屉中，公等自取耳。时以宾从杂沓，书室多古器，已严扃。且夹屉高仅二寸，碗高三四寸许，断不可入。疑其妄，姑呼钥启视，则碗置案上，换贮佛手五。原贮佛手之盘，乃换贮鱼脍，藏夹屉中，是非搬运术乎？理所必无，事所或有，类如此。然实亦理之所有。狐怪山魈，盗取人物，不为异；能劾禁狐怪山魈者，亦不为异；既能劾禁，即可以役使，既能盗取人物，即可以代人取物，夫又何异焉。

旧仆庄寿言，昔事某官，见一官侵晨至，又一官续至，皆契交也。其状若密递消息者，俄皆去，主人亦命驾递出，至黄昏乃归。车殆马烦，不胜困惫。俄前二官又至，灯下或附耳或点头，或摇手或蹙眉或拊掌，不知所议何事。漏下二鼓，我遥闻北窗外吃吃有笑声，室中弗闻也。方疑惑间，忽又闻长叹一声，曰：何必如此。始宾主皆惊，开窗急视，新雨后泥平如掌，绝无人迹，共疑为我呓语，我时因戒勿窃听，避立南荣外花架下，实未尝睡，亦未尝言，究不知其何故也。

永春丘孝廉二田，偶憩息九鲤湖道中，有童子骑牛来，行甚速。至丘前小立，朗吟曰：来冲风雨来，去踏烟霞去，斜照万峰青，是我还山路，怪村竖哪得作此语，凝思欲问，则笠影出没杉桧间，已距半里许矣。不知神仙游戏，抑乡塾小儿闻人诵，而偶记也。 莆田林教谕霈，以台湾俸满北上。至涿州南，下车便旋，见破屋墙外，有磁锋划一诗曰：骡纲队队响铜铃，清晓冲寒过驿亭，我自垂鞭玩残雪，驴蹄缓踏乱山青。款曰罗洋山人。读讫自语曰：诗小有致，罗洋是何地耶？屋内应曰：其语似是湖广人，入视之，惟凝尘败叶而已。自知遇鬼，惕然登车，恒郁郁不适，不久竟卒。

景州李露园基塙，康熙甲午孝廉，余僚婿也。博雅工诗，需次日，梦中作一联曰：鸾翮嵇中散，蛾眉屈左徒。醒而不能自解。后得湖南一令，卒于官，正屈原行吟地也。 先祖母张太夫人，畜一小花犬，群婢患其盗肉，阴扼杀之。中一婢曰柳意，梦中恒见此犬来啮，睡辄呓语。太夫人知之，曰：群婢共杀犬，何独衔冤于柳意？此必柳意亦盗肉，不足服其心也。考问果然。 福建汀州试院，堂前二古柏，唐物也。云有神。余按临日，吏曰当诣树拜。余谓木魅不为害，听之可也，非祀典所有，使者不当拜。树枝叶森耸，隔屋数重可见。是夕月明，余步阶上，仰见树梢两红衣人，向余磬折拱揖，冉冉渐没。呼幕友出视，尚见之。余次日诣树各答以揖，为镌一联于祠门曰：参天黛色常如此，点首朱衣或是君。此事亦颇异。袁子才尝载此事于新齐谐，所记稍异，盖传闻之误也。

德州宋清远先生言，吕道士不知何许人，善幻术，尝客田山虇司农家，值朱藤盛开，宾客会赏，一俗士言词猥鄙，喋喋不休，殊败人意。一少年性轻脱，厌薄尤甚，斥勿多言。二人几攘臂，一老儒和解之，俱不听，亦愠形于色。满座为之不乐，道士耳语小童取纸笔，画三符焚之，三人忽皆起，在院中旋折数四，俗客趋东南隅坐，喃喃自语，听之乃与妻妾谈家事，俄左右回顾若和解，俄怡色自辩，俄作引罪状，俄屈一膝，俄两膝并屈，俄叩首不已；视少年则坐西南隅花栏上，流目送盼，妮妮软语，俄嬉笑，俄谦谢，俄低唱浣纱记，呦呦不已，手自按拍，备诸冶荡之态；老儒则端坐石凳上讲孟子齐桓晋文之事一章，字剖句析，指掠顾盼，如与四五人对语，忽摇手曰不是，忽嗔目曰尚不解耶，咯咯痨嗽仍不止。
众骇笑。道士摇手止之。比酒阑，道士又焚三符，三人乃惘惘凝坐，少选始醒，自称不觉醉眠，谢无礼。众匿笑散。道士曰：此小术，不足道。叶法善引唐明皇入月宫即用此符，当时误以为真仙，迂儒又以为妄语，皆井底蛙耳。后在旅馆，符摄一过往贵人妾魂，妾苏后登车，识其路径门户，语贵人急捕之，已遁去。此周礼所以禁怪民欤。

交河老儒及润础，雍正乙卯乡试。晚至石门桥，客舍皆满。唯一小屋，窗临马枥，无肯居者，姑解装焉。群马跳踉，夜不得寐。人静后忽闻马语，及爱观杂书，先记宋人说部中有堰下牛语事，知非鬼魅，屏息听之。一马曰：今日方知忍饥之苦，生前所欺隐草豆钱，意在何处。一马曰：我辈多由圉人转生，死者方知，生者不悟，可为太息。众马皆呜咽。一马曰：冥判亦不甚公，王五何以得为犬？一马曰：冥卒曾言之，渠一妻二女并淫滥，尽盗其钱与所欢，当罪之半矣。一马曰：信然，罪有轻重。姜七堕豕，身受屠割，更我辈不若也。及忽轻嗽，语遂寂。及恒举以戒圉人。
